places.name: VOG Kyoto
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→options): Shared kitchen baking wheat naan/puri/gyoza; GF curries on request only → options. Absent from curated JP guides. — Prior: extensive dedicated GF menu, items labeled
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian menu; most dishes made fully vegan on request
places.notes: Dedicated Veg/Vegan/GF (also Jain) Indian. Soy-meat curries, GF naan & gyoza, soy-milk lassi. Downtown (Nakagyo). Spice 1–5.

chef_bio.origin: Downtown Kyoto (Nakagyo), a short walk from Shijo Kawaramachi
chef_bio.background: VOG is Kyoto's all-in-one vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free Indian kitchen, where the name itself spells out the mission: Vegetarian & Vegan, Organic, Gluten-free. The whole menu is meat-free and egg-free, built around Kyo Yasai, the heirloom vegetables that Kyoto farmers have cultivated for centuries. The owner is very much the heart of the place: he is multilingual, switching between English, Japanese and another Asian language, and he likes to come to the table to walk diners through the food himself, so even people new to vegan eating feel looked after. Guests often mention ending up in long conversations with him about his life and his family, which gives the small dining room a warm, personal feel. Dishes are served on hand-crafted local ceramics, Kyo-yaki, Kiyomizu-yaki and Shigaraki-yaki, and a complimentary soup arrives in a traditional wooden kuksa cup.
chef_bio.philosophy: A nourishing, plant-based approach the kitchen sums up with its 'No White Food' campaign: brown rice, whole-grain and gluten-free flours, and natural, unprocessed ingredients over refined white ones, leaning on Kyoto's seasonal heirloom vegetables for flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based Indian cooking made entirely vegetarian and vegan, with a wide range of dishes that can also be prepared gluten-free or Jain. Diners single out the spinach (palak) curry and dal, garlic naan, samosas, paneer-style butter masala, the vegan roll cake, and the salted lassi.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the multilingual owner personally explaining the dishes, so even those unfamiliar with vegan food feel reassured, and several mention having heartfelt conversations with him about his life and family.
The restaurant frames its mission around the 'No White Food' idea and Kyoto's heritage Kyo Yasai vegetables, serving food on local Kyo-yaki, Kiyomizu-yaki and Shigaraki-yaki ceramics.

gf_cross_contamination.text: VOG is a fully gluten-free-oriented kitchen that uses whole-grain and gluten-free flours, and gluten-free items are labeled on the menu. No source documents a specific cross-contamination protocol or a dedicated fryer, so celiacs should confirm prep separation directly with the owner, who is hands-on and explains dishes personally.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms whether any soy-based sauces used (for example in the 'butter soy meat' style curry) are wheat-free. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so a strict celiac should verify the specific sauce with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is vegetarian with no meat, fish or egg on the menu, which removes the usual animal cross-contact risks for vegans.
A reviewer advises ordering vegan choices through a person rather than the QR-code system to make sure specifications reach the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The multilingual owner personally explains the dishes and is described as understanding vegan diets well, and reviewers call it super accommodating for gluten-free, vegetarian, vegan and Jain needs, with customizable spice levels.

positives.text: 100% vegetarian and vegan kitchen (no meat, fish or egg), so vegans avoid the typical animal cross-contact concerns.
Strong gluten-free orientation: gluten-free flours and labeled gluten-free items across the menu.
Hands-on, multilingual owner who explains dishes and accommodates vegan, gluten-free and Jain requests.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Shared kitchen baking wheat naan/puri/gyoza; GF curries on request only → options. Absent from curated JP guides.

places.name: Gion Soy Milk Ramen – Uno Yukiko
places.gf_detail: separate cookware, celiac-safe
places.vegan_detail: 100% plant-based
places.notes: The rare fully GF + vegan ramen in Japan. Soy-milk broth, rice-flour noodles, GF gyoza & cakes. Gion. Walk-in; expect a queue.

chef_bio.background: Gion Soy Milk Ramen was founded by Uno Yukiko, a Kyoto-born pastry chef and plant-based cuisine researcher who began her career as a chef before moving into pastry. Her path toward ramen started with people, not recipes: working with customers who had food allergies, she developed a cake made entirely from plant-based ingredients with no wheat, dairy, or eggs. A pivotal moment came when she met a boy with 23 allergies, and she spent years thinking about how to let people who are limited in what they can eat finally enjoy a bowl of ramen. She opened the Gion shop on October 12, 2022. It is an intimate counter restaurant near Gion-Shijo Station where the entire kitchen is built to be free of gluten and animal products. Uno also runs a plant-based sweets shop, Mushiyashanai, in Ichijoji.
chef_bio.philosophy: Uno's stated mission is to remove food barriers and deliver deliciousness to everyone, making all people smile with food. She has described the ramen as gentle enough to be eaten by babies who have finished weaning, and says the moments when allergic families tell her they can finally feel safe eating somewhere reaffirm why she does this work.
chef_bio.specialty: The house bowl is the Soy Milk Ramen 'Yuki' (Snow): a creamy broth built from her own house-made soy milk together with vegetables, mushrooms and kelp, seasoned with gluten-free soy sauce. The noodles are an original recipe made from rice flour kneaded with kelp, giving them a distinctive smooth, slippery texture. The kitchen also offers a spicy soy-milk dan dan noodle bowl and bowls piled with a wide range of Japanese vegetables. Diners are encouraged to finish with rice to turn the remaining broth into a risotto.

anecdotes.text: Uno traces the whole project to one encounter: 'It all started when I met a boy with 23 allergies. For about four years now, I've been thinking that I want people who are limited in what they can eat to enjoy ramen.'

Before opening the ramen shop she developed a fully plant-based cake with no wheat, dairy or eggs; an encounter with a British vegan traveler who loved it helped inspire her broader mission to create dining experiences that matter to people who are usually left out.

The signature 'Yuki' bowl pairs her house-made soy milk broth with original rice-flour-and-kelp noodles seasoned with gluten-free soy sauce, and the shop says the bowl is gentle enough for babies who have finished weaning.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (soymilkramen.com / en.soymilkramen.com) tells the founding story of pastry chef Uno Yukiko: a 100% plant-based 'miracle cake' with no wheat/dairy/eggs, a British vegan traveler who loved it, the mission 'to make all people smile with food', and ramen safe from babies who have finished weaning. Leaf Kyoto confirms the boy with 23 allergies, the ~four-year gestation, the Oct 12 2022 opening near Gion-Shijo, the 1,500 yen 'Snow' bowl, and the Mushiyashanai sweets shop in Ichijoji.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a dedicated gluten-free and plant-based kitchen: the restaurant states all menu items are gluten-free and plant-based, the kitchen contains no gluten or animal products, and customers are asked not to bring those items into the dining area. Because there is no wheat in the kitchen at all, the usual ramen cross-contamination risks (shared noodle water, shared broth) are largely removed.
A celiac-conscious diner highlighted the advantage of an all-gluten-free operation, noting 'not having to worry about cross contamination when it comes to soup especially.'
The noodles themselves are gluten-free, made from rice flour kneaded with kelp rather than wheat; reviewers describe them as smooth and slightly spaghetti-like.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Unlike most Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, this restaurant specifically uses gluten-free soy sauce in its broth and seasoning, so the soup is described as gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire operation is vegan with no animal products in the kitchen, which removes the typical risk of vegan cross-contact from shared meat or dashi-based broths found in conventional ramen shops.
Tabelog reviews describe it as a completely vegan ramen shop where all menu items are plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reviewers praise the allergen transparency, noting items are 'very clearly labeled for all kinds of allergens, such as soy, gluten and wheat.'

The restaurant says it can accommodate other specific allergies on request and offers nut-free and mushroom-free options. Given the soy milk broth, soy-allergic diners should confirm options directly, as the menu is soy-forward.

positives.text: A genuinely dedicated gluten-free and vegan kitchen founded specifically to serve people with allergies and dietary restrictions, which is rare for ramen and unusually safe for celiac diners compared to typical wheat-and-dashi ramen shops.
Gluten-free soy sauce and rice-flour noodles mean the broth and noodles are both made without wheat.
Reviewers report clear allergen labeling and a celiac diner specifically noted not having to worry about cross-contamination in the soup.

noodles.notes: Original gluten-free noodles made from rice flour kneaded with kombu (kelp), giving a smooth, slippery texture. An organic konjac-noodle swap is offered as an alternative.

ramen.signature_bowl: Soy Milk Ramen "Hana" — a creamy soy-milk bowl built on a vegetable-and-mushroom broth seasoned with gluten-free soy sauce, over rice-and-kombu noodles. The spicy Soy Milk Tantanmen "Tsukika" is the loaded sesame-chili alternative.
ramen.chashu: No pork or chicken chashu — protein comes from seasoned soy meat (daizu meat); a seared soy-milk "cheese" over sprouted brown rice is offered as a topping.
notable_toppings: seared soy-milk cheese with sprouted brown rice (add-on)

gf.note: A genuinely celiac-minded kitchen: the noodles are rice flour and kombu (no wheat), the tare uses gluten-free soy sauce, and the menu is built wheat-free throughout. Still worth confirming your needs directly when you order.

vegan.note: Fully 100% plant-based — the broth is vegetable, mushroom and kombu finished with soy milk, with no katsuo, pork or animal fat. A five-pungent-free (gokun-nuki) version is also offered for stricter diets.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional descendant: this bowl trades the usual pork-bone or chicken soul for a creamy soy-milk broth drawn from vegetables, mushrooms and kombu, proving a fully plant-based ramen can still carry real koku. It belongs to the new wave of vegan ramen that lets diners with allergies, vegans and curious travelers all share the same comforting bowl.

places.highlight: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional descendant: this bowl trades the usual pork-bone or chicken soul for a creamy soy-milk broth drawn from vegetables, mushrooms and kombu, proving a fully plant-based ramen can still carry real koku. It belongs to the new wave of vegan ramen that lets diners with allergies, vegans and curious travelers all share the same comforting bowl.

places.name: CHOICE
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→dedicated): Whole kitchen wheat/barley/oat/rye-free, own JP claim + JP reviews + celiac reaction reports. UPGRADE high→dedicated. Flag: buckwheat penne is a separate allergen. — Prior: nearly all dishes GF (tamari; corn penne). Confirm the one pasta
places.vegan_detail: 100% vegan
places.hours_raw: Thu–Tue 9:30–20:00; closed Wed
places.notes: Vegan + GF cafe near Sanjo-Keihan. House-made vegan cheeses, GF pizza, burgers, pancakes. Small portions, premium price.

chef_bio.background: CHOICE was founded by a doctor — a plastic surgeon and cosmetic dermatologist with decades of experience who helped pioneer laser and aesthetic medicine in Japan. The turning point came after March 2011, when concerns about the safety and quality of food set the founder on a path of studying nutrition seriously. That study hardened into a simple conviction: you are what you eat, and many skin and health problems trace back to the plate rather than the symptom. CHOICE grew out of that belief — a whole-foods, plant-based cafe near Sanjo where the entire menu is built to be both gluten-free and vegan, made from organic, natural, unprocessed ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder's guiding idea is that treating a symptom is not the same as curing its cause, and that genuine health starts with what you eat. Just as important is the belief that food should be enjoyed — eating with joy, in their words, helps the body digest and absorb nutrition properly — so the cooking aims to be satisfying and delicious, not merely virtuous. The whole-foods, plant-based approach is framed as good for both personal health and the planet.
chef_bio.specialty: CHOICE is known for being one of Kyoto's go-to spots where vegans, vegetarians and gluten-free eaters can order anything on the menu without worry. Standouts include crisp gluten-free pizzas, pancakes, and two corn-penne pastas: a tomato-sauce version topped with house-made vegan mozzarella, and a pesto penne built on a vibrant spinach-and-chlorella sauce. The kitchen leans on its own fermented vegan cheeses, brown rice, and an array of cakes and sweets, with coffee and pancakes as everyday favorites.

anecdotes.text: The cafe traces its origin to 2011, when worries about food safety led the founder — a longtime plastic surgeon and cosmetic dermatologist — to immerse themselves in the study of food and nutrition, eventually opening CHOICE around the conviction that 'you are what you eat.'

Both of the pasta dishes are made with 100% corn penne — a tomato-sauce penne with homemade vegan mozzarella, and a pesto penne with a spinach-and-chlorella sauce — so even the pasta stays gluten-free and vegan.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hs-choice.com's founder message states the founder is a plastic surgeon/cosmetic dermatologist of 30+ years who pioneered laser surgery and 'medical esthetic' treatment in Japan, and after the March 11 2011 disaster turned to studying food and nutrition ('you are what you eat'; 'eating with joy makes food digested and absorbed well'). The meal page states dishes are gluten-free and vegan and that both pastas use 100% corn penne.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire establishment is 100% gluten-free: the kitchen states it uses no wheat, barley, oats or rye anywhere on the menu. Because no gluten-containing ingredients are present in the building, the usual sources of cross-contamination are largely removed — though the venue is not explicitly described as a certified celiac-dedicated facility, so those with severe celiac disease may wish to confirm prep details directly.
Every dish is gluten-free and vegan, including the two pasta dishes, which are made with 100% corn penne rather than wheat pasta.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: CHOICE uses tamari shoyu (gluten-free soy sauce) in its cooking instead of standard wheat-based soy sauce, and the soup broth is made without fish flakes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, dairy or honey are used anywhere on the menu — so vegan cross-contact risk is inherently minimal since no animal products are present in the kitchen at all.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A reviewer noted that staff were very helpful with allergy information and that English is spoken, making it easier to confirm dietary needs.

positives.text: Widely recommended as one of the best places in Kyoto for vegans, vegetarians and gluten-free eaters, where the whole menu is safe to order from.
A diner summed it up simply: 'Everything is gluten-free!' — the entire menu is both gluten-free and vegan.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Whole kitchen wheat/barley/oat/rye-free, own JP claim + JP reviews + celiac reaction reports. UPGRADE high→dedicated. Flag: buckwheat penne is a separate allergen.

places.name: cafe planet
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→dedicated): Own site excludes wheat/barley/oat/rye whole-kitchen + empacede curated guide ★★★★★. UPGRADE high→dedicated. Open (Tabelog Apr 2026). — Prior: fully GF, reported celiac-friendly
places.vegan_detail: 100% vegan
places.hours_raw: Thu–Tue 9:00–18:00; closed Wed (CASH ONLY)
places.notes: All-vegan, all-GF cafe on the Kamogawa near Kamo Delta. GF pizza, pasta, acai bowls. River-view greenhouse; dog-friendly. Sells out early.

chef_bio.background: cafe planet opened in November 2021 on the banks of the Kamogawa River, just a short walk from the Kamo Delta where the Takano and Kamo rivers meet. It is a bright, simple and modern riverside space with an indoor terrace and river-facing seating. The cafe is the sister establishment of CHOICE, an established gluten-free and vegan cafe in Kyoto, and carries the same plant-based philosophy to this Kamogawa-side location. It is also openly dog-friendly, a direction shaped by the owner's own love of dogs, with hours suited to dog walks and even plant-based dog treats and latte art featuring a pet's face.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe is built on the idea that plant-based, healthy eating should be genuinely delicious -- summed up in its own words as 'Vegan is delicious!' The whole menu is vegan and gluten-free, using organic, additive-free ingredients chosen with care for their origin and production method.
chef_bio.specialty: Known for a fully vegan and gluten-free menu where every item avoids dairy, eggs, and wheat. The signature is a gluten-free pizza built on a rice-flour crust described as chewy right to the edge, topped with a vegan cheese made from fermented cashew nuts and seasonal vegetables on a tomato base. Other favorites include acai bowls, vegetable-packed tomato-based chilled pasta made with corn penne, fresh seasonal salads, and house desserts such as chocolate terrine and pound cake.

anecdotes.text: The entire menu is vegan and gluten-free: in the cafe's own words, all items are plant-derived and use no dairy, eggs, or wheat, with organic, additive-free ingredients selected with attention to origin and production method.
The gluten-free pizza is built on a rice-flour crust and described as chewy all the way to the edge, paired with a vegan cheese made from fermented cashew nuts.
Opened in November 2021 along the Kamogawa River as a sister store of the vegan and gluten-free cafe CHOICE, with a dog-friendly concept reflecting the owner's love of dogs.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese coverage (inumag.jp, life-info.co.jp, cafeplanet.kyoto) confirms cafe planet opened November 2021 on the Kamogawa near Demachiyanagi as the sister shop of the Sanjo GF/vegan cafe CHOICE; the whole menu is vegan and gluten-free (no dairy/eggs/wheat); the signature rice-flour pizza is 'chewy to the edge' with fermented-cashew vegan cheese; it is dog-friendly (dog treats, pet-photo latte art) reflecting the owner's love of dogs. No source mentions a former bank building.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen operates with no wheat in the building at all -- the cafe states that every menu item is gluten-free and contains no wheat, which structurally reduces the risk of wheat cross-contact compared with a mixed kitchen. No specific protocol (separate prep surfaces, shared equipment) is documented, so celiac diners should still confirm details directly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No specific information was found on whether soy sauce or seasonings used are wheat-free. Because most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, anyone with celiac disease should confirm the brand or alternative used before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is vegan -- no dairy, eggs, or other animal products are used anywhere on the menu -- so the risk of animal-product cross-contact is inherently low. Vegan cheese is made from fermented cashew nuts, which is relevant for those with tree-nut allergies.

positives.text: 100% vegan and 100% gluten-free kitchen: every menu item is plant-based and made without dairy, eggs, or wheat, using organic and additive-free ingredients.
Signature gluten-free pizza uses a rice-flour crust and cashew-based vegan cheese rather than wheat dough.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Own site excludes wheat/barley/oat/rye whole-kitchen + empacede curated guide ★★★★★. UPGRADE high→dedicated. Open (Tabelog Apr 2026).

places.name: cafe vegan terrace (Cafe Tea Terrace)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (options→ask): Venue never claims GF (only 'no wheat flour'), silent on traps, default gluten bread on premises, absent from all celiac guides → ask. Better nearby: ぎをん豆乳らーめん. — Prior: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→options. HappyCow + own menu confirm NOT dedicated — wheat bread & burger buns actively used, GF bread only 'on request', small shared hotel kitchen, no celiac protocol. That's options-on-request, fails the high bar. — Prior note: vegan & gluten-free menu
places.vegan_detail: vegan
places.notes: Modern vegan + GF, shojin-style (no garlic/onion). Millet-noodle ramen, curry, vegan 'egg' sandwich. Near Gion Corner (in Gion Elite Terrace hotel).

chef_bio.background: Cafe Vegan Terrace (also listed as Cafe Tea Terrace) is a small, calm vegan cafe tucked inside the Gion Elite Terrace hotel in Kyoto's Higashiyama / Gion district. Set just off the busy streets, it serves a fully plant-based menu to hotel guests and walk-in diners alike, and is known as a quiet retreat for an unhurried lunch in the heart of historic Gion.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking follows the principles of shojin ryori (Buddhist temple cuisine): dishes are built entirely from plants and deliberately exclude the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, leek, chives, shallots), so the menu also suits diners avoiding those ingredients for religious reasons. Much of the menu is gluten-free, with gluten-free options offered for the sandwich and burger.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan ramen is the standout: a surprisingly rich, savory broth built without garlic, leeks, onions, or chives, served with millet noodles that hold their texture better than rice noodles. The kitchen also turns out soy-sauce and yuba-yuzu ramen, a teriyaki soy-meat burger, turmeric-yellow vegetable curry, and sweets like matcha pudding and dango.

anecdotes.text: A diner who tried the ramen called it one of their favorites in Kyoto, noting the broth was 'super flavorful, which was a surprise because it was made without garlic, leeks, onions, or chives to follow the principles of Shojin Ryori,' and that the kitchen uses millet noodles for better texture.

Reviewers describe it as a 'cute cafe away from the hustle and bustle of nearby streets,' a calm and cosy spot attached to a hotel with very friendly staff, and note a gluten-free sandwich and a burger on gluten-free bread are available on request.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The cafe advertises a vegan and gluten-free menu, with most dishes gluten-free and gluten-free options offered for the sandwich and burger. However, no source describes a dedicated gluten-free prep area, separate cookware, or cross-contamination controls, so celiac diners should confirm handling directly before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is offered in soy-sauce (shoyu) versions. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no source confirms whether this kitchen uses a wheat-free tamari. Celiac and wheat-allergic diners should ask specifically whether the soy sauce used in the ramen and teriyaki burger is wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fully vegan, so there is no animal-product cross-contact to worry about; the entire menu is plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe takes reservations (reportedly in advance) and can accommodate special dietary requests, including avoiding particular ingredients for religious reasons. Diners are advised to flag ingredients to avoid when booking.

positives.text: Fully plant-based menu with no alliums (onion, garlic, leek, chives, shallots), prepared along shojin ryori lines, making it well suited to vegans and to diners avoiding those ingredients.
Largely gluten-free menu with gluten-free options offered for the items that would normally contain gluten, such as the sandwich and burger.
Staff described as friendly and willing to accommodate dietary needs when given advance notice.

noodles.notes: Gluten-free millet noodles rather than the usual wheat-and-kansui ramen noodle; reportedly hold their texture better than rice noodles and resist clumping.

ramen.signature_bowl: Vegan shojin-style ramen: a clean, allium-free plant broth (no garlic, onion, leek or chives) served with gluten-free millet noodles.

gf.note: Genuinely gluten-free: the ramen uses millet noodles instead of wheat, and the kitchen builds dishes to be gluten-free (sandwiches/burgers offered with GF options). Confirm tare/seasoning details and cross-contact directly, as celiac safety is not explicitly certified.

vegan.note: Fully plant-based kitchen. The ramen follows shojin ryori principles with a vegetable-based broth and no animal dashi, meat, or animal fat; toppings such as tofu and seaweed are vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, fully plant-based bowl rooted in Buddhist shojin-ryori restraint rather than any regional broth lineage, its soul is a quiet vegetable stock built without the alliums and animal dashi that define mainstream ramen. Paired with gluten-free millet noodles, it is a Kyoto temple-cuisine reimagining of the ramen bowl for vegan and gluten-sensitive diners.

places.highlight: A modern, fully plant-based bowl rooted in Buddhist shojin-ryori restraint rather than any regional broth lineage, its soul is a quiet vegetable stock built without the alliums and animal dashi that define mainstream ramen. Paired with gluten-free millet noodles, it is a Kyoto temple-cuisine reimagining of the ramen bowl for vegan and gluten-sensitive diners.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Venue never claims GF (only 'no wheat flour'), silent on traps, default gluten bread on premises, absent from all celiac guides → ask. Better nearby: ぎをん豆乳らーめん.

places.name: musubi cafe
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→options): Operator ADMITS spelt wheat + oats on premises, no CC protocol, miso soup no tamari assurance → options. My English run wrongly kept high. Only Arashiyama open. — Prior: gluten-free options marked
places.vegan_detail: vegan options marked
places.hours_raw: Wed–Mon 10:00–17:00; closed Tue
places.notes: Arashiyama cafe popular for vegan + GF lunch sets (mapo tofu, chickpea curry), vegan cakes & coffee. Near bamboo grove/monkey park.

chef_bio.background: Tucked just off the tourist bustle of Arashiyama, near Hankyu Arashiyama Station, Musubi began with a simple idea built into its name: musubi, the act of connecting people to one another. The cafe pairs a relaxed, homely space (including a tatami room that families love) with a mission of being "kind to heart and body." Unusually for a cafe, it was set up with active travelers in mind too, offering showers and lockers so that runners, hikers, and climbers exploring the hills and river around Arashiyama have a welcoming place to refuel. Its sister operation, Musubi Sweets Factory, grew out of a wish that people who normally cannot eat cake, those with egg, dairy, or wheat allergies, and especially children, could finally sit down with family and friends and enjoy dessert too.
chef_bio.philosophy: Food should be gentle on both mind and body, made with locally sourced ingredients, and shared in a way that connects people: "when people connect, happiness spreads." The team also believes no one should be left out of a treat, building a dessert program specifically so allergy sufferers and children can enjoy cake alongside everyone else.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward, health-conscious cooking served as lunch and dinner sets, with daily blackboard specials where one option is always vegan-friendly. Signature savory plates include chickpea and tomato curry, butternut squash and Manganji pepper keema curry, taco rice, and vegetable pasta. The cafe is best known, though, for its desserts: a lineup of sweets that are 100% vegan and gluten-free, including a musubi parfait and butter-style sandwich, made so that those with egg, dairy, or wheat allergies can enjoy them.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen is known for going out of its way for guests: one visitor recalled the staff being incredibly friendly and still preparing them a vegan lunch set even though it had technically sold out for the day.

Beyond a meal, the cafe doubles as a base for active travelers, with shower booths and lockers (day-use around 500 yen) so runners, hikers, and climbers can clean up and refuel after exploring Arashiyama.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (musubi-cafe.jp) frames the name around 'when people connect, HAPPY chains onward', the tagline 'kind to heart and body', and describes Musubi Sweets Factory's 100% vegan & gluten-free sweets for those with egg/dairy/wheat allergies. The service page confirms shower booths, fitting rooms and lockers (about 500 yen day-use) supporting runners and hikers based near the Togetsukyo Bridge.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No reviews or sources describe the kitchen's cross-contamination practices, separate prep surfaces, or whether the savory menu is celiac-safe. The gluten-free claim is documented mainly for the desserts; for celiac diners, the safety of hot savory dishes cannot be confirmed from available sources.
Reviewers confirm the desserts are gluten-free, but none address whether they are prepared in a fully separate, dedicated gluten-free environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source specifies whether the soy sauce or other seasonings used in savory dishes (such as the taco rice or curries) are wheat-free. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so celiac diners should confirm directly before ordering savory items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is reported to be clearly labeled, making it easy to see which items are vegan, and staff are said to understand what vegan cooking ("vegan ryori") means.
Daily blackboard specials follow a pattern where one option is always vegan-friendly.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are repeatedly described as exceptionally friendly and accommodating, including preparing a vegan set that had sold out for one guest.

The cafe's sister dessert operation was built specifically around egg, dairy, and wheat allergies, indicating allergy awareness is central to the business, though no formal cross-contamination protocol is documented.

positives.text: All desserts are 100% vegan and gluten-free, with nine such desserts available, and the dessert program was created expressly so people with egg, dairy, and wheat allergies can enjoy cake.
Vegan options are clearly marked and easy to identify on the menu, and staff understand vegan cooking.
The cafe emphasizes locally sourced ingredients and food that is gentle on body and mind, with vegan curry, taco rice, and pasta available.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Operator ADMITS spelt wheat + oats on premises, no CC protocol, miso soup no tamari assurance → options. My English run wrongly kept high. Only Arashiyama open.

places.name: stope. Vegan cafe
places.gf_detail: GF & 'oriental' options available
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.hours_raw: Wed–Mon 12:00–19:00 (Thu to 18:00, Sun from 11:00); closed Tue
places.notes: All-vegan cafe near Nijo Castle. Rare vegan cheeseburger, Kumamoto ramen, Buddha bowl, gateau chocolat. GF & oriental options.

chef_bio.background: stope. is a small, fully plant-based cafe tucked near Nijo Castle, run as a family affair by a husband-and-wife team who often have their young daughter around. Before opening their own place, the owner cooked at Ain Soph, one of Japan's best-known vegan restaurant groups, and that pedigree shows up in the cooking. The result is a warm, low-key neighborhood spot where regulars and travelers alike are greeted personally and the kitchen is happy to tweak dishes or whip up an off-menu drink on request.
chef_bio.philosophy: 100% plant-based cooking served in a relaxed, family-run setting, with friendly, accommodating service.
chef_bio.specialty: An all-vegan menu spanning comfort-food savories and indulgent desserts: a much-praised vegan cheeseburger (grilled onion, avocado, lettuce, tomato), a rich 'milky' black-garlic ramen, a teriyaki Buddha bowl, Japanese curry, and minestrone soup, followed by parfaits, gateau chocolat, and big vegan sundaes. Many dishes can also be made gluten-free.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe it as a cute spot run by a friendly husband and wife who have a darling little daughter, with a chill, welcoming atmosphere.
Regulars note the owner previously worked at Ain Soph, a well-known vegan restaurant, before opening stope.
The kitchen is known for going the extra mile, including making customers a special drink that isn't on the menu and confirming details before serving.

gf_cross_contamination.text: stope. is a fully vegan kitchen, but it is a small shared kitchen rather than a dedicated gluten-free facility. Gluten-free dishes are prepared alongside wheat-based items (ramen noodles, burger buns, baked desserts), so cross-contact is possible. No celiac-specific separation protocol is documented; if you have celiac disease, confirm preparation directly with the owners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Savory dishes here lean on Japanese flavors (teriyaki Buddha bowl, Japanese curry, ramen) that typically use soy sauce. Most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat, so unless a dish is specifically made gluten-free, assume it may contain wheat-derived soy sauce. Ask whether a gluten-free tamari is used for any dish you order.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cafe is 100% plant-based, so there is no animal-product cross-contact to worry about. The entire menu, including cheese, ramen broth, and desserts, is vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Service is repeatedly described as friendly and accommodating; staff have custom-made off-menu drinks and confirmed details with customers before serving, which suggests they are open to dietary requests. Still, no formal allergen-handling procedure is documented, so celiac and severe-allergy diners should explain their needs clearly.

positives.text: Gluten-free options are available and specifically praised by diners ('I love that they offer gluten-free food as well').
Fully vegan menu, so plant-based diners can order anything without checking for animal ingredients.
Welcoming, family-run service that customers describe as friendly and willing to make special accommodations.

noodles.notes: Noodle specifications are not documented. As a fully plant-based kitchen, the bowl is built around vegetable-driven broth rather than a defined regional noodle cut.

ramen.signature_bowl: Black garlic ramen (the kuro-mayu bowl) from the all-plant-based menu

gf.note: The kitchen is 100% plant-based and offers gluten-free versions of many items (cakes, parfaits, ice cream). A gluten-free ramen is not specifically documented, so confirm noodle and tare before ordering if you are celiac.

vegan.note: Entirely plant-based cafe: every dish, including the ramen, is vegan with no fish dashi, animal tare, or pork. The broth is vegetable-based.

ramen.pantheon: A wholly plant-based bowl from a small Kyoto cafe, its black-garlic version channels the toasty, bittersweet punch of mayu-style ramen without a drop of animal stock. This is modern, compassionate ramen-making: the soul of the soup is coaxed entirely from vegetables, so you taste depth built from the garden rather than the bone.

places.highlight: A wholly plant-based bowl from a small Kyoto cafe, its black-garlic version channels the toasty, bittersweet punch of mayu-style ramen without a drop of animal stock. This is modern, compassionate ramen-making: the soul of the soup is coaxed entirely from vegetables, so you taste depth built from the garden rather than the bone.

places.name: Towzen
places.gf_detail: ask (rice-noodle ramen)
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.hours_raw: Reservation via LINE; hours vary — check
places.notes: Vegan ramen house in a garden home, Shimogamo (near Shimogamo Shrine). Soy-milk & chlorella ramen, eggplant sushi, soy pudding. Tiny — book ahead.

chef_bio.background: Towzen began in 2004, when Minoru Yonekawa opened a small ramen shop near Shimogamo Shrine in northern Kyoto. The Yonekawa family had deep roots in tofu-making, and that heritage led to a single, defining experiment: building a ramen broth not on pork or chicken bones, but on soy milk. That creamy soy-milk base became the soul of the shop. Towzen bills itself as the shop that developed the first vegan ramen in Japan, and over two decades it has grown into one of the country's longest-running and most celebrated vegan ramen establishments. Tucked into a house in the quiet suburbs north of the city, it has built a devoted following and even taken its bowls beyond Kyoto, to kitchens as far as Kuala Lumpur, Batam and Sydney.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yonekawa calls his concept 'World Peace Ramen' — exceptional flavor married with compassion and cultural integrity. The cooking draws on Buddhist shojin ryori (temple cuisine), prepared without animal products or alliums, intended as nourishment for both body and spirit.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-milk ramen built on a 100% organic soy-milk broth. The signature 'Musashi' bowl is a creamy, velvety soy-milk broth flavored with kelp and soy sauce, dusted with sansho pepper and topped with slices of yuba, simmered mushrooms, and a dab of tart plum paste. The 'tantanmen' uses the same broth swirled with chili paste and savory simmered soy-bean 'meat' — spicy and rich without being heavy.

anecdotes.text: The whole restaurant grew out of one experiment rooted in the Yonekawa family's tofu tradition: replacing the usual meat-bone ramen broth with soy milk. That creamy soy-milk base is what makes the bowl so distinctively rich.
Towzen describes itself as the original vegan ramen shop, having developed and served what it calls the first vegan ramen in Japan — operating since July 2004.

The Musashi bowl pairs a kelp-and-soy-sauce-flavored soy-milk broth with sansho pepper, yuba, simmered mushrooms and a dab of plum paste; the tantanmen swirls in chili paste and simmered soy-bean meat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists the opening day as 2004.7.26 and quotes the shop calling itself the original vegan ramen shop that 'developed and invented the first vegan Ramen in Japan, and in fact the world', using 100% organic soy milk in a tonkotsu-style soy-milk broth. towzenramen.com confirms founder Minoru Yonekawa, the family tofu tradition, 'World Peace Ramen', shojin-style no-animal/no-allium cooking, and branches in Kyoto, Kuala Lumpur, Batam and Sydney.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gluten-free rice noodles are available as a substitute for the standard wheat ramen noodles (some noodle swaps carry a small fee). One reviewer specifically described ordering 'tantan ramen with soy meat and rice noodles (gluten free noodles).'
The shop also notes gluten-free options on its menu and offers gluten-free cakes, indicating real awareness of gluten-free diners.

No information is documented about a dedicated gluten-free cooking station, separate noodle-boiling water, or cross-contamination protocols, so strict celiac diners should confirm equipment handling directly with staff.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Unlike most ramen shops, Towzen states it uses gluten-free soy sauce, which it boils down (along with mirin) to reduce alcohol content below 0.1%. This addresses the usual wheat-in-soy-sauce concern that makes most ramen broths unsafe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen has been fully vegan (100% plant-based) since 2018, with broth built on organic soy milk rather than meat or fish stock — so there is no cross-contact with animal products.
The cooking follows shōjin ryōri principles, prepared without animal products or alliums (onion/garlic), which also makes it suitable for stricter Buddhist and oriental-vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Orders are highly customizable — diners can choose flavor, spice level, portion size and noodle type, and reviewers note staff speak good English, making it easier to communicate dietary needs.
The menu lists oriental-vegan, halal and gluten-free options, showing the staff routinely accommodate dietary requests.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen with no animal-product cross-contact — a rare confident choice for plant-based diners.

Genuine gluten-free pathway: gluten-free rice noodles plus gluten-free soy sauce, which is unusual for a ramen shop. Celiac diners should still confirm noodle-boiling and shared-equipment handling in person.

noodles.notes: Standard wheat ramen noodles; a gluten-free noodle option is documented as a substitution for those who need it.

ramen.signature_bowl: The soy milk ramen (the classic 'Musashi' bowl): a creamy soy-milk broth seasoned with kelp and soy sauce, dusted with sansho pepper. The tantanmen (Si Chuan Tan Tan Men) is the other star, building sesame and peanut paste with chili oil over the same plant-milk base.
ramen.chashu: No pork chashu. The "heart" is plant-based: simmered seasoned soy-bean mince (vegan "meat") on the tantanmen, and yuba (tofu skin) plus simmered mushrooms on the classic bowl.

gf.note: A gluten-free noodle option is documented as an available substitution. Confirm the tare and broth seasoning when ordering, as soy-sauce-based seasoning typically contains wheat.

vegan.note: The entire menu is fully vegan and free of animal products. Broths are built on soy milk, walnut milk, or oat milk with kelp dashi (no katsuo/pork); proteins are soy mince, yuba, and mushrooms. The kitchen also avoids onion and garlic in the shojin-inspired style.

ramen.pantheon: A pioneer of plant-based ramen, this bowl trades the meat-fat soul of conventional ramen for a velvety soy-milk broth lifted by kelp umami and a fragrant dusting of sansho. Rooted in the shojin Buddhist tradition (no animal products, no garlic or onion), it is a distinctly Kyoto reinvention of the form rather than a regional gotochi style.

places.highlight: A pioneer of plant-based ramen, this bowl trades the meat-fat soul of conventional ramen for a velvety soy-milk broth lifted by kelp umami and a fragrant dusting of sansho. Rooted in the shojin Buddhist tradition (no animal products, no garlic or onion), it is a distinctly Kyoto reinvention of the form rather than a regional gotochi style.

places.name: Cafe & Bar Maru @Keika
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (dedicated→high): Kitchen wheat-discipline is genuine BUT bar pours regular BARLEY beer (Asahi/Premium Malt's)+whiskey on premises + own site tells severe cases to refrain. '小麦'-scoped claim narrower than GF → high, not dedicated. My English run wrongly kept dedicated. — Prior: whole menu GF; chef is GF; GF soy sauce
places.vegan_detail: primarily GF (serves meat); a few vegan dishes
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 18:00–02:00; Sat to 03:00; Sun to 24:00; closed Mon
places.notes: Dedicated-GF bar by the Kamogawa (Kiyamachi, 3F). GF tonkatsu, karaage, rice-flour bread, cocktails. Best for mixed GF/omnivore groups.

chef_bio.background: Maru is a fully gluten-free cafe and bar tucked away on the third floor of a building in Kiyamachi, one of Kyoto's liveliest streets near Shijo-Kawaramachi. The owner opened it after developing a wheat allergy about ten years ago, having suffered sudden, full-body hives. Wishing there were an accessible, welcoming place to eat well without wheat, she created exactly that: a kitchen where no wheat products are handled at all, and where even bringing wheat-containing items into the premises is not allowed. The head chef is Japanese and follows a gluten-free diet herself, and she leans on Kyoto ingredients wherever possible, seasoning with fermented staples like salt koji and rice miso rather than reaching for wheat-based shortcuts.
chef_bio.philosophy: That eating without wheat should be easy, delicious, and unremarkable. By keeping the entire kitchen and room free of wheat, the owner offers people with wheat allergies and celiac disease a place where they can relax and simply enjoy a meal or a drink.
chef_bio.specialty: An all-gluten-free menu where wheat flour is swapped for rice flour throughout. Diners single out the gluten-free fried chicken, the curry, vegan-friendly stews and grilled vegetables, soy milk ice cream, and a full bar of carefully chosen gluten-free drinks and cocktails. The kitchen seasons with salt koji, rice miso, and gluten-free soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The owner developed a wheat allergy roughly ten years ago after a sudden bout of full-body hives, and opened Maru because she wished there were an accessible place to eat well without wheat nearby. No wheat products are handled in the kitchen, and wheat items are not allowed onto the premises at all.

The head chef is Japanese and follows a gluten-free diet herself; the kitchen uses Kyoto ingredients where possible, seasoned with salt koji, rice miso, and gluten-free soy sauce, and the whole menu, including bar-time dishes, is gluten-free.
Diners highlight the gluten-free fried chicken, curry, and even gluten-free cocktails, noting the bar is 'a hidden gluten-free' spot near Shijo-Kawaramachi.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: maru.keika.kyoto states no wheat products are handled in the kitchen and wheat may not be brought onto the premises; the owner developed a wheat allergy ~10 years ago after sudden full-body hives; the kitchen uses salt koji, rice miso and gluten-free soy sauce and rice flour instead of wheat. It also notes complete absence of gluten cannot be absolutely guaranteed for every sourced ingredient.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a fully dedicated gluten-free establishment: the kitchen handles no wheat products at all, and wheat-containing items are not allowed onto the premises, which strongly limits in-house cross-contamination.

The owner cautions that, because trace wheat contamination during the manufacturing of Japanese ingredients cannot be completely ruled out, those with severe wheat allergy should refrain (重度の方はお控えくださいませ). Highly sensitive celiac diners should weigh this and confirm directly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen uses gluten-free soy sauce rather than standard wheat-based soy sauce, and seasons with fermented staples like salt koji and rice miso.
Multiple sources confirm gluten-free soy sauce is used throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan options exist but are limited; the place is gluten-free first, and most of the menu contains animal products. Reviewers report vegan versions of curry and white stew, plus salad, soup, grilled vegetables, and soy milk ice cream.

One reviewer described roughly four or five vegan dishes available, suggesting vegans should ask which items are prepared vegan; no specific reports on vegan cross-contact were found, so confirm with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner is personally wheat-allergic and the head chef follows a gluten-free diet herself, so allergy awareness is central to how the place operates.
The owner openly advises that severe wheat-allergy cases should refrain, showing transparency about the limits of what a no-wheat-on-premises kitchen can guarantee.

positives.text: Entirely gluten-free cafe and bar: wheat flour is replaced with rice flour, gluten-free soy sauce is used, and even the bar-time dishes and drinks are gluten-free.
No wheat is handled in the kitchen or permitted on the premises, making it one of the safer gluten-free options in Kyoto and frequently recommended for celiac diners.
Diners praise the gluten-free fried chicken, curry, desserts, and gluten-free cocktails, plus friendly staff and a relaxed atmosphere.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Kitchen wheat-discipline is genuine BUT bar pours regular BARLEY beer (Asahi/Premium Malt's)+whiskey on premises + own site tells severe cases to refrain. '小麦'-scoped claim narrower than GF → high, not dedicated. My English run wrongly kept dedicated.

places.name: Kuru Kuru
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (options→ask): 'Dedicated' rested entirely on mutually-echoing English aggregators; zero JP substantiation, absent from every curated JP celiac guide + Tabelog. Celiac-owner all-GF concept keeps above 'no' but handling unverified → ask. — Prior: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→options. Only aggregator listings (FMGF, Atly) support it — no own site, absent from every major celiac Kyoto guide, so celiac-grade corroboration is missing; one FMGF review = barely-reheated frozen crust + 'may not be safe for celiac' disclaimer. — Prior note: owner gluten-intolerant
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian options (veg curry, rice-flour pizza) (Menu review: 1 vegan meal.)
places.notes: Dedicated-GF cafe near Ginkaku-ji / Philosopher's Path. Rice-flour pizza, veg curry, chiffon cake. Free cake samples.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan — near the Philosopher's Path, a short walk from Ginkakuji (the Silver Pavilion)
chef_bio.background: Kuru Kuru is a tiny, warmly personal cafe tucked along the route to Ginkakuji, run single-handedly by a woman who is gluten-intolerant herself. Because she cannot eat gluten, she built the whole place around that need: every single item on the menu is gluten-free, made by her own hands. The room is small and homey, and regulars describe the whole project as a genuine labor of love. Because she runs it alone, there can be a short wait at busy times — part of the charm of a place where one person is cooking, baking and serving everything.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is gluten-free, made from scratch by the owner so that someone who shares her intolerance can eat the entire menu without worry — hospitality grounded in her own lived experience.
chef_bio.specialty: She is best known for her cakes — light, airy chiffon and rice-flour cakes in a rotating range of flavors (around four at a time) that diners single out as the highlight. On the savory side she makes a rice-flour pizza and a vegan chickpea curry, and the menu also includes gluten-free spaghetti. Drinks lean refreshing and homemade, including a plum soda, matcha, and ginger, lemon and rooibos teas.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the owner as a lovely, very kind and helpful woman who runs the tiny cafe largely on her own, and who has been known to surprise guests with complimentary treats — and to offer samples when a customer mentions they also eat gluten-free.
Visitors call it a nice discovery on the walk toward Ginkakuji, singling out that the curry and pizza are great and describing the owner as 'so lovely.'
Several diners note that the rice-flour pizza won over even the gluten-eaters in their group, with one calling it the best gluten-free crust they had had.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is gluten-free because the owner is gluten-intolerant herself, which removes wheat-flour from the core of the kitchen and is the biggest single point in the cafe's favor for GF diners.

Sources disagree on how strictly cross-contamination is controlled: one gluten-free directory lists it as celiac-friendly with dedicated appliances, while another states plainly it is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for celiac disease. Celiac diners should confirm preparation directly with the owner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: At least one reviewer warns that the bento box is not safe for celiacs because it contains soy sauce — a reminder that standard Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. Ask which items use soy sauce before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: There are dedicated vegan options, notably a vegan chickpea curry, and reviewers mention plenty of vegan items; no specific reports were found on vegan cross-contact in a kitchen that also handles dairy cakes and cheese, so vegan diners should confirm shared-equipment handling with the owner.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe is run by a single owner who is gluten-intolerant herself and is described as kind and helpful, which makes direct conversation about your needs straightforward — but it also means there is no separate trained allergy team, so clear, item-by-item questions are worthwhile.

positives.text: Everything on the menu is gluten-free, made from scratch by an owner who shares the intolerance — rare and reassuring for GF diners.

Homemade chiffon and rice-flour cakes are widely praised as light and fluffy, and many diners report the rice-flour pizza impressed even gluten-eaters; a vegan chickpea curry is available for plant-based guests.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: 'Dedicated' rested entirely on mutually-echoing English aggregators; zero JP substantiation, absent from every curated JP celiac guide + Tabelog. Celiac-owner all-GF concept keeps above 'no' but handling unverified → ask.

places.name: waco crepes
places.gf_detail: all GF
places.vegan_detail: vegan options (confirm vegan when ordering)
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 10:00–15:00; closed Sun–Mon
places.notes: Dedicated-GF crepe shop in Miyagawasuji (Gion). Savoury + sweet crepes, plant-based cream, coconut latte. Vegan & omnivore options.

chef_bio.background: waco crepes is a tiny, six-seat creperie tucked into Miyagawa-cho, one of Kyoto's historic geisha quarters in the Higashiyama district, where you can still cross paths with maiko and geiko on the lanes outside. The shop set out to do something unusual: make every single item gluten-free and vegan without it feeling like a compromise. The crepes are built on an original fermented rice-flour batter using house-blended organic rice flour, organic soy milk, and organic apples and vegetables, which gives them their distinctive mochi-like chew with crisp edges. Each batter is mixed fresh and each crepe griddled individually to order. The standout is a handmade soy whipped cream made from tofu and vanilla bean, and the kitchen leans on dairy-free cheese and chocolate alternatives so the roughly fifty menu options stay fully plant-based. Despite its six-seat footprint, the shop has built a devoted following among celiac and vegan travelers, and sells its own original merchandise such as tenugui hand towels and eco-bags.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking that is gentle on the body: a wholly plant-based, gluten-free menu built on organic ingredients, with each crepe batter mixed and griddled to order.
chef_bio.specialty: Fermented rice-flour crepes (mochi-textured, crisp at the edges) that are entirely gluten-free and plant-based, finished with a signature handmade soy whipped cream made from tofu and vanilla bean. Around 50 sweet and savory variations, from matcha and azuki to avocado-and-vegan-cheese and a much-loved smoked-salmon-style filling.

anecdotes.text: Staff often draw cute little hand-drawn messages on the crepe wrapping paper, a small touch reviewers love.
A celiac travel writer called it 'an excellent 100% gluten free crepe restaurant in Kyoto' and was so taken with the smoked salmon and cream cheese crepe that she ordered two in one sitting.
The handmade soy whipped cream, made from tofu and fragrant vanilla beans, is singled out as a here-alone delicacy with a delicate tofu sweetness.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: life-info.co.jp and the wacocrepes.com/about + vegewel listings confirm a six-seat Miyagawa-cho creperie where every item is gluten-free and vegan, built on an original fermented organic rice-flour batter (with organic soy milk, apples and vegetables), ~50 crepe variations, a mochi-like chewy texture, and a signature tofu-based soy whipped cream with vanilla beans. mynekotabi confirms original merchandise (tenugui, eco-bags). No source seen confirms a November 2021 opening date or a house gluten-free soy sauce for sale.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire shop is dedicated gluten-free: every item on the menu is gluten-free, with crepes built on a fermented rice-flour batter rather than wheat. Because there is no wheat flour used anywhere, the risk of gluten cross-contact is inherently low.
A celiac travel writer describes it as 'an excellent 100% gluten free crepe restaurant' and ate two crepes there without noting any issue, treating it as a safe dedicated venue.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: This is a creperie, not a soy-sauce-heavy kitchen, and the shop actually sells bottles of gluten-free soy sauce, indicating awareness of the wheat-in-soy-sauce issue. Savory crepes are gluten-free across the board, but it is still worth confirming with staff that any savory sauce on your specific crepe is the gluten-free version.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The whole menu is plant-based, so there is no animal dairy or egg used in the standard dough; the crepe batter itself is naturally vegan and dairy-free cream, cheese and chocolate alternatives are used throughout.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reviewers across platforms consistently describe the staff as kind, helpful and attentive to allergies and dietary needs.

positives.text: A fully dedicated gluten-free and vegan kitchen using a fermented organic rice-flour batter, which removes wheat from the premises entirely.
Celiac travelers single it out as one of the safer, dedicated gluten-free stops in Kyoto.
Vegan diners praise the abundant plant-based options, including the homemade soy-milk whipped cream and vegan cheese.

places.name: Koma Gallery Cafe
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→high. Multiple celiac reviewers corroborate GF safety (one 4 visits, never glutened), but restaurant's OWN first-party GF claim unverifiable (English site SSL expired; main domain is artist portal) and JP Tabelog omits GF, so 'dedicated' not fully evidenced. — Prior note: all rice-flour, celiac-safe
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian; ask for vegan items
places.hours_raw: Wed–Mon 10:00–19:00; closed Tue
places.notes: All-GF cafe with Yasaka Pagoda view. Rice-flour bread/milk roll, egg-cucumber sandwich, matcha latte. Quiet, beautiful.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (Higashiyama-ku, near Kodaiji / Yasaka Pagoda)
chef_bio.background: Koma Gallery Cafe is run by the arts organization NPO Koma (NPO高麗), founded by the artist, photographer and writer Keiko Koma (高麗恵子), who established the organization after her parents' deaths and is reported to have funded it from her inheritance, gathering roughly 100 volunteers. Keiko Koma works alongside the musician Idaki Shin (いだきしん, whose real name is reported as Saito Tadamitsu), and the cafe operates as a combined art gallery, cafe and cultural-event space rather than a conventional restaurant — a first-floor cafe where you can hear Idaki Shin's music and a second-floor gallery of Koma's work. Japanese portal material ties the cafe to Koma's cultural projects such as her Goguryeo ('Koma') legends writing and concert work. The cafe is noted for coffee the NPO brings in from Africa (Ethiopia and Tanzania), and Koma has written about 'messages' from African nature and coffee. It is a dedicated gluten-free cafe, serving gluten-free cakes, and looks out on a quiet garden with a view toward the Yasaka Pagoda.
chef_bio.philosophy: Art, coffee and cultural exchange as a path toward a society where people can live in true peace and happiness; the cafe doubles as a gallery and event venue expressing Keiko Koma and Idaki Shin's artistic activities.
chef_bio.specialty: A dedicated gluten-free cafe: gluten-free cakes and sweets (chiffon, matcha and other seasonal cakes, some also dairy-free) and coffee (beans sourced from Africa — Ethiopia and Tanzania — via the NPO), served in a gallery setting with a garden view of the Yasaka Pagoda.

anecdotes.text: The founder Keiko Koma established the NPO behind the cafe after her parents' deaths and reportedly funded it from her own inheritance, gathering about 100 volunteers from various fields.

The cafe's coffee is brought in from Africa (Ethiopia and Tanzania) as part of the owners' NPO work, and Koma has written about 'messages' received from African nature and coffee; guides list it as a 100% gluten-free cafe with cakes and a Yasaka Pagoda garden view.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official portal (komagallerycafe.com) presents the cafe as part of the 'Idaki Shin / Keiko Koma' cultural world and NPO Koma, describing Keiko Koma (artist/photographer/writer) working with musician Idaki Shin, the NPO's ~100 volunteers, its African (Ethiopia/Tanzania) coffee and Ethiopia support work, and Goguryeo-legends concert/writing projects; the cafe sits beneath the Yasaka Pagoda. Tabelog lists it as a Gion-Shijo/Higashiyama gallery cafe with a pagoda garden view; findmeglutenfree and atly list it as a dedicated (100%) gluten-free cafe with chiffon/matcha cakes.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a fully dedicated gluten-free kitchen — no gluten ingredients are used anywhere on the premises, so the menu is built to be celiac-safe rather than relying on a few special items. As with any kitchen, confirm directly with staff if you have a severe reaction.
Everything here is made with rice flour, including the cakes, pastries and the rice bread used for sandwiches, so there is no wheat flour in the building.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is dessert- and sweets-focused (cakes, dorayaki, matcha sweets, rice-bread sandwiches) rather than savory soy-sauce-based dishes, so wheat-containing soy sauce is unlikely to be a primary concern here. Still worth asking about any savory or seasoned item.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan suitability is uncertain — the cafe's sweets and sandwiches commonly include dairy and egg (for example a ham-and-egg rice-bread sando), and no source confirms dedicated vegan preparation. Vegan diners should confirm specific items with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described by visitors as welcoming and accommodating, and the cafe is run as a dedicated gluten-free space, which suggests genuine familiarity with celiac needs.

positives.text: Carries a dedicated-facility / celiac-safe designation: 100% gluten-free with no gluten ingredients used on site.

Everything on the menu is gluten-free and made with rice flour, so the whole menu is open to celiac diners rather than just a handful of items — popular picks include the dorayaki, matcha sweets, cakes and rice-bread sandwiches.

gf_review.why: Multiple celiac reviewers corroborate GF safety (one 4 visits, never glutened), but restaurant's OWN first-party GF claim unverifiable (English site SSL expired; main domain is artist portal) and JP Tabelog omits GF, so 'dedicated' not fully evidenced.
red_flags: No retrievable first-party GF claim
JP-facing Tabelog zero GF mention; reputation lives only in English listings
Savory items with no soy/miso/seasoning statement
No documented dedicated-prep/CC protocol
gf_review.plain_rice: Unknown; seasoned Koma rice bowl exists, plain rice not confirmed. Safest = cakes/dorayaki/coffee reviewers vouch for.

places.name: Gluten-Free Deli AL CAMINO
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→ask. 'Dedicated' rests on a SINGLE source: own marketing. Zero independent celiac-grade corroboration for this newly-reopened (fall 2025) shop, inside a former 17-year wheat wood-fired pizzeria. Single self-claim can never be dedicated/high. — Prior note: owner GF (some packaged items share a facility)
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian pizza/panini (cheese)
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 11:30–18:00; closed Mon–Tue
places.notes: Dedicated-GF Italian deli (Mibu/Nakagyo) with wood-fired GF pizza & panini. Vegetarian options; confirm vegan.

chef_bio.background: AL CAMINO is part of chef Watanabe Takesho's il Viale family of Italian restaurants in Kyoto. His path to cooking began almost by accident: at 21 he traveled to Italy hoping to see Ferraris and Ducati motorcycles, but it was the food that captured him, and he came home determined to cook it. After training at Italian kitchens in Kyoto and Tokyo, he opened his first restaurant, Cucina il Viale, in 1999, and a stint working on a farm in Kyoto's Kamigamo district turned him into a devotee of organic and heirloom Kyoto vegetables. He opened Pizzeria AL CAMINO in 2008, serving wood-fired pizza made with all-Italian ingredients for well over a decade. More recently he reinvented the space entirely, retiring the pizza oven and relaunching AL CAMINO as a gluten-free specialty deli built around rice-flour bread.
chef_bio.philosophy: A conviction that good food wins people over on its own terms. When early diners balked at vegetable-forward plates, Watanabe held the line on the belief that truly delicious things are eventually recognized, the same patient approach he brought to rebuilding AL CAMINO entirely around gluten-free cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour bread panini are the heart of the menu, led by their signature panino, alongside versions like Parma prosciutto and caprese. The kitchen rounds things out with Italian small plates, rice-flour-and-olive-oil chocolate cake and other rice-flour desserts, Italian cookies, fresh-squeezed orange juice, Italian wines, and even a gluten-free Italian beer.

anecdotes.text: Chef Watanabe first went to Italy at 21 to look at Ferraris and Ducati motorcycles, and ended up falling for the food instead, which set him on the path to becoming an Italian chef.

AL CAMINO opened in 2008 as a wood-fired pizzeria (its former name was Pizzeria AL CAMINO) and has since been reborn as a gluten-free specialty deli centered on rice-flour bread panini, with pizza service ended.
A formative period working on a Kamigamo farm in Kyoto turned Watanabe into a champion of organic and Kyoto vegetables, shaping the produce-forward style across his il Viale restaurants.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: watanabechef.com (Japanese) recounts Watanabe Takesho's trip to Italy at 21, training in Kyoto and Tokyo, opening Cucina il Viale in 1999, his Kamigamo farm work and Kyoto-vegetable focus, and lists Pizzeria AL CAMINO (2008) among his il Viale restaurants; the AL CAMINO page states pizza service has ended and it now serves rice-flour panini. No source names a pizzaiolo 'Stefano' or a specific relaunch year.

gf_cross_contamination.text: AL CAMINO presents itself as a gluten-free specialty shop, with rice-flour bread panini as the core menu and no wheat-based pizza or bread program (pizza service has ended). However, the available sources do not document its cross-contamination handling, so confirm preparation details directly before dining.

Existing dietary notes indicate the owner is gluten-free, but flag that some packaged items may share a facility. Celiac diners should verify which items are made in-house versus sourced, and ask about shared equipment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an Italian deli where cheese and prosciutto feature heavily; vegan options are limited. The sources do not detail dairy-free or vegan preparation or cross-contact handling, so vegan diners should confirm specifics with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop's listing states it can accommodate various allergies and that allergy information is available; ask staff for ingredient details on the day.

positives.text: A dedicated gluten-free deli built around rice-flour bread, rather than a conventional restaurant adding a gluten-free option, which means the menu is designed without wheat from the ground up.
Rice-flour desserts (such as a rice-flour and olive-oil chocolate cake), Italian cookies, and a gluten-free Italian beer are offered, giving GF diners full-meal-plus-dessert range.

gf_review.why: 'Dedicated' rests on a SINGLE source: own marketing. Zero independent celiac-grade corroboration for this newly-reopened (fall 2025) shop, inside a former 17-year wheat wood-fired pizzeria. Single self-claim can never be dedicated/high.
red_flags: Only own site asserts GF-only; no celiac reviewer corroborates
Brand new inside a former wheat pizzeria; oven/prep wheat-free status undocumented
Panino deli fillings — no wheat-free soy/barley-free miso statement
Parent (Watanabe Chef) runs multiple WHEAT Italian venues
TripAdvisor stale 'temporarily closed' banner — call to confirm
gf_review.plain_rice: Not available — panino shop; GF item is rice-flour panino, not plain rice.

places.name: SINLESS CAFÉ / Sinless Lab
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→options. 'SINLESS' = guilt-free about SUGAR, not gluten — core concept is low-sugar/low-carb; a JP primary source confirms GF is per-item not cafe-wide. Menu includes wheat quiche and scones in the same kitchen → not dedicated; no first-party celiac/CC claim verifiable. — Prior note: all GF, oat-free, often sugar-free
places.vegan_detail: several plant-based sweets; ask
places.notes: Dedicated-GF patisserie (Nakagyo). Rice-flour cakes, canele, cheesecake, cookies. Owner runs it solo; check IG/Google for hours.

chef_bio.background: SINLESS CAFÉ / Sinless Lab grew out of founder Hisae Sakamoto's years living and working in the food world in Singapore, a country known for its health-forward approach to eating. There, Sakamoto became a researcher of low-sugar cooking and went on to lecture on the subject. The café in central Kyoto is the expression of that research: a 100% gluten-free, sugar-conscious place where the healthier version is the whole point rather than an afterthought. The kitchen leans into ingredients suited to the body, including distinctly Kyoto touches like cakes made with sake lees from the city's Sasaki Sake Brewery.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is sweets you can enjoy without the guilt or the after-effects: 'regulating the body with sweets.' Sweetness is coaxed from plant-based ingredients rather than refined sugar, so a full afternoon tea spread carries only about the sugar of a single rice ball and stays easy on the stomach.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the canelé, reworked the Sinless way: made with rice flour instead of wheat and without white sugar, with the rice flour's amount and distribution carefully calibrated to keep a mellow, rich flavor while holding the sugar down. A boxed set of five low-sugar, gluten-free canelés is the most popular thing they make. Beyond the canelé, expect creative sweets, breads, cakes, puddings, cookies, and a reservation-based afternoon tea set built around bite-sized sweets and a Victoria cake.

anecdotes.text: The flagship canelé is built on rice flour instead of wheat flour and skips white sugar entirely; the founder tuned the amount and distribution of the rice flour to keep a mellow, rich flavor while keeping sugar low.

The owner spent many years in the food business in Singapore researching low-sugar dishes, and built the café around sugar-free sweets 'you won't feel guilty about eating,' with sweetness created from plant-based ingredients.
Some cakes feature sake lees from Kyoto's Sasaki Sake Brewery, part of a deliberate choice of ingredients suited to the Japanese body.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Leafkyoto (JP/EN) names owner Hisae Sakamoto, a low-sugar cookery researcher and university lecturer with years in Singapore's food scene, and describes the rice-flour, no-white-sugar canelé, cakes made with Sasaki Sake Brewery sake lees, and an afternoon tea whose sugar is roughly that of a single rice ball.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Described by sources as a 100% gluten-free café, meaning the whole kitchen is dedicated gluten-free rather than offering GF items alongside wheat ones. This greatly reduces cross-contamination risk, but specific protocols are not documented, so celiac diners should still confirm directly.
The signature canelé and baked goods are made with rice flour in place of wheat flour, consistent with a flour-free (wheat-free) kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: This is a sweets-focused bakery and café (canelés, cakes, puddings, cookies, afternoon tea), not a savory shokudo. No soy-sauce-based dishes are documented; if any savory items are added, ask whether wheat-free (gluten-free) soy sauce is used, since most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

positives.text: A dedicated, fully gluten-free café where everything on offer is gluten-free, so there is no wheat flour in the kitchen to cross-contaminate with.
Baked goods are also low-sugar or sugar-free, with sweetness from plant-based ingredients instead of refined sugar.

gf_review.why: 'SINLESS' = guilt-free about SUGAR, not gluten — core concept is low-sugar/low-carb; a JP primary source confirms GF is per-item not cafe-wide. Menu includes wheat quiche and scones in the same kitchen → not dedicated; no first-party celiac/CC claim verifiable.
red_flags: Concept is sugar-free/low-carb, GF incidental per-item
Wheat items (quiche, scones) → shared-kitchen CC
No dedicated GF kitchen/CC protocol
First-party GF claim unverifiable; 'dedicated' from aggregators
Canele uses soybean flour — soy allergen
gf_review.plain_rice: Not available — pastry cafe; rice only as rice flour in bakes.

places.name: Toshoan
places.gf_detail: all GF (rice-flour souffle pancakes, GF beer)
places.vegan_detail: ask for plant-based items
places.notes: Dedicated-GF cafe/sweets shop (Sanjo, near Nijo). Famous GF souffle pancakes, red-bean sweets, GF beer. Cafe + packaged takeaway.

chef_bio.background: Toshoan grows out of one of Kyoto's old anko houses: a sweet red-bean-paste maker established in 1950, rooted in the city's traditional confectionery trade. In 2012 that bean-paste heritage was reimagined as a modern shop and little cafe a short walk south of Nijo Castle along Horikawa Street, with a refreshed reopening in 2018. The idea is simple but unusual: take the family's craft of making fine 'nama-an' (fresh, smooth red bean paste) and build an entire menu of sweets around it without using any wheat flour at all. The result is a rare thing in Japan, a place where every cake, cookie and pancake is made gluten-free from the ground up rather than as a special-request substitute.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing the craft of fresh red bean paste, and proving that anko-based wagashi can be made entirely without wheat flour so that gluten-free guests can enjoy classic Japanese sweets.
chef_bio.specialty: Gluten-free sweets built on freshly made red bean paste. The cafe is best known for its fluffy gluten-free souffle pancakes, made to order (they take about 30 minutes) and often served with anko and salty butter. The shop side is known for its anko-forward treats: An de Cookies, the An de cheesecake (cream cheese folded with fresh red bean paste), Yokan for Coffee designed to pair with a cup of coffee, sakura anmitsu, and the Miyako Monaka wafer sandwich.

anecdotes.text: The whole shop is wheat-flour-free, with the souffle pancakes as the standout. They are made to order and take about half an hour, but visitors describe them as well worth the wait and a chance to try Japan's famous fluffy pancakes in a gluten-free form.

Toshoan grew from a bean-paste manufacturer founded in 1950; the cafe opened in 2012 and was renewed in 2018, and specializes in treats made from fresh 'nama-an' red bean paste such as An de Cookies, An de cheesecake and Yokan for Coffee.
The matcha souffle pancake in particular is singled out by gluten-free travelers as unforgettable.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms Toshoan descends from a bean-paste maker founded in 1950, opened as a cafe on 3 Mar 2012 and renewed 9 Nov 2018, sits about 5 minutes south of Nijo Castle on Horikawa Street, and makes all its sweets without wheat flour from fresh nama-an (An de Cookies, An de cheesecake, Yokan for Coffee, Miyako Monaka).

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire shop is gluten-free / wheat-flour-free, so it is one of the safer choices in Kyoto for celiac and gluten-intolerant diners. The one documented exception: the Miyako Monaka has cross-contact on its production lines, which the shop states on the menu, so celiacs should avoid that specific item. Everything else is made without wheat flour.
Described by gluten-free travel guides as a completely gluten-free bakery and pancake cafe, including gluten-free versions of Japan's famous souffle pancakes.
All sweets in the shop are made without wheat flour.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan options are limited. While the menu is built on plant-based red bean paste, many signature items contain dairy, including the An de cheesecake (cream cheese) and pancakes/treats served with butter or cream. Vegan diners should confirm which specific items are dairy-free before ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Because the shop is entirely wheat-flour-free, the menu itself flags the one cross-contact item (Miyako Monaka), which suggests allergen awareness in how items are labeled. No further detail on staff training was found in available reviews, so confirm directly for severe allergies.

positives.text: A rare 100% gluten-free / wheat-flour-free shop and cafe, well regarded by celiac travelers; staff are repeatedly described as gentle, polite and helpful.
Signature gluten-free souffle pancakes are made to order and consistently praised; a safe way to enjoy a treat that is normally off-limits to gluten-free diners.

places.name: ZIRAEL Vegan Restaurant
places.gf_detail: ask (many GF dishes)
places.vegan_detail: 100% vegan
places.hours_raw: Hours vary by day ~12:30–21:00; closes when sold out
places.notes: Tiny all-vegan spot in Gion (Ukrainian/European). Big salad sets, dumplings, soy 'meat', hamburger steak, canele. ~8 counter seats.

chef_bio.origin: Gion, Kyoto — in an alley near Yasaka Shrine
chef_bio.background: ZIRAEL is the work of Anna, a Russian founder who opened this tiny plant-based spot in September 2022 in a quiet alley near Yasaka Shrine in Gion. She had been living a vegan lifestyle since 2006 and spent several years in Japan before settling in Kyoto, drawn by the natural surroundings she discovered while traveling the country. She built ZIRAEL around the produce she fell in love with here, sourcing vegetables from growers across the region — Ohara, Tango, Kyotamba, and Kameoka among them. The room seats only a handful of guests, and the warm, personal welcome from the owners is something diners mention again and again.
chef_bio.philosophy: Anna wanted to make plant-based eating in Kyoto effortless for international visitors, who can struggle with Japanese-only labeling elsewhere. As she put it, she would love for foreigners to feel at home here — a place to enjoy a fully plant-based meal without worry. The cooking is 100% plant-based and built first and foremost around showcasing local vegetables.
