chef_bio.specialty: Kyoto-vegetable plates are the heart of the menu, with a daily salad that regulars call some of the best they have had, alongside generous mains like soy hamburg steak, ginger-fried soy meat, and vegan dumplings that travelers single out as standouts. The kitchen also reaches back to Anna's roots with dishes such as Ukrainian borscht and a lentil-tomato stew. The plates change day to day with the season and what the farms send, and popular dishes can sell out, so the menu is never quite the same twice.

anecdotes.text: Anna had lived as a vegan since 2006 and moved to Kyoto in 2022, captivated by the region's vegetables, which she sources from farms in Ohara, Tango, Kyotamba, and Kameoka.
She designed ZIRAEL as a welcoming space for non-Japanese speakers, saying she would love for foreigners to use the place and enjoy plant-based meals without concern.
Guests describe the daily salad as among the best they have ever had and the dumplings as the best they have had in Japan, with the owners remembered for their warm, personal hospitality.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Leafkyoto confirms owner Anna is Russian, vegan since 2006, spent about four years in Japan before moving to Kyoto in 2022, opened ZIRAEL on 17 Sep 2022 in an alley near Yasaka Shrine, and sources seasonal vegetables from Ohara, Tango, Kyotamba and Kameoka; HappyCow reviews confirm the ~8-seat counter, the standout salad and dumplings, and the owners' warm hospitality.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen does not publish a dedicated-fryer or cross-contamination policy, and the room is tiny with a shared cooking space, so celiac diners should confirm preparation directly before ordering.

Note that the soy hamburg steak plates and the burger-style mains are NOT on the restaurant's gluten-free list, and a reviewer reported that a hamburger here was not gluten-free — fried and breaded soy items in particular should be treated as containing gluten unless confirmed otherwise.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-meat and stir-fried dishes are commonly seasoned with soy sauce, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. The restaurant does not document a wheat-free tamari substitution, so celiac diners should ask which seasonings are used on any soy-meat or savory plate.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is 100% plant-based — every dish uses exclusively vegan ingredients — so there is no risk of cross-contact with animal products on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff speak English and the owner is fluent in Japanese, and the menu maintains a labeled gluten-free section, suggesting allergy questions can be discussed directly. The restaurant asks that allergen specifics be confirmed with them (zirael.q@gmail.com) since dishes contain soy products.

positives.text: ZIRAEL explicitly offers a gluten-free menu and labels specific gluten-free items, including the ZIRAEL Plate, ginger-fried soy meat, ice cream, a vegan gluten-free canele, and most drinks.
The restaurant advertises dedicated vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, oriental-vegetarian, and Muslim-friendly menus, indicating real experience accommodating dietary needs.
Being entirely plant-based makes it straightforward and reliable for vegan diners, with consistently warm reports about the welcoming owners.

places.name: AIN SOPH. Journey Kyoto
places.gf_detail: GF items marked; shared fryer (not celiac-safe)
places.vegan_detail: 100% vegan
places.hours_raw: ~11:30–20:00; closed Sat; irregular — check Instagram
places.notes: Popular vegan chain. Souffle/matcha pancakes, burgers, karaage, curry, taco rice. Central (near Shinkyogoku). Allium-free items marked.

chef_bio.background: AIN SOPH. Journey Kyoto is the Kansai outpost of the AIN SOPH. group, the all-vegan restaurant family founded by Yuki Shirai, who opened the first AIN SOPH. in Tokyo in 2009 and has been a driving force in Japan's plant-based dining scene ever since. Shirai grew up between two faiths and worldviews: her father's family followed Shingon esoteric Buddhism while her mother's family was Catholic, an upbringing she credits with teaching her to respect and live close to nature. A homestay with a vegan family in Canada introduced her to plant-based living firsthand, and those encounters set her on the path to building AIN SOPH. The Kyoto branch sits on a quiet backstreet just off bustling Kawaramachi, in a building with deep local roots: it formerly housed the Maruni food hall and still honors a guardian tanuki spirit, 'Shichibe-san,' enshrined on site and tied to the nearby Nishiki Tenmangu shrine. The result is a cozy, fully plant-based cafe where Tokyo classics meet Kyoto-only creations.
chef_bio.philosophy: For Shirai, food is a way to reconnect people with nature and with their truest selves. The name 'Ain Soph' is Hebrew for the infinite, which she encountered during a spiritual experience and felt drawn to; her company name, the single character 'Shin' meaning 'truth,' reflects a commitment she draws from tea ceremony to 'not lying to myself and living in truth.' Everything is 100% plant-based - no meat, fish, dairy, eggs or honey - and she frames veganism not as a mandate but as a cuisine that deserves a place alongside Italian, French and Japanese cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan comfort food and 'junk food done right' - the kitchen is known for hearty plant-based burgers, including a Ripple cheese burger and a cutlet (katsu) burger, plus convincing vegan kara-age (fried 'chicken'), onion rings and curries. On the sweet side they serve the group's signature Heavenly vegan pancakes and a Kyoto-original gluten-free matcha pancake, along with seasonal parfaits and vegan brownies and ice cream.

anecdotes.text: Founder Yuki Shirai grew up between her father's Shingon Buddhist family and her mother's Catholic family, and a homestay with a vegan family in Canada helped lead her toward opening an all-vegan restaurant.

The name 'Ain Soph' is Hebrew for the infinite; Shirai heard the phrase during a spiritual experience and felt a deep connection to it, while her company name 'Shin' (truth) reflects a tea-ceremony-inspired vow to 'not lie to myself and live in truth.'
The Kyoto location occupies a building that once held the Maruni food hall and still enshrines a guardian tanuki spirit, 'Shichibe-san,' connected to the nearby Nishiki Tenmangu shrine.
A diner praised the vegan kara-age as so convincing they didn't realize it wasn't chicken, in a writeup of the cafe's plant-based comfort food.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: delicious-japan.com records founder Yuki Shirai's dual Shingon-Buddhist/Catholic upbringing, her Canada vegan homestay, AIN SOPH's July 2009 Tokyo founding, the Hebrew 'Ain Soph' (infinite) name and the company name 'Shin' (truth) drawn from tea ceremony; ain-soph.jp confirms the Kyoto branch occupies the former Maruni Shokudo and enshrines the Shichibei tanuki deity linked to Nishiki Tenmangu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Specific gluten-free dishes are offered and clearly labeled (for example the gluten-free matcha pancakes), but the kitchen also fries and serves wheat-based items like burgers, cutlets and onion rings, and no source confirms a separate dedicated fryer or a celiac-safe protocol - so cross-contact in shared frying oil and prep areas should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise.

At least one diner specifically came looking for a gluten-free restaurant and found options here, and reviewers note a gluten-free menu is available - but availability of GF items is not the same as celiac safety.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms whether the soy sauce or seasonings used here are wheat-free. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so savory dishes (burgers, cutlets, curries, kara-age) should be treated as potentially containing wheat-derived gluten unless staff confirm a wheat-free soy sauce is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is 100% plant-based - no meat, fish, dairy, eggs or honey are used anywhere in the kitchen - so vegan cross-contact with animal products is not a concern here.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy information is made available and dishes are clearly labeled - reviewers note, for example, that allium-free (onion/garlic-free) items are marked on the menu - though the restaurant states that allergen information does not guarantee against a reaction and asks customers to make the final decision themselves.

positives.text: Fully vegan, 100% plant-based menu, so there is no risk of animal-product cross-contact for vegan diners.
Dedicated gluten-free items exist and are clearly marked on the menu, and the restaurant publishes allergen information.
Allium-free (onion- and garlic-free) options are clearly identified on the menu, which diners with those sensitivities appreciated.

places.name: Vegan Restaurant F
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (ask→options): Identity confirmed; signature pasta is spelt=wheat, GF bread/noodle only on request, no dedicated kitchen → options. (English run said ask; JP run says options — GF rice-flour bread + enzyme brown rice on request lift it one notch.) — Prior: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→ask. Kitchen ROUTINELY handles gluten: own site says it uses spelt/ancient-wheat flour for pasta (spelt = gluten); GF only 'prepared separately' on advance request, no celiac-verified CC protocol, unknown soy-sauce type — fails 'high'. App profile ('ramen & pancake near Gyoen') appears conflated; real place is reservation-only Italian-fusion vegan course near Karasuma-Gojo. — Prior note: all dishes GF except the (spelt) pasta
places.vegan_detail: vegan
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 11:30–13:30 & 18:00–19:00 (reservation); closed Mon–Tue
places.notes: Refined French-style vegan near Kyoto Station. Lunch set; dinner = chef's-choice course, reservation only. House-made vegan cheeses.

chef_bio.background: Effe is the work of chef-owner Toshiyuki Fujimatsu, who was born in Kyoto in 1962. After graduating from the Tsuji Culinary School in Osaka, he trained at a long-established Kyoto Italian restaurant before moving to Italy at age 24. There he cooked at acclaimed kitchens including the two-Michelin-starred Guido and Balzi Rossi, as well as Boccon di Vino, a restaurant tied to the Slow Food movement. He first opened Cucina Naturale Effe in February 2018, then, after deepening his commitment to plant-based cooking and earning a Raw Food Meister certification, reopened in August 2020 as the vegan kitchen known today as Saishoku Ryori Effe, tucked into the Gojo-Karasuma neighborhood of Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fujimatsu cooks by a simple credo: today's cooking creates tomorrow's body. Meals, he believes, are central to health and a full daily life, and his vegetable dishes are meant to carry the gentleness of nature's blessings.
chef_bio.specialty: A vegan kitchen built around vegetables, with as many as 30 varieties appearing across a single meal, sourced from trusted farmers and seasoned with house-made touches like salt koji and amazake. Signature plates include the gluten-free rice-flour bread and the enzyme brown rice, cooked with red beans and sea salt and served at its peak texture on the third day. The cooking is free of meat, fish, dairy, eggs, refined white sugar, and wheat flour, with the one exception of an ancient-grain (spelt) pasta.

anecdotes.text: Before opening his own place, Fujimatsu spent his twenties cooking in Italy at two-Michelin-starred kitchens including Guido and Balzi Rossi, and at Boccon di Vino, a restaurant connected to the Slow Food movement.
He began as an Italian chef, opening Cucina Naturale Effe in 2018, then reoriented entirely toward plant-based cooking and reopened as a fully vegan restaurant in 2020.
The enzyme brown rice is cooked with red beans and sea salt and is considered to reach its best texture on the third day.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: naturale-f.com (concept page) states Toshiyuki Fujimatsu was born in Kyoto in 1962, graduated from Osaka's Tsuji Culinary School, trained at the Kyoto Italian restaurant Fukumura, went to Italy at 24 to cook at Michelin-starred Guido and Balzi Rossi and Slow-Food-linked Boccon di Vino, opened Cucina Naturale Effe in Feb 2018 and reopened vegan (Raw Food Meister certified) in Aug 2020 at Gojo-Karasuma, using 30+ vegetables, spelt pasta, rice-flour bread and third-day enzyme brown rice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is wheat-flour-free throughout, with one exception: an ancient-grain spelt pasta. Spelt is a wheat variety and contains gluten, so it is not safe for celiacs, and there is no documentation of separate cookware or surfaces to keep this pasta from contaminating other dishes. Celiac diners should confirm directly how the spelt pasta is handled and ask for it to be left out.

One reviewer noted that gluten-free accommodation is available, but the spelt pasta still contains gluten, so 'gluten-free' here should be confirmed dish by dish with staff rather than assumed for the whole menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a fully vegan kitchen with no meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or refined white sugar used anywhere, so the risk of animal-product cross-contact is structurally very low.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant states it cannot accommodate vegetable allergies or oriental-style vegan requests (such as excluding the five pungent vegetables), and that gluten-free needs should be arranged in advance. Staff communicate in Japanese, with English menus available.

positives.text: Aside from a single spelt-pasta option, the entire kitchen is built without wheat flour, meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or refined sugar, which removes many common allergens from the table.
House-baked rice-flour bread is offered as a gluten-free alternative to wheat bread.
Gluten-free meals can be arranged with advance notice.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Identity confirmed; signature pasta is spelt=wheat, GF bread/noodle only on request, no dedicated kitchen → options. (English run said ask; JP run says options — GF rice-flour bread + enzyme brown rice on request lift it one notch.)

places.name: Veg Out
places.gf_detail: some items marked GF (rice bowls); not dedicated
places.vegan_detail: 100% vegan
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 9:00–10:30 & 11:30–17:00; Sun to 14:00; closed Mon
places.notes: Riverside vegan cafe near Kyoto Station. Buddha bowls, tomato-coconut curry, BLT-tempeh, cakes; good coffee. Opens early.

chef_bio.background: Veg Out sits on the first floor of the cheerful yellow TAMISA building beside the Kamo River, a short walk from Keihan Shichijo Station. The cafe is paired with the TAMISA yoga studio upstairs, and the two were conceived together as one place: somewhere you could move, eat well, and unwind. The founders describe wanting to create a space where you could relax gently, as peacefully as a plant basking in the light. Large windows open onto the river and greenery, flooding the room with natural light, and the whole spot is built around the idea that healthy food and a calm atmosphere belong side by side. A sister location sits near the Nishiki Market in central Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: The team frames veganism not as restriction but as a way of living rooted in gratitude (kansha) for the natural cycles that sustain us. They draw on the spirit of Buddhist shojin (temple) cuisine and its stance of avoiding unnecessary harm, and they care about three connected things: animal welfare, personal health (food free of additives and chemical seasonings), and the environment. Rather than demanding perfection, they encourage eating consciously and plant-based when you can, even if not every day, and they say each dish is prepared with all of their hearts.
chef_bio.specialty: A fully plant-based menu built on naturally farmed, organically grown vegetables (including Kyoto's famous Kyo-yasai) and grains, with no animal products, additives, or artificial seasonings. The house favorite is the Veg Out Plate; other signatures include the Buddha Bowl with homemade falafel that's crispy outside and soft within, served over brown rice with seasonal greens, an obanzai-style vegetable plate, a tempeh sandwich, open sandwiches with vegan taco meat and guacamole, and a tomato-coconut curry. Drinks lean into Bonsoy and oat milk, with natural wine and craft beer in the evenings.

anecdotes.text: Veg Out sits beside the Kamo River, paired with the TAMISA yoga studio upstairs in the same building, so diners can both eat and take a class in one place.
The signature Buddha Bowl features homemade falafel, crispy on the outside and soft on the inside, with seasonal greens and brown rice.
The cafe draws on the spirit of Buddhist shojin cuisine and frames plant-based eating as gratitude for nature's cycles rather than strict abstinence.

Everything is made without fish, meat, eggs, or dairy, using naturally farmed organic vegetables and grains with no additives or chemical seasonings; the Veg Out Plate is the house's top recommendation.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tamisa-yoga.com and JapanTravel confirm Veg Out is the ground-floor (1F) cafe of the riverside TAMISA building, 2 minutes from Keihan Shichijo Station, attached to the TAMISA yoga studio; it is fully plant-based using naturally grown vegetables including Kyo-yasai with no additives or chemical seasonings, draws on shojin cuisine and gratitude for nature, and recommends the Veg Out Plate. The specific opening year and the yoga studio's floor are not stated in the sources checked.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a vegan cafe, not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. Some items (such as rice bowls) are marked gluten-free, but wheat-based items like sandwiches, tartines, pancakes, muffins, cakes and tarts are made in the same kitchen, so cross-contact is possible. Celiac diners should confirm preparation directly with staff.
A gluten-free menu is listed among the cafe's options, but no detail on cross-contamination protocols is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information on whether the soy sauce or seasonings used are wheat-free is published. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so celiac diners should ask whether tamari or a wheat-free alternative is used in any given dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is plant-based: no fish, meat, eggs, or dairy are used anywhere on the menu, so there is no risk of animal-product cross-contact.
All dishes are described as fully vegan, with soy and oat milk alternatives used for drinks.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific allergy-handling protocols are published. English-speaking staff and an English menu are available, which can help when discussing allergies in person.

positives.text: 100% vegan kitchen, making this an easy and very low-risk choice for vegan diners.
Cooking focuses on naturally farmed organic vegetables and grains with no additives or artificial seasonings.
Some rice-based dishes are marked gluten-free, and a gluten-free menu is listed, giving GF diners a starting point to discuss with staff.
English-speaking staff and an English menu make it easier to confirm ingredients.

places.name: mumokuteki cafe & foods
places.gf_detail: ask (some GF; uses tamari on request)
places.vegan_detail: vegan/vegetarian
places.hours_raw: Thu–Tue 11:30–19:00; closed Wed
places.notes: Spacious vegan cafe above a lifestyle shop (Teramachi). Steamed-veg gozen, soy-milk ramen, vegan cutlets, tiramisu. Halal-friendly set.

chef_bio.background: mumokuteki cafe sits on the upper floor of the mumokuteki goods & wears shop, just off the Teramachi shopping arcade in downtown Kyoto. It is run by the mumokuteki group, which builds everything it does around the idea of 'creating life' — and that idea reaches all the way back to the soil. The group farms its own land at mumokutekifarm in Miyama, in the mountains north of Kyoto, where it grows pesticide-free rice, and sources its vegetables from contracted farmers across the Kinki region who use careful, low-impact methods. That farm-to-table chain is the quiet backbone of the kitchen: the food you eat here started as their rice and their growers' produce.
chef_bio.philosophy: Eat with peace of mind. The kitchen uses absolutely no animal-derived ingredients — no eggs, dairy, refined sugar, meat, fish, or chemical seasonings — so that whether you have allergies, are vegan or vegetarian, or simply care a little about your health, you can enjoy a meal here without hesitation. Even the dashi is plant-based, made from kombu and shiitake rather than animal stock.
chef_bio.specialty: A fully plant-based Japanese menu built so that vegans, vegetarians, and people with allergies can order without second-guessing. The signature is the 'mumokuteki Special Set of 3' — a hamburger built on tofu and okara (soy pulp), okara karaage (fried okara), and rich miso katsu. They are also known for inventive desserts that use no dairy at all: a tiramisu and seasonal cheesecake built on cashews instead of cheese, and a soy-milk soft serve.

anecdotes.text: The group grows its own pesticide-free rice at mumokutekifarm in Miyama, Kyoto, and brings in vegetables from contracted Kinki-region farmers who use safe, low-impact methods.
The miso soup is made entirely from kombu and shiitake dashi, with no animal stock — part of the kitchen's all-vegan, gentle-on-the-body approach.
Desserts reimagine dairy classics without any animal products: a tiramisu and a seasonal cheesecake made with cashews instead of cheese.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mumokuteki.com and Tabelog confirm an entirely animal-product-free menu (no eggs, dairy, refined sugar, meat, fish or chemical seasonings), kombu-and-shiitake dashi, pesticide-free rice from mumokutekifarm in Miyama (Nantan) and vegetables from contracted Kinki-region farmers, the tofu-hamburger / okara-karaage / miso-katsu set, and cashew-based tiramisu and cheesecake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The cafe offers some gluten-free menu items (including a Sesame Miso Soy Milk Ramen Set and several desserts marked gluten-free), but states plainly that the kitchen is NOT a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and that utensils and dishes may carry traces of wheat. This means it cannot be considered safe for celiac diners — treat the GF items as lower-gluten, not zero-risk, and confirm directly if you are highly sensitive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Several dishes use a soy-sauce base (e.g. the fried okara-konjac in the signature set). Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) typically contains wheat, so soy-sauce-seasoned items should be assumed to contain wheat unless staff confirm a wheat-free tamari is used. The existing note that tamari may be available on request points to this being a per-dish, ask-the-staff situation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cross-contact risk for vegans is structurally low: the entire kitchen is animal-product-free — no eggs, dairy, meat, or fish are used anywhere — so there are no animal ingredients in the kitchen to cross-contaminate with.
Even the dashi/stock is plant-based (kombu and shiitake), removing the most common hidden-animal pitfall (fish/bonito stock) found in Japanese cooking.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The English menu clearly lists information important to vegans, such as whether fish stock is used, which makes it easier to identify what is safe before ordering.

The cafe explicitly welcomes guests with allergies alongside vegans and vegetarians, framing the whole menu around being able to dine 'with peace of mind' — but for specific allergen questions it is best to confirm with staff, as detailed cross-contamination protocols are not published.

positives.text: 100% vegan kitchen — no animal-derived ingredients of any kind, so animal-product cross-contact is not a concern for vegan diners.
Also avoids eggs, dairy, refined sugar, and chemical seasonings, and uses plant-based kombu/shiitake dashi.
Offers a dedicated set of gluten-free menu items and is upfront about the kitchen not being a dedicated GF environment, which lets sensitive diners make an informed choice.

noodles.notes: Standard wheat ramen noodles by default; rice-flour (komeko) noodles are offered so the bowl can be made gluten-free.

ramen.signature_bowl: Veggie Soy-Milk Ramen (tonkotsu-style) - a creamy, plant-based bowl whose soy-milk broth mimics the rich body of pork-bone tonkotsu. The weekday-limited Sesame-Miso Soy-Milk Ramen and a Sichuan-style tantanmen round out the noodle lineup.
ramen.chashu: No pork or chicken chashu - this is a fully plant-based kitchen, so the bowl is dressed with vegetables and plant proteins rather than braised or grilled meat.

gf.note: Ramen can be ordered with rice-flour noodles and a gluten-free soy sauce, making a celiac-friendly bowl genuinely possible - confirm preparation when ordering.

vegan.note: Entirely vegan kitchen: the soy-milk broth, tare, oils and toppings contain no meat, fish, egg or dairy, so the ramen is fully plant-based.

ramen.pantheon: A rare fully plant-based ramen: a creamy soy-milk broth engineered to echo the rich, clingy body of Hakata-style tonkotsu without a trace of animal fat, deepened with sesame and miso. Its soul is the soy-milk-and-miso lineage of modern Japanese vegan cooking rather than any regional gotochi tradition.

places.highlight: A rare fully plant-based ramen: a creamy soy-milk broth engineered to echo the rich, clingy body of Hakata-style tonkotsu without a trace of animal fat, deepened with sesame and miso. Its soul is the soy-milk-and-miso lineage of modern Japanese vegan cooking rather than any regional gotochi tradition.

places.name: Nijiya
places.gf_detail: confirm (some fried/soy items)
places.vegan_detail: vegan
places.notes: 7-seat vegan izakaya (Kiyamachi). Soy-meat yakitori/yakiniku, obanzai, dumplings, miso rice balls. Owner cooks solo; queue.

chef_bio.background: Nijiya (虹屋) is a tiny, fully vegan izakaya tucked into Kyoto's Kiyamachi neighbourhood, a short walk from Sanjo Keihan Station. It is run by two women who cook everything themselves in a snug counter space of only about six or seven seats. The intimate, no-reservations setup means you eat almost shoulder-to-shoulder with the cooks, and conversation flows easily across the counter. The kitchen is part of the Vegan Farm project, and the warmth of the two owners, their easy smiles and attentiveness, is the thing guests mention as often as the food.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking leans on a wholesome, body-friendly idea of vegan Japanese food: a dashi built from konbu (kelp), dried shiitake, daikon and burdock rather than fish, brown rice grown without pesticides, and gyoza made without alliums so nothing lingers on your breath. The owners pair this with a hands-on, personal hospitality that the small counter makes possible.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is genmai dashi chazuke, a bowl of pesticide-free brown rice over which a deeply savoury, entirely plant-based broth is poured at the table (you are often invited to taste the dashi on its own first). Beyond that, Nijiya reimagines izakaya classics without any animal products: soy-meat yakitori and yakiniku skewers that even meat-eaters say are hard to tell from the real thing, gyoza made deliberately without leek, shallot or garlic, yuzu tofu with yuba, an obanzai assortment of Kyoto small plates, grilled miso rice balls, and a chocolate yokan for dessert.

anecdotes.text: The dashi poured over the brown-rice chazuke is made from konbu (kelp), dried shiitake mushroom, daikon and burdock, and guests are encouraged to taste it on its own before pouring it over the rice.
The gyoza are made without leek, shallot or garlic, so there's no lingering smell on your breath afterwards.
Two women prepare all of the dishes themselves in the tiny seven-seat counter space, serving a set 'nijiya course' alongside a la carte plates.
The soy-meat yakitori and yakiniku skewers are praised as tender and flavourful, with diners noting they were hard to distinguish from meat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Review sources (visitjapan-vegetarian, herbivoresheaven, itravelforveganfood) confirm Nijiya is a roughly seven-seat counter izakaya near Sanjo/Kiyamachi run by two women and 'produced by Vegan Farm', serving genmai dashi chazuke with kombu/shiitake/daikon/burdock dashi over pesticide-free brown rice, allium-free gyoza, and soy-meat yakitori/yakiniku. A specific opening date is not stated in the sources checked.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-aware caution, not a celiac-safe rating. The menu includes gyoza (wheat-flour wrappers), soy-meat skewers (commonly seasoned with soy sauce and may be wheat-based), and deep-fried tofu in the obanzai assortment, so wheat and shared frying/cooking surfaces are present. No source documents gluten-free preparation, a dedicated fryer, or celiac protocols. Treat as not celiac-safe unless you confirm specific dishes and handling directly with the cooks.
The obanzai assortment is described as including deep-fried tofu, indicating frying happens in the kitchen; no information confirms whether the fryer is dedicated or gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The gyoza are served with house-made soy sauce, and soy-meat skewers are part of the menu. Most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, and no source states this soy sauce or seasoning is wheat-free. Assume it contains gluten unless the cooks confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Nijiya is a fully vegan kitchen with no animal products on the premises, so the risk of animal-product cross-contact is effectively nil. The dashi is built from konbu, dried shiitake, daikon and burdock rather than fish, so it is plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A reviewer reported the staff have good English and are very attentive, and will be accommodating with sensitivities such as garlic and spices.
With only the two owner-cooks running a six-to-seven-seat counter, requests can be discussed directly, though the very small kitchen may limit how strictly cross-contamination can be controlled.

positives.text: Entirely vegan menu with a plant-based dashi (konbu, dried shiitake, daikon, burdock) instead of fish stock, so no hidden animal products.
Gyoza are made without leek, shallot or garlic, which helps diners avoiding alliums.
Attentive, English-speaking staff described as accommodating to sensitivities like garlic and spices.

places.name: MERCY Vegan Factory (Kyoto Station)
places.gf_detail: ask (shojin curry, gimbap)
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.notes: All-vegan spot by Kyoto Station (Elite Inn B1F). Vegan 'egg' sandwiches, shojin curry, gimbap, cakes. Reserve at busy times.

chef_bio.background: MERCY Vegan Factory opened its Kyoto Station location in September 2024 as a branch of the all-vegan MERCY Vegan Factory that started in Osaka. Tucked into the basement floor (B1F) of the Elite Inn near Kyoto Station, it brought the brand's playful, comfort-food take on plant-based eating to Kyoto, including a rare-for-Japan early-morning breakfast service alongside dinner.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for crowd-pleasing vegan comfort food rather than austere temple fare: a much-loved vegan egg salad sandwich, deep-fried kurumafu (a chewy wheat-gluten mock meat that fans rave about), Korean-style gimbap, hearty curries, and a 'criminally good' matcha pudding. The bakery case rounds it out with chocolate-filled melon pan and cinnamon rolls.

anecdotes.text: Diners single out the vegan egg salad sandwich, with one writing they had 'been craving an egg salad sandwich for years and this was perfect,' and the matcha pudding earning a 'criminally good' verdict.

A visitor notes the Kyoto branch opens early for breakfast, which stands out because 'not many vegan spots open early in Japan,' with house curries, fried kurumafu, melon pan and cinnamon rolls on offer.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms MERCY Vegan Factory Kyoto opened on 2024-09-12 in the B1F of Elite Inn Kyoto Station (Shimogyo-ku, Kamayacho 3, ~716 m from Kyoto Station); HappyCow reviews confirm it is a branch of the Osaka original, note breakfast plus dinner service, and praise the vegan egg-salad sandwich, matcha pudding, fried kurumafu, curries, melon pan and cinnamon rolls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-heavy kitchen, so it is not a safe choice for celiac diners. Several signature items are built on wheat gluten or wheat flour: the popular deep-fried kurumafu is a wheat-gluten product (one reviewer describes it as 'a little bit like seitan'), and the bakery (melon pan, cinnamon rolls) and the egg-salad sandwich on white bread are all wheat-based.

With wheat gluten (kurumafu/fu) and breads central to the menu, flour is present throughout the kitchen and airborne/surface cross-contamination is likely. Anyone with celiac disease should confirm directly with staff before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu includes curries, gimbap and other savory Japanese/Korean dishes that typically rely on soy sauce; most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. GF gluten content of the soy seasoning has not been documented, so celiac diners should confirm whether wheat-free tamari is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is fully vegan, with an entirely plant-based menu and no animal products used on premises, so cross-contact with meat, dairy or egg is not a concern here.

positives.text: Because the entire kitchen is vegan, plant-based diners can order freely across the menu without worrying about animal-product cross-contact.

places.name: Vegginy
places.gf_detail: ask (falafel/salads naturally GF)
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.notes: Basement vegan cafe near Karasuma-Oike. Big Buddha bowls, slow-cooked vegan burger, hibiscus lemonade. Each dish made to order.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (founder's culinary outlook shaped by five years in New York)
chef_bio.background: Vegginy is the project of Tomomi Horii, who spent five years living in New York. It was there, surrounded by the city's wildly diverse vegetarian and vegan cooking, that her whole idea of plant-based food shifted: she realized it could be rich, satisfying, and genuinely fun rather than an exercise in going without. She brought that energy home to Kyoto, opening Vegginy in the Gosho-minami area just south of the Imperial Palace, a short walk from Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae station. The space is small and cozy, tucked away with a counter and a couple of tables, and Horii cooks with a New York sensibility translated for a Kyoto audience.
chef_bio.philosophy: Horii's aim is to bring Japan more casual, fun, and delicious vegan food (in her words, '日本にももっと気軽で、楽しくておいしいヴィーガン料理を') - plant-based eating that feels abundant and welcoming rather than restrictive.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegginy is known for its special vegan burger: a spiced chickpea-paste patty bulked out with three kinds of mushroom, grains, and spices for an umami-rich, hearty bite, layered on a soft homemade bun with roasted onions, salted cucumber, and tomato so you get sweetness, salt, and acidity all at once. Beyond the burger, the kitchen turns out colorful, generous plates like a power Buddha bowl and bibimbap, plus weekly-changing vegan specials and homemade baked goods made with coconut oil.

anecdotes.text: Five years in New York transformed Tomomi Horii's view of vegetarian food after she experienced the city's many international plant-based cuisines and found them 'so rich and satisfying' - the inspiration behind Vegginy.

The signature burger's patty is built on a chickpea base mixed with three varieties of mushroom and spices for deep umami, served on a slightly sweet, moist homemade bun with roasted onions and salted cucumber.
Horii applies her New York experience to create weekly-changing vegan menus and homemade baked goods made with coconut oil.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: leafkyoto and life-info confirm owner Tomomi Horii spent five years in New York, where diverse plant-based food reshaped her view of vegan cuisine as 'rich and satisfying', and opened Vegginy in Gosho-minami near Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae; they confirm her quote '日本にももっと気軽で、楽しくておいしいヴィーガン料理を', the chickpea-and-three-mushroom patty on a sweet moist homemade bun with roasted onions and salted cucumber, weekly-changing menus and coconut-oil baked goods.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature vegan burger is served on a homemade bun made from wheat flour and whole wheat flour, so the burger as served is not gluten-free. Wheat is baked in-house, which means flour is handled in the kitchen.

Some components are naturally gluten-free in themselves - the patty is chickpea-paste based, and the kitchen also offers salad-style and bowl dishes (Buddha bowl, bibimbap) - but no dedicated gluten-free preparation or cross-contamination controls are documented. Celiac diners should confirm directly with the owner before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No specific soy sauce or seasoning ingredient details are documented. As is typical in Japan, any soy sauce used likely contains wheat unless confirmed otherwise, so wheat-sensitive and celiac diners should ask about sauces and seasonings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegginy is a fully vegan kitchen - the entire menu is plant-based with no animal products on premises - so animal cross-contact is not a concern. The bun uses soy milk rather than dairy.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reviewers describe the staff as very friendly and the owner as passionate about her work and a great English speaker, which should make it easy to ask about ingredients and accommodations. No specific allergy-handling protocol is documented, so discuss your needs directly when ordering.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen, so no risk of animal-product cross-contact; the menu is entirely plant-based.
Friendly, English-speaking owner who is hands-on and passionate, making it straightforward to discuss ingredients and dietary needs.
Food is made largely from scratch - homemade buns, patties, and baked goods - so the owner knows the ingredients in each dish.

places.name: Hachidori Table
places.gf_detail: confirm (curry udon contains wheat)
places.vegan_detail: vegan/vegetarian
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:00–14:00; closed Mon
places.notes: Beloved vegan cafe south of Kyoto Station (Minami). Vegan 'katsu' bowl, veg curry, plum soda, great coffee. Next to a vegan grocer.

chef_bio.background: Hachidori Table (ハチドリTable) is a small plant-based light-meal and coffee shop in Kyoto's Minami-ku, located near Toji Temple (Hachijo-cho 438, about a 14-minute walk from Kyoto Station). Japanese listings describe it as essentially 100% plant-based, using ingredients such as soy meat, and free of the 'five pungent' Buddhist-avoided ingredients (green onion, onion, garlic, Chinese chives and rakkyo), which aligns it with shojin/Buddhist-influenced vegan cooking. The menu is built around easy everyday dishes like curry rice and udon. Daytime hours are about 11:00-15:00 with an evening reservation-only slot (17:00-19:00), closed Mondays; the specific owner/chef is not named in available public sources.
chef_bio.philosophy: Approachable, essentially fully plant-based everyday food that also excludes the five pungent roots, making it suitable for strict vegetarian and Buddhist diners.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based comfort dishes such as soy-meat curry rice and udon; coffee.

anecdotes.text: The shop avoids the 'five pungent' ingredients (negi, onion, garlic, nira, rakkyo) in addition to being essentially fully plant-based, using ingredients such as soy meat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Vegewel and Japan VegeMap describe the Kyoto Hachidori Table as a near-100% plant-based light-meal/coffee shop near Toji (Minami-ku, Hachijo-cho 438), five-pungent-free, using soy meat, serving curry rice and udon; hours ~11:00-15:00 lunch plus reservation-only evenings (17:00-19:00), closed Monday. A separately named 'Hachidori table' in Tottori is a different establishment. No owner name is published, and no source checked confirms an adjacent 'DAICHU' shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Some menu items are offered as gluten-free, but the kitchen also serves wheat-based dishes such as curry udon and ramen, so cross-contact with wheat is possible. There is no documented dedicated gluten-free prep area or fryer. If you have celiac disease, contact the cafe before visiting and confirm how each dish is prepared.
The curry udon and noodle dishes contain wheat and are not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms whether the soy sauce or seasonings used are wheat-free. As most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, celiac diners should ask staff directly which dishes use wheat-free seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is primarily plant-based, but a few items contain dairy and/or eggs. Vegans should tell the staff to exclude milk and eggs from their order.

The cafe describes its food as 100% plant-based and free of the five pungent vegetables (onion, garlic, chives, leeks, shallots), though some listed items note dairy/egg use, so confirm when ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe states it will accommodate allergies as much as possible and invites diners to contact them in advance with dietary needs.
Staff will do their best to accommodate dietary requirements; food is prepared from scratch and cooked fresh to order, which can make custom requests easier.

positives.text: A dedicated plant-based cafe near Toji Temple and Kyoto Station, with vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and oriental-vegetarian (no five pungent vegetables) options on the menu.
An English menu is available and the staff are described as welcoming, with dishes made fresh to order.

places.name: Shio Veg Cafe
places.gf_detail: confirm (bakes own bread)
places.vegan_detail: vegan/vegetarian
places.notes: Spacious vegan coffee shop (Higashiyama/Gojo). House-baked bread, tofu/avocado/hummus toasts, cookies, muffins.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (Higashiyama ward, near Sanjusangendo temple)
chef_bio.background: Shio Veg Cafe was founded by Harsha Sharma, who brings close to a decade of experience in the cafe world to this Kyoto venture. The cafe opened in September 2024 and sits in a wonderfully convenient spot for sightseers: just a few steps from the Sanjusangendo temple and directly across the street from the Kyoto National Museum, in a space that blends an old-Kyoto atmosphere with a bright, modern plant-based kitchen. The aim from the start has been to make vegetarian and vegan food welcoming to everyone, vegetarians and meat-eaters alike, with an emphasis on sustainability and food made from scratch rather than anything pre-packaged.
chef_bio.philosophy: A from-scratch, nothing-artificial approach to plant-based cooking, with a stated emphasis on sustainability, mindful eating, and making vegetarian and vegan food approachable for a wide audience.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based cafe fare made fresh in-house. Diners single out the tofu sandwich, the avocado and guacamole toasts (one visitor called it the best they had ever had), and the baked goods, especially the banana walnut and pumpkin nutmeg muffins and chewy cookies. They bake their own bread, including sourdough, and pour matcha lattes and specialty coffee (oat milk available).

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits a few steps from Sanjusangendo temple and directly across from the Kyoto National Museum, blending an old-Kyoto feel with a fresh plant-based menu.

Founder Harsha Sharma launched the cafe in September 2024 after around nine years in the cafe industry, with the goal of introducing vibrant vegetarian and vegan flavours to a broad audience while emphasizing sustainability.
All of the food is made from scratch, with diners praising the freshly made tofu sandwiches, guacamole toast and house-baked muffins and cookies.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a from-scratch bakery-style cafe that bakes its own bread and sourdough on site and serves toasts, muffins and cookies, so wheat flour is heavily present in the kitchen. No gluten-free preparation or cross-contamination protocols are documented in any source. Celiac diners should treat the cafe as not verified celiac-safe and confirm options directly with staff before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a vegetarian cafe rather than an all-vegan kitchen. Reviewers note the sandwiches are vegan (including the bread), several items can be ordered vegan, but some toasts contain cheese and are vegetarian only. Dairy is present in the kitchen, so strict vegans should specify a vegan order and confirm there is no cross-contact.
The listing states the cafe offers vegan and egg-free menu options.

positives.text: The cafe has a clearly marked vegan and egg-free menu, and sandwiches including the bread are vegan, with several other items available made vegan on request.
Food is made from scratch with no pre-packaged ingredients, and reviewers consistently praise the freshness of the tofu sandwiches, guacamole toast and baked goods.

places.name: Cafe Gallery Sun
places.gf_detail: confirm (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.notes: Cosy all-vegan cafe (Shimogyo). Soy-milk-cheese avocado toast, sandwiches, mushroom cream pasta, cakes & smoothies.

chef_bio.background: Cafe Gallery Sun (燦) occupies a kyomachiya, a traditional Kyoto wooden townhouse, that dates back to around 1916, when it was first built as a private home. More than a century later it was taken over by architect Takeshi Yamazaki, who spent about six months redesigning the space alongside his regular work before opening the cafe on 22 October 2022. The result is part cafe, part design studio and part gallery, all under one roof on Gokomachi Street in Shimogyo. One of the most-admired details is the ceiling, finished with an embossed washi (Japanese paper) pattern made by the renowned Kyoto paper house Kyo Karakami Maruni, giving the bright, calm room a distinctly Kyoto craft sensibility.
chef_bio.philosophy: An architect's eye runs through the whole experience, pairing a restored century-old townhouse and handcrafted Kyoto materials with a small, all-vegan menu where, as guests often note, everything is made with a lot of care.
chef_bio.specialty: A fully plant-based menu of unfussy, carefully made dishes. Regulars come for the avocado sandwich, the open-faced avocado sandwich, the ham-and-vegetable sandwich and the pasta of the day served with a baguette and salad. The bread comes from Slō, a much-loved Kyoto bakery. Sweets round things out, with carrot, banana and chocolate cakes, plus vanilla and rum-raisin ice cream.

anecdotes.text: The cafe lives inside a roughly 107-year-old kyomachiya built in 1916, which architect Takeshi Yamazaki redesigned over about six months and opened as Cafe Gallery Sun on 22 October 2022.

The ceiling is a signature feature, finished with an embossed washi pattern made by Kyoto paper company Kyo Karakami Maruni, and the bread served with the sandwiches and pasta comes from the popular Kyoto bakery Slō.
The space doubles as the architect's design studio and a small gallery, and visitors describe a calm, bright, stylish room where the all-vegan menu is small but everything is made with great care.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free menu or cross-contamination protocol is documented in available sources. The menu leans heavily on wheat: sandwiches, baguette, and pasta of the day are all core items, so the kitchen routinely handles wheat. This cafe should not be considered celiac-safe; a celiac diner would need to confirm directly with the staff before eating.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information found on whether soy sauce or other seasonings used are wheat-free. As most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, anyone strictly avoiding gluten should ask the staff directly about seasonings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is fully vegan, so there is no animal-product cooking line to cross-contact with. This makes it a comfortable choice for vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific reports on how staff handle allergy requests. Ordering is counter-service (order and pay first, then take a seat), so flag any allergy clearly at the counter when ordering.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen, so every dish is plant-based with no risk of animal-product cross-contact.
Small, made-with-care menu that guests consistently praise; the owner is attentive and responds personally to guest feedback.

places.name: Smoothie etc
places.gf_detail: smoothie bowls naturally GF; ask
places.vegan_detail: vegan
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 9:00–15:00; closed Mon–Tue
places.notes: Vegan smoothie & bowl bar (Sanjo, in Tamisa yoga studio, 2F). Smoothie bowls, soy lattes. Opens early for breakfast.

chef_bio.background: Smoothie etc lives on the second floor of the TAMISA yoga studio in the Sanjo-Teramachi neighbourhood, a calm pocket tucked above one of central Kyoto's busiest shopping streets. The cafe began as a way to nourish the studio's yoga students from the inside out, offering food and drink meant to be kind to both mind and body rather than relying on artificial products. It later widened its doors to travellers and neighbours looking for honest, plant-based fare. In August 2018 it was reborn from the earlier Cosy Cafe under the name Smoothie etc, built around the idea of being 'Mindful and Convenient': conscious daily choices that are still easy to fold into real life. Regulars affectionately shorten the name to 'Sumueto.'
chef_bio.philosophy: Food and drink should be 'Mindful and Convenient' - kind to body and mind, made from additive-free, plant-based, organic and fair-trade ingredients, and easy to bring into everyday life.
chef_bio.specialty: Smoothies built on organically grown and pesticide-free (or reduced-pesticide) fruit and vegetables sourced from around Kyoto, each laced with ginger and warming spices meant to aid digestion. Signatures include Super Green, Beet Ginger, Spice Nut Milk, and the Cacao Energy smoothie made with banana, amazake, cacao and soy milk. The smoothie bowl, topped with banana, berries, house-made granola and nuts, has become a draw, alongside gluten-free rice-flour muffins sweetened with brown sugar, maple syrup, dried fruit and nuts instead of refined white sugar.

anecdotes.text: The cafe grew out of TAMISA yoga studio as a way to support yoga students from a dietary perspective, before expanding to welcome travellers and other visitors seeking accessible vegan food and drink.
Formerly known as Cosy Cafe, it was rebranded in August 2018 as Smoothie etc around the concept 'Mindful + Convenient.'

Smoothies use organically grown, pesticide-free or reduced-pesticide produce from around Kyoto, with ginger and spices added to aid digestion, and rice-flour muffins are sweetened with brown sugar, maple syrup, dried fruit and nuts rather than white sugar.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The cafe is fully plant-based and offers a number of gluten-free items, including smoothie bowls, rice-flour muffins, cakes and cookies. No information on shared prep surfaces or flour-dust cross-contamination is published, so a strict celiac should confirm preparation directly with staff before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: This is a smoothie- and baked-goods-focused vegan cafe rather than a savory soy-sauce kitchen; documented menus emphasize fruit, vegetable and spice smoothies, soups, vegan bread and rice-flour baked goods. No specific soy-sauce details are published. Soy milk and amazake do appear in several smoothies, which is relevant for soy-sensitive diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is described as 100 percent vegan and plant-based, with all items additive-free, so animal-product cross-contact risk is low.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A diner with a nut allergy reported a serious incident: a smoothie bowl arrived garnished with visible walnuts, and after sending it back, staff offered only to scoop the nuts out rather than remake the dish, assuring him it was 'just the garnish.' He nonetheless had an allergic reaction, with nuts apparently present in the bowl or an accompanying cookie. The reviewer felt the cafe did not show due diligence in guiding a nut-allergy sufferer through the menu.

Nuts are used heavily across the menu - nut milks, walnut and granola smoothie-bowl toppings, and nut-studded muffins - so anyone with a nut allergy should be explicit with staff and exercise strong caution.

positives.text: A reviewer noted the cafe offers many kinds of gluten-free muffins.
Fully vegan kitchen using additive-free, plant-based, organic and pesticide-free ingredients, which simplifies things for plant-based diners.

places.name: Padma
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: all vegan (Indian-inspired)
places.hours_raw: Tue–Wed & Fri–Sun 12:00–19:00 (to 20:00 wknd); closed Mon & Thu
places.notes: All-vegan set meals with Indian influence near Demachiyanagi (Sakyo, riverside). Multiple curries, salad, tempura, chai. Vintage-music vibe.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Demachiyanagi); the owner relocated from Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Padma is the work of Hisano Kuno, who came to plant-based cooking through a deep interest in Buddhism and yoga. Years ago she traveled to India, where alongside her spiritual practice she learned to cook with spices and to prepare vegetarian food. Back in Japan she studied macrobiotics and Buddhist temple cuisine (shojin ryori), but found that a rigid macrobiotic approach didn't suit her own body, so she gradually shaped a gentler, more personal style of cooking. Drawn by Kyoto's people, its young, conscientious farmers, and the quality of its vegetables, she left Tokyo and settled in the city, opening Padma with the help of friends who helped renovate the space. The name Padma means lotus flower, an Eastern symbol of purity and awakening, and the room carries a warm, lived-in atmosphere that blends Japanese and early-20th-century Western touches.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kuno calls her food 'heiwateki gohan' - peaceful meals: dishes that everyone can enjoy regardless of dietary needs, without animal products, made so simply that a guest who asks how something is cooked can actually understand the answer. She leans on fermented seasonings - miso, plum vinegar and sesame paste - as her three pillars for depth, prioritizes organic and pesticide-free Kyoto vegetables and brown rice, and aims to cook gentle, healing food adapted to the person in front of her.
chef_bio.specialty: A fully plant-based, Kyoto-vegetable-centered table built around Kuno's 'Peaceful Meal Plate' - a generous seasonal spread of many small vegetable dishes. Signature items include a tempeh cutlet/croquette and a katsudon made with kuruma-fu (wheat gluten) standing in for meat, plus Indian-influenced spice cooking. A point of pride is the handcrafted RUSTO tempeh, made in Shiga from soybeans and natural groundwater with no preservatives, by an Indonesian maker who built his own well and workshop.

anecdotes.text: Kuno's path to the kitchen began with Buddhism and yoga: a trip to India years ago, where she learned both spiritual practice and how to cook vegetarian food with spices.

She studied macrobiotics but found a strict version didn't agree with her own body - root vegetables bothered her pancreas - so she built her own gentler 'peaceful meals' style instead of following any single doctrine.

She moved from Tokyo to Kyoto after being won over by the city's young farmers and the quality of its vegetables, saying Kyoto vegetables are abundant and delicious and that the soil's energy feels like it circulates naturally.
A signature ingredient is RUSTO tempeh, hand-made in Shiga from soybeans and natural groundwater with no preservatives, by an Indonesian maker who dug his own well and built his own workshop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe kitchen. Several of Padma's signature dishes are built on wheat: a katsudon made with kuruma-fu (wheat gluten / fu) and a wheat-gluten 'meat' substitute (seitan/fu) appear as mains, so wheat is handled and cooked throughout the kitchen.
The menu also includes fried, battered items such as tempura broccoli and a vegetable cutlet, which typically use wheat flour batter and a shared fryer - an additional gluten cross-contact risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source documents the use of wheat-free tamari. As with most Japanese kitchens, assume standard soy sauce and fermented seasonings (the kitchen relies on miso and plum vinegar) may contain wheat unless confirmed with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Padma is a fully plant-based kitchen - no meat, fish, eggs or dairy are used anywhere - so the risk of animal-product cross-contact is minimal. The menu is explicitly vegan/oriental-vegetarian.
Even desserts are animal-free, e.g. macrobiotic sweets such as blueberry amazake pudding rather than dairy-based items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner describes cooking that adapts to the individual - serving gentler meals for guests avoiding raw foods, reducing salt, or with particular health needs - which suggests she is willing to accommodate requests, though no formal allergy protocol is documented.

positives.text: 100% plant-based menu, so vegan diners can order freely without checking for hidden animal ingredients.

Ingredient-conscious cooking: organic, pesticide-free Kyoto vegetables, brown rice, and deliberately simple recipes the owner says a guest can understand when they ask how a dish is made - helpful when discussing what's in your food.

places.name: MIMI – Vegan Sweets & Restaurant
places.gf_detail: Newly opened (June 2025) 'food barrier-free' (食のバリアフリー) vegan restaurant near Tofukuji; gluten-free baked goods (e.g. あんバタースコーン) are confirmed and, being fully vegan, cross-allergen risk is lower. Its pasta/pizza GF status is per-dish, not whole-kitchen, so confirm each item and cross-contact with staff. Listed GF on Vegewel; opened via 2025 launch. Raised from 'ask' to 'options' on the Japanese-first discovery pass 2026-07-02.
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.hours_raw: Wed–Mon 11:00–20:00; closed Tue
places.notes: All-vegan restaurant & sweets near Tofuku-ji (between Kyoto Stn & Fushimi). Margherita pizza, teriyaki 'chicken', soy pilaf, ramen, treats.

chef_bio.background: MIMI was opened in 2025 by Kyozen Co., Ltd., a Kyoto company led by representative director Motoki Mimino, in the Tofukuji area within walking distance of Fushimi Inari. The name plays on the Japanese 'fukumimi' (lucky ears), the idea of bringing good fortune and good news to diners. The founding vision is 'food barrier-free' dining: a place where vegans, people with allergies, the health-conscious, and people from every dietary background can all sit at the same table and eat with peace of mind. The dining room is built around natural materials with a modern Japanese feel, garden views, and a mix of counter seats, tables, and a private space.
chef_bio.philosophy: Chef Komura describes the cooking as drawing out the maximum potential of carefully selected ingredients, especially seasonal vegetables from Kyoto, and pursuing the depth and possibilities of vegan cuisine to nourish both body and spirit. The restaurant prioritizes organic and pesticide-free Kyoto vegetables and traditional plant-based ingredients, aiming to connect local producers with diners and support sustainable agriculture.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based versions of comforting, familiar food. The signature is the 'King's Margherita' pizza, built on a hand-stretched dough made from a Hokkaido specialty wheat (crisp outside, chewy inside), topped with a custom-made vegan mozzarella and fresh basil. The kitchen is also known for its sweets, especially the red-bean butter scone pairing smooth vegan butter with house-made red bean paste, plus dishes like hayashi rice, pasta, and a teriyaki 'chicken'-style plate. Everything is 100% plant-based, with no meat or dairy.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name is a play on 'fukumimi' (lucky ears) - the idea of delivering good, delicious news to diners' ears.

Founder Motoki Mimino frames MIMI as a commitment to meeting diverse dietary needs so that all people can enjoy a meal with peace of mind and warmth - the 'food barrier-free' table where no one is left out.

Chef Komura says he pursues the depth and possibilities of vegan cuisine by drawing out the full potential of carefully selected seasonal Kyoto vegetables, delivering colorful dishes that nourish body and spirit.
The signature King's Margherita is built on hand-stretched dough made from a Hokkaido specialty wheat and topped with a house-developed vegan mozzarella and fresh basil.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-forward kitchen: the signature pizza uses a hand-stretched dough made from Hokkaido wheat, and the menu includes pasta and baked goods. Wheat flour is handled regularly, so even where a gluten-free dish exists, airborne flour and shared prep surfaces are a real cross-contact concern. No source documents how (or whether) gluten-free items are isolated from wheat handling. Celiac diners should not assume it is celiac-safe and must confirm preparation directly with staff.
A separate gluten-free menu is listed as available, but no details are published on dedicated equipment, separate prep areas, or cross-contamination controls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source specifies whether the soy sauce or seasonings used are wheat-free. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so savory dishes (e.g., the hayashi rice or teriyaki-style plate) should be assumed to contain wheat unless staff confirm a wheat-free soy sauce or tamari is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is 100% plant-based with no meat or dairy on site, which substantially lowers the risk of animal-product cross-contact for vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant's stated concept is 'food barrier-free' dining for people with allergies and varied dietary needs, and it advertises a gluten-free menu, suggesting allergy awareness. However, no concrete allergen-handling or cross-contamination protocols are published; confirm specifics with staff.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen - all dishes are 100% plant-based with no meat or dairy, and an English menu is available.
A dedicated gluten-free menu is offered, and the restaurant explicitly markets itself to diners with allergies and dietary restrictions.
Ingredient sourcing emphasizes organic and pesticide-free Kyoto vegetables, which the restaurant highlights as a core priority.

noodles.notes: No ramen or documented ramen-style noodle bowl is served. The kitchen is a plant-based Western-leaning operation (hand-stretched pizza dough, pasta, hayashi rice), so no ramen noodle specification exists.

gf.note: The shop markets itself as food barrier-free and lists a dedicated gluten-free menu, but no gluten-free ramen exists here because no ramen is served. Confirm specific GF dishes on arrival.

vegan.note: Fully plant-based kitchen with no animal products, dairy, or fish dashi anywhere on the menu. However this applies to its Western-style fare (pizza, pasta, hayashi rice), not to a ramen bowl, which it does not offer.

ramen.pantheon: This is a plant-based Japanese-Western kitchen near Tofukuji rather than a ramen house, so it sits outside the ramen world entirely. Its soul is hand-stretched margherita pizza and comforting hayashi rice made without any animal products, not a simmered soup.

places.highlight: This is a plant-based Japanese-Western kitchen near Tofukuji rather than a ramen house, so it sits outside the ramen world entirely. Its soul is hand-stretched margherita pizza and comforting hayashi rice made without any animal products, not a simmered soup.

places.name: Sunny Place
places.gf_detail: GF on request with ~1-day notice
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.hours_raw: Hours vary — call ahead
places.notes: Tiny owner-run vegan cafe near Demachiyanagi (Tanaka). Obanzai/curry lunch sets with refillable rice & curry. GF accommodated on notice.

chef_bio.background: Sunny Place is one of Kyoto's longest-running plant-based kitchens, serving since 2001. It began in the Kitayama district in the north of the city and was later carried on from mother to daughter, with Yumi now at the stove. She does the cooking herself in a tiny, cosy, wood-trimmed countryside-style room near Demachiyanagi, and her guiding wish is simple: to serve food made from safe, trustworthy ingredients that proves vegan cooking is far more than just a plate of vegetables.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yumi sources with real conviction, building the menu around organic and pesticide-free vegetables (much of it from a Kyoto-area organic grower) and pesticide-free rice from Hiroshima. She wants every diner, vegan or not, to leave satisfied and surprised by how generous plant-based cooking can be.
chef_bio.specialty: A daily lunch plate built around her signature tempeh tempura, with three changing seasonal side dishes, a salad, enzyme brown rice, and miso soup. The kitchen is also known for crispy fried mock 'chicken' karaage, a tempeh teriyaki rice bowl, and a hearty millet/tempeh burger. Drinks lean just as carefully sourced: domestically roasted organic coffee ground to order, roasted-grain (brown rice) coffee, soy-milk chai, and seasonal smoothies.

anecdotes.text: The cafe has been going since 2001 and was passed from mother to daughter; Yumi, the current owner, has run the kitchen herself with an unwavering focus on safe, organic ingredients.
Beyond the daily lunch, Yumi teaches international vegan cooking classes roughly every couple of months and caters plant-based food for events.

English-speaking visitors describe food 'prepared by a lovely woman' who had been vegan for over 20 years, who explained dishes to non-Japanese guests and happily recommended other vegan spots around the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The owner can accommodate gluten-free diners when given about a day's notice ahead of your visit, since she cooks everything herself and can plan around it. No dedicated gluten-free prep area or formal cross-contamination protocol is documented, so celiac diners should confirm details directly when they call to arrange it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information is available on whether the soy sauce used is wheat-free. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and tempeh/teriyaki and miso-based dishes here may involve it, so celiac diners should ask specifically about the soy sauce and seasonings when arranging a gluten-free meal.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fully plant-based, so animal-product cross-contact is not a concern; there is no meat, fish, dairy, or egg cooking on site to mix with vegan dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner cooks all dishes herself and speaks English, and reviewers note she explains menu items to non-Japanese guests, which makes it easy to discuss ingredients and allergies in person.

positives.text: Entirely vegan menu, so plant-based diners can order anything without worrying about hidden animal products.
Strong emphasis on organic and pesticide-free vegetables and pesticide-free rice, reflecting the owner's focus on safe, trustworthy ingredients.
A single owner-cook who handles the whole kitchen and speaks English makes it straightforward to communicate dietary needs and arrange gluten-free meals in advance.

places.name: hale
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan set lunch
places.hours_raw: Thu–Sun 12:00–14:30; closed Mon–Wed
places.notes: Calm vegan set-lunch spot steps from Nishiki Market. Tofu dishes, yuba, seasonal sides in a traditional setting with a small garden.

chef_bio.background: hale occupies a quiet Kyoto machiya townhouse tucked down a narrow alley off the bustle of Nishiki Market. The building is the family home of the owner's grandmother, who ran a kombu (kelp) shop here until sometime before World War II. The owner, Mr. Kondo, converted that space into a vegetarian dining room, opening hale as a place where the calm of an old townhouse and its small Japanese garden let you forget the crowded market just outside.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kondo's aim is food so good that people enjoy it without even realizing it is vegan: 'The ideal is when ordinary people eat it without realizing it's vegan.' His guiding principle is 'as much as possible' — as safe, as local, and as pesticide-free as he can manage — favoring local Kyoto produce and long-established neighborhood suppliers over rigid rules.
chef_bio.specialty: hale is a lunch-only restaurant built around Nishiki Market ingredients, especially yuba (tofu skin) and nama-fu (fresh wheat-gluten cake), alongside vegetables from the Kyoto area. The signature dish is a yuba donburi (湯葉丼): a bowl of rice topped with yuba and mushrooms in a golden, lightly sweet ankake (starchy) sauce, served with side dishes such as okara and leafy greens. Once a daily special, customer demand turned it into the centerpiece of the set lunch.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sits in the townhouse that was the home of the owner's grandmother, who ran a kombu (kelp) shop here until before World War II.
Owner Mr. Kondo opened hale as a vegetarian restaurant about 14 years before 2018, and says his ideal is food so natural that ordinary diners don't realize it is vegan.
hale sources from long-established Kyoto purveyors, including a tofu shop with over a century of history and Ippodo tea (founded 1717).

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe restaurant. The kitchen openly works with wheat-based ingredients — nama-fu (fresh wheat-gluten cake) is a featured ingredient — so gluten is present throughout the cooking, and there is no indication of separate gluten-free preparation.
The set menu is fixed and changes daily, with no published gluten-free option, so a celiac diner cannot rely on a safe alternative being available.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cooking is built on traditional Japanese dashi and seasoning, and the signature yuba donburi uses a Japanese-style ankake sauce. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so these dishes should be assumed to contain wheat-based soy sauce unless the kitchen confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: hale is a fully vegetarian/vegan kitchen with no animal products used, which greatly reduces the risk of animal cross-contact for vegan diners. The dashi is plant-based (kombu) rather than fish-based.

positives.text: The entire menu is vegan/vegetarian with no animal products, so vegan diners can order freely without parsing ingredients.
The owner emphasizes safe, local, pesticide-free ingredients sourced from trusted Kyoto suppliers, reflecting genuine care about what goes into the food.

places.name: Pelgag
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian/vegan, made to order
places.notes: Tiny owner-cooked spot (Nakagyo) with global-fusion vegan curry & 'Bali' noodles, fresh herbs and pickles. Books to draw while you wait.

chef_bio.background: PELGAG is a small, one-person operation tucked onto the 3rd and 4th floors of a building in downtown Nakagyo, run from open to close by its owner, Mr. Hashimoto, who cooks everything from scratch himself. He opened the cafe in 2018 after a stint cooking at a vegan restaurant, and built it around a simple trio of ideas: vegetables, a drink, and a book. The space reflects that spirit. It is intimate and eclectic, lined with books and even stocked with sketchbooks and markers so guests can draw while they wait. Hashimoto is upfront that he is not strictly vegan himself; what he loves is vegetables, and he wanted a place where, in his words, it doesn't matter who you are, where you are from, or what you eat. The name PELGAG itself comes from a song by an artist he likes, chosen simply because it had a nice ring to it.
chef_bio.philosophy: A welcoming community table open to everyone regardless of diet, pairing spice- and herb-forward, vegetable-centered cooking with a relaxed, book-filled space.
chef_bio.specialty: Known for a daily rotating plate built around vegetables, served with turmeric rice, a coconut-milk or beet soup, and several side dishes that each get their own distinct seasoning, an approach Hashimoto takes further than most. Signature items include falafel balls with herb tartar sauce, tempeh in peanut sauce, and fried vegetables like broccoli, eggplant and carrot. The vegetable curry and a vegan chocolate cake with rum and Cuban mint are also favorites.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's name comes from a song by a musician Hashimoto enjoys; he picked it simply because it had a nice ring to it.
Hashimoto, who cooked at a vegan restaurant before opening his own, says he is not vegan himself but loves vegetables and wanted a place where it doesn't matter what you eat.
Each side dish on the daily plate is given its own unique seasoning, an uncommon touch.
A one-person operation where the owner makes everything from scratch, in a small book-filled room with sketchbooks and markers out for guests.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No specific gluten-free or cross-contamination information is documented. The kitchen is a one-person operation cooking many dishes from scratch in a small space, so shared equipment is likely; celiac diners should confirm directly before relying on any dish.
Several menu items are wheat-based or fried (falafel balls, fried tempeh and vegetables, noodle dishes), so a strictly gluten-free meal is not something the sources confirm.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information is available on whether the soy sauce or seasonings used are wheat-free. As with most Japanese kitchens, standard soy sauce typically contains wheat; ask the owner about specific sauces if you are celiac.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Despite an existing note of 'fully vegan,' sources indicate PELGAG is a mixed menu: it serves non-vegan items (including meat) alongside clearly labeled vegan options, all prepared in the same small one-person kitchen. Vegan dishes are available but shared prep means some cross-contact is possible.
Vegan options are clearly marked on the menu (an English menu is available), so you can identify plant-based dishes, but the venue is not an all-vegan kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The listing advises guests to tell staff about vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies when choosing a dish, suggesting the owner will accommodate on request.
Reviewers describe the owner as friendly and hands-on, but there are no specific reports about formal allergy protocols.

positives.text: Vegan dishes are clearly labeled and an English menu is available, making it easy to identify plant-based options.
The owner cooks everything from scratch and is approachable, so questions about ingredients can be raised directly.
The staff invite diners to share vegan and allergy needs before ordering so a suitable dish can be chosen.

places.name: Vegan Cafe (Philosopher's Path)
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan (limited menu, e.g. tofu bowl)
places.hours_raw: Hours vary — check (near Philosopher's Path)
places.notes: Small couple-run vegan cafe on the Philosopher's Path (Sakyo). Tofu donburi, mushroom udon, grape juice. One meal per person.

chef_bio.background: This light-filled little vegan dining room sits on the second floor above an art gallery in the quiet Jodoji neighborhood, a short stroll from Ginkaku-ji and the Philosopher's Path. It is the personal project of owner-chef Mr. Katou, who had spent more than a decade cooking plant-based food, including a stint at the well-known Kyoto yuba and vegan kitchen Hale, before opening his own place here. By his own telling he is not strictly vegan himself; he simply fell in love with vegetables and cooking and wanted to give more people a welcoming spot to eat vegetable-centered meals. Much of his produce comes from around Kyoto, with extra fresh vegetables grown on his sister's farm in Mie Prefecture, and the menu actually tells you where each ingredient came from.
chef_bio.philosophy: Mr. Katou deliberately builds each meal around contrasting textures and tastes so the experience stays engaging from bite to bite, leans on local and seasonal produce, and is open about sourcing so guests know exactly where their food comes from. His broader aim is simply to create more places where people can enjoy good vegetable-based cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Contemporary vegan Japanese cooking served as a colorful set plate built from many small dishes, with a rotating main course that changes roughly every month and a half depending on the season and the vegetables on hand. Diners single out his playful range of textures and flavors in a single meal, with dishes like freeze-dried (koya) tofu fried and dressed in a spring-onion-and-amazake sauce, and a chilled soy-milk cream soup of burdock and enoki mushroom.

anecdotes.text: Before opening this cafe, Mr. Katou cooked at the established Kyoto vegan kitchen Hale and had been making plant-based dishes for over ten years; he describes himself as not fully vegan but always drawn to vegetables and cooking, wanting to give people more places to eat vegetable-based meals.

The main dish changes about every month and a half based on the season and the vegetables he has, and the menu notes where each ingredient is sourced, including produce from his sister's farm in Mie Prefecture.
Guests describe a colorful lunch plate full of small dishes, including freeze-dried tofu fried with a spring-onion and amazake sauce and a cold soy-milk cream soup of burdock and enoki mushroom.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: This is contemporary vegan Japanese cooking, which typically relies on soy sauce, miso and amazake-style seasonings. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation here, so celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not assume dishes are wheat-free and should confirm each item directly with the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fully plant-based and describes itself as serving contemporary vegan Japanese food, so the risk of animal-product cross-contact is inherently low.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Listings recommend telling the staff about your vegan needs and any potential allergies before ordering.
Staff are repeatedly described as friendly and accommodating; one diner reported they kindly offered to make a plate of rice balls with pickled vegetables even after regular service hours.

positives.text: Fully vegan menu, so all dishes are free of meat, fish, dashi, dairy and egg.
The menu states where ingredients are sourced, which makes it easier to ask about specific components.
Friendly, accommodating staff who are willing to adapt for guests.

places.name: Cafe Phalam
places.gf_detail: GF matcha cake; ask
places.vegan_detail: vegan options (Buddha/smoothie bowls)
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri & Sun 9:30–17:00; closed Sat
places.notes: Cozy plant-leaning cafe near Nijo/Nishinokyo. Buddha bowls, smoothie bowls, soy chai, GF matcha cake. Japan-meets-health-food.

chef_bio.origin: Nijo, Kyoto — a short walk north on Senbon-dori from JR Nijo Station
chef_bio.background: Cafe Phalam has been a quiet fixture near Nijo Station for around twelve years. Its name comes from the Sanskrit word phalam (फलम्), meaning "fruit" — a fitting choice for a kitchen built around plants, produce, and where ingredients come from.
