The space is roomy and warm, with wooden interiors and low tatami-style kneeling tables alongside regular seating. It has enough character that it was used as a filming location for the 2020 Japanese film 'Beyond the Infinite Two Minutes' (Droste no Hate de Bokura). Staff are comfortable in English and there's an English menu, which makes it an easy landing spot for visitors.
chef_bio.philosophy: A thoughtful approach to sourcing — paying attention to ingredient origin, the producers behind them, fair trade, traditional methods, and self-grown ingredients — carried through from the food to the drinks and desserts.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based cafe cooking and specialty coffee. The kitchen is known for its generous, colorful vegan plates — a morning/lunch plate that fans out lentil salad, beet-pink potato salad, shiso-seasoned hijiki, carrot rapé, plant-based meatballs, steamed vegetables and fruit, with homemade apple jam and vegan butter, plus a sweet potato potage and plant-based yogurt with granola. On the sweets side it's beloved for vegan cheesecake with bright lemon notes, carrot cake, brownies, scones, and muffins — the matcha muffin in particular gets singled out by regulars. Drinks include an organic matcha latte and specialty coffee, with soy milk available.

anecdotes.text: The name Phalam comes from the Sanskrit word for "fruit," and the cafe centers its menu on plant-based food and carefully sourced, organic and fair-trade ingredients.
The cafe's distinctive interior was used as a filming location for the 2020 Japanese film 'Beyond the Infinite Two Minutes' (Droste no Hate de Bokura).

A visitor described the generous morning vegan plate — lentil salad, beet-pink potato salad, shiso hijiki, carrot rapé, plant-based meatballs, steamed vegetables, fruit, homemade apple jam and vegan butter, sweet potato potage, and plant-based yogurt — praising the vegan butter as rich enough to rival the real thing.
Diners call the staff genuinely vegan-aware, with vegan dishes central to the menu, and single out the matcha muffin and matcha latte as standouts.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source documents gluten-free preparation, a separate prep area, or cross-contamination controls. This is a cafe whose baked goods include wheat-flour items (for example whole-wheat cinnamon rolls and muffins), so flour is present and airborne in the kitchen. A celiac diner should not assume any item is gluten-free without confirming directly. The one item flagged as GF (a matcha cake) should be verified with staff before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source specifies whether savory dishes use wheat-containing soy sauce or wheat-free tamari. As with most Japanese kitchens, assume standard soy sauce contains wheat unless staff confirm otherwise. Confirm directly if avoiding gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a vegan-forward kitchen — reviewers describe vegan dishes as central to the menu and staff as genuinely vegan-aware, with plant-based mains, desserts, and soy milk widely available. No source raises vegan cross-contact concerns.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff speak English and there is an English menu, which makes asking about ingredients straightforward. However, no source documents a specific allergy-handling protocol, so allergic diners should describe their needs clearly and confirm individual dishes.

positives.text: Strongly vegan-aware: plant-based dishes are central to the menu and reviewers consistently note knowledgeable, accommodating staff.
Soy milk and plant-based options are readily available for drinks and desserts.
English-speaking staff and an English menu make it easier to communicate dietary needs.

places.name: Fune Ethical Lifestyle
places.gf_detail: confirm (Menu review: 2 gluten-free meal options on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: vegan
places.hours_raw: Thu–Tue 12:00–18:00; closed Wed
places.notes: Zen-modern vegan cafe + ethical shop (Nakagyo). Rice-porridge lunch set, cacao tea, wasabi-chocolate dessert. Small — booking advised.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (Gosho-minami / Imperial Palace area, Nakagyo)
chef_bio.background: Fune sits in a traditional Kyoto townhouse just south of the Imperial Palace, and it is more than a cafe — it is a small "ethical lifestyle" house spread over two floors. The ground floor holds the cafe alongside a retail corner of hemp clothing, upcycled-kimono pieces and natural goods, while the second floor is a tatami-floored open room used for yoga, bodywork sessions, workshops and small gallery shows. The place is run by the maker Hinode Products, a company perhaps best known outside food circles for producing a natural scallop-shell cleaning powder, and Fune is the home where they put their whole "eat, wear, live gently on the earth" idea under one roof. Even the name carries the intention: written 普廻, it joins the characters for "universally" and "to circulate," expressing a wish to cherish every living thing in nature without distinction — a phrase the owners link to the Buddhist idea of giving equally to all beings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking follows a clear principle: keep food close to how the earth gives it. Many dishes are prepared raw and unheated so that vitamins, minerals and enzymes are not destroyed, and the kitchen leans heavily on fermentation and lactic-acid cultures for gut health. The food is fully plant-based — no eggs, no dairy — and the sweets avoid wheat and added sugar entirely. As the cafe frames it, the meal is meant to "build a future body" by caring for the gut and calming body and mind, an extension of the house ethos of circulating kindness through everything you eat, wear and use.
chef_bio.specialty: Fune is known for its 未来粥 ("Future Porridge") set: a savory five-grain brown-rice okayu rich in lactic-acid bacteria, served with seasonal organic vegetables, house-made sesame tofu, roasted natto crackers, fermented vegetables and house miso and sauces. The other signature is the 御所の杜 range of raw vegan sweets — fruit "terrazzo," a "forest medley" and raw chocolate built on cashews — made without eggs, dairy, wheat or refined sugar. Matcha lattes, kombucha and organic teas and coffee round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's name, 普廻 (Fune), is built from the characters for "universally" and "to circulate," chosen to express cherishing all things in nature without discrimination — the owners tie it to the Buddhist notion of distributing kindness equally to all living beings.

The signature 未来粥 ("Future Porridge") is built on organic rice and live lactic-acid bacteria and is described as nourishment to create "a future body," served with sesame tofu, natto crackers and fermented vegetables.
The raw vegan sweets are made unheated specifically to preserve vitamins, minerals and enzymes, and are free of eggs, dairy, wheat and added sugar.
A visitor described the menu as "very experimental for my tastebuds, but felt very healthy and overall interesting experience."

gf_cross_contamination.text: No documented gluten-free protocol or cross-contamination procedure was found. The raw vegan sweets are explicitly made without wheat, but the savory menu (porridge sets) includes grains and items like natto crackers, so a strict celiac diner should confirm wheat-free status and shared-surface handling dish by dish directly with staff.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cafe makes its own miso and sauces and uses fermented ingredients, but no source specifies whether the seasonings (including any soy sauce/tamari) are wheat-free. Since most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, a celiac diner should ask which house seasonings are used on the porridge and savory sides.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The food and beverages are described as 100% vegan, with no eggs or dairy used in the kitchen, which lowers the risk of animal-product cross-contact in the food.
Note that the attached retail corner has been reported as vegetarian rather than strictly vegan, so check individual products if buying goods.

positives.text: The kitchen is fully plant-based (no eggs, no dairy), which is reassuring for vegan diners.
The 御所の杜 raw vegan sweets are explicitly free of eggs, dairy, wheat and added sugar.
The menu lists gluten-free and sugar-free options, including the wheat-free raw desserts, giving GF diners documented choices to start a conversation from.

places.name: MERCY Vegan Bakery
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: all vegan bakery
places.hours_raw: Wed–Mon 7:00–15:00; closed Tue
places.notes: All-vegan bakery near Kyoto Station (Minami, hotel lobby). Sweet & savoury breads, vegan curry bread, brownies. Mostly takeaway.

chef_bio.background: MERCY Vegan Bakery opened on January 24, 2026 in the Kyoto Station area, tucked into Hotel Elite Kyoto Station a short walk from JR Kyoto Station and Kujo Station. It is the bakery sibling of MERCY Vegan Factory, the much-loved fully plant-based kitchen in the Tanimachi neighborhood of Osaka that became a magnet for vegan travelers. Both are run by Easy Link, an Osaka-rooted company that has spent years exploring how far plant-based cooking can go without any animal-derived ingredients. The bakery was the team's answer to a simple wish: that people eating gently, or living with dietary restrictions, should still get to enjoy the everyday pleasure of fresh bread. Everything is baked fresh each morning, with more than 30 kinds of bread on the shelves daily, and the shop sells until it sells out.
chef_bio.philosophy: Plant-based without compromise: every bread is made with no animal-derived ingredients at all. The aim is gentle, body-friendly flavors that fit into daily life, made from carefully selected ingredients, so that dietary restrictions never mean giving up the joy of good bread.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked, 100% plant-based breads, around 30 varieties a day, spanning hearty meal breads, savory deli breads, doughnuts and sweet pastries. Standouts that diners gravitate to include the Mushroom Cream Gratin bread (soy-cream gratin over mushrooms), an Eggplant and Tomato Pizza Bread with vegan cheese, a Potato Yuzu-Pepper Mayo bread, a Daily Vegetable Cutlet Burger built on seasonal vegetables, and a fragrant Cinnamon Doughnut. The shop is also known for sweet classics like anpan and melon bread, and a soy-milk vegan pudding.

anecdotes.text: The bakery grew out of MERCY Vegan Factory in Osaka, a fully vegan restaurant that earned a devoted following among plant-based diners before the Kyoto bakery opened.
Every morning the team bakes more than 30 kinds of bread fresh, and the shop simply closes for the day once the bread sells out.
A monthly bread ranking from the shop put the Mushroom Cream Gratin bread at number one, ahead of the Eggplant and Tomato Pizza Bread and a Potato Yuzu-Pepper Mayo bread.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-flour bakery. The shop describes its breads as made from carefully selected ingredients centered on domestic wheat flour, so gluten is present throughout the kitchen and gluten-free or celiac-safe options should not be assumed. Flour dust and shared baking surfaces make cross-contamination effectively unavoidable in a working bakery of this kind.
No gluten-free product line or dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented in any source. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should confirm directly with the shop before eating.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Several breads lean on soy-based ingredients (soy cream, soy milk pudding, vegan mayo), so soy is a common allergen here even though it is a plant-based shop. No information is published on whether any seasonings contain wheat-based soy sauce; confirm with staff if you avoid wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire bakery is 100% plant-based, with no animal-derived ingredients, no honey, and no refined white sugar processed with bone char, so there is no animal product on site to cross-contaminate vegan items.
Because the shop is fully vegan, there are no eggs, dairy, or animal products used anywhere, removing the usual cross-contact risks for vegan diners.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen with a clear ingredient ethos: no animal products, no honey, and no bone-char-processed white sugar, which makes it a low-worry choice for strict vegan diners.
Bread is baked fresh daily in small batches and sold same-day, so ingredients and turnover are transparent and current.

places.name: Nicot & Mum's (vegan donuts)
places.gf_detail: GF sweets/cookies available
places.vegan_detail: vegan donut options
places.hours_raw: Fri 12:00–18:00; Sat 11:00–18:00 (limited days)
places.notes: Vegan donut & sweets shop (Nakagyo). Vegan donuts plus rice-flour GF cookies/sweets. Limited opening days — check before visiting.

chef_bio.background: nicotto & mam began with a parent making egg-free donuts at home for their own child, who had an egg allergy. That simple act of love grew into a tiny doughnut shop near Karasuma Oike in Nakagyo Ward, where every doughnut is still made without eggs or dairy so that children and grown-ups with allergies can enjoy them too. The shop's name captures the image at the heart of it all: a child laughing and saying 'oichii!' while devouring a whole doughnut, and a mother smiling warmly as she watches. They bake many kinds in small batches, so there's usually something new to discover on each visit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Beyond simply selling doughnuts, the makers say they want to deliver love and a moment of happiness, hoping that everyone who eats one smiles, even for just a moment. Allergy-conscious recipes and carefully chosen, mostly domestic ingredients are central to that aim.
chef_bio.specialty: Egg-free, dairy-free doughnuts with a signature fluffy-yet-chewy (fuwafuwa-mochimochi) texture, made from 100% domestic Japanese wheat (Hokkaido flour), the shop's own natural yeast, and rice koji sourced from Kyoto's Sasaki Sake Brewery. Each doughnut takes up to about 12 hours from mixing to shelf, fermenting slowly at low temperature for a deep, gently sweet flavor that isn't overly sugary. Regular flavors include plain, black-bean kinako, chocolate, and strawberry, alongside rotating seasonal varieties.

anecdotes.text: The shop was founded by a parent who first created egg-free donuts for their own child with an egg allergy, and the name 'nicotto & mam' comes from the image of a child laughing while eating a whole doughnut and a mother smiling as she watches.

Doughnuts take up to about 12 hours from start to shelf, using the shop's own natural yeast and slow, low-temperature fermentation, plus rice koji from Kyoto's Sasaki Sake Brewery and 100% domestic (Hokkaido) wheat, for a natural sweetness and a fluffy, chewy texture.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: doughnut.jp (official) confirms the egg-/dairy-free founding story for an allergic child, the name's meaning (a child laughing 'oichii' while a mother smiles), 100% domestic Hokkaido wheat, the shop's own natural yeast, Sasaki Sake Brewery rice koji, up-to-12-hour low-temperature fermentation, and the regular flavors (plain, black-bean kinako, chocolate, strawberry). The Karasuma Oike/Nakagyo location is given as a 2-minute walk from Karasuma Oike Station exit 1.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The doughnuts are made from 100% domestic Japanese wheat flour, so the standard doughnuts are NOT gluten-free and are not safe for celiac diners. The shop's own site describes the dough as made with domestic wheat, natural yeast, and rice koji.

A separate gluten-free menu is listed as available, but because wheat flour is the base of the regular doughnuts and is handled throughout the kitchen, cross-contamination is a real risk for celiac diners. No dedicated gluten-free prep area or fryer is documented — confirm directly with the shop before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All doughnut dough is made without eggs and without dairy, using natural yeast and rice koji, so the doughnuts are suitable for vegan diners. This is core to the shop's recipe rather than a special-request item.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop was started specifically to make egg-free, dairy-free doughnuts for a child with an egg allergy, so allergy-awareness is built into the business. Even so, no published details confirm how they handle gluten or other allergens in the kitchen — ask staff directly about your specific allergy.

positives.text: Egg-free and dairy-free across the doughnut range, which removes two of the most common allergens and makes the doughnuts vegan-friendly by default.
Ingredients are carefully chosen and mostly domestic (Hokkaido wheat, the shop's own natural yeast, rice koji from a Kyoto sake brewery), reflecting an ingredient-conscious, allergy-aware approach.
A gluten-free menu and low-sugar options are listed alongside the vegan/vegetarian offerings, indicating they cater to multiple dietary needs.

places.name: Vegan Ramen UZU Kyoto
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: all vegan
places.notes: Design-forward all-vegan ramen (Nijo) by teamLab's restaurant arm. Spicy miso ramen, vegan sushi, soy ice cream; digital-art communal table.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Gosho-Minami, Nakagyo Ward, near Kyoto City Hall), opened 2020
chef_bio.background: Vegan Ramen UZU Kyoto opened in 2020 in the quiet Gosho-Minami neighborhood near Kyoto City Hall, born from a collaboration with teamLab, the art collective known for its immersive digital installations. The idea grew out of teamLab's interest in how people might live more thoughtfully alongside the natural world, and they chose vegan food, with its long roots in Japan, as the way to express that. The concept later spawned a Tokyo location inside teamLab Planets in Toyosu. Diners sit at a polished black table built around teamLab's artwork 'Reversible Rotation - Black in White,' which appears to flow across both the walls and the tabletop, so the meal unfolds inside the artwork itself. The space was made for people to reflect on the simple acts of eating and living.
chef_bio.philosophy: 'Earth-friendly dining' is the guiding mission: showing that plant-based eating can be a refined pleasure rather than a sacrifice, and that the act of eating connects us to the wider world. The kitchen works to minimize food waste. In the Michelin Guide Kyoto-Osaka the restaurant earned a Green Star for its sustainability commitment — the first awarded in the ramen category — alongside a Bib Gourmand.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based ramen made entirely without meat or fish. The signature Vegan Ramen Shoyu is built on a broth of Rausu kelp and Japanese shiitake dashi, deepened with vegetable umami, and finished with a topped tomato that breaks down as you eat to add brightness; a Spicy Miso and Sake Kasu ramen is also offered. Broths are steeped slowly, with kelp, shiitake and vegetables left in water for around 12 hours. The noodles are made from Hokkaido flour blended with organic whole grain flour from the town of Aya in Miyazaki Prefecture. Meals are served as a course.

anecdotes.text: The concept was created by the digital art collective teamLab, whose mission for the restaurant is 'Earth-friendly dining'; the idea of eating ramen while surrounded by art grew from teamLab's belief that eating connects us to the world.
teamLab designed the immersive art installation for the Kyoto branch when it opened in 2020, and the collaboration was a hit that drew long lines.

The signature Vegan Ramen Shoyu uses a Rausu-kelp-and-shiitake dashi enhanced with vegetable umami, with a topped tomato that breaks down during eating to add tang; the restaurant earned a Michelin Green Star and Bib Gourmand.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a gluten-free or celiac-safe restaurant. The ramen noodles are made from wheat (Hokkaido flour blended with organic whole grain flour), so the core dish contains gluten.

No gluten-free menu, dedicated gluten-free preparation area, or cross-contamination protocol is documented in any available source. A celiac diner should not assume any dish is safe without confirming directly with the restaurant.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen is a shoyu (soy sauce) ramen. Most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, which would add gluten on top of the wheat already in the noodles. There is no documentation that a wheat-free tamari is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen and menu are vegan, made without any meat or fish, so there is no animal-product cross-contact concern for vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific allergen-handling procedures or staff allergy accommodations are documented in the available sources. For any allergy beyond animal products, contact the restaurant directly before your visit.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen: all dishes are made without meat or fish, making it a reliable choice for strict vegan and vegetarian diners.
Broths are built from plant-based dashi (Rausu kelp, shiitake mushrooms and vegetables) rather than any animal stock.

noodles.notes: Made from Hokkaido wheat and organic pesticide-free whole-grain flour from Aya, Miyazaki; whole-grain character gives a firm, full-flavored bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Soy Sauce & Fermented Mugwort Ramen (the shoyu bowl on the UZU Signature Course), built on a 12-hour Rausu kelp, shiitake and vegetable broth seasoned with sweet Inoue-brand soy sauce.

gf.note: Fully plant-based kitchen and the kelp/shiitake/vegetable broth carries no animal dashi, but the noodles are wheat-based and the shoyu tare contains wheat, so it is not celiac-safe. Confirm directly if you need strict gluten-free.

vegan.note: The entire restaurant is 100% vegan. The broth is built from Rausu kombu, domestic shiitake and vegetables with no katsuo or pork, and the tare (soy or miso/sake-lees) and oils are plant-based throughout.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original, fully plant-based bowl that rewrites the ramen playbook from Kyoto: instead of pork or chicken, its soul is a 12-hour Rausu-kombu and shiitake dashi whose deep glutamate-rich umami stands in for animal richness. Seasoned with mellow Inoue soy or a sake-lees miso and threaded with vegetable aroma oils, it tastes of refined shojin/Zen vegetarian lineage rather than any single regional gotochi style.

places.highlight: A modern-original, fully plant-based bowl that rewrites the ramen playbook from Kyoto: instead of pork or chicken, its soul is a 12-hour Rausu-kombu and shiitake dashi whose deep glutamate-rich umami stands in for animal richness. Seasoned with mellow Inoue soy or a sake-lees miso and threaded with vegetable aroma oils, it tastes of refined shojin/Zen vegetarian lineage rather than any single regional gotochi style.

places.name: Utsuwa to Cafe Bar Mokumoku
places.gf_detail: GF donuts/sweets; not dedicated (confirm cross-contact)
places.vegan_detail: reported fully vegan
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 8:30–16:00; closed Sun–Mon
places.notes: Vegan cafe/pottery shop near Yasaka Pagoda. GF+vegan tofu donut, lunch sets, parfaits, homemade lemonade. Opens early; tiny.

chef_bio.background: Mokumoku is a small cafe tucked into Kyoto's Higashiyama district, within easy reach of Kiyomizu-dera and Gion. The name pairs 'utsuwa' (うつわ, meaning vessels or pottery) with 'cafe bar,' and that combination is the heart of the place: lunches and sweets are served on handmade artist's pottery that you can also buy from the front of the shop. It is a calm, intimate room with jazz playing and a cosy, welcoming feel, remembered in reviews for food 'made with care and love.' As of September 2025 the kitchen turned fully vegan, doubling as a small art gallery and ceramics shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: A conviction that plant-based and gluten-free food should never mean compromising on taste or quality. They lean on seasonal vegetables dressed with homemade condiments and spices, and present everything on handcrafted pottery, tying the food to a respect for traditional craft.
chef_bio.specialty: Best known for their tofu donuts, which reviewers repeatedly single out as the standout, alongside vegan muffins and scones (a gluten-free lemon muffin is often mentioned) and amazake pudding with strawberry jam. Lunch comes as a plant-based set with rice, an assortment of vegetable dishes, vegan karaage and house-made sauces, plus a vegetable curry. Gluten-free options are marked on the menu.

anecdotes.text: Lunches and sweets are plated on handmade artist's pottery that diners can also purchase from the front of the cafe, blending the meal with a small ceramics shop.
Visitors describe a cosy room with jazz, friendly staff and food 'made with care and love,' calling out the tofu donuts as a gem; the cafe went fully vegan as of September 2025.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gluten-free donuts and bread are offered and praised by GF diners, but no source documents whether they are prepared in a dedicated, gluten-free-only area or fryer, so cross-contact cannot be confirmed. Celiac diners should confirm prep directly with staff.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source specifies whether the soy sauce or seasonings used are gluten-free. As most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, anyone avoiding gluten should ask which sauces are used on the lunch set and vegan karaage.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen has been fully vegan since September 2025, with no animal products on the menu, so the risk of animal-derived cross-contact is low by design. Plant milks (oat and soy) are available for drinks.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as friendly and the food as made with care, and menu items are labelled as vegan/gluten-free, but no specific allergy-handling protocol is documented in the sources.

positives.text: Reviewers consistently praise the gluten-free tofu donuts and gluten-free set as very good.
Fully vegan kitchen with clearly labelled items and seasonal, house-made cooking; the philosophy is that vegan and gluten-free should not mean compromising on taste.

places.name: Shigetsu (Tenryu-ji)
places.gf_detail: shojin; soy sauce may contain wheat (request adaptation) (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Buddhist vegan
places.hours_raw: Daily 11:00–14:00; closed Thu (garden ticket required)
places.notes: Shojin-ryori inside Tenryu-ji's garden, Arashiyama. Multi-course seasonal vegan temple cuisine; seated on tatami. Reserve ahead.

roles: Run by Tenryu-ji (Rinzai Zen head temple)
chef_bio.origin: Arashiyama, Kyoto — inside the grounds of Tenryu-ji Temple, Sagano
chef_bio.background: Shigetsu is run directly by Tenryu-ji, the head temple of the Tenryu-ji branch of Rinzai Zen Buddhism and a UNESCO World Heritage site in Arashiyama. The restaurant sits within the temple grounds, so a meal here comes paired with views over the famous Zen garden, tatami seating, and the cherry blossoms and autumn foliage of the season. The food is shojin-ryori, the Buddhist vegetarian cuisine whose roots reach back to Chinese Zen and which was carried to Japan along with Zen teachings in the thirteenth century. Because it is the temple's own kitchen rather than an independent restaurant, dining at Shigetsu is less about a celebrity chef and more about tasting a centuries-old monastic tradition kept alive in the place where it belongs.
chef_bio.philosophy: In Zen, cooking and eating are themselves forms of spiritual practice. The cuisine is meant to harmonize with Buddhist ideals — creating nourishing, satisfying meals from vegetables, herbs, and wild plants while taking no animal life — and to let each ingredient speak for itself through simple, mindful preparation. Food cuttings are repurposed as compost.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal shojin-ryori served as a single set meal in three sizes — Yuki (Snow), Tsuki (Moon), and Hana (Flower) — built around one soup and five to seven side dishes that change with the season. Everything is entirely plant-based: Kyoto seasonal vegetables, wild mountain herbs, tofu and seaweed, with broths drawn from kombu, shiitake, and soybeans rather than any animal stock. Diners consistently note how colorful and genuinely satisfying the food is, upending the idea that temple vegetarian cooking is plain.

anecdotes.text: Shojin-ryori has its origins in Chinese Zen and was brought to Japan together with Zen teachings in the thirteenth century; at Shigetsu, cooking and eating are treated as forms of spiritual practice that take no animal life.
The kitchen builds its broths entirely from plants — kombu, shiitake mushrooms, and soybeans — alongside Kyoto's seasonal vegetables, wild herbs, tofu and seaweed, with no animal products at all.

The menu comes in three seasonal sizes — Yuki, Tsuki, and Hana — of one soup with five to seven side dishes, and is recognized in the Michelin Guide Kyoto and Osaka with both a Bib Gourmand and a Green Star for sustainable gastronomy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source documents a gluten-free protocol or how shared cookware is handled. As traditional shojin-ryori, dishes are generally light and plant-based, but gluten-containing items are common in this style of cooking — wheat-gluten products such as fu (wheat gluten / nama-fu) and seitan-like ingredients are staples of Buddhist temple cuisine, so individual dishes may contain wheat. Celiac diners should not assume the meal is gluten-free and must confirm dish by dish with staff.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths are made from kombu, shiitake, and soybeans, and soy-based seasonings are typical of shojin-ryori. Most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) is brewed with wheat, so seasoning here likely contains wheat unless a wheat-free tamari is specifically used. No source confirms a wheat-free soy sauce, so celiac diners should request an adaptation and confirm directly before dining.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Multiple sources confirm the cuisine uses no animal products whatsoever — dashi is drawn from kombu, shiitake, and soybeans rather than fish or meat — so the menu is fully plant-based and well suited to vegans.

As a Buddhist vegetarian (shojin-ryori) kitchen, the entire operation observes the rule that no animal products are used, lowering the risk of animal-derived cross-contact compared with a conventional restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No reviews or sources describe how staff handle specific allergy requests. The set menus change seasonally and reservations are required in advance, so diners with allergies (especially wheat/gluten) should call ahead to ask whether the meal can be adapted.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based, animal-product-free shojin-ryori with plant-based dashi (kombu, shiitake, soybean) — a strong baseline for vegan diners.
Reservations are required in advance, which gives diners a natural opportunity to discuss dietary needs before the visit.

places.name: Ajiro
places.gf_detail: shojin; request GF adaptation
places.vegan_detail: Buddhist vegan
places.hours_raw: Thu–Tue 11:00–19:00; closed Wed (reservation)
places.notes: Michelin-listed shojin-ryori near Myoshin-ji (Hanazono). Refined vegan course (yuba etc.). Reservation required.

roles: Official shojin-ryori purveyor to Myoshin-ji temple
chef_bio.background: Ajiro has been the official purveyor of shojin-ryori to Myoshin-ji, the head temple of the Rinzai Zen sect, for more than half a century. Founded in 1962 (Showa 37) by Mineo Yoshitaka — who trained in Myoshin-ji's own kitchen, learning both its cooking and its Buddhist ceremonial practice — the house has spent generations cooking the vegetarian Buddhist meals served at the temple's observances, memorial rites and ceremonies. That long temple connection shapes everything on the table: no animal products at all, and a menu built around the vegetables, yuba, tofu and fu (wheat gluten) that define Kyoto's Buddhist kitchen. The cooking faithfully follows the old shojin discipline of the 'five tastes, five cooking methods and five colors,' using each ingredient fully and in season. Sitting at the gate of Myoshin-ji, it is the kind of place pilgrims and temple-goers have relied on for ceremonial meals for decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house takes its name from the ajiro-gasa, the woven traveling hat worn by Zen monks, and the proprietor frames the cooking as a humble, earnest spiritual practice rather than a luxury. Its stated principles are to use seasonal ingredients, to make the fullest use of each material, and to put one's heart into the cooking. The food is meant to be gentle on the body and made with sincere hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Multi-course shojin-ryori built on Kyoto's temple staples, presented as a seasonal kaiseki-style meal. The courses showcase yuba (tofu skin), alongside delicately seasoned tofu preparations such as hirousu (ganmodoki-style tofu dumplings studded with ginkgo nuts and other vegetables). The house is also known for a morning rice porridge (asagayu) experience tied to temple visits, served thick and piping hot with refills, and for seasonal vegetable courses arranged for color as much as flavor.

anecdotes.text: Ajiro has served as the official shojin-ryori purveyor to Myoshin-ji, the Rinzai Zen sect's head temple, since its founding in 1962, cooking for the temple's ceremonies and memorial services.
The restaurant's name refers to the ajiro hat worn by traveling Zen monks; the proprietor frames the cooking as humble, earnest spiritual practice.

Diners describe the morning porridge as thick and piping hot with refills, and praise the light, gentle hirousu tofu dish made with ginkgo nuts, enjoyed after viewing the weeping cherry blossoms at Myoshin-ji's Taizoin sub-temple.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ajiro-s.co.jp/about (official) confirms the 1962 (Showa 37) founding, founder Mineo Yoshitaka's training in Myoshin-ji's kitchen, the Myoshin-ji purveyor role, the ajiro-gasa name origin, and the three cooking principles (seasonal ingredients, full use of materials, putting one's heart in). A tabelog review corroborates the asagayu porridge and hirousu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is shojin-ryori, which is fully vegetarian/vegan but NOT inherently gluten-free. Fu (wheat gluten) is a defining ingredient of Kyoto temple cuisine and appears across shojin courses, so the standard menu is not celiac-safe and a dedicated gluten-free preparation would have to be arranged in advance.

Courses are built around yuba, tofu and seasonal vegetables; while some items may be naturally wheat-free, no gluten-free protocol or allergen labeling is published, so cross-contact in a wheat-gluten kitchen should be assumed unless confirmed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shojin-ryori relies heavily on soy sauce and seasoned broths. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so dishes should be assumed to contain wheat-derived seasonings unless the kitchen confirms a wheat-free soy sauce is used. No source specifies a wheat-free soy sauce here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: As temple shojin-ryori, the cooking uses no animal products at all and relies on plant-based dashi (kombu/shiitake) rather than fish stock, making it well suited to vegans. This reflects the cuisine's Buddhist discipline rather than a separately certified vegan kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen-handling policy, allergy menu, or cross-contamination procedure was found. The restaurant is reservation-only and does accommodate custom arrangements by request, so allergy needs should be discussed directly when booking.

positives.text: Fully vegetarian/vegan Buddhist cuisine with no animal products, light seasoning, and dishes described as gentle and easy on the stomach.
Reservation-only with the menu changing monthly and custom arrangements available by request, which gives room to communicate dietary needs ahead of the visit.

places.name: Izusen (Daitoku-ji, Daiji-in)
places.gf_detail: shojin; request adaptation
places.vegan_detail: Buddhist vegetarian/vegan
places.notes: Shojin-ryori in the Daitoku-ji temple complex (Kita). Courses served in nested red lacquer bowls; beautiful zen setting.

chef_bio.background: Izusen is a long-established Kyoto purveyor of shojin-ryori (Buddhist temple cuisine) whose roots reach back roughly 150 years, to its founding around 1868. Its Daiji-in branch sits inside the grounds of Daitoku-ji, the head temple of the Rinzai Zen school, where it has served visitors since 1963. Diners are seated on tatami in a sub-temple setting, with garden views accompanying the meal. The food is entirely plant-based: by Buddhist precept it uses no meat and no seafood, drawing only on grains, legumes, vegetables, seaweed, tofu and Kyoto's prized yuba (the delicate skin lifted from simmering soy milk).
chef_bio.philosophy: Izusen frames its cooking around Zen teaching on food. Seasoning is kept restrained so the natural flavor of each ingredient comes through, and ingredients are used completely without waste. The kitchen invokes the spirit of Dogen's three minds of the temple cook: kishin, the joyful heart that takes genuine pleasure in preparing food; roshin, the nurturing heart that treats every grain of rice as a precious life; and daishin, the great, magnanimous heart. The aim is to bring the heart of Zen together with the traditions of Kyoto cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is shojin teppatsu (iron-bowl) cuisine. The teppatsu is a round iron alms bowl that mendicant monks once carried to receive food, a form said to have begun in India and reached Japan in the Nara period. Izusen serves its seasonal vegetarian dishes in nested vessels shaped after that historic bowl, so the meal itself echoes the monastic alms tradition. Course menus are named for flowers — Ayame, Yuri and Botan. Reviewers single out the grilled eggplant with miso as a standout.

anecdotes.text: The signature dishes arrive in vessels modeled on the teppatsu, the round iron alms bowl carried by mendicant monks, a form said to have originated in India and reached Japan in the Nara period.

True to Buddhist precept, the cooking uses no meat or seafood and only grains, legumes and vegetables, with seasoning kept minimal so each ingredient's own flavor leads and nothing is wasted; the Daiji-in branch has operated within Daitoku-ji since 1963, offering flower-named courses Ayame, Yuri and Botan.
Among the courses, reviewers point to the grilled eggplant with miso as a must-order.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-izusen.com (official) confirms the teppatsu alms-bowl form (round iron bowl monks used to receive food, said to have begun in India and reached Japan in the Nara period), the no-meat/no-seafood precept and minimal seasoning, Dogen's kishin/roshin principles, the Daiji-in branch operating within Daitoku-ji since 1 March 1963, and the flower-named courses Ayame (¥3,800), Yuri (¥4,800) and Botan (¥6,200); web search corroborates the ~150-year-old house founded around 1868.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cuisine is built on soy-based ingredients (soy sauce, miso, tofu, yuba), and the sources do not state whether a wheat-free (tamari-style) soy sauce is used. Most Japanese soy sauce and miso contain wheat, so unless confirmed, the seasonings should be assumed to contain gluten. Celiac diners must confirm directly with the restaurant before relying on any dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is fully plant-based by Buddhist precept: no meat and no seafood, using only grains, legumes, vegetables, seaweed, tofu and yuba. As shojin-ryori, the dashi is vegetarian (no bonito/fish stock), making it well suited to vegans.

positives.text: Entirely vegetarian shojin-ryori with no meat or fish anywhere on the menu, so animal cross-contact risk is inherently low.
Seasoning is deliberately kept light to let natural ingredient flavors lead, which can make it easier to identify what is in each dish.

places.name: Yoshuji
places.gf_detail: shojin; request adaptation
places.vegan_detail: Buddhist vegan
places.hours_raw: Wed–Mon 10:00–17:30; closed Tue
places.notes: Shojin-ryori by Kurama-dera (far north Sakyo). Seasonal mountain-vegetable sets, tofu, dango. Great after the Kurama–Kifune hike.

chef_bio.background: Yoshuji sits halfway up the stone steps leading to the Sanmon gate of Kurama-dera, the mountain temple north of Kyoto. It occupies a traditional Japanese farmhouse with tatami seating and an irori (sunken hearth) at its centre, a cozy and surprisingly refined retreat for such a small mountain village. Its name, Yoshuji, nods to Yoshu, an old poetic name for the region around Kyoto. The setting makes it a natural stop for walkers heading up to, or coming down from, the temple and the wooded Kurama-to-Kibune trail.
chef_bio.specialty: Buddhist vegetarian shojin-ryori, served either as set meals or as simpler noodle bowls. Signature dishes are built around mountain vegetables, tofu, and house preparations such as goma-dofu (chilled sesame tofu), mashed tofu with wild greens, and konnyaku presented sashimi-style with a dark miso sauce. A smoky, fragrant tea typically comes with the meal.

anecdotes.text: The set lunches lean on the mountain's seasonal bounty: rice, miso soup, pickles, enoki mushrooms, chilled sesame tofu, mashed tofu with wild greens, mashed yam with seaweed, and konnyaku served like sashimi with a dark miso sauce, finished with a wonderfully smoky tea.
Diners describe it as the nicest place to eat in Kurama, a surprisingly sophisticated restaurant whose delightful incongruity with the tiny village only adds to its charm.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a shojin-ryori (Buddhist vegetarian) kitchen, not a dedicated gluten-free one. Handmade soba (buckwheat noodles, which in Japan are commonly cut with wheat flour) is a core part of the menu, so wheat is regularly handled in the kitchen and a celiac should not assume the soba is safe.

Set meals include items seasoned with miso and soy-based sauces (for example konnyaku with a dark miso sauce and white-miso preparations). These typically contain wheat, so dishes are not inherently gluten-free; an adapted plate would need to be requested and confirmed in advance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes here rely on soy sauce and miso, the bulk of which in Japan is brewed with wheat. Assume soy sauce and miso contain wheat unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is largely vegan, but a diner reported that one side dish contained dashi (fish stock) and had to request it be removed, so not every plate is vegan by default.

Reports conflict on stock: a more recent review and on-site signage state the food is fully vegan with no fish stock and no animal ingredients, while an earlier report flagged dashi in a side. Confirm fish-stock use when ordering rather than relying on the menu's vegan framing.
Dairy is present for some items: milk is used for drinks, and the ice cream desserts are not vegan. Stick to the shojin sets and noodle bowls and skip the ice cream for a vegan meal.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff speak some English and accommodated a request to remove a dashi-containing side dish, suggesting requests can be made, though you should be explicit about needs.

positives.text: An entirely meat- and fish-free shojin-ryori menu, which removes many cross-contact risks that come with mixed kitchens, making it a comfortable choice for vegetarians and a strong starting point for vegans who confirm stock and dairy.

places.name: Daitokuji Ikkyu
places.gf_detail: shojin; request adaptation (Menu review: 3 gluten-free meal options on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Buddhist vegan
places.hours_raw: Sat–Sun 12:00–14:00 & 17:00–18:00 (reservation); weekdays by booking
places.notes: Centuries-old shojin restaurant at Daitoku-ji gate (Kita). Private rooms overlooking a zen garden; kaiseki-style vegan. Reserve ahead.

chef_bio.background: Daitokuji Ikkyu has stood at the gate of Daitoku-ji temple for more than five centuries. The family traces its work back to the Bunmei era (1469-1487), when an ancestor began cooking for the great Zen monastery, eventually earning the honor of preparing meals for the temple's most distinguished guests and delivering dishes to its many sub-temples. The name itself was a gift: the Zen master Ikkyu bestowed it on the family, who chose the characters 一久 (Ikkyu) out of humility rather than using the master's own name directly. For generations the kitchen cooked for visiting daimyo and cultural figures; only after temple tourism took off in the early 1960s did they begin serving guests on-site. Today the proprietress Tsuda Hiroko runs the house together with her husband, daughter, son-in-law and grandchildren - six family members and no outside staff.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking follows the strict Buddhist precepts of shojin-ryori - no meat, dairy, eggs or pungent plants - and the Daitokuji tradition of balancing the five flavors (pungent, sweet, salty, sour, bitter). The family preserves its recipes through isshi soden, one-heir succession, passing the techniques only within the bloodline. A scroll reading 'originally nothing exists' (本来無一物) hangs in the house, expressing the Zen spirit behind the food and their year-round dedication.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic Daitokuji shojin-ryori: an entirely plant-based, multi-course procession of the seasons built on tofu, fresh wheat gluten (namafu), yuba (tofu skin) and seasonal vegetables. Signature dishes include their sesame 'tofu,' fresh namafu simmered in soy sauce, and their famous Daitokuji natto - a dark, salty, dry fermented soybean, trademarked and made once a year in the summer heat in wooden barrels from soybeans, barley and salt, of which they are the sole authorized makers. The house is also celebrated for its pickles, including wasabi-accented pickled turnip rolls.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name was a gift from the Zen master Ikkyu; the family felt using the same character as his name would be presumptuous, so they adopted the characters 一久 instead.

The proprietress Tsuda Hiroko notes that if her family ever stopped making it, traditional Daitokuji natto would vanish - they are its sole authorized makers, fermenting it once a year in wooden barrels during the hottest months from just soybeans, barley and salt.

The family has served Daitoku-ji since the Bunmei era (1469-87), once cooking for visiting daimyo and delivering meals to the temple's sub-temples, and only began on-site dining after temple tourism grew in the early 1960s; it is the oldest surviving shop of its kind in Kyoto, over 500 years old.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: murasaki-style.rental-space-kyoto.com confirms the proprietress Tsuda Hiroko (津田紘子), the six-family-member operation with no outside staff, the Bunmei-era origin, the name 一久 adopted out of deference to Zen master Ikkyu, single-heir succession (一子相伝), the hanging scroll 本来無一物, and that they are the sole nationwide maker of trademarked Daitokuji natto, fermented annually in midsummer in wooden barrels from soybeans, barley and salt.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe restaurant. Fresh wheat gluten (namafu) is a central, recurring ingredient in the shojin-ryori course - it appears sliced, molded into plum-blossom shapes, and inside dumplings across multiple courses.

A detailed course documented multiple gluten-containing dishes (fresh gluten with soy sauce, fresh gluten plum blossom, kenchin-maki dumplings wrapped in fried yuba), confirming wheat is woven throughout the meal rather than confined to one dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce seasons several dishes and a white (saikyo) miso soup anchors a course; both standard Japanese soy sauce and miso typically contain wheat, adding further gluten exposure on top of the namafu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cooking follows strict Buddhist precepts - no meat, dairy, eggs or pungent alliums - and the broths are vegetable-based, so the menu is fully plant-based by design. Reviewers consistently describe the meals as entirely vegan with no animal products.
The sesame 'tofu' is made from kuzu (arrowroot) starch and ground sesame rather than soybeans, and the kenchin dumpling is served in a vegetable dashi (no bonito/fish stock).

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reviewers describe the kimono-clad family hosts as extremely accommodating and willing to explain how to eat each dish, though English is limited; no reviewer documented how the kitchen handles specific allergies.

positives.text: Fully vegan, multi-course Buddhist temple cuisine - a reliable choice for plant-based diners avoiding meat, fish, dairy, eggs and alliums.
Cooked entirely by family members with no outside staff, with reservations required - a setting where you can speak directly with the hosts about your needs when booking.

places.name: TOMONA
places.gf_detail: confirm (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian/vegan Japanese
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–19:00; closed Mon
places.notes: Refined vegetarian/vegan Japanese in Sagano (near Arashiyama). High-quality seasonal small plates; calm, stylish room.

chef_bio.background: TOMONA (ともな) is a plant-based restaurant in the Saga district near Arashiyama, in a quiet residential pocket of Kyoto's Ukyo Ward. It is a young kitchen with a clear point of view: every dish is built entirely from vegetables, with no animal products, eggs, or dairy. The meal follows a traditional Japanese rhythm of one soup, one rice, and nine small vegetable plates served alongside pickles, so a single sitting becomes a tour through many colors, textures, and seasonal preparations rather than one big main course. There is a calm, tea-house feel to the experience, with Chinese tea poured to accompany the food.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking that celebrates vegetables on their own terms, drawing flavor and variety from produce alone rather than from animal ingredients, and treating staples like rice as carefully as the showpiece dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: A multi-dish vegetable set: one soup, one rice, and nine small plates plus pickles (一汁一飯・九菜・香の物), leaning on locally sourced seasonal produce. The rice itself is a signature point of pride, a pesticide-free 'Hibiki' variety from Wakasa in Fukui Prefecture, cooked as brown rice with refills. Plates that recur on the table include vegetable tempura and fritters, a tofu 'hamburger' finished with a house-made onion sauce, salads dressed with amazake (sweet sake), snap peas with a curry soy-milk mayo, and a tomato-and-plum marinade.

anecdotes.text: Every dish at TOMONA is made only from vegetables, using no animal-derived ingredients, eggs, or dairy, and the meal is presented as one soup, one rice, and nine vegetable plates with pickles, built around locally sourced seasonal produce.

The rice is a pesticide-free special-cultivation variety called 'Hibiki' from Wakasa in Fukui Prefecture, served as brown rice with refills, while plates range from vegetable tempura and a tofu hamburger with house-made onion sauce to amazake-dressed salad.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com (Japanese) confirms the vegan, no-egg/no-dairy creative-vegetable concept; the one-soup/one-rice/nine-vegetable-plates-plus-pickles (一汁一飯・九菜・香の物) format; the pesticide-free 'Hibiki' brown rice from Wakasa in Fukui, served with refills; and specific plates (vegetable tempura, tofu hamburger with house-made onion sauce, amazake-dressed salad, snap-pea curry soy-milk mayo, tomato-plum marinade). The owner's name is not disclosed.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not celiac-safe based on documented cooking. The menu features vegetable tempura and fritters, which in Japanese kitchens are battered with wheat flour, creating a strong risk of gluten in fried items and shared frying oil. No information confirms a dedicated gluten-free fryer or separate prep, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sauces and dressings appear throughout the menu (for example dishes finished with soy-based sauces and a curry soy-milk mayo). Standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so seasonings here likely contain gluten unless the restaurant confirms a wheat-free tamari. Celiac diners should verify each sauce directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Strongly vegan-friendly by design: the kitchen uses only vegetables and explicitly avoids animal-derived ingredients, eggs, and dairy across the whole menu, so the baseline risk of animal-product cross-contact is low. No information is documented on shared oil or surfaces, so strict vegans who avoid trace contact should still confirm.

positives.text: An entirely plant-based kitchen that avoids animal products, eggs, and dairy, which makes it a comfortable choice for vegan diners.
Whole-food, vegetable-forward cooking with brown rice and many small seasonal plates, so diners can see and ask about each component.

places.name: M's Bento
places.gf_detail: macrobiotic, often GF; confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan (macrobiotic)
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:00–18:30; closed Sun–Mon
places.notes: Sister-run vegan macrobiotic bento by Saga-Arashiyama station. Two-tofu set, sushi rolls, miso soup. Great pre/post bamboo grove.

chef_bio.background: M's Bento is a small vegan-macrobiotic kitchen tucked away on the third floor of a building in Saga-Arashiyama, a short walk from the Sagano Romantic (Torokko) Train and JR Saga-Arashiyama stations. It is run by two sisters who look after every guest personally: one greets and serves diners (and speaks English well, happily explaining each ingredient and its health benefits), while the other concentrates on the cooking. Their devotion shows in the pace of the kitchen, where preparing the day's offerings is treated as slow, careful work rather than volume cooking. The heart of the place is chef Miho, a registered dietitian and vegan-macrobiotic cooking instructor with roughly 40 years of experience, who cooks to nourish both body and mind.
chef_bio.philosophy: A macrobiotic approach focused on wholesome, nutritious food meant to nourish the body and mind, with care given to ingredient quality and to explaining what goes into each dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Japanese-style vegan macrobiotic bento boxes are the signature: a balanced spread built around whole grains, seasonal vegetables, miso soup, tofu, vegetable sushi, pickles and a fruit jelly. Diners single out the tofu as some of the best they have eaten, and most items can be prepared allium-free. Beyond bento, the menu also stretches to pasta and toasties. The cooking is gentle and light, favoring the natural flavor of good ingredients over heavy seasoning.

anecdotes.text: Run by two sisters who take amazing care of their customers; the chef, Miho, is a registered dietitian and vegan-macrobiotic cooking instructor with 40 years of experience, aiming for her cooking to nourish her customers' bodies and minds.
Diners describe the tofu as among the best they have ever eaten, and praise the educational menu that details each ingredient and its nutritional benefits.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a fully vegan macrobiotic kitchen built around whole grains and vegetables, and it is reported to offer a gluten-free option, with some diners describing a gluten-free set lunch that the kitchen can prepare if given advance notice. However, no source documents a dedicated gluten-free preparation area or specific cross-contamination protocols, so celiac diners should confirm directly before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen is noted for using traditionally made soy sauce. Most traditional Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so unless a wheat-free tamari is confirmed, the soy sauce here should be assumed to contain wheat. Celiac diners should ask specifically whether a gluten-free soy sauce or tamari is used in their dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The food is vegan and macrobiotic. One note flags that a single item on the broader menu may contain cheese, so vegans should confirm their specific dish (the bento boxes are the plant-based mainstay).

staff_allergy_handling.text: English-speaking staff will explain the ingredients used and their health benefits, and the kitchen can make most things on the menu allium-free (no garlic/onion).
Staff are described as welcoming questions about the food, and have accommodated strict requests such as Jain dietary needs.

positives.text: Fully vegan macrobiotic menu with detailed ingredient and nutrition information provided in multiple languages, making it easy to see what is in each dish.
A gluten-free option is reported to be available, and the kitchen is willing to prepare a gluten-free set lunch with advance notice.
Most menu items can be made allium-free on request, and staff have a track record of handling restrictive diets thoughtfully.

places.name: KYOCRAFT (Ren vegan cafe)
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan/medicinal (yakuzen)
places.hours_raw: Thu–Tue 10:00–17:00 (Ren cafe; reservation); closed Wed
places.notes: Crafts house in Umezu (Ukyo) with 3F vegan 'Ren' cafe serving Taiwan-influenced medicinal (yakuzen) shojin courses. Reservation.

chef_bio.background: Cafe Ren grew out of one woman's own brush with serious illness. The founder, Okawa (大川月霞), came to Japan from Taiwan in her early twenties to study the language, married, and built a life here, until a life-threatening liver condition required major surgery. Recovering taught her how deeply food and health are connected, so she turned to the Taiwanese Buddhist vegetarian tradition she had grown up around and rebuilt her own well-being through it. After roughly a quarter-century of restored health she opened Ren in 2016 (its first shop in Wakayama, later growing to a small group that includes this Kyoto Horikawa branch), with the simple wish that everyone from small children to the elderly could taste how wonderful shojin cooking can be. What sets the place apart is its strict Taiwanese-shojin approach: no meat, fish, eggs or dairy, and none of the five pungent vegetables (onion, scallion, garlic, chives and the like), which this tradition holds can burden the body and cloud the mind.
chef_bio.philosophy: Health through food, and sharing the value of shojin cuisine with people of every age, cooking entirely without animal products or the five pungent vegetables.
chef_bio.specialty: Taiwanese-style vegan shojin cooking. The kitchen is best known for its medicinal herbal broths, in which more than fifteen kinds of Chinese herbs are simmered for over two hours, served as the base for soups aimed at warming the body and easing fatigue. Other signatures include plump water gyoza in a soy-milk broth, a plant-based shojin 'unagi' (mock eel) rice bowl that mimics the look and taste of the real thing, Chinese-style sticky rice, daikon cakes and tofu nuggets.

anecdotes.text: The founder arrived from Taiwan at age 22 to study Japanese, married a Japanese partner, and after surviving a life-threatening liver illness and major surgery rebuilt her health through vegetarian eating before opening Ren to pass that on.

Every soup starts from a medicinal broth of more than fifteen Chinese herbs simmered for over two hours, and the shop excludes the five pungent vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion, leek and the like) that Buddhist practice holds burden the organs.
At the Kyoto Horikawa branch, among the most talked-about dishes are the water gyoza in soy-milk broth and a convincing plant-based shojin 'unagi' rice bowl (精進うな重).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: cafe-ren.therestaurant.jp (official) confirms the Taiwanese founder who came at 22 to study Japanese, married, survived a life-threatening liver illness/surgery and recovered over ~25 years; the exclusion of animal products and the five pungent vegetables (五葷); and the 15+ Chinese-herb broth simmered 2+ hours. japanese-heart.com names the owner as 大川月霞 (Okawa, Taiwan-born), dates the first (Wakayama) shop to 2016 with the Kyoto Horikawa shop as a branch, and lists the water gyoza in soy-milk broth and the mock-eel rice bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe kitchen. The cuisine leans heavily on wheat: water gyoza are wrapped in wheat dough, and mock-meat items such as the shojin 'unagi' are typically wheat-gluten based. A diner-facing description explicitly states that some dishes contain wheat (and peanut) ingredients.

No gluten-free certification, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or cross-contamination protocol is documented anywhere in the available sources. A truly gluten-sensitive or celiac diner should confirm specific dishes and preparation directly with the staff before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is soy- and seasoning-forward (soy-milk gyoza broth, tofu nuggets, Taiwanese vegetarian seasonings sold in-house). Most Japanese and Taiwanese soy-based seasonings contain wheat, and no wheat-free or tamari soy sauce is mentioned, so assume soy seasonings here contain wheat unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan diners are very well served: the entire menu is plant-based, with no meat, fish, eggs or dairy, and even the five pungent vegetables (onion, garlic, scallion, chives) are excluded, so cross-contact with animal products is a non-issue across the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as willing to accommodate dietary restrictions, preparing modified versions of dishes or suggesting allergen-free alternatives on request.

positives.text: Fully vegan menu rooted in Taiwanese shojin cooking, with no animal products at all.
No five pungent vegetables, which also helps diners avoiding onion and garlic.
Staff are open to modifying dishes or recommending alternatives for allergies.

places.name: Ikkyu-an (一休庵, Kodai-ji)
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian/vegan set menu
places.hours_raw: Hours vary — check (Kodai-ji south gate)
places.notes: Traditional Kyoto restaurant by Kodai-ji's south gate (Higashiyama). Set menus with vegetarian & vegan options; temple-view setting.

chef_bio.background: Tucked into a traditional Japanese house directly in front of Kodai-ji temple's south gate, Ikkyu-an specializes in fucha ryori, a style of Buddhist vegetarian cuisine carried to Japan from China by Zen monks in the Edo period. Where Japanese shojin ryori tends toward austerity, fucha ryori is a little warmer and more generous, drawing on vegetable oil and bolder seasoning, which makes it approachable even for first-timers. The setting is intimate and quiet, with tatami rooms that feel more like dining in a private temple residence than a restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking that lets the natural flavor of seasonal vegetables come forward, in the Zen Buddhist spirit of simple, mindful, meat-free dining.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal plant-based courses in the fucha ryori tradition. Signature dishes include silky black sesame tofu (goma dofu), eggplant dengaku glazed with miso, kudzu vegetable soup, and a vegetable tempura that diners single out for being remarkably light and not at all greasy.

anecdotes.text: The fucha ryori served here was brought to Japan by Chinese monks during the Edo period; compared with Japanese shojin ryori it uses more vegetable oil, which many diners find makes the flavors easier to enjoy.
Diners are charmed to find a restaurant devoted to fucha cuisine sitting right in front of Kodai-ji temple, and praise how light and clean the vegetable tempura tastes.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a Buddhist vegetarian (shojin/fucha ryori) kitchen, not a gluten-free one. Traditional shojin and fucha menus regularly include wheat gluten (fu) and soy-based items, and Japanese seasonings like soy sauce typically contain wheat, so the cuisine should not be considered celiac-safe without direct confirmation.
Vegetable tempura is a signature dish, and tempura batter is wheat-based; no source describes a separate gluten-free fryer or station, so assume shared frying oil and surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms the type of soy sauce used. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and shojin/fucha cooking relies heavily on it for seasoning, so a celiac diner should confirm directly before eating here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cuisine is plant-based by tradition: as Buddhist vegetarian cooking, the menu avoids meat and fish, and fucha ryori is rooted in fully meat-free Zen practice. This aligns with a vegan-friendly style, though specific accommodations are not documented.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based menu in the Buddhist vegetarian tradition, so vegan diners can eat broadly across the courses.
Small, owner-run setting with private tatami rooms, which can make it easier to have a direct conversation about your dietary needs.

places.name: Ramen Kazu – Kinkaku-ji
places.gf_detail: sub the GF rice noodles (vegan ramen page)
places.vegan_detail: full vegan ramen & side menu
places.notes: Local ramen shop near Kinkaku-ji serving regular meat ramen AND a whole vegan page; order GF rice noodles. Vegan masala-curry ramen, soy karaage, gyoza.

chef_bio.background: A small, cafe-like ramen shop tucked just a few minutes' walk behind Kinkaku-ji (the Golden Pavilion), Ramen Kazu has been quietly serving the neighborhood since opening in 2005 and is now marking its 20th year. It's run on an intimate scale by just two people, and diners often note that the chef cooks right in front of them at the counter. The owner is frequently described as warm and welcoming, speaks English, and is happy to steer first-timers toward a recommendation. What sets the place apart is its rare dual identity: alongside a classic light Kyoto-style shoyu ramen, it keeps a full separate menu of plant-based bowls, making it a genuine refuge for vegan and vegetarian travelers in a temple district where meat-free ramen is hard to find.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as being particular about its vegetables and runs a modern, cafe-like room that welcomes solo diners, couples and families alike, with an English menu for the many international visitors who find it on the way to the Golden Pavilion.
chef_bio.specialty: Known for a light, mild soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen, and for a limited daily chashu bowl topped with grilled pork (only about 10 servings a day). On the plant-based side, the shop is loved for three vegan ramens (miso, shoyu and curry), its fried 'soy meat' mock-meat bites, and veggie gyoza that more than one diner has called the best they've ever had; rice noodles can be swapped in for the vegan bowls.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe it as 'a little gem' near the temple, 'a small place run by just two people' where 'the chef cooks next to you' and 'the owner was very friendly.'
The vegan lineup is a standout: 'They have three different vegan ramens, plus fried mock meats,' with the veggie miso ramen and veggie gyoza repeatedly singled out as excellent.
The shop has served light Kyoto-style shoyu ramen since 2005 and offers a limited daily bowl topped with grilled chashu, capped at about 10 servings a day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a ramen shop where standard bowls use egg noodles and soy-sauce-based broths, prepared in a small shared kitchen run by two people. A gluten-free rice-noodle swap is offered, but no source documents separate pots, utensils, or surfaces, so cross-contact with wheat noodles and shared broth is likely. It should not be considered celiac-safe.
The signature ramen is a light soy-sauce (shoyu) broth; soy-sauce-based and miso broths in ramen typically contain wheat, so even the rice-noodle bowls may not be free of gluten in the soup itself.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house style is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. There is no documentation that a wheat-free tamari is used, so celiac diners should assume the shoyu and miso broths contain gluten and confirm directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: There is a dedicated separate menu for vegetarian and vegan dishes, and vegan bowls are made by swapping the egg noodles for rice noodles. Reviewers report a reliably plant-based experience, including three vegan ramens and fried mock 'soy meat,' though no source describes whether vegan and non-vegan items share cooking equipment.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner speaks English and is described as friendly and attentive; diners ask staff directly to swap egg noodles for the rice noodle option. No formal allergy protocol is documented, so describe your needs clearly when ordering.

positives.text: Maintains a genuine separate vegan/vegetarian menu with three vegan ramens (miso, shoyu, curry) plus fried mock meat and veggie gyoza, which vegan diners praise highly.
A gluten-free rice-noodle option is available to replace the standard egg noodles on request.
English-speaking, hands-on owner in a tiny two-person shop makes it easy to ask questions about your order before you commit.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick straight noodles, chosen to suit the light, clean Kyoto-style broth rather than to grip a heavy soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kinkaku Ramen — a Kyoto-style clear shoyu bowl crowned with a generous helping of charcoal-grilled chashu (a daily limited offering).
ramen.chashu: Sumibi (charcoal-grilled) pork chashu — char-grilled over open flame rather than slow-braised, giving a smoky, caramelized edge; it is the signature heart of the bowl.

gf.note: The shop documents a celiac-friendly / gluten-free ramen option, which is genuinely rare for ramen (wheat noodles + wheat shoyu tare). Confirm preparation and cross-contact directly when ordering.

vegan.note: Vegetarian and vegan ramen options are documented on the shop's official site. Confirm at order that the broth and tare are fully plant-based with no fish dashi or animal fat.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto comfort ramen in its gentlest register: a clear, soy-kissed broth built on a pork-and-seafood blend that drinks light yet carries quiet depth, the local palate's answer to heavier regional bowls. Its soul shows in the contrast — a delicate, clean soup set against smoky charcoal-grilled chashu that lands like a flame across the bowl.

places.highlight: This is Kyoto comfort ramen in its gentlest register: a clear, soy-kissed broth built on a pork-and-seafood blend that drinks light yet carries quiet depth, the local palate's answer to heavier regional bowls. Its soul shows in the contrast — a delicate, clean soup set against smoky charcoal-grilled chashu that lands like a flame across the bowl.

places.name: KYOTO ENGINE RAMEN
places.gf_detail: confirm noodles
places.vegan_detail: vegan & vegetarian broth options
places.notes: Hugely popular downtown ramen with full vegan/vegetarian 'Zesty' broth alongside pork. Vegan dumplings. Long lines — go at opening.

chef_bio.origin: Kawaramachi, Kyoto (with a sister Engine Ramen in Osaka, and reportedly in Tokyo)
chef_bio.background: Kyoto Engine Ramen (京都炎神) grew out of a deeply personal motivation: the owner's daughter has celiac disease. That family experience shaped a ramen shop built so that someone who can't eat wheat doesn't have to sit out one of Japan's most beloved comfort foods. Tucked near Kawaramachi Station in central Kyoto, it's a small, intimate spot on an upper floor where the whole menu is designed around inclusion rather than wheat-based ramen with a token alternative. The shop has since grown beyond Kyoto, with a larger sibling Engine Ramen in Osaka (and, per gluten-free travel guides, a Tokyo location as well).
chef_bio.philosophy: A place where vegan and gluten-free diners are first-class guests, not an afterthought: because the soups are made in-house, the kitchen aims to offer a genuinely wheat-free experience, and the team is candid about its limits (for example noting it can offer pork-free chicken- or vegan-based bowls but cannot meet strict halal requirements).
chef_bio.specialty: Ramen where every bowl can be made gluten-free, with rice noodles available as a substitute and broths built in-house from carefully chosen ingredients (including brown-rice and bean bases). Standout vegan bowls include the KYOTO BEANing Vegan Ramen, a creamy soup made from edamame, rice, daikon and soy-milk cream finished with shiso, fried carrot, corn, green onion and sesame, and the Zesty (spicy miso) Ramen for Vegan. They even pour gluten-free beer.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant exists because the owner's daughter has celiac disease, which inspired a ramen shop where every bowl can be made gluten-free.
Because the soup ingredients are made in-house, the kitchen says it can ensure a wheat-free dining experience, and it serves gluten-free beer alongside the ramen.
The vegan KYOTO BEANing Ramen is built on a creamy broth of edamame, rice, daikon and soy-milk cream, finished with shiso, fried carrot, corn, green onion and sesame.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Reviewers report the kitchen boils the gluten-free noodles separately in a dedicated gluten-free noodle pot and uses separate cooking ware for gluten-free orders.
Gluten-free ramen is served in a different-colored bowl to flag it as gluten-free, and place-setting markers are used to prevent mix-ups for gluten allergies.
This is not a dedicated gluten-free facility, and some reviewers note there is still a chance of cross-contamination in the shared kitchen.

A gluten-free travel writer chose not to dine here on her visit, citing caution about cross-contamination at non-dedicated ramen kitchens after being glutened at a ramen shop in Tokyo, though she noted it gets rave reviews and that she would likely try it next time.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ramen seasonings and dashi in Japan commonly rely on soy sauce, most of which contains wheat. The shop emphasizes in-house soups and a wheat-free experience, but the specific soy sauce/tare used is not documented in these sources, so celiac diners should confirm the seasoning is wheat-free directly with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Two clearly labeled vegan ramen options are offered and staff are described as knowledgeable about vegan accommodations.
Some users reported dishes labeled vegan that they felt weren't fully vegan, and that staff aren't always sure about every ingredient, so vegans should double-check specifics with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are repeatedly described as friendly, attentive to allergies, and as clearly understanding the needs of a celiac customer.

English-speaking staff and English menus with allergen information are available, and the kitchen is transparent about its limits (e.g., it can do pork-free chicken- or vegan-based bowls but cannot meet strict halal needs).
Some reviewers caution that staff are not always certain about every ingredient, so confirm specific allergen concerns directly.

positives.text: Unlike most ramen shops, every bowl here can be made gluten-free using rice noodles (typically +200 yen, subject to stock), and the soups are prepared in-house with the stated goal of a wheat-free experience.
The shop was founded with celiac diners in mind because the owner's daughter has celiac disease, and reviewers generally report feeling safe and well cared for.
Concrete safety practices reported by diners include a separate pot for boiling gluten-free noodles, separate cooking ware, and different-colored bowls to distinguish gluten-free orders.
Gluten-free beer is available, a rare find at a ramen shop.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick, high-hydration crimped (chijire) noodles in the Sapporo tradition, made to grip the thick miso soup; the shop also serves fresh egg noodles made in-house and offers a rice-flour noodle swap for a gluten-free bowl.

ramen.signature_bowl: Houjun Miso Ramen (芳醇味噌ラーメン) — the flame-finished signature miso bowl; spice-lovers go for the Kiwami Kara-Miso (極み辛味噌), and the shop's celebrated plant-based KYOTO BEANing Vegan Ramen (edamame/soy-milk miso) is the must-order for vegan diners.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu served in generous, chunky pieces (the vegan bowls omit pork entirely).

gf.note: Unusually for ramen, a gluten-free path exists: a rice-flour noodle swap is offered (about +200 yen) alongside the plant-based bowls. The standard miso tare and W-soup are not certified gluten-free, so a strict celiac should confirm tare and stock ingredients before ordering.

vegan.note: A genuine plant-based bowl is a house specialty: the KYOTO BEANing Vegan Ramen uses a miso broth built from edamame, rice, daikon and soy milk with no animal stock or pork, dressed with corn, buckwheat groats, shiso, scallion and sesame; a spicy ZESTY vegan version is also offered.

ramen.pantheon: Kyoto Engine is the Kyoto outpost of Sapporo's cult 炎神, where a blend of Hokkaido misos is literally scorched with a 1300°C flame to toast the miso and emulsify the lard into a thick, potage-like soup over a fish-and-animal double stock — pure Sapporo miso lineage you taste in the silky, fragrant body. What sets this branch apart is a rare twin identity: that fiery, fatty miso classic sitting beside one of Japan's most credible fully vegan, soy-milk-and-bean miso bowls.

places.highlight: Kyoto Engine is the Kyoto outpost of Sapporo's cult 炎神, where a blend of Hokkaido misos is literally scorched with a 1300°C flame to toast the miso and emulsify the lard into a thick, potage-like soup over a fish-and-animal double stock — pure Sapporo miso lineage you taste in the silky, fragrant body. What sets this branch apart is a rare twin identity: that fiery, fatty miso classic sitting beside one of Japan's most credible fully vegan, soy-milk-and-bean miso bowls.

places.name: UNO RAMEN – Kyoto Station
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→dedicated): Same owner/protocol as Gion, confirmed by official JR West press release. UPGRADE high→dedicated. Flag: sits inside 京都拉麺小路 food court surrounded by wheat ramen (stall kitchen clean, shared common area unverified). — Prior: gluten-free options
places.vegan_detail: vegan/vegetarian soy-milk ramen
places.notes: Soy-milk ramen specialist on Kyoto Station's 10F ramen street; vegan & GF options, gyoza, croquettes, bento. (Sister of Gion/Uno GF ramen.)

chef_bio.background: This Gion soy-milk ramen shop is the work of Yukiko Uno, a Kyoto-born chef (1975) known as the world's first "Soy Milk Patissiere." She trained for six years in Japanese restaurant kitchens before spending another six years learning pastry, then opened her flagship patisserie, Mushiyashinai, in the Ichijoji area of Kyoto at age 30. Over the years she built a name for plant-based, allergen-free baking, using soybeans in place of flour, eggs and dairy. In 2016 she went a step further and started her own farm so she could control ingredient quality from the ground up. The Gion ramen shop, which opened in October 2022, carries that same mission from the dessert counter into a bowl of ramen: a fully plant-based, fully gluten-free ramen restaurant built so that people with dietary restrictions can sit down and eat ramen without worry.
