chef_bio.philosophy: Uno's guiding idea is "making everyone smile with food" — bringing joy to the family table so that people with food limitations can taste the same delicious things everyone else does. As she has put it, one of her dreams is to help people with food restrictions eat the dish or dessert they wish they could have.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-milk-based vegan ramen served with rice-flour noodles instead of wheat. The signature is the Tonyu Ramen "Yuki" (a creamy soy-milk bowl), alongside a soy-milk dan dan noodle "Gekka" and seasonal Kyoto-vegetable bowls built on many varieties of vegetables. The kitchen also turns out gluten-free gyoza and okara (soy-pulp) croquettes, plus her gluten-free, vegan cakes — a nod to her patisserie roots.

anecdotes.text: Her allergen-free path crystallized around a four-year-old boy with 23 allergies who asked for "a cake like in fairytales" — strawberries and fluffy white cream. After much experimentation she created it without flour, eggs or dairy, and he loved it; that became her "Miracle Cake," the spirit of which carries into the ramen shop.
In 2016 Uno founded her own farm so she could grow and oversee her own ingredients, ensuring quality and safety for people eating allergen-free.
The Gion bowl is built around soy milk and a generous mix of Japanese vegetables, with rice-flour noodles standing in for wheat ramen noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a fully dedicated gluten-free kitchen: it is described as an entirely wheat-free kitchen that does not use any gluten ingredients, with rice noodles used in place of wheat noodles. Because no wheat or gluten is handled anywhere in the kitchen, the usual ramen risk of shared boiling water, surfaces and utensils does not apply here.
Reviewers report that everything served is gluten-free and that they did not have to worry about cross-contamination, including in the soup.

As with any venue, practices can change and some residual cross-contamination risk can remain even at verified gluten-free restaurants — confirm directly with staff before ordering, especially if you are highly sensitive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Because the entire kitchen is gluten-free and uses no wheat ingredients, the seasonings (including any soy sauce used) are gluten-free rather than standard wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce. This is consistent with the restaurant's 100% gluten-free designation, though the specific soy sauce brand is not documented; confirm with staff if you need certainty.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is fully vegan — the broth is soy-milk based and dishes are entirely plant-based — so there is no animal product in the kitchen to cross-contact a vegan order.
Many dishes are also allium-free, which can help diners avoiding onion and garlic.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A reviewer noted the menu is very clearly labeled for all kinds of allergens, such as soy, gluten and wheat.

Suppliers are carefully selected and staff are described as trained in safe handling to protect guests with celiac disease or gluten intolerance; guests are also asked not to bring gluten-containing foods into the restaurant.

positives.text: A rare find: a fully dedicated 100% gluten-free and vegan ramen restaurant, frequently described as one of the safest ramen options in Japan for people with celiac disease.
Fried items are made in this wheat-free kitchen, so the gyoza and okara croquettes are gluten-free — reviewers called the fried gyoza among the best they have had and the croquettes incredible.
The rice-flour gluten-free noodles drew praise for being cooked al dente and genuinely delicious rather than a compromise.
The shop also offers gluten-free, vegan cakes, reflecting the owner's background as an allergen-free pastry chef.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles of rice flour kneaded with kombu (kelp) — gluten-free, with a smooth, slippery, springy texture rather than the snap of wheat-and-kansui noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hana (花) — the creamy soy-milk ramen: a koku-rich, creamy soy-milk soup built on a vegetable-and-mushroom veggie broth seasoned with gluten-free soy sauce, crowned with 15 colorful vegetables and soy meat over rice-flour-and-kombu noodles. (Their Tsuki/月 is the dandan/tantan-style sibling.)
ramen.chashu: No pork or chicken chashu — the protein is plant-based soy meat (soy-based meat substitute) in keeping with the fully vegan kitchen.

gf.note: Genuinely gluten-free: the entire menu is built around gluten-free soy sauce in the tare and house noodles made from rice flour and kombu rather than wheat-and-kansui. The kitchen is dedicated to gluten-free, plant-based cooking, so this is one of the rare ramen shops a wheat-avoider can eat at — confirm cross-contact needs directly if celiac.

vegan.note: The whole shop is 100% plant-based and vegan — the soul soup is creamy soy milk over a vegetable-and-mushroom (veggie broth) base with kombu and shiitake, no katsuo/pork dashi, gluten-free soy-sauce tare, and soy meat in place of chashu. No animal products anywhere on the menu.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto creation that rewrites the genre from the broth up: instead of the city's classic se-abura pork-bone tradition, the soul here is a creamy, plant-only soy-milk soup drawn from vegetables, mushrooms and kombu and seasoned with gluten-free soy sauce — paitan-style richness with no animal in the bowl. You taste a deliberately barrier-free lineage, where the comfort of a creamy ramen is reimagined for vegan and gluten-free diners alike.

places.highlight: A modern-original Kyoto creation that rewrites the genre from the broth up: instead of the city's classic se-abura pork-bone tradition, the soul here is a creamy, plant-only soy-milk soup drawn from vegetables, mushrooms and kombu and seasoned with gluten-free soy sauce — paitan-style richness with no animal in the bowl. You taste a deliberately barrier-free lineage, where the comfort of a creamy ramen is reimagined for vegan and gluten-free diners alike.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Same owner/protocol as Gion, confirmed by official JR West press release. UPGRADE high→dedicated. Flag: sits inside 京都拉麺小路 food court surrounded by wheat ramen (stall kitchen clean, shared common area unverified).

places.name: UNO RAMEN – Sanjo (Vegan & GF Sushi)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→dedicated): Same owner/wheat-free-kitchen protocol, JP review corroboration. UPGRADE high→dedicated. — Prior: gluten-free ramen & sushi
places.vegan_detail: vegan ramen & sushi
places.notes: Vegan & gluten-free ramen + sushi near Sanjo/Gion. Creamy soy-milk broth, GF gyoza. Set menus often need online booking.

chef_bio.background: UNO RAMEN is the work of Yukiko Uno, a Kyoto-born pastry chef and plant-based cuisine researcher who also runs the sweets shop Mushiyashinai in the Ichijoji district. Uno trained first in Japanese cooking and then in pastry, where she became known for creating cakes made without wheat, dairy, or eggs. Her turn toward ramen began with a personal encounter: she has described meeting a young boy living with 23 allergies, which set her on a years-long mission to let people who are limited in what they can eat enjoy ramen, one of Japan's soul foods and a Kyoto specialty. She opened her original soy-milk ramen shop in Gion in October 2022, and in March 2025 added this second branch near Keihan Sanjo Station, set inside a renovated roughly 100-year-old Kyoto townhouse with a garden, sunken-kotatsu seating, and a private room with painted sliding doors. Everything served is fully plant-based and gluten-free.
chef_bio.philosophy: Uno's guiding idea is food without barriers that is delicious for everyone. She has spoken about wanting guests with allergies to be able to say I can eat here safely, drawing on Kyoto's local produce to make plant-based, gluten-free cooking that does not feel like a compromise.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy milk ramen is the heart of the menu, built on a mushroom-and-vegetable broth enriched with soy milk and gluten-free soy sauce, served with original house-made noodles made from rice flour and kelp rather than wheat. The signature bowls go by names like Hana, Yuki, and Snow, alongside a spicy soy-milk tantanmen. The Sanjo branch is also known for vegan and gluten-free sushi (yuba, plant-based salmon and squid, inari, pickles), dim sum and gyoza, house-made tofu made in the shop, and Uno's soy-milk desserts.

anecdotes.text: Uno traces the restaurant back to meeting a boy with 23 allergies: for about four years she had been thinking she wanted people who are limited in what they can eat to be able to enjoy ramen, a Japanese soul food and one of Kyoto's specialties.

A vegan traveler from Britain who loved her allergen-free cake helped inspire her broader mission to delight people with food made without the usual restrictions, which grew into the gluten-free, plant-based ramen project.
The Sanjo branch, the second location of her Gion shop, opened in March 2025 inside a renovated roughly 100-year-old townhouse with a Japanese garden, just a short walk from Keihan Sanjo Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen contains no gluten or animal products whatsoever, and the restaurant states it never brings wheat or animal-based ingredients into the shop, so cookware, utensils, and surfaces are used only for gluten-free cooking.
Because the kitchen handles no wheat at all, guests are asked to cooperate with a no-bring-in policy and not bring gluten or animal-based items into the dining area at table seating.
The Sanjo branch is described as fully vegan and fully gluten-free, presented as a venue designed specifically for guests with multiple dietary restrictions.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Unlike most Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, the soy-milk ramen broth here is made with gluten-free soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every menu item is 100% plant-based and the kitchen handles no animal products whatsoever, so there is no animal-ingredient cross-contact in the shop.
Reviewers describe the food as fully vegan, including sushi made with plant-based fish, with vegans fully catered for.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Beyond its gluten-free and vegan baseline, the restaurant says it can customize dishes for additional needs such as nut-free and mushroom-free when guests notify them in advance.
The owner is a pastry chef who built the business specifically around allergy-restricted diners, and notes that guests with allergies telling her they can eat safely is central to her mission.

positives.text: The wheat-free, gluten-free design runs through the whole concept: original noodles are made from rice flour and kelp rather than wheat, and the broth uses gluten-free soy sauce.
A dedicated all-gluten-free, all-vegan kitchen with no wheat or animal products on site makes this a strong choice for celiac and vegan diners; reservations are recommended.
Reviewers call it one of the best vegan restaurants they have been to, with gyoza that have a delightful crunch and plant-based sushi that tastes delicious.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Same owner/wheat-free-kitchen protocol, JP review corroboration. UPGRADE high→dedicated.

places.name: MOON and BACK Ramen Bar & Brunch
places.gf_detail: GF ramen, sides, beer & desserts marked
places.vegan_detail: vegan ramen, dumplings & desserts
places.notes: Australian-style ramen bar (Nakagyo). Wagyu ramen plus clearly-marked vegan & GF ramen, sushi, GF beer, vegan matcha brownie. Multilingual staff.

chef_bio.background: MOON and BACK was founded by chef Tatsuya Fukuda, who was born in Osaka in 1988. He trained in traditional Japanese kappo cooking from a young age, then moved abroad to Australia, where his career took off. He spent about seven years as head chef at a fine-dining modern Japanese restaurant near the Sydney Opera House, and went on to launch and produce several restaurants across Sydney, the Gold Coast, and Brisbane. Living and cooking overseas, where diners routinely flagged allergies and dietary restrictions, reshaped how he thought about Japanese food. He brought that perspective back to Kyoto and built MOON and BACK as a place where meat lovers, vegans, and gluten-free diners can all sit at the same table and share a meal, with an Australian-inflected, sunny cafe feel that nods to his years down under.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fukuda's aim is to spread Japanese food culture to the world by combining vegan, gluten-free, and traditional Japanese cooking into a new style of Japanese taste, and to make ramen that everyone can enjoy with allergies and dietary needs taken into account, so that groups with very different diets never have to eat apart.
chef_bio.specialty: Ramen reimagined so almost anyone can eat it. The signature is gluten-free ramen built on special rice-based noodles that Fukuda spent years developing so the bowl still satisfies without wheat, alongside a fully plant-based vegan ramen made with naturally fermented miso and soy milk and a custom-blended miso and soy sauce for umami depth. The kitchen also serves meat-forward bowls like grilled Japanese Wagyu shoyu ramen and teriyaki chicken, plus a brunch-and-cafe side with desserts such as a rich Basque cheesecake. By day it runs as a brunch cafe; in the evening it shifts into a ramen bar.

anecdotes.text: Fukuda spent about seven years as head chef at a fine-dining restaurant near the Sydney Opera House before producing restaurants across Sydney, the Gold Coast, and Brisbane, then returning to open MOON and BACK in Kyoto.
Fukuda spent years developing a gluten-free ramen on rice-based noodles that still satisfies without wheat; diners have told him it was the first ramen they had ever been able to eat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen builds its gluten-free ramen on special rice-based noodles developed specifically to be wheat-free, rather than swapping noodles in a shared wheat-ramen line, which lowers a major source of gluten cross-contact for a ramen shop.

A gluten-free guide lists the venue as celiac friendly with low risk of cross-contamination and notes the use of dedicated appliances for gluten-free preparation; this is community-reported, not formal third-party certification, so confirm current practice with staff.

The restaurant's own site and listings do not spell out kitchen-level protocols such as a dedicated fryer, separate noodle-boiling water, or separate utensils, so celiac diners should ask directly about these specifics before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: For the vegan and gluten-free bowls the kitchen uses a custom-blended miso and soy sauce rather than standard ramen seasoning; the site does not explicitly state the soy sauce is wheat-free or gluten-free, so celiac diners should confirm the soy sauce used in their specific dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan ramen is described as completely free from any animal ingredients, built on naturally fermented miso and soy milk. However, the same kitchen also cooks Wagyu, teriyaki chicken, and other meat dishes, so vegans concerned about shared equipment or cross-contact should confirm preparation with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant asks guests to let them know about any food allergies or intolerances when booking, indicating they collect allergy information at reservation.
A gluten-free guide reports the venue has staff knowledgeable about celiac safety and offers a gluten-free omakase set with careful service for gluten-sensitive guests.

positives.text: This is a rare ramen specialist designed from the ground up for gluten-free and vegan diners, with dedicated wheat-free rice noodles and a fully plant-based bowl, making it far more accommodating than a typical wheat-and-dashi ramen shop.
Guests with wheat allergies and gluten intolerance are explicitly welcomed, and reviewers praise the gluten-free ramen and dumplings, with some calling it the best gluten-free ramen they have found.

noodles.notes: Thick wheat noodles (about 150g) cut on a 14-blade for a substantial, chewy bite; a separate rice-flour noodle is offered for the gluten-free bowls.

ramen.signature_bowl: Wagyu Ramen - a clear oyster-and-chicken broth perfumed with shrimp oil, crowned with A5 domestic wagyu sirloin seared tableside with a torch (eat it first with a touch of salt).
ramen.chashu: The heart is A5 domestic wagyu sirloin, char-grilled tableside with a blowtorch and served rare rather than braised; a pork-belly chashu also appears as a supporting topping.
notable_toppings: soft-boiled egg branded with the shop's rabbit logo

gf.note: A dedicated gluten-free ramen is on the menu, built on rice-flour noodles for guests avoiding wheat; confirm tare/broth ingredients with staff if you are strictly celiac.

vegan.note: Two documented plant-based bowls: a soy-milk creamy ramen on a base of naturally fermented miso and soy milk with no animal ingredients, and a spicy sesame (tantan-style) vegan ramen with bok choy and tofu skin.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional throwback, this bowl chases elegance over heaviness: a clear shellfish-and-chicken broth lifted by fragrant shrimp oil, with the soul-stirring flourish of A5 wagyu sirloin torched at your table. It is a globally-minded, all-welcome ramen bar where carnivores, vegans, and gluten-free diners each get a genuinely considered bowl.

places.highlight: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional throwback, this bowl chases elegance over heaviness: a clear shellfish-and-chicken broth lifted by fragrant shrimp oil, with the soul-stirring flourish of A5 wagyu sirloin torched at your table. It is a globally-minded, all-welcome ramen bar where carnivores, vegans, and gluten-free diners each get a genuinely considered bowl.

places.name: MOON and BACK – Kiyamachi Alley
places.gf_detail: GF & vegan items marked
places.vegan_detail: vegan ramen, dumplings, dairy-free ice cream
places.notes: Kiyamachi branch of Moon and Back. Vegan & pork ramen, dairy-free ice cream, cocktails. Friendly, multilingual.

chef_bio.background: MOON and BACK was founded by owner-chef Tatsuya Fukuda, who spent seven years as head chef at a high-end modern Japanese restaurant overlooking the Sydney Opera House in Australia before bringing his craft back to Kyoto. That Australian chapter still shows up in the room, which leans into a playful Down-Under theme with imagery like the Opera House and a koala. Tucked into the Kiyamachi/Kawaramachi area a short walk from Nishiki Market, the restaurant grew out of a simple but ambitious idea: build a table where meat lovers, vegans, and gluten-free diners can all sit down and eat the same kind of meal together, without anyone being relegated to a token side dish.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fukuda's stated mission is to deliver safe and delicious food to people whose options are limited by nationality, religion, or allergies, and to connect with the world through food by making a space where everyone shares the same table naturally. The kitchen frames its work around health, safety, and deliciousness using Japanese cooking techniques.
chef_bio.specialty: Wagyu beef ramen is the calling card here, including a grilled Japanese wagyu shoyu ramen, alongside a parallel lineup of vegan ramen built on traditional Japanese ingredients like kelp, miso, and soy sauce. The kitchen is especially known for its gluten-free ramen and gyoza, and even runs a gluten-free omakase-style set so that celiac and gluten-sensitive guests get a full, considered meal rather than a stripped-down one.

anecdotes.text: Before opening in Kyoto, Fukuda spent seven years as head chef at a high-end modern Japanese restaurant in front of the Sydney Opera House, and the shop carries that Australian influence through to its koala-and-Opera-House decor.

The restaurant pitches itself plainly as a place where meat lovers, vegans, and gluten-free diners can all enjoy a meal together, with wagyu ramen, vegan ramen, and brunch dishes side by side on one menu.

Vegan dishes are built on traditional Japanese pantry staples such as kelp, miso, and soy sauce, reflecting the kitchen's aim to make restriction-friendly food that still tastes like proper Japanese cooking.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is one of the rare ramen shops that takes gluten-free seriously: it offers a dedicated gluten-free ramen lineup (and a gluten-free omakase set) rather than just removing items, which is unusual for a wheat-heavy dish like ramen.
Reviewers report the kitchen uses separate cooking water for the gluten-free noodles and prepares allergy meals carefully.

It is not a fully dedicated gluten-free facility — gluten-free items run alongside regular wheat ramen — so some cross-contact risk remains in a shared kitchen. One reviewer flagged a concern about cross-contamination from wooden ramen spoons.

Reports on the fryer are mixed: at one location staff confirmed a separate fryer, while another review noted no dedicated fryer — so confirm fryer arrangements for fried items at the specific location before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen specifically uses gluten-free soy sauce for its gyoza and dim sum, which directly addresses the usual problem that most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Still confirm gluten-free soy is used on any dish you order, as standard wheat-based soy sauce is common in Japanese kitchens.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan ramen is a core offering built on kelp, miso, and soy sauce rather than meat or fish stock, but it is prepared in the same kitchen that handles wagyu and other meats, so strict vegans should confirm separate handling.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff ask about allergies and intolerances when you order and reportedly understand what celiac disease means, which is notable for a Japanese ramen shop.
The reservation process explicitly invites guests to flag any food allergies or intolerances in advance.
A reviewer described the team as super sharp about celiac, cross-contamination, and safe preparation practices.

positives.text: Widely praised as one of the best gluten-free ramen experiences in Kyoto, with reviewers saying the gluten-free ramen and dumplings tasted amazing.
Gluten-free and vegan items are clearly available across the menu, with the shop offering good choices for many diets.
Despite ramen being a wheat-heavy dish, this kitchen was built around inclusive dining and treats gluten-free and vegan guests as a primary audience rather than an afterthought.

noodles.notes: Made by a noted Kyoto noodle maker; whole-grain wheat noodles with a substantial, chewy bite. A separate rice-flour gluten-free noodle is offered for the gluten-free bowls.

ramen.signature_bowl: Grilled Japanese Black Wagyu Shoyu Ramen — premium A4 wagyu sirloin seared tableside with a blowtorch over a shoyu-tare broth built on oyster-and-shrimp dashi with chicken stock.
ramen.chashu: The heart is not braised chashu but slices of Japanese black wagyu sirloin seared to order with a torch at the table; a piece of slow-cooked pork belly also rides alongside.
notable_toppings: soft-cooked ajitama with the shop's rabbit logo branded on top (add-on)

gf.note: Genuinely uncommon: the shop is built around dietary inclusion and offers a dedicated gluten-free ramen using rice-flour noodles plus gluten-free seasoning, including a gluten-free omakase set. Confirm cross-contact and tare details when booking, and note the standard bowls do contain wheat.

vegan.note: A dedicated vegan ramen is on the menu, built on a base of naturally fermented miso and soy milk with a blend of several misos and soy sauce, completely free of animal ingredients. Pair with the gluten-free noodle option if needed.

ramen.pantheon: A thoroughly modern, original Kyoto bowl rather than a regional classic: its soul is a delicate seafood-forward soup of oyster-and-shrimp dashi rounded with chicken stock and finished with shoyu tare, a clean canvas for the showpiece of A4 black wagyu sirloin torched at your table. It is the rare ramen counter where a wagyu indulgence, a soy-milk-miso vegan bowl, and rice-flour gluten-free noodles all share one menu.

places.highlight: A thoroughly modern, original Kyoto bowl rather than a regional classic: its soul is a delicate seafood-forward soup of oyster-and-shrimp dashi rounded with chicken stock and finished with shoyu tare, a clean canvas for the showpiece of A4 black wagyu sirloin torched at your table. It is the rare ramen counter where a wagyu indulgence, a soy-milk-miso vegan bowl, and rice-flour gluten-free noodles all share one menu.

places.name: Nishiki Market Alley by MOON and BACK
places.gf_detail: GF ramen & items marked
places.vegan_detail: vegan ramen & dumplings marked
places.notes: Moon and Back branch by Nishiki Market (3F). Sushi/fish plus clearly-marked vegan dishes & GF ramen. Accommodates allergies; private karaoke room.

chef_bio.background: Nishiki Market Alley is part of the MOON and BACK family of ramen bars, the work of owner-chef Tatsuya Fukuda. Before returning home to Japan, Fukuda spent seven years as head chef at a high-end modern Japanese restaurant set right in front of the Sydney Opera House, and went on to help produce restaurants in Korea as well. That time cooking abroad, where so many guests asked for food they could safely eat, shaped the whole idea behind MOON and BACK: ramen that meat lovers, vegans, and gluten-free diners can all sit down and enjoy at the same table. The Kyoto company behind it, The Infinity Inc., was founded in 2019, and the Nishiki location sits just a short walk from the main entrance of Nishiki Market, with English-speaking staff used to welcoming travelers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fukuda's stated aim is to deliver safe and delicious meals to people who have limited food options because of nationality, religion, or allergies, and to build a food culture where, as he puts it, 'no one is left behind.' He has said he created the rice-noodle ramen after long years of experimentation specifically so that no one would have to miss out, and points to guests with wheat restrictions telling him it was the first bowl of ramen they had ever had.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is a Grilled Japanese Wagyu shoyu ramen, with the marbled beef blowtorched tableside, alongside a spicy dandan-style noodle. What really sets the kitchen apart, though, is that the vegan and gluten-free bowls are not afterthoughts: the vegan ramen is built on a base of naturally fermented miso and soy milk with no animal ingredients, and the gluten-free ramen uses special rice-based noodles that Fukuda developed so guests with a wheat restriction could finally have a real bowl of ramen.

anecdotes.text: Fukuda developed the gluten-free ramen using special rice-based noodles so guests who cannot eat wheat could still have a satisfying bowl; he recounts customers telling him 'this is the first ramen I've ever had in my life.'

Before opening in Kyoto, Fukuda spent seven years as head chef of a modern Japanese fine-dining restaurant directly in front of the Sydney Opera House, and returned to Japan on a mission to serve authentic, safe Japanese food to people with dietary restrictions.
The restaurant bills itself as a place 'where meat lovers, vegans, and gluten-free diners can all enjoy together,' a few steps from Nishiki Market.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a ramen shop with a mixed kitchen, not a dedicated gluten-free facility, so cross-contact is possible and severe celiacs should confirm protocols directly. That said, reviewers report the gluten-free noodles are boiled in a separate boiler from the regular wheat noodles.
At the Nishiki Market Alley location, reviewers report staff confirmed they use a separate fryer to help prevent cross-contamination.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce in Japanese ramen typically contains wheat. MOON and BACK offers gluten-free soy sauce on request, so ask for the gluten-free version with your order rather than assuming the default broth or sushi sauce is wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is mixed, serving meat alongside vegan dishes. One vegan reviewer noted that most other diners order the raw Wagyu beef ramen that is blowtorched tableside, which fills the small space with 'meat air' - worth knowing if that bothers you, though it does not affect the vegan ingredients themselves.
The vegan ramen is described as made entirely from naturally fermented miso and soy milk with no animal ingredients, and the menu labels vegan items clearly.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Multiple reviewers report staff immediately ask about allergies and dietary restrictions when you sit down, and that most staff speak good English, making it easier to discuss your needs.

Coeliac reviewers report that staff know what celiac disease means and ask about allergies when you order, and several said they felt very safe eating there - but always confirm your own protocols, as it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen.

positives.text: The menu is clearly labeled for what is vegan and what is gluten-free, and dedicated vegan and gluten-free ramen are standing menu items rather than improvised substitutions.

Gluten-free noodles are rice-based, gluten-free soy sauce is available on request, and reviewers report a separate noodle boiler and a separate fryer at the Nishiki location - meaningful precautions for a non-dedicated kitchen.
English-speaking staff and a stated whole-restaurant philosophy of safe food for guests with allergies and dietary restrictions make it an unusually accommodating ramen stop near Nishiki Market.

noodles.notes: Flat whole-wheat noodles sourced from Kyoto's celebrated Menya Teigaku, with notably strong body and elasticity that stand up to the clear seafood-chicken broth. A separate gluten-free, rice-based noodle is available for the gluten-free and vegan bowls.

ramen.signature_bowl: Seared A4 Kuroge Wagyu shoyu ramen (aburi kokusan kuroge wagyu shoyu ramen) — slices of premium Japanese black wagyu torched tableside and laid over a clear shoyu broth.
ramen.chashu: The heart is not a conventional braised chashu but thin slices of A4 Kuroge Wagyu beef, blowtorch-seared to order right at the table so the fat just melts; a soft slow-braised pork belly also rides alongside in the wagyu bowl.

gf.note: A dedicated gluten-free ramen made with special rice-based noodles is offered for wheat-sensitive guests; confirm the tare/broth are also wheat-free for strict celiac needs, as standard shoyu tare contains wheat.

vegan.note: A fully plant-based ramen is on the menu, built on a kombu broth with soy milk and a blend of naturally fermented miso and soy sauce, explicitly free of any animal ingredients; vegan gyoza, sushi and tempura are also clearly marked.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, original Kyoto bowl rather than a regional throwback: a clear, refined shoyu soup built from chicken stock layered with oyster-and-bonito seafood dashi, crowned by A4 wagyu seared in front of you so the beef fat enriches every spoonful. It reads like a fine-dining take on ramen, a chef's-counter luxe spin on the clean Kyoto shoyu idiom.

places.highlight: A modern, original Kyoto bowl rather than a regional throwback: a clear, refined shoyu soup built from chicken stock layered with oyster-and-bonito seafood dashi, crowned by A4 wagyu seared in front of you so the beef fat enriches every spoonful. It reads like a fine-dining take on ramen, a chef's-counter luxe spin on the clean Kyoto shoyu idiom.

places.name: Wagyu Ramen & Steak MOKUMOKU
places.gf_detail: ask
places.vegan_detail: vegan items available
places.notes: Wagyu ramen & stone-bowl steak (Nakagyo) with GF and vegan options. Soy-sauce broth, gyoza, sake flights. English-speaking, interactive service.

roles: owner-operator / operating group (chef name not published)
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (downtown Nakagyo-ku, near Karasuma Oike)
chef_bio.background: Wagyu Ramen & Steak MOKUMOKU is a downtown Kyoto restaurant (near Karasuma Oike) built inside a machiya the shop markets as a roughly 100-year-old former samurai house, decorated with authentic samurai armor. Its signature experience is A5 wagyu served both as ramen and as steak, with a table-side sizzling stone-bowl ('ishiyaki') presentation, an English menu and walk-in-friendly service aimed largely at international visitors. MOKUMOKU is promoted on the fireramen.com website alongside Kyoto's Menbaka Fire Ramen (Menbakaichidai), indicating it is operated or promoted by the same group behind Menbaka, a Kyoto fire-ramen restaurant that has run since 1984. No individual head chef or owner is named in available public sources.
chef_bio.philosophy: A theatrical, tourist-facing take on premium Kyoto beef: high-grade A5 wagyu presented as both ramen and steak with tableside stone-bowl searing in a historic machiya setting.
chef_bio.specialty: A5 wagyu ramen and A5 wagyu steak, cooked/finished tableside on a hot stone bowl.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a machiya it describes as a ~100-year-old samurai house complete with displayed samurai armor.
MOKUMOKU is listed on the same fireramen.com site as Menbaka Fire Ramen, whose fire-ramen concept dates to 1984, suggesting shared operation/promotion.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Public listings (fireramen.com English site, Instagram @moku_moku_kyoto, Tripadvisor) describe MOKUMOKU as an A5 wagyu ramen & steak restaurant in a historic Kyoto machiya near Karasuma Oike, associated with the Menbaka Fire Ramen group. No Japanese-language source found naming an individual chef/owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wagyu ramen and gyoza house, so wheat is central to the kitchen: standard ramen noodles and gyoza wrappers are made from wheat flour. Even though gluten-free noodles are offered on request, shared pots, water, and prep surfaces mean cross-contact is likely. Treat it as not safe for celiac unless staff can confirm a fully separate preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen and seasonings are soy-sauce based, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. The menu mentions gluten-free seasoning options on request, but unless that is specifically confirmed for your dish, assume the default sauces contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Plant-based dishes are advertised, but this is a beef- and broth-focused kitchen (wagyu steak, wagyu ramen). No information confirms separate utensils, grills, or pots for vegan items, so cross-contact with meat and animal-based broth is possible. Confirm preparation with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff speak English and there is a full English menu, which makes it easier to discuss allergies directly. No specific allergy-handling protocol is documented, so explain your needs clearly and ask how each dish is prepared.

positives.text: The restaurant states that gluten-free noodles and seasoning options are available upon request.
Specially crafted plant-based dishes are offered for vegetarian and vegan diners.
The broth is described as 100% chicken-based with no pork, and the wagyu is halal-certified, which can help diners avoiding pork.
English-speaking staff and a full English menu make it easier to communicate dietary restrictions.

noodles.notes: Noodle specifics are not documented in detail. The shop also offers gluten-free noodles on request, suggesting standard wheat-and-kansui noodles as the default.

ramen.signature_bowl: A5 Wagyu chicken-broth ramen, finished tableside in a sizzling hot stone bowl so the aromas bloom as it arrives.
ramen.chashu: The heart is A5 Japanese wagyu beef rather than pork chashu - tender, well-marbled slices prized for their melt-in-the-mouth richness and deep beef umami, laid over the bowl as the centerpiece.

gf.note: Unusual for ramen: the shop documents gluten-free noodle and seasoning options on request. Confirm cross-contact directly if you are celiac, as the kitchen also handles standard wheat noodles.

vegan.note: The shop documents specially crafted plant-based dishes alongside its beef-and-chicken menu. Confirm the ramen broth itself is plant-based (no chicken/fish dashi) when ordering, as the signature bowls are built on a chicken broth.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, original Kyoto bowl that swaps the usual pork chashu for prized A5 wagyu, ladling a clean, pork-free chicken broth around melting beef. Its soul is the gentle, refined chicken stock - a luxe take on Kyoto's chicken-and-soy lineage rather than any single regional tradition.

places.highlight: A modern, original Kyoto bowl that swaps the usual pork chashu for prized A5 wagyu, ladling a clean, pork-free chicken broth around melting beef. Its soul is the gentle, refined chicken stock - a luxe take on Kyoto's chicken-and-soy lineage rather than any single regional tradition.

places.name: GYUMON (Tomikoji main)
places.gf_detail: confirm (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: vegan spicy ramen option
places.notes: Hugely popular halal wagyu ramen (Nakagyo). Pork-free broths plus a vegan spicy ramen; spice 1–10. Prayer room. Very busy.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Nishiki Market / Tomikoji, Nakagyo-ku) — part of the GYUMON group founded in Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: GYUMON began in 2007 in Shibuya as what the group describes as Japan's first halal wagyu yakiniku restaurant, built around the idea that Muslim travelers and others with dietary restrictions should be able to enjoy premium Japanese beef without compromise. After years of refining their craft, the group turned that pork-free, A5-wagyu philosophy toward ramen, completing their halal wagyu ramen concept in 2023. The Kyoto location opened on October 12, 2024, a minute from the bustle of Nishiki Market, following sister shops in Tokyo and Osaka. The space is dressed in traditional Japanese furnishings and includes a clean, quiet prayer room — a deliberate touch so Muslim guests can sit down to a proper bowl of Japanese ramen in comfort. Alongside the beef bowls, the kitchen also serves vegan and vegetarian ramen, so mixed groups with very different diets can all eat at the same table.
chef_bio.philosophy: To share the flavors of Japanese cuisine — yakiniku, ramen, gyukatsu — with people from all over the world, using only A5-grade halal wagyu and keeping the kitchen pork-free so that Muslim diners, and others who are often left out, can fully take part.
chef_bio.specialty: Halal wagyu ramen built on a rich, creamy beef-bone broth (or an original spicy miso broth), topped with marbled A5 halal wagyu and homemade chewy, medium-thick wavy noodles made from carefully selected domestic wheat. The signature is the Wagyu Ramen & Gyukatsu set, pairing the ramen with a sirloin beef cutlet. There are also two vegan ramen — a plain and a spicy version finished with soy bits for texture.

anecdotes.text: The group opened its first restaurant in Shibuya in 2007 as Japan's first halal wagyu yakiniku house, and only later perfected a halal wagyu ramen, which it completed in 2023.
The Kyoto shop opened on October 12, 2024, just off Nishiki Market, and pairs its dining room with a dedicated prayer room for Muslim guests.
The broth is simmered for hours from beef bones, the noodles are made in-house from selected domestic wheat, and bowls are crowned with marbled A5 halal wagyu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-based ramen shop and is not celiac-safe. The house noodles are made from selected domestic wheat, and the kitchen's two signature broths include a spicy miso broth (miso commonly contains barley or wheat). Even non-noodle items share a kitchen built around wheat noodles, so cross-contamination is very likely. No gluten-free menu or dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.
The restaurant states allergy information is available on request and staff can help with dietary needs, but no specific gluten-free protocol, separate cookware, or celiac accommodation is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop uses a spicy miso broth and standard Japanese seasonings; like most Japanese soy sauce and miso, these typically contain wheat. Assume the seasonings are not gluten-free unless staff confirm a wheat-free version.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dedicated vegan ramen (a plain and a spicy version) is on the menu, so plant-based diners do have real options here — but this is primarily a beef and halal wagyu ramen kitchen, so vegan bowls are cooked alongside meat dishes and wheat noodles. Strict vegans concerned about shared equipment or broth should confirm preparation with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant offers an English menu with photos and says allergy information is available on request, with staff available to help address dietary needs. No specifics on how allergens are isolated in the kitchen are published.

positives.text: Pork-free, halal-certified kitchen using only A5-grade halal wagyu, with vegan and vegetarian ramen options and a prayer room — a genuinely inclusive setup for Muslim, vegan, and vegetarian diners.
A vegan diner praised the spicy vegan ramen as filling and full of flavor, with soy bits adding texture (note: feedback on the plain vegan ramen was mixed).

noodles.notes: Handmade chewy, curly medium-thick noodles made from selected domestic wheat, built to grip the heavy beef-bone broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Creamy Wagyu Beef-Bone Ramen (gyukotsu paitan) crowned with marbled A5 wagyu
ramen.chashu: Not classic pork chashu. The headline protein is sliced, marbled A5 wagyu beef (calbi-style cut) laid over the bowl, finished with a hidden blend of spices; no pork is used anywhere.

gf.note: Wheat-based handmade noodles and miso/beef seasonings are not gluten-free; no documented rice- or konjac-noodle option. Ask staff if celiac.

vegan.note: The shop brands itself as Halal Wagyu & Vegan Ramen and lists a dedicated vegan bowl on the menu, though the plant-broth composition is not published; confirm details when ordering.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, halal-original creation rather than any regional lineage: instead of pork or chicken, the soul here is a rich, creamy beef-bone (gyukotsu) broth simmered long enough to turn milky and emulsified, its body drawn entirely from wagyu bones and beef. Crowned with marbled A5 wagyu and offered in both a creamy and a spicy-miso form, it is one of the few ramen anywhere built around beef rather than the usual tonkotsu or tori.

places.highlight: A modern, halal-original creation rather than any regional lineage: instead of pork or chicken, the soul here is a rich, creamy beef-bone (gyukotsu) broth simmered long enough to turn milky and emulsified, its body drawn entirely from wagyu bones and beef. Crowned with marbled A5 wagyu and offered in both a creamy and a spicy-miso form, it is one of the few ramen anywhere built around beef rather than the usual tonkotsu or tori.

places.name: GYUMON Kyoto (Tatsumicho)
places.gf_detail: confirm (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: vegan ramen option
places.notes: GYUMON branch near Kyoto Station (Shimogyo). Halal wagyu ramen + vegan ramen; white-broth wagyu shoulder. Calm, English-friendly.

roles: Pork-free, Muslim-friendly kitchen with prayer room
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Tatsumicho, Shimogyo-ku) branch of a group founded in Tokyo (Shibuya), 2007
chef_bio.background: GYUMON began in 2007 in Tokyo's Shibuya, where a team of Japanese and Muslim chefs opened what they describe as Japan's first halal wagyu yakiniku restaurant. The aim was simple but ambitious: let Muslim visitors and travelers enjoy the flavors of Japanese cooking without compromise. Over the following years the group grew across Tokyo, Osaka and Kyoto, and in 2023 they finally landed on a halal wagyu ramen they were proud to serve. The Kyoto Station branch, tucked into Tatsumicho in Shimogyo-ku a short walk from the station, opened on February 1, 2026, carrying that same idea: entirely pork-free, halal-certified cooking, with a quiet prayer room kept available for guests at all times.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen is built around heartfelt hospitality and the belief that Japanese food should be open to everyone: every ingredient, seasoning and cooking method is kept halal-compliant under certification, and they take particular pride in sourcing A5 wagyu with a marbling score rarely seen on the Japanese market.
chef_bio.specialty: Wagyu ramen is the headliner here, built on a broth simmered from beef bones (there is also a spicy original-miso version) and crowned with marbled A5 halal wagyu rather than the usual pork chashu. The dish people come for is the Wagyu Ramen and Gyukatsu set, pairing the noodles with a crisp beef cutlet cut from premium A5 halal wagyu. Beef runs through everything they do, from the gyukatsu to the broth, and the kitchen also serves gyoza and tempura alongside vegan-friendly options.

anecdotes.text: The group traces back to 2007 and the opening of Gyumon Shibuya, which it bills as Japan's first halal wagyu yakiniku restaurant, created by Japanese and Muslim chefs working side by side.

After years of experimenting following their 2007 yakiniku debut, the team says they finally perfected a halal wagyu ramen in 2023, offering both a rich beef-bone broth and a spicy original-miso version topped with A5 wagyu instead of pork.
The Kyoto Station branch opened on February 1, 2026 in Tatsumicho, Shimogyo-ku, about seven minutes from the station, with a prayer room kept available for guests at all times.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a ramen and gyukatsu (breaded, deep-fried beef cutlet) house, so the kitchen routinely handles wheat noodles, flour and breadcrumbs. There is a high baseline risk of gluten cross-contact in shared pots, fryers and prep areas, and no source documents a gluten-free protocol.

The core dishes are not gluten-free or celiac-safe: standard ramen noodles are wheat-based, gyukatsu is breaded and fried, and tempura is wheat-battered. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the signature menu here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ramen broths and Japanese seasonings of this style typically rely on soy sauce and/or miso, most of which contain wheat. No source confirms a wheat-free soy sauce or tamari is used, so assume the seasonings contain wheat unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan ramen options are advertised at this location, but no source describes how the vegan dish is prepared. Because the kitchen is built around beef-bone broth, wagyu and shared cookware, vegan diners should ask whether the vegan ramen is cooked in separate water/pots and kept away from beef broth.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Tabelog listing notes that allergy information is available, and the venue offers English menus and English-speaking staff, which makes it easier to discuss ingredients before ordering.

As a halal-certified kitchen, the group states that all ingredients, seasonings and cooking methods are vetted for compliance, indicating an ingredient-aware kitchen, though this is a religious-dietary standard and is not the same as gluten-free or allergen-free handling.

positives.text: Entirely pork-free and halal-certified, with vegan and vegetarian options marketed at this branch, so the kitchen is used to accommodating dietary needs.
English menus and English-speaking staff make it practical to confirm ingredients and ask about cross-contact in person.
Halal certification means ingredients, seasonings and cooking methods are documented and vetted, so staff are likely able to answer specific ingredient questions.

noodles.notes: House-made chewy (mochi-mochi) medium-thick wavy noodles milled from select domestic wheat, built to grip the creamy beef broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Wagyu Beef Ramen — the rich creamy beef-bone (gyu-paitan) bowl crowned with A5 halal wagyu; a spicy-miso version with the house original spicy miso is the other pillar.
ramen.chashu: A5 marbled halal wagyu short rib (karubi) rather than pork; richly fatty beef laid over the broth instead of a conventional braised pork chashu.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the seasonings are wheat-based; no documented rice or konjac noodle option, so not celiac-safe. Ask staff if avoiding gluten.

vegan.note: A dedicated vegan ramen is on the menu (the shop brands itself Halal Wagyu & Vegan Ramen) with plant-based components and no pork anywhere in the kitchen; confirm the broth contains no animal stock when ordering.

ramen.pantheon: A rare beef-bone (gyu-paitan) ramen rather than the usual pork or chicken — long-simmered wagyu bones build a creamy, deeply savory soup whose soul is pure beef koku, finished with marbled A5 wagyu. As a fully halal, pork-free kitchen with a parallel vegan bowl, it sits outside any regional gotochi lineage, a modern Kyoto original speaking to travelers and Muslim diners alike.

places.highlight: A rare beef-bone (gyu-paitan) ramen rather than the usual pork or chicken — long-simmered wagyu bones build a creamy, deeply savory soup whose soul is pure beef koku, finished with marbled A5 wagyu. As a fully halal, pork-free kitchen with a parallel vegan bowl, it sits outside any regional gotochi lineage, a modern Kyoto original speaking to travelers and Muslim diners alike.

places.name: GYUMON Volcano
places.gf_detail: confirm (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: vegan ramen option
places.notes: Spicy-focused GYUMON branch (Shimogyo, near Kawaramachi). Halal wagyu + vegan ramen, spicy karaage. Non-spicy options too.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Kawaramachi (part of a group founded in Shibuya, Tokyo)
chef_bio.background: GYUMON began in Shibuya, Tokyo in 2007 as one of Japan's first halal wagyu yakiniku restaurants, built around a single idea: that Muslim travelers and residents shouldn't have to miss out on premium Japanese beef. Over the years the team kept experimenting with how to turn that same A5 halal wagyu into a great bowl of ramen, and in 2023 they finally landed on a recipe they were proud to serve. GYUMON Volcano in Kawaramachi is the spicy-focused expression of that journey, sitting about three minutes from Kawaramachi Station and pairing the kitchen with a thoughtfully designed prayer room so Muslim guests can relax comfortably.
chef_bio.philosophy: To share the flavors of Japanese cuisine, whether yakiniku, ramen, or gyukatsu, with people from all over the world, and to give Muslim and other guests who are often left out a delicious Japanese ramen experience they can enjoy without compromise.
chef_bio.specialty: Halal wagyu ramen topped with marbled A5 halal wagyu beef. The kitchen offers a rich, creamy beef-bone broth and a spicy 'Volcano' broth made with their original spicy miso, both served over homemade, chewy, medium-thick curly noodles. Gyukatsu (breaded, fried wagyu cutlet) is the other house specialty, and a vegan ramen option is also on the menu.

anecdotes.text: The group opened what it describes as Japan's first halal wagyu yakiniku restaurant in Shibuya back in 2007, then spent years chasing a halal wagyu ramen worthy of the same name before perfecting it in 2023.
Each bowl is finished with marbled A5 halal wagyu and a special blend of spices, and the Kawaramachi location includes a dedicated prayer room designed for Muslim guests to relax comfortably.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a ramen shop whose bowls are built on homemade, medium-thick curly wheat noodles, so the broth and serving area are inherently wheat-heavy. It should not be considered celiac-safe, and no source documents a gluten-free noodle, a separate gluten-free prep area, or any cross-contamination controls. Celiac diners should confirm directly before visiting.
The other house specialty, gyukatsu, is breaded and deep-fried, which adds wheat flour/breadcrumbs and frying-oil cross-contact to the kitchen. No source confirms whether a dedicated fryer is used.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source specifies the soy sauce or seasonings used. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and the spicy 'Volcano' broth is built on an original spicy miso (miso can also contain wheat/barley), so gluten-free diners should assume wheat is present in the seasonings unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A vegan ramen option is advertised, but no source describes how it is prepared relative to the wagyu and beef-bone-broth dishes. Strict vegans should ask whether the vegan bowl is cooked with separate pots, utensils, and noodle water, since none of that is documented.

positives.text: The kitchen is pork-free and oriented around halal preparation, and the menu explicitly includes vegan options, so the staff are accustomed to accommodating dietary restrictions and are a reasonable group to discuss your needs with directly.
The Kawaramachi location lists a vegan option directly on its profile, signaling that plant-based requests are part of normal service rather than an unusual ask.

noodles.notes: House-made, chewy, medium-thick curly (wavy) noodles built to grip the rich beef broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Spicy Wagyu Ramen (the namesake "Volcano") - a spicy beef-bone broth seasoned with the shop's original spicy miso, crowned with marbled A5 Wagyu and a house spice blend; a creamy (non-spicy) beef-bone version is also offered.
ramen.chashu: No traditional braised pork chashu - the bowl's heart is slices of marbled A5 Wagyu beef laid over the ramen (the shop is entirely pork-free/halal).

gf.note: Noodles are house-made wheat-and-kansui and the spicy bowl is seasoned with miso, so it is not celiac-safe; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: A dedicated plant-based vegan ramen is on the menu - a savory vegetable broth packed with fresh vegetables and plant toppings, separate from the beef bowls. Confirm noodle/tare specifics on the day.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, one-of-a-kind Wagyu ramen rather than any regional gotochi style: instead of pork or chicken, the soul here is a rich beef-bone broth, the same lineage as the kitchen's Wagyu yakiniku roots. The signature Volcano pours that beefy depth together with a fiery original miso and crowns it with marbled A5 Wagyu - a luxurious, halal take on the bowl found nowhere in the traditional ramen map.

places.highlight: A modern, one-of-a-kind Wagyu ramen rather than any regional gotochi style: instead of pork or chicken, the soul here is a rich beef-bone broth, the same lineage as the kitchen's Wagyu yakiniku roots. The signature Volcano pours that beefy depth together with a fiery original miso and crowns it with marbled A5 Wagyu - a luxurious, halal take on the bowl found nowhere in the traditional ramen map.

places.name: KAWARAMACHI BEEF-TEI
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan & vegetarian items (tagged)
places.notes: Halal wagyu steak & burger house (Kawaramachi) that also tags vegan/vegetarian options. Cheese-torch show; serves alcohol. Pricey.

chef_bio.background: Kawaramachi Beef-tei is the Kyoto home of a family whose Wagyu story reaches back to 1962. The first generation began as a Wagyu merchant - a craftsman who stood face to face with the cattle, judged the quality of the meat by eye and hand, and passed that skill down through the family as the business grew from meat wholesaling into butcher shops and, eventually, restaurants. The proprietor today is the fifth generation, raised quite literally alongside meat. The turning point came on a trip to the United States: the 'Wagyu' he met there was nothing like the Wagyu he had grown up with - cattle from Australia and America were being sold under the same name, and diners were eating it none the wiser. That shock became the reason to open a restaurant of his own, to put genuine Japanese Wagyu in front of travelers from around the world. The kitchen serves it as familiar, approachable food - steak, burgers and ramen - so the quality of the beef speaks for itself. The restaurant is alcohol-free and built to welcome everyone, with the meat sourced as halal-certified Wagyu.
chef_bio.philosophy: To share the experience of real Japanese Wagyu with the world, choosing only meat he is confident in and presenting it through everyday dishes so its quality is unmistakable - in a comfortable, alcohol-free room open to all.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% Japanese Wagyu served in familiar forms - the Japanese Wagyu cheeseburger (a Wagyu patty layered with cheese, lettuce, tomato and house sauce) and a bigger double-cheese version are the signatures, alongside Wagyu steak and Wagyu ramen. The beef is halal-certified Wagyu, hand-selected by the owner.

anecdotes.text: Since 1962 the family has built a Wagyu legacy; as the fifth generation, the owner grew up with authentic Wagyu, and after being shocked by fake 'Wagyu' sold abroad in the U.S., opened a store to share genuine Japanese Wagyu - serving familiar dishes like steak and burgers to showcase its quality.

The founding story traces to 1962, when the first generation worked as a Wagyu merchant honing his eye for meat; the fifth generation, raised around it, was moved to act after seeing Australian and American cattle circulated as 'Wagyu' in America.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant markets itself as offering Halal, Vegan and Gluten-Free options, but no published source describes how gluten-free items are kept separate from wheat-based ones (buns, ramen noodles, sauces) during prep. A celiac diner should confirm dedicated handling with staff before ordering.

The menu includes a shared French fries item served alongside burgers; whether fries share a fryer with any battered or breaded item is not documented, so cross-contact in the fryer cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Burgers are served with a house 'sauce' and the menu also features ramen; the specific ingredients of these sauces and broths are not published. As most Japanese soy sauce and many Japanese sauces contain wheat, anyone strictly avoiding gluten should verify each sauce and the ramen broth/noodles directly with the restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is fundamentally a Wagyu beef restaurant (steak, Wagyu burgers, ramen) that advertises a vegan option. Any vegan dish would be prepared in a kitchen that primarily handles beef, so shared grills, utensils and surfaces are a realistic cross-contact concern; confirm separate preparation with staff if strict.

staff_allergy_handling.text: For allergen questions the restaurant directs guests to phone the store directly rather than relying on the online listing, indicating allergen details are handled case by case with staff (store phone listed on its delivery page).

positives.text: The restaurant explicitly advertises Halal, Vegan and Gluten-Free options, so accommodating these diets is part of its stated offering - a good starting point for a conversation with staff.
As an alcohol-free, Muslim-friendly venue that hand-selects halal-certified Wagyu, the kitchen is already used to sourcing-conscious, restriction-aware service.

places.name: Kyoto Wagyu Steak Sandwich Pizza (Halal/Vegan/GF) – Kiyomizu
places.gf_detail: gluten-free options advertised
places.vegan_detail: vegan options advertised
places.notes: Wagyu steak/sandwich/pizza spot below Kiyomizu-dera explicitly offering halal, vegan AND gluten-free options. Rice bowls, burgers, pizza.

chef_bio.background: This restaurant belongs to the Beef-Tei / 5W family of Wagyu houses, whose story begins in 1962 when the first generation set out as a Wagyu merchant — a craftsman who stood before the cattle, judged the quality of the meat, and honed his eye and his knife. Over the decades the family business grew through meat wholesale, butcher shops, and finally restaurants, and the fifth generation was, quite literally, raised alongside the meat. For him Wagyu was never a once-a-year luxury; he had known its taste since childhood. The turning point came in his thirties on a trip to America, where he found beef from Australia and the United States being sold and celebrated as 'WAGYU' — utterly different from the real thing he had grown up with. That realization became the family's mission today: to put genuine 100% Japanese Wagyu in front of travelers from around the world, in an approachable form everyone can enjoy.
chef_bio.philosophy: Real Japanese Wagyu should be something everyone can enjoy, not a rarefied once-a-year treat — and not the imitation 'Wagyu' sold abroad. The family's aim is to share authentic 100% Japanese Wagyu with visitors of every background and dietary need.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% Japanese Wagyu served in casual, traveler-friendly forms — the signatures here are the Wagyu steak sandwich and Wagyu pizza, alongside the group's hamburgers, steaks and ramen. The Wagyu is sourced halal, and the kitchen also puts out vegan and gluten-free versions of its dishes.

anecdotes.text: The family's Wagyu trade dates to 1962, when the first generation began as a Wagyu merchant — a craftsman who faced the cattle, discerned the quality of the meat, and honed his skills; the business grew into wholesale, butcher shops and restaurants over five generations.

In his thirties the fifth-generation owner traveled to America and was shaken to find Australian and American beef circulating as 'WAGYU,' completely unlike the real Wagyu he had known since childhood — the experience that shaped the restaurant's mission to serve genuine 100% Japanese Wagyu.
The Kyoto houses serve 100% Japanese Wagyu in approachable forms — steak sandwich, pizza, hamburger, steak and ramen — with halal-sourced Wagyu and vegan and gluten-free options.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a Wagyu steak, sandwich, pizza, burger and ramen house — an environment built around wheat (bread, pizza dough, ramen, breading) and shared cooking surfaces. Gluten-free items are advertised, but no source confirms a separated gluten-free prep area or how cross-contact is controlled, so celiac diners should not assume it is celiac-safe and should confirm in person.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wagyu, ramen and burger dishes here are typically seasoned with Japanese-style sauces, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No source specifies whether a wheat-free/tamari soy sauce is used, so anyone strictly avoiding gluten should ask which sauces and seasonings are wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan options are advertised, and the group is known to offer a vegetarian tofu-based burger, but this is primarily a Wagyu beef kitchen where vegan items are likely cooked alongside meat. No source describes separate utensils or surfaces for vegan dishes, so cross-contact with beef should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise.

positives.text: The restaurant openly advertises halal, vegan and gluten-free options and is set up to accommodate travelers with varied dietary needs, so staff are accustomed to such requests.
The Wagyu is sourced halal, and the group offers gluten-free buns and a plant-based/tofu burger alternative, giving GF and vegan diners a starting point to discuss with staff.

places.name: Wagyu Steak & Hamburger (Halal/Vegan/GF) – Arashiyama
places.gf_detail: gluten-free options advertised
places.vegan_detail: vegan options advertised
places.notes: Riverside Arashiyama wagyu burger/steak house advertising halal, vegan & gluten-free options. Cheese-torch burger; great views.

chef_bio.background: This is the flagship (honten) of the OAGARI Group, tucked near the Katsura/Hozu River within walking distance of the Togetsukyo Bridge and Tenryu-ji Temple. The whole place is built around one idea: that everyone, whatever their dietary needs or beliefs, should be able to sit down at the same table and share a real Kyoto wagyu meal. To make that possible the restaurant offers halal, vegan, vegetarian and gluten-free options side by side, keeps an on-site prayer room with ablution facilities for Muslim guests, and uses no alcohol in its cooking. Counter seats look out over the riverfront, and staff work in multiple languages, so it has become a go-to for international visitors exploring Arashiyama.
chef_bio.philosophy: As the restaurant puts it, they prepare a wide range of food options 'so that everyone, whatever their allergies, religion, or lifestyle, can enjoy a meal together' - aiming to create a space and a moment where all guests can dine side by side with peace of mind.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 'Wagyu Volcano' (和牛火山) - A4-rank or higher Japanese black wagyu, seared and dramatically flamed atop rice, then finished tableside as a hitsumabushi by pouring over a natural broth made from kombu and shiitake mushrooms. The kitchen is particular about the rice too, cooking it in a traditional hagama (kamado-style) rice pot for a naturally sweet flavor to pair with the beef.

anecdotes.text: Hidden by the river in scenic Arashiyama, OAGARI serves Kyoto's 'Wagyu Hitsumabushi' - melt-in-your-mouth A4 wagyu flaming up like a volcano atop fluffy rice, finished with a rich kombu and shiitake broth. An on-site prayer room with ablution facilities plus a halal menu let Muslim guests dine with peace of mind, while vegan and gluten-free options aim to welcome everyone.

The house broth poured over the wagyu is described as a natural dashi made from kombu (kelp) and shiitake mushrooms, turning the dish into a hitsumabushi-style rice bowl, and the rice is cooked in a traditional hagama pot for a naturally sweet flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant openly states that gluten-free items are prepared in the same kitchen as foods containing gluten. They use separate cooking equipment, such as frying oil and pans, and clean equipment thoroughly before each preparation - but because the kitchen is shared, a risk of trace cross-contact remains and the restaurant does not claim suitability for celiac disease.

For gluten-free dishes the restaurant says it uses ingredients that do not contain wheat, barley, rye, or non-gluten-free oats, and that it checks manufacturers' publicly available ingredient lists for seasonings. This relies on manufacturer declarations rather than independent testing or certification.

Gluten-free needs must be communicated to staff before ordering; the restaurant states it cannot take responsibility for orders where no prior notification was given. If you are celiac, flag it clearly when you arrive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The published gluten-free policy says wheat, barley and rye are excluded from gluten-free dishes and that seasoning ingredient lists are checked, which implies a wheat-free seasoning is used for those dishes - but the sources do not specifically name the soy sauce or confirm it is wheat-free. Since standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, confirm directly with staff which seasoning is used on your dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan and vegetarian options are offered alongside meat dishes, but the kitchen is shared with wagyu and other animal products, so strict vegans should expect possible cross-contact and confirm preparation with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff aim to accommodate allergies where possible and ask guests to inform them at the time of ordering. They note, however, that because the kitchen shares equipment, severe allergies may not be fully supported.

Halal handling is taken seriously: staff are trained in halal procedures, the menu uses carefully selected halal-certified wagyu, no alcohol is used in cooking, and an on-site prayer room with ablution facilities is provided.

positives.text: This is a genuinely allergy- and diet-aware restaurant: it maintains a dedicated gluten-free commitment that uses separate frying oil and pans for gluten-free orders and cleans equipment before each preparation.

Halal, vegan, vegetarian and gluten-free options are all available on one menu, with multilingual staff and menus (English, Chinese, Korean), making it unusually accommodating for travelers with dietary restrictions.
No alcohol is used in the dishes, which the restaurant states as part of its halal and inclusive approach.

places.name: Chikurintei
places.gf_detail: gluten-free option available
places.vegan_detail: two vegan ramen options
places.notes: Arashiyama ramen shop (near bamboo grove) known for creamy chicken broth, but also two vegan ramen and a GF option. Quaint, fast.

chef_bio.background: Tucked into the Saga side of Arashiyama, a short walk from the Torokko Saga railway, this small counter ramen shop is run by a husband-and-wife team in which the owner is a trained Japanese-cuisine (washoku) craftsman, with his wife — who hails from Mie Prefecture — working alongside him. That washoku background shapes everything in the bowl: rather than a heavy, rustic ramen, the kitchen treats the broth like a refined Japanese soup, layering Nagoya Cochin and Oyama chicken bones with aromatic vegetables, kombu and bonito for a clean, clear stock. The seasonings read more like a fine-dining pantry than a typical noodle bar — Guérande sea salt from Brittany for the salt bowls, and naturally brewed 'white tamari' (shiro-tamari) aged in wooden barrels from Iga in Mie. The room itself nods to its name and setting, with big glass windows and walls and doors dressed in split bamboo, echoing the famous Arashiyama bamboo grove.
chef_bio.philosophy: Apply the discipline of Japanese washoku cooking — careful stock-making and high-grade, named ingredients — to a bowl of ramen.
chef_bio.specialty: Light, refreshing clear-broth (tori-seitan) chicken ramen topped with additive-free Kyoto-pork rare chashu is the signature. The kitchen also offers richer creamy chicken (tori-paitan) bowls and a premium Ise (spiny) lobster paitan made by simmering large quantities of lobster heads into a deep, creamy broth. Noodles are sourced from the Kiyokawa noodle works in Chichibu, Saitama.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run by a husband-and-wife team; the owner is a Japanese-cuisine (washoku) craftsman, and the proprietress is originally from Mie Prefecture — the same region that supplies the naturally brewed white tamari used to season the broth.

The signature clear chicken broth is built from Nagoya Cochin and Oyama chicken bones with aromatic vegetables, kombu and bonito, and finished with either Guérande salt from Brittany or barrel-aged white tamari from Iga, Mie; a premium bowl uses Ise spiny-lobster heads for a creamy paitan.
The proprietress being from Mie is described as the connection behind the shop's ingredient choices.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Osumituki confirms the washoku-craftsman owner and wife, the Nagoya Cochin/Oyama chicken broth with aromatic vegetables, kombu and bonito, Guérande salt, Iga white tamari, Ise-lobster paitan, additive-free Kyoto-pork rare chashu, and Chichibu Kiyokawa noodles; the livedoor blog confirms the proprietress is from Mie.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop, so it should be treated as NOT celiac-safe. Even the vegan ramen is explicitly made with whole-wheat (zenryufun) noodles, meaning every bowl on the menu contains wheat. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.

Wheat noodles are sourced from a noodle maker (Kiyokawa Seimen in Chichibu); the same noodle pots and broths serve all bowls, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable for a strict celiac diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The salt-based bowls are seasoned with 'white tamari' (shiro-tamari), a naturally brewed seasoning from Iga, Mie. White tamari is typically brewed mostly from wheat, so it should be assumed to contain wheat/gluten unless the shop confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A dedicated vegan ramen is on the menu (including a 100% vegan Shinshu-miso version and a 'tonkotsu-style' vegetable bowl), and the shop reportedly gets frequent vegan inquiries from international visitors. However, the broths for the regular menu are made from chicken bones, pork and bonito/dashi, and there is no documented separation of cookware or noodle water, so vegan diners should confirm how the vegan bowl is cooked relative to the meat and fish broths.

positives.text: A genuine, clearly labeled vegan ramen is part of the standard menu, and the shop is used to fielding vegan questions from overseas guests — a good sign for being able to discuss dietary needs.

noodles.notes: Thin, springy noodles sourced from Kiyokawa Seimen in Chichibu, Saitama. A whole-grain noodle is used for the vegan bowl.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ise-ebi shiro-yu (spiny-lobster paitan) ramen with additive-free Kyoto-pork rare chashu — a thick, creamy chicken-and-pork paitan deepened with a generous pour of spiny-lobster head extract, finished with sea-urchin terrine and your choice of salt or white-tamari soy tare.
ramen.chashu: Additive-free Kyoto-pork (kyo-buta) loin served as a seared rare chashu, prized for its sweet, well-marbled fat.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles plus white-tamari soy tare; no documented rice or konjac noodle option, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A dedicated vegan ramen with a vegetable-based broth and whole-grain noodles is on the menu, offered for the many international visitors to Arashiyama. Confirm tare and oil are fully plant-based when ordering.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, chef-driven bowl from a washoku craftsman who treats ramen like kaiseki — Nagoya-cochin and Daiyama chicken bones simmered with Ibaraki pork into a velvety paitan, then lifted by a luxurious flood of spiny-lobster essence you can taste in every spoonful. It belongs to the new wave of premium Japanese-cuisine ramen rather than any regional gotochi lineage, its soul a refined seafood-and-poultry richness seasoned with Breton Guerande salt or Iga white tamari.

places.highlight: A modern, chef-driven bowl from a washoku craftsman who treats ramen like kaiseki — Nagoya-cochin and Daiyama chicken bones simmered with Ibaraki pork into a velvety paitan, then lifted by a luxurious flood of spiny-lobster essence you can taste in every spoonful. It belongs to the new wave of premium Japanese-cuisine ramen rather than any regional gotochi lineage, its soul a refined seafood-and-poultry richness seasoned with Breton Guerande salt or Iga white tamari.

places.name: Falafel Garden
places.gf_detail: falafel/hummus/salads naturally GF (pita is not)
places.vegan_detail: many clearly-marked vegan dishes
places.hours_raw: Thu–Tue 11:30–20:30 (Fri–Sun to 21:00); closed Wed
places.notes: Middle-Eastern cafe near Demachiyanagi (Tanaka). Falafel, hummus, mixed plates; lots of marked vegan options + chicken. Sourdough pita.

chef_bio.background: Falafel Garden is widely regarded as Kyoto's Israeli restaurant — one of the best-known places for Middle Eastern food in the wider Kinki region. It was founded by an Israeli owner (who speaks fluent English) and first opened on September 1, 2004. In April 2014 it relocated to its current, roomier home near Demachiyanagi Station, a traditional Kyoto machiya townhouse that quietly blends Japanese and Israeli sensibilities. There's a garden out the back, an upstairs balcony where you can watch the little trains rattle past, and a small grocery corner stocked with homemade sweets and imported Israeli goods. The kitchen grows its own herbs and leans on fresh produce, giving the whole place a relaxed, healthy, slightly bohemian feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is to feed people food made from earth-friendly ingredients — grown without additives or pesticides — and to cook without chemical seasonings, so that 'all kinds of people' (meat-eaters, vegetarians, and vegans alike) can sit down and enjoy a meal together.
chef_bio.specialty: Falafel — golden chickpea croquettes (the chickpeas are blended with their skins on for extra fiber), served in pita sandwiches packed with fresh vegetables, alongside chicken kebab plates, hummus, baba ganoush, and Israeli sweets like baklava.

anecdotes.text: The owner is Israeli and speaks fluent English, and the restaurant is set in a cozy traditional machiya townhouse that blends Japanese and Israeli traditions, with a garden and balcony seating.
The owner's wish is for many people to eat earth-friendly ingredients that don't use additives or pesticides; the kitchen grows its own herbs and cooks without chemical seasonings.
The falafel are made by blending chickpeas with their skins on for extra dietary fiber, and have been praised by diners as 'the most delicious falafels I have ever had.'

The restaurant opened on September 1, 2004 (open date listed on Tabelog), and later moved north of Demachiyanagi on Kawabata, reopening on April 28, 2014, in a bigger, more comfortable space with a large garden out back.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Falafel is deep-fried, and the menu also serves wheat-based pita and fried sides (fries) in the same kitchen. No source describes a separate fryer or dedicated gluten-free prep area, so cross-contamination in the oil and on shared surfaces should be assumed possible. Falafel, hummus, and salads are naturally gluten-free by recipe, but the pita is not — celiac diners should confirm fry-oil separation with staff before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: This is Middle Eastern / Israeli cooking (falafel, hummus, kebab, tahini-based sauces) rather than Japanese cuisine, so wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce is not a typical ingredient. No source confirms an entirely soy-free or wheat-free sauce lineup, so anyone highly sensitive should still verify sauces directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant openly serves vegetarian, vegan, and omnivore diners side by side, with vegan items clearly marked on the menu and pita sandwiches described as well suited to vegans. Chicken kebab is cooked in the same kitchen, so strict vegans concerned about shared grills or utensils should ask, but the place is set up to handle plant-based orders.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One source notes the menu offers egg-free, milk-free, wheat-free, and 'allergen-free' options and marks vegan dishes clearly, suggesting staff are accustomed to dietary requests. This is a menu-labeling claim, not a verified allergen-control protocol, so celiac and severe-allergy diners should still confirm specifics in person.

positives.text: Cooks without chemical seasonings and emphasizes additive- and pesticide-free ingredients, with vegan dishes clearly labeled on the menu.
The owner speaks fluent English and there is a picture menu, which makes discussing dietary needs and allergies much easier for international diners.
Core dishes — falafel, hummus, baba ganoush, and vegetable-packed salads/pita fillings — are naturally plant-based and, apart from the wheat pita, naturally gluten-free by recipe.

places.name: Telaviv
places.gf_detail: many naturally GF (falafel, hummus, salads)
places.vegan_detail: falafel/hummus/veg mezze are vegan
places.notes: Warm owner-run Israeli izakaya (Sanjo). Falafel, hummus and mezze (vegan) alongside grilled meats; ingredients shipped from Israel.
