chef_bio.background: TEL AVIV is the small, lively Israeli kitchen of Moshe, a chef born in 1982 just outside Tel Aviv and a father of two. He landed in Japan in December 2006 - famously stepping off the plane in summer clothes into the winter cold, speaking not a word of Japanese - and never left Kyoto. Today he is a long-time local who chats in fluent Kansai dialect, and his place tucked into the Sanjo dining alley feels exactly like what he set out to build: somewhere you can eat, drink and walk out feeling lighter than when you came in. It is intimate, with just a handful of counter seats downstairs and a small room of tables upstairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: Moshe cooks to coax out aroma and natural flavor with herbs and spices rather than over-processing ingredients, leaning on quality vegetables and olive oil. He describes the experience he wants to give guests as the comfort of drinking at a friend's home crossed with the thrill of being somewhere foreign - a warm, energizing spot to land after a hard day.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic Israeli home and street cooking built on imported spices, good vegetables and olive oil: hummus (plain, with meat, or with mushrooms), falafel, shawarma, sabich, shakshuka, baba ganoush, the bright Tel Aviv salad, and warm pita. There is also a small list of Israeli wines that are hard to find anywhere else in town.

anecdotes.text: Moshe arrived in Japan in December 2006 dressed for summer and unable to speak Japanese, and has lived in Kyoto ever since, now bantering with guests in Kansai dialect.
Guests describe the room as feeling like drinking at a friend's house - lively, welcoming, and run by an owner clearly passionate about cooking the food of home.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No specific reports about fryer sharing or cross-contamination in the kitchen were found, so celiac diners should confirm directly with the owner. Note that several signature items are not gluten-free as served - pita bread is wheat, and sabich and shawarma are typically served in or with pita.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: This is Israeli cooking built on spices, vegetables and olive oil rather than Japanese soy-sauce or dashi bases, so wheat-containing soy sauce is far less likely to appear than in a typical Japanese eatery - but no source confirms the seasonings used, so confirm with staff if you are strictly avoiding wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen serves meat dishes (shawarma, meat hummus) alongside vegan items, and no source describes how preparation is separated, so vegan diners avoiding all animal contact should ask the owner how dishes are cooked.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner Moshe is hands-on, speaks fluent Japanese and English, and the menu is multilingual, which makes it easy to discuss ingredients and dietary needs in person.

positives.text: Naturally strong for plant-based diners: the menu explicitly recommends its healthy, satisfying vegetable dishes for vegan and vegetarian guests, and staples like hummus, falafel, baba ganoush and salads are vegetable-forward.
The listing notes that vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free and kosher options are offered, so there is room to ask for accommodations.

places.name: Rin Halal Japanese & Vegetarian
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegetarian; ask for vegan
places.notes: Halal Japanese spot in Gion with a vegetarian menu. Halal wagyu bowls/curry plus veg options; affordable. Confirm vegan/GF on the day.

chef_bio.background: Rin Halal Japanese & Vegetarian is a Muslim-friendly restaurant in Kyoto's Gion area, presenting Japanese cuisine (including wagyu beef) prepared to halal standards alongside vegetarian options, aimed at Muslim and vegetarian travelers visiting Kyoto. The restaurant maintains a public Instagram presence under the name 'RIN HALAL JAPANESE RESTAURANT' (@rinhalaljapan), but no individual owner or head-chef name, nor a detailed founding history, is published in available sources.
chef_bio.philosophy: Making Japanese dining, including wagyu, accessible to Muslim and vegetarian visitors to Kyoto through halal-conscious preparation.
chef_bio.specialty: Halal-prepared Japanese dishes (including wagyu beef) with vegetarian options.
chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: No Japanese-language press or owner biography located. The restaurant is documented mainly through its own Instagram (@rinhalaljapan), which frames it as a Gion halal Japanese/vegetarian spot. A general Kyoto Muslim-dining directory (muslim-guide.jp) and a halal-food directory did not carry a live listing at check time. Owner and founding details are not published.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a Kyoto-style izakaya whose signature dish is a wagyu offal hot pot (horumon nabe) built on a white-miso broth. Miso, broth seasonings, and izakaya cooking like this almost always involve wheat-containing ingredients and shared pots and surfaces, so it should not be considered celiac-safe. Confirm every ingredient directly with the kitchen before relying on it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes here lean on miso and typical Japanese seasonings; most Japanese soy sauce and miso-based broths contain wheat. Assume gluten is present unless staff confirm a specific wheat-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on wagyu and offal hot pot, and broths in this style are typically made with bonito/meat-based dashi. There is no documented dedicated vegan menu or separate-prep process, so vegan diners should confirm in advance what, if anything, can be made plant-based.

places.name: Vermillion Cafe (Fushimi Inari)
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan items (hummus plate, muffins, oat milk)
places.notes: Melbourne-style brunch cafe by Fushimi Inari, pond-side terrace. Avo toast, hummus plate, vegan muffins, oat milk. Lovely recharge spot.

chef_bio.background: Vermillion is a family story rooted at the foot of Fushimi Inari Shrine. Shigeo Kimura is the ninth generation of a family whose tea house, Yakurikitei, has welcomed shrine visitors and worshippers for over a hundred years. After spending around 18 years in Melbourne, Australia, where he met his wife and co-founder Miwa, the couple was encouraged by Australian friends who had visited Kyoto to bring their love of Melbourne cafe culture home. Shigeo returned to his hometown in 2012, and the following year they opened Vermillion - espresso bar & info. (2013), at a time when there was no specialty espresso coffee or English-speaking cafe near the shrine. They followed it with Vermillion - cafe. in 2016 and later the SHU. by Vermillion gift shop in 2019, all while keeping the original family tea house going.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe's guiding idea is "Coffee, Culture, and Communication" - blending the social warmth of a Melbourne cafe with the hospitality legacy of the family tea house, creating a welcoming space where travelers can relax, recharge, and connect over good coffee, good food, and good company.
chef_bio.specialty: Australian-style brunch and Melbourne-style espresso coffee, served with a strong vegetable and vegetarian focus. The cafe is known for its specialty coffee, Uji matcha lattes, smashed avocado toast, a hummus plate with seasonal vegetables, and house cakes and sweets, enjoyed on a spacious terrace overlooking Shin-Ike pond near the shrine.

anecdotes.text: The cafe takes its name from the vivid vermilion color of the thousands of torii gates lining the paths of neighboring Fushimi Inari Shrine.

Shigeo and Miwa Kimura met in Melbourne and were inspired to return to his hometown by Australian friends who told them how special it would be to build something in a place like Kyoto; he came back in 2012 after about 18 years abroad.
Shigeo is the ninth generation of the Kimura family, whose Yakurikitei tea house has served visitors to Fushimi Inari Shrine for over 100 years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No specific gluten-free protocols or cross-contamination information was found in available sources. The menu is bread- and baked-goods-forward (avocado toast, sandwiches, baguettes, muffins, cakes, banh mi), so anyone needing strict gluten-free should confirm individual ingredients and preparation directly with staff before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No reviewer comments about vegan cross-contact were found. Note that avocado toast is described as vegan only upon request, so vegan diners should specify their needs when ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff generally speak English and accommodate requests; reviewers mentioned being able to request alternative milks (soy milk available for a fee, oat milk also offered) and substitutions such as honey.

positives.text: Clearly marked and reliably available vegan options, including cauliflower soup, granola, a hummus plate with vegetables, vegan banana-chocolate muffins, beetroot and mushroom vegan sandwiches, and a vegan banh mi.
Plant-based milk alternatives (soy milk for a fee, oat milk) are available for coffee and matcha drinks.
The cafe places particular emphasis on vegetables and vegetarian dishes on its brunch menu, with at least one vegan or vegetarian option always available.

places.name: Arashiyama Garden Cafe
places.gf_detail: confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan/vegetarian options available
places.notes: All-day cafe in Arashiyama (Sagano). Curry, sandwiches, pizza, breakfast with vegan/vegetarian options. One item per person rule.

chef_bio.background: Arashiyama Garden Cafe is a casual cafe in the heart of Kyoto's Arashiyama sightseeing district, described as located within the Arashiyama Square West Park area. It serves an original house curry, hot dogs and a range of drinks, and is listed by some guides under pizza/Italian and curry categories as a light, walk-in tourist cafe. It is a distinct business from the nearby, better-known 'Bread, Espresso & Arashiyama Garden' bakery-cafe. No individual owner or chef name is published in available sources.
chef_bio.philosophy: Casual, accessible cafe fare for Arashiyama sightseers, centered on an original curry and easygoing menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Original house curry, hot dogs and drinks (also listed for pizza/Italian-style light dishes).

anecdotes.text: The cafe is often confused with the adjacent 'Bread, Espresso & Arashiyama Garden,' which occupies a renovated ~210-year-old former Kobayashi family house designated a Kyoto cultural property; Arashiyama Garden Cafe is a separate, more casual venue.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Autoreserve and Tabelog list Arashiyama Garden Cafe as a casual Ukyo-ku/Arashiyama cafe serving original curry, hot dogs and drinks in the Arashiyama Square West Park area. GLTJP confirms the separate 'Bread, Espresso & Arashiyama Garden' occupies a ~210-year-old Kobayashi-family cultural-property house. No Japanese-language owner biography or founding story located.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a casual counter in an open-air food court where curry, hot dogs and other quick bites are served side by side. Curry sauces are usually thickened with wheat flour and hot dogs come on wheat buns, so a shared cooking and prep area like this carries a real risk of gluten cross-contact. Treat it as not safe for celiac diners unless staff can confirm a specific gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information about the soy sauce or seasonings used here is documented. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so assume curry, sauces and toppings may include wheat-based seasoning until staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan-specific dishes or preparation details are documented for this spot, and the menu leans on curry and hot dogs. Shared equipment in a food-court setting makes cross-contact with meat and dairy likely. Vegan diners should confirm ingredients and handling directly with the counter.

places.name: Kacto
places.gf_detail: staff tailor menu to GF; confirm
places.vegan_detail: vegan/vegetarian items; staff advise
places.notes: Western riverside cafe (Kiyamachi). Pancakes/brunch by day, Mediterranean small plates by night; staff walk you through GF/veg options.

chef_bio.background: Kacto sits in a renovated two-story machiya (traditional townhouse) wedged between two waterways: the broad, tranquil Kamo River and the cherry-blossom-lined Takase River, just a step away from the Shijo-Kawaramachi crossing. It opened on February 1, 2023 as the first Kansai venture of TYSONS & COMPANY, the Tokyo restaurant group founded in 1997 with the opening of T.Y. HARBOR Brewery on Tennozu Island. The building keeps the nostalgic bones of an old Kyoto house while threading in modern touches: a lively kitchen-centered ground floor and a calmer upstairs built around a bar counter. From May through the end of September it puts out its 'noryo-yuka' riverside terrace, the summer dining-platform tradition for which Kyoto's Kamogawa is famous, so the room you sit in changes with the season.
chef_bio.philosophy: The parent group's idea is that a restaurant should be 'a warm atmosphere loved by all the locals' rather than just a place to eat, a space where customers and staff alike can express themselves and where people of different generations and backgrounds are all welcomed equally. At Kacto that translates into a place meant to be lingered in, letting guests feel the passage of the seasons through the waterside scenery, from morning coffee by the sparkling Kamo River to dinner and drinks at night.
chef_bio.specialty: A modern American cafe-restaurant 'with a Japanese twist' that runs from morning to night. It's best known for thick buttermilk pancakes (people famously queue for them) and brunch plates like truffled eggs, wagyu burgers, and steak and eggs, alongside salads and comfort-food classics. Coffee comes from The Roastery by Nozy Coffee, and the drinks lean on the group's own craft beer, including limited-run T.Y. HARBOR brews made with hops grown in Yosano, Kyoto, plus Japanese wines and original cocktails.

anecdotes.text: Kacto opened on February 1, 2023 in a two-story machiya beside the Kamo River, marking TYSONS & COMPANY's very first opening in the Kansai region after building its name in Tokyo.

The parent group traces back to 1997 and the opening of the T.Y. HARBOR Brewery restaurant in Tennozu; the group later brought the brewery-restaurant back from the brink of closure and grew into a portfolio of distinct neighborhood restaurants.
Reviewers single out the pancakes above all, with one noting the pancakes they 'waited an hour for were absolutely delicious.'
Overlooking the Kamo-gawa, the restaurant is especially prized for its truffled eggs and thick pancakes, with a riverside yuka terrace that costs a little extra but is well worth it on warm days.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is built around wheat-heavy American comfort food: buttermilk pancakes, burgers, and similar dishes are the signatures here. A kitchen that turns out pancakes, burger buns, and battered items shares prep surfaces and equipment, so cross-contamination with gluten should be assumed and Kacto should not be treated as celiac-safe without a direct conversation with the staff.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The food is described as modern American 'with a Japanese twist,' so Japanese-style seasonings are used in some dishes. Most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) is brewed with wheat, meaning sauces, marinades, and Japanese-accented dishes are likely sources of hidden gluten. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on eggs, dairy (pancakes, smoothies, truffled eggs), beef burgers, and steak. There is no documented vegan menu or vegan prep separation, so cross-contact with egg, dairy, and meat should be expected and any plant-based request needs to be confirmed in advance with the staff.

positives.text: Service is described as friendly and generally efficient, and most servers speak some English, which makes it easier to ask detailed questions about ingredients and allergens before ordering.

places.name: CoCo Ichibanya – Hachijo (near Kyoto Stn)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→ask): Sealed low-allergen retort curry is the only celiac-safe hot item, well-documented in JP, but not stocked chain-wide and no JP source confirms THIS branch stocks it. Wheat roux + shared fryer + shared rice scoop on premises. ask pending phone call. — Prior: The dedicated 'allergen-free curry' (7 allergens including wheat removed) arrives in a sealed, pre-packaged heated pouch opened at the table — reviewers consistently report it celiac-safe. Rice is the only cross-contact point (shared rice cooker). GF spoon provided sealed.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable curry base available (pork-free on request); confirm no hidden pork/chicken stock in sauce on the day. No dedicated vegan menu but staff will advise via allergen sheet.
places.notes: Chain curry restaurant near Kyoto Stn south exit. The 'allergen-free curry' (小麦・卵・乳・そば・落花生・えび・かに free) comes in a sealed heated pouch — the best-documented GF-safe fast option in Japan. Under ¥500. Spice level 0–10. English tablet ordering. Order two if you're hungry — it's a standard portion.

chef_bio.chef_name: Tokuji Munetsugu (宗次徳二) — chain founder
chef_bio.origin: Chain founded in Nagoya area, Aichi, Japan; this branch in Kyoto (near Kyoto Station, Hachijo)
chef_bio.background: This is a branch of CoCo Ichibanya (Curry House CoCo Ichibanya), Japan's dominant curry-rice chain, operated by Ichibanya Co., Ltd. The chain was founded by Tokuji Munetsugu together with his wife Naomi: the couple opened a coffee shop called 'Bacchus' in the Nagoya area in 1974, where Naomi's homemade curry proved so popular that they specialized, opening the first Curry House CoCo Ichibanya in 1978 in Nishi-Biwajima-cho on the outskirts of Nagoya. Munetsugu, who had a difficult childhood (placed in an orphanage as an infant, adopted at three, and raised in poverty by his adoptive father), built the company into a major nationwide curry chain — today well over 1,400 shops — before retiring in 2002 at age 53; he later founded the 'Yellow Angel' NPO (2003) and built Munetsugu Hall for classical music in Nagoya (2007). As a standardized franchise curry house, the individual Kyoto-Hachijo branch does not have a distinct head chef; food follows the chain's build-your-own system (choice of rice size, spice level and toppings).
chef_bio.philosophy: Founder Munetsugu is known for a hospitality-and-cleanliness-driven management ethos and a build-your-own curry format that lets each customer choose portion, spice level and toppings; his post-retirement motto 'classical music in your daily life' reflects the same everyday-accessibility outlook.
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order Japanese curry rice with customizable spice levels and toppings (katsu, vegetables, etc.).

anecdotes.text: CoCo Ichibanya grew out of the Munetsugus' 1974 Nagoya coffee shop 'Bacchus,' whose popular homemade curry led them to open the first dedicated curry house in 1978.
Founder Tokuji Munetsugu retired at 53 in 2002 and afterward built Munetsugu Hall in Nagoya, hosting near-daily classical concerts under the slogan 'classical music in your daily life.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: English-language chain histories and the Munetsugu foundation column corroborate the 1974 'Bacchus' origin, the January 1978 first shop in Nishi-Biwajima-cho near Nagoya, founders Tokuji and Naomi Munetsugu, retirement at 53 in 2002, Yellow Angel NPO (2003) and Munetsugu Hall (2007), and Ichibanya's growth to 1,400+ shops (1,000th shop Dec 2004). This is franchise-standard information; the specific Kyoto-Hachijo branch has no separately documented chef.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The allergen-free curry is a fully cooked retort product: it arrives in a sealed, factory-prepared heated pouch opened at the table, so the curry sauce itself never touches the regular kitchen line. This sealed-pouch service is the main reason gluten-free diners report it dependable.
The principal remaining cross-contact point is the rice, cooked in a shared rice cooker used for all orders. Very sensitive diners may want to ask about this or skip the rice.
The allergen-free curry is documented as free of wheat, dairy, egg, buckwheat, crab and shrimp.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce concern surfaced for the allergen-free curry, which is wheat-free by formulation. The regular menu curry sauces are a different matter and are not wheat-safe, so order specifically from the allergen-free option rather than a standard curry.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A meat-free 'vegetarian curry' sauce is offered (no animal meat, fish, shellfish, egg, dairy or honey), but it shares a production line with dairy, so trace dairy is possible. Strict vegans should be aware of this.

The standard curry sauce is NOT vegan or vegetarian (contains meat, fish extract, milk, cheese and honey). Always order from the marked vegetarian section rather than assuming a plain curry is meat-free.
Watch out for non-vegan items that look plant-based: keema sauce contains chicken and the udon soup contains fish extract.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain publishes a full allergen matrix and store menus carry detailed allergen descriptions, and travelers report staff being helpful in flagging which sides can be prepared safely when you state your dietary need at ordering.

positives.text: A sealed, pre-packaged allergen-free curry that is opened at the table is unusually reassuring for celiac and multi-allergy diners, and it is inexpensive and widely available.
Beyond gluten-free, a clearly marked meat-free curry exists, so the same restaurant can serve both gluten-free and vegetarian/vegan diners in one stop.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Sealed low-allergen retort curry is the only celiac-safe hot item, well-documented in JP, but not stocked chain-wide and no JP source confirms THIS branch stocks it. Wheat roux + shared fryer + shared rice scoop on premises. ask pending phone call.

places.name: CoCo Ichibanya – Karasuma Gojo
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (options→ask): Same chain logic; English aggregator listing can't establish a rating and no JP source confirms branch stock. ask. — Prior: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→options. Only ONE branch-specific source (FMGF, 403/index only) confirms low-allergen curry here; HappyCow & TripAdvisor for this branch are SILENT on it. Fails ≥2-source 'high'. GF curry likely available (nationwide item) but unconfirmed; own site warns 'some stores do not carry it'. — Prior note: Same chain-wide allergen-free sealed-pouch curry as all CoCo Ichibanya locations. QR-code allergen sheet available in English. Rice cross-contact possible.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable curry base; confirm pork-free on the allergen sheet.
places.hours_raw: Daily 11:00–midnight
places.notes: CoCo Ichibanya branch near Karasuma-Gojo. Sealed allergen-free curry pouch — GF-safe. English menu via QR code. One reviewer had an allergic reaction after not checking the allergen sheet, so check before ordering even vegetarian options.

chef_bio.background: CoCo Ichibanya grew out of a small Nagoya coffee shop called Bacchus, which Tokuji Munetsugu opened with his wife, Naomi, in 1974. They added Naomi's homemade curry to the menu to boost sales, and it became so popular that the couple decided to specialize in curry alone. In January 1978 they opened the first Curry House CoCo Ichibanya in Nishi-Biwajima-cho, a then-rural suburb of Nagoya surrounded by rice fields, where neighbors doubted it would survive. It thrived. The chain has since grown to more than 1,400 restaurants and in 2013 was certified by Guinness World Records as the world's largest curry restaurant chain. The Karasuma Gojo branch in Shimogyo, a minute from Gojo Station, serves that same made-to-order curry on two floors, open from late morning until the early hours.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name comes from the founder's exclamation 'Koko ga ichiban ya!' — roughly 'This is the number-one place!' — reflecting his conviction that his curry was the best he had tasted. The company is known for three guiding principles for its staff: Nico (always smile), Kibi (move with energy), and Haki (speak clearly).
chef_bio.specialty: Build-your-own Japanese curry: a deliberately neutral, mild base curry that becomes a personal dish through dozens of toppings, adjustable spice levels, and customizable rice portions. The chain is famous for the sheer number of possible combinations, and this location is noted in particular for its chicken dishes, including chicken-cutlet (katsu) curry, alongside seasonal offerings such as spring-vegetable curry.

anecdotes.text: The whole company started as a side dish: Tokuji and Naomi Munetsugu ran a coffee shop named Bacchus in 1974 and added Naomi's homemade curry to lift sales. It outshone everything else they served, so they gave up the cafe and went all-in on curry.

The first Curry House CoCo Ichibanya opened in January 1978 in Nishi-Biwajima-cho on the rural edge of Nagoya, where locals were skeptical a curry shop in the rice fields could last. Watching customers leave happy on opening day convinced the founder to quit his real-estate work and commit fully to curry.
The brand name is the founder's own delighted exclamation, 'Koko ga ichiban ya!' ('This is the best place!'), which he reportedly felt after comparing his curry to others he tried while traveling.
In January 2013 the chain was certified by Guinness World Records as the largest curry restaurant chain in the world.

gf_cross_contamination.text: CoCo Ichibanya's standard curry roux contains wheat, so the regular menu is not gluten-free. The chain's dedicated low-allergen curry is the route for celiac and GF diners — confirm with staff that you are being served the allergen-free product, not the standard sauce.
Rice and other menu items are prepped in a shared kitchen that also handles wheat, so cross-contact on rice and shared surfaces is possible even when ordering the allergen-free curry.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard curry and many toppings can contain soybean and wheat. As with most Japanese kitchens, soy-based seasonings here are likely to contain wheat — not safe to assume otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian and vegan curry options exist within the CoCo Ichibanya brand, but they are prepared in a kitchen that also handles meat, dairy, egg and fish, so cross-contact is possible. Vegans avoiding trace animal contact should ask staff how the vegetarian curry is prepared and served.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain publishes allergen information and offers a dedicated allergen-free (low-allergen) curry, which signals a company-wide awareness of allergies; ask staff for the allergen sheet (an English QR-code version is available) before ordering.

positives.text: CoCo Ichibanya offers a dedicated allergen-free curry that is free of wheat, dairy, egg, buckwheat, crab and shrimp — including a children's version — which is unusually accommodating for a Japanese curry chain and a genuine option for wheat-avoiding and celiac diners (served as a separate, pre-prepared product).
An allergen information sheet is available, with an English version accessible by QR code, so you can check ingredients per item before ordering.
Curry is made to order with adjustable toppings, spice and rice, making it easier to leave out specific ingredients you need to avoid.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Same chain logic; English aggregator listing can't establish a rating and no JP source confirms branch stock. ask.

places.name: CoCo Ichibanya – Kawaramachi Sanjo
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→ask. Standard curry roux contains wheat. Only GF item is sealed low-allergen retort curry, which CoCo admits is NOT stocked at all branches and there's no official locator to confirm per-branch stocking. Cannot verify this branch carries it, so 'high' unearned. Open (#2490). — Prior note: Standard CoCo Ichibanya sealed allergen-free curry. Central downtown location.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable/veg curry base; confirm with allergen sheet.
places.notes: Downtown CoCo Ichibanya (Kawaramachi/Sanjo). 2 floors, English & multilingual tablet ordering. Sealed allergen-free curry — GF-safe chain-wide option. Vegetarian/vegan curry base available; customise spice 0–10 and toppings.

chef_bio.background: CoCo Ichibanya grew out of a small Nagoya coffee shop called Bacchus, run in the early 1970s by Tokuji Munetsugu and his wife Naomi. Naomi's homemade curry rice became so popular with customers that the couple decided to specialize in it, and in 1978 Tokuji opened the first Curry House CoCo Ichibanya. From that single shop the family built one of the world's largest curry-rice chains, with well over 1,400 locations across Japan and abroad. This downtown Kyoto branch, just off the Kawaramachi-Sanjo intersection, carries the same made-to-order formula the founders established.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founders' guiding idea, carried through to today, is to make safe, dependable food that customers can enjoy with confidence at an everyday price.
chef_bio.specialty: Japanese curry rice built entirely around the diner's choices: you pick the base (pork, beef, or vegetarian), the rice portion, the spice level, and then layer on toppings such as breaded pork cutlet, fried chicken, spinach, or cheese. The chain is known for letting guests dial spice up the scale and for consistent, affordable curry served quickly.

anecdotes.text: The whole chain traces back to a recipe from the kitchen of the founders' home: Naomi Munetsugu's homemade curry, served at the couple's Nagoya coffee shop, drew such a following that they reopened as a dedicated curry house in 1978.

After building the curry empire, founder Tokuji Munetsugu retired in 2002 and turned to philanthropy, later opening Munetsugu Hall for classical music in his hometown of Nagoya under the motto 'classical music in your daily life.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: CoCo Ichibanya offers a dedicated curry made without the seven specified allergens (egg, dairy, wheat, buckwheat, peanut, shrimp, crab). To reduce the risk of cross-contact in the kitchen, this allergen-free curry sauce is served sealed in its original retail pouch rather than plated from the regular line.
The company notes this allergen-free curry is not an officially certified medical food, so celiac diners should treat it as a low-allergen option and confirm preparation with staff.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard pork and beef curry sauces are wheat-based and are not gluten-free. Only the separately listed allergen-free curry is formulated without wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The plant-based 'CoCo Ichi Veggie Curry' sauce uses no animal-derived ingredients, but dairy is flagged as present on shared factory production lines, so it is not suitable for those who must strictly avoid any dairy contact.

Veggie curry is available without advance reservation at any branch, which makes this Kawaramachi location a practical option for plant-based diners; confirm toppings, since standard toppings are not vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: CoCo Ichibanya publishes allergen, calorie, and ingredient-origin information, accessible via PDF and in-store QR codes, so diners can check specifics before ordering.

positives.text: A genuinely useful feature for allergy-sensitive diners: the seven-allergen-free curry is delivered in a sealed pouch specifically to avoid kitchen cross-contact.
Both a low-allergen curry and a no-animal-ingredient veggie curry are on the standard menu chain-wide, available without booking ahead.

gf_review.why: Standard curry roux contains wheat. Only GF item is sealed low-allergen retort curry, which CoCo admits is NOT stocked at all branches and there's no official locator to confirm per-branch stocking. Cannot verify this branch carries it, so 'high' unearned. Open (#2490).
red_flags: standard curry roux is wheat-thickened
per-branch GF retort stocking unverifiable online
shared rice scoop / topping CC
officially not a medical food
gf_review.plain_rice: Plain white rice available (chain-standard) but naturally-GF only, no CC guarantee

places.name: CoCo Ichibanya – Keihan Shichijo (near Higashiyama)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→ask. Same chain issue: GF only via sealed low-allergen retort curry, not carried chain-wide, unverifiable per branch. No source confirms this branch stocks it. One HappyCow wrong-order report signals order-accuracy risk. Open (#1337). — Prior note: Standard CoCo chain sealed allergen-free curry. Staff at this location noted for being attentive to allergy questions.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable curry base; confirm allergen sheet.
places.notes: CoCo Ichibanya near Kyoto National Museum / Higashiyama. Opens earliest (10:45). Good stop after Kiyomizu-dera or Gion. Reviewer praised staff for allergy attentiveness. QR code allergen menu.

chef_bio.background: CoCo Ichibanya grew out of a love story and a homemade recipe. In 1974, Tokuji Munetsugu and his wife Naomi, who had met as colleagues at a real estate company and started their own brokerage, opened a small coffee shop called Bacchus in Nagoya for steadier income. Naomi's homemade curry became the runaway favorite with regulars, and the couple leaned into it. In 1978 Munetsugu, then 30, closed the real estate business and opened the first Curry House CoCo Ichibanya in the rice-field suburbs of Nagoya. That single shop built on a wife's home recipe grew into one of the world's largest curry chains. This Keihan Shichijo branch, a short walk from Keihan Shichijo Station near Higashiyama, carries that same build-your-own-plate tradition into Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder built the company on a spirit of gratitude and mutual help. Munetsugu has described giving as 'helping each other' rather than charity, an outlook that traces back to the chain's modest, family-run beginnings.
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order Japanese curry rice you customize yourself: choose your rice portion, spice level, and toppings. Beyond the classic curries, the chain's hand-breaded fried chicken cutlet topping is a favorite that regulars come specifically to add.

anecdotes.text: Naomi Munetsugu's homemade curry was so popular among regulars at the couple's Nagoya coffee shop Bacchus that they decided to specialize, leading to the first CoCo Ichibanya in 1978.
Founder Tokuji Munetsugu opened his first curry shop at age 30 in the rice-field suburbs of Nagoya and walked away from his real estate business to pursue restaurant work.
Regulars at the Keihan Shichijo branch single out the hand-breaded fried/crispy chicken cutlet topping.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Munetsugu foundation column corroborates the 1974 'Bacchus' coffee-shop origin, Naomi's home curry, and the 1978 first shop opened by Munetsugu at age 30.

gf_cross_contamination.text: CoCo Ichibanya's standard curry sauce/roux is wheat-based, so the regular curry is NOT gluten-free or celiac-safe. The chain does offer a separate sealed/retort low-allergen curry made without designated allergens, which is the only gluten-conscious option; confirm with staff that it is the one being served and ask how it is heated and plated to avoid contact with the standard roux.

This is a shared kitchen serving primarily wheat-based curry, plus fried items and curry ramen (wheat noodles). For a strict celiac, cross-contact risk from the standard roux, shared utensils, and fryers should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: CoCo Ichibanya offers vegetarian/vegan-leaning curry options at some locations, but they are prepared in a kitchen that also handles meat, dairy and egg toppings. Vegan diners should confirm the specific curry base is plant-based and ask about shared cooking surfaces and utensils.

positives.text: The chain publishes allergen information and offers a dedicated low-allergen (allergen-free) curry product, which is more than many independent curry shops provide.

gf_review.why: Same chain issue: GF only via sealed low-allergen retort curry, not carried chain-wide, unverifiable per branch. No source confirms this branch stocks it. One HappyCow wrong-order report signals order-accuracy risk. Open (#1337).
red_flags: wheat curry roux
GF retort stocking unverifiable for this branch
documented wrong-order incident
shared rice CC

places.name: CoCo Ichibanya – Enmachi (near Nijo)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→ask. Same chain issue: safe option is only the sealed low-allergen retort curry, not carried everywhere, not verifiable for this branch. No source confirms or denies stocking here. Open (#1106). — Prior note: Standard CoCo chain sealed allergen-free curry. Convenient for Kinkaku-ji and Nijo Castle area.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable curry base; confirm allergen sheet.
places.notes: CoCo Ichibanya near Nijo/Enmachi — useful for Kinkaku-ji or Daitoku-ji days. Sealed allergen-free curry GF-safe. Comic books on shelf. Staff cooperative with dietary questions.

chef_bio.chef_name: Tokuji Munetsugu (宗次徳二) — chain founder
chef_bio.origin: Chain founded in Nagoya area, Aichi, Japan; this branch in Kyoto (Enmachi, near Nijo)
chef_bio.background: This is a branch of CoCo Ichibanya (Curry House CoCo Ichibanya), Japan's largest curry-rice chain, operated by Ichibanya Co., Ltd. Founders Tokuji Munetsugu and his wife Naomi opened a Nagoya-area coffee shop, 'Bacchus,' in 1974; the runaway popularity of Naomi's homemade curry led them to open the first Curry House CoCo Ichibanya in 1978 in Nishibiwajima-cho outside Nagoya. Tokuji Munetsugu, who was placed in an orphanage as an infant and later raised by his stepmother, expanded the company to over 1,400 shops and retired in 2002 at age 53, subsequently launching the 'Yellow Angel' NPO and building Munetsugu Hall for classical music in Nagoya. As a standardized franchise curry house, the individual Enmachi/Nijo branch has no separately documented head chef; dishes follow the chain's customizable build-your-own curry system.
chef_bio.philosophy: Founder Munetsugu's approach centers on hospitality, cleanliness and a customer-customizable curry format (portion size, spice level, toppings), reflecting an everyday-accessibility ethos he later carried into public classical-music programming.
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order Japanese curry rice with adjustable spice levels and a wide range of toppings.

anecdotes.text: The chain began when the Munetsugus' Nagoya coffee shop 'Bacchus' (1974) became known for Naomi's homemade curry, prompting them to specialize and open the first CoCo Ichibanya in 1978.
Founder Tokuji Munetsugu retired at 53 and later opened Munetsugu Hall in Nagoya, a venue devoted to near-daily classical concerts.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Chain histories and the Munetsugu foundation column corroborate the 1974 'Bacchus' origin, 1978 first shop, founders Tokuji and Naomi Munetsugu, ~1,450-shop scale, orphanage childhood, 2002 retirement at 53, and Munetsugu Hall. This is franchise-standard information; the specific Enmachi/Nijo branch has no separately documented chef.

gf_cross_contamination.text: CoCo Ichibanya offers an allergen-free curry that uses none of Japan's 7 major allergens (egg, dairy, wheat, buckwheat, peanut, shrimp, crab). To limit cross-contamination, the curry sauce is brought to the table sealed in an unopened retort pouch that you open yourself, rather than being plated from shared kitchen containers.

The company openly states that contamination can occur both in the factory and in-store through shared equipment, utensils, and hand contact during cooking. The standard menu is not prepared in a celiac-safe environment.
The allergen-free curry is not certified as a medical or special-purpose food, and not every branch stocks it. Confirm availability at this Enmachi location before relying on it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard Japanese curry roux is wheat-thickened, and most Japanese soy sauce used in seasonings contains wheat. The regular curry sauce is therefore not gluten-free; only the dedicated sealed allergen-free pouch is formulated without wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An animal-ingredient-free version of the allergen-free curry is reported to exist, which can suit vegan diners. However, it is prepared in a kitchen that handles meat, seafood, dairy, and egg, so trace cross-contact is possible.

staff_allergy_handling.text: For the allergen-free curry, staff serve the sauce in its sealed pouch and provide a disposable plastic spoon instead of a shared metal utensil, reducing secondary cross-contact.
Detailed per-item allergen and nutrition charts are published on the official site, so you can check specific ingredients before ordering or show staff your requirements.

positives.text: The sealed, unopened allergen-free curry pouch is one of the more reassuring fast-curry options in Japan for avoiding the 7 major allergens, since the sauce never touches shared serving equipment.
Convenient location near Enmachi for visits to Nijo Castle and the Kinkaku-ji / Kitano-Tenmangu area, open daily with long hours.

gf_review.why: Same chain issue: safe option is only the sealed low-allergen retort curry, not carried everywhere, not verifiable for this branch. No source confirms or denies stocking here. Open (#1106).
red_flags: wheat curry roux
GF retort stocking unverifiable for this branch
shared rice CC
not a medical food

places.name: MOS Burger – Shijo Nishinotoin
places.gf_detail: 'Low allergen' menu: pork rice-burger and pork hot dog, both on rice-flour buns, in sealed packaging — certified free of wheat, egg, milk, shrimp, crab, peanut, buckwheat. Very small (children's size); order 2. Fries NOT safe (shared fryer). Allergen chart in English on website.
places.vegan_detail: Avocado basil plant-based burger available; cooked on shared grill with meat. Fries and onion rings vegan. Not GF.
places.notes: MOS Burger downtown. Japan's most allergy-conscious fast-food chain. The low-allergen pork rice-burger and pork hot dog come in sealed packs (GF, egg-free, dairy-free) — show the picture on the website menu to order. They are very small (child-sized); get two each. Note: the vegan plant burger is NOT GF (wheat bun, shared grill). Cannot combine GF + vegan in one item here.

chef_bio.origin: Japan (chain founded 1972; this branch in Shimogyo/Downtown Kyoto near Shijo)
chef_bio.background: MOS Burger is a hamburger chain born and raised in Japan, founded in 1972. The name is an acronym for Mountain, Ocean, and Sun — a founding philosophy of standing tall and firm like a mountain, having a heart broad and deep as the ocean, and carrying passion like the sun that never burns out. The chain built its reputation by blending Japanese culinary traditions with American hamburger culture, and today operates roughly 1,300 stores across Japan. This downtown Kyoto branch near Shijo is a small, made-to-order counter shop that opened in December 2022.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on taste, safety, and health, using fresh vegetables sourced from partner farms and assembling each order to order rather than in advance.
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order burgers prepared fresh after you order, with no pre-made items sitting under heat lamps. MOS is best known for its signature meat-sauce burger (a tangy fresh-tomato and beef-and-pork sauce) and for reimagining Japanese flavors — teriyaki, tonkatsu, fish — as burgers, plus its distinctive rice-flour-bun rice burgers. The Fish Burger and Teriyaki Burger are popular picks at this location.

anecdotes.text: The MOS name stands for Mountain, Ocean, and Sun — the founder's wish that the company stand firm like a mountain, stay broad and deep as the ocean, and burn with passion like the sun.
This Shijo branch opened on December 14, 2022, as a compact counter shop, and keeps to the chain's principle of cooking every item fresh once you order rather than holding pre-made stock.

gf_cross_contamination.text: MOS offers a 'low allergen' line — a pork rice-burger and a pork hot dog, both on rice-flour buns — that is certified free of wheat, egg, milk, shrimp, crab, peanut, and buckwheat, and served in sealed individual packaging to limit cross-contact. These items are children's-size and small, so celiac diners often order two.

MOS states its allergen information reflects product ingredients only, and that ingredients not normally used in an item may be introduced through contact or mixing during factory manufacturing or in-store cooking — so the allergen list is not an absolute guarantee.

MOS notes that in-store kitchens use the same standard cooking equipment and utensils and also prepare other products, meaning shared-surface cross-contact is possible for anything not in the sealed low-allergen packaging.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard menu items such as the Teriyaki Burger use sauces that typically contain wheat-based soy sauce, so popular non-low-allergen items are not wheat-free. Rely only on the certified low-allergen items and the published allergen chart for a strictly gluten-free order.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore burger shop with no documented dedicated vegan menu at this branch. MOS does offer plant-based 'Green' burgers chain-wide, but they are cooked on shared equipment alongside meat and dairy items, so cross-contact is likely — confirm specifics with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: An allergen chart is available in English on the MOS website (downloadable PDF), and the company directs customers with allergy concerns to check the list and ask store staff directly.

positives.text: The certified low-allergen pork rice-burger and pork hot dog are free of wheat, egg, milk, shrimp, crab, peanut, and buckwheat and arrive in sealed packaging — a rare, genuinely usable option for celiac diners at a Japanese burger chain.
An English-language allergen chart is published online, making it easier to verify ingredients before ordering.
Every item is made to order rather than pre-assembled, so you can speak with staff about your order at the counter when you place it.

places.name: MOS & CAFE – Nijo Station
places.gf_detail: Same low-allergen sealed-pack rice-burger and hot dog as all MOS Burger Japan. Opens early (8am) for breakfast.
places.vegan_detail: Plant-based burger option; shared grill, not GF.
places.notes: MOS & CAFE near Nijo Station — opens at 8am, good for breakfast. Cafe-style interior with seating. Same low-allergen sealed rice-burger/hot dog as all MOS Japan. English menu and self-order terminal. 'Meatless' burger and rice burger options too.

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (1972); this branch in Nijo, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: MOS Burger is the homegrown Japanese burger chain founded in March 1972 by Satoshi Sakurada, who opened the very first tiny shop in Narimasu, Tokyo. Sakurada had fallen for cook-to-order American hamburgers — especially Original Tommy's — while posted to the United States with a Japanese securities firm, and he came home determined to build something that fused that made-to-order spirit with Japanese taste. The name MOS stands for Mountain, Ocean, Sun: standing tall like a mountain, a heart as deep as the ocean, and passion that never fades like the sun. The chain grew into one with stores in all 47 prefectures, and this Nijo Station branch carries the same kitchen philosophy — everything cooked fresh to order rather than held pre-made, with vegetables from partner farms. It is a handy, friendly stop right by JR Nijo Station, popular with locals grabbing a bite before a movie at the nearby cinema.
chef_bio.philosophy: Every order cooked fresh, never pre-made, with fresh vegetables sourced from partner farms — a Japanese take on the hamburger that prizes flavor and care over speed.
chef_bio.specialty: The Teriyaki Burger — introduced in 1973 and one of Japan's first — pairs a beef patty with lettuce and mayonnaise in a teriyaki sauce built on Japanese flavors. The chain is also known for its made-to-order approach and its rice-bun burgers. At this branch, reviewers also call out the shrimp katsu burger and the vegetable burger.

anecdotes.text: Founder Satoshi Sakurada started MOS Burger in March 1972 in a small shop in Narimasu, Tokyo, inspired by the cook-to-order American hamburgers he had eaten while working in the United States.
The name MOS stands for Mountain, Ocean, Sun — standing tall like a mountain, a heart as deep as the ocean, and passion that never burns out like the sun.
In 1973 MOS introduced the Teriyaki Burger, fusing traditional Japanese teriyaki sauce with a beef patty, lettuce and mayonnaise — an instant hit and one of Japan's first.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This branch carries the same chain menu as MOS Burger Japan-wide, including the low-allergen sealed-pack rice-burger and hot dog, which are prepared and sealed off-site to limit allergen contact. No location-specific cross-contamination protocol is documented for the open kitchen, so confirm handling with staff on the day.

MOS publishes an English allergen list (PDF) and notes that ingredients can vary by store and that information is not fully guaranteed, so celiac diners should rely on the sealed low-allergen items rather than the standard menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: MOS's signature Teriyaki Burger sauce is built on Japanese teriyaki, which is made with soy sauce that typically contains wheat — so the regular burgers, sauces and most of the standard menu are not celiac-safe.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A vegetable burger (MOS Yasai Burger) is on the menu, but it is not certified vegan and is prepared in a shared kitchen alongside meat and dairy items; standard sauces and buns may contain egg, milk or animal-derived ingredients. Vegans should ask staff to check ingredients and preparation.

positives.text: The chain-wide low-allergen rice-burger and hot dog are certified gluten-free and free of wheat, egg and milk, and arrive in sealed plastic packaging — a rare reliable option for celiac and multi-allergy diners at a burger shop.
MOS provides an allergen list in English and cooks each item fresh to order, which can make it easier to ask about and confirm ingredients.

places.name: MOS Burger – Karasuma Oike
places.gf_detail: Same low-allergen rice-burger/hot dog sealed packs as all MOS Japan. Central location.
places.vegan_detail: Plant-based burger (Green Burger / soy patty); not GF.
places.notes: MOS Burger Karasuma-Oike, central Kyoto. Opens 8am. Reviewers praise the vegan Green Burger and soy patty options. Low-allergen sealed GF rice-burger available. Good location for a quick stop en route to Nijo Castle or Imperial Palace.

chef_bio.background: MOS Burger is a Japanese-born hamburger chain founded in 1972, built around the idea that a burger could carry Japanese flavors and be made fresh to order rather than sitting under a heat lamp. The Karasuma Oike branch is a centrally located outpost of that chain, open early for a morning menu and right by the subway in central Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand describes itself as valuing taste, safety and health, and prepares each item fresh upon order with no pre-made stock.
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order burgers using vegetables sourced from partner farms in Japan, including the MOS Chicken, cheeseburgers, the Avocado Burger and premium Kuroge Wagyu burger. The chain is also known nationwide for its rice-flour-bun rice burgers and a low-allergen kids' menu.

anecdotes.text: MOS Burger has operated since its 1972 opening as a hamburger specialty shop born in Japan that values Japanese flavors, preparing each dish fresh to order with vegetables from local partner farms.

gf_cross_contamination.text: MOS Burger's low-allergen burger and hot dog arrive in individually sealed packaging, and the chain states there is no chance for allergens to get into the package at the store, which lowers cross-contamination risk for these specific items. However, the kitchen is not a dedicated gluten-free facility, so the regular menu shares wheat-handling equipment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Most standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Coverage of MOS Burger's allergen program notes that allergens can hide even in soy sauce, so any item with regular sauces should be assumed to contain wheat unless the sealed low-allergen item is chosen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan-specific cross-contact information was documented. The low-allergen burger and hot dog are pork-based, and vegan status is not addressed in the sources, so ingredients should be confirmed with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The low-allergen menu is designed to exclude egg, milk, wheat, shrimp, crab, soba (buckwheat) and peanut, and items are prepared and sealed so allergens cannot enter the package at the store.

positives.text: The low-allergen burger and hot dog use rice-flour buns instead of wheat and come in pre-sealed packs, making them a relatively safe option for wheat avoiders; the buns are described as soft and surprisingly filling.
These low-allergen items are part of the kids' menu but can be purchased by adults as well.

places.name: Kura Sushi Plus – Kawaramachi (downtown)
places.gf_label: GF options — allergen menu online (not dedicated GF)
places.gf_detail: Full allergen menu in English on website (kurasushi.co.jp); ~1/4 of menu items free of wheat. Bring your own tamari/GF soy sauce (house soy sauce contains wheat). Conveyor belt means possible cross-contact from passing dishes. Not celiac-safe but manageable for non-celiac GF with allergen menu.
places.vegan_detail: Cucumber/daikon/avocado maki, tofu sushi, fried corn, edamame, soy-milk ice cream. Order via tablet to avoid conveyor cross-contact.
places.notes: Kura Sushi Plus (premium branch) downtown Kyoto B1F. Conveyor-belt sushi from ¥120/plate. Check allergen menu at kurasushi.co.jp before going — ~1/4 of items are wheat-free. BRING YOUR OWN GF soy sauce (tamari). Order direct from tablet, not off the belt, to reduce cross-contact. Fun capsule-toy game every 5 plates. Long waits weekends — book online or go weekday 3–5pm.

chef_bio.origin: Sakai, Japan (chain founded 1977; this branch in Kawaramachi/Teramachi, Kyoto)
chef_bio.background: Kura Sushi traces back to Kunihiko Tanaka, who in the 1970s was selling vinegar to sushi shops across western Japan. He came away convinced the trade was broken: quality was inconsistent, prices changed depending on who you were, and a proper sushi meal was a once-in-a-while luxury most families couldn't justify. In 1977 he opened his own restaurant in Sakai to do it differently, later moving to a conveyor-belt format and formally founding the company in 1995. That idea grew into one of Japan's largest sushi chains, and this Kawaramachi branch, just off Teramachi near Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station, is one of its newest, a bright, casual kaiten-zushi spot where plates of sushi glide past on the belt for around 150 to 230 yen each.
chef_bio.philosophy: A founding belief that good sushi should be honest and affordable, free of unnecessary chemicals. Kura built its kitchens around removing four common additives, chemical seasoning (MSG), artificial sweeteners, synthetic coloring, and artificial preservatives, and draws flavor instead from natural kombu and bonito. Even the vinegar, soy sauce, and wasabi are custom-made in-house to keep that additive-free promise.
chef_bio.specialty: Affordable conveyor-belt sushi: plates of toro (fatty tuna), seared (aburi) fish, seasonal catches like yuzu flounder, and playful creative rolls such as salmon-avocado-cheese, all at a fixed low price per plate. The chain is also known for its arcade-style touch: clear plates dropped into the table chute, animations, and the chance at a capsule-toy prize between dishes.

anecdotes.text: The whole company grew out of a vinegar salesman's frustration: visiting sushi shops in the 1970s, Tanaka decided the industry's high prices and uneven quality needed an overhaul, borrowed roughly the equivalent of a few months' wages, and opened his first restaurant in 1977.

To stay true to its no-additives idea, Kura makes its own vinegar for the rice, its own soy sauce, and its own wasabi rather than buying off-the-shelf seasonings, leaning on kombu and bonito for flavor.

Part of the fun at the table is mechanical: diners slot finished plates into a chute that can trigger an animation and a shot at a capsule-toy prize, and runners and a delivery robot help bring out orders.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a conveyor-belt (kaiten-zushi) restaurant, so plates of many different items pass continuously over your seat. That open belt creates real potential for cross-contact, so it is best treated as manageable for non-celiac gluten-avoiders rather than celiac-safe.

An allergen and ingredient chart is available in English on the Kura Sushi website; roughly a quarter of menu items are free of wheat, so a careful diner can build a meal, but should verify each item on the chart.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kura makes its own soy sauce in-house as part of its no-additives policy, but this does not make it gluten-free, standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat. Assume the house soy sauce contains wheat and bring your own tamari or certified gluten-free soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Some plant-forward plates exist (for example avocado-based rolls), but on a shared conveyor belt that carries fish and seafood, incidental cross-contact is likely; strict vegans should confirm preparation with staff.

positives.text: Kura publishes a detailed allergen and ingredient chart in English on its website, which makes it easier than at most casual sushi spots to identify which items are free of wheat and other allergens.

The chain's founding principle is to avoid four common additives, MSG, artificial sweeteners, synthetic coloring, and artificial preservatives, drawing flavor from kombu and bonito instead, which appeals to diners watching what goes into their food.

places.name: Kura Sushi – Teramachi
places.gf_label: GF options — allergen menu online (not dedicated GF)
places.gf_detail: Same allergen menu as all Kura Sushi. Teramachi location — handy for Nishiki Market area.
places.vegan_detail: Cucumber/avocado/daikon maki, tofu sushi, fried corn, edamame, soy-milk ice cream.
places.notes: Kura Sushi on Teramachi, 2F — convenient for Nishiki Market and Kawaramachi shopping. Same conveyor-belt format, bring own GF soy sauce, order via tablet. Allergen menu online in English. Unlimited free matcha at the table.

chef_bio.background: Kura Sushi began in 1977, when Kunihiko Tanaka opened a single small restaurant in Sakai City, Osaka. Tanaka had spent his early career as a vinegar peddler, supplying sushi shops across western Japan, and that ground-level view convinced him the sushi business of the era was broken: quality was inconsistent, and the same meal could cost different people different amounts, putting good sushi out of reach for ordinary diners except on special occasions. Borrowing about three million yen, he set out to make honest, affordable sushi an everyday pleasure rather than a luxury. That first shop grew into Kura Corporation (formally founded in 1995) and one of Japan's largest revolving-sushi chains. The Teramachi-area Kyoto branch is a modern 'Plus-type' conveyor-belt store near the Shinkyogoku/Sanjo shopping arcades, a short walk from Nishiki Market, built around the chain's signature touch-screen ordering, automated plate-counting and the playful capsule-prize system that rewards finished plates.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tanaka's founding idea was that sushi should be consistent, transparent and affordable for everyone, not priced differently from customer to customer. The 'Muten' (literally 'nothing added') approach - no MSG, no artificial sweeteners, no synthetic coloring and no artificial preservatives - grew out of that same commitment to honest food at a fair price.
chef_bio.specialty: Affordable conveyor-belt (kaiten) sushi served plate by plate, built on the company's 'Muten' promise: dishes made without the four additives Kura swore off from the start - chemical seasonings (MSG), artificial sweeteners, synthetic coloring and artificial preservatives. The chain is also known for its self-service, technology-forward dining: touch-panel ordering, a covered conveyor that protects each plate, and a gachapon-style prize game triggered by stacking finished plates.

anecdotes.text: Founder Kunihiko Tanaka started out peddling vinegar to sushi shops before borrowing roughly three million yen to open his first restaurant in Sakai City in 1977 - the seed of what became a billion-dollar revolving-sushi empire.

Kura built its identity on a 'Muten' (nothing-added) principle, eliminating the four major additives - chemical seasoning, artificial sweetener, synthetic coloring and artificial preservative - from its products.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kura Sushi states its restaurants are not allergen-free or gluten-free environments, and that menu items may be prepared using shared equipment and fryers, allowing cross-contact with allergens including gluten. Even when an item is specially ordered, the company says it cannot guarantee it will be free of any allergen.

A conveyor-belt sushi setting means many shared surfaces and shared preparation areas, so celiac diners should treat the location as high cross-contact risk and confirm directly with staff before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Reports for Kura note that some locations may stock gluten-free soy sauce but availability is not guaranteed, so celiac diners are advised to bring their own. Sauced items such as unagi (eel) are coated in soy-sauce-based glazes that contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kura offers some plant-based and vegetable items, but the kitchen prepares fish, shellfish, egg, dairy and other animal products on shared equipment, so vegan diners cannot rely on the absence of animal cross-contact. Confirm specific items with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: An allergen menu and per-item allergen labeling are available to help diners identify which of the major allergens each dish contains, but staff and the company explicitly state they cannot guarantee any item is free of a given allergen.

positives.text: The chain provides an allergen menu and labels the major allergens on its online menu, which makes it easier to screen dishes before ordering.

Dishes are made under the company's 'Muten' policy without MSG, artificial sweeteners, synthetic coloring or artificial preservatives - useful for diners avoiding those additives (note: this is separate from gluten/allergen safety).

places.name: Sushiro – Kawaramachi Takoyakushi
places.gf_label: GF options — allergen info available; not dedicated GF
places.gf_detail: Sushiro publishes allergen information; many plain nigiri (salmon, tuna, shrimp) are wheat-free without sauce, but soy sauce on the table contains wheat. Bring your own tamari. Shared preparation area — not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Limited: cucumber/avocado maki, edamame, some desserts. Primarily a fish/meat restaurant.
places.notes: Sushiro downtown Kyoto — Japan's largest kaiten-zushi chain, generally better quality/value than Kura (per most reviews). Order via tablet. Bring GF soy sauce. Use their app to queue before arriving; waits can be 45+ min on weekends at 6pm but much faster mid-afternoon. Allergen info available but shared kitchen.

chef_bio.origin: Osaka, Japan (brand origin); Kawaramachi, Kyoto (this location)
chef_bio.background: Sushiro grew out of a single Osaka sushi shop opened by the Shimizu brothers, Yoshio and Yutaka, who formally established the conveyor-belt company in 1984. Their idea was simple but ambitious: bring honest, good-quality sushi to everyday diners at an approachable price, with plates gliding past on a belt. From that one shop the brand became Japan's largest conveyor-belt sushi chain, with hundreds of restaurants. This Kawaramachi branch, which opened in March 2020, is one of the more unusual outposts in the chain. Tucked away on the basement (B1) floor just north of the busy Shijo-Kawaramachi crossing, you reach it by heading down a flight of stairs rather than into the typical roadside two-story Sushiro building, which gives it a slightly hidden, urban feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make genuinely good sushi accessible to everyone at an everyday price.
chef_bio.specialty: Affordable conveyor-belt nigiri and maki, with the kitchen taking pride in its fish, including premium offerings such as natural bluefin tuna. The brand is built on serving fresh, recognizable sushi quickly and cheaply.

anecdotes.text: A standout quirk of this branch is a dedicated express conveyor lane for touch-panel orders, which whisks your ordered plates straight to your seat quickly and with little chance of a mix-up, instead of the leisurely main belt. As a central, compact city-center store, plates here run about 20 yen more than the standard Sushiro pricing.

The brand traces back to a sushi shop opened by the Shimizu family in Osaka, with the conveyor-belt company formally founded in 1984; it has since grown into Japan's largest conveyor-belt sushi chain with hundreds of locations.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese Akindo Sushiro / Sushiro histories confirm founder Yoshio Shimizu (with brother Yutaka) opened the first 'Sushi Taro' shop in Toyonaka, Osaka in June 1984 (company established October 1984), preceded by Yoshio's 1975 individual sushi shop in Abeno.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sushiro's own allergen guidance states that ingredients are handled in factories and stores alongside other allergens, so cross-contact is possible even when an item's recipe does not list wheat. As conveyor-belt sushi prepared in a shared kitchen with wheat-containing items, this location is not celiac-safe.

The plain sushi rice and most plain fish nigiri (such as salmon, tuna, and shrimp) without sauce are not made with wheat, but they are prepared in an environment shared with wheat-containing products.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard soy sauce provided at the table is brewed from soybeans and wheat, so it contains gluten. Celiac and gluten-free diners should bring their own tamari or gluten-free soy sauce, as the restaurant is unlikely to stock it.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Some plant-based items exist (for example inari and vegetable maki such as cucumber), but they are prepared and delivered in a fish- and seafood-heavy conveyor-belt environment, so cross-contact with animal products is likely. There are no documented vegan-dedicated preparation procedures.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific reports about how staff at this branch handle allergy requests were found. Sushiro publishes brand-wide allergen tables but relies on diners to check them and confirm details; for serious allergies, confirm directly with staff before ordering.

positives.text: Sushiro publishes a detailed, regularly updated allergen chart covering the legally specified allergens plus additional recommended ones, marked per menu item, which lets diners check ingredients before ordering.
Ordering is done via a tablet/touch panel at the table, making it easier to review items and select specific dishes rather than relying only on what passes on the belt.

places.name: Sushiro – Gion
places.gf_label: GF options — allergen info available; not dedicated GF
places.gf_detail: Same chain-wide allergen info as all Sushiro. Gion location — smaller, can be less crowded than downtown.
places.vegan_detail: Cucumber/avocado maki, edamame, some vegetable sides.
places.notes: Sushiro in Gion B1F — handy after Higashiyama sightseeing. Conveyor-belt sushi, affordable (¥150/plate). Bring GF tamari soy sauce. Book via app to avoid long waits. Good non-seafood options too (beef sukiyaki sushi, chicken teriyaki).

chef_bio.origin: Toyonaka, Osaka (chain origin); this branch in Gion, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Sushiro began in June 1984, when the Shimizu brothers opened a small sushi shop called Sushi Taro in Toyonaka, just outside Osaka. At the time, conveyor-belt sushi had a reputation for being cheap but second-rate, and the brothers — led by a trained sushi craftsman — set out to prove the opposite: that genuine, well-made sushi could also be affordable and fun. That idea grew into Sushiro, which became Japan's largest conveyor-belt sushi chain. The Gion branch carries that same approach into one of Kyoto's most famous neighborhoods, a short walk from Gion-Shijo Station. Being slightly off the busiest downtown strip, it can feel a little calmer than the city-center shops while still serving the full standardized menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make sushi that is both delicious and affordable, overturning the old idea that conveyor-belt sushi has to mean lower quality.
chef_bio.specialty: Conveyor-belt (kaiten) sushi at accessible, mostly flat-rate pricing — a rotating lineup of nigiri, gunkan, and rolls alongside ramen, side dishes, and desserts ordered from a touch-panel and delivered to your seat. The chain is known for keeping fish fresh and turnover fast, including seasonal and limited-time fairs and frequent themed collaborations.

anecdotes.text: Sushiro traces its roots to a single shop, Sushi Taro, opened by the Shimizu brothers in Toyonaka, Osaka, in June 1984 with the goal of making honest, good sushi affordable to everyone.
From that one Osaka shop the company grew into Japan's largest conveyor-belt sushi chain by turnover, with hundreds of restaurants across Japan and an expanding international presence.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Akindo Sushiro's corporate history confirms the first 'Sushi Taro' shop opened June 1984 in Toyonaka, Osaka; founder Yoshio Shimizu had run an individual sushi shop since 1975.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a busy conveyor-belt sushi shop where many different dishes — including breaded, fried, and wheat-based items like ramen and rolls — share the same kitchen, conveyor, and shared equipment. That makes meaningful cross-contact with wheat very likely, so it should not be considered celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) almost always contains wheat, and the table/conveyor soy sauce at conveyor-belt sushi chains is conventional shoyu. Plain nigiri may look gluten-free, but the soy sauce typically is not — bring your own certified gluten-free tamari if you are celiac.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A handful of items (such as inari, cucumber rolls, certain sides and desserts) may appear plant-based, but everything rides a shared conveyor and is prepared in a fish- and seafood-heavy kitchen, so cross-contact with fish, shellfish, and egg is expected. Sauces and broths frequently use dashi (fish stock). Confirm ingredients item by item.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Sushiro publishes chain-wide allergen information for its menu items, which can help identify which dishes contain major allergens; however, no location-specific allergy-handling practices for the Gion branch were found, so confirm directly with staff before ordering.

positives.text: The menu and ordering are touch-panel based with published chain-wide allergen charts, so you can look up listed allergens for individual items before ordering.
As a slightly off-center Gion location, it can be calmer than the busiest downtown shops, which may make it easier to take time and ask questions about ingredients.

places.name: Ikkakuju Karasuma (100% GF izakaya — okonomiyaki, karaage, yakitori)
places.gf_detail: Entire menu is gluten-free (rice flour). 100% GF except some drinks. Celiacs report eating safely. Note: traditional okonomiyaki here contains fish (Osaka-style with bonito flakes/fish meat) — confirm if vegan.
places.vegan_detail: Menu includes meat/fish (GF karaage, yakitori, wagyu). Vegan options limited — ask staff. Bonito flakes top most dishes; okonomiyaki contains fish. Dedicated vegans should check carefully.
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] Hidden gem B1F izakaya near Karasuma-Gojo. Entire menu in rice flour — GF okonomiyaki, GF karaage, GF yakisoba, GF yakitori, wagyu. One of the very few places in Kyoto offering beloved Japanese comfort food (okonomiyaki!) safely for celiacs. Reservation essential — small, very popular. Evenings only. Note: fish/meat throughout menu; limited vegan options.

chef_bio.background: Ikkakuju Karasuma occupies a hideaway basement space near Shijo-Karasuma and grew into what it calls Kyoto's first fully gluten-free okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house. The kitchen rebuilt classic Kansai griddle cooking from the ground up so that children and people with wheat allergies could eat with peace of mind. The okonomiyaki batter swaps wheat flour for a specially cultivated 'Nikomaru' rice flour, bound with grated mountain yam (tsukune imo) and rich dashi, after what the restaurant describes as repeated trial and error to get both the safety and the flavor right. As of early 2024 the entire food menu, down to the seasonings, is gluten-free.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea, in the owner's own words on their site, is that children and anyone with a wheat allergy should be able to eat here with peace of mind, but it has to be genuinely delicious or there is no point. They aim to deliver smiles through every element of the meal, from the food and drink to the custom dishware, the room, and the service.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour okonomiyaki cooked by dedicated grill masters, prized for a crispy exterior and a fluffy, creamy interior. Beyond the okonomiyaki they are known for teppanyaki including wagyu sirloin steak, rice-flour yakisoba/udon, takoyaki, karaage, and a signature Kyo-yuba (Kyoto tofu skin) okonomiyaki made in collaboration with a yuba house founded in 1885.

anecdotes.text: The okonomiyaki is built on a specially cultivated 'Nikomaru' rice flour with grated mountain yam and dashi, perfected through repeated trial and error so that wheat-allergic diners could finally enjoy it.
Their signature collaboration is a Kyo-yuba okonomiyaki made with tofu skin from the long-established Kyoto yuba house Yubasho, founded in Meiji 18 (1885).
As of February 2024 the restaurant's entire food menu, including its seasonings, became completely gluten-free.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site and JP listings confirm the 'Kyoto's first gluten-free rice-flour okonomiyaki' claim, the Nikomaru rice-flour + mountain-yam + dashi batter, the all-menu-including-seasonings gluten-free scope, and the Kyo-yuba okonomiyaki collaboration with Kyoto yuba maker Yubasho (founded Meiji 18 / 1885). The exact 2011 opening year could not be independently confirmed.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant presents itself as 100% gluten-free across its entire food menu, which greatly reduces the risk of wheat in the kitchen; however, no source documents specific cross-contamination or shared-surface protocols, so confirm griddle/teppan handling with staff if you are highly sensitive.
One diner reported it is '100% gluten free except for some of the drinks,' so check beverages separately.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant states that all dishes are gluten-free including the seasonings, which implies a wheat-free (gluten-free) soy sauce is used rather than standard wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan-friendly: the okonomiyaki is Osaka style and contains fish meat even in plain orders, and most items are topped with bonito flakes. As one reviewer warned, 'they contain fish meat (even if you order a plain flavour) as they are Osaka style. They also top most items with bonito flakes.'
The okonomiyaki batter itself is made with dashi (fish/bonito broth), so the base is not vegan.
The complimentary starter is fried tofu topped with bonito flakes, another reason vegans should confirm options directly with staff.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant was founded specifically so that children and people with wheat allergies could eat safely, and developed its rice-flour recipes with that goal, indicating allergy-aware cooking.
Staff are described by visitors as friendly and professional, though one review noted occasional miscommunication, so be clear when ordering.

positives.text: Entirely gluten-free food menu using specially cultivated 'Nikomaru' rice flour in place of wheat, with okonomiyaki, takoyaki, rice-flour noodles, karaage and teppanyaki all gluten-free.
Celiac and gluten-free diners report eating safely here; one called it a 'fantastic little place' that is 100% gluten free apart from some drinks.
The restaurant states all dishes are gluten-free down to the seasonings, so condiments like soy sauce are accounted for.

places.name: Kerala Indian Restaurant
places.gf_detail: Celiac travellers report staff understanding of GF needs and many naturally GF dishes (dals, rice, curries). Confirm naan/bread is separate. Not a dedicated GF kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: South Indian menu has naturally vegan dishes — dals, vegetable curries, rice. Confirm no ghee. English menu available.
places.notes: [Omnivore · both options] South Indian restaurant on Kawaramachi. Celiac travel guides specifically cite this location as having GF-aware staff and an English menu. Many South Indian dishes are naturally vegan and GF (dals, rice, coconut curries). 2F location above Kawaramachi street. Busy at weekends.

chef_bio.background: Kerala has been a fixture of central Kyoto's dining scene since 1986, tucked away on the second floor of a building just off Kawaramachi near Sanjo. Named for the coconut-palm-rich state on India's southwestern coast, it is run with chefs from South India who cook the food of their homeland. While most Indian restaurants in Japan lean toward North Indian cooking, Kerala built its reputation on bringing genuine South Indian dishes to the city, alongside Northern and pan-Indian favourites, in an intimate room dressed with colourful cloths and Indian art. Decades on, it is regarded by many as one of the best Indian restaurants in Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking the flavours of a South Indian homeland with attention to vegetables and fish, and a staff who happily guide diners through a long menu rather than leaving them to choose alone.
chef_bio.specialty: South Indian cooking is the heart of the kitchen: crisp masala dosa (a rice-flour crepe stuffed with spiced potato, served with coconut chutney and sambar) and a Kerala-style local prawn curry built around small prawns with dried mango for a gentle sourness. The kitchen is also known for Kerala-style fried fish and a house Kyoto tofu curry, plus a generous, well-priced thali set that takes the guesswork out of ordering.

anecdotes.text: Although named after a South Indian state, Kerala serves both Northern and Southern Indian food, including crisp dosas, and has been a Kyoto institution since 1986 (over 35 years).

One diner called it 'the best Indian food I've eaten in Kyoto,' praising a charming, soft-spoken waiter who helped with recommendations across dishes like fish tikka, mutton saagwala and vegetable jalfrezi.
The kitchen's Kerala local prawn curry, made with small prawns and dried mango, is noted for having topped curry rankings in the Kansai region.
