places.vegan_detail: No vegan offerings. Signature dishes (白梅麺 with simmered pork, 紅梅麺 with chicken, gyoza, Chinese noodle dishes) are meat- and animal-based, with dashi/soy-sauce broths. No documented plant-based menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:00–23:00 (hours and closing days subject to change)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent shop. Coverage describes it as a 個人店 town-Chinese run by the owner with no branches or chain/noren-wake affiliation. Described as a 47-year, second-generation family-run business but operated as a tiny owner-driven small shop; classified independent_solo given the one-person small-shop frami 12 seats. Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio).

chef_bio.background: Seiyoken opened in 1970, the year of the Osaka Expo, in the Nishijin neighborhood near Kitano Tenmangu. Its origins are unusually charming: the founder had run a women's undergarment shop in postwar Kyoto, and when that business fell on hard times he was invited by a traveling chuka-soba stall master to try running a noodle shop instead. He started on a shoestring with almost no capital and no formal training. The stall master sent an apprentice to help for just three days, and the founder learned the craft by watching and imitating. The shop has stayed in the family for two generations. The current owner, Takada Kei, joined at age 19 when the place was only three years old and has run it largely on his own ever since. Step inside and you walk into an early-Showa time capsule, nostalgic furnishings, old cooking equipment, and a homey warmth that regulars describe as feeling like a real-life film set, a survivor of a fast-disappearing style of old Kyoto town-Chinese cooking.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner is refreshingly unpretentious about it all, describing himself as having no particular culinary obsessions and simply making each morning's broth fresh, a quiet, daily-grind devotion to a neighborhood that has changed around him.
chef_bio.specialty: The shop is known for its old-fashioned chuka soba: a clean, carefully skimmed chicken-and-pork-bone broth with straight, thin yellow noodles and thick-cut chashu, plus hand-made gyoza with a fresh, homey vegetable filling. The signature plays on the plum blossoms of nearby Kitano Tenmangu: Hakubai-men (white-plum noodles, with pork and pickled plum) and Kobai-men (red-plum noodles, with chicken and pickled plum). The broth is made fresh each morning from blanched bones rather than topped up from a running pot.

anecdotes.text: The founder previously ran a women's undergarment shop; when it struggled, a chuka-soba stall master invited him to open a noodle shop, and he learned the trade in just three days of watching the master's apprentice, with almost no starting capital.

Current owner Takada Kei joined the shop at age 19, when it had been open only three years, and has run it solo ever since, making a fresh batch of broth from blanched pork and chicken bones every morning.
The signature white-plum (Hakubai-men) and red-plum (Kobai-men) noodles are named for the famous ume blossoms of the nearby Kitano Tenmangu shrine and the local Hakubaicho district.
Regulars describe the interior as feeling like a real-life Showa-era drama set, with original mid-20th-century furnishings preserved intact.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Yahoo News expert (Kyoto machi-chuka writer) reports Seiyoken opened 1970 near Kitano Tenmangu; the founder had run a women's undergarment shop, was talked into noodles by a chuka-soba stall owner, started with roughly ¥3,000 and three days of watching the master's apprentice; second-generation owner Takada Kei joined at 19 (~1973) and runs it solo, boiling fresh bone broth each morning. kyotopi confirms Hakubai-men/Kobai-men named for Kitano's plum blossoms and the preserved Showa interior.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-friendly local Showa-era Chinese-noodle shop where almost no tourists come and little to no English is spoken, so a foreign student should arrive with basic Japanese (or a translation app) and a relaxed, respectful attitude toward the chatty owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a town-Chinese noodle and gyoza shop. The core menu is wheat-based: chuka soba (ramen) and thin udon are wheat noodles, and the hand-made gyoza wrappers are wheat. Shared pots and surfaces mean heavy cross-contamination risk for anyone with celiac disease. Treat as not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broths and table condiments at a shop like this are soy-sauce based, and most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) is brewed with wheat. The signature noodle bowls and gyoza dipping should be assumed to contain wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made from pork and chicken bones, and the noodle toppings include pork (chashu) and chicken, so the core dishes are not vegan and the kitchen handles meat throughout. No vegan options are indicated.

places.name: 友楽菜館 (Yuraku Saikan)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a wheat/soy/dashi-heavy town-Chinese menu — ramen/中華そば, fried rice, ankake yakisoba, mapo tofu, tori-ten all rely on wheat noodles/flour, soy sauce, and shared woks/fryers. No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Treat as unsuitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering. Menu is meat- and animal-based (chicken, pork, egg fried rice, meat sauces) with soy-sauce/dashi seasoning and shared cooking surfaces. No documented vegetarian/vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–22:00 (hours and closing days subject to change; confirm ahead)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent town-Chinese shop near Mototanaka Station. Reviews/blogs describe it run by the proprietress (女将) — one source says "女将さんと娘さん" (proprietress and possibly her daughter), so it is a small owner-operated shop. No chain or noren-wake affiliation found anywhere. Classified independent_solo becaus 20 seats. Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio).

roles: Owner-operator (daughter of the founding generation, runs the kitchen and front solo)
chef_bio.background: Yuraku Saikan is a town-Chinese (machi-chuka) institution that has stood in Kyoto's Motoranaka neighborhood for more than 50 years, just off Higashioji-dori near Eizan Railway's Motoranaka Station. Today it is kept alive single-handedly by the daughter of the previous owner, who runs the whole place on her own. The interior wears its decades openly: a nostalgic Showa-era room with a counter and a few tables, comfortably worn-in rather than polished. In a student-heavy district, it has been a generous, affordable refuge for hungry locals and university students for generations, with bowls big enough to fix a student's vegetable deficit and ramen for around 550 yen.
chef_bio.philosophy: A home-style, handmade approach the regulars describe as 'okaasan no aji' - mom's cooking - generous in portion, gentle in flavor, and unpretentious. The mapo tofu, for instance, is made in a milder, soupy, Japanese-leaning style rather than a fiery one.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen is best known for its ankake (thick, glossy gravy) cooking. The standout is ankake yakisoba: thick noodles fried until crisp and fragrant, then smothered in a vegetable-rich Chinese gravy loaded with napa cabbage along with pork, ham, carrot, chikuwa and wood-ear mushrooms. Other house favorites include chuka-don and mabo-don, tenshinhan, a dorm-sized bowl of egg soup, an egg-and-meat stir-fry (tamago niku itame) with silky, fluffy egg, gingery chicken tempura (tori-ten), and old-style soft, juicy gyoza. The chicken-bone (tori-gara) base anchors many of the set meals.

anecdotes.text: The shop has been a Motoranaka fixture for over 50 years and is now run entirely on her own by the daughter of the previous owner - very much a one-person, mom's-cooking machi-chuka.

The signature ankake yakisoba is built on thick noodles griddled until crisp and fragrant, then blanketed with a five-vegetable gravy heavy on napa cabbage, with pork, ham, carrot, chikuwa fish cake and wood-ear mushroom - the chikuwa being a classic neighborhood-Chinese touch.

The tamago niku itame (egg and meat stir-fry) is praised for perfectly fluffy, silky egg, and the chicken tempura comes out crisp and gingery - everyday home-style Chinese food in a comfortably worn Showa room.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan-kyoto reports Yuraku Saikan has served Motoranaka (by Eizan's Motoranaka station, on Higashioji-dori) for 50-plus years and is now run solo by the previous owner's daughter, in a worn Showa room, with fluffy tamago-niku-itame (¥650) and gingery chicken tempura. osumituki details the ankake yakisoba (¥600) - crisped thick noodles under a napa-cabbage gravy with pork, ham, carrot, chikuwa and wood-ear - and its student-friendly ~¥600 pricing near Kyoto University.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only, dinner-only local Chinese diner run by a proprietress with no sign of English menus or service, so a foreign student should expect to order in basic Japanese (pointing works) and bring cash.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-, soy- and dashi-heavy town-Chinese (machi-chuka) menu and is not celiac-safe. Core dishes rely on wheat noodles and flour: ramen/chuka-soba, the signature ankake yakisoba (crisp wheat noodles), fried rice, gyoza wrappers, and battered chicken tempura (tori-ten). No gluten-free accommodation is indicated anywhere.

The kitchen is a small single-operator setup using shared woks and frying oil, so cross-contact between wheat-floured/battered items and everything else should be assumed. Treat as unsuitable for strict gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ankake gravies, stir-fries and set meals are seasoned in the standard Japanese-Chinese style, which means soy sauce - and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. The sweet-savory tamago niku itame and the ankake sauces should be treated as containing wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not suitable for vegans. Many savory bases lean on a chicken-bone (tori-gara) stock, and even vegetable-forward dishes mix in pork, ham, chikuwa fish cake or egg (e.g., the ankake yakisoba gravy and the egg dishes). Shared woks would also carry meat/seafood cross-contact.

noodles.notes: Standard town-Chinese style noodles served in a light broth; the shop is also known for its ankake yakisoba using thicker noodles griddled until crisp and aromatic. Exact ramen noodle gauge not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: チャーシューめん (chashu-men) — the chuka soba dressed up with extra sliced pork; the everyday 中華そば (550 yen) is the soul of the place
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork in the classic home-style chuka soba manner; preparation details (loin vs belly, exact braise) not documented.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a soy-based seasoning make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners; no gluten-free options documented at this family-run town-Chinese kitchen.

vegan.note: The broth and bowl lean on animal stock (chicken and pork) and pork chashu, with no documented plant-based option.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl from the vanishing world of Kyoto's old neighborhood Chinese kitchens, where ramen means a gentle, clear shoyu chuka soba simmered from chicken and pork — the kind of homey, unfussy soup that has fed Kyoto University students for half a century. You taste the lineage not in any trendy technique but in its warmth: light, savory, and deeply nostalgic.

places.highlight: A bowl from the vanishing world of Kyoto's old neighborhood Chinese kitchens, where ramen means a gentle, clear shoyu chuka soba simmered from chicken and pork — the kind of homey, unfussy soup that has fed Kyoto University students for half a century. You taste the lineage not in any trendy technique but in its warmth: light, savory, and deeply nostalgic.

places.name: まるせ (Maruse)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. The core dish is 中華そば — wheat-based ramen noodles in a soy- and dashi-based broth (蒲鉾/kamaboko fishcake also typically contains wheat starch). No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Treat as unsafe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Signature bowl features 豚バラチャーシュー (pork belly), pork/fish-based broth, and 蒲鉾 (fishcake). No plant-based menu or substitutions documented; broth is animal-based throughout.
places.hours_raw: Not listed on Tabelog (business-hours field blank; page states to contact the shop directly). Phone: 075-691-2685.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Presents as a single-location, individually run neighborhood 町食堂 (machi-shokudo) in a residential area. No evidence of branches, brand, franchise, or noren-wake network found in any source. Classified independent_solo; could be family-run (independent_family) but no documentation confirms family operation, so the more  Seats not published (small shop). Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio).

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Minami Ward, Nishi-Kujo (near Jujo Station)
chef_bio.background: Maruse is a tiny, well-loved neighborhood noodle shop in the Nishi-Kujo area of Kyoto's Minami Ward, run by an elderly couple who cook for a mostly local crowd. The shop has deep roots in the area: a longtime customer recalls patronizing it some thirty years ago when it operated over on Aburakoji Street, near his old workplace, before it settled into its current spot. Inside it is small and snug, with compact tables set in a row and a quiet, almost timeless feel. There is no television or radio playing, just the hush of regulars keeping their voices low, and a kitchen service window with takeout containers stacked near the door for the brisk local delivery trade.
chef_bio.specialty: Honest, generous, everyday Kansai-style noodle and rice fare at gentle prices. The kitchen turns out udon (kitsune udon with sweet fried tofu, niku/meat udon, nabeyaki udon), soba, and a lineup of donburi rice bowls (oyakodon, tempura shrimp bowl, curry meat bowl). The curry meat udon is a standout favorite that regulars rave about for being both cheap and genuinely delicious.

anecdotes.text: A longtime regular remembers the shop from about thirty years ago, when it operated on Aburakoji Street near his workplace and he frequented it often, a sign of how long this couple has been feeding the neighborhood.
Diners describe the room as enveloped in silence, with no TV or radio, just regulars keeping conversation soft, an unusually calm, old-fashioned atmosphere for a casual noodle shop.
One reviewer's highlight: curry meat udon for around 490 yen, called out as both inexpensive and delicious.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists Maruse at 56 Nishi-Kujo Nishi-Yanagi-no-Uchi-cho, Minami-ku (287m from Jujo), a small elderly-couple shop with counter seating, no TV or radio, dine-in and takeout/delivery, most items ~¥490-570. Reviews recall it operating on Aburakoji-dori ~30 years ago and single out the cheap, delicious curry meat udon.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny residential-neighborhood town shokudo with no English signage, menu, or online English presence — a foreign student should be comfortable with basic Japanese (or a translation app) and a polite, low-key approach, as this is a very local spot unaccustomed to tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-forward Japanese noodle shop. The menu is built on udon and soba (wheat noodles), with broths that are soy- and dashi-based and donburi that include tempura. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, and shared pots, broth, and prep surfaces make cross-contamination essentially unavoidable. Treat as not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and donburi sauces rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which typically contain wheat. No wheat-free soy alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan or vegetarian-specific options are documented. Dashi broths are fish/bonito-based and many dishes include meat, egg, or fishcake, so even noodle dishes are not vegan. Shared broth and prep make vegan cross-contact likely.

places.name: 六波羅飯店 (Rokuhara Hanten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic Chinese diner whose dishes (ramen, gyoza, fried rice, ankake bowls, the signature 六波羅丼) rely heavily on wheat-based noodles/wrappers, soy sauce, and shared woks/fryers. No gluten-free accommodation documented; cross-contamination is essentially unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering. Menu is meat- and egg-centric (karaage, pork, egg-thickened dishes) and broths/sauces are animal/dashi-based. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 11:00–21:00; closed Tue
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Confirmed family-run by a second-generation owner ("2代目の大将") together with his mother, per a local Kyoto food blog. Single location near Rokuhara-mitsuji Temple; no branches or brand network found. 28 seats. Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio).

chef_bio.background: Rokuhara Hanten is a classic Showa-era town Chinese diner tucked along Matsubara-dori in Kyoto's Higashiyama, a short walk from Gion and right in the shadow of the historic Rokuhara Mitsuji Temple. It is a true family operation, run by the second-generation owner together with his mother, who together keep the unpretentious neighborhood-eatery spirit alive. Step inside and it feels frozen in a gentler era: a snug room of counter and table seats, a TV murmuring in the corner, and the clatter and aromas drifting from the open kitchen. Locals, temple-goers, and tourists wander in side by side for the same generous, affordable plates that have made the place a fixture for decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: A working-class, come-as-you-are neighborhood Chinese restaurant: big portions, gentle prices, and a relaxed, unpretentious atmosphere where anyone can drop in without ceremony.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty, old-school Chinese diner cooking served in famously generous portions. The curry chanpon is a signature: thick, straight egg noodles under a rich, almost gravy-like curry ankake sauce loaded with vegetables. The 六波羅丼 (Rokuhara-don) is the house namesake bowl, an ankake-style dish of stir-fried vegetables and pork in a glossy thickened sauce over rice. Their fried chicken is another favorite, fist-sized chunks of juicy thigh meat in a fluffy egg batter. In summer they're also known for a gloriously retro Showa-style cold ramen, thick chilled egg noodles in a slightly sweet vinegar-soy base, plated with cucumber, egg ribbons, ham, char siu, and a vintage canned cherry at the center.

anecdotes.text: It's the kind of place where a relaxed, easygoing air fills the room, kitchen sounds and cooking aromas included, and where a chicken-tempura set meal is exactly the comfort you came for.
The summer cold ramen is a showpiece of Showa nostalgia: thick chilled egg noodles fanned out with cucumber, egg ribbons, ham, and char siu around a single vintage canned cherry in the middle.
The fried chicken arrives in 'fist-sized' chunks, juicy thigh meat under a fluffy egg batter, part of a menu where everything is known for generous portions.
The shop is run by the second-generation owner together with his mother, keeping the retro town-Chinese eatery going near Rokuhara Mitsuji Temple.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan-kyoto describes Rokuhara Hanten as a Showa town-Chinese diner on Matsubara-dori by Rokuhara Mitsuji, run by the second-generation owner with his mother (~28 seats), famed for curry chanpon (¥800) and fist-sized egg-battered fried chicken (¥750). kyotopi details the summer cold ramen (¥750) with a canned cherry centered among cucumber, egg ribbons, ham and char siu, plus the ankake Rokuhara-don; dosue confirms the ankake curry chanpon and Rokuhara-don rice bowl. No source substantiates celebrity patronage, so that claim was removed.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming but very local, Japanese-only spot — a foreign student should be comfortable here with basic Japanese (or a translation app) and cash, since there are no English menus and cards are not accepted.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a classic town Chinese diner built around wheat: ramen and chanpon egg noodles, gyoza wrappers, and shared woks and a single fryer used for fried chicken, gyoza, and fried rice. Cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable, so the kitchen is not celiac-safe.

Signature dishes such as the curry chanpon use thick wheat egg noodles, and the namesake Rokuhara-don is an ankake (thickened-sauce) bowl typically built on soy sauce and starch, so even the rice dishes are not safe for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cold ramen is served in a vinegar-soy base and ankake dishes lean on soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) is brewed with wheat, so these sauces should be assumed to contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered on pork, chicken, egg, and seafood-based Chinese cooking with shared woks; even vegetable-forward dishes are cooked alongside meat and egg, so there is no documented vegan option and cross-contact is likely.

noodles.notes: Standard machi-chuka egg-style noodles. The well-documented cold reimen uses shikoshiko springy thick straight egg noodles served over ice (paired creatively with thin rice vermicelli); the hot chuka soba and curry champon noodles are softer-cooked, with the curry version held in a thick, clingy ankake-style sauce.

ramen.signature_bowl: Curry champon (curry chanpon-men) — softer noodles under a thick, concentrated curry roux loaded with cabbage, carrot and bamboo shoot; for a plain bowl, the classic chuka soba
ramen.chashu: Old-school roasted/braised pork (cha-shu) of the town-Chinese kind; it appears as a topping including on the cold reimen. Specific cut and preparation not documented.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy (wheat) tare and a curry roux thickened with flour; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented at this classic town-Chinese diner.

vegan.note: A chicken-bone (torigara) stock underpins the soups and the curry, and pork/ham/egg appear throughout the noodle dishes; no documented plant-only broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic Showa-era neighborhood Chinese diner tucked beside Rokuhara-mitsuji temple, where the soul of the bowl is an honest, light chicken-bone soy broth in the old machi-chuka tradition rather than any one regional ramen lineage. Its true signature voice is the hearty curry champon — soft noodles cloaked in a thick, vegetable-packed curry roux — a comfort-food riff that locals have leaned on for generations.

places.highlight: A nostalgic Showa-era neighborhood Chinese diner tucked beside Rokuhara-mitsuji temple, where the soul of the bowl is an honest, light chicken-bone soy broth in the old machi-chuka tradition rather than any one regional ramen lineage. Its true signature voice is the hearty curry champon — soft noodles cloaked in a thick, vegetable-packed curry roux — a comfort-food riff that locals have leaned on for generations.

places.name: ギョーザ泰三 (Gyoza Taizo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. Gyoza wrappers are wheat-flour based and the signature miso dipping sauce plus typical seasonings involve soy/wheat. No indication of GF accommodation; assume cross-contamination in a small kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The core product is meat gyoza (Kagoshima black-pork mince). No vegan items documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 17:00–21:00; closed Tue (some sources instead list 16:00–22:00 with irregular days off)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single independent owner-operated shop. Chef 古屋泰三 relocated his own long-running Kobe-Motomachi shop "餃子専門店 古屋" (founded 1988, ~23 years in Kobe) to Kyoto, reopening under the name ギョーザ泰三 on 2020-06-01. No branches or noren-wake network found; this is a one-location, owner-run shop. 10 seats. Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio). Map pin is approximate (chō-level geocode in a dense downtown block); use the Maps link for exact location.

chef_bio.background: Gyoza Taizo carries on a recipe that began in 1988, when its owner opened a small gyoza specialty shop called Furuya in Kobe. After more than thirty years of perfecting handmade dumplings in Hyogo, he brought that craft to Kyoto, reopening just off Kawaramachi in June 2020 under the new name Gyoza Taizo. It's an intimate, tucked-away counter spot on the second floor of the Shinobu Kaikan building, near Ura-Teramachi, with only about eight seats, so expect a cozy, made-to-order pace.
chef_bio.philosophy: Wrap to order, not in advance. The owner makes each gyoza fresh once it's ordered so the thin dough keeps its crispness, even though it means a slower, small-batch rhythm.
chef_bio.specialty: Thin-skinned, made-to-order gyoza. The kitchen wraps each dumpling only after you order so the delicate skin stays crisp, then chars them and pairs them with a rich house miso dipping sauce (with optional Sichuan pepper). Fillings lean on Kagoshima black pork plus prominent domestic garlic and ginger, and fresh vegetables. Beyond the classic, look for creative originals: negi gyoza topped with scallions and egg yolk, shiso (perilla) gyoza with a touch of sudachi citrus, and Hong Kong-style noodles finished with cilantro.

anecdotes.text: The signature thin-skin gyoza is so flavorful from garlic and ginger that the shop suggests trying a bite without any sauce first, just to appreciate the wrapper and filling on their own.
A standout original is the negi gyoza, crowned with scallions and a glossy egg yolk and dressed in the house miso sauce for a sweet, umami-rich, creamy bite.
The dumplings are wrapped only after you place your order to keep the thin dough at its crispiest, a hallmark of the shop carried over from its Kobe days.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osaka-gourmet reports the recipe began at a 1988 Hyogo gyoza specialist called Furuya, which after 30-plus years relocated to Kyoto in June 2020 as Gyoza Taizo, in the Shinobu Building 2F ~62m from Kawaramachi Station, an L-shaped counter seating ~8, using Kagoshima black pork, generous domestic garlic/ginger and a Sichuan-pepper miso sauce. kyotopi confirms the wrap-to-order thin skins and the negi (egg-yolk), shiso-sudachi, and Hong Kong cilantro-noodle originals.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny (10-seat, ~4-tsubo) owner-run counter shop where almost everything operates in Japanese, reservations are not accepted, and gyoza orders require a 2-person minimum — friendly but best approached with basic Japanese or a Japanese-speaking companion.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The gyoza here are wheat-flour wrappers, and the shop's whole identity is thin-skinned dumplings, so wheat is central to nearly every dish.

This is a tiny roughly ten-seat counter kitchen wrapping and char-cooking wheat dumplings to order on shared surfaces, so flour and cross-contact should be assumed throughout. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dipping sauce is a rich miso-based sauce, and miso plus typical Japanese seasonings commonly contain wheat and soy. Treat the sauces and seasonings as containing wheat and soy unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not suitable for vegans. The dumplings are built around Kagoshima black pork, and originals add egg yolk (negi gyoza). Fillings, sauces, and the shared kitchen all involve animal products.

places.name: 三宝の餃子 (Sanpo no Gyoza)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour gyoza wrappers (皮から手作り = skins made from scratch from wheat flour); dishes also involve soy sauce and typical Chinese seasonings. No gluten-free accommodation documented. Avoid if gluten-intolerant. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering documented. Gyoza, Chinese dishes and ramen are meat/animal-based; gyoza commonly contain pork and the broths use animal stock. No evidence of vegan menu items. (Menu review: 1 vegan meal.)
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 17:00–04:00 / Sun 17:00–02:00; closed Mon
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single location near Kitano Tenmangu. Multiple sources (Tokyo Gyoza food blog, local guild page) describe a homemade, family-run shop decorated with handwritten posters and children's artwork, beloved by locals. Listed in the 上七軒匠会 (Kamishichiken Takumikai), a local merchants'/artisans' association — not a restaurant c 20 seats. Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio).

chef_bio.origin: 北野白梅町／北野天満宮 (Kitano Hakumeicho / Kitano Tenmangu), Nishijin district, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Tucked into the old Nishijin weaving district a short walk from Kitano Tenmangu shrine, Sanpo no Gyoza is a neighborhood gyoza specialist that has quietly earned a devoted local following over the years. It opens in the evening and keeps going deep into the night, making it a beloved late-night refuge for students, locals, and night owls in the Kitano Hakumeicho area. Part of its charm is the room itself: the walls are covered with the owner's own hand-drawn posters, menu cards, and illustrations, alongside nostalgic manga, giving the place a warm, lived-in, distinctly handmade personality. The owner is known for being friendly, attentive, and welcoming to families and regulars alike.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made gyoza with skins made from scratch, at a remarkably low price. The wrappers have a real handmade character, chewy and pliable, turning crisp and deeply golden when pan-fried for that prized contrast of crunchy outside and chewy inside. The filling leans on cabbage, letting its natural sweetness come through, rounded out with garlic, so the dumplings are flavorful enough to enjoy on their own even before any sauce. Small and made to be eaten in a single bite, they deliver crispy, chewy, and juicy all at once. Beyond gyoza, the shop is also known for a surprisingly good tonkotsu-miso ramen, combination sets pairing ramen, gyoza, and rice, and a signature garlic-miso condiment for varying the flavor.

anecdotes.text: The gyoza skins are made from scratch in-house, and the chewy-then-crispy wrappers paired with cabbage-forward filling let the natural sweetness of the vegetables shine, so the dumplings are tasty even without dipping sauce.
A favorite among long-time residents of the Nishijin neighborhood, prized for handmade-skin gyoza at an exceptionally affordable price, with locals regularly stopping in for takeout.
The owner's artistic touch shows throughout the shop in hand-drawn posters, menus, and signage covering the walls.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms Sanpo no Gyoza near Kitano Tenmangu (Kitano Hakumeicho) makes from-scratch handmade skins - crispy-chewy-juicy, one-bite, cabbage-and-garlic forward and good without sauce - plus tonkotsu-miso ramen, gyoza/ramen/rice sets and a garlic-miso condiment, with 5pm-late (to 4am) hours and owner-drawn wall art. tokyogyoza confirms the ~¥250 bargain handmade-skin gyoza, brisk takeout trade, Nishijin regulars and the hand-drawn signage.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, local, late-night family gyoza shop with no documented English support — bring basic Japanese or a translation app, and a simple konnichiwa plus pointing at the menu will be warmly received.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not celiac-safe. The core product is gyoza with wheat-flour skins made from scratch in-house, and the wrappers are pan-fried on the same surfaces used for the rest of the menu, so gluten is unavoidable and cross-contact is effectively certain.
The menu is built around wheat-heavy items (gyoza, ramen, Chinese dishes), so a shared cooking environment with flour and wheat-based noodles should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gyoza are typically served with a vinegar-and-soy-sauce dip and chili oil. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the dipping sauce should be treated as a gluten source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan. The gyoza filling contains meat, and the kitchen serves tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen and other meat-based Chinese dishes, so animal products and shared cooking surfaces are pervasive.

places.name: ミスター・ギョーザ (Mr. Gyoza)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a gyoza (dumpling) specialty shop — the wrappers are wheat flour and the menu centers on gyoza, ramen (wheat noodles), and karaage (typically flour-dredged). Seasonings are soy-based. No gluten-free options are advertised; high cross-contamination risk. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options advertised. Signature gyoza contain pork/meat filling and the menu is meat- and broth-centric (ramen, karaage). No documented plant-based dish; vegans should not rely on this venue.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Wed, Fri–Sun 11:30–20:30 (last order 20:00); ends when gyoza sell out
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single restaurant location in Kyoto's Karahashi Takadacho since 1971, no branch restaurants. They distribute frozen/fresh gyoza through department-store and supermarket retail partners and an online shop, but that is product retail, not multi-branch restaurant operation, so it does not constitute a restaurant chain. Fo 20 seats. Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio).

chef_bio.background: Mr. Gyoza opened in 1971 and has spent more than 50 years as a tiny, dumpling-devoted shop tucked into Kyoto's Minami-ku, just a short walk from the World Heritage Toji Temple. It is a true neighborhood institution run under Kitakawa Enterprises, with only a handful of counter seats and a single-minded focus. Famously, the shop makes only as many gyoza as it can prepare that day and simply closes once they sell out, so the dumplings always reach customers at their freshest. Word of mouth has carried its reputation across the city and into TV programs and magazines, and it is regularly named among Kyoto's very best gyoza shops.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners say what keeps them going is the simple joy of hearing customers say 'that was delicious,' and their goal is to hold true to the same flavor they have served since the day they opened. Making only the day's batch and closing when it's gone is the practical expression of that commitment to freshness and consistency.
chef_bio.specialty: Pan-fried gyoza are the star: crisp, golden-brown bottoms and a chewy, elastic wrapper around a light, savory filling that leads with the natural sweetness of cabbage and pork. They are easy to eat in quantity and come with a choice of a miso-based dip or a vinegar-soy sauce with chili oil. Alongside the gyoza, the shop serves ramen in soy, salt, miso, and roasted-pork styles, a juicy karaage seasoned with mayonnaise, mustard and pepper, and a beloved whole pickled cucumber that regulars order to go with a beer.

anecdotes.text: The shop makes only the quantity of gyoza it can prepare that day and closes as soon as they sell out, a deliberate practice so every order arrives at peak freshness.
Open since 1971 and still run with the same recipe, the owners say the joy of customers calling the gyoza delicious is what drives them to keep the founding taste unchanged.

The gyoza are praised for crisp, golden bottoms and a chewy, springy wrapper, with a filling that leads with the sweetness of cabbage and pork; they come with a miso dip or a vinegar-soy-and-chili-oil dip.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site and about page state Mr. Gyoza opened in 1971 (Showa 46), is run by 有限会社喜多川エンタープライズ (Kitakawa Enterprises) at Karahashi Takada-cho 42, Minami-ku, sells only the day's batch and closes when sold out to serve gyoza at their best, and aims to keep its founding taste alive on the joy of customers' praise; it lists genuine TV (TBS Lavit!, Yomiuri/Kansai TV) and magazine (Hanako, Monthly Kyoto) features. gcjapan confirms the crisp-bottomed chewy gyoza with miso or vinegar-soy-chili dips.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, cash-only (no cards/e-money/QR), no-reservation local gyoza shop where ordering is in Japanese and queues are common, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the simple menu, pay cash, and use basic Japanese or a phone translator.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a gyoza specialty shop whose core menu is built on wheat: the dumpling wrappers are wheat flour, the ramen uses wheat noodles, and karaage is typically flour-dredged. Gyoza, ramen and fried items are prepared in the same small kitchen, so cross-contamination with wheat is essentially unavoidable. Not celiac-safe.
No gluten-free options are advertised and there is no indication of allergen-aware preparation; the menu centers entirely on wheat-based gyoza and ramen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The gyoza are served with a vinegar-soy sauce (plus chili oil) as one of the dip options, and ramen broths include a soy (shoyu) style. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so these seasonings are not gluten-free.
Ramen is offered in soy, salt, miso, and roasted-pork styles; the soy and miso bases are typically wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered on pork-and-cabbage gyoza, meat ramen broths, and fried chicken, with no advertised vegan or vegetarian options. Even the side soup shares the same broth used for ramen. There are no documented plant-based dishes.

places.name: やきとり雷 円町店 (Yakitori Kaminari – Enmachi)
places.gf_detail: Charcoal yakitori. Tare-glazed skewers contain wheat soy sauce; salt-grilled (shio) skewers avoid the tare but share a grill. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Grilled chicken skewers over binchōtan. No vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu–Sun 17:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:00). Closed Wed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop — binchōtan yakitori] The Enmachi branch of 雷 (the original honten is in Mibu, near Shijō-Ōmiya). ~20 seats, binchōtan charcoal, Hakata salt; evening-only, open late to midnight. Reviews assessed as overwhelmingly Japanese (proxy from Tabelog presence, not a counted ratio).

chef_bio.background: Yakitori Kaminari is a casual, unpretentious yakitori spot tucked just down Saido-dori from JR Enmachi Station, with a folk-craft-style izakaya feel. The Enmachi shop is one of a small local pair, sharing its name with a sister location near Shijo-Omiya. The room is built around an L-shaped counter with a couple of tables tucked in the back, the kind of neighborhood place where you watch the skewers cook over the coals in front of you.
chef_bio.philosophy: Reasonably priced, genuinely good yakitori - approachable food cooked over real charcoal rather than anything fussy.
chef_bio.specialty: Binchotan charcoal-grilled chicken skewers at friendly prices, prized for the toasty, fragrant char the charcoal gives off. Salt-grilled skewers are finished with Hakata salt. The lineup runs from negima (chicken and scallion) and seseri (chicken neck) to tsukune meatballs and kawa (skin), and the house signature is the Kaminari-don rice bowl topped with negima and nori.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen grills over binchotan charcoal and seasons its shio skewers with Hakata salt, leaning on the aromatic char of charcoal-fire cooking; skewers run roughly 270-440 yen each.
The signature Kaminari-don is a rice bowl crowned with negima skewers and nori, alongside a menu of negima, salted seseri, ginger-tinged tsukune, kawa and heart.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi describes Yakitori Kaminari near JR Enmachi (Nakagyo-ku), with a Shijo-Omiya sister shop, an L-shaped counter plus back tables, binchotan-grilled skewers seasoned with Hakata salt (~¥270-440 each), a lineup of negima, seseri, tsukune, kawa and heart, and the signature Kaminari-don rice bowl topped with negima, Kyoto negi and nori.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local yakitori counter near JR Enmachi (smoking allowed); Japanese menu. A little Japanese or pointing works; order skewers by the piece.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a charcoal yakitori grill. Tare-glazed skewers are brushed with a sweet soy-based sauce that contains wheat, and salt (shio) skewers, while sauce-free, are cooked on the same shared grill as the tare skewers. Cross-contact from shared grill surfaces and basting is likely. Not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare (basting glaze) on yakitori is a soy-sauce-based sauce, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Tare-glazed skewers should be treated as containing wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a chicken yakitori specialist with no documented vegan offerings; the menu centers on chicken skewers, and even vegetable items are cooked on a grill shared with meat. Not suitable for vegans.

positives.text: Salt-grilled (shio) skewers are seasoned only with Hakata salt rather than the wheat-containing tare, so the seasoning itself is wheat-free - but they share the grill with tare skewers, so this does not make them celiac-safe. Confirm directly with staff.

places.name: 自家製麺 天狗 (Jikaseimen Tengu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. The shop centers on udon and soba (wheat and buckwheat noodles) and homemade wheat noodles; broths typically use soy sauce and dashi. High wheat and soy content — unsuitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu confirmed. Japanese noodle dishes here almost certainly use dashi (commonly bonito/fish-based) and may include meat/egg toppings; a vegan diner would need to ask directly, and even plain noodles risk fish-based broth.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:00, 17:30–19:30 / holidays 11:30–14:00; ends when sold out
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tabelog describes it as a single-house restaurant (一軒家レストラン) with no mention of branches; appears to be a single independent location. Could not definitively confirm family vs solo operation, but no chain/brand affiliation found.

chef_bio.origin: 神宮丸太町 / 上京区, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: A cherished old-fashioned neighborhood udon shop tucked along Kawaramachi-dori, just north of the Kojin-guchi crossing near the Kyoto Imperial Palace and the prefectural university hospital. Long-running and steeped in a homey, lived-in atmosphere, it draws a loyal crowd of locals who come as much for the warmth as for the noodles. One charming touch regulars love: the shop keeps a guest comment notebook at the tables, and the staff actually write back to messages left on earlier visits, turning a quick lunch into an ongoing little correspondence.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to making its own noodles in-house ('jikaseimen') and pairing them with a clean, light Kyoto-style dashi, in the unfussy spirit of a classic neighborhood noodle shop.
chef_bio.specialty: The house is built around its homemade noodles, above all the signature 'sasamen' (ささめん) thin noodles made in-house. Diners single out the homemade thin noodles for their pleasant firmness and bite, paired with a light, fragrant Kyoto-style dashi broth. A standout winter dish is the 'shimoori' sasamen/udon simmered in a warming sake-lees (kasu) broth, loaded with pork, vegetables, shrimp tempura, and a poached egg. Beyond noodles, the kitchen turns out an extensive menu including a well-loved oyako-don (chicken and egg rice bowl), curry-dashi dishes, rice bowls, and daily lunch sets.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a wonderfully old-fashioned udon shop frequented by locals, with the homemade thin noodles ('sasamen') showing a pleasant firmness and pairing excellently with a sake-lees broth in the winter 'shimoori' bowl.

A visitor near the prefectural university hospital praised the Kyoto-style light dashi with firm hand-made noodles, friendly staff, and a delightful guest comment book at the tables that the staff write back into on return visits.
The shop is known for homemade thin noodles ('sasamen'), udon, and soba, with a hearty sake-lees broth 'frost' udon as a winter specialty.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gnavi lists Jikaseimen Tengu in Kamigyo-ku on Kawaramachi-dori just above Kojin-guchi (near Jingu-Marutamachi station), specializing in homemade sasamen thin noodles, udon and soba, with a winter-only sake-lees 'frost' udon full of shrimp tempura. Tabelog reviewers praise the light Kyoto dashi and firm handmade noodles and note the guest comment notebook that staff write back into.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local Japanese noodle shop with no listed website and Japanese-only reviews, so a foreign student should ideally go with a Japanese-speaking guide or be prepared to point at the menu and use basic Japanese.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a homemade-noodle (jikaseimen) shop centered on udon and soba (wheat and buckwheat noodles) plus in-house thin wheat 'sasamen' noodles. With fresh wheat noodles made and boiled on-site, flour dust and shared noodle-boiling water make heavy gluten cross-contamination effectively unavoidable. Not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broths are a Kyoto-style dashi typically seasoned with soy sauce, and there are curry-dashi dishes and tempura items as well. Most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the broths and seasonings should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes documented in sources rely on dashi (typically bonito/fish-based), pork, egg (poached egg, oyako-don), and shrimp tempura, so the broths and most dishes are not vegan. Vegan suitability is unconfirmed and should be checked directly with the shop.

positives.text: Reviewers describe friendly, attentive staff (including a charming guest comment notebook the staff write back into), which may make it easier to ask questions about ingredients in person, though no allergen accommodations are documented.

places.name: 麺処 美松 (Mendokoro Mimatsu)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat udon noodles; dashi is made from 昆布(kombu)・鰹(bonito)・いりこ/niboshi (dried small fish) and dishes use soy-based seasonings. Wheat and fish/dashi are central; no dedicated gluten-free preparation. Cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Dashi is animal-derived (bonito/niboshi), oden and menu items rely on fish-based stock, and no plant-based broth is mentioned. Not suitable for vegans/vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri–Sun 10:30–14:30, 17:00–20:00 / Wed 10:30–14:30; closed Thu. Hours and closing days may change, so confirm with the shop before visiting.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single independent location opened 2019-07-11 in Kyoto. Family lineage: original shop 三松 founded in Imabari (Ehime) in 1945/昭和25年(1950) per sources; 2nd generation switched to homemade noodles and renamed 美松; current owner is the 3rd generation (三代目), trained under the 2nd genera

chef_bio.origin: Imabari, Ehime Prefecture (Shikoku) — now in Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Mimatsu's roots reach back to 1950 in Imabari, on the udon-loving island of Shikoku, where the family's first shop opened under the name 三松 (Mimatsu). The second generation made the defining change that still shapes the bowls today: switching to meticulously made in-house noodles, a commitment so central that the shop was renamed 美松. The current owner is the third generation to carry the trade. A longtime Kyoto resident, they trained under the second-generation master back in Imabari before bringing the family's noodles to Kyoto, opening 麺処美松 in the Enmachi area in July 2019 and later settling near Kitano-Hakubaicho. Three generations on, the owner keeps refining the flavors little by little to suit how people eat today, while holding onto the foundations passed down from Imabari.
chef_bio.philosophy: Stay true to the handmade-noodle craft handed down through three generations from Imabari, while gradually tuning the flavors to meet the tastes of today's diners.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade udon with a soft, springy, mochi-like (モチモチ) bite — gentler than the sharp firmness of classic Sanuki udon — bathed in a broth built from kombu, bonito, and niboshi (dried small fish) that leans subtly sweet. Beyond the noodles, the shop is known for its 出汁おにぎり (dashi rice balls), a self-service oden counter (a rarity in Kyoto) featuring ingredients sourced from the family's home of Imabari, and a curry udon seasoned with a distinctive curry salt. The 天ざるうどん, served with shrimp and chicken tempura, is a signature plate.

anecdotes.text: The family business began in 1950 in Imabari as 三松; when the second generation switched to its own handmade noodles, the shop was renamed 美松 — and the third-generation owner now carries that tradition in Kyoto.
A rarity for Kyoto: a self-service oden counter, stocked with ingredients brought from the owner's home region of Imabari, sits alongside the udon.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi reports Mimatsu traces to 1950 Imabari, Ehime, as 三松; the second generation switched to handmade noodles and renamed it 美松, and the third-generation owner trained under the second generation in Imabari before opening the Kyoto shop (Enmachi) in July 2019. It confirms the mochimochi soft udon (softer than Sanuki), a kombu/bonito/niboshi slightly-sweet broth, dashi onigiri, a self-service oden with Imabari ingredients, curry-salt udon, and the tenzaru udon (¥850) with shrimp and chicken tempura.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny ~10-15 seat counter-only neighborhood udon shop with self-serve oden, likely Japanese-only menu and staff, so a foreign student should be comfortable with basic ordering customs and pointing, ideally bringing a Japanese-reading friend or translation app.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not celiac-safe. The core product is handmade wheat udon, so wheat and gluten are central to nearly every dish and cross-contamination is unavoidable in a noodle-focused kitchen.
Tempura (shrimp and chicken) is a signature offering, which adds wheat-flour batter and likely shared frying oil to the gluten picture.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use soy-based seasonings, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — an additional gluten source beyond the noodles themselves.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broth is built from bonito and niboshi (dried small fish) alongside kombu, so dashi-based dishes contain fish. Tempura includes shrimp and chicken.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific allergy-handling reports were found in available sources. Diners with allergies should confirm directly with the shop before ordering.

places.name: 本格手打うどん 大河 (Honkaku Teuchi Udon Taiga)
places.gf_detail: NOT celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour handmade udon; the kitchen handles flour throughout and tempura is served, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Dashi-based broths and soy-based seasonings (soy sauce/tsuyu) are standard. No gluten-free menu confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu confirmed. Udon broth/tsuyu is typically made with bonito/fish (and often kombu) dashi, and signature items include chicken, shrimp, and chikuwa tempura. A vegan would need to ask directly; no plant-based dashi option is documented.
places.hours_raw: Fri–Sun 11:00–14:00 (last order 14:00; arrive and sign the wait list by 14:00); closed Mon–Thu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location, owner-operated shop. The owner (大将) is described in multiple sources as having trained in Sanuki udon in Shikoku. No evidence of a chain or multiple branches; treated as an independent owner-operated business. Family vs solo not definitively confirmed, classified

chef_bio.background: Taiga opened in the Fukakusa neighborhood of Fushimi in May 2012. The owner, Kishino Tatsuya, learned his craft over a two-year apprenticeship at Ikegami Seimen in Kagawa Prefecture, the legendary Sanuki udon shop known to fans as 'Rumi-baachan's' place. He came to udon from a previous career in sales, and he carried over a single guiding idea: make what the customer actually wants, rather than what you have decided to serve. Wanting udon to be talked about with the same seriousness as ramen, he set out to do things the hard way that almost no one else bothered with.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kishino deliberately makes the noodles in a way few shops use: hand-kneaded with no mixer and no foot-treading, a high-hydration dough rested overnight in the cold, then cut and boiled to order throughout service so the udon is as fresh as possible on the plate. His stated aim was to be a pioneer precisely because 'no one else does it,' and to serve what the guest in front of him wants.
chef_bio.specialty: Honest hand-cut Sanuki-style udon with a famously chewy, springy bite, paired with big, crisp tempura cut and fried to order. The house plate, the Taiga-mori bukkake, comes loaded with shrimp tempura, chicken tempura, chikuwa and shiso-leaf tempura. The chicken tempura (made from breast rather than thigh) and the shrimp tempura are particular favorites, and seasonal Fukakusa bamboo shoots appear as tempura and rice in spring.

anecdotes.text: Owner Kishino Tatsuya trained for two years at Ikegami Seimen in Kagawa, the Sanuki udon shop affectionately known as 'Rumi-baachan's,' before opening Taiga in Fukakusa in 2012.

He wanted udon to be discussed as seriously as ramen and chose the laborious path on purpose: 'Since no one else does it, I'll become the pioneer.' The dough is hand-kneaded with no machine mixer and no foot-treading, rested overnight, and cut and boiled to order during service.
Coming from a sales background, his approach is to serve what the customer genuinely wants rather than forcing a fixed menu on them.
The signature Taiga-mori bukkake piles on shrimp, chicken, chikuwa and shiso-leaf tempura; the chicken tempura is made from breast that is marinated for 24 hours before frying.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hotpepper Mesitsu (interview) confirms Kishino Tatsuya left a sales career and apprenticed two years at Ikegami Seimen in Kagawa before opening Taiga in Fukakusa, Fushimi in May 2012; he hand-kneads without a mixer or foot-treading, rests a high-hydration dough overnight, and cuts and boils (~13 min) to order, wanting udon taken as seriously as ramen ('since no one else does it, I'll be the pioneer'). The Taiga-mori bukkake carries shrimp, chicken, chikuwa and shiso tempura, the chicken from breast marinated 24 hours in daikon juice and Rausu kelp.

cultural_comfort.note: A small 15-seat Japanese udon counter shop open only Fri-Sun with a sign-in name board (you must arrive and write your name by 14:00); going with a Japanese-speaking friend or having key phrases ready helps, as English support is unlikely.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a handmade wheat-flour udon shop where flour is kneaded, rested and cut to order throughout service, so airborne and surface flour contact is unavoidable. It is not celiac-safe.
Tempura is a core part of the menu and the batter is wheat flour mixed with dashi, fried in shared oil alongside the udon operation, so fried items are not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and bukkake/tsuyu sauces are dashi- and soy-sauce based. Most Japanese soy sauce and tsuyu contain wheat, so the seasonings should be assumed to contain gluten unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi-based broths contain seafood derivatives (bonito/kelp dashi), and the tempura batter is made with secondary dashi, so even vegetable tempura is not vegan. No vegetarian or vegan menu is documented.

positives.text: Seasonal vegetable tempura, including local Fukakusa bamboo shoots in spring, is a highlight for diners without wheat restrictions.

places.name: 六条新町 招福亭 (Rokujo Shinmachi Shofukutei)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is wheat-based (udon, donburi) and soba/茶そば is typically blended with wheat flour. Dishes rely on soy-sauce and dashi-based broths (soy + fish dashi). No gluten-free preparation indicated.
places.vegan_detail: Soba/udon broths and donburi use fish-based dashi (katsuo/kombu blends) and the signature dishes include カツ丼 and 親子丼 (pork/chicken/egg). No vegan menu indicated; even noodle broth is not vegan.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 11:00–16:00, 16:30–19:00; closed Mon and Tue
places.neighborhood: 六条新町 / 下京区 (Shimogyo-ku), near Gojo Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent neighborhood 食堂 known for 茶そば (tea soba); no chain/brand affiliation found. Treated as independent family-run based on local-eatery descriptions.

chef_bio.chef_name: Shimizu Yusuke (third generation, kitchen); Shimizu Hisayuki and his wife (second generation, front of house)
chef_bio.background: Shofukutei is a family soba-and-udon house that has stood on Shinmachi-dori, one of Kyoto's old Heian-era streets, for generations. The shop opened in 1938 (Showa 13) and has been run by the same family ever since, growing into a neighborhood institution that locals have quietly loved across the decades. Today it is a true multi-generational operation: the second generation, Shimizu Hisayuki and his wife, look after the dining room, while the third generation, Shimizu Yusuke, works the kitchen. In October 2016 the family moved the shop from its longtime spot directly across Shinmachi-dori into a larger, refreshed space, but the homey, unpretentious mood of an old Kyoto everyday eatery carried right over.
chef_bio.philosophy: The family talks about choosing ingredients with the diner's wellbeing in mind, paying attention to where things come from and to freshness, and cooking with customers' health in mind. The kitchen leans on a refined, slightly sweet Kyoto-style dashi as the heart of its bowls.
chef_bio.specialty: They are best known for their house-made green tea soba (chasoba), kneaded with Uji matcha so the noodles come out a vivid green with a gentle matcha fragrance and a touch of sweetness. The dish that draws people in is the Fukusoba ("good-fortune soba"): tea soba in a softly sweet, dashi-forward Kyoto-style broth, crowned with colorful, auspicious toppings, prawns, kamaboko fish cake, shiitake, spinach and egg. Locals have a saying that when it comes to chasoba, you think of Shofukutei.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 1938 and is now in its third generation: Shimizu Yusuke runs the kitchen while the second generation, Shimizu Hisayuki and his wife, look after the dining room. The proprietress describes their cooking as choosing ingredients by origin and freshness with the customer's health in mind.

Their signature green tea soba is made in-house with Uji matcha, giving the noodles a bright color and a subtle matcha aroma and sweetness; the much-loved Fukusoba arrives with colorful, lucky toppings, prawns, kamaboko, shiitake, spinach, nori and egg, in a gently sweet, dashi-rich Kyoto broth.
Locals say "when it comes to green tea soba, think of Shofukutei" - the one-of-a-kind chasoba is held up as the secret to the shop's lasting popularity as a beloved everyday eatery.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: KBS Kyoto (2023) and muchi2 confirm the family, chasoba and Fukusoba; multiple Kyoto listings (kyotonikanpai, mypl) give the founding as Showa 13 (1938), and Tabelog records the 2016-10-03 relocation/reopening across the street.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, inexpensive local soba/donburi diner where a foreign student would be comfortable, but staff likely speak little English and there is no English menu, so a translation app or pointing at photos helps.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a soba/udon/donburi house and is not celiac-safe. Udon and the donburi (rice-bowl) sets are wheat-based, and the house green tea soba (chasoba) is a matcha-blended noodle that, like nearly all Japanese soba, is typically cut with wheat flour. Noodles are boiled in shared water, so cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broths are a sweet, dashi-forward Kyoto style built on soy sauce and fish dashi. Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat, so the broths should be assumed to contain gluten. No wheat-free soy sauce or tamari is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not suitable for vegans. The dashi-based broths are made with fish stock, and the signature dishes feature egg, prawns, kamaboko (fish cake) and other animal products. There is no indication of a separate vegan broth or preparation.

places.name: いのうえの餃子 (Inoue no Gyoza)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is gyoza (wheat-flour wrappers) plus ramen (wheat noodles) and Chinese dishes; soy sauce and likely pork/dashi-based seasonings are pervasive. No documented gluten-free accommodations.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan options. Gyoza, ramen, and Chinese dishes here are meat-based (pork) with soy/animal-based seasonings; no plant-based menu indicated.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:00–20:00; closed Mon
places.neighborhood: 出町柳 / 上京区 (Demachiyanagi, Kamigyo-ku)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent shop opened March 3, 2023 by veteran chef Sadahiro Inoue (井上定博), who previously ran the well-known "Gyoza no Osho Demachi branch" before retiring it in 2020. No chain affiliation; the current shop is his own independent venture.

chef_bio.background: Inoue no Gyoza is the second act of Inoue Sadahiro, the beloved owner-cook known around the neighborhood simply as "Demachi no occhan" (Demachi's uncle). For decades he ran the Gyoza no Ohsho branch in Demachi as its franchise owner and manager, becoming a local institution. When he reached the chain's mandatory retirement age, that branch closed at the end of October 2020 at age 70, to the regret of regulars. About two and a half years later, on March 3, 2023, he came back on his own terms, opening his own tiny counter shop, Inoue no Gyoza, right in the Demachi Masugata shopping arcade. It is a small spot of roughly nine counter seats where he cooks the same nostalgic town-Chinese food he is known for.
chef_bio.philosophy: Inoue is famous for a standing offer he carried over from his Ohsho days: anyone genuinely short on money can eat their fill for free in exchange for washing dishes for 30 minutes afterward (for those 18 and over who are struggling). His guiding belief, in his own Kyoto dialect, is that going hungry and cold today is no way to reach tomorrow.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-fashioned, town-Chinese style gyoza with a crisp, fragrant skin and a juicy, vegetable-sweet pork filling, turned out continuously on a small gyoza machine. The signature order is the gyoza set meal (gyoza with fried chicken, salad, soup and rice). The shop also serves a light, nostalgic soy-based ramen, char siu ramen, fried chicken, and gyoza curry.

anecdotes.text: For many years Inoue ran the Gyoza no Ohsho Demachi branch as its franchise owner, becoming the neighborhood's beloved "Demachi no occhan." That branch closed at the end of October 2020 when he hit the chain's retirement age, and about two and a half years later, on March 3, 2023, he reopened independently as Inoue no Gyoza in the Demachi Masugata arcade.

Inoue is best known for a sign he posted at Ohsho and revived at his own shop: people without meal money can eat their fill for free, on the condition they wash dishes for 30 minutes after eating, for those 18 and over who are having trouble affording food.
He reopened in Kyoto's Demachi neighborhood with the dishwashing-for-a-free-meal service intact, run out of a small counter shop in the Masugata shopping street.
Rather than being hand-folded, the gyoza are produced continuously on a small gyoza machine and aim for an old-fashioned, classic gyoza flavor.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter shop run by a friendly veteran chef famous for his dishwashing-for-a-free-meal kindness, but basic Japanese helps since the menu and service are Japanese-only with no documented English support.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe restaurant. The core menu is gyoza with wheat-flour wrappers, plus wheat-noodle ramen and other town-Chinese dishes, so gluten is unavoidable and there are no documented gluten-free options.

Fried items such as fried chicken (often wheat-battered or flour-dusted) are served alongside gyoza and curry from a small shared kitchen, so heavy cross-contamination of wheat should be assumed; no separate gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is described as soy-sauce (shoyu) based, and Chinese-style cooking here relies on soy sauce, which in Japan almost always contains wheat. Assume soy sauce throughout the menu is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The menu is built around pork gyoza, fried chicken, char siu ramen and meat-based dishes, with pork and likely meat or fish-based broths and dashi seasonings pervasive. No plant-based options are documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen handling, ingredient sourcing, or dietary-accommodation information is documented for this shop. Diners with celiac disease or strict dietary needs should confirm directly with the owner before visiting.

places.name: 龍園 (Ryuen (Gyoza no Mise Ryuen))
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour gyoza (the shop is famous for its house-made/hand-stretched wheat skins), plus ramen, fried rice and Tianjin dishes; soy sauce and vinegar-soy dipping are standard. Heavy wheat and soy exposure; no gluten-free accommodation documented.
places.vegan_detail: Gyoza filling is described as light and vegetable-centered, but no confirmation it is meat-free, and Chinese dishes/ramen typically use pork/meat broth. No vegan menu documented; a vegan would need to ask directly. Treat as not confirmed vegan.
places.hours_raw: Closed Mon / Tue–Sun 11:00–19:00
places.neighborhood: 中京区 壬生 (Mibu, Nakagyo-ku); near JR Nijo Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location shop founded in 1962 (Showa 37), run by the same family for over 60 years; multiple sources describe the "mothers/grandmothers" hand-wrapping gyoza on site using a vintage noodle/skin machine. No chain or brand affiliation found.

chef_bio.background: Ryuen has been grilling gyoza in the quiet, residential Mibu neighborhood west of Nijo Station since 1962, making it a genuine neighborhood institution loved by locals for more than 60 years. It is a small, unpretentious family operation, with counter seating wrapped around the kitchen so you watch everything happen in front of you. The kitchen is run by a trio of women: an older mother who works the grill, her daughter who hand-makes the gyoza, and a third member helping with service. A portrait of an earlier generation hangs on the wall, a quiet reminder of how long this family has been at it. What sets the shop apart is its hand-stretched skins (te-uchi no kawa), prepared so the dough comes out chewy and mochi-like rather than thin and crisp. The filling leans heavily on vegetables (cabbage in particular), with a moderate amount of ground meat and glass noodles, giving the gyoza a lighter, sweeter, umami-forward profile instead of a heavy, greasy one. Everything is made to order. The shop is firmly old-school: cash only, no reservations, Showa-era retro decor, and a reputation captured in one local description as 'unpretentious but undeniably delicious.'
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-stretched gyoza with chewy, mochi-like wheat skins and a light, vegetable-forward filling; also Tianjin rice (tenshinhan), fried rice, and ramen

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1962, Ryuen has been cherished by locals for over 60 years as a long-standing neighborhood gyoza shop.

The kitchen is run by a family of women: an older mother works the grill while her daughter hand-makes the gyoza, with a third staff member helping; a photo of an earlier generation hangs on the wall.

The signature is the hand-stretched skin (te-uchi no kawa), with a chewy, mochi-like (mochi-mochi) texture and a cabbage- and vegetable-forward filling; locals call it 'unpretentious but undeniably delicious.'

The gyoza are lightly seasoned to highlight the natural sweetness of the vegetables, with glass noodles and moderate ground meat, and are served with house-made chili oil and a miso-based dipping sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny (10-seat) old-school neighborhood gyoza shop with a Japanese-only menu and staff, so a foreign student should be comfortable ordering by pointing or with a few words of Japanese and not expect English support.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe restaurant. The core product is hand-stretched wheat-flour gyoza, and the menu also includes ramen, fried rice, and Tianjin rice, all of which involve wheat and/or soy. No gluten-free accommodation is documented, and with hand-made wheat skins prepared daily in a small open kitchen, airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination effectively unavoidable.

The gyoza filling includes glass noodles and the skins are wheat; the kitchen is a small counter operation where the same surfaces and grill are used for all dishes, so flour cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gyoza here are served with standard dipping condiments including a soy/vinegar-style dip and a house miso-based sauce. Most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and miso may also contain barley or wheat, so the dipping sauces are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not suitable for vegans. The gyoza filling contains ground meat, the menu features egg dishes (Tianjin rice, egg soup) and meat-based Chinese dishes, and there are no documented vegan options. Even the vegetable-forward gyoza contain meat and are cooked on a shared grill.

places.name: 蕎麦 ろうじな (Soba Roujina)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core product is soba; even juwari (100% buckwheat) noodles are prepared in a kitchen handling wheat, and the dipping sauce (そばつゆ) is dashi-based, typically containing soy sauce (wheat + soy) and bonito dashi. No gluten-free or dedicated allergen-safe preparation is documented. Cross-contamination from wheat is likely.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. Soba is typically served with a katsuo/kombu dashi-based tsuyu (bonito = non-vegan), and the menu features items such as duck (鴨) and izakaya-style dishes. A vegan would need to ask directly; plain zaru soba might be possible only if the dashi-based sauce is omitted, which is not confirmed.
places.neighborhood: 中京区 (Nakagyo-ku, near Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae / Mar
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location specialist soba shop with no indication of being part of a chain or brand. Small 14-seat owner-run establishment; treated as independent_solo, though family involvement could not be confirmed.

chef_bio.chef_name: Oshige (大重) [given name reported as Shigeru; surname and Nakajin training confirmed]
chef_bio.background: Soba Roujina is a small handmade soba house tucked into the Gosho-minami / Maruyacho neighborhood south of the Kyoto Imperial Palace, opened on June 27, 2014 by chef Oshige, who trained at the well-regarded soba kitchen Nakajin before striking out on his own. The room is warm and intimate, all wood and quiet calm, the kind of place built around one craftsman's hands rather than a big operation. Oshige sources the season's best buckwheat from whichever region is at its peak, and grinds it fresh on his own stone mill each day, milling only as much as he expects to serve.
chef_bio.philosophy: In the evening the restaurant leans into a sake-and-soba philosophy: a rotating lineup of pure-rice (junmai) sakes meant to be enjoyed slowly alongside small dishes before the noodles arrive, treating soba less as fast food and more as the close of a relaxed meal.
chef_bio.specialty: The house is known for its juwari soba (100% buckwheat noodles made with no wheat binder), and especially its coarse-ground (arabiki) soba, which carries a deeper, more rustic buckwheat aroma. Diners can take the noodles either with a bonito-rich dipping tsuyu built on katsuobushi from Makurazaki in Kagoshima, or simply with salt to taste the buckwheat itself. Beyond the noodles, the kitchen turns out roast duck, conger-eel aspic (anago no nikogori), warm sobagaki, and seasonal tempura.

anecdotes.text: Chef Oshige opened Roujina in June 2014 after training at the soba restaurant Nakajin, and grinds the day's buckwheat fresh on his own stone mill, milling only the amount he needs that day.
The noodles are 100% buckwheat with no binding agent, made from the season's best-sourced buckwheat and served either with a Makurazaki bonito tsuyu or simply with salt.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and mec-h confirm the 2014-06-27 opening, own-stone-mill juwari/arabiki soba, Makurazaki bonito tsuyu and junmai-sake program; Nakajin's own shop diary lists Roujina among alumni, corroborating that the owner (surname Oshige, 大重) trained at Nakajin. Bib Gourmand / Tabelog Soba 100 (2025) listed.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, quiet 14-seat owner-run soba shop where staff likely speak limited English; a foreign student should know a few Japanese phrases and be comfortable ordering by pointing at the menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a soba specialist whose entire focus is buckwheat noodles. Even though the soba is juwari (100% buckwheat, no wheat binder), the kitchen is small and also fries tempura, which is wheat-battered, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make wheat cross-contact likely. No gluten-free or dedicated allergen-safe preparation is documented. Not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a dashi-based sauce built on bonito (katsuobushi); like most Japanese soba tsuyu it would typically contain soy sauce, which is normally brewed with wheat. There is no documented wheat-free tsuyu. Note that the noodles can also be ordered simply with salt rather than tsuyu, which avoids the soy-sauce element (but not the kitchen-wide wheat cross-contact).

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tsuyu and broths are built on bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, so the default dipping sauce is not vegan. Vegetable dishes and tempura are on the menu, but no dedicated vegan preparation or fish-free dashi is documented, so vegans should confirm directly with the restaurant which items can be made without dashi, fish, or other animal products.

positives.text: Because the noodles are juwari (100% buckwheat with no wheat binder), the noodle itself contains no added wheat flour, and the option to eat the soba with salt instead of the soy-based tsuyu exists. This does not make it celiac-safe given shared-kitchen wheat from tempura, but diners with milder sensitivities may find it useful to discuss with the chef.

places.name: やまが (Yamaga (Echizen Soba Yamaga))
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is soba; many shops cut buckwheat with wheat flour (二八), and tempura, ソースカツ丼, and soy-based つゆ/dashi all contain wheat and soy. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is advertised. Cross-contamination from shared broth and frying is highly likely.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering advertised. Soba broth (つゆ) is dashi-based, typically using 鰹節 (bonito); listed dishes feature bonito flakes, sauce pork cutlet, grilled mackerel sushi, and tempura. A vegan would need to ask directly, and even plain soba is likely served with fish-dashi tsuyu, so options are minimal. Default to "ask"; realistically limited.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat 11:00–15:00, 18:00–21:30 / Wed 11:00–15:00 / Sun and holidays 11:00–15:30, 17:30–21:30 (per Tabelog). Gurunavi also lists 'Wednesday evening' as a closed time.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Appears to be a single-location independent neighborhood soba shop; no chain affiliation or multiple branches found across Tabelog, Gnavi, Hot Pepper, or Retty listings. Family/owner-operated assumption; no incorporation/brand evidence found.

chef_bio.origin: Fukui Prefecture (Ono region), via Kyoto's Shugakuin / Ichijoji area
chef_bio.background: Yamaga is a small neighborhood soba shop near Shugakuin that brings a slice of Fukui Prefecture to northeast Kyoto. It is run by a proprietress from Fukui who serves the rustic Echizen-style soba of her home region, a style that is genuinely rare in Kyoto. The shop leans into its Fukui roots, drawing on ingredients and water tied to the famous spring water of the Ono area, and even makes fresh noodles that can be sent out by chilled courier for people to enjoy at home.
chef_bio.philosophy: To serve the everyday soul foods of Fukui (Echizen soba and sauce katsudon) honestly and approachably in a Kyoto neighborhood setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Echizen soba: dark, whole-grain, thick-cut buckwheat noodles with a notably firm, slightly chewy bite, classically served as oroshi soba topped with grated daikon, bonito flakes, and scallion, then mixed together before eating. The other house signature is sauce katsudon (a Fukui soul food of thin pork cutlet dressed in a tangy sauce over shredded cabbage and rice), often ordered as a set alongside warm or cold Echizen oroshi soba. The menu runs broad, with more than 70 items spanning soba, udon, and donburi.

anecdotes.text: Echizen soba is the soul food of Fukui Prefecture and a genuinely uncommon find in Kyoto; Yamaga's noodles are the rustic, dark, whole-grain Echizen style with a firm, slightly chewy bite rather than the lighter Edo-style noodles more typical of the city.
The signature oroshi soba pairs the thick noodles with grated daikon and bonito flakes, eaten by mixing the broth, toppings, and noodles together vigorously before the first bite.
Beyond soba, Yamaga is known for sauce katsudon, another Fukui specialty, frequently served as a set with warm or cold Echizen oroshi soba.

cultural_comfort.note: A small (24-seat) local soba shop with no evidence of English menus or staff, so a foreign student should arrive prepared with basic Japanese phrases or a translation app and point at the (very extensive) menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a soba shop whose core product is buckwheat noodles, and traditional soba is very commonly cut with wheat flour (nihachi, roughly 20% wheat). No gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation is advertised, so the soba itself should be treated as containing wheat unless the shop confirms otherwise.

The kitchen also turns out tempura and breaded sauce katsudon, both wheat-based and deep-fried; the same boiling water, broth pots, and frying setup are shared across dishes, so cross-contamination with wheat is highly likely. Not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping/pouring tsuyu and dashi are soy-sauce based, and most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat. The sauce on the sauce katsudon is likewise soy/wheat based. Assume the broths and sauces contain both wheat and soy.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The soba broth and dashi are bonito/fish based (oroshi soba is finished with katsuobushi), and the menu is built around pork katsudon and tempura, so vegan diners should confirm directly. Even a plain soba would normally sit in a fish-dashi tsuyu.

places.name: 六曜社 地下店 (Rokuyosha Chikaten (Rokuyosha Basement S)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature items are homemade donuts (wheat flour) and the shop is a coffee/café; no gluten-free menu is documented. Wheat-based pastries are central. Coffee itself is naturally GF but shared-kitchen wheat handling makes celiac-safe assurance impossible. Treat as not suitable for strict celiacs without direct confirmation.
