places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Black self-roasted hand-drip coffee is plant-based; however the famous donuts contain egg/dairy and milk is offered for coffee. No confirmed vegan food options — a vegan visitor should ask, and realistically only black coffee is reliably suitable. (Menu review: 0 vegan meals; 5 vegan items but no full main.)
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 12:00-23:00 / Wed 18:00-23:00 (cafe 12:00-17:00 / bar 18:00-23:00). Cafe last order 16:30, bar last order 22:30. Donuts served from 12:00, no takeout.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Kawaramachi Sanjo (Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded 1950 (Showa 25), family-run by the Okuno family. The basement shop is run by second-generation Osamu Okuno and the first-floor shop by third-generation Kumpei Okuno. Only the first-floor and basement shops in the same building at Daikoku-cho, Kawaramachi Sanjo Sagaru; no chain expansion. Qualifies as an independent, single-location family business.

chef_bio.background: Rokuyosha opened in 1950 at the Kawaramachi-Sanjo area, founded by Okuno Minoru after his family had run a coffee shop in Manchuria and returned home to Kyoto. The basement room came first; the ground floor was added in 1965 when bar service began. It has stayed a family business across three generations. The second-generation owner, Osamu Okuno, started working here around 1970 alongside family and still runs the basement room today. He is also known outside the shop as a singer-songwriter (Okuno Osamu). His son, the third-generation Kohei Okuno, joined the family business in 2013 after training for years at Maeda Coffee and running his own cafe in Higashiyama-Sanjo; he now mainly looks after the ground-floor kissaten while his father tends the basement.
chef_bio.philosophy: Coffee roasted in-house and brewed cup by cup, in a quiet, timeless basement room where attentive, unhurried service lets guests linger.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted, made-to-order coffee that is ground fresh and hand-dripped one cup at a time, with the basement leaning toward the deeper roasts. The other beloved signature is the homemade donut: a firm, densely packed, only-lightly-sweet ring made fresh each day and available from around noon, served gently warmed in the basement.

anecdotes.text: The shop's roots trace to a coffee shop the founding family ran in Manchuria before returning to Kyoto and opening Rokuyosha in 1950.

The now-famous donut came from the second-generation owner's mother, who began making them years after the basement opened, inspired by the donuts at Osaka's long-running Hiraoka Coffee and refined through trial and error.
Second-generation owner Osamu Okuno, who has worked here since around 1970 and roasts the coffee himself, is also a working singer-songwriter.
Third-generation Kohei Okuno trained about seven and a half years at Maeda Coffee and ran his own cafe before joining the family shop in 2013.

cultural_comfort.note: A historic, locals-loved retro kissaten where staff likely speak mostly Japanese and English menus are not guaranteed, so a foreign student should be ready to point at items or use a translation app, but the relaxed cafe atmosphere makes a short visit easy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a traditional kissaten whose signature item is a homemade wheat-flour donut made fresh on-site daily. Wheat baking and frying happen in a small shared space, so a strict celiac cannot be assured of a gluten-free experience here. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but cross-contamination from the donuts cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The donuts and other sweets (pound cake, roll cake) are wheat- and dairy/egg-based baked goods, so they are not vegan. No vegan-specific menu is documented; black coffee with no milk would be the safest choice, but confirm directly.

positives.text: Coffee is brewed plain, one cup at a time, and ordered black it contains no gluten ingredients.

places.name: 築地 (Tsukiji)
places.gf_detail: No mention of gluten-free accommodation. A classic coffee shop where light meals and Western pastries assume the use of wheat. The Vienna coffee itself is coffee plus whipped cream, but the shop offers no cross-contamination control or gluten-free guarantee, so it cannot be considered safe for celiac patients.
places.vegan_detail: No explicit vegan accommodation stated. The signature Vienna coffee uses dairy (whipped cream). Black coffee and similar drinks are plant-based and may be drinkable, but whether milk-free options are available needs to be confirmed.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Shijo Kawaramachi / Kawaramachi Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A single, independent shop founded in 1934 (Showa 9). A multi-generational family-run coffee shop where the founder's philosophy has been passed down to the second and third generations; not a chain or affiliate.

chef_bio.chef_name: 原田正志（3代目）／創業者：原田邦雄
chef_bio.origin: 京都・四条河原町／河原町駅
chef_bio.background: 1934年（昭和9年）創業の老舗喫茶。芝居の世界に憧れた初代・原田邦雄が、東京の前衛劇団「築地小劇場」にちなんで名づけたと伝わります。建築を学んだわけではない初代が自らの感性で職人に思いを伝えてつくり上げた空間で、漆喰の壁、色とりどりの不揃いなタイル、柱や幅木の彫刻、油彩画や木彫レリーフ、装飾皿や時計などの重厚な調度が並び、ヨーロッパの古城に迷い込んだような雰囲気をたたえています。当初は2階が住居でしたが、1951〜52年頃に店舗として改装されました。創業から三代にわたって受け継がれ、現在は3代目・原田正志さんが店を守っています。京都の喫茶文化が芽吹いた1930年代から続く、現存する市内最古級の喫茶店のひとつです。
chef_bio.philosophy: 粗悪品や紛い物の材料は使わない——「インチキのものだと混ぜても泡立たない。だからホイップして浮かべて出すことで、うちの材料は純正だと示す」という創業者の考え方を、三代にわたって守り続けています。
chef_bio.specialty: 京都で初めてウインナー珈琲を出した店として知られます。戦後まもなく、粗悪品や混ぜ物が出回るなか「うちの材料は純正です」と示すため、本物の生クリームをホイップして珈琲に浮かべたのが始まりと伝わります。深煎り豆をネルドリップで落とした珈琲に、きちんと泡立てた本物の生クリームを合わせるのが身上。ムースケーキやクリームソーダ、ウインナー珈琲とケーキのセットも親しまれています。

anecdotes.text: 店名は、芝居に憧れた初代・原田邦雄が東京の前衛劇団『築地小劇場』にちなんで名づけたと伝わります。
建築を学んだわけではない初代が、自らの感性を職人に伝えてつくり上げた内装。色とりどりの不揃いなタイルや、柱・幅木の彫刻が空間を彩ります。
ウインナー珈琲は戦後、『偽物だと混ぜても泡立たない。だからホイップして浮かべることで材料が純正だと示した』のが始まりと、現主人が語っています。
店内にはハイドンなどクラシックの楽曲が流れ、ヨーロッパの古城のような雰囲気をつくっています。

cultural_comfort.note: 英語メニューや英語対応の記載はなく現金のみ・喫煙可の昭和レトロな純喫茶のため、外国人学生は基本的な日本語フレーズやガイド情報を用意して訪れると安心です。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリー対応や専用調理に関する記載は確認できませんでした。クラシックな喫茶店であり、ムースケーキなど提供される洋菓子・軽食は小麦の使用が前提です。セリアック（小麦アレルギー）の方には安全とは言えません。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 看板のウインナー珈琲は珈琲＋ホイップ生クリームで、乳製品を含みます。植物性ミルク等の代替や、ヴィーガン対応の記載は確認できませんでした。対応可否は来店前に直接確認してください。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応や除去食、コンタミ管理に関する具体的な情報は見つかりませんでした。詳細はスタッフに直接ご確認ください。

places.name: COFFEE Cattleya （コーヒーカトレヤ）【旧店名】祇園喫茶 カトレヤ (COFFEE Cattleya (Coffee Katoreya / Gion )
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. A retro kissaten centered on coffee and sandwiches; sandwiches and toast use wheat bread, and typical menu items (sandwiches, cream soda, sweets) are not gluten-free. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Cross-contamination with wheat is highly likely. A celiac should avoid food items here; black coffee itself is plausibly fine but unverified.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling is documented. Black coffee (brewed with sacred-water-vein water per sources) and some drinks may be vegan, but signature items like sandwiches and cream soda contain egg/dairy. A vegan would need to ask in person; nothing confirmed.
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama-ku, Gion-Shijō Station / Gion (near Yasaka Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Independent single-location kissaten founded 1944 near Yasaka Shrine. Operated across generations (interview references a 3rd-generation owner). The original shop closed in May 2024 and reopened July 3, 2024 under continued/new management, deliberately preserving the historic nam

chef_bio.background: Cattleya is one of Kyoto's pioneering kissaten, tracing its roots to the early 1940s and a spot just steps from Yasaka Shrine in Gion. For roughly eight decades it was a quiet grassroots fixture of the city's old coffee-house culture, in the same lineage as long-running Kyoto institutions. The original kissaten closed in May 2024, but rather than let the name disappear, it was handed on: a new proprietor reopened the shop on July 3, 2024, keeping the historic name and the original interior intact. The space still carries its early-Showa character, with high counters and high chairs, vintage lamps, fresh flowers throughout the room, and artwork on the walls, so the feel of the old Gion coffee house lives on even as the coffee program has been modernized.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honor the old room and the tradition behind it while embracing modern coffee. The brewing is treated as craft over volume: as the shop has put it, simply adding more beans won't make a better cup, what matters is technique, water temperature and steaming time, with each cup given individual attention.
chef_bio.specialty: Coffee is the heart of the place. In its long-running form the shop was known for carefully hand-extracted coffee brewed cup by cup using soft groundwater drawn from its own well, fed by the same underground vein as Yasaka Shrine, along with a deep, bitter signature pour and the bright cream soda and light kissaten fare you'd expect of a classic Kyoto coffee house. Under its current owners the focus is contemporary specialty coffee, espresso drinks such as Americanos and cafe lattes, plus toast and cake, alongside beer and wine.

anecdotes.text: The name Cattleya comes from the orchid known as the 'queen of orchids,' native to Central and South America. It's a fitting name for a coffee house: the cattleya is the national flower of coffee-growing countries including Costa Rica, Colombia and Brazil.

The shop's coffee was famously brewed with soft 'sacred' water from its own well, drawn from the same water vein as nearby Yasaka Shrine. The owner explained they don't carry water from the shrine itself but pump and filter their own groundwater for the kitchen, a naturally soft water said to bring out the coffee's full character.

When the original kissaten closed in May 2024 after nearly 80 years, the name and interior were preserved rather than lost: a new proprietor reopened it on July 3, 2024, blending today's specialty-coffee culture with the retro early-Showa space.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Gion kissaten where basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu will carry you comfortably, though staff may have limited English and the atmosphere is quiet and old-fashioned.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a coffee house centered on toast, cake and light cafe fare, all of which involve wheat. Wheat is heavily present in the kitchen and cross-contact is highly likely, so the food menu is not celiac-safe.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan options or dairy-free preparation are documented. Menu items center on coffee, espresso drinks, toast and cake; lattes and baked goods use dairy, and vegan suitability would need to be confirmed directly with the shop.

positives.text: Black brewed coffee and espresso (Americano) without milk are plausibly free of gluten and animal products, though this is not confirmed by any source and should be verified with staff.

places.name: ジャンボ (Jambo)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Okonomiyaki and yakisoba are wheat-flour based (batter and wheat noodles), and are typically seasoned with soy-sauce-based sauce and often dashi — all wheat/soy/dashi-heavy. No gluten-free preparation is indicated on the page.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan information on the page. Okonomiyaki and yakisoba at such shops standardly contain egg, pork/meat, and bonito-based dashi/toppings, plus okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan options indicated; treat as not vegan-friendly.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, Tōjiin Minami-machi (Tōjiin / Ritsumeikan University Kinugasa Campus-mae Stn / Kitano-Hakubaichō)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent neighborhood okonomiyaki/yakisoba shop near Ritsumeikan University's Kinugasa campus, known for large portions (大盛り). No indication of being part of a chain or brand on the Tabelog page.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Kita-ku, Tojiin Minami-cho (near Ritsumeikan University's Kinugasa campus)
chef_bio.background: Jambo (ジャンボ) has been a neighborhood okonomiyaki and yakisoba institution in Kyoto's Tojiin Minami-cho since 1974, sitting right in the student belt near Ritsumeikan University's Kinugasa campus and local schools. For decades it was the kind of place every student in the area knew by name - an affordable, fill-you-up shop run by a family that wanted young people and neighbors to be able to eat their fill. It built a fiercely loyal local following over more than 40 years, with regulars who have come back dozens of times, and its reputation eventually reached a national audience. In July 2018 the shop stopped offering dine-in service and moved to takeout only (you can order it pre-cooked or as raw batter to grill at home), but it has kept its signature flavor and its over-the-top portions intact.
chef_bio.philosophy: Generosity above all - serving portions big enough to truly fill up students and neighbors at a price they can afford, which is the spirit the shop has carried since it opened.
chef_bio.specialty: Jambo lives up to its name: the okonomiyaki and yakisoba are famously enormous, routinely big enough for two or three people to share for under 1,000 yen. The mixed okonomiyaki is the standout. The batter is built on dashi and grated mountain yam (yamaimo), and the okonomiyaki is loaded with cabbage, tenkasu (tempura scraps), beni-shoga, pork belly, seasoned ground beef, generous egg, and notably oversized squid. It was named to a national okonomiyaki “Top 100” list in 2018 and 2019.

anecdotes.text: Jambo earned a spot on Japan's nationwide Top 100 okonomiyaki list in both 2018 and 2019.
The portions are so large that two people have been known to struggle to finish a single okonomiyaki plus one yakisoba between them.

A long-running student-district favorite founded in 1974, it became the rare shop 'not one student didn't know about,' with the okonomiyaki batter made from dashi and grated mountain yam and piled with cabbage, tempura scraps, pork, beef, egg and oversized squid.

cultural_comfort.note: Estimate for a foreign student: this is a casual, cash-friendly local shop with menus and reviews in Japanese and currently takeout-only, so bring a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend and be ready to order in basic Japanese.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not celiac-safe. Okonomiyaki here is a wheat-flour batter and yakisoba uses wheat noodles, both cooked on a shared teppan (flat grill) - the core menu is wheat-based with no gluten-free option indicated.
The okonomiyaki batter is described as being made with dashi and grated mountain yam in addition to wheat flour, so even the batter carries dashi (commonly fish-based) on top of the gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki and yakisoba are finished with okonomiyaki/soba sauce, which is typically soy-sauce based; most Japanese soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce contain wheat, adding another gluten source beyond the batter and noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki batter contains dashi and egg, and the dishes are built with pork belly, seasoned ground beef and squid; the shared teppan also cooks meat and seafood.

places.name: からす (Karasu)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Okonomiyaki and yakisoba are wheat-flour-based; the batter and dishes use bonito-kombu dashi and chicken dashi, and sauces blend Kyoto/Osaka/Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauces (soy- and wheat-containing). Spicy items use doubanjiang/yanyanjiang (soy/wheat). Heavy wheat, soy, and dashi presence throughout. No gluten-free accommodation documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. Menu is centered on pork okonomiyaki, beef tendon, and other meat/seafood teppanyaki items, with dashi (bonito-kombu, chicken) used as a base. A vegan would need to ask directly, and given the dashi-based batter, suitable options are unlikely without special accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri–Sun 17:00–22:30
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single independent location owner-operated by 川村万馬 (Kawamura Banma), who previously ran a bar with his wife before opening this okonomiyaki/teppanyaki shop. Tabelog Magazine and other sources describe it as a single hidden-gem local shop, not a chain or branded franchise.

chef_bio.background: Karasu is a teppanyaki and okonomiyaki shop tucked into the Shogoin neighborhood of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, a short walk from Jingu-Marutamachi and the Kumano-jinja-mae bus stop. It is named after the Yatagarasu, the three-legged crow revered as the divine guide of nearby Kumano Shrine. The owner, Kawamura Kazuma, ran a bar together with his wife before opening Karasu in 2004. He had only a little food-service experience, so he taught himself okonomiyaki from scratch, refining his recipes year after year while running the shop. More than two decades on, it has become a warm, homey neighborhood institution loved by locals and by visitors who travel from farther afield. Kawamura and his wife (the okami) run the room with a personable, welcoming style, and the shop is known for staff who tend to stay rather than drift away.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kawamura's guiding principle is uncompromising sourcing: he selects premium ingredients and travels in person to suppliers to buy them, as he puts it, going to the source himself rather than ordering sight unseen.
chef_bio.specialty: The house okonomiyaki is the butatama, a light, refined pork-belly version made with Kyoto pork, grated mountain yam, and a batter built on bonito-and-kombu dashi plus chicken stock, finished with a sauce blending Kyoto, Osaka, and Hiroshima styles. Other signatures include a spicy beef-tendon suji-yaki using Matsusaka beef seasoned with a spicy doubanjiang-based blend, a yakisoba cooked without tenkasu and bound with pork fat until the ingredients meld, and a fried rice made with shiba-zuke pickles from the Murakami Shigehonten shop. The kitchen also leans on a wide range of vegetable-forward small plates.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named after the Yatagarasu, the three-legged crow that serves as the divine messenger and guide of the nearby Kumano Shrine.
Owner Kawamura Kazuma ran a bar with his wife before opening Karasu in 2004, and taught himself okonomiyaki, improving his recipes while running the shop.
Kawamura insists on carefully selected ingredients, saying he always travels to the source in person to buy them.
The homey atmosphere is shaped by the personalities of Kawamura and the okami, with staff who, it is said, have never quit partway through.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small Japanese-language local teppanyaki/okonomiyaki shop with no documented English support, so a foreign student should expect to order in Japanese (or with translation help) and be ready for a casual, intimate counter-and-table setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is an okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop, and it is not celiac-safe. The okonomiyaki and yakisoba are built on wheat flour, the batter is bound with bonito-kombu dashi and chicken stock, and dishes are finished with a blend of Kyoto, Osaka, and Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauces that contain soy and wheat. There is heavy wheat, soy, and dashi presence throughout, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented.
Everything is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat-based okonomiyaki and yakisoba, so cross-contact with gluten is essentially unavoidable even on non-batter items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The finishing sauces blend Kyoto, Osaka, and Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauces, which are soy- and wheat-containing; most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.
Spicy items such as the beef-tendon suji-yaki use a doubanjiang and yanyanjiang (chili-bean paste) blend, which commonly contains soy and wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is known for a range of vegetable-forward small plates, but the kitchen relies heavily on bonito-kombu dashi, chicken stock, pork fat, and eggs, so vegetable dishes are not inherently vegan and should be confirmed dish by dish with the owner.
All cooking is done on a shared teppan also used for pork, beef, and shrimp, creating animal-product cross-contact for any plant-based order.

positives.text: The owner Kawamura and his wife run the shop personally and are warm and talkative, so diners with dietary questions can speak directly with the people who source and cook the food.
Ingredients are carefully selected and bought in person from suppliers, so staff can speak knowledgeably about what goes into each dish.

places.name: よりみち (Yorimichi (Okonomiyaki Yorimichi))
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Okonomiyaki/beta-yaki and manbo-yaki use a wheat-flour batter; the standard sauce is soy/Worcester-based and the batter/broth is dashi-heavy. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented and cross-contamination on a shared teppan is likely.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Core dishes contain egg in the batter, pork/meat and bonito-based dashi/sauce are standard for beta-yaki and hoso-yaki. No vegan menu is documented; a foreign vegan diner could not be reliably accommodated. (Menu review: 0 vegan meals; 2 vegan items but no full main.)
places.neighborhood: Shimogyō-ku, Sūjin area (1F of municipal housing / around Tōfukuji Stn and JR Kyoto Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Multiple sources (Tabelog area listing and a 2022 relocation feature on mach-no-osusume.com) describe the shop as run by a gentle husband-and-wife team (夫婦経営). Single location, no chain affiliation found.

chef_bio.origin: Sujin area, Shimogyo Ward, Kyoto (near Kyoto Station / Tofukuji Station)
chef_bio.background: Yorimichi is a small family okonomiyaki shop tucked into the Sujin neighborhood of Kyoto's Shimogyo Ward, a short walk from Kyoto Station and near Tofukuji Station. It is now run by a third-generation owner who took over after his grandmother and mother, carrying on the family's recipes and the down-to-earth, Showa-era teppan atmosphere they built. The shop settled into its current spot in the Sujin municipal housing block on July 1, 2022, in a part of town long known as a cluster of neighborhood okonomiyaki counters.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep the family's flavors intact across the generations and serve them in an unpretentious, neighborhood-counter setting.
chef_bio.specialty: The house is known for a thin-batter, layered beta-yaki style rather than the mixed Kansai style: a thin crepe of batter piled with a generous mound of cabbage and steam-cooked on the teppan, often built up with stir-fried noodles (soba-iri), beef tendon (suji), egg, and pork. Two signatures stand out: manbo-yaki (まんぼ焼き), the shop's signature layered okonomiyaki, and the salt-seasoned hoso-yaki (beef offal), praised for the sweetness of the fat and its umami. Sauce is sweet or spicy, brushed on by the diner at the griddle counter and finished with aonori and bonito flakes.

anecdotes.text: The current proprietor told a visiting writer that he is the third generation, working hard to carry on after his grandmother and then his mother ran the shop.

The shop's beta-yaki is made by sandwiching a large quantity of cabbage between thin layers of batter and steam-cooking it on the teppan, a style distinct from typical okonomiyaki; the fatty-offal (hoso) topped version was singled out as tender and richly flavored.
After relocating on July 1, 2022, the shop is still known for its namesake manbo-yaki and for hoso (beef tripe) served simply with salt, sauce or soy.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, Showa-era family okonomiyaki shop where you cook/season at the teppan and almost everything (menu, reviews, staff) is in Japanese, so a foreign student should bring a Japanese-speaking friend or a translation app and a few basic phrases.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-batter okonomiyaki shop cooked on a shared teppan (griddle), where noodles, batter, and multiple dishes are prepared on the same surface. It is not celiac-safe, and cross-contamination on the shared griddle is very likely. No gluten-free preparation is documented.
The signature dishes (manbo-yaki, beta-yaki, hoso-yaki) are built on a wheat-flour batter and frequently include wheat noodles (soba-iri).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The okonomiyaki sauce is described as sweet (or spicy) and is brushed on by the diner at the counter; this style of sauce is soy/Worcester-based and Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. Treat the sauce as containing gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan-friendly. Dishes are dashi-heavy (bonito-based) and toppings center on pork, beef tendon, egg, and offal; finished with bonito flakes. No vegan preparation is documented and the shared teppan handles animal products throughout.

places.name: 京都 下町のお好み焼き 昌・べた焼きの専門店 (Kyoto Shitamachi no Okonomiyaki Masa (Be)
places.gf_detail: NOT celiac-safe. Betayaki/okonomiyaki centers on a wheat-flour batter, the sauce contains wheat and soy (from soy sauce), and dashi is also used. Because wheat, gluten, soy, and dashi are used heavily, celiac accommodation is not possible. No dedicated gluten-free information available.
places.vegan_detail: No explicit vegan accommodation information. Okonomiyaki/betayaki normally contains egg, pork, seafood, and bonito dashi, and there are also grilled-meat menu items. No plant-only options could be confirmed; whether accommodation is possible must be inquired about. Presumed non-vegan by default.
places.hours_raw: No hours listed on Tabelog itself. Kiyamachi Kyoeikai site: 20:00-27:00 (i.e. until 3:00 the next day). Ekiten/Gurunavi-type listings: 19:00-4:00 (reservations only for private buyout). Evening only, late-night hours.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Kamiya-cho (Nishi-Kiyamachi-dori Shijo Agaru, Kiyamachi / Pontocho area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A single-location, hideaway-style betayaki specialist. No sign of chain or brand expansion. Judged to be a small, individually run shop based on operating traits such as cash-only and reservations only for private buyout. As the strict distinction between family and individual ownership could not be confirmed from sources, independent_family is tentatively assigned.

chef_bio.origin: 京都・木屋町（西木屋町通四条上る、紙屋町）
chef_bio.background: 四条から西木屋町を上がった、夜の店が並ぶ雑居ビルの一角にある、知る人ぞ知る隠れ家的な一軒。提供するのは大阪風でも広島風でもない『京都下町のべた焼き』ひと筋。キャベツは控えめ、生地はしっかりめに焼き上げ、鉄板の上で香ばしく仕上げるのが身上です。店主がカウンターに立ち、目の前の鉄板で一枚ずつ焼いてくれる、小さなお好み焼き屋ならではの距離感が魅力。木屋町・先斗町エリアで、深夜まで暖簾を上げ続けています。
chef_bio.philosophy: べた焼きは熱々こそが命。だからこの店では基本的に箸を出さず、テコ（コテ）やヘラで食べてもらうのが流儀です。金属のコテなら生地の温度が下がりにくく、最後まで熱々のおいしい状態で味わえるという、店主のこだわりが理由。初めての人もこの作法でべた焼きを味わうのがお約束です。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板はずばり『べた焼き』。たっぷりの刻み九条葱をのせるのがこの店の流儀で、油カス・玉子・キムチなどのトッピングを合わせて楽しめます。焼き上がりはカリッと香ばしく、軽やかな食べ口。前菜には、てっちゃん（ホルモン）の野菜焼き、山芋の塩焼き、牛肉の薄焼きなど、鉄板ならではの一品も。

anecdotes.text: この店では基本的にお箸が出てきません。べた焼きはテコ（コテ）やヘラですくって食べるのが流儀。金属のコテで食べると生地の温度が下がりにくく、最後まで熱々のおいしい状態で楽しめるから、というのが理由です。
大阪風でも広島風でもない京都下町のべた焼き。キャベツ控えめで生地はしっかりめ、鉄板でカリッと香ばしく焼き上げ、たっぷりの刻み葱をのせるのがこの店ならではのスタイルです。

cultural_comfort.note: 深夜営業・現金のみ・カウンター数席の隠れ家的なローカル店で英語対応や外国語メニューの情報がないため、外国人留学生は日本語が話せる人と一緒に、または案内役と訪れるのが安心です。

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a betayaki (Kyoto-style okonomiyaki) specialist. The core dish is built on a wheat-flour batter cooked on a shared griddle (teppan), so it is not celiac-safe. Gluten is central to virtually every dish here.
Everything is cooked together on a single shared teppan (griddle), so cross-contact with wheat batter is unavoidable. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki/betayaki sauce is wheat- and soy-based, and most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat. There is no indication of a wheat-free or soy-free sauce option here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Betayaki batter and toppings commonly include egg, and the menu features meat and pork-offal items (oil cas/aburakasu, beef, horumon). The single shared griddle cooks animal products, so vegan diners face heavy cross-contact. No vegan options are documented; confirm directly.

places.name: べた焼き節ちゃん (Betayaki Setsu-chan)
places.gf_detail: Okonomiyaki/betayaki is built on a wheat-flour batter, the sauce typically contains wheat and soy, and the dashi usually contains seafood-based ingredients (bonito flakes, etc.). No gluten-free menu is listed, and celiac accommodation should be considered not possible.
places.vegan_detail: A teppan okonomiyaki shop where the batter standardly uses egg, ingredients include pork and squid, and toppings include bonito flakes and egg. No vegan menu is specified. Whether accommodation avoiding egg, dairy, and animal dashi is possible must be confirmed directly at the time of visit.
places.hours_raw: Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 17:30-22:00
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A single-location, individually run small teppan okonomiyaki shop. According to a Tabelog review (unverified), it was originally a karaoke bar that the daughter took over as a betayaki shop after the mother's passing, so it is judged to be family-run. No sign of chain or brand expansion.

chef_bio.origin: 京都市伏見区中島中道町
chef_bio.background: べた焼き節ちゃんは、店主が学生だった頃に母・節子さんが同じ「節ちゃん」という屋号で営んでいたべた焼き店を受け継いだお店です。その後この場所は一時カラオケ店になっていましたが、お母様が亡くなられた後、娘である現店主がべた焼きの鉄板を再び始め、店内を改装してカウンターとテーブル席のお店として2024年に開店しました。母への敬意と感謝の気持ちを込めて「節ちゃん」の店名を引き継いだ、母娘二代にわたる京都・伏見の下町お好み焼き店です。
chef_bio.philosophy: 母が営んでいた「節ちゃん」の名を、敬意と感謝を込めて受け継ぐこと。
chef_bio.specialty: 店名の由来でもある「べた焼き」が看板。豚肉・脂かす・生卵・刻みネギをのせた京都ならではの薄焼きスタイルで、ほかにイカ焼き、じゃがチーズ、梅肉と柚子胡椒を添えたせせり焼き、豆腐ステーキなど鉄板焼きの一品も評判です。

anecdotes.text: 店主自身の言葉によれば「私が学生の頃、べた焼き節ちゃんと言う名で母がやってました。母が節子なので。その母へのリスペクトと感謝の思いで節ちゃんと言う店名を引き継ぎました」。店主はこのお店を2024年7月27日に開店したとしている。
以前はカラオケ屋だった場所で、お母様が亡くなられた後に娘さんがべた焼きの鉄板を始め、店内を改装してカウンターとテーブル席の店として再出発した。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログの口コミが、以前カラオケ店だった場所を母の死後に娘が改装しべた焼き店として開業したと記載。母『節子』への敬意と2024年開店は店主自身のInstagram投稿による。住所・卓上鉄板・メニューは食べログ店舗ページで確認。

cultural_comfort.note: 地元客中心の小さな鉄板お好み焼きの個人店で英語メニューや英語対応は期待しにくいため、留学生はガイドや日本語の話せる友人と一緒に行き、指差し注文や簡単な日本語を準備しておくと安心。

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a betayaki/okonomiyaki shop where the batter is wheat-flour based. Betayaki and okonomiyaki are cooked on a shared teppan (iron griddle) alongside other wheat-containing dishes, so gluten cross-contact on the griddle is effectively unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or preparation is documented. This restaurant should be treated as not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki/betayaki sauce typically contains wheat and soy, and most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat. No wheat-free sauce or soy is indicated, so assume the standard sauce and seasonings contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature betayaki includes pork, fat scraps (脂かす) and raw egg, and the kitchen also grills meats, chicken (せせり) and seafood (イカ焼き) on the shared griddle. Dashi/bonito (鰹節) is typical in this style of cooking. There is no documented vegan menu, and cross-contact with animal products on the shared teppan should be expected.

places.name: 嘗〜miso〜 (Sho ~miso~)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The cuisine is centered on miso (made from soybeans, and many miso varieties also contain wheat/barley koji), and the oden broth is dashi-heavy (described variously as chicken-bone + kombu dashi, or kombu + bonito dashi). The signature dish is generously topped with white miso, and the menu includes 生麩 (fresh wheat gluten) dengaku. Heavy soy, dashi, and wheat-gluten exposure means it is unsuitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Several plant-based items exist (daikon, atsuage/fried tofu, mushrooms, lotus root, rolled Chinese cabbage), but the oden broth uses animal-derived dashi (chicken bone and/or bonito), so dishes are not strictly vegan as served. A vegan would need to ask whether any items can be prepared without the meat/fish dashi.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–14:00 (food last order 13:30), 17:00–23:00 (food last order 22:00, drinks 22:30)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Multiple independent sources describe it as a single standalone miso-specialty restaurant opened in 2016, run by a proprietress who is passionate about miso; no chain branches found. It sources white miso from the established supplier 山利商店 (Yamari Shoten) but is not a chain outle

chef_bio.background: Sho ~miso~ opened in 2016 as a restaurant built around a single idea: that Kyoto has a deep white-miso culture worth celebrating, and that miso can be both delicious and nourishing. The kitchen sources its miso from Yamari Shoten, a long-established miso maker in Kyoto's Miyagawacho district whose products also reach ryotei and high-end restaurants around the city, and the whole menu is designed to let diners experience that artisan miso through a run of inventive dishes. The room itself is intimate, with table seating and a U-shaped counter, giving it the feel of a neighborhood specialist rather than a big operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: Miso as everyday medicine and pleasure at once: the restaurant frames its food around the idea that good miso is both healthy and delicious, and aims to share Kyoto's white-miso tradition in an approachable way, even for diners new to white-miso cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: The house is best known for its miso oden: a lineup of roughly 25 varieties generously finished with Yamari's sweet, mellow white miso. What sets it apart is that the white miso is brushed over the simmered items like a sauce or condiment rather than cooked into the broth. The standout is the 'Tokudai Waraji Momo,' a palm-sized, fall-apart-tender braised chicken thigh so soft you can cut it with chopsticks. Beyond the oden, the kitchen threads miso through the rest of the menu, from a cappuccino-style frothed miso soup with clams and grated yam to a croquette-shaped potato salad (Koropo) finished with charred breadcrumbs and white-miso sauce, and fresh-wheat-gluten (nama-fu) dengaku in flavors such as mochi-fu and mugwort. Winter brings seasonal items like cabbage rolls.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's miso comes from Yamari Shoten, a long-established miso shop in Kyoto's Miyagawacho whose miso is also used by upscale restaurants and ryotei; the white miso is described as gently sweet and rich, and is brushed over the oden like a sauce rather than mixed into the simmering broth.

Among about 25 kinds of miso oden, the signature is the 'Tokudai Waraji Momo,' a palm-sized chicken thigh said to be so tender you can cut it with chopsticks; winter brings seasonal items like cabbage rolls.

Opened in 2016, the restaurant was conceived to share Kyoto's white-miso culture as food that is both tasty and healthy, with miso threaded through everything from a cappuccino-style frothed miso soup to fresh wheat-gluten dengaku.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・kyototwoが、2016年開業、山利商店(宮川町)の白味噌をソース的に使う点、約25種のおでんと特大わらじもも、カプチーノ仕立ての味噌汁、生麸田楽、ミシュランビブグルマンを報じている。

cultural_comfort.note: A small (18-seat) traditional Kyoto miso-oden izakaya with little English presence, so a foreign student should be comfortable with basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu, though the warm proprietress and Michelin Bib Gourmand recognition make it welcoming.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a miso-specialty restaurant, and miso is central to nearly every dish. Many Japanese miso varieties are made with wheat or barley koji, so the kitchen is heavily reliant on an ingredient that is frequently not gluten-free. Not celiac-safe.

The menu includes fried items such as miso-marinated fried chicken and shrimp-and-cheese rolls, plus a deep fryer in regular use; with no documented dedicated gluten-free fryer, fried foods carry cross-contamination risk.

The menu features 生麩 (nama-fu / fresh wheat gluten) dengaku, which is essentially pure wheat gluten and must be strictly avoided by celiac diners; its presence in the kitchen also raises broader cross-contact concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The food is built around miso (a soybean ferment) and the menu uses plum-miso, house 'name-miso,' and miso marinades throughout; combined with typical Japanese seasonings, gluten and soy exposure is heavy. Diners with soy allergy or wheat/gluten restriction should treat the menu as high-risk.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The oden dashi is animal-based. Sources describe it as a kombu-and-bonito (fish) combination or a chicken-bone-and-kombu stock, so the broth is not vegan even before the miso topping.

The signature oden and many dishes feature chicken (the giant chicken thigh, miso fried chicken), pork (white-miso braised pork belly), and seafood (clams in the cappuccino miso soup, shrimp rolls), so most plates are not vegan and vegetable items risk cross-contact with the shared animal-based dashi and pots.

positives.text: There are a few naturally plant-leaning dishes on the menu, such as seasonal vegetables with house-made name-miso, that a vegan or vegetarian diner could ask about; given the miso focus, confirm with staff which items avoid the animal-based dashi.

places.name: だるまときんぎょ (Daruma to Kingyo)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is an oden specialist: oden simmers ingredients in a dashi broth typically built on soy sauce (wheat-containing) and bonito/kombu dashi, and many oden items (chikuwa, hanpen, satsuma-age, fried items) contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or labeling is documented. A celiac should not assume safety here.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan/vegetarian menu is documented. Oden broth is overwhelmingly dashi-based (bonito/fish stock) and seasoned with soy, so even vegetable oden items are typically cooked in animal-derived broth. Some vegetable dishes exist (e.g., the signature potato salad uses egg and oden broth), but nothing is confirmed plant-only. A strict vegan would need to ask directly and likely find few or no compliant options.
places.hours_raw: Daily, including holidays and the days before and after holidays, 18:00–00:00 (food last order 23:30, drinks 23:45)
places.neighborhood: 寺町通高辻 (Teramachi-dori Takatsuji), 下京区 Shimogyo-k
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent oden bar opened in 2010 by a proprietor who previously managed the Kyoto izakaya group "五十棲" before going independent. No evidence of multiple locations or chain affiliation. Classified independent_solo as it is an owner-run single establishment; could

chef_bio.background: Daruma to Kingyo is a snug oden izakaya on Teramachi-dori at Takatsuji in Shimogyo-ku, easy to spot by the large lantern painted with a daruma doll. It opened in 2010, founded by an owner who had cut their teeth helping run a well-known Kyoto izakaya before striking out to build their own place. What sets it apart is that oden is the year-round star here rather than just a winter dish, and the kitchen has grown into one of the names that diners point to when they talk about Kyoto's oden scene. The room is small and intimate, with counter and table seats for roughly twenty guests, and the mood leans warm and lively.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking centers on the dashi as a foundation, letting vegetables lend a natural sweetness for a soft, gentle seasoning rather than anything heavy-handed.
chef_bio.specialty: Oden simmered in a gentle dashi is the heart of the menu, running from the classics like daikon and egg to playful creative pieces such as tomato and avocado. The house signature is a potato salad made from the very same potatoes and eggs simmered in the oden broth, giving it an umami you won't find elsewhere. Another favorite is the nankan-age, a Kumamoto-style fried tofu that arrives fluffy, juicy, and soaked through with dashi. The kitchen also likes to weave its dashi into unexpected dishes, and many nights end with a finishing bowl of ramen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is marked out on Teramachi-dori by a large lantern illustrated with a daruma doll, a fitting nod to its name.
The much-loved potato salad is built from the same potatoes and eggs simmered in the oden pot, giving it a depth of flavor regulars say can't be found anywhere else.
Opened in 2010 by an owner who had previously helped run a popular Kyoto izakaya, the place has become known for serving oden all year round.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan・kyoto-optionが、2010年開業、有名京都居酒屋を支えた店主、通年提供のおでん、おでん出汁で作るポテトサラダ、南関揚げ、だるま提灯、約20席、締めのラーメンを報じる。

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small (17-seat), late-night local oden izakaya where reviews are in Japanese and no English menu or service is documented, so a foreign student should expect a Japanese-only experience and benefit from basic Japanese phrases or a translation app.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is an oden specialist, and oden is the core of the menu. Oden is simmered in a shared dashi broth, so ingredients sit and cross-contaminate in one communal pot. No gluten-free menu or allergen labeling is documented, and a celiac should not assume any item is safe here.
Wheat-containing oden items are central to the offering, including the nankan-age (fried tofu) and other fried pieces, all of which share the same dashi pot.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden dashi is typically built on soy sauce, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. The broth here is described as the foundation of nearly every dish, including the signature potato salad and dishes where the dashi is worked into unexpected preparations, so wheat-based seasoning is likely present throughout. Not celiac-safe.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is a traditional oden broth, which generally relies on bonito (katsuobushi) alongside kombu, making it not vegan even for vegetable items. Vegetable oden such as daikon, tomato, and avocado is simmered in this same animal-based broth, so vegans should confirm directly and treat these as not vegan by default.

positives.text: There is a genuine focus on fresh, seasonal vegetable oden (daikon, tomato, avocado and more), so plant-forward diners without a strict animal-product or gluten restriction have appealing options to discuss with staff.

places.name: 空蝉亭 (Karasemitei)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is wheat-flour-dredged and panko (wheat breadcrumb) coated pork cutlet; sauces and the izakaya/Western menu typically involve soy sauce and dashi. Heavy wheat and soy exposure with high cross-contamination risk in shared frying. No gluten-free accommodation documented.
places.vegan_detail: Core menu is pork tonkatsu. Editorial coverage notes a bagna cauda appetizer made with organic Kyoto vegetables and use of rapeseed oil rather than lard, but bagna cauda traditionally contains anchovy/dairy, so it is not reliably vegan. No vegan menu is advertised; a vegan diner would need to ask directly, and options would at best be very limited. (Menu review: 1 vegan meal.)
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:00), 17:00–20:00 (last order 19:00) / Sat, Sun 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:00), 17:00–21:00 (last order 20:00)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Described in editorial coverage as a single standalone machiya (traditional townhouse) restaurant run by its owner (店主); no evidence of multiple branches or chain/brand affiliation. Classified as independent; family-vs-solo distinction not explicitly documented.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Nishijin area (Horikawa-Shimotachiuri, near Nijo Castle)
chef_bio.background: Karasemitei sits in a quietly restored Kyoto machiya near Nijo Castle in the historic Nishijin weaving district, with a front earthen-floored entry and a raised tatami seating area looking onto a tended garden. It opened in 2018 and has since reinvented itself as a specialist in aged-pork tonkatsu, a focus the house describes as its 'second chapter.' Rather than the usual quick fry, the kitchen treats each cutlet almost like a slow-cooked European dish, prizing patience and precise heat over speed.
chef_bio.philosophy: Coax out the pork's natural sweetness, umami, and smooth mouthfeel through meticulous temperature control, and serve it light. Nearly everything is made in-house, from the tonkatsu sauce that balances vegetable savoriness with fruit sweetness, to grated-daikon ponzu made with Kyoto spring water, to a bagna cauda of organic Kyoto vegetables served in place of the usual shredded cabbage.
chef_bio.specialty: Aged-pork tonkatsu. The signature is the house 'hibernation aging' (冬眠熟成) method: the pork is cleaned in warm water, tightened in ice water, marinated in a house fermentation extract, then vacuum-sealed and aged at roughly minus 1 degree for 21 to 45 days, like an animal hibernating through winter, to concentrate its umami. Premium pork is featured, including Kagoshima Nanashu and Chiba Hayashi SPF, across cuts such as thick-cut loin, rib loin, fillet, and a rare Chateaubriand. Cutlets are fried low and slow at around 150 degrees in cholesterol-free rapeseed oil (not lard) with a fine breadcrumb coating, then finished with residual heat so the interior stays a delicate sakura-pink while fully cooked. The result is meant to taste light and clean rather than heavy.

anecdotes.text: Karasemitei ages its pork using a proprietary 'hibernation aging' technique, sealing the meat and holding it at about minus 1 degree for 21 to 45 days, the idea being that aging it in winter-like conditions, as a bear hibernates, deepens its umami before it is fried low and slow.

The owner's obsession is fire control, achieving a sakura-pink interior that is never raw, frying in cholesterol-free rapeseed oil rather than lard, and serving a house bagna cauda of organic Kyoto vegetables instead of the usual cabbage.

Set in a renovated Kyoto machiya near Nijo Castle, the restaurant offers aged loin, fillet, rib loin and a rare Chateaubriand cut, with the pork vacuum-aged at sub-zero temperatures and fried at a gentle 150 degrees to keep its flavor.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・食べログ・JR東海travelが、2018年開業『第二章』の熟成豚とんかつ、冬眠熟成(−1度前後で21〜45日)、鹿児島南州・千葉林SPF、リブロースやシャトーブリアン、150度の菜種油低温揚げ、バーニャカウダを記載。

cultural_comfort.note: A small 12-seat Japanese tonkatsu restaurant where staff and the 487 reviews are predominantly Japanese-speaking, so a foreign student should be comfortable with basic Japanese (konnichiwa-level may suffice for ordering) and should reserve ahead since it is popular.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a tonkatsu specialist: the pork cutlets are coated in wheat-flour dredge and panko (wheat breadcrumbs) and deep-fried, so the dish is inherently wheat-based and not celiac-safe.
Even the egg specialty (玉かつ / tamago-katsu) is a breaded, deep-fried item, meaning the shared fryer carries breaded wheat coating; no gluten-free preparation or dedicated fryer is documented.
The house uses cholesterol-free rapeseed oil rather than lard, but this concerns the fat type, not gluten; the oil still fries wheat-breaded cutlets, so cross-contact with gluten remains.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on tonkatsu with a house tonkatsu sauce and house ponzu, and ponzu/sauce in Japanese cooking typically involve soy sauce, which usually contains wheat; no wheat-free soy product is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a pork tonkatsu specialist; no vegan or vegetarian main is documented. A bagna cauda of organic Kyoto vegetables is served, but bagna cauda is traditionally made with anchovy and is not vegan, and it is plated in a kitchen handling pork and fried wheat coating.

positives.text: Frying is done in cholesterol-free rapeseed oil instead of lard, and sauces are made in-house without chemical seasonings, which can help diners who want to know exactly what is in the food, though this does not make any dish gluten-free or vegan.

places.name: 熟成豚 かわむら (Jukuseibuta Kawamura)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is breaded with wheat panko (糖度低めのパン粉 from Tokyo's 中屋パン粉工場) and deep-fried; accompanying sauces are soy-based (屋久島あごだし醤油, おぢかソース). Shared fryer and wheat/soy throughout. No gluten-free options indicated.
places.vegan_detail: A tonkatsu specialist centered entirely on aged pork cutlets; no vegan or vegetarian menu items indicated. Frying uses lard (and lard/canola blend per reports). Not suitable for vegans/vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Wed, Fri–Sun, holidays, and days before and after holidays 10:00–20:30 (last order 20:00) (per Tabelog). Note: the official site lists 11:00–20:00 (last order 19:30), differing from Tabelog.
places.neighborhood: Yamashina-ku, Takehana Nishinokuchi-chō / Keihan Yamashina Stn and Yamashina Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single independent location started by 川村悠爾 (Kawamura Yuji) in 2014, formerly named とんかつ食堂 熟豚, renamed ~2022. Has its own standalone official site (kdn7900.gorp.jp) and Instagram; no chain or multi-branch references found.

chef_bio.origin: Yamashina-ku, Kyoto (near Keihan Yamashina / Yamashina Station)
chef_bio.background: Kawamura is a small tonkatsu specialist tucked into Yamashina, on the eastern edge of Kyoto. It began as a humble shop called Jukuton (熟豚, later とんかつ食堂熟豚) before being reborn under the current name 熟成豚 かわむら. The whole shop is built around one idea: aged pork. The pork is selected by farmers and then entrusted to a carcass-aging specialist in Kyoto's Fushimi district, where it is dry-aged for roughly three to five weeks so its aroma and flavor deepen before it ever reaches the fryer. The room is small and bright, almost cafe-like, with just a handful of counter and table seats, and it tends to fill the moment the doors open, even on weekdays. Word has spread well beyond Kyoto, and the little shop has been recognized in the Michelin Guide's Bib Gourmand.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let the pork speak. Rather than leaning on heavy sauce, the kitchen ages the meat to concentrate its natural umami and encourages diners to start with just the house-blend salt to taste the pork itself.
chef_bio.specialty: Tonkatsu made from dry-aged pork, double-fried for a crisp crust and tender, juicy interior

anecdotes.text: The pork is dry-aged for about three to five weeks at a specialist aging house in Kyoto's Fushimi district, a step meant to deepen its aroma and flavor before frying.

The tonkatsu is cooked in two stages: first gently in pure lard at low temperature, then finished in a blend of medium-heat lard and canola oil, for a crisp crust over tender meat. The panko is sourced from the Nakaya Panko Factory in Tokyo.
Beyond the regular Kyoto-area pork, the shop rotates rare heritage breeds such as Tokyo X, Umeyama pork, and Saddleback, so the offering changes with what is at its peak.

Diners are given several ways to finish each cut: a sweet sauce, a spicy sauce, sesame dressing, a Yakushima ago-dashi soy sauce, and a house-blend salt, with regulars saying the salt best brings out the meat's natural umami.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ・gorp・genjitsutouhiが、旧『熟豚』、伏見の熟成庫での3〜5週間熟成、ラードと菜種油での二度揚げ、東京・中屋パン粉、ミシュランビブグルマン、東京X・梅山豚・サドルバック等の稀少豚、塩など複数の薬味を記載。約12席。

cultural_comfort.note: A small (about 12-18 seat) counter-focused, Japanese-only specialty tonkatsu shop where ordering and signage are in Japanese, so a foreign student should arrive ready to point at the menu and use a few basic phrases; reservation/announcement info is via Instagram and the shop front.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a tonkatsu specialist: pork is coated in wheat panko (from the Nakaya Panko Factory in Tokyo) and deep-fried, so it is not celiac-safe. There is no indication of a gluten-free menu or a dedicated fryer.
The breading is wheat panko and the cutlets are deep-fried, meaning gluten is present throughout the core cooking process.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Among the table condiments is a Yakushima ago-dashi soy sauce; most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so it should be treated as containing gluten.
Sauces offered include a soy-based ago-dashi shoyu and a demi-glace-style sauce, both wheat/soy-derived rather than gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered entirely on deep-fried pork cutlets, and accompaniments include miso soup and pork-based sides, so there are no vegan mains. The frying oil includes lard, making the deep-fryer unsuitable for vegan diners.

places.name: とんかつ山本 (Tonkatsu Yamamoto)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is pork breaded in wheat-flour panko and deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce and accompaniments typically contain wheat and soy. High cross-contamination risk in a small shared kitchen. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.
places.vegan_detail: Core menu is deep-fried pork cutlet (tonkatsu); the shop is built around pork. No vegan options indicated and the broader Japanese/Western menu likely uses dashi, egg, and meat. Not suitable for vegans.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location shop founded 1968 (1968年創業), run by the proprietor with his wife handling service (奥様の接客). No evidence of a chain or branch locations. Selected for Tabelog's とんかつ百名店 (Top 100 Tonkatsu).

chef_bio.background: Tonkatsu Yamamoto has been frying cutlets in an alley near Teramachi Nijo, just a short walk from Kyoto City Hall, since 1968 — more than half a century. The shop is run by an owner couple known for their warm hospitality, and it is the kind of place Kyotoans return to again and again. Tucked into a narrow lane, it has the classic Kyoto 'unagi no nedoko' (eel's-bed) layout — long and slender, opening from a calm storefront marked by a noren curtain bearing a single character, 技 ('technique/skill'). Inside there are 13 counter seats where you can watch the work up close, plus a quiet tatami room in the back.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen is built around 技 — craft — emphasizing the lightness of the coating and precise control of the frying so the cutlet stays tender at the center with minimal heaviness afterward. They source quality pork without leaning on premium brand labels, letting meat quality and technique do the talking, and even keep the menu in old-fashioned characters rather than katakana to underline that traditional spirit. Regulars are encouraged to taste the meat on its own first, before the house-made sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut loin cutlets (ロースかつ) and tenderloin cutlets (ヘレかつ) are the heart of the menu, with a minced cutlet (みんちかつ) also on offer. The pork loin is unusually thick — said to be about 1.5 times a normal cutlet — yet cooked all the way through while staying tender, wrapped in a fine, thinly applied panko coating that fries to a crisp, fragrant golden brown. Sets come with rough-chopped (rather than julienned) cabbage that's crunchy and naturally sweet, rice, pickles, and a standout pork-and-miso soup built on a white miso base with a hidden note of sansho pepper. By advance reservation, seasonal luxuries like lobster and abalone are available.

anecdotes.text: The house-made sauce is made right in front of guests — vegetables can be seen simmering away in a pot at the counter — and the shop pairs its cutlets with a small lineup of around seven sake, including Nagaoka's Asahi-yama.

The cabbage is served in rough chunks rather than the usual fine shreds, described by diners as crisp with a natural sweetness, and the pork miso soup hides a touch of sansho pepper in its white-miso base.
The loin cutlet is notably thick — roughly 1.5 times a standard cutlet — yet stays tender to the center under a light, crisp panko crust fried to a clean golden color.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan・kyotogourmetが、1968年創業、夫婦経営、『技』の暖簾、うなぎの寝床の間取り、13席+座敷、通常比約1.5倍のロースかつ、ざく切りキャベツ、山椒を効かせた白味噌の豚汁、目の前で作るソース、約7種の日本酒、要予約の伊勢海老・鮑を記載。

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional 13-seat counter shop where staff likely speak mainly Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases and a willingness to order set meals (定食) will make your visit smooth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a tonkatsu specialist: the cutlets are pork coated in wheat-flour panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the core dishes are not gluten-free and are not safe for celiac diners.
Frying is done in shared oil used for breaded, wheat-coated cutlets and minced cutlets, with no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer, so cross-contamination risk is high in this small kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house-made tonkatsu sauce and the accompaniments (pickles, miso soup) typically contain wheat and/or soy; most Japanese soy sauce and sauce bases are brewed with wheat, so assume gluten is present throughout the meal.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a pork tonkatsu house; the menu centers on deep-fried pork cutlets, the soup is a pork-and-miso soup, and no vegetarian or vegan dishes are indicated. The kitchen is not suitable for vegan diners and cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

places.name: とんかつ処 勝裕（かつひろ） (Tonkatsu-dokoro Katsuhiro)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is breaded (wheat panko + wheat flour batter) and deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce contains wheat and soy; accompaniments (rice, miso soup, curry) involve soy/dashi. Shared fryer means cross-contamination with wheat. No gluten-free offering indicated.
places.vegan_detail: A tonkatsu specialist whose core menu is breaded deep-fried pork; sides and sauces use animal-derived and dashi-based ingredients. No vegan options indicated on any source.
places.neighborhood: 下京区 西七条 / 西大路駅 (Nishioji Station, 909m)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] No branch or chain/系列店 information found on any source. Multiple sources reference the owner directly (店主のおすすめ / owner's recommended dishes) and describe a homey, family-style (家庭的で温かい雰囲気) atmosphere, consistent with a single-location owner-operated independent shop. Classified i

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Shimogyo Ward (Nishi-Shichijo, near the Nishioji-Shichijo intersection)
chef_bio.background: Katsuhiro is the kind of neighborhood tonkatsu shop that locals quietly treasure. Tucked near the Nishioji-Shichijo intersection in Kyoto's Shimogyo Ward, it has the warm, homey feel of an old-fashioned family dining room, with regulars and families filling the seats and a feeling that, as one writer put it, you've wandered onto the set of a TV drama. It looks at first like an ordinary little shokudo, but the cooking is what keeps people coming back year after year.
chef_bio.philosophy: Careful preparation and frying of the meat and breading, an affordable price, and a welcoming local atmosphere — straightforward home-style tonkatsu done with care.
chef_bio.specialty: Crisp, thinly-breaded tonkatsu is the heart of the menu. The fillet (hire) cutlet is famous for being so tender that, as one reviewer joked, you barely need teeth, while the loin (rosu) cutlet, croquettes, and a generous jumbo fried shrimp round out the lineup. The signature is the Katsuhiro-zen set, pairing an extra-large fried shrimp with fillet tonkatsu, served with cabbage, pickles, pork miso soup, and rice with free refills. A nice touch: a mortar of sesame seeds arrives first so you grind your own and blend it into the sauce while your cutlet is freshly fried.

anecdotes.text: The fillet cutlet is so tender that one reviewer joked you don't even need teeth to eat it, and the batter is praised as thin and shatteringly crisp.
Before the food arrives, diners are handed a mortar of sesame seeds to grind and mix into their own tonkatsu sauce — a small ritual that's part of the experience here.
The room has a homey, warm atmosphere that fills quickly with families, and the place is clearly beloved by locals as a classic old-style neighborhood shop.
The most popular order is the Katsuhiro-zen set, which pairs an extra-large fried shrimp with the fillet tonkatsu.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・o-ensokuが、西七条の家庭的なとんかつ食堂、擂りごまをソースに混ぜる趣向、名物『勝裕膳』(特大海老フライ+ヒレかつ)、歯が要らないほど柔らかいヒレ、薄く軽い衣、地元に愛される雰囲気を記載。

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-light neighborhood tonkatsu shop (QR payment accepted but no credit cards) where reviews and signage are in Japanese and staff likely speak little English, so a foreign student should arrive with a translation app or some basic Japanese phrases.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a tonkatsu specialist: cutlets are coated in wheat flour and wheat panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the core menu is not gluten-free and is not safe for celiacs.

The kitchen fries a wide range of breaded items — pork loin and fillet cutlets, croquettes, fried shrimp, and oyster fry — in shared oil, so cross-contamination with wheat breading is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkatsu sauce contains wheat and soy, and the set meals come with miso soup (pork miso/tonjiru) and rice; miso and most Japanese soy sauce typically contain wheat, so seasonings and accompaniments add further gluten exposure.

vegan_cross_contact.text: There is no vegan or vegetarian offering: the menu is built on pork and seafood cutlets, the soup is a pork-based miso soup, and dashi/soy-based seasonings are used throughout.

places.name: ぼんじゅーる (Bonjour)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Menu centers on yoshoku hambagu and steak, which typically rely on wheat (flour binder/breadcrumbs, demi-glace sauce), soy sauce, and dashi-based seasonings. No gluten-free accommodation is indicated on the page; cross-contamination likely.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. This is a meat-focused Western-style restaurant (hamburg steak, beef steak); dishes use animal protein, dairy, and meat-based sauces. The page lists no plant-based menu items.
places.hours_raw: Thu–Sun 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:30), 18:00–22:00 (food last order 20:00, drinks 21:00)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Small single-location establishment with only 16 seats (8 counter, 4 two-top tables) and a limited 4-day operating week; no chain or brand indicators on the page. Treated as an independent owner-operated shop, though staffing details are not explicitly documented.

chef_bio.background: Bonjour has been part of the Karasuma Rokkaku neighborhood since 1951, and after roughly 75 years it remains a quietly beloved old-school yoshoku (Japanese-Western) house run by a husband-and-wife team. The current proprietor is the second generation to stand at the small counter, where just sixteen seats (eight at the counter, plus four tables for two) let regulars watch the cooking up close. The chef trained in French cuisine before turning that technique toward the comforting Western-style classics Kyoto diners have loved here for over seven decades. It is unhurried, homestyle, and personal — when the couple is working solo, a single dish can take its time to reach the table, because everything is made by hand to order.
chef_bio.philosophy: A stated commitment to keep making food that pleases the guest — premium ingredients and careful handwork offered at neighborhood prices, so quality cooking stays within reach of regulars rather than reserved for fancy occasions.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a hand-formed hamburg steak built from a blend of premium A5-grade Japanese Black wagyu, minced and mixed in-house, that comes out so tender it almost crumbles — regulars call it ほろほろジューシー — releasing a deep, rich umami the moment you bite in. It is classically plated yoshoku style, crowned with a fried egg and a rich demi-glace sauce. The other house pillar is the curry rice, a slow-simmered Japanese Black wagyu beef-tendon curry that has been a signature 'since founding.' The kitchen also does a beef cutlet (bifukatsu) using Shinshu Premium A5 Japanese Black wagyu, and weekday lunches pair the hamburg set (with rice and soup) with a long list of fryer sides like shrimp fries, scallop fries, and house-made sausage.

anecdotes.text: The hamburg is a hand-formed blend of premium A5-grade Japanese Black wagyu, minced and blended in-house for an extremely tender, umami-rich patty.
One regular calls it simply the most delicious hamburg they know — 'ほろほろジューシー,' meltingly tender and juicy, with umami that spreads the instant it hits your mouth.
The wagyu hamburg is served the classic yoshoku way, topped with a fried egg and a rich demi-glace sauce, and is likened to the refined quality of a high-end establishment.
The chef came up through French cooking before opening this Western-style counter run by the couple ('元々フレンチ出身のシェフ' / '夫婦で経営').

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ・kyotopi・gcjapan・公式が、1951年創業、フレンチ出身シェフの夫婦経営・二代目、16席、A5黒毛和牛を配合した手ごねハンバーグ(目玉焼き+デミ)、創業以来の黒毛和牛すじ肉カレーを記載。信州プレミアムA5和牛はビフカツに使用。

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 16-seat counter-and-table yoshoku shop with no indication of English support, so a foreign student should ideally come with a Japanese-speaking friend or a translation app and be ready to order in Japanese.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a classic yoshoku (Western-style) house centered on wagyu hamburg steak and curry rice. Both are wheat-heavy by nature: hamburg patties typically use a breadcrumb/flour binder, the signature topping is demi-glace (a flour-thickened, slow-cooked sauce), and the curry rice is built on a wheat-flour roux. It is not celiac-safe, and no gluten-free accommodation is indicated.

The lunch menu features many breaded, deep-fried sides — shrimp fries, scallop fries, and house-made sausage — which use wheat breading and share fryer oil. There is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer, so cross-contamination is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yoshoku seasoning and demi-glace-style sauces in Japan are commonly built with soy sauce and stock-based seasonings, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free or tamari alternative is indicated, so assume soy sauce here contains wheat unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around wagyu beef, fried egg, seafood fries, and house-made sausage, with no vegan or vegetarian options indicated. The shared griddle and fryer used for meat and seafood make plant-only meals impractical here.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information about allergy handling or allergen labeling appears on the restaurant's own pages or in diner reports. Because this is a small two-person operation cooking everything by hand to order, it is best to phone ahead and discuss any allergy directly before visiting.

positives.text: It is a tiny, hands-on counter where the husband-and-wife team cook to order and the owner is known for explaining the dishes to guests — a setting where it is easy to ask questions about ingredients in person.

places.name: 洋食 おがた (Yoshoku Ogata)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. As a 洋食 (Japanese-Western) restaurant, dishes rely heavily on wheat-based flour, breading, demi-glace/roux sauces, and likely soy and dashi-based seasonings; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on premium beef (hamburger, meat dishes) and French-technique meat/dairy preparations; no vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and dairy/eggs/meat stocks are integral to 洋食 cooking. (Menu review: 0 vegan meals; 3 vegan items but no full main.)
places.neighborhood: 中京区・京都市役所前駅/烏丸御池 (Nakagyo-ku, near Kyoto Shiyaku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent restaurant opened in 2015 by chef-owner Ogata Hiroyuki; no branches or chain affiliation found.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (Nakagyo-ku, near Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae / Karasuma Oike)
chef_bio.background: Yoshoku Ogata opened near Kyoto City Hall in October 2015 as the independent restaurant of chef Hiroyuki Ogata, a Kumamoto native who built his craft the long way around. He trained for roughly a decade in classical French cuisine, including time at the luxury Hotel Europe in Nagasaki's Huis Ten Bosch, before becoming head chef at Bistro Sept, a beloved Kyoto bistro famous for its monthlong reservation waits. When he struck out on his own, he carried that French foundation into yoshoku, Japan's own tradition of Western cooking, in the very city long regarded as the birthplace of Japanese Western cuisine. The result is a tiny counter-focused room where guests watch Ogata and his team work, the kitchen running as a tight, visible piece of teamwork that regulars say is part of the charm. Within two years of opening it earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand, and reservations now routinely fill up a month ahead.
chef_bio.philosophy: Ogata describes wanting to run a Western restaurant that feels like a kappo counter, ingredient-first cooking where the produce comes before the recipe (食材ありきの、割烹のような洋食屋). He sources from trusted producers around the country and lets their stories shape each plate, leaning on French fundamentals of careful heat control, sauce-making, and garnish to keep flavors clean and bright.
chef_bio.specialty: Ogata is best known for its hamburger, a blend of aged Shiga beef and Kagoshima pork finished with a deep, faintly bitter house demi-glace. Beyond it the daily board runs to 80-plus options that change with what arrives: a thick-cut pork ginger using saddleback pork from Fukudome Komaki Farm, a live-aji (horse mackerel) fry served rare with tartare, ebi fry, omurice, chicken macaroni gratin, potato salad, and a small mini-curry that many diners order as a gentle finale to the meal.

anecdotes.text: Chef Hiroyuki Ogata trained about ten years in French cuisine, including at the Hotel Europe in Nagasaki, then ran the kitchen as head chef at Kyoto's Bistro Sept before opening his own place in 2015.

Ogata frames his cooking as 'ingredient-first, kappo-like Western food,' weaving the stories of the producers he buys from into dishes such as a thick-cut pork ginger made with saddleback pork from Fukudome Komaki Farm.
The hamburger blends aged Shiga beef and Kagoshima pork under a deep demi-glace, served alongside crowd favorites like the ebi fry and a sweet, onion-forward mini curry.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: dancyu・タケマシュラン・食べログが、2015年10月開業、熊本出身・緒方博行、約10年のフレンチ修業(ハウステンボス ホテルヨーロッパ)、ビストロセットの料理長、開業2年でビブグルマン、熟成近江牛と鹿児島豚のハンバーグ、80種超の品書き、食材ありきの割烹のような洋食を記載。

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, highly popular (Tabelog Top-100 Western) counter-style restaurant where reservations and basic Japanese are effectively expected, so a foreign student should ideally go with a Japanese-speaking guide or someone who can phone-book and order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a classic yoshoku (Japanese-Western) kitchen built around wheat. Core dishes such as the ebi fry and live horse-mackerel fry are panko-breaded and deep-fried, and the signature hamburger is finished with a flour-thickened demi-glace, so gluten is central to the menu rather than incidental.

No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol is documented anywhere in available sources. With breaded fried items and roux/demi-glace sauces sharing the same small kitchen, cross-contamination should be assumed. This restaurant is not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasonings are not itemized publicly, but as a Japanese kitchen it is likely to use standard soy sauce (which typically contains wheat) and dashi-based seasonings in various dishes. Anyone strictly avoiding gluten should not assume any savory dish is wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: There is no vegan or vegetarian offering documented. The menu centers on meat, seafood, eggs, and dairy (hamburger, fries, gratin, omurice), and even vegetable-forward items like the soup and salad are prepared in a kitchen dominated by animal-based stocks, butter, and demi-glace. Vegan cross-contact is effectively unavoidable here.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-accommodation policy is documented in available sources. Given the counter format and a daily board of 80-plus changing specials, diners with serious allergies should call ahead in Japanese to confirm before booking; the restaurant is reservation-only and books up roughly a month in advance.

positives.text: The chef sources ingredients personally from trusted producers nationwide and emphasizes quality and food safety, and the open counter lets guests see the kitchen at work, which can make it easier to ask questions about a specific dish in person.

One dish, the mini curry, is noted as being made without added oil or flour, making it gentler on digestion, though this is not a verified gluten-free preparation and the rest of the menu remains wheat-heavy.

places.name: 洋食の店　みしな (Yoshoku no Mise Mishina)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic yoshoku/fry-set restaurant: signature dishes are crab cream croquettes, shrimp/fry sets, and beef stew — all built on wheat (breading/batter, roux-based brown sauce, flour-thickened cream). No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Cross-contamination from a small shared fry kitchen is highly likely.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. The menu centers on meat (beef stew), seafood (shrimp, crab croquettes) and likely dairy/egg in cream-based and breaded dishes. A vegan diner would need to ask directly and should not expect dedicated options; potage and side vegetables may contain meat/dairy/dashi.
places.neighborhood: 東山区・二年坂/清水寺周辺 (Higashiyama-ku, Ninenzaka / near
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent establishment run by the founder's daughter and son-in-law together with the chef (三品), descended from the original "つぼさか" (Tsubosaka) shop in Gion. No chain or multi-location indication found.

chef_bio.origin: 東山区・二年坂/清水寺周辺 (Higashiyama-ku, Ninenzaka, near Kiyomizu-dera), Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Mishina's roots reach back to 1948, when it opened in the heart of Gion on Hanamikoji as a Western-style restaurant called Tsubosaka. For decades it was a beloved haunt of geiko and maiko, and of literary figures said to include novelist Junichiro Tanizaki. When the Gion shop finally closed at the turn of the Heisei era, the family didn't let the cooking end there. They converted their own residence on Ninenzaka, on the hillside lanes leading up to Kiyomizu-dera, into a tiny ten-seat counter restaurant and reopened in 1994 as Mishina. Today it is run by the family across two generations, with Masako Mishina as the current proprietress, tucked down a quiet back lane so well hidden that regulars treasure it as a secret.
chef_bio.philosophy: Guarding the old-fashioned flavors and cooking earnestly and carefully, the kitchen describes its motivation simply: the joy of hearing a customer say 'delicious.'
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Kyoto yoshoku. The signature is a beef stew built on a demi-glace sauce simmered patiently for two full weeks to draw out the depth of vegetables and meat. The fried sets are the most popular order, anchored by delicate crab cream croquettes and shrimp fry made with an ultra-fine house breadcrumb. A charming Gion legacy lives on in the menu: bite-sized cream croquettes and a 'hito-kuchi katsu' (one-bite cutlet), originally sized so that maiko could eat neatly without spoiling their makeup. Meals finish in a distinctly Kyoto way, with ochazuke (green tea over rice) with pickles and seaweed seasoning.

anecdotes.text: The shop began life in 1948 as Tsubosaka on Gion's Hanamikoji, a Western-food favorite of geiko, maiko, and literary regulars; when it closed at the start of the Heisei era the family reopened it on Ninenzaka in 1994 as a ten-seat counter restaurant carved out of their own home.
Bite-sized cream croquettes and a one-bite cutlet were put on the menu so that small-mouthed maiko could eat them gracefully without disturbing their appearance.

The demi-glace that defines the beef stew is made over two weeks to coax out the umami of the vegetables and meat, in keeping with the kitchen's aim of protecting its time-honored taste and cooking sincerely and carefully.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: discoverjapan・2nenzaka・ダイナースが、1948年祇園花見小路の『つぼ坂』に始まり谷崎潤一郎ら文人・芸舞妓に愛され、平成初頭の閉店後1994年に二年坂の自宅を改装し十席のカウンターで再開、女将・三品雅子と家族が営むこと、二週間仕込むデミグラスのビーフシチュー、舞妓向けの一口サイズ、締めの茶漬けを記載。

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter-style traditional yoshoku spot in a tourist area where reservations are required and no English support is documented, so a foreign student would be most comfortable going with a Japanese-speaking guide or someone who can phone-book and order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a classic yoshoku fry restaurant built on wheat: crab cream croquettes, shrimp fry and fried sets use wheat-flour breading and batter, the signature beef stew is built on a roux-based two-week demi-glace, and the cream filling is flour-thickened. It is not celiac-safe and no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

The kitchen is a single small counter (about ten seats) where breaded fried items and flour-based sauces are the core of the menu, so cross-contamination of any non-fried dish with wheat is highly likely. There is no evidence of a separate fryer or allergen-segregated prep.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Meals are finished with ochazuke and pickles in the Kyoto style, and Japanese sauces/seasonings are used throughout. As with most Japanese restaurants, soy sauce and similar seasonings here very likely contain wheat; assume wheat unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a meat-and-seafood yoshoku house centered on beef stew, fried shrimp, croquettes and cutlets; there are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes, and shared fryer/grill surfaces make animal-product cross-contact likely. Vegan diners should confirm directly before visiting.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures, allergen menus, or accommodation policies are documented in any available source. With reservation-only seating and a tiny owner-run kitchen, the best path is to phone ahead and discuss needs directly.

positives.text: The restaurant is small, owner-run and reservation-based, so staff can be spoken with directly about ingredients before or at booking, the kind of personal kitchen where a careful conversation is possible.

places.name: 呑龍 淀店 (Donryu Yodo-ten (Tonryu Yodo))
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. A 町中華 menu centered on gyoza, ramen, fried rice, stir-fries, and set meals relies heavily on wheat (gyoza wrappers, ramen noodles), soy sauce, and likely chicken/pork-based dashi and shared frying oil. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Assume cross-contamination throughout.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan/vegetarian options are documented on any source. Dishes described are meat- and seafood-heavy (fried chicken, shrimp tempura, crab omelette, fried dumplings), with vegetable stir-fries that still use meat-based broth/soy/oils. A vegetable dish might be requested, but a strict vegan must ask directly; no confirmed plant-based offerings.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Wed, Fri–Sun 11:00–14:30 / 16:00–21:00; closed Thu. Note: Gurunavi and others sometimes list continuous hours 'Mon–Wed, Fri–Sun 11:00–21:00.'
places.neighborhood: Fushimi-ku, Yodo Honmachi (near Yodo Stn; residential area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Independently operated single location. The 京都ぴあ article and search sources note that several 呑龍 shops in the Yodo area were "元々親戚筋のお店ながらそれぞれ独立経営" (originally connected by family ties but each now independently managed). This 淀店 is its own independent family-run establishment, no

chef_bio.origin: Yodo (Yodo-Honcho), Fushimi-ku, Kyoto — near Yodo Station and Kyoto Racecourse
chef_bio.background: Donryu Yodo-ten is a longtime neighborhood Chinese spot tucked into the residential streets of Yodo, a short walk (about two minutes) from Yodo Station and a short stroll from Kyoto Racecourse. It is the kind of unpretentious machi-chuka (everyday town Chinese) eatery that locals lean on for a quick, filling, affordable meal, and it draws extra crowds on race days. The room is casual and roomy, and regulars describe a warm, comfortable welcome that makes it easy to settle in.
chef_bio.specialty: Generous, wallet-friendly lunch sets are the draw, with most plates priced well under 1,000 yen. The kitchen is known for fragrant, well-seasoned fried rice (one diner found it fluffier than expected), pan-fried gyoza, ramen and mini-ramen sets, and crisp, juicy karaage that anchors many of the combination set meals. Other regulars include sweet-and-sour pork, tenshinhan, chuka-don, and yakisoba sets, plus a hearty hiyashi chuka in summer.
