anecdotes.text: Regulars point to the cozy, comfortable atmosphere and warm service as part of the appeal, calling it a relaxing place to eat.
Diners single out the fragrant, well-seasoned fried rice, with one noting it had a fluffier texture than expected.
Value is a recurring theme: one diner remarked that being able to eat a set meal for under 1,000 yen in today's economy is precious.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ・Rettyが、淀本町(淀駅至近・京都競馬場近く)の町中華、1000円未満の定食、香ばしい焼飯、餃子・唐揚げ・酢豚・天津飯などのメニュー、居心地の良さと値打ちを記載。店主名は非公表。

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, cash-friendly local Chinese diner in a quiet Yodo residential area with a Japanese-only menu and clientele, so a foreign student should be comfortable but should expect to point at menu items or use a translation app and know basic Japanese greetings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a town-Chinese (machi-chuka) kitchen built around gyoza, ramen, fried rice, stir-fries, and fried dishes like karaage and sweet-and-sour pork. These rely heavily on wheat (gyoza wrappers, ramen noodles, batter/flour dusting) and soy-based sauces, and everything is cooked on shared woks, surfaces, and frying oil. Assume cross-contamination throughout; it is not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sauces and seasonings across the menu (fried rice, stir-fries, gyoza dipping, ramen broth, tenshinhan) are soy-sauce based, and standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat. No gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. Broths and dishes at a machi-chuka spot are typically pork/chicken-based, and the kitchen shares woks and oil across meat dishes, so cross-contact with animal products should be assumed. Vegan diners should confirm directly with staff.

positives.text: Reviewers describe warm, comfortable service and note that staff can be flexible with some menu modifications, so it is worth speaking with them directly about your needs.

noodles.notes: Standard machi-chuka chuka-soba noodles, served thin; exact specs not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: The chuka-soba (ramen), most popularly enjoyed as the mini-ramen included in a lunch set alongside fragrant fried rice (yakimeshi), fried gyoza, and karaage.
ramen.chashu: Not documented; if present, expect simple sliced braised pork in the old-school town-Chinese style.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus soy-sauce (wheat) tare; a town Chinese kitchen with shared woks and no documented GF options.

vegan.note: Light shoyu broth built on chicken/pork in a Chinese kitchen; no documented plant-based bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood comfort bowl from a long-standing town-Chinese kitchen by Yodo Station: a clear, light soy-sauce soup of the everyday Japanese-Chinese (chuka-soba) lineage, the kind of unfussy, nostalgic ramen meant to round out a plate of fried rice and gyoza rather than steal the show.

places.highlight: A neighborhood comfort bowl from a long-standing town-Chinese kitchen by Yodo Station: a clear, light soy-sauce soup of the everyday Japanese-Chinese (chuka-soba) lineage, the kind of unfussy, nostalgic ramen meant to round out a plate of fried rice and gyoza rather than steal the show.

places.name: まるい食堂 (Marui Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is wheat-based ramen/udon noodles, breaded (flour) fried dishes, and soy-sauce/dashi-based broths and seasonings; no gluten-free accommodation indicated. High cross-contamination risk.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. Menu centers on meat/fish (charsiu, pork cutlet, breaded fish, chicken) and broths/sauces almost certainly made with animal-based dashi (katsuo/niboshi) or pork. No documented plant-based or dashi-free dishes.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–15:00, 15:00–19:30 / Sat 11:00–14:00; closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: 四条大宮 / 阪急大宮駅 (Shijo-Omiya)
places.cuisine: Neighborhood Chinese (machi-chūka), chūka soba, casual set-meal diner
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tabelog lists a single location; a Kyotopi feature describes the shop as run by an elderly husband-and-wife couple, indicating a family-operated independent business with no chain affiliation.

chef_bio.background: Marui Shokudo is a Showa-era neighborhood diner tucked along Omiya-dori just south of the Bukkoji intersection, a short walk from Shijo-Omiya. It has been part of the area long enough that regulars affectionately call it a local "cultural heritage" of Shijo-Omiya. There is no flashy storefront sign; you find it by the noren curtain at the door and the handwritten whiteboard of daily specials. Inside, the room is unmistakably old-school and homey, with table seats and a small tatami area, and the place tends to fill with neighborhood regulars soon after it opens. It's a cash-only, everyday kind of spot built around honest, affordable home-style cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: A classic machi-chuka and shokudo all-rounder. It is best known for its chuka soba (Chinese-style ramen): a chicken-based, soy-sauce broth that locals describe as gentle and soothing, with firm noodles that keep their bite, topped with seasoned pork (chashu), bamboo shoots, hand-cut green onion, and a generous hit of black pepper. Beyond ramen it spans miso chanpon, soy, miso and black ramen variations, plus a wide everyday menu of udon, soba, rice bowls (donburi), and set meals. Homey set meals are a draw too, such as a dashimaki (rolled-omelet) teishoku served with rice, miso soup, pickles and side dishes for around 500 yen.

anecdotes.text: Locals regard Marui Shokudo as a kind of Showa-retro "cultural heritage" of the Shijo-Omiya neighborhood, prized for its simple, comforting ramen in an unchanged old-fashioned setting.
The signature chuka soba pairs a chicken-and-soy-sauce broth, described as gentle and soothing, with firm noodles, seasoned pork, bamboo shoots, green onion and black pepper.
A regular notes the dashimaki (rolled-omelet) set meal comes with rice, miso soup, pickles and side dishes for around 500 yen at this long-running set-meal diner.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・ekiten・食べログが、四条大宮の昭和レトロな食堂、暖簾と日替りホワイトボード、鶏ガラ醤油の中華そば(約550円/チャーシュー・メンマ・葱・胡椒)、味噌ちゃんぽん等の変化、約500円のだし巻き定食、現金のみを記載。

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny old-school Showa-era neighborhood diner run by an elderly couple with no indication of English support, so basic Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe restaurant. The core menu is built on wheat: chuka soba and ramen, udon, soba, and breaded/fried items like tonkatsu and fried fish. With shared pots, broths, and fryer in a small everyday diner, cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable.
Documented signature dishes are wheat-based ramen and udon in soy-sauce broths, with no indication of any gluten-free preparation or separate cooking surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broths and seasonings are soy-sauce based (the chuka soba is a chicken-and-soy-sauce broth). Standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi typically contain wheat, so the soups and sauces here should be assumed to contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not suitable for vegans. The kitchen centers on chicken-based broths, pork (chashu), egg dishes, and dashi, all prepared in shared equipment; there is no indicated vegan option and high risk of animal-product cross-contact.

noodles.notes: Crisp, springy bite with good snap (歯切れよく、ぷりっと) — better than typical canteen noodles, holding up against the clear shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (チャーシューメン) — the chicken-bone shoyu chuka soba loaded with extra slices of braised pork; a generous black-pepper shake is part of its character.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork (煮豚) — firm with a satisfying bite rather than melting-tender, sliced generously and prized for how well it harmonizes with the broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option documented at this old-school canteen.

vegan.note: Chicken-bone broth with shoyu tare and braised pork throughout; no plant-based broth bowl documented.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic Showa-era canteen chuka soba: a clear chicken-bone broth perfumed with soy sauce, the kind of comforting, unpretentious bowl that seeps into you and once defined neighborhood ramen across Japan. Hand-cut scallions, thick braised pork, and a generous shake of black pepper give it that timeless old-school shokudo soul.

places.highlight: A nostalgic Showa-era canteen chuka soba: a clear chicken-bone broth perfumed with soy sauce, the kind of comforting, unpretentious bowl that seeps into you and once defined neighborhood ramen across Japan. Hand-cut scallions, thick braised pork, and a generous shake of black pepper give it that timeless old-school shokudo soul.

places.name: マルシン飯店 (Marushin Hanten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is gyoza (wheat dumpling wrappers), ramen (wheat noodles), tenshinhan and other Chinese dishes relying on soy sauce and wheat/soy-based seasonings; heavy gluten and soy exposure with high cross-contamination risk. No gluten-free accommodation documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The signature gyoza use aged pork; Chinese dishes typically use meat, egg, and pork/chicken-based stocks. A vegan would need to ask on-site, and options are unlikely. No labeled plant-based items confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 11:00–05:45 (last order 05:45)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent town-Chinese eatery in Higashiyama. It runs an adjunct raw-gyoza retail shop and online/vending gyoza sales under the same brand at the same address, but the restaurant operation itself is one location, not a chain. Family/solo distinction not explicit

chef_bio.background: Marushin Hanten opened in June 1977 under the catchphrase "the sleepless Marushin" (ねむらないマルシン) — a町中華 (neighborhood Chinese diner) that, unusually for its time, served from midday straight through until early the next morning, year-round. The founder built it into a Higashiyama institution. The current owner, Maekawa Ryushiro, grew up living on the second floor above the shop until he was about five. He didn't set out to run it: he spent four years in Tokyo as a construction site supervisor before coming back to Kyoto to help his father. In 2004, when Maekawa was 27 and knew nothing about running a restaurant, his father suddenly told him "I'm handing the shop over to you" (お前に店を譲る) — and then stepped back entirely, letting his son sink or swim. Maekawa took the reins and has carried the place forward, keeping the Showa-era room and its through-the-night spirit intact while building the gyoza into a destination of its own.
chef_bio.philosophy: Aiming to be the best casual Chinese restaurant in Kyoto — sourcing fresh ingredients daily from the Kyoto Central Market and serving food that is delicious, quick, and affordable, so that customers leave smiling. The owner talks about getting back to the fundamentals of dining and earning loyalty through quality and warmth.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the tenshinhan (天津飯) — a crab-style egg-over-rice topped with a glossy, generously poured sauce, the egg cooked silky and fluffy (three eggs go into a single serving). The other banner item is the gyoza, available pan-fried, boiled, or deep-fried, with a premium "aged pork" version made using meat from the renowned Kyoto aged-meat purveyor Nakasei (京都中勢以). Hearty wok dishes like beef-and-green-pepper stir-fry round out the menu, all served fast, affordable, and at almost any hour.

anecdotes.text: The owner, Maekawa Ryushiro, took over the restaurant in 2004 at age 27 — knowing nothing about running a business — after his father abruptly told him "I'm handing the shop over to you" (お前に店を譲る) and then stepped back completely, refusing to interfere.
He grew up in the family quarters on the restaurant's second floor until he was about five, and only returned to the business after four years working as a construction site supervisor in Tokyo.

Opened in June 1977 with the catchphrase "the sleepless Marushin," it was unusual at the time to run from midday until early morning all year round — a tradition the shop still keeps, serving until roughly 6 a.m.
The aged-pork gyoza uses meat from the renowned Kyoto aged-meat purveyor Nakasei (京都中勢以), giving the filling a rich depth beneath a golden-brown, crisp exterior.
The famous tenshinhan is built on three eggs per serving, cooked silky and fluffy, with the glossy sauce poured on so generously it nearly overflows the bowl.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official about page (marushinhanten.com) confirms the June 1977 opening, the ねむらないマルシン catchphrase, the 11:00–翌6:00 through-the-night hours, and owner Maekawa Ryushiro taking over in 2004 at age 27 after his father said 「お前に店を譲る」. Takemachelin and Leaf KYOTO confirm the premium gyoza uses 京都中勢以 (Nakasei) aged pork; genjitsutouhi confirms the 天津飯 uses three eggs with sauce that nearly overflows the bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, casual late-night Kyoto town-Chinese diner where ordering is straightforward but staff and menus are mainly Japanese, so a foreign student should be ready to point at dishes or use basic Japanese and expect possible queues.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat- and soy-heavy町中華 (Chinese diner): the core menu is gyoza (wheat dumpling wrappers), ramen (wheat noodles), tenshinhan and wok dishes built on soy sauce and wheat/soy-based seasonings. It is not celiac-safe, and cross-contamination risk is high.
Gyoza is offered pan-fried, boiled, and deep-fried, and dishes are cooked in shared woks and a busy, fast-turnover kitchen — there is no documented gluten-free preparation or separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes such as the tenshinhan rely on a heavily seasoned soy-based sauce; most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat, so these dishes should be assumed to contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The kitchen centers on pork gyoza, egg, wagyu beef and other meat dishes cooked in shared woks, so cross-contact with animal products should be expected. Vegan diners should confirm directly with staff.

noodles.notes: Old-school town-Chinese chuka-soba noodles: soft and thin. The shop highlights using carefully chosen (kodawari) noodles, but specific specs are not published.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (the plain shoyu chuka-soba); the Cantonese ramen (広東ラーメン) is the most distinctive bowl — a glossy oyster-and-soy ankake thickened broth heaped with eight kinds of vegetables, seafood and pork over soft thin noodles. For pork lovers, the Char Siu Ramen.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork that is meaty and notably lean, with little fat — a clean, restrained chashu rather than a fatty melt-in-mouth style.
notable_toppings: Cantonese bowl: kikurage, napa cabbage, onion, carrot, shiitake, bamboo shoot, small shrimp, pork and quail egg under a glossy ankake sauce

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; the standout Cantonese ramen also rides on a wheat-thickened oyster-soy ankake. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A meat-and-seafood town-Chinese kitchen: bowls lean on pork/chicken stock, oyster sauce, soy tare and pork or shrimp toppings. No documented plant-only broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A beloved old-school Kyoto town-Chinese institution where the ramen is honest chuka-soba — a clear, gentle soy broth on the humble pork-and-chicken backbone that has fed the neighborhood since the 1970s, the kind of nostalgic everyday bowl that defines Japan's machi-chuka tradition. Its most characterful pour is the Cantonese ramen, a glossy oyster-soy ankake blanketing soft thin noodles with a garden of vegetables and seafood.

places.highlight: A beloved old-school Kyoto town-Chinese institution where the ramen is honest chuka-soba — a clear, gentle soy broth on the humble pork-and-chicken backbone that has fed the neighborhood since the 1970s, the kind of nostalgic everyday bowl that defines Japan's machi-chuka tradition. Its most characterful pour is the Cantonese ramen, a glossy oyster-soy ankake blanketing soft thin noodles with a garden of vegetables and seafood.

places.name: 鉄鍋ぎょうざとお酒の店 杏っ子 (Tetsunabe Gyoza to Osake no Mise Anzukko)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Gyoza wrappers are hand-kneaded wheat dough; fillings use pork and Chinese spices, and an izakaya menu typically involves soy sauce and dashi. No gluten-free options or allergen program documented.
places.vegan_detail: Official site explicitly states pork is used in all gyoza. As an izakaya focused on iron-pan pork gyoza, no vegan or vegetarian gyoza/options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 18:00–23:00 (food last order 22:00, drinks 22:30) / Sat 17:30–23:00 (food last order 22:00, drinks 22:30) / Sun 17:30–22:30 (food last order 21:30, drinks 22:00)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Official site and Tabelog show a single location on the 2F of Le Sizième Building; no chain or brand affiliation found. Classified as independent; family-vs-solo not documented, defaulted to independent_solo.

chef_bio.origin: 京都市中京区, near 京都市役所前駅
chef_bio.background: Anzukko is a small, tucked-away second-floor izakaya in central Kyoto that opened on January 6, 2006. It deliberately keeps an intimate, calm and refined feel, the kind of spot regulars describe as a hidden gem, where you settle in for an evening of dumplings and a drink rather than a quick meal. The kitchen makes its gyoza by hand, and the signature iron-pan dumplings are built around dough that is kneaded and rested overnight before being pan-fried to a fragrant, crispy finish.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen favors nuance over a single dominant flavor, building dumplings with layered spices and aromatic depth so that ingredients like Camembert lend a delicate richness rather than overpowering the bite. The room is run as a quiet, grown-up retreat: a one-drink-per-guest house and no children under 15, keeping the focus on relaxed evening dining.
chef_bio.specialty: Tetsunabe (iron-pan) gyoza is the house signature: hand-kneaded dough rested overnight, pan-fried crisp, with a juicy filling of fresh domestic pork, vegetables and a blend of Chinese spices. The menu fans out into creative variations including shrimp gyoza, shiso-plum gyoza, cheese (Camembert) gyoza and edamame gyoza, alongside izakaya plates like a smoked-egg potato salad, all meant to be paired with craft beer, sake and wine.

anecdotes.text: A regular calls Anzukko the number one gyoza place in Kyoto, praising how the iron-pan, shrimp, shiso-plum, cheese and edamame gyoza each carry their own complex spice blend and aromatic depth instead of one obvious flavor.
The signature iron-pan gyoza is made with dough that is kneaded by hand and left to rest overnight, then filled with fresh domestic pork, vegetables and Chinese spices.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog (JP and EN) confirms the January 6, 2006 opening on the 2nd floor of the Le Sizeme building (中京区恵比須町442-1, ~3 min from 京都市役所前駅), ~16 seats (カウンター9/テーブル7), the one-drink-per-guest rule and the 「15歳未満のお子様のご来店はご遠慮」 policy, and the signature 鉄鍋ぎょうざ made with hand-kneaded dough rested overnight and a juicy domestic-pork filling; varieties include shrimp, Camembert cheese, shiso-plum and yuzu.

cultural_comfort.note: english-friendly

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not celiac-safe. The house signature is gyoza wrapped in hand-kneaded wheat dough, so wheat is central to the core menu and flour is present throughout the kitchen.
No gluten-free menu, allergen program, or cross-contamination protocol is documented on the restaurant's own page or in any review.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: This is a gyoza izakaya; dumplings are typically served with soy-based dipping sauce, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Assume soy sauce here is not gluten-free unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan-friendly. The restaurant's own page states all gyoza contain pork, and the menu is built on pork, seafood and cheese fillings.

places.name: 手打うどん春日井 (Teuchi Udon Kasugai)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour handmade udon; tempura is wheat-battered; dashi/broths and curry udon are very likely to contain soy sauce, wheat, and bonito/dashi. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation indicated. A celiac/gluten-sensitive diner should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu confirmed. Japanese udon broths typically use katsuo/dashi (bonito/fish stock), and tempura is fried; some items (zaru udon, kake) could potentially be made without meat but the broth is unlikely to be vegan. A vegan would need to ask directly about dashi; no documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 17:00–22:00 (per Tabelog)
places.neighborhood: Shimogyō-ku, Gojō-Ōmiya (JR Tambaguchi Stn / subway Gojō Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single, fixed-location independent shop (no chain references found). Multiple independent sources describe a solo 大将 (owner-chef) who single-handedly handles all cooking — udon, tempura, and sides — while female floor staff handle service.

chef_bio.background: This is a true one-man udon shop near the Gojo-Omiya corner, a short walk from JR Tanbaguchi station. The owner runs the kitchen entirely solo, hand-pulling the noodles and cooking everything to order, from the udon itself to the tempura and side dishes. Because each bowl is made individually for the customer in front of him, there can be a wait, and the staff let you know in advance, but regulars say that care is exactly the point. The shop has been serving since late 2007 and has built a devoted local following, to the point that it regularly sells out, so much so that some diners arrive early after being turned away on an earlier visit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality and craft over speed: udon made fresh to order, one customer at a time, with the owner doing all of it by hand himself.
chef_bio.specialty: Kyoto-style udon: thin noodles with a clean firmness (koshi) and a smooth, almost silky texture, served in a refined, elegant dashi broth that locals describe as deeply flavored yet light. The shop is especially loved for its agehana / haikara-style udon (thick grilled aburaage, a runny half-boiled egg, tempura crisps, sudachi citrus, green onion, seaweed and roasted sesame), kitsune udon, curry udon, and tempura such as shrimp and chicken (kashiwa) tempura, plus a tempura assortment. Five-ingredient okowa (sticky-rice) onigiri rounds out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The owner works alone in a tiny space, hand-making the udon in the order tickets come in; diners describe being impressed watching him work methodically through each order.
Every bowl is hand-pulled and cooked individually, which is why service takes time, and the staff tell customers about the wait in advance as a courtesy.
The shop is famous for selling out; one reviewer noted they were turned away on a previous visit because it had sold out, and came back earlier the next time.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Living Kyoto confirms 「このお店では店主さんが一人で手打ちうどんを作られています。そのため、提供までには時間がかかります」 and that staff give advance notice of the wait; the mypl feature calls it a 「売り切れ御免の超人気うどん店」 at the 五条大宮角. Tabelog places it at 下京区下五条町435-3 (near 大宮五条, ~10 min from 丹波口), opened Dec 19, 2007, 15 seats, with kitsune/kashiwa-ten/curry udon and a 9-item tempura assortment; the agehana/haikara udon (grilled aburaage, half-boiled egg, sudachi, tenkasu) is a signature.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, popular local udon shop where reviews and signage are in Japanese and the solo chef is busy, so basic Japanese phrases (or a translation app) will make ordering smoother — but it is approachable for a foreign student willing to point at the menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not celiac-safe. The core product is handmade wheat-flour udon, and the menu centers on wheat-based dishes; flour and shared cooking water make cross-contamination effectively unavoidable in a one-person kitchen.
Tempura (shrimp, chicken/kashiwa, assorted) is wheat-battered and deep-fried; no dedicated gluten-free fryer or separate preparation is indicated in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are served in a dashi broth, and Japanese udon broths and curry udon are typically built on soy sauce (which usually contains wheat) plus bonito/kombu dashi. Treat the broth, curry udon, and any tsuyu as containing wheat and fish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. The broth is a bonito/dashi-based stock, kitsune and agehana udon include egg and aburaage, and there is no indication of plant-based dashi. Vegan diners should confirm directly before visiting.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy protocols, allergen information, or accommodation practices appear in any available source. With a single owner cooking everything to order, ask directly and well in advance about any allergy needs.

places.name: お数家いしかわ (Okazuya Ishikawa)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Cuisine is obanzai/Japanese home cooking built on dashi, soy sauce, miso and likely wheat-containing seasonings; no gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation is advertised. Cross-contamination with soy/wheat should be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on seasonal-vegetable simmered dishes (~100 obanzai recipes), so plant-based dishes likely exist, but most are simmered in fish/bonito dashi and the genre is listed as 居酒屋・海鮮 (seafood). No vegan labeling is provided; a vegan diner would need to ask and confirm dashi/animal content per dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, days before and after holidays 11:30–13:30 (last order 13:00), 17:00–22:00 (food last order 21:00, drinks 21:30) / Sat, Sun, holidays 17:00–22:00 (food last order 21:00, drinks 21:30)
places.neighborhood: Shimogyō-ku, Shijō-Karasuma (Takakura-dōri Shijō, heading south; right by Karasuma Stn / Shijō Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Independent restaurant opened in 2006 by proprietress Chiyoyo Ishikawa (女将 石川千代世), serving obanzai learned from her mother. A sister tempura restaurant ("Tempura Obanzai Ishikawa") exists under the same family, but each is a distinct single location, not a chain. Treated as indep

chef_bio.background: Tucked down a narrow alley near Shijo-Karasuma, Okazuya Ishikawa lives inside a traditional Kyoto machiya (wooden townhouse) that has been quietly reworked into a calm, chic izakaya. The room is run by a female proprietor (the okami) whose cooking grows straight out of her own childhood and her memories of her mother's home cooking. That nostalgic 'mom's okazu' became the whole idea of the place: warm, unfussy Kyoto food meant to be eaten at the end of the day with a cup of sake or a bowl of plain rice. The counter looks straight into the kitchen and its old-style cooking hearth (okudo-san), so you can watch the day's dishes come together. The restaurant has been a neighborhood fixture for years, and there is now a sister shop, Tempura Obanzai Ishikawa, carrying the same family name nearby.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is approachable, comforting home cooking - food simple enough to feel like family dinner, in a room welcoming enough that a woman can drop in alone in the evening without a second thought.
chef_bio.specialty: Obanzai - Kyoto-style home cooking - is the heart of the menu: a rotating daily lineup drawn from a repertoire of around 100 recipes that changes with the seasons. The kitchen happily crosses genres, mixing Japanese standards with Western and Chinese-influenced original plates. Alongside the obanzai, fresh fish is a real point of pride - sashimi cut from seafood sent in daily from around the country - paired with a deep, frequently rotating sake list that runs to limited and rare bottles.

anecdotes.text: The signature obanzai is built on the owner's nostalgic memories of her mother's cooking, with a daily lineup chosen from a repertoire of around 100 seasonal recipes spanning Japanese, Western and Chinese styles.
The shop sits in a renovated traditional Kyoto townhouse down an alley near Shijo-Karasuma, with counter seats facing the kitchen and its old-style cooking hearth (okudo-san).
Fresh fish delivered daily from around Japan is served as carefully selected sashimi, rounded out by a rich, rotating selection of sake including limited and rare bottles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (okazuya-ishikawa.com) confirms a 女将-run machiya izakaya down an alley in 下京区高倉通, the concept of casual home-style cooking so 「女性が夜ひとりでも気負いなく来られる」, an open counter facing the kitchen and its おくどさん hearth, a genre-crossing 和・洋・中 obanzai repertoire of roughly 100 recipes, daily fish/sashimi and a rotating sake list with limited/rare bottles, plus the sister shop Tempura Obanzai Ishikawa. The specific '20-plus' sake count and 'eight or more' daily-dish figure were not stated in sources and have been softened.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, intimate machiya counter spot expressly designed so solo diners (including women) feel at ease, but ordering and conversation are in Japanese, so basic Japanese or a translation app will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is an obanzai (Kyoto home cooking) and seafood izakaya whose dishes are built on dashi, soy sauce and miso - seasonings that in Japan almost always contain wheat. It is not celiac-safe, and cross-contact with wheat and soy should be assumed across the menu. No gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation is advertised.
The owners also run a sister tempura shop (Tempura Obanzai Ishikawa), and tempura batter is wheat-based; any fried items here carry a high risk of gluten and shared-oil cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is grounded in soy sauce, dashi and miso typical of Kyoto obanzai. Most Japanese soy sauce (and many dashi and seasoning blends) contains wheat, so soy-sauce-based dishes should be treated as containing wheat unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi made from bonito (katsuo) and/or other fish is a foundational ingredient in obanzai, so even vegetable-looking dishes are likely to contain fish-based stock. Fresh fish and sashimi are also central to the kitchen. Vegans should ask directly about every dish; vegan status is unconfirmed.

positives.text: Counter seating faces the open kitchen and traditional hearth, so diners can see how dishes are prepared and have a direct line to ask the staff about ingredients.
Daily dishes are drawn from a large rotating repertoire of about 100 recipes, so staff can describe exactly what is in each plate when asked.

places.name: ろじうさぎ (Roji Usagi)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The cuisine is おばんざい/和食 built around dashi (fish/kombu stock), miso, soy sauce, pickles, and dishes like だし巻き (dashi egg roll) and 塩さば (salted mackerel) — dashi, soy and wheat-containing seasonings are pervasive. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented; not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented on the official site or Tabelog. Set meals are fish/meat-centered with vegetable おばんざい sides, but dashi and miso typically contain fish; sweets (parfait, anmitsu, sweet-potato ice cream) may suit vegetarians but not strict vegans. A vegan would need to ask directly; nothing confirmed. (Menu review: 0 vegan meals; 5 vegan items but no full main.)
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama-ku, Miyagawachō / Gion (an alley in Shimoyanagi-chō, ~6 min walk from Keihan Gion-Shijō Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single independent machiya cafe run by a solo owner-operator who opened it in July 2012; no chain or additional branches found. The owner is a former Kyoto tour guide and the ~1,000 books in the shop are from the owner's personal collection.

chef_bio.origin: 京都・東山区 宮川町／祇園（下柳町の路地）
chef_bio.background: ろじうさぎは、宮川町・祇園の路地裏にたたずむ築100年あまりの京町家を活かしたカフェ・甘味処です。2012年7月に開業し、昔ながらの京町家の風情と、生活感さえ漂うほっこりとした空気をそのまま残した空間で、生粋の京都人である女性店主が切り盛りしています。畳敷きの座敷や昭和を感じさせる古い設えが残り、観光向けに作り込まれた華やかな京カフェとは一線を画す、素朴で家庭的な居心地のよさが魅力です。芸妓・舞妓の街として知られる宮川町の路地を進んだ先にあり、地元の人にも旅人にも愛されています。
chef_bio.philosophy: 生粋の京都人の店主が、ひと品ひと品を「丁寧に丁寧に」手作りする家庭的なもてなしを大切にしています。体にやさしく素材を生かした献立が特徴です。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は「京のあさごはん」。焼き鮭か焼き鯖を選べる京の朝ごはんセット、地元の豆腐店の濃い豆乳と生湯葉をたっぷりのせた豆乳湯葉粥、ふわふわのだし巻きが主役のだし巻きセットなど、朝から丁寧に作られた純和風の献立が揃います。昼は色とりどりのおばんざいを並べた御膳が看板で、だし巻き・お椀・季節の小鉢・香の物までひと皿ひと皿手をかけた京の家庭料理が味わえます。午後は手作りのプリンやプリンパフェ、季節のケーキなど、やさしい甘味も人気です。

anecdotes.text: 焼きたての焼き鯖は皮目がパリッパリで、京の朝ごはんとして評判です。焼き鮭か焼き鯖を選べ、お汁も数種類から選べます。
「古き良き日本を感じられる素敵な場所」「京の町家で純和風なランチ」と評され、昭和の佇まいを残す座敷でくつろげます。
地元の豆腐店の濃い豆乳と生湯葉をたっぷり使った豆乳湯葉粥が季節の名物です。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトとTabelogにより、2012年7月1日開業・築100年の京町家（東山区下柳町、宮川町の路地裏、全24席）で、生粋の京都人の女性店主（healthy meals crafted by a genuine Kyoto woman chef）が切り盛りすることを確認。看板は「京の朝ごはん」と、地元豆腐店の濃い豆乳＋生湯葉の豆乳ゆば粥、焼き鯖／焼き鮭、だし巻き、おばんざい御膳、プリン・プリンパフェ。『バターを使わないパウンドケーキ』『うさぎクッキー添えのアイス』は出典で確認できず削除。

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, foreigner-friendly machiya cafe where the chatty owner (a former Kyoto guide) often shares sightseeing tips, but staff communication is likely mostly Japanese, so a few basic phrases will help a foreign student.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a traditional Kyoto obanzai / washoku cafe built around dashi (fish and kombu stock), miso, soy sauce, and pickles. Signature dishes such as the dashi-maki (dashi egg roll), salted mackerel and grilled salmon breakfast, soy-milk yuba porridge, and the colorful obanzai sets all rely on dashi and wheat-containing seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free menu or dedicated allergen handling, so it is not safe for celiacs.

Even seemingly lighter or vegetable-forward items (yuba porridge, obanzai side dishes, miso and kasu soups, white-miso椀) are typically prepared with fish-based dashi and soy seasonings, so cross-contact with gluten-bearing ingredients is pervasive in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu makes heavy use of soy sauce and soy-based seasonings (obanzai, dashi-maki served with sauce, simmered dishes). Most Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) is brewed with wheat, so dishes seasoned with it should be assumed to contain wheat/gluten unless the staff confirm a wheat-free tamari is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan status is uncertain and should be confirmed directly. Even plant-forward items like the soy-milk yuba porridge and obanzai sides are generally made with fish-based dashi (katsuobushi/iriko), and the kitchen also prepares grilled fish, egg dishes (dashi-maki), and dairy desserts, so cross-contact with animal ingredients is likely.
Sweets such as the pudding, pudding parfait, pudding a la mode, and ice cream contain egg and dairy. One cake is noted as made without butter, but that does not make it vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen labeling, gluten-free, vegetarian, or vegan accommodations are documented on the cafe's own site or in diner reviews. Allergy needs should be discussed directly with the staff before ordering.

positives.text: Diners can choose between grilled salmon or mackerel and from several soup varieties, and some sets allow customization, so it may be possible to discuss simple substitutions with the staff.
Dishes are described as carefully homemade and 'gentle' (e.g., a pound cake made without butter), suggesting an attentive owner who may be willing to talk through ingredients on request.

places.name: 京肴 ふくや (Kyozakana Fukuya)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Menu centers on dashi-based broths, soy sauce, sabazushi (vinegared mackerel sushi), and tempura (wheat batter) — wheat- and soy-heavy throughout. No gluten-free menu or accommodation stated on official site or listings. (Menu review: 4 gluten-free meal options on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Seafood-focused izakaya (sashimi, fish dishes, dashi broths). No vegan or vegetarian menu information found on the official site, Tabelog, or Hot Pepper. Dashi is typically fish/katsuobushi-based, so even vegetable dishes are likely not vegan.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Wed, Fri–Sun, holidays, and days before and after holidays 17:00–01:00 (last order 00:00) / closed Thu (per Tabelog). Hot Pepper lists Mon–Wed, Fri–Sun 17:00–23:00, so the two differ on the closing time.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single location confirmed (only one address listed; no chain/brand affiliation found). Official site states "京料理、和食を愛する店主が切り盛りするアットホームな当店" (a proprietor-run homey establishment). The target brief describes it as couple-run (夫婦2人で切り盛り); a 16-seat counter izakaya is consistent with

chef_bio.background: Tucked into the lively Sanjokai shopping arcade near Nijo Castle in Kyoto's Nakagyo ward, Fukuya is an intimate izakaya run by a husband-and-wife team who opened the doors in April 2017. It draws an easygoing local crowd, from people stopping in after work to families, couples, and solo diners. Every morning the kitchen sources fresh fish and seafood from Kyoto's central wholesale market and Maizuru Fish Port on the Sea of Japan, and pairs them with seasonal Kyoto vegetables, building menus around whatever is at its best that day.
chef_bio.philosophy: A self-described lover of Kyoto and Japanese cuisine, the proprietor runs a small, welcoming room built on careful craftsmanship and the kind of dashi that anchors traditional Japanese cooking, where the quality of the kombu, bonito flakes, and the cook's precision make all the difference.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is bo-zushi, a pressed sushi of thick, vinegar-marinated mackerel laid over rice. The owners are confident enough in it that they hope every guest will try it, prizing the mackerel's natural umami and a clean balance with the rice. Beyond that, the kitchen turns out daily sashimi and seafood platters, grilled fish, tempura of seasonal Kyoto vegetables, obanzai home-style side dishes, and dishes featuring chicken, beef, and pork, alongside a sake selection.

anecdotes.text: The owners single out their bo-zushi (pressed mackerel sushi) as the dish they most want guests to try, made from thick marinated mackerel chosen to bring out its natural umami in balance with the rice.
The kitchen restocks each morning from Kyoto's central wholesale market and Maizuru Fish Port, then builds the day's menu around the freshest seasonal seafood and Kyoto vegetables.
A husband-and-wife team opened the izakaya in the Sanjokai shopping arcade in April 2017, welcoming everyone from after-work regulars to families and solo diners.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (k-fukuya.com) confirms the signature 棒鯖寿司 (「肉厚な鯖本来の旨味を活かした味」) and the owner's devotion to Kyoto/Japanese cuisine and good dashi. Tabelog confirms the April 6, 2017 opening, 16 seats, and the morning restock from the central wholesale market and Maizuru Fish Port; the Sanjokai arcade page and multiple listings describe it as a husband-and-wife-run izakaya using seasonal 京野菜 for tempura and obanzai.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small 16-seat local Japanese izakaya with Japanese-language listings and no documented English support, so a foreign student would be most comfortable with at least some basic Japanese (a translation app and a friendly attempt at konnichiwa will go a long way).

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a celiac-safe restaurant. The menu is built around wheat- and soy-heavy Japanese cooking: dashi broths, soy sauce, vinegared mackerel sushi, and tempura fried in a wheat batter. The official site and listings state no gluten-free menu and no accommodation.
Tempura of seasonal vegetables is a core part of the menu, which means a shared fryer and wheat batter in the kitchen; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is used throughout, and most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat. Dishes such as sashimi, simmered items, and the mackerel sushi rely on soy sauce and dashi, so gluten is present across the menu. No wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a seafood-forward izakaya whose dashi is made from bonito (fish) flakes and kombu, and the kitchen centers on fish, meat, and soy sauce. There is no stated vegan or vegetarian menu, and even vegetable dishes are likely seasoned with fish-based dashi or soy sauce.

places.name: 旬菜 いまり (Shunsai Imari)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Cuisine is dashi-heavy (fish-based broth in obanzai, miso soup) and soy-heavy (miso, soy sauce, pickles); wheat is likely present (soy sauce, possible tempura batter, miso variants). No gluten-free/allergen accommodation is published. Phone (075-231-1354) for any dietary needs; cross-contamination not addressable for celiac. (Menu review: 4 gluten-free meal options on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling published. Most dishes use dashi (fish stock) and animal products; menu includes some plant-based-looking items (Dutch-style braised eggplant ¥500, simmered greens & yuba/tofu skin ¥500, corn tempura ¥580, potato salad ¥680) but these likely contain dashi and are not confirmed vegan. Breakfast set is fixed and includes fish, egg, miso. Vegans should call 075-231-1354 to confirm preparation. (Menu review: 1 vegan meal.)
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu–Sun 07:30–11:00, 17:30–23:00 (last order 22:00) / closed Tue and Wed. Mornings are fully reservation-only (last reception 9:30). The official site lists morning 07:30–10:00 and evening 17:30–23:00.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent establishment, no chain affiliation found across Tabelog/official site/gnavi/hotpepper. Hitosara chef profile states chef Sawai joined after being invited by the restaurant's owner (his former school classmate), confirming owner-operated independent bu

chef_bio.background: Shunsai Imari is a Kyoto obanzai-and-sake house set inside a restored machiya, one of the city's traditional wooden townhouses, in the quiet streets off Rokkaku-dori near Karasuma-Oike. The name 'Shunsai' (旬菜) means 'seasonal vegetables,' and that idea runs through everything: the kitchen builds its menu around Kyoto vegetables (京野菜) and fresh seafood that change with the season, with the ingredients personally sourced rather than ordered in. The mood is relaxed and homey rather than formal kaiseki, a place to settle in over good sake and a spread of home-style Kyoto dishes in a calm, traditional interior of counter and table seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to bring out the natural taste of each ingredient and finish dishes with a gentle flavor (素材の味を活かして優しい味わいに仕上げる), keeping things approachable so guests can casually enjoy seasonal cooking, while paying attention to safety and quality by sourcing the ingredients themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: The house is known for its obanzai, the rotating lineup of traditional Kyoto home-style side dishes, with roughly 10 to 15 kinds offered on any given day, simmered in dashi-based broths. Its signature is the clay-pot tai-meshi (sea bream rice) cooked with Koshihikari rice from the Kyotango area and sea bream broth. Other favorites include kamo-nasu (Kyoto round eggplant) dengaku in two-color miso, dashimaki tamago, and Saikyo miso-marinated grilled fish. The restaurant is also notable for its reservation-only Kyoto breakfast (朝ごはん) built around freshly cooked clay-pot rice (Tanba Koshihikari), grilled fish, obanzai, dashimaki, miso soup and pickles. Alongside the food sits a rotating selection of more than 20 hand-picked sake from around Japan.

anecdotes.text: The signature clay-pot tai-meshi (sea bream rice) is cooked to order in a donabe with Koshihikari rice from the Kyotango region and sea bream broth, so the rice arrives freshly steamed at the table.

Imari is as well known for its quiet, reservation-only Kyoto breakfast as for dinner: a morning set of fresh clay-pot rice (Tanba Koshihikari), seasonal obanzai, dashimaki egg, Saikyo-grilled fish, miso soup and pickles, with the rice timed to be cooked just as you arrive.

The kitchen describes its approach as finishing dishes with a gentle flavor that draws out the ingredient's own taste, and says it personally goes out to source seasonal ingredients with attention to safety and quality.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms the 京の町家のおばんざい酒膳 in a 中京区六角通 machiya, self-sourcing of 京野菜・新鮮魚介 「安心・安全にも気を使い、食材は自ら足を運んで仕入れております」, the 「素材の味を活かして優しい味わい」 ethos, and 10-15 obanzai daily. The morning page confirms the reservation-only breakfast with donabe 丹波産コシヒカリ, Saikyo-grilled fish, dashimaki, miso soup and pickles, timed to arrival. Tabelog confirms 20+ sake, kamo-nasu two-color dengaku, and the signature clay-pot 鯛めし using 京丹後産コシヒカリ (correcting the earlier 'Tamba/Kyotango'); opened Jan 25, 2013, 24 seats.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a Japanese-language, locals-oriented machiya obanzai spot where a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese (and reservations, since breakfast is fully reservation-only) will do best; little English support is documented, so come prepared to order in Japanese or with help.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not celiac-safe. This is a dashi-heavy, soy- and miso-heavy Kyoto obanzai kitchen. Obanzai and miso soup are built on dashi, and dishes lean on soy sauce, miso and pickles, so gluten exposure is broad and cross-contact is hard to control for celiacs.

The menu includes fried items that are wheat-based or batter-based, including 串カツ (kushikatsu, breaded skewers) and 生麩の揚げ出し (agedashi nama-fu, fried wheat gluten). Nama-fu is made from wheat gluten and is not gluten-free, and breaded/fried items share oil and prep surfaces.

No gluten-free or allergen accommodation is published on the restaurant's site or listings. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or separate prep for gluten-free guests, so a dedicated fryer cannot be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce, miso and Saikyo miso marinades feature heavily (e.g. 銀鱈 西京漬け silver cod in Saikyo miso, 牛頬肉の赤味噌 braised beef cheek in red miso). Most Japanese soy sauce and many miso products contain wheat, so these dishes should be treated as containing gluten unless individually confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan is very difficult here. Obanzai and miso soup are made with fish-based dashi, the breakfast and many dishes include egg (dashimaki tamago) and grilled fish, and seafood is central to the menu. Even vegetable dishes are likely simmered in dashi.
No vegetarian or vegan options or labeling are published. A diner avoiding all animal products should call ahead to ask whether any vegetable dishes can be prepared without dashi, egg or fish.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen policy or gluten-free menu. For any dietary needs, call the restaurant directly at 075-231-1354 to discuss before visiting; celiac-level cross-contamination cannot be assured.

positives.text: The kitchen says it personally sources seasonal ingredients and pays attention to safety and quality, and cooks to order (e.g. clay-pot rice), which can make it easier to have a direct conversation about ingredients when you book ahead.

places.name: 天ぷら 京星 (Tempura Kyoboshi)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tempura is by definition a wheat-flour batter deep-fried preparation; cross-contamination in shared frying oil is inherent. Although the house "京星流" batter is described as egg-free with a thin coating, it still relies on wheat flour. Dipping is served with lemon juice, salt, and grated daikon (no flour-thickened tentsuyu), but the core dish remains wheat-based. Soy/dashi exposure also likely in an accompanying Japanese course. Avoid for gluten intolerance/celiac.
places.vegan_detail: A fixed reservation-only tempura course built around seafood and seasonal items; no vegan menu is advertised. Some vegetable tempura exists but the batter and shared frying oil, plus likely dashi/seafood elements, make a fully vegan meal unconfirmed. Would need to ask the restaurant directly in advance — and given the completely reservation-only kaiseki-style course format, accommodation is uncertain.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00–22:00 (fully reservation-only; last entry 20:00)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location, family-run establishment now operated by the third-generation owner (surname Sakakibara/榊原), per kyotopi.jp. No chain or branch locations found.

chef_bio.origin: Founded 1947 in Gion, Kyoto; later based in Ginza, Tokyo, and returned to Gion by the third generation (current location since around 1991)
chef_bio.background: Tempura Kyoboshi traces back to 1947, when it was founded in Gion; the shop later spent years in Ginza, Tokyo, and is now in the hands of its third-generation owner, Toshinori Sakakibara. His path to the counter was not gradual: he took over the family business at just 23 after his father fell ill, having trained at the long-established Tokyo tempura house Banhann. Under his stewardship the restaurant came back from Ginza to a quiet spot just east of Hanami-koji in Gion, where today only a handful of guests are welcomed each night at its small, roughly ten-seat counter. It is an intimate, single-counter room where the entire experience is built around watching one craftsman work the oil.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sakakibara works in the old tradition that 'craftsmen learn by observation,' not by being told. He describes his trade as competing 'solely with tempura' and judges the oil by touch alone, reading its temperature from the feel of the cooking chopsticks, a craft he says is nearly impossible to put into words. He sources ingredients himself across the Central Market, Nishiki Market, and the Ohara morning market, trusting his own eye over brand names or pedigree.
chef_bio.specialty: The house is known for its delicate 'Kyoboshi-style' (京星流) tempura: an egg-free batter of essentially just flour and water, applied with a silk-thin, almost translucent coating so the flavor of each ingredient comes through clean rather than being masked. The frying is done in a secret oil blend. Rather than a flour-thickened tentsuyu, pieces are finished simply with the kitchen's own house-blended salt, grated daikon, and lemon juice. Signature creations include simmered turban shell (煮栄螺) and a sweet-potato preparation the chef calls 丸十.

anecdotes.text: Sakakibara took over the restaurant at age 23 after his father became ill, having trained at the long-established Tokyo tempura house Banhann, and later brought the restaurant back from Ginza to Gion.
He judges the frying oil's heat purely by the tactile sensation of inserting his cooking chopsticks, describing it as a craft almost impossible to articulate in words.
The owner personally shops across the Central Market, Nishiki Market, and the Ohara morning market, choosing ingredients by his own judgment rather than by brand or origin.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog, gorp and Gnavi/hitosara confirm 三代目 榊原俊徳 took over at 23 after his father fell ill, having trained at the Tokyo tempura house 'Banhann', and the signature egg-free 粉と水のみ silk-thin batter, secret oil blend, house salt + grated daikon + lemon finish, and 煮栄螺/丸十 specialties, in a ~10-seat counter serving a few groups a day. Sources establish the shop was 昭和22年（1947）founded in Gion, later moved 祇園→銀座, and the third generation set up the current Gion shop ~26 years ago (~1991) — correcting the earlier 'originally in Ginza'.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a completely reservation-only, high-end (￥15,000+) 8-seat Michelin-starred tempura counter in Gion, so a foreign student should come only with a Japanese-speaking guide or fluent companion, ideally with the reservation and any dietary needs arranged in advance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a tempura specialty house, so the core of every course is wheat-flour batter deep-fried in shared oil. The house 'Kyoboshi-style' coating is described as essentially just flour and water, applied very thin, but it is still wheat-based. It is not celiac-safe.
All pieces are fried in the same oil, so even non-battered items would share frying oil with wheat batter. There is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tempura here is finished with house-blended salt, grated daikon, and lemon juice rather than a soy-based tentsuyu, so the tempura course itself avoids dipping sauce. However, an omakase-style Japanese course of this type commonly includes soy- and dashi-based elements, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Confirm with the kitchen before relying on this.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan or vegetarian options are documented in any source. The batter is egg-free, but the kitchen serves a fixed tempura omakase fried in shared oil with seafood, so cross-contact with animal ingredients is likely. Vegan status should be confirmed directly with the restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information was found in available sources about how staff handle allergy or dietary requests. As a small counter serving a fixed course, customization is likely limited, so contact the restaurant in advance to discuss any needs.

positives.text: The batter is made without eggs, which is helpful for diners with an egg allergy (though shared frying oil and seafood mean cross-contact cannot be ruled out).

The tempura is dressed only with house salt, grated daikon, and lemon juice rather than a flour-thickened or soy-based dipping sauce, so the fried pieces themselves carry no added soy or sauce-borne wheat.

places.name: 富小路 天ぷらまつ井 (Tominokoji Tempura Matsui)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tempura is wheat-flour battered and deep-fried, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is dashi/soy-based (wheat-containing soy sauce). Cross-contamination in shared frying oil is highly likely. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly as a whole: the menu is seafood-centric (live shrimp, fish) and the batter/dipping sauce rely on dashi (fish/bonito stock). Vegetable tempura exists but is fried in shared oil and served with dashi-based sauce, so it is not reliably vegan. A dedicated vegan would need to ask in advance; suitability is not confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri, Sun 11:30–14:30, 18:00–20:30 / Sat 11:30–14:30, 18:30–20:30 (open days and hours may change by season)
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku, Tominokōji-Sanjō, heading south (~5 min walk from Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location counter-style tempura restaurant opened 2019-10-25, run by owner-chef 松井基宏. No chain or multiple-location affiliation found on Tabelog or Hitosara.

chef_bio.origin: 中京区 富小路三条下る, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Tominokoji Tempura Matsui opened in 2019 on Tominokoji, just south of Sanjo in central Kyoto, as the namesake counter of chef Matsui Motohiro. Born in Hokkaido in 1977, Matsui first trained as a Japanese (washoku) cook. His path turned toward tempura when he was put in charge of a tempura specialty restaurant in Pontocho, which drew him into the world of Kyoto-style tempura. After honing his craft at well-regarded establishments, he opened this intimate ten-seat counter, where he fries to order in front of guests in a calm, Japanese-inspired room.
chef_bio.philosophy: Matsui pairs the discipline of classical Japanese cooking with Kyoto tempura technique, frying slowly in low-temperature oil to draw out each ingredient's flavor. His hospitality is deliberately unpretentious (kata-hiji harasenai omotenashi) - an easy, approachable counter where he talks through the day's ingredients and pairings with guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Kyoto-style (kyofu) tempura fried to order from fresh seasonal ingredients, carefully cooked in low-temperature oil so each item keeps its own character under a light, crisp coating. The menu changes with the seasons, and the kitchen is known for tempura that eats light rather than heavy. At lunch, the tendon (tempura rice bowl) is a popular draw.

anecdotes.text: Matsui began as a washoku (Japanese cuisine) chef and only moved into tempura after being entrusted with a tempura specialty restaurant in Pontocho, which sparked his interest in Kyoto-style tempura.
He fries Kyoto-style tempura carefully in low-temperature oil so ingredients keep their character while the coating stays light and crisp, served at the counter in front of guests.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hitosara's chef profile confirms 松井基宏 was born in 北海道 in 1977, started in 日本料理, and moved into 京風天ぷら after being 「先斗町の天ぷら専門店を任された」, active at 富小路 天ぷらまつ井 from 2019, with low-temperature frying. Tabelog confirms the Oct 25, 2019 opening and 10 counter seats (席数10席 カウンター10席), seasonal fresh-fried tempura and an affordable lunch donburi.

cultural_comfort.note: A small 10-seat counter where the chef fries each piece in front of you, so some basic Japanese (or a translation app) and comfort with quiet, attentive counter dining will help a foreign student feel at ease.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a tempura specialist: items are coated in wheat-flour batter and deep-fried, so the food is not celiac-safe. A shared frying oil handling many wheat-battered items makes gluten cross-contamination effectively unavoidable.
No dedicated gluten-free fryer or separate-oil arrangement is documented in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tempura is served with tentsuyu (a dashi-and-soy dipping sauce) and/or salt; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and tentsuyu and any dashi base typically include wheat-containing soy and/or other wheat-derived seasonings. Treat the sauces as gluten-containing unless the kitchen confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura exists, but the batter, the shared frying oil (used for fish, prawn and other animal items), and dashi/tentsuyu (made with bonito and other fish stock) mean vegetable items are not vegan and carry heavy animal cross-contact. No vegan-specific preparation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No documented allergy or gluten-free accommodation was found in available sources. The chef does discuss the day's ingredients with guests at the counter, so allergen questions can be raised directly - but confirm any needs in advance.

positives.text: Small ten-seat counter where the chef fries to order in front of you and talks guests through the day's ingredients, making it easy to ask about preparation directly.
Tempura is fried low and light, and is noted as eating less heavy than typical tempura - though this does not change its gluten or animal-product content.

places.name: 市川屋珈琲 (Ichikawaya Coffee)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Signature menu items are fruit sandwiches on wheat bread (gluten) and baked goods; coffee shop with no advertised gluten-free menu. No verified GF accommodation found. A foreign student with celiac disease should avoid the bread items.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature fruit sandwiches use fresh dairy cream (生クリーム) and bread; coffee can be taken black. No confirmed plant-milk options found, so a vegan would need to ask on-site. Status set to 'ask' (unconfirmed). (Menu review: 0 vegan meals; 5 vegan items but no full main.)
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 08:00–17:00
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama-ku, Kanei-chō (Shibutani-dōri, west of Higashiōji / Umamachi–Kiyomizu-Gojō Stn area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent cafe opened in 2015 by owner Mr. Ichikawa, who converted his grandfather's former Kiyomizu-ware pottery workshop (a ~200-year-old machiya) into the cafe. No chain or brand affiliation found.

chef_bio.background: 市川屋珈琲は、京都・東山の清水焼の窯元が集まっていた界隈に2015年に開いた喫茶店です。建物は店主・市川陽介さんの祖父がかつて営んでいた清水焼の工房兼住居で、市川さん自身もここで暮らしていました。築200年を数えるこの京町家はいったん取り壊しの話が持ち上がりましたが、市川さんは『この場所を残したい』という想いから、新築の約4倍という費用をかけて全面改修を決断。京町家作事組の手で、坪庭を望む大きな町家カフェとして生まれ変わりました。市川家は祖父・父ともに清水焼の陶芸家で、兄も焼物の道を継いでいます。市川さんだけは学生の頃から趣味で焙煎をするほどコーヒーを愛し、名門イノダコーヒで18年修業したのち、独立してこの店を開きました。
chef_bio.philosophy: 『一杯のコーヒーができること』を考え抜いた店づくり。市川さんは、来店した人がコーヒーを楽しむうちに自然と美しい器に出会えるよう、『一杯のコーヒーが、工芸への入り口に』なることを願っています。ネルドリップのなめらかで甘い一杯と、清水焼の器が、日常を少しだけ特別なものにしてくれます。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は、月ごとに移ろう旬のフルーツをふんわりした食パンとコクのある生クリームで挟んだ『季節のフルーツサンド』。果物の見極めや切り方、クリームの甘さの加減まで丁寧に仕立てられ、開店前から行列ができるほどの人気です。コーヒーは店内の焙煎機で日々自家焙煎し、ネルドリップでじっくり抽出。市川屋ブレンド・青磁ブレンド・馬町ブレンドの3種があり、それぞれ父や兄が手がけた清水焼のカップで供されます。市川屋ブレンドのカップは飲みやすさと保温性を考えて縁を薄く底を厚く、青磁ブレンドは香りが立つよう口を広く、と一杯ごとに器まで設計されています。卵サンドやデミオムライスといったメニューもあります。

anecdotes.text: 市川家は祖父・父・兄が清水焼の陶芸家という家系。市川さんだけは学生時代から趣味で焙煎をするほどコーヒーが好きで、自分の淹れたコーヒーを喜んでもらえることに魅力を感じ、名門イノダコーヒで18年修業してから独立しました。
祖父の工房だった築200年の町家が取り壊されかけたとき、市川さんは『この場所を残したい』という想いから、新築の約4倍の費用をかけて全面改修に踏み切りました。
コーヒーは父や兄が作る清水焼の器で提供され、市川屋ブレンドのカップは縁を薄く底を厚く保温性を高めるなど、一杯ごとに器まで考え抜かれています。
看板の季節のフルーツサンドは月替わりで旬の果物を使い、開店前から行列ができるほどの人気。『フルーツサンドのお店』として広く知られています。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 中川政七商店のストーリー記事が、店主・市川陽介さんは清水焼の陶芸家（祖父・父）の家系で兄も焼物を継ぎ、市川さんだけが名門イノダコーヒで18年修業したこと、祖父の工房兼住居だった築200年の町家を『残したい』一心で新築の約4倍の費用をかけ京町家作事組が全面改修したこと、市川屋／青磁（兄作）・馬町（父作）ブレンドを清水焼のカップで供することを確認。co-tripは月替わりの季節のフルーツサンドの人気を裏付ける。2015年開業。

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed Japanese-language machiya cafe popular with tourists; basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu will get you by, though staff may not speak much English, so a few Japanese phrases help.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a coffee shop whose signature items are fruit sandwiches and egg sandwiches made on wheat bread, plus baked goods. There is no advertised gluten-free menu and no verified GF accommodation, so it is not celiac-safe. A diner with celiac disease should avoid the bread and baked items and confirm any other order directly with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on dairy (fresh cream, milk-based coffee drinks) and egg sandwiches, so vegan options are not advertised; vegan diners should ask staff what, if anything, can be prepared without animal products.

places.name: しまお珈琲 (Shimao Coffee)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Signature offerings are wheat-based (バタートースト, ケーキ) prepared in a shared kitchen; no gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented. Toast/cake-centric café — assume wheat exposure throughout.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan/vegetarian options documented. The flagship morning plate contains bacon, fried egg, butter, and yogurt; cakes and toast use dairy/eggs. None of the fetched sources mention vegan or vegetarian accommodation. A vegan would need to ask, with low expectation of suitable options. (Menu review: 0 vegan meals; 5 vegan items but no full main.)
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun, holidays, and days before and after holidays 09:00–17:00 / closed Tue (per Tabelog). Note: some secondary sites list 11:00–17:00, so the hours conflict and should be confirmed.
places.neighborhood: Fushimi-ku, Minamihama-chō (Keihan Chūshojima Stn / in front of Teradaya, along the Uji-gawa canal)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent café opened 2023-05-21 across from 寺田屋. Run by 大嶋さん (Oshima); shop name "しまお" reflects the owner. No chain/brand affiliation found.

chef_bio.origin: Fushimi-ku, Minamihama-cho, Kyoto — across from Teradaya, near Keihan Nakashojima Station, along the Uji River branch
chef_bio.background: Shimao Coffee opened on May 21, 2023, in a bright, glass-fronted spot in Fushimi's Minamihama district, sitting directly across from the historic Teradaya inn with the Uji River branch flowing just outside. Through the floor-to-ceiling windows you can watch the traditional jukkoku-bune sightseeing boats glide past amid the greenery and seasonal hydrangeas, which gives the room a calm, waterside feel. The café was created by its owner, Oshima, who built it around a very personal idea: he loves the classic Japanese kissaten 'morning' set and wished he could eat it at any hour, not only first thing in the day. That wish became the heart of the place.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Oshima's guiding idea is that the comforting kissaten 'morning' should be available all day, not just in the morning — he is passionate enough about morning-café culture that he travels to Nagoya, famous for it, for inspiration. The result is an unhurried, all-day morning experience paired with the changing river view.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 'Anytime Morning Plate' (いつでもモーニングプレート) served with a drink throughout opening hours — a generous roughly 30cm plate piled with colorful salad, slow-roasted crispy bacon, sunny-side eggs, well-textured butter toast, and yogurt. Alongside it the café is known for its freshly brewed coffee (a deep-roast Bitter Blend and a medium-roast Mild Blend), café au lait, and a rotating selection of cakes, including a distinctive kinako (roasted soybean flour) and bamboo-charcoal cake. Simple plates like butter toast round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The owner, Oshima, conceived the café around the wish to enjoy a traditional kissaten morning set at any time of day, not only in the morning — and is so devoted to morning-café culture that he travels to Nagoya to experience it, which directly shaped the all-day 'Anytime Morning Plate'.

Set directly across from the historic Teradaya inn with the Uji River branch outside, the glass-walled room looks out on the jukkoku-bune sightseeing boats passing through fresh greenery and hydrangeas, giving a refreshing waterside atmosphere.
A standout among the cakes is a kinako (roasted soybean flour) and bamboo-charcoal cake, served alongside the café's Bitter and Mild coffee blends.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ALCO and KYOTOLIFE (life-info) confirm しまお珈琲 opened 2023年5月21日 across from 寺田屋 in 伏見区南浜町278 (中書島/Keihan Nakashojima area) with a glass-walled room overlooking the 宇治川派流 and 十石舟. 店主は大嶋さん, who created the 「いつでもモーニングプレート」(850円, drink included, ~30cm) from the wish 「喫茶店のモーニングを朝だけじゃなくいつでも食べたい」, inspired by Nagoya morning-café trips; menu confirms ビターブレンド（深煎り）／マイルドブレンド（中煎り）and 「きな粉と竹炭のケーキ」.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local riverside café with Japanese-only menu and no listed English support, so a foreign student should be comfortable with a few words of Japanese or a translation app, but the all-day morning plate makes ordering simple.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a toast- and cake-centric café whose signature items are wheat-based (butter toast, the all-day morning plate, and cakes), prepared in a shared kitchen. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented, so wheat exposure should be assumed throughout. Not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No specific information on soy sauce was found in the sources. The salad-topped curry on the menu would conventionally be roux-thickened (wheat); confirm ingredients directly if you are gluten-sensitive.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan menu or handling is documented. Core offerings center on butter toast, bacon, eggs, yogurt, café au lait, and butter/dairy-style cakes, so animal products are pervasive and cross-contact with vegan items cannot be assumed avoided. Note the kinako cake contains soybean flour but other cakes/toast are not vegan.

places.name: 馬耳東風 (Baji Tofu)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. As a yakitori izakaya, the menu relies on a tare (sweet soy-based glaze) aged ~20 years, which contains wheat and soy; soy sauce, dashi, and shared grills are pervasive. Salt-only (塩) skewers reduce but do not eliminate cross-contamination and gluten exposure risk. No gluten-free program documented.
places.vegan_detail: A charcoal yakitori specialist centered on chicken (skewers from Tajima/Okayama birds); no vegan menu or documented plant-based options. Dashi and animal-based seasonings are standard. Vegans would have effectively nothing reliable here.
places.neighborhood: 室町四条 (Muromachi Shijo), 中京区, near 烏丸駅/河原町駅
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location independent yakitori shop run by an owner-proprietor; no chain/brand affiliation found across Tabelog, Gnavi, Hitosara, or Retty listings. Classified independent_solo as no family-operation detail is documented.

chef_bio.background: Baji Tofu is the work of Kenji Kobayakawa, born in Kyoto in 1960. He came to grilling by an unusual route: he started cooking at 21 in a Western/French-style kitchen, and at 27 took on running a franchise restaurant. In 2006 he opened his own place, choosing yakitori precisely because it is a cuisine that is widely and warmly loved across generations, and wanting to build a restaurant that could be cherished for the long haul. The result is an intimate, dimly lit counter room on Muromachi Street where the light comes mainly from the glow of the charcoal, so the focus stays on the skewers in front of you.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kobayakawa deliberately chose yakitori as an accessible, long-beloved genre in which to test his craft, and his approach centers on freshness and care at every step: chicken bought fresh each day, skewers strung to order, charcoal grilling tuned by feel, and finishing salt chosen with intent.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori. Kobayakawa sources fresh domestic chicken daily from trusted suppliers and skewers each order individually rather than in advance, grilling over Kishu binchotan charcoal to keep the meat tender while concentrating its umami, with the heat and timing carefully judged for aroma. Signature skewers include the tori-negi (chicken and scallion), prized for its overwhelming portion of meat layered with vegetables and served with a house tare refined over some 30 years; the kimo (liver), grilled to a rich, almost rare-centered texture; and the seseri (chicken neck), valued for its springy, marbled bite and finished with Miyakojima sea salt.

anecdotes.text: Kobayakawa began his career at 21 in a Western/French-style restaurant before taking over a franchise location at 27, and in 2006 opened Baji Tofu of his own, picking yakitori because it is widely and lastingly loved.

Each skewer is strung to order and grilled over binchotan charcoal to keep the meat tender and the umami concentrated; the seseri (neck) is finished with Miyakojima sea salt and the tori-negi is served with a house tare developed over roughly 30 years.
The counter room is kept dim, lit largely by the bright flames of Kishu binchotan charcoal, a quiet, grown-up space for savoring skewers one at a time.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hitosara's chef profile confirms 小早川賢治 was born in 京都 in 1960, began cooking at 21 in a Western/French kitchen, ran a franchise at 27, and opened 馬耳東風 in 2006, choosing yakitori as a widely-loved genre; it details the 鶏ねぎ (圧倒的なボリューム, 30-year 秘伝のタレ), kimo, and せせり finished with 宮古島の塩, grilled to order over 備長炭. Gnavi/search confirm the Muromachi-dori location and the dim, Kishu-binchotan-lit adult counter atmosphere. The 'suguki welcome pickle' and specific ground-floor seat count were unverifiable and removed.

cultural_comfort.note: A popular, reservation-recommended Japanese yakitori counter where walk-ins are often turned away and menus/reviews are in Japanese, so a foreign student should book ahead and be ready to order in basic Japanese.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a charcoal yakitori izakaya where skewers share a single grill and the menu leans heavily on soy-based seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free program, and cross-contamination on the shared grill should be assumed.
Even salt-only (塩) skewers, while reducing direct gluten exposure, cannot be considered celiac-safe given the shared grill surface and the pervasive use of soy sauce and tare nearby.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori glaze (tare) and soy-based seasonings are central to the menu, and most Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat. Tare skewers should be treated as containing gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a chicken-focused yakitori restaurant; there is no vegan program and even non-meat items (such as grilled vegetables) are cooked on the same charcoal grill used for chicken, so cross-contact with animal products should be assumed.

positives.text: Chicken is sourced fresh daily and skewers are prepared and grilled to order over binchotan charcoal, so guests can order item by item and ask about preparation; salt (塩) seasoning is available as an alternative to tare for those wanting to reduce soy/wheat exposure, though it is not gluten-free.

places.name: くちばしモダン (Kuchibashi Modan)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a yakitori (grilled chicken skewer) restaurant; standard yakitori preparation relies on a soy-sauce-and-wheat-based tare glaze, and soy sauce itself typically contains wheat. Dashi and soy are heavily used. No gluten-free accommodation is documented on any page fetched. A celiac diner should not assume safety here.
places.vegan_detail: Poultry-focused omakase yakitori counter using 京赤地鶏 and フレッシュなひね鶏. No vegan or vegetarian menu items are documented on any source fetched (Tabelog, Gnavi, Hitosara). A plant-based diner is not accommodated. (Menu review: 0 vegan meals; 5 vegan items but no full main.)
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri–Sun 18:00–00:00 (last entry 22:00); closed Wed and Thu
places.neighborhood: Shimogyō-ku, Nagahara-chō (Shijō-Kawaramachi, Ayanokōji; 5 min walk from Kawaramachi Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single-location, owner-operated yakitori counter. Descriptions reference "店主こだわりの串" and "大将が丁寧に焼き上げる" (the master/owner grills at the counter). No evidence of chain or multi-branch group affiliation found across Tabelog, Gnavi, Hitosara, or Ikyu. Classified as independent_solo on

chef_bio.background: Kuchibashi Modan opened in August 2013 on Nagahara-cho in the heart of Kyoto's Shijo-Kawaramachi district, a short walk from Kawaramachi Station. The owner came up through traditional Japanese-cuisine (washoku) kitchens before striking out with this charcoal yakitori counter, and that grounding shows from the very first dish: even the otoshi (opening small plate) is made with care. The result is a stylish, almost wine-bar feel where you can settle in casually at the counter or a table and watch the skewers cook over binchotan charcoal.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hospitality-forward and detail-driven: regulars note that the owner and staff put real care into both the cooking and the welcome, and that the meticulousness of a Japanese-cuisine background carries through every plate.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen is built around 'hinedori' (mature laying hens) prized for their deeper flavor and firmer, more satisfying bite than young chicken. It is served as a single omakase course (around seven grilled items plus a few small plates and a finishing dish), running through cuts like seseri (neck), negima, tebakawa (wing skin) and jidori thigh. The signature is the tsukune (chicken meatball), browned and fragrant on the outside, fluffy within, rolled through a rich egg yolk and brightened with grated daikon. Raw chicken preparations, including chicken sashimi, round out the experience.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe a tsukune with a fragrant browned surface and fluffy center, eaten by rolling it through a rich egg yolk and refreshing grated daikon.
The owner trained in traditional Japanese cuisine, and diners note that even the opening otoshi plate is full of care; the skewers are grilled over charcoal in a stylish, bar-like room.
The course is described as a 'beautifully composed yakitori course where the flavor of hinedori (mature hen) stands out,' and the shop is a repeat Tabelog 'Yakitori WEST Top 100' selection.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog: opened 7 Aug 2013 in Shimogyo-ku (Nagahara-cho), ~26 seats, omakase-only ~6,600 yen with 7 grilled items + 3 sides + finish, hinedori focus, Yakitori WEST Top 100 (2022-2025). Hitosara review chorus: owner trained at a washoku restaurant, careful otoshi, tsukune with rich yolk and grated daikon, chicken sashimi available.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, Japanese-language counter yakitori spot serving a chef's-choice (omakase) style with no documented English support or English reviews, so a foreign student would be most comfortable bringing a Japanese-speaking friend or guide and using a translation app.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a charcoal yakitori (grilled chicken skewer) restaurant. Standard yakitori is glazed with a tare made from soy sauce and mirin, and most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the skewers and sauces should be assumed to contain gluten. No gluten-free preparation is documented on any source.
Raw chicken preparations (chicken sashimi, tori no otsukuri) are typically served with soy-based dipping sauces, another likely source of wheat-containing soy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce and tare are central to a yakitori menu like this one, and most Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat. A celiac diner should not assume any skewer, dipping sauce, or finishing dish is wheat-free here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a chicken-focused omakase counter (skewers, raw chicken, meatballs); the menu is built entirely around chicken with no vegan course documented. Shared charcoal grill and dashi/soy seasonings make cross-contact with animal products unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant asks guests to share any allergies, disliked ingredients, or special requests at the time of reservation, so it is worth contacting them directly in advance to discuss needs.

positives.text: Reservations invite advance notice of allergies and dislikes, and reviewers consistently praise the owner and staff for attentive, careful hospitality, suggesting they will engage seriously if you raise dietary concerns ahead of time.

places.name: 鳥さき (Torisaki)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Yakitori is glazed/seasoned with tare (a soy-sauce-based sauce containing wheat) and the omakase relies on soy and dashi-based seasonings; cross-contact on shared grill is likely. Gluten-free cannot be assured. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: A reservation-only chicken yakitori omakase house; the entire fixed course centers on chicken and other animal products with no vegan course. Not suitable for vegans. (Menu review: 1 vegan meal.)
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku (2 min walk from Karasuma-Oike Stn; Oshikōji-dōri, east of Muromachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Single Kyoto location opened Nov 2019. It is a 暖簾分け (noren-wake / independent spinoff) of 鳥しき in Tokyo Meguro — the chef trained there before opening his own independently-operated shop. It is not a corporate chain or multi-outlet brand owned by one company, so treated as an inde

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (Nakagyo Ward, near Karasuma Oike Station)
chef_bio.background: Tori Saki is the Kyoto yakitori counter of chef Tomoki Hata, a Kyoto native born in 1983. Hata entered the world of yakitori at 19, and later moved to Tokyo alone to apprentice for three years under Yoshiteru Ikegawa at the Michelin-starred Meguro restaurant Tori Shiki, one of the most respected yakitori houses in Japan. He brought that technique home, opening Tori Saki in November 2019 inside a renovated Kyoto townhouse (machiya) with a counter seating about fifteen, plus a small private room, where guests watch the grilling up close.
