The room is intimate and reservation-only, and the cooking centers on a single hearth of Kishu binchotan charcoal.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on fire: maximizing each ingredient through strong, close-flame grilling and careful, skewer-by-skewer heat control, in the tradition Hata trained in.
chef_bio.specialty: Chicken, cooked over fierce, close binchotan heat in the high-flame style Hata learned at Tori Shiki, which sears the surface fast while keeping the inside juicy. The omakase walks through the bird cut by cut, finished in salt or tare depending on the piece, with skewers such as kashiwa (thigh), seseri (neck), sunagimo (gizzard), tsukune, and grilled vegetables like shiitake. The meal opens with pickles and closes with a rice bowl, with chicken soboro-don and oyakodon appearing at the finish.

anecdotes.text: Hata started in yakitori at age 19 and later apprenticed for three years under Yoshiteru Ikegawa at the Michelin-starred Tori Shiki in Meguro, Tokyo, before returning to his hometown of Kyoto to open his own counter.
Tori Saki opened in 2019 in a renovated Kyoto machiya, working from a single hearth of Kishu binchotan charcoal and the high-heat grilling method carried over from Tori Shiki.
Diners describe the kitchen's hallmark as exceptional yakitori grilled over close, strong heat, with each skewer landing at a consistent, careful level of doneness.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog (EN): chef-owner Tomoki Hata, born 1983 in Kyoto, entered yakitori at 19, moved to Tokyo to apprentice 3 years under Yoshiteru Ikegawa at 1-star Tori Shiki (Meguro), opened Tori Saki in Kyoto in 2019; counter for ~15 plus a 4-person private room; grilled-chicken omakase with rice-bowl finishes; multiple Tabelog Award/Top-100 selections.

cultural_comfort.note: This intimate reservation-only counter omakase runs in Japanese, so a foreign student should ideally come with a Japanese-speaking friend or some Japanese ability.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a single-counter yakitori omakase where every skewer is grilled on the same shared binchotan hearth. Both salt and tare (a wheat-containing soy-based sauce) skewers come off the same grill, so cross-contact is effectively unavoidable. Gluten-free cannot be assured and the meal is not celiac-safe.

The set is a fixed omakase that moves cut by cut through the bird and finishes with rice bowls such as soboro-don and oyakodon, leaving little room to remove gluten-containing components without unraveling the course.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Many skewers and the finishing rice bowls are seasoned with tare and soy-based sauces; standard Japanese soy sauce and yakitori tare contain wheat. The omakase leans on these soy- and dashi-based seasonings throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a dedicated chicken restaurant; the omakase is built around chicken skewers, chicken soup, and egg-and-chicken rice bowls, with vegetables grilled on the same chicken-fat-coated grill. There is no vegan or vegetarian path through the course.

places.name: 京都焙煎屋 (Kyoto Baisenya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature tsukemen noodles contain wheat, and wheat is explicitly listed as an allergen for the dish. Whether the soy sauce/seasonings used are wheat-free is not confirmed. A celiac or gluten-avoiding diner should not rely on this venue without direct confirmation; the noodle dish is the only food item offered.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. The signature dish is vegan tsukemen (dipping noodles) served with a drink (drip coffee), and the menu contains no animal ingredients. The soup is built on a donko (dried shiitake) and kombu dashi with a soy-milk base, so the broth itself is plant-based with no fish dashi. Offered in limited quantities, reservation-only.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-15:00, reservation-only (lunch strictly by pre-booking). Specific open days are not published; hours are irregular and depend on accepted reservations.
places.neighborhood: Minami Ward, just east of To-ji Temple (To-ji Higashimon / e
places.cuisine: Vegan ramen / tsukemen and self-roasted coff
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] A small café near the To-ji World Heritage Temple that doubles as a coffee roaster. The name 焙煎屋 ("the roastery") reflects that the staff directly import green coffee beans from overseas and roast them in-house. The shop previously built a following for its an

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, near the To-ji Temple east gate in Minami Ward
chef_bio.background: A small café near the To-ji World Heritage Temple that doubles as a coffee roaster. The name 焙煎屋 ("the roastery") reflects that the staff directly import green coffee beans from overseas and roast them in-house. The shop previously built a following for its animal-free vegan ramen, then reopened in June 2023 as a reservation-only venue with a renewed vegan tsukemen.
chef_bio.philosophy: An entirely animal-free kitchen offering food that is gentle on the body, paired with carefully self-roasted coffee; deliberately small-batch and by-reservation so each bowl is made with care.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan tsukemen (dipping noodles) in a soy-milk-based broth made with donko (dried shiitake) and kombu dashi, served as a set with a drink such as their own drip-roasted coffee.

anecdotes.text: Reopened in June 2023 as a reservation-only spot, reviving its previously popular animal-free vegan ramen as a renewed vegan tsukemen.
The shop directly imports green coffee beans from overseas and the staff handle the bean selection and roasting themselves, hence the name 'Baisenya' (the roastery).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Vegemap: near To-ji (58 Toji-higashi-monzen-cho, Minami-ku), 12-15:00 reservation-only from June 2023, vegan tsukemen + drink set (1,500 yen) in a soy-milk base with donko and kombu broth; a renewal of the shop's earlier animal-free vegan ramen; reservations web-only.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature and essentially only food dish is wheat noodle tsukemen; wheat is present in the kitchen, so any gluten-free request cannot be considered safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Whether seasonings or any soy sauce in the broth are wheat-free is not stated; wheat is listed as an allergen for the tsukemen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is vegan with no animal ingredients, so risk of cross-contact with meat, fish, dairy or egg is effectively eliminated. The broth uses donko (dried shiitake) and kombu dashi with soy milk, not fish dashi.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen, so vegan diners do not need to worry about hidden animal ingredients or shared meat/fish surfaces.
Plant-based broth using shiitake and kombu dashi instead of fish/bonito dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run vegan café where reservations are made through an online booking system (the shop does not take phone bookings), so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, but the all-vegan single-dish format makes ordering simple.

noodles.notes: Wheat noodles (the dish is documented as containing wheat), so not gluten-free. Served tsukemen-style for dipping into the soy-milk-and-mushroom dipping broth. Exact thickness, shape and hydration are not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Vegan Tsukemen (ヴィーガンつけ麺) - a fully plant-based dipping noodle, served by reservation only and bundled with a cup of the cafe's own drip coffee. The dipping broth is built on a soy-milk base layered with donko (dried shiitake) and kombu dashi.

gf.note: The dish is explicitly documented as containing wheat; the noodles are standard wheat noodles. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented, so it is not celiac-safe. Diners with allergies are asked to inform staff when reserving.

vegan.note: This is the shop's flagship: a fully animal-free vegan tsukemen with a broth of soy milk, donko (dried shiitake) and kombu - no katsuo/fish dashi, no pork, no animal tare or fat. The entire cafe menu is vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, original vegan bowl with no traditional regional lineage - its soul is a velvety soy-milk dipping broth deepened with the dark, almost meaty umami of donko dried shiitake and the oceanic minerality of kombu, a plant-kingdom answer to the richness usually coaxed from pork or chicken. Tucked into a self-roasting coffee cafe a few steps from To-ji, it pairs the bowl with a cup of drip coffee, a quietly distinctive Kyoto take on what tsukemen can be without a single animal product.

places.highlight: A modern, original vegan bowl with no traditional regional lineage - its soul is a velvety soy-milk dipping broth deepened with the dark, almost meaty umami of donko dried shiitake and the oceanic minerality of kombu, a plant-kingdom answer to the richness usually coaxed from pork or chicken. Tucked into a self-roasting coffee cafe a few steps from To-ji, it pairs the bowl with a cup of drip coffee, a quietly distinctive Kyoto take on what tsukemen can be without a single animal product.

places.name: 菜食料理エッフェ (Saishoku Ryori F (Effe))
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. They avoid regular wheat flour and instead use rice flour for breads and sweets, but their pasta is made from ancient-grain/spelt (spelled) flour, which DOES contain gluten and is unsafe for celiacs. Gluten-free pasta can be accommodated only when requested in advance with your reservation (multiple diners confirmed GF accommodation when booked a few days ahead). If you are gluten-free, you must notify the restaurant directly when reserving; no cross-contamination protocol is published, so ask about shared cookware. No published detail on whether soy sauce used is wheat-free.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. The couple who run it exclude all meat, fish, dairy, eggs and refined white sugar from every dish. Vegetables are sourced from trusted farmers. Note: they explicitly state they do NOT cater to "Eastern-style" veganism that excludes the five pungent spices (garlic/onion etc.), nor do they accommodate vegetable allergies, so confirm if you avoid alliums.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 18:00-21:00 (last entry 19:00, L.O. 19:30). Open Wed-Sun. Closed Mon & Tue plus irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Shimogyo Ward, near Karasuma-Gojo; a few minutes' walk from
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Saishoku Ryori F (Effe) is a small plant-based restaurant run by a couple in a calm, traditional Kyoto machiya (townhouse) near Karasuma-Gojo. They opened in 2018, initially known for gluten-free and vegan pasta, and became a fully vegan kitchen in 2020.

chef_bio.background: Saishoku Ryori F (Effe) is a small plant-based restaurant run by a couple in a calm, traditional Kyoto machiya (townhouse) near Karasuma-Gojo. They opened in 2018, initially known for gluten-free and vegan pasta, and became a fully vegan kitchen in 2020.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners believe food is essential to health and a fulfilling daily life, emphasizing the link between good nutrition and wellbeing. They buy their vegetables from trusted farmers and aim to let each ingredient's natural flavor shine, so dishes feel 'alive.'
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal vegetable courses with a European bent - handmade vegan cheeses, ancient-grain/spelt spaghetti, lentil patties and burgers, and dishes such as roasted black carrots with basil sauce, shiitake isobe-age, egg-free zucchini quiche, and soy-meat 'beef' stroganoff. Breads and sweets are made with rice flour and no refined sugar.

anecdotes.text: The name 'F (Effe)' is simply the Italian pronunciation of the letter F.

Diners praise the couple's handmade vegan cheeses and the multi-course meals with French and Italian influences; reviewers note the chef coaxes natural flavor from vegetables so the food feels 'alive.'
The tiny machiya seats only a handful of guests (two 4-tops and three 2-tops) and is so popular it can fill within about 20 minutes of the lunch opening.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: eight-trees: couple-run vegan cafe near Gojo Station (Karasuma line, Shimogyo-ku), opened 2018, fully vegan from 2020; no meat/fish/dairy/egg/refined sugar; GF pasta and rice-flour breads/sweets; seating two 4-tops + three 2-tops, fills ~20 min after lunch open; spelt pasta set; food described as uniquely 'alive.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: No cross-contamination protocol is published. The kitchen makes pasta from ancient-grain/spelt flour (gluten-containing) alongside rice-flour items, so shared surfaces/cookware are a risk for celiacs; ask directly when reserving.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No published information on whether the soy sauce used is wheat-free (tamari) - confirm directly if you are gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fully vegan kitchen with no meat, fish, dairy or eggs on the premises, so animal cross-contact is essentially eliminated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff ask each guest about allergies and accommodate various dietary needs when arranged in advance.
The restaurant explicitly states it does NOT accommodate vegetable allergies, and does not cater to allium-free ('five pungent spices') veganism.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based menu - no meat, fish, dairy, eggs or refined white sugar used in any dish.
Regular wheat flour is avoided; breads and sweets are made with rice flour, and gluten-free pasta can be provided when requested ahead of time.
Gluten-free requests have been successfully accommodated when reserved a few days in advance, per diner reviews.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available, but the staff speak only Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases (and booking ahead) will make the visit much smoother.

places.name: ヴィーガンラーメン ウズ キョウト (Vegan Ramen UZU KYOTO)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The noodles in all ramen dishes are made from Hokkaido wheat flour and organic whole-grain flour from Aya, Miyazaki — so they contain wheat/gluten. The signature ramen also uses soy sauce; the source pages do not state whether a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce is used, so a celiac/gluten-sensitive diner should assume standard wheat-containing soy sauce and confirm directly. No dedicated gluten-free menu or GF noodle option was found.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely plant-based kitchen — every dish is vegan. The shared broth used across all ramen is a 12-hour infusion of Rausu kelp (kombu), shiitake mushrooms and vegetables, with no meat, fish or animal dashi. The flagship is the UZU Signature Course featuring a choice of Soy Sauce & Fermented Mugwort ramen or Spicy Miso & Sake Kasu ramen, plus vegan appetizers and dessert. Because the entire venue is vegan, there is no animal-product cross-contact in the kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Fri, Sat, Sun & public holidays: 12:00-15:00 (L.O. 13:30) and 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Tue/Wed/Thu.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Gosho-Minami area; ~6 min walk from Kyoto Shiy
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Vegan Ramen UZU is a plant-based ramen and course-dining restaurant set inside an immersive art space. teamLab's digital artwork 'Reversible Rotation' is projected on the wall and reflected across a polished black communal table, so guests eat surrounded by mo

chef_bio.origin: Opened in 2020 in the Gosho-Minami area of central Kyoto, near the Imperial Palace.
chef_bio.background: Vegan Ramen UZU is a plant-based ramen and course-dining restaurant set inside an immersive art space. teamLab's digital artwork 'Reversible Rotation' is projected around a polished black communal table, so guests eat surrounded by moving art on both the wall and the reflective tabletop. It became the first ramen restaurant in the world to receive a Michelin Green Star and has also been a Michelin Bib Gourmand selection.
chef_bio.philosophy: An 'earth-friendly dining' mission — the space is designed to let people reflect on the acts of eating and living, treating a fully plant-based meal as both an environmental act and an artistic experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan ramen built on a 12-hour broth of Rausu kelp, shiitake mushrooms and vegetables, with noodles made from Hokkaido flour and organic whole-grain flour from Aya, Miyazaki. The signature course offers a choice of Soy Sauce & Fermented Mugwort ramen or Spicy Miso & Sake Kasu ramen, alongside vegan appetizers and dessert.

anecdotes.text: Recognized as the first ramen restaurant in the world to earn a Michelin Green Star, awarded for sustainability, and a Bib Gourmand selection.
Diners are seated at one large communal table wrapped around teamLab's 'Reversible Rotation' artwork, so the artwork appears on both the wall and the reflective tabletop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official (EN): opened 2020 in Gosho-Minami, Kyoto; teamLab 'Reversible Rotation' around a polished black table; world's first Green Star in the ramen category plus a Bib Gourmand (2026 Kyoto-Osaka guide); all-ramen broth of Rausu kelp, shiitake and vegetables steeped 12 hours; noodles from Hokkaido flour and organic whole-grain flour from Aya, Miyazaki; Soy Sauce & Fermented Mugwort and Spicy Miso & Sake Kasu ramens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ramen noodles are made with wheat flour (Hokkaido flour plus organic whole-grain flour from Aya, Miyazaki), so the kitchen routinely handles wheat/gluten and is not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen uses soy sauce; sources do not specify whether it is wheat-free (tamari), so assume standard wheat-containing soy sauce unless confirmed with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire restaurant is fully vegan, so there is no meat, fish or dairy in the kitchen and no animal-product cross-contact. The broth uses kelp and shiitake rather than animal dashi.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reviewers describe staff as friendly and knowledgeable, explaining dishes and even offering descriptions in other languages; no formal allergy protocol is documented, so confirm specific allergen needs directly.

positives.text: Fully plant-based kitchen means vegans can order anything on the menu with confidence and no animal cross-contact.
Broth is a clean 12-hour kelp, shiitake and vegetable infusion with no fish or meat dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: English-friendly: staff are known to explain the course in multiple languages and the online booking platform is in English, so a foreign student can reserve and dine comfortably.

noodles.notes: Made with wheat flour from Hokkaido and organic whole-grain flour from Aya, Miyazaki, lending an earthy, hearty character. Exact thickness/shape not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Soy Sauce & Fermented Mugwort ramen (part of the UZU Signature Course; a Spicy Miso & Sake-kasu bowl is the alternate choice)

gf.note: Noodles are made from wheat flour (Hokkaido + organic whole-grain), so the standard bowls are not gluten-free; the kitchen runs a fully curated plant-based course and may accommodate dietary needs, so inquire when reserving.

vegan.note: Fully plant-based shop. The broth is a clear dashi steeped 12 hours from Rausu kombu, shiitake and vegetables with no katsuo or animal products; all bowls, tare, toppings and the full course are vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, original vegan creation rather than any regional lineage: the soul lives in a crystal-clear dashi coaxed over twelve slow hours from Rausu kelp, shiitake and vegetables, proving that umami depth can come entirely from the plant kingdom. The signature soy-and-fermented-mugwort bowl, dressed with oven-roasted tomato and crisp grilled maitake, reads like Kyoto kaiseki restraint translated into a noodle bowl.

places.highlight: A modern, original vegan creation rather than any regional lineage: the soul lives in a crystal-clear dashi coaxed over twelve slow hours from Rausu kelp, shiitake and vegetables, proving that umami depth can come entirely from the plant kingdom. The signature soy-and-fermented-mugwort bowl, dressed with oven-roasted tomato and crisp grilled maitake, reads like Kyoto kaiseki restraint translated into a noodle bowl.

places.name: 菜食光兎舎 (Saisyoku KOUSAGISHA)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The set plate leans heavily on soy-based and seasoned items (miso, soy milk, tofu, simmered/seasoned vegetables) where Japanese soy sauce and seasonings typically contain wheat, so gluten is very likely present and not separately controlled. There is no mention of wheat-free soy sauce, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocols. A celiac/GF diner should contact the restaurant directly in advance; the menu is largely Japanese-only, so communication may require Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. The single signature offering is a one-plate lunch (around 2,100-2,500 yen) of 10+ small seasonal dishes containing no meat, fish, eggs or dairy. Components reported include miso potage made with soy milk, homemade mugwort/koya (freeze-dried) tofu, simmered Oki seaweed, mountain vegetables and pickles, and a fresh-fruit dessert. Vegetables are pesticide-free/reduced-pesticide and sourced from trusted farmers, largely in northern Kyoto Prefecture. The owner is open about not being dogmatic ("enjoy delicious vegetables without becoming too strict"), but the dishes themselves are reported as entirely plant-based.
places.hours_raw: Wed-Sat 11:30-15:00 (open until sold out; food last order ~14:30-15:00). Closed Mon, Tue, Sun.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo-ku, near Ginkakuji (Silver Pavilion) along Shirakawa-d
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Saisyoku Kousagisha is a small, quietly hidden vegan lunch room on the second floor of a craftsman-built wooden building near Ginkakuji, with an art gallery run by the owner's brother on the ground floor. Chef Yuki Kato had been cooking plant-based food for mo

chef_bio.background: Saisyoku Kousagisha is a small, quietly hidden vegan lunch room on the second floor of a craftsman-built wooden building in the Jodo-ji area near Ginkakuji, with an art gallery run by the owner's younger brother on the ground floor. Chef Yuki Kato had long cooked plant-based food before opening here; he is connected to the traditional yuba (tofu-skin) restaurant Hale in Kyoto's Nishiki Market.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kato wants guests to feel the simple joy of eating genuinely delicious vegetables rather than approaching veganism or organic eating as a strict rulebook. He emphasizes that carefully grown pesticide-free vegetables can be beautiful in their own right, letting the natural flavor of each seasonal ingredient lead the plate.
chef_bio.specialty: A single, beautifully composed vegan plate lunch of 10+ seasonal small dishes built around pesticide-free vegetables and wild mountain plants from trusted farmers, with touches like homemade mugwort tofu and simmered Oki seaweed.

anecdotes.text: The dining room was built with a craftsman's care, including a single-plank table by woodworker Kawai Yu and sukiya-style carpentry, and looks out toward the Daimonji mountain.
There is no obvious sign for the cafe, so even many locals walk past the building without realizing a restaurant is inside.
Kousagisha is operated by the owner-chef connected to the yuba-don restaurant Hale in Nishiki Market.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Rurubu: vegan cafe by chef 加藤祐基 (Yuki Kato) on the 2nd floor of a wooden building in Jodo-ji near Ginkakuji, brother's art gallery below; single-plank table by woodworker Kawai Yu, sukiya carpentry, view of the Daimonji 'dai'; pesticide-free vegetables from trusted farmers; signature 2,500-yen plate with 10+ vegetable dishes, homemade mugwort tofu and Oki seaweed. HappyCow: run by the owner-chef of the yuba-don restaurant Hale in Nishiki Market.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling or cross-contamination protocols are documented; the seasoned, soy-based dishes most likely contain wheat-derived soy sauce/seasonings, so gluten should be assumed present.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce and miso-based seasonings are used in the plate; there is no mention of wheat-free tamari, so wheat is likely present in the seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely plant-based, so there is no meat, fish, or egg cross-contact in the dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reservations are required and dishes are made to order, so allergy needs are best raised in advance by phone or email; however, the menu is mainly Japanese-only and only a little English is spoken, which can make detailed allergy communication harder.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen with no animal products used, removing any risk of meat, fish, egg, or dairy cross-contact.
Vegetables are pesticide-free / reduced-pesticide and sourced from trusted local farmers, with a strong seasonal, ingredient-led focus.

cultural_comfort.note: Staff are friendly and a little English is spoken, but the food menu is mainly in Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases (and an advance reservation) will make your visit smoother.

places.name: alt. coffee roasters（オルト コーヒー ロースターズ） (alt. coffee roasters)
places.gf_detail: Multiple sources describe the cafe as offering gluten-free options, and several items (such as certain cakes and ice cream) are noted as gluten-free. However it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and not all items are GF (e.g. bagel sandwiches contain wheat). There is no published information on soy sauce wheat content, dedicated fryers, or flour cross-contamination, so a strictly gluten-free diner should confirm specific items with staff on the day. GF travelers are advised to ask which sweets are wheat-free.
places.vegan_detail: The Nijo Castle (Nakagyo) location is fully plant-based: the kitchen uses no milk, eggs or other dairy. Cow's milk was replaced with organic oat milk (Minor Figures) for lattes and other drinks, and the sweets and food are all vegan. Owner Chihiro Nakamura, a certified nutritionist, also avoids white sugar (using amazake / brown sugar / fermented seasonings). Signature items include a vegan "cheesecake" (brown sugar, homemade miso, fermented yuzu-lemon sauce, purple carrot), chocolate cake, seasonal-fruit cakes, and open bagel sandwiches with seasonal vegetables. Note: a separate sister branch in Yawata ("Iwashimizu") reportedly uses egg in an omelet-rice dish, but the Nijo location matching this listing is described as entirely vegan. Staff invite guests to mention vegan needs and allergies when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–18:00 (last order 17:45); irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, just south of Nijo Castle and Shinsen-en garde
places.cuisine: Specialty light-roast coffee cafe with vegan
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] alt. coffee roasters opened in 2019 in front of Nijo Castle as one of Kyoto's early specialty light-roast roastery cafes. Owner-roaster Chihiro Nakamura came to coffee by an unusual route: a former childcare/nursery teacher and certified nutritionist, she had

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan; concept rooted in the owner's experience with Melbourne's specialty coffee scene
chef_bio.background: alt. coffee roasters opened in 2019 in front of Nijo Castle as one of Kyoto's early specialty light-roast roastery cafes. Owner-roaster Chihiro Nakamura came to coffee by an unusual route: a former childcare/nursery teacher and certified nutritionist, she had actually disliked coffee until she tasted specialty light-roast coffee in Melbourne, Australia, which changed her mind and inspired her to open her own shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Serve food and drink that is delicious and kind to the body while ensuring everyone in the supply chain is treated fairly. The shop sources ethically-produced, environmentally-conscious beans, went fully vegan by swapping cow's milk for organic oat milk, and avoids white sugar in its sweets.
chef_bio.specialty: Light-roast single-origin specialty coffee served in wine glasses to highlight its fruit-like character, plus an entirely plant-based food menu: vegan 'cheesecake' (brown sugar, homemade miso, fermented yuzu-lemon, purple carrot), chocolate cake, seasonal-fruit cakes, and open bagel sandwiches with seasonal vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The owner originally disliked coffee and only became passionate about it after encountering specialty light-roast coffee in Melbourne, which led her to open one of Kyoto's first dedicated light-roast roastery cafes.
The cafe went fully vegan by switching from cow's milk to organic barista oat milk (Minor Figures), and serves coffee in wine glasses so guests can focus on its changing aroma and fruity flavor.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: lifehugger: owner Chihiro Nakamura, former childcare-facility teacher and certified nutritionist, disliked coffee until tasting specialty light-roast coffee during her time in Melbourne; opened alt. coffee roasters near Nijo Castle; light-roast single-origins served in wine glasses; fully plant-based, no dairy/egg/white sugar; fair-trade beans. zenbird: switched cow's milk to organic Minor Figures oat milk to go fully vegan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No published information on flour/wheat cross-contamination in the kitchen; gluten-containing items (e.g. bagel sandwiches) are made on-site alongside GF sweets, so a strict GF diner should confirm specific items with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The Nijo/Nakagyo kitchen is fully plant-based with no milk, eggs or dairy used, which greatly reduces the risk of animal-product cross-contact.
Cow's milk was eliminated and replaced with organic oat milk for all drinks, so even lattes and desserts are dairy-free.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop asks guests to tell staff about vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies before ordering so a suitable item can be chosen.
Staff speak English, making it easier for foreign visitors to discuss ingredients and allergies.

positives.text: Entirely vegan menu run by a nutritionist owner, with sweets made without white sugar using amazake, brown sugar and fermented seasonings.
Gluten-free options are available, with several cakes and ice cream noted as gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: The cafe has English-speaking staff and is used to foreign vegan visitors, so a foreign student can comfortably order and ask about ingredients in English.

places.name: 素食カフェ Ren 四条大宮店 (Soshoku Cafe Ren Shijo Omiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. The kitchen leans heavily on soy/wheat-based mock meats ("vegan eel," fried soy-meat), tempura, curry bun and apple cake, all of which typically contain wheat. Brown rice and the herbal medicinal soup are served, which may be safer bases, but standard Japanese soy sauce (usually wheat-containing) is likely used in seasoning. A gluten-free diner should ask directly about each dish; assume wheat is present unless confirmed otherwise.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is plant-based: the kitchen uses no meat, fish, eggs or dairy at all. It follows Taiwanese Buddhist "su si" (素食/精進料理) principles, which also exclude the five pungent vegetables (onion, garlic, leek, green onion, chives). This means the food is effectively vegan and additionally allium-free, which is helpful for diners avoiding onion/garlic. Signature plant-based dishes include "vegan eel" made from tofu, a daily vegetarian set meal with multiple mains and sides, yakuzen (medicinal) curry, and fried soy-meat with mugwort and lotus root. Because soy-meat (mock meat) and tempura/fried items are central to the menu, dishes are flavorful and clearly meat-free, but see GF notes for wheat caution.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-20:00 (last order 20:00); closed Sundays and holidays. (One source lists service until 21:00 with last order 20:00.)
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Mibu area; about a 4-minute walk west of Hanky
places.cuisine: Taiwanese Buddhist-style vegetarian · Shojin (Buddhist vegetarian cuisine)
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Soshoku Cafe Ren is run by a Taiwanese owner who emigrated to Japan at age 22. After recovering from a serious liver illness, she embraced vegetarianism and Taiwanese Buddhist 'su si' cooking, eventually opening Ren to share that cuisine. The brand has grown t

chef_bio.background: Soshoku Cafe Ren is run by a Taiwanese woman who came to Japan at age 22 for language study and settled after marrying a Japanese husband. After recovering from a serious liver illness that required major surgery, she embraced vegetarianism and Taiwanese Buddhist 'su si' cooking, eventually opening Ren to share that cuisine. The brand has grown to three locations across Kyoto plus one in Wakayama, with this Shijo-Omiya branch set in a renovated machiya townhouse on a quiet residential lane.
chef_bio.philosophy: A strict no-animal-products ethos (no meat, fish, eggs or dairy) rooted in Buddhist vegetarian practice, also excluding the five pungent vegetables, paired with a wish to share the wonder of vegetarian food with everyone from children to the elderly, and a stated commitment to animal welfare and the environment. Vegetables are grown without pesticides or chemical fertilizers in Wakayama and Kyoto.
chef_bio.specialty: Taiwanese Buddhist-style vegetarian cuisine: a daily set meal with two mains, five sides, brown rice (mixed with black rice) and an herbal medicinal soup simmered from more than 15 Chinese herbs for about two hours; yakuzen (medicinal) curry; and playful mock dishes such as 'vegan eel,' fried soy-meat with mugwort and lotus root, and pumpkin tempura.

anecdotes.text: The owner turned to vegetarian cooking after recovering from a serious liver disease, and now wants 'everyone from children to the elderly to know the wonderfulness of Buddhist vegetarian cuisine.'
The signature medicinal soup is made by slowly simmering more than 15 kinds of Chinese herbs for around two hours, and the produce is grown pesticide-free in Wakayama and Kyoto.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: cafe-ren.therestaurant.jp: owner is a Taiwanese woman who came to Japan at 22 for language study, married a Japanese man, recovered from a severe liver disease/major surgery, then became vegetarian (25 years) and opened Ren to spread Buddhist vegetarian cuisine 'from children to the elderly.' kyoto-kanko: Shijo-Omiya machiya branch, su-si (no five pungent vegetables), 15+ herb soup simmered ~2 hrs, pesticide-free Wakayama/Kyoto vegetables, two-mains/five-sides brown-rice set, one Wakayama + three Kyoto shops.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around soy-meat mock dishes, tempura and baked goods (curry bun, apple cake) that typically contain wheat, so shared frying and wheat cross-contact are likely; no gluten-free handling is advertised.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasoning likely uses standard Japanese soy sauce (usually wheat-containing); the sources do not confirm a wheat-free tamari, so a gluten-free diner should ask.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fully plant-based, using no meat, fish, eggs or dairy at all, which removes animal cross-contact risk for vegan diners.

positives.text: Completely animal-product-free kitchen following Buddhist 'su si' principles, with food also free of the five pungent vegetables (onion, garlic, leek, green onion, chives) and made from pesticide-free, self-grown produce.
Counter seating makes it easy and comfortable for a solo vegan/vegetarian traveler to dine.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, welcoming townhouse cafe with counter seating that is easy to visit solo; staff are used to vegetarian/vegan diners, though a little basic Japanese or a translation app helps since English support is not confirmed.

places.name: 素食カフェRen 堀川商店街店 (Soshoku Cafe Ren (Horikawa Shotengai bra)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published by the cafe or on listing sites. The menu leans heavily on wheat-based items — dumplings, gyoza, radish cakes, glutinous-rice dumplings and noodles — and Taiwanese mock meats often contain wheat gluten (seitan), so cross-contact with wheat is likely. Soy sauce is presumably standard (wheat-containing) but not confirmed. Gluten-sensitive diners should ask directly about each dish; nothing is confirmed safe. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based kitchen. The cafe states it uses no animal products whatsoever (no meat, fish, eggs or dairy) and follows Taiwanese Buddhist vegetarian (素食/精進) tradition. It also avoids the "five pungent vegetables" (onion, scallion, garlic, chives and related alliums), so dishes are free of onion and garlic. Mock-meat items (e.g. vegan "eel" rice bowl made from okara and seaweed, vegan takoyaki, dumplings, radish cakes) are made from plant ingredients. Everything on the menu is suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 12:00–20:00 (last order 19:00), closed Sundays (per official site). Tabelog lists 11:00–20:00, Mon–Sat.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo Ward, on Horikawa Shopping Street along Horikawa-dor
places.cuisine: Taiwanese Buddhist-style vegetarian · Shojin (Buddhist vegetarian cuisine)
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Soshoku Cafe Ren is a small, casual fully-vegan cafe tucked into the covered Horikawa Shopping Street in Kyoto's Kamigyo Ward, run by a Taiwanese owner who turned to vegetarian cooking after surviving a serious liver illness around 28 years ago. The food draws

chef_bio.background: Soshoku Cafe Ren is a small, casual fully-vegan cafe tucked into the covered Horikawa Shopping Street in Kyoto's Kamigyo Ward, run by a Taiwanese owner who turned to vegetarian cooking after surviving a serious liver illness around 28 years ago. The food draws on Taiwanese Buddhist vegetarian (素食) tradition.
chef_bio.philosophy: Strictly Buddhist vegetarian: no animal products and none of the 'five pungent vegetables' (onion, garlic, chives, scallions and related alliums). The owner wants everyone from children to the elderly to discover how wonderful and healthful Buddhist vegetarian cuisine can be, framing the kitchen as a wish for a peaceful world.
chef_bio.specialty: Taiwanese plant-based comfort food served food-stand style: vegan 'eel' rice bowl made from okara and seaweed, vegan takoyaki-style veggie balls, steamed dumplings (miso, curry, tofu), gyoza, radish cakes, glutinous-rice dumplings, hot pot, curries and noodles, plus soy-milk soft-serve ice cream. Soups are simmered for two-plus hours with 15+ medicinal herbs.

anecdotes.text: The owner adopted a vegetarian diet after recovering from a severe liver illness about 28 years ago, and now wants to share the value of Buddhist vegetarian cooking with people of all ages.
The menu is deliberately varied and snackable — vegan takoyaki, soy-milk ice cream, dumplings — so guests can graze as if visiting a food stand.
Every soup is made by simmering more than 15 medicinal herbs for over two hours, reflecting the cafe's medicinal/wellness focus.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: cafe-ren-horikawa.therestaurant.jp: Taiwanese owner came to Japan at 22, nearly died of a severe liver illness/surgery, has lived healthily as a vegetarian for 28 years; wants all ages to appreciate Buddhist vegetarian cuisine; no five pungent vegetables; soups of 15+ herbs simmered 2+ hrs; Taiwanese vegan snacks (vegan eel, takoyaki-style balls, soy-milk soft serve) in the Horikawa Shopping Street.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free information is published. The menu centers on wheat-based dumplings, gyoza, radish cakes, glutinous-rice dumplings and noodles, and Taiwanese mock meats commonly contain wheat gluten, so wheat cross-contact is likely. Gluten-sensitive diners should ask about each dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Whether the soy sauce is wheat-free is not stated anywhere; assume standard wheat-containing soy sauce until confirmed in person.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is fully plant-based with no meat, fish, eggs or dairy used at all, so there is no animal-product cross-contact — a strong positive for vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests are advised to inform staff of dietary restrictions and allergies before ordering; the owner/staff are reported as friendly and English-speaking, which helps with allergy questions.

positives.text: 100% vegan kitchen — no meat, fish, eggs or dairy anywhere on the menu, eliminating animal cross-contact entirely.
Allium-free cooking: no onion, garlic, scallion or chives are used (Buddhist 'five pungent vegetables' avoided), helpful for those sensitive to alliums.
Friendly, English-speaking owner/staff who serve a complimentary herbal soup, making it easy to discuss what's in each dish.

cultural_comfort.note: Reviewers repeatedly describe the owner and staff as friendly and English-speaking, so a foreign student can order comfortably in English.

places.name: 食堂 山小屋 (Shokudo Yamagoya)
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners. The fixed menu leans heavily on wheat: signature items include wheat gluten ("fu") and wheat-gluten katsu, plus tempura and vegetable pakora (battered/fried), all of which contain wheat flour. Japanese soy sauce (almost certainly wheat-containing) is used in the cooking. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and the single-set format leaves little room to adapt for celiac/GF needs. No information was found confirming any GF accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Effectively a fully vegan kitchen. There is a single fixed daily lunch set built around organic brown rice and local Uji vegetables, and reviewers confirm everything is vegan apart from one dessert (a cake) that is clearly labeled as containing egg and dairy. Savory dishes (tempeh, wheat-gluten "fu", tempura, vegetable pakora, miso soup, pickles) are plant-based. Because there is essentially one set menu, customization is very limited.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-16:00 (lunch only); open Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun; closed Wed & Thu. Cash only.
places.neighborhood: Uji (Uji-shi), near Byodoin Temple; about a 6-minute walk fr
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Shokudo Yamagoya is a husband-and-wife cafe in the old castle town of Uji, a short walk from the World Heritage Byodoin Temple. It occupies a converted Japanese townhouse that previously served as a glassworks and a dango (rice-dumpling) shop, now given a warm

chef_bio.background: Shokudo Yamagoya is a husband-and-wife cafe in the old town of Uji, a short walk from the World Heritage Byodoin Temple. It occupies a converted Japanese townhouse that previously served as a glassworks and a dango (rice-dumpling) shop, now given a warm, retro feel. Owner Kazuhiro Oji ran a record store for about 25 years, and the dining room is filled with his vinyl collection and self-roasted coffee; his wife Atsuko, who cooked in Tokyo restaurants and at Kyoto vegan establishments, runs the kitchen.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners describe their approach as 'treat each day with care,' favoring a simple 'one vegetable, one dish' honesty over elaborate technique, and cooking with organic produce sourced from nearby Uji-area farmers.
chef_bio.specialty: A single organic brown-rice vegan lunch set, with the rice (blended daily with ancient grains) as the star and the side dishes chosen to bring out its flavor: soft house-made tempeh, fragrant vegetable pakora, crisp fried wheat-gluten (fu), seasonal Uji vegetables, beans, miso soup, and pickles. Vegan cakes and tarts and home-roasted coffee round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: Kazuhiro spent roughly 25 years running a record store before opening the cafe, and guests eat to music he selects from his vinyl collection while he roasts the coffee himself.
The building was once a glassworks and later a dango shop before being remodeled into the current retro brown-rice cafe.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: vegewel: run by Kazuhiro Oji (応治和博) and Atsuko Oji (応治淳子) in Uji, ~6 min from Uji Station and ~10 min from Byodoin; building was formerly a glassworks then a dango shop; Kazuhiro ran a Shin-Kyogoku record store for 25 years and now curates vinyl and roasts coffee; Atsuko cooked at Tokyo restaurants and Kyoto vegan spots; ~1,100-yen brown-rice set (blended with ancient rice) with tempeh, fu, pakora and pesticide-free local vegetables.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is wheat-heavy (wheat gluten/fu, tempura, vegetable pakora), and fried items share oil, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable for a strict GF diner. No dedicated fryer is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese cooking using soy sauce; no wheat-free/tamari soy sauce is advertised, so assume the soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Reviewers report everything is vegan except one clearly-labeled cake containing egg and dairy; that single dairy/egg dessert is the only animal product in the room, so cross-contact with meat, fish or dashi is minimal.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Family-run with a single fixed set menu and no English menu or English-speaking staff, so accommodating specific allergies on the spot is limited; communicate needs in Japanese in advance.

positives.text: Essentially a fully plant-based kitchen built on organic local Uji vegetables and brown rice, so vegans can order the set with confidence and very low risk of animal-product cross-contact.
Uses organic produce from nearby farmers, house-made tempeh, and self-roasted coffee, with vegan cakes/tarts also available.

cultural_comfort.note: A small family-run spot with no English menu or English-speaking staff, so a few words of Japanese (or a translation app) will help, but the owners are warmly described as very kind and the single vegan set makes ordering simple.

places.name: 玄gen (Gen)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→dedicated): Entire product line 100% rice flour, GF & vegan — no wheat on premises. Multi-year JP own claim + independent JP bakery guide + 2026 blog confirms open. UPGRADE vs my English run (which had cut it to high for lacking English reviews). Dedicated. — Prior: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→high. Real whole-kitchen-GF takeout shop in Nishijin (no wheat on premises per own site: 'すべてグルテンフリー&ヴィーガン', 100% rice-flour bread), open — structurally low CC. But 'dedicated' fails: NO explicit celiac claim anywhere and NO independent celiac-grade corroboration (only HappyCow, a vegan directory; absent from every dedicated celiac Kyoto guide). Takeout-only, irregular hours weakens top-tier trust. — Prior note: A fully gluten-free, wheat-free kitchen: no wheat flour is used anywhere. Their signature bread is made from 100% rice flour with no binders, and all sweets are gluten-free. This is a dedicated GF environment rather than a mixed kitchen with GF options, so cross-contamination from wheat flour is minimal. The brand of soy sauce/seasoning is not specified online, so a celiac-level diner should still confirm the soy sauce is a wheat-free (tamari-style) one, as standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.
places.vegan_detail: Everything sold is 100% plant-based. The kitchen uses no animal products at all (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs), and also avoids chemical seasonings and refined white sugar. Menu includes Ayurvedic-style spice curries, bento boxes, salad wraps, quiche, tofu karaage, soups, 100% rice-flour breads, and desserts; pre-order vegan nigiri sushi is also available. Because the whole operation is vegan, there is no animal-based cross-contact in the kitchen. Note: dashi and the specific soy sauce used are not documented online; confirm directly if you need to verify the seasoning is also free of animal-derived katsuo (fish) dashi.
places.hours_raw: Official site: 11:00-20:00 daily with irregular closures. Some aggregator listings show 12:00-19:00, closed Sun & Mon. Hours vary; check their Instagram/business calendar before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo Ward (上京区), about a 15-minute walk from Imadegawa St
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] 玄gen is a fully vegan and gluten-free takeout shop in Kyoto's Kamigyo Ward, run by a chef with extensive experience in plant-based and allergy-conscious cooking. The shop manager previously worked as a head chef at the well-known Kanto vegan restaurant Ain Sop

chef_bio.background: 玄gen is a fully vegan and gluten-free takeout shop in Kyoto's Kamigyo Ward, run by a chef with extensive experience in plant-based and allergy-conscious cooking. The shop manager previously worked as a head chef at the well-known Kanto vegan restaurant Ain Soph, where he helped develop its famous pancakes, before traveling to India to study curry.
chef_bio.philosophy: A guiding principle of inclusivity: everyone - vegans, people with food allergies, and the health-conscious - should be able to share a meal together with joy. The kitchen avoids animal products, wheat, chemical seasonings and refined white sugar entirely, and the chef emphasizes compassionate, sustainable eating without animal exploitation, stocking materials from the Animal Rights Center.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% rice-flour bread made without binders, Ayurvedic-inspired spice curries, bento boxes incorporating Chinese-medicine ingredients, plus quiche, salad wraps, tofu karaage, soups and gluten-free vegan sweets like cheesecake. Pre-order vegan nigiri sushi is also offered.

anecdotes.text: The shop manager previously served as head chef at the Kanto vegan restaurant Ain Soph and helped develop its famous pancakes, then studied curry in India before opening Gen.
Their signature bread is made from 100% rice flour with no binders, part of a fully wheat-free, vegan menu that also excludes chemical seasonings and white sugar.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hachidory: 100% vegan and gluten-free takeout in Kamigyo-ku; shop manager was head chef at the Kanto vegan restaurant Ain Soph and helped develop its famous pancakes, then studied curry in India; curries (incl. vegan butter-chicken), 100% rice-flour bread with no binders, bento, tofu karaage, GF vegan sweets, pre-order vegan nigiri; displays Animal Rights Center materials and frames veganism as non-exploitation of animals. Owner name not published.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entirely wheat-free kitchen - no wheat flour is used anywhere, and the signature bread is 100% rice flour with no binders, so wheat cross-contamination risk is minimal.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The specific soy sauce/seasoning brand is not documented online. Since the kitchen is fully gluten-free, the seasonings are presumably wheat-free, but a celiac-level diner should confirm a tamari (wheat-free) shoyu is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire operation is 100% vegan with no animal products handled, so there is no risk of animal-based cross-contact.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop explicitly caters to people with food allergies as part of its core mission, and the chef has years of experience making gluten-free, allergy-conscious foods.

positives.text: Fully vegan AND fully gluten-free dedicated kitchen - no meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or wheat, plus no chemical seasonings or refined white sugar - making it one of the safer choices in Kyoto for both vegan and GF travelers.

cultural_comfort.note: A small Japanese-run takeout shop deeply versed in vegan and allergy-friendly cooking; basic Japanese helps, but the all-vegan, all-gluten-free concept makes ordering safe and simple, so a few polite phrases go a long way.

gf_review.basis: Japanese-first re-rating (Japanese sources establish; English corroborates/denies)
gf_review.why: Entire product line 100% rice flour, GF & vegan — no wheat on premises. Multi-year JP own claim + independent JP bakery guide + 2026 blog confirms open. UPGRADE vs my English run (which had cut it to high for lacking English reviews). Dedicated.

places.name: 毘沙門堂 勝林寺 (Bishamondo Shourin-ji (Shourin Temple))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. Shojin ryori commonly includes wheat-based items (seasoned soy sauce, fu/wheat gluten, tempura batter, noodles) and the matcha is served with wagashi that often contain wheat or wheat-derived ingredients. Soy sauce used in shojin cooking is typically standard wheat-containing shoyu unless otherwise arranged. A gluten-free diner must ask in advance (call 075-561-4311 or use the reservation form); nothing here is confirmed GF-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Shourin-ji is a Rinzai Zen temple (a sub-temple of Tofukuji). Food served to visitors follows shojin ryori (精進料理) tradition, which is plant-based by religious rule: no meat, fish, eggs, dairy or honey, and dashi (stock) is made from kombu (kelp) and dried shiitake rather than bonito. Two food offerings exist: (1) matcha green tea served with Japanese sweets after a zazen/sutra-copying session (a choice of dry sweets / higashi for about 800 yen or fresh sweets / namagashi for about 1000 yen added to the experience), and (2) a full shojin ryori meal experience available by advance reservation (must book by 16:00 two days before). Because it is a temple kitchen, the meal itself is reliably vegan; the accompanying wagashi sweets are traditionally vegan but the exact sweets vary, so confirm ingredients of the specific sweet on the day. No menu page independently itemizes every ingredient, so vegans should still state their requirement when booking.
places.hours_raw: Viewing hours 10:00–16:00. Experiences (zazen, sutra/Buddha-image copying, shojin ryori meal) by reservation; shojin ryori must be booked by 16:00 two days prior. Generally open to the public during special spring and autumn viewings; otherwise individual/group experiences by advance reservation.
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama Ward, near Tofukuji; about an 8-minute walk from
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Shourin-ji (毘沙門堂 勝林寺) is a Rinzai Zen sub-temple (tatchu) of the great Tofukuji temple complex in Kyoto's Higashiyama district, founded in 1550. Its principal deity is Bishamonten, a guardian of fortune and victory, and the temple historically protected the no

roles: Provider of shojin ryori and matcha/wagashi to visitors
chef_bio.background: Shourin-ji (毘沙門堂 勝林寺) is a Rinzai Zen sub-temple (tatchu) of the great Tofukuji temple complex in Kyoto's Higashiyama district, founded in 1550. Its principal deity is Bishamonten, a guardian of fortune and victory, and the temple historically protected the northern direction of Tofukuji. In recent years it has become well known to visitors for its seasonal flower-water basins (hanachozu), goshuin calligraphy, and hands-on Zen experiences.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rooted in Zen Buddhist practice, the temple frames eating, meditation and copying scriptures as a single mindful experience open even to first-time and solo visitors.
chef_bio.specialty: Zazen (seated meditation) and sutra/Buddha-image copying sessions, finished with matcha and Japanese sweets; a full shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian) meal is available by advance reservation. The plant-based meal follows Zen kitchen tradition, using kelp- and shiitake-based dashi instead of any animal stock.

anecdotes.text: The temple is widely featured for its photogenic seasonal hanachozu (flower-filled water basins) and goshuin, alongside zazen, sutra-copying and Buddha-image copying experiences that beginners and solo visitors can join by reservation.

After a meditation or copying session, visitors are served matcha with a choice of dry sweets (about 800 yen) or fresh sweets (about 1000 yen); a separate shojin ryori meal (from about 3,630 yen) can be reserved up to 16:00 two days in advance.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: souda-kyoto / ja.kyoto.travel: 勝林寺 founded 1550, a normally-closed tatchu of Tofukuji enshrining Bishamonten and protecting the northern direction; zazen (adult 1,500 yen) plus sutra/Buddha-image copying, hanachozu and goshuin. shourin-ji.org: matcha with dry sweets 800 yen or fresh sweets 1,000 yen; shojin ryori from 3,630 yen reservable by 16:00 two days in advance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling information is published. Shojin ryori and accompanying wagashi commonly include wheat (soy sauce, fu/wheat gluten, batter, sweets), so cross-contamination cannot be ruled out and must be discussed when reserving.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No statement that wheat-free tamari is used. Shojin cooking typically uses standard wheat-containing soy sauce unless specially arranged, so assume soy sauce contains wheat unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: As a Zen temple kitchen serving only shojin ryori, no meat, fish, eggs or dairy are used on site, which greatly reduces animal cross-contact compared with an omnivore restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: VegeMap notes that visitors should inform staff of dietary requirements and allergies before ordering; the temple takes reservations by phone (075-561-4311) and web form, which is the channel to arrange a vegan and/or allergy-aware meal.

positives.text: Listed as fully vegan (all-vegan) on Japan VegeMap, consistent with the temple's shojin ryori tradition that excludes all animal products and uses kelp/shiitake dashi rather than fish stock.
Plant-based meals and matcha-with-sweets are prepared in a temple kitchen with no animal-protein cooking, so the risk of meat or fish cross-contact is inherently low.

cultural_comfort.note: A foreign student is welcome but should expect a Japanese-language temple setting; reservations are made through the website form, and basic Japanese or a translation app helps, especially for arranging a vegan shojin ryori meal.

places.name: ブランジュリ ロワゾー・ブルー (Boulangerie L'Oiseau Bleu)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→no. This is a WHEAT bakery — specialty is ancient-wheat (spelt/kamut/einkorn) sourdough baked on-site daily. Spelt/kamut/einkorn are ALL wheat species containing gluten; airborne flour dust + shared oven = severe CC. A small rice-flour 'GF' line is made in this same wheat kitchen. Own blog uses misleading allergy framing; reportedly states bread is 'not safe for those with celiac disease.' False 'high' is dangerous. — Prior note: The bakery's core line is gluten-free, built on rice flour and sorghum (the "Life Bread" and many baked goods are explicitly gluten-free). However, the shop also bakes breads from ancient wheats (spelt/Kamut/einkorn), which DO contain gluten — these are marketed as "ancient wheat" rather than gluten-free. Gluten-free and ancient-wheat products are made in the same small kitchen, so a celiac diner should ask the owner which specific items are gluten-free and discuss cross-contact. No dedicated gluten-free-only facility is confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: 100% vegan bakery — all products are free of eggs, dairy and honey, with no meat, fish or any animal ingredients (Vege Project / VegeMap certified as all-vegan). Breads are made from organic, pesticide-free rice flour, sorghum (takakibi), and ancient wheats. The signature "Life Bread" (命のパン) is a slow-fermented enzyme bread.
places.hours_raw: Official site: Tue 11:30-17:00, Sat & Sun 11:00-17:00; closed Mon/Wed/Thu/Fri. Schedule is variable and announced monthly via Instagram; may close early once sold out.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku (Kamigamo / Omiya area), northern Kyoto; nearest sta
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] The bakery was opened in August 2014 by owner-baker Keiko Hirooka, who transitioned away from conventional baking after immersing herself in vegan and gluten-free cooking. She holds qualifications as a bread coordinator, whole-grain expert and herb coordinator

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (Kamigamo / Kita-ku area, west of Kamigamo Shrine near Misono Bridge).
chef_bio.background: The bakery was opened in the mid-2010s by owner-baker Keiko Hirooka (弘岡桂子), who moved away from conventional baking after immersing herself in vegan and gluten-free cooking. She is a certified whole-grain (zakkoku) expert with additional bread- and herb-related food credentials, and does almost all of the baking herself in the early-morning hours.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hirooka's ethos, echoed in the shop's name (l'oiseau bleu, the 'bluebird' whose happiness is close at hand), is that bread connects people with joy (パンは人とつながる喜び) — she bakes to give to the people she cares about, keeping the method very simple and treating both ingredients and people with care while continually refining health-conscious, additive-free recipes.
chef_bio.specialty: Gluten-free, all-vegan breads built on organic pesticide-free rice flour and sorghum, alongside a separate line of breads from ancient wheats (spelt, Kamut, einkorn). The signature item is the slow-fermented vegan gluten-free enzyme 'Life Bread' (命のパン); other favorites include a gluten-free superfood bread with cranberries, raisins and walnuts, rice-flour pound cake with homemade almond flour, and mugwort and organic-chocolate doughnuts.

anecdotes.text: The 'Life Bread' (命のパン) — a vegan, gluten-free enzyme bread of naturally-cultivated pesticide-free rice flour and sorghum — earned its name because customers reported feeling better eating it, including people with irritable bowel syndrome, thanks to a long, slow fermentation that activates enzymes and develops natural amino acids; the owner says everyone from children to the elderly can eat it with peace of mind.

Reviewers describe the owner as exceptionally kind and passionate, doing nearly all the baking herself in the early morning, with bread so fluffy it 'tastes like normal non-gluten-free bread'; lines often form before opening and the shop closes early once everything sells out.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: lmaga.jp and the official site confirm owner Hirooka Keiko (弘岡桂子), the vegan gluten-free 命のパン enzyme bread (rice flour + sorghum, slow fermentation, IBS/all-ages framing) and the 'bread connects people with joy' motto; organicstory-jpn confirms the Kamigamo location and bluebird motto; trip.kyoto/search results confirm her whole-grain (zakkoku) expert qualification.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bakery sells both gluten-free (rice-flour/sorghum) breads and ancient-wheat breads (spelt, Kamut, einkorn) that contain gluten, baked in the same small owner-run kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat flour is possible; no dedicated gluten-free-only facility is confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is 100% vegan/plant-based — all products are free of eggs, dairy and honey and there are no animal ingredients on site, so there is no animal cross-contact risk.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop states all products are free from eggs, dairy and honey and are made to suit people with allergies and dietary restrictions, and the owner is described as very accommodating and willing to discuss ingredients.

positives.text: Fully vegan (Vege Project / VegeMap certified all-vegan) with a gluten-free core line using organic, pesticide-free rice flour and sorghum; some items are also Low-FODMAP certified.
No animal products anywhere in the kitchen means vegan diners face essentially zero animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run bakery where the friendly owner does almost all the baking herself; English isn't confirmed, so a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the all-vegan, mostly gluten-free goods will go a long way.

gf_review.why: This is a WHEAT bakery — specialty is ancient-wheat (spelt/kamut/einkorn) sourdough baked on-site daily. Spelt/kamut/einkorn are ALL wheat species containing gluten; airborne flour dust + shared oven = severe CC. A small rice-flour 'GF' line is made in this same wheat kitchen. Own blog uses misleading allergy framing; reportedly states bread is 'not safe for those with celiac disease.' False 'high' is dangerous.
red_flags: ancient WHEAT (spelt/kamut/einkorn) is the on-site specialty = maximal flour-dust CC
HappyCow spreads dangerous 'gluten-free spelt' misinformation — spelt is NOT GF
GF items made in shared wheat kitchen, no CC protocol
allergy framing conflated with celiac safety
gf_review.plain_rice: Rice-flour/millet bread line exists but baked in the wheat kitchen; no sealed/naturally-GF item bypasses shared-kitchen risk

places.name: いただき繕 京都西陣店 (Itadakizen Kyoto (Itadaki Zen Kyoto Nish)
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen in Kyoto (the brand's London branch is fully gluten-free, but Kyoto is not). Some dishes can be made gluten-free — the tofu steak can be prepared GF and there is reportedly a GF option for some items — but fried items share oil, so they are not celiac-safe. Soy sauce/wheat content of seasonings is not confirmed; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so a strict GF or celiac diner should explicitly inform staff and ask whether wheat-free tamari is used. Staff are described as accommodating with allergies — notify them when booking and confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen — no animal products, dairy, eggs or honey. The menu is entirely plant-based, built around fermented soy (house-made miso), soy milk, tofu and seasonal Kyoto vegetables. Signature plant dishes include vegan sushi (a dinner-only specialty topped with fresh Kyoto vegetables), vegetable-and-soy-milk vegan ramen, grilled onigiri, tofu steak, enmen soy-milk noodles, and Korean-style bibimbap and kimbap. Vegetables are organic/pesticide-free and locally sourced. Because the entire kitchen is animal-free, dashi is plant-based (no fish stock).
places.hours_raw: Mon/Wed/Fri 18:00-22:30; Thu/Sat/Sun 12:00-15:00 and 18:00-22:30; Tue closed (last entry 21:00, last order 21:30, 2-hour table limit). Hours fluctuate and closing days are irregular — confirm via Instagram/booking site before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo Ward (上京区), in the historic Nishijin textile distric
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Itadaki Zen started in Europe with a 2009 London flagship and grew into a small group of vegan restaurants. The Kyoto branch sits in the historic Nishijin district inside a renovated machiya — a traditional wooden townhouse over 100 years old — north of downto

chef_bio.origin: Founded by a Japanese family who had relocated to Europe; the London flagship opened in 2009. The Kyoto branch is run by the founder's son.
chef_bio.background: Itadaki Zen started in Europe with a 2009 London flagship and grew into a small group of vegan restaurants. The Kyoto branch sits in the historic Nishijin district inside a renovated machiya — a traditional wooden townhouse over 100 years old — north of downtown Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name reflects 'itadakimasu' — gratitude, rooted in Buddhist tradition, for everything that gave nourishment — paired with the owner's reading of 'zen' as 'to repair,' the idea that food heals the body physically, spiritually and mentally. The concept is 'simple yet luxurious,' using organic, pesticide-free, locally sourced produce and traditional East Asian techniques, with the goal of making vegan food available to a wider public.
chef_bio.specialty: All-vegan Japanese cuisine: a dinner-only vegan sushi topped with fresh seasonal Kyoto vegetables, vegetable-and-soy-milk vegan ramen (enmen), grilled onigiri, tofu steak, plus house-made miso and Korean-influenced dishes like bibimbap and kimbap.

anecdotes.text: The owner has been vegan his whole life and frames the restaurant as a mission to make vegan food more available to the mass public, with ambitions to expand globally.
The kitchen makes its own miso in-house, described as much richer than typical miso soup found elsewhere.
The vegan sushi is an evening-only specialty using fresh Kyoto vegetables as toppings, changing with the season for a crisp texture and delicate vegetable flavor.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyototwo.jp (JP) confirms the evening-only vegan sushi topped with seasonal Kyoto vegetables and the soy-milk 'enmen' ramen; digistyle-kyoto (EN) confirms the 2009 London flagship, the founder's-son operator, the 100-year Nishijin machiya, the name's meaning and the in-house miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen in Kyoto; fried items share oil, so fried dishes are not celiac-safe even when a gluten-free version of an item exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat content of soy sauce/seasonings is not confirmed in available sources; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so a strict GF diner should ask whether wheat-free tamari is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is fully vegan (no animal products, dairy or eggs used), so there is no animal cross-contact — a strong positive for vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as accommodating to allergies; notify them in advance, ideally when booking online, and confirm specifics directly.

positives.text: 100% plant-based kitchen using organic, pesticide-free, locally sourced vegetables and house-made fermented ingredients — no risk of hidden fish dashi or animal stock.
Tofu steak can be prepared gluten-free, and some items have a gluten-free option, so GF-flexible diners have safe choices when staff are informed.

cultural_comfort.note: Run by an internationally-minded vegan family (with a London origin) and used to foreign vegan travelers, so English is generally fine — but book online in advance since phone lines are limited and seating is small.

places.name: ゴマクロサロン (gomacro Salon)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free kitchen. Many savory dishes (sesame dandan noodles, curry noodles/ramen, pasta) are wheat-based, and at least one gluten-free diner reported being unable to eat any of the savory options and settling for a nut pie dessert. There is no documented confirmation that the soy sauce is wheat-free or that a dedicated fryer is used. A celiac/GF traveler should ask item by item; safe choices are limited.
places.vegan_detail: The café kitchen is plant-based: dishes are made without eggs, dairy, or other animal ingredients. The one honest caveat is that a few dressings/items contain honey, and these are clearly marked on the menu, so strict vegans should ask or watch for the honey labels. Sesame is central to nearly every dish (it is the antenna café of sesame-oil maker Yamada Seiyu), so sesame-allergic diners should avoid. Dashi-based soups use sesame miso rather than fish dashi.
places.hours_raw: Shop 11:00-18:00; Café 11:30-18:00 (L.O. 17:00); Breakfast Fri-Sun 8:00-10:00 (L.O. 9:15, reservations prioritized). Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, at the southeast corner of Oike-dori and Shinm
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] gomacro Salon is the Kyoto antenna café and community space of Yamada Seiyu (山田製油), a long-established Kyoto sesame-oil maker. The ground floor pairs a café with a shop of carefully chosen sesame products and seasonings, while the second floor hosts a macrobio

roles: Antenna café of sesame-oil maker Yamada Seiyu
Macrobiotic cooking school and food workshop space
chef_bio.background: gomacro Salon is the Kyoto antenna café and community space of Yamada Seiyu (山田製油), a long-established Kyoto sesame-oil maker. The ground floor pairs a café with a shop of carefully chosen sesame products and seasonings, while the upper floor hosts a macrobiotic cooking school and food workshops (classes are sometimes run in English).
chef_bio.philosophy: Built on the concept of protecting the children who will build the future and the mothers raising them, the salon emphasizes food safety, seasonal local vegetables and environmental awareness, using sesame as its signature ingredient and metaphor.
chef_bio.specialty: Sesame-forward plant-based dishes: the rich, mellow sesame dandan noodles, spicy original-spice curry noodles, white curry and creamy-noodle lunch sets, tofu steak sets, and bibimbap, all served with brown rice, sesame miso soup and table-side sesame condiments to customize.

anecdotes.text: One visitor described eating during a snowfall as feeling like being inside a Ghibli movie, sitting at the table with snow falling outside and gentle music playing.
Reviewers note nearly every dish revolves around sesame, giving the food a full-bodied flavor, and that the retail section of sesame products is a worthwhile bonus.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The gomacro Salon site (gomacro.jp) confirms it is sesame-oil maker Yamada Seiyu's antenna café, with a ground-floor café/shop and upstairs cooking school and food-safety/cooking/yoga workshops, and the 'protect the children and the mothers' concept centred on sesame; HappyCow reviews confirm the sesame-forward dishes and retail shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a gluten-free kitchen; wheat-based noodles and pasta are central to the menu and one gluten-free diner could not eat any of the savory options.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The café cooks without eggs, dairy or other animal ingredients, so animal cross-contact is inherently low; the main non-vegan exception is honey in a few dressings, which is clearly marked on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff offer an English menu and have been praised for explaining dishes and their ingredients in English.

positives.text: Fully plant-based menu means a vegan diner can order almost anything (just avoiding the honey-marked items), with low risk of animal cross-contact.
Café of an established Kyoto sesame-oil maker with an attached shop of vetted ingredients, emphasizing food safety and seasonal local vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: Staff are used to international visitors and reviewers have been served an English menu with dishes and ingredients explained in English, so a foreign student can dine comfortably.

places.name: ナチュラルフード ビレッジ (Natural Food Village)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised, and several signature items contain or likely contain wheat: soy meat / soy "cutlet," tempeh tempura (batter), Thai fried noodles, spring rolls, and curry. Soy meat and seitan-style proteins are wheat-based, and there is no confirmation that a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce is used. Gluten-sensitive diners should ask directly about each dish; assume standard wheat-containing soy sauce and batters unless confirmed otherwise.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan (plant-based) kitchen — described as one of the oldest all-vegan restaurants in Kyoto (est. 2001). The entire menu is plant-based, built around organic, pesticide-free vegetables, ancient grains, and house seasonings, with groundwater drawn from Kitashirakawa. Signature dishes are the Rainbow Bento (虹色御膳, ~¥1,200, seven deli sides + ancient rice + soup) and soy-cutlet/soy-meat rice bowls. No animal products are used, so dairy/egg/fish cross-contact is inherently minimal.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat: 12:00–15:00 (lunch), 18:00–24:00 (dinner). Sun: 12:00–15:00 lunch only. Closed Mondays. (Some sources list dinner start as 18:30.)
places.neighborhood: Sakyo-ku, Ichijoji area (near Ichijoji Station / northeast K
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Natural Food Village was opened in 2001 by Tatsu, a musician who spent about four years backpacking around Japan and the world before settling in the Ichijoji neighborhood of Kyoto. It is widely cited as one of the oldest fully vegan restaurants in the city, f

chef_bio.background: Natural Food Village was opened in 2001 by Tatsu, a musician who spent about four years backpacking around Japan and the world before settling in the Ichijoji neighborhood of Kyoto. It is widely cited as one of the oldest fully vegan restaurants in the city, founded back when 'vegan' and 'organic' were still unfamiliar words in Japan. Tatsu describes the restaurant's aesthetic as 'stateless' rather than multicultural, blending the many cultures he encountered while traveling.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tatsu's guiding idea is that 'food creates the body, and music and art create the heart and spirit' — he sees good health as the foundation for truly enjoying music and life. The kitchen prioritizes organic, pesticide-free vegetables, carefully chosen safe seasonings, and groundwater sourced from Kitashirakawa.
chef_bio.specialty: International creative vegan cuisine; the signature Rainbow Bento (虹色御膳) with seven deli-style sides, ancient grains and soup, plus soy-cutlet rice bowls, Thai-style fried noodles, tempeh tempura, spring rolls and curry.

anecdotes.text: The space doubles as a live-music venue; weekend nights often feature local musicians performing or jamming, reflecting Tatsu's background as a musician.
Tatsu travels personally to draw groundwater from Kitashirakawa for the restaurant, and some longtime regulars have been inspired by the food ethos to take up farming themselves.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: vegewel.com (JP) confirms the 2001 opening, owner Tatsu's musician background and ~4-year travels, the 'stateless' aesthetic, the Kitashirakawa groundwater, the Rainbow Bento and regulars turning to farming; HappyCow confirms the live-music nights.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free program advertised; menu features wheat-based proteins (soy meat / soy cutlet) and battered/fried items (tempeh tempura) and noodles, so shared frying oil and surfaces likely carry gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No confirmation that wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce is used; assume standard wheat-containing soy sauce in seasonings and sauces unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely plant-based kitchen with no meat, fish, dairy or eggs used, so risk of animal-product cross-contact is inherently very low.

positives.text: Fully vegan since 2001 — every dish is plant-based, making it a safe and easy choice for vegan and vegetarian diners.
Uses organic, pesticide-free vegetables, carefully selected seasonings and Kitashirakawa groundwater, with an English menu and friendly owner.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the owner Tatsu and staff are repeatedly described as friendly and welcoming, so foreign students should feel comfortable dining here.

places.name: 好文 (Yoshifumi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The macrobiotic/grain-based menu leans heavily on rice (enzyme rice, seven-grain rice) and miso/soy-based seasoning, but wheat-containing items (soy sauce, possibly dumpling wrappers, seven-grain blends) are likely present. Whether the soy sauce or miso is wheat-free, and whether dishes can be made gluten-free, is unconfirmed and must be asked directly in advance. (Menu review: 2 gluten-free meal options on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Listed by Japan VegeMap as an all-vegan eatery (飲食店（全てヴィーガン）), meaning no meat, seafood, eggs, dairy, or honey. Described as a 穀物菜食 (grain-based/macrobiotic) home cafe serving seasonal vegetable set meals. A reported lunch set includes vegetable dumplings in a soymilk soup, fried daikon, pickles, and a choice of enzyme rice or seven-grain rice, with dessert and tea. Everything served is plant-based.
places.hours_raw: Hours are irregular and have varied by source. Historically lunch was served mainly on Fridays (often tied to a yoga class on the premises), around midday; one aggregator listing showed 9:00-18:00 Thursday-Sunday. Confirm directly before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Sagano, Ukyo-ku — near Seiryoji Temple (Saga Shakado), about
