places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Yoshifumi (好文) is a tucked-away, home-style plant-based cafe in the quiet residential lanes of Sagano, just east of the main gate of Seiryoji Temple (Saga Shakado) in Kyoto's Arashiyama district. The setting is a modest house with a deliberately understated si

chef_bio.background: Yoshifumi (好文) is a tucked-away, home-style plant-based cafe in the quiet residential lanes of Sagano, just by Seiryoji Temple (Saga Shakado) in Kyoto's Arashiyama/Ukyo-ku district. The setting is a modest house with a deliberately understated sign; guests eat at low tables on tatami, giving the feel of being welcomed into someone's home rather than a commercial restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe frames plant-based eating as a way to face and care for your own body — visitors describe dishes of 'dynamic, alive-feeling vegetables' and leaving with a sense that mind and body have been cleansed. The space doubles as a venue for wellbeing: the second floor hosts yoga, calligraphy, and rakugo storytelling, reinforcing a holistic, slow-living ethos.
chef_bio.specialty: Grain-based, macrobiotic-leaning vegan set meals (穀物菜食) built around seasonal vegetables and rice — for example vegetable dumplings in a soymilk soup, fried daikon, pickles, and a choice of enzyme rice or seven-grain rice, finished with a small dessert and tea.

anecdotes.text: Lunch has historically been offered chiefly on Fridays, when the owner's pop-up also operates from the space, blending the meal with the building's wellness program rather than a conventional restaurant schedule.
The owner also runs a vegan pop-up food stall (described as the 'Wataridori' / migratory-bird stall), extending the cafe's plant-based cooking beyond its home base.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi.jp (JP) confirms the Sagano home-cafe near Seiryoji/Saga Shakado, the tatami low-table setting, the grain-based macrobiotic Friday lunch set, the upstairs yoga/calligraphy/rakugo program and the owner's 'Wataridori' pop-up stall.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information published on whether the soy sauce or miso used is wheat-free; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, so gluten-sensitive diners should assume wheat is present unless they confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Japan VegeMap classifies the cafe as fully vegan (飲食店（全てヴィーガン）), meaning the kitchen uses no meat, seafood, eggs, dairy, or honey — so animal cross-contact risk is inherently low across the menu.

positives.text: Fully plant-based kitchen, so the entire menu is suitable for vegans and no animal-derived ingredients are used.
Macrobiotic/grain-based cooking centered on rice and seasonal vegetables, with simple, whole-food preparations.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, low-key Japanese home cafe with no evidence of English service, so a few basic Japanese phrases and confirming the irregular opening days in advance (ideally by phone) will go a long way.

places.name: Cacao∞Magic（カカオマジック） (Cacao Magic)
places.gf_detail: No explicit gluten-free information is published. Raw chocolate bars themselves are cacao/nut/superfood based and many are naturally grain-free, but the signature raw chocolate tart has a crust whose ingredients are not documented as gluten-free. Because soy sauce/dashi are not part of this dessert-focused menu, the main gluten concern is tart crusts and baked items. A coeliac should ask per item; no dedicated GF claim exists.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based raw chocolate specialty shop. All chocolate and sweets are organic, dairy-free, egg-free and made with no animal products whatsoever ("動物を一切使っていない"), no white sugar, no additives or preservatives. Raw process keeps everything below 48°C. Cafe items (raw chocolate tart, cacao smoothie, iced raw chocolate, ice cream) use plant milks such as almond milk. Suitable for vegans without modification.
places.hours_raw: Sat, Sun, Mon 12:00–17:00 (café/eat-in service is irregular)
places.neighborhood: Sakyo-ku, near Ginkaku-ji (Silver Pavilion) and the Philosop
places.cuisine: Raw vegan chocolate / cacao sweets cafe
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] A raw-chocolate specialty shop (ローチョコレート専門店) set in a nostalgic old house in Sakyo-ku near Ginkaku-ji and the Philosopher's Path. The shop and its refined aesthetic reflect the sensibility of owner Sumire; the chocolate packaging was designed by graphic design

chef_bio.background: A raw-chocolate specialty shop (ローチョコレート専門店) set in a nostalgic old house in Sakyo-ku near Ginkaku-ji and the Philosopher's Path. The shop and its refined aesthetic reflect the sensibility of owner Sumire; the chocolate packaging was designed by graphic designer Midori Kawano.
chef_bio.philosophy: Treats chocolate as a way to experience cacao itself rather than just a sweet. Everything is raw (processed at low temperature to preserve cacao's natural nutrients and polyphenols), organic, free of dairy, white sugar, additives and preservatives, and contains no animal products at all.
chef_bio.specialty: Raw chocolate and cacao sweets made with organic raw cacao, plus a signature raw chocolate tart, cacao smoothies, iced raw chocolate and plant-based ice cream served in an irregularly-open eat-in space.

anecdotes.text: LUNA SEA / X JAPAN guitarist SUGIZO publicly praised the shop's chocolate tart, saying it is a dish he would choose as a final meal.
The back room is a single zashiki (tatami) space facing the garden, and reviewers single out the raw chocolate tart as the best item in the shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto.tips (JP) confirms the Sakyo-ku raw-chocolate shop near Ginkaku-ji, owner Sumire, the packaging by designer Midori Kawano, the low-temperature raw/organic/no-animal-product method and SUGIZO's praise of the chocolate tart.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fully plant-based kitchen: all chocolate and sweets are made with no animal products whatsoever, dairy-free and egg-free, so the risk of animal cross-contact is minimal.

positives.text: Entirely vegan, organic, raw operation with no dairy, eggs, white sugar, additives or preservatives, making it a low-risk choice for vegan diners.
Menu uses plant milks (e.g. almond milk) for smoothies and chocolate drinks.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, artisanal shop in a nostalgic old house; some basic Japanese helps, though the all-vegan menu and chocolate focus make it easy to navigate for a foreign student.

places.name: 泉仙 大慈院店 (Izusen Daijiin-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are made. Shojin cuisine relies heavily on wheat-based ingredients: seitan/wheat-gluten (fu, namafu), tempura/fried items, and Japanese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat). Set courses are not adjustable a la carte. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should ask in advance and treat soy sauce, fu, and fried elements as containing wheat unless individually confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: This is traditional shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian cuisine) served within the grounds of Daitoku-ji temple. By definition shojin uses no meat, fish, or seafood, and the kitchen also avoids the "five pungent" aromatics (onion, garlic, etc.). The official site explicitly states no meat, fish, or seafood are used and that dashi is plant-based rather than animal/fish stock — making the food effectively vegan/vegetarian throughout. Diners with strict vegan needs should note shojin traditionally permits ingredients like dairy/honey rarely, but the cuisine here is plant-centered; confirm any specific concern when reserving.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–16:00 (last order). Open daily (closed Dec 28–31)
places.neighborhood: Kita Ward (Kita-ku), inside the Daitoku-ji temple complex in
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Izusen has served Kyoto's Buddhist vegetarian (shojin) cuisine since 1963 from a tranquil sub-temple, Daijiin, within the grounds of Daitoku-ji — one of the head temples of Rinzai Zen. Guests dine looking out over a temple garden, eating from tetsubachi: round

chef_bio.background: Izusen has served Kyoto's Buddhist vegetarian (shojin) cuisine since 1963 from a tranquil sub-temple, Daiji-in, within the grounds of Daitoku-ji — one of the head temples of Rinzai Zen. Guests dine looking out over a temple garden, eating from tetsubachi: round iron bowls that, in the Buddhist tradition, monks once carried to receive alms of food.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows the Zen ideal of three hearts: kishin (a joyful heart in cooking), roshin (a respectful heart that wastes nothing, attending to each grain of rice), and daishin (a compassionate, magnanimous heart). Meat, fish and seafood are excluded entirely, seasonings are kept restrained to let natural flavors speak, and every part of an ingredient is used.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is shojin tetsubachi-ryori — seasonal vegetable, grain and legume dishes presented in nested iron-bowl vessels, offered as fixed seasonal courses (Ayame, Yuri, Botan). Tea-kaiseki (chakaiseki) is also served.

anecdotes.text: The tetsubachi (iron bowl) at the heart of the meal traces back to the alms bowls of Buddhist monks, an implement said to have come from India and reached Japan in the Nara period — here repurposed as elegant tableware merging Zen with Kyoto cuisine.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Izusen site confirms the 1963 Daiji-in shojin restaurant within Daitoku-ji, the tetsubachi iron-bowl format (traced to Indian alms bowls that reached Japan in the Nara period), the three hearts (kishin/roshin/daishin) and the Ayame/Yuri/Botan seasonal courses.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free program is advertised; wheat-gluten (fu/namafu) and tempura are staple shojin ingredients, so cross-contact with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese soy sauce is standard in shojin seasoning and normally contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an entirely plant-based shojin kitchen using no meat, fish or seafood, so animal cross-contact is inherently very low.

positives.text: Fully vegetarian/vegan-leaning Buddhist cuisine where no meat, fish or seafood is prepared anywhere in the kitchen, and dashi is plant-based — a reassuring environment for vegans regarding animal cross-contact.
Long-established (since 1963) temple-grounds restaurant with a clear, published culinary philosophy.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional temple-grounds restaurant accustomed to tourists; basic Japanese greetings and the English-listed course names help, but staff English may be limited, so a few polite Japanese phrases go a long way.

places.name: KYONO JUICE（キョウノ ジュース） (Kyono Juice)
places.gf_detail: No source states a gluten-free policy. The core product — cold-pressed juices from raw fruit and vegetables — is inherently free of wheat/gluten, and smoothies and cold-pressed sodas are likewise plant/fruit based. However, there is no published allergen information; VegeMap explicitly says to confirm allergens directly with the shop. A celiac diner should ask about any cereal/grain powders, granola toppings, or syrups before ordering. Treat as likely-safe-but-unconfirmed.
places.vegan_detail: A cold-pressed juice and smoothie specialty stand whose menu is built entirely on fresh fruit and vegetables, cold-pressed sodas and smoothies — listed as fully vegan (すべてヴィーガン) on Japan VegeMap. No dairy or animal products are mentioned in any source describing the menu. Note that smoothies and any blended items may contain plant milk or other add-ins, so a strict vegan should confirm specific blends in person. The shop is the antenna store of Sun Chlorella's subsidiary, so chlorella/spirulina superfood additions appear in some drinks (all plant-based).
places.hours_raw: 10:00–19:00, closed Wednesdays (per official Sun Chlorella opening notice and a Jan 2025 listing). Some third-party listings show 11:00–18:00 or 11:00–19:00; treat hours as subject to change.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward, Shimogamo — at the northwest corner of the Shimo
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Kyono Juice opened in 2018 as the antenna shop of Chlorelland Corp., a wholly-owned subsidiary of the Kyoto health-food company Sun Chlorella. It sits on the busy northwest corner of the Shimogamo Honmachi and Kitaoji intersection in a striking modern storefro

chef_bio.background: Kyono Juice opened in 2018 as the antenna shop of Chlorelland Corp., a wholly-owned subsidiary of the Kyoto health-food company Sun Chlorella. It sits on the busy northwest corner of the Shimogamo Honmachi and Kitaoji intersection in a striking modern storefront, with a second-floor seating area for lingering over a drink.
chef_bio.philosophy: The team set out to bring cold-pressed juice culture — popular among health- and beauty-conscious people and vegetarians in the US — to Japan, spreading it 'from Kyoto's Shimogamo,' with a focus on drinks that are gentle on the body and deliver fruit-and-vegetable nutrition in an easily absorbed form. They also run a juice cleanse program.
chef_bio.specialty: Cold-pressed juices made by slowly applying heavy pressure to raw seasonal fruit and vegetables without any heat, preserving live enzymes and water-soluble fiber; the lineup also includes seasonal smoothies (peach, melon, kabocha squash and other flavors) and cold-pressed sodas.

anecdotes.text: Billed in local food press as one of Kyoto's first cold-pressed juice shops, it pitched the then-novel idea of nutrient-dense raw juice with live enzymes and only the soluble fiber, marketed for quick absorption and lighter digestion.

It is operated by a small team and serves as the retail 'antenna store' for Sun Chlorella's juice line, run by operating company Chlorelland Corp. under representative director Nakayama Futoshi (中山太).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Sun Chlorella's press release confirms KYONO JUICE opened in 2018 as the antenna shop of subsidiary Chlorelland at the Shimogamo Honmachi–Kitaoji corner; the Kyoto city business directory (kigyo.city.kyoto.lg.jp) confirms Chlorelland's representative director Nakayama Futoshi (中山太); ippin.gnavi describes the cold-press method, second-floor seating and juice cleanse.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Listed on Japan VegeMap as a fully vegan (すべてヴィーガン) cold-pressed juice and smoothie shop, so the kitchen handles essentially no animal products, greatly reducing the risk of animal cross-contact.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen handling info; the VegeMap listing explicitly advises confirming allergen and ingredient details directly with the shop.

positives.text: Menu is centered on raw cold-pressed fruit-and-vegetable juices, smoothies and cold-pressed sodas — naturally plant-based and, for the juices, inherently free of wheat/gluten.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood juice stand where pointing at the menu and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; staff are unlikely to conduct detailed allergen conversations in English, so know your key Japanese food words.

places.name: 精進鉄鉢料理 泉仙 紫野店 (Izusen Murasakino (Shojin Teppachi Ryori)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Signature dishes rely on wheat-derived ingredients — yuba (the house specialty), fu (wheat gluten) and ordinary wheat-based soy sauce are central to the cuisine and cannot be removed. No GF accommodation is indicated. Celiac/GF diners are not safe here.
places.vegan_detail: Shojin ryori is fully plant-based by Buddhist precept — no meat or seafood. The official site states dishes use only grains, legumes and vegetables, and Tabelog states animal-derived broths are avoided, so meals are intended to be vegetarian/vegan. However, this is traditional shojin (not a self-described "vegan" kitchen): a HappyCow reviewer historically reported a bonito/fish flavour in the dashi and said staff once confirmed fish was used for broth (later reviewers reported no fish). Yuba, tofu and seasonal vegetables are the mainstays. Vegans should confirm at booking that no katsuo/fish dashi is used in their course.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00; closed Wednesdays (open if a public holiday)
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, at the front gate of Daitoku-ji Temple, Murasakino;
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Izusen's Murasakino branch has stood at the front gate of Daitoku-ji — a great head temple of the Rinzai Zen school — since December 1977, with the wider Izusen house tracing its roots back generations and a sister branch operating inside the Daiji-in sub-temp

chef_bio.background: Izusen's Murasakino branch has stood at the front gate of Daitoku-ji — a great head temple of the Rinzai Zen school — since December 1977, with the wider Izusen house tracing its roots back generations and a sister branch operating inside the Daiji-in sub-temple since 1963. It is one of Kyoto's long-established purveyors of shojin ryori, the vegetarian cuisine developed by Zen monks.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rooted in Zen Buddhist precepts: cuisine built only from grains, legumes and vegetables, prepared with care and gratitude, letting guests taste the depth of the seasons within a quiet temple setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Teppachi (iron-bowl) shojin cuisine: a series of small seasonal dishes served in lacquered, stackable iron bowls that echo the begging bowls Zen monks carry on their rounds. House signatures include yuba (tofu skin) kaiseki, the 'Unsui' and 'Ayame' set courses, and seasonal vegetable, tofu and grain preparations — no meat or seafood.

anecdotes.text: Diners eat on tatami in a serene corner by the Daitoku-ji temple complex, with dishes presented in nested iron bowls modeled on the alms bowls Zen monks use — a format that gives the meal its name, teppachi ryori.
The cuisine uses only grains, legumes and vegetables, with no meat or seafood, so guests can experience authentic Buddhist vegetarian dining at the gate of Daitoku-ji.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Izusen site confirms the Murasakino branch at Daitoku-ji's gate since 1977, the sister Daiji-in branch since 1963, the teppachi iron-bowl shojin format and the yuba-kaiseki, Unsui and Ayame courses (grains/legumes/vegetables only, no meat or seafood).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is integral to the cuisine — yuba, fu (wheat gluten) and standard soy sauce are used throughout — so a shared, wheat-heavy kitchen makes this unsuitable and unsafe for gluten-free or celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) and other wheat-derived elements such as fu are described as essential to Izusen's cooking and cannot be modified.

vegan_cross_contact.text: As traditional shojin, the kitchen handles no meat and the menu is plant-based, which sharply reduces animal cross-contact — but it is not a certified vegan kitchen. A reviewer historically reported a bonito/fish flavour in the broth and said staff confirmed fish was once used for dashi; later reviewers reported no fish. Strict vegans should confirm no fish/katsuo dashi when booking.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Recent reviewers describe the service as very kind and attentive; however, an older account suggested staff were initially evasive about ingredient questions, so be clear and specific about dietary needs.

positives.text: Fully plant-based shojin menu with no meat or seafood per the restaurant's own description — grains, legumes and vegetables only — making it broadly vegetarian/vegan-friendly with low animal cross-contact.
Long-praised, authentic shojin experience in a tranquil Daitoku-ji temple setting, well regarded by international visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional temple-gate shojin restaurant popular with international visitors; staff are used to foreign diners and English menus/listings exist, but a few words of Japanese and politeness go a long way in this formal Zen setting.

places.name: ミレット (Cafe Millet)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free guarantee is published. The set lunch is bread- and wheat-forward (stone-oven house bread, vegan pizza, quiche), so gluten is present in the standard course. There is no documented dedicated gluten-free menu or stated wheat-free soy sauce. Because it is reservation-only and the kitchen tailors the course, gluten-free or wheat-restricted diners should phone or email ahead (075-888-0878 / millet@cafemillet.jp) to ask what can be adapted; nothing specific is confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. Cafe Millet serves a single multi-course organic vegan lunch built around vegetables grown on their own pesticide-free farm in Shizuhara. No animal products are used in the food (one optional addition is local honey served with the house-baked bread, which strict vegans may decline). Typical courses include grilled seasonal vegetables, tofu, vegan quiche, squash/vegetable soup, vegan pizza, salad, brown rice with aduki beans, stone-oven bread, and an apple & sweet potato tart, plus herb tea and coffee. The map listing notes it is an all-vegan establishment; staff recommend telling them about vegan needs and allergies when you reserve so the menu can be tailored.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (lunch only, reservation required). Closed Wednesdays. Sundays also host an 8:00-12:00 farmers market with stone-oven bread and coffee.
places.neighborhood: Shizuhara, Sakyo Ward — rural satoyama near Ohara/Kurama, no
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Cafe Millet is an organic vegan restaurant, stone-oven bakery and working natural farm set in a rustic renovated farmhouse in Shizuhara, a quiet satoyama (rural mountain) hamlet about 20 minutes north of central Kyoto toward Kurama, near the temple town of Oha

chef_bio.background: Cafe Millet is an organic vegan restaurant, stone-oven bakery and working natural farm set in a rustic renovated farmhouse in Shizuhara, a quiet satoyama (rural mountain) hamlet north of central Kyoto toward Kurama, near the temple town of Ohara. Diners eat in a light-filled farmhouse dining room overlooking the farm's own rice paddies, a river and the surrounding mountains.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to 'maximize vegetables' natural power' using pesticide-free, sustainably grown produce and to move toward self-sustaining cultivation where life cycles naturally. The owners emphasize creating irreplaceable, unhurried time for families and friends amid birdsong, rustling cherry and zelkova trees, and the sound of streams.
chef_bio.specialty: A single seasonal multi-course organic vegan lunch built from vegetables grown on the farm — grilled vegetables, tofu, vegan quiche and pizza, vegetable soup, brown rice with aduki beans, salad, stone-oven house bread, and an apple & sweet potato tart, with herb tea and coffee.

anecdotes.text: The farmhouse bakes its natural-yeast bread in a stone oven, and the cafe doubles as a venue for art exhibits, music performances and workshops on bread-baking, herb cultivation and traditional crafts.
On Sundays the farm runs an 8:00-noon farmers market selling stone-oven bread and coffee.
Honey served (optionally) with the house bread comes from the farm's own beehive.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (cafemillet.jp) confirms the Shizuhara farmhouse setting, the reservation-only seasonal vegan lunch, the Sunday 8:00-noon farmers market and the own-apiary honey; HappyCow reviews confirm the stone bread oven and the art/music/workshop program (no source found for a named oven architect, so that detail was removed).

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol is published, and the standard course is wheat-heavy (stone-oven bread, vegan pizza, quiche), so cross-contact with wheat throughout the kitchen should be assumed for gluten-sensitive diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely vegan and farm-to-table, so there is no meat, fish, dashi or dairy on the premises to cross-contaminate dishes — a strong positive for vegans. The only animal product on offer is optional farm honey served alongside the bread, which can be declined.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Because lunch is reservation-only and tailored, staff ask that you tell them about vegan needs and any allergies when booking so the menu can be adjusted.

positives.text: Fully vegan, organic kitchen using vegetables from the restaurant's own pesticide-free farm, eliminating animal cross-contact concerns.
English-speaking, accommodating owner and a reservation-based format that allows dietary needs to be communicated in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner speaks English and hosts are described as warm and accommodating, so a foreign student can reserve and dine comfortably in English.

places.name: 閑臥庵 (Kanga-an)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is made. Fucha ryori relies heavily on deep-fried dishes (a defining feature is its skillful use of oil) and on tofu/seitan-style preparations, plus standard Japanese soy sauce, mirin and sweetened glazes, all of which typically contain wheat. Tempura batter and the imitation-meat/eel dishes are likely wheat-based. A gluten-free diner would need to discuss the menu in advance; gluten avoidance cannot be assumed safe here. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Kanga-an serves fucha ryori (普茶料理), the Obaku Zen school's form of shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) cuisine, which by tradition contains no meat, fish, or animal products. Courses are multi-dish (10+ items) and famously include plant-based imitation dishes such as "vegan unagi (eel)" made from tofu, seaweed, and burdock. Shojin/fucha cooking traditionally uses kombu (kelp) and shiitake-based dashi rather than fish/bonito dashi, so it is generally plant-based throughout. That said, the kitchen does not publish an explicit allergen/vegan certification, so strict vegans should confirm when reserving that no honey, dairy, or egg is used in any specific course.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-15:00 (last entry 13:00); Dinner 17:30-21:00 (last entry 19:00); Salon/bar 13:00-21:00. Open year-round except New Year holidays.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, ~3 min walk from Kuramaguchi Station (Karasuma subw
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Kanga-an is a Zen temple of the Obaku (Obakushu) school, full name 黄檗禅宗 瑞芝山 閑臥庵, founded in 1671. It was established when the guardian deity Chintaku Reifujin was brought from Kifune Shrine to protect the imperial capital, and the temple is tied to Emperor Go-

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (Obaku Zen Buddhist temple, founded 1671)
chef_bio.background: Kanga-an is a Zen temple of the Obaku (Obakushu) school, full name 黄檗禅宗 瑞芝山 閑臥庵, founded in 1671. It was established when the guardian deity Chintaku Reifujin was brought from Kifune (Kibune) Shrine to protect the imperial capital, and the temple is tied to Emperor Go-Mizunoo, who favored it as a peaceful retreat. For more than 300 years it has carried on the fucha ryori tradition of the Obaku monks.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fucha means 'serving tea widely to many people,' and the cuisine embodies communal, egalitarian dining — dishes are traditionally shared among diners seated together, with no one superior or inferior. Unlike austere temple fare, Kanga-an's fucha is colorful and celebratory, while remaining rooted in Zen vegetarian principles and the Obaku monks' reputation for healthful longevity through this oil-rich plant-based diet.
chef_bio.specialty: Fucha ryori (普茶料理) — the Obaku school's distinctive plant-based Buddhist banquet cuisine, served as multi-course set meals (Murasaki-zen, Take-zen, Shu-zen, Kuro-zen). Signature dishes include a remarkable plant-based 'vegan eel' crafted from tofu, seaweed and burdock, deep-fried preparations, and colorful kaiseki-style presentations.

anecdotes.text: The Obaku monks who ate fucha cuisine continuously were known for their longevity, attributed to the healthy plant-based, oil-conscious cooking.
Within the temple grounds is a hidden bar overlooking the Japanese garden, where guests can relax with a drink after the course meal.
A signature dish is a plant-based 'unagi (eel)' made from tofu, seaweed and burdock, showcasing the inventive imitation dishes central to fucha ryori.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kangaan.jp and visitjapan-vegetarian confirm the 1671 Obaku Zen temple, its tie to Emperor Go-Mizunoo and the Kibune guardian deity Chintaku Reifujin, the shared/egalitarian fucha ryori, the tofu-seaweed-burdock 'vegan eel' and the garden bar.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fucha ryori is defined by its skillful use of oil and includes deep-fried dishes (tempura, fried imitation items); these batters are typically wheat-based, so gluten cross-contact in fryers is likely. Not confirmed whether any GF accommodation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese temple kitchen, the cuisine uses standard soy sauce and sweet glazes, which normally contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is advertised. Assume soy seasonings contain gluten unless confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a Buddhist (shojin/fucha) kitchen serving no meat, fish or animal products, so animal cross-contact is inherently minimal — a strong positive for vegans and vegetarians.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Meals are completely reservation-only and arranged 2-3 days in advance, which gives the kitchen the opportunity to note dietary needs; however, no published allergen protocol was found, so specific allergies should be raised directly when booking.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based Buddhist temple cuisine — no meat or fish in the kitchen — making it one of the safest choices in Kyoto for vegans and vegetarians.
Shojin/fucha tradition uses kombu and shiitake dashi rather than fish-based dashi, so the umami base is plant-derived.

cultural_comfort.note: It is a traditional temple kaiseki experience that is completely reservation-only and best arranged in advance (often via an English booking concierge), so a few words of Japanese or an interpreter-style booking service smooths the visit.

places.name: リトルヘブン (LITTLE HEAVEN)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free-friendly spot. The kitchen uses fresh namafu (生麩, a wheat-gluten product) and serves house-made natural-yeast bread, so wheat/gluten is central to the menu. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised, and the owner states they normally do not accommodate food allergies. Soy sauce is used but there is no confirmation it is wheat-free (standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat) — gluten-sensitive diners should treat this as unsafe unless they confirm directly. A celiac/GF traveler should ask before booking and assume gluten is present.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen: 100% plant-based with no dairy, eggs, fish, meat, or honey used anywhere. Dishes are built around seasonal Kyoto vegetables plus fresh yuba (tofu skin) and namafu (fresh wheat gluten). Everything served is vegan, so plant-based diners can order any item on the (reservation-only) course without modification.
places.hours_raw: 12:00 onward (reservation only). Hours and open days vary; sources list roughly 12:00 onward, with some reporting Tue–Sat ~12:00–18:00 and course meals on certain days only.
places.neighborhood: Ukyo-ku, Sagano/Uzumasa area — about a 5-minute walk from Ka
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Little Heaven is a tiny (around ten-seat) reservation-only vegan restaurant tucked into the Sagano/Uzumasa side of Ukyo-ku, near Katabiranotsuji. It serves a fully plant-based, multinational take on Kyoto cooking — a modern, westernized cousin of shojin ryori

chef_bio.background: Little Heaven is a tiny (around ten-seat) reservation-only vegan restaurant tucked into the Sagano/Uzumasa side of Ukyo-ku, near Katabiranotsuji. It serves a fully plant-based, multinational take on Kyoto cooking — a modern, westernized cousin of shojin ryori — built around seasonal Kyoto vegetables together with fresh yuba (tofu skin) and namafu (fresh wheat gluten).
chef_bio.philosophy: A quality-over-volume ethos: the kitchen deliberately limits how many guests it takes so each course can be prepared with care and presented as 'a work of art.' Everything is 100% plant-based — no dairy, eggs, fish, meat or honey — using Kyoto's seasonal produce.
chef_bio.specialty: Multi-course vegan tasting menus. Signature dishes include a much-praised plate of vegan 'sushi' that mimics eel, tuna and scallops, beautifully arranged seasonal small plates, yuba prepared several ways, house-made natural-yeast bread, smoked tofu, a vegan meatloaf main, and soy-cream desserts such as a matcha tofu 'cheesecake.'

anecdotes.text: Reviewers repeatedly single out the faux-sushi plate, which convincingly replicates eel, tuna and scallops, and a remarkably creamy matcha tofu cheesecake, describing the cooking as creative and meticulously presented.
A diner noted the owner's attentive, customer-centered hospitality, including thoughtful care for an elderly guest with mobility difficulties.

The owner reportedly has food allergies themselves and understands the challenges, occasionally making accommodations when no other guests are present, though they state they normally do not handle allergy requests.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: HappyCow and Tabelog confirm the ~10-seat reservation-only Sagano/Ukyo-ku vegan restaurant near Katabiranotsuji, its yuba/namafu and faux-sushi (eel/tuna/scallops), the matcha tofu 'cheesecake', and the owner's own food allergies with a stated policy of normally not handling allergy requests (no safety guarantee is implied).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central to the menu: fresh namafu (wheat gluten) and house-made natural-yeast bread are signature ingredients, so gluten is present throughout the kitchen and cross-contact with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is used in the cooking but there is no confirmation it is wheat-free; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, so assume gluten unless confirmed directly with the restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is 100% vegan — no dairy, eggs, fish, meat or honey are used anywhere — so the risk of animal-product cross-contact is essentially eliminated.
Vegewel and HappyCow both list the restaurant as fully plant-based / 100% vegan with all dishes safe for vegans.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner states they normally do not accommodate food allergies, but has made exceptions when no other guests are present; the owner also has food allergies and understands the difficulties. Discuss any needs at the time of (mandatory) reservation.
English-speaking staff and an English menu are available, which makes communicating dietary questions easier.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based kitchen, so vegan diners can order anything on the course menu without worrying about hidden animal products, dashi or honey.
Intimate, low-volume operation where dishes are prepared with care; reviewers praise the creativity, presentation and the owner's attentive hospitality.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff and an English menu are available, and reservations are accepted by email in English, so a foreign student can book and dine comfortably — just remember booking ahead is mandatory.

places.name: 篩月（しげつ） (Tenryuji Shigetsu)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is advertised. The cuisine is plant-based and many components (rice, simmered vegetables, sesame tofu, pickles) are naturally wheat-free, but standard Japanese soy sauce and miso glazes typically contain wheat, and some dishes use wheat gluten (fu) or tempura-style preparations common in shojin ryori. A celiac/strict-GF diner should ask in advance — the temple takes reservations by phone (3 days ahead for the larger courses) where dietary requests can be raised. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Shojin ryori prepared by Tenryu-ji temple's own kitchen using no animal-based ingredients whatsoever ("動物性の素材を一切使用せず") — built on vegetables, mountain vegetables (sansai), wild plants, and seaweed. Traditional shojin cuisine also excludes the five pungent vegetables (garlic, onion, etc.). Dashi is plant-based (kombu seaweed) rather than fish-based, so the kitchen is genuinely vegan, not merely vegetarian. Signature dishes include goma-dofu (sesame tofu), yuba (tofu skin) in clear broth, miso-glazed eggplant, and seasonal simmered/pickled vegetables. The full course is a one-soup, five/six/seven-side-dish set depending on the tier.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–14:00 (lunch only); closed Thursdays
places.neighborhood: Saga-Arashiyama, Ukyo Ward — inside the World Heritage Tenry
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Shigetsu is the dining hall of Tenryu-ji, the head temple of the Tenryu-ji branch of Rinzai Zen Buddhism and a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Arashiyama. The restaurant occupies the Ryumontei pavilion within the temple's celebrated Sogen Pond garden and is run

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan — Saga-Arashiyama, on the grounds of Tenryu-ji temple.
chef_bio.background: Shigetsu is the dining hall of Tenryu-ji, the head temple of the Tenryu-ji branch of Rinzai Zen Buddhism and a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Arashiyama. The restaurant occupies the Ryumontei pavilion within the temple's celebrated Sogen Pond garden and is run directly by the temple, serving the shojin ryori that grew out of Zen monastic practice.
chef_bio.philosophy: Shojin ryori embodies the Buddhist principle of reverence for life: meals are made entirely from plants — no meat, fish, or animal products — and traditionally without the five pungent vegetables, harmonizing with nature and the seasons. Diners are invited to eat mindfully, reflecting the Zen view of eating as part of spiritual training.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal shojin ryori multi-dish set meals (Yuki, Tsuki, Hana) centered on goma-dofu (sesame tofu), yuba, miso-glazed and simmered seasonal vegetables, mountain vegetables, and pickles, served in private tatami rooms overlooking the garden.

anecdotes.text: Recognized in the MICHELIN Guide Kyoto-Osaka as a Bib Gourmand (exceptional food at moderate prices) and awarded a MICHELIN Green Star for its sustainable, plant-based ethos.

A vegan diner described being 'stunned before we even tasted one bite of food,' served roughly 11–12 artfully arranged dishes — chilled sesame tofu, yuba in clear broth, miso-glazed eggplant — while dining shoeless in a private tatami room with shoji doors opening onto the Tenryu-ji gardens.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tenryu-ji's official pages and Kyoto tourism listings confirm Shigetsu is temple-run, served in the Ryumontei hall beside the Sogenchi pond garden, offering the Yuki/Tsuki/Hana shojin sets, and note its Michelin Bib Gourmand + Green Star listing.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce and miso glazes used in shojin ryori typically contain wheat; the restaurant does not advertise wheat-free seasonings, so strict-GF diners should confirm in advance. (General shojin/Japanese cuisine norm; not specifically stated by the venue.)

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fully plant-based — no animal-based ingredients are used at all ('動物性の素材を一切使用せず') — so there is no meat or fish cross-contact in the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Meals are reservation-based by phone (075-882-9725, 9:00–17:00), giving an opportunity to communicate allergies or dietary needs ahead of the visit; no formal allergy-handling program is published.

positives.text: Entirely vegan kitchen: a temple-run shojin ryori operation that uses zero animal products, with plant-based (kombu/seaweed) dashi rather than fish dashi — eliminating dairy, egg, meat and fish cross-contact concerns.
Set-course format with consistent, well-documented dishes (sesame tofu, yuba, simmered seasonal vegetables) makes it easy to know what you are eating.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous tourist temple that welcomes many foreign visitors and has an English website, but the dining service itself is traditional and Japanese-run, so a few polite Japanese phrases (and a phone reservation, ideally with help) smooth the experience.

places.name: 白雲庵 (Hakuunan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free by default. Fucha ryori centers on wheat-heavy preparations: seasoned tempura (wheat-flour battered and fried), mock-meat "modoki" dishes that often use wheat gluten (fu/seitan), and Japanese soy sauce that typically contains wheat. There is no published gluten-free menu and no information about a dedicated fryer. A celiac/gluten-sensitive diner would need to discuss this directly when reserving (reservation required by 5pm the previous day), and even then safe options would likely be very limited. No GF accommodation is confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Hakuunan serves traditional Obaku fucha ryori (普茶料理), a Chinese-derived form of Buddhist shojin cuisine that is plant-based by tradition: no meat, fish, eggs or dairy. The standard course is built on "two soups and six dishes" (二汁六菜) and includes sesame tofu (goma-dofu), yoshino-ni stew, seasonal vegetables and seasoned tempura. Classic shojin/fucha cooking generally avoids animal products, but some traditional kitchens use bonito (katsuo) dashi unless kombu-only stock is requested, so vegans should state their needs when reserving. The restaurant's listing advises telling staff about vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies so the menu can be adjusted, and both vegan and vegetarian course options are noted as available. Treat it as plant-based-by-default but confirm dashi and any honey at booking.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (last order 14:00). Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays (open on public holidays, closed the following day). Reservation required by 5:00pm the previous day. Cash only.
places.neighborhood: Gokasho, Uji City (Obaku area) — directly in front of the ma
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Hakuunan is a long-established fucha ryori restaurant set directly across from the main gate of Obaku-san Manpuku-ji, the head temple of the Obaku Zen school in Uji — an area famous as a birthplace of Uji tea. The Obaku school was founded in Japan by the Chine

chef_bio.background: Hakuunan is a long-established fucha ryori restaurant set directly across from the main gate of Obaku-san Manpuku-ji, the head temple of the Obaku Zen school in Uji — an area famous as a birthplace of Uji tea. The Obaku school was founded in Japan by the Chinese monk Ingen (Yinyuan Longqi), who arrived in 1654 and brought with him 'fucha ryori,' a Chinese-style Buddhist vegetarian cuisine distinct from native Japanese shojin ryori.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithful to the Obaku fucha tradition introduced from Ming-dynasty China, the kitchen turns carefully selected seasonal vegetables and plant proteins into refined, colorful dishes — vegetarian cooking treated as a guest-honoring art rather than austere abstinence.
chef_bio.specialty: Obaku fucha ryori served as a 'two soups and six dishes' course: sesame tofu (goma-dofu), yoshino-ni stew, seasonal vegetable preparations and seasoned tempura, plated to be visually elegant and rich in flavor despite being entirely plant-based.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sits at the gate of Manpuku-ji, founded by the Chinese monk Ingen, who introduced fucha ryori to Japan in the 17th century — making Hakuunan a place to taste one of Japan's four great schools of Buddhist vegetarian cuisine in its home setting.
Its signature course follows the classic '二汁六菜' (two soups, six dishes) format, featuring goma-dofu (sesame tofu) and seasoned tempura among the carefully selected seasonal dishes.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official hakuunan.com describes it as a fucha ryori (精進料理) 老舗 at Manpuku-ji's gate serving a 二汁六菜 course of goma-dofu, yoshino-ni and 味付天麩羅, and notes it accommodates even single diners.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fucha courses include seasoned tempura, deep-fried in wheat-flour batter; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is documented, so shared frying oil is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free soy sauce (tamari) is advertised; standard Japanese shoyu used in shojin/fucha cooking typically contains wheat. Confirm at booking if gluten is a concern.

vegan_cross_contact.text: As a dedicated Buddhist fucha/shojin kitchen, the menu is plant-based by tradition with no meat, fish, eggs or dairy prepared on site, greatly reducing animal cross-contact.
Some traditional shojin kitchens use bonito-based dashi unless kombu-only stock is requested; vegans should confirm the dashi when reserving.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant's listing advises diners to tell staff about vegetarian/vegan requirements and any allergies so the menu can be chosen accordingly, indicating willingness to accommodate stated needs.

positives.text: Fully plant-based Buddhist temple cuisine: no meat, fish, shellfish, dairy, eggs or honey by tradition, so no risk of animal-protein contamination from a mixed menu.
Reservation-only (by 5pm the prior day) means the kitchen can prepare for your dietary needs in advance rather than improvising on the spot.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional, reservation-only temple-cuisine restaurant where some basic Japanese and advance booking (by phone or online the day before) will smooth things, though a polite konnichiwa and clearly stated dietary needs go a long way.

places.name: 東林院 (Tōrin-in)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. Shojin ryori relies heavily on regular Japanese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) for simmered dishes and dressings, and may include wheat gluten preparations (fu) and tofu products marinated in soy sauce. There is no indication of wheat-free soy sauce or a dedicated GF kitchen. A gluten-sensitive or celiac diner must inquire directly in advance; assume soy sauce contains wheat unless confirmed otherwise.
places.vegan_detail: Tōrin-in serves traditional shojin ryori (Buddhist temple cuisine), which by religious practice excludes all meat, fish, and animal products — it is plant-based throughout. The abbot Nishikawa Genbō, a published shojin cookbook author, prepares the meals. Documented dishes include rice, mushroom-and-mitsuba clear soup, chrysanthemum-greens shiraae (tofu dressing), simmered carrot and burdock, furofuki daikon, and pickles; seasonal events such as the Azuki-gayu (red-bean rice porridge) gathering serve azuki porridge, simmered black beans, daikon with abura-age, rape-blossom shiraae, and salted kelp. Shojin ryori traditionally uses kombu (kelp) and shiitake dashi rather than fish (bonito) dashi, so the broth is plant-based — but specific ingredient confirmation is not published, so a strict vegan should still confirm on the day.
places.hours_raw: Regular shojin ryori lunch is by reservation; weekly cooking classes Tue & Fri 10:00-13:00. Special seasonal openings (no reservation): "Admiring the Sal Blossoms" gathering mid-June to early July and "Azuki-gayu" gathering in mid-to-late January, roughly 9:30-15:00 (last entry 14:30), serving shojin meals. The temple is otherwise normally closed to the general public.
places.neighborhood: Ukyo Ward, Hanazono — inside the Myoshin-ji temple complex (
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Tōrin-in is a sub-temple of the great Rinzai Zen complex of Myoshin-ji, founded in 1531 by Hosokawa Ujitsuna to honor his late father and later rebuilt by the warlord Yamana Toyokuni. Its main image is Kannon Bosatsu. The temple is famous as the 'temple of the

chef_bio.origin: Hanazono, Kyoto — within the Myoshin-ji temple grounds
chef_bio.background: Tōrin-in is a sub-temple of the great Rinzai Zen complex of Myoshin-ji, founded in 1531 by Hosokawa Ujitsuna to honor his late father and later rebuilt by the warlord Yamana Toyokuni. Its main image is Kannon Bosatsu. The temple is famous as the 'temple of the sal trees' (sarasōju), with more than a dozen Japanese stewartia in the front garden whose white blossoms fall in a single day during the rainy season — the source of its celebrated June-July 'Admiring the Sal Blossoms' gathering.
chef_bio.philosophy: The abbot teaches not just cooking but the spirit of shojin — wasting no ingredient and minimizing waste of water and electricity — treating the meal as Zen practice. Guests describe gentle, refined, distinctly Kyoto flavors.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal shojin ryori (Zen vegetarian temple cuisine): rice, clear soups, tofu shiraae dressings, simmered vegetables, furofuki daikon, and the wintertime azuki red-bean porridge served at the New Year Azuki-gayu gathering.

anecdotes.text: The resident abbot Nishikawa Genbō entered Zen training at Ryoan-ji and Zuiryu-ji and became head priest of Tōrin-in in 1984; he opened the normally-closed temple to the public, offering shojin meals, temple lodging, and weekly cooking classes, and has authored several shojin cookbooks including works on Zen temple home cooking.
Every Tuesday and Friday the abbot personally teaches a shojin ryori class in the temple's training hall, sharing both recipes and the mindful, anti-waste ethos behind them.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyounoryouri.jp confirms Nishikawa Genbō is abbot of Tōrin-in at Myoshin-ji, trained at Ryoan-ji and Zuiryu-ji, became head priest in 1984 and is a published shojin author; kyoto-kankou lists his Tuesday/Friday shojin-ryori training classes; shukuken confirms the sal-tree and Azuki-gayu events.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information is published on whether the soy sauce, dressings, or simmering liquids are wheat-free; standard Japanese soy sauce used in temple cooking normally contains wheat, so gluten-sensitive diners should assume wheat is present unless confirmed.

positives.text: This is a fully plant-based shojin ryori kitchen run by a Zen temple, so by religious practice no meat or fish is used — there is no animal cross-contact in the cooking, a strong baseline for vegan diners.
Meals are prepared by the abbot himself, a published shojin cookbook author, with documented all-vegetable menus (e.g., shiraae tofu dressings, simmered vegetables, furofuki daikon, azuki porridge).

cultural_comfort.note: This is a working Zen sub-temple run by a Japanese-speaking abbot with a formal reservation-by-postcard system and no published English support, so a foreign student should arrange a Japanese-speaking helper and basic temple etiquette in advance.

places.name: apelila（アペリラ） (apelila)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-forward bakery. The signature products are breads and baked goods made from house-milled Kyoto whole-wheat flour, so gluten is central to nearly everything. No gluten-free menu, certification, or dedicated preparation is mentioned by any source. Not suitable for celiacs or those avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: 100% plant-based (vegan) bakery and café. All breads and baked goods use no butter, eggs, or dairy. Bread is made from house-milled Kyoto whole-wheat flour and a natural sourdough starter kept going for ~20 years. The café serves lunch including a vegan cheeseburger plate (Kyoto whole-wheat bun, chickpea patty, fermented soy yogurt), plus vegan donuts, melon bread, caramel cream pastries, cookies and waffles. Soy-based cream and plant milks are used in place of dairy. No animal products are used anywhere in the kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Approx. 11:00–16:00 (lunch from 12:00), or until sold out; days vary — most sources indicate open Wed–Sun, closed Mon–Tue; check Instagram @apelilapan.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward (左京区), Takano area near Demachiyanagi station and
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] apelila is a 100% plant-based bakery and café tucked into the Takano neighborhood of Sakyo Ward, a short walk from Demachiyanagi station and Shimogamo Shrine, sitting along the river on Kawabata-dori. It was opened in 2015 by owner-baker Takahashi-san, who pre

chef_bio.background: apelila is a 100% plant-based bakery and café tucked into the Takano neighborhood of Sakyo Ward, a short walk from Demachiyanagi station and Shimogamo Shrine, sitting along the river on Kawabata-dori. It was opened in 2015 by owner-baker Takahashi Sayaka, who had previously taught bread-making classes before turning her craft into a shop offering more than 20 kinds of bread.
chef_bio.philosophy: Takahashi-san is reportedly not vegan herself; she chooses to bake entirely without animal-derived ingredients because she wants people to discover how delicious naturally-fermented yeast and simple, well-sourced ingredients can be. The shop leans on sustainability and quality: in-house milled Kyoto-grown whole wheat, organic dried fruit, and a decades-old starter.
chef_bio.specialty: Naturally-leavened vegan breads and baked goods made with a house-milled Kyoto whole-wheat flour and a sourdough starter that has been continuously fed for roughly two decades; plus a café lunch headlined by a vegan cheeseburger plate with a chickpea patty and fermented soy yogurt, alongside donuts, melon bread, caramel cream pastries, cookies and waffles.

anecdotes.text: The bread is built on a natural sourdough starter that has been continuously fed and kept alive for roughly 19–20 years, paired with whole-wheat flour milled in-house from Kyoto-grown grain.
Takahashi Sayaka opened apelila in 2015 after previously running bread-making classes, and keeps animal ingredients out of her bread to let customers taste the umami of fermentation.
The café has a calming atmosphere with a small zen garden and a back seating area where diners remove their shoes.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: orangepage confirms the ~20-year continuously-fed natural starter and in-house-milled Kyoto whole wheat at this Takano/Sakyo-Ward vegan bakery; LEE names owner Takahashi Sayaka, her 2015 opening, prior bread-making classes and her focus on fermentation's umami.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is everywhere: this is a whole-wheat bakery with house-milled flour, so flour dust and gluten cross-contact are unavoidable throughout the kitchen. Not safe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely plant-based kitchen with no butter, eggs, or dairy used in any product, so there is no animal cross-contact to worry about.

staff_allergy_handling.text: English is limited and menu labels are mostly Japanese, but staff are reportedly eager to help answer ingredient questions.

positives.text: 100% vegan bakery and café — every bread, baked good, and lunch item is plant-based, so vegans can order anything on the menu with confidence.
Uses soy-based cream and plant milks plus fermented soy yogurt in place of all dairy.

cultural_comfort.note: Menus and labels are mostly in Japanese with limited English, but staff are reportedly eager to help with ingredient questions, so a few Japanese phrases will smooth your visit.

places.name: 豆乳ラーメン専門店 豆禅 (MameZen (Tonyu Ramen Senmonten Towzen))
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated GF kitchen, but genuinely GF-aware: gluten-free rice noodles are available as a swap (+250 yen), and the official menu states a wheat-free soy sauce is used when the rice noodles are selected. Standard ramen noodles contain wheat. The kitchen uses a single boiling pot but separate preparation can be requested, so a celiac should explicitly ask for the rice-noodle/wheat-free-soy preparation and confirm separate cooking.
places.vegan_detail: Everything served is 100% vegan, no animal products at all. Since 2014 the kitchen follows an "Oriental Vegan" approach rooted in shojin (Buddhist temple) cuisine, excluding eggs, fish stock, the five pungent alliums (no green onion/garlic/onion), and using no fish dashi. Signature dishes are the Musashi soy-milk ramen (yuba/tofu skin, mizuna, pickled plum, yuzu, sansho mushrooms, seaweed) and a soybean-meat tantan ramen.
places.neighborhood: Shimogamo, Sakyo-ku, near Shimogamo Shrine; about 2 min from
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] MameZen (also styled TowZen) opened on July 26, 2004 in Kyoto's Shimogamo district as a hidden, speakeasy-style soy-milk ramen house. Its founder, Minoru Yonekawa, spent about ten years managing a Kyoto tofu restaurant before reframing his cooking as food heal

chef_bio.background: MameZen (also styled TowZen) opened on July 26, 2004 in Kyoto's Shimogamo district as a hidden, speakeasy-style soy-milk ramen house. Its founder, Minoru Yonekawa, spent about ten years managing a Kyoto tofu restaurant before reframing his cooking as food healing cuisine.
chef_bio.philosophy: Completely animal-free cooking grounded in Zen shojin-ryori principles, avoiding the five pungent alliums, and pursuing the healing that food brings, framing veganism as kinder to health and environment.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-milk-based ramen, the signature Musashi bowl topped with yuba, mizuna, pickled plum, yuzu, seaweed and sansho mushrooms, plus a soybean-meat tantan ramen; vegan sushi by advance reservation.

anecdotes.text: Began as a hidden retreat pairing soy-milk ramen with flute performances and Zenbient meditation, transitioning around 2009 into a community gathering space.
Went fully 100% vegan on January 1, 2014, removing eggs, fish stock and onions; celebrated its 20th anniversary in July 2024.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mamezen.com's About page states founder Minoru Yonekawa managed a Kyoto tofu restaurant ~10 years before opening TowZen on 2004/7/26 as a hidden retreat with flute/Zenbient events, opened up around April 2009, and went fully vegan on 2014/1/1 (removing eggs, onions and fish broth).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen uses a single boiling pot, but separate preparation is available on request, important for a celiac ordering gluten-free rice noodles alongside wheat ramen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A wheat-free soy sauce is used when the gluten-free rice noodles are selected; standard ramen uses wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is 100% vegan with no animal products on premises, so there is no meat, fish or dairy cross-contact.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Menu explicitly offers gluten-free rice noodles and notes separate preparation can be requested.

positives.text: Fully vegan, animal-free kitchen rooted in shojin cuisine, no fish dashi, eggs or dairy anywhere, very low-risk for vegan travelers.
Offers a genuine gluten-free path (rice noodles plus wheat-free soy sauce), uncommon for a ramen shop.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains a full English website and English menu and is well-known among foreign vegan travelers, so an English-speaking student should feel comfortable here.

noodles.notes: Thin straight wheat noodles are standard; the shop also offers fully gluten-free rice-flour noodles and a chlorella (algae) noodle as alternatives. Soup richness can be adjusted, and there are charcoal-infused and chlorella-infused broth variations.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tonyu (soy milk) ramen — organic soy milk simmered with a plant-based dashi into a thick, creamy, silky white broth. The "Musashi" bowl (mushroom and Japanese pepper topped with fresh yuba and plum paste) is the house standard; a soy-meat tantan version seasoned with miso is the other pillar.
ramen.chashu: No pork or chicken chashu. The protein heart is plant-based: fresh yuba (tofu skin) in the Musashi bowl, or seasoned soy meat in the tantan-style bowl.

gf.note: A genuinely gluten-free path exists: rice-flour noodles plus gluten-free soy sauce in the tare. Confirm preparation with staff, as the default noodle is wheat-based and shared equipment may apply.

vegan.note: The entire menu is plant-based shojin (Zen Buddhist) cooking with no animal products: organic soy milk and a vegetable/mushroom dashi, miso and gluten-free soy sauce tare, yuba and soy meat in place of pork. A fully vegan bowl is the default, not an exception.

ramen.pantheon: A rare Kyoto original built on Zen shojin principles, where a tonkotsu-like richness is conjured entirely from organic soy milk simmered down with a plant dashi until thick and silky — no pork, no fish, no animal fat. You taste the soul of the bowl in that creamy soy depth: a meditative, meat-free reinvention of paitan ramen rather than any regional lineage.

places.highlight: A rare Kyoto original built on Zen shojin principles, where a tonkotsu-like richness is conjured entirely from organic soy milk simmered down with a plant dashi until thick and silky — no pork, no fish, no animal fat. You taste the soul of the bowl in that creamy soy depth: a meditative, meat-free reinvention of paitan ramen rather than any regional lineage.

places.name: 喫茶ホーボー堂 (Kissa Hobodo (Cafe Hobodo))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is made by the cafe and none of the sources document GF accommodation. The menu includes clearly wheat-containing items (homemade flatbread, spring rolls/gyoza wrappers, falafel, sweets) and Japanese set meals typically use standard miso and soy sauce, which usually contain wheat. Brown-rice and rice-based dishes (e.g. edamame rice, some ohagi) may be naturally gluten-free, but no dedicated GF preparation or wheat-free soy sauce is confirmed. A gluten-sensitive diner should ask in person about each dish; the staff speak some English.
places.vegan_detail: A fully plant-based (vegan) cafe and bookshop. Multiple sources confirm everything on the menu is vegan ("全てヴィーガンOK") - no meat, fish, dairy, eggs or honey. The owner's wife, who runs the kitchen, follows a plant-based lifestyle and the cafe was founded so "everyone can eat together without worry." Food centers on seasonal vegetables sourced from local organic/natural farms: a daily set meal (brown or edamame rice, miso soup, and several vegetable side dishes), plus dishes like vegetable-and-bean curry, pumpkin-carrot soup, spring rolls/gyoza, falafel, and six varieties of brown-rice ohagi (sweet rice cakes). Because the kitchen is entirely vegan, there is no animal-protein cross-contact.
places.hours_raw: Fri-Sun, doors from 10:00, food service 11:00/11:30-17:00 (last order ~16:30). Closed Mon-Thu. Also closes irregularly on some open days - check Instagram (@hobodocafe) for the monthly schedule.
places.neighborhood: Sakyō-ku, on Niōmon-dōri just west of Higashiōji-dōri; about
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Hobodo is a tiny vegan cafe-and-bookshop run by a husband-and-wife team, Daijiro and Misayo Satoh, who opened it in spring 2016. Daijiro handles the coffee (using beans from a local Kyoto roaster, with Italian Blend and Panama varieties), while Misayo, who liv

chef_bio.background: Hobodo is a tiny vegan cafe-and-bookshop run by a husband-and-wife team, Daijiro and Misayo Satoh, who opened it in 2014. Daijiro handles the coffee (using beans from a local Kyoto roaster, Cafe de Corazon, with an Italian Blend and Panama variety), while Misayo, who lives a plant-based lifestyle, cooks the food. The couple deliberately keep the place small, book-filled and open only a few days a week so guests can linger and read while they eat.
chef_bio.philosophy: They named the cafe after early-20th-century American 'hobos,' embracing a free, independent spirit. The kitchen is entirely plant-based so that, in their words, everyone can sit down and eat together without worry; they prioritize seasonal vegetables from local organic and natural farms and balance the shop around family life.
chef_bio.specialty: A daily-changing vegetable set meal (brown or edamame rice, miso soup and several seasonal vegetable side dishes), plus several kinds of brown-rice ohagi in unusual flavors (egoma/perilla, rum-raisin, hazelnut, roasted-tea and more), spring rolls, falafel and homemade flatbread.

anecdotes.text: The couple chose the name 'Hobodo' in homage to American hobos of the early 1900s, embracing that wandering, independent spirit for their homey DIY cafe.
Misayo creates a fully vegan menu specifically so that 'everyone can eat together without concern,' sourcing produce directly from organic and naturally-cultivated farms.

Their weekly-changing brown-rice-and-seasonal-vegetable bento is popular as a takeaway to carry to the rooftop terrace of the Kyoto City KYOCERA Museum of Art (the 'Higashiyama Cube'), a short walk away.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: karafuneya and other Kyoto listings say the Satoh couple opened Hobodo in 2014, open only a few days a week; Daijiro sources beans from roaster Cafe de Corazon; the name honors early-1900s American hobos; crea.bunshun recommends buying its brown-rice/vegetable bento to eat at the nearby Kyocera Museum rooftop terrace.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol is documented; wheat-based items (homemade flatbread, gyoza/spring-roll wrappers, falafel, sweets) are prepared in the same small kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat should be assumed unless confirmed in person.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals include miso soup and seasoned vegetable dishes; no source confirms wheat-free tamari is used, so standard wheat-containing soy sauce and miso should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is plant-based, so there is no risk of cross-contact with meat, fish, dairy, eggs or honey - every item served is vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner-couple speak some English and provide an English menu, making it feasible to ask about ingredients; no formal allergen protocol is documented, so confirm specific needs directly with them.

positives.text: 100% vegan cafe and bookshop - all menu items are confirmed vegan, eliminating animal-product cross-contact concerns.
Produce is sourced from local organic and naturally-cultivated farms, and the menu is built around fresh seasonal vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: The friendly couple who run it speak some English and have an English menu, so a foreign student can manage comfortably with just a little Japanese.

places.name: 西源院 (Seigen-in (Saigen-in))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The core dish is tofu, vegetables and the seven herbs of yudofu, which can be naturally GF, but the dipping sauce and any soy sauce / seasonings are an unknown gluten risk (typical Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and the dipping sauce is reportedly fish-based and otherwise unspecified). The shojin set may include other components. A gluten-sensitive diner must ask directly about the sauce, soy sauce and condiments; staff speak only limited English, so a written allergy card in Japanese is advisable.
places.vegan_detail: Seigen-in serves a seven-herb yudofu (hot tofu pot, ~2,000 yen) and a shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian) set with tofu pot (~4,000 yen). The food itself is plant-based tofu-and-vegetable temple fare, so the bulk of the meal is suitable for vegetarians and largely for vegans. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: multiple HappyCow reviewers report that the dipping sauce served with the yudofu is fish-based (not vegan) — so despite the VegeMap "all-vegan" tag, a strict vegan should explicitly decline the dipping sauce and ask what condiments contain. As a temple-style (shojin) kitchen it should avoid meat, but vegans cannot assume the dashi/sauce is plant-based and must ask. Best treated as vegetarian-friendly with vegan possible on request.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (food served within temple opening hours); closed Wednesday and Thursday. Note that some sources list slightly different hours, so confirm seasonally.
places.neighborhood: Ukyo-ku, on the grounds of Ryoan-ji Temple (World Heritage r
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Seigen-in is a sub-temple dining hall set inside the grounds of Ryoan-ji, where guests eat in a traditional tatami room looking out over a lush garden and the thousand-year-old Kyoyochi pond. Diners traditionally buy temple admission and then settle in for a q

chef_bio.origin: Within the grounds of Ryoan-ji, the World Heritage Zen temple famous for its dry rock garden in northwest Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Seigen-in is a sub-temple dining hall set inside the grounds of Ryoan-ji, where guests eat in a traditional tatami room looking out over a lush garden and the historic Kyoyochi pond. Diners traditionally buy temple admission and then settle in for a quiet tofu lunch as part of a visit to the rock garden.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rooted in shojin ryori, the meat-free Buddhist temple cuisine tradition: simple, seasonal, plant-centred food eaten slowly while contemplating the temple garden.
chef_bio.specialty: Seven-herb yudofu (nanakusa yudofu) — a simmered hot-tofu pot with vegetables and seven herbs — and a shojin ryori (Buddhist temple cuisine) set built around the same tofu pot, served with house-made sauce and condiments.

anecdotes.text: Guests dine on tatami in a traditional temple room overlooking a well-kept garden and the Kyoyochi pond, with the experience valued as much for the serene setting as for the food itself.
The two mainstays are a seven-herb yudofu pot and a tofu-centred shojin ryori set, served with house-made dipping sauce and condiments.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-kankou confirms Seigen-in inside Ryoan-ji serves 七草湯豆腐 and a shojin set (¥4,000, or ¥2,000 for the tofu pot alone) with house-made sauce and 薬味, in a garden-view setting.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce / seasoning ingredients are not disclosed; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so a gluten-sensitive diner must ask whether the dipping sauce and any soy sauce are wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Reviewers report the yudofu dipping sauce is fish-based (not vegan); a strict vegan should decline the standard dipping sauce and ask which condiments are plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: An English menu is available but staff speak limited English; a written Japanese allergy note is recommended for clear communication about sauce and dashi.

positives.text: A temple shojin-style kitchen centred on tofu and vegetables, so meat and most animal cross-contact is inherently low — the main animal-derived risk is the dipping sauce rather than the food itself.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the temple setting is used to foreign visitors, but staff speak only limited English, so a few Japanese phrases and a written allergy note will help — and note you must pay the Ryoan-ji temple admission to reach the restaurant.

places.name: 鴨川ベーカリー 京都本店 (KAMOGAWA BAKERY Kyoto Honten (Kamogawa B)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery — breads and bagels are made from domestic (Hokkaido) wheat flour, so products are not gluten-free and the environment is heavily flour-dusted. No gluten-free items are advertised. Not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Roughly 80% of the lineup is plant-based: the kitchen does not use butter, milk or eggs in its core breads, instead using soy-milk butter and confirming its beet sugar is animal-free. Most loaves (soy-milk butter "new white bread"/shokupan made with Hokkaido Haruyokoi wheat) and many bagels are vegan, and there is an egg-allergy-friendly "hobo-tama" sandwich. However it is NOT a fully vegan shop: some bagel sandwiches contain salmon, shrimp/avocado and cream cheese. Vegans should select the plant-based loaves/bagels and the plant-based burger and confirm fillings, since animal-based sandwich options share the counter.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (closes when sold out). Opened October 31, 2020.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo Ward, near Kawaramachi-Marutamachi; about 5 minutes'
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] KAMOGAWA BAKERY opened on October 31, 2020 in Kyoto's Kamigyo Ward, just west of the Kamogawa River near the Imperial Palace. It was created so that vegetarians, vegans and people with egg or dairy allergies could enjoy genuinely delicious bread alongside ever

chef_bio.background: KAMOGAWA BAKERY opened on October 31, 2020 in Kyoto's Kamigyo Ward, just west of the Kamogawa River near the Imperial Palace. It was created so that vegetarians, vegans and people with egg or dairy allergies could enjoy genuinely delicious bread alongside everyone else, baking with 100% Japanese wheat and plant-derived ingredients instead of the butter and milk usually relied on in baking.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's theme is 'ONE TABLE' - the idea that bread lovers and allergy sufferers can all sit at the same table and enjoy the same bread. Around 80% of the lineup is plant-based, and the bakery also pursues sustainability, using compostable cups for takeaway drinks, ethically sourced coffee, and supporting food-donation and children's-meal programs.
chef_bio.specialty: Chewy bagels made with Hokkaido 'Yumechikara' wheat, a soy-milk-butter 'new white bread'/shokupan made with Hokkaido 'Haruyokoi' wheat, and bagel sandwiches including a Kyoto shibazuke-pickle bagel using pickles from the established Kawakatsu Sohonke.

anecdotes.text: The bakery substitutes soy-milk butter for dairy so its signature 'new white bread' tastes sweet and creamy with no milk or butter, and it uses plant-based ingredients across roughly 80% of its lineup.
There is a second-floor cafe space where customers can sit and eat in, and an egg-allergy-friendly 'hobo-tama' (almost-egg) sandwich on the menu.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: leafkyoto confirms the Oct 31 2020 opening in Kamigyo-ku, the 'ONE TABLE' inclusive concept, ~80% vegan lineup, Yumechikara bagels, Haruyokoi soy-milk-butter shokupan, and the Kawakatsu-Sohonke shibazuke bagel; kamogawabakery.com confirms 100% domestic wheat and the hobo-tama sandwich.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-flour bakery; all bread and bagels contain wheat and the kitchen is flour-heavy, so there is no gluten-free safe option and high airborne flour cross-contact.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Roughly 80% plant-based, but not a fully vegan shop: some bagel sandwiches contain salmon, shrimp and cream cheese, so animal products are handled in the same kitchen. Choose the plant-based loaves, bagels and plant-based burger and confirm fillings.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop was founded with allergy sufferers in mind, avoids butter, milk and eggs in its core breads, and offers an egg-allergy-friendly sandwich, indicating staff awareness of egg/dairy allergies.

positives.text: Core breads use no butter, milk or eggs and rely on soy-milk butter and animal-free beet sugar, so plant-based options are clearly labeled and the menu is intentionally inclusive ('ONE TABLE').

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood bakery where you point at the bread you want, so a few words of Japanese and gesturing get you by, though English signage is limited.

places.name: ikuri café（イクリカフェ） (ikuri café)
places.gf_detail: Some naturally gluten-free items but not a GF-safe kitchen. The 100% buckwheat (Dattan) soba is naturally gluten-free, and a wheat-free plant-based soy sauce option is offered with the noodles. However, the brown rice udon contains wheat (roughly 30% whole brown-rice flour mixed with wheat flour), and there is no stated dedicated gluten-free preparation protocol or separate cooking water. A celiac diner should confirm cross-contact directly; soba and udon are likely boiled in shared water.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based whole-foods café — the entire menu is vegan. Dishes are built from unrefined, unprocessed ingredients with no animal protein, fat, or excess sugar/oil/salt. Offerings include an energy plant-based whole-food salad set, a soy-meat burger, brown rice udon, 100% buckwheat (Dattan) soba, brown-rice rice balls, daily vegetable soups, raw cakes/tarts (e.g. raw matcha tiramisu), sourdough toast, and soy/oat-milk beverages and smoothies. Broths use kombu (kelp) dashi only — no fish dashi. The kitchen also accommodates 五葷抜き (no five pungent alliums) on request.
places.hours_raw: Reception 9:00–17:00 (by appointment), closing 19:00. Closed Tuesdays and the 2nd, 4th & 5th Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Kita Ward, Shichiku area near the Kamogawa river; closest is
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] ikuri café is the plant-based whole-foods kitchen attached to Lu Cocon ikuri, a fully private natural beauty salon tucked into a green, residential pocket of Kita Ward near the Kamogawa river. Kaori Nonomura, who leads the business, took the helm after the fou

chef_bio.background: ikuri café is the plant-based whole-foods kitchen attached to Lu Cocon ikuri, a fully private natural beauty salon tucked into a green, residential pocket of Kita Ward near the Kamogawa river. Kaori Nonomura, who leads the business, took the helm as representative director after the founder — her husband — passed away, and over the years broadened ikuri's vision of beauty from hair and skin to the food that nourishes them from within.
chef_bio.philosophy: A whole-food ethos of using every part of the ingredient and choosing food that is 'kind to both people and the planet' (人と地球に優しい) — no animal protein or fat and minimal added sugar, oil and salt — so that what you eat supports your body's own natural healing power, skin and hair.
chef_bio.specialty: Unrefined, unprocessed plant-based whole-food dishes: an energy whole-food salad set, a soy-meat burger, 100% buckwheat soba and brown-rice udon, brown-rice rice balls, daily vegetable soups, and raw desserts, plus soy- and oat-milk smoothies.

anecdotes.text: The café's plant-based whole-foods direction grew out of the director's October 2019 visit to the Hippocrates Health Institute in West Palm Beach, Florida, after which ikuri committed to serving unrefined, unprocessed plant-based meals.

The café sits inside Lu Cocon ikuri, a completely private natural beauty salon offering henna coloring, head spa and skincare, framing food and beauty as one continuous idea of caring for body, skin and hair.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ikuri.jp confirms director Kaori Nonomura trained at the Hippocrates Health Institute (West Palm Beach) in Oct 2019, driving the whole-food plant-based menu at the salon-attached café; the message page states she became representative director after founder (husband) Ikumin Nonomura died in 2008, and uses the '人と地球にやさしい' ethos.

gf_cross_contamination.text: 100% buckwheat (Dattan) soba is naturally gluten-free, but brown rice udon on the same menu contains wheat (about 30% brown-rice flour mixed with wheat), and no separate cooking water or GF protocol is documented — likely shared boiling water between wheat and non-wheat noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A wheat-free plant-based soy sauce option is offered to accompany the buckwheat noodles, but it is not confirmed to be used throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is 100% plant-based, so there is no meat, fish, egg or dairy cross-contact; no fish dashi is used (kombu/kelp broth only).

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen accommodates 五葷抜き (omitting the five pungent alliums) on request, indicating willingness to adapt for dietary needs, though no formal allergy protocol is stated.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based, whole-foods menu with no artificial ingredients or additives — every dish is vegan, removing all animal-product cross-contact risk.
Soups use kombu (kelp) dashi only, with no hidden fish stock.
