cultural_comfort.note: Calling ahead is recommended since the café shares space with a private appointment-based salon; a few words of Japanese help when reserving, though the all-vegan menu makes ordering easy for foreign students.

places.name: 担担麺 胡 YEBISU 円町店 (Tantanmen Yebisu (Ebisu) Enmachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles, and tantanmen seasonings typically include wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free soy sauce indicated. A celiac or strict GF diner is not accommodated here. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Primarily a meat-based tantanmen restaurant, but it offers one dedicated vegan dish: the Vegetable Tantanmen (たっぷり野菜の担担麺), in which Kyoto tofu replaces the usual minced pork and the broth is made with no animal ingredients (per the VegeMap listing for this branch). Plenty of vegetables top it. Noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles. Most other items contain meat/dairy, so the vegan traveler should specifically order the vegetable/tofu tantanmen and confirm no animal products (動物性なし) when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 daily (L.O. 22:00), open year-round with occasional temporary closures.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō Ward, Nishinokyō Enmachi area — about 2 minutes' wal
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Tantanmen Yebisu is a Kyoto-born tantanmen specialty shop founded in 2008 in the Yamashina district, which has since grown to several branches around Kyoto and into Osaka, including this Enmachi location near JR Enmachi Station.

chef_bio.background: Tantanmen Yebisu is a Kyoto-born tantanmen specialty shop founded in 2008 in the Yamashina district, which has since grown to several branches around Kyoto and into Osaka, including this Enmachi location near JR Enmachi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated mission is to spread tantanmen culture from Kyoto and to dispel the assumption that tantanmen must be fiery, letting diners dial heat from none upward.
chef_bio.specialty: House tantanmen with adjustable spice levels; the signature for vegan diners is the Vegetable Tantanmen, where Kyoto tofu stands in for minced meat and the soup is built without animal ingredients.

anecdotes.text: To break the tantanmen-equals-spicy stereotype, the shop offers fully customizable spice levels starting from no spice at all.
The Enmachi branch's vegan vegetable tantanmen swaps Kyoto tofu in for the minced meat and keeps the broth free of animal ingredients.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-yebisu.com states '2008 京都山科創業', now multiple Kyoto branches plus an Osaka (Takatsuki) shop, with a mission to dispel that tantanmen must be spicy via spice levels adjustable from zero; vegemap documents the Enmachi branch's tofu-based vegan vegetable tantanmen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-noodle ramen kitchen; tantanmen noodles and soy-based seasonings contain wheat, with no gluten-free accommodation indicated, so heavy gluten cross-contact is expected.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free tamari indicated; standard Japanese wheat-containing soy sauce should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen primarily prepares meat tantanmen alongside the single vegan tofu bowl, so shared surfaces and utensils mean possible animal cross-contact; the vegan dish itself is formulated without animal ingredients per VegeMap.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific allergy-handling protocol is published; VegeMap recommends consulting staff before ordering regarding dietary restrictions.

positives.text: The vegetable tantanmen is intentionally built as a vegan dish, with Kyoto tofu instead of minced meat and a broth with no animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local noodle shop with table and counter seating; a few words of Japanese plus pointing at the vegetable tofu tantanmen will get you served comfortably, though staff may not speak much English.

places.name: オーシーブリトー（旧店名：ラ セグンダ ホヤ SUINA室町店） (OC Burrito (formerly La Segunda Jolla, S)
places.gf_detail: Corn tortillas are available as an alternative to flour/wheat tortillas, and the Vegan Taco BOX is built on corn tortillas, so naturally gluten-free components (corn tortilla, rice, black beans, salsa, guacamole, chips) exist. However this is a casual shared-prep counter with flour tortillas, cheese and house salsas on the same line, and no dedicated gluten-free or fryer protocol is documented. Whether the chips are fried in a shared fryer and whether the salsa/lime cream contain wheat is not confirmed. A celiac diner should ask staff directly and request corn tortillas.
places.vegan_detail: An omnivore Chipotle-style counter restaurant with clearly labeled plant-based items: a Vegan Taco BOX (3 corn tortillas, green rice/Arroz Verde, black beans, house salsa and chips) and a Vegan Burrito with chips and salsa. Note the separate "vegetarian" quesadilla/nachos/burrito are NOT vegan, since the standard build includes cheese and lime (sour) cream and flour tortillas; order specifically from the vegan items or ask for cheese and lime cream to be omitted. Beans, rice, guacamole and fresh salsa (tomato, lettuce, cilantro, pickles) are the plant-based core. Soy/tofu meat substitute is not documented; the plant filling is vegetables and beans rather than mock meat.
places.neighborhood: Shimogyo-ku, at the Shijo-Karasuma / Shijo-Muromachi crossin
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] A Southern California-style Mexican grill in the basement food area of the SUINA Muromachi building at the Shijo-Karasuma crossing, originally opened as La Segunda Jolla and now operating as OC Burrito. It runs Chipotle-style: you order at the counter, choose

chef_bio.background: A Southern California-style Mexican grill in the basement food court of the SUINA Muromachi building at the Shijo-Karasuma crossing, originally opened as La Segunda Jolla and now operating as OC Burrito. It is a casual counter spot — you order at the counter (via ticket machine), your food is cooked to order, and takeout is available — serving generous California-Mexican plates.
chef_bio.philosophy: Emphasizes fresh vegetables prepared daily and organic/natural ingredients (guacamole and salsa made fresh), with a stated effort to cater to vegetarian and vegan diners alongside its omnivore menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Burritos, tacos, quesadillas and nachos; the burrito is described as being 'like a large spring roll,' and the menu includes dedicated vegan options — a vegan taco set and a vegan burrito built on beans and vegetables.

anecdotes.text: A food-court counter spot at SUINA Muromachi serving California-Mexican fare with daily-made fresh guacamole and salsa, it explicitly offers vegan and vegetarian menu options and lets diners customize at the counter.
Uses high-grade jasmine (Thai fragrant) rice blended with brown rice, with black beans featuring prominently and refreshing hibiscus tea offered alongside the food.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: KPC describes La Segunda Jolla (now OC Burrito) at SUINA as South-California-style Mexican with organic ingredients, a jasmine+brown-rice blend, black beans, fresh guacamole, burritos 'like large spring rolls' and hibiscus tea; RETRIP confirms the basement food-court counter format (ticket machine, cooked to order) and its vegan/vegetarian options.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Casual shared-line counter where flour (wheat) tortillas are handled alongside corn tortillas; no dedicated gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contact with wheat is possible. Request corn tortillas and ask staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan and vegetarian items are prepared on the same counter line as meat, cheese and dairy lime cream; the explicitly Vegan Taco BOX and Vegan Burrito are designed to be plant-based, but shared utensils/surfaces mean some animal cross-contact is possible at a busy counter.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff have accommodated plant-based requests off-menu, including cooking rice separately without chicken stock for a vegan guest, indicating willingness to adjust on request.

positives.text: Offers clearly labeled vegan items (Vegan Taco BOX on corn tortillas with green rice, black beans, salsa and chips; Vegan Burrito), so plant-based diners do not have to fully improvise.
Emphasizes fresh vegetables prepared daily and made-fresh guacamole and salsa.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual order-at-the-counter spot popular with international visitors, with an English-readable build-your-own menu, so a foreign student can point and customize easily.

places.name: ロンハーマンカフェ 京都店 (Ron Herman Cafe Kyoto)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or GF labeling was found on the official site, the cafe menu pages, or listing directories. The kitchen cooks to order and welcomes allergy requests, so some dishes may be adaptable, but cross-contact with wheat (baked goods, cakes, sauces) is likely in a general cafe kitchen. A gluten-free diner should ask staff directly about each dish and seasonings.
places.vegan_detail: An omnivore California-style cafe that offers a small set of clearly labeled vegan dishes made without any animal-derived ingredients. Documented vegan items include a vegan rice bowl (black rice with 8 kinds of vegetables, around 1,350 yen), a vegan avocado cake (around 550 yen), and oat-milk banana juice made with plant-based milk (around 900 yen, with toppings like matcha, cacao nibs, mint). The cafe says it plans to expand its vegan lineup. Specific details on whether the vegan dishes use a wheat-free soy sauce or any dashi were not published; ask staff to confirm seasonings. Because the cafe cooks to order and explicitly invites guests to flag allergies and disliked ingredients, customization is realistic.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (food L.O. 19:00, drinks/dessert L.O. 19:30) daily
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, on Kawaramachi-dori just south of Sanjo; insid
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Ron Herman began in 1976 as a specialty fashion store on Melrose Avenue in Los Angeles, and its Japanese arm extended the lifestyle brand into cafes. The Kyoto cafe sits on the 6th floor of the Kyoto BAL building on Kawaramachi-dori, designed as an airy, light

chef_bio.background: Ron Herman began in 1976 as a specialty fashion store on Melrose Avenue in Los Angeles, and its Japanese arm extended the lifestyle brand into cafes. The Kyoto cafe sits on the 6th floor of the Kyoto BAL building on Kawaramachi-dori, designed as an airy, light-filled space with skylights, long communal tables and sofa seating.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe's ethos is 'more delicious, more healthy,' centered on vegetables. It runs a project in which contract farmers and the cafe work together from soil preparation onward to grow pesticide-free organic vegetables, and it deliberately operates without a rigid service manual, on the belief that hospitality has no single correct answer.
chef_bio.specialty: A relaxed California-style cafe menu built around fresh, seasonal vegetables, including dedicated vegan dishes such as a vegan rice bowl of black rice with eight kinds of vegetables, a vegan avocado cake, and oat-milk banana juice.

anecdotes.text: The cafe states it provides vegan menus made without any animal-derived ingredients and plans to expand the lineup further.
Because food is cooked after the order is placed, staff explicitly invite guests to mention food allergies or disliked ingredients so dishes can be adjusted as much as possible.
The cafe partners with contract farmers from soil preparation onward to grow pesticide-free organic vegetables for its dishes.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ronherman.jp/journal/34 confirms the vegan bowl (black rice + 8 vegetables), vegan avocado cake and oat-milk banana juice, the cook-to-order allergy accommodation, and the soil-up contract-farm pesticide-free vegetable program; bal-bldg.com confirms the Kyoto BAL 6F location.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free menu or dedicated GF preparation was documented; as a general cafe serving cakes and baked goods, wheat cross-contact is likely. Gluten-sensitive guests should confirm each dish with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore kitchen that also serves meat and dairy dishes, so plant-based items are not prepared in an animal-free environment; shared equipment cross-contact is possible. Cooked-to-order preparation does reduce some shared-batch risk.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff explicitly ask guests to tell them about food allergies and disliked ingredients, and because dishes are cooked to order they say they can accommodate requests as much as possible.

positives.text: Several clearly labeled vegan dishes are offered with no animal-derived ingredients, including a vegetable rice bowl, avocado cake and oat-milk drinks.
The cafe emphasizes pesticide-free organic vegetables grown with contract farmers from the soil up.
Cook-to-order kitchen with a stated willingness to adjust dishes for allergies and dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: An international, design-forward cafe inside a department store where some English is likely workable, though a few Japanese phrases to confirm vegan ingredients will help.

places.name: ペッティロッソ 京都 (Pettirosso Kyoto)
places.gf_detail: Gluten-free sweets are explicitly offered, and one reviewer described "tons of gluten-free options." However there is no published detail on whether the soy sauce / seasonings are wheat-free, no dedicated GF prep area is mentioned, and no celiac cross-contamination protocol is documented. A celiac diner should call or message ahead (English is fine) and confirm specific dishes and soy-sauce wheat content.
places.vegan_detail: Since relocating to Kita-ku in May 2023, the kitchen is entirely plant-based — all dishes contain no animal products (the earlier Shimogyo location had served local fish, but that is no longer the case). The lunch is a one-plate vegan meal with four deli-style sides, salad and a choice of main; dinner is a multi-course vegan set. More than 30 kinds of organic vegetables (from Ohara, Hanase and other Kyoto areas) are used, with nuts for richness, fermented foods for depth and spices as accents. Vegan cakes/sweets made without eggs, dairy or refined sugar are available.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-16:00 (last order 14:30); Dinner 17:30/18:00-21:00. Closed Mondays and Tuesdays (open on national holidays). Lunch limited to ~15 servings/day. Sources differ on closed days (some older listings say Monday only), so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, near the Kamogawa (Kamo River); about a 9-minute wa
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Pettirosso is a tiny owner-run plant-based restaurant created by chef Ayako Hayashi and her Italian husband, Marco. Ayako met Marco while studying to become a chef in Italy, and the two run the place themselves. After working in vegan cooking and operating a r

chef_bio.background: Pettirosso is a tiny owner-run plant-based restaurant created by chef Ayako Hayashi and her Italian husband, Marco. Ayako met Marco while studying to become a chef in Italy, and the two run the place themselves. After working in vegan cooking and operating a restaurant in the Shijo Tominokoji area of central Kyoto for about five years, they relocated in May 2023 to a quiet residential pocket of Kita-ku, just a few minutes' walk from the Kamo River, and made the kitchen fully vegan.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deep appreciation for local, seasonal Kyoto vegetables expressed through an Italian-Japanese fusion: nuts add richness, fermented foods deepen flavor, and spices act as accents. The couple chose the new location to feel nature nearby, encouraging guests to take a vegan lunch box to enjoy along the Kamo River. The menu rotates roughly every two weeks to follow what is in season.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal one-plate vegan lunches and multi-course vegan dinners built from more than 30 kinds of organic Kyoto vegetables (from Ohara, Hanase and nearby areas). Signature dishes include a vegan broccoli-and-almond lasagna and a vegan carrot cake; egg-, dairy- and refined-sugar-free vegan sweets are a highlight.

anecdotes.text: Chef Ayako Hayashi met her Italian husband Marco while studying culinary arts in Italy; the couple now runs the entire restaurant by themselves.
They relocated in May 2023 to be near nature, and recommend taking a vegan lunch box just a few minutes' walk to the Kamo River.
The kitchen uses over 30 kinds of organic Kyoto vegetables, with a menu that changes about every two weeks to follow the season.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Leaf Kyoto and DigiStyle Kyoto confirm chef Ayako Hayashi and Italian husband Marco, the ~5-year Shijo Tominokoji predecessor, the May 24 2023 relocation to Izumoji Tatehoncho in Kita-ku near the Kamo River, the fully vegan turn, 30+ organic Kyoto vegetables, biweekly seasonal menu, and the almond lasagna and carrot cake.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely plant-based with no animal products used, which greatly reduces the risk of meat, fish or dairy cross-contact.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Listings recommend telling the staff about your vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies when choosing from the menu, and the owners speak good English so this is easy to do.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen since May 2023 - no animal products at all, so no meat/fish/dairy in the kitchen.
Owners speak very good English and the vegan menu is well marked, making it easy to ask about ingredients.
Gluten-free sweets are offered and a reviewer noted plenty of gluten-free options alongside the vegan ones.

cultural_comfort.note: The owners speak very good English and the menu is foreigner-friendly, so a foreign student can comfortably ask about ingredients and reserve in English (Instagram DM works well).

places.name: 本と野菜 OyOy (Hon to Yasai OyOy (Book and Vege OyOy))
places.gf_detail: Listings indicate a gluten-free menu / gluten-free options are available, but no detail is published on dedicated preparation, cross-contamination control, or whether the soy sauce used is wheat-free. A gluten-sensitive diner should ask directly about each dish, since carrot cake and other baked desserts likely contain wheat.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is fully vegetarian (no meat or fish on the menu). The signature OyOy Seasonal Vegetable Plate (about 1,850 yen) is made entirely from plant-based ingredients and is explicitly recommended for vegans; this full plate is served Fri-Mon (Monday lunch only). On other weekdays only lighter snacks, desserts and drinks are available, and some of those items (and some desserts) are vegetarian-but-not-vegan, containing dairy or egg (e.g. egg custard). Vegans should aim for Fri-Sun (or Mon lunch) for the full vegan plate and confirm individual desserts. Specific soy sauce wheat content and dashi sourcing are not documented publicly; the cuisine is vegetable-forward and uses soy products like tofu.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu 11:00-20:00 (food L.O. 19:00); Fri-Sun 11:00-21:00 (food L.O. 20:00). Monday is lunch-focused (full vegetable plate Fri-Mon, Mon lunch only). Closed Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, inside the Shinpuhkan complex, a 1-minute walk
places.cuisine: Plant-based Japanese vegetable cafe / book c
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] OyOy opened in June 2020 on the first floor of the Shinpuhkan complex by Karasuma Oike Station as a hybrid bookshop, cafe and vegetable shop. It is a collaboration between Saka no Tochu (坂ノ途中), a Kyoto organic-vegetable company that sources seasonal produce gr

chef_bio.background: OyOy opened in June 2020 on the first floor of the Shinpuhkan complex by Karasuma Oike Station as a hybrid bookshop, cafe and vegetable shop. It is a collaboration between Saka no Tochu (坂ノ途中), a Kyoto organic-vegetable company that sources seasonal produce grown without pesticides or chemical fertilizers from partner farms, and Oraido (鴎来堂), the team behind Kamome Books in Kagurazaka, Tokyo, who curate the in-store library.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name OyOy (oioi) evokes an unhurried pace - gradually and gently bringing vegetables and books into everyday life. The shop treats vegetables as living things, each with its own flavor, form and daily change, and pairs them with a curated shelf of books on food, farming and travel to enrich daily life.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature OyOy Seasonal Vegetable Plate, built entirely from plant-based seasonal vegetables and changing with what the farms harvest, alongside homemade desserts like seasonal tofu douhua and carrot cake, in-house fruit cordials, and Umi no Mukou coffee from Saka no Tochu's coffee division.

anecdotes.text: Half the shop is lined with curated bookshelves spanning food, agriculture, daily life and travel essays, so diners can browse books and buy organically grown vegetables alongside their meal.
The full plant-based vegetable plate is offered Friday through Monday (lunch only on Monday), with the philosophy that each seasonal vegetable's individual character should be showcased.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: On-the-slope (Saka no Tochu's own project page) and vegewel confirm the June 2020 Shinpuhkan first-floor opening, the Saka no Tochu x Oraido (Kamome Books) collaboration, the plant-based seasonal vegetable plate served Friday-Monday, tofu douhua and carrot cake desserts, and Umi no Mukou coffee.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is vegetarian with no meat or fish, so there is no animal-protein cross-contact from meat or seafood; the main risk for vegans is dairy and egg used in some desserts and light items, not shared cooking surfaces with meat.

positives.text: Fully vegetarian kitchen using organic, pesticide-free seasonal vegetables from partner farms, with a clearly designated all-plant-based vegetable plate for vegans.
Reviewers praise the vegan platter's many distinct vegetable preparations as a highlight, in a calm bookstore setting with soft jazz.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, tourist-accustomed book-cafe in a central shopping complex where a little Japanese helps, but the plant-based plate and menu are easy to navigate and staff are welcoming to foreign visitors.

places.name: ソーシャル・キッチン（Social Kitchen） (Social Kitchen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The café serves cakes, cookies, curries and lunch plates, and standard Japanese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is the regional default, so wheat/gluten should be assumed present unless you ask. Because the kitchen rotates among different operators by day, any gluten-free accommodation would need to be confirmed directly with whoever is cooking that day. Treat as "ask" — no dedicated GF handling confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Social Kitchen's 1F café is a community-minded eatery centered on locally grown ingredients and low-waste cooking, and it offers plant-based dishes (vegan items such as a South Indian bean curry have appeared on its menu). Important: the café now runs as a "share café," with different cooks/operators taking different days of the week, so vegan availability and the exact menu vary day to day. If you want a guaranteed vegan meal, check that day's operator in advance. Not a fully vegan kitchen — animal-based dishes are also served depending on the day.
places.hours_raw: Hours vary by operating day (share café). Listings have shown ranges including Sun–Wed approx 11:30–16:00, Thu approx 9:30–15:30, Sat 11:30–21:00, and evening service Fri–Sat 18:30–21:00; older descriptions list 13:00/14:00–23:00. Schedule is irregular and changes with the day's operator and events.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo-ku, just outside the south gate of Shokoku-ji temple
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Social Kitchen opened in September 2010 in a building just outside the south gate of Shokoku-ji temple in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto. It was created by the Kyoto collective Hanare, which had been running a weekly community café since 2006. Conceived as a '21st-century

chef_bio.background: Social Kitchen opened in September 2010 in a building just outside the south gate of Shokoku-ji temple in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto. It was created by the Kyoto collective Hanare, which had been running a weekly community café since 2006. Conceived as a '21st-century community center,' the three-floor space pairs a ground-floor café and bookstore (Not Pillar Books) with a second-floor event hall for lectures, discussions, workshops and exhibitions, and a third-floor shared office for designers, programmers and art groups.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café is framed as 'a project exploring the possibilities that expand from food.' It emphasizes locally grown ingredients and a deliberately low-waste system, treating eating as part of a broader practice of community, sustainability and dialogue rather than just a meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily-changing lunch plates, soups, curries (including plant-based dishes such as a South Indian bean curry), plus cakes, cookies and drinks served in a relaxed café-and-bookstore setting.

anecdotes.text: The space is run by the Kyoto collective Hanare, which describes itself as pursuing practical, exciting approaches to all aspects of life — food, art, politics, economics and architecture — and had operated a weekly community café since 2006 before opening Social Kitchen in 2010.
Today the café functions as a 'share café,' with multiple cooks and small operators each taking different days of the week, so the menu and atmosphere change depending on who is in the kitchen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Shift.jp.org confirms the September 2010 opening near Shokoku-ji in Kamigyo-ku, the Hanare collective (weekly community cafe since 2006), the three-floor layout with the ground-floor cafe and Not Pillar Books, second-floor event hall and third-floor shared office, and the locally-sourced, low-waste ethos.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is not fully vegan and rotates among different operators by day, so animal ingredients may be used on the same equipment depending on who is cooking; confirm with the day's operator.

positives.text: Plant-based dishes (such as a vegan South Indian bean curry) have been part of the café's offering, and the venue's ethos centers on local ingredients and low-waste cooking.

cultural_comfort.note: An open, internationally-minded community space used to hosting events and foreign visitors, so a friendly greeting and simple English will usually get you by, though the rotating operators may not all be fluent.

places.name: 京大食堂 カフェテリアルネ (Kyodai Shokudo Cafeteria Rene)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a standard Japanese cafeteria, soy sauce and seasonings almost certainly contain wheat, and there is no indication of GF-dedicated prep or wheat-free options. A celiac/GF diner should ask staff directly and not assume the certified-vegan items are also gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: An omnivore university cafeteria (open to the public, not just students) that offers a dedicated lineup of plant-based dishes meeting VegeProject Japan's vegan certification standard (no meat, fish, shellfish, dairy, eggs, or honey). It also runs a halal counter with items like a soy-fried tofu wrap. Vegan dishes are clearly marked, but they share a busy cafeteria line with omnivore food, so vegan offerings are a subset of the menu rather than the whole kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–22:00; Sat 11:00–19:30; Sun 11:00–14:00; closed on public holidays. Hours may change during long university holidays.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward, on Kyoto University's Yoshida (west-side) campus
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Cafeteria Rene is the Kyoto University Co-op's flagship dining hall, on the 2nd floor of the west-side co-op building (named 'Rene' after the camphor tree, the university's symbol). It reopened in its current form in 2013 and is open to the general public, not

chef_bio.background: Cafeteria Rene is a Kyoto University Co-op dining hall on the 2nd floor of the west-side co-op building known as 'Rene.' After roughly twenty years of service the west building was renovated and reopened in April 2013, and the cafeteria is open to the general public, not only students.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafeteria's plant-based offerings grew out of a student-led push to make vegan and vegetarian food normal and accessible in university dining. Kyoto University is recognized as the birthplace of VegeProject Japan, the NPO whose vegan certification mark these dishes carry, reflecting an ethos of inclusive eating across cultures and diets.
chef_bio.specialty: Certified plant-based set dishes and a halal counter, alongside the usual cafeteria fare; popular vegan/halal items include a soy-fried tofu wrap and kebab-style wraps and plates.

anecdotes.text: Kyoto University is described as the birthplace of VegeProject Japan; the NPO that issues Japan's widely used vegan certification mark originated from a student initiative there to introduce vegan options into the dining halls.
The cafeteria deliberately serves people of varied backgrounds, pairing a VegeProject-certified vegan lineup with a halal counter offering kebab wraps and a soy-fried tofu wrap.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The National Federation of University Co-ops (univcoop) confirms the west-building 'Rene' reopened in April 2013 after about twenty years of service; vegemap and VegeProject confirm the VegeProject-certified vegan menu and Kyoto University as VegeProject's birthplace; hotozero confirms the halal kebab counter and soy-fried tofu wrap.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen documentation was found; as a standard Japanese cafeteria, soy sauce and seasonings should be assumed to contain wheat unless staff confirm otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Certified-vegan dishes are prepared and served on a shared cafeteria line alongside meat and fish dishes, so incidental cross-contact is possible despite the vegan certification of the recipes themselves.

positives.text: Plant-based dishes meet VegeProject Japan's vegan certification standard, meaning the recipes themselves contain no meat, fish, shellfish, dairy, eggs, or honey.
Vegan and halal items are clearly labeled and the venue is open to the public, making it an easy, low-cost option for plant-based diners visiting the campus area.

cultural_comfort.note: As a Kyoto University cafeteria used to international students, staff are welcoming and vegan/halal items are clearly marked, but it is a fast self-service line where a few words of Japanese (or pointing) helps.

places.name: 京のカレーうどん 味味香 祇園店 (Mimikou (Kyo no Curry Udon Mimikou, Gion)
places.gf_detail: This is fundamentally a wheat-udon shop, so the signature udon and ramen dishes are NOT gluten-free (udon noodles are wheat). Some directory listings tag a "gluten-free menu," but the only plausibly gluten-free item is the curry rice with vegetables, and even then the curry sauce and the regular dashi are seasoned with soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and the shared frying oil handles battered/wheat items. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free setup or wheat-free soy sauce. A celiac diner should ask directly and treat the noodle dishes as off-limits.
places.vegan_detail: Mimikou is an omnivore curry-udon shop (it also serves meat and fish), but it keeps a separate English vegetarian/vegan menu. The vegetarian udon broth is made without fish stock, using a kombu/seaweed (kelp) base instead of the usual katsuobushi (bonito) dashi found in their regular curry udon. Vegan choices include vegetarian/vegan curry udon, vegan dashi ramen, and curry rice with vegetables, customizable with vegetable tempura and deep-fried soy meat ("soy nuggets"). On the vegan menu, egg is an optional add-on, so order without it. Note one important caveat reported by a 2025 diner: staff said all frying (tofu, meat, fish) is done in the same oil, so fried toppings such as tempura and soy meat are not free of animal cross-contact. Confirm "no egg" and ask about the frying oil when ordering.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30). Irregular holidays; closes early when sold out. (Some sources list slightly different hours such as 11:30-20:30; confirm before visiting.)
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama Ward, Gion district — about a 10-minute walk fro
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Mimikou (味味香) began in 1969 as a Kiyamachi food stall opened by Kokichi Shimada, a former Japanese-restaurant owner, and grew into a Kyoto curry-udon institution. The shop relocated to its present spot in Gion near Yasaka Shrine in June 2010, and its curry udo

chef_bio.background: Mimikou (味味香) began in 1969 as a Kiyamachi food stall opened by Kokichi Shimada, a former Japanese-restaurant (ryotei) owner, and grew into a Kyoto curry-udon institution. The shop relocated to its present spot in Gion near Yasaka Shrine in June 2010, and its curry udon was recognized in 2015 by Japan's Cool Japan 'The Wonder 500' selection.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honoring the founder's original recipe while refining it: the second-generation owner, who trained in French cuisine, spent years perfecting the dashi (kombu and dried bonito for the regular broth, and a fish-free version for vegetarian diners) and the balance of spices, treating the curry udon as Kyoto 'soul food.'
chef_bio.specialty: Kyoto-style curry udon: chewy udon in a deeply flavored ankake (thick) curry broth. The signature 'fox' (kitsune) curry udon is a house favorite; for plant-based diners they offer a vegetarian/vegan curry udon, a vegetarian ramen, and curry rice topped with vegetable tempura.

anecdotes.text: The curry udon was chosen for Japan's Cool Japan 'The Wonder 500' in 2015 as a distinctive regional product.

Founded 1969 in Kiyamachi by Kokichi Shimada and moved to the current Gion location near Yasaka Shrine in June 2010; the second-generation owner trained in French cooking before refining the family curry-udon recipe.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official mimikou.jp confirms the 1969 Kiyamachi stall founding, the June 2010 move next to Yasaka Shrine in Gion, the 2015 Wonder 500 selection, and the kombu/bonito house broth; Leaf Kyoto confirms founder Kokichi Shimada (ex-ryotei owner) and the French-trained successor; vegewel confirms a vegetarian/vegan curry-udon and rice-bowl menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Primarily a wheat-udon shop; udon and ramen noodles are wheat, and battered tempura shares the kitchen, so high gluten cross-contact for any non-noodle order.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth/sauce is seasoned with soy sauce (regular Japanese shoyu, typically wheat-based); no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A May 2025 diner reported staff disclosed that everything is fried in the same oil (tofu, meat and fish), so fried toppings like tempura and soy meat carry animal cross-contact and are not strictly vegan.
It is an omnivore restaurant serving meat and fish dishes alongside the vegetarian menu, so shared cookware/oil should be assumed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff openly disclosed the shared-oil situation when a vegan customer asked, indicating they will answer ingredient/preparation questions if you ask.

positives.text: There is a separate English vegetarian/vegan menu, and the vegetarian udon broth is explicitly made without fish stock, using a kombu/seaweed base instead of bonito dashi.
Vegan can be ordered by declining the optional egg add-on; vegan curry udon, vegan dashi ramen and vegetable curry rice are available with soy-meat and vegetable toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: There's an English vegetarian/vegan menu and the shop is used to tourists, so a few words of Japanese plus pointing at the English menu will get you a vegan curry udon comfortably.

places.name: サロン・フ (Salon Fu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information could be confirmed for this venue. Macrobiotic cooking in Japan typically relies on wheat-based ingredients such as standard soy sauce (shoyu), miso, seitan and wheat gluten (fu), so gluten exposure is likely. A celiac/GF diner must ask directly; whether tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) is used is unknown.
places.vegan_detail: Listed on the curated Japan VegeMap directory as a macrobiotic cafe offering vegan options (not a fully vegan kitchen). Macrobiotic cooking generally avoids meat, eggs, dairy and refined sugar, so plant-based choices are likely available, but the menu is not confirmed to be entirely vegan. The listing itself is marked "not verified," so diners should tell staff about vegan needs and confirm individual dishes (especially regarding dashi, bonito, and any animal-derived seasonings) before ordering. No specific vegan dish list could be confirmed from public sources.
places.hours_raw: Not published / unknown
places.neighborhood: Ukyo Ward, Utano area (northwest Kyoto, near Omuro / Ninna-j
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Listed on the curated Japan VegeMap directory as a macrobiotic cafe offering vegan options (not a fully vegan kitchen). Macrobiotic cooking generally avoids meat, eggs, dairy and refined sugar, so plant-based choices are likely available, but the menu is not c

chef_bio.background: Salon Fu (サロン・フ) is described as a macrobiotic cafe in the Utano / Omuro area of Ukyō-ku, northwest Kyoto, near Ninna-ji. It has no findable website and only minimal presence on Japanese review and vegan-directory listings. Web research did not surface any reliable source naming the owner/cook or documenting the shop's founding, so no biographical claims can be responsibly asserted. (A Japanese Ameblo blog registered as 'salon-fu' turned out to belong to an unrelated Tokyo-based cooking instructor and does not describe this Kyoto cafe.)
chef_bio.philosophy: Macrobiotic / plant-forward cooking (implied by its category as a macrobiotic cafe; not otherwise sourced).
chef_bio.specialty: Macrobiotic cafe fare (unconfirmed in detail).
chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese macrobiotic/vegan directory searches place a macrobiotic cafe of this name in the Utano area, but no source provides owner or origin details; an identically named Ameblo blog is a different, unrelated Tokyo business.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The directory listing advises customers to tell staff about their vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies and to choose a menu item accordingly, implying ordering decisions should be confirmed with staff at the venue.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small local macrobiotic cafe with little English-facing presence, so a few words of Japanese and a clearly written note about your vegan/allergy needs will help; phone ahead (075-461-5911) before visiting since hours could not be confirmed.

places.name: 京大食堂 北部食堂 (Kyodai Shokudo Hokubu Shokudo (Kyoto Uni)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. This is a high-volume cafeteria serving fried foods, noodles (udon/ramen) and breaded items alongside vegan dishes, so cross-contact with wheat is likely. Menu cards do display allergen information (wheat is one of Japan's mandatory labeled allergens), so a diner can check individual items, but GF travelers should ask staff directly. Japanese soy sauce and standard dashi-based seasonings commonly used in cafeteria cooking typically contain wheat; assume soy sauce is NOT wheat-free unless confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: This is the North Cafeteria (Hokubu Shokudo) of Kyoto University's co-op, an omnivore student/staff canteen that is open to the public. It carries a certified vegan menu as part of the VegeProject initiative that began at Kyoto University in 2013. VegeMap lists it as having certified vegan options, where "vegan" is defined as containing no meat, seafood, eggs, dairy or honey. Vegan dishes are a small dedicated portion of an otherwise meat- and fish-heavy cafeteria menu, so it is a cafeteria-style pick-your-tray operation rather than a vegan restaurant. Vegan items are labeled, and menu cards display allergen information. Staff recommend telling them about your vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies before choosing.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-21:00, Saturday 11:00-14:00, closed Sundays and public holidays (per VegeMap). Tabelog lists weekdays from 8:00; hours follow the academic calendar.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward, Kyoto University North (Yoshida) Campus; nearest
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] The North Cafeteria is one of the Kyoto University co-op dining halls on the Yoshida campus and is open to the public, not just students and staff. It is part of the dining system that became the birthplace of Japan's vegan-menu movement: in 2013, a student-le

chef_bio.background: The North Cafeteria is one of the Kyoto University co-op dining halls on the Yoshida campus and is open to the public, not just students and staff. It is part of the dining system that became the birthplace of Japan's vegan-menu movement: in 2013, a student-led effort to put certified vegan dishes onto the university cafeteria trays grew into VegeProject, later the nonprofit VegeProject Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make plant-based eating an everyday, accessible default in ordinary dining halls so that vegan, vegetarian and curious diners alike can always find a clearly labeled option.
chef_bio.specialty: Certified plant-based set meals served alongside the standard Japanese cafeteria fare, containing no meat, seafood, eggs, dairy or honey.

anecdotes.text: Kyoto University is widely cited as the birthplace of VegeProject, which began in 2013 to introduce a vegan menu to the university cafeteria and later grew into a nationwide nonprofit.
VegeProject was started by Haruko Kawano, who studied at Kyoto University and launched the cafeteria vegan-menu project in her final year of graduate school.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: VegeProject's own pages and corroborating searches confirm Kyoto University as VegeProject's birthplace, the 2013 start of the cafeteria vegan menu, and that director Haruko Kawano launched the project in her final graduate year; the specific faculty she studied in is not clearly published, so it is left unstated.

gf_cross_contamination.text: High-volume cafeteria serving fried foods and wheat noodles alongside vegan dishes; wheat cross-contact is likely and no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free soy sauce is confirmed; standard Japanese soy sauce and cafeteria seasonings typically contain wheat, so assume soy sauce is not gluten-free unless verified with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan dishes are prepared and served in a shared omnivore cafeteria line; the vegan menu itself is certified to contain no animal-derived ingredients, but cross-contact in a shared kitchen is possible.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Menu cards display nutritional balance and allergen information; staff recommend telling them about vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies before choosing a dish.

positives.text: Carries a VegeProject-certified vegan menu defined as containing no meat, seafood, eggs, dairy or honey, with items clearly labeled.
Open to the public (not only students), making the certified vegan options accessible to travelers.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual university canteen with labeled vegan dishes and allergen-marked menu cards; basic Japanese or pointing helps, and as the birthplace of VegeProject it is welcoming to vegan and international diners.

places.name: ブラウニーブレッドアンドベーグルズ (Browny Bread & Bagels)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery built around bagels, bread and focaccia made from domestic wheat with long fermentation. No gluten-free products, separate prep, or GF accommodations were found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners.
places.vegan_detail: A wheat bakery offering 20-30 bagel varieties daily plus white bread and focaccia. Many of the plainer and savory items (made with domestic wheat, water, oil) are vegan-friendly, and soy milk is available. However, a large share of the lineup is explicitly non-vegan: cheese, cream cheese, gorgonzola-honey, tiramisu, bacon-cheese and similar varieties. There is no dedicated vegan menu or in-shop vegan labeling found, so plant-based diners should ask staff which specific bagels are egg-free and dairy-free. Confirmed as "has vegan options" rather than fully vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, closed Wednesdays and Sundays
places.neighborhood: Sakyo-ku (Shogoin area), just south of the Higashioji-dori x
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Browny Bread & Bagels is a well-loved Kyoto bagel specialist that began on Kitaoji-dori in the north of the city before relocating in 2016 to its current spot in the Shogoin neighborhood of Sakyo-ku, a short walk south of the Higashioji and Marutamachi interse

chef_bio.background: Browny Bread & Bagels is a well-loved Kyoto bagel specialist that began on Kitaoji-dori in the north of the city before relocating in 2016 to its current spot in the Shogoin neighborhood of Sakyo-ku, a short walk south of the Higashioji and Marutamachi intersection near Heian Shrine and Kyoto Zoo.
chef_bio.philosophy: The bakers emphasize carefully selected ingredients and extended, long-time fermentation to bring out the dough's fragrance, flavor and texture rather than relying on heavy fillings, with everything baked fresh on site each day.
chef_bio.specialty: Bagels above all: the shop keeps 20-30 varieties in stock at any time, made from domestic wheat with long fermentation and house-made yeast for a chewy, moist crumb and a deep wheat aroma. Signature flavors run from a popular cinnamon-apple-walnut with cream cheese to gorgonzola with honey and walnuts, kinako-mochi with red bean, sweet-potato cream cheese and bacon-cheese, alongside other savory breads. Bagels are graded by texture (firmer T-60 vs. softer T-70).

anecdotes.text: The shop consistently keeps 20-30 kinds of bagels on the shelves at once, spanning savory to dessert styles, and even labels them by texture grade (T-60 firmer, T-70 softer) so customers can pick the chew they like.
Originally on Kitaoji-dori, Browny moved to its current Shogoin location in 2016, settling into a small storefront near Heian Shrine and Kyoto Zoo.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: dmatcha confirms the 20-30 rotating bagel varieties, the T-60/T-70 firmness grades, domestic wheat with long fermentation, and flavors including gorgonzola-honey-walnut, kinako-mochi red bean, sweet-potato cream cheese and cinnamon-apple-walnut; kyotopi confirms the 2016 move from Kitaoji to Shogoin near Heian Shrine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Dedicated wheat bakery making bagels, bread and focaccia from domestic wheat; gluten is pervasive throughout the kitchen and there are no gluten-free items, so this is unsafe for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: At least one variety is a soy-sauce mochi cheese bagel; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many bagels and breads are made and baked alongside cheese, cream cheese, honey and dairy varieties on shared equipment, so vegan items are prepared in the same kitchen as animal-product items. Ask staff which specific bagels are egg-free and dairy-free.

positives.text: A HappyCow vegan-bakery directory entry notes a domestic-wheat bakeshop whose main products are bagels, white bread and focaccia, many of which are vegan-friendly, and that it offers soy milk for drinks.
Everything is baked fresh on site and the small storefront is known for friendly service.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood Japanese bakery with a takeaway counter; staff are described as friendly, but menus and bagel labels are mostly in Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases help when asking which bagels are egg- and dairy-free.

places.name: ごはんぱん工房つぶつぶ (Gohan Pan Kobo Tsubutsubu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The rice bread is cooked rice kneaded together with wheat flour (Hokkaido wheat), so all breads contain wheat/gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Rice-bread bakery whose dough kneads freshly cooked Kyoto-blend rice into wheat flour. The base bread is plant-based, using soy milk and rice oil instead of eggs and dairy, and many items contain no eggs, dairy, or honey. Items with animal products are clearly labeled, so vegan diners can select safe options. Not all-vegan, as some products contain eggs, dairy, and honey.
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Gohan Pan Kobo Tsubutsubu grew out of a rice shop (Okui Riso Mai-ten) and opened after debuting at the Kamigamo Shrine handmade market in summer 2012. The owners knead freshly cooked, just-polished Kyoto-blend rice into the dough with a mochi-pounding machine

chef_bio.background: Gohan Pan Kobo Tsubutsubu is tied to the rice shop Okui Riso Mai-ten (奥井理想米店), which handles its sales, and it debuted at the Kamigamo Shrine handmade market in summer 2012. The bakers knead freshly cooked, just-polished Kyoto-blend rice into the dough with a mochi-pounding machine rather than using rice flour.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name Tsubutsubu references ryuryu-shinku (粒粒辛苦), honoring the grain-by-grain effort of farmers and the value of every grain of rice; the shop emphasizes domestic rice and food self-sufficiency, using Hokkaido wheat chosen to avoid post-harvest pesticides.
chef_bio.specialty: Chewy, fluffy gohan pan (rice bread) made by mixing cooked rice with wheat flour for a naturally sweet, mochi-like texture; many varieties use soy milk and rice oil instead of eggs and dairy.

anecdotes.text: The bread is made from actual cooked rice pounded into the wheat dough, not rice flour, which gives its signature mochi-mochi texture and gentle sweetness.
The business began as a stall at the Kamigamo Shrine handmade market in summer 2012 and still appears at Kyoto handmade markets monthly.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms that the bakery uses freshly cooked Kyoto-blend rice (not rice flour) pounded into the dough, Hokkaido wheat chosen to avoid post-harvest pesticides, soy milk and rice oil in place of eggs and dairy, the ryuryu-shinku name meaning, and that Okui Riso Mai-ten handles sales.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not gluten-free: every bread is made with wheat flour (rice is kneaded in alongside the wheat), so gluten is present throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The facility also produces items containing eggs, dairy, peanuts, and honey, so there is cross-contamination risk for strictly plant-based diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Breads containing eggs or dairy are clearly labeled, helping customers avoid animal products and choose plant-based alternatives.

positives.text: The base bread is plant-based, using soy milk and rice oil instead of eggs and dairy; many items are free of eggs, dairy, and honey.
Listed on a vegan/ethical directory as a bakery with many egg-, dairy-, and honey-free items that welcomes plant-based customers.

cultural_comfort.note: Vegan-friendly spot; many such places in Kyoto are used to international visitors.

places.name: カフェ フロッシュ (Café Frosch)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or options are documented. The café's identity centers on house-baked bread made with whole-grain wheat flour and natural koji yeast, so wheat/gluten is pervasive in the kitchen. There is no information confirming wheat-free soy sauce or any gluten-free preparation. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should treat this as not gluten-safe and ask directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore café (it serves a German sausage sandwich and dishes that may contain dairy/eggs), but it offers a clear set of dedicated vegan options. Reported vegan items include a vegan aubergine (eggplant) burger set served with soup, side, salad and pickles; koji-bread plates with a vegan soup option; an onigiri rice-ball vegan set; and a "little bread" set with vegan soup and salad. The house bread is risen German-sourdough style using koji (a plant-based fermentation culture), and is described as entirely plant-based. Some vegetarian dishes contain dairy or eggs but can often be modified by removing animal products. Staff explicitly recommend telling them your vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies so they can guide your order.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00; closed Thursdays and Fridays (per official/Tabelog listing; HappyCow lists Thu plus 2nd Friday). Available for private reservations after 17:00 on request. Koji bread baked in-house and sold on Wednesdays and Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo-ku, Nishijin weaving district — about 3 minutes east
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Café Frosch opened on May 1, 2009, in Kyoto's historic Nishijin textile district, in a renovated traditional machiya (wooden townhouse) that sits within a still-active weaving neighborhood and looks out over a small park. It is set about three minutes east of

chef_bio.background: Café Frosch opened on May 1, 2009, in Kyoto's historic Nishijin textile district, in a renovated traditional machiya (wooden townhouse) about three minutes east of the east gate of Kitano Tenmangu shrine. It was created by two sisters to bring cultural exchange to the historic neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café describes itself as a place 'without boundaries' — a space where people of any nationality or age can freely come together to enjoy culture and art. It emphasizes natural ingredients, locally grown Kyoto vegetables, and naturally fermented bread, and welcomes plant-based diners.
chef_bio.specialty: House-baked bread made with whole-grain flour and natural koji yeast — koji bread, bagels, and fig-walnut bread — alongside vegan plates such as a vegan aubergine burger set, koji-bread plates with vegan soup, and an onigiri vegan set. Bread is baked in-house and sold on Wednesdays and Sundays.

anecdotes.text: The café was created as a borderless gathering place for cultural and artistic exchange, hosting events such as English nights, live performances and workshops, and offering rental space.
HappyCow describes it as a café created by two sisters in the historic Nishijin area, combining cultural exchange with vegan-friendly dining and a bakery selling bread on Wednesdays and Sundays.
Its signature bread is risen German-sourdough style using koji, a plant-based fermentation culture, and is made with whole-grain flour and natural yeast.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The café's own Japanese about page confirms the May 1 2009 opening in a Nishijin machiya three minutes east of Kitano Tenmangu's east gate, the borderless culture-and-art concept, and koji bread of whole-grain flour and natural yeast sold Wednesdays and Sundays; HappyCow confirms the two-sister founding and the vegan menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is bread-focused, baking with whole-grain wheat flour and koji yeast, so wheat/gluten is pervasive; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore kitchen that also serves meat dishes such as a German sausage sandwich, so vegan items are prepared in a shared kitchen; no dedicated vegan-only prep area is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff recommend that you tell them about your vegetarian/vegan needs and any potential allergies before choosing a menu so they can guide your order, and some vegetarian dishes can be modified to remove dairy/eggs.

positives.text: Offers a clearly defined set of vegan dishes — a vegan aubergine burger set, vegan koji-bread and onigiri sets — with plant-based, naturally fermented bread.
Reviewers praise the vegan meals as 'fantastic' with thoughtful meal combinations.

cultural_comfort.note: Founded as a borderless cultural-exchange café with international staff who prepare weekend global dishes, and it hosts English nights, so a foreign student should feel comfortable communicating in English.

places.name: おめんNIPPON 四条先斗町店 (Omen NIPPON Shijo Pontocho)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-udon restaurant — the signature noodles are made from Japanese wheat flour and are not gluten-free. The dipping sauce/tare and any soy sauce used are conventional (wheat-containing); no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free noodle option was found. Tempura batter also contains wheat. Celiac/strict GF travelers cannot eat the core dishes here.
places.vegan_detail: Omen is an omnivore udon house, but it maintains a dedicated vegan/vegetarian menu available on request. The plant-based set comes with a vegan dashi (fish-free, kelp/vegetable-based dipping broth) instead of the standard bonito dashi, an assortment of fresh and pickled seasonal Kyoto vegetables, ground sesame, and egg-free vegetable tempura. Omen was noted as one of the first noodle shops to explicitly offer a fish-free (vegetarian) dashi. You must ask for the vegan/vegetarian version when ordering, since the default menu is not plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:00), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed 2nd and 3rd Thursdays of the month. (Source: Tabelog. Note: HappyCow lists Wed closed / slightly earlier evening close — hours may vary; confirm before visiting.)
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, in Pontocho just off Shijo-dori, a short walk
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Omen is one of Kyoto's most beloved udon institutions, with its flagship shop near Ginkaku-ji (the Silver Pavilion) on the Philosopher's Path. The name 'Omen' is an honorific dialect word from eastern Japan meaning 'noodles' (men/麺). This Shijo Pontocho branch

chef_bio.background: Omen is one of Kyoto's most beloved udon institutions, with its flagship shop near Ginkaku-ji (the Silver Pavilion) on the Philosopher's Path. The name 'Omen' combines the honorific 'o' with 'men' (麺, noodles). This Shijo Pontocho branch, branded Omen NIPPON, brings the same udon to central Kyoto just off the lively Pontocho lane.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on seasonal Kyoto vegetables and quality domestic wheat, presenting udon as an elegant, vegetable-forward meal rather than a quick noodle bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick, chewy udon made from Japanese wheat, served hot or cold with a dipping tsuyu and a generous spread of seasonal Kyoto vegetables (boiled and pickled) plus freshly ground sesame. The house tsuyu is a fish (bonito-and-kombu) broth; for plant-based diners the kitchen prepares, on request, a vegan version with a fish-free vegetable/kelp dashi and egg-free vegetable tempura.

anecdotes.text: Omen NIPPON keeps a separate vegetarian menu: on request the kitchen prepares a vegan udon with a fish-free vegetable/kelp dashi and vegan (egg-free) vegetable tempura, which reviewers single out for its broth and tempura.

The signature 'omen' set pairs hot or cold udon with a colorful assortment of seasonal Kyoto vegetables such as daikon, gobo, eggplant, greens, ginger and scallions, plus ground sesame to stir into the broth.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Omen.co.jp confirms the Ginkaku-ji flagship and a house broth of Hokkaido kombu and bonito; HappyCow confirms that the Omen NIPPON Pontocho branch offers a request-only vegan udon with a fish-free dashi and egg-free vegetable tempura. The standard broth is fish-based, so the vegan version must be requested.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-udon kitchen; the noodles, tempura batter and standard tare all contain wheat, so there is heavy gluten presence throughout. No gluten-free preparation or separate handling was described — not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free soy sauce or tamari was mentioned; the dipping sauce/seasonings should be assumed to contain wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan set uses a separately prepared vegan dashi and egg-free tempura, but the kitchen also handles meat and seafood (e.g., prawn tempura). One reviewer reported being mistakenly served the non-vegan version with prawns, so confirm your order.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Vegan/vegetarian orders are accommodated on request with a distinct broth and sides, and an English menu is available, but plant-based is not the default and at least one order mix-up has been reported — communicate your needs clearly.

positives.text: A dedicated vegan/vegetarian set is available with a fish-free (kelp/vegetable) dashi, fresh and pickled seasonal vegetables, ground sesame and egg-free vegetable tempura.
English menu and staff experienced with international visitors make it easy to request the vegan option.

cultural_comfort.note: There is an English menu and the staff are used to international visitors, so a foreign student can order the vegan set comfortably in English — just clearly request the vegetarian/vegan version.

places.name: インド食堂TADKA（タルカ） (Indo Shokudo TADKA (Tadka))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. South Indian cooking here is largely rice- and lentil-based (rice meals, dosa, sambar, rasam, chutney), which is naturally wheat-free, but breads such as roti/parotta contain wheat and asafoetida (hing) used in many dishes is often wheat-cut. There is no published information on dedicated GF prep, soy-sauce contents, or dashi. A celiac/GF traveler must ask directly about each dish.
places.vegan_detail: The standard/default lunch menu at TADKA1 is vegetarian (South Indian "Meals"), and the kitchen will prepare vegan versions on request ("定番メニューがベジタリアンで、ヴィーガン対応もしてもらえます"). The VEG MEALS set (around 9 dishes made "with beans and vegetables only") and a SIMPLE VEG SET are vegetable/legume based. Because South Indian vegetarian cooking commonly uses ghee, yogurt, paneer and dairy, vegan diners should explicitly ask for dairy-free preparation. No published per-dish ghee/dairy breakdown was found, so confirm in person or via Instagram (@indosyokudo_tadka).
places.hours_raw: Tue-Thu 12:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); Fri-Sat 08:00-10:00 (breakfast/dosa) and 12:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); Sun & Mon closed
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, on Oshikoji-dori; about a 4-minute walk from K
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] TADKA's Japanese owner-chef trained at Mughal, a long-established Indian restaurant in Kyoto, before opening TADKA1 in January 2012 as a street-stall-style South Indian lunch spot on Oshikoji-dori. A second, more refined dinner location, TADKA2, followed in De

chef_bio.origin: From Aichi Prefecture, Japan; based in Kyoto
chef_bio.background: TADKA's owner-chef, Dai Okonogi (小此木 大), originally from Aichi Prefecture, trained at Mughal, a long-established Indian restaurant in Kyoto, before opening TADKA1 in 2012 as a street-stall-style South Indian lunch spot on Oshikoji-dori. A second, more refined dinner location, TADKA2, followed in December 2021 nearby.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef travels to India annually to experience regional cooking in homes, temples, street stalls, cafeterias and restaurants, and prizes the 'diversity' of how a single dish changes by region and context. Spice blends are custom-mixed per curry rather than built on a standardized base, and menus rotate daily with what is seasonal and available.
chef_bio.specialty: South Indian 'Meals' (thali-style vegetable set meals) served with rasam, sambar and chutney; weekend morning dosa/tiffin service.

anecdotes.text: TADKA was awarded a Bib Gourmand in the Indian-cuisine category of the Michelin Guide Kyoto 2023.
Roughly three times a year, for South Indian festivals such as Pongal, Vishu and Onam, TADKA serves an all-vegetarian Sadya of about 20 dishes on a banana leaf.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese listings (search-ethnic, Tabelog) identify the owner-chef as 小此木 大 (Dai Okonogi), from Aichi, who trained at the Kyoto Indian restaurant Mughal before opening TADKA in 2012; the official site confirms TADKA1 (Oshikoji-dori lunch) and TADKA2 (Dec 2021 dinner) plus the annual India travel and the ~20-dish banana-leaf Sadya; the Michelin Kyoto 2023 Bib Gourmand is confirmed by Japanese Michelin round-ups.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen also prepares non-vegetarian dishes (especially at the TADKA2 dinner location), so shared cookware/cross-contact is possible; vegan diners should specify dairy-free and meat-free preparation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reviewers report the standard menu is vegetarian and that the kitchen will accommodate vegan requests when asked, but no formal allergy protocol is published.

positives.text: The standard daily VEG MEALS set is built from beans and vegetables only (about 9 items), making it an easy, largely plant-based default.
Much of the South Indian repertoire (rice, sambar, rasam, chutney) is naturally wheat-free and based on rice and lentils.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual lunch counter popular with international diners and listed in English-language guides, so basic Japanese helps but you can get by with a few words and pointing at the vegetarian meals set.

places.name: ゆば料理 東山ゆう豆 (Yuba Ryori Higashiyama Yuzu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are published. Soy sauce (very likely standard wheat-containing) appears in dishes such as the egg-yolk soy sauce yuba rice, and tempura/fried items and seasonings may contain wheat. Yuba and tofu-milk dishes themselves are naturally wheat-free, but cross-use of regular soy sauce and the lack of any stated gluten protocol means a celiac/GF diner must ask directly and cannot assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore restaurant built around house-made yuba (tofu skin), which is itself plant-based. Many signature elements are vegetable- and soy-forward: the "tofu milk hot pot" with Kyoto vegetables, fresh raw yuba, and yuba-based dishes. However, the kaiseki courses are NOT vegan — they incorporate Miyama-raised Kyoto jidori chicken, free-range egg (e.g. yuba rice topped with egg-yolk soy sauce), and very likely fish-based dashi in soups and seasonings. There is no published dedicated vegan or vegetarian course. A vegan or vegetarian diner would need to phone ahead (075-532-1019) and request a plant-only arrangement; the restaurant explicitly asks for advance notice of allergies and dietary needs, so accommodation may be possible but is not guaranteed. Treat as "ask."
places.hours_raw: Breakfast 9:00-10:15 (LO 9:30), Lunch 11:00-15:00 (LO 14:00), Dinner 16:00-22:00 (Mon from 17:30; entry until 20:30, food LO 21:00, drinks LO 21:30). Tue: no dinner service. Closed Thursday (official site also lists Monday closed; schedules differ, so confirm before visiting).
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama Ward, Gion (south side of Gion-machi, near Yasak
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] The restaurant is the direct-run dining room of 京・美山ゆば ゆう豆, a yuba (tofu skin) maker based in the mountain village of Miyama in Nantan, northern Kyoto — described as 'the inner sanctum of Kyoto,' about 90 minutes from Kyoto Station near the headwaters of the Y

chef_bio.background: The restaurant is the direct-run dining room of 京・美山ゆば ゆう豆, a yuba (tofu skin) maker based in the mountain village of Miyama in Nantan, northern Kyoto — described as 'the inner sanctum of Kyoto,' about 90 minutes by car from Kyoto Station near the headwaters of the Yura River. The story begins not with cooking but with farming: the company grows its own soybeans and works with neighboring Nantan, Kyoto-north and Tanba growers, using exclusively domestic, Kyoto-centered soybeans (including Tamba black soybeans for its rare black yuba). In 2015 they opened this Gion-Higashiyama restaurant so guests could taste their mountain-made yuba directly.
chef_bio.philosophy: Artisanal craft over efficiency: the makers deliberately produce yuba only 'to the point where it genuinely tastes best eaten fresh,' adjusting daily to temperature, humidity and bean quality by feel rather than machine standardization, and refusing anti-foaming agents (消泡剤未使用) for a concentrated, umami-rich result.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made Miyama yuba served fresh; signature dishes include a soy-milk hot pot with colourful Kyoto vegetables and 'yuba meshi' (yuba rice). Courses are built around the maker's own additive-free yuba, presented as a Japanese-Western fusion kaiseki.

anecdotes.text: The brand supplies its handmade, additive-free Miyama yuba to high-end Kyoto restaurants and hotels, and opened the Higashiyama dining room in 2015 to let the public experience it firsthand.

Evening service offers three kaiseki courses named for the neighbourhood — 'Higashiyama,' 'Gion' and 'Yasaka' — using Miyama yuba, Kyoto soybeans and Miyama water alongside seasonal Miyama and Tanba ingredients.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The maker's site miyamayuba.com confirms the Miyama/Nantan base ~90 min from Kyoto Station, use of only domestic Kyoto-centered soybeans (Tamba black for black yuba), the no-anti-foaming-agent method, and the 2015 Higashiyama restaurant. Hitosara/search confirm the Higashiyama/Gion/Yasaka evening kaiseki courses using Miyama yuba, Kyoto soybeans and Miyama water.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as the yuba rice topped with egg-yolk soy sauce indicate standard (wheat-based) soy sauce is used in seasoning; no wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore kitchen: courses include Kyoto jidori chicken and free-range egg, so plant-based dishes are prepared in a kitchen that also handles animal products. Fish dashi is also likely in soups. Vegan diners should request a fully plant-based arrangement in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant requests advance notice of allergies and dietary requirements at the time of reservation, suggesting some willingness to accommodate, though no formal vegan/GF protocol is published.

positives.text: The kitchen centers on house-made, additive-free yuba and a tofu-milk hot pot with Kyoto vegetables — plant-based, soy-forward dishes that form a strong vegetarian-friendly base if animal garnishes (egg, chicken, dashi) are omitted on request.

cultural_comfort.note: A refined Gion kaiseki-style spot popular with international visitors via online reservation platforms; some English support and tourist familiarity exist, but a few words of Japanese and a phoned-ahead dietary request will help, especially for vegan/allergy needs.

places.name: 泉仙 嵯峨野店 (Izusen Sagano)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-friendly. Wheat-based soy sauce and fu (wheat gluten) are essential, core ingredients in Izusen's cooking and cannot be modified, per InsideKyoto. Tempura batter and other course components also typically contain wheat flour. A celiac or strictly gluten-free diner should not rely on this restaurant; there is no dedicated GF menu or accommodation noted.
places.vegan_detail: Izusen serves shojin ryori (Buddhist temple vegetarian cuisine) that is entirely plant-based, with no meat or fish. As traditional shojin, it also excludes the five pungent vegetables (onion, garlic, etc.). The signature is "teppatsu shojin ryori" — a multi-course set served in stacked red-lacquer iron bowls, featuring seasonal vegetables, sesame tofu (goma-dofu), tofu fritters, vegetable tempura, simmered and vinegared dishes, and matcha with Japanese sweets to finish. Set menus (Ran ¥2,600, Hana ¥3,300, Housenka ¥3,500, Ayame ¥3,800) change with the season. Note: authentic shojin does not use fish or katsuo dashi — broth is plant-based (kombu/shiitake) — but confirm directly if you have a strict requirement.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00; closed Tuesdays (open if Tuesday is a national holiday)
places.neighborhood: Ukyo Ward, Sagano/Toriimoto area near Arashiyama — about a 2
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Izusen (泉仙) is a long-established Kyoto purveyor of shojin ryori — Buddhist Zen temple vegetarian cuisine — with roots in the city's monastic catering tradition. The Sagano branch sits in the historic Sagano/Toriimoto district near Arashiyama, in a refined woo

roles: Sagano/Arashiyama branch of the Izusen company
chef_bio.background: Izusen (泉仙) is a long-established Kyoto purveyor of shojin ryori — Buddhist Zen temple vegetarian cuisine — with roots in the city's monastic catering tradition. The Sagano branch sits in the historic Sagano/Toriimoto district near Arashiyama and serves the same teppatsu (iron-bowl) shojin style for which the company is known across its Kyoto locations (including its temple branch at Daiji-in within the Daitoku-ji complex).
chef_bio.philosophy: Rooted in Zen Buddhist practice, the food is entirely plant-based, seasonal, and prepared with the shojin ethos of simplicity, gratitude, and non-killing — eating in harmony with nature and the temple calendar.
chef_bio.specialty: Teppatsu shojin ryori — a course meal served in a set of nested red-lacquer (朱塗り) bowls (typically seven, graded large to small) modeled on the teppatsu, the iron alms bowl monks historically carried to receive offerings. Signature items include sticky, nutty sesame tofu (goma-dofu), tofu fritters, vegetable tempura (shojin-age) such as shiitake, and simmered and vinegared/dressed seasonal vegetables, finished with matcha and a Japanese sweet.

anecdotes.text: Meals are served in stacked, luminous red-lacquer bowls and the menu shifts with the seasons — bamboo shoots and asparagus in spring, mushrooms and gingko/chestnuts in autumn — reflecting shojin cuisine's deep tie to the temple year.

The 'teppatsu' presentation uses bowls modeled on the iron alms bowl (鉄鉢) that Buddhist monks historically carried to receive food offerings, tying the modern dining experience to centuries-old monastic practice; the Sagano branch layers original shojin dishes across large-and-small nested bowls.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-izusen.com (Sagano and kodawari pages) and Japanese search confirm 精進鉄鉢料理 served in 朱塗り (red-lacquer) bowls modeled on the monk's iron alms bowl (鉄鉢), with goma-dofu, shojin-age, simmered and dressed vegetables in fucha-ryori style. Inside Kyoto corroborates the seasonal menu and signature sesame tofu/shiitake tempura.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is structurally central to the cuisine (soy sauce and fu/wheat gluten are core ingredients; tempura uses wheat batter), so gluten is present throughout the kitchen and cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable for a GF diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce used here is conventional wheat-containing soy sauce and is described as essential to Izusen's cooking and not modifiable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is a fully shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) operation serving no meat or fish, which greatly reduces the risk of animal cross-contact.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based shojin ryori — no meat, no fish — so vegan and vegetarian diners can eat the standard set menus without modification.
Traditional shojin excludes the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, etc.), helpful for diners avoiding those.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, tourist-frequented shojin spot in Sagano where set courses make ordering simple; basic Japanese greetings help, but the fixed vegetarian menus mean little language is needed to dine comfortably.

places.name: タローベーカリー (Taro Bakery)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bakery's identity is built on domestic (Hokkaido) wheat and natural-yeast wheat bread, so wheat is in essentially everything and the kitchen handles flour throughout. No dedicated gluten-free or wheat-free line was confirmed by reliable sources. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore bakery, but plant-based options are a core part of what they make. The base/plain bread (and a number of the daily loaves) is genuinely vegan: it is made from just four ingredients — Hokkaido domestic wheat, natural (wild) yeast, salt and water — with no butter, vegetable oil, milk, eggs or sugar, slow-fermented 15–18 hours. The owners say they put out "egg-free, dairy-free, sugar-free" items every day specifically for vegans and people with allergies. Seasonal sandwiches use pesticide-free Ukyo-grown vegetables (burdock, lotus root, shiitake, onion), but note that other pastries/sweets on the shelf may contain dairy/egg, so ask which specific items are plant-based on the day.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–19:00 (Wed–Fri); 9:00–16:00 (Sat); closed Sun, Mon, Tue. Phone 075-861-3015.
places.neighborhood: Ukyo Ward (Tokiwa), about a 1-minute walk from Tokiwa Statio
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Taro Bakery is run by Hideyasu Kito and his wife Sumiko. Kito trained as a bread craftsman in Aichi Prefecture, developed a love for honest, simple bread, and chose Kyoto to open his own shop in Tokiwa, Ukyo Ward (the shop opened in 2005).

chef_bio.background: Taro Bakery is run by Hideyasu Kito and his wife Sumiko. Kito trained as a bread craftsman in his native Aichi Prefecture, developed a love for honest, simple bread, and chose Kyoto to open his own shop in Tokiwa, Ukyo Ward (the shop opened around 2005).
chef_bio.philosophy: The couple's ethos is to use ingredients that are 'as delicious and as safe as possible.' They deliberately bake in small batches across many varieties so customers don't tire of the lineup, and they make a point of offering egg-free, dairy-free and sugar-free items every day for vegans and people with allergies.
chef_bio.specialty: Long-fermented natural-yeast loaves made with domestic Hokkaido wheat. The signature plain pain de mie and country breads (campagne) are built from just wheat, wild yeast, salt and water, slow-fermented 15–18 hours for a deep, simple flavor; seasonal sandwiches use pesticide-free vegetables grown locally in Ukyo.

anecdotes.text: The base plain bread is intentionally stripped to four ingredients — Hokkaido wheat, natural yeast, salt and water — with no butter, oil, milk, eggs or sugar, fermented 15–18 hours from the night before.
The shop has a cozy zakka-style (general-store) feel, and the owner's mother's hand-knitted items are sold alongside the bread, miso and preserves.
Sandwiches feature pesticide-free, organically grown vegetables from the surrounding Ukyo area — burdock, lotus root, shiitake and onion.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: digistyle-kyoto (2018) confirms baker Hideyasu Kito trained in native Aichi and opened ~13 years earlier (~2005) in Tokiwa, Ukyo Ward, with wife Sumiko; the 'as delicious and safe as possible' motto; deliberate small-batch variety (少量多種); daily egg/dairy/sugar-free breads; the four-ingredient Hokkaido-wheat loaf fermented 15–18 hours; and pesticide-free Ukyo vegetables in sandwiches.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat bakery: domestic wheat flour is used throughout and is handled across the whole kitchen, so flour cross-contact is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan loaves are baked in the same kitchen as breads that contain dairy/egg, so shared-equipment cross-contact is possible. Ask staff which specific items are fully plant-based that day.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owners deliberately put out egg-free, dairy-free and sugar-free items every day for vegans and people with allergies, indicating allergy awareness — though communication is in Japanese.
