positives.text: The plain/base bread is genuinely vegan, made from only Hokkaido wheat, natural yeast, salt and water — no butter, oil, milk, egg or sugar.
Emphasis on safe, simple ingredients and locally grown pesticide-free vegetables in the sandwiches.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood bakery run by a Japanese family; staff are warm but service is in Japanese, so a few polite phrases and pointing at the loaves will carry you a long way.

places.name: 夢や (Yume-Ya)
places.gf_detail: The defining draw per the VegeMap listing is "gluten- and dairy-free doughnuts," so at least the doughnuts are made gluten-free. This is not a confirmed fully-dedicated gluten-free kitchen, and there is no information on cross-contamination controls, shared equipment, or whether other items contain wheat. Celiac diners should confirm preparation directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Listed by Japan VegeMap as offering vegan option(s). The standout item is gluten- and dairy-free doughnuts paired with house-roasted coffee, which suggests plant-based-friendly baked goods. However, the VegeMap listing carries no menu detail and is flagged by VegeMap itself as unverified, so exactly which items are fully vegan (no eggs/honey) is not confirmed. A diner should tell staff their requirements and confirm each item before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Not published on the available source.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, near the Teramachi shopping arcade and Rokkaku-d
places.cuisine: Coffee shop / doughnut and bake stand
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Listed by Japan VegeMap as offering vegan option(s). The standout item is gluten- and dairy-free doughnuts paired with house-roasted coffee, which suggests plant-based-friendly baked goods. However, the VegeMap listing carries no menu detail and is flagged by

chef_bio.background: Yume-ya (written 夢屋, 'coffee shop 夢屋') is a small kissaten-style coffee and bake shop at 116 Yaoya-chō, Nakagyō-ku, Kyoto, in the Rokkaku-dōri / Teramachi arcade area. It operates roughly 10:30-18:00 and is typically closed Mondays (open on public holidays). Alongside coffee it serves house-made baked goods including doughnuts, scones and mini brownies, offered as part of lunch sets. Published listings confirm the address, hours and menu but do not name the proprietor or give a founding date.
chef_bio.specialty: Coffee with house-made bakes: doughnuts, scones and brownies (including a rice-flour baked-doughnut set), served in lunch sets.

anecdotes.text: Lunch sets pair drip coffee with a rotating selection of small house baked goods (doughnuts, scones, mini brownies).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese shop directories (always.fan, tabelog, kyotodekuraso donut roundup, Retty) confirm the Nakagyō-ku address (八百屋町116), ~10:30–18:00 hours, Monday closures (open on holidays), and the coffee-plus-baked-goods menu, but contain no owner biography or founding date.

staff_allergy_handling.text: VegeMap advises informing staff about dietary requirements before ordering, as the listing is unverified and offerings may differ from what is shown.

positives.text: The shop's signature offering is described as gluten- and dairy-free doughnuts alongside house-roasted coffee, indicating a deliberate focus on gluten-free and dairy-free baking.

cultural_comfort.note: A small Kyoto coffee-and-doughnut spot; basic Japanese or pointing will help, and a friendly "konnichiwa" plus showing your dietary needs should be enough for a quick takeaway order.

places.name: 発酵食堂カモシカ (Hakkō Shokudō Kamoshika (Fermented Food )
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or GF information is published. The kitchen's seasonings center on traditional Japanese fermented products — soy sauce (醤油), miso, and amazake. Their dressing ingredient list includes soy sauce, and standard Japanese shoyu and most miso contain wheat, so dishes should be assumed to contain gluten unless confirmed. Rice and many pickles are naturally gluten-free, but cross-use of wheat-containing soy sauce/miso is likely. A celiac/gluten-sensitive diner must ask directly; safety cannot be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The flagship dish, the "8-kinds fermented set meal" (発酵8種定食), contains fish: mackerel heshiko, pufferfish pickled in rice bran, and konjac dressed with fish sauce. However the kitchen leans heavily on plant-based fermentation, so vegan/plant-based choices exist: koji-natto served in/over deep-fried tofu, fermented vegetable pickles (nukazuke), white-miso soup, and salads dressed with their house brown-rice amazake dressing (explicitly labeled vegan/植物性: brown-rice amazake, rapeseed oil, onion, vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, plum, salt). A vegan diner should ask staff to omit the fish/fish-sauce components of the set or order plant-based items à la carte. White miso soup may use dashi — confirm it is kombu-based and not bonito.
places.hours_raw: Tuesday–Saturday 11:30–17:00 (lunch service until 15:00; 15:00–17:00 light meals/café). Closed Sunday and Monday. Reservations recommended during the lunch rush.
places.neighborhood: Ukyo Ward, Saga-Arashiyama; about a 1-3 minute walk from the
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Founded in May 2014 in Saga-Arashiyama, Kamoshika is a restaurant-and-shop devoted to fermented food, opened by Megumi Seki. The name カモシカ comes from 醸し家 (kamoshi-ya, 'brewing house'). It pairs a ground-floor dining room with a 2nd-floor 'Fermentation Marché'

chef_bio.background: Founded in May 2014 in Saga-Arashiyama, Kamoshika is a restaurant-and-shop devoted to fermented food, opened by Megumi Seki. The name カモシカ comes from 醸し家 (kamoshi-ya, 'brewing house'), reflecting the in-house fermentation. It pairs a ground-floor dining room with a 2nd-floor 'Fermentation Marché' (発酵マルシェ) selling house-made and nationwide fermented goods, plus DIY fermentation kits and online workshops.
chef_bio.philosophy: Their motto is 'returning fermented foods to the kitchen' (発酵食を台所に取り戻す) and proposing a '発酵のある暮らし' (a life with fermentation), emphasizing health, flavor, and sustainability through traditional, additive-free fermented seasonings.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 発酵8種定食 (8-kinds fermented set meal), in which everything but the rice is fermented — including koji-natto, nukazuke pickles, white-miso soup, and Awa-bancha fermented tea. Popular plant-based items include koji-natto over deep-fried tofu and salads with house brown-rice amazake dressing.

anecdotes.text: The shop name カモシカ is a play on 醸し家 ('brewing house'), reflecting that the food is fermented (醸す) on-site under its all-fermentation theme.
Beyond dining, Kamoshika runs a 2nd-floor Fermentation Marché and online fermentation workshops, treating fermentation as a whole lifestyle rather than just a meal.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: co-trip and kamoshika.kyoto.jp confirm founder Seki Megumi (関恵), the May 2014 Saga-Arashiyama opening, the 醸し家 → カモシカ name origin, the 1F dining / 2F 発酵マルシェ structure with online workshops, and the 発酵8種定食 in which everything but the rice is fermented (koji-natto, nukazuke, white-miso soup, Awa-bancha tea).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional Japanese soy sauce and miso (core to this kitchen) typically contain wheat; no GF accommodation is published, so gluten cross-contact via seasonings should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house brown-rice amazake dressing ingredient list includes 醤油 (soy sauce); there is no indication it is a wheat-free tamari, so it should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore kitchen: the signature set meal includes mackerel heshiko, pufferfish nukazuke, and fish-sauce konjac, so fish is handled in the same kitchen as plant-based dishes. White miso soup may use a fish (bonito) dashi — confirm it is kombu-only.

positives.text: Strong focus on traditional, additive-free fermented seasonings (naturally brewed soy sauce, miso, mirin, house-made koji and brown-rice amazake), and clearly identified vegan/plant-based items such as the brown-rice amazake dressing, koji-natto with fried tofu, and vegetable nukazuke.

cultural_comfort.note: An official English website exists, but it's a small traditional fermented-food restaurant; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will help when asking to omit the fish components or to check for wheat/dashi.

places.name: ORTO（オルト） (ORTO)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu is course-based and bread/pasta feature heavily (e.g. shiitake bread, grilled negi bread, maitake pasta), so wheat is prevalent. The restaurant asks guests to declare all allergies and dislikes in advance of the visit (it cannot accommodate requests after arrival), which means a gluten-free adaptation would have to be negotiated when reserving. Whether soy sauce is wheat-free and whether any dashi is used in the vegan course are not stated — confirm directly. Treat GF as ask/by-arrangement only.
places.vegan_detail: ORTO is an omnivore innovative restaurant (its standard tasting course features fish, octopus, duck and house-made bacon) but it offers a dedicated, fully vegan multi-course menu and a separate vegetarian course — one of the few high-end Kyoto restaurants with a genuine 100% vegan set course. The kitchen defines vegan as no meat, fish, shellfish, dairy, eggs or honey. The cooking centers on seasonal Kyoto vegetables (kyoyasai), edible flowers and herbs, with restrained salt and oil. Per VegeMap, vegan reservations are accepted at lunch only and must be arranged in advance — you cannot order the vegan course on the spot. Always reserve the vegan/vegetarian course ahead and state your needs clearly when booking.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun & public holidays 18:00-22:00; closed Tue & Wed. (Vegan course reservations noted as lunch/noon only by VegeMap — confirm when booking.)
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, central Kyoto — on Koromodana-dori just south of
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Owner-chef Shinji Tanimura grew up surrounded by Kyoto's famous vegetables (kyoyasai). After culinary school he trained in Italian cuisine, then rediscovered the power of vegetables while cooking at a French restaurant. At 31 he traveled to Italy before return

chef_bio.origin: Born 1976 in Kumiyama, just south of Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Shinji Tanimura grew up surrounded by Kyoto's famous vegetables (kyoyasai). After culinary school he trained in Italian cuisine, then rediscovered the power of vegetables while cooking at a French restaurant. At 31 he traveled to Italy before returning home to open ORTO in central Kyoto in August 2008. In March 2016 he reworked the restaurant around its 'vegetable garden' identity.
chef_bio.philosophy: 'ORTO' is Italian for 'vegetable garden.' Tanimura cooks to let seasonal ingredients breathe, deliberately restraining salt and oil so the natural strength of each vegetable comes through all five senses, transcending the boundaries of any single cuisine.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Kyoto-vegetable course cuisine blending Italian and French technique. Signature plant-forward dishes include a 'garden' (菜園) salad of around 30 vegetables, herbs and edible flowers, and house-baked breads such as grilled negi (leek) bread, alongside seasonal vegetable-led pastas. Beyond the omnivore tasting menu, ORTO is one of very few Kyoto restaurants offering dedicated vegetarian and full vegan multi-course menus.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant dropped 'Ristorante' from its signage, signaling a deliberate move beyond traditional Italian boundaries into genre-transcending, vegetable-led cuisine.

After the 2016 renovation the 'vegetable garden' concept became central, and ORTO began offering dedicated vegetarian and vegan menus so diners could experience the quality of Kyoto vegetables through all five senses.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: italianweek100 confirms chef Tanimura was born 1976 in Kumiyama, trained in Italian then French cuisine (where he rediscovered vegetables), went to Italy at 31, opened ORTO in Aug 2008, and renovated in March 2016 dropping 'Ristorante' to focus on seasonal Kyoto vegetables with reduced salt/oil and dedicated vegetarian/vegan menus. takemachelin corroborates the ~30-vegetable garden salad and in-house negi bread.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread and pasta are core to the menu (shiitake/negi breads, maitake pasta), so wheat and gluten are heavily present in the kitchen; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore kitchen whose standard course includes fish, octopus, duck and house-made bacon, so animal ingredients are handled alongside the vegan course; the vegan menu is prepared to order rather than in a separate animal-free kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant asks guests to inform them of all allergies and dislikes in advance and states it cannot accommodate requests made after arrival — so any dietary needs must be arranged at the time of reservation.
The kitchen offers a true 100% vegan course and defines vegan strictly as no meat, fish, shellfish, dairy, eggs or honey, indicating clear understanding of plant-based requirements.

positives.text: One of the few high-end Kyoto restaurants offering a genuine full vegan multi-course menu built around seasonal Kyoto vegetables, herbs and edible flowers.
Because the vegan course is reserved and prepared individually in advance, the chef can plan a fully animal-free plate rather than improvising on arrival.

cultural_comfort.note: A refined reservation-only course restaurant that appears in English vegan guides and takes online bookings; a little Japanese or a clearly-worded advance reservation stating your vegan needs will make the experience smooth.

places.name: ケララ (Kerala)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program documented. Rice dishes (saffron/basmati rice) and rice-flour dosas are naturally wheat-free, but naan, papad, pakora batter and some curry thickeners may contain wheat, and shared cookware means cross-contact is likely. No information on dedicated fryers or wheat-free soy sauce. A GF-sensitive or celiac diner must ask staff directly about each dish.
places.vegan_detail: Omnivore Indian restaurant with a strong vegetable focus rooted in Kerala/Ayurvedic cuisine, offering many plant-based choices. Reviewers report a range of vegan-adaptable dishes: tofu curry, chana masala, aloo gobi, dal, vegetable pakoras, and masala dosa. Vegan items are not labeled on the menu, but English-speaking staff can advise which dishes can be made vegan by omitting dairy and eggs (and confirm whether chutneys/accompaniments are vegan). Naan and many breads contain wheat; the soybean/lotus-root curry and rice-based dosas are good plant-based picks. Confirm ghee, cream (used in some curries), and dairy in breads when ordering.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun: Lunch 11:30-14:00, Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Monday. Some guides note irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, downtown Kyoto near Kawaramachi-Sanjo; about 2
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Kerala is one of downtown Kyoto's longstanding Indian restaurants, tucked on the second floor of the KUS Building just north of Kawaramachi-Sanjo. It is named for the South Indian state of Kerala, the birthplace of Ayurveda, and serves both South and North Ind

chef_bio.background: Kerala is one of downtown Kyoto's longstanding Indian restaurants, tucked on the second floor of the KUS Building just north of Kawaramachi-Sanjo. It is named for the South Indian state of Kerala, the birthplace of Ayurveda, and serves both South and North Indian cooking from a famously compact, corridor-like dining room decorated with Indian furnishings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on Kerala and Ayurvedic ideas of light, vegetable-rich, easy-to-digest food, which gives plant-based diners an unusually wide field of choices for a non-vegetarian Indian restaurant; most vegetarian dishes can be made vegan on request by asking for no dairy or egg.
chef_bio.specialty: Crispy rice-based dosas (the masala dosa is a favourite) and an extensive curry menu (often described as 40-plus varieties), with vegetable-forward options like chana masala, aloo gobi and dal. The house tomato (and cream) soup is a signature, and the good-value lunch sets pair curries with saffron rice and bread.

anecdotes.text: Guides describe the space as little more than a cramped corridor lined with tables, yet praise it as one of the best places downtown to take a break from Japanese food while shopping.
The restaurant frames its menu around Ayurvedic principles from Kerala state, offering healthy, vegetable-abundant dishes that are light and easy to digest.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: k021500.gorp confirms the restaurant is named for Kerala state (birthplace of Ayurveda), serves authentic South Indian cooking with 40-plus curries, and that tomato-cream soup is a top seller (28 seats total). HappyCow reviews confirm both South and North Indian dishes, dosas (masala dosa), chana masala/aloo gobi/dal, and that most vegetarian dishes can be made vegan on request. Inside Kyoto confirms the 2F KUS Building location and cramped-corridor space.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free program is documented; naan, papad and fried items share a kitchen with wheat, so a wheat-sensitive diner should ask before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore Indian kitchen that also serves meat and dairy dishes; many curries use cream or ghee, so vegan diners should request no dairy/eggs and confirm each dish.

staff_allergy_handling.text: English-speaking staff can tell which dishes can be made vegan and will confirm whether dips and accompaniments like chutneys are dairy/egg-free.

positives.text: A wide range of vegetable-based and adaptable plant dishes (tofu/soybean curry, chana masala, aloo gobi, dal, vegetable pakoras, masala dosa), with staff used to advising vegan diners.
Vegetable-forward, Ayurveda-inspired menu makes plant-based ordering easier than at a typical non-vegetarian Indian restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff are used to explaining which dishes can be made vegan, so it's comfortable for a foreign student to ask about ingredients in English.

places.name: ぴっぱら (Pippara)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Wheat chapati and battered fried snacks are core to the set; no GF program or dedicated fryer. A GF diner could potentially eat turmeric rice with a vegetable curry, but cross-contamination from shared wheat handling and frying makes it unsuitable for strict celiacs without careful confirmation. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Omnivore Indian/Nepalese curry counter, not vegan-dedicated. The standard set includes fish (often mackerel, varies daily) and dairy (lassi, yogurt, paneer snack, possibly ghee). It is vegetarian-friendly: the plate contains several plant-based vegetable and legume curries (potato-tomato, chana/chickpea). A vegan should explicitly ask to skip fish/meat and all dairy (no yogurt/lassi, confirm oil vs ghee, check the coconut chutney). Onion/garlic likely present. Vegan is possible-on-request, not a fixed menu.
places.hours_raw: Fri, Sat, Sun 12:00-18:00 (may close earlier if sold out). Closed Mon-Thu, plus the day before and day of the Spring and Autumn Equinoxes.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo-ku (Kitashirakawa), down an alley just east of the Kyo
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Pippara is a famously tiny (about 10-seat) home-style Indian/Nepalese curry counter tucked down an alley just east of the Kyoto University Faculty of Agriculture, where it has long been a gathering spot for students. Open only Friday through Sunday, it serves

chef_bio.background: Pippara is a famously tiny (about 10-seat) home-style Indian/Nepalese curry counter tucked down an alley by the Kyoto University Faculty of Agriculture, where it has long been a gathering spot for students. Open only Friday through Sunday, it serves an all-in set plate of several curries with turmeric rice, freshly baked chapati, a fried snack and a small lassi for an unusually low price (around 780 yen).
chef_bio.philosophy: The name comes from pippala (Sanskrit for the bodhi/sacred fig tree under which the Buddha is said to have attained enlightenment), framing the little shop as a place for seekers, fitting for its university crowd. The owner runs it with deliberate, almost monastic house rules: greet loudly on entry, wait for your order to be taken, order decisively, eat cleanly and finish within about 60 minutes.
chef_bio.specialty: An all-in-one multi-curry set plate: typically several curries that rotate daily (including a chana/legume curry and a fish option), served with turmeric rice, freshly griddled chapati and a fried snack.

anecdotes.text: The name Pippara derives from the bodhi (pippala) tree under which the Buddha attained enlightenment, casting the shop as a meeting place ('the bodhi tree where Kyoto University students gather').

The proprietor is known for strict but affectionate house rules; in one anecdote she scolded a regular who asked for a bigger lassi — not for asking, but for not noticing she had already poured him an extra-large one as a service ('I always give you the large one without asking! Don't you understand?').
The shop is a set-meal place for people who order quickly, eat cleanly, and don't linger (within about 60 minutes).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The ameblo report confirms the pippala/bodhi-tree name and the 'bodhi tree where Kyoto U students gather' framing, ~10 seats, Fri–Sun opening, the ~780-yen all-in set (curries + turmeric rice + chapati + fried snack + small lassi), the lassi-scolding anecdote, and the ~60-minute rule; search corroborates the strict entry etiquette (greet, wait for the order to be taken, order '一通り').

gf_cross_contamination.text: Freshly baked chapati (wheat) is part of the standard set and fried snacks use batter, so wheat is handled openly in the kitchen; no gluten-free accommodation is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore kitchen that also cooks fish/meat curries and dairy items (lassi, yogurt, paneer) in the same small space, so vegan dishes are prepared alongside animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Vegetarian options are explicitly available, but the owner runs a fast, no-reservations counter with strict house rules and limited time per customer, so make any vegan/allergy requests quickly and clearly.

positives.text: Vegetarian options are confirmed available, and the daily plate already includes multiple plant-based vegetable and legume curries (e.g. potato-tomato and chana/chickpea), giving a vegetarian or flexible-vegan diner real choices.
Curries are described as light, home-style and gentle on the body rather than heavy, with an inexpensive all-in set plate.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, quirky student-favorite counter run by a famously strict but caring owner who expects a polite greeting, a quick decision, clean eating and leaving within about 60 minutes — a few words of Japanese and good manners go a long way, so come ready to order promptly.

places.name: 茶房 半兵衛
places.gf_detail: Ask about gluten-free options. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: (Menu review: 3 vegan meals.)
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Hanbeifu was founded in 1689 (Genroku 2) by Tamaoki Hanbei and has handcrafted Kyoto fu (wheat gluten) and yuba (tofu skin) for over three centuries, at the south-east foot of the Gojo Ohashi bridge along the Kamo River. Within its renovated machiya complex, a

roles: Long-established (since 1689) Kyoto fu and yuba maker
Tea house serving plant-based fu/yuba cuisine
chef_bio.background: Hanbei-Fu was founded in 1689 (Genroku 2) by Tamaoki Hanbei and has handcrafted Kyoto fu (wheat gluten) and yuba (tofu skin) for over three centuries, at the south-east corner of the Kawabata-Gojo intersection by the Gojo Ohashi bridge, overlooking the Kamo River. Within its machiya complex, a Showa-era building (designed by architect Ryo Tanahashi) houses the tea house, Sabo Hanbei.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fu and yuba were introduced to Japan by Zen monks, and the house's cooking belongs to the shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian) lineage, centered on plant-based proteins and respect for water, ingredients and craftsmanship.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature offering is the 'Mushiyashinai' course (むしやしない / 虫養い), a Kyoto-dialect name for a light meal that gently staves off hunger, showcasing an array of fu and yuba dishes.

anecdotes.text: The signature yuba-and-fu course is called 'Mushiyashinai' — a Kyoto word (虫養い) for a light bite that gently nourishes the stomach.
Fu is made from wheat flour mixed with water, kneaded and washed to remove the starch, and was brought to Japan by Zen monks as part of shojin ryori.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kanpai-japan and hanbey.co.jp confirm the 1689 founding by Tamaoki Hanbei, the 300-plus-year fu/yuba craft, the location at the SE corner of Kawabata-Gojo by Gojo Ohashi over the Kamo River, the Showa-era building (by architect Ryo Tanahashi) housing the Sabo Hanbei tea house, fu as wheat gluten introduced by Zen monks, and the 'Mushiyashinai' course. (The romanization was corrected from 'Mushi Yoinai.')

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is built around fu, which is concentrated wheat gluten, so the kitchen is inherently wheat-heavy and not suitable for gluten-avoidant or celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free soy sauce was confirmed; standard Japanese soy sauce and seasonings typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Default broth/dashi is not confirmed vegan; a vegan-friendly soup stock must be requested at least three days in advance and cannot be arranged same-day.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant will accommodate a vegan broth if notified at least three days ahead, indicating they handle dietary requests with advance notice.

positives.text: Core dishes are plant-based fu (wheat gluten) and yuba (tofu skin) in the shojin vegetarian tradition, so the menu is largely free of meat and fish.

cultural_comfort.note: Vegan-friendly spot; many such places in Kyoto are used to international visitors.

places.name: すし善 (Sushizen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. Standard sushi-shop soy sauce (shoyu) typically contains wheat, and there is no indication of tamari or wheat-free options. The rolls and rice are not documented as gluten-free. A gluten-sensitive diner should ask in advance and bring their own GF soy sauce; cross-contact in a busy fish-prep kitchen is likely.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional seafood sushi shop, not a vegan/vegetarian establishment. The menu is overwhelmingly fish-based (chirashi, nigiri, tekka/anago/saba). The only plant-based maki are the cucumber roll (kappa-maki, ¥900) and the plum-shiso roll (ume-shiso-maki, ¥900), which are vegan; shinko-maki (pickled radish roll, ¥900) is also plant-based. Futomaki and tamago-maki contain egg and are not vegan. There is no published confirmation of whether the sushi rice/vinegar, pickled radish, or rolls use animal- or wheat-free seasonings, so a strict vegan should ask directly. A vegan diner would have very few choices here.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 10:00–18:00 (open until sold out); closed Saturday, Sunday, and public holidays
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, near Karasuma Oike subway station, on Sanjo-dori
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Sushizen is a long-established Kyoto sushi shop founded in 1939, tucked on Sanjo-dori in the Karasuma Oike area of Nakagyo-ku. It has served the same neighborhood for generations, with regulars said to span three generations of families.

chef_bio.background: Sushizen is a long-established Kyoto sushi shop that opened as a sushi restaurant in 1939 (Showa 14), having started as a fish shop, on Sanjo-dori (Shinmachi) in the Karasuma-Oike area of Nakagyo-ku. It has served the same neighborhood for generations, long loved by local families.
chef_bio.philosophy: An everyday, good-value neighborhood shop rather than a high-end counter; the rice is given a sharper vinegared edge to balance the toppings, a deliberate Kyoto take on chirashi.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is a colorful chirashi sushi: a mound of golden shredded egg crepe (kinshi-tamago) blanketing seafood — tuna, sea bream, amberjack, grilled anago, octopus and squid — over seasoned rice, finished with a house sweet sauce. It also offers nigiri, futomaki, and Kyoto-style pressed (hako-zushi/oshi) sushi.

anecdotes.text: Recognized in the MICHELIN Guide as a Bib Gourmand selection (since the 2023 Kyoto & Osaka guide) for good-quality, good-value cooking.
The most popular item is the seafood chirashi (around 1,700–1,850 yen), topped with shredded egg crepe over an abundant bed of fish, finished with a house sweet sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese search (amakara, kyotopi, tabelog magazine) confirms Sushizen opened as a sushi shop in 1939 from a former fish shop, on Sanjo-Shinmachi in Nakagyo-ku, with a signature kinshi-tamago chirashi over tuna/sea bream/amberjack/grilled anago/octopus/squid finished with a sweet sauce, and all-day 10:00–18:00 service. The Michelin Guide lists it as a Bib Gourmand selection (first in the 2023 Kyoto & Osaka guide).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce details published; standard sushi-shop shoyu generally contains wheat. Assume wheat-containing soy sauce unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a seafood-focused sushi kitchen; vegan rolls (cucumber, plum-shiso) are prepared in a space that primarily handles raw fish, so cross-contact with fish is likely.

positives.text: A couple of genuinely plant-based maki are on the menu: cucumber roll (¥900) and plum-shiso roll (¥900).

cultural_comfort.note: A long-standing neighborhood sushi shop popular for takeaway; an English menu exists on listing sites but staff communication is likely mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases help.

places.name: プレマルシェ・ジェラテリア 三条本店 (Premarché Gelateria Kyoto Sanjo (Honten))
places.gf_detail: The shop states none of its gelato contains gluten-containing ingredients, and a gluten-free option is offered. Standard cones/cups should be confirmed, but they sell an organic mochi (rice-based) gluten-free waffle cup (about ¥100) specifically for guests with wheat allergies. Because gelato itself is gluten-free, the main GF concern is the serving vessel — request the mochi gluten-free cup. No in-house wheat/soy-sauce cooking is involved (this is a dessert shop), reducing gluten cross-contact risk.
places.vegan_detail: A natural/organic gelato specialist offering 40+ flavors at any time, including one of Japan's largest selections of vegan and non-dairy ("non-milk") gelato. The menu mixes dairy gelato with clearly-labeled vegan options: pure vegan gelato (no animal products, honey or added oils) and fruit-and-sugar sorbetto, plus signature vegan flavors such as premium Sicilian pistachio and organic olive oil & almond, and non-milk flavors like black sugar with organic kinako. Diners pick from a rotating case, so vegan availability is genuine and broad but not the whole menu — ask staff to point out the vegan vs. milk flavors.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu 12:00–18:00; Fri/Sat/Sun & holidays 12:00–20:00; closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, along Sanjo-kai Shotengai (covered shopping arca
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan/vegetarian] Premarché Gelateria grew out of Premarché Organics, a Kyoto-based natural and organic food company. The team channeled its organic-ingredient expertise into making gelato, opening this Sanjo main store as the flagship along the Sanjo-kai shopping arcade near N

chef_bio.background: Premarché Gelateria grew out of Premarché Organics, a Kyoto-based natural and organic food company. The team channeled its organic-ingredient expertise into making gelato, opening this Sanjo main store as the flagship along the Sanjo-kai shopping arcade near Nijo Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: Inclusivity through dessert: offering many types of gelato so that people with allergies and dietary restrictions can gather and enjoy ice cream together — including those who previously could never eat it.
chef_bio.specialty: Natural, organic Italian-style gelato and sorbetto with one of Japan's largest vegan and non-dairy selections (40-plus rotating flavors) — signature vegan flavors include premium Sicilian pistachio and organic olive oil & almond, plus non-milk flavors like black sugar with organic kinako.

anecdotes.text: The head gelato maker placed 3rd internationally in a vegan gelato contest run by the Italian Gelato Association (and 10th for non-milk gelato), reportedly only the third Japanese gelatiere to earn such recognition.
Staff recount a parent bringing an allergic child who had never been able to eat ice cream, finally getting to share the experience — a story the shop ties to its inclusive mission.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: vegewel confirms Premarché Gelateria grew out of Premarché Organics, opened on the Sanjo-kai arcade near Nijo Castle, and offers Japan's largest vegan/non-dairy gelato line (40+ flavors) including Sicilian pistachio, organic olive-oil & almond, and black-sugar kinako. It states the head maker placed 3rd for vegan gelato and 10th for non-milk at the Italian Gelato Association contest as only the third Japanese individual so recognized, and recounts the allergic-child story.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop states none of its gelato contains gluten-containing ingredients; main residual concern is the serving vessel (standard cones/waffle cups), so request the rice-based gluten-free option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan and dairy gelato are scooped from the same case, so shared scoops/utensils mean some dairy cross-contact is possible; fine for dietary vegans but worth asking if you have a milk allergy.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy information is available and the shop explicitly accommodates food allergies on request, with alternative serving vessels for wheat-allergic guests.

positives.text: All gelato is gluten-free and a dedicated gluten-free organic mochi waffle cup (~¥100) is offered for wheat-allergic guests.
One of Japan's largest selections of vegan and non-dairy gelato, with clearly distinguished pure-vegan and sorbetto options made from fruit and sugar only.
Award-winning vegan gelato (3rd place in an international Italian Gelato Association contest) signals genuine expertise in plant-based recipes.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up gelato counter used to international and allergy-conscious customers; pointing at flavors works, and a few words of Japanese or English go a long way for asking which flavors are vegan or gluten-free.

places.name: アラシのキッチン (Arash's Kitchen)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program confirmed. Naturally GF-leaning items exist (Persian rice dishes, dal, falafel is typically chickpea-based, hummus, eggplant dips), but falafel can contain wheat flour as a binder and naan/pizza/some appetizers contain wheat. Tabelog lists "gluten-free options" as a tag but no preparation/cross-contamination detail is published, and a shared kitchen frying naan/breaded items is likely. Celiac travelers should call ahead (+81 80-5366-5573) to confirm each dish. No information on soy sauce or dashi (this is Persian/Indian cuisine, so Japanese wheat soy sauce/dashi are unlikely to be central, but unconfirmed). (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Omnivore Halal restaurant with a meat-forward kabab/curry menu but a solid roster of plant-based dishes. Vegan-likely items include falafel, hummus tahini, baba ghanoush, kashk-e-bademjan (eggplant; note kashk is a fermented whey product so ask), salad shirazi, dal (lentil) curry, and several Persian rice dishes (basmati, zereshk polo, baghali polo). Some "vegetarian" items contain dairy (palak paneer, mast-o-khiar yogurt dip, vegetarian pizza cheese), so distinguish vegan from vegetarian when ordering. The menu does not visibly label dishes vegan/vegetarian, so confirm dairy/egg/butter (ghee) per dish; the kitchen advertises egg-free and dairy-free accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:00-23:03 (L.O. 22:30); Tue-Thu, Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:30); Fri 11:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00). Lunch ~11:00-15:00, dinner thereafter. Open year-round (no regular closing day).
places.neighborhood: Shogoin, Sakyo-ku — near Jingu-Marutamachi Station (Keihan l
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Chef Arash Sodagaran moved to Kyoto from Iran in 2009 and opened Arash's Kitchen in 2013. He trained at renowned Tehran establishments including the Hani and Nayeb restaurants and holds a hotel-housekeeping diploma from Hotel Evin, then honed his craft across

chef_bio.background: Chef Arash Sodagaran moved to Kyoto from Iran in 2009 and opened Arash's Kitchen in 2013. He gained early experience at renowned Tehran establishments including the Hani and Nayeb restaurants and holds a hotel-housekeeping diploma from Hotel Evin, then honed his craft across Persian and Indian restaurants in Kyoto and Tokyo before launching his own venue.
chef_bio.philosophy: A Halal kitchen built on 'high-quality food and authentic flavors' that blends Persian, Indian, Turkish and Arabic traditions and makes a point of welcoming diverse dietary needs, including egg-free and dairy-free guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic Persian/Iranian cooking — kababs, slow-cooked stews, saffron and barberry rice dishes (zereshk polo, baghali polo) — alongside Indian curries, naan, and mezze such as hummus, falafel, baba ghanoush and kashk-e-bademjan.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant hosts a free belly-dance performance every Saturday at 19:30 with no cover charge, giving the dining room a festive Middle-Eastern atmosphere.
Chef Arash trained at Tehran's Hani and Nayeb restaurants and worked in Persian and Indian kitchens in Kyoto and Tokyo before opening his own place in 2013.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: arashskitchen.com/about-us confirms chef Arash Sodagaran was born in Iran, trained at Tehran's Hani and Nayeb restaurants, holds a Hotel Housekeeping diploma from Hotel Evin, moved to Kyoto in 2009, worked in Persian and Indian restaurants in Kyoto and Tokyo, and opened Arash's Kitchen in 2013 as a Halal kitchen spanning Persian/Iranian/Indian/Turkish/Arabic cuisine with egg-free and dairy-free options. Tripadvisor and the site confirm the free Saturday 19:30 belly-dance show.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen prepares naan and other wheat-based items alongside dishes; no dedicated gluten-free preparation or fryer is documented, so cross-contact is likely. Celiac guests should confirm directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a Halal omnivore kitchen cooking meat kababs and curries; vegan dishes are made in the same kitchen, so shared-surface/utensil contact with meat and dairy is possible. Some 'vegetarian' items contain dairy (paneer, yogurt, cheese) — confirm vegan vs vegetarian per dish.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant publicly states it accommodates vegetarian, egg-free and dairy-free diners and invites guests to contact them about dietary needs by phone or email.

positives.text: Wide selection of naturally plant-based mezze and mains (falafel, hummus, baba ghanoush, salad shirazi, dal, and several Persian rice dishes) makes a satisfying vegan meal feasible.
Halal kitchen with no pork on premises and explicit willingness to prepare egg-free and dairy-free dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: The Iranian owner-chef and staff operate an English-friendly, international (Halal) restaurant with an English website and menu, so a foreign student can order comfortably in English.

places.name: ケ・パサ (QUÉ PASA)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free-friendly spot. The core menu is burritos (flour/wheat tortillas) and the kitchen makes heavy use of wheat, so there is no documented gluten-free offering or accommodation. Corn-tortilla tacos may be naturally wheat-free, but no dedicated GF preparation, fryer, or cross-contamination control is documented; a celiac diner should not assume safety. Ask directly before relying on any item. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: Omnivore Mexican burrito shop with solid vegan choices. The vegetarian burrito and vegetarian taco can be made vegan by asking for no cheese and no sour cream; bean burritos, tacos, nachos, and three fresh salsas are available with modifications. The owner understands veganism and has made custom tofu and shiitake-mushroom burritos on request. There is also an all-vegan menu run roughly once a month. Filling proteins like beef and pork are NOT vegan, so always specify vegan / no cheese / no sour cream when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 17:30-21:30 (open daily per listing; some sources note evening hours vary by day)
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo-ku, on Kawaramachi-dori near Kojinguchi, about a 4-m
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] QUÉ PASA started because its owner missed the burritos eaten constantly while a student in the United States. After graduating from a Japanese university and moving to Kyoto to help with the family's bok choy (pak choi) wholesale business, the owner began sell

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan (owner shaped by student years in the U.S.)
chef_bio.background: QUÉ PASA started because its owner missed the burritos eaten constantly while a student in the United States. After graduating from a Japanese university and moving to Kyoto to help with the family's bok choy (pak choi) wholesale business, the owner began selling burritos despite having no prior restaurant experience, self-teaching through YouTube and customer feedback.
chef_bio.philosophy: To create a casual, come-anytime place where anyone can get a consistently delicious meal whether grabbing a quick bite or gathering with friends, faithfully recreating authentic American-Mexican burrito flavors in Kyoto.
chef_bio.specialty: Generously sized California-style burritos and tacos, with vegetable-forward and made-to-order vegan versions (including custom tofu and shiitake burritos).

anecdotes.text: The shop reopened in April 2024 at the same Kawaramachi-Kojinguchi spot after the building was rebuilt, keeping its relaxed American-diner atmosphere that draws many international visitors.
The owner is happy to accommodate vegans, having created off-menu items like tofu and shiitake burritos on request, and runs an all-vegan menu about once a month.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Leaf Kyoto (Japanese lifestyle magazine) confirms the owner studied in the U.S., missed burritos there, and after helping the family pak-choi wholesale business opened QUÉ PASA with no restaurant background, refining recipes via YouTube and customer feedback; it reopened April 2024 after the building was rebuilt at the Kawaramachi area (荒神口/Kōjinguchi) location.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-heavy burrito kitchen (flour tortillas); no gluten-free preparation or cross-contamination control is documented, so celiac diners should treat it as unsafe unless they confirm details directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: It is an omnivore kitchen serving beef and pork alongside vegan items, so shared surfaces and utensils are likely; ask for clean preparation if strict.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owner is described as English-speaking, knowledgeable about veganism, and accommodating, willing to omit cheese/sour cream and build custom vegan burritos (e.g., tofu, shiitake) on request.

positives.text: Vegan diners can get a substantial, flavorful burrito or taco with three fresh salsas, and the staff actively understands and supports vegan requests.
A dedicated all-vegan menu is offered roughly once a month for fully animal-free dining.

cultural_comfort.note: English is comfortable here, with an American-diner vibe and staff who speak English and readily handle custom and vegan requests.

places.name: ダ・マエダ (da Maeda)
places.gf_detail: The official site and dietary directories list a gluten-free menu as available, but the core of the restaurant is Italian (wood-fired pizza, pasta, breaded items), so wheat is central to the standard offerings and gluten-free dishes must be requested. There is no published statement confirming a dedicated gluten-free preparation area, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce, so a celiac diner should call ahead (075-465-5258) and confirm cross-contamination handling in person. Treat as gluten-free-by-request, not dedicated.
places.vegan_detail: da Maeda is an organic Italian restaurant in a renovated Kyoto townhouse that offers a clearly marked vegan and vegetarian menu alongside its regular dishes — it is not a fully plant-based kitchen. Vegan plant-based plates include a soy patty in porcini red-wine demiglace, a breaded soy cutlet with almond-caper sauce, chickpea croquettes, and a vegetable curry/stew with pita, plus a vegetarian pizza. Vegetables come largely from the owner's own organic farm in Nantan, Kyoto. Staff speak English and ask you to state vegan/vegetarian needs and allergies when ordering so they can guide the menu. The same family runs a separate, fully-vegan concept, Lapis (vegan sushi kaiseki), by reservation at the same machiya.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed 11:30-14:30; Fri-Sun 11:30-15:00 and 17:30-22:00; Closed Thursday. (Sister concept Lapis has separate reservation-only hours.)
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo-ku, near Senbon-dori x Nakadachiuri, west of the Kyo
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] The Maeda story begins in the soil. After more than 35 years cooking — including training in Italy at fine-dining and Michelin-starred kitchens — Takamitsu Maeda turned toward organic, plant-forward food. He launched an organic online shop in 2007, took on far

chef_bio.background: The Maeda story begins in the soil. After more than 35 years cooking — including training in Italy at fine-dining and Michelin-starred kitchens — Takamitsu Maeda turned toward organic, plant-forward food. He launched an organic online shop in 2007, took on farmland in Nantan, Kyoto in 2009 to grow his own vegetables, and opened the vegan restaurant Organic House Salute near Kyoto Station in 2013. da Maeda is his organic Italian table set inside a roughly 110-year-old renovated Kyoto townhouse (machiya), where the vegetables he farms become wood-fired pizza, pasta, and plant-based plates.
chef_bio.philosophy: A circular, soil-to-soil ethos: 'starting with soil cultivation, through farming and dining, and returning to the soil.' Food should be kind to the body and to the planet, built on organic and naturally-farmed ingredients sourced from the chef's own land and like-minded producers across Japan.
chef_bio.specialty: Organic Italian cooking from the chef's own farm, with standout vegan plates such as a breaded soy cutlet in almond-caper sauce, a soy patty in porcini red-wine demiglace, chickpea croquettes, and wood-fired vegetable pizza.

anecdotes.text: In 2019 Maeda opened a sister concept at the same machiya — Lapis, a fully-vegan sushi kaiseki served entirely by reservation, where a 15-course plant-based meal is plated on antique Japanese dishes and lacquerware.
In 2021 he created WAoWA, billed as Japan's first trademark-registered fermented coffee brand, extending the fermentation-and-circulation theme that runs through the kitchen.
A guest singled out the breaded soy cutlet with almond-caper sauce as surprisingly rich and satisfying — substantial enough that they wished for a larger portion.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: da-maeda.com and its Lapis page confirm Maeda's 30+/35-year cooking career with Michelin-starred Italian training, the 2007 organic online shop, 2009 Nantan farmland, 2013 Organic House Salute, the ~110-year-old machiya, the 2019 reservation-only 15-course vegan sushi kaiseki 'Lapis' served on antique dishes, and the 2021 WAoWA fermented coffee (described as Japan's first trademark-registered fermented coffee), all under a 'soil-to-soil' circular philosophy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a working Italian restaurant centered on wood-fired pizza and pasta, so wheat flour is in heavy use; a gluten-free menu is offered but no dedicated gluten-free prep area or fryer is published, so cross-contamination cannot be ruled out without asking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: da Maeda is not a fully-plant-based kitchen — meat/animal dishes are prepared alongside the vegan menu, so vegan diners should state their requirements clearly when ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant explicitly asks guests to tell staff about vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies before choosing a dish, and offers English staff and menus to help.

positives.text: Vegetables are organic and largely from the chef-owner's own farm in Nantan, Kyoto, and clearly-marked vegan and vegetarian options are part of the standard menu.
For fully-vegan diners, the same owner runs Lapis at this address — a 100%-vegan sushi kaiseki (reservation only) with gluten-free options on most menus, eliminating animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff and English menus are available, and the team is used to guiding diners through vegan, vegetarian, and gluten-free choices — just tell them your needs when you order.

places.name: 黒豆珈琲のbean's goody（ビーンズグッディ） (Bean's Goody)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The black bean coffee itself is naturally gluten-free (just brewed black soybeans), but food items such as the vegetable burger, sandwiches and baked goods are wheat-based. Whether any GF options exist is unconfirmed; a gluten-sensitive diner should ask directly or stick to the black bean coffee/tea and black bean jelly.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore machiya cafe (the sandwich menu includes ham & cheese, tuna and egg) but it offers several plant-based choices. The signature black bean coffee (黒豆珈琲) is made purely from Tanba black soybeans, is naturally caffeine-free and inherently plant-based. Cafe au lait can be made with soy milk instead of dairy. There is a vegetable burger, and the cafe has sold vegan baked goods from "Seiun Oyatsu" (星雲おやつ), which use no animal-derived ingredients. Confirm individual items (e.g. whether burger bun/sauce and any cakes contain egg/dairy) when ordering, as most desserts (ice cream, cakes) appear to be standard dairy-based.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–17:00, closed Wednesdays and Thursdays
places.neighborhood: Kita Ward, Kinugasa area, near Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Bean's Goody is a small cafe set inside a renovated traditional Kyoto townhouse (machiya) in the Kinugasa neighborhood of Kita Ward, near Kinkaku-ji. It opened in August 2015 and is built entirely around one unusual specialty: black bean coffee (黒豆珈琲), a caffe

chef_bio.background: Bean's Goody is a small cafe set inside a renovated traditional Kyoto townhouse (machiya) in the Kinugasa neighborhood of Kita Ward, near Kinkaku-ji. It opened in August 2015 and is built entirely around one unusual specialty: black bean coffee (黒豆珈琲), a caffeine-free brew made from roasted Tanba black soybeans rather than coffee beans.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe centers on making a gentle, caffeine-free black bean coffee accessible while preserving its quality, and on showcasing local Tanba black soybeans. The owner has publicly expressed gratitude to longtime patrons for their steady support ('これも、変わらず支えていただいた皆様のおかげです').
chef_bio.specialty: Caffeine-free black bean coffee made from Kyoto Tanba black soybeans, plus black bean jelly and black bean coffee jelly; light fare includes a vegetable burger and 'dog-style' sandwiches.

anecdotes.text: The black bean coffee is naturally caffeine-free, and after brewing, the spent black beans can be reused to make black bean tea, so guests can taste both from the same beans.
The cafe has hosted art fairs and events where it sold its black bean coffee drip bags alongside vegan baked goods from Seiun Oyatsu (星雲おやつ), which use no animal-derived ingredients.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: beans-goody.com confirms the Kinugasa/Kita-ku machiya cafe opened August 2015 around caffeine-free black bean coffee (黒豆珈琲) from Tanba black soybeans, black bean and coffee jellies, a vegetable burger and 'dog' sandwiches, the reuse of spent beans for black bean tea, and the owner's published thanks to loyal patrons ('これも、変わらず支えていただいた皆様のおかげです').

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore kitchen serving ham & cheese, tuna and egg sandwiches alongside its plant-based items, so cross-contact with animal products is possible for vegan diners; confirm preparation when ordering.

positives.text: The signature black bean coffee is made purely from Tanba black soybeans, is caffeine-free and inherently plant-based and gluten-free, and soy milk is available as a dairy alternative for cafe au lait.
The cafe offers a vegetable burger and has stocked fully vegan baked goods (Seiun Oyatsu) made without animal-derived ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local machiya cafe near Kinkaku-ji where a little Japanese helps; staff are welcoming, but menus and explanations are primarily in Japanese, so learning to ask "Is this vegan?" (これはヴィーガンですか) is useful.

places.name: ザ・リッツ・カールトン京都 (The Ritz-Carlton, Kyoto)
places.gf_detail: La Locanda marks gluten-free items (GF) on its menu, and the hotel sources a 100% gluten-free amazake granola made locally for its breakfast — so GF awareness is real and staff handle allergy disclosures formally. However there is no dedicated GF kitchen or fryer documented; the Japanese outlets (tempura, dishes using soy sauce/dashi) carry wheat/gluten risk. GF diners should declare their needs at booking and confirm each dish; cross-contamination controls are not published.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The Ritz-Carlton Kyoto is a luxury hotel with several dining outlets, and vegan diners are accommodated on request. The clearest plant-based access is at the Italian restaurant La Locanda, whose menu explicitly marks items (VG) for vegan, (GF) for gluten-free and (LF) for lactose-free. Across outlets the hotel asks guests to inform their server of allergies/vegetarian-vegan needs and will customize dishes (reported examples include edamame, vegetable sushi, and miso soup). Dashi-free and wheat-free soy sauce status are not documented and must be confirmed per dish with the kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Dining outlets vary. La Locanda (Italian): Tue-Sat 12:00-15:30 & 18:00-21:30, Sun 12:00-15:30, Mon closed. Mizuki Japanese restaurants (kaiseki/sushi/tempura/teppan): Mon, Thu-Sun 11:30-15:00 & 17:30-21:00, Tue-Wed closed. The Lobby Lounge: daily 11:00-18:00.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, on the Kamogawa river at Nijo-Ohashi bridge; ~3
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] The Ritz-Carlton, Kyoto opened in 2014 on the west bank of the Kamogawa river at the Nijo-Ohashi bridge, designed around a contemporary Japanese aesthetic and traditional garden setting in central Kyoto. Its dining portfolio spans an Italian restaurant (La Loc

chef_bio.background: The Ritz-Carlton, Kyoto opened in 2014 on the west bank of the Kamogawa river at the Nijo-Ohashi bridge, designed around a contemporary Japanese aesthetic and traditional garden setting in central Kyoto. Its dining portfolio spans an Italian restaurant (La Locanda), a cluster of Japanese counters under the Mizuki name (kaiseki, sushi, tempura, teppanyaki), a lobby lounge, a bar, a chef's table, and a Pierre Hermé Paris boutique.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hospitality built around personalized service, including accommodating dietary preferences such as vegan, vegetarian and gluten-free when guests notify staff.
chef_bio.specialty: Refined Italian cuisine using local Kyoto produce at La Locanda, and multi-discipline Japanese fine dining at Mizuki; afternoon tea in the riverside Lobby Lounge.

anecdotes.text: La Locanda's menu explicitly flags dishes as (VG) vegan, (GF) gluten-free and (LF) lactose-free, making plant-based and allergen choices identifiable rather than guesswork (guests are still asked to notify staff of any allergies).
The hotel sources a 100% gluten-free amazake granola made locally in Kyoto for its breakfast service, a small sign of attentiveness to gluten-free guests.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official La Locanda menu page confirms the (VG) vegan / (GF) gluten-free / (LF) lactose-free notation (and an allergy-notification request); the Ritz-Carlton dining materials and independent reviews confirm the 2014 opening on the Kamogawa at Nijo-Ohashi, the La Locanda / Mizuki / Pierre Hermé line-up, and the 100%-gluten-free Kyoto-made amazake granola at breakfast.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests are asked to inform their server of any food allergies or vegetarian/vegan needs so the kitchen can accommodate them; allergy disclosure before ordering is part of the standard service.

positives.text: La Locanda's menu clearly labels vegan (VG), gluten-free (GF) and lactose-free (LF) dishes, so diners can identify safe options instead of guessing.
A locally made, 100% gluten-free amazake granola is offered at breakfast, indicating genuine gluten-free sourcing awareness.
English-speaking, professionally trained luxury-hotel staff make it straightforward to communicate and customize dietary requests.

cultural_comfort.note: As an international luxury hotel, English-speaking staff are standard and dietary requests are handled professionally — a comfortable, low-stress option for a foreign student (though pricing is high-end).

places.name: asian chample foods goya（アジアンチャンプルフーズ ゴヤ） (Asian Chample Foods Goya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. The cuisine relies on soy sauce, miso, and fried items (tempura, croquettes), and there is no published statement on whether the soy sauce is wheat-free or whether a dedicated fryer is used. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should contact the restaurant directly and confirm ingredients in person. (Menu review: 1 gluten-free meal option on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: An omnivore Okinawan/pan-Asian restaurant that clearly marks vegan and vegetarian dishes on its menu. Signature plant-based items include vegan taco rice (soy-meat with avocado instead of cheese), vegan banana tarte, spicy Vege Goreng, Nasi Campur / mixed seasonal vegetable plates with Okinawan seaweed, summer rolls, vegetable croquettes, and seaweed tempura. Staff proactively confirm whether cheese, egg or dashi are acceptable, so a strictly vegan meal is achievable but you should specify your needs when ordering since meat and animal-based Okinawan dishes are also served.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00, 17:30-24:00 (last orders vary; HappyCow lists evening close ~23:00). Closed Wednesdays. Check Facebook for irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo-ku, Jodoji area near Kyoto University and Ginkaku-ji (
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Owner Nakagawa opened the restaurant around 2014 out of a love for Okinawan and pan-Asian cooking, settling in the Jodoji neighborhood near Kyoto University. The name plays on goya (bitter melon) and Goya/Koza, the Okinawan city seen as the birthplace of 'cham

chef_bio.background: Owner Nakagawa opened the restaurant around 2014 out of a love for Okinawan and pan-Asian cooking, settling in the Jodoji neighborhood near Kyoto University. The name plays on goya (bitter melon) and Goya/Koza, the Okinawan city seen as the birthplace of 'chample' (mix-it-all-together) culture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Nakagawa aims for a '食籍なき食卓' - a 'food-passport-free table' where vegans, vegetarians and meat-eaters can all sit and eat together. He didn't set out to build a vegan menu; it grew naturally as he responded to requests from the foreign residents and international students in the neighborhood, reflecting Okinawa's blended Chinese-Japanese-American 'chample' spirit.
chef_bio.specialty: Okinawan 'chample' dishes given a vegan-friendly twist - vegan taco rice with soy meat and avocado in place of cheese, Nasi Campur seasonal vegetable plates with Okinawan seaweed and Kume-island miso soup, goya champuru, island-tofu dishes, and vegan banana tarte.

anecdotes.text: The vegan menu wasn't planned - Nakagawa kept saying yes to requests from international students and foreign residents near Kyoto University until plant-based dishes naturally became a large part of the offering.
He notes that Okinawan ingredients have such strong flavor that even non-vegetarians enjoy the veg dishes ('沖縄食材は味がしっかりしているので、ベジじゃない方にも喜ばれてます').
Reviewers describe a cozy, authentic wooden machiya setting with very friendly staff; one diner was sent off with complimentary Okinawan chopsticks.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Vegewel's interview confirms owner Nakagawa opened around 2014 in Jodoji near Kyoto University, the goya/Goya-Koza chample naming, the '食籍なき食卓' (food-passport-free table) ethos, the organic growth of the vegan menu from international residents' requests, signature vegan taco rice (soy meat + avocado, no cheese), Nasi Campur, Kume-island miso soup and island tofu, and the quote that strongly-flavored Okinawan ingredients please non-vegetarians too.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information is published on whether the soy sauce used is wheat-free; standard Japanese shoyu typically contains wheat. Confirm directly if avoiding gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat-based Okinawan dishes are prepared in the same kitchen, so a fully vegan order should be requested explicitly.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff routinely ask diners whether cheese, egg or dashi are acceptable, showing solid awareness of vegan and vegetarian dietary needs when taking orders.
English-speaking staff and an English menu make it easier to communicate dietary restrictions.

positives.text: Vegan dishes are clearly marked on the menu, so plant-based diners can choose confidently.
Staff proactively confirm dashi/egg/cheese, which is a strong allergy-awareness signal for an omnivore restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff and an English menu are available, and the place is used to international students and travelers, so a foreign student can order vegan items comfortably in English.

places.name: 野菜ダイニング 薬師 (Yasai Dining Kusushi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The menu includes clearly wheat-containing items (pizza), and as a Japanese izakaya it likely uses standard wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings. There is no indication of gluten-free accommodation; a celiac or gluten-sensitive diner would need to inquire in advance and should treat cross-contamination as unmanaged unless the kitchen confirms otherwise. (Menu review: 2 gluten-free meal options on the documented menu — but the kitchen is NOT certified GF; confirm cross-contamination, and for grilled/teppan items ask for a freshly-cleaned grill; if you can't be sure it's clean, don't risk it.)
places.vegan_detail: This is a vegetable-centric omnivore izakaya ("yasai dining"), not a vegan restaurant. Its concept is showcasing rare Kyoto vegetables and produce from named farmers nationwide, but the regular menu includes non-vegan items (e.g. cheese-tomato bakes, pizza topped with cheese) and it operates as an izakaya with wine. The vegan map listing notes vegan service is available "by advance reservation" only, so vegans should call ahead (075-212-4313) to arrange a plant-only course. No published confirmation of whether dashi, soy sauce, or other animal-derived seasonings are avoided in vegan requests, so confirm directly when booking. (Menu review: 1 vegan meal.)
places.hours_raw: 17:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); closed Wednesdays. (Some listings show last order 23:30 / close 24:00.)
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, just south of Sanjo on Kawaramachi-dori (Daikoku
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] A vegetable-focused dining bar ("yasai dining") in central Kyoto, just off Kawaramachi-dori south of Sanjo. The restaurant builds its menu around seasonal Kyoto heirloom vegetables - Shogoin daikon and turnip, Horikawa burdock, Kujo green onions, Kyoto carrots

chef_bio.background: A vegetable-focused dining bar ("yasai dining") in central Kyoto, just off Kawaramachi-dori south of Sanjo. The restaurant builds its menu around seasonal Kyoto heirloom vegetables - Shogoin daikon and turnip, Horikawa burdock, Kujo green onions, Kyoto carrots - alongside rare produce sourced from farmers across Japan, paired with wine.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 薬師 (Kusushi), an old Japanese word for a physician/healer, captures the owner's ethos that vegetables are medicine: the goal is to help guests eat many kinds of vegetables and become healthier, using only safe, traceable produce from farmers whose faces they know.
chef_bio.specialty: Dishes that put unusual and seasonal vegetables front and center: a 20-vegetable salad, Kujo-negi and burdock kinpira pizza, and Inca-potato tomato-cheese bakes, plus a rotating lineup of rare vegetables from around the country.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named after 薬師 (kusushi), an archaic word for a doctor, reflecting the belief that eating a wide variety of vegetables makes you healthier.
Vegetables are chosen for safety and trust - sourced from across Japan where 'the farmer's face is visible,' with a focus on Kyoto heirloom varieties.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own site (kdh9700.gorp.jp) confirms the 薬師/kusushi name (archaic word for a doctor) and 'vegetables are medicine' ethos, sourcing safe produce from farmers 'whose faces are visible' with a focus on Kyoto heirloom vegetables (Shogoin daikon, Horikawa burdock, Kujo negi), plus a 20-vegetable salad, Kujo-negi/burdock kinpira pizza and Inca-potato tomato-cheese bake, on the 3rd floor near Kawaramachi south of Sanjo.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an omnivore izakaya, not a vegan kitchen: the regular menu includes dairy (cheese bakes, cheese-topped pizza) and the kitchen handles meat and fish, so a vegan order shares equipment and surfaces with animal products. Vegan dishes are prepared only on advance request, so cross-contact handling depends entirely on arranging this ahead.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The vegan map listing states vegan service is available 'by advance reservation,' implying the kitchen will tailor a plant-based course if you call ahead (075-212-4313) - but no formal allergen or gluten protocol is published.

positives.text: The whole concept is vegetable-forward, so plant-based diners have a genuinely large palette of seasonal Kyoto and nationwide vegetables to draw from when a vegan course is arranged in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: A local Kyoto izakaya with primarily Japanese-language service and menu; a foreign diner can manage with a few Japanese phrases, and a vegan should phone ahead (basic Japanese helpful) to arrange a plant-based course.

places.name: モンパン食堂 (Monpan Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu. The signature dish, monpan, is a wheat flatbread, and the kitchen also serves yakisoba, pasta, noodles and Korean pancakes, so wheat is widely used. No information was found on whether the soy sauce is wheat-free or whether sauces are gluten-free. One reviewer reported the owner accommodated their allergies when handed an explanation written in Japanese, so individual accommodation may be possible, but a GF diner must communicate needs clearly (ideally in writing/Japanese) and verify each item.
places.vegan_detail: A homey omnivore eatery with extensive plant-based offerings. There are separate vegetarian and non-vegetarian menus, and vegan choices are clearly marked; staff will also veganize many dishes on request. Vegan-friendly items include vegan yakisoba, veggie/tofu monpan, buddha bowls, stir-fries, noodles, curry with rice, and jijimi (Korean pancake). VegeMap and HappyCow both tag it as "vegan options" rather than fully vegan, and meat is served in the same kitchen, so confirm each dish when ordering. No information confirming whether dashi or other animal stock is avoided in default broths — best to ask.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree and the restaurant notes "open until sold out." Tabelog lists: Tue 18:00-22:00; Thu-Sun 10:00-22:00; closed Mon & Wed. Other listings cite roughly Thu-Sun 11:30-20:00. Hours are unreliable — confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward, near Kyoto University Hospital; about a 20-25 mi
places.notes: [VegeMap-sourced · vegan options] Monpan Shokudo is a small, home-style eatery in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, run by a family team (often described as a warm, grandmotherly host) out of what feels like the owners' own house. It opened in August 2016.

chef_bio.origin: Japan / Kyoto, with a globe-spanning, Mongolian-inspired menu
chef_bio.background: Monpan Shokudo is a small, home-style eatery in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, run by a family team (often described as a warm, grandmotherly host) out of what feels like the owners' own house. It opened in August 2016.
chef_bio.philosophy: An inclusive table: the menu deliberately spans meat, vegetarian and vegan so that vegans, vegetarians and meat-eaters can all eat together, and the kitchen will adapt many dishes to be vegan on request.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is the 'monpan' (Mongolian bread) — a flatbread filled with vegetables and tofu, also offered pizza-style, with cheese, or as a sweet version. Other favorites include vegan yakisoba, buddha bowls, stir-fries, curry with rice, and jijimi (Korean savory pancake).

anecdotes.text: The dining room mixes a small, cute outdoor space with counter, table and tatami floor seating; in winter the table becomes a heated kotatsu with an attached blanket.

One diner noted the owner understood their vegan order and accommodated allergies when given an explanation written in Japanese, and described the warmth as like eating at 'a beloved grandma's for dinner.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: English review sources (summernightdream, HappyCow) confirm the August 2016 opening in Sakyo Ward, the home-style family/elderly-couple operation, the signature 'monpan' (Mongolian bread) with vegetable/tofu, cheese, pizza-style and sweet versions, separate meat/vegetarian/vegan menus adaptable on request, jijimi/yakisoba/buddha bowls/curry, the winter kotatsu, and allergy accommodation when given a Japanese-language note.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature monpan is a wheat flatbread and the kitchen also makes yakisoba, noodles, pasta and Korean pancakes, so wheat is handled throughout; no gluten-free preparation protocols are documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information found on whether the soy sauce or sauces used are wheat-free; assume standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce unless confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat dishes are served from the same home kitchen as the vegan options, so some cross-contact is possible; ask for dishes to be made vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A reviewer reported the owner understood a vegan order and took allergies into account when handed an explanation written in Japanese — individual accommodation appears possible if you communicate clearly.

positives.text: Vegan items are clearly marked on the menu and staff will veganize many dishes on request, so plant-based diners have genuine, varied choices.
Small, attentive owner-run kitchen where food is prepared to order, which makes custom dietary requests easier to handle.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy home-style spot run by a small family team; some English is reported and an English menu has been mentioned, but bringing your dietary needs written in Japanese (as one allergic diner did) will make ordering smoother.

places.name: 麺屋 極鶏 一乗寺本店 (Menya Gokkei Ichijoji Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The ramen is built on wheat noodles, and the ultra-thick chicken broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce-based tare (Japanese shoyu almost always contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle or gluten-free preparation is offered, and the kitchen is a single-concept ramen shop with no dedicated allergen handling. Diners requiring strict gluten avoidance cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire menu is anchored on an ultra-concentrated chicken (paitan) broth made by simmering chicken carcasses to a near-pottage consistency, plus chicken-based toppings (鶏だく = extra chicken). There is no plant-based broth or vegan bowl. Vegetarians and vegans cannot be accommodated.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–22:00 (Tue–Sun); closed Mon plus irregular days off. Ends when the soup runs out, so it may close early even within stated hours.
places.neighborhood: Ichijōji (~5 min walk from Eizan Railway Ichijōji Stn / Ramen Street), Sakyō-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2011 on Ichijoji's famous "Ramen Street" (ラーメン街道) in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, Menya Gokkei staked out its own corner of one of Japan's most competitive ramen districts. The name 極鶏 ("Gokkei") fuses 極 (extreme/perfected) with 鶏 (chicken), and the shop has stayed laser-focused

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2011 on Ichijoji's famous "Ramen Street" (ラーメン街道) in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, Menya Gokkei staked out its own corner of one of Japan's most competitive ramen districts. The name 極鶏 ("Gokkei") fuses 極 (extreme/perfected) with 鶏 (chicken), and the shop has stayed laser-focused on a single idea ever since.
chef_bio.philosophy: A one-bowl, one-idea shop: rather than chase variety, Gokkei pours everything into perfecting a single, unmistakable chicken broth — its own self-described 'one-of-a-kind soup to savor.'
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 極鶏 鶏だく: an ultra-concentrated chicken paitan so thick it is often described as 'doro-kei' (muddy) and famously dense enough that a spoon can stand upright in it. Chicken carcasses are simmered to an extreme, near-pottage consistency to create what the shop calls a 'soup you eat rather than drink.' Variations build on the same base: 赤だく (fiery red chili), 黒だく (fragrant black mayu garlic oil), and 魚だく (a fish-infused twist).

anecdotes.text: The broth is so heavy it is regularly described as standing a spoon upright — closer to a chicken pottage than a conventional ramen soup.
Set on Ichijoji's Ramen Street, the shop draws lines from before opening, and regulars advise arriving early; the day can end abruptly once the broth runs out.
The shop frames its broth as a '超濃厚肉濁鶏白湯' (ultra-rich, meat-clouded chicken paitan) and sells frozen versions to ship its signature soup beyond the counter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's official amebaownd site confirms the '超濃厚肉濁鶏白湯', the 鶏だく 'spoon stands upright' thickness, the 'one-of-a-kind soup to eat', the 赤だく/黒だく/魚だく variations and frozen mail-order; the arafuka blog confirms the 2011 opening on Ichijoji's Ramen Street, the chicken-pottage texture, lines before opening and daily sell-out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-concept ramen shop: wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken broth are central to every bowl, with no gluten-free option or separate preparation. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce-based tare; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so the soup itself is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on chicken broth and chicken toppings; there is no plant-based broth, so vegan diners cannot be served.

cultural_comfort.note: A fast-moving, ticket-machine ramen shop with frequent queues; basic Japanese and reading the ticket machine help, but pointing and a friendly greeting will get you through.

noodles.notes: Firm, low-hydration medium-gauge straight noodles chosen to stand up to the spoon-standing, potage-thick broth; served with a deliberate chew so they don't drown in the soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏だく (Tori-daku) — the original ultra-concentrated chicken paitan, a broth so thick a spoon stands upright in it.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, sliced thin and laid over the gravy-like broth — a soft, simmered slice rather than aburi/char-grilled.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-based tare make this celiac-unsafe; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire concept is a whole-chicken broth boiled to a gravy, finished with chicken fat and topped with pork chashu — no plant-based bowl exists.

ramen.pantheon: In the ramen-battleground of Ichijoji's Ramen Highway, Gokkei pioneered the 'doro-kei' (mud-style) chicken paitan: whole chickens boiled down until the soup is less a liquid than an edible potage that holds a spoon upright. It is the soul of Kyoto's modern tori-paitan movement — extreme body and chicken sweetness with almost no fishiness, offered white (tori-daku), red with chili (aka-daku), or black with aromatic oil (kuro-daku).

places.name: セアブラノ神 壬生本店 (Seaburano Kami Mibu Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a back-fat niboshi ramen and mazesoba specialist: bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with pork/fish-based broth and a wheat-containing shoyu tare, plus tsukemen and mazesoba (also wheat noodles). No gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation is indicated anywhere. Celiac diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core of every signature bowl is animal-based: pork back fat (背脂), niboshi (dried sardine) and pork-bone broth, low-temperature pork chashu and braised pork toppings. No vegan or vegetable broth option is indicated.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:30 (last order 15:00) / 18:00–22:30 (last order 22:00); closed Jan 1 (New Year's Day)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] セアブラノ神 壬生本店 is a small, much-loved Kyoto ramen counter in the Shijo-Omiya / Mibu neighborhood, tucked into the ground floor of a building on Mibu-Aioi-cho a short walk from Hankyu Omiya Station. It built its reputation on a niche idea: a niboshi (dried-sardine) and pork-bone brot

chef_bio.background: セアブラノ神 壬生本店 is a small, much-loved Kyoto ramen counter in the Shijo-Omiya / Mibu neighborhood, tucked into the ground floor of a building on Mibu-Aioi-cho a short walk from Hankyu Omiya Station. It built its reputation on a niche idea: a niboshi (dried-sardine) and pork-bone broth crowned with a generous lid of pork back fat (背脂) — a style that nods to Niigata's Tsubame-Sanjo ramen — and on bold Taiwan-style mazesoba.
