chef_bio.philosophy: Balance over heaviness — the back fat is used for richness and sweetness, not to overwhelm, so the niboshi and the broth still come through clean. Hearty, generous bowls (rare and braised pork, onion, nori, free rice refills) at a neighborhood price.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is the back-fat niboshi soba (背脂煮干そば; the 'special' version runs around ￥1,150): a broth that looks rich from the cap of back fat but drinks surprisingly clean and clear, with a pronounced sardine aroma, served over springy medium-to-thick noodles and finished with low-temperature-cooked pork. The mazesoba (Taiwan-style mixed noodles) is an equally celebrated draw, built on a long-simmered pork-bone base and meant to be stirred from the bottom before eating.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the back-fat cap as deceptively light: despite the visible fat the soup drinks clean with a clear sardine fragrance, over chewy thick or medium-thick noodles likened to good Okinawan soba.
Local food writers single out the mazesoba as a standout — 'mix it from the bottom and it's delicious; among the mazesoba I've been into lately, this is top-tier.'
The name セアブラノ神 ('god of back fat') points straight at the kitchen's calling card: the generous use of pork 背脂 (back fat) in its bowls.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: takemachelin (a Tabelog Top-100 review) confirms the Mibu location near Hankyu Omiya, the Tsubame-Sanjo-style niboshi + pork-back-fat 'special' soba at ￥1,150 that drinks surprisingly clean with a clear sardine aroma, the choice of medium/thick springy noodles likened to Okinawan soba, and low-temperature pork; kyotogourmet.jp praises the Taiwan-style mazesoba and explains the 'god of back fat' name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (ramen, tsukemen and mazesoba) shared in the same kitchen and water; high cross-contact risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu/back-fat-shoyu-style tare is used; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are pork-bone and niboshi (fish) based with pork back fat and pork toppings throughout; no vegan or vegetable option indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese and there is no reservation system; come ready to queue and order simply, ideally with a few words of Japanese or by pointing.

noodles.notes: Custom-made for the shop by Kyoto's Menya Teigaku. Famously thick — roughly udon-width — with a springy, gummy-chewy bite that grabs the back-fat and niboshi soup. A medium-thick option is also offered, the signature being the thick noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei se-abura niboshi soba (特製背脂煮干しそば) — the deluxe back-fat dried-sardine soba topped with the full set of chashu, ajitama and menma
ramen.chashu: Two kinds in the deluxe bowl: thin rare-style pork and a braised pork whose fat takes on a crisp, almost crackling edge — four slices total, half of each.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; pork back-fat and niboshi run through the whole bowl. No gluten-free option documented.

vegan.note: Built on a chicken-and-pork-bone clear soup blended with a dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi, finished with pork back-fat and chashu. No plant-based bowl documented.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl carries the Niigata Tsubame-Sanjo lineage — a clear chicken-and-pork soup married to a punchy cold-brewed sardine dashi, then blanketed in glistening clouds of melted pork back-fat — re-tuned to Kyoto tastes. You taste the soul in the contrast: bracing niboshi umami and round shoyu cut through fat so rich the udon-thick noodles need every bit of their springy chew to stand up to it.

places.name: 新福菜館 本店 (Shinpuku Saikan Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto chuka soba shop built around wheat noodles in a dark soy-sauce-based broth (soy sauce contains wheat), with a pork-and-chicken-bone foundation. There is no gluten-free noodle or menu option and no indication of any allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on a pork-and-chicken-bone broth with chashu (roast pork) toppings; the signature fried rice is also typically cooked with pork fat and egg. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-20:00 (last order 19:30). Closed Wednesdays; additional 2-3 irregular closure days each month.
places.neighborhood: Kyoto Station (Shiokōji-dōri / Takabashi; near the Hachijō exit of Kyoto Station), Shimogyō-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shinpuku Saikan began life in 1938 as a humble street stall (yatai) near Kyoto Station and has grown into one of the founding names of Kansai ramen. The honten (main shop) still stands on Higashi-Shiokoji Street by the Takabashi crossing, a short walk from Kyoto Station, and rema

chef_bio.background: Shinpuku Saikan began life in 1938 as a humble street stall (yatai) near Kyoto Station and has grown into one of the founding names of Kansai ramen. The honten (main shop) still stands on Higashi-Shiokoji Street by the Takabashi crossing, a short walk from Kyoto Station, and remains a cash-only, family-run institution that locals and travelers line up for.
chef_bio.philosophy: Captured in the shop's own line — roughly, 'a bowl that makes you want to eat it again and again' — the focus is on that one addictive, instantly recognizable black broth rather than menu breadth.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is chuka soba in a startlingly jet-black, deeply colored soy-sauce broth built on a chicken- and pork-bone base — the original 'Kyoto Black.' Despite the intense dark appearance, regulars describe the soup as surprisingly mild, clean, and easy to drink, which is why it doubles as a beloved asa-ra (morning ramen) from the 9:00 opening. The pork-fat fried rice (yakimeshi), often ordered alongside a small bowl, is nearly as famous as the noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so identified with Kyoto Station that it advertises itself as the 'Kyoto Takabashi chuka soba specialist,' referencing the nearby Takabashi crossing where the main shop sits.
Opening at 9:00 a.m., it is one of the city's classic spots for asa-ra (morning ramen) — the broth is gentle enough to enjoy first thing in the day, and it stays busy from open to close.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: shinpukusaikan.net confirms the 1938 founding as a yatai near Kyoto Station, the Takabashi honten, cash-only operation, the 9:00 opening and the deeply-colored soy 'Kyoto Black' chuka soba that surprises first-timers yet keeps them coming back; kyotopi confirms its status as a classic morning-ramen (asa-ra) spot and the celebrated pork-fat yakimeshi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish is wheat ramen noodles, so wheat is present and boiled in shared water; no gluten-free preparation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature black broth is built on a soy-sauce tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, making the broth itself unsafe for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork- and chicken-bone base and chashu (roast pork) is a standard topping; nothing on the menu is vegan or vegetarian.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, no-frills old-school ramen counter where ordering in simple Japanese (or by pointing at the short menu) and paying cash makes the visit smoothest.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick noodles with a smooth, springy (tsuru-mochi) bite that soaks up and carries the dark, savory soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (their one signature bowl); order it Tokudai with extra thin-sliced chashu and a side of their famous black yakimeshi.
ramen.chashu: Generous, thinly sliced braised pork chashu, firmly seasoned and tender with a well-judged balance of lean and fat — soft enough to melt and good enough to eat on its own with a drink.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui Chinese-style noodles plus a wheat-based dark soy sauce tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented, so this bowl is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soup is built on chicken bones and pork and finished with a meat-and-soy tare, with pork chashu on top; no plant-based broth bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: Open since 1938 near Kyoto Station, this is widely held as the forebear of Kyoto's jet-black shoyu ramen: a soup so dark it looks fierce yet tastes mellow and clean, where chicken-and-pork stock and a Kyoto-brewed dark soy tare melt into one deep, gently sweet umami. You taste old-Kyoto town-Chinese lineage in every sip — bold color, surprisingly light body, almost no surface oil.

places.name: 吟醸らーめん 久保田 (Ginjo Ramen Kubota)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The entire menu is built on thick wheat noodles (the signature tsukemen noodles are notably thick and udon-like), and the bowls use miso/shoyu tare almost certainly made with wheat-based soy sauce, served with pork- and seafood-based broths. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten avoidance. No allergen menu or dedicated gluten-free handling is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core offerings — miso tsukemen, meat-miso ramen, and chicken-shoyu ramen — all rely on animal products (ground pork, pork/seafood broth, chicken). There is no vegetable or plant-based ramen on the menu, and side rice dishes (chirimen-sansho rice, takana rice) also include animal/fish ingredients.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:00–15:00, 18:00–22:30 / Tue–Sun 11:00–15:00, 18:00–22:00 (last order lunch 14:30, dinner 21:30). Noodles take about 15 min to boil. Open daily from September; closed only year-end/New Year.
places.neighborhood: Gojō, Shimogyō-ku (Kyoto Municipal Subway Gojō Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small ramen-ya near Gojo in Kyoto's Shimogyo ward, a stretch close to Kyoto Station that has surprisingly few standout eateries, which helped Kubota stand out and draw a steady mix of locals and international visitors. The dining room is intimate — roughly ten counter seats div

chef_bio.background: A small ramen-ya near Gojo in Kyoto's Shimogyo ward, a stretch close to Kyoto Station that has surprisingly few standout eateries, which helped Kubota stand out and draw a steady mix of locals and international visitors. The dining room is intimate — roughly ten counter seats divided by partitions, a layout regulars liken to a see-through version of the famous solo-booth chains.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking leans into a made-to-order ideal — the noodles are boiled fresh to order — prioritizing texture and the quality of the noodle itself over speed or piling on toppings.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the ginjo miso tsukemen: thick, glossy, springy noodles — often compared to Sanuki udon for their substance and chew — served with a rich, grainy-textured miso dipping broth layered with seafood umami and ground pork, finished with a hint of chili-oil spice that some have likened to Kansai-style spice curry. A ginjo shoyu (chicken-soy) ramen rounds out the lineup, with a marinated egg and rice-bowl sides as add-ons.

anecdotes.text: Selected for Tabelog's Ramen WEST 'Tabelog 100' (2025) and regarded as one of the Kyoto region's standout ramen destinations.

Diners describe the partitioned ten-seat counter as a 'see-through Ichiran,' and the freshly boiled noodles are praised as delicious enough to enjoy on their own, though the made-to-order boil means a wait.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: takemachelin confirms the ~ten partitioned counter seats likened to a 'see-through Ichiran' near Gojo, the made-to-order noodle boil (hence a wait), the Sanuki-udon-like thick springy noodles, and the grainy miso tsukemen with seafood/ground-pork depth and a Kansai-curry-like spice; Tabelog confirms the shop's Ramen WEST 'Tabelog 100' (2025) selection and the ginjo-shoyu ramen. (The earlier vacan article is no longer available.)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu ramen and miso tare are made in the standard Japanese ramen style, which relies on wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths and toppings center on pork, chicken and seafood; there is no separate vegetable broth or plant-based preparation line.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, popular counter shop with individual partitioned seats that already draws many overseas visitors; a few words of Japanese and patience for the line and the long noodle-cook time go a long way.

noodles.notes: Substantial, springy-chewy wheat noodles often compared to Sanuki udon for their thickness and bite; curly/wavy strands that cling to the thick dipping sauce on the tsukemen. Served default at a soft al dente.

ramen.signature_bowl: Miso tsukemen — the house's famous order: tightly-kneaded thick noodles with a rich, grainy miso-forward dipping sauce built on the seafood-and-poultry broth, studded with finely minced meat and sakura shrimp; add an ajitama (soft-boiled egg) for a little more. The clear "ginjō shoyu rāmen" is the elegant alternative.
ramen.chashu: A single thin slice of braised pork — tender with a gentle bite, fresh porcine flavor deepened by a savory braise; lean rather than fatty belly.
notable_toppings: minced meat and sakura shrimp (in the miso tsukemen sauce)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus blended-soy-sauce and miso tare make every bowl wheat-laden; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on whole chicken and chicken bones with bonito, dried squid and dried shrimp, and bowls carry pork chashu and minced meat/sakura shrimp; no plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is a refined Kyoto take where the soul lives in a long-simmered whole-chicken-and-bonito-kombu broth pulled clear and brown, finished with blended soy — you taste the dashi lineage of Kansai in the soup rather than the heavy pork-back-fat shoyu Kyoto is usually known for. Its renown, though, rests on a rarer-in-Kyoto miso tsukemen whose grainy, faintly spicy dipping sauce wraps thick udon-like noodles.

places.name: 中華そば 高安 (Chuka Soba Takayasu)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free accommodation. The signature bowl is a creamy chicken-and-pork-bone white broth served with custom wheat-kansui noodles, and the menu includes wheat-battered, curry-dusted karaage. There is no indication of gluten-free options, allergen handling, or a dedicated fryer; the karaage is deep-fried in shared oil. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The core product is a long-simmered pork-bone and chicken-bone broth, with pork chashu, beef-sinew (suji) ramen, and fried chicken as headline items. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or advertised. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–02:00 (continuous service, lunch through late night); irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Ichijōji (~4 min walk from Eizan Railway Ichijōji Stn / Ramen Street), Sakyō-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Takayasu is a fixture of Ichijoji's famed "Ramen Street" in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, a neighborhood packed with competing ramen shops and a steady stream of students. It is widely credited as one of the shops that introduced the rich, white-cloudy pork-and-chicken-bone style to Kyoto,

chef_bio.background: Takayasu is a fixture of Ichijoji's famed "Ramen Street" in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, a neighborhood packed with competing ramen shops and a steady stream of students. It is widely credited as one of the shops that introduced the rich, white-cloudy pork-and-chicken-bone style to Kyoto, and it has remained a perennial line-out-the-door destination.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames its goal as serving not just good ramen but a whole experience -- atmosphere and space included -- aiming to bring a sense of excitement and an enriched table to every customer.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature Chuka Soba is built on a milky, creamy white broth simmered for around 24 hours from pork bones and chicken bones, paired with custom noodles whose kansui balance is tuned to the soup. The other crowd-pleaser is the oversized, curry-dusted karaage (fried chicken), crisp outside with juices that burst on the first bite; a beef-sinew (suji) ramen runs as a limited item.

anecdotes.text: The dining room has an unusually varied, almost cafe-like layout -- window seats, wall seating, a curved counter, and tables under bright lighting -- which, together with generous portions and low prices, makes it a favorite in this student town.
Unusually for the area, the shop offers two parking spaces, a notable convenience since most Ramen Street shops have none, and it runs continuous service from late morning into the early hours.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: takayasuramen.com confirms the white-cloudy pork-and-chicken-bone broth simmered about 24 hours, the limited beef-sinew (suji) ramen, the curry-dusted karaage and the 'more than food, a whole experience' ethos; kyotopi confirms the Ichijoji Ramen Street location, the varied cafe-like seating and the two parking spaces.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles boiled in shared water; signature broth served over wheat-kansui noodles. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured items are pork/chicken-bone broth based with pork, beef-sinew, and fried-chicken toppings; no vegan/vegetarian preparation noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, cash-only neighborhood ramen shop on Ichijoji's "Ramen Street" geared toward locals and students; expect a queue and minimal English, so basic Japanese and pointing at the menu help.

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered straight thin noodles with a kansui (alkaline-water) ratio tuned to grip and carry the milky broth; clean slurp.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka Soba — the milky pork-and-chicken white broth with straight thin noodles, sliced pork chashu, Kujo negi and menma. The limited Suji Ramen (braised tendon) is the house's top recommendation when available.
ramen.chashu: Thinly sliced braised pork that stays moist and gives up its umami into the soup; an extra-chashu bowl (Chashu-men) is offered.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-leaning tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core broth is pork-bone and chicken simmered together, finished with animal tare and pork chashu; no plant-based broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A pillar of Kyoto's Ichijoji ramen row, this bowl pours a 24-hour pork-and-chicken broth that arrives milky white yet stays clean and unctuous rather than heavy — the smooth, naturally sweet, gameless paitan that defines the city's tori-tonkotsu lineage. Straight thin noodles and a fistful of crisp Kujo negi let that creamy soup, not seasoning fireworks, carry the soul of the bowl.

places.name: ラーメン荘 夢を語れ (Ramen-sou Yume wo Katare)
places.gf_detail: This is a Jiro-style shop built on ultra-thick hand-kneaded wheat flour noodles (極太平打ち手もみ麺), a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a pork-bone broth — there is no gluten-free option and the noodles are the entire point of the bowl. No allergy accommodation is advertised. Not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten or wheat.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is centered on a non-emulsified pork-bone (豚骨) shoyu broth with pork chashu and back fat (背脂), plus a wheat-based tare. There are no vegan or vegetarian options, and the only free toppings are garlic and more fat (アブラ). Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 17:00–23:00 / Sat 12:00–15:00, 17:00–23:00; closed Sun and Mon (hours differ slightly by source; some list a 22:45 close)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in October 2006 in the student town of Ichijoji, this is widely regarded as the pioneer of Kansai-style Jiro ramen. Founder Nishioka Tsuyoshi apprenticed in the Ramen Marji lineage (itself descended from the legendary Ramen Jiro in Tokyo) before opening his own counter he

chef_bio.background: Founded in October 2006 in the student town of Ichijoji, this is widely regarded as the pioneer of Kansai-style Jiro ramen. Founder Nishioka Tsuyoshi apprenticed in the Ramen Marji lineage (itself descended from the legendary Ramen Jiro in Tokyo), serving as branch manager at Marji's Nishi-Arai shop before opening his own counter here. In 2012 he carried the style abroad, opening 'Yume Wo Katare' in Boston — the first Jiro-style shop to expand overseas.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name means 'tell your dream.' True to it, the shop gives diners who wish to a space, after finishing their bowl, to speak their dreams aloud — turning a bowl of ramen into a small ritual of encouragement.
chef_bio.specialty: A single, towering bowl: ultra-thick hand-kneaded flat wheat noodles in a non-emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth with a concentrated, subtly sweet depth, crowned with thick pork chashu, a mountain of bean sprouts and cabbage, and back fat. Free toppings of garlic and extra fat are called out at the counter.

anecdotes.text: After eating, customers who wish are invited to stand and 'tell their dream' (夢を語る) — the practice that gives the shop its name.
Founder Nishioka took the Ichijoji style to Boston in October 2012, making it the first Jiro-style ramen shop to open overseas.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ja.Wikipedia and osumituki confirm: founder 西岡津世志 (b. 1979, Omihachiman, Shiga) trained in the Ramen Jiro/Marji lineage and became manager of Marji Nishi-Arai; opened this shop in Ichijoji on 20 Oct 2006; opened the Boston branch in Oct 2012 as the first overseas Jiro-style shop; the group has since grown to ~9 shops across Kyoto/Osaka/Hyogo. Extra-thick hand-kneaded flat noodles, non-emulsified tonkotsu-shoyu, L-counter ~10 seats, ticket machine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bowl is built on ultra-thick hand-kneaded wheat noodles, a wheat-based shoyu tare, and pork-bone broth; gluten is unavoidable and there is no GF preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) seasoned pork-bone base; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl contains pork chashu, pork-bone broth, and back fat (背脂); no vegan or vegetarian preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, fast-paced Jiro-style counter where you order at the ticket machine and call your toppings in Japanese — knowing a few words (ニンニク/ヤサイ/アブラ/カラメ) and the unwritten etiquette of finishing your bowl makes the visit far smoother.

noodles.notes: Extra-thick flat hand-massaged (temomi) self-made noodles with good wheat flavor; default 300g, with 200g small and 400g large options. Holds firm even under the mountain of toppings.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (default 300g) with the full call: yasai (vegetables), ninniku (garlic), abura (back fat), karame (extra tare). A sweet, fat-slicked non-emulsified pork-and-soy bowl buried under a mountain of bean sprouts and cabbage. A shiru-nashi (soupless) version is also offered.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork dominated by tender red lean meat (with a slice of fat), mild in smell, cut slightly smaller than other Jiro shops but generous and rough-hewn rather than refined.

gf.note: Jiro-style wheat noodles with kansui plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare and pork-bone broth. No gluten-free option exists; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is pork bone with soy tare, topped with pork back fat and red-lean chashu. No plant-based broth or bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: The flagship of Kansai's Jiro lineage and the first Ramen-Jiro-inspired shop in the region, this bowl carries the unmistakable Jiro soul: a sweet, non-emulsified pork-bone-and-soy soup slicked with melted back fat, hidden beneath a towering mound of bean sprouts and cabbage you eat your way down to. Yet the broth here is a touch more harmonious and milder than its wilder siblings, letting the thick hand-massaged noodles and that pork-fat sweetness sing rather than overwhelm.

places.name: 山﨑麺二郎 (Yamazaki Menjiro)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac. Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-sauce (shoyu) or salt tare; shoyu tare almost always contains wheat, and house-made noodles share the same kitchen. No gluten-free menu or accommodation found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: All bowls are built on chicken-bone broth plus Japanese dashi (bonito/seafood stock) and topped with pork chashu. There is no vegetable-based or vegan broth, and no vegan/vegetarian options were found.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree. Older/Michelin-era listings: 11:30–14:00, 18:00–22:00, closed Tuesdays. More recent Tabelog listing: Thu–Sun 11:30–15:30, closed Mon–Wed. Verify locally before visiting.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, quietly run ramen counter near Emmachi station, kept deliberately modest: just nine seats along an L-shaped counter, worked by the owner and one assistant. Despite earning a Michelin Bib Gourmand, the shop has held onto unusually low prices, with the basic bowl staying i

chef_bio.background: A small, quietly run ramen counter a few minutes from JR Emmachi station, kept deliberately modest: just nine seats along an L-shaped counter, worked by the owner and one assistant. Despite earning a Michelin Bib Gourmand (2016), the shop has held onto unusually low prices, with the basic soy bowl staying around 650 yen while most acclaimed ramen shops have drifted well past 1,000 yen.
chef_bio.philosophy: Refinement without spectacle: a restrained, balanced bowl at an everyday price, prioritizing clean broth and good noodles over flash or markup.
chef_bio.specialty: An 'assari' (light, clean) style: the soy-sauce ramen layers a chicken-bone base with Japanese dashi, while the salt (shio) ramen pairs chicken and seafood stock with a lift of yuzu. Tsukemen is served with house-made noodles and a squeeze of sudachi citrus that shifts the flavor as you eat. Chashu is a notably good cut of pork, served sliced or worked into a rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: Even with Michelin recognition, the shop keeps its signature bowl around 650 yen (shio ~750, tsukemen ~700) and stays cash-only with just a nine-seat counter.
The shio ramen finishes with yuzu and the tsukemen comes with sudachi citrus, small aromatic touches that define the shop's light, clean style.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: genjitsutouhi confirms: 9-seat L-counter near JR Emmachi, Nakagyo-ku; Michelin Bib Gourmand (2016); basic shoyu 650 yen, shio 750, tsukemen 700; cash only; shoyu = soy + chicken + washi dashi 'trinity', shio = chicken + seafood + yuzu, tsukemen with sudachi + bonito; owner works with one younger staffer. The source notes waits are usually short (~10 min at dinner), so no long-queue claim is asserted.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl and the shoyu tare is wheat-based; a nine-seat single-counter kitchen means no separation from gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard ramen uses a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken-bone plus fish/seafood dashi (bonito), and bowls are topped with pork chashu; there is no plant-based broth on offer.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat counter shop run by the owner and one assistant; little to no English, lines are common, and the quiet workflow rewards ordering simply and eating without fuss.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles are matched to the bowl: a thin, smooth, straight noodle for the shoyu, and a flatter, thicker, higher-hydration chewy noodle for the shio. Tsukemen uses a chewier thick cut.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shio ramen (salt) — the bowl regulars rave about; the shoyu ramen is the equally celebrated alternate.
ramen.chashu: Large, generously marbled pork belly slices, low-temperature/braised so they stay tender and rich.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui house noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a chicken-and-dried-seafood (bonito/kelp/dried fish) dashi, finished with pork chashu. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A refined, crystal-clear chintan that reads as modern Kyoto tanrei rather than the city's old se-abura tradition: a gentle chicken stock laced with a deep multi-fish-and-kombu dashi and rounded by a quiet soy or salt seasoning. You taste the lineage in a soup that is delicate yet startlingly savory, carried by house-cut noodles tuned to each tare.

places.name: 麺屋 猪一 (Menya Inoichi)
places.gf_detail: The signature dashi soba uses house-made noodles milled from stone-ground whole wheat flour, and the tare is white or black soy sauce (typically wheat-fermented). Both the noodles and the seasoning contain wheat, so the bowls are not celiac-safe and no gluten-free option exists. No allergen-handling or dedicated-equipment information was published.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian bowls are offered. While the signature broth is uniquely a 100% seafood dashi with no pork or chicken fat, it is fish-based rather than plant-based, and the wheat noodles and soy tare rule out a vegan meal.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–14:30 / 17:30–21:00; irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Kyoto-Kawaramachi (south side of Shijō-dōri, along Teramachi-dōri), Shimogyō-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺屋 猪一 opened in July 2013 and moved to its current location on Teramachi-dori, just south of Shijo near Kawaramachi, in 2019. It has grown into one of Kyoto's most recognized ramen destinations, earning the Michelin Bib Gourmand for roughly nine years running.

chef_bio.background: 麺屋 猪一 opened on 3 July 2013 and relocated on 1 May 2019 to its current spot on Teramachi-dori in Shimogyo-ku, just south of Shijo near Kawaramachi. It has grown into one of Kyoto's most recognized ramen destinations, earning the Michelin Bib Gourmand for many consecutive years.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is built around dashi culture rather than heavy tonkotsu trends: the broth is a 100% seafood stock with no animal fat such as pork or chicken, drawn from several carefully selected premium dried-fish (fushi) varieties, extracted slowly at low temperature to coax out a clean, layered umami.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the dashi soba, served in two styles: white (shiro-shoyu) for a clear, delicate, umami-forward bowl, and black (kuro-shoyu) for a mild yet richer one. House-made thin-but-firm noodles are milled from stone-ground whole wheat.

anecdotes.text: The broth contains no pork or chicken fat at all — it is built entirely from a blend of top-grade dried fish slowly drawn at low temperature, a deliberate stand for Kyoto's dashi tradition.
The noodles are made in-house from stone-ground whole wheat flour, described as thin but firm.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: leafkyoto (EN) and kyo-soku confirm: 100% fish/shellfish broth with no animal fat; house-made stone-ground whole-wheat noodles, thin but firm; white (shiro-shoyu) and black (kuro-shoyu) dashi soba; opened 3 Jul 2013, relocated 1 May 2019 to Teramachi-dori 542, Shimogyo-ku; Michelin Bib Gourmand for multiple consecutive years (sources cite 7 straight 2016-2022 for the honten; a sister shop '離れ' opened Nov 2017).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house-made whole-wheat noodles plus white or black soy sauce tare; no gluten-free preparation is offered and shared cooking water makes the kitchen unsuitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining seasoning is white (shiro) or black (kuro) soy sauce tare, which is normally wheat-fermented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on a seafood (dried-fish) dashi, so no broth is free of animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: The broth uniquely contains no pork or chicken fat — a 100% seafood dashi — which may matter to diners avoiding mammalian/poultry products specifically.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy 16-seat counter shop with no reservations and frequent queues; a few Japanese greetings and patience with the line will serve you well, though the central tourist location means staff are used to visitors.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles milled from stone-ground whole wheat flour; thin but notably firm, cut to stand up cleanly against the delicate clear dashi without muddying it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Dashi soba (chuka soba) — choose the black (kuro shoyu) for a deeper, rounder seasoning or the white (shiro shoyu/shio-leaning) for the cleanest expression of the dashi; the black is the house signature. A separate tsukemen version is also a draw.
ramen.chashu: Lean, gently cooked pork presented thin and restrained so it does not fight the seafood broth; preparation details are not firmly documented.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (stone-ground whole wheat) and the tare is soy-sauce based, so the bowl is not celiac-safe. No documented rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare option.

vegan.note: The defining broth is 100% seafood dashi (Rausu kombu plus multiple dried fish/katsuobushi), so even the no-animal-fat base is not plant-only. No documented kombu/shiitake vegan bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original built entirely on the soul of Japanese dashi: a crystal-clear seafood broth coaxed slowly from Rausu kombu and a chorus of dried fish, deliberately free of pork or chicken fat. You taste the lineage of the washoku dashi pot rather than any regional ramen tradition, with white and black soy-sauce versions framing that katsuobushi-driven clarity from delicate to deep.

places.name: 中華そば ほそかわ 花屋町店 (Chuka Soba Hosokawa (Hanayacho))
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is almost certainly wheat-based, served in a chicken-bone-and-back-fat / pork broth. There is no gluten-free noodle or menu option, and the kitchen is a single small ramen line with shared water and equipment, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire menu is animal-based: the broth is simmered chicken bones and pork with floating back fat (背脂), the only dishes are chuka soba and chashu (roast pork) ramen, and the noodles sit in a meat-and-soy broth. There are no plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (Mon-Sat); closed Sunday. Note: one source lists hours to 20:00 — confirm locally.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop opened in 1985 after the owner spent eight years training at Masutani (ますたに), the Kyoto institution credited with the city's back-fat shoyu ramen style. He carried that foundation forward but added his own touches to build an independent identity rather than a copy.

chef_bio.background: The shop opened in 1985 after the owner (Mr. Hosokawa) spent eight years training at Masutani (ますたに), the Ginkakuji institution credited with Kyoto's back-fat shoyu ramen style. He carried that foundation forward but added his own carefully selected back fat to build an independent identity rather than a copy.
chef_bio.philosophy: Conservative and assured: a quintessential, standard Kyoto ramen that respects the regional tradition and avoids needless innovation. The aim is the 'back-fat chacha' look with a surprisingly refined, drink-it-to-the-bottom finish.
chef_bio.specialty: A classic Kyoto chuka soba: a glistening back-fat soy-sauce broth that looks rich but finishes clean, built on chicken bones with a smooth pork umami, poured over fine straight noodles. The menu is deliberately tight — chuka soba and a chashu (roast pork) version — and the house chili-miso (辛味噌) and vinegar can be stirred in partway through.

anecdotes.text: The founder trained for eight years at Masutani, the lineage often credited with originating Kyoto's back-fat shoyu ramen, before opening his own shop in 1985 near Hankyu Nishikyogoku.
Despite the heavy-looking back-fat-and-soy broth, regulars note it drinks surprisingly light and clean thanks to the chicken-bone base, so you can finish the bowl without it feeling heavy.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-gourmet, kyotopi and arukikata confirm: owner Hosokawa trained 8 years at 銀閣寺ますたに, then opened in 1985 on Hanayacho-dori near Hankyu Nishikyogoku; inherits Masutani's soup/noodle tradition but uses his own selected 背脂 so the bowl looks こってり yet eats さっぱり; back-fat shoyu over chicken-bone base, medium-thin straight noodles, standard ~700 yen / large ~800; chili powder, spicy miso and vinegar offered.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small ramen line built around wheat noodles boiled in shared water; wheat cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth and tare are soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which is virtually always wheat-fermented; no gluten-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl uses a chicken-bone, pork and back-fat broth with meat toppings; nothing on the menu is plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is simple and Japanese is expected; point at the menu and a few words will carry you through.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles with a firm bite that grab the back-fat shoyu broth; firmness can be specified at ordering and a large bowl doubles the portion.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (ramen) — chicken-bone shoyu broth blanketed with fine pork back-fat, finished with a dusting of ichimi chili
ramen.chashu: Roasted pork loin, sliced thin (three slices); lean and lightly firm rather than melting fatty belly

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken bones and pork, with shoyu tare and a generous layer of pork back-fat; no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is textbook Kyoto se-abura shoyu — a dark, soy-tinged chicken-and-pork broth blanketed with a snowfall of fine pork back-fat, the lineage that runs back through Ginkaku-ji Masutani where the owner trained. The looks promise heaviness, but a clean chicken-bone clarity beneath the fat lets you drink it to the bottom, with a pinch of ichimi chili for a Kyoto bite.

places.name: 和醸良麺 すがり (Wajo Ryomen Sugari)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core dish is built on wheat: house-made flat wheat noodles (one of the two noodle choices is explicitly "whole wheat," the other yuzu-flavored — both wheat), plus a rich chicken-paitan-and-seafood dip that in this style of shop is seasoned with wheat-based shoyu tare. Offal (seared hoso tripe) is the headline ingredient, but the broth and noodles make this a hard no for gluten avoidance. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergen info is published. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is animal-based: chicken-bone paitan broth blended with seafood dashi (bonito-forward), and rare seared beef hoso (offal/tripe) as the signature topping. Only two menu items exist — motsu tsukemen and motsu ramen — both meat/fish-based. No vegetable or plant-based bowl is offered, and there is no published vegan accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:50) & 18:00–22:00 (last order 21:50); Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:50) & 18:00–21:00 (last order 20:50). Both services end early when sold out. Closed only year-end/New Year per search summary.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, down a hidden alley near Shijo-Karasuma (Shijo /
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sugari is part of the well-loved Kyoto ramen family centered on "Takakura Nijo," with this Wajo Ryomen Sugari branch occupying a renovated century-old machiya (wooden townhouse) tucked down an easily-missed alley near Shijo-Karasuma. You enter along a stone-paved corridor, buy a

chef_bio.background: Sugari is part of the well-loved Kyoto ramen family centered on 'Takakura Nijo,' this being that group's third shop, occupying a renovated century-old machiya (wooden townhouse) tucked down an easily-missed alley near Shijo-Karasuma. You enter along a stone-paved corridor past a small courtyard, buy a ticket from a vending machine that sits almost incongruously inside the rustic space, then pass through to a small, elegantly spotlit 10-seat counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: A two-dish, do-one-thing-well house: rare offal treated with care, paired with a deeply concentrated chicken-and-seafood broth and noodles made in-house, served fast in an intimate machiya setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Motsu tsukemen — house-made flat noodles served alongside a thick, intensely rich dip of chicken-bone paitan blended with seafood dashi, crowned with rare seared hoso (a prized cut of offal that is lightly torched with a blowtorch before serving for a fragrant finish). Accompanied by garlic buds and grilled leeks; a seasoned egg is a popular add-on. A motsu ramen (offal in broth) is the only other option.

anecdotes.text: The hoso offal is lightly seared with a blowtorch before serving, giving it a fragrant, smoky aroma that defines the bowl.
Noodles come in two styles — a whole-wheat noodle and a yuzu-scented noodle — both house-made and flat, designed to carry the thick dip.
The shop sits down a hard-to-find alley near the Kawaramachi/Shijo-Karasuma area, inside a century-old townhouse reached via a stone corridor and small courtyard.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: naox0812 and mckee-blog confirm: century-old machiya down an inconspicuous alley near Shijo-Karasuma, entered via stone corridor + courtyard; 10-seat counter, spotlit, condiment drawers; vending-machine tickets; motsu tsukemen (~900 yen) with rare 希少もつ blowtorch-seared, chicken-paitan + seafood dip; two noodle choices (whole-wheat / yuzu); also motsu soba; part of the 麺や高倉二条 group (Sugari = 3rd shop); Tabelog 100-Best 2023.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish uses house-made wheat noodles (both noodle options — whole-wheat and yuzu — are wheat) cooked in shared water, making the kitchen unsuitable for gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen or tare ingredient disclosure is published; as a standard motsu tsukemen shop the seasoning base is expected to include wheat-based soy sauce, but this is not confirmed on any fetched page.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Both menu items are animal-based (chicken-paitan + seafood dashi broth with beef hoso offal); no plant-based option exists, so all dishes involve meat/fish.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter down a hard-to-find alley with a Japanese ticket-machine ordering system and brisk, locals-paced service — comfortable if you can manage a little Japanese and the kiosk, but not set up for English hand-holding.

noodles.notes: House-made whole-wheat (zenryufun) thick flat noodles, mochi-mochi chewy and firm, built to grab the heavy dipping broth. A yuzu-infused version of the noodles can be chosen as an aromatic alternative.

ramen.signature_bowl: Motsu tsukemen (もつつけ麺) — thick whole-wheat dipping noodles with a rich, creamy chicken-and-pork-bone broth deepened with seafood dashi and a touch of sweet-sour, served warm in a small Staub pot, crowned with charred beef offal.
ramen.chashu: No conventional chashu — the heart is motsu: prized domestic-beef offal (hoso/hosomotsu, of which only a few kilos come from a whole animal), torched/seared so it is juicy and smoky and bursts in the mouth.

gf.note: Wheat-based whole-grain noodles and a shoyu-style seasoning over a pork/chicken/seafood broth — not celiac-safe, and no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken and fish (bonito) dashi, and the signature topping is beef offal; no plant-based broth or noodle option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional classic: a creamy chicken-pork-bone tonkotsu-gyokai dipping broth (the now-ubiquitous "W-soup" lineage of Tokyo-style tsukemen) given a distinctly Kansai twist by crowning it with charred domestic-beef offal. You taste the gyokai-tonkotsu soul in the soup, but the smoky motsu is what makes this bowl its own.

places.name: 麺や高倉二条 (Menya Takakura Nijo)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat noodles in a long-simmered pork-bone and dried-fish/bonito broth seasoned with soy-based tare, so gluten is present throughout. No gluten-free noodles or accommodation is advertised, and the kitchen is a single tight counter. Treat as off-limits for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The core broth is a 15-hour pork-bone (tonkotsu) and seafood (niboshi/bonito) stock, and signature toppings are motsu (offal), char-roasted pork, or wagyu. There is no plant-based broth or vegan ramen option here.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00, 18:00-21:00 (holidays L.O. 14:50 / 20:50). Open daily; closed only year-end/New Year.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺や高倉二条 is the flagship of a small, tightly run Kyoto ramen family that also includes the Sugari shops and a couple of sibling counters. The shops are deliberately low-key: several have no noren curtain and no signboard, so from the street they read more like a quiet machiya townh

chef_bio.background: 麺や高倉二条 is the head shop of a small, tightly run Kyoto ramen family that also includes the Sugari shops and a couple of sibling counters. The shops are deliberately low-key: the storefront carries almost no signage — just a small wooden nameplate on a stylish, machiya-like facade — so from the street it reads more like a quiet townhouse than a ramen joint, and it fills mostly by word of mouth.
chef_bio.philosophy: The group cooks under the banner of 医食同源 (ishoku-dogen) — 'food and medicine share one source' — aiming for bowls that nourish as much as they satisfy, presented with an understated, distinctly Kyoto restraint.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a rich tonkotsu-gyokai (pork-bone and dried-fish/bonito) bowl, served as both ramen and the group's celebrated tsukemen. The dipping broth combines long-simmered pork bones with niboshi and katsuobushi dashi, balanced toward a deep finish; the noodles are house-made, flat and silky (with a yuzu-noodle option), and the prized topping is motsu (offal), alongside char-pork and wagyu choices.

anecdotes.text: The shopfront is intentionally almost unmarked — a stylish, ryotei-like facade with only a small wooden nameplate — so first-timers often walk past it; it has become a quiet local favorite found largely through word of mouth.
Across the group's counters, chopsticks and condiments are tucked into a little drawer at each seat — a small signature quirk of the Takakura Nijo / Sugari shops.
The tsukemen dipping broth is built from long-simmered pork bones blended with dried-fish and bonito dashi, and motsu is the topping most often recommended to newcomers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: webmist, the gnavi group interview, and magazine.vacan confirm: head shop of the 高倉二条/すがり family; near Karasuma-Oike / Marutamachi at Takakura-dori × Nijo-dori; almost no signage (small wooden plaque), hard-to-find entrance, ~8-seat counter; philosophy 医食同源; tonkotsu-gyokai broth heavy on niboshi/bonito dashi, house-made flat noodles with yuzu option; tsukemen toppings motsu / chashu / wagyu; seat-drawer for chopsticks; Tabelog Ramen WEST 100 (2022).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles are the core of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; no gluten-free option exists, so cross-contamination is unavoidable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen is typically brewed from wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (15-hour pork-bone stock plus fish dashi); there is no separate vegan preparation, so any item would contact pork and fish stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, unsigned counter shop where locals slip in and out quietly; a little Japanese and ramen-counter etiquette go a long way, though the staff are used to visitors who found them by word of mouth.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles produced in an upstairs noodle room. Whole-grain (zenryufun) flour gives a soba-like, nutty, slightly grainy character with a smooth, silky bite; on the slimmer side for a tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen. A citrus yuzu noodle option is also offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Noko tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen (thick pork-bone & seafood dipping noodles) — best ordered with the motsu (braised offal) or wagyu topping
ramen.chashu: Char-grilled (aburi) pork chashu with a fragrant seared edge; cubed/diced chashu is also worked into the dipping broth. Signature upgrades swap in tender braised motsu (offal) or thinly shredded premium wagyu beef.

gf.note: Whole-grain wheat noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Core broth is pork-bone and fish (katsuo/seafood) dashi with animal proteins and char-grilled pork; no documented plant-based broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of the modern thick tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen movement, this bowl pairs a viscous pork-bone soup with a deep, almost gritty pounding of bonito and seafood — the seafood-and-pork soul you taste in every dip. Hidden in a tiny Kyoto machiya, it leans into a local twist of recent years: tender braised offal alongside the noodles.

places.name: 京都鶏白湯そば 純 (Kyoto Tori-Paitan Soba Jun)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is wheat-noodle chicken-paitan ramen; the broth and tare carry the usual wheat risk, and no allergen menu or GF accommodation is published. The signature bowl is built on wheat noodles in an emulsified chicken (and small amount of pork-bone) broth — not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The core product is chicken-paitan broth made from Kagoshima chicken (with small amounts of Miyazaki/Oita pork bone), char siu, eggs, and seven kinds of dried seafood. Some lighter/"vegetable" menu structure is mentioned but no genuine vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered, and broths are animal-based throughout.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Wednesday evenings (per official site).
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, near Marutamachi Station (Karasuma Line), south
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A modern chicken-paitan specialist tucked into a residential pocket of Nakagyo-ku, a short walk south of the Imperial Palace and steps from Marutamachi Station. The owner came up as a Japanese-cuisine (washoku) chef before opening this ramen shop, and that fine-dining training sh

chef_bio.background: A modern chicken-paitan specialist tucked into a residential pocket of Nakagyo-ku, steps from Karasuma-Marutamachi Station. The owner, Sakakimoto, came up as a Japanese-cuisine (washoku) chef before opening this ramen shop, and that fine-dining training shows in the careful, clean presentation. The honten deliberately serves its neighborhood rather than chasing tourists, even as a second branch opened in busy Shijo-Karasuma in August 2025.
chef_bio.philosophy: Depth and texture through technique rather than long brute-force simmering — emulsifying the broth to coax out umami and a creamy mouthfeel — paired with the polish of a trained washoku cook and a focus on regulars over tourist trade.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori-paitan soba — a creamy, foamy chicken-broth ramen. The kitchen builds the soup mainly from Kagoshima chicken (with a small amount of Miyazaki and Oita pork bone) and finishes each bowl by force-emulsifying it in a blender (kyousei nyuuka), whipping the broth into a smooth, almost espuma-like foam that clings to the medium-thick noodles and carries added seafood umami. The flagship is the rich foamy tori-paitan (with Tajima chicken-breast chashu); variations include a salt (shio) seafood version with clam and multiple fish stocks, a winter miso paitan, and a seafood-leaning bowl made with seven kinds of dried fish. The tokusei (special) bowl piles on extra char siu and egg.

anecdotes.text: Each bowl is individually blasted in a blender to force-emulsify the broth, which is what gives the soup its signature foamy, creamy body and deeper umami.
Lemon is set out so diners can add acidity partway through and shift the broth's flavor — an aji-hen (flavor-change) ritual the shop encourages.
The owner moved from a career as a Japanese-cuisine chef into running this ramen shop, and keeps the focus on serving local residents rather than tourists despite the central location.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: chukasoba-jun (EN), osumituki and kyotopi confirm: owner 榊本 was a washoku chef before opening; foamy 鶏白湯 built by blender 強制乳化; Kagoshima chicken + a little Miyazaki/Oita pork bone; Tajima chicken-breast chashu; menu of tori-paitan, seafood paitan (7 dried fish), shio seafood (clam), winter miso paitan, plus sides; lemon offered tableside for aji-hen; honten at Karasuma-Marutamachi (三本木町462-5), 2nd branch opened 13 Aug 2025 at Shijo-Karasuma; deliberately local-focused.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood ramen counter that has an English website and is used to local diners; basic Japanese greetings smooth things along, but you can manage with pointing and a few words.

noodles.notes: Custom medium-thick flat-cut (中太平打ち) noodles with a firm bite, chosen to grip and carry the thick foam-emulsified broth. Mazesoba uses a chewier, larger (1.5x) portion.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏白湯そば (Tori Paitan Soba) — the foam-style creamy chicken-bone bowl. Squeeze the lemon mid-bowl to lift it, and finish with the "dive rice" in the leftover soup.
ramen.chashu: The heart is a slab of slow-braised pork belly, lightly aburi (seared) so the edge chars and the fat melts — it collapses into tender, sweet pieces against the rich broth. A rare-seared Tajima chicken-breast chashu is also offered as an alternative.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus shoyu/miso tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken bones with added pork bone, and bowls carry pork/chicken chashu, egg and clams. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on the awa-kei (foam-style) tori-paitan movement: Kagoshima chicken bones with a touch of Miyazaki/Oita pork are force-emulsified in a blender until the soup turns into a creamy, cappuccino-like froth, so you taste pure poultry koku rather than any one regional lineage. It is a bowl built around texture and roundness — rich and mellow, with a Hokkaido-onion shio or a winter Sapporo-miso tare to season the foam.

places.name: 重厚軍団 (Juko Gundan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The noodles are homemade wheat noodles, the mazesoba uses a sweet soy-sauce (typically wheat-based) dressing, and the tsukemen broth is a rich pork-bone and fish stock. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is indicated. A celiac should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes are built on pork-bone and fish broth with pork-belly chashu, marinated egg, and meat toppings. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:45-13:30 / 12:00-13:00, Dinner 18:00-22:30. Irregular holidays. Stays limited to under 20 minutes due to the small space.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner ran a ramen website before opening the shop in 2007, turning a fan's obsession into a kitchen.

chef_bio.origin: Ichijoji, Kyoto — the city's famous ramen street, a short walk from Ichijoji Station.
chef_bio.background: The owner ran a ramen website before opening the shop in 2007, turning a fan's obsession into a kitchen.
chef_bio.philosophy: Big, unapologetic flavor and serious volume aimed at hungry diners — the kind of intense, no-frills bowl that put Ichijoji-style tsukemen and mazesoba on the map.
chef_bio.specialty: Two signature bowls anchor the shop: a rich tsukemen with thick, homemade straight noodles (standard 400g) dipped in a heavy pork-bone broth deepened with fish stock and finished with pork-belly chashu, green onion and a marinated egg; and the J-mazesoba, brothless extra-thick wavy noodles tossed with a sweet soy-based dressing, a hit of garlic, a pile of vegetables and a chunky slab of meat.

anecdotes.text: The shop is credited as a birthplace of Ichijoji's tsukemen and mazesoba scene, and remains a local landmark drawing ramen fans and hungry students; on a recent visit roughly 80% of customers ordered the mazesoba.
In April 2025 the small interior was refreshed with new floors and walls while the heavy, rich bowls stayed front and center.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan-kyoto confirms: owner was formerly a ramen-website administrator, opened 2007; 6-min walk from Ichijoji Station; counter-only ~9 seats; tsukemen with 400g house-made straight noodles in pork-bone + seafood broth, chashu and marinated egg; J-mazesoba brothless thick wavy noodles with sweet soy sauce, garlic, vegetables and meat (most popular with students, ~80% ordered it); interior renovated April 2025.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The J-mazesoba is dressed in a sweet soy-sauce base, which in standard ramen tare is wheat-containing shoyu; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on pork-bone and fish broth with meat and egg toppings, so the kitchen is not set up for plant-based diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat counter shop with a 20-minute time limit, mostly local students, and no reservations; basic Japanese and quick ordering will make the visit smoother.

noodles.notes: Homemade (jikasei) straight thick noodles for the tsukemen, with a chewy, wheat-forward bite that clings to the heavy dipping broth; the mazesoba uses extra-thick crinkled (chijire) noodles. Standard tsukemen portion is a generous 400g.

ramen.signature_bowl: Juko Tsukemen — thick homemade straight noodles served alongside a dense, viscous gyokai-tonkotsu dipping broth (the alternative house favorite is the J-Mazesoba topped with a fist-sized chunk of pork).
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly (buta-bara) chashu; the mazesoba instead crowns the bowl with a fist-sized chunk of meat.

gf.note: Tsukemen and mazesoba both center on wheat-and-kansui noodles, and the broth/tare lean on pork bone, seafood dashi and a soy-based sauce — wheat throughout. No rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and fish-dashi broth finished with pork belly chashu and pork toppings. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A heavyweight of Ichijoji's fabled ramen street, this counter-only specialist lives up to its name (juko = 'weighty/substantial'): the signature is not a soup bowl but a tsukemen whose dipping broth is a glossy, gravy-thick fusion of pork bone and seafood dashi. You taste the modern gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen lineage — born of Tokyo's dipping-noodle boom — rendered here at maximum density and clung to by chewy house-made noodles.

places.name: 天天有 本店 (Tentenyu Honten)
places.gf_detail: A 1971-founded chicken-paitan ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat noodles in a chicken-and-vegetable broth, with a soy-based tare that almost certainly contains wheat. No gluten-free options, no allergen menu, and no dedicated preparation are advertised. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on a chicken-and-vegetable broth simmered for 18+ hours, topped with chashu pork and egg. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen, and the broth itself is animal-based, so there is nothing for a strict plant-based diner here.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-22:00; closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Sakyō-ku, Ichijōji (Eizan Railway Ichijōji Stn / Ichijōji Ramen Street)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1971 in Ichijoji, the neighborhood now famous as Kyoto's ramen street, Tentenyu Honten is the original shop that gave its name to a wider Tentenyu lineage, including a sister restaurant in Osaka's Suminoe district. More than fifty years on, it remains a fixture of the

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1971 in Ichijoji, the neighborhood now famous as Kyoto's ramen street, Tentenyu Honten is the original shop that gave its name to a wider Tentenyu lineage, including a sister restaurant in Osaka's Suminoe district. More than fifty years on, it remains a fixture of the area's intensely competitive ramen scene.
chef_bio.philosophy: Depth without heaviness, drawing the natural sweetness out of vegetables and chicken over a long simmer, and letting each diner fine-tune the bowl by choosing noodle thickness and firmness, tare strength, scallion amount, and a soft-boiled or onsen egg.
chef_bio.specialty: A sweet, creamy chicken paitan: chicken and vegetables simmered for roughly 18 hours or more into a rich yet surprisingly gentle broth, paired with medium-thin straight noodles. The signature order is the nitamago chashu-men, a bowl topped with roasted pork and a soft-cooked egg.

anecdotes.text: The chicken paitan broth is built over roughly 18 hours of simmering chicken and vegetables, giving it a sweetness diners describe as concentrated yet light.
This Ichijoji location is the original honten of the Tentenyu name, which later extended to a franchise-style sister shop in Osaka's Suminoe district.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan-kyoto and tabelog confirm: 天天有 honten opened 1971 in Ichijoji (左京区一乗寺西杉ノ宮町49); chicken-and-vegetable paitan simmered ~18h+, sweet yet light; medium-thin straight noodles; diners adjust firmness, broth strength, scallion, egg; signature nitamago chashu-men (~1000 yen); counter + tables + 8-person booth; sister shop in Osaka Suminoe.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, popular local ramen counter with no English menu or allergen support expected, so a guide or some Japanese helps with ordering and customizing your bowl.

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium-thin straight noodles chosen to carry the thick, faintly sweet chicken broth, which clings to the strands. Firmness is adjustable to taste, and a flat-noodle (hiramen) option appears in limited quantities.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chicken paitan ramen (chashu-men for the full version) — the lone broth at the original shop, a thick, mild, faintly sweet tori-paitan over thin straight noodles, finished with chashu, green onion and egg.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork thigh (momo) — leaner and cleanly seasoned in an old-school style, sliced tender; the chashu-men piles on thick, substantial slices.
notable_toppings: optional kotteri finish of back-fat + ichimi togarashi
beni-shoga (pickled red ginger) on the side

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles; chicken-and-vegetable broth with no documented rice/konjac-noodle or certified GF option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is long-simmered chicken (plus chicken-thigh chashu); no documented plant-only bowl. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A 1971 pioneer of Ichijoji's ramen row, this bowl predates the tori-paitan boom: chicken and vegetables simmered for some 18 hours into a thick, ivory broth whose hallmark is an unusual, gentle sweetness rather than heavy fat. You taste that mellow, almost soothing chicken depth in every sip — a distinctly Kyoto reading of tori-paitan that feels nourishing rather than punishing.

places.name: 珍遊 一乗寺本店 (Chinyu Ichijoji Honten)
places.gf_detail: A traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop: wheat ramen noodles in a chicken/pork-bone broth with back fat and (typically) wheat-based shoyu tare. There are no gluten-free noodles or menu items, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiacs. Side dishes like fried rice (yakimeshi) and karaage are also breaded/wheat-seasoned. Confidence: no.
places.vegan_detail: The signature and effectively the entire menu is built on animal stock — a chicken-bone broth enriched with pork back fat — plus chashu pork and meat-based sides. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–24:00 (last order 23:30); closed Wed (open if it falls on a holiday)
places.neighborhood: Sakyō-ku, Ichijōji (Eizan Railway Ichijōji Stn / Ichijōji Ramen Street)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 珍遊 (Chinyu) opened in 1950 (Showa 25) and is one of the founding old-guard ramen houses of Kyoto, now anchoring the famous ramen street of Ichijoji in the city's northeast. Over more than seventy years it has grown from a single counter shop into a small Kyoto family of three loc

roles: head store of a small three-shop Kyoto group
chef_bio.origin: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto — a neighborhood that became one of Japan's most celebrated ramen battlegrounds, partly on the strength of long-running shops like this one.
chef_bio.background: 珍遊 (Chinyu) opened in 1950 (Showa 25) and is one of the founding old-guard ramen houses of Kyoto, now anchoring the famous ramen street of Ichijoji in the city's northeast. Over more than seventy years it has grown from a single counter shop into a small Kyoto family of three locations, with this Ichijoji shop remaining the honten (head store).
chef_bio.philosophy: Their tagline sums up the bowl they aim for: '見た目こってり、後味さっぱり' — it looks rich and indulgent, but the finish is clean and light.
chef_bio.specialty: Their signature chuka-soba: a chicken-bone broth finished with rendered pork back fat (背脂) and a soy tare, served with straight medium-thin noodles. The shop frames this back-fat shoyu style as one of the roots of Kyoto ramen. Diners can adjust noodle firmness, back-fat richness, and green onion to taste, and popular set meals pair the bowl with fried rice (yakimeshi) or karaage.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1950, Chinyu is treated as one of the originators of the Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen style, and the shop itself calls this bowl a source of Kyoto ramen.
The Ichijoji head store sits on Kyoto's renowned ramen street and offers customer parking (about 15 affiliated spaces), unusual for an old city ramen shop and a sign of how much volume it draws.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: chinyu.jp confirms: 珍遊 founded 1950 (Showa 25), 70+ years; chicken-bone broth + 背脂 + soy tare, medium-thin straight noodles, framed as a source of Kyoto ramen; tagline 見た目こってり後味さっぱり; three Kyoto locations, Ichijoji honten open 11:00-24:00 (closed Wed); sets pair the bowl with yakimeshi, karaage or gyoza; honten offers affiliated parking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen uses a traditional soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in a shop of this style is wheat-based; no gluten-free tare or noodles are offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based throughout — a chicken-bone stock enriched with pork back fat — so there is no non-animal stock prepared on site.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, no-frills old-school Kyoto ramen shop with Japanese-only menu and ordering; manageable for a confident solo visitor but best approached with a guide or some Japanese, especially for any dietary requests.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin, low-hydration straight noodles milled from Hokkaido wheat, chosen to grab and carry the back-fat-laced broth; firmness can be ordered to taste.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the chicken-bone white broth bowl finished with shoyu tare and a generous scatter of fresh pork back-fat; the back-fat amount, scallion, and noodle firmness can all be dialed in.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-based ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (wheat) tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu — animal products run through every layer, and no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A founding bowl of Kyoto ramen, simmered since 1950 on the city's Ichijoji ramen street: cloudy chicken-bone broth lacquered with shoyu and a snowfall of fresh pork back-fat, so it looks heavy yet finishes clean — the kotteri-but-light se-abura shoyu lineage that defines the Kyoto north-side style. You taste that old-school chicken-and-lard soul in every sip, the back-fat melting into the soy backbone.

places.name: 麺屋 さん田 (Menya Sanda)
places.gf_detail: The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles with a sesame-based dan dan sauce or a concentrated chicken broth, and Japanese ramen tare typically contains wheat soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or preparation is offered, and nothing in available sources suggests celiac-safe accommodation. Not suitable for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-broth (鶏白湯) and chicken-based shop: the broth is made from chicken and water, the dan dan mazesoba is topped with minced chicken, the ramen carries chicken chashu, and a marinated egg is a common topping. There are no vegetarian or vegan bowls. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 18:00–21:30; open daily (no regular closing day; temporary closures announced on X/Twitter)
places.cuisine: Dan dan mazesoba / rich chicken paitan ramen and tsukemen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺屋 さん田 opened in the Saiin district of Kyoto's Ukyo Ward in 2017, founded by an owner who came up through the well-known Kyoto shop Ginjo Ramen Kubota (吟醸らーめん久保田) before going independent. It rose quickly in a fiercely competitive Kyoto ramen scene to become one of the city's mos

chef_bio.background: 麺屋 さん田 opened in the Saiin district of Kyoto's Ukyo Ward in 2017, founded by an owner who came up through the well-known Kyoto shop Ginjo Ramen Kubota (吟醸らーめん久保田) before going independent. It rose quickly in a fiercely competitive Kyoto ramen scene to become one of the city's most talked-about noodle shops, and has been listed on Tabelog's ramen 100-best since 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on noodle craftsmanship and a clean, concentrated broth: the chicken paitan is described as deeply concentrated yet surprisingly light, and the house-made noodles are cut with a textured surface so they grab the thick soup or sauce. The menu is deliberately noodle-focused with few side dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: The two pillars are a concentrated chicken paitan (鶏白湯) — ramen and tsukemen built on a broth made from little more than chicken and water, finished with low-temperature chicken-breast chashu, diced menma and a fragrant caramelized onion-and-garlic oil — and the signature 担担まぜそば (dan dan mazesoba): house-made thick straight noodles coated in a rich, sticky sesame sauce with minced chicken, nuts, onion and bok choy, a numbing-spicy balance, and a small bowl of rice to finish the leftover sauce.

anecdotes.text: The dan dan mazesoba is the shop's cult favorite — a rich sesame sauce with a balance of spice, numbing sensation and umami, finished by mixing the remaining sauce into a bowl of rice.

The chicken paitan is made from essentially just chicken and water for a concentrated but clean flavor, paired with low-temperature chicken-breast chashu and a caramelized onion-garlic oil; the shop regularly draws lines at peak hours.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (hePrB, B4hca) confirms: opened 2017 in Saiin, Ukyo-ku; owner formerly at 吟醸らーめん久保田; chicken paitan from chicken + water, concentrated yet clean, with 鶏油, low-temp chicken-breast chashu ('top-class'), diced menma and caramelized onion-garlic oil; house-made medium-thin rough-surface noodles; 担担まぜそば with sesame sauce (spice/numb/umami) finished into rice; long lines at peak; Tabelog 100-best ramen since 2019.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All bowls use house-made wheat noodles boiled in shared water, with sesame dan dan sauce or chicken broth and (typically) wheat-based soy sauce tare; there is no gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese ramen tare for chicken/shoyu-style bowls is normally made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is chicken-based throughout (chicken paitan broth, minced-chicken and chicken-chashu toppings, egg), so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy counter-only ramen shop with lines at peak times; a few words of Japanese and ramen-shop etiquette (order, eat, free your seat) will go a long way.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles milled from domestic wheat with a noticeable whole-wheat character; medium-thick and straight with a smooth, slurpable surface and firm, springy bite that stands up to the heavy chicken broth. The tsukemen version is iced for extra snap. Strong wheat aroma is part of the appeal.

ramen.signature_bowl: Rich chicken paitan tsukemen (濃厚鶏白湯つけ麺) — thick, concentrated chicken dipping broth with house-made wheat noodles served plain so the soup leads.
ramen.chashu: Steamed chicken breast (mune) chashu — pale, fine-grained and tender rather than braised pork; served as bite-sized slices.

gf.note: Noodles are house-made from wheat (with whole-wheat) and kansui, and the seasoning is not gluten-free. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire menu is built on a chicken-only paitan broth with chicken chashu and chicken-fat aroma oil; the mazesoba carries minced meat as well. No plant-based broth or bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan specialist whose soul is pure chicken: the dipping broth is chicken and water simmered down until it turns velvet-thick, yet it finishes clean rather than cloying, with a slick of golden chicken fat and a whisper of scorched garlic carrying the aroma. It sits in the contemporary nou-kou (ultra-rich) chicken-paitan lineage rather than any old gotochi school, letting the bird's umami — not pork bone or heavy oil — do all the talking.

places.name: らーめんや亜喜英 (Ramen-ya Akihide)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop built around an ultra-thick pork-and-chicken broth, served with chunky chewy wheat noodles and chashu pork; the tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce. Nothing in any source indicates gluten-free handling, and the tiny one-counter operation has no allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is an intensely rich pork-bone and chicken-bone broth with pork chashu toppings. There are no plant-based bowls and no source mentions vegetarian or vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Irregular days off and irregular hours. Basically closed; opens only on days announced on Twitter (@eigyoakihide for lunch / @eigyoakihide_2 for dinner). Usually from around 18:00 until sold out (some days last order around 23:00). Announced about 30 minutes before opening. One bowl per person (excluding children); numbered-ticket system.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A legendary hole-in-the-wall ramen counter in Kyoto's Ichijoji "ramen street" district, a short walk from Ichijoji Station on the Eizan Electric Railway. The shop is famous less for marketing than for its mystique: it is, in its own words, basically closed, opening only on the da

chef_bio.origin: 一乗寺, 京都 (Kyoto's Ichijoji ramen district)
chef_bio.background: A legendary hole-in-the-wall ramen counter in Kyoto's Ichijoji 'ramen street' district, a short walk from Ichijoji Station on the Eizan Electric Railway. The shop is famous less for marketing than for its mystique: it is, in its own words, basically closed, opening only on the days the owner decides to cook and announcing those days on Twitter — to the point that locals describe it as a hidden gem because no one ever knows when it's open.
chef_bio.philosophy: Self-described as a specialty shop for the 濃厚民族 — the devoted lovers of intensely rich, heavy flavor — focused entirely on delivering maximum depth and coating power rather than subtlety. The owner runs it strictly on his own schedule and his own rules: one bowl per person (children excepted) and a numbered-ticket system for the queue.
chef_bio.specialty: An extraordinarily thick, ultra-rich (極濃こってり) pork-bone and chicken-bone broth so concentrated that a spoon reportedly stands upright in it — closer to a glaze than a soup — coating thick, chewy wheat noodles, topped with generous chunks (not slices) of tender chashu pork.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so unpredictable that it's known as the place where you never know when it's open — it stays closed by default and only posts on Twitter, with separate accounts for lunch and dinner service, when it decides to open.
The broth is so dense that reviewers describe a renge (soup spoon) standing upright in it, and the eating experience feels closer to tsukemen than ordinary ramen.
Bowls come crowned with chunks of chashu that are notably tender and stand up to the heavy broth — described as not a fillet but a chunk of roasted pork.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (CIIF3) and kyoto-kanko confirm: Ichijoji (左京区一乗寺北大丸町5-2); closed by default, opens only when the owner decides and posts on Twitter (separate lunch/dinner accounts); billed as a shop for the 濃厚民族; one bowl per customer, numbered-ticket queue; ultra-rich 極濃こってり pork+chicken broth so thick a spoon stands upright (closer to tsukemen than ramen); thick chewy noodles; chunky, tender chashu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles cooked in a shared pot; broth and tare are not gluten-free. No gluten-free handling described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce, as is standard for this style; no gluten-free tare offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-bone based with pork chashu; no vegan preparation, and animal stock is the core of every bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny nine-seat counter run on the owner's terms with a one-bowl-per-person rule and numbered-ticket queuing, where you'll need to read the Japanese Twitter feed to know it's even open — comfortable for those who can navigate in Japanese.

noodles.notes: Choice of medium-thick straight noodles or a medium-thin cut, both built to grab the heavy emulsified broth; the thick straight option (around 180g) has a chewy, substantial bite that stands up to the sauce-like soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Junnoko / Tokuno rich ramen (純濃厚らーめん) — the single bowl this shop is built around: an extreme chicken-and-pork-bone broth poured at the density you choose, from "doro-doro" (thick enough to coat the ladle) to "mellow." Add garlic and/or a hit of yuzu peel at the counter.
ramen.chashu: Two cuts in one bowl: chunky aburi-style pork belly that is hung and roasted (吊るし焼き) for charred, fragrant edges that crumble apart, alongside a softer braised nibuta. Generous goro-goro chunks rather than thin slices.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-based tare in a pork/chicken broth; nothing here is celiac-safe and no rice/konjac-noodle or GF option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire identity of the bowl is an animal broth boiled down from pork and chicken bones, finished with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or topping option exists.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto super-kotteri specialist where the broth is reduced so far it stands a ladle upright — a chicken-and-pork paitan closer to gravy than soup, the koukon end of Kyoto's rich-ramen lineage rather than the city's classic se-abura shoyu. You taste the bones themselves: hours of pork and chicken simmered into one viscous, glossy emulsion you can dial from doro-doro to mellow.

places.name: つけめん 恵那く (Tsukemen Enaku)
places.gf_detail: A tsukemen specialist built around house-made extra-thick wheat noodles dipped in a rich pork-bone-and-fish broth that is virtually always thickened/seasoned with wheat-containing elements.
