There is no gluten-free noodle, no separate prep, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or those needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is centered on pork-bone (tonkotsu) and fish (gyokai) broth, with meat and egg toppings. There are no vegan or vegetarian bowls. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–15:30 (closes when sold out); closed Tue
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2010 in a quiet residential stretch of Ichijoji, Kyoto's famed 'ramen street,' and grew almost entirely by word of mouth into one of the area's most queued-for tsukemen counters. A tiny L-shaped counter of about a dozen seats wraps around the open kitchen, so you eat ju

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2010 in a quiet residential stretch of Ichijoji, Kyoto's famed 'ramen street,' a two-minute walk from Eizan Railway's Ichijoji Station, and grew almost entirely by word of mouth into one of the area's most queued-for tsukemen counters. A small counter of about a dozen seats (twelve) wraps around the open kitchen, so you eat just steps from where the bowls are built.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately narrow menu — essentially three tsukemen (standard fish-and-pork-bone, spicy, and curry) plus toppings and rice — reflecting a focus on doing one thing exceptionally rather than chasing variety.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 魚介豚骨つけ麺 — silky, chewy extra-thick house-made noodles (milled from Izawa whole-grain flour) served beside a deeply concentrated pork-bone-and-fish dipping broth, balanced with fish powder, oil and a touch of spice. The 特製 version piles on double meat, menma and a half seasoned egg.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 2010 and became a word-of-mouth sensation, drawing lines for its lunch-only service (11:30–15:30, closed Tuesdays).
Noodles are house-made using Izawa whole-grain flour, described as smooth, chewy and extra-thick; a medium (275g) portion is priced the same as regular.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan-kyoto.com confirms: opened 2010, near-instant word-of-mouth fame, 12 counter seats, three tsukemen varieties (fish/spicy/curry), Izawa (井澤製粉) whole-grain house-made thick noodles, concentrated seafood+pork-bone broth, lunch-only, closed Tuesdays, 特製 with double meat/menma/half egg. (Second listed source kyototravels.com returned 403 and could not be re-verified.)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tsukemen is built on house-made wheat noodles dipped in a wheat-seasoned pork-bone-and-fish broth; gluten is unavoidable and no gluten-free option or separate prep is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use pork-bone and fish broth with meat and egg toppings; there is no vegan or vegetarian preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, popular lunch-only counter shop where lines form before opening; ordering is simple but English support is unlikely, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps.

noodles.notes: House-made extra-thick (gokuboso/極太) straight noodles cut with whole-grain flour from Izawa Mills; chewy and silky (tsuru-tsuru/mochi-mochi) with a pronounced wheat sweetness that stands up to the heavy dip. Portions up to medium (around 275g) at one price.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (つけめん) — extra-thick whole-grain noodles with a thick gyokai-tonkotsu/chicken dipping broth; the Tokusei (special) adds extra chashu, menma and a seasoned egg. Spicy (kara) and curry dip versions also offered.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, served in a doubled portion on the special bowl; rolled/sliced (not aburi-charred).

gf.note: Whole-grain wheat noodles with kansui and a shoyu-based dip make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The dip is built on a long-simmered pork/chicken/vegetable animal stock blended with a heavy seafood dashi (mackerel, bonito, sardine, oyster, etc.) plus pork chashu; no plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Ichijoji ramen-street specialist working in the gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen idiom: a thick, double-soup dip where a long-cooked pork-chicken-vegetable stock is married to a deep ocean of mackerel, bonito, sardine and oyster, then finished with fish powder and oil. The soul is in that surf-and-turf concentration clinging to fat, chewy whole-grain noodles — rich enough that you finish it with a soup-wari dilution.

places.name: ラーメン二郎 京都店 (Ramen Jiro Kyoto-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Jiro-style ramen is built on thick wheat noodles, a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, and a pork-and-back-fat broth — all standard wheat-containing components. There is no gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and the kitchen is a single shared boil-and-assemble line. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is a heavy pork (and back-fat) stock, bowls are built around large chunks of chashu pork, and the tare is animal/soy-sauce based. There are no vegetable or plant-based bowls; even toppings like extra vegetables sit in pork broth. Vegans cannot eat here.
places.hours_raw: Reported hours vary by source. Tabelog/official list roughly: weekdays 11:00–14:00 & 18:00–21:30 (lunch service sometimes suspended), Sat 11:00–14:30 & 17:30–21:30, Sun 11:00–14:30 & 17:30–20:30. Closed Wednesdays plus irregular closures. Confirm same-day via the shop's X account (@jiro_kyoto) before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 一乗寺（左京区）/ Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Jiro Kyoto opened on April 2, 2017, as a fully sanctioned 直系 (direct-lineage) branch of the legendary Tokyo Ramen Jiro empire — for several years it stood as the only direct Jiro branch in all of western Japan (until a Kyushu branch later opened). The proprietor trained at

chef_bio.background: Ramen Jiro Kyoto opened on April 2, 2017, as a fully sanctioned 直系 (direct-lineage) branch of the legendary Tokyo Ramen Jiro empire — for several years it stood as the only direct Jiro branch in all of western Japan (until a Kyushu branch later opened). The proprietor trained at the Jimbocho (神保町) Jiro in Tokyo before bringing the style to Ichijoji, a neighborhood already famous as one of Kyoto's great ramen streets.
chef_bio.philosophy: Pure, unapologetic Jiro: enormous portions, bold pork-fat richness, and an efficient eat-and-go counter rhythm. The shop keeps the old-school 二郎 atmosphere intact rather than softening it for a tourist crowd.
chef_bio.specialty: The classic Jiro bowl: a mountain of thick, flat, chewy hand-cut wheat noodles under a tangle of boiled bean sprouts and cabbage, slabs of thick-cut chashu pork, and a rich pork-and-back-fat soy broth. Diners customize at the counter with the Jiro 'call' — ninniku (garlic), yasai (extra veg), abura (back fat), karame (extra tare) — and finish with counter condiments like coarse pepper and chili.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor and assistant came up through the Jimbocho Jiro in Tokyo and are noted Hanshin Tigers fans.

Long described as Kansai's only direct-lineage Jiro; a blogger revisiting in 2025 noted a new Kyushu branch had since ended its status as the sole branch in western Japan, and that the shop keeps its traditional, masculine old-school Jiro vibe with 13 seats along an L-shaped counter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyototravel.info/jirou confirms opening April 2, 2017 as a branch of Ramen Jiro Sanada honten (Minato-ku, Tokyo) in Ichijoji, counter-only. Official ramen-jiro-pc3.com confirms the store-owner/assistant are Jimbocho-branch alumni and Hanshin fans. escapismo (2025) confirms 'Kansai's only chokkei Jiro' status ending after a Kyushu branch, 13-seat L-shaped counter, 280g noodles, ticket system, old-school atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All bowls use wheat noodles boiled in a shared pot with a wheat-based soy tare; there is no gluten-free preparation or separation, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a soy-sauce base typical of Jiro-style ramen, which uses wheat-containing soy sauce; soy sauce is also offered as a counter condiment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork and back-fat stock and bowls include chashu pork; even the vegetable topping (boiled bean sprouts and cabbage) is served in pork broth, so no item is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, fast-moving counter shop with Jiro's distinctive ordering ritual (the "call" for garlic/vegetables/oil/sauce) and frequent queues — comfortable if you know the system or watch others, but there is little English and the pace expects you to order, eat, and leave promptly.

noodles.notes: Trademark Jiro-style flat, thick, low-hydration noodles — almost udon-like in heft. Boiled to a soft, springy 'mochi-mochi' chew rather than firm, so they droop slightly and drink up the fatty broth. Standard portion runs lighter than many Jiro shops (often cited around 280-300g).

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (small/futsu) ordered with the free 'mashi' call — niku-mashi for extra pork, plus yasai (sprouts), ninniku (garlic) and abura (back-fat) at the counter. The half-size or futsu is plenty for most; mashi the toppings, not the noodles, your first time.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork (buta) simmered in the broth — the prized 'kami-buta': one chunk lean and meaty, another meltingly fatty and jiggly. Soft, fall-apart, deeply seasoned by the soup rather than a thin sliced loin.
notable_toppings: Kujo negi (Kyoto green onion, paid add-on)

gf.note: Thick wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a pork-and-shoyu (wheat) broth and tare. No gluten-free option exists; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-boiled pork-bone broth loaded with back-fat, pork chunks and a shoyu tare. No plant-based broth is offered; not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

ramen.pantheon: Kansai's lone direct-lineage Ramen Jiro — the unmistakable Tokyo-born 'Jiro-kei' creed of a thick, fatty, emulsified pork broth, mountains of garlicky sprouts and back-fat, and slabs of melting pork over heavy udon-like noodles. The Kyoto shop pours a notably mellow, richly emulsified soup whose deeper shoyu edge has earned it the nickname 'Kyoto Black,' a heady, garlic-charged bowl unlike anything else in the region.
sources: https://mwanderer.com/ラーメン二郎京都店に行ってきた感想/

places.name: 油そば専門店 ムジコ (Abura Soba Senmonten Mujiko)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free. Abura soba is a brothless wheat-noodle dish: chewy medium-thick wheat noodles tossed with a soy-sauce-based tare (special 醤油ダレ) plus flavored oils. The noodles are wheat and the tare is soy-sauce based (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle or tamari option is mentioned anywhere. Diners with celiac disease should avoid this shop.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The abura soba is built on pork and seafood (fish/niboshi dried-sardine) flavor bases plus a soy tare, and the menu leans into animal components (e.g., a fish-stock niboshi version, and a popular karaage side). No vegan or vegetarian bowl is advertised. A vegan diner could not be accommodated with the standard menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun, holidays, and days before holidays 11:30–15:30 (food last order 15:00) / 18:00–22:30 (food last order 22:00); closed Mon. (Note: Tabelog also lists closed Thu with dinner last order 21:30, so confirming before visiting is recommended.)
places.neighborhood: Nijō, Juraku (Kamigyō-ku; Senbon-dōri Takeyamachi; ~7 min walk from Nijō Stn on the JR and Tōzai subway lines)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ムジコ (Mujiko) is one of the very few abura soba specialists in Kyoto — a city far better known for its kotteri ramen than for brothless oil noodles — which makes it something of a local curiosity. It sits in the quiet Senbon-Takeyamachi/Shuraku neighborhood of Kamigyo, a short wal

chef_bio.background: ムジコ (Mujiko) is one of the very few abura soba specialists in Kyoto — a city far better known for its kotteri ramen than for brothless oil noodles — which makes it something of a local curiosity. It sits on Senbon-dori in the Kamigyo ward, a short walk from Nijo Station, as a small counter-style shop (it shares its building with a spice-curry spot).
chef_bio.philosophy: A focused, do-one-thing-well abura soba bar: a tight menu of four variations on the same idea, customizable at the table, with portion size left up to the diner at no extra charge.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the standard 油そば (abura soba): chewy medium-thick wheat noodles tossed at the bottom of the bowl with a special soy-sauce-based tare and flavored oil, no soup. You mix it vigorously, then dial in the flavor yourself with rice vinegar, chili oil and grated garlic. Portion sizes from small to large are all the same price.

anecdotes.text: The lineup runs four ways on the same theme — the classic abura soba, a fish-stock niboshi (dried-sardine) version, a ginger oil soba, and a numbing-spicy bowl built on the shop's house-made chili oil (痺辛).

One reviewer noted it was their first abura soba in over twenty years and found ムジコ's far more delicious than the version they remembered, the chewy medium-thick noodles clinging perfectly to the special soy tare and oil.
Beyond the noodles, the shop's intensely seasoned karaage (fried chicken) is a favorite side and pairs well with a beer.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: dosue.net confirms: rare Kyoto abura soba specialist on Senbon-dori near Nijo Station (Asada Building, Kamigyo), four variations (standard, niboshi, ginger oil, spicy with house chili oil), medium-thick chewy noodles with soy-based tare and oil, uniform +0-yen pricing across small–large, karaage side, and a reviewer's first abura soba in over 20 years being far tastier than remembered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The abura soba is dressed with a special soy-sauce-based tare (特製醤油ダレ); Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat, and no wheat-free/tamari alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Flavor bases include pork and seafood (fish/niboshi dried-sardine) broths and oils, so the noodles are not vegan even before the soy tare.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood shop run in Japanese with no reservations and cash/QR payment only; a little Japanese or a translation app and quiet patience at the counter will make ordering smooth.

noodles.notes: Chewy, hot medium-thick wavy noodles with a springy, firm bite (mochi-mochi/shiko-shiko) that grip the soy tare and oil pooled at the bowl's bottom. Reportedly supplied by a Kyoto noodle maker (Teigaku/Menya Teigaku). Free portion upsize from small through large (up to ~300g).

ramen.signature_bowl: Teiban Abura Soba (the standard brothless soy-tare abura soba) — pour in the vinegar and chili oil, mix vigorously, and finish with the fridge-cold fresh garlic. Also worth a look: the gyokai-niboshi (seafood and dried-sardine) version and the house chili-oil shibi-kara spicy bowl.
ramen.chashu: Two slices of braised pork chashu served atop the noodles.

gf.note: Abura soba is built on wheat-and-kansui noodles tossed in a soy-sauce (wheat-based) tare — no rice or konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The tare is a lard-enriched soy sauce and the bowl is dressed with pork chashu; a seafood-dashi/niboshi variant also uses fish. No documented plant-only broth or oil. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: One of Kyoto's rare abura soba specialists, Mujiko serves the brothless Tokyo school of the dish: no soup at all, just hot springy noodles dredged through a pool of soy tare and lard at the bowl's base, then woken up with vinegar, chili oil and raw garlic. It is the soul of a ramen distilled to its seasoning and fat — the lineage you taste is the dry-noodle counter culture of Tokyo's student districts, transplanted into a Kyoto backstreet.

places.name: 本家 第一旭 本店 (Honke Daiichi Asahi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature 中華そば is wheat ramen noodles (medium-thick blended-flour noodles aged dry, by Kondo Seimen) in a pork-bone broth seasoned with a wheat-based Kyoto soy sauce tare (生醤油 from Fushimi / Goko Shoyu). Noodles, tare, and gyoza all contain wheat; there is no gluten-free option and no indication of allergen accommodation. Avoid if you require strict gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire menu is built around a pork-bone (豚骨) broth, char siu (roast pork), and pork-fat finish, with soy-sauce tare; toppings include kujo negi, bean sprouts, menma and pork. No vegetable/plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: 6:00–01:00 (next day) (6:00 AM – 1:00 AM); closed Thu
places.neighborhood: Shimogyō-ku, east side of Kyoto Station (Shichijō / Takabashi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born in 1947 near Kyoto Station at the spot locals call "Takabashi," this is the original shop of the celebrated Daiichi Asahi lineage of Kyoto ramen. It started life as a humble eatery (Asahi Shokudo) and grew into a dedicated ramen specialist by the mid-1950s, opening before da

chef_bio.background: Born in 1947 near Kyoto Station at the spot locals call "Takabashi," this is the original shop of the celebrated Daiichi Asahi lineage of Kyoto ramen. It started life as a humble eatery (Asahi Shokudo / 旭食堂) and was renamed 第一旭 in 1956 as it became a dedicated ramen specialist, opening before dawn to feed early-morning workers and travelers near the station — a habit that survives today in its famous 6 a.m. opening.
chef_bio.philosophy: A long-protected, continually refined old-school taste ("守り続け、磨き続けてきた老舗の味") built on careful sourcing — Kyoto-grown Kujo negi and mung-bean sprouts, raw soy sauce from a long-established Kyoto (Fushimi) maker, and char siu from a single specific grade of domestic pork chosen for its lean, clean balance of fat and meat so the soup stays gentle on the palate.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 特製ラーメン (chuka soba): a clear yet deeply savory pork-bone broth seasoned with raw Kyoto soy sauce, piled with thinly sliced char siu, a generous tangle of Kujo green onions, and bean sprouts, over medium-thick dried-aged wheat noodles.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1947 as 旭食堂 at the Takabashi site by Kyoto Station; the shop took the 第一旭 name in 1956 and now draws on the order of 240,000 visitors a year, with lines forming from early morning.
The char siu uses only domestic sows that have farrowed twice (a grade the shop calls 中大貫, around 120 kg), prized for low fat content so no excess grease bleeds into the clear soy broth.

Broth is drawn by simmering pork bones briefly to pull a clear soup of pure umami; noodles are blended-flour, dry-aged medium-thick strands from Kondo Seimen, and the tare uses raw soy sauce from Goko Shoyu, a historic Kyoto maker.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: prtimes confirms 1947 founding and ~240,000 visitors/yr; osumituki confirms it opened as 旭食堂 in 1947 and was renamed 第一旭 in 1956. leafkyoto confirms clear pork-bone broth, Kondo Seimen dry-aged medium-thick noodles, Goko Shoyu (五光醤油) tare, Kujo negi, mung-bean sprouts and 6:00 opening. The official こだわり page (honke-daiichiasahi.info) confirms char siu from twice-farrowed domestic sows (~120kg, the '中大貫' grade) chosen for low fat and a clear soup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles (blended-flour, dried-aged) are the base of every bowl, with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare and gyoza on the menu — a high-gluten kitchen with no gluten-free provision.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is made with raw Kyoto soy sauce (生醤油, Fushimi / Goko Shoyu), which is wheat-fermented; it flavors the broth of the standard bowls.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are pork-bone broth with char siu and pork fat; no plant-based broth is prepared, so vegan separation is not possible.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, no-frills local ramen institution with frequent queues and communal seating; a few basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the simple menu will carry you through comfortably.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker (Kondo Seimen). Firmness is customizable to taste at no charge.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka-soba / Ramen — pork-bone shoyu broth piled with sliced chashu, a mountain of Kyoto kujo-style green onion, and bean sprouts, with raw grated garlic to stir in at the table. The Tokusei (special) or Chashu-men adds a generous extra load of pork.
ramen.chashu: Braised sliced pork chashu, layered generously across the bowl as the headline element of the classic Chashu-men.
notable_toppings: raw grated garlic on the side to add to taste

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a Fushimi soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is a long-simmered pork-bone broth with pork-fat sheen, soy tare and pork chashu — no plant-based broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is the wellspring of Kyoto's Takabashi-style ramen: a clear, golden pork-bone broth seasoned with unsweetened Fushimi soy sauce, deep with pork umami yet finishing light and clean rather than heavy. Crowned with a heap of fragrant Kyoto green onion and stirred with raw garlic, it tastes like the 1947 birthplace of a whole regional lineage — rich body, gentle aftertaste, eaten from dawn.

places.name: 中華そば ますたに 北白川本店 (Chuka Soba Masutani Kitashirakawa Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The core product is wheat ramen noodles in a chicken-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth topped with pork back fat; the soy-based tare is virtually certain to contain wheat. There is no evidence of gluten-free noodles, a dedicated preparation area, or any allergen accommodation. This is not a safe choice for celiac diners — treat as off-limits.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The single signature bowl is built on a chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) broth with pork back fat (背脂) and chashu (roast pork) toppings. The menu is essentially one ramen with no plant-based version, and no vegan broth or substitutions are offered.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 10:00–15:00, 16:00–19:00 (last order before closing; some sources list 10:00–14:45 and 16:00–17:45, so the time slots may vary)
places.neighborhood: Sakyō-ku, Kitashirakawa (Ginkakuji-michi / along Imadegawa-dōri, near Mototanaka Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Masutani's Kitashirakawa flagship has stood near the Ginkakuji-michi area of Kyoto's Sakyo ward since the late 1940s, growing from a humble start into a shop with more than seventy years of history. It is widely regarded as the source of Kyoto's signature back-fat soy-sauce ramen

roles: founding/originating shop of Kyoto back-fat soy-sauce ramen
chef_bio.background: Masutani's Kitashirakawa flagship has stood in Kyoto's Sakyo ward since the late 1940s, growing from a humble start into a shop with more than seventy years of history. It is widely regarded as the source of Kyoto's signature back-fat soy-sauce ramen — the style locals call 背脂チャッチャ, where rich pork back fat is showered over the bowl. Generations of Kyoto students, workers, and visitors have queued at its tiny counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than chase trends, the shop keeps its decades-old recipe unchanged — patrons return precisely for that time-honored, lived-in flavor (食べなれたスープ). One bowl, done the same way, year after year.
chef_bio.specialty: A single, focused bowl: a chicken-bone broth married with soy-sauce tare and finished with a generous fall of pork back fat (背脂), served over fine, straight noodles with chashu, menma, and Kujo green onions. The fat gives body and sweetness over a clear, familiar shoyu base.

anecdotes.text: Regarded as the originator of Kyoto's back-fat soy-sauce ramen style (背脂チャッチャ発祥), the shop has carried more than 70 years of history at its Kitashirakawa home.
Because the noodles are on the soft side, regulars recommend ordering them firm ('麺カタ'); the broth carries a momentary sharp poultry aroma that fades as you eat.

The historic Kitashirakawa flagship is set to go on indefinite hiatus from July 19, 2026, largely due to aging of the building (compounded by rising material and utility costs), with the owner expressing hope to reopen someday; the brand continues at its Umekoji shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (4Q4Y2) confirms Masutani Kitashirakawa as the originator of Kyoto's 背脂チャッチャ back-fat soy ramen, 70+ years of history, late-1940s founding, chicken-bone broth with soy tare and back fat, thin straight noodles, chashu/menma/Kujo negi. kyotopi (kyugyo) confirms the July 19, 2026 indefinite hiatus due to building deterioration plus cost pressures, consolidation at the Umekoji branch, and the owner's wish to someday reopen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a chicken-bone soup combined with soy sauce (鶏ガラでとったスープに醤油を合わせた), and the soy-sauce tare in this style is virtually always wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature bowl uses chicken-bone broth with pork back fat and chashu, with no plant-based version offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English — a few words of Japanese and pointing at the bowl you want will carry you through smoothly.

noodles.notes: Old-school thin, straight, low-hydration noodles cooked on the soft side, designed to drink down easily with the lighter-than-it-looks broth. Diners can request firmer noodles (麺固め) at order.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the single house bowl: chicken-bone shoyu broth blanketed with sweet pork back-fat and a generous heap of Kujo negi. Order the large (大) with extra negi; a pinch of the chili at the bottom shifts the flavor mid-bowl.
ramen.chashu: Traditional pork loin simmered until meltingly tender (horo-horo), sliced into rustic, irregular pieces.
notable_toppings: red chili / ichimi at the bottom of the bowl

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is chicken-bone broth crowned with pork back-fat, with pork-loin chashu; no plant-broth version exists.

ramen.pantheon: This is the wellspring of Kyoto se-abura shoyu ramen — the 1948 original that taught the city to ladle sweet pork back-fat over a chicken-bone soy soup. You taste old Kyoto in it: a deep, faintly animal chicken stock that drinks far lighter than its glistening, fat-capped surface and mountain of Kujo negi suggest.

places.name: らぁ麺 とうひち (Ra-men Touhichi)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles served in chicken broth with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare. The signature bowls are built on a raw-pressed soy sauce (nama-age shoyu) chicken-clear broth, and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat. With wheat noodles plus a wheat-based tare, there is no gluten-free option here and the kitchen is not set up for celiac-safe service. No gluten-free claims were found on any page; treat as not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every signature bowl is built on a chicken broth (tori-seitan clear soup or tori-paitan), finished with chicken oil, and topped with pork chashu. There is no vegan or vegetable-broth ramen on the menu, and no vegan options were found on any page.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sun 11:00-14:30, 18:00-21:00; Tuesday closed. Ticket-vending-machine order; lunch and dinner service may end early when sold out.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop's name とうひち is written 藤七 and read 'touhichi' in the Kyoto dialect. The owner trained at the renowned Kyoto ramen house Senmarushakuriki before opening his own counter in a quiet residential pocket near Kinkaku-ji (Kita Ward) in March 2015. In April 2022 he relocated the

chef_bio.background: The shop's name とうひち is written 藤七 and read 'touhichi' in the Kyoto dialect. After leaving a corporate career in his late 30s, the owner (Sodeoka) trained for about four years at the renowned Kyoto ramen house 京都千丸しゃかりき (Kyoto Senmaru Shakariki) before opening his own counter in a quiet residential pocket of Kita Ward in March 2015. In April 2022 he relocated the shop to its current spot a short walk from Shugakuin Station, into premises that had previously housed his sister shop Suguru.
chef_bio.philosophy: A restrained, transparency-first aesthetic: a simple-looking but deeply layered broth meant to slide cleanly across the palate, with the raw-pressed-soy-sauce chicken-clear style being the direction the owner settled on during his training years.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the tori-shoyu raa-men: a crystal-clear chicken broth (tori-seitan) seasoned with raw-pressed nama-age soy sauce and gilded with fragrant chicken oil, topped with two kinds of chashu, thick menma and chopped green onion. The repertoire also runs to a richer tori-paitan, a niboshi (sardine) seafood bowl, and tsuke-soba dipping noodles served with a kombu-steeped water bath for the noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 藤七 is read 'touhichi' in Kyoto dialect, a local twist that doubles as the shop's identity; the owner trained about four years at 京都千丸しゃかりき before opening in March 2015.
Tsuke-soba is served with a kombu-dashi water bath that the noodles soak in, a refinement on the standard dipping-noodle format.
After the April 2022 move to Shugakuin, the shop took over the premises of its own sister shop, Suguru.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: krkjapan (Vol.37) and corroborating tabemin/osumituki confirm: name 藤七 read 'touhichi' in Kyoto dialect; owner Sodeoka (袖岡) left a corporate job in his late 30s, trained ~4 years at 京都千丸しゃかりき, and opened in a Kita-ku residential area in March 2015; the shop won a ramen-guide grand prize the following year. kyotopi (z9aGR) confirms the April 2022 relocation to Shugakuin into the former Suguru (sister shop) space, tori-shoyu clear broth with chicken oil, dual chashu, and a five-item menu (tori-shoyu ramen/tsukemen, tori-abura mazesoba, tori-paitan, niboshi). Bib Gourmand / Tabelog-100 listed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls are seasoned with raw-pressed (nama-age) soy sauce, which is normally wheat-based; combined with wheat ramen noodles this makes the dishes unsuitable for gluten-free diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based (tori-seitan clear soup or tori-paitan) and bowls are topped with pork chashu, so the kitchen works with animal products throughout; no vegan preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, popular neighborhood ramen counter with a ticket vending machine and no reservations; a few words of Japanese and patience for a possible queue make the visit smooth.

noodles.notes: In-house handmade straight noodles blending domestic wheat with stone-ground, whole-grain, and roasted-wheat-germ flours (no additives). Smooth, glossy mouthfeel with a springy bite and pronounced wheat aroma that stands up to the clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏醤油らぁ麺 (tori-shoyu / chicken shoyu ramen) — a crystal-clear chicken consommé built only from premium jidori (black chicken / Hinai-dori) and water, seasoned with a layered blend of fresh-pressed raw soy sauces and finished with fragrant golden chicken oil. Add the ajitama for the special bowl.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds: a low-temperature pork loin, lean and chewy with fat trimmed so it doesn't muddy the clear soy broth; and a thick, moist, gently-seasoned chicken breast chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-based handmade noodles with kansui and a soy-sauce (wheat) tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken stock with chicken oil, plus pork and chicken chashu and a soy tare; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto refinement of the clear chicken-shoyu lineage: the soul lives in a long-simmered, days-aged jidori consommé so clean it drinks like a crystal-clear broth, lifted by a multi-soy-sauce tare and bright chicken oil. It tastes less like old Kyoto se-abura and more like the precise, ingredient-forward tori-paitan-and-chintan craft that has redefined Japan's top contemporary shoyu bowls.

places.name: らーめん セアブラノ神 壬生本店 (Ramen Seabura no Kami Mibu Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The core bowls are built on wheat noodles (chukamen from Kyoto's Mennya Teigaku / 棣鄂) and a soy-sauce-based tare, served with a chicken-and-pork-bone broth. Standard ramen, mazesoba and tsukemen are all wheat-based, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu or preparation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house style is animal-forward: an ~8-hour clear (chintan) broth made from chicken carcass, whole chicken and pork knuckle bones, finished with pork back fat and small dried sardines (niboshi). Toppings center on chashu pork and egg. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku, Mibu (~6–7 min walk from Hankyū Ōmiya Stn / Shijō-Ōmiya)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Seabura no Kami began almost by accident. After about three months working as a black-suited host in Gion, the founder found his afternoons free and decided to run a ramen shop again — but only at lunch to start. He chose Niigata's Tsubame-Sanjo style as the signature precisely b

chef_bio.origin: 京都 / Kyoto (Tsubame-Sanjo style of Niigata, reinterpreted for Kyoto palates)
chef_bio.background: Seabura no Kami began almost by accident. After about three months working as a black-suited host in Gion, the founder found his afternoons free and decided to run a ramen shop again — but only at lunch to start. He chose Niigata's Tsubame-Sanjo style as the signature precisely because nothing like it existed in Kyoto at the time, and the city had no real niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen culture either. A tasting trip to Hakkaisan in Takatsuki sparked the idea ('this is interesting'), and after consulting the noodle maker Teigaku (棣鄂) and the owner of Ken Ramen's Yamauchi shop, he committed to the concept.
chef_bio.philosophy: Indulgent but not heavy: refuse to merely imitate Niigata's Tsubame ramen and instead build a distinct identity by obsessively refining the back fat itself, aiming for an approachable bowl where the niboshi is present but restrained.
chef_bio.specialty: 背脂煮干そば (seabura niboshi soba) — back-fat dried-sardine ramen. The kitchen takes back fat, which most shops use only as a finishing touch to add aroma and keep soup hot, and 'elevates it to the starring role.' Hand-picked domestic back fat gives an aromatic, surprisingly clean richness over an ~8-hour clear broth of chicken carcass, whole chicken and pork knuckle bones, layered with four kinds of small sardines and finished with rare chashu and a seasoned egg. Diners choose medium or thick wheat noodles from Mennya Teigaku.

anecdotes.text: The owner reframes back fat from a supporting ingredient into the main attraction: 'Back fat is supposed to be an auxiliary ingredient that adds flavor to the soup and keeps it from cooling — this is ramen that sublimates that back fat into the star.'

The decision to build the shop around Tsubame-Sanjo niboshi ramen came from a tasting trip to Hakkaisan in Takatsuki — 'this is interesting' — followed by consulting noodle maker Teigaku and the Yamauchi owner of Ken Ramen before committing.
The Mibu flagship has been supported since opening by proprietor Ido, described as a mother of two who has kept the shop running while raising her children.
From a single lunch-only counter, Seabura no Kami grew into a brand with multiple shops across Kyoto, credited as a pioneer that brought Tsubame-style niboshi ramen to the city.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: krkjapan (Vol.22) confirms the Gion-host origin (~3 months), lunch-only start, choice of Tsubame-Sanjo niboshi style because nothing like it existed in Kyoto, the Hakkaisan/Takatsuki tasting-trip spark, consulting 麺屋棣鄂 (Teigaku) and 拳ラーメン's Yamauchi, the 'back fat as the star' quote, and the ~8-hour broth (chicken carcass, whole chicken, pork knuckle, four kinds of sardines) with rare chashu, egg and Teigaku noodles. rocketnews24 confirms the Tsubame style and multi-shop growth; kyotokurasu (KBS) confirms proprietor Ido, a mother of two, has run the Mibu flagship since opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls use wheat chukamen and a soy-sauce-based tare in a shared ramen kitchen; no gluten-free handling is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature is described as a combination of dried sardine, pork back fat and soy-sauce soup, indicating a standard wheat-containing shoyu base.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is an ~8-hour chicken-and-pork-bone clear stock finished with pork back fat and dried sardines, so all bowls share animal-based components.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy local ramen counter where ordering in Japanese (or by pointing at the ticket/menu) is easiest; staff are accustomed to a brisk lunch and dinner crowd rather than extended English exchanges.

noodles.notes: Specialty thick noodles from an outside maker (Tieou/Teigaku), close to udon diameter; built to stand up to the niboshi-shoyu soup and grab the back fat. A medium-thick option is also offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Seabura Niboshi Soba (special back-fat niboshi soba) with thick noodles
ramen.chashu: Braised pork shoulder-loin (kata-rosu), low-fat and clean, served in a neat cross-section slice

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core soup is built on chicken/pork bones plus dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi and finished with pork back fat and pork chashu; no plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's pioneering take on Niigata's Tsubame-Sanjo seabura-niboshi tradition: a dual soup of long-simmered chicken-and-pork chintan married to a cold-water niboshi extraction, crowned with chunky domestic pork back fat and a heap of minced raw onion. Despite the imposing "god of back fat" name, the bowl drinks surprisingly clean and umami-forward rather than heavy, with sardine depth carrying the soy tare.

places.name: 麺屋 龍玄 (Menya Ryugen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house bowl is a classic chuka-soba built on wheat ramen noodles in a dark soy-sauce tare over a pork-and-chicken broth, in the Shinpuku Saikan tradition. The deep-color tare is soy-sauce based and almost certainly contains wheat, and the signature yakimeshi (fried rice) is also seasoned with soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tamari tare, or celiac accommodation. A celiac should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a meat-forward chuka-soba shop: the broth is a pork-bone and chicken base, the bowls are topped with pork-loin chashu, and the famous yakimeshi is cooked with pork and egg-style seasoning. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu. A plant-based diner has nothing suitable here.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00, 17:00-21:00 (some listings give a continuous 11:30-21:00 with last order around 20:40); closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Ichijoji (Sakyo-ku), a short walk from Eizan Railway Ichijoj
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Ryugen opened in July 2016 on Ichijoji's quieter back lane, just off the neighborhood's famous ramen street, in the space once occupied by another ramen shop that had closed the year before. The owner is a Shinpuku Saikan man through and through: he started training at that

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, in the Shinpuku Saikan lineage
chef_bio.background: Menya Ryugen opened in July 2016 on Ichijoji's quieter back lane, just off the neighborhood's famous ramen street, in the space once occupied by another ramen shop (Menya Kagari) that had closed the year before. The owner is a Shinpuku Saikan man through and through: he started training at that legendary old Kyoto ramen house at age 19 and brings roughly three decades of chuka-soba experience to his own counter. When he opened, Shinpuku Saikan reportedly sent congratulatory flowers, a quiet nod from the parent house to one of its own.
chef_bio.philosophy: Straightforward, traditional, unpretentious ramen that honors the Shinpuku Saikan template rather than chasing trends -- a bowl that, as one writer put it, tastes like the ramen of twenty years ago, in the best way.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a chuka-soba with the unmistakable Shinpuku-style jet-black broth (漆黒のブラックスープ) -- looks intensely dark from the soy but drinks cleaner and lighter than it appears, with pork-and-chicken depth, thin noodles, tender pork-loin chashu, bean sprouts and chopped scallion. The other half of the order is the yakimeshi (fried rice), seasoned dark and aromatic with soy; many regulars come as much for the rice as the ramen, and the ramen-and-yakimeshi set is the classic combination.

anecdotes.text: The owner began his training at Shinpuku Saikan at age 19 and has around 30 years of chuka-soba experience behind the counter.
Shinpuku Saikan, the parent house (along with noodle maker Kondo Seimen), sent congratulatory flowers when Ryugen opened, and the bowl closely mirrors Shinpuku's true-black soy broth.

The shop took over a small Ichijoji space (former ramen shop Menya Kagari, closed 2015) just off the main ramen-street battleground, and despite the area's high turnover it has steadily grown popular and fills up at lunch.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki (73542) confirms: opened July 21, 2016 in the former Menya Kagari space (closed 2015); owner trained at Shinpuku Saikan from age 19 with ~30 years of chuka-soba experience; Shinpuku Saikan and Kondo Seimen sent congratulatory flowers; jet-black soy broth closely resembling Shinpuku's, firm thin noodles, fragrant pork-loin chashu, and a yakimeshi set (many order the fried rice on its own). gcjapan confirms the off-the-main-street Ichijoji location and lunchtime popularity.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining feature of the bowl is a deep, jet-black soy-sauce broth/tare in the Shinpuku Saikan style; soy-sauce tare of this type is standard wheat-based shoyu, so the ramen should be treated as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-bone and chicken base with pork-loin chashu, and the yakimeshi is cooked with pork; there are no plant-based items, so cross-contact with animal products is total.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills neighborhood counter shop where ordering in Japanese is expected; point-and-order works but expect little to no English.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles that drink up the dark soy broth; firm bite that suits the jet-black Kyoto chuka soba.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba — the jet-black chicken-and-pork-bone soy sauce ramen, topped with pork loin chashu, bean sprouts, and chopped green onion (pairs famously with the shop's yakimeshi fried rice).
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin (rosu) chashu — lean sliced loin rather than fatty belly, in keeping with the old-school Kyoto chuka-soba tradition.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce tare (wheat-based); no rice or konjac noodle or gluten-free tare is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-and-pork-bone soy broth with pork loin chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A direct heir to Kyoto's legendary Shinpuku Saikan school, this is the city's signature jet-black chuka soba — a soy-dark broth where chicken bones and pork bone meld so the color reads fierce but the soy aroma stays measured, deep and savory rather than salty. You taste old Kyoto in every sip: an everyday, never-tiring bowl built on a lineage that defined the local style.

places.name: 汁なし担々麺＆麻婆豆腐 ラアノウミ (Shirunashi Tantanmen & Mabodofu Raanoumi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat noodles dressed in a sesame-and-chili-oil tantan sauce, and the menu centers on tantanmen and mabo-men (mapo-style noodles) — all wheat-based. The spicy sauces and mapo preparations typically rely on wheat-containing seasonings (soy sauce, doubanjiang). No gluten-free options or allergen accommodations are advertised. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a tantanmen and mapo tofu specialist; the signature bowls use sesame/chili-oil sauce with minced pork, and the mapo tofu is meat-based. No vegan or vegetarian menu items are advertised. A vegan diner could not reliably eat the signature dishes here.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (last order 15:15 weekdays / 15:30 weekends & holidays); Dinner 17:00–21:30 (last order 21:00). Official site lists irregular closures; Tabelog notes closed only for year-end/New Year holidays.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent noodle shop tucked into the basement of the Zuisho Building near Doshisha University's Shinmachi campus, just a short walk from Imadegawa Station. It bills itself as the only specialty shop in Kyoto City devoted to shiru-nashi tantanmen (brothless dan dan nood

chef_bio.background: A small independent noodle shop tucked into the basement of the Zuisho Building near Doshisha University's Shinmachi campus, just a short walk from Imadegawa Station. It bills itself as the only specialty shop in Kyoto City devoted to shiru-nashi tantanmen (brothless dan dan noodles), and draws a steady student crowd at lunch.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes handmade chili oil and its own custom spice blend, and a commitment to locally sourced (地産地消) Kyoto ingredients, aiming for a balance where the umami of the ingredients is still felt amid the spice.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the shiru-nashi tantanmen: wheat noodles tossed in a house-blended sauce of carefully made chili oil and several spices, finished with the aromatic fragrance of sesame so that umami comes through within the heat. The kitchen also makes a mapo-style noodle (汁なし麻婆麺) and a standalone mapo tofu, both with adjustable spice levels, plus rice that pairs with the spicy sauces.

anecdotes.text: Won the runner-up grand prize (準グランプリ) at the 'Ultimate Ramen Pia Kansai Edition' competition.
Promotes itself as Kyoto City's only shiru-nashi tantanmen specialty shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official ranoumi-ramen.jp confirms it is '京都市内唯一の汁なし担々麺専門店' (Kyoto City's only shiru-nashi tantanmen specialist), a runner-up (準グランプリ) at the Ultimate Ramen Pia Kansai competition, with handmade chili oil, a proprietary multi-spice blend, and 地産地消 local sourcing, plus mapo tofu. Tabelog confirms the location in the basement of the Zuisho Building (Kamitattsuri-cho, Kamigyo), 7 min from Imadegawa Station, 19 seats, opened Oct 29, 2020, specializing in oil-less tantanmen and mapo tofu.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, student-friendly counter-and-table noodle shop near Doshisha; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, though staff communication is in Japanese.

places.name: ラーメン池田屋 京都一乗寺店 (Ramen Ikedaya Kyoto Ichijoji-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for gluten-free or celiac diners. This is a Jiro-inspired ramen shop built around thick, chewy wheat noodles (the shop uses Nisshin Seifun wheat flour), a pork broth finished with a soy-based (wheat-containing) tare, and pork chashu. No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen accommodation is advertised, and the kitchen is a single small counter operation with shared cooking water. Treat as off-limits for celiac needs.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The bowls are based on a rich emulsified pork (tonkotsu/pork-fat) broth with pork back-fat (abura), pork chashu, and a soy-based tare; vegetables like beansprouts and cabbage are piled on top but cooked in/with the pork system. No plant-based broth or vegan option is offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun: 11:30-15:00, 18:00-23:00; Thursday closed. (Source variance: some listings give lunch 11:30-14:30 and weekday dinner ending 22:00, weekends to 23:00; occasional irregular closures noted.)
places.neighborhood: Sakyō-ku, Ichijōji, Takano (near Eizan Railway Ichijōji Stn / Ramen Street)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ikedaya began life outside Kyoto, in the Hokuriku region (linked to Nonoichi in Ishikawa and to Fukui), before the owner brought the shop to Kyoto's Ichijoji district in 2011. Ichijoji is one of Japan's most famous ramen battlegrounds, lined with shops along what locals call the

chef_bio.origin: Hokuriku (Ishikawa/Fukui) origins; established in Kyoto Ichijoji in 2011.
chef_bio.background: Ikedaya began life outside Kyoto, in the Hokuriku region (its first shop was in Nonoichi, Ishikawa), before the owner brought the shop to Kyoto's Ichijoji district in 2011. Ichijoji is one of Japan's most famous ramen battlegrounds, lined with shops along what locals call the 'ramen highway,' and Ikedaya quickly became one of its standout names. The shop later expanded with branches in Fukui and in the Osaka/Amagasaki area, but the small Ichijoji counter near Eizan Railway's Ichijoji Station remains its spiritual home.
chef_bio.philosophy: Ikedaya is proudly an 'inspired' shop rather than an official Jiro branch: the owner never trained at the original and instead developed his own interpretation of the style, chasing maximum pork richness and volume on his own terms. It is unpretentious, ticket-machine, counter-only ramen meant to be eaten with focus rather than slurped daintily.
chef_bio.specialty: A bowl of Jiro-style ramen built on a thick, intensely porky broth that the kitchen emulsifies until it turns rich yet surprisingly smooth, ladled over fat, chewy wheat noodles you order by weight (from a 180g 'petit' up to a 500g 'large'). It comes loaded with tender beansprouts and cabbage and soft pork chashu, and at the counter you call out how much garlic, vegetables, and back-fat (abura) you want piled on.

anecdotes.text: Ikedaya is widely cited as one of the leading Jiro-inspired shops in Kyoto despite never being an official Jiro lineage shop, with fans debating whether it rivals the original style; its melting chashu earns the 'kami-buta' (divine pork) nickname.
The shop opened in Kyoto's Ichijoji ramen district in 2011 after starting in Nonoichi, Ishikawa, later expanding to Fukui and the Osaka area.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kencellara confirms Ikedaya's first shop was in Nonoichi (Ishikawa), the move to Kyoto's Ichijoji in 2011, its 'inspired-by' (non-official-Jiro) status, the owner's own style development, later Fukui and Osaka/Amagasaki branches, an emulsified smooth-but-rich porky broth, 180g–500g by-weight noodles, and garlic/veg/back-fat call-outs. kyotopi (NdqU8) confirms it as one of Kyoto's leading Jiro-inspired shops with prized melting chashu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is thick wheat ramen noodles made with Nisshin Seifun wheat flour, served from a small single-counter kitchen with shared boiling water, making gluten cross-contamination effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in standard ramen contains wheat; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is an emulsified pork-bone/pork-fat base with pork back-fat and pork chashu; vegetables are cooked within this pork-based system, so no vegan-safe preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy, cash-only Jiro-style counter where you buy a meal ticket from a machine and call out garlic/vegetable/fat preferences in Japanese at the counter, so a little Japanese or a phrasebook makes it much smoother.

noodles.notes: Udon-like ultra-thick straight noodles with a firm, chewy bite and a retained core; a distinct wheat-grain aroma. Made in-house. Portions scale from petit (~180-200g) up to dai (500g), with calls to add 100g at a time.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (sho/300g or chu/400g) with the free calls — yasai (piled softened bean sprouts and cabbage), ninniku (coarse raw garlic), abura (back-fat), karame (extra tare). A year-round and summer tsukemen variant is also a fan favorite.
ramen.chashu: Big chunks of soft, flaky braised pork ("horohoro buta") with a reddish hue, plus shredded pieces — both deeply soaked in a sweet-savory braise that falls apart at a touch.
notable_toppings: mountain of bean sprouts (moyashi) and cabbage (yasai)

gf.note: Jiro-style bowl is built on wheat ultra-thick noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare over pork broth — not celiac-safe, and no rice/konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Broth and tare are pork-based with shoyu, finished with pork back-fat and topped with pork chashu. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Kyoto's Jiro-inspired scene on Ichijoji's famed ramen street: a thick, emulsified pork-and-shoyu soup slicked with back-fat, mountained with garlicky vegetables, and built around udon-thick house noodles. The soul here is the creamy, assertive tonkotsu-shoyu — you taste the Tachikawa-Jiro lineage in every viscous, garlic-charged spoonful, tightened by a dusting of chili.

places.name: 麺匠 たか松 本店 (Mensho Takamatsu Honten)
places.gf_detail: The entire concept is built on stone-ground whole-grain wheat noodles, and the chicken-seafood tsukemen broth is finished with a tare that almost certainly contains wheat-based shoyu. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of a dedicated preparation area. This is not a safe choice for anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy. Confirm directly before any visit if cross-contact is a concern.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is an animal-based chicken-and-fish (鶏魚介) stock combining poultry with katsuo, saba and scallop, so the core offering is not vegan and not vegetarian. No plant-based ramen option is advertised. A strict vegan or vegetarian cannot eat the standard bowls here.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sat/Sun/holidays open from 10:30. Tabelog lists last-order based hours as 11:00–22:30 weekdays, 10:30–22:30 weekends.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Takamatsu built its name on tsukemen (dipping noodles) at a time when the style was still uncommon in Kyoto, and the main shop remains a small counter-driven room where the noodles take center stage.

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 2011 in the Shijo-Karasuma district of central Kyoto, this is the flagship (honten) of the Takamatsu noodle house, which has since grown to five shops around the city.
chef_bio.background: Takamatsu built its name on tsukemen (dipping noodles) at a time when the style was still uncommon in Kyoto, and the main shop (on Takoyakushi-dori near Shijo-Karasuma) remains a small counter-driven room where the noodles take center stage.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house motto is 'a single bowl of happiness, every day' (毎日に、一杯の幸せを) — an everyday, repeatable comfort rather than a special-occasion meal.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a tsukemen of thin, roughly 2mm stone-ground whole-grain noodles served with a clean, unthickened chicken-and-seafood dipping broth that blends select chicken with katsuo, saba and scallop. The kitchen leans on whole-grain flour milled from Nagano-grown wheat, giving the noodles a rustic, wheat-forward character closer to country soba than typical glossy tsukemen noodles.

anecdotes.text: Diners are encouraged to shift the flavor mid-meal with a trio of accents — fresh sudachi citrus, a Kyoto black shichimi, and onion — so a single bowl moves from bright and citrusy to deeper and spicier as you eat.
The whole-grain noodles are deliberately thinner than usual tsukemen noodles and show visible rough edges; the shop treats this rustic texture as a point of pride rather than a flaw.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official mensho.net confirms the tsukemen/ramen concept, stone-ground whole-grain noodles, chicken+seafood (katsuo/saba/scallop) broth, motto '毎日に、一杯の幸せを', and multiple shops (honten, Shijo, Kawaramachi, Kyoto Station Ramen Koji, BiVi Nijo). Search corroboration (Tabelog/kyotopi) confirms 2011 founding at Shijo-Karasuma, the honten on Takoyakushi-dori, thin stone-ground whole-grain noodles from Nagano wheat, and five Kyoto shops. genjitsutouhi confirms the ~2mm rustic noodles and sudachi/black-shichimi/onion flavor accents.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles and a wheat-shoyu-based tare are central to every bowl; there is no separate gluten-free preparation and cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable in a small tsukemen kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken-seafood dipping broth is built on a soy-based tare, which in standard Japanese ramen/tsukemen contains wheat unless stated otherwise; no wheat-free shoyu is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use an animal (chicken plus katsuo/saba/scallop) broth; no plant-based stock or vegan option is offered, so there is nothing safe for a strict vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A popular, fast-moving counter tsukemen shop near Shijo-Karasuma; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu is plenty, though staff are used to a steady mix of locals and visitors.

noodles.notes: Self-made noodles from Nagano-grown wheat stone-milled into whole-grain (zenryufun) flour, giving a brown, soba-like cast with a faint grainy bite and a smooth, clean slurp. Thin for a tsukemen, prized for the wheat aroma that comes through when dipped.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (つけ麺) — thin stone-milled whole-wheat noodles served cold beside a thick, hot chicken-and-seafood dipping broth; eaten plain first, then brightened mid-bowl with onion, sudachi, and black shichimi. (The shop also pours a niboshi-forward "煮干し香る らぁ麺" bowl.)
ramen.chashu: Cubed braised pork chashu (gorogoro chunks) submerged in the dipping broth rather than sliced over the noodles.

gf.note: Noodles are stone-milled whole-wheat (wheat + kansui) and the dipping broth is shoyu-tare based, so it contains wheat; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The dipping broth is built on chicken, pork, scallop and assorted seafood dashi, with pork chashu; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tsukemen house whose soul is a dense double-soup of chicken and seafood dashi — pork and scallop deepening the umami — that clings to brown, stone-milled whole-wheat noodles you slurp like fine soba. It belongs to the post-2010 wave of tori-gyokai dipping ramen rather than any regional gotōchi lineage, finished tableside with sudachi and Kyoto's Gen'yokaku black shichimi for a citrus-and-sansho lift.

places.name: 麺屋 優光 (Menya Yukou)
places.gf_detail: The house noodles are made fresh daily from a blend of whole-wheat and udon flour, and the broth uses a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare, which almost always contains wheat. This is a wheat-noodle, wheat-tare ramen and is not safe for celiac diners; no gluten-free option is indicated.
places.vegan_detail: All bowls are built on a shellfish (oyster, clam, shrimp) dashi combined with a soy-based tare, and the signature topping is low-temperature seared rare pork charshiu. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺屋 優光 is a small, independent ramen shop tucked into the Karasuma Oike office district of central Kyoto. Its owner trained at the celebrated Osaka ramen shop 人類みな麺類 (Jinrui Mina Menrui) before opening here. The space deliberately feels more like a bright, stylish cafe than a typi

chef_bio.background: 麺屋 優光 is a small, independent ramen shop tucked into the Karasuma Oike office district of central Kyoto. Its owner trained at the celebrated Osaka ramen shop 人類みな麺類 (Jinrui Mina Menrui) before opening here. The space deliberately feels more like a bright, stylish cafe than a typical ramen counter, with seats at the counter downstairs and tables upstairs, and it welcomes solo diners and a notably high share of women.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself around caring for the customer during the short time they spend eating — making a quick bowl of ramen feel like a considered, hospitable experience.
chef_bio.specialty: A shellfish-based 中華そば (kai-dashi ramen) that is genuinely rare for Kyoto: the soup layers the umami of shellfish (clams and other shellfish) into a soy-based tare for a clean but pronounced, slightly sweet-savory profile. The three regular bowls are named after types of bamboo — 淡竹 (Hachiku), 真竹 (Madake) and 黒竹 (Kurochiku) — with Hachiku the most popular, finished with several thick slices of soft, low-temperature seared rare pork charshiu.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at the famous Osaka ramen shop 人類みな麺類 (Jinrui Mina Menrui) before opening 麺屋 優光.
The noodles are made in-house daily from a blend of whole-wheat flour and udon flour, giving a distinctive aroma and a firm, powerful texture.
A lunch-only 炙りレア焼豚丼 (torched rare pork rice bowl) made with diced charshiu is offered as an add-on (+250 yen).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: takemachelin confirms the owner trained at Osaka's 人類みな麺類, the Karasuma Oike shellfish-broth concept rare for Kyoto, the bamboo-named bowls (Hachiku most popular, Madake, Kurochiku), cafe-like space (counter downstairs, tables upstairs, solo-diner friendly), and a lunch-only torched rare-pork rice bowl (+250 yen). Official yukou-kiyo.com confirms the Karasuma Oike/Nakagyo office-district location, daily in-house noodles blending whole-wheat and udon flour, and the attentive-hospitality philosophy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare, which conventionally contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, cafe-like counter ramen shop in a Kyoto office district; no reservations and cash-only, so a guide or a few words of Japanese helps but it is approachable for solo diners.

noodles.notes: Made fresh daily in the shop's own noodle factory. A proprietary blend of whole-wheat (zenryufun) flour for wheat aroma and udon flour for strong koshi — surprisingly powerful, chewy bite that stands up to the delicate clam broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hachiku (淡竹) — clam-dashi chuka soba in shoyu, topped with three generous slices of seared chashu. Add the torched rare-pork rice bowl with ajitama for great value.
ramen.chashu: Seared (aburi) pork chashu, served as thick slices flame-torched to order; a torched rare roast-pork (rea-cha) is also offered over rice as a side bowl.

gf.note: Noodles are a wheat blend (whole-wheat plus udon flour) and the tare is Kyoto soy sauce — both contain wheat. No documented rice or konjac noodle option; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a shellfish dashi (oyster, littleneck and asari clams) finished with pork chashu — no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original from a chef of the Jinrui Mina Menrui lineage: a clear shoyu chuka soba whose soul is a sweet, rounded shellfish dashi — oyster, hamaguri and asari clams — laced with local Kyoto soy. Clam ramen is a rarity in Kyoto, and here the briny-sweet shellfish umami reads delicate yet deep, a clean counterpoint to the city's heavier se-abura traditions.

places.name: あいつのラーメン かたぐるま 本店 (Aitsu no Ramen Kataguruma Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen and tsukemen specialist. The bowls use wheat-based noodles, the tare is built on pork-bone/salt and soy bases (the dinner menu shifts to soy-pork-bone variations), and broths are long-simmered pork bone with chicken and fish elements. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or dedicated preparation are indicated anywhere. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: A meat- and fish-forward shop: signature broths are pork-bone (豚骨) simmered 10+ hours, with chicken and seafood, and bowls are topped with generous rare chashu (9-11 slices). No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or mentioned.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 10:30–14:15 (last order 14:00) / Dinner 18:00–21:15 (last order 21:00). Irregular days off. Closed over the New Year holidays. Numbered-ticket (via LINE) system.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shichijō, Shimogyō-ku (~10 min walk from JR Tanbaguchi Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained at 俺のラーメンあっぱれ屋 (Ore no Ramen Appareya), the famous ramen shop tucked in the hills of Kyoto, before striking out on his own and opening this shop in the city. He kept the mentor shop's menu DNA — even the dish names nod to Appareya (こくまろ塩 to its こくとん, 濁とろ塩 to its

chef_bio.origin: 下京区西七条、JR丹波口駅から徒歩約10分。2014年5月開業。
chef_bio.background: The owner trained at 俺のラーメンあっぱれ屋 (Ore no Ramen Appareya), the famous ramen shop tucked in the hills of Kyoto, before striking out on his own and opening this shop in the city. He kept the mentor shop's menu DNA — even the dish names nod to Appareya (こくまろ塩 to its こくとん, 濁とろ塩 to its 濁とん, うるとらつけ麺 to its スーパーつけ麺) — while building a distinct identity of his own.
chef_bio.philosophy: A counter-focused, single-diner-friendly shop (おひとり様専門店) that opens unusually early at 10:30 and runs a flavored-oil 'no-leftover' approach so guests can vary the soup's character as they eat — the kind of detail-obsessed, owner-run craft inherited from its Appareya training.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the うるとらつけ麺 (Ultra Tsukemen): a濃厚豚骨, foam-rich dipping broth carrying assertive salt and spice, paired with house-made chewy flat noodles with a smooth finish. The shop is also known for kaiseki-like plating piled with generous rare chashu (often 9-11 slices). By day the lineup leans on salt-based broths; by night it shifts to soy/pork-bone variations.

anecdotes.text: The owner apprenticed at the celebrated mountain ramen-ya 俺のラーメンあっぱれ屋 before opening on his own, and pays homage through parallel dish names while diverging from Appareya's more refined seafood-leaning dip toward a thicker, spicier pork-bone style.
Opened in May 2014 under representative Yasunori Ueshima; styled as an おひとり様専門店 (solo-diner specialty shop) that issues numbered tickets via ticket machine or a store-front LINE QR when full.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト(aitsunoramen.com)とgenjitsutouhi等より、代表・上嶋泰範、2014年5月開業、丹波口駅徒歩約10分、10:30開店、俺のラーメンあっぱれ屋出身、こくまろ塩/濁とろ塩/うるとらつけ麺、おひとり様専門店・LINE整理券制を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, wheat-containing tare (salt and soy/pork-bone bases), and shared broth pots mean gluten is pervasive; no separate gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Evening menu shifts to soy/pork-bone (醤油系) variations; ramen shoyu tare in Japan is standardly wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on pork-bone/chicken/fish broth and topped with chashu; no vegan preparation exists, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, locals-focused single-person-oriented ramen counter that runs a Japanese-language LINE numbered-ticket queue system, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps; staff are used to a fast lunch turnover rather than detailed allergy consultations.

noodles.notes: House-made (jikasei) flat straight noodles, high-hydration, smooth and slippery (tsuru-tsuru) with a chewy, mochi-like bite that catches the thick broth well. Tsukemen portions step up small/medium/large.

ramen.signature_bowl: Daigutton (Kokuton) Ramen — the foam-system rich tonkotsu-and-seafood bowl; or the Ultra Tsukemen, ordered with the rare chashu add. Lunch is served shio, dinner shoyu.
ramen.chashu: Signature low-and-slow rare pork chashu, oven-roasted with salt and pepper and finished with a light char/aburi on the surface; dramatic pink rose-colored centers, thick and meaty (a generous fan of slices in the chashu bowl).

gf.note: House-made wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat shoyu tare at dinner and a pork-bone/seafood broth — no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core broth is whipped pork-bone, chicken, and seafood (gyokai) with pork fat and rare pork chashu throughout. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A foam-system (awa-kei) bowl from a master who trained at Kyoto's revered Appareya: a pork-bone, chicken and seafood broth whipped to a creamy, aerated cloud so the koku hits soft and rich rather than heavy. The lineage shows in the daytime shio's wild, spice-forward punch and the evening shoyu's fish-powder depth, ladled over slippery house-made flat noodles.

places.name: 京つけめん つるかめ 六角 (Kyo Tsukemen Tsurukame Rokkaku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. The house specialty is self-made thick wavy noodles made with whole wheat flour (自家製の全粒粉入り加水麺), served with a seafood-and-pork shoyu dipping broth that uses wheat-based soy sauce tare. Both the noodles and the tare contain wheat; broths are animal/fish based. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or published allergy program were found. A celiac or strict gluten-avoider cannot eat the core dishes here.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. The kitchen is built around fish (dried sardine/bonito) and pork/chicken broths, with chashu pork as a standard topping; the signature is a seafood-pork shoyu tsukemen. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered. A vegan diner has nothing suitable on the menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:30 (food last order 21:00), open daily. Note: some sources list 11:30–22:00 (last order 21:50).
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku, Shinkyōgoku-dōri Rokkaku (Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae Stn / Sanjō–Kawaramachi area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, tucked-away tsukemen specialist on Rokkaku-dori in the Shinkyogoku/Teramachi shopping warren, just a short walk from Shijo-Kawaramachi and Kyoto City Hall-mae Station. The room is famously compact — an L-shaped counter of roughly ten seats and a single table, so tight th

chef_bio.background: A small, tucked-away tsukemen specialist on Rokkaku-dori in the Shinkyogoku/Teramachi shopping warren, just a short walk from Shijo-Kawaramachi and Kyoto City Hall-mae Station. The room is famously compact — an L-shaped counter of roughly ten seats and a single four-top table, so tight that a separate rear door is used to manage the flow of customers — which gives it the feel of a true neighborhood noodle den.
chef_bio.philosophy: Straightforwardly delicious, honest tsukemen — chewy house-made noodles in generous portions with broth that clings to every strand, the kind of bowl regulars return to rather than a one-time novelty.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the gyokai shoyu tsukemen (魚介醤油つけ麺): self-made, thick wavy noodles cut with whole-grain wheat, given free in a large portion, dipped into a concentrated seafood-and-pork shoyu broth balanced between animal umami and dried sardine/bonito notes, finished with tender chashu and a dusting of aonori. The shop also runs ramen, cold mixed noodles, a miso-tantan tsukemen, and rotating limited bowls.

anecdotes.text: The noodles are made in-house from domestic whole-grain wheat for a notably chewy bite, and large portions are offered at no extra charge — with free white rice for orders before 2 p.m., often finished as tamagokake-gohan or charred-chashu rice.
The space is so small it is described as an 極小スペース (ultra-compact space), an intimate counter that gives it an authentic, lived-in ramen-shop atmosphere.
Staff are known for attentively explaining the menu, which makes it approachable even for a first-time tsukemen eater.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・食べログ・Rettyより、六角通(新京極)、L字カウンター約10席＋4人テーブル1の極小スペース、鍵の手の裏口、国産全粒粉の自家製波打ち麺・大盛無料・14時前ライス無料、魚介醤油つけ麺を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth is a shoyu-based seafood/pork tare, which in a Japanese tsukemen shop is made with conventional wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are fish (dried sardine/bonito) and pork/chicken based and chashu pork is a standard topping, so there is no separation from animal products for a plant-based diner.

positives.text: Staff are noted for friendly, attentive menu explanations, so a diner with questions can ask about a dish before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, no-frills counter tsukemen shop where solo diners are welcome and staff will explain the menu, but it is a Japanese-language local spot, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.

noodles.notes: House-made (jikasei) noodles using domestic whole-wheat (zenryufun) flour. High-hydration (takasui), notably mochi-mochi and chewy with real heft and weight on the chopsticks for the dipping bowls; in the soup ramen they read as flatter, medium-thin and silky-smooth. Free oomori (large) at the same price.

ramen.signature_bowl: 魚介醤油つけ麺 (gyokai-shoyu tsukemen) — chewy whole-wheat house noodles served cold with a thick, concentrated chicken-pork-and-seafood dipping broth finished with yuzu.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, two slices, on the firmer side and seasoned to taste of the pork itself rather than a heavy soy glaze.

gf.note: House-made noodles are wheat (whole-wheat) plus kansui and the broth carries a shoyu tare and pork/chicken/fish dashi; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken, pork and seafood (gyokai) dashi with pork-belly chashu; no plant-based kombu/shiitake broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A flagship of Kyoto's own modern tsukemen movement: hefty, chewy whole-wheat house noodles meet a thick, glossy dipping broth where pork and chicken body lead and a wave of seafood dashi follows, lifted at the finish by a fragrant snap of yuzu. The soul here is that gyokai-tonkotsu "W-soup" richness — bold and salty yet clean, an original Kyoto take rather than any inherited regional lineage.

places.name: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ 伏見本店 (Ramen So Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero (Fushimi Ho)
places.gf_detail: This is a Jiro-style ramen shop: thick house-made wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is virtually always wheat-based. Generous garlic and pork chashu round out the bowl. There is no gluten-free noodle option and no indication of any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-forward Jiro-style shop built on pork-bone broth and large amounts of pork chashu/back-fat. Toppings (extra vegetables, garlic, fat, chili) sit on top of an animal broth, so even a "vegetable-heavy" bowl is not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
places.neighborhood: Fushimi-ku, Kyoto (near Kintetsu Momoyama-Goryō-mae Stn / Keihan Fushimi-Momoyama Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2009 as a spinoff of the famous Ichijoji Jiro-inspired shop "夢を語れ" (Yume wo Katare), this Fushimi flagship grew into one of the standard-bearers of the Kansai Jiro-kei (Jiro-style) ramen scene and went on to spawn multiple branches across the region.

chef_bio.origin: Ichijoji, Kyoto (parent shop Yume wo Katare)
chef_bio.background: Opened in 2009 as a spinoff of the famous Ichijoji Jiro-inspired shop "夢を語れ" (Yume wo Katare), this Fushimi flagship grew into one of the standard-bearers of the Kansai Jiro-kei (Jiro-style) ramen scene and went on to spawn multiple branches across the region.
chef_bio.philosophy: Big, generous, made-to-order satisfaction: the kitchen prizes the sharp cut of the soy tare layered over deep umami, and lets each customer dial in the bowl with the classic Jiro call of garlic, vegetables and fat for an "overwhelmingly satisfying" finish.
chef_bio.specialty: A thunderous Jiro-style bowl built on house-made flat, extra-thick noodles (offered 200g/300g/400g) and a non-emulsified pork-bone-and-soy broth, piled with mountains of vegetables, garlic and back-fat. The pork-heavy "豚ラーメン" comes loaded with roughly 200g of chashu.

anecdotes.text: Customers buy a meal ticket and queue; staff ask your topping preferences (vegetables, garlic, fat, chili) just as the noodles are being cooked, in the classic Jiro ordering ritual.
Described as a representative presence of the Kansai Jiro-inspired scene and one of the area's most reliably lined-up shops.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・食べログより、2009年に一乗寺『夢を語れ』系として伏見に開業、関西二郎系の代表格で系列を拡大、食券制＋ヤサイ・ニンニク・アブラ・カラメのコール、麺量200/300/400g、豚ラーメンは約200gのチャーシューを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls center on house-made wheat noodles boiled in a shared pot; there is no gluten-free preparation, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in a Jiro-style shop is virtually always wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls share a pork-bone broth and pork back-fat; vegetable, garlic and fat toppings are added on top of the animal broth, so no bowl is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, high-volume Jiro-style counter where you buy a ticket, then call out your topping preferences (garlic, vegetables, fat) in Japanese when the noodles go in; some basic Japanese and familiarity with the ordering ritual make the visit much smoother.

noodles.notes: Hefty, snappy, low-hydration thick noodles with a bouncy, chewy 'buri-buri patsu-patsu' bite and visible wheaty edges — the muscular Jiro-style noodle. Portions are huge: 'sho' (small) 200g, 'nami' 300g, 'dai' 400g, with extra noodle add-ons available.

ramen.signature_bowl: Buta Ramen (pork ramen) — order it 'nami' size and, when asked, call your toppings: 'yasai-ninniku-abura' (extra vegetables, raw garlic, back-fat) with 'karame' for a stronger tare hit. The Buta Double piles on even more of the shop's prized fatty pork.
ramen.chashu: Big, soft slabs of braised pork — a finely-textured leaner piece alongside outrageously fatty belly chunks (jokingly called '80% fat'), so meltingly tender they go marshmallow-soft in the hot soup. The fat is the draw, not a flaw.
notable_toppings: mountain of boiled vegetables (cabbage + bean sprouts / moyashi)
ichimi togarashi chili (standard on the ramen)

gf.note: Wheat thick noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare and pork-bone broth — no rice/konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is offered. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire bowl is built on pork-bone broth, pork back-fat, and big slabs of pork chashu, with a shoyu tare. No plant-based broth or topping bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A flag-bearer of Kansai's Jiro-inspired scene: a thunderous, non-emulsified pork-bone-and-soy soup whose clear-but-mighty broth carries raw pork umami rather than creamy emulsion. Crowned with a mountain of garlicky vegetables and glistening back-fat over muscular thick noodles, it's the full 'yasai-ninniku-abura' ritual — a bowl you don't so much eat as conquer.

places.name: 貝だし麺 きた田 (Kaidashimen Kitada)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles — the shop uses Hokkaido "yumechikara" wheat for its straight thin noodles — and a salt (shio) tare that, like nearly all Japanese ramen seasoning, is very likely to contain wheat-based ingredients. The shellfish dashi broth itself (hamaguri, asari, hotate) is naturally gluten-light, but the noodles and tare make the dish unsafe for anyone with celiac disease or wheat allergy. No gluten-free menu, no separate prep, and no dedicated equipment were found. A wheat-avoiding diner should not eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: This is a shellfish-broth ramen specialist — every signature bowl is built on a clam/scallop (hamaguri, asari, hotate) clear dashi, and bowls are topped with pork-loin chashu. There is no vegan or vegetarian broth, and no plant-based bowl was found on the menu. Not a destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 7:00–24:00 daily (last order 23:30). Opens at 7:00 for morning ramen (asa-ra). Note: some sources list closing at 22:00 / last order 21:30; hours can vary, so confirm late-night service before a midnight visit.
