places.neighborhood: 下京区・京都駅周辺 (Shimogyo-ku, near Kyoto Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A specialist ramen counter a short walk from Kyoto Station, Kitada built its reputation on one idea done very well: a clear, fragrant broth drawn entirely from shellfish. The shop opens early — its doors are up at 7:00 a.m. — which has made it a favorite stop for 'asa-ra' (mornin

chef_bio.background: A specialist ramen counter a short walk from Kyoto Station, Kitada built its reputation on one idea done very well: a clear, fragrant broth drawn entirely from shellfish. The shop opens early — its doors are up at 7:00 a.m. — which has made it a favorite stop for 'asa-ra' (morning ramen), with a line of regulars and travelers already forming mid-morning.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let the shellfish speak — a clear, delicate broth that is light enough to drink in the morning yet still eats like a full bowl of ramen.
chef_bio.specialty: Kai-dashi shio ramen — a clear, salt-seasoned broth simmered from generous amounts of hamaguri (hard clam), asari (littleneck clam) and hotate (scallop), giving a clean, sweet shellfish umami rather than the usual pork-bone heaviness. The signature 'specialty' bowl and the 蛤らぁ麺 (hamaguri ramen) lead the menu; the regular 貝だし麺 layers in a house-made aromatic shellfish oil for a richer hit. Straight thin noodles milled from Hokkaido 'yumechikara' wheat, pork-loin chashu, red onion and green sprouts round out the bowl, with miso (貝だし辛味噌) and a classic chuka-soba also offered.

anecdotes.text: Kitada opens at 7:00 a.m., which has made it one of Kyoto Station's go-to spots for morning ramen; reviewers describe queues of several people already waiting through the mid-morning hours.

The broth is built on scallop, littleneck clam and hard clam in large quantity, finished with salt to lift the natural sweetness — described as a clear soup carrying the distinct sweet umami of shellfish.
The shop's 蛤らぁ麺 is noted for a concentrated clam broth that stays refreshing thanks to the salt seasoning, paired with thin straight whole-grain noodles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・fantabi・複数レビューより、京都駅近く下京区、朝7時開店の朝ラー人気店、蛤・あさり・帆立の貝だし塩スープ、蛤らぁ麺と貝だし麺(貝香味油入り)、北海道ゆめちから使用の細ストレート麺、貝だし辛味噌も提供を確認。

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local ramen counter near Kyoto Station with no reservations and frequent queues — a warm greeting and a little patience go a long way, but English support should not be assumed.

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight noodles with a smooth surface and a soft, plump, slightly sticky bite from a touch of extra hydration; built to carry and absorb the delicate shellfish soup rather than to chew firm.

ramen.signature_bowl: 貝だし麺 (Kaidashi-men) — the shellfish-dashi salt ramen; the special version adds extra scallop and comes dressed with chashu, ajitama and wonton.
ramen.chashu: A trio of thin-sliced meats rather than a single cut: char-grilled (aburi) pork shoulder loin and pork belly alongside a slice of lean chicken breast, all lightly seared and laid over the soup.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a seasoning tare that includes soy/wheat; the wonton wrappers are wheat too. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire identity of the bowl is a shellfish broth (clam, scallop, hamaguri) finished with pork and chicken chashu and a wonton with pork filling. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A rare Kyoto take on the modern shellfish-dashi school: the soul of this bowl is a broth drawn purely from clams, hamaguri and scallop, gently salt-seasoned so the briny-sweet shellfish liquor reads clean and delicate rather than rich. It belongs to the new wave of single-ingredient kaidashi shio ramen rather than any old-school regional lineage, a quiet contrast to Kyoto's usual heavy se-abura shoyu.

places.name: ゑびす屋 (Ebisuya)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto ramen shop: wheat noodles in a chicken/pork soy-sauce (shoyu) broth, plus a fried-rice set. The shoyu tare is wheat-based and the broth is chicken/pork — there is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no indication of celiac-safe preparation. Not suitable for someone who must strictly avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The core offering is a chicken-gara / pork-bone soy-sauce ramen with back-fat, served with chashu pork; the fried rice is also savory soy-sauce based. There are no vegetarian or vegan ramen options, and the broth itself is animal-based. Not a fit for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:20), 18:00-20:50. Closed Wednesdays. (Some listings show evening service to around 21:00.)
places.neighborhood: Shimogamo-Takagi-chō, Sakyō-ku (near Shimogamo Shrine / ~11 min walk from Matsugasaki Stn, ~2 min walk from 'Shimogamo-Higashi-Honmachi' bus stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ebisuya opened in the Shimogamo neighborhood in January 2013, calling itself a maker of "original Kyoto ramen" (元祖京都らーめん) — a deliberate nod to the back-fat chicken-and-soy-sauce style that defined the city's noodle culture before the wave of creamy tonkotsu shops.

chef_bio.background: Ebisuya opened in the Shimogamo neighborhood in January 2013, calling itself a maker of "original Kyoto ramen" (元祖京都らーめん) — a deliberate nod to the back-fat chicken-and-soy-sauce style that defined the city's noodle culture before the wave of creamy tonkotsu shops.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is unapologetically nostalgic — it preserves the older Kyoto ramen identity its owner grew up with rather than chasing modern trends.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a chicken-gara soy-sauce ramen crowned with pork back-fat (背脂チャッチャ系), built on thin, slightly curly Kyoto-style noodles; a tonkotsu version rounds out the menu. The fried-rice set (焼めしセット) is a local favorite, praised for an aromatic soy fragrance and a gentle, well-made character likened to a proper Chinese kitchen.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the chicken-soy broth as mellow and deeply savory without harsh saltiness, with thin curly noodles that suit it well; the interior is surprisingly roomy behind a modest storefront, and staff stayed kind even to late-arriving customers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: muchi2ブログより、2013年1月に下鴨で開業、『元祖京都らーめん』を掲げ、鶏ガラ醤油＋背脂チャッチャ、細ちぢれ麺、豚骨版(+20円)、焼めしセット、清潔で意外に広い店内・丁寧な接客を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the menu is built around wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Both the ramen tare and the fried rice are soy-sauce based; Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so these dishes contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen broths are animal-based (chicken-gara / pork-bone) and bowls include pork back-fat and chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills neighborhood ramen shop where ordering is in Japanese; staff are described as kind, but expect minimal English and a quick-turnover local atmosphere.

noodles.notes: Straight Chinese-style noodles, on the softer side, served at a medium thickness — accounts differ slightly between Kyoto-thin and slightly-thick, but consistently straight and tender rather than firm or wavy.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-gara shoyu ramen (鶏ガラしょうゆ) — the house bowl: a clear chicken-bone broth crowned with melted back-fat and a sharp soy edge. Often ordered with the fried-rice (yakimeshi) set, and 'negi-large' for a mound of fresh scallion.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin, hand-cut into thick slices so tender they nearly crumble apart; seasoned lightly to let the meat itself come through rather than a heavy soak.
notable_toppings: abundant green onion (negi) — upgradable to negi-large

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui Chinese noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat-based) tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is textbook 'gansō' Kyoto ramen — a clear chicken-bone broth made deep and sweet by a slick of melted pork back-fat (se-abura) over a confident soy tare, the se-abura-chaccha lineage that defines the city's old-school bowls. The fat reads rich on the lips while the soup itself drinks surprisingly clean, a Kyoto balancing act of indulgence and restraint.

places.name: 拳ラーメン (Kobushi Ramen)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic ramen shop built on wheat noodles, wheat-based shoyu tare, and duck/fish (niboshi) broths, plus tsukemen and tantanmen that are likewise wheat-heavy. There is no indication of gluten-free options or any celiac-safe accommodation; not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered on animal-based broths (Kyoto duck, nodoguro and other niboshi fish, pork chashu, chicken) with no vegan or vegetarian ramen offering noted. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 18:00-22:00 (last entry approx 21:13-21:14); closed Wednesdays.
places.cuisine: Niboshi (dried-sardine) chuka-soba / tsukemen / soupless dandan noodles
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 拳ラーメン (Kobushi Ramen) sits a few minutes' walk from Umekoji Park near Umekoji-Kyotonishi Station in Kyoto's Shimogyo Ward. The shop relocated and was renamed from its earlier incarnation 'こぶ志' in March 2011, and has grown into one of the area's most respected ramen counters. Afte

chef_bio.background: 拳ラーメン (Kobushi Ramen) sits a few minutes' walk from Umekoji Park near Umekoji-Kyotonishi Station in Kyoto's Shimogyo Ward. Run by owner-chef Kengo Yamauchi, a former washoku (Japanese-cuisine) cook, the shop was renamed and relocated from its earlier incarnation 'こぶ志,' opening at its current spot on March 28, 2011, and has grown into one of the area's most respected ramen counters. When the owner was hospitalized the shop temporarily closed and later resumed service, and in its most recent chapter it held a renovation grand reopening on November 17, 2025.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen works from a 'fusion of washoku technique and ramen' approach, bringing Japanese-cuisine precision to every element. Pork-shoulder chashu is sliced to order just before serving to keep its aroma and fat at their best, and the bowl pairs medium-thin straight noodles from the well-known Kyoto noodle maker Teigyaku (棣鄂) to suit the delicate broth.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 京鴨とのどぐろ煮干しそば — a niboshi chuka soba built on a base of Kyoto duck stock layered with the rich umami of nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), finishing clean and elegant. Yuzu vinegar is offered on the side to shift the flavor mid-bowl. The shop also serves tsukemen, tantanmen and shiru-nashi (brothless) tantanmen.

anecdotes.text: The shop's washoku-meets-ramen style and its refined Kyoto-duck-and-nodoguro niboshi broth have earned it Michelin Bib Gourmand recognition and repeated selection among Kyoto's top ramen shops.
Chashu is sliced fresh right before each bowl is served so the meat's aroma and fat richness come through at their peak.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ・ラーメンWalker・kyotopiより、店主・山内健吾(和食出身)、2011年3月28日に『こぶ志』から改名移転、棣鄂の中細麺、京鴨とのどぐろ煮干しそば、ミシュラン・ビブグルマン常連。店主入院で一時休業後に再開、2025年11月17日にリニューアルオープンを確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-based ramen tare at Japanese ramen shops is normally made with wheat-containing soy sauce, and the noodles are wheat; no gluten-free preparation is indicated here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and toppings are animal-based (duck, fish niboshi, pork, chicken); no separate vegan preparation is noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local counter shop where ordering in Japanese is expected; non-Japanese diners are welcome but should not expect English support.

noodles.notes: Chu-hoso (thin-to-medium) straight noodles from the long-established Teikaku (棣鄂) noodle maker; pronounced wheat fragrance with a supple, clean-biting texture that suits the delicate clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 京鴨とノドグロ煮干しそば (Kyoto-duck & nodoguro-niboshi soba) with ajitama — a golden, glass-clear bowl where duck umami opens and dried-sardine nodoguro depth follows, finished with a yuzu-vinegar table condiment to brighten the soup mid-bowl.
ramen.chashu: Two proteins: low-temp pork shoulder chashu, tender and lean, served alongside a delicate poached chicken-breast ham — a refined pair rather than a fatty belly slab.

gf.note: Wheat noodles from a conventional noodle maker plus shoyu-based tare; no documented rice or konjac noodle or GF-tare option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A genuine plant-based bowl is documented: a Rausu-kombu kake-soba built only from kombu (kelp) and water with a light non-animal tare and plant oil, created for vegetarian/foreign diners. Confirm current availability, as it may be limited or seasonal.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto bowl that trades the city's classic se-abura shoyu heritage for haute restraint: a crystal-clear soup where Kyoto duck and prized nodoguro dried sardine meet, tasting closer to a fine consomme than a typical noodle broth. Its soul is layered, lingering dashi clarity rather than richness — niboshi depth handled with delicacy.

places.name: セアブラノ神 伏見剛力 (Seabura no Kami Fushimi Goriki)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a Jiro-style ramen specialist: the signature "Kami Jiro" uses thick, heavily-crinkled wheat noodles, and the bowls are seasoned with a wheat-based soy sauce tare (Kyoto Gokko shoyu) over a pork-and-fish broth finished with seasoned back fat. There are no gluten-free noodles, tare, or dedicated preparation, and the kitchen is small and shared. No published allergen handling. Anyone with celiac disease should avoid; a gluten-sensitive diner should treat everything here as containing wheat.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option. The entire menu is built on pork-bone and fish broth, pork back fat, roasted/braised pork, and ground pork toppings. Even the vegetable-forward items (bean sprouts, corn) sit in a meat-and-fish broth. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 18:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Wednesdays and Jan 1. Some limited/"guerrilla" items sell out earlier in the day.
places.neighborhood: Fukakusa-Shibatayashiki-chō, Fushimi-ku (in front of Fushimi Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is the Fushimi outpost of Seabura no Kami, the Kyoto ramen group whose flagship is in Mibu (Shimogyo), with additional shops including a Nishiki location. Set right by Fushimi Station in southern Kyoto, the Goriki branch grew its own identity rather than simply copying the m

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Seabura no Kami group; flagship in Mibu, Shimogyo-ku)
chef_bio.background: This is the Fushimi outpost of Seabura no Kami, the Kyoto ramen group whose flagship is in Mibu (Shimogyo), with additional shops including a Nishiki location. Set right by Fushimi Station in southern Kyoto, the Goriki branch grew its own identity rather than simply copying the main shop, and at one point reworked its concept toward a rougher, more forceful 'Goriki' (powerful) style with notes borrowed from the bold ramen culture of Tsubame-Sanjo.
chef_bio.philosophy: An approachable, Kyoto-tuned read on Jiro: serious volume and back fat, but tempered — finer-than-typical Jiro noodles and a lighter, less heavy broth (often with grated fresh ginger offered in place of mandatory raw garlic) so newcomers can enjoy a Jiro-style bowl, while the renovated 'Goriki' direction keeps it satisfying for hardcore fans.
chef_bio.specialty: Kami Jiro — the shop's own take on Jiro-style ramen, served with a dedicated extra-thick, heavily-crinkled noodle in 200g/300g/400g sizes, over a pork-and-fish broth crowned with back fat that's been seasoned with Kyoto Gokko soy sauce for a sweet, umami finish. The Fushimi shop also runs frequent 'guerrilla' limited bowls (miso Kami Jiro, niboshi back-fat soba, tantan tsukemen and more) alongside its mazesoba.

anecdotes.text: The 'Kami Jiro' began as an occasional limited special before becoming a regular menu item at the Fushimi shop, and uses a noodle made specifically for that bowl.

A renovation pushed the Fushimi branch toward a stronger, rawer 'Goriki' presentation influenced by Tsubame-Sanjo ramen, swapping refined rare chashu for braised pork belly and leaning into bigger, bolder bowls.

Diners describe the Fushimi bowls as deliberately more approachable than hardcore Jiro — finer noodles, gentler white broth, with ginger tempering the richness — positioned as a friendly entry point to the style.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・noodlers・genjitsutouhi・食べログより、壬生本店(＋錦等)を持つグループの伏見店。燕三条風の荒々しい『剛力』へ改装しレアチャーシューをバラ煮豚へ変更、専用麺の神二郎(200/300/400g)、京都五光醤油の背脂、ニンニクの代わりに生姜で万人向けに調整、を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: A dedicated Jiro-style ramen kitchen using wheat noodles and wheat soy-sauce tare in a small shared space; no gluten-free preparation and high risk of wheat throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The back fat and tare are seasoned with Kyoto Gokko (五光) soy sauce, a standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and fish based with back fat and pork toppings; even vegetable items are served in the meat/fish broth, so no dish is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locals-focused Jiro-style ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese and English support shouldn't be assumed; knowing how to call your noodle size and toppings (and a few words of Japanese) makes it smoother.

noodles.notes: The signature se-abura shoyu bowl runs firm, low-hydration noodles — a finer, almost Hakata-leaning straight cut that stands up to the fatty broth, with a medium-thick option also offered. The separate 神二郎 jiro bowl swaps in its own ultra-thick crimped (wavy) noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: 背脂中華そば (se-abura chuka soba) — the Kyoto back-fat soy-sauce bowl: a chicken-and-pork stock laced with dried-sardine dashi, seasoned with sweet-savory Kyoto Gokko shoyu tare and blanketed in melting pork back-fat. Add the 黒バラ海苔 (black laver) for a mid-bowl flavor shift. The bold can instead order the 神二郎, the shop's regularized Jiro-style mountain.
ramen.chashu: Two thick slices of low-temperature rare-cooked pork; the regular menu has at times also run a soft braised pork-belly chashu, and the 神二郎 bowl is dressed with rare chashu.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy-sauce tare; the dried-fish/pork/chicken broth and back fat are also animal-derived. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Built on chicken bones, pork knuckle and dried-sardine dashi, finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's own se-abura shoyu soul — a clear chicken-and-pork stock deepened with dried sardine, sweetened by local Gokko soy tare, then cloaked in a snowfall of melted pork back-fat that clings to firm, fine noodles. The Fushimi branch pushes harder than its siblings, planting a full Jiro-style mountain alongside the classic bowl for those who want the back-fat lineage at maximum volume.

places.name: 麺匠 一粒万倍 寺町店 (Mensho Ichiryu Manbai Teramachi)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen and tsukemen shop. The noodles are wheat-based (the thick tsukemen noodles are made with bran/ふすま), and the soy (醤油) tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu. There is no indication of any gluten-free option or accommodation, and broths are duck/chicken or pork-bone-and-fish based. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: All bowls are built on animal stocks — a light duck-and-Daisen-chicken clear broth for the shoyu/shio bowls, and a rich pork-bone-and-fish (魚介豚骨) base for the tsukemen. Toppings include pork and chicken chashu. No plant-based ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (last order 21:30), open daily
places.cuisine: Light (tanrei) clear-broth ramen with duck and Ōyama chicken
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Kyoto-grown ramen shop. The first 一粒万倍 opened near Sanjo Kawaramachi in 2019, and this Teramachi branch followed in January 2022, on Teramachi street just south of Shijo near Kyoto-Kawaramachi station; sister shops have since opened in Saiin and Gion. The name 一粒万倍 comes from 一

chef_bio.background: A Kyoto-grown ramen shop. The first 一粒万倍 opened near Sanjo Kawaramachi in 2019, and this Teramachi branch followed in January 2022, on Teramachi street just south of Shijo near Kyoto-Kawaramachi station; sister shops have since opened in Saiin and Gion. The name 一粒万倍 comes from 一粒万倍日, an auspicious day in the Japanese calendar meaning that a single grain of rice multiplies ten-thousandfold — a wish for prosperity that the shop extends to its guests.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing good fortune to the diner — the shop leans into its auspicious name to make a bowl of ramen feel lucky as well as satisfying.
chef_bio.specialty: A light, refined (淡麗系) clear broth built on duck and branded Daisen chicken (大山どり, from Tottori), served as both 醤油 (soy) and 塩 (salt) bowls. The premium soy bowl pairs a duck-and-chicken double broth with both pork and chicken-breast chashu. The Teramachi branch also has its own signature: a rich fish-and-pork-bone tsukemen offered in bonito, spicy, and shrimp variations.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits across from a shrine on Teramachi and frames eating its ramen as a small piece of good luck, playing on the meaning of 一粒万倍 — one grain becoming ten thousand.

What began as a single Sanjo-Kawaramachi shop in 2019 has expanded into a small local group, with the Teramachi branch (2022) adding its own exclusive rich fish-pork-bone tsukemen alongside the house clear-broth bowls.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・genjitsutouhiより、2019年に三条河原町へ一号店、寺町店は2022年、西院・祇園にも展開。鴨と大山どりの淡麗清湯(醤油/塩)、特製醤油はWスープに豚と鶏むねチャーシュー、寺町限定の濃厚魚介豚骨つけ麺、京都大神宮前という立地を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the core product and the thick tsukemen noodles contain bran (ふすま); shared noodle-cooking water and a wheat-based soy tare make this a high-gluten environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 醤油 (soy) ramen uses a soy-based tare, which in standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat. No wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth is animal-based (duck/chicken clear soup or pork-bone-and-fish tsukemen) and toppings include pork and chicken chashu; no plant-based preparation exists.

positives.text: An English menu is available and the shop is used to international customers, which can make it easier to ask questions about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the shop is accustomed to international visitors, so ordering should be straightforward for non-Japanese speakers.

noodles.notes: Thin, low-hydration straight noodles with whole-wheat flour worked in, boiled firm for a crisp, snappy (patsu-patsu) bite reminiscent of Hakata-style thin noodles. The branch-exclusive tsukemen instead uses thick, wavy, bran-flecked noodles served cold and firmly set.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Shoyu Ramen — the dark, savory soy bowl over the clear duck-and-Daisen-chicken broth (the shio version is the lighter alternative). The thick seafood-pork-bone tsukemen, including a spicy version, is a branch-exclusive draw.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds: a low-temperature rare pork shoulder-loin chashu, moist with a balance of lean and fat umami and a faint smokiness, plus a tender low-temperature chicken-breast chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on duck and chicken with chicken-fat aroma oil and pork/chicken chashu; the tsukemen adds pork bone and fish powder. No plant-based broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto bowl that trades the city's old pork-back-fat shoyu lineage for a refined, clear duck-and-chicken chintan: the soul lives in a glistening golden soup of duck and Daisen-dori (Tottori) chicken, deep and aromatic yet free of any gamey edge and lifted by fragrant chicken oil. It is tanrei (light-and-clear) ramen at its most elegant, finished tableside with black pepper, sansho, or vinegar to taste.

places.name: 麺屋 優光 河原町 (Menya Yukou Kawaramachi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop: the bowls use wheat noodles, and the shoyu (soy sauce) tare is built on Kyoto soy sauces that almost certainly contain wheat. Broths are shellfish (oyster/clam) and duck/bonito/kombu based. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, separate preparation, or allergen accommodation, so it should be treated as not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every signature bowl is animal-based: shellfish (oyster, clam, shimijiri) dashi shoyu ramen, duck-dashi tsukemen, and bonito/kombu/meat-enriched shoyu ramen, plus chashu pork and soft-egg toppings. No plant-based ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: Official site lists 11:00–22:00, open daily (year-round). A listing aggregator shows extended hours of 11:00–00:30 (last order 00:00); hours may vary, so confirm before a late visit.
places.cuisine: Light shellfish-broth shoyu (soy sauce) ramen / duck-broth tsukemen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Yukou began as a humble yatai (street food stall) called 屋台優光 in Kyoto's Kiyamachi district before relocating to a permanent storefront in the heart of the city at Shijo-Kawaramachi, just south of Takashimaya and beside Good Nature Station. The owner trained at the renowned

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (originally a yatai stall in Kiyamachi, now Shijo-Kawaramachi)
chef_bio.background: Menya Yukou began as a humble yatai (street food stall) called 屋台優光 in Kyoto's Kiyamachi district before relocating to a permanent storefront in the heart of the city at Shijo-Kawaramachi, just south of Takashimaya and beside Good Nature Station. The owner trained at the renowned Osaka ramen shop 人類みな麺類 (Jinrui Mina Menrui) before striking out on his own. The Kawaramachi shop opened on September 23, 2021, and is the flagship of a small group that also runs 麺屋聖~kiyo~, a shop devoted to spreading Kyoto's dashi culture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around Kyoto's dashi culture and freshly made noodles - the team makes noodles fresh each morning and leans on carefully blended Kyoto soy sauces to keep the broths clean and delicate rather than heavy.
chef_bio.specialty: A light, refined shellfish-dashi shoyu ramen (貝らーめん) that condenses the umami of oyster, clam and shijimi over a base of blended Kyoto soy sauces - a shellfish-broth style that is relatively rare in Kyoto. The shop is equally known for its rich duck-dashi tsukemen (鴨つけ麺), a group-first creation limited to roughly 15 bowls a day, and a Kyoto-style shoyu ramen layered with bonito, kombu and meat umami. A torched rare-chashu rice bowl (炙りレア焼豚丼) is a favorite side.

anecdotes.text: The shop grew from a single Kiyamachi yatai (street stall) called 屋台優光 into a permanent Shijo-Kawaramachi storefront, with the owner having trained at Osaka's celebrated 人類みな麺類.
Designed to feel more like a cafe than a typical ramen shop, the interior plays Mr. Children throughout service and explicitly welcomes solo diners, including women.
The duck-dashi tsukemen was a first for the Yukou group and is limited to around 15 servings per day.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式(yukou-kiyo.com)・osumituki・takemachelinより、木屋町の屋台優光から四条河原町(高島屋南2軒)へ移転、2021年9月23日開業、店主は人類みな麺類出身。貝らーめん(牡蠣・あさり・しじみ)を京都醤油で、鴨つけ麺は一日15食限定、炙りレア焼豚丼、カフェ風でミスチルが流れる店内を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and tare are built on blended Kyoto soy sauces, which in Japan are standardly brewed with wheat; combined with wheat ramen noodles this makes the bowls unsuitable for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (shellfish dashi, duck/bonito/kombu, meat-enriched shoyu) and bowls carry pork chashu and egg, so there is no plant-based separation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, welcoming counter ramen shop in a busy central tourist area; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, and solo diners (including women) are explicitly welcomed.

noodles.notes: Springy medium-thick wavy noodles incorporating whole-grain flour, with a satisfying chew that carries the broth well. The shellfish and shio bowls present them in a clear, refined soup; the duck tsukemen pairs them with a concentrated dipping broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 貝らーめん (Shellfish Ramen) — a light, refined Kyoto-shoyu broth drawing oyster, asari clam, and shijimi clam umami; add the rare seared chashu. The duck-fat tsukemen (鴨つけ麺, limited daily) is the standout alternative.
ramen.chashu: The heart is a rare, lightly seared (aburi) pork chashu — soft, pink, and gently charred at the edges; a thin-cut braised pork is also offered, and bowls can be loaded with multiple slices.
notable_toppings: yuzu/citrus accent on the shio bowl

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles with a Kyoto soy-sauce (wheat) tare throughout; no rice or konjac noodle option and no gluten-free tare is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on animal/seafood dashi — shellfish (oyster/clam), bonito-and-kombu, chicken, or duck fat — plus pork or duck toppings. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's clear-soup counterpoint to the city's famous se-abura richness: a delicate kaidashi (shellfish-dashi) shoyu where oyster, asari, and shijimi umami are floated on local Kyoto soy sauce, so you taste the bivalve sweetness rather than fat. Alongside it sits a deeply aromatic duck-fat tsukemen built on kombu-and-bonito, marking the shop as a modern Kyoto refined-shoyu specialist.

places.name: 濃厚京鶏白湯らーめん めんや 美鶴 (Nōkō Kyō Tori Paitan Rāmen Menya Mitsuru)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use standard wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare in the black version, both of which contain wheat. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation are mentioned by any source. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is a chicken-based broth (tori-paitan), simmered for hours from domestic chicken and aromatic vegetables, with toppings of chicken chashu and chicken tsukune (meatballs). There is no plant-based broth or vegan bowl. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 11:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00); Fri-Sat 11:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00); Sun 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Open year-round (sources vary slightly on exact open/close times).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2015 in the Shijo-Karasuma area of central Kyoto, Menya Mitsuru built its reputation on a single idea done well: a rich, collagen-heavy chicken paitan broth. The proprietor came from a mizutaki (chicken hot-pot) specialty background, and that pedigree shows in how the b

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2015 in the Shijo-Karasuma area of central Kyoto, Menya Mitsuru built its reputation on a single idea done well: a rich, collagen-heavy chicken paitan broth. The proprietor came from a mizutaki (chicken hot-pot) specialty background, and that pedigree shows in how the broth is built — fresh domestic chicken simmered in volume over high heat for long hours until it turns creamy and white. Pointedly, the shop uses no pork at all; the depth here is all chicken and aromatics. The shop has since added a sister location in the Gion / Nawate area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Refinement over heaviness — the broth aims to be intensely flavorful yet clean rather than cloying, expressing 'the paitan's original character' in the white version while the black leans into layered umami.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 濃厚鶏白湯そば (rich chicken paitan soba), offered in a salt-based 'white' version that showcases the broth's natural character and a soy-tare 'black' version built to amplify umami. There is also a bonito-enhanced 京鰹節鶏白湯 and an extra-rich 超濃厚 version. Bowls come dressed with chicken chashu, chicken tsukune meatballs, kikurage wood-ear mushroom, and scallion, with medium-thick straight noodles chosen to carry the thick broth.

anecdotes.text: The owner previously worked at a mizutaki (chicken hot-pot) specialty restaurant, bringing that refined chicken-broth craft to a ramen format — and deliberately uses only domestic chicken and aromatic vegetables, with no pork in the broth.
The broth is made by simmering large quantities of fresh domestic chicken over high heat for an extended time to draw out a creamy, white paitan.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto.tips・kyotopiより、2015年に四条烏丸(中京区)で開業、水炊き専門店出身の店主、豚不使用・国産鶏と香味野菜のみの濃厚鶏白湯(白=塩/黒=醤油)、鰹だし版・超濃厚版、祇園縄手に姉妹店、鶏チャーシュー・つくね・きくらげ・葱、中太ストレート麺を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy-tare 'black' version of the paitan soba is seasoned with shoyu, which is wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on a chicken broth with chicken chashu and chicken tsukune toppings; the shared broth and kitchen make any plant-only request unworkable.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, clean counter-only ramen shop near a major station that draws a broad crowd including solo diners and tourists; a few Japanese greetings go a long way but no special preparation is needed.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick straight noodles chosen to stand up to the thick, clinging chicken broth; an extra-rich (super nōkō) version pours an even more viscous soup over the same noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Nōkō tori paitan rāmen (rich chicken paitan) — choose white (shio) or black (shoyu); a super-rich version delivers an even thicker, gravy-like soup.
ramen.chashu: Chicken chashu — moist, juicy, lightly seasoned pieces of poultry (no pork used) that soak up the broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare option; no documented rice or konjac noodle, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and aromatic vegetables finished with bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, and toppings are chicken chashu and tsukune — no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan specialist: pure chicken-and-vegetable bones whipped into a creamy, collagen-thick white soup, then deepened with Kyoto katsuobushi dashi so a quiet seafood hum runs beneath the poultry richness. Choose shio for the cleanest expression of that chicken sweetness or shoyu for a darker, rounder finish — and reach for the Gion black-shichimi to cut the body with citrus and pepper.

places.name: 珍遊 河原町六角店 (Chinyu Kawaramachi Rokkaku)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle chuka-soba shop. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles, a soy-sauce tare (Japanese shoyu almost always contains wheat), and a chicken-bone broth — none of it is celiac-safe, and there is no gluten-free menu or noodle option. Not suitable for anyone needing a strict gluten-free meal.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is a long-simmered chicken paitan (chicken bones, whole chicken, chicken feet) finished with rendered back fat, and bowls are topped with chashu pork. There are no advertised vegan or vegetarian ramen options. Not a fit for plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–24:00 (daily); some listings show 11:00–23:00. Open year-round.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chinyu traces back to 1950 (Showa 25), when it began as a single ramen yatai (street stall) wheeled out in a still-recovering postwar Kyoto. The founder eventually set down roots in an eight-tsubo storefront along what would become Ichijoji's famous 'ramen street,' and the house

chef_bio.background: Chinyu traces back to 1950 (Showa 25), when it began as a single ramen yatai (street stall) wheeled out in a still-recovering postwar Kyoto. The founder eventually set down roots in an eight-tsubo storefront along what would become Ichijoji's famous 'ramen street,' and the house is now counted among the wellsprings of the Kyoto chicken-paitan, back-fat style. The Kawaramachi Rokkaku branch carries that lineage into the center of town, on the first floor of the Rokkaku Kaikan just off Shinkyogoku.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated creed is plain and stubborn: 奇をてらわない、手を抜かない — no gimmicks, no shortcuts — pouring everything into a single honest bowl while still pushing to refine the craft.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka-soba (中華そば): a chicken-bone white broth simmered long with whole chicken and chicken feet for a collagen-rich body, mellowed with an aged soy-sauce tare and finished tableside with a generous scatter of fresh back fat. It looks rich but drinks surprisingly clean, and you can dial in noodle firmness, the amount of green onion, and how much back fat you want.

anecdotes.text: The brand's own telling: born in 1950 from a postwar Kyoto street stall, it grew into a tiny eight-tsubo Ichijoji shop and became one of the source points of Kyoto's chicken-broth, back-fat ramen tradition.
Listings highlight the trademark move of the style — fresh back fat scattered over the bowl to order — and a broth that reads heavy by sight but lands light and mellow on the palate.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式沿革・ホットペッパーより、1950年(昭和25)に屋台で創業、一乗寺の八坪店に定着、京都の鶏白湯・背脂ラーメンの源流の一つ。『奇をてらわない、手を抜かない』を信条に、丸鶏・鶏足の白湯＋熟成醤油ダレ＋後がけ背脂、河原町六角店は六角会館1Fを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the broth/tare contain wheat-based shoyu; a shared ramen kitchen offers no gluten-free path or separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is described as an aged/traditional soy sauce ('老舗の醤油でまろやかな風味に'), and Japanese brewed shoyu is almost always made with wheat — treat the seasoning as wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is chicken paitan (chicken bones, whole chicken, chicken feet) finished with animal back fat, so every bowl is animal-based with no plant-based stock available.

cultural_comfort.note: A roomy, modern, walk-in chuka-soba shop in the central Shinkyogoku/Sanjo tourist zone — easy for a visitor to enter, order at the counter, and slurp a bowl with just a few words of Japanese.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin, low-hydration straight noodles milled from fragrant Hokkaido wheat; they start crisp and firm at the bite and soften into a chew as they drink up the fatty soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka-soba (中華そば) — the house bowl: a chicken-rich, milky soy broth crowned with scattered back-fat, with the amount of se-abura adjustable to taste.
ramen.chashu: Braised roast-pork chashu, sliced and laid over the noodles.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy tare put this beyond celiac-safe; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and topped with pork chashu — no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A founding voice of the Kyoto se-abura shoyu lineage, simmering whole chickens, feet and bones into a milky-rich soup that is sweetened with dark Kyoto soy and showered with melting back-fat. It is the classic Kyoto paradox in a bowl — it looks decadently heavy, yet the finish pulls back clean and light.

places.name: 麺処 鶏谷 (Menjokoro Toritani)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (medium-thick curly and straight flat-cut varieties) served in chicken broth, with the salt and "thick soy" (shoyu/tare) versions almost certainly using wheat-based soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, gluten-free tare, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around chicken — clear chicken broth, concentrated aged chicken paitan, chicken char siu, seared chicken (tataki), and roast-pork butter rice. There are no plant-based broths or vegan bowls. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:15–14:30, 18:05–22:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00–15:00, 18:00–22:00. Closes early when sold out.
places.neighborhood: Saiin, Ukyō-ku (Keifuku Yamanouchi Stn / Hankyū Saiin Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A refined neighborhood ramen-ya in the Saiin area of Kyoto, set between the Keifuku Yamanouchi and Hankyu Saiin stations. The room has a quiet, almost traditional-Japanese-restaurant feel rather than the look of a typical ramen counter, and it draws steady weekday crowds with lin

chef_bio.background: A refined neighborhood ramen-ya in the Saiin area of Kyoto, set between the Keifuku Yamanouchi and Hankyu Saiin stations. The room has a quiet, almost traditional-Japanese-restaurant feel rather than the look of a typical ramen counter, and it draws steady weekday crowds with lines on weekends despite not sitting on a prime corner.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking without chemical seasonings (無化調), leaning on the quality and combination of ingredients, and keeping the kitchen open into the evening (until around 10 PM, later than most local rivals).
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is its chicken soba, offered in two registers: a clear, no-MSG (無化調) tori-soba, and the namesake jukusei (aged) chicken soba — a deeply concentrated, high-viscosity chicken paitan that the kitchen finishes without sweetness, ending on a clean, almost bitter note. The chicken char siu is a standout, praised as approaching proper chicken cookery in its own right, and the kitchen's confidence with chicken extends to seared tataki preparations.

anecdotes.text: House-made noodles — a medium-thick, slightly chewy curly noodle and a straight flat-cut version with firm, mochi-like bite — are matched to the broths so they grip the concentrated soup.
A small side of butter rice with chicken and roast pork (around 300 yen) is singled out as punching well above a typical side dish.
The chicken char siu is described as exceptional, 'reaching the realm of chicken cuisine itself.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・noodlersより、西院(右京区)、和食店のような静かな佇まい、無化調・清湯系、鶏そばと熟成鶏白湯の二本立て(白湯は甘さを抑え後味やや苦め)、『鶏料理の域』と評される鶏チャーシュー・鶏たたき、自家製麺、破格300円の鶏とローストポークのバターごはんを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Ramen here is wheat-noodle based and broths/tare are chicken and (for the soy version) wheat-soy-sauce based; no gluten-free handling is indicated anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu ('thick soy' / 鶏そば soy-style) bowls use soy sauce tare, which in a standard Japanese ramen-ya is wheat-based; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based and toppings include chicken char siu and roast pork; there are no plant-based options, so no meaningful vegan handling exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locals-focused counter ramen shop with no English support expected; ordering is straightforward but basic Japanese or pointing will help most.

places.name: らーめん 鶴武者 (Ramen Tsurumusha)
places.gf_detail: This is a chicken-paitan ramen shop built on wheat. Noodles are made in-house daily from a blend of Kyoto-grown "Kyo-komugi" wheat and Hokkaido "Kitahonami"/"Haruyutaka" wheat flours, and the tare/seasoning is shoyu-based (soy sauce, which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat). The broth itself is chicken plus pork bones with aromatic vegetables. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, gluten-free tare, or celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiac; a gluten-avoider has essentially nothing safe on the core menu. Confirm directly with the shop if you must, but treat as not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: All bowls are built on an animal broth — whole chicken (from Omi) plus pork bones and aromatic vegetables — and the tare uses kombu, shiitake, and dried scallop (dried scallop is animal-derived). Toppings include pork shoulder rare chashu. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Wed/Sat/Sun: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-21:00. Tue/Thu: 11:30-14:00, 18:00-21:00. Fri: closed. (Lunch and dinner service with a mid-afternoon break.)
places.neighborhood: Saiin, Ukyō-ku (Hankyū / Keifuku Saiin Stn / Nishioji-Sanjō)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Yoshiaki Nishimura was originally a programmer. The birth of his second child became the turning point: he wanted to create a ramen shop that parents could comfortably walk into with kids in tow. He trained at the long-established Kyoto ramen house Chinyu before going

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Yoshiaki Nishimura was originally a programmer. The birth of his second child became the turning point: he wanted to create a ramen shop that parents could comfortably walk into with kids in tow. He trained at the long-established Kyoto ramen house Chinyu before going independent and opening his own shop in 2012.
chef_bio.philosophy: So the whole family can eat here with peace of mind. To that end the shop uses no preservatives and no chemical seasonings (MSG), and keeps child seats and child cutlery at the counter, with kids openly welcomed.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a salt-based chicken-paitan (tori-paitan) ramen. The broth is built from whole chicken from Omi plus pork bones and aromatic vegetables, simmered about 30 minutes while the scum is carefully skimmed; the tare is drawn from kombu, shiitake, and dried scallop steeped in water from the day before and slowly cooked down. The result is light on the palate yet creamy and mellow. The rare chashu is pork shoulder seasoned with salt alone to let the meat's own flavor come through, and the house menma is rehydrated over four days then seasoned, never made in advance.

anecdotes.text: The noodles are made fresh every day on the shop's own noodle machine, using a blend of Kyoto-grown Kyo-komugi wheat with Hokkaido Kitahonami and Haruyutaka flours; each portion is hand-massaged and aged into smooth, springy medium-thick wavy noodles.
Nishimura left a programming career to open the shop in 2012 after training at Kyoto's old-guard ramen house Chinyu, specifically wanting a place easy to enter with children.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Amakara Techo profiles Nishimura as an ex-programmer who opened Tsurumusha in 2012 after training at Chinyu, wanting a family-friendly shop; it documents the Omi-chicken shio paitan, the day-ahead kombu/shiitake/scallop tare, the 4-day menma, the Kyo-komugi noodle blend, and the no-preservative/no-MSG child-welcoming policy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: In-house wheat noodles are made daily on the shop's own noodle machine, so the kitchen is a wheat-heavy environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The seasoning/tare and items like the house menma are soy-sauce (shoyu) based; Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is animal-based (whole chicken plus pork bones and vegetables) and the tare includes dried scallop; toppings include pork chashu. No vegan/vegetarian option exists.

positives.text: No preservatives and no chemical seasonings (MSG) are used, reflecting an explicit safe-for-the-whole-family ethos.
Child seats and child cutlery are provided and children are actively welcomed.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, family-run neighborhood counter ramen shop that is unusually welcoming to families and newcomers, but expect Japanese-only ordering, so a few polite words and pointing at the menu will carry you through.

noodles.notes: Milled and cut in-house every day on the noodle machine at the entrance, blending rare Kyoto-grown 'Kyo-komugi' wheat with Hokkaido flour. Chewy, springy and aromatic of wheat.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chicken paitan ramen (鶏白湯らーめん) — a salt-based bowl of creamy Omi-chicken soup rounded out with pork bone and a seafood-kombu dashi; the plain らーめん and seasonal tsukemen round out the menu.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut pork shoulder loin, gently low-temperature cooked so it stays rare, tender and pink, seasoned only with salt to let the meat's own flavor lead.
notable_toppings: soft-boiled ajitama from fresh Kyoto eggs

gf.note: Noodles are house-milled wheat (Kyoto + Hokkaido flour) with kansui, and the tare/soup carry soy and dashi. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is animal- and fish-derived: Omi chicken, pork bone, plus scallop/dried-shrimp/katsuo-style seafood dashi, with chicken-fat aroma and pork chashu. No documented plant-only broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A family-minded Kyoto kitchen that treats the bowl as cooking rather than chemistry — additive-free soup of Shiga's Omi chicken and pork bone laced with a kombu-and-shellfish seafood dashi, served either as a creamy-yet-clean salt paitan or a lighter clear bowl. It is a modern, ingredient-driven house style rather than a classic gotochi lineage, its soul tasted in that layered chicken-and-sea umami carried on house-milled Kyoto-wheat noodles.

places.name: 麺逢 KATAJIKENAI (Men-Au KATAJIKENAI)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature bowls are wheat ramen noodles (sourced from Kyoto's Teikaku / 麺屋棣鄂) served in chicken-paitan or shellfish broth, and the tare almost certainly uses wheat-based soy sauce. There is no celiac-safe option and no published allergen handling; not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. Both signature broths are animal-based — a creamy chicken paitan and a clear shellfish (clam/scallop/oyster) dashi — and toppings include meat. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun: 11:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:30/15:45) and 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:45). Monday: morning ramen service 8:00–10:30 only. (Tabelog also lists a daily 09:00–16:00 / 17:00–21:00 pattern; the morning/asa-ramen slot on Monday and rotating limited bowls mean times can shift.)
places.neighborhood: Saiin / Nishinokyo area, Nakagyo-ku — about 300m (3 min walk
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop opened in late August 2023 in the Saiin area of western Kyoto, near Saiin Station. Its chef trained at 鶏soba座銀 (Tori Soba Zagin), a highly regarded Osaka ramen shop, and carries that pedigree into a calm, design-forward counter space with gray finishes and wood furniture

chef_bio.background: The shop opened in late August 2023 in the Saiin area of western Kyoto, near Saiin Station. Its chef trained at 鶏soba座銀 (Tori Soba Zagin), a highly regarded Osaka ramen shop, and carries that pedigree into a calm, design-forward gray-mortar counter space of about nine seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name KATAJIKENAI (忝い) means 'grateful / much obliged' — an expression of gratitude that frames the shop's hospitality, paired with a focus on clean, refined broths and quality ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: A two-style 'twin-broth' lineup: a creamy chicken paitan (鶏白湯そば) whose foamed, emulsified soup is blended just before serving, and a clear shellfish dashi (貝清湯そば) built from Shinji-ko clams, hamaguri, scallops and oysters for a clean oceanic aroma. A daily-limited tsukemen version of the chicken paitan (鶏白湯つけそば) rounds out the menu. Noodles are sourced from Kyoto maker 麺屋棣鄂.

anecdotes.text: The chef trained at the popular Osaka shop 鶏soba座銀 before opening here in August 2023.
The kai-dashi (shellfish) broth is drawn from a mix of Shinji-ko clams, hamaguri, scallops and oysters for a layered seafood umami and an 'aroma of the sea'.
The chicken paitan soup is foamed/emulsified with an immersion blender (bamix) just before service for a light-yet-creamy texture.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi (two articles) covers the Aug-2023 Saiin opening, the chef's 鶏soba座銀 pedigree, the chicken-paitan and shellfish (Shinjiko-clam/hamaguri/scallop/oyster) broths, and the immersion-blender foaming; Tabelog confirms the daily-limited 鶏白湯つけそば tsukemen.

cultural_comfort.note: A small 9-seat counter ramen shop with a ticket-machine order system and cash-only payment, so a few words of Japanese and basic point-and-order etiquette go a long way; staff are unlikely to handle detailed English allergy requests.

noodles.notes: Specialty noodles supplied by Menya Teigaku, a Kyoto noodle maker known for ramen and tsukemen. Generally medium-thin and high-hydration, boiled deliberately soft to marry with the soup and release a wheaty aroma; the chicken-paitan side is paired with a firmer, thinner cut for a satisfying bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kai-dashi (shellfish) Soba — a clear, intensely savory clam-driven broth; the creamy Tori Paitan Soba is the equally celebrated second pillar, also offered as a limited tsukemen.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, low-temperature rare roast pork (belly/loin) served barely seasoned so the meat's own umami melts into the broth.
notable_toppings: house basil-flavored oil as a flavor-changer

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus wheat-based tare; no rice, konjac, or gluten-free noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Both signatures are animal-based — chicken paitan and a clam/shellfish-and-fish dashi — with rare pork chashu standard. No plant-broth (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, two-souled Kyoto bowl from a chef who trained at Osaka's celebrated Tori-soba ZAGIN: on one side a clear broth wrung from clams and shellfish that tastes of the sea — light yet quietly addictive — and on the other a velvety, blender-whipped chicken paitan with no barnyard funk. A dab of house basil oil is the signature flourish, and the lineage you taste is contemporary tori-paitan-meets-kaidashi rather than any old gotochi school.

places.name: ラーメンムギュ VOL.1本店 (Ramen Mugyu Vol.1 Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles ("chewy, thick noodles") in a chicken/Nagoya-Cochin broth blended with soy sauce (Japanese shoyu, which contains wheat). The signature bowls (Onibara White/Black, rich chicken soba) and the mazesoba all use wheat noodles, and the soy-sauce tare adds wheat. No celiac-safe option exists here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The broth is built on Nagoya Cochin chicken and chicken bones, and the shop is known above all for its char siu (roasted pork belly) — there are no plant-based ramen options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:30 (last order 22:00). Closes early when the day's char siu sells out. Official site lists open year-round; some listings note irregular closures, and one review noted a Wednesday closure, so it is best to confirm before a special trip.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Mugyu opened its Vol.1 main shop in the Enmachi area of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward in January 2017, just a couple of minutes' walk from JR Enmachi Station. It is a small, glass-fronted counter shop that has grown into a local favorite, since expanding to additional Mugyu branches

chef_bio.background: Ramen Mugyu opened its Vol.1 shop in the Enmachi area of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward on January 21, 2017, seconds' walk from JR Enmachi Station. Once a branch of an Osaka-Umeda lineage, it became the brand's main shop after the original stores closed, and the Mugyu name has since expanded to further branches around the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop sums itself up as '自慢のスープ、こだわりの焼き豚' — pride in the soup and devotion to the char siu. The kitchen leans on quality Nagoya Cochin chicken for a clean, deeply savory base, and a small flourish of minced (rather than sliced) onion adds a crisp texture in place of the usual scallion.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowls are the 'Onibara' series — Onibara White, a clear shio ramen built on a Nagoya Cochin broth blended with light (usukuchi) soy sauce, and Onibara Black, a darker soy version — alongside a richer thick chicken soba (濃厚鶏そば). All are crowned with the shop's celebrated char siu: roasted pork sliced generously over chewy, hand-massaged flat noodles. There is also a mazesoba finished with Nagoya-Cochin chicken oil.

anecdotes.text: The char siu is the shop's calling card — one Kyoto ramen reviewer rated the firm, meaty, fat-controlled roasted pork as their No.1 among Kyoto ramen shops, with four generous slices on a regular bowl.
The Onibara White shio bowl is praised for a clear, clean, refined taste, the Nagoya Cochin broth lifted by a blend of light soy sauce, with minced onion worked into the soup.
A musician credited as YAGI MASAHIKO (pen name X.L.H.) — a producer, composer and guitarist — created a theme song for the Mugyu brand, a quirky touch for a ramen shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Mugyu site gives the brand slogan, the X.L.H. theme song and the branch list; kyoto-gourmet documents the Nagoya-Cochin/light-soy Onibara White, the No.1-rated 4-slice char siu and minced onion; osumituki records the Jan-21-2017 Enmachi opening.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is described as Nagoya Cochin chicken blended with light soy sauce (薄口醤油); Japanese soy sauce is normally brewed with wheat, making the tare a wheat source on top of the wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is built on Nagoya Cochin chicken and chicken bones and the shop centers on roasted pork belly char siu, so all bowls contain animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual, popular neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is straightforward; little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu helps.

noodles.notes: Signature bowl uses flat hand-massaged (hira-uchi te-momi) noodles with a chewy, springy bite; the rich tonkotsu-tori soba is paired instead with thin straight noodles, and the maze-style bowl with chewy thick noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Onibara Shiro (おにばら 白) — a clear golden Nagoya Cochin chicken broth seasoned with a light shio/thin-soy tare, finished with finely minced onion folded into the soup itself rather than scattered on top. A black version (Onibara Kuro) swaps in Kyoto-style re-brewed soy for deeper sweetness, and a separate rich, cloudy chicken-and-vegetable tonkotsu-tori soba rounds out the lineup.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly finished firm rather than melt-soft; excess fat is trimmed so the slices keep a meaty chew and restrained sweetness — the shop's proudest element (also sold over rice as the Mugyu-don). The signature bowl carries roughly four slices.
notable_toppings: minced onion blended into the broth
optional nira (chive) paste with mustard seasoning

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus soy-based tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on Nagoya Cochin chicken broth and topped with braised pork belly; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto chicken-clear specialist whose soul is pure Nagoya Cochin: a refined golden chintan where minced onion is dissolved into the soup for natural sweetness and a delicate, oniony crispness instead of being piled on as a garnish. It sits in the contemporary jidori-shio lineage rather than Kyoto's heavy se-abura tradition, letting the bird and a whisper of light soy carry the bowl.

places.name: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ ソラ (Ramen-so Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero Sora)
places.gf_detail: This is a Jiro-style ramen shop: the bowl is built on thick wheat noodles in a sweet-soy (wheat shoyu) tonkotsu broth, so it is not celiac-safe. No gluten-free noodles, broth, or accommodations are mentioned anywhere. A celiac diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: A pork-based Jiro-style shop — tonkotsu (pork-bone) broth, pork back-fat (アブラ), char siu, and pork toppings are central to every bowl. No vegan or vegetarian options are mentioned. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Tue/Thu/Sat 11:00-14:45, 18:00-23:45; Wed/Fri 18:00-23:45; Sun 11:00-21:45; Mon closed. (Some listings give the broader window as 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-24:00 weekdays, Sun 11:00-22:00.)
places.neighborhood: 右京区 西院 (Saiin, Ukyo Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sora is the Saiin branch of the Kyoto Jiro-style chain Ramen-so Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero, which grew out of the group's roots in Kyoto's Fushimi area. This Ukyo Ward location opened on April 24, 2017.

chef_bio.background: Sora is the Saiin branch of the Kyoto Jiro-style chain Ramen-so Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero, which grew out of the group's roots in Kyoto's Fushimi area. This Ukyo Ward location opened on April 24, 2017.
chef_bio.philosophy: Classic inspired-by-Jiro abundance: pile it high, let the diner dial in garlic, vegetables and fat to taste.
chef_bio.specialty: Jiro-style ramen built on thick, chewy house-made noodles (ワシワシとした自家製極太麺) under a towering mound of bean sprouts and cabbage, with char siu, garlic and pork back-fat in a sweet-soy tonkotsu broth. The regular bowl comes with 300g of noodles (a small is 200g), scaling up further for big appetites.

anecdotes.text: Ordering uses the Jiro-style call system, and this chain has its own signature extra-fat call nicknamed 'chomo' that regulars layer onto the standard ニンニク/ヤサイ/アブラ chant — a call that only works at this group's shops.
The Saiin shop opened April 24, 2017 as a branch of the group with roots in Kyoto's Fushimi area.
Even on a rainy day the counter drew about a 40-minute wait, a sign of its local following.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Osumituki records the April-24-2017 Saiin opening and Fushimi-rooted lineage; noodlers.jp documents the house-made 極太 noodles, the 300g-regular/200g-small portioning, the chain-only 'chomo' fat call, and the ~40-minute rainy-day wait.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth/tare is a sweet soy-flavored tonkotsu, which in Jiro-style ramen relies on wheat-containing shoyu; combined with the wheat noodles this bowl is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork is fundamental — tonkotsu broth, pork back-fat (アブラ), and char siu — so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, ticket-machine Jiro-style counter shop run for regulars; you order by calling out toppings in Japanese (ニンニク garlic, ヤサイ vegetables, アブラ fat, カラメ stronger flavor), so a little Japanese and knowing the system helps a lot.

noodles.notes: Flat, thick, springy chewy noodles (平打ち太麺) with a crisp 'puri-patsu' bite, classic Jiro lineage. Free noodle increases (200g/300g/400g) at a fixed topping price.

ramen.signature_bowl: Buta Ramen (pork ramen) — order with the call for yasai (vegetables), ninniku (garlic), abura (back-fat) and karame (extra tare): the full Jiro experience with a mountain of bean sprouts and slabs of pork.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, heavily seasoned braised pork (buta) slabs — tender, fatty, generously portioned in the pork-ramen build.
notable_toppings: one-flavor chili powder over the veg

gf.note: Wheat-based thick noodles and a shoyu (wheat) tare; a fat-laden Jiro bowl with no documented rice or konjac noodle option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Pork tonkotsu broth, pork back-fat, shoyu tare and pork chashu throughout. No plant-based broth option documented; the 'vegetable' call is bean sprouts piled into a meat soup.

ramen.pantheon: A full-throttle bowl in the Ramen Jiro lineage: a thick, glossy pork-tonkotsu shoyu soup slicked with melted back-fat, buried under a towering mound of bean sprouts and raw garlic, with flat chewy noodles built to stand up to it all. The seasoning stays restrained so the porky dashi depth carries the bowl — comfort and excess in equal measure.

places.name: 麺飯食堂 てら田や (Menhan Shokudo Teradaya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The core dishes are wheat-noodle chuka-soba and ramen, and the shoyu/soy-based tare and seasonings almost certainly contain wheat. The signature karaage is flour-coated fried chicken. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no celiac accommodation is advertised. A celiac diner cannot eat the ramen here; call ahead (075-432-8878) if you want to ask about anything, but assume cross-contamination throughout a small single-operator kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The broth is built on pork clear soup plus fish/seafood dashi, bowls come with chashu, wonton and egg, and the headline side is fried chicken. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option. Vegans should choose a different restaurant.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 18:00–22:00 (Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat, Sun). Closed Thursdays plus irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: 中京区壬生坊城町 (Shijo-Omiya / Omiya / Nijo)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Teradaya started life as a wildly popular karaage popup operating out of a shared kitchen (kashikiri / magari) near Kyoto's Sanjo-kai shopping arcade. After the fried chicken built a following, the owner added ramen to the lineup, and on July 1, 2023 the business graduated to its

chef_bio.background: Teradaya started life as a wildly popular karaage popup operating out of a shared kitchen near Kyoto's Sanjo-kai shopping arcade. After the fried chicken built a following, the owner added ramen to the lineup, and on July 1, 2023 the business graduated to its own storefront in the Mibu-Bojo No. 2 housing complex near Shijo-Omiya, rebranding as a sit-down menhan shokudo (a noodle-and-rice diner) while keeping the Teradaya name.
chef_bio.philosophy: A friendly, unpretentious diner doing two things well: a balanced, light-leaning chuka-soba and seriously good fried chicken, with rice bowls and beer to round out a casual meal.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the tokusei chuka-soba: a pork clear broth (chintan) layered with a fish-forward Japanese wafu dashi for a clean, restrained umami, finished with low-hydration, slightly angular noodles prized for a smooth slurp. The 'special' version is a generous spread of extra chashu, three wontons and a soft egg. Alongside it, Teradaya's original soy-garlic karaage — four big, juicy, piping-hot pieces — remains the shop's calling card.

anecdotes.text: The owner has been described, with affection, as looking more like a hip-hop musician than a traditional ramen-ya master.
The karaage is the dish that made the name: four large, soy-garlic-marinated pieces served sizzling and juicy — the kind of plate locals call 'beer-progressing.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi documents Teradaya's Sanjo-kai karaage-popup origins, the July-1-2023 move to the Mibu-Bojo No.2 complex near Shijo-Omiya, the tokusei chuka-soba (pork chintan + fish dashi, with extra chashu/3 wontons/soft egg), the 4-piece soy-garlic karaage, and the solo owner's hip-hop look.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/chuka-soba noodles are the core product, boiled in shared water; the soy-based tare and seasonings almost certainly contain wheat. Not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth/tare is built on soy-based seasoning (chuka-soba shoyu profile), which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork clear soup plus fish/seafood dashi; bowls carry chashu, wonton and egg. No vegan options and pervasive animal stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood noodle-and-rice diner run largely by one operator; little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will smooth ordering.

noodles.notes: Straight medium-thick, low-hydration noodles built for nodogoshi (throat-clearance) over heavy chew, with a clean, slightly firm bite that suits the clear soup; the same noodle is used for both the chuka-soba and the se-abura ramen.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Ramen (special back-fat shoyu ramen) — pork clear broth crowned with generous se-abura, loaded with extra chashu, three handmade wonton, and a soft-set ajitama; the Tokusei Chuka-soba (pork clear broth + fish dashi) is the cleaner alternative.
ramen.chashu: Large, slightly thick-cut braised pork slices — moist and tender, soaking up the soy broth, with full pork sweetness; available as a chashu-men topped variant.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on pork clear broth, with fish dashi in the chuka-soba and pork back-fat and chashu throughout. No plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful heir to Kyoto's beloved se-abura chacha lineage: a soy-forward pork chintan dusted with sweet pearls of back-fat that melt into a mellow, gingery depth — the same hometown soul you taste in the city's classic Omiya-area shops. The companion chuka-soba leans cleaner, marrying that pork clarity with a quiet wave of fish dashi.

places.name: 頑固麺 (Gankomen)
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners. This is a classic chicken-pork-bone ramen shop: the noodles are wheat (sourced from noodle maker Menya Teigaku / 麺屋棣鄂), and the broths and tare almost certainly use wheat-based soy sauce, especially the 濃厚醤油 (rich soy) bowls. There are no gluten-free options and no indication of allergen handling. Avoid if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Every bowl is built on a chicken-and-pork-bone (とりとんこつ) base, with niboshi (dried sardine) used in the 煮干し variant and pork chashu, pork-fat (背脂) mazesoba, and karaage on the menu. Nothing on the menu is plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (lunch only); closed Mondays. Note: one local article listed extended evening hours (11:00-14:30 / 17:30-22:00) and "Monday Ganko Men" special events, but the consistent current listing is lunch-only.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 頑固麺 sits in the Fukakusa stretch of Fushimi, one of Kyoto's most competitive ramen districts, a short walk from Fujinomori Station and the Meishin/Route 24 area. The owner is said to have trained at a well-regarded Fushimi-Momoyama ramen shop before striking out on his own, and t

chef_bio.background: 頑固麺 sits in the Fukakusa stretch of Fushimi, one of Kyoto's most competitive ramen districts, a short walk from Fujinomori Station. The owner trained at a well-regarded Fushimi-Momoyama ramen shop before striking out on his own, and the place has become a lunchtime fixture that draws lines even on weekdays despite being open only midday.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name plays on 頑固 ('stubborn'); the kitchen leans into a single-minded pursuit of a fully-realized, concentrated bowl. A house condiment nicknamed the 'Ganko sauce' is offered as a flavor intensifier so diners can dial the broth up to one of the densest in Kyoto.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a thick chicken-pork-bone bowl (とりとんこつ): chicken slowly simmered down to a rich, almost paste-thick consistency, with pork umami arriving on the finish, yet kept clean and approachable rather than heavy. It is paired with snappy medium-thin wavy noodles from Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂), topped with large rare chashu, menma, onion and a hit of mayu (burnt-garlic oil). Variations include 煮干しとりとんこつ (with dried sardine), 濃厚醤油とりとんこつ (rich soy), 鶏中華そば, and a back-fat 背脂 mazesoba.

anecdotes.text: The chef is reported to have trained at a popular Fushimi-Momoyama ramen shop before opening his own counter in the Fukakusa ramen battleground.
The 'Ganko sauce' on the table lets guests intensify the broth into what one guide calls one of Kyoto's most concentrated bowls.
The broth is built by slowly simmering chicken for richness and body, with pork umami lingering in the finish, yet staying clean without off-flavors.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi (g2OHY, RJYMT) covers the Fushimi-Momoyama training, the 'Ganko sauce', the 麺屋棣鄂 medium-thin wavy noodles, and mayu/large-chashu/menma/onion toppings; kyoto-gourmet confirms the chicken-forward tori-tonkotsu broth, the menu variations, and the lunch-only (11:00–14:00) hours near Fujinomori Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl, sourced from noodle maker Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂); shared noodle-boiling water means heavy gluten cross-contact throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The lineup includes 濃厚醤油とりとんこつ (rich soy chicken-pork-bone) and a 背脂醤油 mazesoba, indicating wheat-based soy tare is used in the broths/seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are chicken-and-pork-bone based, with niboshi (dried sardine), pork chashu, back-fat and karaage on the menu; there is no plant-based preparation line.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy information is provided by the shop or in available write-ups.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, ticket-machine ramen counter where lines form at lunch; little to no English is expected, so basic Japanese or pointing at the ticket machine helps.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick noodles (some descriptions straight, some slightly wavy) built to grip the near-sludgy broth; sourced from a noodle maker, served firm by default. A few special bowls use extra-thick flat high-hydration noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-tonkotsu ramen — the thick, creamy chicken-and-pork-bone bowl in shoyu tare that the shop is known for. A niboshi tori-tonkotsu (dried-sardine version) and limited tsukemen also rotate through.
ramen.chashu: Large, generously cut rare-style chashu — tender, low-temperature pork left pink in the center with crisp rendered fat edges.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered chicken-and-pork-bone broth finished with chicken fat and topped with pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto thick-soup bowl: long-boiled chicken and pork bones emulsified into a near-sludgy, glossy paitan, then seasoned with a deeply aromatic soy tare so the richness lands full but clean. It belongs to the contemporary kotteri tori-tonkotsu wave rather than any one regional lineage, with the umami of the bones — not the seasoning — carrying the soul.

places.name: 麺処 虵の目屋 (Mendokoro Janomeya)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls use the shop's own house-made Japanese wheat noodles, and the soy-based (鶏そば) bowls use wheat soy-sauce tare; no gluten-free noodle or dedicated prep is offered. Even the salt/paitan bowls are served with wheat noodles. Avoid if you require strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken specialist — every signature bowl is built on 100% jidori (local chicken) broth made from chicken gara, whole chicken and water, with chicken/duck chashu toppings. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner came to ramen from fine dining: he started his career in French cuisine and trained at several restaurants before opening his own shop, and he brings those classical kitchen techniques to every bowl.

chef_bio.origin: The shop moved to its current 2nd-floor spot on the narrow Hanayu-koji lane just off Kawaramachi, reopening on December 8, 2020.
chef_bio.background: The owner came to ramen from fine dining: he started his career in French cuisine and trained at several restaurants before opening his own shop, and he brings those classical kitchen techniques to every bowl.
chef_bio.philosophy: Radical simplicity in service of the bird: the broth is built from nothing but chicken bones (gara), whole chicken, and water, letting the flavor of 100% jidori (local heritage chicken) speak for itself. Toppings are charcoal-grilled to add aroma rather than mask the broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Chicken in every form. The two pillars are the 鶏そば — a clear (清湯) chicken broth with a sharp, bright wheat-soy edge and deep chicken-oil richness — and the 鶏白湯, a creamy, concentrated chicken-paitan bowl. A jidori salt bowl rounds out the lineup, all served over house-made Japanese wheat noodles with low-temperature chicken chashu and charcoal-grilled toppings.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on the owner's French-cooking discipline — low-temperature-cooked chicken chashu and charcoal-grilled aromatics show a chef applying restaurant technique to a bowl of ramen.
The house philosophy is summed up as using 'only gara, whole chicken, and water' so the chicken itself carries the flavor.
Reviewers single out the 鶏そば for its balance of bright soy seasoning over a clear chicken broth with real chicken-oil depth, with a side of tori-meshi (chicken rice) as the classic pairing.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Leaf Kyoto describes the owner's French-cuisine background, the 100%-jidori 'gara/whole-chicken/water' broth, low-temp chicken chashu and charcoal-grilled toppings; hikonan-biog reviews the 鶏そば with tori-meshi; osumituki records the Dec-8-2020 reopening on the 2nd floor of Hanayu-koji.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are served over the shop's own house-made Japanese wheat noodles, so wheat is intrinsic to every dish; there is no gluten-free option.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature 鶏そば is a soy-seasoned (醤油) chicken broth; standard Japanese shoyu tare contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are 100% chicken (gara, whole chicken, water) with chicken/duck chashu toppings; there is no meat-free preparation, so any bowl will contain animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small counter-and-table ramen shop tucked on the 2nd floor of a narrow shopping lane; ordering is straightforward but basic Japanese (or a translation app) helps, and it is cash/PayPay only.

noodles.notes: Homemade Japanese wheat noodles. The tori paitan is paired with a medium, flat, smooth noodle prized for its slick throat-feel; the lighter chicken-niboshi soba gets a thinner, straight noodle with a crisp bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori Soba (clear 100% jidori chicken shoyu soba); the rich Tori Paitan is the other half of the house's identity, with a Shio Tori Soba and a chicken-niboshi soba rounding out the lineup.
ramen.chashu: Two proteins: low-temperature-cooked chicken plus pork, finished over charcoal to add a smoky, savory aroma; thinly sliced pork loin is served rare alongside tender chicken breast.

gf.note: Noodles are homemade wheat-and-kansui and the tare is soy-based; nothing rice/konjac-noodle or GF documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken bones and whole birds (and a chicken-niboshi version adds dried sardine), with chicken/pork chashu; no plant-broth bowl exists.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto chicken specialist where a French-trained chef coaxes everything from 100% local jidori — just bones, whole birds, and water — into either a clear, soy-sharpened tori soba or a creamy, concentrated tori paitan. You taste pure poultry rather than any regional lineage, finished with the smoke of charcoal-grilled chashu.

places.name: 京都千丸 しゃかりき 本店 (Kyoto Senmaru Shakariki Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a tsukemen and ramen specialist: wheat-flour noodles served with a pork-bone-and-fish (niboshi) double broth and a tare that is virtually always wheat-based soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, broth, or accommodation, and no dedicated preparation area. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The shop is built around an animal-heavy broth (tonkotsu pork bone plus dried-fish niboshi). The signature bowls are all meat/fish based and there is no listed vegetarian or vegan option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and dinner service in the evening; closed Wednesdays plus occasional irregular closures. Sources differ slightly on evening hours: one lists 17:30-21:00, another lists 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Confirm on the day of visit.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku, Jurakumawari / Senbon-Marutamachi (northwest of Nijō Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2004 near Senbon-Marutamachi, just northwest of Nijo Castle, Shakariki built its name as one of the early standard-bearers for tsukemen (dipping noodles) in Kyoto, a city long defined by its own back-fat shoyu ramen tradition. It has run in the same neighborhood spot fo
