chef_bio.background: Opened in 2004 near Senbon-Marutamachi, just northwest of Nijo Castle, Shakariki built its name as one of the early standard-bearers for tsukemen (dipping noodles) in Kyoto, a city long defined by its own back-fat shoyu ramen tradition. It has run in the same neighborhood spot for over two decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: An obsessive, customize-to-the-bowl approach: rather than one fixed soup, the kitchen offers five broth variations and matches noodle gauge to each, described as a dedication to the single bowl that sets aside pure business calculation.
chef_bio.specialty: The house style is a rich seafood-and-pork double soup: dried sardine (niboshi) aromatics layered over a deep tonkotsu pork-bone base. The flagship is the 濃厚魚介 (rich seafood) tsukemen, served with thick noodles, and the shop deliberately prepares several broth intensities from light to ultra-rich, each matched to a different noodle thickness.

anecdotes.text: Shakariki is credited as a tsukemen pioneer in Kyoto and as a training ground whose lineage includes the Michelin-recognized ramen shop Touhichi (らぁ麺 とうひち) and the related shop Isshoji Boogie, marking it as an influential mentor in the local ramen scene.

The shop draws a notably broad crowd; observers describe not just male regulars but women and even elementary-school children happily slurping the dipping noodles, a sign of how approachable the rich broth is.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Genjitsutouhi documents the 2004 opening near Nijo, Shakariki's status as a Kyoto tsukemen pioneer, its five broths matched to noodle gauges, the niboshi+tonkotsu 濃厚魚介 tsukemen, its mentor lineage to Michelin-recognized らぁ麺とうひち and Isshoji Boogie, and its broad clientele.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a tsukemen and ramen shop, the dipping tare is virtually certain to be standard wheat-based Japanese soy sauce, and the noodles are wheat flour; no gluten-free preparation is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and dried-fish (niboshi) double soup; all bowls are animal-based with no listed vegetarian or vegan option, and shared broth/equipment makes cross-contact unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the counter-style shop is used to a broad, family-friendly clientele, so non-Japanese-speaking visitors should manage comfortably.

noodles.notes: Extra-thick, chewy wavy noodles (極太ちぢれ麺) built to grab and hold the thick dipping broth; springy, mochi-mochi bite. Upsize available.

ramen.signature_bowl: 濃厚魚介つけそば (Nōkō Gyokai Tsukesoba) — extra-thick wavy noodles dipped in a thick, concentrated fish-and-pork dipping broth, with the optional 超濃厚 (super-rich) upgrade.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds served together: a moist braised pork chashu cut from a meaty, juicy portion, plus a chicken chashu — both kept tender rather than dry.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare and pork/fish broth; no rice or konjac noodle option documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on a pork-bone and chicken double soup loaded with niboshi and katsuo fish dashi, plus pork/chicken chashu. No plant-based broth documented.

ramen.pantheon: In a city where ramen means glossy se-abura shoyu, this shop carved its own lane: a thick gyokai-tonkotsu W-soup that marries pork-and-chicken body with the deep, slightly bitter punch of niboshi (maiwashi, sanma, sagoshi) and a finishing dust of fish powder. You taste that fish-forward double-soup lineage most in the tsukemen, where extra-thick noodles drag up a dip you stretch out with a hit of katsuo soup-wari at the end.

places.name: 本家 第一旭 たかばし本店 (Honke Daiichi Asahi Takabashi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Classic Kyoto chuka-soba: wheat noodles plus a wheat-based raw soy sauce (Fushimi nama-shoyu) tare in a pork-bone broth. No gluten-free noodles or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: There is a "肉なし（野菜入）ラーメン" (no-chashu, vegetable) bowl, but it is still served in the pork-bone (chintan) broth with wheat noodles and wheat soy-sauce tare, so it is neither vegan nor vegetarian. No plant-based broth option exists.
places.hours_raw: 6:00-1:00 the next day / closed Thursdays
places.neighborhood: Shimogyo Ward, east of Kyoto Stn (Takabashi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Honke Daiichi Asahi originated in 1947 (Showa 22). Founder Yuji Taguchi learned ramen-making while helping at the neighboring Shinpuku Saikan (founded 1938), regarded as the source of Kyoto ramen, and first opened the popular eatery 'Asahi Shokudo.' When the ramen he served proved unexpectedly popular, he renamed it 'Daiichi Asahi' in 1956 and converted it into a dedicated ramen shop, which is the start of its present form. The main shop stands in Takabashi, about a 5-minute walk from the Karasuma Central Exit of Kyoto Station.

chef_bio.origin: 京都・下京区／京都駅東（高橋）
chef_bio.background: 本家 第一旭の起こりは1947年（昭和22年）。創業者・田口有司が、京都ラーメンの源流とされる隣の「新福菜館」（1938年創業）を手伝いながらラーメンづくりを学び、まず大衆食堂『旭食堂』を開いた。ところが供したラーメンが思いのほか評判を呼び、1956年に『第一旭』と改名してラーメン専門店へと業態転換したのが現在の姿の始まり。京都駅烏丸中央口から徒歩約5分、高橋（たかばし）の地に本店を構える。
chef_bio.philosophy: 夜明けの最初の光（第一旭）のように京都で輝く一杯を——白濁させない澄んだスープと無添加・素材本位の味を、守り続け磨き続ける。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は素朴な一杯の「ラーメン」、そして人気の「特製ラーメン」。豚骨を白濁させる前に引き上げて澄ませた清湯（ちんたん）スープに、京都・伏見の無添加生醤油のタレを合わせ、ニンニクや野菜の臭み消しに頼らない澄んだ醤油味に仕立てる。麺は近藤製麺の特注ブレンドの中太ストレート。チャーシューは脂が控えめで旨みの濃い国産雌豚を使い、赤身（赤）か脂身（白）かを無料で指定できる。たっぷりの京都・九条ねぎを惜しみなく盛るのも持ち味。

anecdotes.text: 京都ラーメンの真の源流とされる老舗『新福菜館』（1938年創業）が本店のすぐ隣に並び、二軒は定休日をあえてずらして（第一旭は木曜、新福菜館は水曜）80年近く隣同士で共存してきた。両店をはしごする『たかばしはしご』は地元の楽しみ方として親しまれている。
年間およそ24万人が訪れる京都ラーメンの代表格で、早朝から行列ができることでも知られる。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: iekei.shop details the 1947 founding by 田口有司, who learned ramen while helping the neighboring 新福菜館 (1938), opened 旭食堂 and renamed it 第一旭 in 1956, plus the staggered Thu/Wed days off and the たかばしはしご custom; PR Times states ~240,000 annual visitors and constant early-morning lines.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and served in a pork-bone broth seasoned with wheat-based soy-sauce tare — no separation from gluten is possible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare uses additive-free raw soy sauce (nama-shoyu) from Fushimi, Kyoto, which is brewed from wheat and soybeans — it contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is pork-bone (chintan) for every bowl, including the no-chashu 'vegetable' ramen, so no bowl is free of animal product.

positives.text: Many free customizations (noodle firmness, broth richness, soy intensity, chashu lean/fatty, extra negi and bean sprouts) let diners adjust a bowl, though none remove the core wheat/pork allergens.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, perpetually busy Kyoto-Station ramen institution with ticket-machine ordering, shared seating when crowded, and frequent foreign visitors — a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will carry you through.

noodles.notes: Custom medium-gauge straight noodles from a local seimen (Kondo) maker, designed to bind cleanly with the clear pork-shoyu broth. Firm but not low-hydration.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashumen (チャーシューメン) — the standard chuka-soba piled with extra sliced pork; ask for a mound of nine-jo green onion (free negi-mori) and red (lean) or white (fatty) chashu.
ramen.chashu: Braised sliced loin/leg from rare domestic female pigs (twice-farrowed sows), lean and deeply savory rather than fatty; diners may request "aka" (red/lean) or "shiro" (white/fatty) cuts at no charge.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a raw soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is offered. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is 100% pork bone and the bowl is built on pork chashu and rendered pork fat; no plant-based broth or topping option exists.

ramen.pantheon: This is the wellspring of Kyoto ramen: a clear, golden 100%-pork-bone broth seasoned with raw, unpasteurized Fushimi soy sauce and glossed with the lard that rises naturally off the kettle. Deceptively light at first sip, it reveals a deep, gentle pork-shoyu soul beneath a snowy heap of nine-jo green onion — the 1947 archetype that spawned Kyoto's whole pork-shoyu lineage.

places.name: 麦の夜明け (Mugi no Yoake)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The whole concept is wheat: custom thin/flat wheat noodles milled from a specific wheat variety, served in a dashi-shoyu broth (soy-sauce tare typically contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle or menu option is offered. Anyone strictly avoiding gluten should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian place. The signature bowls are built on chicken, pork and seafood dashi (and feature scallop, beef or duck), so the broths and toppings are animal-based. No vegan ramen is advertised.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:00-14:00; Tue-Fri 11:00-15:00 & 18:00-20:00; Sat 11:00-15:00 & 17:30-20:00; Sun 10:30-14:00. Times listed are open hours; last orders earlier. Hours and closures are irregular — check the shop's SNS before visiting. (Some listings show different/condensed hours, e.g. closed Mondays and Sunday evenings.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner-chef is a ramen obsessive who started a ramen-research club while at Doshisha University, then trained at respected shops (including Tsurumusha and Menya Yutaka). Before opening, he went to work for a major flour milling company so he could understand wheat and noodle-m

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef is a ramen obsessive who started a ramen-research club while at Doshisha University, then trained at respected shops (including Tsurumusha and Menya Yutaka). Before opening, he went to work for a major flour milling company so he could understand wheat and noodle-making from the grain up — and that wheat-first knowledge is built into everything the shop serves.
chef_bio.philosophy: Pepper as a Kyoto signature, and wheat treated with a miller's precision: the house noodles are thin and flat, milled from a specific wheat variety, with a smooth, almost inaniwa-udon-like texture meant to match the delicate broth rather than overpower it.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the scallop-and-sansho chuka-soba: a clear, refined dashi-shoyu built from chicken, pork and seafood, finished with the bright, citrus-tingly bite of sansho (Japanese pepper). A beef-shoyu-and-sansho version and a sansho tsukemen round out the core lineup, with seasonal limited bowls (a straw-grilled duck-and-sansho ramen, chilled corn ramen, and a sesame cold tsukemen) appearing through the year.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened February 17, 2022 and earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand almost immediately — less than a year in — with the chef in his twenties, described as the youngest to be recognized in the ramen category in Michelin history.

The straw-grilled duck-and-sansho limited bowl is so sought-after that it can sell out almost as soon as the shop opens — one reviewer heard the 'sold out' call while still waiting to be served; reviewers single out the powerful aroma of the straw-fired duck.
Before opening, the chef worked for a flour milling company to master milling so he could develop the shop's custom thin, flat noodles from a chosen wheat variety.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Genjitsutouhi profiles the chef's Doshisha ramen-club start, training at Tsurumusha and Menya Yutaka, his flour-milling stint and the resulting inaniwa-like thin flat noodles, the Feb-17-2022 opening, the near-immediate Bib Gourmand (youngest in the ramen category in Michelin history), and the fast-selling straw-grilled duck-sansho limited bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire kitchen is built around wheat: custom wheat noodles are the centerpiece and are made and handled throughout the space, so flour/gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are dashi-shoyu (soy-sauce based). Standard ramen soy-sauce tare typically contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from chicken, pork and seafood dashi and bowls feature scallop, beef or duck, so there is no animal-free preparation here.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat counter ramen-ya run by a serious young chef; little to no English is expected, so a few words of Japanese and following counter etiquette (one bowl per seat) will go a long way.

noodles.notes: House-milled noodles cut chuhoso (medium-thin) and straight, made from Yamaguchi-prefecture 'Setokirara' wheat. The owner came from the flour-milling trade, and the noodle work is a point of pride: a smooth, springy strand with clean bite, tuned to carry the delicate clear broth without overpowering it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hotate to Sansho no Chuka Soba — scallop-and-sansho chuka soba: a pale, clear triple-soup of jidori chicken, pork and dried scallop seasoned with a white-soy/salt tare, finished with the floral citrus tingle of Wakayama grape sansho. The special (tokusei) adds the full topping spread.
ramen.chashu: Two proteins: a low-temperature-cooked pork (rosu/loin style, served as tender pink slices) alongside braised duck breast — a refined, restraint-forward pairing rather than a fatty braise.
notable_toppings: torch-seared Hokkaido raw scallop marinated in soy (the signature crown)
grape sansho pepper / sansho-kombu paste added to taste

gf.note: House-made wheat noodles ('Setokirara' wheat) plus a soy-based tare and wonton skins make this bowl wheat-throughout; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is an animal-and-seafood broth (chicken, pork, dried scallop) with seafood-driven umami, topped with scallop, duck and pork. No plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto bowl that rewrites chuka-soba in a fine-dining key: instead of the city's classic pork-back-fat shoyu, it ladles a crystal-clear chicken-pork-and-scallop soup whose sweetness is pulled forward by a whisper of white-soy salt. The defining signature is Wakayama grape sansho — a floral, citrus-numbing lift that turns a delicate seafood broth into something unmistakably its own.

places.name: ギオンダックヌードル (Gion Duck Noodles)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The house-made noodles are made with German rye and whole-grain (wheat) flour — rye and wheat both contain gluten. The shoyu tare in this style of ramen is almost certainly wheat-based, and no gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised. People with celiac disease or wheat allergy should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept is built on a free-range duck (Kishu/紀州鴨) broth, with duck-meat toppings (loin or thigh) and marinated egg. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:30 (last order 21:00), open daily (no regular closing days)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A duck-ramen specialist tucked into a narrow Gion back alley a few minutes from Gion-Shijo Station and Yasaka Shrine. It is run by a French-trained chef who brings a fine-dining sensibility to ramen, and is a sister shop to the acclaimed Osaka (Hirakata) noodle bar Menroku (麺麓),

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Gion), as a sister location to Osaka's Menroku
chef_bio.background: A duck-ramen specialist tucked into a narrow Gion back alley a few minutes from Gion-Shijo Station and Yasaka Shrine. It is run by a French-trained chef who brings a fine-dining sensibility to ramen, and is a sister shop to the acclaimed Osaka (Hirakata) noodle bar Menroku (麺麓), which opened in December 2018.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'restaurant-like' (レストランのような) approach to ramen: refined, clean umami over heaviness, an unusually pure duck aroma, and a minimalist identity expressed through an emoji-only sign rather than traditional ramen-shop branding.
chef_bio.specialty: Duck ramen built on a clear, intensely aromatic broth made from branded free-range Kishu duck (紀州鴨) and kombu, served with house-made noodles milled with German rye and whole-grain flour. Diners choose their duck topping — loin (ロース) or thigh (もも) — and there are rich-white-broth tsukemen variations finished with fruit sauces (cherry or mandarin) plus a Western-style duck pilaf.

anecdotes.text: The shop's sign is composed entirely of emoji (a duck and a bowl of ramen) rather than any written name, fitting its understated, fine-dining-inspired identity.
Reviewers single out the broth for a depth of duck aroma rarely found elsewhere — 'the first dish where I felt the fragrance of duck to this degree.'
Organic grape sansho (ブドウ山椒) is offered as a flavor-changer mid-bowl.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi and gcjapan describe the French-trained chef, the sister relationship to Osaka/Hirakata's Menroku (麺麓, Dec 2018), the Kishu-duck + kombu clear broth, German-rye whole-grain noodles, the loin/thigh choice, fruit-sauce tsukemen and Western duck pilaf, the emoji-only sign, and the organic grape-sansho flavor changer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No ingredient breakdown of the tare/soy sauce is published; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based, so wheat should be assumed present.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff are available and the shop regularly serves international visitors, so foreign diners can order comfortably.

noodles.notes: Extremely thin (~1.1mm) handmade straight noodles made daily from German rye whole-grain flour, giving a distinctive grain-forward texture; clings to the duck broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Duck (kamo) ramen: a clear, deeply aromatic broth built on premium Kishu/Wakayama brand duck, served with duck loin and thigh chashu and finished tableside with organic grape sansho. Eat plain first, then stir in the sansho for a bright, citrus-fragrant lift.
ramen.chashu: Duck chashu, offered as loin or thigh — thick-cut, tender slices of brand Kishu duck rather than the usual pork.

gf.note: Handmade noodles are made from German rye flour (wheat-family gluten grain) and the seasoning is a shoyu-based tare, so the bowl is not celiac-safe. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is duck broth and duck chashu, and the optional siphon dashi adds bonito/kombu/shiitake. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, French-trained original rather than any regional gotochi style: where most ramen leans on pork or chicken, here the soul is duck — a clear, elegant kamo shoyu broth perfumed with the bird and lifted by citrusy grape sansho. The rye noodles and tableside fruit-and-wine tsukemen sauces make it one of Kyoto's most refined, chef-driven bowls.

places.name: 新宿めんや風花 (Shinjuku Menya Fuhka)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic ramen shop built on hand-kneaded wheat noodles, served in clear salt (shio) broths blending animal and seafood/vegetable stocks. Even the salt-forward bowls rely on wheat noodles, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented. A diner with celiac disease should not eat here; gluten-avoiders by preference would still be consuming wheat noodles in every bowl.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broths are built on animal stock (the menu includes charcoal-grilled local-chicken ramen and duck-salt ramen) combined with seafood and vegetable stocks, and bowls are topped with chashu pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or documented.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists 11:30–13:50 and 18:00–21:00 daily; some guides list the bands slightly wider (lunch to 14:00, dinner to 22:00). Closures are irregular and announced on the shop's X/Twitter.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born in 1954, Uemura worked for a fish company before moving to Kansai and running several restaurants. He opened Shinjuku Menya Fuhka in Kyoto in 2001 at age 47, at a time when salt ramen was virtually unknown in a city dominated by rich, fatty pork-bone broths — many people war

chef_bio.background: Born in 1954, Uemura worked for a fish company before moving to Kansai and running several restaurants. He opened Shinjuku Menya Fuhka in Kyoto in 2001 at age 47, at a time when salt ramen was virtually unknown in a city dominated by rich, fatty pork-bone broths — many people warned him the concept would fail. He briefly closed early on to refine his recipe after realizing Kyoto's hot, humid summer (the shop opened in August) shifted customers' taste away from the cold-northern, heavily-salted style he had trained in.
chef_bio.philosophy: Uemura rejects a profit-first approach, saying he doesn't think of ramen as a money-making tool and prioritizing quality over margin. He wants guests to taste attentively — phones and reading are discouraged at the counter, and children under elementary-school age are not admitted because he wants diners with a developed enough palate to appreciate the subtlety of the broth.
chef_bio.specialty: The 天然塩らーめん (natural-salt ramen) is the signature bowl — a clear, golden broth built on a rare cooking technique. Uemura whisks egg whites with salt, heats them and repeatedly skims the surface foam, drawing off harshness until a mild, refined clarity remains, then blends animal and vegetable stocks behind it. He builds the salt itself from Ishigaki Island salt blended with two other salts, and the noodles are kneaded by hand each day. The duck-salt ramen, charcoal-grilled local-chicken ramen, and salt tsukemen round out the menu, and the long-cooked, tender chashu is a frequent favorite.

anecdotes.text: When Uemura opened in 2001, Kyoto ramen meant heavy, fatty pork-bone broth and salt ramen barely existed locally; people told him it would fail, and he briefly closed to re-tune his recipe after learning that Kyoto's humid August climate changed how the broth landed on customers' palates compared with the cold northern region where he trained.
His broth uses an uncommon clarifying technique — egg whites whisked with salt, heated, and repeatedly skimmed until the harsh saltiness is drawn off — to leave a mild, transparent golden soup.
He recalculates his hand-made noodle recipe to the flour and the season's humidity and stays in regular contact with the mill, noting the differences between Kansai and Kanto flour.
Now in his early 70s (born 1954), he has been trying to train a successor since around 2022, though earlier apprentices gave up during the demanding broth-preparation stage.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: k4-media interviews Uemura (b. 1954): ex-fish-company, several Kansai restaurants, opened Fuhka in 2001 at 47 as a Kyoto salt-ramen pioneer; it details the August-opening climate stumble and reformulation, the egg-white salt-clarifying technique, Ishigaki + two salts, daily hand-kneaded noodles, the no-phones/no-young-children counter rules, and the successor search underway since ~2022.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Every bowl is built on hand-kneaded wheat noodles; no gluten-free option or allergen handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chashu and some bowls carry a soy-sauce element typical of ramen, which is normally wheat-based; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal- and seafood-based (chicken/duck plus seafood and vegetable stocks) and bowls are topped with chashu pork; no separate vegan preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A serious, owner-run counter shop where mindful eating is expected (no reading or phones at the counter, and young children are not admitted); little to no English, so basic Japanese or a guide helps.

noodles.notes: House-made thick, wavy (chijire) noodles with a springy, chewy bite that grows softer and more delicate as they steep in the broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tennen Shio Ramen (natural salt ramen) — a transparent golden salt broth seasoned with Ishigaki Island sea salt, with house-made thick wavy noodles and hot braised pork chashu.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork belly, simmered until exceptionally tender and fatty; unusually served piping hot rather than chilled, so its fat melts into the broth and deepens its umami as you eat.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles; salt broth is built on a wheat-free shio tare, but the noodles themselves are not gluten-free and no rice/konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken, pork bones, and fish/shellfish dashi (dried shrimp, ago, katsuo, scallop); pork chashu throughout. No plant-only bowl documented.

ramen.pantheon: A specialist shio-ramen house whose soul is a crystal-clear golden broth — chicken and pork bones woven with dried shrimp, flying fish, bonito and a whisper of scallop, finished with natural Ishigaki sea salt. It trades the heavy se-abura of typical Kyoto ramen for delicate, layered umami you taste as a gentle, clean wave rather than a salty punch.

places.name: ねこまた (Nekomata)
places.gf_detail: Abura soba is built on chewy wheat flat noodles dressed with a soy-based (wheat-containing) tare and sesame oil, so the core dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is advertised. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-fish-forward abura soba shop — the soy tare and broth flavorings typically involve animal products, and side dishes include chicken tempura, cutlet skewers and dumplings. No vegan options are advertised. Toppings like chopped onion and green onion exist but the base dish is not vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–20:30 (L.O. 20:00); closed Sundays. Note: some listings show 11:00–21:00 or daily operation with irregular closures; current Instagram indicates 10:00–20:30, Sunday closed.
places.neighborhood: 東山区 清水 (Kiyomizu, Higashiyama), at the foot of Kiyomizu-dera
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ねこまた sits right in the thick of the Kiyomizu tourist district, on the Higashioji/Matsubara-dori approach between the Kiyomizu-yon and Kiyomizu-go intersections at the foot of Kiyomizu-dera. It is described as Kansai's first dedicated abura-soba specialty shop, opening before the

chef_bio.background: ねこまた sits in the thick of the Kiyomizu tourist district, on Matsubara-dori along Higashioji-dori, between the Shijo and Gojo intersections at the foot of the approach to Kiyomizu-dera. It is described as Kansai's first dedicated abura-soba specialty shop, opening before the soupless-noodle craze caught on in the region — which earns it a quiet reputation for the completeness and polish of its bowls. The room is small: roughly eight counter seats plus a few tables, serving both local noodle fans and overseas visitors.
chef_bio.philosophy: A single-minded focus on abura soba — neither ramen nor yakisoba, but its own noodle culture — done simply and well in a small shop.
chef_bio.specialty: Soupless abura soba: thick, flat kishimen-like noodles tossed at the bottom of the bowl with sesame oil and a soy-based tare so the oil, sauce and noodles cling together into a rich, mellow tangle. Three core styles are offered — the soy-based original, a spicy miso (辛味噌), and a shrimp-scented natural-salt version — with menma, diced chashu, negi and narutomaki on top. Complimentary chopped raw onion is added for crunch, and diners thoroughly mix the bowl, then adjust with chili oil and vinegar to taste.

anecdotes.text: Billed as Kansai's first abura-soba specialty shop, started before mazesoba/abura-soba became popular, which is part of why locals praise how dialed-in the bowls are.
Diners are encouraged to load on the complimentary chopped onion and then adjust with chili oil and vinegar, customizing each bowl as they eat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (DLt7w) describes ねこまた as 関西初の油そば専門店 on Matsubara-dori (Higashioji) in the Kiyomizu/Shimizu area near Kiyomizu-dera, offering three styles (醤油ベース原型・辛味噌・海老の天然塩), flat kishimen-like noodles with menma/chashu/negi/narutomaki, free 刻み玉ねぎ, and about 8 counter seats plus tables.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The abura soba is dressed with a soy-based tare; standard Japanese soy sauce/tare contains wheat, and the noodles themselves are wheat — no wheat-free tare is advertised.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, casual abura-soba counter shop in a heavy tourist zone near Kiyomizu-dera; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though staff are used to international visitors.

noodles.notes: Flat, broad ribbon-like noodles likened to kishimen, with a chewy, springy bite that clings to the shoyu tare and oil at the bottom of the bowl. Brothless abura soba, served without soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ganso (original) shoyu abura soba — flat noodles dressed in a shoyu-based tare and aromatic oil; mix thoroughly from the bottom, then add rayu chili oil and vinegar to taste. Also offered in spicy-miso (kara-miso) and ebi-shio (shrimp salt) variations.
ramen.chashu: Angular-cut (sai-no-me / cubed) braised pork chashu tossed through the noodles rather than laid in slices.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare make this brothless dish unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The tare and oil carry bonito (katsuo) and the bowl includes pork chashu and fish cake; no plant-based version is documented. Worth asking, but assume not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A soup-less bowl in the Tokyo-born abura soba lineage — billed as one of the Kansai region's pioneering oil-noodle specialists at the foot of Kiyomizu-dera. Here the soul lives not in a broth but in a glossy pool of shoyu tare and fragrant oil at the bottom of the bowl, which you toss up through thick chewy noodles and finish with your own hit of vinegar and chili.

places.name: 広東料理 鳳泉 (Kanton Ryori Hosen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature karashi-soba (撈麺) is boiled wheat Chinese noodles tossed in mustard-soy sauce, topped with a thickened chicken-bone and kombu broth "an" — wheat noodles plus wheat-based soy sauce. Spring rolls, shumai and fried rice likewise rely on wheat wrappers/seasoning and shared woks. No indication of gluten-free options or accommodation; treat as unsafe for celiac.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly kitchen. Kyoto-Chinese cooking here is built on chicken-bone and kombu dashi broths, pork, and shrimp/chicken (the karashi-soba is officially listed as エビカシワソバ — shrimp and chicken). No vegan dishes are indicated.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun & holidays 11:30–14:30 (last order 14:00) / 17:00–20:00 (last order 19:30). Closed Mon & Tue.
places.neighborhood: 中京区 河原町二条 (Kawaramachi-Nijo), near 京都市役所前駅 Kyoto City Hall S
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hosen carries the soul of 鳳舞, the restaurant widely regarded as the source of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking, founded in 1967 by Cantonese chef Takahashi (高華吉), who softened bold Cantonese cooking into the subtle, dashi-forward 京風中華 that Kyoto came to love. When 鳳舞 closed in 2009 af

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, in the lineage of the landmark Kyoto-Chinese restaurant 鳳舞 (Homai)
chef_bio.background: Hosen carries the soul of 鳳舞, the restaurant widely regarded as the source of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking, founded in 1967 by Cantonese chef Kō Kakichi (高華吉), who arrived in Kyoto in the Taisho era and softened bold Cantonese cooking into the subtle, dashi-forward 京風中華 that Kyoto came to love. When 鳳舞 closed in 2009 after a much-mourned run, its kitchen chiefs carried the recipes forward — Hosen was opened that same year by 鳳舞 kitchen chief 福田功雄 (Fukuda Isao) together with 三野真人 (Mino Masato), so the beloved flavor would not be lost.
chef_bio.philosophy: 京風中華 — keeping the refined, lighter, dashi-driven Kyoto interpretation of Cantonese cooking alive exactly as it was passed down from 鳳舞, rather than chasing mainland intensity.
chef_bio.specialty: カラシソバ (karashi-soba), listed on the menu as 撈麺 / エビカシワソバ: boiled wheat Chinese noodles tossed in a mustard-soy sauce and crowned with a glossy 'an' thickened sauce built on chicken-and-kombu broth, with peeled shrimp, chicken, green onion, lettuce and dried shiitake. The mustard — dissolved in Kyoto vinegar rather than water — is the signature, and the kitchen lets you dial it to taste.

anecdotes.text: Hosen was born the same year 鳳舞 closed (2009), opened by 鳳舞's kitchen chief so the beloved Kyoto-Chinese flavor would survive.

The mustard in the karashi-soba is fully adjustable — regulars order it mild, extra-hot, or even as a 'karashi-soba with no karashi (mustard) at all,' and can request omitting shrimp, chicken or scallion.
The karashi-soba style traces to Kō Kakichi (高華吉), a Cantonese chef who opened 鳳舞 in 1967 and reworked his cooking to suit Kyoto's subtle, dashi-forward palate.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Brutus names Hosen's founders as 福田功雄 (鳳舞 kitchen chief) and 三野真人 (opened 2009), and traces 鳳舞 to 高華吉 (Kō Kakichi), a Cantonese chef who founded it in 1967 and lightened his cooking for Kyoto; karashi-soba = 撈麺 with mustard dissolved in Kyoto vinegar, fully adjustable. kyotopi (wP9Ta) confirms Hosen was opened in 2009 by 鳳舞's last kitchen chief preserving the flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature dish is wheat Chinese noodles tossed in mustard-soy sauce under a chicken/kombu broth an; spring rolls, shumai and fried rice are cooked in shared woks — gluten is pervasive throughout the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The karashi-soba is dressed in カラシ醤油 (mustard soy sauce); standard Japanese/Chinese soy sauce is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from chicken bone and kombu, and the signature noodle is shrimp-and-chicken (エビカシワソバ); no plant-only preparations indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen will adjust the amount of mustard to order, including making the dish with no mustard at all — showing willingness to customize, though this is a seasoning preference and not an allergen-handling policy.

cultural_comfort.note: A beloved, busy local Kyoto-Chinese counter-and-table spot where the owner is known as warm and easygoing; a few words of Japanese and patience at peak lunch/dinner go a long way, and cash is essential.

noodles.notes: Nostalgic thin, straight noodles that drink up the gravy. This is not a soup-ramen noodle but an ankake (thickened-gravy) lo-mein style; the soul is the karashi (Japanese mustard) that is worked through the dish, giving a piri-piri sharpness against a sweet-salty soy gravy.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashi Soba / Ebi-Kashiwa Soba (からしそば・エビカシワソバ) — thin straight noodles under a thick, sweet-salty soy ankake gravy laced with Japanese mustard, eaten lo-mein style (撈麺) rather than as a soup.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based (wheat) ankake gravy thickened with starch; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The signature dish is built on a chicken-based gravy with shrimp and chicken; no plant-broth/animal-free noodle bowl is documented at this Cantonese-Kyoto shop.

ramen.pantheon: This is not soup ramen at all but the soul of Kyoto-Chinese (京風中華): the famous Karashi Soba, a lineage dish carried over from the legendary Houmai, where thin straight noodles are bound in a glossy sweet-salty soy gravy and cut through with a bold streak of Japanese mustard. You taste the city's gentle, refined Chinese cooking in the ankake — chicken and dried shiitake umami rather than a punchy ramen tare.

places.name: らーめん千の風 四条河原町店 (Ramen Sen no Kaze Shijo-Kawaramachi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen and tsukemen use wheat noodles, the broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based, and a shoyu (soy sauce, normally wheat-containing) tare is used for the 京の醤油 bowl. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or preparation is offered, and there is no information indicating any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base and the bowls are topped with grilled chashu pork; signature items are meat-based. No vegetable/vegan ramen option was found.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu-Sun 12:00-21:00; closed Tue & Wed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, long-running ramen counter tucked into the Shinkyogoku covered arcade just off Shijo-Kawaramachi, open for roughly fifteen years and a familiar fixture in one of central Kyoto's busiest shopping streets.

chef_bio.background: A small ramen counter tucked into the Shinkyogoku covered arcade just off Shijo-Kawaramachi, open since June 2010 — roughly fifteen years — and a familiar fixture a minute's walk from Kawaramachi Station in one of central Kyoto's busiest shopping streets.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for a refined, easy-drinking bowl: richness without heaviness, so the soup stays pleasant from first spoonful to last.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 京の塩 (Kyo-no-shio, Kyoto salt ramen): a broth that is creamy in body yet surprisingly light and clean to the last sip, topped with chashu pork. A 京の醤油 (Kyoto soy sauce) version and tsukemen dipping noodles round out the menu, with gyoza on the side.

anecdotes.text: The little Japanese-fronted shop has become a hit with overseas visitors and often has a queue of around thirty minutes at lunch.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists らーめん千の風 四条河原町店 as opened 2010-06-24 in the Shinkyogoku arcade (中之町580), a 15-seat counter/table shop about 1 min from Kawaramachi Station, signature 京の塩. Retty describes the 京の塩 as a creamy-yet-surprisingly-light broth, praised by overseas guests, with roughly 30-minute lunch queues.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 京の醤油 (Kyoto soy sauce) ramen is built on a soy-sauce tare, which in standard Japanese ramen contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact, tourist-friendly counter shop in the busy Shinkyogoku arcade that regularly draws international visitors, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will get you through fine.

noodles.notes: Standard medium chuka-soba style noodles paired with the creamy broth; tsukemen uses a thicker dipping noodle. Exact gauge not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kyo no Shio (京の塩) — the creamy yet light salt-tare tonkotsu in a milky-white broth, topped with torched chashu
ramen.chashu: Aburi (torched/grilled) chashu — surface charred for a fragrant, caramelized edge while the interior stays soft and melt-in-the-mouth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus shoyu/miso tare; the soy-sauce bowl is explicitly soybean-and-wheat based. No rice or konjac noodle and no GF-tare option is documented, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on pork bones and/or bonito-and-kombu seafood dashi, and bowls carry pork chashu. A 'su-ramen' (plain) exists but its broth is still animal/fish-based, and no documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A self-styled "gentle, natural" Kyoto bowl that softens the tonkotsu idea into a creamy-white yet remarkably clean soup — rich on the tongue but free of the heavy back-fat slick, finished with fragrant torched chashu. Alongside it sits a clear bonito-and-kombu chintan ("Sen no Shio") that leans into Kyoto's love of delicate dashi, so the shop straddles milky comfort and crystalline restraint rather than belonging to any one regional lineage.

places.name: 篠田屋 (Shinodaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The signature chuka-soba is wheat ramen noodles in a soy-based (wheat shoyu) broth, and the famous sara-mori features a breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet plus a curry-udon-style ankake sauce — all wheat-heavy. This is a traditional 1904 family diner with no allergen program; not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The chuka-soba broth is chicken-based, the tare is soy/wheat shoyu, and the menu centers on pork cutlet, beef-and-onion curry sauce, and other meat/fish dishes. No vegan menu or substitutions noted.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon–Fri & Sun 11:30–15:00; Saturday closed. (A secondary source lists 11:00–15:00 / 16:30–19:00 with Friday closing at 15:00; hours may vary — confirm locally.)
places.neighborhood: 東山区・三条京阪 (Sanjo Keihan / Sanjo Ohashi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1904 (Meiji 37) and now run by the fourth generation, Shinodaya is a beloved retro shokudo just east of the Sanjo Ohashi bridge, steps from Sanjo Keihan Station. The shop name comes from Shinoda Village in Aichi, the founder's home. The current owner Kutsumi Shuichi wo

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1904 (Meiji 37) and now run by the fourth generation, Shinodaya is a beloved retro shokudo just east of the Sanjo Ohashi bridge, steps from Sanjo Keihan Station. The shop name comes from Shinoda Village in Aichi, the founder's home. The current owner Kutsumi Shuichi works the kitchen and counter alongside his wife Midori and his mother Tsugiko — three generations keeping a sturdy old wooden building (its thick central pillar reputedly kept every dish in place during the 1995 Hanshin earthquake) and its recipes essentially unchanged for over a century.
chef_bio.philosophy: Continuity and care: traditional cooking methods (rice cooked in a clay okudosan oven), unchanged recipes, and a warm family-run welcome where Kyoto families have built memories across generations.
chef_bio.specialty: Two signature bowls define the place. The chuka-soba is a clear, gentle chicken-and-soy broth with thin, custom-made straight noodles — a plain, nostalgic ramen that regulars come back for again and again. The other icon is the sara-mori: a heaping plate of rice with a lard-fried pork cutlet, blanketed in a piping-hot, curry-udon-style ankake sauce with green onion and beef. It looks like katsu curry but tastes nothing like roux curry.

anecdotes.text: The famous sara-mori began as an off-menu request from Keihan Railway workers about 30 years ago — rice topped with a cutlet and curry-udon sauce. Customers asked for it on a plate so it would cool faster, and it joined the official menu roughly 25 years ago.
The shop's solid traditional construction, anchored by a heavy central pillar (daigokubashira), is said to have come through the 1995 Hanshin-Awaji earthquake without a single dish falling.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hot Pepper Mesitsu confirms 篠田屋 was founded in 1904 (Meiji 37), is 4th-generation, run by Kutsumi Shuichi with wife Midori and mother Tsugiko; the name derives from Shinoda Village, Aichi; it sits seconds from Keihan Sanjo Station by the Kamogawa; sara-mori arose ~30 years ago from Keihan railway workers and joined the menu ~25 years ago; the heavy central beam kept every bowl in place in the 1995 Hanshin-Awaji quake; rice is cooked on an okudosan clay oven.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba broth is a soy-based (shoyu) chicken stock; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, century-old neighborhood lunch counter run by an older family; little to no English, so it is most comfortable with a guide or some Japanese and a relaxed, respectful manner.

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered (tokuchu) thin, straight noodles with moderate firmness; finished over the broth pot so they reach the bowl piping hot.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the old-school clear chicken-bone soy noodle bowl that, alongside the curry-ankake 皿盛, is one of the shop's two signature plates.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-based tare put gluten in the bowl; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented at this classic shokudo.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-bone broth with a soy tare; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) broth is offered, so it is not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl frozen in time: this is the gentle, crystal-clear chicken-gara soy "Chinese noodle" of a century-old Kyoto town shokudo, the kind of unfussy chuka soba that has all but vanished elsewhere. You taste the lineage of pre-war shop-style ramen in its quiet, dashi-forward soup — simplicity, not richness, is the point.

places.name: 山の家 (Yamanoya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The chuka-soba is built on wheat noodles in a soy-tare/pork-style broth, and the kitchen also turns out udon, katsu curry and other wheat-and-fry dishes. There is no celiac-safe offering and no indication the kitchen accommodates gluten avoidance. Not suitable for anyone who needs to avoid wheat or gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly place. The signature chuka-soba comes with char siu (pork) and kamaboko (fish cake) over an animal-based broth, and the wider menu is meat-, fish- and egg-centric (katsu curry, nabeyaki udon, rice bowls). No dedicated vegan or vegetable-only ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-22:00; Sat 11:00-16:30; Sun & public holidays closed. (Hours and closed days may change; check before visiting.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yamanoya is an old-school neighborhood eatery (machi-chuka shokudo) that has been feeding the Shijo-Kawaramachi crowd for well over half a century, tucked onto the Ayakoji-Miyukicho corner right behind the Fujii Daimaru department store. Despite sitting in one of Kyoto's busiest

chef_bio.background: Yamanoya is an old-school neighborhood eatery (machi-chuka shokudo) that has been feeding the Shijo-Kawaramachi crowd for around seventy years, tucked onto the Ayakoji-Miyukicho corner right behind the Fujii Daimaru department store. Despite sitting in one of Kyoto's busiest shopping districts, it has kept the unpretentious feel of a working-people's lunch counter rather than a specialist ramen bar.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tasty, genuinely cheap, everyday food you could happily eat any day — the comfort of a real local diner over any specialist gimmick.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka-soba: a gentle, everyday bowl of springy, chewy wheat noodles in a mild broth topped with bean sprouts, green onion, char siu and kamaboko (fish cake) — a homey, cafeteria-style take rather than a fashionable ramen.

anecdotes.text: Regulars and writers single out the Showa-retro atmosphere: it feels like a nostalgic neighborhood gohan-ya, and the chuka-soba's char siu and fish-cake toppings mark it as a homey diner dish rather than a serious ramen-ya creation. The broth is praised as so gentle you could eat it every day.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (KLAw8) describes 山の家 as an over-70-year-old machi-chuka behind Fujii Daimaru at the Ayakoji/Miyukicho corner, with a Showa-era casual feel; its chuka-soba has chewy, flavorful noodles topped with bean sprouts, negi, char siu and kamaboko, and a gentle broth 'you could eat every day.'

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba uses wheat noodles in a soy-style broth; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat. No wheat-free preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills neighborhood shokudo with a Showa-retro feel and little English; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.

noodles.notes: Firm-cooked Chinese-style wheat noodles (固めの中華麺) with a crisp bite and chewy mochi-like texture; the noodles carry real flavor of their own.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) — the classic clear shoyu Chinese-noodle soup, finished with a generous shower of black pepper at service.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork chashu (roasted/braised loin-style), in the modest old-school shokudo manner.

gf.note: Chinese-style wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based (wheat) shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF option is documented at this traditional shokudo.

vegan.note: Traditional Showa-era shokudo broth and shoyu tare with pork chashu, fish-cake kamaboko, and animal-stock soup; no documented plant-based bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl of pure Showa nostalgia: this is old-Kyoto machi-chuka soba — a clear, soy-tinged soup that is gentle yet quietly deep, the kind you could eat every day. The fish-cake kamaboko alongside the sprouts and pork marks it as a neighborhood shokudo's chuka soba rather than a specialist's ramen, and the heavy crack of black pepper at the finish is its signature flourish.

places.name: 四川亭 (Shisentei)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac. The core dishes are tantanmen and shiru-nashi tantanmen built on straight wheat noodles (sourced from a noodle factory), a sesame-paste tare, and Sichuan seasonings that typically include wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle, menu, or preparation is offered, and the tiny 10-seat counter kitchen has no separate gluten-free workflow.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. Every dish centers on sweet ground pork: the tantanmen, shiru-nashi tantanmen, mapo tofu/mapo rice, and Chinese-style broth all use meat. There is no plant-based bowl on the menu.
places.neighborhood: 左京区 田中里ノ内町 / 元田中駅 (Mototanaka Station, Eizan Railway)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A specialist tantanmen counter shop near Mototanaka Station in Kyoto's Sakyo ward, relocated to its current Tanaka Satononaicho address in January 2024. It keeps a deliberately tight focus: just ten counter seats and a short menu built around Sichuan-style dan dan noodles.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Sakyo-ku (Mototanaka), specializing in Sichuan-influenced tantanmen.
chef_bio.background: A specialist tantanmen counter shop near Mototanaka Station in Kyoto's Sakyo ward, relocated to its current Tanaka Satononaicho address in January 2024. It keeps a deliberately tight focus: just ten counter seats and a short menu built around Sichuan-style dan dan noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: A single-minded focus on tantanmen done well: rich sesame depth balanced against stimulating Sichuan spice, served from a small counter where each bowl gets full attention.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dan dan noodles): straight wheat noodles topped with a mountain of green onions, a sweet ground-pork mince, sesame, and Sichuan peppercorns, with adjustable heat across five levels. The brothy tantanmen (rich sesame Chinese-style broth) and a mini mapo-rice set are equally popular.

anecdotes.text: The shop is repeatedly singled out as one of Kyoto's best tantanmen specialists, with the soupless version cited as the dish that turns first-timers into regulars.
A professional chef who runs a Korean restaurant is quoted calling Shisentei 'Kyoto's best (京都一) dan dan noodles.'
Reviewers note the heat is adjustable across five levels; the brothy tantanmen is approachable while the soupless tantanmen is noticeably spicier and considered the more flavor-concentrated choice.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: anna-media confirms 四川亭 is a 10-seat counter shop at Tanaka Satononaicho, Sakyo-ku, with a soupless tantanmen (green onion + Sichuan pepper, 'addictive'), a brothy sesame tantanmen offered as a mini-mapo-rice set, 5 adjustable heat levels, and a Korean owner-chef's endorsement calling it '京都一.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: All bowls use straight wheat noodles sourced from a noodle factory; there is no gluten-free noodle and the entire menu is wheat-based.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dishes are Sichuan-style tantanmen and mapo, which conventionally use wheat-based soy sauce in the tare and sauces; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish uses sweet ground pork and Chinese-style meat broth, including the tantanmen, soupless tantanmen, and mapo tofu/rice; no plant-based option exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat, cash-only neighborhood counter shop with a short Japanese menu and no reservations: comfortable if you can order simply in Japanese and follow counter etiquette.

noodles.notes: Straight medium-thin (中細) noodles sourced from the Teigaku (定額) noodle maker, a supplier used by several well-regarded Kyoto shops; they cling well to the sesame-rich sauce. In the soup-less version the noodles are dressed rather than soaked.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (汁なし担担麺) — soup-less dandan noodles piled with green scallion and a snow of huājiāo/sansho over chunky minced meat, with a notably spicier sesame sauce than the soupy bowl; the brothy 担々麺 with a creamy sesame Chinese soup is the other house pillar. Selectable spice levels (up to five).
notable_toppings: bok choy (chingensai, on the soupy bowl)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui Chinese noodles and a sesame/Chinese-soup tare built on standard seasonings; no rice- or konjac-noodle or documented gluten-free option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl carries minced meat and a meat-forward Chinese broth/sesame tare; no plant-broth (kombu/shiitake) version is documented. The mince could in theory be omitted but the soup base would remain non-vegan — ask only if you must, expect no.

ramen.pantheon: A Sichuan-specialist's take on tantanmen rather than any Japanese gotōchi ramen: the soul here is a sesame-paste sauce — koku first, heat a beat behind — laid over a creamy Chinese soup, while the soup-less sibling trades broth for an addictive mince-and-scallion tangle finished with a numbing dusting of huājiāo. It tastes of Chengdu more than Kyoto, the màlà tingle balanced so it stirs appetite rather than scorches.

places.name: ラーメン 大栄 本店 (Ramen Daiei Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is a traditional Kyoto ramen shop: wheat noodles (sourced from a local noodle maker), a soy-sauce or miso tare almost certainly made with wheat-based shoyu/miso, and a pork-based broth. No dedicated fryer, no allergen menu, and no indication of celiac awareness. Not safe for celiac diners; cross-contamination from shared noodle water and a single small counter kitchen is essentially certain.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is pork-based (豚骨/豚清湯) and bowls are built around generous chashu pork; free toppings are chives and grated garlic. There is no vegetable or vegan broth option. Vegans and vegetarians have no viable bowl here.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:00–14:30, 18:30–21:30 / closed Sun (some sources note that Saturday dinner service is shortened)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood ramen institution in the Kujo area just south of Kyoto Station, in business for more than 40 years (locally cited as founded in 1981). It is the original 'honten' of the Daiei name and remains a small, independently run counter shop that draws lines even on weekday

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood ramen institution in the Kujo area just south of Kyoto Station, in business for more than 40 years (founded in the early 1980s). It is the original 'honten' of the Daiei name and remains a small, independently run counter shop that draws lines even on weekday afternoons.
chef_bio.philosophy: A minimalist, value-driven menu (essentially shoyu and miso) executed consistently for decades, prized for volume, balance, and a clean pork broth rather than novelty.
chef_bio.specialty: Generously loaded bowls built on a pork-based broth: the shoyu ramen and the miso ramen are the signatures. Both come piled with thinly sliced chashu (often more than ten slices, even on a regular bowl), local Kujo green onions, and bean sprouts, over medium-thick springy noodles from nearby Kondo Seimen. Free chives and grated garlic at the counter let regulars tune the flavor.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the bowls as overflowing with thin-sliced chashu and topped with Kujo negi, with free chives and grated garlic at the counter to change up the flavor mid-meal.
The miso ramen is noted for a subtle acidity and a clean, refreshing finish over a pork broth ('ほんのり酸味を感じ、スッキリとした美味しさ').

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (bAeHF) describes ラーメン大栄本店 in Kujo/Minami-ku as a 40-plus-year honten with a compact ~10-seat counter, shoyu and miso ramen piled with 10+ chashu slices, Kujo negi and bean sprouts, noodles from nearby 近藤製麺 (Kondo Seimen), free chives and grated garlic, and a miso ramen with 'ほんのり酸味' and a clean finish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water in a single small counter kitchen; gluten cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and miso ramen are the house specialties; the soy-sauce/miso tare is almost certainly wheat-based, as is standard for this style. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-based and bowls are built around chashu pork; no vegan or vegetarian broth is offered, so all items share animal-based stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy, cash-only neighborhood counter shop with a Japanese-only menu and no English service expected; comfortable for visitors who can order simply in Japanese or point at the menu.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick noodles with a smooth, springy bite (tsuru-tsuru, shiko-shiko) that cling to the broth. Most accounts describe a low-hydration straight noodle, though one report calls them slightly wavy; sourced from a local noodle maker rather than house-made.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (チャーシュー麺) in shoyu, large size — a clear pork broth piled with thin-sliced pork heaped like a chashu-don. The miso version is the prized cold-weather alternative.
ramen.chashu: Loin pork simmered/braised nyu-buta style, sliced thin and stacked generously (10-plus slices on a chashu-men); tender and soft, soaking up the broth rather than charred or aburi.
notable_toppings: table condiments: chili-miso, grated garlic, white pepper

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a shoyu/miso tare that both carry wheat; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built purely from pork bone and pork meat, and every bowl is dressed with pork chashu. No plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto stalwart since the early 1980s, this bowl reads as the lighter, clearer face of the Kyoto pork tradition: a glistening, clean pork-bone soup whose soul is rendered meat rather than fat, finished with shoyu (or a tangy-sweet miso). You taste the lineage in a broth that drinks easy yet runs deep, crowned with a chashu-don's worth of pork and a tangle of local Kujo negi.

places.name: 中國菜 大鵬 (Chuugokusai Taihou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. The tantanmen and ramen use wheat noodles (the dry tantanmen uses thin Kagawa-style wheat udon noodles), and the kitchen is a Sichuan/Chinese operation built on soy-sauce-based sauces, fried dishes, and wheat-containing seasonings. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no allergen handling claims found in any source. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian operation. The menu centers on meat and animal-based dishes: glazed-pork teridon, beef mapo tofu, Shanghai sweet-and-sour pork, drunken chicken, and meat/pork-and-chili tantanmen. No vegan ramen or dedicated plant-based options were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–14:15 / 17:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Tuesdays, with occasional additional Wednesday closures some months.
places.neighborhood: 中京区 / 二条駅前 (Nijo Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1974 near Nijo Station, the shop reached its 50-year milestone in 2024. It is run by a parent-and-child team: the first generation built its reputation as a beloved casual neighborhood Chinese restaurant (machichuka), and the second generation studied Sichuan cooking,

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1974 near Nijo Station, the shop reached its 50-year milestone in 2024. It is run by a parent-and-child team: the first generation built its reputation as a beloved casual neighborhood Chinese restaurant (machichuka), and the second generation studied Sichuan cooking, layering authentic Sichuan flavors over the original homey menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: A flavor for everyone, from children to seniors: sweet-and-savory comfort cooking that keeps the room full at both lunch and dinner, with the second generation's Sichuan training giving the menu real depth and heat alongside the nostalgic favorites.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the teridon (照り丼) family of glazed-pork rice bowls, especially the teridon kinshi topped with silky egg ribbons and richly glazed pork in a sweet-savory sauce. On the noodle side, the kitchen offers both a brothed tantanmen and a sesame-free dry tantanmen (汁なし担々麺) built on thin Kagawa wheat noodles dressed with Sichuan chili and Sichuan pepper aromatics; the beef mapo tofu is another standout.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has earned the Michelin Bib Gourmand, recognition for great food at a friendly price.
Locals consider it an indispensable name in Kyoto Chinese cooking; lines regularly form for both the midday and evening services.
The dry tantanmen is made without sesame and uses thin Kagawa-prefecture wheat noodles topped with Sichuan chili and Sichuan pepper.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: v-trip confirms 中國菜 大鵬 was founded in 1974 (50th anniversary in 2024) near Nijo Station, holds a Michelin Bib Gourmand, is loved across all ages, and is famous for its teridon glazed-pork bowls (six variations including teridon-kinshi with egg strips). gcjapan documents the sesame-free 汁なし担々麺 on thin Kagawa noodles with Sichuan chili and pepper.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are wheat-based (the dry tantanmen uses thin Kagawa wheat noodles), and the kitchen runs Sichuan/Chinese dishes with soy-based sauces and fried items, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat-forward (glazed pork, beef mapo tofu, chicken, pork tantanmen); no vegan dishes or separated plant-based prep were documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, locals-packed neighborhood Chinese spot with no indication of English service; basic Japanese or a translation tool will make ordering smoother.

noodles.notes: Springy, gently crimped medium noodles in the soup tantanmen with a bouncy puri-puri bite. The separate shiru-nashi (soupless) version is built on thin Kagawa-grown udon rather than ramen noodles, dressed in Sichuan chili and sansho.

ramen.signature_bowl: 担々麺 (tantanmen) — a sesame-rich, chicken-stock-based bowl with sansho-forward spiced minced pork and balanced chili oil; or the shiru-nashi (soupless) tantanmen on thin Kagawa udon.

gf.note: Tantanmen rides on wheat-kansui noodles and the soupless version on wheat udon, with soy and sesame-based seasonings throughout. No celiac-safe option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-stock base with spiced minced pork; no documented plant-broth, meat-free tantanmen. As a made-to-order Sichuan kitchen, worth asking, but nothing vegan is on record.

ramen.pantheon: This is tantanmen as told through a Sichuan kitchen rather than a ramen counter: a deeply sesame-laden, chicken-stock soup where the heat comes not from brute chili but from the citrusy, tongue-numbing tingle of sansho woven into the minced pork. It tastes of a half-century-old Kyoto machiya Chinese house that sources its spices from China itself, so the mala aroma reads authentic rather than tamed.

places.name: 西麻布カレーつけ麺 なんだっ亭 (Nishiazabu Curry Tsukemen Nandattei)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free. Every signature dish is wheat-noodle based — the noodles are custom wheat noodles from Asakusa Kaikaro — and the curry broths and tare standardly contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no published allergen handling. Avoid if you require strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian option. The curry broths are built on beef cheek and a seafood/fish dashi base, and the dishes center on meat-and-fish curry. No vegan or vegetarian bowls are advertised, and the kitchen is animal-broth based.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: 11:30–16:00, 18:00–21:00. Closed days: irregular. Blog sources note earlier hour formats (e.g. 11:30–15:00, 17:00–23:00 with Monday closures); hours have varied over time, so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 中京区 / 京都河原町駅 (Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station), Shinkyogoku arcade
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A curry-noodle specialist that carries the Nishi-Azabu name in its full title — 西麻布カレーつけ麺 なんだっ亭. The owner trained at a Tokyo Nishi-Azabu curry-ramen specialist before opening here, and is described as a former French chef who crossed over into noodles, which shows in the layered

chef_bio.background: A curry-noodle specialist that carries the Nishi-Azabu name in its full title — 西麻布カレーつけ麺 なんだっ亭. The owner is a former French chef who crossed over into ramen, training at a Tokyo Nishi-Azabu curry-ramen specialist (しゅういち) before opening here — a background that shows in the layered, refined curry broths. The shop sits down a quiet Shinkyogoku-area side street near Kyoto-Kawaramachi, an unassuming little counter that has built a loyal regular following.
chef_bio.philosophy: A small, focused counter built around one idea executed carefully — curry-and-noodles done with a chef's attention to broth-building rather than a big menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Curry tsukemen — the signature bowl. The dipping broth is a curry built on slow-cooked beef cheek and a seafood/fish dashi base, balancing gentle sweetness against spice, with a milky, almost coconut-like roundness. It is paired with custom medium-thick, chewy wheat noodles ordered from the long-established Asakusa Kaikaro (開花楼). Curry ramen (カレーらぁ麺) and curry abura-soba round out the lineup; a finishing scoop of rice into the leftover broth is a beloved ritual that shows off the soup's depth.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a former French chef who retrained in ramen at a Nishi-Azabu (Tokyo) curry-ramen specialist (しゅういち) before opening in Kyoto.
Diners are encouraged to drop a small bowl of rice into the leftover curry dipping broth at the end — locals say that finish is where the quality of the soup really shows.
The noodles are custom-made by Asakusa Kaikaro (開花楼), a renowned long-established noodle maker, giving them a notably chewy, substantial texture.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kareota confirms なんだっ亭's owner is a former French chef who transitioned to ramen after training at Nishi-Azabu curry-ramen shop しゅういち; the curry broth uses 牛ほほ肉・魚介 (beef cheek + seafood) with coconut-milk smoothness; noodles are Asakusa 開花楼's medium-thick curly type; menu spans curry tsukemen, curry ramen and curry abura-soba, finished by adding rice to the leftover broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All signature dishes use custom wheat noodles from Asakusa Kaikaro; there is no gluten-free noodle option and no separate preparation, so cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Curry broths are made from beef cheek and a seafood/fish dashi base, so all bowls share an animal-and-fish broth; no vegan/vegetarian preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 13-seat counter shop tucked in a Shinkyogoku side street; comfortable for an independent traveler but expect Japanese-language ordering and a no-frills local pace.

noodles.notes: Flat (hira-uchi) medium-thick noodles from the renowned Asakusa Kaikarou maker; slightly wavy with a chewy bite that grips and carries the creamy curry broth. On the thinner side for tsukemen.

ramen.signature_bowl: Curry tsukemen (カレーつけ麺) — flat Kaikarou noodles dipped in a mild, coconut-rounded beef-and-seafood curry broth; finish by adding the small rice (shime-meshi) into the leftover soup. Also offered as curry ramen (carry-rāmen) and curry abura-soba.
ramen.chashu: Thick slab of pork braised low and slow for about seven hours until meltingly tender — the bowl's standout, falling-apart soft.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a curry tare/roux; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Curry roux itself is typically thickened with wheat flour.

vegan.note: Broth is built on beef cheek and seafood dashi, and the bowl centers braised pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto specialist devoted to one idea — curry as ramen soul — where a beef-cheek stock is married to a fish-forward dashi and rounded with coconut milk into a light, fragrant, gently spiced curry. It owes nothing to the old gotōchi regional schools; the lineage you taste is the W-soup curry-tsukemen movement, finished by tipping rice into the bowl to chase every last drop.

places.name: 一盌 清右衛門 (Ichiwan Seiemon)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is wheat-noodle ramen, and the signature bowl is dressed with a blend of two soy sauces (from a local Gosho-nishi maker) — Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat. The kitchen is a single small counter operation with no documented gluten-free accommodation or dedicated prep, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options found. The signature bowls are built on a dashi-forward broth with chashu made from Iga mochi pork; the miso bowl uses rendered beef fat. Animal protein is central to every bowl on the menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (lunch service only; evening service suspended)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained as a kappo chef in Japanese haute cuisine before his love of ramen grew strong enough that he opened this small counter shop near Imadegawa to pursue it full-time.

chef_bio.background: The owner trained as a kappo chef in Japanese haute cuisine before his love of ramen grew strong enough that he opened this small counter shop near Imadegawa to pursue it full-time.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let the umami of carefully chosen ingredients carry the bowl rather than leaning on heavy seasoning — ramen approached with the restraint and ingredient focus of a kappo kitchen.
chef_bio.specialty: The 京醤油らぁ麺 (Kyoto soy sauce ramen) is the signature bowl: a refined, dashi-forward broth seasoned with a blend of two soy sauces from the noted Gosho-nishi maker Sawai Shoyu (澤井醬油本店), topped with chashu made from prized Iga mochi pork prepared with traditional kappo technique. The shop also offers a 極塩らぁ麺 (salt) and a spicy miso らぁ麺 using white and red miso from a historic maker with Sichuan pepper and star anise.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor's kappo (Japanese haute cuisine) training shows in the details: the chashu is cut from Iga mochi pork and finished with traditional Japanese culinary technique, and the soy tare blends two soy sauces from the Gosho-nishi maker Sawai Shoyu for a mellow, smooth mouthfeel.
He opened the shop because his passion for ramen outgrew his kappo career, building a small counter near Imadegawa to serve refined, dashi-led bowls at lunch.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (WjuV5) confirms 一盌 清右衛門's owner trained in 割烹料理 before opening near Imadegawa; the 京醤油らぁ麺 blends two soy sauces from 御所西 maker 澤井醬油本店 for a mellow, dashi-like broth topped with Iga mochi-pork chashu; the lineup also has 極塩らぁ麺 and a spicy miso らぁ麺 using white/red miso (石野) with Sichuan pepper and star anise.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small counter kitchen serving wheat-noodle ramen; no dedicated gluten-free prep or separate equipment is documented, so wheat cross-contact is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature Kyoto soy sauce bowl is seasoned with a blend of two soy sauces from a local maker; Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and toppings are animal-based (dashi-forward broth, Iga mochi pork chashu; the miso bowl uses rendered beef fat), so no vegan-safe path on the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny lunch-only counter shop run by a chef who is described as bright and attentive; basic Japanese for ordering will go a long way, and there is no indication of English support.

noodles.notes: Thin, lightly wavy noodles from a respected Kyoto noodle maker; good bite and a clear wheat flavor, sized to carry the delicate dashi-forward broth without overpowering it.

ramen.signature_bowl: 京醤油らぁ麺 (Kyo-shoyu raamen) — the Kyoto soy-sauce bowl: a clear, gentle chicken-and-dashi broth seasoned with locally brewed Kyoto soy sauce.
ramen.chashu: Pork chashu from Mie-raised Iga-mochi pork, braised with washoku technique (cooked with Japanese tea to tame any porky odor) for moist, tender, refined slices.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken and fish dashi (mackerel/bonito and kombu) with pork chashu; no plant-only bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: Built by a former kaiseki chef, this bowl treats ramen like a clear Japanese soup: chicken broth married to bonito-mackerel-kombu dashi and seasoned with gentle, locally brewed Kyoto soy sauce at roughly half the usual saltiness. It sits apart from Kyoto's famous heavy se-abura shoyu lineage — a delicate, dashi-forward original that you taste as restraint and clean umami rather than fat.

places.name: 京都担担麺 金鵄楼 本店 (Kyoto Tantanmen Kinshiro Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tantanmen shop (the noodles are hand-cut crimped wheat noodles from Sano, Tochigi), and tantanmen tare/sauce typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free offering and no allergen information published. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Tantanmen here is built on a sesame-rich creamy broth and topped with coarsely ground meat miso (pork), so the signature bowls are not vegan. No vegan or plant-based option is advertised; ask if you want to know whether the broth can be modified.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) daily except Sunday; dinner service 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00) Thursday and Friday only. Closed Sundays.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 京都担担麺 金鵄楼 (Kinshiro) opened on March 25, 2024 in the quiet Gosho-minami neighborhood at the Sakaicho-Ibikawa corner of Nakagyo Ward, a stylish counter-only shop that quickly drew lunchtime lines from its 11:00 opening.

chef_bio.background: 京都担担麺 金鵄楼 (Kinshiro) opened on March 25, 2024 in the quiet Gosho-minami neighborhood at the Sakaicho-Ibikawa corner of Nakagyo Ward, a stylish counter-only shop (about six seats) that quickly drew lunchtime lines soon after opening.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focused single-dish specialist: do tantanmen well, let guests dial in their own heat and numbing spice, and keep an intimate counter where solo diners feel welcome.
chef_bio.specialty: Tantanmen offered three ways - with broth (汁あり), without broth (汁なし), and chilled (冷やし). The signature bowl is a sesame-rich, lightly whipped creamy white broth that stays surprisingly light on the palate, paired with chewy thick (rather than the usual thin) noodles for a strong textural presence, and crowned with a praised coarsely ground meat miso. Diners customize noodle firmness, chili oil (rayu) and Sichuan pepper (花椒) levels on an order slip.

anecdotes.text: Became a local craze soon after opening, with lines forming regularly - the shop earned a reputation for being addictively good among Kyoto tantanmen fans.
Goes against the tantanmen norm by using chewy thick noodles - hand-cut crimped wheat noodles sourced from Sano City, Tochigi - that hold their own against the creamy broth.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi (yQyRk) confirms 金鵄楼 is a counter-only (~6-seat) shop in Nakagyo's Gosho-minami area serving tantanmen three ways (汁あり/汁なし/冷やし) with a sesame-rich, slightly frothy creamy broth, thick noodles, and a standout 肉みそ, all customizable. gcjapan (kinshilou) gives the 2024-03-25 opening and states the noodles are hand-cut crimped noodles from Sano City, Tochigi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tantanmen tare is typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published for this shop, so wheat should be assumed present in the sauce as well as the noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is sesame-rich and bowls are topped with pork meat miso; no separate vegetarian/vegan preparation is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only specialist popular with locals and solo diners; ordering is via a customization slip, so a few words of Japanese and patience with possible lines help, but it is approachable.

noodles.notes: Handmade thick, irregularly crimped flat noodles sourced from Sano in Tochigi Prefecture — unusual for Kyoto tantanmen, which typically uses thin noodles. Chewy and springy (mochi-mochi) with a rough, textured surface that grips the creamy sesame soup and the minced-meat sauce. Best eaten at normal firmness rather than hard.

ramen.signature_bowl: 汁あり担担麺 (shiru-ari / soup tantanmen) — the rich creamy sesame bowl. The shiru-nashi (soupless) version is the alternative.
notable_toppings: finely ground pork niku-miso (minced-meat sauce pooled at the bottom of the bowl)

gf.note: Handmade wheat-and-kansui noodles and a sesame-based tantanmen seasoning make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The bowl is built on creamy sesame (and likely soy milk) rather than an obvious fish or bone stock, and aromatics/chili/negi/niku-miso are customizable on an order sheet — but the meat sauce is ground pork and no plant-only broth is documented. You would have to ask whether they can omit the meat and confirm the soup base contains no animal stock; treat as not confirmed.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on Sichuan-style tantanmen built on a pale, lusciously creamy sesame broth — silky enough that regulars suspect soy milk — rather than the chili-soaked heat many expect. The thick, handmade crimped Sano noodles are a deliberate break from Kyoto's usual thin tantanmen strands, and you tune the rayu and sansho yourself, so the sesame koku stays the soul and the spice rides on top.

places.name: 盛華亭 (Seikatei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. This is a wheat-heavy Chinese kitchen: gyoza/shumai/spring-roll wrappers, Chinese noodles and fried rice seasonings, and wheat-based soy sauce/seasoning are central. The signature sesame gyoza even has roasted sesame kneaded into a wheat wrapper. Not suitable for celiac diners; no dedicated handling found.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. Pork is central to the menu (gyoza, shumai, fried rice, noodle dishes), and dishes are seasoned with soy oil, sake and likely meat/seafood-based stocks. No vegan-specific options or labeling were found.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays (except days before a holiday) 17:00–23:00 (last order 22:30). Closed Mon, and the 1st & 3rd Tue.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Seikatei opened in 1982 in the quiet temple district near Ginkaku-ji. Its roots run back to 盛京亭 (Seikyotei), a Beijing-style restaurant founded in 1951 in the heart of Gion's hanamachi (geisha district). The founder's father served as head chef at Seikyotei and was sent to run a

chef_bio.background: Seikatei opened in 1982 in the quiet temple district near Ginkaku-ji. Its roots run back to 盛京亭 (Seikyotei), a Beijing-style restaurant founded in 1951 in the heart of Gion's hanamachi (geisha district). The founder's father served as head chef at Seikyotei, and the son went on to open this independent shop, carrying most of the original menu with it.
chef_bio.philosophy: A Kyoto take on Chinese food: because the original Gion shop cooked for geiko and refined patrons, it deliberately dialed back heavy aromatics like garlic and excess oil. Seikatei keeps that restraint — light white-pressed soybean oil (shirashime-yu), local Matsui-brewery sake instead of cooking wine, and clean, delicate flavors over punch.
chef_bio.specialty: Goma-gyoza (sesame dumplings): roasted sesame is kneaded right into the wrapper, with a filling of only cabbage, Chinese chives, pork and sake, then pan-fried in a heavy cast iron pan until the surface turns crisp. It began as an off-menu request from a regular and became the house signature.

anecdotes.text: The famous sesame gyoza started life as an off-menu dish made for a customer's request before becoming the shop's signature.

The shop traces back to 盛京亭, opened in Gion's flower district in 1951, where the founder's father was head chef; the garlic-light, oil-light style was developed to suit the refined palates of Gion's patrons.
