chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: serai confirms 盛華亭 opened in 1982 near Ginkaku-ji (Jodoji), founded by the son of a head chef from 盛京亭 (Gion, est. 1951); current owner 佐々木幸司 (Sasaki Koji); the kitchen avoids strong aromatics, uses 白絞油 (white-pressed soybean oil) and 京都松井酒造 sake; the 胡麻餃子 has sesame kneaded into the wrapper, a cabbage/chive/pork/sake filling, cast-iron pan-fry, and began as a customer request.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is pervasive: gyoza, shumai and spring-roll wrappers, Chinese noodles, and fried-rice/stir-fry seasonings all involve wheat, with shared woks and fryers typical of a town-Chinese kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the Chinese-Japanese style; standard soy sauce/seasonings contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork is used throughout (gyoza, shumai, fried rice, noodle dishes) and stir-fries share oil and pans, so genuine vegan separation is not provided.

positives.text: Cooking is intentionally light on garlic and heavy oil — a gentler, cleaner style of Chinese food, though this is about palate rather than allergen safety.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local Kyoto-Chinese restaurant where Japanese is the working language; a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner will serve you well.

noodles.notes: No ramen noodles are served. The only noodle dish is gomoku yakisoba — thin, crisp pan-fried noodles topped with a vegetable-rich ankake (starch-thickened) sauce, not a soup bowl. No documented soup-ramen noodle exists here.

ramen.signature_bowl: This is a Kyoto-style Beijing-cuisine town-Chinese restaurant, not a ramen shop — it serves no ramen bowl. The kitchen's heart is its gentle, vegetable-forward cooking: the closest noodle dish is gomoku yakisoba (crisp fried noodles under a light vegetable ankake). Other signatures are the homemade-wrapper sesame gyoza, kashiwa (chicken) karaage, harumaki spring rolls, kurozu su-buta, and gomoku fried rice.
notable_toppings: gomoku (mixed vegetable) ankake on the yakisoba

gf.note: A wheat-based town-Chinese kitchen — fried noodles, gyoza/spring-roll wrappers and soy-based seasonings all contain wheat. No gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Vegetable-forward Kyoto-style cooking with restrained oil, but soups lean on chicken bone and most dishes use animal protein or soy seasonings; no certified plant-only broth or dish is documented. A diner would need to ask which vegetable preparations can be made without meat/dashi.

ramen.pantheon: Not a ramen house at all but a quiet residential-Kyoto town-Chinese kitchen practicing kyo-fu chuka — Beijing cookery softened by Kyoto restraint, where oil is held back and salt-and-sake seasoning lets vegetables speak. If you came for ramen you have walked into the wrong genre; what is poured here are clear Chinese soups and an ankake fried-noodle plate, not a tare-and-broth ramen bowl.

places.name: 中華のサカイ 本店 (Chuka no Sakai Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional town-Chinese (machi-chuka) noodle shop whose signature bowls are wheat-based: the famous cold noodles (冷麺) use custom wheat noodles dressed in a vinegar-and-sesame sauce, and the chuka-soba is a wheat-noodle soy-broth noodle. Wheat is fundamental to the core menu and the sauces, with no gluten-free preparation indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The house specialty cold noodles are topped with roast pork (焼豚), cucumber, and seaweed, and the kitchen is a meat-and-fish Chinese kitchen. No vegan ramen or vegan menu is indicated. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Wed-Fri 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:45) & 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat-Sun 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30) & 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Lunch may end earlier if sold out. Closed Mon & Tue (open if a public holiday).
places.neighborhood: Murasakino, Shin-Ōmiya shopping street (near Daitoku-ji), Kita-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1939 (Showa 14) as a coffee shop, the business reinvented itself during wartime as a Western-style restaurant and, after the war, became a specialist Chinese noodle shop. Traces of those earlier lives still linger on the menu in the form of kissaten-style cafe items ke

chef_bio.origin: Murasaki / Shin-Omiya shopping street, Kita-ku, Kyoto, near Daitoku-ji
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1939 (Showa 14) as a coffee shop, the business reinvented itself during wartime as a Western-style restaurant and, after the war, became a specialist Chinese noodle shop. Traces of those earlier lives still linger on the menu in the form of kissaten-style cafe items kept on as a nod to the past.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quietly perfectionist about the basics: the cold-noodle sauce is built on honjozo vinegar of the grade used in Kyoto's high-end kaiseki kitchens, and the noodles themselves were dialed in over dozens of trial batches in collaboration with a local noodle maker so they would marry perfectly with the sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: The legendary cold noodles (冷麺) — roast pork, cucumber and seaweed over custom wheat noodles in a vinegar-and-sesame sauce. Unusually, the shop serves them year-round rather than only in summer, and roughly 90% of customers order them. The other pillar is a classic soy-based chuka-soba.

anecdotes.text: The signature cold noodles were created by the current owner's parents, who refined an earlier recipe that had included shrimp and egg ribbons down to a spare trio of pork, cucumber and seaweed; getting the custom noodles right reportedly took dozens of failed attempts and plenty of arguing with the noodle manufacturer.
A favorite insider move: pay about 100 yen extra to swap your rice for fried rice, then pour the leftover cold-noodle sauce over it for a surprisingly delicious finish.

Because it started life in 1939 as a cafe before becoming a Chinese restaurant, the shop has long kept some kissaten-style cafe dishes (like omurice and cream soda) on the menu as a remnant of its origins.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba is a traditional soy-based Chinese noodle soup; soy sauce in this style of cooking is conventionally wheat-based, though the shop publishes no specific allergen details.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is a meat-and-fish Chinese kitchen and the signature cold noodles are topped with roast pork, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive.

cultural_comfort.note: A beloved local town-Chinese institution where most ordering is in Japanese and lines of regulars are common; a little Japanese or pointing at the famous cold noodles goes a long way.

noodles.notes: The hot 中華そば uses a plain, everyday yellow Chinese noodle that suits the nostalgic broth — distinct from the shop's famous custom-made reimen noodle (a thick, chewy, faintly gardenia-yellow specialty strand). Exact thickness/shape of the chuka-soba noodle is lightly documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: The house icon is the year-round 焼豚冷麺 (yakibuta reimen) — chilled noodles under a creamy, tangy mustard-vinegar-and-broth dressing. For a hot bowl, order the 中華そば: a clear, gentle chicken-gara shoyu Kyoto machi-chuka soba.
ramen.chashu: House roasted pork (yakibuta), the same sliced pork that crowns the famous reimen — lean, savory roast pork rather than a fatty rolled belly braise.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui Chinese noodles and a soy-based shoyu/sauce throughout; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Chicken-bone broth, roasted pork, and (on the reimen) a homemade egg mayonnaise dressing are core to the dishes; no documented plant-only broth or topping set.

ramen.pantheon: A Showa-era Kyoto machi-chuka institution open since 1939, where the chuka soba is the old-school clear chicken-gara shoyu broth that locals call simply "Chinese noodles" — light, faintly sweet, and deeply nostalgic rather than modern or assertive. Its true claim to fame, though, is the chilled noodle (reimen) it has served year-round since the 1950s, draped in a creamy mustard-vinegar dressing found nowhere else.

places.name: にぼ魂ラーメン 祇園本店 (Nibodamashii Ramen Gion Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a niboshi (dried sardine) ramen specialist serving thick curly wheat noodles in a fish-based broth. As with virtually all Japanese ramen, the noodles are wheat, the tare is almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu, and there is no indication of any gluten-free option or accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance. No dedicated GF preparation, menu, or cross-contamination controls are advertised.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is built on a dried-sardine (niboshi) fish broth, with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, and nori as standard toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on offer, and the broth itself is fish-based, so even broth-free items cannot be assumed plant-based. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists: Tue/Wed/Thu/Fri/Sat/Sun (& holidays) 22:00–07:00 (last order 06:30); closed Mon & Sun. May close early. Opening-era shop/blog info also lists 20:00–05:00 (next day), closed Sun & Mon; as it is a late-night operation, hours may vary.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] にぼ魂 opened on August 8, 2025 in the heart of Gion, just north of Gion-Shijo Station off Nawate-dori, taking over a space that previously housed a katsudon-style shop. It was conceived from the start as a deep-night niboshi-ramen stop — a bowl to land in after an evening out in Gi

chef_bio.background: にぼ魂 opened on August 8, 2025 in the heart of Gion, just north of Gion-Shijo Station off Nawate-dori, taking over a space that had until then housed a kasu-udon shop (かすうどん). It was conceived from the start as a deep-night niboshi-ramen stop — a bowl to land in after an evening out in Gion's bars and teahouses, when most kitchens have long closed, running from around 8pm into the early-morning hours.
chef_bio.philosophy: Lean into the niboshi rather than tame it — a punchy, sea-forward bowl built for the late-night palate, served bright and clean in a small room. Diners can dial the flavor themselves with the tabletop kit of spiced tare, black and white pepper, and niboshi vinegar, and free extra-large noodle portions reward a hungry post-drinking crowd.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the Special Nibodamashii Ramen (特製にぼ魂ラーメン): an unapologetically bitter, dried-sardine (niboshi) broth ladled over thick, curly noodles, finished with marbled chashu, a soft-boiled egg, nori, and a single whole niboshi as garnish. The kitchen deliberately leaves the sardine heads and innards in the stock rather than removing them to soften the bitterness.

anecdotes.text: Where many niboshi shops carefully pull the sardine heads and guts to tame the bitterness, にぼ魂 leaves them in the broth on purpose, embracing the sharp bitterness as the point of the bowl rather than a flaw to correct.
Every table is set with a niboshi-forward condiment kit — spiced tare, black pepper, white pepper, and niboshi vinegar — so guests can tune the intensity of the dried-sardine flavor to taste.
Opened August 8, 2025 as a late-night destination for Gion's after-hours crowd, occupying a former kasu-udon shop and running into the early morning.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle ramen shop with fried side dishes prepared in a tiny shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling or separation is indicated, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a Japanese ramen seasoning, which is overwhelmingly a wheat-containing shoyu base; no wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on a dried-sardine fish broth with pork chashu and egg; there is no plant-based broth or vegan preparation, so no item can be considered free of animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only late-night ramen counter in Gion with no sign of English support — basic Japanese and pointing at the menu will go furthest, and quiet ordering etiquette is appreciated.

noodles.notes: Thick, flat, crinkled noodles (太麺の平打ち縮れ麺) that grab and carry the bitter niboshi soup. Free large-portion (omori) upgrade offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Special Nibodamashii Ramen (特製にぼ魂ラーメン) — the niboshi soba dressed with rare marbled chashu, ajitama, and nori (around 1,200 yen). The plain Nibodamashii Ramen runs about 1,000 yen.
ramen.chashu: Rare-style chashu (レアチャーシュー) cut with moderate fat marbling, springy and tender rather than fall-apart braised — a low-temp pork slice that stays pink and juicy.
notable_toppings: ajitama (7-min soft-boiled flavored egg, runny yolk)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-style tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire bowl is built on a dried-sardine (niboshi) fish broth and topped with pork chashu and egg. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A devout niboshi-soba that lets the dried sardines speak in full voice — the soup carries the deliberate, unmasked bitterness of pure niboshi rather than smoothing it away, the hallmark of the hardcore dried-sardine school. Set just off Gion's lantern-lit lanes and open till dawn, it's a clean, sardine-forward clear soup made to be the soulful finish to a Kyoto night.

places.name: 元祖らーめん大栄 (Ganso Ramen Daiei)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto pork-bone-shoyu ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (custom-made by Kondo Seimen) plus a soy-based tare, which is virtually always wheat-containing. There is no indication of any gluten-free options, gluten-free noodles, or celiac handling. Not safe for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a pork-and-bone broth (described as crystal-clear shoyu tonkotsu made from premium Iga pork), and chashu pork is the signature topping. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu; even a plain bowl uses animal broth. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:00 / 15:30–18:00; Sun & holidays 11:30–14:00 (closes when sold out). Closed the 2nd, 4th & 5th Sun.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The original Ramen Daiei was founded in 1980 in Kyoto's Minami ward. The current owner is the founder's nephew, who apprenticed under him for about 20 years before opening this Kawaramachi-Marutamachi shop in 2003 to carry the lineage forward. When the founder passed away in 2017

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (lineage from Daiei Ramen, Minami ward)
chef_bio.background: The original Ramen Daiei was founded in 1980 in Kyoto's Minami ward. The current owner is the founder's nephew — raised by his uncle from childhood and apprenticed to him for close to two decades — who then went independent and opened this Kawaramachi-Marutamachi shop around 1998 (it reached its 20th year in 2018) to carry the lineage forward. When the founder passed away in 2017, he left a charge to keep the recipe alive.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder's words guide the shop: 'ラーメンはスープが命' (the broth is the soul of ramen). The kitchen keeps the broth pure and clean - made only from premium pork and bones, with no cloudiness, bitterness, or gaminess - aiming for a refreshing, never-tiring bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: A clear, aromatic Kyoto-style shoyu tonkotsu ramen crowned with a mountain of freshly hand-sliced chashu and a generous heap of Kujo negi. The popular order is the shoyu chashu-men with extra negi; a miso version is also offered, and a yaki-meshi (fried rice) set rounds out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The broth uses Iga mochibuta premium pork and noodles made to order by Kondo Seimen; the chashu is sliced fresh to order so it stays tender and full of umami.
Beneath the generous Kujo negi, crisp green mung bean sprouts add a textural contrast, and free condiments at the counter include spicy nira, beni-shoga, and fresh garlic to customize each bowl.
Just steps from the Kawaramachi-Marutamachi intersection, it keeps a clean, unpretentious counter atmosphere beloved by locals and ramen fans rather than tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles plus a soy-based tare; the shop and broth are not designed around gluten avoidance, so cross-contamination from shared pots and noodle water should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature is a shoyu (soy sauce) tonkotsu broth; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so the tare should be treated as wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use a pork-and-bone broth and pork chashu; there is no vegan/vegetarian preparation, so no vegan-safe option exists.

positives.text: The broth is made only from premium pork and bones with no additives or muddiness, so ingredients are simple and transparent; toppings like Kujo negi, mung bean sprouts, and chashu are clearly identifiable.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, cash-only local counter ramen shop favored by regulars; a friendly greeting and a few words of Japanese will go a long way, with no English menu expected.

noodles.notes: Custom-made thin noodles from a dedicated noodle maker (Kondo Seimen), matched to the clear broth for smooth texture and good soup pickup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (shoyu) — the clear pork-bone soy bowl piled with thin-sliced Iga-mochibuta pork loin chashu.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin from Iga-mochibuta brand pork: blocks seared hard over high heat to render off excess fat and odor, then rested in a long-maintained house soy tare, sliced thin to order and heaped generously over the bowl.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built solely from pork and pork bones, the tare and chashu glaze are pork/meat-based, and chashu tops the signature bowl; no plant-based broth offered.

ramen.pantheon: A local-favorite take on Kyoto's pork-bone shoyu lineage, but brewed clear rather than the city's heavy back-fat style: simmered only from fresh Kyoto pork and bones into a bright, odor-free soup where soy depth and clean pork umami carry the bowl. You taste old-Kyoto comfort without the grease — a refined, never-tiring version of the genre.

places.name: 東龍 北白川本店 (Tonryu Kitashirakawa Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Both signature bowls use house-made egg (wheat) noodles, and the 福仙楼そば is shoyu-seasoned (wheat-based soy sauce). Broths are pork-bone and chicken-gara based. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle substitution, and no allergen handling is mentioned in any source. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. The 東龍そば broth is built on pork bone, chicken carcass and maple-leaf (chicken feet) gelatin; the 福仙楼そば is a pork-bone/chicken paitan. Toppings include pork-belly chashu and braised tendon/minced pork. Noodles are egg noodles. No plant-based bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–14:45 (last order 14:45) / 18:00–21:00 (last order 21:00). Closed Tue & Wed.
places.neighborhood: Kitashirakawa, Sakyō-ku (Chayama-Kyoto Geijutsu Daigaku Stn / toward Ginkaku-ji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 東龍 opened in December 2000 in the Kitashirakawa neighborhood of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward. The owner had previously worked as a manager at Karako, a well-known ramen shop in Higashiyama, and brought that pedigree to his own counter on the quiet north-eastern edge of the city.

chef_bio.background: 東龍 opened in December 2000 in the Kitashirakawa neighborhood of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward. The owner had previously worked as a manager at Karako, a well-known ramen shop in Higashiyama, and brought that pedigree to his own counter on the quiet north-eastern edge of the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on hand-made work and fresh ingredients runs through the kitchen, down to the rice — the owner serves Koshihikari grown on his family's farm in Kutsuki.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is 東龍そば, described as a kind of 'kaiseki in a bowl' — a creamy salt-based broth simmered from pork bone, chicken carcass, chicken-feet gelatin and around ten kinds of vegetables, served with house-made curly egg noodles, Kyotamba-highland pork-belly chashu and Kujo green onion. A second house specialty, 福仙楼そば, is a Taiwanese-street-stall-inspired pork-and-chicken paitan in a shoyu key with slow-braised tendon and minced pork over flatter, thicker egg noodles.

anecdotes.text: A sister shop, Fukusenro, operated next door from April 2009 until May 2020; when it closed, its Taiwanese-style bowl was folded into the main 東龍 menu, which is why diners can now order both the salt 東龍そば and the shoyu 福仙楼そば under one roof.
The owner studied Taiwanese noodle dishes in order to recreate the street-stall flavors of the 福仙楼そば.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 福仙楼そば is described as a 醤油味 (soy-sauce-seasoned) bowl; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (pork bone, chicken carcass, chicken-feet gelatin) and noodles are egg noodles, so the entire ramen line is cooked in shared animal-derived stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood ramen counter where a little Japanese smooths things along; no English service is indicated, and the parking-friendly suburban setting draws mostly local regulars.

noodles.notes: House-made egg noodles (tamago chijiremen), curly and wavy to grip the thick creamy broth. The Taiwan-style bowl gets a flatter, slightly thicker handmade noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: 東龍そば — a creamy, salt-seasoned pork-and-chicken-bone broth thickened with a long-simmered medley of vegetables (an early Kyoto example of the "veji-pota" vegetable-potage style), served with the house wavy egg noodles.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly cut from Kyotamba Kogen-buta (a Kyoto-brand highland pork) — soft, fatty rolled belly rather than lean loin.
notable_toppings: for the Taiwan-style bowl: minced spiced pork and bean sprouts

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui house egg noodles plus a salt tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or documented gluten-free option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The signature broth is built on pork bone, chicken carcass and chicken feet (momiji), and bowls are dressed with pork chashu — no documented plant-only broth.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a gotochi classic: where the old city tradition leans on glossy se-abura shoyu, Tonryu pours a porridge-thick, milky pork-chicken-and-vegetable shio broth that drinks almost like a savory potage — one of the area's pioneering "veji-pota" bowls. The vegetables don't just season the soup; simmered down with the bones they become its creamy body, so you taste the garden as much as the pig.

places.name: つけ麺 夢人 (Tsukemen Yumenchu)
places.gf_detail: Tsukemen specialist. Homemade wheat noodles, fish-and-pork-bone (魚介豚骨) dipping broth typically built on wheat-based shoyu tare, and side dishes finished with soy reduction. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built on a fish-and-pork-bone broth, with pork chashu toppings and butter rice. No plant-based bowls or vegetarian options indicated. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri/Sat 11:00–14:30 (last order 14:30) / 18:00–21:00 (last order 21:00); Sun 11:30–14:30 (last order 14:30). Closed Wed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yumenchu is a much-loved Yamashina tsukemen shop with a comeback story. The original shop near Miryoji closed for roughly three years while the owner recovered from an injury; it reopened on October 1, 2018, in a roomier space near Kyoto Pharmaceutical University, drawing a line

chef_bio.background: Yumenchu is a much-loved Yamashina tsukemen shop with a comeback story. The original shop near Miryoji closed for roughly three years while the owner recovered from an injury; it reopened on October 1, 2018, in a roomier space near Kyoto Pharmaceutical University, drawing a line of around 50 people on opening day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing the heaviest possible broth, the shop aims for a cleaner, balanced dip where both the fish and pork-bone umami read clearly, with a touch of acidity and citrus, an approachable style meant to win over tsukemen newcomers as much as devotees.
chef_bio.specialty: Tsukemen only. The house bowl pairs thick, angular, homemade noodles with a fish-and-pork-bone broth, including a charred burnt-oil version (焦がしつけ麺) that adds aromatic depth. A torch-seared butter rice topped with chashu and a soy reduction is a popular side.

anecdotes.text: After about three years dark while the owner recovered from injury, the shop returned on October 1, 2018, with roughly 50 people queuing on reopening day.
The dip is described as cleaner and more balanced than a typical heavy tonkotsu tsukemen, framed as friendly to first-time tsukemen eaters.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tsukemen-only shop: homemade wheat noodles in a fish-and-pork-bone broth with wheat-based soy seasoning typical of the style. No gluten-free handling or separation is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Side dishes such as the butter rice are finished with a soy (shoyu) reduction; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari/GF soy is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-and-pork-bone based with pork chashu toppings; no plant-based preparation exists, so cross-contact is inherent to the whole menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, queue-heavy local tsukemen counter where ordering and a few words of Japanese go a long way; no English service is indicated.

noodles.notes: House-made in small daily batches from a proprietary flour blend with visible wheat germ flecks (the shop's motto is 'wheat impact'); angular extra-thick noodles with a smooth, chewy bite that stand up to the dip. Shop closes when the day's noodles sell out.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen — thick house-made noodles served with a hot, concentrated fish-and-pork-bone dipping broth. The most popular order is the Kogashi (scorched) tsukemen, finished with a fragrant burnt oil; the owner himself recommends the Gyokai Tantan tsukemen for a richer, sesame-deepened dip.
ramen.chashu: Two styles appear across the menu: thick, fork-tender braised slices that "melt like butter," and a moist low-temperature rare chashu (pork). Three generous slices come standard.

gf.note: Wheat is the whole point here — the house noodles are flour-based with kansui and the broth is finished with a soy-based tare. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the dip is a fish-and-pork-bone broth simmered for days, with pork chashu and animal fats throughout. No plant-based broth or noodle is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen in the Tokyo-born thick-dip lineage, transplanted to a quiet Yamashina back street: days-simmered pork bones married to a wave of seafood umami, brightened by a whisper of citrus and acidity so the richness lands clean rather than leaden. You taste that fish-and-bone marriage in the dip, and the kitchen's obsession with its own germ-flecked noodles is what makes the bowl sing.

places.name: 油そば ねこまた (Abura Soba Nekomata)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. Abura soba is built on wheat (中華麺) noodles tossed in a soy-sauce-based tare (almost always wheat-containing shoyu) with sesame oil; toppings include pork chashu and menma. There is no published gluten-free menu or dedicated handling, and the entire dish is wheat-centric. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The standard bowls use pork chashu, a soy/animal-savory tare, and a popular onsen-egg topping; this is a meat-and-egg abura soba specialist with no advertised vegan or vegetarian version. Confirm directly if you must, but plan to eat elsewhere.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–20:00 (last order 19:45); irregular closures. Note: other sources list 11:00–21:00 closed Wednesdays, and a 10:00–15:00 daily window — hours appear to vary, so confirm before visiting.
places.notes: ねこまた bills itself as Kansai's first dedicated abura-soba specialist, tucked into the slope below Kiyomizu-dera on the well-trodden path between Gojo and Shijo along Higashioji-dori. It is the kind of small, serious noodle counter that holds its own amid the souvenir shops and tem

chef_bio.background: ねこまた bills itself as Kansai's first dedicated abura-soba specialist, tucked into the slope below Kiyomizu-dera on the well-trodden path between Gojo and Shijo along Higashioji-dori. It is the kind of small, serious noodle counter that holds its own amid the souvenir shops and temple crowds, drawing both local regulars and curious travelers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Eat it the shop's way: dig the tare and oil up from the bottom and toss everything together so the noodles, oil, and toppings bind into one concentrated, savory mouthful — the chilled raw onion is kept cold until service for crunch and contrast.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 元祖油そば (ganso abura soba) — a brothless bowl where flat, chewy kishimen-like noodles are dressed at the bottom of the bowl with a fragrant dashi oil and a soy-based tare, then mixed thoroughly with pork chashu, menma, scallion, naruto and a generous handful of cold, crisp diced raw onion. Variations include a spicy miso (辛味噌) version and an ebi-shio (shrimp salt) version. Adding an onsen egg and mixing it through turns the bowl rich and creamy, close to carbonara.

anecdotes.text: Despite being surrounded by Kiyomizu tourist traffic, it keeps genuine noodle-shop credentials, pulling in both neighborhood enthusiasts and international visitors.
The onsen-egg topping is a fan favorite: mixed into the noodles it turns the bowl into something close to a cream sauce, carbonara-like.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Abura soba is fundamentally a wheat-noodle dish in a soy/wheat-based tare; no gluten-free handling is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is described as a soy-sauce (醤油) based seasoning, which in Japan is virtually always wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard bowls include pork chashu and an onsen-egg topping; no vegan/vegetarian version is advertised.

positives.text: English menu available, easing communication for non-Japanese-speaking diners.

cultural_comfort.note: Sitting in the heart of the Kiyomizu tourist district, the shop is used to international visitors and offers an English menu, so a non-Japanese-speaking diner can order comfortably.

noodles.notes: Flat, ribbon-like noodles compared to kishimen, with a notably chewy, mochi-mochi bite that grips the oil and tare when mixed.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shin-Ganso Abura Soba (original shoyu) — brothless flat noodles dressed in a soy-based tare and aromatic oil; mix thoroughly from the bottom so tare, oil, toppings and noodles unite. Spicy-miso and shrimp sea-salt versions also offered.
ramen.chashu: Cubed/diced pork chashu, cut into bite-sized pieces so it folds into the noodles as you mix.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce tare make this brothless bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Pork chashu and a bonito/fish-flavored oil and tare run through every variation; no documented plant-based bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A brothless abura soba shop billed as one of the Kansai region's first dedicated to the style: instead of soup, chewy flat kishimen-like noodles are tossed in a concentrated soy tare and savory oil, then sharpened by a scatter of cold raw onion. The soul lives in the mix rather than the simmer — Tokyo-born abura soba transplanted into Kyoto's temple district.

places.name: ラーメン藤 京都本店 (Ramen Fuji Kyoto Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free. As a noodle-factory-direct shop (近藤製麺), the bowls center on wheat ramen noodles, and the soy-based tare/broth almost certainly contains wheat-based shoyu. No gluten-free noodle or menu option is indicated, and there is no allergen-handling information. Should be avoided by anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature bowl is built on a pork-bone soy (豚骨醤油) broth with pork toppings; gyoza and pork rice (豚めし) are also pork-based. No plant-based ramen is offered. A vegan diner could not be reliably accommodated here.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (closed Thursdays; 3rd Wednesday closes earlier ~17:00). Note: Tabelog lists 10:00-22:00, but the official site and multiple recent blog reports state 10:00-19:00 continuous service — the 19:00 close reflects current operation.
places.neighborhood: Higashi-Kujō, Minami-ku (near Jūjō Stn on the Karasuma Subway Line, along Shin-Jūjō-dōri)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A beloved old-guard Kyoto ramen shop founded in the early 1970s (1973, with some sources citing 1972), Ramen Fuji has fed the south-Kyoto neighborhood for over half a century. It is the flagship of a small local family of shops, with branches around the city (Fushimi Ote, Gojo, F

chef_bio.origin: Minami-ku, Kyoto — near Jujo Station along Shin-Jujo-dori
chef_bio.background: A beloved old-guard Kyoto ramen shop founded in the early 1970s (1973, with some sources citing 1972), Ramen Fuji has fed the south-Kyoto neighborhood for over half a century. It is the flagship of a small local family of shops, with branches around the city (Fushimi Ote, Gojo, Fukuchiyama, Kyotanabe).
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's edge is the noodle itself: it is run as a direct outlet of Kondo Seimen (近藤製麺), the family noodle factory a few minutes' walk away. That factory has long supplied the noodles behind some of Kyoto's most storied ramen names, so the springy, well-cut house noodle is the heart of every bowl here.
chef_bio.specialty: A classic Kyoto-style pork-bone-and-soy bowl: a 豚骨醤油 broth in the clear (清湯) idiom — rich yet transparent, savory with a clean soy finish — piled with green onion and bean sprouts over thin, straight noodles. Beyond the standard ramen there is a 特製 (special), a miso version, and a menma bowl, with gyoza and a pork rice bowl (豚めし) on the side.

anecdotes.text: Ramen Fuji is the direct retail face of Kondo Seimen, the Kyoto noodle maker said to supply heavyweights of the local scene such as Daiichi Asahi and Shinpuku Saikan — so the noodles are made by people who have shaped Kyoto ramen for decades.
The room keeps a thick Showa-era mood: a wooden counter with an open kitchen — a working-class Kyoto ramen counter rather than a polished modern shop.
A playful local tradition: on days when the rain probability is 70% or higher, the shop has offered free noodle refills (kaedama) — a small wink to its neighborhood regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A noodle-factory-direct shop built around wheat ramen noodles; flour is central to the operation and cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-based 豚骨醤油 tare; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, so the soup base should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are pork-based (pork-bone soy broth, pork rice, gyoza); no plant-based options noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, cash-only neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is done verbally in Japanese; a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude go a long way, and there is no English support to rely on.

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight, low-hydration noodles supplied by the shop's own affiliated noodle maker (Kondo Seimen). They drink up the soup, soften fairly quickly, and give off a wheaty aroma with a smooth finish.

ramen.signature_bowl: The standard Ramen (the classic bowl) — a clear pork-and-soy broth crowned with a generous mound of Kyoto kujo scallions, fresh bean sprouts, and several thin slices of pork.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced, lightly marbled pork (the same pork simmered to build the broth), laid on in many slim, soup-soaked sheets rather than thick blocks — tender and meant to be wrapped with the scallions and sprouts.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles and a soy-based (wheat) tare; an animal pork broth. No rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth and tare are built on pork (and pork fat), with pork chashu standard. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A living piece of Kyoto's old-school ramen lineage since 1972: a clear pork-and-soy soup where the shoyu sings out front and a quiet sweetness of back-fat hums beneath, finished Kyoto-style with a mountain of kujo scallions and crisp sprouts. You taste the city's classic clear-tonkotsu-shoyu soul rather than the heavy, cloudy tonkotsu of Kyushu.

places.name: ラーメンとまぜそばのお店 Lee (Ramen to Mazesoba no Omise Lee)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core menu is wheat ramen and mazesoba noodles (sourced from Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku / 棣鄂) served with wheat-based shoyu and curry sauces. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation is advertised, and a busy single-counter kitchen means heavy shared-equipment cross-contact. There is no indication staff can accommodate a gluten allergy.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. Ramen broths are chicken and pork-bone (and chicken-seafood) based; mazesoba and the keema curry use ground pork/meat, homemade bacon, egg yolk, cheese and pork back fat. There is no plant-based bowl on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays: 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:30), 18:00–23:00 (last order 22:30). Closed Mon.
places.neighborhood: Nagitsuji, Yamashina-ku (Nagitsuji Stn on the Tōzai Subway Line / along the Outer Ring Road)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Lee started life in May 2021 as 'Curry Shop Lee,' a curry-focused spot on the gaikanjō ring road in the Natsutsuji neighborhood of Yamashina, near Aeon Town. Over time the kitchen leaned more and more into noodles, and in May 2024 it fully rebranded into a ramen-and-mazesoba shop

chef_bio.background: Lee started life in May 2021 as 'Curry Shop Lee,' a curry-focused spot on the gaikanjō ring road in the Natsutsuji neighborhood of Yamashina, near Aeon Town. Over time the kitchen leaned more and more into noodles, and in May 2024 it fully rebranded into a ramen-and-mazesoba shop while keeping its curry roots front and center.
chef_bio.philosophy: Curry expertise carried into the noodle bowl — the keema curry is built to be spice-forward but balanced, 'spicy without being overwhelming,' reflecting the shop's years as a curry kitchen. The clean, café-like counter room is a deliberate departure from the typical ramen-shop feel.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the keema curry mazesoba: springy Y-shaped 'wing' noodles from the long-established Kyoto maker Teigaku (棣鄂) tossed with a layered keema curry, finished with egg yolk, cheese, house-made bacon, pork back fat, tomato and parsley. Staff ask each guest about garlic before serving it. Alongside it sit a gyokai-shoyu mazesoba and a Taiwan mazesoba, plus three ramen styles: tori-niboshi (chicken-sardine), tori-tonkotsu (rich chicken and pork bone), and gyokai tori-tonkotsu.

anecdotes.text: The keema curry mazesoba layers egg yolk, cheese, house-made bacon, pork back fat, tomato and parsley over Teigaku's grippy 'wing' noodles, and the counter asks your garlic preference before it goes out.

Lee began as 'Curry Shop Lee' in May 2021 and completed its turn into a dedicated ramen-and-mazesoba shop in May 2024; the curry-based mazesoba is popular enough that it can sell out, leaving only the chilled and niboshi bowls late in service.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen and mazesoba noodles from Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku (棣鄂) are the base of every bowl, cooked in a shared single-counter kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls use wheat-based shoyu/curry sauces (e.g. gyokai-shoyu mazesoba and shoyu-seasoned ramen), which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken/pork-bone based and mazesoba/keema use ground meat, house-made bacon, egg yolk, cheese and pork back fat; no plant-based preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small 11-seat counter ramen shop in a residential Yamashina neighborhood; expect Japanese-only ordering and menu, so a translation app or pointing helps.

noodles.notes: Noodles from the long-established Kyoto maker Teikaku (麺屋棣鄂). For mazesoba a distinctive Y-cross-section 'wing' noodle whose grooves grab the sauce; for ramen a medium-thick, slightly flat cut served firm (katame). Brisk, springy bite that gives the bowls real snap.

ramen.signature_bowl: Keema Curry Mazesoba (キーマカレーまぜそば) — brothless wing-noodles tossed with house keema curry, crowned with egg yolk, melting cheese, house-made bacon, back-fat and parsley, with finishing rice (shime-gohan) to mop up the leftover sauce. Garlic added to taste.
ramen.chashu: Shoulder-loin (kata-rosu) chashu, leaner and light in flavor with little fat; the curry mazesoba instead leans on house-made bacon and back-fat as its meaty heart.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen/mazesoba noodles plus shoyu-based sauces and curry roux; the curry mazesoba also carries bacon and dairy. No documented rice- or konjac-noodle or GF option — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken-pork or fish/niboshi stock with pork back-fat, bacon, chashu and egg yolk; the curry mazesoba adds cheese. No documented plant-based broth or bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional throwback: born from a former curry shop, Lee fuses two worlds — a creamy, consommé-scented chicken-and-pork paitan and a punchy keema-curry mazesoba — so you taste the kitchen's curry roots in the bowl as much as any ramen lineage. A quieter chicken-niboshi soba rounds out the range with a sweeter shoyu kaeshi and an animal-fish balance for purists.

places.name: Nippon Ramen 凛 KYOTO (Nippon Ramen Rin Kyoto)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (blends such as Haruyokoi and Kitahonami) and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is brewed from wheat. There is no gluten-free noodle or tare option and no indication of allergen accommodation, so a strict gluten-free or celiac diner cannot safely eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broths are chicken-based (clear chicken shoyu/shio, plus a chicken-paitan variant), finished with chashu pork, wonton, and similar animal toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl on the menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–21:30 (closed Tue). Schedule changes are announced on Instagram/Twitter.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Producer Hiroshi Hiraoka built his reputation in Sapporo with Lab Q, known for crystal-clear, multi-layered broths. He travels around Japan to meet producers in person and source ingredients, then works out the flavor pairings and techniques himself before they reach the bowl.

chef_bio.origin: An offshoot of the celebrated Sapporo shop Japanese Ramen Noodle Lab Q, brought to Kyoto as the brand's first Kansai venture; the Shijo-Karasuma shop (Nakagyo-ku) opened on April 9, 2022.
chef_bio.background: Producer Hiroshi Hiraoka built his reputation in Sapporo with Lab Q, known for crystal-clear, multi-layered broths. He travels around Japan to meet producers in person and source ingredients, then works out the flavor pairings and techniques himself before they reach the bowl.
chef_bio.philosophy: Summed up in the shop's motto 温故知新・日本の食材・醸造文化・伝統を丼の中に — honoring the old to create the new by gathering Japanese ingredients and the country's fermentation (brewing) culture into a single bowl, a modern reconstruction of classic Japanese shoyu ramen.
chef_bio.specialty: Clear chicken shoyu ramen (醤油らぁ麺) built from prized Japanese poultry — jidori from around the country such as Hinai-jidori (Akita) and Nagoya Kochin (Aichi) — alongside a salt (shio) version and a limited chicken-paitan; bowls come plain or dressed up with wonton or chashu, on fine house-made Hokkaido-wheat noodles that stay firm.

anecdotes.text: Hiraoka's stated wish was to 'gather ingredients from all over Japan and make the very best ramen possible,' which is why he personally visits producers nationwide rather than buying off a supplier list.
The dining room is deliberately calm — one craftsman works the line with a couple of helpers, and staff even see guests to the door — so the focus stays on the bowl rather than bustle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are served on house-made wheat noodles (wheat blends such as Haruyokoi and Kitahonami), so wheat is central to the kitchen and there is no gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base; Japanese shoyu is brewed from wheat, so the broth itself contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken-based (clear chicken shoyu/shio and a chicken-paitan), with pork chashu and wonton among the toppings; the kitchen is built around animal products with no vegan option.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, refined counter ramen-ya in central Kyoto that draws international visitors; staff are gracious but the room is calm and Japanese-first, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu go a long way.

noodles.notes: Made fresh in-house daily from a blend mainly of Hokkaido wheat; prized for a smooth, slippery (tsuru-tsuru) texture that carries the clear chicken broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 醤油らぁ麺 (shoyu ramen) — a crystal-clear chicken chintan with golden chicken oil; or the 醤油ちゃあしゅう麺 to feature both chashu. The shio version showcases the same clear broth with a lighter, more delicate seasoning.
ramen.chashu: Dual chashu: a moist, tender braised mochi-pork loin alongside a separate chicken chashu — lean and gentle rather than fatty belly.
notable_toppings: ajitama (Hinai-jidori soft-boiled egg, on wonton bowl)

gf.note: Noodles are handmade from Hokkaido wheat and the tare uses honjozo brewed soy sauce (wheat-based); standard bowls are not celiac-safe and no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a clear soup built from premium jidori chicken (Hinai-jidori, Nagoya Cochin, etc.) with chicken oil and pork/chicken chashu; no plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A transparent, golden-flecked bowl in the Sapporo-born tanrei (light-and-clear) lineage, transplanted to Kyoto: its soul is a luxurious chicken chintan layered from several heritage jidori breeds and seasoned with honjozo raw soy sauce, so you taste refinement and restraint rather than heaviness. This is connoisseur's chicken shoyu/shio — clarity, fragrance and depth held in perfect balance.

places.name: 麺屋 やまひで 京都聖護院店 (Menya Yamahide Kyoto Shogoin-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a Taiwanese maze-soba (brothless mixed noodle) specialist built on house-made medium-thick wheat noodles, finished with a savory tare and spicy pork mince — all classic wheat-and-soy components. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or allergen accommodation is mentioned on the official site, the shop's listings, or any review. Not suitable for celiacs; assume heavy gluten throughout.
places.vegan_detail: The core dishes are animal-based: the signature 台湾まぜそば is topped with spicy minced pork and raw egg yolk, and the other house specialty is a chicken paitan (鶏白湯) ramen. No vegan or vegetarian option is advertised anywhere. Not a place a vegan can eat.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An offshoot of a Nara maze-soba house, this Kyoto outpost opened on November 26, 2022 in the quiet Shogoin neighborhood near Kumano Shrine and Kyoto University Hospital, a short walk from Jingu-Marutamachi Station. It took over a former beauty salon in a residential apartment blo

chef_bio.origin: A branch of Menya Yamahide, whose main shop is in Nara.
chef_bio.background: An offshoot of a Nara maze-soba house, this Kyoto outpost opened on November 26, 2022 in the quiet Shogoin neighborhood near Kumano Shrine and Kyoto University Hospital, a short walk from Jingu-Marutamachi Station. It took over a former beauty salon in a residential apartment block, and the room keeps that pale-wood, almost cafe-like calm rather than the usual steamy ramen-shop bustle.
chef_bio.philosophy: A noodle-first shop: the kitchen makes its own noodles and treats the texture and how the sauce grips them as the whole point of maze-soba.
chef_bio.specialty: Taiwanese maze-soba (brothless mixed noodles): house-made medium-thick noodles deliberately scuffed as they boil so the surface turns glossy and clings to the sauce, then piled with spicy minced pork, raw egg yolk, garlic, nori and bonito for a punchy, savory-spicy mix you stir together yourself — with a scoop of rice to chase the leftover sauce at the end. A chicken-paitan shio ramen and a chuka soba round out the board.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a converted former hair salon in an apartment building, giving it an unusually bright, white-wood, cafe-like feel for a ramen counter, and it draws both neighborhood regulars and travelers staying at nearby guesthouses.
The medium-thick noodles are scratched at the surface while boiling so they turn silky and hold the heavy maze-soba sauce, served at around 200g per bowl.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Maze-soba tare is built on a soy-sauce-based savory seasoning, which in Japan is overwhelmingly brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowl is topped with spicy minced pork and raw egg yolk, and the alternate house bowl is a chicken paitan; the kitchen is animal-broth and meat based throughout.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in-only noodle counter where ordering is simple and a few friendly words of Japanese go a long way; it sits in a guesthouse-heavy pocket near Kumano Shrine so foreign diners are not unusual.

noodles.notes: House-made medium-thick straight noodles with a pronounced wheat fragrance, strong koshi and a chewy, mochi-mochi bite engineered to grab and hold the thick mince-and-tare dressing.

ramen.signature_bowl: Taiwan Mazesoba (台湾まぜそば) — 200g of chewy house-made noodles dressed with spicy-savory ground-pork mince, a raw egg yolk at the center, garlic chives (nira), scallion, nori, fish powder and raw garlic; you mix it all vigorously, then finish the leftover sauce with the complimentary oimeshi rice.
ramen.chashu: No chashu — the protein heart is taiwan mince: a spicy-savory wok-fried ground-pork crumble seasoned with chili and garlic.

gf.note: The dish is built on wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu/fish-powder mince sauce; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Core elements are pork mince, raw egg yolk and fish powder; no documented plant-based broth or tare version.

ramen.pantheon: A Nagoya-born Taiwan mazesoba: a soupless bowl where chewy house-made noodles are bound by a fiery garlic-and-chili pork mince, raw egg yolk, fish powder and garlic chives, all stirred together until the dressing coats every strand. The lineage shows in the punchy garlic-chili-gyofun trinity and the ritual oimeshi rice that mops up what the noodles leave behind.

places.name: つけ麺 京都わたなべ (Tsukemen Kyoto Watanabe)
places.gf_detail: A wheat-noodle tsukemen specialist. The dipping broths are built on a pork-chicken bone and seafood base (the five options include rich seafood, vegetable-potato, spicy minced meat, curry, and shrimp), and tsukemen tare almost always contains wheat-based soy sauce. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or stated allergen handling, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian establishment. All broths derive from a pork-chicken bone stock with seafood umami, and even the vegetable-potato (ベジポタ) option is built on the same animal base. No vegan or vegetarian bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:00–22:30 / Sun & holidays 11:00–21:30. Closed Mon.
places.neighborhood: Ukyō-ku, Saiin (1 min walk from Hankyū Saiin Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tsukemen specialist tucked on a backstreet a one-minute walk from Hankyu Saiin Station. The shop has long roots in the Saiin neighborhood, where it formerly operated under the name Watanabe Seimen (渡辺製麺) before becoming Tsukemen Kyoto Watanabe.

chef_bio.background: A tsukemen specialist tucked on a backstreet a one-minute walk from Hankyu Saiin Station. The shop has long roots in the Saiin neighborhood, where it formerly operated under the name Watanabe Seimen (渡辺製麺) before becoming Tsukemen Kyoto Watanabe.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'new-sensation' tsukemen format where the diner customizes broth and noodle to taste, served continuously from late morning through the evening.
chef_bio.specialty: Choose-your-own tsukemen: five dipping broths built on a pork-chicken bone and seafood base — rich seafood (濃厚魚介), vegetable-potato (ベジポタ), spicy minced meat (辛肉ミンチ), curry (カレー), and shrimp (えび) — paired with a choice of three noodle styles: thick, medium-thick, and flat thick.

anecdotes.text: At lunch the shop offers a complimentary small bowl of rice (一口ライス) so diners can finish their leftover dipping broth risotto-style.
The counter is lined with autographed cards from entertainers, a mark of the shop's local fame.
Formerly known as Watanabe Seimen, the shop has been a familiar fixture in the Saiin area for years.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsukemen dipping tare is conventionally based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published for this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All five broths share a pork-chicken bone and seafood base, including the vegetable-potato option, so no broth is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally beloved counter-and-table tsukemen shop on a backstreet by the station; English support is not advertised, so a little Japanese or pointing at the broth/noodle choices helps.

noodles.notes: Three choices: 太麺 (thick, chewy/mochi-mochi), 中太麺 (slightly slimmer, smooth-slurping), and 平打ち太麺 (flat thick noodle with firm bite and wheat flavor). The thick 太麺 is the standard for the dip. The shop's former name was Watanabe Seimen (a noodle-maker), pointing to a noodle-forward identity.

ramen.signature_bowl: 濃厚魚介つけ麺 (Nōkō Gyokai Tsukemen) — thick noodles dipped in the concentrated pork-and-chicken-bone broth blended with seafood tare; the shop lets you pick noodle type and one of five dip blends (rich seafood, vegetable-potage, spicy minced-meat, curry, or shrimp), with rich seafood being the flagship.
ramen.chashu: 味付き焼豚 (seasoned braised/roast pork), served as several slices atop the noodles; can be upgraded with a chashu add-on. Exact cut/preparation not documented.

gf.note: Tsukemen noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the dip relies on a shoyu-style seafood tare over pork/chicken bone stock; no rice/konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on pork-and-chicken bone stock plus seafood (gyokai) dashi, so the standard bowls are not vegan. A ベジポタ (vegetable-potage) dip blend exists, but it is still served over the same animal-based base unless modified — would need to ask whether a fully plant-based broth and noodle (no katsuo/pork) can be prepared; not documented as a true vegan offering.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on the rich gyokai-tonkotsu dipping noodle: pork and chicken bones simmered long into a thick, clingy broth, then married to a concentrated seafood tare so the dip coats every strand of the chewy house noodle. You taste the W-soup lineage of contemporary tsukemen — the deep animal body and the punch of dried fish working as one — with the playful twist that you choose your own noodle and dip blend.

places.name: 中国四川料理 駱駝 (Chugoku Shisen Ryori Rakuda)
places.gf_detail: This is a Sichuan noodle and stir-fry house: the tantanmen and soupless tantanmen are wheat noodles, and Sichuan staples lean on doubanjiang (broad-bean/chili paste), tianmianjiang (sweet wheat-flour paste) and soy-based seasonings that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Core dishes are built on pork mince, dried shrimp, scallop and animal-based broths (tantanmen mince blends scallop, dried shrimp, sweet miso and pork; mapo tofu and yun-bai-rou are meat dishes). No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented; not a plant-based-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-13:45 (last order); Dinner 17:30-20:45 (last order). Closed Mondays (open if holiday) and 1st & 3rd Tuesdays (open if holiday).
places.neighborhood: Sakyo-ku, Kitashirakawa / Chayama (near Kyoto University of
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tiny, long-running Sichuan counter tucked into the Kitashirakawa/Chayama neighborhood beside Kyoto University of the Arts. The owner-chef trained in Yokohama's Chinatown before opening Rakuda, which has been serving authentic Sichuan food in the same spot for well over fifteen

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Sakyo-ku); cuisine rooted in Sichuan, China, by way of Yokohama Chinatown training.
chef_bio.background: A tiny, long-running Sichuan counter tucked into the Kitashirakawa/Chayama neighborhood beside Kyoto University of the Arts. The owner-chef trained in Yokohama's Chinatown before opening Rakuda, which has been serving authentic Sichuan food in the same spot for well over fifteen years.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking real, unapologetically numbing-and-spicy Sichuan food in an intimate room rather than chasing a crowd — the kind of place enthusiasts mean when they say 'in Kyoto, there is Rakuda.'
chef_bio.specialty: Tantanmen and the soupless tantanmen (汁なし担々麺) drenched in fragrant chili oil, plus a benchmark mapo tofu made with local Kyoto tofu, Sichuan peppercorn and doubanjiang. The yun-bai-rou (雲白肉, 'cloud white meat') is the most popular appetizer.

anecdotes.text: Among Sichuan-food lovers there is a saying, '京都に駱駝あり' ('In Kyoto, there is Rakuda'), nodding to its reputation as a destination for the real thing.
The owner trained in Yokohama's Chinatown before opening Rakuda, which has run for around 17 years.

The soupless tantanmen uses chili oil as its tare, with the star-anise aroma of genuine Sichuan cooking clinging to every noodle; the soup tantanmen runs intensely hot, its mince layered with scallop, dried shrimp, pickled mustard greens, sweet miso, star anise, cinnamon and peanuts.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan seasonings used here (doubanjiang, sweet wheat-flour paste, soy-based sauces) typically contain wheat, and the tantanmen noodles are wheat; assume wheat throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on pork, dried shrimp and scallop; the tantanmen mince and broths are animal-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood Sichuan counter where Japanese is the working language; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way.

noodles.notes: Chinese-style wheat noodles that the rich sesame-and-chili soup clings to well; exact gauge and hydration not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen (担々麺) — a soup-style Sichuan dandan noodle in a rich sesame-paste broth fired with real chili and tongue-tingling huajiao (Sichuan pepper).

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a sesame-and-soy-based Sichuan seasoning make this unsafe for celiacs; no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The tantanmen carries ground-pork niku-miso and a meat/chicken-stock-leaning broth typical of authentic Sichuan cooking; no plant-based version is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is tantanmen as cooked by a true Sichuan kitchen rather than a ramen shop — a deep, sesame-thick soup where the heat is not just hot but numbing, the genuine ma-la lift of huajiao and dried chili that defines Chengdu cooking. You taste the Sichuan lineage in every clinging, fragrant spoonful, a world apart from the milder Japanese tantanmen.

places.name: 中華そば らんたん (Chuka Soba Rantan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses custom wheat noodles made with whole-grain flour (全粒粉入りの特注麺), and the signature 鰹昆布水つけ麺 noodles incorporate tapioca but are still wheat-based. Broths are chicken/bonito/dried-sardine (鶏・鰹・煮干) and the tare is aged soy sauce (熟成醤油), which is conventionally wheat-fermented. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. A celiac diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every bowl is built on animal-derived dashi — chicken, bonito (鰹), and dried sardines (煮干) — and menu items feature duck fat, shellfish dashi (貝ダシ), and egg-based dishes such as TKM and the "まんげつ濃厚卵." There is no vegan or vegetable-broth option noted.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Sanjo Kiyamachi (Sanjo Stn / Kiyamachi Sanjo Agaru)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chuka Soba Rantan is a light, clear-broth (tanrei) chuka soba specialist that opened on May 31, 2024, at Sanjo Kiyamachi (Kiyamachi-dori Sanjo Agaru). With a calm, deep interior reminiscent of a Japanese restaurant, it aims to offer a bowl to be savored as a dish of dashi rather than merely ramen.

chef_bio.origin: 京都・三条木屋町（三条駅近く、木屋町三条上ル）
chef_bio.background: 中華そば らんたんは2024年5月31日に三条木屋町（木屋町通三条上ル）にオープンした淡麗系の中華そば専門店です。和食店を思わせる落ち着いた奥行きのある店内で、ラーメンというより一杯の出汁料理として味わえる一品を目指しています。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「わざわざ食べに行きたいお店」として、味のクオリティーだけでなくおもてなしと高級感のある趣を大切にしている。秋田県大潟村直送のあきたこまちや濃厚な『まんげつ濃厚卵』など素材にもこだわる。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は鰹昆布水つけそば。北海道産がごめ昆布と土佐清水の鰹（宗田節）からとった昆布水に全粒粉入りの特注中細麺を浸し、熟成醤油と国産鴨脂を合わせたつけ汁で味わう。レギュラーの熟成醤油中華そば（900〜1000円）は国産鶏・鰹・煮干から引いたクリアで力強いスープに柚子が香る淡麗系の一杯。

anecdotes.text: 全粒粉入りの特注麺は中細でツルっとした食感とすすり心地のよさが特徴。
鰹昆布水つけそばは、北海道産がごめ昆布と土佐清水産の鰹（宗田節）でとった昆布水につけ、熟成醤油と国産鴨脂のつけ汁で食べる京都ではまだ貴重な昆布水つけ麺。
国産鶏と鰹・煮干から抽出されるクリアながらも力強い味わい、秋田県大潟村直送のあきたこまち、濃厚な『まんげつ濃厚卵』を使用。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyo-soku と kyotopi が2024年5月31日開店・木屋町三条・全粒粉低加水麺・熟成醤油鰹昆布水つけそばを確認。公式PR Times（prtimes 023）が北海道産がごめ昆布と土佐清水の宗田節による昆布水、国産鴨油、大潟村直送あきたこまち、まんげつ濃厚卵を明記。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Custom wheat noodles made with whole-grain flour (全粒粉入りの特注麺) are the core of every bowl; the kitchen is a wheat-noodle ramen operation with no gluten-free handling noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen is built on an aged soy sauce tare (熟成醤油 中華そば), and the tsukemen dipping sauce uses aged soy sauce (熟成醤油) — conventionally wheat-fermented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (国産鶏・鰹・煮干 — chicken, bonito, dried sardine); the tsukemen uses 国産鴨脂 (domestic duck fat) and shellfish dashi (貝ダシ) and egg dishes appear on the menu. No vegetable broth offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small 14-seat counter-style ramen shop near Sanjo-Kiyamachi where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way, though the simple point-and-order format makes it manageable for visitors with little Japanese.

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered chuhoso (medium-thin) noodles with whole-wheat flour in the blend; smooth, slurpable texture that pairs cleanly with the clear broth. The kombu-water tsukemen uses a separate specialty noodle with tapioca for extra chew.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei jukusei shoyu chuka soba (special aged-soy chuka soba) — the flagship clear chicken-and-fish bowl; the aged-soy ginger version and the shellfish-dashi shio soba are the other pillars, with a katsuo-kombu-water tsukemen for dipping.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds: a large slice of low-temperature pork loin rare chashu, tender with a balance of fat and lean, plus a moist, gently poached domestic chicken-breast chashu.
notable_toppings: table katsuobushi / kombu vinegar / sansho / chili for self-seasoning

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus an aged-soy-sauce (wheat) tare run through every bowl; the whole-wheat custom noodles and tapioca tsukemen noodles are still wheat-based. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: All broths are built on chicken plus katsuo/niboshi/shellfish dashi, and bowls carry pork and chicken chashu; the shio uses scallop and clam. No documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake) bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A refined Kyoto take on the Japanese-style clear chuka soba, where you taste the lineage in a tanrei soup that marries domestic-chicken depth with katsuo and niboshi sea-umami, lifted by an aged-soy tare and a whisper of yuzu. It sits in the elegant, dashi-forward modern-classic camp rather than Kyoto's heavier se-abura tradition — clarity and aroma over fat.

places.name: 京都二条 麺屋 啜る (Kyoto Nijo Menya Susuru)
places.gf_detail: A ramen specialist based on wheat chuka noodles and an aged soy-sauce (wheat-derived) tare; no gluten-free accommodation could be confirmed. The soup is centered on Japanese dashi such as chicken, bonito flakes, and dried sardines, and wheat noodles are used across the board, including tsukemen and tantan. No bowl that can be safely served to celiac/gluten-intolerant guests could be confirmed; consider it fundamentally not possible.
places.vegan_detail: Two dedicated animal-free items are permanently offered: 'Vegan Paitan Ramen' (soy-milk and aromatic-vegetable soup, topped with ganmodoki and red mizuna / about 1,200 yen) and 'Vegan Tantan Ramen' (about 1,250 yen). However, the same kitchen prepares large volumes of animal-based dishes such as chicken clear soup, dried sardine, A5 wagyu, and pork gyoza, and there is no stated management of shared noodle-boiling water, shared utensils, or cross-contact. They can be ordered as plant-based, but strict vegans or those with severe allergies should always confirm at the time of visit.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Nijo (Jurakumawari Minami-cho / ~3 min walk from JR Nijo Stn Exit 1)
places.notes: Opened October 16, 2025, in Jurakumawari Minami-cho close to JR Nijo Station, a new ramen specialist. It is run by Efried Co., Ltd. (representative Ryota Adachi, founded 2019), headquartered in Nagoya, opening under the slogan 'Ramen connecting the world and Kyoto.'

chef_bio.background: 2025年10月16日、JR二条駅からほど近い聚楽廻南町に開業した新しいラーメン専門店。名古屋に本社を置く株式会社エフリード（代表・安達亮太、2019年5月設立）が手がけ、『世界と京都をつなぐラーメン』を掲げてオープンしました。
chef_bio.philosophy: 『伝統と革新』がコンセプト。日々の一杯を求める地元客、思い出の一食を求める旅行者、本物の日本文化に触れたい訪日客の三者すべてに応えることを目指しています。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は『熟成醤油らぁ麺』。国産鶏からていねいに引いた澄んだ鶏清湯に、鰹節・煮干し・昆布の和出汁を重ね、まろやかな熟成醤油のかえしを合わせた一杯。クリアでありながら奥行きのある旨味と、熟成醤油の豊かな香りが身上です。

anecdotes.text: 看板の熟成醤油らぁ麺に加え、濃厚鶏白湯、自家製胡麻だれと香味油の胡麻担々、A5ランクの和牛ローストビーフをのせた『A5和牛らぁ麺』まで、幅広い一杯を揃える。さらに動物性不使用の『ヴィーガン白湯らぁ麺』『ヴィーガン担々らぁ麺』も常設し、プラントベースの来店者にも対応する。
開業を記念し、10月16〜17日にはInstagramフォロワー向けに看板の熟成醤油らぁ麺を通常900円のところ500円で提供するなど、地域とSNSを通じたオープン企画も実施した。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式PR Times（prtimes 057）と kyo-soku が2025年10月16日開店・中京区聚楽廻南町・JR二条駅至近・メニュー（熟成醤油らぁ麺900円、鶏白湯、胡麻担々、A5和牛1800円、ヴィーガン各種）・500円オープン企画を確認。運営は株式会社エフリード（名古屋、2019年5月設立、代表 安達亮太）。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 中華麺（小麦）と熟成醤油のかえしが基本で、グルテンフリー麺や専用調理ラインの記載は確認できない。小麦の交差接触は避けられないと考えるのが安全。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 看板メニューは『熟成醤油』のかえしを用いる。一般的な醸造醤油は小麦を含むため、醤油由来の小麦混入を前提に判断するのが妥当。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 『ヴィーガン白湯』『ヴィーガン担々』は動物性不使用をうたう専用メニューだが、同一厨房で鶏清湯・煮干し・A5和牛・豚餃子などを調理しており、麺の茹で湯や調理器具の共用、交差接触の管理についての明記はない。

positives.text: 豆乳と香味野菜でつくる動物性不使用のヴィーガン白湯らぁ麺（トッピングはがんもどき・赤水菜）と、ヴィーガン担々らぁ麺を常設しており、プラントベースの一杯を注文できる。

cultural_comfort.note: 「世界と京都をつなぐ」をテーマに観光客・訪日客も意識した新店で、英語での来店にも比較的対応しやすい雰囲気。

noodles.notes: Whole-grain (zenryūfun) noodles, cut slightly short, with a notably mochi-mochi chewy bite and a pronounced wheat fragrance; they grip the clear broth well.

ramen.signature_bowl: 熟成醤油らぁ麺 (Aged-Soy Shoyu Ramen) — a clear chicken-and-seafood dashi married to richly fragrant matured soy tare. The deluxe (tokusei) version layers on three kinds of chashu, an ajitama, and nori; partway through you can stir in a sachet of katsuobushi to lift the dashi aroma even further.
ramen.chashu: Three different chashu styles are served on the deluxe bowl; the heart is roast pork-style cuts (the kitchen also showcases A5 wagyu roast beef on its separate premium bowl, signaling a roast/low-temp approach rather than a heavy braise).
notable_toppings: side sachet of katsuobushi to finish

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (whole-grain flour + kansui) and the matured soy tare is wheat-derived; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A dedicated ヴィーガン白湯らぁ麺 is built on a soymilk and aromatic-vegetable broth (no katsuo/pork), and a vegan tantanmen is also offered. Confirm the noodles and tare contain no animal/fish products when ordering.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than the city's classic back-fat-and-soy school: its soul is a crystal-clear chintan of domestic chicken bound to katsuobushi and niboshi, so you taste a delicate Japanese dashi lineage rather than a regional gotōchi style. Aged soy tare gives it depth and a soy-sauce perfume that lingers over the clean, layered umami.

places.name: 麺屋 一空 (Menya Ikku)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard ramen shop built on wheat noodles (sourced from Menya Teigyaku), wheat-based shoyu tare, and a long-simmered chicken (tori-paitan) broth. Wheat is present in the noodles and almost certainly in the soy-based tare. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, gluten-free tare, a dedicated fryer, or any celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around chicken (tori-paitan / chicken clear soup made from three heritage chicken breeds including Nagoya Cochin simmered ~8 hours) plus pork and chicken chashu toppings. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options noted; even the broths are animal-based.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-14:30 and 18:00-21:00; Sat 11:00-14:30 (lunch only); closed Sunday. (Some sources list slightly different evening/Saturday hours; Tabelog's listing used here.)
places.neighborhood: 南区 唐橋 / JR西大路駅前 (Karahashi, Minami Ward, by JR Nishioji Stat
places.cuisine: Rich chicken paitan ramen / spicy mazesoba
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Ikku opened on April 10, 2018, right beside JR Nishioji Station as the second shop and sister brand of Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo, a well-regarded ramen maker from the Fukakusa neighborhood of Fushimi Ward. Its debut was marked by a storefront crowded with congratulatory flowe

roles: second shop / sister brand of Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo
chef_bio.background: Menya Ikku opened on April 10, 2018, about a one-minute walk from JR Nishioji Station as the second shop and sister brand of Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo, a well-regarded ramen maker based in Fushimi Ward. Its debut was marked by a storefront crowded with congratulatory flowers. The space is deliberately stylish and modern, closer to a cafe-bar than a traditional ramen counter, with an upper floor above the ground-floor dining room.
chef_bio.philosophy: Coax maximum depth from quality regional chicken through long, careful simmering, presented in a clean, design-forward setting rather than a rough-and-ready ramen joint.
chef_bio.specialty: A rich chicken paitan (tori-paitan) ramen. The broth concentrates the umami of three heritage chicken breeds, including Nagoya Cochin, simmered with aromatics like garlic and ginger for roughly eight hours. The shop offers chicken shoyu, chicken shio, and paitan variations, plus a spicy mazesoba (karauma mazesoba). Bowls are finished with pork and chicken chashu, green onion, and premium hosaki menma (tender bamboo shoot tips) over house noodles from Menya Teigyaku.

anecdotes.text: The broth is built from three heritage chicken breeds including Nagoya Cochin, simmered roughly eight hours with aromatic vegetables to concentrate the chicken umami.
Opened in 2018 about a one-minute walk from JR Nishioji Station, with the debut storefront surrounded by many congratulatory flowers and styled like a cafe-bar rather than a typical ramen shop.
Noodles are sourced from Menya Teigyaku (麺屋棣鄂), and bowls use premium hosaki menma (bamboo shoot tips) rather than standard menma.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi（QkH9w）が2018年4月開店・JR西大路駅徒歩1分・開店祝いの花・カフェバー風の内装・2階の存在・穂先メンマを確認。osumituki（106373）が名古屋コーチンなど地鶏3種を約8時間炊いた鶏白湯、ニンニク・生姜、麺屋棣鄂の麺を確認。親ブランドは伏見の深草製麺食堂。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on shoyu (soy-sauce-based) and shio chicken ramen; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based, and no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (long-simmered chicken paitan/clear chicken soup) and bowls are topped with pork and chicken chashu, so vegan diners cannot be accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local counter ramen shop with no English noted and cash-only payment, so a little Japanese or a translation app and a willingness to order at the counter will make the visit smooth.

noodles.notes: Wheat-forward noodles that drink up the glossy broth. The paitan and clear bowls use straight medium-thickness noodles with a clean wheat aroma; the tsukemen and mazesoba switch to thick, high-hydration noodles with a gummy, elastic chew (roughly 200g) built to carry the concentrated dip.

ramen.signature_bowl: Noukou tori paitan ramen (rich chicken white soup), available in shoyu or shio — the milky bowl with chicken-fat and back-fat sheen is the shop's heart. Clear chicken shoyu/shio bowls are the lighter alternative.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds in one bowl: pork shoulder-loin cooked low-and-slow (around 36 hours) into a moist rare slice, and tender low-temperature chicken breast. The pork is juicy with soft fat; both are served rare rather than braised-dark.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus shoyu-based tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is an 8-hour chicken (Nagoya Cochin and other local breeds) paitan finished with chicken fat and pork back-fat, topped with pork and chicken chashu. No plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan specialist that simmers Nagoya Cochin and local chickens for eight hours into a milky, concentrated soup laced with chicken fat, back-fat, and a whisper of garlic and ginger — you taste the whole bird in the bowl. It plays both sides of the chicken spectrum, offering the same poultry pulled clear into a delicate chintan shoyu or shio when you want lighter.

places.name: 麺屋 聖 ～kiyo～ (Menya Kiyo (Ichijoji))
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a shoyu ramen specialist built on wheat: house-made wheat noodles fresh each morning, a wheat-based soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, and a dashi/shellfish broth. Gyoza and other sides also contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and noodles cook in shared water. A celiac diner cannot be safely accommodated here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core bowls are built on a pork (chashu) and shellfish dashi (oyster/clam/shrimp) broth, and the signature draw is the customer-thickness-cut pork chashu simmered over two hours. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered; even side dishes center on pork. A strict vegan would have nothing suitable on the menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:30-22:00; closed Thursdays (irregular closures may occur)
places.neighborhood: 左京区 一乗寺 (Ichijoji, Sakyo Ward)
places.notes: 麺屋 聖 ～kiyo～ opened on October 1, 2018 as the second shop of Kyoto's Menya Yukou group, planting its flag on Ichijoji's famously competitive ramen street. Its stated mission is to carry Kyoto's dashi (出汁) culture from this neighborhood out to Japan and the wider world.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Ichijoji, Sakyo Ward); second branch of the Menya Yukou group founded in Kawaramachi-Oike, Kyoto.
chef_bio.background: 麺屋 聖 ～kiyo～ opened on October 1, 2018 as the second shop of Kyoto's Menya Yukou group, planting its flag on Ichijoji's famously competitive ramen street. Its stated mission is to carry Kyoto's dashi (出汁) culture from this neighborhood out to Japan and the wider world.
chef_bio.philosophy: The parent group runs on the idea that staff are not employees but companions (仲間); the original shop was even named after its manager rather than its owner to honor that spirit. At kiyo the kitchen wants guests simply to taste the depth of the dashi and shoyu, with the chashu fat melting into the broth.
chef_bio.specialty: A mellow Kyoto-style shoyu chuka-soba served as three signature bowls (each around 800 yen). The flagship 'Gifto' is built on a shellfish-and-flying-fish dashi — asari clam and tobiuo — married to a soy-sauce kaeshi and balanced so neither the dashi nor the soy overpowers. The house-made noodles are pressed fresh every morning at the group's own noodle factory for a chewy, firm (モチモチとしたコシのある) bite, and the slow-simmered pork chashu is cut to the thickness each guest prefers right at the counter. The three bowls carry playful names: Gifto, Simple (シンプル), and Shirushi (しるし).

anecdotes.text: The pork chashu is tenderized by a unique method and simmered for over two hours, then sliced to the customer's chosen thickness at the counter.
kiyo was the Menya Yukou group's second shop, opened October 1, 2018 on Ichijoji's fiercely competitive ramen street with the aim of spreading Kyoto's dashi culture to Japan and the world.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式（yukou-kiyo.com）が麺屋優光グループ2号店、仲間思想、店長名を店名にした由来、自社製麺所で毎朝製麺、2時間以上煮込みカウンターで厚さを選べるチャーシューを確認。kyoto.tips と検索結果が2018年10月1日開店、一乗寺、1番人気『ギフト』がアサリと飛び魚出汁の醤油ラーメン、メニューはギフト／シンプル／しるし各800円と確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are boiled to order in shared ramen water; no gluten-free preparation exists, so cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth's defining element is a special soy-sauce (shoyu) tare made with traditional Kyoto soy sauce; Japanese soy sauce is normally brewed with wheat, so the broth contains wheat-derived gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on shellfish dashi (oyster, clam, shrimp) and the shop's centerpiece is pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian bowl is offered, so there is no animal-free option to protect from cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy counter ramen shop on Ichijoji's competitive ramen street; ordering is typically in Japanese and the flow is quick, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, though pointing at the menu works fine.

noodles.notes: House-made daily on-site, blended with whole-wheat flour for a chewy, springy (mochi-mochi) bite with good body; medium thickness with a gentle curl that carries the clear seafood-shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Gift (ギフト) — the mellow shoyu chuka soba built on a clear clam (asari) and flying-fish (tobiuo) dashi over Kyoto-style soy tare
ramen.chashu: Extra-thick (about 2cm) slow-braised pork belly, simmered over two hours until tender and juicy; cut to your preferred thickness at the counter.

gf.note: House-made wheat noodles (with whole-wheat flour) plus a wheat-based soy tare — not celiac-safe. No rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on clam and flying-fish seafood dashi and finished with pork fat, with braised pork-belly chashu. No plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A refined, seafood-forward take on Kyoto chuka soba: instead of the city's classic heavy se-abura shoyu, this bowl pulls a clear, golden broth whose soul is the gentle sweetness of clams (asari) braced with flying fish, then rounded with Kyoto soy and a whisper of pork fat. Elegant and tanrei rather than rustic — a modern original standing apart on Ichijoji's famously hard-fought ramen street.

places.name: 名前のないラーメン屋 (Namae no Nai Ramen-ya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe for celiacs. This is a classic ramen/tsukemen shop: wheat noodles, a soy-based tare (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat), and meat/fish broths. Even the signature bowls that mention burdock or "less wheat-heavy" texture are not gluten-free, and no GF menu, GF noodles, or dedicated prep is advertised.
