Anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy should treat this shop as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around animal-based broths (seafood/niboshi, chicken, and pork) and the proteins offered are offal (motsu), chashu pork, and wagyu beef. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option, and the tare/broth foundations are animal-derived. Vegans cannot be accommodated here.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–15:00, 18:00–22:00 / Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:50), 18:00–21:00 (last order 20:50). Irregular days off.
places.cuisine: Rich seafood / niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen / tsukemen / curry tsukemen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked into a B1F space along the Takasegawa canal at Kiyamachi-Sanjo, this is the famous "ramen shop with no name" — there is no signboard and no storefront marking, just an unmarked staircase leading underground. It is run as a sister project of well-known Kyoto ramen shops Tak

roles: sister shop of Takakura Nijo and Sugari
chef_bio.background: Tucked into a B1F space along the Takasegawa canal at Kiyamachi-Sanjo, this is the famous "ramen shop with no name" — there is no signboard and no storefront marking, just an unmarked staircase leading underground. It is run as a sister project of well-known Kyoto ramen shops Takakura Nijo and Sugari, and the same obsessive attention to detail carries through here.
chef_bio.philosophy: Understated elegance over conventional ramen-shop styling: the room reads more like a hidden cafe, chopsticks, napkins and condiments are tucked away in drawers beneath the counter for a minimalist presentation, and bowls are served on premium tableware (Royal Copenhagen, Staub). Diners are encouraged to adjust the flavor themselves using condiments kept at the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: A modular bowl ordered from a ticket machine: choose your noodle style (ramen, tsukemen, or curry tsukemen), your broth (delicate, layered, or rich), and your protein (offal, chashu, or wagyu beef). The layered seafood-leaning broth is a signature — described as so clean and balanced you can drink it straight — finished with charred and roasted vegetables, including roasted tomato, as accents.

anecdotes.text: The shop deliberately has no name and no sign — you reach it by an unmarked underground staircase beside the Takasegawa canal, which is part of its hidden-gem appeal.

Condiments, chopsticks and napkins are stored neatly in drawers rather than displayed, and food is served on Royal Copenhagen and Staub tableware — the space feels like a hidden cafe rather than a ramen shop.
It is run as a sister/subsidiary shop of the popular Kyoto ramen establishments Takakura Nijo (麺屋高倉二条) and Sugari (和醸良麺すがり).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi（5WVKH）が屋号も看板もないB1F店・木屋町三条・高瀬川沿い・券売機での麺/スープ/具の選択・ロイヤルコペンハーゲン/ストウブの器・ローストトマトなどを確認。ぐるなび取材と系列解説が麺屋高倉二条・和醸良麺すがりと同系列であることを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard ramen/tsukemen shop with wheat noodles and a shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling, dedicated equipment, or separate prep is advertised anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broths are described as soy-based; Japanese soy sauce used in ramen tare almost always contains wheat, so the tare should be assumed to contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (seafood/niboshi, chicken, pork) and proteins are offal, chashu pork, and wagyu beef; there is no vegan option and animal products are pervasive in the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A signboard-less, locals-in-the-know spot with a touch-panel ticket machine and a modular Japanese-language menu; some Japanese ability or studying the menu beforehand makes ordering much smoother.

noodles.notes: Whole-grain (zenryufun) noodles with a fragrant wheat aroma and firm, satisfying chew; the kitchen is known for working organic burdock (gobo) into the dough. Straight cut that clings well to the thick rich broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: The rich (noko) seafood-chicken paitan ramen, or the spice-laced curry tsukemen — both crowned with luxurious grilled motsu. The shop runs a modular build-your-own system: pick light (tanrei) clear chicken, rich, or blended (juso) broth, then a noodle format and a protein (motsu, chashu, or wagyu).
ramen.chashu: Standard option is a clean, reliable braised pork chashu, but the shop's signature heart is generously piled grilled motsu (pork/beef giblets) — torched for melting texture, sweetness, and no funk; wagyu beef is also a selectable protein.
notable_toppings: rice + grated cheese risotto finish for tsukemen

gf.note: Whole-grain wheat noodles with kansui and a wheat-based shoyu seasoning; nothing rice- or konjac-based is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on chicken bones and seafood dashi, and proteins are motsu, chashu, or wagyu. No documented plant-only kombu/shiitake/vegetable bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A nameless, signless basement hideaway in Kyoto's Kiyamachi-Sanjo that plays in the modern-original key rather than any gotochi lineage: its soul is a glossy, deeply emulsified seafood-and-chicken paitan whose dashi depth you taste in every clinging spoonful. The luxury of torched giblets, a curry-tsukemen with a cheese-risotto finish, and burdock-laced whole-grain noodles mark it as a chef-driven Kyoto original (a sibling of the Takakura-Nijo / Sugari kitchens), not a regional revival.

places.name: 広東御料理 竹香 (Kanton Goryori Takeka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is indicated. This is a Cantonese restaurant whose signature dishes (spring rolls in wheat wrappers, sweet-and-sour pork, fried rice seasoned with soy sauce) rely heavily on wheat: wheat-flour wrappers, soy-sauce-based seasonings, and likely wheat starch/flour for frying. No sources mention gluten handling, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free options. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The kitchen does cook some vegetable-forward dishes — notably a water-spinach (空心菜) stir-fry and other seasonal-vegetable plates praised for light, gentle Kyoto-style seasoning — but these are prepared in a meat-and-seafood kitchen and the seasonings are soy/oyster-style sauces of unknown composition. A vegan could potentially eat a vegetable plate but only after asking carefully; no dedicated vegan menu exists.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:20); banquets/course parties until 21:30. Closed Tuesdays. Lunch banquets available by reservation for parties of 10+.
places.notes: Takeka opened in 1966 at the foot of Gion Shinbashi, beside the Shirakawa stream, and has spent close to six decades cooking for the geiko and maiko, teahouse patrons, and locals of the district. Its first owner trained at Fuyo-en (芙蓉園), one of the foundational houses of Kyoto's

roles: owner-proprietor (female proprietor, descendant of the geisha Takeka)
chef_bio.background: Takeka opened in 1966 at the foot of Gion Shinbashi, beside the Shirakawa stream, and has spent close to six decades cooking for the geiko and maiko, teahouse patrons, and locals of the district. Its first owner trained at Fuyo-en (芙蓉園), one of the foundational houses of Kyoto's distinctive Cantonese tradition, before going independent. The shop's name comes from Takeka, the proprietor's great-grandmother, a celebrated Gion geisha of the Taisho and early Showa eras; her vintage hairpins and combs are still displayed in the restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: A gentle, light, 'Kyoto-fied' Cantonese cooking that deliberately omits pungent aromatics — garlic, onion, ginger — so that the food would never interfere with a geiko's or maiko's work. The result is a refined, understated cuisine built around seasonal vegetables and clean flavors.
chef_bio.specialty: Spring rolls (春巻き) are the house signature, said to be ordered by roughly 80% of customers: a homemade crepe-like wrapper packed with finely shredded bamboo shoots, pork and crab, then cut into eight small pieces specifically so geiko could eat them without smudging makeup or staining a kimono. Also renowned are the sweet-and-sour pork (酢豚), the glossy sweet-vinegar meatballs, the roast pork (焼豚), and a fried rice (やきめし) that the kitchen has quietly refined over the years.

anecdotes.text: The spring rolls are deliberately cut into eight small bites so that geiko and maiko could eat them cleanly without ruining their makeup or kimono.
Dishes such as the sweet-and-sour pork are made without garlic, onion or ginger so the lingering aromas would not interfere with the geiko's evening work.
The restaurant is named after Takeka, the present proprietor's great-grandmother, a famous Gion geisha; her old hairpins and combs are still on display in the shop.
Cultural figures including Kitaoji Rosanjin (北大路魯山人) and the painter Hashimoto Kansetsu (橋本関雪) are said to have visited the establishment.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotokurasu（19091）が1966年創業・祇園白川沿い・初代の芙蓉園修業・曾祖母である祇園芸妓『竹香』に由来する店名と現存する簪や櫛・芸妓のためにニンニク/玉ねぎ/生姜を使わない調理・八つに切る春巻き・酢豚・焼豚・北大路魯山人や橋本関雪の来店を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No sources address gluten or wheat cross-contamination. As a Cantonese kitchen serving wheat-wrapper spring rolls and soy-sauce-seasoned dishes alongside fried items, cross-contact with wheat should be assumed pervasive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes such as the roast pork are seasoned with soy sauce, and the soy sauce is not specified as wheat-free; standard Japanese/Chinese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes (e.g., the water-spinach stir-fry) are cooked in a kitchen handling pork, crab and other seafood; no vegan separation is indicated.

positives.text: The kitchen's gentle, aromatics-free Kyoto-Cantonese style means many dishes are lightly seasoned and free of garlic, onion and ginger, which may help diners avoiding those specific ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-established Gion neighborhood Chinese restaurant beloved by geiko and maiko; service is in Japanese and the experience suits a diner comfortable navigating a traditional local establishment, ideally with some Japanese or a guide.

noodles.notes: Not a ramen shop. This is a long-established Cantonese restaurant whose noodle offerings are Chinese-style dishes, not ramen: yakibuta-iri soba (Chinese chuka-men with roast pork), yakisoba (stir-fried noodles), seasonal hiyashi soba (chilled), and a winter gomoku ankake soba (noodles under a thickened vegetable-and-pork gravy with bamboo shoot and cabbage). No noodle shape/thickness/hydration is documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: No ramen is served. The closest noodle dishes are Cantonese-style preparations such as gomoku ankake soba (noodles napped in a clear, gently thickened vegetable-and-pork gravy) and yakibuta soba (roast-pork noodles). The house signature dishes are non-noodle Cantonese plates.

gf.note: Noodle dishes use wheat-based Chinese noodles and standard sauces; no gluten-free or rice/konjac noodle option is documented. Not a setting built for celiac-safe dining.

vegan.note: A Cantonese house known for deliberately omitting garlic and lard and for light, vegetable-forward Kyoto-style cooking; some vegetable dishes may suit, but noodle gravies typically include pork and the kitchen does not document a verified plant-only broth, so confirm directly.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen house but a long-standing Gion Cantonese kitchen, and its noodles reflect that lineage: gentle, clear-tasting Kyoto-Chinese cooking that famously forgoes garlic and lard, so its soba arrive under a light ankake gravy rather than in a tonkotsu or shoyu ramen broth. For ramen as a genre, this address is out of scope.

places.name: ラー麺 陽はまた昇る 伏見稲荷駅前本店 (Ra-men Hi wa Mata Noboru Fushimi Inari Ekima)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls center on house-made wheat noodles (now a whole-wheat flat thick noodle for tsukemen) and a fermented soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, served over chicken-and-pork-bone broth. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free noodle/tare options were found; a celiac-safe meal is not realistic here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The house specialty is a chicken-and-pork-bone (tori-tonkotsu) double broth simmered with ~130kg of bones per batch; a beef-bone broth variant is also offered. Toppings include chashu pork belly and house-made pastrami. No vegetable- or plant-based broth or vegan menu was found.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00; closed Wednesdays. A Jiro-style option is offered after 18:00.
places.neighborhood: Fukakusa, Fushimi-ku (in front of Keihan Fushimi-Inari Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An independent neighborhood ramen shop on the busy approach street near Fushimi Inari, run by representative Shunji Otsubo under his company Hinobo. The shop relocated to its current spot by the Keihan Fushimi Inari station front, growing into a local favorite that now also draws

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Fushimi-ku, Fukakusa), near Fushimi Inari Taisha
chef_bio.background: An independent neighborhood ramen shop on the busy approach street near Fushimi Inari, run by representative Shunji Otsubo under his company Hinobo (株式会社ヒノボ). The shop relocated to its current spot by the Fushimi Inari station front, growing into a local favorite that now also draws the steady stream of shrine visitors.
chef_bio.philosophy: A stated belief in valuing the natural flavor of the ingredients and keeping food safe enough for children to eat: the kitchen avoids leaning on chemical umami additives, relying instead on careful preparation and heat control to get a broth that is rich but clean-finishing rather than heavy, seasoned with naturally fermented soy sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori-tonkotsu ramen and a rich tsukemen: a chicken-and-pork-bone double broth simmered 8+ hours from roughly 130kg of bones per batch, paired with house-made noodles. The tsukemen broth is kept hot in a stone bowl and topped with seared pork-belly chashu and house-made pastrami, menma and green onion.

anecdotes.text: The broth is built from about 130kg of chicken and pork bones simmered for over eight hours per batch, aiming for a result that is concentrated yet elegant with a clean aftertaste.

The tsukemen dipping broth is served in a heated stone bowl to hold its temperature, with whole-wheat flat thick noodles and house-made pastrami plus seared pork-belly chashu among the toppings; it has become a draw for local ramen fans despite the touristy location.
The shop relocated from near Kintetsu Fushimi station to its current spot by Fushimi Inari, where it sits on the busy Sandan-kaido approach drawing both tourists and locals.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトが代表 大坪俊次・運営 株式会社ヒノボ・1回の仕込みで鶏ガラと豚骨130kgを8時間強火で炊く・うま味調味料を極力排除・子どもも安心・天然醸造醤油を確認。kyotopi（p7WqE）が近鉄伏見駅前から伏見稲荷への移転、参道（三段街道）立地、石鍋のつけ麺、自家製パストラミ、全粒粉入り平打ち太麺を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles and a fermented soy-sauce tare, so the dish is wheat-based throughout; no gluten-free preparation or separate handling is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasoning uses naturally fermented soy sauce (and a tamari-style soy sauce for the beef-bone variant), which is wheat-containing unless specified otherwise; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (chicken-and-pork bone, plus a beef-bone option) and toppings include pork chashu and pastrami; there is no separate vegetable broth line, so vegan separation is not possible.

positives.text: The kitchen states it avoids chemical seasoning additives and prioritizes ingredient safety so that even children can eat the food, relying on careful preparation rather than synthetic umami enhancers.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy counter ramen shop on the main approach near Fushimi Inari that draws both tourists and locals; basic Japanese greetings help but you can manage with minimal Japanese, and there is an English site/menu.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from domestic wheat, with a pronounced wheat fragrance and a firm, chewy bite that clings to the thick broth. Most accounts describe a thin-to-medium straight cut for the standard bowls; the tsukemen is served with thicker, heartier house noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-tonkotsu ramen (とりとんこつ) — the house double-soup of chicken bones and pork bones simmered long and hard into a thick, creamy bowl.
ramen.chashu: Black-pepper-rubbed low-temperature rare pork chashu — lean rather than fatty, the cured pepper crust framing concentrated, tightly-packed pork flavor in tender rosy slices.

gf.note: Wheat house-made noodles plus a brewed-soy-sauce tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken-and-pork bone or beef-bone broth (the fish variant adds katsuo/seafood dashi) with animal-protein chashu. No plant-based broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto chicken-pork paitan: chicken and pork bones cooked for hours into one velvety, thick soup where neither animal dominates, yet it lands clean rather than cloying. It sits in the contemporary tori-tonkotsu lineage rather than old-school Kyoto se-abura shoyu — and a fish-laced variant lets you taste a seafood-deepened version of the same soul.

places.name: 京都中華 ハマムラ 府庁前店 (Kyoto Chuka Hamamura (Fuchomae))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature noodle dishes — 中華そば and the famous からしそば — use wheat noodles, and the からしそば is finished with a wheat-thickened ankake sauce and mustard. Chinese-style cooking here relies on soy sauce and wheat-based seasonings throughout. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The Kyoto-Chinese cooking is built on chicken-bone and kombu dashi plus pork, and broths/sauces are animal-based. No dedicated vegan menu was found. Core items are not plant-based.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, across from the Kyoto Prefectural Office (Fuchom
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hamamura was founded in 1924 (Taisho 13) and is widely credited as the birthplace of Kyoto Chinese (kyo-chuka). What began as a humble noodle-and-Chinese shop grew into one of the city's defining culinary lineages, now run by the founder's grandson, the third-generation Hamamura

chef_bio.chef_name: Hamamura Hozo (founder, 濱村保三); third-generation Hamamura Yoshiyuki (濱村吉行)
chef_bio.background: Hamamura was founded in 1924 (Taisho 13) and is widely credited as the birthplace of Kyoto Chinese (kyo-chuka). What began as a humble noodle-and-Chinese shop grew into one of the city's defining culinary lineages, now run by the founder's grandson, the third-generation Hamamura Yoshiyuki. The Fuchomae branch sits across from the Kyoto Prefectural Office and moved to this Marutamachi spot from Kawaramachi around 2014.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kyoto Chinese holds back the garlic and heavy oil of mainland Chinese cooking, leaning instead on chicken-bone and kombu dashi for a clean, restrained flavor. The style is said to have grown out of Kyoto's geisha districts, where strong-smelling foods were discouraged.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is karashi-soba: noodles bound in a glossy ankake sauce sharpened with vinegar-loosened Japanese mustard, described as spicy yet paradoxically light and refined. Their straightforward chuka-soba is the other anchor of the menu.

anecdotes.text: Hamamura is regarded as a legend of Kyoto Chinese, tracing back to the original Shina-ryori Hamamura credited with founding the kyo-chuka genre in 1924.
The karashi-soba is celebrated as spicy from the mustard yet still light and refined, a one-plate symbol of Kyoto Chinese.
The Fuchomae location relocated from Kawaramachi around 2014 and is known as an easy place to drop into, even for a solo diner.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: shinise.tv が1924年（大正13年）創業・京都中華発祥の店・創業者 濱村保三・三代目 濱村吉行・ニンニクや油を控え鶏がらと昆布の出汁を使う京都中華・芸妓文化に由来する薄味・看板のからしそばを確認。kyotopi（7lvle）が府庁前店は約5年前（2014年頃）に河原町から移転したことを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, wheat-thickened ankake sauce, and soy/wheat seasonings are central to the menu; no gluten-free handling or dedicated equipment information was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Chinese soy-sauce-based seasonings are used throughout; assume wheat-containing shoyu unless the shop confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and sauces are built on chicken-bone and kombu dashi with pork dishes prominent; shared woks and stocks make vegan separation unlikely.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, long-running neighborhood Chinese restaurant where solo and first-time visitors fit in easily; a little menu guidance helps since service is in Japanese.

noodles.notes: Thin straight noodles served under a thick ankake (starch-bound) sauce rather than in a loose soup; one source notes the noodles are seared on one side before the sauce is ladled over them.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashi soba (からしそば) — thin straight noodles bound in a light, glossy ankake sauce over chicken-and-kombu stock, loaded with pork and vegetables and finished with a fierce sheet of hot Japanese mustard (karashi). A Kyoto-Chinese specialty served as a dinner item; an evening-only offering at this location.
ramen.chashu: No braised or grilled chashu in the ramen sense; the protein is sliced pork simmered into the ankake topping alongside the vegetables.

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a soy-based, starch-thickened ankake sauce; nothing rice- or konjac-noodle and no GF tare is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The signature bowl is built on chicken-gara stock with sliced pork in the ankake; no documented kombu/shiitake plant-only broth bowl. The kitchen does run a vegetable-forward, oil-controlled style, so a modified veg dish could be worth asking about, but nothing vegan is confirmed.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto-Chinese, not gotochi ramen: a house that has cooked the genteel, oil-restrained Chinese of the old geisha quarters since 1924, where chicken and kombu do the work that garlic and lard do elsewhere. Its karashi soba is the soul of that lineage — a delicate, almost dashi-clear ankake draped over thin noodles, then jolted awake by a roaring hit of hot mustard, refinement and shock in one bowl.

places.name: ラーメン また此処で (Ramen Mata Koko de)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for gluten-free or celiac diners. The signature draw is house-made extra-thick wheat noodles (極太手揉み麺), and the tare is a soy-sauce (wheat-based) seasoning over a pork-bone broth. No gluten-free noodles, separate prep, or allergen accommodation is offered or mentioned.
places.vegan_detail: This is a Jiro-style ramen shop built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with back fat and pork toppings; the bowls are fundamentally pork-based with mounded vegetables on top. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered or mentioned anywhere.
places.hours_raw: Hours 11:00–14:00 / 17:00–22:00; Fri 17:00–22:00; Sun 11:00–16:00. Closed Mon. (Note: Tabelog listing shows 11:00–15:00 / 18:00–22:00, closed Mon & Thu — local/shop sources give the times used here.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is the realization of a 13-year dream for owner-chef Takuya Kozasa, who trained for 13 years at the influential Kyoto Jiro-style house 'ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ／そら' (Ramen Sho 'Chikyu-kibo de Kangaero' / Sora) before striking out on his own. It opened on October 6, 2025.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Nishi-Shichijo, Shimogyo Ward) — opened in the former premises of the ramen shop Akamon (赤もん)
chef_bio.background: The shop is the realization of a 13-year dream for owner-chef Takuya Kozasa, who trained for 13 years at the influential Kyoto Jiro-style house 'ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ／そら' (Ramen Sho 'Chikyu-kibo de Kangaero' / Sora) before striking out on his own. It opened on October 6, 2025, with 12 counter seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: Despite the big, brawny Jiro format, the cooking aims for balance — the soy tare is kept deliberately restrained in saltiness to let the broth's natural pork sweetness come through, tuned toward Kyoto palates, with garlic and a touch of chili (一味) for punch. The chef keeps refining the recipe by tracking room temperature and humidity day to day.
chef_bio.specialty: Jiro-style (二郎系) ramen built on house-made extra-thick, hand-massaged noodles (極太手揉み麺) cut on the shop's own noodle machine, in a sweet-edged pork-bone broth crowned with mounded vegetables, back fat (アブラ), garlic, and thick chashu. The standard bowl is 300g of noodles with two slices of pork; a flagship 'また此処ラーメン' with special toppings tops the menu. A brothless 'sojiru-nashi/tsukemen' version is also offered.

anecdotes.text: The name 'また此処で' (mata koko de — 'see you here again') doubles as the staff's parting line to guests: 'また此処でお待ちしています' ('we'll be waiting for you here again').

On opening, the shop announced the chef's roots plainly on social media: '元ソラの人です！地球規模で13年間修行してました！' ('I'm a former Sora guy — I trained for 13 years at Chikyu-kibo'), framing the launch as '13年間追い続けた夢が遂に動き出します' ('the dream I chased for 13 years finally takes off').

A call board (コール表) is posted at the counter so even newcomers to Jiro-style can pick their toppings — extra vegetables (野菜マシ), extra seasoning (カラメ), extra garlic — without having to call them out by heart.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi（eptC6）と osumituki（223370）が店主は地球規模／そら出身で13年修行・2025年10月6日開店・下京区西七条（赤もん跡地）・カウンター12席・極太手揉み麺の二郎系・コール表を確認。検索で店主名 小笹琢也 と300g/豚2枚の基本構成を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core product is house-made extra-thick wheat noodles boiled in shared pots; gluten is unavoidable and there is no indication of any gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a soy-sauce-based (kaeshi) seasoning — standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — kept light to let the pork-bone broth's sweetness show.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) with back fat and pork toppings; vegetables are cooked into and mounded over the same animal-based bowl, so no component is free of animal contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, counter-only neighborhood Jiro-style shop with a ticket machine and a posted call board, so a first-timer can point and order without much Japanese, though staff interaction is in Japanese and you'll be asked the standard "garlic?" question at the counter.

noodles.notes: Extra-thick, wavy self-made noodles with a strong wheat aroma and substantial chew; ~300g in the regular size. Slurpable rather than punishingly firm despite the bulk.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (並ラーメン) - 300g of thick wavy noodles in an emulsified sweet pork-bone shoyu broth, crowned with the Jiro mountain of bean sprouts and cabbage, back-fat (seabura), and grated garlic. Call your toppings at the counter (mashi for extra veg/fat/garlic). A tsukemen version is also offered.
ramen.chashu: Thick, melt-in-the-mouth braised pork (buta), served 2 slices standard with a 5-slice option; tender, gelatinous edges in the Jiro tradition.

gf.note: Jiro-style bowls are built on wheat: thick wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare over a pork broth. No rice/konjac noodle or GF option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core of the bowl is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with pork back-fat and braised pork; shoyu tare and pork are pervasive. No plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful piece of the Ramen Jiro universe transplanted to Kyoto by a chef who spent over a decade in a celebrated Jiro-style kitchen: a sweet, emulsified pork-bone soup buried under a mountain of bean sprouts, cabbage, back-fat and garlic. What sets it apart is a distinctly Kyoto restraint in the shoyu seasoning, dialing back the usual Jiro saltiness so the broth's natural pork sweetness leads.

places.name: あぶらそば 油珉 (Abura Soba Yumin)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. All bowls use wheat noodles (the regular and spicy abura soba use house-made wheat noodles; the back-fat vegetable bowl uses a thick Jiro-style wheat noodle), and the oil/tare sauce is a typical abura-soba sauce that contains wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and no indication of allergy handling. A celiac diner should avoid this shop.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan option. This is an abura soba (brothless oil noodle) shop whose bowls center on a wheat-based oil/tare sauce, pork fat (背脂), char siu / fatty pork toppings, eggs, and mayo-based extras. The "背脂野菜油そば" (back-fat vegetable) bowl is built on pork fat in a Jiro style. No plant-based bowl is offered; a vegan diner could not be safely accommodated here.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–22:30 (Friday dinner until 22:00). Sources vary slightly on lunch close (one lists 11:00–14:30). Irregular closures announced on Instagram; some sources note Monday evening closed.
places.neighborhood: 百万遍・田中門前町（左京区、京大近く） / Hyakumanben, Sakyo-ku, near Kyoto Univ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yumin is the abura-soba spin-off of Bishiya (美しが), a ramen shop in Ichijoji known for drawing lines. The team opened this second, oil-noodle-focused shop in the Hyakumanben student quarter just north of the Hyakumanben crossing near Kyoto University, taking over a space that had

chef_bio.background: Yumin is the abura-soba spin-off of Bishiya (びし屋), a ramen shop in Ichijoji known for drawing lines. The team opened this second, oil-noodle-focused shop in the Hyakumanben student quarter just north of the Hyakumanben crossing near Kyoto University, taking over a space that had been a curry restaurant. Staff from the Ichijoji parent moved over to run it. It opened on October 10, 2025, and was generating buzz before it even opened.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make oil soba approachable: lighter on the palate than its rich look suggests, with a good balance of acidity and aromatics, and table signage that walks first-timers through how to eat it. Diners mix everything themselves, dial in garlic to taste, and use the counter condiments (vinegar, chili oil, pepper, diced onion) to shift the flavor partway through the bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: Abura soba — brothless wheat noodles tossed with an oil-and-tare sauce. Three bowls anchor the menu: the standard abura soba and a spicy version (辛味あぶらそば), both on house-made noodles, plus a 背脂ヤサイあぶらそば (back-fat vegetable) bowl built in a Jiro style on thick, chewy noodles with pork fat and a mound of vegetables. The base bowl is about 750 yen, with cheap noodle upsizes up to 540g.

anecdotes.text: The shop took over a former curry restaurant in Hyakumanben and was already a talking point among Kyoto ramen fans before opening, on the strength of the Bishiya name.
One writer summed up the bowl as 'lighter than it looks, with a good balance of acidity and seasonings,' and noted the table signage guiding newcomers through mixing and topping their noodles.
The back-fat vegetable bowl deliberately breaks from the plain look of typical oil soba, piling on Jiro-style pork fat and vegetables on a thick crinkly noodle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi（taoZO）が一乗寺の行列店『びし屋』の2号店・百万遍（京大近く）のカレー店跡地・2025年10月10日開店・開店前から話題・3種（あぶらそば/辛味/背脂ヤサイ）・自家製麺・基本750円・食べ方POP・卓上調味料を確認。osumituki（223472）が背脂ヤサイの二郎系仕立てを確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are abura soba dressed in an oil-and-tare sauce of the standard type, which is based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on pork fat (背脂), fatty pork, eggs and mayo-based toppings shared across the kitchen; there is no plant-based bowl and no separation from animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual student-district ticket-machine shop where ordering and eating are wordless and self-directed, so basic Japanese (or just pointing at the machine) is plenty, but there is no English support or allergy accommodation.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from 100% domestic wheat, mochi-mochi chewy with a springy bite. The standard abura soba and spicy version share a common house noodle; the back-fat vegetable bowl uses a separate extra-thick, wavy, jiro-style noodle with a wari-wari chew. Base portion around 180g, scalable up to roughly 540g.

ramen.signature_bowl: 背脂ヤサイあぶらそば (Se-abura Yasai Abura Soba) — the brothless back-fat vegetable bowl on the thick jiro-style noodle; the shoyu-leaning standard あぶらそば is the lighter all-rounder.
ramen.chashu: A single thick-cut slab of tender braised pork chashu tops the signature vegetable bowl.

gf.note: Brothless abura soba built on wheat-and-kansui house noodles with a shoyu-forward (wheat) tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Bowls are dressed with pork back fat, braised pork chashu and a savory shoyu tare; no documented plant-only build. The vegetable variant still rides on back fat and meat, so it is not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A brothless Kyoto abura soba where the soul lives in the tare-slicked noodle rather than a soup: a shoyu-forward sauce with a faintly creamy sweetness, finished tableside with vinegar, garlic and chili oil. Its flagship leans into the city's back-fat heritage and a jiro-style mountain of cabbage, sprouts and lard on a thick wavy noodle — a "soupless Jiro shio" you mix, not sip.

places.name: 台湾まぜそば専門店 脳天喜。 (Taiwan Mazesoba Senmonten Noutenki (Kyoto Me)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Taiwanese mazesoba built on wheat noodles (the shop even lets you choose between two wheat-flour noodle types), tossed with a soy-sauce-based Taiwanese mince — both standard wheat-containing components. No gluten-free noodle, tare, or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Celiac diners should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu centers on pork — signature 東坡肉 (skin-on braised pork belly / dongpo pork), spicy Taiwanese pork mince, chashu rice bowls, and a chicken-paitan ramen. Eggs (味玉) feature prominently. No plant-based bowl is advertised.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–14:00, 18:00–21:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00–21:00. Closed: irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyō-ku, Senbon-Demizu (Nijō Stn / northwest of Nijō Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A specialty mazesoba shop that opened on November 20, 2024, on Senbon-dori. The signature dongpo-pork dish is prepared under a Chinese chef who keeps to an authentic recipe while tuning it to Japanese palates.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Kamigyo-ku (Senbon-Demizu, northwest of Nijo Castle)
chef_bio.background: A specialty mazesoba shop that opened on November 20, 2024, on Senbon-dori. The signature dongpo-pork dish is prepared under a Chinese chef who keeps to an authentic recipe while tuning it to Japanese palates.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality ingredients plus customization: the owner frames the two noodle choices as 'selecting the two ultimate options so you can enjoy Taiwanese mazesoba's deliciousness to the very end,' and sets out 10-plus condiments (garlic vinegar, kombu/anchovy vinegar and more) to encourage mid-meal 味変 (flavor-shifting).
chef_bio.specialty: 東坡肉台湾まぜそば — Taiwanese mazesoba crowned with dongpo pork (皮付きの豚の角煮, glossy skin-on braised pork belly slow-cooked with star anise). The house bills itself as Japan's first mazesoba shop that lets you pick your noodle, choosing between a medium-thick full-wheat noodle and a wavy 'wing' noodle so you can enjoy the bowl your way to the last bite.

anecdotes.text: The shop sources top-grade Kujo negi normally reserved for high-end kaiseki restaurants, and 'triple-folate' eggs from the Tanba/Mizuho area.
Beyond mazesoba the counter also serves a chicken-paitan ramen (泡鶏白湯拉麺) and chashu rice, with Taiwanese beer on offer to pair with the bowls.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: digistyle-kyoto（48193）が2024年11月開店・千本通（千本出水付近）・中国人シェフによる東坡肉まぜそば・台湾まぜそば専門店で初の麺選択（全粒粉ブレンド中太麺と波状麺）・高級料亭向けの九条ねぎ・丹波（瑞穂）の葉酸3倍たまご・10種の調味料を確認。kyo-soku（notenki）が泡鶏白湯拉麺・チャーシュー飯・台湾ビールを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-based Taiwanese mince; no gluten-free handling or separate prep is described, so heavy gluten cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Taiwanese mazesoba tare is conventionally a wheat-based soy-sauce mince; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is pork- and egg-forward (dongpo pork, pork mince, chashu, 味玉); no vegan bowl, and shared equipment with meat dishes should be assumed.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 6-seat neighborhood noodle counter with no indication of English service, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will make ordering smooth.

noodles.notes: Two noodle choices: a chunky medium-thick noodle blending whole-grain and wheat flour, or a rippled "wing" noodle (from a renowned Kyoto noodle maker) whose wavy surface grips the spicy mince and sauce. The wing noodle is the popular pick for how well the ingredients cling.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tonporo Taiwan Mazesoba (東坡肉台湾まぜそば) — brothless mixed noodles with spicy Taiwan mince, crowned with a slab of melting braised pork belly. Mix everything, then finish with a rice ball (追い飯) dunked into the leftover sauce.
ramen.chashu: The heart is not a typical ramen chashu but Chinese-style 東坡肉 (Dongpo/tonporo) braised pork belly — slow-cooked by a Chinese chef until quivering-tender, sweet-savory, with the star anise kept gentle for the Japanese palate. A diced-pork rice bowl and ajitama-chashu version are also offered.
notable_toppings: 10 table condiments incl. Aomori garlic-vinegar and niboshi-vinegar

gf.note: Both noodle options are wheat-based (whole-grain/wheat blend and wing noodle), the Taiwan mince and tare carry soy/wheat seasoning, and the rice finisher shares the same sauce. No gluten-free noodle or tare is documented.

vegan.note: Core dish is built on pork mince, braised pork belly, raw egg yolk and fish powder; the side ramen is chicken paitan. No documented plant-based broth or topping set.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto temple to Nagoya-born Taiwan mazesoba — the brothless, garlic-and-chili mince bowl you toss together yourself — but with a twist: a Chinese chef tops it with quivering Dongpo braised pork belly instead of the usual char siu. Choose your noodle, mix in the raw yolk, then dunk a rice ball into the lingering spicy-savory sauce for the classic two-act finish.

places.name: 力餅食堂 柿仲 (Chikara Mochi Shokudo Kakichu)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Showa-era noodle diner. The signature 中華そば uses fine wheat ramen noodles in a dashi-style broth, and the menu is built around wheat-based noodles (udon, soba, chūka soba), rice bowls and inari sushi. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Broth and tare almost certainly involve wheat-containing soy sauce. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish family diner: the 中華そば comes with thick char siu pork, two kinds of fish-cake kamaboko, and a dashi-based broth. Rice bowls and set meals are similarly animal-based. No vegan options are advertised and the kitchen is not set up for plant-based requests.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (some listings note 10:00-17:30), closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Fushimi-ku, Fukakusa shopping street (JR Fujinomori / Keihan Fukakusa Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Part of the historic Rikimochi (力餅) lineage that began in 1889 in Toyooka as a confectionery and spread across Kansai as a franchise of homey eateries; fewer than a handful of these old shops survive today. This Kakichu branch was opened independently in 1966 (Showa 41) by the fo

chef_bio.chef_name: Seiji Kakichu (founder of this branch; run today by the proprietor couple)
chef_bio.background: Part of the historic Rikimochi (力餅) lineage that began in 1889 in Toyooka as a confectionery and spread across Kansai as a franchise of homey eateries; fewer than about ten of these old shops survive today. This Kakichu branch was opened in 1966 (Showa 41) by Seiji — the father of today's proprietor — under the Rikimochi name, and remains a fixture of the Fukakusa shopping street.
chef_bio.philosophy: あっさりと飽きのこない家庭的な味 — a light, never-tiring homestyle flavor served in an unpretentious old-fashioned shokudo where everything stays under 1,000 yen and a solo diner is always welcome.
chef_bio.specialty: 中華そば (chūka soba) at around 680 yen: a deliberately light, pale broth closer to a gentle udon dashi than a heavy ramen soup, with fine straight noodles, thick-cut char siu, two kinds of kamaboko fish cake, generous Kyoto negi and menma. Their oversized inari sushi (about 150 yen), packed with black sesame, carrot and burdock, is a beloved side.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps a classic Showa-era atmosphere down to the television in the corner, and the owner couple know their regulars personally, trading easy small talk as customers come and go.
The 中華そば broth is so pale and gentle that it reads more like a soothing udon dashi than a typical ramen soup, the kind of bowl described as quietly soaking into your body.
Opened in 1966 by Seiji under the historic Rikimochi name, it is one of the dwindling number of these old Kansai diners still in operation.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi（GZjPd）が力餅の系譜（1889年豊岡の菓子屋に始まりフランチャイズで関西へ、現在10店未満）・この深草店は1966年（昭和41年）にせいじさんが開業・約680円の淡麗な中華そば（うどん出汁のよう、細ストレート麺、厚切りチャーシュー、2種のかまぼこ、京ねぎ、メンマ）・約150円の大きないなり（黒ごま・人参・ごぼう）・テレビのある昭和の雰囲気と常連客を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-noodle-centric diner (chūka soba, udon, soba) sharing one small kitchen; no gluten-free accommodation noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowl uses char siu pork, fish-cake kamaboko and a dashi-based broth; no plant-based preparation indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood Showa-era diner where the owner couple knows the regulars by name and casual Japanese greetings are the norm, so basic Japanese or a guide helps; little to no English is expected.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles that slide smoothly and easily into the mouth — light and unfussy, matched to the delicate broth rather than a chewy modern ramen noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (chuka soba) — the house bowl: a pale, gentle light-shoyu soup with thin straight noodles, served the old-shokudo way for under 1000 yen.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu, cut surprisingly thick for such a light bowl — a homely, tender slice rather than an aburi/char-grilled or melt-and-collapse style.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui chuka noodles in a soy-sauce (wheat) broth; this is a traditional town shokudo with no documented rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option.

vegan.note: The bowl is built on an animal/dashi base, soy tare, fish-cake kamaboko and pork chashu; no plant-broth (kombu/shiitake/veg) version is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is chuka soba in its most nostalgic, home-cooked form: the kind of pale, gentle light-shoyu bowl an old Kyoto neighborhood eatery has ladled out for generations, closer in spirit to a clear udon-soba broth than to any modern ramen specialist. Its soul is restraint — a clean, kind soup you could eat every day without tiring of it, dressed with two slices of kamaboko, menma and bright Kujo negi.

places.name: 中華そば 六 (Chuka Soba Roku)
places.gf_detail: This is a refined chuka-soba (Chinese-style ramen) specialist. The dish is built on custom-made wheat noodles, almost certainly served with a wheat-based shoyu/Chinese-style tare, and a meat broth drawn from five bone stocks (duck, local chicken, venison, beef, pork). Wonton toppings are wheat-wrapped. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or wheat-free option is advertised on any source reviewed. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is centered on an animal broth extracted from five types of bone (duck, free-range chicken, venison, beef, pork), and the signature wontons contain pork. There is no vegetarian or vegan ramen on the menu and no plant-based broth option. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / Dinner 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closes early when the broth runs out.
places.notes: Roku is produced by chef Yuji Iwasaki, who earned two Michelin stars at his high-end Chinese restaurant VELROSIER. The shop began as 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU (kamo-dashi / duck-broth chuka-soba) and was renewed and renamed to 中華そば 六 when it reopened on the second floor of GOOD NATURE STATION

chef_bio.background: Roku is produced by chef Yuji Iwasaki, who earned two Michelin stars at his high-end Chinese restaurant VELROSIER. The shop began as 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU (kamo-dashi / duck-broth chuka-soba) and was renewed and renamed to 中華そば 六 when it reopened on the second floor of GOOD NATURE STATION on May 13, 2023, narrowing its focus to a single, refined Chinese-style noodle soup.
chef_bio.philosophy: Applying the techniques of fine Chinese cuisine to ramen to create what the shop calls a 唯一無二 (one-of-a-kind) soup — an elevated, restaurant-grade take on the everyday chuka-soba.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 中華そば (Chuka Soba, around ¥1,100): a clear yet deeply layered broth that draws umami from five kinds of bone stock — duck, free-range chicken, venison, beef and pork — and builds further depth with porcini, longan fruit, tomato and dried shiitake. Straight noodles are custom-made to match the clear soup, and hand-made wontons (pork, onion, arrowhead and celery) are added partway through the bowl as a second act.

anecdotes.text: A two-Michelin-star Chinese chef (VELROSIER) channeling his craft into a tiny counter ramen shop, the bowl is engineered around Chinese stock technique rather than typical ramen broths — five animal bones layered with porcini, longan, tomato and shiitake.
The shop has been recognized in Michelin's Bib Gourmand listing for its chuka-soba.
Originally 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU, the restaurant relaunched as 中華そば 六 inside GOOD NATURE STATION on May 13, 2023, refocusing entirely on its Chinese-style noodle soup.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: goodnaturestation・kyotokurasu・lmaga・kyotopi より、ミシュラン二つ星『VELROSIER』オーナーシェフ岩崎祐司氏プロデュース、鴨・地鶏・鹿・牛・豚の5種のガラ＋ポルチーニ/龍眼肉/トマト/干し椎茸、追いワンタン（豚・玉ねぎ・クワイ・セロリ）、GOOD NATURE STATION 2Fで2023年5月13日リニューアル、ビブグルマン掲載を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish is wheat ramen noodles in a meat broth, with wheat-wrapped wontons, prepared in a small 12-seat shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is advertised. Treat as not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese-style chuka-soba, the tare is almost certainly soy/shoyu-based, which standardly contains wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth itself is animal-based (five bone stocks: duck, chicken, venison, beef, pork) and wontons contain pork, so there is no vegan-compatible base; cross-contact is moot because no plant-based option exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small 12-seat counter ramen shop inside a modern, visitor-friendly retail complex (GOOD NATURE STATION) by Kyoto Kawaramachi Station, so it is approachable with basic greetings even if deep English support is not guaranteed.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight custom-made noodles with a firm bite, chosen to define against the silky clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — a crystal-clear duck-forward chukasoba built on a five-bone stock; optionally finished with the table-side trio of pork-and-water-chestnut wontons.
ramen.chashu: Pork chashu cooked low in a convection oven to stay moist and juicy rather than braised soft, served refined and clean to match the delicate soup.
notable_toppings: side wontons (pork, onion, water chestnut, celery)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a shoyu-style tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free seasoning is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is drawn from duck, chicken, venison, beef and pork bones with animal-fat finishing oil and pork chashu; no plant-only bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A chef-driven, modern-original take on chukasoba: instead of one signature bone, the soup is a layered consommé pulled from duck, chicken, venison, beef and pork and rounded with porcini, dried shiitake, tomato and longan, so you taste depth without weight. It reads less like a regional gotochi ramen and more like a refined-Chinese-kitchen interpretation of the clear-soup Kyoto bowl, where finesse and aromatics carry the lineage.

places.name: 京・清水 油そば ねこまた (Kyo-Kiyomizu Abura Soba Nekomata)
places.gf_detail: Abura soba is a brothless wheat-noodle dish built on a soy-based (wheat) tare with sesame oil, topped with chashu pork — wheat is present in both the thick flat noodles and the tare. No gluten-free noodle or tamari option is advertised, and there is no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on animal ingredients: diced chashu pork, soy-based tare, and toppings such as naruto (fish cake) and menma. No vegan or vegetarian version is mentioned. The side of 卵かけご飯 (egg-on-rice) is also non-vegan. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–20:00 (last order 19:45), daily (Tabelog). Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama-ku, Kiyomizu (Matsubara-dōri / near the entrance to the Kiyomizu-dera approach; near Kiyomizu-Gojō Stn and 'Kiyomizu-michi' bus stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Billed as Kansai's first abura-soba specialty shop, this small noodle counter sits in the thick of the Kiyomizu tourist district, on Matsubara-dori between Gojo and Shijo, near the approach to Kiyomizu-dera.

chef_bio.background: Billed as Kansai's first abura-soba specialty shop, this small noodle counter sits in the thick of the Kiyomizu tourist district, on Matsubara-dori between Gojo and Shijo, near the approach to Kiyomizu-dera.
chef_bio.specialty: Abura soba — 'ramen without the soup.' Thick, flat kishimen-like noodles are tossed with a soy-based tare and fragrant sesame oil pooled at the bottom of the bowl, dressed with thick menma, diced chashu, green onion and naruto. The house signature is the 新元祖油そば (around 800 yen), and the lineup runs across soy, spicy-miso, and shrimp-salt versions, with a side of egg-on-rice (卵かけご飯) a popular add-on.

anecdotes.text: The shop hands you a little dish of minced raw onion to scatter on top — the way to eat it is to lift the tare up from the bottom of the bowl and mix everything thoroughly so noodles, toppings and sauce come together before you dig in; the onion adds sharpness and crunch.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi と食べログより、関西初の油そば専門店、松原通（五条〜四条）の清水観光地、きしめん風の平打ち麺、太メンマ・角切りチャーシュー・ねぎ・なると、新元祖油そば800円、辛味噌・海老塩などのバリエーション、卵かけご飯300円、刻み玉ねぎを添えて底からよく混ぜる食べ方を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based abura soba noodles and a soy (wheat) tare are the core of every bowl; no gluten-free preparation is offered or mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is built on a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in standard Japanese practice contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls include diced chashu pork plus naruto fish cake and menma; no vegan/vegetarian version is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual tourist-area noodle counter where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly greeting will carry you through; do not expect English allergen support.

noodles.notes: Flat, wide kishimen-like noodles with a chewy mochimochi bite, built to grab and hold the tare and oil pooled at the bottom of the bowl. You mix vigorously from the bottom so noodles, oil, tare, and toppings become one.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ganso (original) shoyu abura soba — the brothless house bowl: thick flat noodles dressed in sesame oil and a soy-based tare, finished at the table with chili oil and vinegar to taste. Spicy-miso and ebi (shrimp) shio versions are the other two pillars.
ramen.chashu: Cubed/diced roasted pork tossed through the noodles rather than laid as sheets, so it folds into each bite as you mix.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-soy tare; abura soba is fundamentally a wheat dish with no documented rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option.

vegan.note: Pork chashu, soy-based tare and likely fish-derived seasoning run through every bowl; no documented plant-based version. The ebi (shrimp) and naruto fish cake further rule it out.

ramen.pantheon: A brothless bowl in the Tokyo-born abura soba lineage, set down in the lanes below Kiyomizu: there is no soup, so the soul lives in the marriage of nutty sesame oil and a punchy soy tare clinging to thick, chewy flat noodles. You build the flavor yourself with chili oil and a splash of vinegar, and the dish reveals itself only after a hard mix from the bottom.

places.name: コムギノキラメキ 上桂前田町本店 (Komugi no Kirameki Kamikatsura Maedacho Hont)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen and mazesoba shop with its own on-site noodle factory, so the noodles are wheat-based; the chicken-paitan ramen and Taiwan mazesoba are built on wheat noodles plus a shoyu/tare seasoning that typically contains wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, dedicated equipment, or any celiac accommodation. Not suitable for someone who needs to avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The core menu is animal-based: chicken-paitan (tori-paitan) broth simmered from chicken, and Taiwan-style mazesoba topped with minced pork, egg yolk, chives and garlic, plus a back-fat "小麦のチカラ" with pork chunks. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered, and broths/tare are meat-based. Not a vegan-friendly option.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30) / Closed: none (may close temporarily on occasion)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Komugi no Kirameki (Komugi) is one of the shops of the Kyoto-born ramen chain 'Kirameki Group' (operator: Kirameki no Mirai Co., Ltd.), a spacious suburban-style location along National Route 9 in Kamikatsura Maeda-cho. It is part of a group that expanded from its flagship brand 'Kirameki no Tori' in Marutamachi to across Kyoto City, Fukuchiyama, Ayabe, and further to Osaka (Suita, Takatsuki). A notable feature is the attached in-house noodle factory, visible through glass, where they make their own noodles.

roles: キラメキグループ店舗
chef_bio.background: コムギノキラメキ〈小麦〉は、京都発のラーメンチェーン「キラメキグループ」（運営：キラメキノ未来株式会社）の店舗のひとつで、上桂前田町の国道9号沿いに構える広々とした郊外型の一軒。本店ブランドである丸太町の「キラメキノトリ」から京都市内・福知山・綾部、さらに大阪（吹田・高槻）へと勢力を広げてきたグループの一翼で、店内にはガラス越しに見える自家製の製麺所を併設し、麺を自前で打っているのが大きな特徴。
chef_bio.philosophy: 自家製麺と濃厚な一杯で“小麦（コムギ）”そのものの力を主役にする、というコンセプトを店名に込め、子ども向けラーメンの用意やポイント制度など、地元のファミリーや一人客が気軽に通える普段使いの一杯を目指している。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は二本立て。長時間炊き出した鶏のうまみが凝縮した白濁スープの『鶏白湯ラーメン』（醤油・塩あり）と、豚ミンチ・卵黄・ニラ・にんにくをのせたガツンと濃い『台湾まぜそば』。さらに背脂系で麺量を最大500gまで増やせる『小麦のチカラ』など、自家製の太麺を主役にした食べごたえ重視の品が揃う。

anecdotes.text: ガラス張りの製麺所を併設し、自家製の麺をその場で作っている様子が客席から見える。
鶏白湯スープは白濁してコクが強く、飲んだ後に舌へ粉末状のうまみがザラッと残るほど鶏が凝縮していると評される。
キラメキグループ5店舗目として国道9号沿いの上桂に出店し、使い勝手のよい郊外型の店として紹介されている。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: genjitsutouhi・kyotopi・公式より、キラメキグループ（キラメキノ未来株式会社）の5店舗目、上桂・国道9号沿いの郊外型店、ガラス張りの自家製製麺所（グループの製麺工房を兼ねる）、鶏白湯（醤油・塩）と台湾まぜそば、麺量最大500gの『小麦のチカラ』を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Self-described as a wheat-noodle shop ('コムギ／小麦') with an on-site noodle factory, so wheat is central and pervasive throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free option exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken-paitan ramen is offered in a soy-sauce (醤油) variety; standard ramen shoyu tare is typically wheat-based, and no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All signature bowls are animal-based — chicken broth, minced pork, egg yolk; no vegan items are prepared, so there is no separated vegan line.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, family-friendly suburban ramen shop with a ticket-vending machine ordering system and no English menu, so a guide or translation help is useful for navigating toppings and noodle-portion choices.

noodles.notes: Made fresh in-house at a glassed-in noodle factory using a blend of Hokkaido/Nagano/Australian wheat. The chicken paitan ramen gets medium-thick noodles with firm koshi to stand up to the rich broth; the Taiwan mazesoba and Jiro-style 小麦のチカラ use very thick (around 3mm), short-cut straight noodles with a dense chew.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏白湯らーめん (chicken paitan ramen) — order it shoyu to show off the concentrated chicken; the Taiwan mazesoba (台湾まぜそば) is the co-flagship, with the Jiro-style 小麦のチカラ for big appetites.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu in large slices that drape across the bowl; the thick-cut tender, fatty pork belly piled on the 小麦のチカラ mazesoba is the bolder, fattier version.

gf.note: Wheat-based handmade noodles (kansui) are the house specialty and a shoyu tare is used; nothing rice/konjac-based is offered, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is simmered chicken, topped with pork chashu (and ground pork on the mazesoba); no documented plant-based broth or topping option.

ramen.pantheon: A tori-paitan house in the modern Kyoto idiom: the soup is built from chicken alone, simmered long into a milky, umami-dense bowl that still drinks surprisingly clean, dressed with deliberate restraint so the bird stays center stage. Alongside it sits a thick, chewy Taiwan mazesoba and a Jiro-leaning, back-fat-heavy noodle plate, all riding house-milled, in-house-cut noodles.

places.name: 中華 六六 (Chuka Rokuroku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature chuka-soba uses standard wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (醤油) broth, and the wider menu (gyoza, fried chicken/karaage, shumai, stir-fries) leans heavily on wheat flour and wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, no celiac accommodation, and no allergen handling is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian specialist. This is a meat-and-seafood town-Chinese kitchen — char siu, fried chicken, shrimp dumplings, crab omelette, pork trotters — and the ramen broth is animal-based. A few vegetable dishes exist (e.g. a zha cai/celery/dried-tofu salad, mapo-style and stir-fried items) but they typically use meat-based seasoning, soy sauce and shared woks, so a vegan would have to ask directly and expect very limited options.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–13:45 (last order) / Dinner 17:30–21:00 (last order). Closed Mon. Hours are not clearly stated on Tabelog and some sources note irregular days off, so confirming via Facebook etc. before visiting is recommended.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 中華 六六 sits in a renovated Kyoto machiya just west of the Horikawa-Kitaoji crossing, in the Murasakino district a short walk from Daitoku-ji temple. The building once housed a Japanese restaurant; its high ceilings and shoes-off, eel's-nest layout — a few tables by the entrance an

chef_bio.background: 中華 六六 sits in a renovated Kyoto machiya just west of the Horikawa-Kitaoji crossing, in the Murasakino district a short walk from Daitoku-ji temple. Its high ceilings and shoes-off, eel's-nest layout — a few tables by the entrance and a long counter deep in the back — make a warm, retro setting for Chinese home-style cooking that locals treat as a townhouse take on neighborhood Chinese.
chef_bio.philosophy: A neighborhood kitchen that prizes authentic ingredients — its dried tofu is specially sourced from mainland China — and leans on volume and comfort over flash, the sort of filling, hospitable lunch that keeps regulars coming back.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for hearty, generous lunch sets around 1,000 yen — a fried-chicken (yurin-chi) set, mapo and weekly-rotating set menus — alongside a soy-sauce chuka-soba. That bowl leads with a clean shoyu base carrying a gentle tang and the sweetness of scallion and onion, medium straight noodles, a thick slab of char siu, and menma that one local guide called the best they had ever eaten.

anecdotes.text: The traditional machiya architecture is as much the experience as the food — you take your shoes off at the entrance — the kind of townhouse Chinese ('町家中華') that sets it apart from a plain 町中華.
One diner stumbled on the shop walking back from Daitoku-ji, drawn in purely by the machiya frontage, and singled out the bamboo-shoot menma as the finest they had tasted.
A Kyoto food guide praised the volume-packed sub-1,000-yen lunches — 'お腹パンパン、ボリューム満点のランチ' — in a clean, nostalgic space favored by locals.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-gourmet・kyotopi・digistyle より、紫野西御所田町（堀川北大路の西）の京町家を改装したレトロな中華店、靴を脱ぐ造り、1,000円前後・満腹のランチ、醤油中華そばの厚切りチャーシューと絶品メンマ、干し豆腐は本場中国から取り寄せ、を確認。前身が日本料理店だったとの記述は出典で確認できず削除。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled and animal/soy-based broth, gyoza and karaage are prepared in the same small kitchen, so heavy wheat cross-contact is unavoidable for a celiac.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba broth is built on a soy-sauce (醤油) base, which in a town-Chinese kitchen is standard wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat- and seafood-centered (char siu, fried chicken, shrimp dumplings, crab omelette, pork trotters) with shared woks; vegetable dishes likely use animal-based seasonings.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood machiya Chinese spot where you remove your shoes at the door and order from a Japanese-only menu, so basic Japanese or a translation app and a relaxed attitude help a lot.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick, straight, translucent noodles of the old-school machi-chuka type — soft and not the focus of the bowl; the soup and toppings carry it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the house clear soy-sauce noodle bowl
ramen.chashu: A thick, single cut of braised pork chashu with a notably good lean-to-fat balance.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare in a townhouse Chinese kitchen with no documented rice/konjac-noodle or GF option — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A meat-stock soy-sauce broth with pork chashu and no documented plant-based bowl; not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: This is a town-Chinese chuka soba rather than a specialist's ramen: a clear, gently sweet soy-sauce soup whose soul is the perfume of scallion and the mellow sweetness of slow-cooked onion, served the old machiya way. It leans nostalgic and homey — light and easy-drinking, defined more by its tender housemade-style menma and a generous slab of chashu than by any regional ramen lineage.

places.name: キラメキノトリ (Kirameki no Tori)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The ramen is built on in-house wheat noodles (made from 100% Hokkaido wheat at the group's own noodle workshop), and the menu also features Taiwan mazesoba (wheat) and karaage (wheat-dredged fried chicken). The chicken-paitan broth comes in shio/shoyu/miso tares; the shoyu and miso versions involve wheat-based soy sauce/miso. There is no published gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen-handling information. A celiac diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is chicken: the broth is a chicken-bone paitan simmered with chicken thigh meat and skin, toppings include chicken-thigh chashu, and the shop is paired with karaage. No vegetable or plant-based broth or noodle option is published.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (last order 14:30) / 18:00–23:00 (last order 22:30). Open daily, with occasional temporary closures.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo-ku, Demizu-cho — west of the Kyoto Imperial Palace (
places.notes: This is the original flagship (一号店) of Kirameki no Tori, opened in 2013 near the Kawaramachi-Marutamachi crossing in Kyoto's Kamigyo ward. From this single counter the brand grew into a chicken-focused group (operated by Kirameki no Mirai Co.) with around two dozen shops across K

chef_bio.background: This is the original flagship (一号店) of Kirameki no Tori, opened in 2013 just north of the Kawaramachi-Marutamachi crossing (Demizu-cho, Kamigyo ward) in Kyoto. From this single counter the brand grew into a chicken-focused group (operated by Kirameki no Mirai Co.) with around 30 shops across Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga and Nara, plus a noodle workshop in Nishikyo ward — but the Demizu-cho shop remains where it all started.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything revolves around the chicken: the broth is built from chicken bones simmered with thigh meat and skin for a rich, sweet umami that is full-bodied without being heavy, and the noodles are made in-house from 100% Hokkaido wheat for a fragrant, crisp bite. The group's stated aim is to keep taste and service at a high level even as it expands.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori-paitan ramen — a creamy chicken white-broth bowl offered in shio, shoyu and miso. The signature shio version is finished with chicken-thigh chashu, white leek, mizuna, thread chili, menma and a slice of lemon for brightness. Taiwan mazesoba and karaage round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The broth is made by carefully simmering chicken bones together with chicken thigh meat and skin, giving a soup with clear chicken richness and sweetness that avoids feeling overly heavy.
The noodles are produced at the group's own noodle workshop in Nishikyo ward using 100% Hokkaido wheat, prized for their aroma and crisp texture.
Opened in 2013 as the very first shop, it became the seed of a chicken-ramen group that now spans Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga and Nara.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi と公式（キラメキノ未来）より、2013年開業の出水町一号店（河原町丸太町すぐ）、西京区の自社製麺工房・北海道産小麦100%、塩ラーメンの具（鶏もも肉チャーシュー・白髪ねぎ・水菜・糸唐辛子・レモン・メンマ）、京都・大阪・滋賀・奈良に展開する鶏白湯グループ（公式店舗一覧では約33店）を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen handles wheat ramen noodles, Taiwan mazesoba (wheat noodles) and wheat-dredged karaage, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable in this environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken-paitan bowls are offered in shoyu and miso versions, whose tare uses standard wheat-containing soy sauce/miso; even the shio version is a wheat-noodle dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a chicken-bone paitan simmered with chicken meat and skin and topped with chicken chashu; there is no plant-based broth or noodle option, so no vegan service is possible.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local ramen counter with ticket-machine ordering and self-service water; a little Japanese or pointing helps, and no special accommodations should be expected.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from the group's own noodle factory using 100% Hokkaido wheat; medium-thick straight cut with a chewy, mochi-mochi bite that clings to the creamy broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori paitan ramen, shio version (the salt tare best showcases the chicken umami, finished with lemon); the night-only Taiwan mazesoba is the other house specialty.
ramen.chashu: Chicken thigh chashu — tender and moist, served with a seared skin for a bit of char and bite (no pork in the signature bowl).

gf.note: Noodles are wheat (100% Hokkaido wheat) plus kansui, and the shoyu/miso tare add wheat; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered chicken paitan with chicken chashu and chicken fat; no plant-based broth or vegan bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern tori-paitan specialist whose soul is a creamy, deeply emulsified chicken broth simmered to a milky richness yet finished clean enough to drink to the bottom. Order it shio with a squeeze of lemon and you taste the lineage of the contemporary chicken-paitan movement rather than any one regional tradition — pure poultry umami carried by chewy Hokkaido-wheat noodles and a skin-seared chicken chashu.

places.name: 御旅飯店 (Otabi Hanten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is an old-school neighborhood Chinese diner whose core dishes — 中華そば (wheat ramen noodles), fried rice, gyoza, ankake/oyster-sauce stir-fries over rice (the signature 御旅丼) — all rely on wheat noodles, wheat-flour dumpling skins and soy-based (wheat-containing) seasonings, typically cooked on shared surfaces and woks. No dedicated gluten-free menu, prep, or fryer was found. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. The kitchen centers on pork, egg and seafood (the signature 御旅丼 is stir-fried pork, cabbage, carrot, onion, wood-ear and egg over rice; menu spans yakiniku, fried shrimp and gyoza). Broths and tare are meat/seafood based. No vegan dishes were identified, and there is no indication the kitchen can prepare a meaningful plant-based meal. A vegan diner should ask, but should not expect a workable option here.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–14:30, 17:30–23:00 (closed Wed). Some sources give lunch starting at 11:30 and dinner ending at 22:00.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyō-ku, Kitaōji-Ōmiya (Funaokayama / in front of Daitoku-ji; Kuramaguchi Subway Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 御旅飯店 is a long-standing neighborhood Chinese diner (町中華) tucked into the old Kitaoji-Omiya quarter near Funaokayama and the Daitokuji temple precinct. Its name comes from the adjacent 御旅所 (otabi-sho) — the resting place for the portable shrine during Imamiya Shrine's festival eac

chef_bio.background: 御旅飯店 is a long-standing neighborhood Chinese diner (町中華) tucked into the old Kitaoji-Omiya quarter near Funaokayama and the Daitokuji temple precinct. Its name comes from the adjacent 御旅所 (otabi-sho) — the resting place for the portable shrine during Imamiya Shrine's festival each May. This pocket of the city flourished as a merchant district in the Edo period and quieted after the early Showa era as the Nishijin textile trade declined, leaving behind exactly the kind of unhurried local shop the restaurant still embodies.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hearty, affordable, community-first cooking — big portions, free rice refills, and a deep two-sided menu of Chinese standards (fried rice, gyoza, noodles, fried and grilled dishes) priced for everyday eating.
chef_bio.specialty: The house pride is the 御旅丼 (Otabi-don), a local bowl that is, in the words of one writer, 'barely known outside the neighborhood': stir-fried pork with cabbage, carrot, onion, wood-ear mushroom and egg, bound in a Chinese-style sauce over a generous mound of rice (said to hold roughly three servings). The shop also serves a proper 中華そば and offers ramen as an add-on to many dishes.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits right by the otabi-sho used during Imamiya Shrine's May festival, which is where its name '御旅' comes from.

It is a narrow, old-school place with counter seats and small tatami sections, stocked with comics, magazines and a TV, full of neighborhood workers and university students; staff move briskly through the lunch rush.
Rice refills are free, and the kitchen still runs deliveries the old way using traditional okamochi carrying boxes.
Known locally for big, well-balanced portions, including the signature 御旅丼.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi と gcjapan より、今宮神社の御旅所が店名の由来、江戸期に商家町として栄え昭和期に西陣衰退で静かになった一帯の町中華、御旅丼（豚肉・野菜・卵の中華餡でご飯約3杯分・かなりローカル）、ご飯おかわり無料、岡持ちでの出前、カウンター＋小上がりで漫画・雑誌・TVありの雰囲気を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, wheat-flour gyoza skins and soy-based seasonings dominate the menu, cooked on shared woks and surfaces in a small kitchen — high cross-contact risk for gluten throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned with Chinese-style soy/oyster sauces (the 御旅丼 sauce is described as a Chinese sauce/oyster-sauce base); standard Japanese and Chinese soy sauces contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking centers on pork, egg and seafood across stir-fries, fried rice and gyoza; broths and sauces are meat/seafood based, so any vegetable dish would share equipment and seasonings with animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local Chinese diner where Japanese-language ordering and casual local manners are the norm; a friendly spot but expect little to no English support.

noodles.notes: Standard old-school 中華そば noodles in the neighborhood-Chinese idiom; specific gauge, shape and hydration are not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (the house Chinese soba) — a chicken-bone shoyu bowl; note the shop's true headliner is actually the 御旅丼 rice bowl, with the noodle dishes a beloved sideline. Also offered as a mini-ramen add-on (+¥200) to a rice set.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based soy tare; no rice/konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented at this neighborhood Chinese diner. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The bowl is built on a chicken-bone broth with a soy tare; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) broth option is documented. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: An everyday Kyoto 町中華 (neighborhood Chinese) bowl rather than a destination ramen: a clear chicken-bone shoyu soup, seasoned bold and on the stronger side, in the homey Showa-era 中華そば tradition you find in the back-streets near Funaokayama and Daitoku-ji. You taste the lineage of the all-purpose Chinese kitchen — the same stock that anchors its famous rice bowls — rather than a single regional school.

places.name: いいちょ ラーメン (Iicho Ramen)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles (from Rairaiken/Kairaikian seimen) and a wheat-based soy sauce tare (Sawai twice-fermented "double-koji" shoyu), with no gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation noted anywhere. Treat as off-limits for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. The signature broth is a chicken-bone and pork-bone blend (roughly 7:3) with back fat and sliced chashu pork; the popular yakimeshi side uses egg and pork fat. No plant-based option is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 15:00); closes early if sold out. Lunch-only operation since February 2025. Closed Wednesday and Thursday plus occasional irregular closures. (Prior to Feb 2025 also served dinner 17:30-21:00, L.O. 20:30.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Iicho opened in 1998 in the Shimogamo neighborhood of Sakyo-ku, a short walk east of Kitaoji across the Kamogawa. The shop carries on the lineage of the so-called 'phantom Iwakura ramen' (幻の岩倉ラーメン), a Kyoto street-cart ramen whose flavor passed to Yamasan Ramen before that shop e

chef_bio.background: Iicho opened in 1998 in the Shimogamo neighborhood of Sakyo-ku, a short walk east of Kitaoji across the Kamogawa. The shop carries on the lineage of the so-called 'phantom Iwakura ramen' (幻の岩倉ラーメン), a Kyoto street-cart ramen whose flavor passed to Yamasan Ramen before that shop eventually moved away to Shiga; the founder learned the style from that tradition and brought it into the modern era.
chef_bio.philosophy: The proprietor sums up his approach as 'people are moved by heart,' treating each day's open as the start of a fresh battle and refining the bowl through constant, patient improvement.
chef_bio.specialty: An assari shoyu chuka-soba: a lightly cloudy broth of chicken bone (specifically the shin) and pork bone in roughly a 7:3 ratio, finished with back fat, thin chashu, and Kyoto negi. The tare is Sawai shoyu, a twice-fermented 'double-koji' soy sauce that gives the bowl its punchy backbone. Medium-thin straight noodles from Rairaiken keep an old-Showa chuka-soba character. The egg-and-pork-fat yakimeshi, with heavily caramelized edges, is a beloved companion.

anecdotes.text: The recipe descends from the legendary Iwakura cart ramen, later carried by Yamasan Ramen; Iicho opened in 1998 to keep that disappearing flavor alive.
The broth leans on aged chicken rather than young birds for deeper flavor, with small pork bones added gradually to build umami and exacting quality checks on the ingredients.
The kitchen has used the same Rairaiken (来々軒) noodles since opening, aiming to keep a strong nostalgic Showa-era chuka-soba feel while still tasting contemporary.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトと osumituki より、1998年下鴨（左京区）開業、幻の岩倉ラーメン→山さんラーメン（後に滋賀へ移転）の系譜、『人は心で動く』の理念、鶏（すね）と豚骨7:3・老鶏使用・背脂・薄切りチャーシュー・京ねぎ、澤井醤油の二度仕込みダレ、来々軒の中細ストレート麺、玉子と豚脂の焦がし焼き飯を確認。麺屋名の別表記『Kairaikian』は出典になく削除。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles and a wheat soy-sauce (Sawai shoyu) tare, so gluten is central to the dish; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is Sawai shoyu, a twice-fermented double-koji soy sauce, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone and pork-bone based with back fat and chashu; the yakimeshi uses egg and pork fat. No vegan/vegetarian item exists, so all cooking surfaces and stock are animal-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, classic neighborhood ramen counter where a few words of Japanese and an easygoing attitude go a long way; no English service is advertised, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu work best.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles from a local Kyoto noodle maker, with a slightly soft, traditional mid-century bite (細めのストレート). Made by an outside noodle factory, not in-house.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu chuka soba (醤油ラーメン) — the chicken-and-pork blended soy-sauce bowl; add chashu for the chashu-men, and pair with the shop's signature fried rice (yakimeshi) as a set.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced pork chashu braised for about half a day in the shop's own Sawai soy-sauce tare before serving (薄切りチャーシュー).

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with a soy tare and pork back-fat, and chashu tops the bowl. No plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A traditional Kyoto-style soy bowl that carries the lineage of the long-gone Iwakura ramen: a chicken-and-pork blend simmered to a faintly clouded, deeply chicken-scented broth, seasoned with a punchy artisanal Kyoto soy sauce and finished with melting back-fat. You taste the old Rakuhoku neighborhoods in every sip — comfort-food shoyu rather than flashy modern ramen.

places.name: 麺屋 誉 (Menya Homare)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen and mazesoba shop. Noodles are wheat (from maker 麺屋棣鄂), the tare is a soy-sauce (typically wheat-containing) shoyu base, and the broth is a concentrated pork-bone-and-chicken stock. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, dishes, or accommodation. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Every documented bowl is built on an animal base: a concentrated pork-bone (豚骨) and chicken (鶏ガラ) broth, with toppings such as chashu pork, ajitsuke (egg) tamago, and karaage. No vegetarian or vegan ramen option is mentioned anywhere in the sources.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Wed 11:00–15:00 (last order 14:30) / Fri/Sat 11:00–15:00 & 18:00–21:30 (last order 21:00) / closed Thu & Sun. Tabelog also lists Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri/Sat 11:00–14:00, 17:30–21:00 and Wed 11:00–14:00, 17:00–21:00; hours differ by source.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺屋 誉 (Menya Homare) opened on October 22, 2025 as the reborn version of a neighborhood favorite, フカクサ製麺食堂 (Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo). That predecessor began life humbly as a mazesoba specialist tucked into the corner of a parking lot, later moved into a proper storefront, and buil

chef_bio.background: 麺屋 誉 (Menya Homare) opened on October 22, 2025 as the reborn version of a neighborhood favorite, フカクサ製麺食堂 (Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo). That predecessor began life humbly as a mazesoba specialist tucked into the corner of a parking lot, later moved into a proper storefront, and built a following on chicken-paitan (鶏白湯) ramen. When it closed at the end of September 2025, the shop reopened weeks later under the new Homare name with a completely retooled bowl.
chef_bio.philosophy: A carefully tended broth above all: 豚骨と鶏ガラを手間暇かけた濃厚スープは雑味が少なく、まろやかでとろみのある — a long-simmered pork-and-chicken stock prized for being rich yet clean, with little harshness and a smooth, slightly thick body. Bowls can be tuned to the guest, with noodle firmness, broth saltiness, and chicken-oil volume adjustable to taste.
chef_bio.specialty: 特製濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメン — a concentrated pork-bone-and-chicken soy-sauce ramen with a soy-forward, punchy, smooth and full-bodied broth.
