The default bowl arrives with two kinds of chashu (a thick, smoky pork and a black-pepper pastrami-style loin), a half-cooked ajitsuke egg, a blend of spinach/komatsuna greens, and sheets of nori, over straight medium-thick custom noodles from the long-established maker 麺屋棣鄂 (Teigaku).

anecdotes.text: The shop carries forward two things from its フカクサ製麺食堂 days: the popular まぜそば, and a per-piece karaage that guests order by the number (about ¥100 each).
A student discount lets those with a student ID upgrade to a large noodle portion for free — and it applies to the mazesoba too.
The new 濃厚豚骨醤油 bowl reads less like classic Iekei family-style ramen and more like a straight, intense pork-bone-soy ramen, with the soy notes leading.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki・kyotopi・noodlers より、2025年10月22日開業、前身フカクサ製麺食堂（2025年9月末閉店、鶏白湯で人気）のリニューアル、豚骨＋鶏ガラの濃厚豚骨醤油、低温調理の2種チャーシュー（燻香の豚と黒胡椒パストラミ）、ほうれん草・白ねぎ・海苔3枚、麺屋棣鄂（1931年創業）の中太ストレート麺、まぜそばと1個約100円の唐揚げを継続、学割で麺大盛り無料、伏見深草・藤森駅近くを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, a soy-sauce-based tare, and shared broth/cooking water make cross-contamination with gluten effectively unavoidable for a celiac diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowl is a 濃厚豚骨醤油 (pork-bone soy-sauce) ramen; the tare is soy-sauce based, which in standard ramen contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths and dishes are animal-based (pork bone and chicken stock with chashu, egg, and karaage); no vegan preparation or separation is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local counter ramen shop with cash-only payment and no reservations; some basic Japanese helps for ordering and customization, with little to no English support expected.

noodles.notes: Custom noodles from the venerable Kyoto maker Teigaku (棣鄂), founded 1931 — medium-thick, mostly straight with a very subtle wave, cut slightly thinner than typical Hakata noodles. Springy, chewy and built for slurping, with enough body to stand up to the heavy soup. Firmness is adjustable to order.

ramen.signature_bowl: 特製濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメン (Tokusei rich tonkotsu-shoyu ramen) — the deluxe bowl loaded with both chashu styles, an ajitama, greens and nori.
ramen.chashu: A two-style pairing: smoky low-temperature braised pork with deep savory richness, plus a pastrami-style cured loin crowned with cracked black pepper — both cooked sous-vide-tender.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare over a pork-and-chicken broth; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered pork-bone and chicken broth dressed with shoyu tare, pork back-fat/chicken oil and pork chashu — animal-derived throughout. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A thick, velvety pork-and-chicken paitan lacquered with a bold soy tare — this bowl leans into the Yokohama Iekei spirit of muscular tonkotsu-shoyu, but pushes the richness and salt-forward punch harder than Kyoto's gentler local style. You taste the deep, faintly sweet collagen of slow-rendered pork bones, sharpened by a generous soy backbone and a table-side hit of grated garlic and niboshi vinegar.

places.name: 和麺ぐり虎 京都本店 (Wamen Guriko Kyoto Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The custom noodles are made from domestic wheat flour, and the kitchen handles wheat throughout. While the bowls are salt-based (shio) rather than soy-sauce-based, there is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation area, and the same broth/noodle line is used for all dishes. Cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable. The shop also offers a separate fresh-soy-sauce (生醤油) ramen, which would contain wheat. No GF accommodation is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Both signature bowls are animal-based: the chicken-salt (鶏塩) broth is an eight-hour chicken consommé (with added shijimi clam at the Kyoto branch), and the sea-bream-salt (鯛塩) broth is built on wild domestic sea bream. The shio tare also layers in bonito (katsuo) dashi. Toppings include chashu pork and egg. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 daily (L.O. 21:30); through-service. May close earlier when the soup/ingredients sell out.
places.notes: Chef Sho Isogai came up as an Italian cook before turning to ramen, apprenticing at celebrated Tokyo shops including Mensho. In 2017 he opened his own 麺処ぐり虎 (Mendokoro Guriko) in Okayama, which went on to earn a Michelin Bib Gourmand and repeat recognition across the Kyoto, Osaka

chef_bio.origin: Okayama (original shop); the chef trained in Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Chef Sho Isogai came up as an Italian cook before turning to ramen, apprenticing at celebrated Tokyo shops including Mensho. In 2017 he opened his own 麺処ぐり虎 (Mendokoro Guriko) in Okayama, which went on to earn a Michelin Bib Gourmand and repeat recognition across the Kyoto, Osaka and Okayama guides, plus top finishes at national ramen festivals and appearances at the Tokyo Ramen Show. 和麺ぐり虎 is his second brand, and this Kyoto branch on Rokkaku-dori opened on July 23, 2025 as his first foray into Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: Isogai approaches ramen 'like a course,' marrying Italian and Japanese technique and deliberately restraining seasoning so the integrity of carefully chosen ingredients comes through. The 和麺 (wamen) concept centers on preserving and nurturing Japan's exceptional ingredients and food culture.
chef_bio.specialty: Two salt (shio) bowls define the shop. The 鶏塩 (chicken-salt) is a clear, eight-hour chicken consommé — deepened at the Kyoto branch with shijimi clam — finished with a house salt tare that blends salts from Japan, Italy, France and Mongolia and a layered dashi of kombu, shiitake and bonito. The 鯛塩 (sea-bream-salt) showcases wild domestic sea bream cooked low and slow for a clean, aromatic, umami-deep broth, with a tare built on Okinawan and Niigata sea salts. Custom thin straight noodles made from 100% domestic wheat are matched to the soups.

anecdotes.text: The chef's Italian-kitchen roots shape the salt tare, which draws on salts from Japan, Italy, France and Mongolia — an unusually cosmopolitan seasoning for a Japanese shio ramen.
The Kyoto branch's chicken-salt bowl adds shijimi clam to the eight-hour chicken consommé for extra depth while keeping the seasoning restrained.
Raw/boiled egg and nori are offered freely at the tables alongside any bowl.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: PR TIMES・gcjapan より、イタリアン出身で麺庄（Mensho）で修業した礒貝翔氏、2017年岡山で麺処ぐり虎を開業しビブグルマン・東京ラーメンショー出場、第二ブランド和麺ぐり虎の京都本店が2025年7月23日六角通に開業、鶏塩は8時間の鶏コンソメ＋しじみ・日本/イタリア/フランス/モンゴルの塩ダレ、鯛塩は国産天然真鯛・沖縄/新潟の塩、国産小麦100%の細麺を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Custom noodles are made from 100% domestic wheat flour and are handled throughout the kitchen; there is no gluten-free option or separate preparation, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls are salt-based (shio) rather than soy-sauce-based; however, a separate fresh-soy-sauce (生醤油) ramen is also offered, which would contain wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (chicken consommé with clam, or wild sea bream) and the tare includes bonito dashi; no plant-based broth is prepared, so there is no vegan-safe bowl.

positives.text: Bowls are shio (salt) tare rather than the usual wheat-shoyu tare, so the base seasoning is comparatively lower in soy/wheat than many ramen — though the wheat noodles still rule it out for celiacs.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, welcoming counter-and-table ramen shop where ordering is done by QR code at the table, so a few words of Japanese and a friendly greeting go a long way, though the visual menu makes it manageable.

noodles.notes: Special noodle of 100% domestic wheat; medium-thin straight cut renewed in April 2025 to be slightly thicker for a more satisfying bite. Notable for a pronounced wheat aroma, supple texture, firm clean bite and smooth throat-feel.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-shio ramen (chicken-salt) — the house signature; also celebrated for the tai-shio ramen made with whole domestic wild red sea bream. Order the 特製 (special) version to get the full topping set.
ramen.chashu: Low-temperature-style braised pork chashu, moist and tender, releasing more meat umami the longer you chew.

gf.note: Wheat noodles are 100% domestic wheat plus kansui, and the dashi/tare layer in bonito (katsuo) and other umami — not celiac-safe. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broths are built on chicken, sea bream, clam/shellfish dashi, and bonito flakes, with pork chashu — no documented plant-based bowl. The shiitake/kombu in the tare sit atop animal stocks, not a standalone vegan broth.

ramen.pantheon: A polished modern-Japanese kaiseki take on shio ramen, where the soul lives in a crystal-clear chicken broth deepened with delicate clam dashi — or, in the sister bowl, the sweet umami of whole wild red sea bream. The salt tare, aged with Okinawa and Niigata sea salts plus kombu, shiitake and bonito, gives a soft, refined salinity that tastes unmistakably of careful Japanese dashi craft rather than any single regional lineage.

places.name: 麺や 鳥の鶏次 KYOTO 四条河原町店 (Men-ya Tori no Keiji KYOTO Shijo Kawaramachi)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen: wheat (medium-thick wavy) noodles plus shio/shoyu tare that typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated-handling options are advertised, and rice-bowl sides also involve sauces. Not safe for celiac diners; treat as not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-paitan specialist; the entire concept is a broth simmered for 8+ hours from domestic chicken and water, with chicken chashu toppings. There are no plant-based broth or noodle options noted. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-00:30 daily (L.O. food 00:00); closes when soup runs out. Lunch sets 10:30-15:00, dinner sets 15:00-21:00.
places.notes: An Osaka-born chicken-paitan specialist that built a reputation for long queues at its original shop and grew into a small group (with locations across the Osaka/Sakai area, Nara and Tokyo) before opening this, its first Kyoto outpost, on September 12, 2025, on a busy corner near

chef_bio.background: An Osaka-born chicken-paitan specialist that built a reputation for long queues at its original shop and grew into a small group (with locations across the Osaka/Sakai area, Nara and Tokyo) before opening this, its first Kyoto outpost, on September 12, 2025, on a busy corner near Shijo-Kiyamachi just steps from Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let the chicken speak for itself: a broth built from only chicken and water, then aerated so the richness lands light and silky rather than heavy.
chef_bio.specialty: The house style is an 泡系 (foam-style) chicken paitan: fresh domestic chicken and water simmered for over eight hours into a thick, creamy stock that is whipped/aerated to order into a frothy, cappuccino-like soup, served over smooth medium-thick wavy noodles with low-temperature chicken chashu (both breast and thigh). It anchors a lineup of variations including tori-shio and tori-shoyu, Fromage (cheese), Truffle, Red (chili oil), Black (mayu/burnt garlic oil) and Green (matcha), plus chicken rice-bowl sides.

anecdotes.text: Described as a popular Osaka shop known for daily queues, making this Kyoto location its first foray into the city, opening September 12, 2025.
The broth is made from only fresh domestic chicken and water, simmered for over eight hours, then blended/aerated just before serving into an 'awa-awa' (foamy) finish.
The Kyoto store launched with upgraded versions of the signature chicken paitan and items available only at this location.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi・keiji-project より、大阪発の行列人気店、京都初出店は2025年9月12日（四条木屋町・京都河原町駅すぐ）、国産鶏と水のみを8時間以上炊きブレンダーで泡立てる泡系鶏白湯、中太ウェーブ麺・低温調理の鶏チャーシュー（むね/もも）、鶏塩・鶏醤油・Fromage・Truffle・Red・Black・Greenのバリエーションを確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tori-shoyu (soy-sauce) bowls use a soy-based tare; Japanese shoyu is normally brewed with wheat. No gluten-free tare is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based (chicken + water simmered 8+ hours) and toppings include chicken chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation; cross-contact with animal stock is inherent to every bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, central ramen shop with QR-code mobile ordering, so a tourist can manage with minimal Japanese, but the interface and staff are primarily Japanese-oriented.

noodles.notes: Smooth, slightly flat medium-thick noodles with good elasticity and strong broth pickup; sources vary between straight and gently wavy. Built to carry the aerated, creamy chicken broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏そば (塩) — chicken shio paitan, the core bowl that best showcases the pure chicken broth. Flavored variations include 鶏次レッド (chili oil), 鶏次ブラック (black-garlic mayu), 鶏次グリーン (matcha), 鶏そばフロマージュ (cheese), and トリュフそば (truffle).
ramen.chashu: Two kinds of house-made low-temperature chicken rare chashu — tender, juicy, odor-free thigh and breast; mild-flavored so it complements rather than competes with the broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu-tare option mean these bowls are not celiac-safe. No rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire identity is chicken: broth is domestic chicken and water only, tare and chashu are chicken-based, so all bowls are animal-derived. No plant-broth option is offered (the shop is notably popular with Muslim diners precisely because everything is chicken, not pork).

ramen.pantheon: A foam-style tori paitan in the modern Osaka lineage — fresh domestic chicken and water simmered over eight hours, then blitzed tableside-fresh into an aerated, cappuccino-like froth so creamy and sweet it tastes almost dairy-rich, with no vegetables or seafood to dilute the pure poultry soul. The shio reads cleanest, while playful black-garlic, chili-oil, matcha, cheese and truffle riffs push the genre into dessert-like indulgence.

places.name: ぎょうざ処 高辻 亮昌 本店 (Gyoza-dokoro Takatsuji Sukemasa Honten)
places.gf_detail: A gyoza specialist whose signature Sukemasa gyoza is wrapped in a springy special wheat skin; no gluten-free accommodation. The blended dipping sauces of Japanese dashi and miso as well as seasonings are highly likely to contain wheat-derived components, so it is not suitable for those requiring strict gluten-free such as celiac disease. No official information on gluten-free or wheat-allergy accommodation could be confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: The main product is meat gyoza using minced Kyo-mochibuta pork, and the Japanese dashi is bonito-based. Many Kyoto vegetables such as Kujo leek are used, but no gyoza or menu for vegans/vegetarians could be confirmed. There is no clear plant-based menu; whether accommodation is possible must be inquired about.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:30-14:00 (L.O.) / 17:00-22:00 (L.O.); Sat, Sun & holidays 11:30-22:00 (L.O.); no regular closing day (open daily). Times shown are on a last-order basis.
places.neighborhood: Shimogyo Ward, Takatsuji / near Shijo Karasuma, Karasuma Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Kyoto-gyoza specialist proclaiming 'born and raised in Kyoto,' with its main shop along Takatsuji-dori close to Shijo Karasuma. Operated by MASTERMIND Inc., it insists on making gyoza from Kyoto-produced ingredients as a matter of local production for local consumption.

roles: テイクアウト・冷凍餃子販売
chef_bio.background: 「京都生まれ京都育ち」を掲げる京餃子の専門店で、四条烏丸にほど近い高辻通沿いに本店を構えます。運営はMASTERMIND社で、地産地消を信条に京都産の食材で餃子をつくることにこだわっています。
chef_bio.philosophy: 中華の調味料ではなく和のだしと味噌で仕上げる『和のぎょうざ』。にんにくの匂いを抑え、たれをほとんど付けずに素材の味で食べられるよう、女性や幅広い層が楽しめる上品な味わいを目指しています。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板の『亮昌ぎょうざ』。鰹だしをベースにした合わせ和だしで上品に仕立てた京もち豚のミンチに、香り豊かな九条ねぎを合わせ、隠し味に老舗『山利』の味噌をほんのり効かせ、もちもちの特製の皮で包んでいます。

anecdotes.text: 京都市長賞を受賞した伏見・中嶋農園のキャベツや京もち豚、京の伝統野菜である九条ねぎなど、できる限り京都の地のものを使用している。
黒く焙煎した七味と千鳥酢のたれが添えられ、にんにくの匂いを抑えて食べやすいと評される。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト・v-trip・食べログより、運営MASTERMIND社・エグゼクティブシェフ毛利亮氏、中華調味料を使わず鰹だしと山利の田舎味噌で仕立てる和のぎょうざ、にんにくの匂いを抑えた上品な味、京もち豚・九条ねぎ・伏見中嶋農園（京都市長賞）のキャベツなど地産地消、黒煎り七味と千鳥酢のたれを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 餃子専門店であり、すべての主力商品が小麦の皮を使用するため、厨房は小麦が常用される環境です。グルテンフリー対応・分離調理に関する記載は確認できませんでした。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 和だしと味噌をベースにしたたれや調味料を使用しており、醤油・味噌など小麦を含みうる発酵調味料が用いられている可能性があります。具体的な原材料の公開情報は確認できませんでした。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 京もち豚のミンチや鰹だしを中核に使用する肉餃子の専門店で、植物性メニューの分離・専用調理に関する情報は確認できませんでした。

positives.text: 英語メニューを用意しており、にんにく不使用の餃子のため、にんにくを避けたい人には案内しやすい。

cultural_comfort.note: 英語メニューを用意しており、四条烏丸の人気餃子専門店として観光客にも慣れているため、英語のみでも比較的安心して利用できます。

places.name: 中華料理 大鵬 (Chuka Ryori Taiho)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a Chinese kitchen built around wheat: the ramen and tantanmen use wheat noodles, dumplings/dim sum are wheat-wrapped, and dishes are seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and Chinese sauces. Broths and stir-fries are meat/seafood based and not designed for celiac safety. No dedicated fryer or allergen protocol is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. The menu centers on pork (the signature teri-don is glazed pork), wagyu mapo tofu, chicken, shrimp and other seafood, and meat/seafood-based broths and tare. Even ostensibly vegetable dishes are likely cooked with shared woks, meat fats and animal-based seasonings. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:15 (lunch L.O. ~14:20), 17:30-21:00 (dinner L.O. ~20:30); closed Tuesdays plus irregular/biweekly Wednesdays. Sources vary slightly on the dinner close time (20:30-22:30 reported across listings); confirm by phone for late dinner.
places.neighborhood: 中京区 西ノ京 / 二条駅すぐ (Nishinokyo, near JR Nijo Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Taiho opened in 1974 near Nijo Station and has been a fixture of Kyoto's machi-chuka (neighborhood Chinese) scene for about half a century. It began as a classic town-Chinese counter serving ramen, fried rice, gyoza and other everyday favorites; over the years a second-generation

chef_bio.background: Taiho opened in 1974 near Nijo Station and has been a fixture of Kyoto's machi-chuka (neighborhood Chinese) scene for about half a century. It began as a classic town-Chinese counter serving ramen, fried rice, gyoza and other everyday favorites; over the years the second-generation cook traveled to Sichuan in China and brought authentic Sichuan techniques back into the kitchen, gradually turning the shop into a place where old-school Kyoto machi-chuka and genuine Sichuan cooking sit side by side on the same menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bridging humble Kyoto town-Chinese comfort food with serious Sichuan cooking - keeping the beloved old standards like teri-don while layering in authentic regional Chinese flavors.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the 'teri-don' (teridon) - a rice bowl crowned with sweet-savory teriyaki-glazed pork. It comes in several forms, most famously the 'teri-don kinshi' topped with shredded egg, plus an oversized 'jigoku-mori' (hell portion) for big appetites. Alongside it the menu spans ramen and chuka-soba, dan-dan noodles, hot-and-sour noodles, fried rice, tenshin-han and Chinese rice bowls, and Sichuan plates like mapo tofu made with Omi-area wagyu and sweet-and-sour pork.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1974 and marking its 50th anniversary, Taiho has earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand and is known as a small shop with a near-constant queue at both lunch and dinner.

The second-generation cook visited Sichuan and introduced new dishes, transforming what was a standard machi-chuka into a place where town-Chinese and Sichuan cuisine are combined; the original teri-don remains the signature.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: v-trip・kyotopi・gcjapan より、1974年創業・二条駅近く・ミシュランビブグルマン・親子2代の行列町中華で、創業当初は町中華の定番中心だったが2代目が中国四川に足を運び四川料理を導入、名物てりどん／てりどんきんし／地獄盛り、近江牛の麻婆豆腐、担々麺・酸辣湯麺を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based noodles (ramen, chuka-soba, dan-dan, hot-and-sour noodles) and wheat-wrapped dumplings/dim sum are core to the menu, with heavy use of wheat soy sauce and Chinese sauces in a shared wok kitchen - high gluten exposure throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chinese-style cooking with soy and bean-paste seasonings; standard wheat-containing soy sauce should be assumed. No wheat-free tare or sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on pork, wagyu, chicken and seafood, with meat/seafood broths and shared woks; vegetable dishes are very likely cooked with animal fats and meat-based seasonings.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy local Chinese eatery with Japanese-language menus and no indication of English support, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps; come ready to queue and order quickly at peak times.

noodles.notes: The dry tantanmen rides on thin, non-kansui straight noodles (described as Kagawa-style fine udon) rather than typical kansui ramen noodles, chosen to soak up the sesame-chili sauce pooled at the bottom of the bowl. Toss top-to-bottom before eating. Thickness/shape for the soup tantanmen is not separately documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen — order the shiru-nashi (dry) tantanmen for the house's most distinctive bowl; a soup tantanmen is also offered. (Note: this is a Sichuan town-Chinese kitchen whose true icons are teridon and Omi-beef mapo tofu, with tantanmen as its standout noodle dish.)

gf.note: Wheat noodles throughout (the dry tantanmen uses non-kansui wheat udon-style noodles) plus a soy/sesame-based Sichuan sauce; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The tantanmen is built on minced pork and a dashi-backed broth, and the kitchen's repertoire leans heavily on pork, Omi beef and chicken. No documented plant-broth, meat-free bowl.

ramen.pantheon: This is no ramen-counter bowl but a Sichuan kitchen's tantanmen: a sesame-rich, faintly sweet broth wired tight with dashi and a whisper of vinegar, then lit up by sweat-coaxing mala heat and the citrus-numbing perfume of huajiao. The prized dry version pools that chili-sesame sauce beneath slick thin noodles, peanuts and minced pork — closer to Chengdu's street dandan than to any Japanese gotochi lineage.

places.name: 京都和麺 諭吉本舗 (Kyoto Wamen Yukichi Honpo)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy/dashi tare and chicken-scallop broth; no gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised. Avoid if you require strict GF.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The entire concept centers on chicken-and-scallop dashi broth topped with A5 wagyu beef steak; there is no vegan ramen or plant-based offering.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku, Teramachi-dōri Takoyakushi (~3 min walk from Kyoto-Kawaramachi Stn)
places.notes: The Yukichi brand grew out of a father-and-son project — its noodles and broth were developed together over long experimentation, with the founders describing their signature as a creation 'built by my father and me.' The Kyoto Teramachi branch was opened by a veteran restaurateu

roles: owner-operator (from Kyotango / Amino, north Kyoto Prefecture)
chef_bio.origin: An offshoot of the 諭吉 (Yukichi) ramen group, which began in Mito, Ibaraki as 特選中華蕎麦諭吉 and has grown into a multi-city brand; this Teramachi shop opened on September 24, 2024.
chef_bio.background: The Yukichi brand grew out of a father-and-son project — its noodles and broth were developed together over long experimentation, with the founders describing their signature as a creation 'built by my father and me.' The Kyoto Teramachi branch was opened by a veteran restaurateur from the Kyotango (Amino) area as part of the group's urban expansion.
chef_bio.philosophy: Nearly all components — noodles, broth, tare — are made in-house, with noodles served the same day they are made for maximum freshness; the bowl is conceived as a high-end, theatrical fusion of teppan wagyu and ramen.
chef_bio.specialty: 和麺 (Wamen) — a refined, wafū-style chuka-soba finished with truffle oil for an almost French aroma, and crowned with A5 wagyu beef seared tableside on a hot iron griddle (charred outside, rare within).

anecdotes.text: The flagship gesture is a slab of A5 wagyu flambeed tableside over flame on a large iron griddle, seared outside and rare within, then set over the bowl.
The founders describe their ramen as 'a confident creation built by my father and me' (父と私で作り上げた自信作です), with almost every ingredient homemade and noodles served same-day.
The Teramachi shop was opened by a senior restaurateur from Kyotango City's Amino district as part of the brand's move into the city.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト(m-yukichi.jp)は水戸の特選中華蕎麦諭吉が父子で作り上げた自家製麺・スープであることを記載。kyo-soku/kusamotoが寺町本舗の2024年9月24日開業、A5和牛の鉄板焼き、トリュフオイル、京丹後網野出身の経営者を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls use a soy-sauce / dashi tare typical of Japanese chuka-soba, which standardly contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is advertised.

cultural_comfort.note: A small counter-only shop with a premium, theatrical wagyu-and-ramen format; basic Japanese helps and the bill runs high, so set expectations before going.

noodles.notes: Self-made noodles cut fresh daily, with a choice of thin, thick, or hand-kneaded (te-momi) styles matched to the soup; the thin straight cut is the default for the wafu chuka soba.

ramen.signature_bowl: Wamen (和麺) — the wafu chuka soba built on a chicken-and-shellfish dashi; premium versions crown it with a seared A5 wagyu steak finished tableside and a wash of truffle oil.
ramen.chashu: On the premium bowls the headline protein is seared A5 wagyu steak rather than traditional braised pork; the standard chuka soba's pork chashu is not clearly documented.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-leaning tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken bones blended with clam/shellfish dashi, and bowls feature egg and wagyu, so there is no plant-based option.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto wafu original rather than a regional throwback: its soul is a delicate double soup of chicken-gara cut with clam-and-shellfish dashi, seasoned light with shio and shoyu so the marine umami stays clear and elegant. The shop then gilds that restrained Japanese base with luxury flourishes — a tableside-seared A5 wagyu steak and a perfume of truffle oil — pushing the bowl toward a near-French extravagance.

places.name: 麺屋 坂本 (Menya Sakamoto)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen and tsukemen shop. The noodles are sourced from a renowned Tokyo wheat-noodle maker (Asakusa Kaika-ro), the salt/shoyu tare is almost certainly wheat-based, and the broth is a chicken-and-seafood clear soup. There is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is built on a chicken-based (鶏清湯) broth balanced with seafood (sardine, bonito, mackerel, kombu, clam). Signature bowls are chicken ramen, salt ramen, clam ramen, and tsukemen. No vegan or vegetarian options are indicated; the broth itself is animal-based, so even plain noodles would carry meat/fish stock.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (last order 14:30) / 17:30–22:00 (last order 21:30). Closed Wed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in April 2019 on Karasuma street just south of Kuramaguchi subway station, a short walk from Doshisha University. The owner previously cooked at Gokukei, a well-known rich-broth ramen shop in Kyoto's Ichijoji district, and deliberately struck out in the opposite direction

chef_bio.background: Opened in April 2019 on Karasuma street just south of Kuramaguchi subway station, a short walk from Doshisha University. The owner previously cooked at Gokukei (極鶏), a well-known rich-broth ramen shop in Kyoto's Ichijoji district, and deliberately struck out in the opposite direction with his own place.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing richness, the shop pursues simplicity and elegance — meticulously crafted bowls where a clean, delicate broth is the point.
chef_bio.specialty: A light, refined clear soup (淡麗系) built on a chicken base brought into exquisite balance with seafood — dried sardine, bonito, mackerel and Rausu kombu — described as having the elegance of a kaiseki soup course. Signature bowls include the chicken chuka soba, salt soba, clam (shijimi) soba, and tsukemen, paired with thick noodles from the famous Tokyo maker Asakusa Kaika-ro. A seasonal cold salt soba uses a salt tare aged for a month.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at Gokukei (極鶏), a popular heavy-broth shop in Ichijoji, then opened Menya Sakamoto with a markedly different, light-and-refined direction.
The clear broth balances chicken with sardine, bonito, mackerel and Rausu kombu, aiming for a taste likened to the soup course of a traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant.
Noodles are sourced from Asakusa Kaika-ro, a renowned Tokyo noodle maker.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi記事が2019年4月開業、烏丸鞍馬口(同志社近く)、店主の極鶏出身、淡麗系鶏清湯+魚介(煮干・鰹・鯖・羅臼昆布)、浅草開化楼の麺、塩冷やしそば・1ヶ月熟成塩ダレを確認。

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter ramen shop where cash is the only payment and a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the ticket/menu) will carry you through; come prepared rather than expecting English support.

noodles.notes: Noodles sourced from the famed Tokyo mill Asakusa Kaika-ro (浅草開化楼) — rarely seen in Kansai — slightly flat, chewy straight noodles with substantial bite, chosen to carry the delicate clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori Chuka Soba (鶏中華そば) — clear chicken-and-seafood shoyu ramen; also celebrated for the chilled shio soba (塩冷やしそば) in warmer months, finished with sudachi citrus.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu served in the Tokyo style; specific cut (loin vs belly) not documented.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken plus fish dashi (niboshi, katsuo/saba-bushi, kombu) with pork chashu; no documented plant-based broth or topping set.

ramen.pantheon: A clean-soup (chintan) shop in the refined Tokyo tradition: a base of whole heritage chickens (momiji-dori, sakura-dori) layered with niboshi, bonito, mackerel flakes and Rausu kombu, then a slightly sweet shoyu kaeshi or a month-aged shio tare. You taste the lineage in the clarity — the chilled shio version is praised as elegant enough to recall a kaiseki broth, a delicate counterpoint to Kyoto's heavier se-abura tradition.

places.name: びし屋 (Bishiya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house-made noodles are wheat (100% domestic / Hokkaido wheat flour) and the broth is a pork-bone-and-chicken tonkotsu-shoyu, with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that in Japan almost always contains wheat. Tsukemen and mazesoba are likewise wheat noodles. No gluten-free options are indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The core menu is built on a pork-bone and chicken-carcass broth simmered for about two days, with chashu pork and other animal toppings; tsukemen uses a tonkotsu-seafood (gyokai) dipping broth. No plant-based or vegetable broth is indicated.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00, 18:00-24:00 (L.O.); closed irregularly
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Bishiya opened on December 10, 2011 in Ichijoji, Kyoto's famous "ramen battleground" street near Eizan Railway's Ichijoji Station. It occupies a quirky, irregularly shaped corner space that gives the room an unconventional layout, with counter seats wrapping the open kitchen in a

chef_bio.background: Bishiya opened on December 10, 2011 in Ichijoji, Kyoto's famous "ramen battleground" street near Eizan Railway's Ichijoji Station. It occupies a quirky, irregularly shaped corner space that gives the room an unconventional layout, with counter seats wrapping the open kitchen in a "し" curve (about 12 seats). It has grown into one of the district's reliably busy, queue-forming shops.
chef_bio.philosophy: Though regulars and review sites often peg it as a 'family-style' (iekei) shop because of the thick straight noodles and tonkotsu-shoyu look, the shop is clear that it does not call itself iekei and operates as its own tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: as they put it, the taste is entirely different despite the visual resemblance. The house-milled noodles are described as flat and notably silky, a departure from Kyoto's typically firmer style.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is a rich tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: pork bones and chicken carcass simmered for about two days into a deep, soy-forward broth, paired with house-made noodles milled from 100% domestic (Hokkaido) wheat. The tsukemen is equally beloved, with a thick pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth carrying a subtle sweetness, four thick chashu slices, and an optional yuzu-scented finishing rice. Crispy karaage is a popular side.

anecdotes.text: Bishiya pushes back on being labeled a family-style (iekei) ramen shop, insisting its tonkotsu-shoyu is a different thing entirely despite looking similar.
The broth is built by simmering pork bones and chicken carcass for roughly two days, and the noodles are made in-house from 100% domestic wheat with an unusually flat, silky texture.
The tsukemen pairs a rich tonkotsu-seafood dipping broth with a subtle sweetness, four thick chashu slices, and an optional yuzu-flavored finishing rice.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki/genjitsutouhiが2011年12月10日開業、家系を名乗らず豚骨醤油、豚骨+鶏ガラ2日炊き、国産(北海道)小麦の自家製麺(つるつる平打ち)、し字カウンター約12席、つけ麺(チャーシュー4枚・柚子ごはん)を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a tonkotsu-shoyu (soy-sauce based) style; soy-sauce tare in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat, on top of the wheat-flour noodles themselves.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-carcass based with pork chashu and animal toppings throughout; no separate vegetable/vegan preparation is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, ticket-machine local ramen shop where staff focus on the line and the bowls, so a little Japanese and ramen-counter familiarity help; no English service is indicated.

noodles.notes: In-house noodles milled from Hokkaido domestic wheat; thick, straight (near hira-uchi/flat) for the tsukemen with strong wheat fragrance and a chewy, elastic bite that stands up to the thick broth. The ramen uses a slightly slimmer medium-thick straight noodle. Tsukemen portions chosen 200-300g.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tonkotsu-shoyu ramen — the house bowl — or the pork-bone-and-seafood (tonkotsu-gyokai) tsukemen with thick handmade noodles and yuzu-dashi finishing rice.
ramen.chashu: Generous thick-cut braised pork slices, soft and tender with notably high-quality, well-rendered fat; four substantial pieces in a typical bowl.

gf.note: Wheat noodles are milled in-house from domestic wheat, and the broth is a pork/seafood/chicken stock with a soy (wheat-based) tare. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken bones and fish/seafood dashi, topped with pork chashu. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: In the dense ramen corridor of Ichijoji, this is a thick, concentrated pork-bone-and-soy bowl whose soul is a deeply rendered tonkotsu rounded out with chicken and a clear current of seafood umami — rich and faintly sweet, yet scrubbed of any barnyard pork funk. Though its thick straight noodles, spinach and nori echo the look of Yokohama iekei, the kitchen pushes its own modern blend: a sharper soy edge, a gyokai sweetness, and a thick-noodle tsukemen on house-milled Hokkaido wheat finished with yuzu-scented dashi rice.

places.name: 中華そば 双鳩 (Chuka Soba Soukyu)
places.gf_detail: This is a single-bowl old-school chuka soba shop. The noodles are wheat, and the shoyu tare and chicken-pork broth would almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, broth, or accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The one signature bowl is built on a chicken-gara / pork-bone shoyu broth and is topped with grilled pork (chashu). There is no vegan or vegetarian option; the shop serves essentially a single classic meat-and-bone ramen.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–14:30, 17:00–21:00. Closed Wed (from April 2026, also closed Thu).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 双鳩 (Soukyu) opened on November 11, 2022, in the exact spot once occupied by 珍元 (Chingen/Chinmoto), a legendary Kyoto chuka soba shop near Shijo-Omiya that closed in 2018. The owner was not a trained restaurateur but a devoted regular who ate at 珍元 for some 30 years; driven by the

chef_bio.background: 双鳩 (Soukyu) opened on November 11, 2022, in the exact spot once occupied by 珍元 (Chingen), a legendary Kyoto chuka soba shop near Shijo-Omiya that closed in 2018. The owner was not a trained restaurateur but a devoted regular who ate at 珍元 for some 30 years; driven by the wish to taste that bowl again, he reopened the space and rebuilt the recipe with guidance from the original shop's family (the 珍元 owner's daughter).
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithful revival rather than reinvention: recreate the nostalgic, comforting flavor of a lost local institution as closely as possible, keeping the offering simple and honest.
chef_bio.specialty: A single classic chuka soba: medium-thin wheat noodles in a chicken-and-pork-bone shoyu broth (with back fat) lifted by a clear note of ginger, topped with hand-cut green onion, grilled pork, and menma (bamboo shoots) — light enough to eat every day.

anecdotes.text: The predecessor 珍元 is said to have been a model for Juzo Itami's 1985 ramen film 'Tampopo'; 双鳩 carries that lineage forward in the same location.
The owner spent about 30 years as a 珍元 regular and learned the flavor from the original shop's daughter, refining the broth over time to bring it ever closer to the remembered taste.
The shop collaborates with neighbors — for example a curry ramen created together with nearby curry shop ムジャラ (Mujara).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi/osumitukiが2022年11月11日開業、珍元跡地(2018年閉店)、30年通った常連が珍元の娘さんに教わり思い出の味を再現、鶏ガラ豚骨背脂醤油に生姜、映画『タンポポ』縁の店を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles boiled in shared water, with a shoyu-based tare and chicken/pork broth — gluten is inherent to the dish and unavoidable here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a shoyu (soy sauce) base; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal-based throughout — chicken-and-pork-bone broth with grilled pork topping; no vegan preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood ramen shop run by locals; expect a Japanese-only menu and a quiet, regulars-focused atmosphere, so a little Japanese or a translation app goes a long way.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles with a crisp bite and pronounced wheat flavor, chosen to bind tightly with the clear shoyu broth in the old-school chuka soba idiom.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — a nostalgic chicken-and-pork-bone soy sauce bowl accented with ginger; upgrade to the chashu-men for a generous layer of pork.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly (nibuta) — tender, fatty rolls of bara cut, sliced over the bowl in the classic Showa style.
notable_toppings: spicy miso condiment on the side

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with a shoyu tare, and the bowl is dressed with pork belly and back-fat; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A loving revival of a beloved neighborhood chuka soba that once stood on this Mibu corner, the bowl pours a clear chicken-and-pork shoyu broth brightened by ginger and rounded with a gentle melt of pork back-fat — the unmistakable Kyoto soul of light-yet-savory se-abura shoyu. It tastes like the everyday Showa-era ramen that locals built their afternoons around, simple and quietly addictive.

places.name: ラーメン 霽レ空 (Ramen Haresora)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls use wheat ramen noodles (the Chinese soba uses thin Hokkaido-wheat noodles) and a wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) tare, so the noodles, tare, and likely the broth all contain gluten. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated, and cross-contact in a single shared ramen kitchen would be unavoidable. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Broths are animal-based (the current Chinese soba uses a clear pork broth; earlier versions used chicken and oyster), and bowls feature meat toppings such as chashu and Omi-beef tendon rice. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, design-conscious ramen shop tucked into a quiet block west of Nijo Station in Nakagyo Ward. The name 霽レ空 (Haresora) evokes the clear sky that breaks open after rain, and the interior carries that mood: bright, minimal, and calm, with a short counter and a couple of table

chef_bio.background: A small, design-conscious ramen shop tucked into a quiet block west of Nijo Station in Nakagyo Ward, with a tidy 6-seat counter and one table. The name 霽レ空 (Haresora) evokes the clear sky that breaks open after rain, and the interior carries that mood: bright, minimal, and calm, welcoming solo diners.
chef_bio.philosophy: An ingredient-driven, fragrance-first approach: regulars describe being pulled inside by the aroma of soy as they walk in, and the bowls are praised for their clean, gentle beauty rather than heaviness. The team is willing to evolve the menu rather than coast on a single signature, retiring the famous oyster bowl when sourcing made it untenable.
chef_bio.specialty: The shop built its reputation on a clear, elegant chuka soba, originally famous for an oyster-and-niboshi bowl in which a clear chicken broth was perfumed with oyster, balanced by Maruta (丸大) soy sauce and thin-medium noodles milled from Hokkaido wheat. When the oyster bowl was later retired for sourcing reasons, the kitchen reorganized around three pillars: a refined Chinese soba (reworked from a clear chicken broth into a clear pork broth), a rich Negi (green onion) ramen promoted from a beloved limited-run dish to a permanent fixture, and a spicy maze soba.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature was once a one-day-limited oyster soba, with a clear chicken broth scented by dried oyster and an oyster paste diners could dissolve gradually as they ate, balanced by Maruta soy and Hokkaido-wheat noodles.

After dropping the oyster bowl (discontinued over ingredient sourcing, reflected by early 2025), the menu was rebuilt around three mains: the Chinese soba changed from a clear chicken broth to a clear pork broth, the popular Negi ramen graduated from a limited item to a regular, and the spicy maze soba's extra heat became a paid option.
Diners note the noodles are flat, medium-thin, and have a satisfying grip, with black pepper and a chili condiment on the counter to adjust the bowl.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapanが牡蠣そば(澄んだ鶏ガラ+牡蠣煮干し・牡蠣ペースト・丸大醤油・北海道小麦中細麺、6席+1卓)を、noodlers(2025年2月記事)が牡蠣そば終売と中華そば(鶏→豚清湯)・ネギラーメン定番化・辛まぜそば辛さ増し有料化を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small ramen kitchen serving wheat-noodle bowls with shoyu tare; shared boiling water and prep make gluten cross-contact effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, noted as using Maruta soy sauce, which is wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (clear pork broth in the current Chinese soba; earlier chicken/oyster), with meat toppings, so no vegan-safe preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small counter-focused independent shop with ticket-machine ordering and cash-only payment; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the machine will get you through comfortably.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin noodles. The chuka soba uses flat, rectangular-cut noodles that give a pleasant tongue-feel and clean bite; the negi ramen uses medium-thin straight noodles that the thick, viscous pork-shoyu broth clings to well.

ramen.signature_bowl: Negi Ramen — a rich pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu bowl crowned with a mound of seasoned shiraga-negi (fine white-scallion threads); for a lighter classic, the chuka soba is a clear chicken-and-soy bowl.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork belly on the Negi Ramen; the chuka soba carries three thin slices of conventional braised chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-based (wheat) shoyu tare run through every bowl; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on chicken bones plus seafood/dried-fish dashi, with pork-back-fat and braised-pork toppings throughout. No documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A Nijo neighborhood shop with two souls in one room: a clear, nostalgic chicken-and-soy chuka soba in the gentle Kyoto-meets-Tokyo tradition, and a richer pork-bone shoyu Negi Ramen finished with a snowfall of seasoned white-scallion threads and a slick of back-fat that nods to Kyoto's love of se-abura shoyu. You taste the kitchen's careful, additive-light hand in the balance of soy depth and clean stock.

places.name: 担担 四条本店 (Tantan Shijo Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a tantan-men (dandan noodle) specialist. The bowl is built on wheat egg-style ramen noodles in a sesame-based broth seasoned with a soy/miso meat tare, which conventionally contains wheat. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, broth, or menu option, and no allergen handling is advertised. Not suitable for celiacs or those needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The signature tantan-men is topped with seasoned meat miso (pork) and the broth/tare are animal-derived; chashu and crab/egg fried-rice sides are also meat- and seafood-based. No vegan or vegetarian version of the noodles is offered or advertised. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–15:00 (closes early when sold out). Closed Thu; irregular Tuesday closures. One source also lists a 17:30–21:00 dinner service, but this could not be consistently confirmed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running, much-loved tantan-men specialist tucked into a small storefront on Shijo-dori, in the Mibu quarter of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward between Shijo-Omiya and Saiin. It is a tiny, counter-only neighborhood shop where regulars line up at lunch, and many say that once they've h

chef_bio.background: A long-running, much-loved tantan-men specialist tucked into a small storefront on Shijo-dori, in the Mibu quarter of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward between Shijo-Omiya and Saiin. It is a tiny, counter-only neighborhood shop where regulars line up at lunch, and many say that once they've had this tantan-men they can't eat it anywhere else.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focused, single-dish craft: a sesame-cream broth balanced so it stays drinkable to the last drop rather than overwhelming with chili heat.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature tantan-men: thin noodles in a creamy, sesame-forward broth crowned with seasoned meat miso, bok choy and green onion. The flavor leads mild and mellow, then reveals a gentle, umami-rich heat as you eat. In warmer months a chilled hiyashi tantan-men appears, swapping broccoli in for the greens at a slightly milder spice level. The crab-ankake fried rice and the shop's lettuce fried rice are beloved companions to the noodles.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the broth as mild and so well-balanced that they finish it every time, and say the shop's tantan-men has spoiled them for any other.
Frequently cited as one of the noodle shops that defines Kyoto's tantan-men, with a creamy sesame broth that clings to its thin noodles.
In summer a chilled version substitutes broccoli for the greens; the crab-ankake fried rice and lettuce fried rice are popular side orders.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi/retty/58-gourmetが京都を代表する担担麺、細麺にごまクリーミースープ、マイルドで毎回飲み切れる評判を確認。tabelog口コミが夏の冷やし担担麺(ブロッコリー)、蟹あんかけチャーハン・レタスチャーハンを確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tantan-men is built on wheat noodles with a sesame broth and a soy/miso-based meat tare that conventionally contains wheat; no gluten-free preparation is offered or noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The bowl is topped with pork meat miso and uses an animal-based broth/tare; no vegan or vegetarian version is available.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills counter shop run as a local couple's operation with cash-only payment and no reservations; a little Japanese and an easygoing, queue-friendly attitude will make the visit smoother.

noodles.notes: Thin-gauge noodles with springy elasticity that grip the thick sesame broth well; a thick-noodle (futomen) substitution is offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu tantanmen — the creamy sesame tantanmen, optionally dressed up with chashu; a cold (hiyashi) version appears in summer.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu offered as an add-on; the bowl's protein heart is its seasoned ground pork (niku-miso style) stirred into the broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a sesame-paste tantan base built on Chinese seasonings; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Sesame-cream broth carries an animal (chicken/pork) backbone and the bowl is finished with seasoned ground pork; no documented plant-only broth. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto take on tantanmen that prizes a deeply creamy, sesame-rich umami over fire — built by a chef out of the Cantonese kitchen, so the soup drinks more like a refined Chinese broth than a chili-oil gut-punch. Heat is left to the diner via table condiments, letting the nutty sesame and savory ground pork carry the soul of the bowl.

places.name: 京都らーめん おおの (Kyoto Ramen Ono)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto-style ramen shop: wheat-flour noodles in a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is virtually always made with wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no rice-noodle option, and no indication of allergen handling. The kitchen is a small counter operation cooking noodles, gyoza, karaage, and fried rice in shared equipment, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on an animal base — pork-bone broth, back fat, chashu pork, and soy tare — and the side menu centers on karaage, gyoza, and chashu-egg rice. There is no plant-based broth or vegan ramen offered, and no menu flexibility advertised. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: Saiin, Ukyō-ku (within walking distance of Hankyū Saiin Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, lively neighborhood ramen counter serving the old-school Kyoto style, with a handful of counter seats and a couple of tables. It draws a steady crowd of regulars who come for time-honored, comforting bowls rather than trend-chasing.

chef_bio.origin: Saiin, Ukyo Ward — a short walk from Hankyu Saiin Station along Gojo-dori in western Kyoto.
chef_bio.background: A small, lively neighborhood ramen counter serving the old-school Kyoto style, with a handful of counter seats and a couple of tables. It draws a steady crowd of regulars who come for time-honored, comforting bowls rather than trend-chasing.
chef_bio.philosophy: Classic, value-driven Kyoto ramen done the way it always has been — generous sets pairing the bowl with karaage and fried rice, an everyday-dining feel that sits somewhere between a shokudo, an izakaya, and a ramen-ya.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 'Ono Ramen' — a rich, deeply savory bowl of back-fat soy-sauce (seabura shoyu) ramen, the quintessential Kyoto style. The broth is pork-bone based and seasoned with a punchy soy tare, finished with a scattering of back fat, plenty of green onion, tender chashu, and menma. The straight, low-hydration noodles have a firm, crisp 'patsun' bite that stands up to the bold broth. A lighter, smoother 'Kyoto Ramen' is also offered, and you can lean toward assari (lighter) or kotteri (richer).

anecdotes.text: Bloggers describe it as 'natsukashii shinise no aji' — the comforting, time-honored flavor of an old-school shop — and note a weekday perk of a complimentary boiled egg for each customer.
One visitor characterized the room's easygoing, hybrid atmosphere as 'part cafeteria, part izakaya, part ramen shop,' making it an easy casual stop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: v-trip/initialdmenが西院・五条通(阪急西院近く)、背脂醤油の『おおのラーメン』(豚骨ベース)、パツッとした低加水ストレート麺、あっさりの『京都ラーメン』、平日ゆで卵サービス、食堂/居酒屋/ラーメン屋的な雰囲気を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, gyoza, karaage, and fried rice are all cooked in a small shared kitchen, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining seasoning is a back-fat soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; Japanese soy sauce is virtually always brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use a pork-bone broth with back fat and chashu; no plant-based option exists, so vegan diners cannot be safely accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, regulars-driven neighborhood ramen counter with no English support expected — point at the photo menu, order, pay cash, and you'll be fine.

noodles.notes: Thin, firm noodles that soak up the sharp, dark shoyu broth so the seasoning lands just right; served kime (on the firmer side).

ramen.signature_bowl: Oono Ramen (おおのラーメン) — the sharp, dark-shoyu version crowned with melted back-fat (se-abura) and a heap of green scallions; the milder 京都ラーメン is the gentler sibling of the same broth.
ramen.chashu: Tender braised/roasted pork (焼豚), soft sliced loin/belly that yields into the fatty broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken and pork bones, finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu — no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's hometown soul-food: the se-abura chaccha-kei bowl where a cloudy chicken-and-pork broth is hit with a sharp, sweet-free dark shoyu and blanketed in melted back-fat. You taste the old Kyoto lineage in that punchy-yet-mellow soup — rich and fatty up front, the shoyu cutting clean behind it, the way the city's ramen has tasted for generations.

places.name: らー麺創房 ぱこ (Ramen Soubou Pako)
places.gf_detail: A classic Kyoto ramen counter built on wheat: wheat noodles in every bowl, and the niboshi/bonito fish broths and shio/shoyu tare are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated gluten-free preparation, and no indication staff can accommodate celiac needs. Not safe for celiac or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian option. The signature bowls are built on dried-sardine (niboshi), mackerel, and bonito fish broth, and the shop also serves pork-based Chinese noodles and gyoza. There is no plant-based ramen on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 11:30–14:00 / 17:30–23:00; Sat 11:30–15:00 / 17:30–23:00; Sun & holidays 11:30–15:00 / 17:30–22:00. Closed Mon (open when it falls on a holiday). (Tabelog also lists 11:30–14:00 / 17:30–22:00; lunch and dinner closing times may vary.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running independent ramen counter in Kyoto's Saiin district, near Saikyogoku, that has been a fixture of the Saiin-Katsura ramen scene long enough to outlast its own name. It originally opened as らー麺創房 日本一 ぱこぱこ before slimming down to the current らー麺創房 ぱこ.

chef_bio.background: A long-running independent ramen counter in Kyoto's Saiin district, near Saikyogoku, that has been a fixture of the Saiin-Katsura ramen scene long enough to outlast its own name. It originally opened as らー麺創房 日本一 ぱこぱこ before slimming down to the current らー麺創房 ぱこ.
chef_bio.philosophy: Refined and approachable rather than loud — the kitchen leans on Kyoto ingredients and a delicate hand, prizing balance over bold intensity.
chef_bio.specialty: The shop is best known for its 極上塩らーめん (premium salt ramen) — a clean, restrained shio bowl with a light, elegant broth and slightly crinkled smooth thin noodles, often finished with Kyoto mizuna greens. A second pillar is the 超にぼし中華そば (super-niboshi chuka soba), a gentle dried-sardine-forward soy broth.

anecdotes.text: The shop dropped a syllable over the years, going from らー麺創房 日本一 ぱこぱこ to simply らー麺創房 ぱこ — one of the 'pako's quietly disappeared along the way.
Regulars prize the 極上塩らーめん, an airy, restrained salt bowl topped with Kyoto-style mizuna greens.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: rettyが旧名『らー麺創房 日本一 ぱこぱこ』→現名『らー麺創房 ぱこ』、あっさりスープに縮れ細麺+水菜の極上塩らーめん、優しい醤油の超煮干中華そば、右京区西院六反田町を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are central to every bowl and are boiled in shared water; the broths and shio/shoyu tare are not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-based (shoyu/chuka soba) bowls are on the menu; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are built on dried sardine, mackerel, and bonito (fish), and pork-based noodles and gyoza are served; no plant-based preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood ramen counter where ordering in Japanese (or by pointing at the menu) is expected; English support is unlikely.

noodles.notes: Straight medium-width noodles in the signature shio bowl; thinner, firmer noodles paired with the niboshi-forward chuka soba to stand up to the punchy broth. No documentation that noodles are made in-house.

ramen.signature_bowl: Gokujo Shio Ramen (premium salt ramen) — a clear chicken-and-fish broth seasoned with shio tare; the niboshi-forward Cho-Niboshi Chuka Soba is the other pillar, and a shio tsukemen rounds out the lineup.
ramen.chashu: Thinly sliced braised roast pork (four to five slices), moist and tender with a deep, well-developed flavor.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-component tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and niboshi/seafood dashi with pork chashu and chicken fat throughout; no plant-only bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This Saiin shop chases clarity over fat: a limpid Kyoto-red-chicken and dried-sardine broth tuned bright with a delicate shio tare, the kind of restrained, dashi-driven bowl that lets the niboshi's umami sing. Its alter ego, a punchy niboshi chuka soba, pushes that dried-fish lineage to the front with an aroma that hits before the first slurp.

places.name: さいラーメン (Sai Ramen)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop and not safe for celiacs. The noodles are straight medium-thin wheat noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker (Teigaku), and the signature bowls use a pork-bone broth with braised wagyu beef tendon; ramen tare is virtually always wheat-based shoyu. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no allergen handling is advertised. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The core product is a rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth, the signature topping is braised wagyu beef tendon, and other bowls feature chashu pork and karaage (fried chicken). There is no vegetable-based broth or plant-based bowl on offer.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu–Sun 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–20:30; closed Tue & Wed. (One source lists evening start as 17:00.) Year-end/New Year break 12/31–1/8.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner-chef, known as Sai-san, spent about 15 years making ramen at Takayasu (高安), the famous, perennially queued shop on Ichijoji's 'Ramen Street' in Kyoto. He then ran Gion's Menya Ichijo (麺屋一乗) as an owner-operator before striking out fully on his own, opening this shop nea

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef, known as Sai-san, spent about 15 years making ramen at Takayasu (高安), the famous, perennially queued shop on Ichijoji's 'Ramen Street' in Kyoto. He then ran Gion's Menya Ichijo (麺屋一乗) as an owner-operator before striking out fully on his own, opening this shop near Sanjo-Omae in May 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carry forward the Takayasu legacy of richly satisfying broth and crisp karaage, served from an intimate single-counter shop where the owner can look after every guest personally; a complimentary spicy chive topping is offered.
chef_bio.specialty: Wagyu beef-tendon ramen (和牛すじラーメン): a sweet, creamy pork-bone broth crowned with slow-braised wagyu beef tendon whose own sweetness melts into the soup. A salt (shio) ramen and a set with karaage and rice round out the short menu.

anecdotes.text: Sai-san honed his craft over roughly 15 years at Ichijoji's celebrated Takayasu before opening here, carrying that shop's signature thick broth and karaage into his own bowl.
Opened on May 21, 2024, the shop quickly drew attention as a notable new entry in the Sanjo-Omae area for its thick, umami-laden soup.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki/kyo-sokuが店主さい氏の高安15年・祇園『麺屋一乗』オーナー歴、2024年5月21日三条御前(二条駅近く)開業、和牛すじラーメン(甘くクリーミーな豚骨)、無料の辛ニラ、カウンター8席を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (sourced from the Kyoto noodle maker Teigaku) in a pork-bone broth; ramen tare of this style is wheat-based shoyu, so the dish contains wheat throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and toppings include wagyu beef tendon, pork chashu, and fried chicken; no plant-based preparation is offered, so cross-contact with animal products is total.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run counter ramen shop with no English menu advertised; a friendly greeting in Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.

noodles.notes: Straight medium-thin noodles from a local Kyoto noodle maker, with a smooth surface and a firm, slightly snappy bite that holds up in the thick broth; regulars suggest ordering them on the firmer side.

ramen.signature_bowl: Wagyu-suji ramen — the thick chicken-and-pork paitan crowned with slow-braised wagyu beef tendon and a sweet Korean-style chili-and-sesame soy accent; the plain ramen and the karaage-and-rice set are the other ways regulars work the bowl.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, fall-apart braised pork chashu (some bowls served lightly seared/aburi for an aromatic char), paired with the shop's signature slow-stewed pork cartilage (nankotsu) so tender it needs no chewing — a soki-soba-like touch.
notable_toppings: karaage and rice on the set
free spicy chive oil at the counter

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-based tare; the broth is built on long-simmered chicken and pork bones. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a heavy chicken-and-pork bone paitan, dressed with pork chashu, cartilage and beef tendon. No plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A thick, collagen-rich chicken-and-pork paitan carrying the DNA of Ichijoji's famed Takayasu, where the chef spent fifteen years at the stockpot — you taste that lineage in the pottari, almost gravy-like sweetness of the soup. This is Kyoto's heavyweight, modern kotteri tori-tonkotsu rather than the city's older thin se-abura shoyu, pushed even richer with a deeper animal punch than the parent shop.

places.name: ラーメン たぬき屋 (Ramen Tanukiya)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core menu is built on wheat: ramen and mazesoba use wheat noodles supplied by the Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku (麺屋棣鄂), and the bowls rely on standard (wheat-based) shoyu/tare seasonings. Broths are pork-bone (aged tonkotsu) and chicken paitan, with rich garlic-heavy mazesoba sauces. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation is advertised, and the kitchen is a single shared 9-seat counter operation. A strict gluten-free or celiac diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. Every signature item is animal-based: aged pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken paitan broths, thick chashu pork, and meat-laden mazesoba/tantan/curry bowls. There is no plant-based broth or vegan option, and no vegetarian menu is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 / 18:00-22:00. Closed Thursdays and the first Sunday of each month. Closing time may shift depending on the day. (Some sources list the evening service ending around 21:00.)
places.neighborhood: Fushimi-Momoyama / Chūshojima area (near Fukakusa and Fushimi Stns)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small owner-run counter shop tucked near Fushimi Station, beneath the Kintetsu rail overpass in the Fukakusa area of Fushimi. The owner trained at earlier ramen shops (known locally as Dainaka and Gankokumen) before going independent and opening Tanukiya around 2015-2016; it ha

chef_bio.background: A small owner-run counter shop tucked near Fushimi Station, beneath the Kintetsu rail overpass in the Fukakusa area of Fushimi. The owner trained at earlier ramen shops (known locally as Dainaka and Gankokumen) before going independent and opening Tanukiya around 2015-2016; it has since grown into one of the most loved ramen stops in the Fushimi neighborhood, reaching roughly its 10th year in 2025.
chef_bio.philosophy: Big, satisfying, unabashedly rich bowls done in a tiny nine-seat space, with broth given time to mature rather than rushed.
chef_bio.specialty: Two faces define the shop. By day and night it pours an aged tonkotsu ramen made by simmering pork bones over high heat for hours and then resting the broth to deepen its umami into a thick, almost porridge-like richness, served over chewy medium-thick straight noodles and limited to about 20 bowls per service. Its other headline is the mazesoba: extra-thick noodles coated in a punchy tare with thick slabs of chashu and a heap of garlic. A genuinely full-sized house fried rice (chahan) rounds out the order and is a destination in its own right.

anecdotes.text: The shop explains the origin of its name, Tanukiya, on a sign at the storefront — a small touch of the owner's attention to detail.
The aged tonkotsu ramen is offered as a daily limited run of roughly 20 bowls at each service.
A frozen vending machine stands outside the shop selling chashu, fried rice, and mazesoba to take home.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopiが伏見深草・近鉄高架下(深草泓ノ壺町)、大中/頑固麺出身、カウンター9席、熟成豚骨ラーメン・極太まぜそば・チャーハン、店名の由来を店頭掲示と確認。totteoki/tabelogが1日約20杯限定と冷凍自販機を記載。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles for ramen and mazesoba come from a Kyoto noodle maker and are boiled in the same shared pots; with a single nine-seat counter kitchen, gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls use standard Japanese shoyu/tare seasoning, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free or tamari alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths and bowls are pork- and chicken-based with chashu and meat toppings; no plant-based preparation exists, so any item will contain or contact animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, busy local counter shop where ordering and a little basic Japanese go a long way; no English service is indicated, so a guide or a few Japanese phrases will smooth the visit.

noodles.notes: Noodles from the renowned Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂). The aged-tonkotsu bowl uses a medium-thick straight noodle with a firm, substantial bite to stand up to the thick broth; the lighter chuka-soba uses a thinner straight cut with a pleasant wheat aroma and crisp snap.

ramen.signature_bowl: Jukusei Tonkotsu Ramen (aged pork-bone ramen) — the kitchen's limited specialty, capped at about 20 bowls each at lunch and dinner. Go DX for extra chashu and a half ajitama, and consider the house "tanuki" meat-miso or keema-style curry topping stirred in.
ramen.chashu: Thick, roughly hand-cut chunks of braised pork belly rather than thin slices — soft and crumbly with fatty seams that melt into a sweet pork-fat richness, glazed in a soy-based tare. The lighter chuka-soba instead gets a tender, well-seasoned chicken chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker plus a soy-based (wheat) tare run through every bowl; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the menu is long-simmered pork-bone and chicken broth with pork-belly and chicken chashu; no plant-based broth or vegan bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto crowd-pleaser that refuses one lane: its limited-run star is a creamy, potage-thick pork-bone soup, simmered hard then rested a full day so the umami deepens without any barnyard funk, finished with a forward hit of garlic that rises through the nose on the first sip. Alongside it the kitchen runs a clean, almost nostalgic chicken-shoyu chuka-soba and a much-loved mazesoba, so one counter quietly covers light-and-clear to rich-and-rib-sticking.

places.name: 拉麺 へんてこ (Ramen Henteko)
places.gf_detail: This is a small ramen counter serving wheat noodles in chicken-paitan and shoyu-tare broths, with rotating specials like a spice/curry ramen and a carbonara-style bowl. Wheat noodles plus a wheat-based soy tare mean it is not celiac-safe, and no published source mentions gluten-free options or any allergen handling. A celiac diner should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around chicken-bone broth (a 6-hour chicken-and-aromatics paitan) with pork chashu and menma toppings; signature bowls are the Henteko Shoyu and Henteko Shio, plus meat-and-cheese specials. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is offered or mentioned by any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays plus occasional irregular closures. (One source lists lunch/dinner as roughly 11:00-14:30 / 18:00-21:30.)
places.neighborhood: 深草・墨染 (Fukakusa / Sumizome), Fushimi Ward
places.cuisine: Jiro-style / rich tonkotsu / chicken paitan ramen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Henteko is a tiny eight-seat ramen counter tucked down a narrow alley in the Fukakusa/Sumizome area of Fushimi, opened in March 2019 and reopened after a renovation in spring 2024. The owner is reported to have trained in the lineage of Meimon, a Kyoto establishment long associat

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Fushimi), in the Meimon ramen lineage
chef_bio.background: Henteko is a tiny ramen counter tucked down a narrow alley in the Fukakusa/Sumizome area of Fushimi (near Sumizome Station / Fujimori Shrine), opened around 2019 and reopened after a renovation in 2024 that expanded its original six-seat counter with a couple of tables. The owner trained at Meimon (名門), the Kyoto establishment often called the founder of the city's rich tonkotsu style, and he carries that grounding into his own shop while constantly tinkering with new ideas.
chef_bio.philosophy: A minimalist, quality-first operation: a small counter, a carefully simmered chicken broth, and straightforward bowls rather than fuss, paired with a steady stream of playful limited specials.
chef_bio.specialty: The house bowl is the Henteko Shoyu: a creamy chicken-paitan broth married to a soy tare, topped with a few thick slices of chashu, tender menma, and medium-thick noodles with a firm bite. A salt (shio) version sits alongside it.

anecdotes.text: The owner is often described as a warm, smiling, soft-spoken host whose personality seems to come through in the bowl itself.
True to the name 'henteko' (quirky/unconventional), the shop runs frequent limited creations, such as a house spice-curry ramen topped with chashu, melted cheese, fried onion and garlic.
The owner comes out of the Meimon lineage — 京都こってりラーメンの始祖『名門』 — the Kyoto shop credited with founding the city's signature rich tonkotsu tradition.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopiが店主の名門(京都こってりラーメンの始祖)出身、へんてこ醤油(鶏白湯+醤油)、限定スパイスカレーラーメン(チャーシュー・チーズ・フライドオニオン・自家製スパイス)、6席→2024年改装で卓追加を確認。kyoto-gourmetが深草・墨染の路地、鶏白湯醤油、穏やかな店主を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature bowls are built on a soy (shoyu) tare; standard Japanese ramen soy tare is wheat-based, and no source indicates otherwise here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a long-simmered chicken-bone paitan and bowls carry pork chashu, so the kitchen is animal-based throughout with no vegan preparation noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood counter down a narrow alley with a ticket-machine ordering system and cash-only payment, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the ticket machine) goes a long way.

noodles.notes: Medium / medium-thin noodles with a chewy, springy bite that lets the wheat flavor read through; sources differ on whether they run straight or gently wavy. Cooked a touch firmer for takeout orders.

ramen.signature_bowl: Henteko Shoyu (へんてこ醤油) — the chicken-paitan shoyu bowl. Variants worth knowing: the niboshi-charged "煮干しへんてこッ" and the seafood "魚介へんてこッ."
ramen.chashu: Sources differ: some describe thick chicken chashu blanched shabu-style to shed excess fat while keeping a juicy bite; others describe house slow-braised pork belly, served three generous slices. Either way it's a soft, fatty, melt-leaning cut rather than a lean loin.
notable_toppings: playful fish-shaped snack crackers (おっとっと) on the seafood bowl

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on long-simmered chicken bones and feet, with chicken fat and meat chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A tori-paitan house from the Fukakusa-Sumizome backstreets of Fushimi, where chicken feet and bones are boiled hard for hours into a milky, gently rich soup that drinks mellow rather than heavy. Its soul is the playful, research-minded spirit of the shop — a clean chicken-cream base finished with shoyu and bent toward niboshi or seafood riffs, a modern Kyoto original rather than any one regional lineage.

places.name: せんぼんぐらばー館 (Senbon Gurabaa-kan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The house specialties — Nagasaki champon and sara-udon — are both wheat-flour noodle dishes served in pork/seafood broth or wheat-thickened gravy, and the broader menu (gyoza, sweet-and-sour pork, fried rice, Chinese rice bowls) relies on wheat noodles, dumpling skins, and standard Chinese seasonings that contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no published allergen handling, no dedicated preparation, and nothing to suggest a celiac diner could eat safely here. Treat as unsafe for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The signature champon and sara-udon are built on pork, shrimp, squid, fishcake and a meat/seafood broth, and the rest of the menu is centered on pork, dumplings and egg-fried rice. No vegetable-only or plant-based versions are advertised. A vegan would have nothing reliable to order; at most one could ask, but no animal-free option is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri 11:30-14:00 & 17:00-20:00; Sun 17:00-20:00 (dinner only); closed Wed & Sat. (Blog sources variously cite lunch from ~11:45 and dinner to ~21:00 with last order ~20:40; listing hours used here.)
places.cuisine: Machi-chūka (neighborhood Chinese) / Nagasaki champon / sara-udon (crispy fried noodles)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A genuine Showa-era town-Chinese shop that has fed the Nishijin neighborhood for some 30-40 years, dating back to when the Senbon district was a buzzing entertainment quarter known as 'Kawaramachi in the east, Senbon in the west' (東の河原町、西の千本), with locals out for an evening 'Senb

chef_bio.background: A genuine Showa-era town-Chinese shop that has fed the Nishijin neighborhood for some 40 years, dating back to when the Senbon district was a buzzing entertainment quarter known as 'Kawaramachi in the east, Senbon in the west' (東の河原町、西の千本), with locals out for an evening stroll. As the surrounding district quieted over the decades, the shop stayed busy and beloved.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hearty, no-frills Nagasaki-style Chinese cooking done the same honest way for decades — generous portions, a hot wok, and a welcoming counter for solo diners.
chef_bio.specialty: Nagasaki champon and Nagasaki sara-udon — the house signatures. The champon is a milky, umami-rich broth piled with chewy noodles, pork, shrimp, squid, fishcake (kamaboko/chikuwa), cabbage, bean sprouts and carrot; the sara-udon layers ultra-thin crispy fried noodles under a glossy, vegetable-and-seafood gravy. Regulars also love the 'Gurabaa bento' set (sweet-and-sour pork, sara-udon, fried chicken and rice).

anecdotes.text: The name 'ぐらばー (Gurabaa)' nods to Nagasaki's Glover House, signaling that this is Nagasaki-style Chinese cooking rather than the usual Kyoto Chinese fare.
In the kitchen the owner works the wok with flames leaping up ('火柱あげて中華鍋を振り'), in a snug space of around a dozen counter and table seats.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi/muchi2が西陣・千本通の約40年続く町中華、『東の河原町、西の千本』と呼ばれた昭和の繁華街、長崎ちゃんぽん(エビ・イカ・カマボコ・チクワ・豚バラ・野菜)と皿うどん(極細揚げ麺)、ぐらばー=グラバー邸由来、火柱をあげる中華鍋、約13席、ぐらばー弁当(酢豚・皿うどん・唐揚げ・ご飯)を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central throughout: champon and sara-udon are wheat noodles, dumplings have wheat skins, and a single shared wok and fryer handle all dishes, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable in a kitchen this size.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese town-Chinese kitchen, dishes are seasoned with standard soy sauce and the gravy/seasonings expected to contain wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and stir-fries are built on pork and seafood, and the wok is shared across all orders, so any 'vegetable' dish would still carry animal-derived broth and cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, well-loved local machi-chuka where the owner cooks over roaring wok flames; little to no English is expected, so a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, regulars-friendly attitude go a long way.

noodles.notes: Distinctive thick yellow Nagasaki-champon noodles with a chewy, springy bite that holds up against the loaded broth and toppings; these are champon noodles (made with a unique alkaline lye water), not standard ramen noodles. The sara-udon by contrast uses hair-thin noodles deep-fried crisp.

ramen.signature_bowl: Nagasaki champon (長崎ちゃんぽん) — a milky, umami-deep bowl piled with stir-fried pork, shrimp, squid, chikuwa, naruto, cabbage, bean sprouts, onion and carrot over thick chewy champon noodles. (The sara-udon, with hair-thin crispy fried noodles under a chicken-stock veg-and-seafood ankake, is the equally famous companion specialty.)

gf.note: Champon and sara-udon noodles are wheat-based, and the stir-fried sauce/broth typically carries soy and seafood; no rice- or konjac-noodle or documented GF option exists here.

vegan.note: The signature champon broth is built on pork and chicken bones plus seafood (shrimp, squid), and the bowl is loaded with pork and seafood; no plant-only broth or vegan bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen shop but a beloved old Nishijin town-Chinese counter whose soul is Nagasaki champon — a Kyushu-born dish where pork-and-chicken bones, the sweetness of stir-fried cabbage and the brine of shrimp and squid melt into one milky, gently salted broth. You taste Nagasaki's port-city Chinese lineage rather than any Kyoto ramen tradition: a single warming bowl that is meal, soup and noodle all at once.

places.name: なるかみ (Narukami)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is almost certainly wheat-based, and the menu also features gyoza (wheat wrappers) and an ebimeshi/shrimp fried rice often seasoned with soy sauce.
