There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen accommodation noted. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broth is made from chicken bones (鶏ガラ100%) finished with scattered back fat (背脂), topped with chashu pork; gyoza typically contain pork. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen offerings.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–14:00 / 17:30–21:00 (last order). May close early when the soup runs out. Closed Mondays and Thursdays; schedule can vary.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Narukami is a small, family-run chuka-soba and gyoza specialist tucked along Senbon-dori in Kyoto's Kamigyo ward, a short walk from the Kitano-Hakubaicho area near Kitano Tenmangu. It is run as an intimate husband-and-wife operation with just fourteen seats split between a counte

chef_bio.background: Narukami is a small, family-run chuka-soba and gyoza specialist tucked along Senbon-dori in Kyoto's Kamigyo ward, a short walk from the Kitano-Hakubaicho area near Kitano Tenmangu. It is run as an intimate husband-and-wife operation with just fourteen seats split between a counter and a couple of tables.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on chicken bones alone, slowly drawn out, aiming for a broth with real depth that is still approachable enough to eat every day rather than a one-time heavy hit.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a classic Kyoto-style chuka soba: a broth built from carefully selected chicken bones simmered patiently (鶏ガラ100%), finished with a scattering of back fat (背脂) over a soy tare. Despite its rich, glistening look it drinks surprisingly light and clean, with a hidden touch of ichimi togarashi for lift, served over medium straight noodles with chashu. Alongside the ramen, the shop is known for its gyoza and a popular ebimeshi (shrimp fried rice).

anecdotes.text: Though the back-fat surface suggests a heavy, kotteri bowl, regulars note it eats clean and light, with a subtle ichimi spice as a hidden accent.
Beyond ramen, the ebimeshi (shrimp fried rice) is a beloved side that locals order to round out the meal.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Gnavi lists it as a 中華そば・餃子専門店 with a 鶏ガラ100%スープに背脂を散らした bowl kept light with a ぴりっとした一味 hidden accent; Tabelog gives 14席（カウンター10・テーブル4）, ご夫婦で営まれているスタイル, and reviews highlighting えびめし.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, gyoza with wheat wrappers, and a likely wheat-based soy tare are all prepared in the same small kitchen; no gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) style tare, which in standard Japanese ramen is brewed with wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is 100% chicken bones with pork back fat and chashu pork; gyoza typically contain pork. No animal-free preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny husband-and-wife counter shop with no English-language presence; basic Japanese and a relaxed, point-at-the-menu approach will serve you best.

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight noodles that play a supporting role to the soup; sources describe them as crisp and springy, sitting low-to-medium hydration with a clean bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Se-abura (back-fat) shoyu chuka-soba — the house chicken-bone soy bowl scattered with pork back fat and a peppery hit of ichimi chili. Pair it with the famous ebi-meshi (shrimp rice) on the side.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork belly, served a couple of slices to a bowl, with a firm, chewy texture that releases meat umami rather than melting away.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Chicken-bone broth, pork back-fat oil, and pork-belly chashu are core to every bowl; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook bowl of Kyoto's se-abura shoyu lineage: a chicken-bone soy soup that stays clear and surprisingly light beneath a snowfall of pork back fat, so you taste depth without heaviness. What sets this one apart is the unapologetic dusting of ichimi chili, giving the old-school Kyoto soul a bright, peppery edge.

places.name: 新大宮 中華のサカイ 本店 (Shin-Omiya Chuka no Sakai Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The signature reimen and other noodle dishes use wheat noodles, and the cold-noodle sauce is a sesame-mustard-soy style dressing that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. As a traditional machi-chuka serving fried rice, gyoza (wheat wrappers), spring rolls and fried chicken, the kitchen relies heavily on wheat and shared cooking surfaces. Not suitable for celiac diners; no dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation was found.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The famous reimen is topped with chashu (roast pork), and the broth/fried rice are enriched with chicken stock. Menu centers on pork, chicken and egg dishes (omurice, fried rice, fried chicken, spring rolls). No vegan options were identified, and there is no indication of plant-based broths or animal-product-free preparation.
places.hours_raw: Wed-Fri: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:45) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat-Sun: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Monday and Tuesday (open if a public holiday falls on those days).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1939, Chuka no Sakai began life as a kissaten (Western-style teahouse) on the Shin-Omiya shopping street just north of Kitaoji, near Daitoku-ji temple in Kyoto's Kita ward. After the war, food shortages pushed the family to reinvent the shop as a neighborhood Chinese d

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1939, Chuka no Sakai began life as a kissaten (Western-style teahouse) on the Shin-Omiya shopping street just north of Kitaoji, near Daitoku-ji temple in Kyoto's Kita ward. After the war, food shortages pushed the family to reinvent the shop as a neighborhood Chinese diner (machi-chuka), while quietly keeping a few of its teahouse-era Western dishes on the menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: A relaxed, unpretentious local institution that resists rushing diners despite long lunch queues, seating even small parties at big tables and letting people linger.
chef_bio.specialty: The house reimen (cold noodles): uniquely thick, chewy house-made noodles in a creamy sesame-based sauce with a pronounced mustard kick, balanced by refreshing acidity and a touch of sweetness, topped simply with chashu, nori and cucumber. Famously served year-round, not just in summer.

anecdotes.text: The reimen has built a nationwide following, drawing fans from across Japan to this small shopping-street shop.
The omurice is a holdover from the shop's pre-war teahouse days, with chicken-stock-enriched fried rice wrapped in a thin omelet and ketchup sauce.

The walls are covered in autographed photos of celebrities who have visited over the decades, and a sister branch called Misonobashi Sakai serves a slightly different soup flavoring (taxi drivers sometimes mix the two up).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan-kyoto confirms 1939 founding as a kissaten that turned machi-chuka after wartime shortages, the reimen with 全国にファン, and the omurice inherited from the teahouse era; takemachelin confirms the celebrity autographs on the walls and the taxi-driver confusion with the Misonobashi Sakai branch.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, queue-prone neighborhood Chinese diner where staff focus on turning tables quickly; expect a Japanese-only menu and little English, so a guide or pointing at dishes works best.

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered, exclusive to the shop: notably thick, glossy-translucent and intensely springy/mochi-mochi — closer to a tsukemen-grade noodle than a typical chilled-Chinese strand, and very much the co-star of the bowl.

ramen.signature_bowl: Reimen (cold noodle) — the year-round signature; choose the chashu (yakibuta) version over the ham version. A hot chuka-soba is also offered but is a secondary item.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork (yakibuta) is the standard heart; a ham version is offered as the alternate protein.

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a vinegar/soy-leaning sesame-mustard dressing; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The signature cold bowl carries pork (or ham) and an umami-rich dashi-laced dressing; no plant-only broth or topping set is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Showa-born Kyoto town-Chinese institution whose soul lives not in a hot soup but in a chilled bowl: thick, springy custom noodles bound in a creamy sesame-mustard-vinegar dressing so beloved that regulars drink the leftover sauce. In a country where ramen means hot broth, this is Kyoto's definitive answer to what a cold noodle can be — its own lineage, found nowhere else.

places.name: みその橋サカイ (Misonobashi Sakai)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The house specialty is cold noodles (冷麺) made with thick wheat ("ムチムチ太麺") noodles, and the menu centers on Chinese noodle dishes (中華そば) — all wheat-based. The signature sesame dressing blends sweet/sour and mustard notes and almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce/seasonings; broth-based dishes use pork. There is no published allergen or gluten-free information. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The flagship bowls are 焼豚冷麺 (roast-pork cold noodles) and ham cold noodles, and the kitchen is a Chinese-cuisine shop built around pork. The sesame dare and other dishes have no stated plant-based versions, and no vegetarian/vegan menu is advertised.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun & holidays: 11:00–15:00, 17:00–21:30. Closed Mon & Tue.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, Misonobashi (west end of Misono Bridge; toward Kitaōji / Kamigamo Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Misonobashi Sakai traces back to a Sakai family Chinese kitchen founded in Kyoto's Kita ward in the prewar/early-Showa era; this branch opened in 1982 as an independent offshoot of the original 'Chuka no Sakai' near Daitokuji, settling by the Misonobashi bridge toward Kamigamo.

chef_bio.background: Misonobashi Sakai traces back to a Sakai family Chinese kitchen founded in Kyoto's Kita ward in the prewar/early-Showa era; this branch opened in 1982 as an independent offshoot of the original 'Chuka no Sakai' near Daitokuji, settling by the Misonobashi bridge toward Kamigamo.
chef_bio.philosophy: A neighborhood institution built on one perfected dish, served year-round — captured in the local saying that when you think cold noodles you think Sakai, and when you think Sakai you think cold noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Sesame-sauce cold noodles — the 焼豚冷麺 (roast-pork cold noodles): plump, chewy thick noodles bound in a thick, emulsified pale-yellow sesame dare that balances sweetness, tang, and a mustard kick, finished with sliced roast pork; a ham version is also offered.

anecdotes.text: The cold noodles are eaten year-round rather than only in summer, and the shop is so identified with the dish that it has become its calling card across Kyoto.
The signature sesame dare is described as a thick, emulsified dressing combining sourness, sweetness, and mustard pungency over notably plump, chewy thick noodles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms the 1982 opening as an offshoot of 中華のサカイ near Daitokuji, the Misonobashi/Kamigamo location, the 焼豚冷麺 (¥720) with a yellow sesame sauce of 酸味・甘味・マスタードの辛味 over thick chewy noodles plus a ham version, and the reputation as 京都一有名な冷麺 with the saying 冷麺といえばサカイ、サカイといえば冷麺.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes use wheat noodles and wheat-based Chinese seasonings; high cross-contact risk in a shared noodle kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sesame cold-noodle dare and Chinese dishes are very likely seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is pork/ham-centered (roast pork, ham cold noodles); animal products are pervasive.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, locally loved Kyoto neighborhood Chinese shop where ordering is easiest with a little Japanese, though the menu is simple and pointing works fine.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick straight noodles in the traditional Kyoto machi-chuka mold; the broth clings to them. (The shop's signature cold-noodle dish uses far thicker, chewy, glossy egg noodles, but the hot chuka-soba runs medium straight.)

ramen.signature_bowl: The house is famous for its year-round cold noodles (reimen), but the hot bowl to order is the chuka-soba — a Kyoto-style pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu ramen; chashu-men adds extra roast pork.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised/roast pork (yakibuta) in the old-school machi-chuka manner; the same roast pork that crowns the celebrated cold noodles.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; the cold-noodle sauce also contains soy and egg. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork-bone and chicken stock and the bowls are dressed with roast pork; the cold-noodle sauce likewise lists chicken extract, pork extract and egg. No documented plant-based broth.

ramen.pantheon: A beloved old-guard Kyoto neighborhood Chinese kitchen near Misonobashi whose hot chuka-soba speaks pure Kyoto lineage — a clear, soy-tinged soup drawn from pork bones and chicken that coats medium-thin straight noodles. Its true legend, though, is the secret-recipe cold noodle eaten all year, a chilled bowl found nowhere else in the same form.

places.name: 一番星 (Ichibanboshi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop serving wheat ramen noodles in a pork-and-chicken broth with soy-based tare, which is virtually always made with wheat-containing shoyu. There is no indication of any gluten-free option, gluten-free noodles, or allergen accommodation. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here. The only side is white rice, and the house-made pickles served with the bowl could in theory be safe, but the shop offers no allergen information and the kitchen handles wheat noodles throughout, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: The single signature bowl is built on a pork-bone and chicken-carcass broth and topped with house-made chashu (pork). There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen, and the menu is essentially one item plus rice. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Fri/Sat/Sun 11:00–15:00, 17:00–19:00 / Tue 11:00–15:00 (no dinner) / closed Wed & Thu. Some sources list the dinner service as 16:30–19:00.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A craftsman ramen shop born from a yatai (hand-pushed food cart). Founder Ryohei Morikawa, originally from Ishikawa Prefecture, had worked as a materials dealer and a taxi driver before learning to make ramen by volunteering for five months — late nights and days off, unpaid — at

chef_bio.origin: 京都市左京区・岡崎（平安神宮の北東）
chef_bio.background: A craftsman ramen shop born from a yatai (hand-pushed food cart). Founder Ryohei Morikawa, originally from Ishikawa Prefecture, had worked as a materials dealer and a taxi driver before learning to make ramen by volunteering unpaid for five months — late nights after his own shift work — at a shop called Homin (芳眠) in Kamikatsura. In 1972 he began pushing a hinoki-wood cart along Marutamachi-dori in Okazaki, opening each evening around the time the first star appeared, which gave the shop its name, Ichibanboshi ("first star"). After about three years he took over a brick-walled space left by a closing nearby steakhouse and put down permanent roots; the ivy-covered, café-like brick exterior near Heian Shrine is now the local landmark.
chef_bio.philosophy: Morikawa credits his customers for the recipe itself: "お客さんのほうがラーメンにくわしいんです。こだわりのあるお客さんのアドバイスをたくさん取り入れて、今の味になりました" — "Customers know ramen better than I do; I took in a lot of advice from discerning regulars, and that's how today's flavor came to be." He says the taste evolved over the years alongside the regulars who eat it.
chef_bio.specialty: A single bowl: chuka-soba. A clear (seiyu) broth of pork bones blended with chicken carcasses, soy-based and a touch sweet, with fine, soft noodles, house-made chashu and house-made menma, plus bean sprouts and green onion. House-made pickles are served alongside to refresh the palate after the bowl. Rice is the only other thing on the menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named for the cart's opening time: Morikawa rolled out his hinoki-wood yatai around dusk, when the first star (一番星) appeared in the sky.
He learned the craft by working unpaid for five months at a shop called Homin in Kamikatsura, taking the late shifts after his own day's work.
He shaped the broth around long-time regulars' feedback, adjusting it over the years until it settled into today's flavor.
The brick, ivy-covered storefront — taken over from a closed steakhouse — feels more like a retro kissaten than a ramen shop: "レトロなレンガ調で、まるで喫茶店のような店内."

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hankei500 (detailed profile) confirms Morikawa's Ishikawa origin, prior work as a materials dealer and taxi driver, five months' unpaid training at 芳眠 in Kamikatsura, the 1972 hinoki yatai on Marutamachi-dori in Okazaki opening ~6:30pm when the first star appears, the move after ~3 years into a former steakhouse's brick building, the soy-based pork-bone-and-chicken broth with house chashu and menma, and the quote お客さんのほうがラーメンにくわしいんです…今の味になりました; kyotopi describes the retro, kissaten-like brick interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-item wheat-noodle ramen shop with one small kitchen; wheat noodles are handled throughout, so any dish would face cross-contamination. No gluten-free handling is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-based (醤油/清湯) chuka-soba; Japanese shoyu used in such tare is virtually always wheat-containing. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-carcass based with house-made pork chashu; the kitchen is built entirely around animal products, so no vegan separation exists.

positives.text: House-made pickles (お漬物) are served with the bowl to refresh the palate, and chashu and menma are made in-house — a sign of a careful, hands-on kitchen, though not an allergen accommodation.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only, decades-old neighborhood ramen counter run by an attentive owner with a single set menu and no English or allergen support, so a first-time GF/vegan visitor should not attempt it without a Japanese-speaking guide.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles with a slightly soft, gentle bite that cling to and soak up the soy-pork broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the one bowl this shop makes: a pork-bone-and-chicken soy broth over thin straight noodles, served with a side of house pickles.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, rested a full day until tender and juicy, with clearly defined lean and fat layers; the fat carries a subtle sweetness.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce tare made with wheat; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option exists here.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-bone and chicken broth with a touch of pork fat, finished with chashu — no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic survivor of old Kyoto's noodle tradition: pork bones and chicken simmered with onion into a soy-darkened soup that drinks light and mellow yet leaves a faint clinging richness on the lips. It is the gentle, everyday face of Kyoto ramen — pork-bone soy with a whisper of fat rather than the heavy back-fat curtain of the city's louder shops.

places.name: 慈永 (Jiei)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle Kyoto chukasoba shop. The bowl is built on wheat (komugi) straight noodles plus a tonkotsu-shoyu broth whose tare is almost certainly standard wheat-based soy sauce, with no gluten-free menu, no separate prep, and a shared boiling pot. Not suitable for celiac diners. No GF information is published anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: A single-bowl pork-based shop: the broth is tonkotsu-shoyu (pork bone) and every bowl is topped with pork-belly chashu, with rice/egg-over-rice as the only sides. There is no vegetarian or vegan ramen option and no published accommodation.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–14:30 / 17:30–22:00 (closed Mon, with additional irregular days off)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Jiei opened on February 4, 2016 in a quiet residential pocket of Mototanaka in Sakyo Ward, just north of Kyoto University's agriculture campus, taking over a small storefront that earlier sources describe as a former bistro/izakaya space. Now approaching its tenth year, it has be

chef_bio.background: Jiei opened in February 2016 in a quiet residential pocket of Mototanaka in Sakyo Ward, about five minutes' walk from Mototanaka Station and just north of Kyoto University's agriculture campus, taking over a small storefront that earlier housed a Western-style (yoshoku) restaurant called Kitchen Gorilla. Now approaching its tenth year, it has become a low-key neighborhood fixture rather than a tourist destination.
chef_bio.philosophy: Do one bowl, and do it carefully. The taciturn owner is described as working quietly and methodically, plating a nostalgic, old-fashioned (昔ながらの) Kyoto ramen meant to feel homely and warming rather than flashy.
chef_bio.specialty: A single classic Kyoto-style chukasoba (中華そば) in a style that reviewers liken to the Takara-ya (宝屋) / Taiei (大栄) Kyoto-ramen tradition: a tea-colored tonkotsu-shoyu broth with mellow, comforting umami, served over medium-thin straight noodles and topped with thick-cut pork-belly chashu, menma (bamboo shoots) and freshly hand-cut scallions. Offered as regular or large, plus a chashu version; rice rounds out the short menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a space that earlier housed a Western-style restaurant called Kitchen Gorilla before becoming Jiei, part of its low-profile, residential-street character.
The owner is reported to have previously worked as a manager at the Kyoto ramen shop Daioh Ramen (大王ラーメン), which reviewers tie to Jiei's nostalgic, Takara-ya/Taiei-style Kyoto ramen.
The space leans into Showa-era nostalgia, down to a television playing in the corner of the small counter room.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms the 2016 opening in Mototanaka (~5 min from Mototanaka Station), the single tonkotsu-shoyu chukasoba with medium-thin straight noodles, large pork-belly chashu, menma and hand-cut scallions, the taciturn proprietor and TV-in-the-corner nostalgia, and that the space previously held a Western restaurant 'Kitchen Gorilla'; osumituki reports the owner was formerly manager at 大王ラーメン and likens the ramen to 大栄ラーメンや宝屋系.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowl is built on wheat straight noodles boiled in a shared pot, with a tonkotsu-shoyu broth and (standard wheat) soy-based tare; no gluten-free preparation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork bone + soy sauce) base; soy sauce in a traditional Kyoto chukasoba tare is wheat-based unless stated otherwise, and no allergen labeling is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl uses pork-bone broth and is topped with pork-belly chashu; there is no animal-free option.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood ramen shop with a quiet owner, cash only, no English and no menu accommodations expected — best approached with a guide or basic Japanese and a willingness to just order the one bowl.

noodles.notes: Chuhoso (medium-thin) straight noodles with a clean, crisp bite and clear wheat character; described as silky and supple, gripping the tea-colored broth well.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu Ramen — the standard ramen (around 750 yen) topped with extra slices of the tender pork belly chashu.
ramen.chashu: Long, thin slices of braised pork belly, simmered until they crumble apart at the touch of chopsticks; tender and full-flavored without being heavy.
notable_toppings: thick-cut crunchy menma with a gentle sweetness

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-based (wheat) tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with a soy tare, finished with pork back fat and topped with pork belly chashu; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic bowl in the classic Kyoto se-abura shoyu tradition: a tea-colored broth of chicken and pork bones seasoned with dark soy and floated with a thin veil of pork back fat, so you taste the old-school Kyoto lineage (a Takaraya-style soul carried over from the owner's years at a veteran Kyoto ramen-ya) in every mellow, deep-umami sip. It is the comforting, lived-in Kyoto shoyu — rich and well-developed but never cloying — that defines the city's everyday ramen culture.

places.name: 昆布と麺 喜一 (Konbu to Men Kiichi)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The noodles are wheat — a custom whole-grain blend built on Hokkaido "Haruyo Koi" first-grade flour — so the core dish contains gluten. Unusually for ramen, the signature kombu ramen broth is described by the shop as using no meat and no kaeshi (no soy-sauce tare), built only from blended ma-/rausu-/rishiri kombu plus seafood and bonito; that removes the usual wheat-shoyu tare risk from the broth, but the wheat noodles themselves rule out a gluten-free meal. No gluten-free noodle option and no dedicated GF handling is indicated.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan and not vegetarian. Although the signature kombu ramen broth uses no meat stock (a kombu-and-seafood dashi), it is built on bonito (katsuo) flakes and other seafood, so it is not vegetarian/vegan. The gagome kombu tsukemen is also described with pork chashu and a soy-sauce tare in at least one account. There is no stated vegan or vegetarian option.
places.hours_raw: 3-seating reservation system: ① from 11:00, ② from 12:00, ③ from 13:00 (three time slots, 10 seats each, reservation-only), each seating ~45 minutes. Arrive by the 10-minutes-prior assembly time or entry is denied. Tabelog lists Sunday closed; official site notes irregular closures — confirm via Instagram @kiichi5224 before going.
places.neighborhood: 上京区・西五辻東町 (Kitano-Hakubaicho / Kitano Tenmangu area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kiichi is the ramen room of Itutuji no Konbu, a Kyoto kombu specialist founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) on Nishi-Itsutsuji in Kamigyo-ku. The ramen counter sits on the second floor above the family's historic kelp shop, near Kitano Tenmangu.

roles: Operated by 五辻の昆布 (Itutuji no Konbu)
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, in the Kitano-Hakubaicho / Kitano Tenmangu district of Kamigyo-ku.
chef_bio.background: Kiichi is the ramen room of Itutuji no Konbu, a Kyoto kombu specialist founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) on Nishi-Itsutsuji in Kamigyo-ku. The ramen counter sits on the second floor above the family's historic kelp shop, near Kitano Tenmangu.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop exists to help people rediscover the value of kombu: 昆布専門店が『昆布の価値を再認識してもらう』ために作った『昆布らぁめん』 — a kombu specialist's answer to falling kelp consumption and changing kitchens.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 昆布らぁめん (kombu ramen) is built on a broth blending ma-kombu, rausu and rishiri kelp, lifted with bonito and seafood (squid and shellfish) and — unusually for ramen — made with no meat stock. The other headline bowl is the がごめ昆布つけ麺 (gagome kombu tsukemen), whose dipping water leans on sticky gagome kelp; whole-grain wheat noodles built on Hokkaido 'Haruyo Koi' first-grade flour are made to the shop's own spec.

anecdotes.text: Each seating is a guided experience, not just a meal: diners first water-taste three kelp varieties side by side, then watch oboro-kombu shaved fresh by hand and laid over the noodles, finishing with a kombu rice ball and the shop's tsukudani.
Since June 2023 a 'One Kombu' rule asks every guest to buy at least one product from the kelp shop downstairs, tying the ramen experience back to the 120-year-old family trade.
The fresh oboro kombu is described as just-shaved and still soft (削り出された出来立てホヤホヤのおぼろ昆布), demonstrated live with the shop's own equipment.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: itutuji (official) confirms 五辻の昆布 founded Meiji 35 (1902) at 西五辻東町, the 2nd-floor ramen counter (reservation, three seatings of ~10), the 昆布らぁめん on ma/rausu/rishiri kelp with bonito, squid and shellfish and no meat stock/additives over 春よ恋 Hokkaido first-grade whole-wheat noodles, the three-kelp water tasting, hand-shaved oboro kombu, kombu tsukudani rice ball, and the ワンコンブ制 since June 2023; noodlers describes the just-shaved oboro kombu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish is wheat-noodle ramen/tsukemen with no gluten-free alternative noted, so cross-contamination is moot for celiacs — the meal itself contains gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature kombu ramen is stated to use no kaeshi / no soy-sauce tare (かえしは不使用), so the flagship broth avoids the usual wheat-shoyu seasoning; however, an account of the gagome kombu tsukemen describes a soy-sauce tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth relies on bonito and seafood (and at least one bowl uses pork chashu), so it is not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

positives.text: The signature kombu ramen broth uses no additives and no meat stock — a purely kombu-and-seafood dashi (添加物、肉類、かえしは不使用), unusually transparent and animal-stock-free for ramen.

cultural_comfort.note: A reservation-only, fixed-timing seating with a guided kombu tasting and a one-product purchase rule — smoothest if you can manage the online booking and follow along in Japanese, though the experience is welcoming.

noodles.notes: Whole-grain (zenryūfun) noodles made in-house/for the shop by ramen maker Tōhichi using first-grade Hokkaido Haruyokoi wheat. The bowl (ramen) uses a medium straight whole-grain noodle; the seasonal gagome-kombu tsukemen pairs a thicker, chewier whole-grain noodle chilled in cold water for firm, elastic bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: 昆布らぁめん (Kombu ramen) — a clear shio bowl built entirely on kombu and seafood dashi, topped with the shop's hand-shaved oboro kombu. In warmer months (roughly May–October) the kitchen switches to the がごめ昆布つけ麺 (gagome-kombu tsukemen), with a sticky kombu-rich dip and thick whole-grain noodles.
ramen.chashu: For the ramen, the "chashu" is domestic young chicken breast gently cooked in kombu dashi and salt — lean, delicate, and in keeping with the no-meat-in-the-broth philosophy. The seasonal tsukemen instead uses soft, tender braised pork (nibuta).
notable_toppings: Hand-shaved oboro kombu (true kombu, shaved to order)

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based whole-grain (Haruyokoi) men with kansui, and the tsukemen dip adds shoyu — both contain wheat/gluten, so not celiac-safe. No documented rice or konjac noodle option.

vegan.note: The broth is uniquely meat-free and additive-free, built on three kombu varieties plus seafood (bonito, dried squid, shellfish). The kombu dashi base itself is plant-derived, but the soup includes katsuo/seafood and the toppings include chicken or pork, so as served it is not vegan. A kombu-and-vegetable-only bowl is not advertised — worth asking, but do not assume.

ramen.pantheon: A one-of-a-kind bowl from a 150-year-old Kyoto kombu house: the soup is pure kelp-and-sea umami — true, Rausu, and Rishiri kombu cold-steeped in soft water, layered with bonito, dried squid and shellfish, and seasoned with nothing but salt. There is no meat and no kaeshi in the pot, so what you taste is the clean, glutamate-rich lineage of dashi itself, finished with kombu shaved over the bowl by hand.

places.name: 麺屋 聖〜kiyo〜 (Menya Kiyo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The shop is a shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen specialist serving house-made wheat noodles produced fresh daily, in a soy-sauce tare that in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. Broth and chashu add further risk. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, tare, or any celiac accommodation. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature bowls are built on an animal-based dashi (described as drawing on shellfish such as oysters, clams and shrimp) combined with Kyoto soy sauce, and topped with slow-cooked pork chashu. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is advertised.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun 11:00-15:00, 17:30-22:00; Thursday closed (plus occasional irregular closures).
places.notes: Menya Kiyo opened on October 1, 2018, as the second shop of the Kyoto-born Menya Yukou group, planting its flag in Ichijoji, one of Japan's most fiercely contested ramen districts. The aim from the start was to carry Kyoto's dashi (broth) culture to the neighborhood, the rest of

chef_bio.background: Menya Kiyo opened on October 1, 2018, as the second shop of the Kyoto-born Menya Yukou group, planting its flag in Ichijoji, one of Japan's most fiercely contested ramen districts. The aim from the start was to carry Kyoto's dashi (broth) culture to the neighborhood, the rest of Japan, and the wider world.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop mills and makes its noodles fresh every morning at an in-house noodle facility, chasing a chewy texture that clings to the soy-based broth, and treats the spacious counter as a place where guests can slow down and relax over a bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style shoyu (soy-sauce) chuka-soba: a clear, mellow soy-sauce broth layered over a delicate dashi, paired with house-made noodles. The pork chashu is tenderized by the shop's own method and simmered for over two hours, then sliced to each diner's preferred thickness right at the counter.

anecdotes.text: Chashu is slow-simmered for more than two hours and then carved to the customer's chosen thickness at the counter.
Noodles are produced fresh each morning in the group's own in-house noodle factory for a chewy bite that suits the soy-sauce broth.
Opened October 1, 2018 as the second branch of the Kyoto Menya Yukou group, with the stated mission of spreading Kyoto's dashi culture.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: yukou-kiyo (official) confirms Kiyo as the Menya Yukou group's 2nd shop with the Kyoto dashi-culture mission, in-house morning-milled chewy noodles, three soy-sauce soups, and chashu simmered 2+ hours cut to preference; miyatsu (kyoto.tips) and osumituki confirm the October 1, 2018 Ichijoji opening date.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat ramen noodles are produced fresh daily; the kitchen is built around wheat noodles with no gluten-free line, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop is a shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen specialist whose broth is finished with Kyoto soy sauce; Japanese soy sauce / shoyu tare is conventionally brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth relies on an animal/shellfish-based dashi and bowls are topped with pork chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation path.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly neighborhood ramen counter in Kyoto's competitive Ichijoji ramen district; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, as English support is not guaranteed.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles milled and cut fresh each morning at the shop's adjoining in-house noodle workshop; medium-thick with a springy, chewy, firm bite (puri/mocchiri) that grips the shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Okurimono ("Gift" / 贈り物) — the clear, dashi-forward shoyu chuka-soba, the bowl that defines the shop. The broth is finished tableside as the rich pork-fat from the chashu melts in and rounds out the soy.
ramen.chashu: The heart of the bowl: thick-cut braised pork, simmered gently for over two hours until meltingly tender, then sliced to the diner's chosen thickness right at the counter — a slab so substantial it eats more like a meat dish set in the ramen than a topping.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth leans on fish dashi (bonito/seafood) and the bowl is finished with pork fat and topped with braised pork; no documented plant-based broth or vegan bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A polished Kyoto take on the city's dashi-and-shoyu soul: a clear, refined soy broth layered with bonito, kombu and seafood umami, then rounded out tableside as melting pork fat enriches the soup — restrained and elegant where Tokyo shoyu is sharper. You taste Kyoto's beloved sweet, dashi-deep lineage in every sip, anchored by a slab of slow-braised chashu cut to order.

places.name: 稲穂食堂 (Inaho Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. The kitchen leans on naturally rice-based Southeast Asian dishes — Khao Man Gai (jasmine rice with poached chicken), pho and pad thai (rice noodles), and green curry over rice — which can be lower-gluten than the wheat-noodle ramen the street is known for. However, Thai/Vietnamese cooking commonly uses fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy-based seasonings, and no cross-contamination handling is documented. A celiac diner must ask about each sauce; treat as not verified safe.
places.vegan_detail: Some plant-forward items exist (green salad, chilled tofu, vegetable salads, and green curry described as having a vegetarian option), and the building's ground floor is an organic vegetable market that supplies the kitchen. But Thai/Vietnamese dishes here typically use fish sauce, chicken broth, and egg, so a strict vegan must confirm preparation; full vegan is not guaranteed.
places.neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku (near Ichijoji Station, Eizan Railway)
places.notes: Inaho Shokudo sits on the second floor of a small building in Ichijoji, above a ground-floor organic vegetable market and below a guesthouse — a layout that shapes the whole experience: produce comes from just downstairs, and travelers staying upstairs drift in, giving the room a

chef_bio.background: Inaho Shokudo sits on the second floor of a small building on the Takano/Ichijoji fringe of Sakyo Ward, above a ground-floor organic vegetable market and below a guesthouse — a layout that shapes the whole experience: produce is sold just downstairs, and travelers staying upstairs drift in, giving the room an easy international mix. Rather than a ramen counter, it is an Asian 'no-borders' (mukokuseki) dining bar leaning hard into Thai and Vietnamese home cooking, making liberal use of fresh herbs and seasonal vegetables.
chef_bio.philosophy: Lunch is framed as Asian street food in a plant-filled, greenery-draped space; evenings turn into a relaxed dining bar with music, drinks, and food. The cooking emphasizes fresh herbs and seasonal vegetables, with an organic vegetable market right downstairs in the same building.
chef_bio.specialty: Khao Man Gai — jasmine rice cooked in concentrated chicken broth, topped with poached chicken and served with Thai chili and scallion sauces — is the dish reviewers single out. Khao Soi (Chiang Mai curry noodles), pho, pad thai, and green curry round out the noodle-and-rice section, with crispy herb-filled spring rolls and Yam Woon Sen among the small plates.

anecdotes.text: The building stacks an organic vegetable market on the ground floor, the restaurant on the second, and a guesthouse on the third — so produce is sold in the same building and the crowd skews international.
Despite standing near Ichijoji's famous ramen street, the kitchen serves Thai and Vietnamese-inspired dishes — Khao Man Gai, Khao Soi, pho, green curry — rather than ramen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto.tips describes the building as an organic vegetable market (1F), the restaurant (2F) and a guesthouse (3F) at Takano Tamaoka-cho, a mukokuseki/Thai-leaning kitchen using fresh herbs, ~25 seats, and highlights the カオマンガイ (Khao Man Gai); Tabelog's menu lists Khao Soi, pho, pad thai, green curry, spring rolls and Yam Woon Sen.

positives.text: Many menu items are naturally rice-based rather than wheat-noodle — Khao Man Gai (jasmine rice + poached chicken), pho and pad thai (rice noodles), and curries over rice — which can suit a gluten-avoiding diner better than the wheat ramen the street is known for, though sauces should still be checked.
An organic vegetable market on the ground floor supplies the kitchen, and the menu includes plant-forward salads, chilled tofu, and a green curry with a vegetarian option.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, foreigner-friendly spot (a guesthouse occupies the floor above, so international guests are common), but English support is limited — a few words of Japanese go a long way.

places.name: コムギノキラメキ〈小麦〉 (Komugi no Kirameki (Komugi))
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The house specialty is in-house-milled, in-house-made wheat noodles (a four-wheat blend), and the bowls pair them with chicken-paitan broth and typically wheat-based shoyu/miso tare. The flagship dish, Komugi no Chikara, is a thick-noodle Jiro-style mazesoba — entirely wheat. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation was found, and no allergen program is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly stop. The broth is a chicken-paitan (creamy chicken) base and the Taiwan mazesoba is built on pork mince, egg yolk, and chicken-derived components; the Komugi no Chikara mazesoba features braised pork and back fat. No plant-based broth or vegan option was found on the menu.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Kami-Katsura neighborhood ramen shop in Kyoto's Nishikyo Ward, this is one branch of the fast-growing Kirameki group, which began with its flagship Kirameki no Tori in Marutamachi and has spread across Kyoto and into Osaka. The shop sits about a 7-minute walk from Kamikatsura S

chef_bio.background: A Kami-Katsura neighborhood ramen shop in Kyoto's Nishikyo Ward, this is one branch of the fast-growing Kirameki group, which began with its flagship Kirameki no Tori in Marutamachi and has spread across Kyoto and into Osaka. The shop sits about a 7-minute walk from Kamikatsura Station on the Hankyu Arashiyama line, with on-site parking.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name means roughly 'the sparkle of wheat,' and the shop leans hard into the noodle itself: noodles are milled and made in-house in a glass-fronted production room, using a custom wheat blend prized for its bite — thick and chewy for mazesoba, medium for ramen.
chef_bio.specialty: Two pillars: a creamy tori-paitan (chicken) ramen offered in salt, soy, and miso, and richly built mazesoba. The signature bowl is Komugi no Chikara ('the power of wheat'), a Jiro-style thick-noodle mazesoba piled with bean sprouts, menma, and big chunks of tender pork and back fat. The Taiwan mazesoba layers pork mince, egg yolk, garlic, chives, scallion, nori, and fish powder.

anecdotes.text: Komugi no Chikara, the house Jiro-style mazesoba, is offered at only a handful of the group's Kyoto shops, making this branch one of the places to try it.
Noodles are produced on-site behind glass in the shop's own noodle room, which gives it its 'sparkle of wheat' identity.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: genjitsutouhi confirms the Kamikatsura branch of the Kirameki group (flagship Kirameki no Tori in Marutamachi), 7-min walk from 上桂駅 on the Hankyu Arashiyama line, on-site parking, glass-fronted in-house noodle production, tori-paitan (soy/salt) with thick white chicken broth, the Taiwan mazesoba 'Chokutaro' and Komugi no Chikara oil-based mazesoba (up to 500g); osumituki notes Komugi no Chikara is available at only some group shops.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House noodles are wheat (a four-wheat blend), milled and made in-house, and used across every bowl; there is no gluten-free noodle and no allergen-handling program advertised, so cross-contamination from shared noodle water and prep is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy- and miso-tare ramen are on the menu; standard ramen tare and miso in Japan are wheat-containing, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-paitan and dishes feature pork, egg yolk, and fish powder; no plant-based options were found, so vegan diners cannot eat here safely.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual ticket-machine ramen shop — buy a meal ticket at the vending machine before sitting; a smile and a few Japanese words go a long way, though the system is easy to navigate visually.

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from the shop's glass-walled noodle workshop using 100% Hokkaido wheat. The standard paitan bowls use a smooth, chewy (tsuru-mochi) medium-thin straight noodle that suits the delicate-yet-rich soup. The Jiro-style mazesoba 'Komugi no Chikara' instead uses an ultra-thick (3mm, #10 cutter) square straight noodle with strong bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shio no Kirameki (salt tori-paitan) — the cleanest expression of the rich, creamy chicken-paitan soup; the soy (Shoyu no Kirameki) and miso versions share the same broth. The shop-exclusive Jiro-style mazesoba "Komugi no Chikara" is the brothless counterpart for big-noodle, back-fat cravings.
ramen.chashu: Seared chicken-thigh chashu — thick, tender slices of thigh meat with a lightly torched/seared surface, served on the paitan bowls. (The Jiro-style Komugi no Chikara mazesoba instead tops with tender pork and back-fat.)

gf.note: Noodles are house-made 100% Hokkaido wheat with kansui and the soy/miso tare contain wheat; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a long-simmered chicken-bone paitan, with chicken or pork chashu and animal-based tare/oil. No plant-broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern tori-paitan specialist whose soul is a long-simmered chicken broth boiled until it turns milky, creamy and clinging — the cloudy, emulsified style that defines contemporary chicken-paitan ramen rather than any old regional lineage. You taste that pure poultry richness most clearly in the restrained salt (shio) bowl, where the velvety soup, not the seasoning, carries the bowl.

places.name: ラーメン ビスカ (Ramen Viska)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tonkotsu shop with no gluten-free offering. The noodles are wheat, the tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu, and the menu (kotteri/assari/tsukemen) is built around standard ramen components. No celiac-safe option and no published allergen handling. Not suitable for anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth and topped with roast-pork chashu; there is no vegetable or vegan broth and no meat-free option on the menu. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 18:00-20:00 (sources also list the dinner block as 17:30-19:30). Open until sold out. Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays plus irregular holidays.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Viska opened in summer 2023 (July 14) in a quiet residential pocket behind Takano Post Office, on the fringe of Ichijoji's famously crowded ramen battleground. It is a true one-person shop: the owner cooks, serves, and chats single-handedly. The space is a renovated former

chef_bio.background: Ramen Viska opened on July 14, 2023, in a quiet residential pocket behind Takano Post Office, on the fringe of Ichijoji's famously crowded ramen battleground. It is a true one-person shop: the owner cooks, serves, and chats single-handedly. The space is a renovated former snack bar with a glossy high bar-style counter of about five seats, and the owner greets guests in a suit and tie rather than the usual ramen-cook garb. There is no noren curtain out front.
chef_bio.philosophy: A minimalist, single-minded devotion to tonkotsu done well — quality broth over a broad menu, served personally and explained in detail to anyone curious.
chef_bio.specialty: Super-rich kotteri tonkotsu: a pork-bone broth simmered until thick enough that, as locals joke, the spoon could stand up in it, yet it stays mild and clean on the palate with no porky funk, drinking almost like a potage. The lineup is deliberately spare — kotteri (rich), assari (a remarkably clear, transparent tonkotsu, finished with yuzu zest), a Bisuro (ビス郎) bowl, and tsukemen — paired with medium-straight noodles and well-seasoned roast-pork chashu. The focus is entirely the bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'Viska' comes from Catalan, used as a celebratory cheer meaning roughly 'long live!' / 'banzai / hooray!'
Among the table condiments sits an unexpected jar of harissa, the North African chili paste, which regulars say melds surprisingly well with the rich pork broth.
The owner works solo, and diners can self-serve rice from a cooker and add takana and pickled ginger, with free small meat and green-onion top-ups offered.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms the summer-2023 opening behind Takano Post Office, no noren, bar-like interior, suit-and-tie owner, the こってり 'spoon might stand up' mild tonkotsu 'like potage', and the harissa on the table; noodlers confirms the one-person operation, ex-snack-bar space, assari/kotteri/Bisuro/tsukemen, clear yuzu-accented assari, self-serve rice with takana and beni-shoga and free top-ups; Retty listing gives the July 14, 2023 opening and the Catalan 'banzai' meaning of the name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water, making the broth and any shared utensils unsuitable for strict gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare/seasoning is standard ramen-shop shoyu, which is wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are pork-bone based and every bowl is cooked in the same tonkotsu kitchen with pork chashu; no vegan-safe preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter run single-handedly by an approachable owner; ordering is done in Japanese when he comes out to greet you, and a little Japanese goes a long way in this snug local spot.

noodles.notes: House offers a choice of thick or thin noodles. The default with the kotteri is a medium-thick, fairly straight noodle that clings well to the heavy broth; the thick option has a wavy, hand-cut-feeling, springy-chewy texture. Thin noodle also available.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kotteri pork-bone ramen — the ultra-thick emulsified tonkotsu the shop is built on. A clear assari version, an urume-sardine tsukemen, and a Jiro-style "Bis-ro" (mashimashi) are the alternates.
ramen.chashu: Lean, red-meat-forward pork chashu cooked more like a roast than a fatty braise, so it drinks up the heavy broth rather than melting into it.
notable_toppings: harissa chili paste at the table

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare and a pork-bone broth; nothing rice- or konjac-based is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Built entirely on a long-simmered pork-bone broth with pork chashu and a shoyu tare; no plant-based bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto kotteri specialist in the Ichijoji ramen battleground, this bowl chases pure pork-bone density — a broth simmered so far down it turns velvety and potage-thick, the kind a spoon nearly stands up in, all umami and no gaminess. It is a contemporary tonkotsu original rather than a regional copy, with a clear assari counterpart and a North-African harissa kicker that signals the kitchen's playful streak.

places.name: 麺家 澤希 (Menya Sawaki)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen here uses wheat noodles (Goto noodles for the iekei bowl) and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that almost certainly contains wheat, plus pork broth. No gluten-free noodles or menu are offered, and there is no indication of celiac-safe handling.
places.vegan_detail: All bowls are built on pork-bone (tonkotsu) or pork-clear broth with chashu and other animal toppings; no vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sat: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-23:30; Tue: 11:00-14:00; Sun: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-22:00 (one source lists 11:00-14:00, 18:00-22:00, closed Mondays). Hours subject to change.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Sawaki sits on Ichijoji's famous 'ramen street,' a stretch in northeast Kyoto packed with competing shops. It first opened in 2021 as Chuka Soba Nishiki (中華そば 錦) and was rebranded as Menya Sawaki in October 2023, making it one of the area's notable iekei (Yokohama-style) en

chef_bio.background: Menya Sawaki sits on Ichijoji's famous 'ramen street,' a stretch in northeast Kyoto packed with competing shops. It first opened in 2021 as Chuka Soba Nishiki (中華そば 錦) and was rebranded as Menya Sawaki in October 2023, making it one of the area's notable iekei (Yokohama-style) entrants in a district better known for Kyoto's heavier back-fat shoyu and assari styles.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately wide-ranging menu - iekei, Hakata, Kyoto shoyu, classic chuka soba and more - so that a single counter can satisfy very different ramen cravings, with evening service leaning toward small plates for a casual izakaya-style stop.
chef_bio.specialty: Iekei ramen: a shoyu-tonkotsu (pork-bone-and-soy) bowl described as rich yet surprisingly clean, served over thick Goto-style noodles and topped with pork chashu, spinach, nori, chopped white negi and a quail egg. A popular add-on set brings karaage and rice. The kitchen also ranges across Hakata yatai-style thin-noodle tonkotsu and a classic chuka-soba shoyu bowl.

anecdotes.text: Diners single out the karaage as unusually fluffy and soft rather than the typical crisp-shelled style, a small signature alongside the ramen.
The iekei bowl is praised as 'looks thick but tastes clean,' a balance the shop aims for in its shoyu-tonkotsu broth.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan confirms the 2021 opening as 中華そば 錦, the October 2023 rebrand to Menya Sawaki, the wide menu (iekei, Hakata, Kyoto shoyu, chuka soba and more), and the iekei bowl on Goto-made thick noodles with chashu, spinach, negi, nori and quail egg plus a karaage set; note (yk317ss) describes the soft karaage and the 'looks thick, tastes clean' iekei broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are shoyu-tonkotsu based; the soy-sauce tare used in this style of ramen standardly contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local ramen counter on Ichijoji's ramen street; little to no English, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will smooth the visit.

places.name: 煌力 (Goriki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a tantanmen specialist: wheat noodles (curly noodles from the noted maker Teikaku/棣鄂), a sesame-and-nut tare that in dandan noodles is typically built on wheat-based soy sauce and miso, and pork. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised, and the standard rice-based finish is cooked together with the same sauce and toppings. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The bowls are built around ground pork and char siu, and there is no advertised vegan or vegetarian tantanmen. The shop highlights a vegetable-forward dumpling and some vegetable-topped variations, but the broth/sauce and proteins are meat-based throughout. No plant-based option is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 / 17:30-21:00. Monday is lunch service only (no dinner). Closed for Obon (around Aug 16) and New Year holidays, plus occasional temporary closures.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku / Karasuma-Oike Stn (Nishinotōin-dōri, just south of Anekōji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Goriki opened in 2016, back when dedicated tantanmen counters were still a rarity in Kyoto, taking over a space that had previously run as a night-only spot before expanding into lunch service. It has since grown into a fixture among the city's dandan-noodle lovers.

chef_bio.background: Goriki opened in 2016, back when dedicated tantanmen counters were still a rarity in Kyoto, taking over a space that had previously run as a night-only spot before expanding into lunch service. It has since grown into a fixture among the city's dandan-noodle lovers.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for a broth that is concentrated and smooth (noko de maroyaka) rather than punishingly hot, letting the Sichuan-pepper aroma and sesame depth carry the bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is its tantanmen built on a thick yet mellow sesame-and-nut sauce, balancing the numbing-and-spicy 'karashibi' character with nutty richness rather than chasing pure heat. The lineup runs from THE Tantanmen to a brothless (shirunashi) version, dipping-style tsukemen, the chili-heavy Karashibi, and a stamina version, plus seasonal limiteds.

anecdotes.text: Goriki's signature move is the shime finish: after you have worked through most of the noodles, the staff scrape the remaining meat and toppings into a bowl and cook it down with rice and a raw egg over the table's built-in burner, turning it into a fragrant, bibimbap-style 'second dish' whose egg mellows the lingering spice.
The brothless tantanmen uses curly noodles from the well-regarded Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku (棣鄂), tossed with minced meat, char siu, bean sprouts, scallions and nuts.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms the 2016 opening, the five tantanmen varieties (THE担々麺, 汁なし, つけ麺, カラシビ, スタミナ) plus seasonal limiteds, the spice-fragrant sesame sauce, the 汁なし bowl on Teikaku (棣鄂) noodles with minced meat, char siu, bean sprouts, scallions and nuts, and the tabletop-IH-burner rice-and-raw-egg bibimbap-style shime finish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dandan-noodle tare is conventionally built on wheat-based soy sauce and miso; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built around ground pork and char siu and share a meat-based sauce; no plant-based separation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood noodle counter with no indication of English support, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will smooth the visit.

noodles.notes: Semi-translucent high-hydration thin noodles sourced from a renowned Kyoto noodle maker (Menya Teikaku); springy, snappy 'puri-puri' bite that stands up to the thick sesame soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: THE 担々麺 (THE Tantanmen) — the house standard: a thick, smooth sesame-and-nut soup with freshly ground sesame and house-made chili oil, finished with fragrant Kyoto sansho. Order the tsuke-tantanmen version (served over a tabletop IH burner that keeps the dipping soup hot) for the shop's playful twist, and add the lunch rice set to finish the leftover soup as a stone-baked bibimbap-style kuppa.
ramen.chashu: No sliced chashu — the protein is a spicy miso-seasoned minced-pork soboro stirred into the soup.

gf.note: Wheat noodles with kansui and a sesame/soy-based tantan tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The soup is built on animal stock and the bowl is topped with minced pork; no documented plant-broth, meat-free version.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tantanmen specialist where the soul is the soup: a deep, velvety river of freshly ground sesame and nuts riding a savory meat stock, balanced across umami, sweetness, salt and a gentle chili heat. What sets it apart is the bright, fruity numbing perfume of Kyoto sansho instead of dried Sichuan pepper — a distinctly local, aromatic take on the genre.

places.name: まる担 おがわ (Marutan Ogawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a tantanmen (dandan noodle) specialist. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (straight thin noodles from a noodle supplier) in a sesame/miso/pork-based spicy broth, which in this style virtually always contains wheat-based shoyu/miso tare. No gluten-free options or gluten-free preparation were found, and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or any cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-based tantanmen shop: bowls feature ground meat (挽肉) and the broth is built on a pork/sesame/miso base. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is advertised. Not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: Katsura / Kashihara (Nishikyo-ku) — a residential street nea
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Marutan Ogawa began life as a yatai-style operation, with the owner serving tantanmen from the garage/parking space of his own home in a quiet residential pocket of Katsura. A few years ago, after renovating the house, the food-cart setup was converted into a proper indoor shop o

chef_bio.background: Marutan Ogawa began life as a yatai-style operation, with the owner serving tantanmen from the garage/parking space of his own home in a quiet residential pocket of Katsura. A few years ago, after renovating the house, the food-cart setup was converted into a proper indoor shop on the same spot — but it has kept the intimate, handmade, hole-in-the-wall feel of its food-cart roots.
chef_bio.philosophy: A small, personal kitchen focused on doing one thing — tantanmen — well, in a casual, home-style setting that grew straight out of a neighborhood food cart.
chef_bio.specialty: Spicy 'uma-kara' (delicious-spicy) tantanmen. The miso tantanmen pairs a miso-and-sesame broth with ground meat, bamboo shoots, Chinese greens and bean sprouts over straight thin noodles; seasonal bowls include a chilled tantanmen served over ice and topped with kimchi and crunchy vegetables, and a brothless (shiru-nashi) version.

anecdotes.text: What started as the owner serving noodles out of his own home garage in food-cart style was turned into an indoor shop a few years ago when he renovated the house.

The shop sits in a residential neighborhood near Route 9 that you basically need a car to reach; regulars include students from the nearby Kyoto University Katsura campus, who are on friendly terms with the owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tantanmen here is made with wheat noodles in a sesame/miso/pork-based broth; this style of tare is wheat-based, so the dish is not gluten-free and no GF handling is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are meat-based (ground meat plus a pork/sesame/miso broth), so vegan diners cannot be accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood tantanmen counter on a residential street; expect a Japanese-only experience and a warm, regulars-driven atmosphere, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help.

noodles.notes: Thin, firm straight noodles supplied by the Teigaku noodle maker; they hold up against the rich, chili-oil-forward broth and tangle well with the minced-pork niku-miso. Some reviewers note a slight curl. Kaedama (noodle refills) are popular.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen (担々麺) at spice level 1 — the house standard. The umami-spicy soup is built on a fragrant, slightly bitter house-made chili oil with rich koku, dressed with niku-miso (minced pork), pak choi, bean sprouts and menma. A miso tantanmen and a vinegar-sharp sanratan (hot-and-sour) version are the main alternatives; wonton and seasonal chilled bowls also appear.
ramen.chashu: No sliced chashu — the meat is niku-miso, a savory minced-pork miso crumble spooned over the noodles in place of a chashu slice.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a miso/soy-leaning tare and minced-pork niku-miso; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The build centers on a minced-pork niku-miso and a meat/dashi-based broth; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake, no katsuo/pork) bowl is documented. Spice level is adjustable, but the soup and topping are animal-based.

ramen.pantheon: A yatai-style tantanmen worked out of a residential Nishikyo driveway, this is a Japanese-tuned dandan bowl whose soul is its house-made chili oil — fragrant, faintly bitter and built for koku rather than searing heat. With adjustable spice and a savory minced-pork niku-miso over pak choi and sprouts, it sits in the everyday, addictive-comfort lane of Japanese tantanmen rather than the numbing Sichuan or shiru-nashi schools.

places.name: 京亀 (Kyogame)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The lunch bowls are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the kitchen is built around motsunabe with chicken/garlic broths and soy-based seasonings — no gluten-free noodles, dedicated handling, or allergen accommodation is advertised. Cross-contamination with wheat is effectively guaranteed in a shared ramen and hotpot kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The house identity is beef offal (motsu) and the ramen leans on offal, clams, duck, and chicken broth; even the cabbage-heavy "hormone ramen" is built on meat/animal stock. No plant-based ramen or vegan set is offered.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). Wed & Thu dinner only (no lunch). Ramen is a lunch-only offering.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kyogame occupies a restored Kyoto machiya townhouse along Imadegawa-dori, a short walk from Demachiyanagi. The owner, known affectionately as Kame-san, runs it less like a polished restaurant and more like a friend's home — courtyard garden, tatami rooms where you can stretch you

chef_bio.background: Kyogame occupies a restored Kyoto machiya townhouse along Imadegawa-dori, a short walk from Demachiyanagi. The owner, known affectionately as Kame-san, runs it less like a polished restaurant and more like a friend's home — courtyard garden, tatami rooms where you can stretch your legs, and the unhurried feel of an old popular izakaya.
chef_bio.philosophy: Generous, unfussy, and deliberately affordable — the lunch bowls are priced so that students and regulars can come often, in a room meant to feel like dropping by someone's house rather than dining out.
chef_bio.specialty: The house centerpiece is a hearty garlic motsunabe: select beef offal in a quality chicken-bone broth, offered in white-miso, soy, or salt versions, alongside other izakaya sides. At lunch the kitchen turns to ramen — the signature being a cabbage-piled hormone (offal) ramen, alongside a light clam (asari) salt bowl and, in summer, a cold duck-and-vegetable bowl, all served with a generous 1.5 portions of noodles.

anecdotes.text: A writer who once worked at a related shop describes the owner Kame-san as encouraging and supportive of his staff's ambitions, the kind of proprietor people stay loyal to.
The cabbage-heavy hormone ramen and the affordable lunch sets are pitched as a welcome deal for students.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi and genjitsutouhi confirm a machiya motsunabe-ya on Imadegawa-dori run by 'Kame-san': garlic motsunabe (white-miso/soy/salt, ~14 pieces offal per 2-serving order), a spicy cabbage-piled hormone ramen (850 yen), clam salt and cold-duck lunch bowls with 1.5 noodle portions; official kamerin.jp confirms the garlic motsunabe in chicken broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Lunch ramen uses standard wheat noodles and shares a kitchen with motsunabe and fried sides, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable; no gluten-free option is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and seasonings are soy- and miso-based (a soy-shoyu motsunabe option is offered), which in Japan normally means wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even the vegetable-forward cabbage 'hormone ramen' is built on beef offal and animal-based broth; all advertised bowls contain meat, clams, duck, or chicken stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood machiya izakaya where ordering and any dietary questions will go most smoothly in Japanese; an English menu is not advertised.

places.name: らぁ～めん京 祇園本店 (Raamen Miyako Gion Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use the house 低温熟成細麺 (low-temperature aged thin wheat noodles), a paitan broth built on chicken and vegetables, and tare options including soy (醤油, typically wheat-based) and miso, finished with pork chashu. There is no gluten-free menu, no published allergen handling, and no indication of a dedicated gluten-free preparation area. Not safe for celiac diners; cross-contamination from shared noodle water and broth is effectively unavoidable. Confirm directly with the shop if you must, but treat as not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is a chicken-and-vegetable paitan (鶏白湯) and bowls come standard with pork chashu, egg, and other animal toppings; the broth itself is animal-based. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen offering noted on any source. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu & Sun 11:00–22:00 (last order 21:30) / Fri & Sat 11:00–03:00 next day (last order 02:30 next day). Note: hours may be temporarily changed for the time being (please confirm). No regular closing days.
places.neighborhood: Gion, Higashiyama-ku (just north of Yasaka Shrine) / ~8 min walk from Gion-Shijō Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The Gion honten is the flagship of the Raamen Miyako (らぁ～めん京) group, which has since grown to additional locations including Hyogo and Shiga, and runs a much-praised stall in Kyoto Station's Kyoto Ramen Koji (ramen alley), where it opened in June 2023.

roles: Gion flagship of a small Kyoto-based group
chef_bio.origin: Gion, Higashiyama — the main shop sits just north of Yasaka Shrine in the heart of Kyoto's old geisha district.
chef_bio.background: The Gion honten is the flagship of the Raamen Miyako (らぁ～めん京) group, which has since grown to additional locations including Hyogo and Shiga, and runs a much-praised stall in Kyoto Station's Kyoto Ramen Koji (ramen alley), which opened in June 2023.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen states it uses only carefully selected ingredients and does meticulous prep every day, slowly drawing chicken umami and vegetable sweetness into a broth that is rich yet light enough to drink to the bottom.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 'ぎをん白湯' (Gion paitan) — a chicken-and-vegetable white broth, slightly enriched with back-fat for body without heaviness, paired with the shop's own low-temperature aged thin noodles. The 'みやこらぁ～めん' tops it with a wide slice of chashu, soft egg, scallion and menma.

anecdotes.text: The broth is described as having solid body from chicken and a touch of back-fat, yet staying savory rather than heavy — 'finished enough to drink down as the close of a meal.'
The shop developed its own 'low-temperature aged thin noodles' (低温熟成細麺) prized for a chewy-yet-smooth texture that clings to the Gion paitan broth.
Its side-dish fried rice uses rice from the noted Kyoto brand Hachidaime Gihey, and the group's bowl was a top seller at Kyoto Station's ramen alley.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house wheat thin noodles boiled in shared water, with soy/miso tare and chicken-vegetable broth; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy (醤油) is one of the standard tare options; ordinary Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is animal (chicken paitan) and bowls include pork chashu and egg; no vegan option is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy walk-in ramen counter beside Yasaka Shrine in Gion; tourists are common, but a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, and it is cash-only.

noodles.notes: Signature low-temperature aged thin noodles (低温熟成細麺), developed in-house over years. Described as mochi-mochi/springy yet smooth (もちっとプリっとした歯切れの良い食感), they coat well with the emulsified Gion chicken broth. Larger portion (omori) available.

ramen.signature_bowl: Miyako Ramen (みやこらぁ～めん) — the house chicken-paitan bowl, the "Gion white broth" topped with ajitama, scallion, and menma. Gion-honten exclusive: the white-miso version blending Kyoto's traditional Saikyo white miso into the chicken paitan; an extra-rich kotteri (特濃こってり) option is also offered.
ramen.chashu: Long-braised pork chashu cut thick, chosen for a good balance of fat and lean; sweetly seasoned, very tender and meltingly soft in the mouth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu/miso tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a long-simmered chicken broth finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on tori-paitan: a creamy, emulsified chicken broth — the shop's "Gion white broth" — built on chicken and plenty of vegetables, then lifted with a touch of pork back-fat so it stays deep yet drinkable rather than heavy. It nods to Kyoto's love of rich, fatty soup while threading its own house identity, even folding the city's traditional Saikyo white miso into a signature variant.

places.name: ラーメン 麺対軒 (Ramen Mentaiken)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen and town-Chinese shop. The bowls are built on medium-thick wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare over an animal-based broth, plus fried rice and other Chinese dishes — all standard sources of gluten. Soy-sauce tare is essentially always wheat-containing, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs; anyone strictly gluten-free should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: The core menu is built on an animal-based (pork/meat) dashi broth with pork chashu toppings, and the shop is a meat-forward town-Chinese ramen place. No vegan or vegetable-broth ramen is indicated. Not a vegan-friendly option.
places.hours_raw: 11:30〜22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Sundays: Uber Eats delivery only.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mentaiken is a small neighborhood 'machi-chuka' (town-Chinese) ramen shop in central Kyoto, the kind of unpretentious local counter where regulars come for a comforting bowl. It is run essentially as a near one-person operation, with the owner-chef handling most of the cooking.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Shimogyo ward, near the Shijo-Horikawa intersection and Omiya Station)
chef_bio.background: Mentaiken is a small neighborhood 'machi-chuka' (town-Chinese) ramen shop in central Kyoto, the kind of unpretentious local counter where regulars come for a comforting bowl. It is run essentially as a near one-person operation, with the owner-chef handling most of the cooking.
chef_bio.philosophy: In-store signage sums up the aim as making 'memorable ramen that stays in your heart' — a straightforward, regulars-first approach to a satisfying everyday bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the ginger ramen (生姜ラーメン / 生姜中華そば): an animal-based broth seasoned with soy-sauce tare and finished with a generous amount of freshly grated ginger, so that a soup which looks mild actually delivers a real punch of ginger warmth. It is served with medium-thick, low-hydration straight noodles that have a chewy bite (extra-firm available on request) and sliced pork chashu with chopped green onion. The shop also serves a 'Kyoto black' deep-soy-sauce ramen, ramen-and-fried-rice sets, spicy mala noodles, and negi-chashu.

anecdotes.text: The shop expanded its set options with a 'nami-nami set' (regular ramen plus regular fried rice) so customers could finally order full-size portions of both together, rather than only the smaller combinations offered before.
The ginger ramen is described as especially popular with women, its grated-ginger soup giving a flavor punch that belies the broth's mild appearance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ramen is built on wheat noodles and a soy-sauce tare, and the kitchen also makes fried rice and Chinese dishes, so shared cookware and gluten exposure throughout are inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen, including a 'Kyoto black' deep-soy-sauce style; soy-sauce tare for ramen is essentially always wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based dashi with pork chashu; no vegan/vegetable-broth option is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locals' town-Chinese ramen counter where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the menu) is expected; no English service indicated.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick round noodles with a soft, tender surface rather than firm and chewy; the jet-black broth clings to and fully coats them. The noodles play a supporting role to the soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu ramen (醤油ラーメン) — a lacquer-black soy bowl — best taken as the famous set with the matching jet-black fried rice (yakimeshi). The clear shio ginger ramen (生姜ラーメン) is the standout alternative.
ramen.chashu: Thin slices of lean braised pork loin, minimal fat — modest, old-school town-Chinese style rather than a thick fatty centerpiece.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a wheat-based dark soy tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free options are documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with pork chashu and a meat-forward soy tare; no plant-based broth bowl is offered. A vegetable ramen exists on the menu but sits in the same animal broth.

ramen.pantheon: A 1975-founded neighborhood town-Chinese counter pouring Kyoto's signature lacquer-black shoyu ramen — the Shinpuku-lineage bowl whose soup looks like ink yet drinks gentle, the dark soy hitting first before a quiet chicken-and-pork-bone umami rises underneath. The same broth tints the legendary black fried rice that turns this into one of Kyoto's classic ramen-plus-meshi sets.

places.name: らーめん杉千代 (Ramen Sugichiyo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a classic Kyoto-style ramen shop: the noodles are wheat, the tare (seasoning) is soy-sauce based (typically wheat-containing shoyu), and the broth is a pork-bone and chicken soy-sauce base. There is no published allergen handling, no gluten-free noodle option, and no dedicated prep area. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Every bowl is built on an animal broth (pork bone / chicken bones) and topped with chashu (braised pork). The menu is limited to ramen, chashu ramen, rice and green onion add-ons; there is no plant-based broth or vegetable ramen.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:30-15:00 / 17:00-23:00; Saturdays & holidays roughly 11:30-23:00; Sunday until 22:30. Sources vary on exact midday break and on a possible Monday closure — confirm locally before a special trip.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sugichiyo is the revival of a legendary Kyoto ramen shop. The original opened in 1999 in the Uzumasa area of western Kyoto and drew daily lines for roughly a decade before it closed at the end of 2009 — reportedly because the crowds it attracted had become difficult to sustain. A

chef_bio.background: Sugichiyo is the revival of a legendary Kyoto ramen shop. The original opened in 1999 in the Uzumasa area of western Kyoto and drew daily lines for roughly a decade before it closed at the end of 2009 — reportedly because the crowds it attracted had become difficult to sustain. After about twelve years dormant, the name returned in June 2021 in a renovated spot down a Takase River alley just west of Shijo-Kawaramachi, in the heart of central Kyoto's nightlife district. The current team is said to have inherited the complete original recipe directly from the founder.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithfully carry forward a beloved recipe rather than reinvent it — a nostalgic, unpretentious bowl served with quiet, courteous hospitality instead of the high-intensity style of many modern ramen shops.
chef_bio.specialty: Chashu ramen — the bowl that built the shop's reputation. The pork-bone soy-sauce broth carries the deep richness of Kyoto-style ramen with a thin veil of back fat on top, yet drinks lighter and cleaner than its color suggests; the soy tare is comparatively restrained, letting the broth's umami lead. It comes over low-hydration straight thin noodles, finished with melt-tender homemade chashu (made by the founder's original slow-braise method), menma, nori and green onion.
