A touch of chili pepper is added by default, giving a gentle heat from the first sip.

anecdotes.text: Sugichiyo's flavor was famous enough that Nissin Foods produced it as a cup-noodle product — a rare nod from a major manufacturer.
The original shop closed in 2009 reportedly because of complaints about the long lines it generated, and was reborn in 2021 at Shijo-Kawaramachi.
The current operators say they received the full original recipe from the founder 'without any leaks,' aiming to reproduce the legendary taste exactly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare base; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and the shop publishes no gluten-free or wheat-free tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use animal broth (pork bone / chicken) and pork chashu; no separate vegetable stock or vegan prep is mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, classic counter ramen shop where ordering is straightforward but staff and menu are Japanese; a little Japanese or pointing goes a long way.

noodles.notes: Low-hydration thin straight noodles with a chewy, mochi-like bite that holds up against the clinging back-fat broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (the standard ramen topped with extra braised pork belly) — the cloudy back-fat shoyu bowl that built the shop's reputation.
ramen.chashu: Pork belly braised until meltingly tender, collapsing under chopsticks.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare make this bowl unsafe for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-and-chicken bone broth finished with pork back fat and braised pork belly — no plant-based option exists.

ramen.pantheon: A revived legend of the Kyoto se-abura tradition: a milky, pork-and-chicken bone broth seasoned with soy and dusted with melted back fat, yet drinking far lighter than its opaque white surface suggests. A default scattering of ichimi chili gives the heritage Kyoto bowl a quiet warmth.

places.name: 鶏そば223 (Torisoba 223 (Tsutsumi))
places.gf_detail: This is a salt ramen specialist built on wheat noodles (curly noodles supplied by Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku/麺屋棣鄂), so the core dish is not gluten-free. The broth is chicken-based and the seasoning is a salt (shio) tare rather than a wheat-shoyu tare, which avoids one common wheat source, but the noodles themselves contain wheat and there is no indication of a gluten-free noodle option, dedicated prep, or celiac-level cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiac diners; a wheat-allergic diner should treat it as off-limits unless they confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a chicken broth (described as roughly 99% chicken stock, using Tanba black chicken bones and Kyoto red-breed chicken) with chicken chashu and egg toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu and the entire concept is animal-based, so it does not work for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 08:00–15:00; additional evening service Sunday 18:00–21:00; closed Mondays. May close early when the day's soup stock runs out.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Torisoba 223 is a one-man salt-ramen specialist that opened in 2020 in a renovated storefront just south of the Kyoto Botanical Garden, a short walk from Kitaoji. The shop's odd name is a play on the owner's surname: 2-2-3 reads as 'tsu-tsu-mi' (Tsutsumi).

chef_bio.background: Torisoba 223 is a one-man salt-ramen specialist (seven counter seats) that opened in 2020 in a renovated storefront just south of the Kyoto Botanical Garden, a short walk from Kitaoji. The shop's odd name is a play on the owner's surname: 2-2-3 reads as 'tsu-tsu-mi' (Tsutsumi).
chef_bio.philosophy: Tsutsumi openly states his goal is to be 'No.1 in salt ramen.' He is a certified salt sommelier and blends four salts (including Andes rock salt and a sea salt) to build a smooth, layered savoriness rather than a sharp saltiness, and avoids chemical seasonings.
chef_bio.specialty: An unusually delicate, additive-free (無化調) salt ramen built on a clear chicken broth made from Tanba black-chicken bones and Kyoto red-breed chicken meat, finished with fragrant chicken oil and paired with curly wheat noodles from Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku. Bowls carry playful Italian-flavored names (e.g. 'Milano no Asahi'), and a table-side olive/special sauce lets diners shift the flavor mid-bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 223 is read 'Tsutsumi' after the owner's surname.
The owner is a certified salt sommelier who blends four kinds of salt (including Andes rock salt) to design the broth's seasoning.
It opens early at 8:00 for ramen-as-breakfast and is treated as a morning-ramen spot in Kyoto.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are seasoned with a salt (shio) tare rather than a soy-sauce tare, so wheat soy sauce is not the primary seasoning; however this is not a substitute for being gluten-free because the noodles are wheat.

positives.text: No chemical seasonings are used (無化調); the broth is a clear chicken stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 7-seat counter shop run by one owner where Japanese is the working language; basic Japanese or a translation app helps, and ordering is straightforward once seated.

noodles.notes: Thin, low-hydration noodles with a slightly crimped/hand-kneaded wave, made by Kyoto noodle-maker Teigaku (棣鄂). They have a clean bite on arrival and soften gracefully as they drink up the chicken broth; good slurp and throat-feel.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-shio soba — the clear chicken salt ramen. Order "ミラノの朝陽 (Milano no Asahi)" for the cleaner, lighter version, or "フィレンツェの夕日 (Firenze no Yuhi)" for the deeper, richer koku bowl. Both are the same chicken-shio soul at different intensities.
ramen.chashu: Not pork — the protein is sliced tori-hamu (low-temperature chicken-breast ham), salt-cured and seasoned with herbs/spice in a French-leaning style. Tender, moist, gently pink, served as a few slices.
notable_toppings: house olive sauce condiment (clam, basil, shallot, olive oil, black pepper) for mid-bowl flavor change

gf.note: Wheat-kansui ramen noodles from a noodle-maker; not celiac-safe. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented. The shio tare itself is salt-based, but the noodles rule it out.

vegan.note: Broth is essentially 99% chicken/jidori and chicken-feet stock, and the protein is chicken ham. No plant-only broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto chicken-shio specialist that breaks from the city's usual se-abura shoyu lineage: the soul here is a painstaking clear jidori-and-chicken-feet broth, collagen-silky yet clean, seasoned by a salt tare its chef honed studying French cuisine (a three-salt blend of white, pink and black). The French-Italian flourishes — chicken-breast ham, a clam-and-basil olive sauce, a knob of butter — make it a refined modern tori-soba rather than any regional classic.

places.name: 麺屋 しな埜 (Menya Shinano)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not suitable for celiacs. This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles served in a chicken paitan or fish-soy broth finished with soy-sauce-based tare (Japanese soy sauce / white soy sauce, both wheat-containing). Tsukemen (dipping noodles) is also wheat. No gluten-free menu, no documented allergen accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. The signature bowls are built on a chicken-based broth (whole chicken and chicken bones), with chashu, flavored egg, and other animal toppings; the fish-soy ramen uses fish-flake dashi. Nothing on the menu is plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Wednesday and Sunday evenings closed (calendar-dependent). Lunch served daily.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺屋 しな埜 began life in February 2020 as TETSUpan Style+ しな埜, a yakisoba specialty shop, before reinventing itself as a ramen-focused restaurant in March 2021 with chicken paitan as its new signature. A further full renovation in September 2024 refreshed the menu and the space. It s

chef_bio.background: 麺屋 しな埜 began life in February 2020 as TETSUpan Style+ しな埜, a yakisoba specialty shop, before reinventing itself as a ramen-focused restaurant in March 2021 with chicken paitan as its new signature. A further full renovation in September 2024 refreshed the menu and the space. It sits, almost unexpectedly, in a quiet residential pocket of Matsugasaki near the ward office, drawing queues to a converted white single-family house with sunlit counter seating and a breezy balcony area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than the heavier bowls Kyoto is often known for, the shop leans on technique and restraint, using the whipped-foam broth and carefully drawn dashi to keep flavors smooth and the finish clean.
chef_bio.specialty: The house bowl is the 泡白湯 (awa paitan): a broth simmered from whole chicken and chicken bones, then whipped into a creamy, mousse-like foam and finished with a Japanese-style soy tare for a surprisingly clean, light aftertaste. The kitchen also offers a chicken paitan, a fish-soy (gyokai-shoyu) ramen built on a low-temperature dashi of three fish flakes, kombu and dried shiitake with chicken oil, and a rich fish-pork tsukemen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a renovated white house with second-floor counter seating and a beach-house-style balcony, and it has become a quiet favorite among neighbors and nearby university students who line up despite the out-of-the-way residential location.

Visitors note the broth's creamy, foamed texture is achieved with an espuma-style whipping technique, accented with coarse black pepper, giving a lighter, less-salty profile than typical for the city.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi and osumituki confirm the timeline (yakisoba TETSUpan Style+ しな埜 opened 16 Feb 2020, ramen pivot 22 Mar 2021, full renovation 1 Sept 2024), the Matsugasaki white-house/balcony setting, the 泡白湯 whipped-foam chicken broth with Japanese soy tare, a three-fish/kombu/shiitake gyokai-shoyu, and a pork-fish tsukemen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broths are finished with soy-sauce-based tare (Japanese soy sauce / white soy sauce), which is wheat-containing, and the noodles are wheat ramen noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: A small residential-neighborhood ramen shop with friendly, attentive staff; ordering is straightforward but expect a Japanese-only experience, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles cooked slightly firm (yaya-katame) to cut through the aerated chicken broth; some bowls use a medium-thick wavy noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ajitama Awa-hakutou ramen (foam chicken-paitan with seasoned egg) — the aerated, cappuccino-creamy chicken paitan finished with a Japanese-style soy/umami edge.
ramen.chashu: Rare, low-temperature-cooked pork (shoulder loin), sliced thin — two slices laid over the bowl rather than a fatty braised or char-grilled cut.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-based tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is a long-simmered chicken-bone-and-whole-chicken paitan with chicken-fat aroma and pork chashu; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan that whips its slow-simmered chicken-bone-and-whole-chicken broth into a cappuccino-like foam, so the soup lands creamy and feather-light on the tongue rather than heavy. Its soul is the twist of finishing that rich white broth with a Japanese soy-and-umami edge, giving a clean, refined aftertaste that sets it apart from the dense, gravy-thick tori-paitan that made the city famous.

places.name: 中華そば アッキー (Chuka Soba Akky)
places.gf_detail: This is a pork-broth chuka soba (ramen) shop built around wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, both of which contain wheat. The signature bowls are a back-fat "black" soy ramen and a back-fat tonkotsu-shoyu ramen. There is no published gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no statement about gluten handling. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot safely eat the core menu here.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's entire identity is a 100% pork broth ("豚100％のスープ"), with back fat, chashu pork, and pork-bone soy bases across every bowl. There are no vegetarian or vegan ramen options on the published menu and no plant-based broth offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon 10:30–14:30; Tue closed; Wed–Fri 10:30–14:30 and 17:00–20:30; Sat 10:30–14:30; Sun 08:00–14:30. Note: evening service is suspended for the time being, so currently lunch-only daytime hours apply.
places.neighborhood: Shimogamo, Sakyō-ku (Matsugasaki Stn / Shimogamo-Honmachi bus stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Akky is a Kyoto native who became well known across the city's ramen scene as a food creator, running the YouTube channel 'Mr.京都アッキーのぶらり飲食店巡り' along with TikTok, Instagram and X, where he tirelessly reviewed Kyoto ramen shops. Before opening this place he spent about two years ru

chef_bio.chef_name: Mr.京都アッキー (Mr. Kyoto Akky)
chef_bio.background: Akky is a Kyoto native who became well known across the city's ramen scene as a food creator, running the YouTube channel 'Mr.京都アッキーのぶらり飲食店巡り' along with TikTok, Instagram and X, where he tirelessly reviewed Kyoto ramen shops. Before opening this place he spent about two years running a ramen shop on borrowed premises in Fushimi, learning the craft hands-on, before launching his own standalone shop in Shimogamo in August 2025.
chef_bio.philosophy: Akky builds everything on a 100% pork broth simmered carefully from early morning, drawing out the depth of pork bone and pork meat while keeping the finish clean rather than heavy. He describes the goal as a soup that is rich yet sharp ('コクがありながらもキレのある'), an everyday bowl that people of all ages can enjoy.
chef_bio.specialty: His signature is the 'Triple GO' back-fat black chuka soba (トリプルGO 背脂ブラック中華そば) - a dark soy-and-back-fat bowl in the classic Kyoto 'black' style - alongside a back-fat tonkotsu-shoyu chuka soba.

anecdotes.text: His shop is a personal homage to the three Kyoto ramen styles he loves most, blending the black broth of Shinpukusaikan, the pork-bone style of Dai-ichi Asahi, and the back-fat-forward approach of Masutani into one bowl of what he himself most wants to eat.
Among Kyoto ramen fans he is described as someone 'there isn't a single ramen lover in Kyoto who doesn't know' - a familiar face who turned his fandom into his own shop.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, including a dark-soy 'black' version; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth is a 100% pork soup with back fat and pork toppings, so there is unavoidable animal content throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood ramen shop run by a local owner; expect Japanese-only service and a quick, casual lunch flow, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin (chuuboso) straight noodles supplied by a Kyoto noodle maker, with springy bite and smooth slip. The mazesoba bowl instead uses thick, flat, chewy crimped noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Seabura Black Chuka Soba (back-fat black soy ramen) — a 100% pork broth with a dark, deep tamari-shoyu tare and a generous slick of back fat; the lighter Seabura Tonkotsu Shoyu is the southern-Kyoto-style sibling.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly (sanmai-niku), tender and juicy, cut in large pieces to play up pork umami and aroma.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based tamari shoyu tare over a pork broth — not celiac-safe, and no rice/konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on a 100% pork broth with pork back fat and pork-belly chashu; no plant-based broth option is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl lives in the Kyoto se-abura shoyu tradition: a 100% pork broth kept clear and clean-finishing rather than cloudy, married to a dark, deep tamari-soy tare and crowned with a melt of back fat. You taste southern-Kyoto lineage in the soup — soy sharpness cut by sweet pork fat, rich but never heavy.

places.name: あんびしゃす花 (Ambitious Hana)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a tsukemen specialist: the signature noodles are wheat-based (with tapioca flour blended in, but still wheat), and the rich pork-and-fish (豚骨魚介) dipping broth is built on wheat-containing shoyu/miso tare — the spicy "辛醤つけめん" is miso-based. There is no mention anywhere of gluten-free options, a separate fryer, or any celiac accommodation. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core product is a thick pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) dipping broth, and add-ons include raw egg; the menu centers on animal-based tsukemen and ramen with no plant-based broth or noodle option mentioned. No vegan offering is advertised.
places.neighborhood: Shimogamo-Nishihonmachi (Kitayama Stn / Rakuhoku-Kōkō-mae bus stop area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent tsukemen specialist tucked into the Shimogamo neighborhood of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, just west of where Shimogamo-hondori meets Kitaoji. It has built a loyal following among nearby students and locals as a destination for serious, rich dipping noodles.

chef_bio.background: A small, independent tsukemen specialist tucked into the Shimogamo neighborhood of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, just west of where Shimogamo-hondori meets Kitaoji. It has built a loyal following among nearby students and locals as a destination for serious, rich dipping noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: Generous and unfussy — large portions (up to 400g) are offered at the same price as a regular serving, and diners customize noodle thickness and garlic to taste.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a thick, rich pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) tsukemen with a pronounced seafood depth, served with chewy noodles made by blending tapioca flour into the dough. Its calling card is the dipping broth served bubbling in a clay/stone pot (土鍋・石鍋): the pot is brought to a boil and its heat retention keeps the tsukejiru piping hot from the first bite to the last.

anecdotes.text: The defining feature is the screaming-hot dipping broth: it is brought to the table in a clay/stone pot kept at a rolling boil, so the tsukejiru stays piping hot (アツアツ) right to the end of the meal instead of cooling off the way ordinary tsukemen broth does.

The noodles are made with tapioca flour worked into the dough for a distinctive chew, and the broth is a thick pork-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) style; portions run up to 400g at the same price, making it a favorite of the area's students.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth is a thick pork-bone-and-seafood tare-based tsukejiru, and the spicy version is miso-based (辛醤つけめん); these standard ramen/tsukemen seasonings contain wheat-based soy/miso.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, ticket-machine ramen counter popular with local students; minimal English and a fast-paced lunch flow, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the machine) goes a long way.

noodles.notes: Signature extra-thick (gokubuto) noodles with strong bite, often likened to Sanuki udon; a thick or medium-thick option is offered, and a 400g large portion is the same price as regular. Some accounts mention tapioca-blended noodles for extra chew.

ramen.signature_bowl: Koku-uma noukou tsukemen (rich concentrated dipping noodles) — the thick seafood-and-pork dipping broth served in a hot stone pot kept actively simmering at the table so it never cools, paired with the shop's signature extra-thick noodles. Finish with a soup-wari (dashi dilution).
ramen.chashu: Large, thick slices braised until melt-in-the-mouth tender, kakuni-style (resembling braised pork belly) with a deep, sweet-savory flavor.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen/tsukemen noodles and shoyu-based broth; no documented rice or konjac noodle or GF tare option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork/chicken bones and seafood (gyokai) dashi with braised pork chashu; no documented plant-based broth option.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tsukemen specialist in the rich tonkotsu-gyokai (pork-and-seafood double-soup) lineage that swept Japan in the 2000s, but with its own signature theatrics: the concentrated dipping broth arrives in a hot stone pot, simmering at your table so the last mouthful is as scorching as the first. Pair that intense, slightly sweet soup with the shop's udon-thick noodles and you taste a bowl built for the heavy-hitter crowd rather than the delicate sea-abura shoyu Kyoto is usually known for.
sources: https://muchi2.com/2018/08/25/京都で話題のつけ麺店で食べる/

places.name: タンポポ (Tanpopo)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto pork-and-chicken-bone soy ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat noodles with a soy-sauce tare and a pork/chicken-bone broth enriched with pork back-fat, so it is not celiac-safe. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, a gluten-free tare, or any allergen accommodation, and the kitchen is a single shared ramen line. A celiac diner should treat this as off-limits; even the signature chili topping and the soy-and-pepper fried rice involve wheat-based seasoning.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on a long-simmered pork-bone and chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat, and the bowls come with pork chashu and menma; the fried rice is cooked with pork and egg-style seasoning. There is no plant-based broth or vegan option here. A vegan diner has nothing to eat at this shop. The chili topping can be omitted on request, but that does not change the animal-based broth.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, Murasakino / Kinugasa (Senbon-Kitaōji, Kitayama-dōri / toward Kinkaku-ji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tanpopo is a Kyoto ramen institution out near the western end of Kitayama-dori, in the Murasakino/Kinugasa pocket toward Kinkaku-ji. Sources describe it as a shop loved by Kyoto residents for around 70 years, with a founding commonly cited as 1947, making it one of the old-guard

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, in the Kita-ku Murasakino area near Bukkyo University and Kitayama-dori.
chef_bio.background: Tanpopo is a Kyoto ramen institution out near the western end of Kitayama-dori, in the Murasakino/Kinugasa pocket toward Kinkaku-ji. Sources describe it as a shop loved by Kyoto residents for around 70 years, with a founding commonly cited as 1947, making it one of the old-guard shops that carry the history of Kyoto-style ramen into the present.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deceptively dramatic, comforting bowl: the chili makes a strong first impression, but the flavor underneath is the deep, fatty, soy-rich savoriness of classic Kyoto ramen. The shop leans into a warm, unpretentious neighborhood feel rather than polish.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a Kyoto back-fat soy-sauce ramen famous for a topping of red chili spread across the entire surface, so the bowl arrives looking fiery red. The broth is a rich pork-bone soup simmered for many hours and finished with sweet pork back-fat; the chili sits on top rather than being cooked into the soup, so the actual heat is mild-to-moderate rather than punishing. Medium noodles, pork-belly chashu that turns crumbly-tender, green onion and menma round it out. The other house favorite is an old-fashioned, hearty fried rice (yakimeshi) with pork and onion, popular as a ramen-and-fried-rice set.

anecdotes.text: The bowl is famous for being blanketed in red chili so it looks intensely spicy, but because the chili is sprinkled on top rather than mixed into the broth it is only mildly spicy, and you can order it with the chili left off entirely while the soup stays delicious on its own.

Described as loved by Kyoto people for about 70 years and as a legend shop conveying the history of Kyoto ramen, with a retro exterior, humorous handwritten signs around the room, and a regular crowd of locals rather than tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are cooked in shared boiling water on a single ramen line, so any bowl carries gluten and cross-contact risk; no gluten-free preparation is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop is a Kyoto soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen specialist; the tare is a standard soy-based seasoning, which in this style is wheat-containing soy sauce, with no gluten-free alternative noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is built on a multi-hour pork-bone and chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and topped with pork chashu, so the cooking line is fundamentally animal-based with no separate vegan preparation.

positives.text: The signature chili topping can be omitted on request, since it is sprinkled on top rather than cooked into the broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, cash-only neighborhood ramen counter run for locals with no English service, so come prepared to point at the menu and keep it simple.

noodles.notes: Smooth, slurpable straight noodles; descriptions range from slightly thin to medium, cooked on the softer side to soak up the back-fat shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen blanketed with the shop's signature red togarashi (chili) powder over a pork-and-soy broth thick with floating back-fat — order it as the classic bowl, optionally with a side of yakimeshi (fried rice). Chashu-men for those who want extra pork.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, simmered until it crumbles apart at the touch of chopsticks, releasing a sweet, rich pork fattiness.
notable_toppings: red togarashi (chili) powder blanketing the surface

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles with a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare and a pork broth; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Pork-bone broth, soy tare, pork back-fat and braised pork-belly chashu are central to every bowl; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Kyoto's se-abura shoyu lineage: a clean pork-and-soy soup made indulgent by a thick raft of melted back-fat, its gentle shoyu carrying that mellow porky sweetness rather than fighting it. The shop's signature flourish — a fiery-looking cap of red togarashi powder that tastes far milder than it appears — has made this long-running neighborhood bowl a Kyoto institution.

places.name: ラーメン ゆう (Ramen Yu)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto-style soy-sauce ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a pork-driven broth — none of which is gluten-free, and there is no indication of any GF accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs. The only other listed item is gyoza (wheat wrappers). Assume cross-contamination throughout a single shared kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is limited to shoyu and miso ramen built on a pork-based (旭系) broth with chashu pork, plus gyoza. There are no vegan or vegetarian options and no plant-based broth. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–19:00. Closed Mondays and Thursdays. (One listing notes possible irregular Sunday closures; confirm before a Sunday visit.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, time-worn ramen counter tucked into the Shichijo-Omiya block near Umekoji Park and Umekoji-Kyoto-Nishi Station. It belongs to Kyoto's beloved 'Akira-kei' (旭系) lineage — the nostalgic Kyoto soy-sauce ramen tradition associated with the famous Daiichi-Asahi. The owner is s

chef_bio.origin: 京都市下京区 七条大宮・梅小路
chef_bio.background: A small, time-worn ramen counter tucked into the Shichijo-Omiya block near Umekoji Park and Umekoji-Kyoto-Nishi Station. It belongs to Kyoto's beloved 'Akira-kei' (旭系) lineage — the nostalgic Kyoto soy-sauce ramen tradition associated with the famous Daiichi-Asahi. The owner is said to have trained at a shop called Taiki before opening his own counter, and the place has the lived-in, slightly aged storefront of a long-standing local fixture.
chef_bio.philosophy: A taciturn owner with a quiet, single-minded devotion to ramen — no frills, no trend-chasing, just an honest bowl in the old Kyoto idiom. Regulars describe the flavor as deeply nostalgic, evoking long-gone neighborhood shops.
chef_bio.specialty: Kyoto-style shoyu ramen — a clear, pork-forward broth where the pig's umami and richness are balanced against a restrained soy edge, served over soft medium-thick straight noodles that soak up the broth, finished with loin chashu, a generous pile of scallions and bean sprouts. A miso version and a notably good spicy miso are also offered.

anecdotes.text: Regulars often pitch it as the under-the-radar alternative to the famous station-side Daiichi-Asahi: its broth is described as sitting between the intensity of Daiichi-Asahi and Ganso Ramen Taiee, refreshing yet rich.
The clientele skews to local regulars, salarymen, and older customers rather than a young crowd — a true neighborhood ramen stop.

One Kyoto food guide notes the bowl tastes remarkably close to a now-defunct beloved shop ('Big Ramen') near the Higashino (東野) intersection in Yamashina, and singles out the abundant loin chashu and excellent spicy miso.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: priceless-kyoto confirms the 旭系 lineage, the owner having trained at 'Taiki', a 12-seat counter of regulars/salarymen/older locals, and a broth judged between Daiichi-Asahi and Ganso Ramen Taiee; kyoto-gourmet.info likens the taste to a defunct shop ('Big Ramen') near the Higashino intersection in Yamashina and praises the loin chashu and spicy miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small counter kitchen built entirely around wheat ramen noodles, wheat-based shoyu tare, and gyoza; no separation possible and no GF handling mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining flavor is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in a traditional Kyoto ramen shop is wheat-based; assume the broth/tare contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-based with chashu pork; no plant-based broth or vegan options, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school neighborhood counter shop frequented by taxi drivers, locals, and older regulars; a little basic Japanese and easygoing ramen-shop etiquette will go a long way, and English support should not be expected.

noodles.notes: Medium, mostly straight noodles with a firm, springy 'patsu-patsu' bite that drink up the salty soy broth; some sources describe them as thinner. Slight curl noted in places.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu chuka soba (soy-sauce ramen), upgraded to the chashu-men for the extra belly slices
ramen.chashu: Tender, melt-in-the-mouth braised pork belly, several slices per bowl, lightly salted to play against the salty broth; some bowls feature leaner loin cuts

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat soy-sauce tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bones and chicken with pork-fat richness and pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic neighborhood bowl in the Kyoto soy-sauce lineage: pork bones and chicken simmered into a deep, savory, lightly fatty broth seasoned with a punchy shoyu tare, the kind of unpretentious chuka soba locals have leaned on for decades. Its soul is the balance of porky umami and assertive soy salt, slicked with rendered fat and crowned by famously tender melt-away chashu.

places.name: 京一 本店 (Kyoichi Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Showa-era Chinese-noodle diner. The signature dishes are wheat-noodle bowls — curry chuka soba and chuka soba — built on a chicken-and-secret-ingredient broth with curry roux (curry roux is itself wheat-thickened) plus, typically, wheat-based seasonings. There is no indication of any gluten-free accommodation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners; assume wheat throughout.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-broth-centered old-style shokudo: the broth is chicken-bone based, the curry chuka is finished with braised wagyu beef, and other mains include katsudon. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered, and there is no sign of plant-based broth or accommodation. The dessert menu (zenzai, parfaits) is a legacy of the shop's origins as a sweet-bean (zenzai) shop but is not a vegan offering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–20:00. Closed Thu, and the 3rd & 4th Wed.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku, Shijō-Ōmiya (right by Hankyū Ōmiya Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1948 (Showa 23) at Shijo-Omiya, Kyoichi Honten has anchored the same corner for over 75 years. The shop began life as a zenzai (sweet red-bean) shop before becoming the bustling everyday diner it is today; the current building was rebuilt in 1976 but keeps its retro, t

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1948 (Showa 23) at Shijo-Omiya, Kyoichi Honten has anchored the same corner for over 75 years. The shop began life as a zenzai (sweet red-bean) shop before becoming the bustling everyday diner it is today; the current building was rebuilt in 1976 but keeps its retro, time-capsule feel. It is run by the Miyazaki family, with a hands-on female proprietor who happily steers customers toward her favorite pairings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's long-standing motto is 'umai, hayai, yasui' — delicious, fast, and cheap — the creed of a true neighborhood diner serving locals, laborers and students alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Curry chuka soba (カレー中華) — Chinese wheat noodles buried under a glossy, spicy-sweet curry an (sauce) built from a long-simmered broth, multiple curry rouxs and secret seasonings, finished with braised wagyu beef for depth. The sauce is famously so generous it hides the noodles, and regulars drink it to the last drop. The clearer chicken-bone chuka soba is the other house favorite.

anecdotes.text: The shop's origins as a zenzai (sweet-bean) house live on in its unusually rich dessert lineup — zenzai and several parfaits — and the proprietor likes to suggest a cream dessert as the perfect cool-down after the spicy curry chuka.

The first-generation owner has remarked that along this stretch almost nothing has changed over the decades except themselves, the 'Hattori' coffee shop and the 'Yaoiso' fruit stand — a snapshot of how rooted the place is in the Shijo-Omiya streetscape.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat ramen noodles and a wheat-thickened curry roux sauce; gluten is unavoidable across the menu and no gluten-free handling is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone based and the signature curry chuka is finished with braised wagyu beef; the kitchen is built around animal stocks and meat, with no plant-based option noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A genuine local Showa-era diner where regulars, workers and students drop in — friendly and casual, but expect a Japanese-only ordering experience and no special accommodation, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way.

noodles.notes: Smooth-textured (tsurun) house noodles that slip easily; the curry-chukasoba uses custom-made thinner noodles chosen to cling to the thick curry-an while resisting sogginess.

ramen.signature_bowl: Curry Chukasoba (カレー中華) — house ramen noodles drowned in a thick, dashi-rich Japanese-style curry an, gently spiced and sweetened by braised wagyu beef. The plain Chukasoba (中華そば) is the classic clear-soup alternative.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork-belly chashu (about three pieces) in the standard chukasoba.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; the curry an is also flour-thickened. No rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The clear soup is built on chicken bones blended with udon dashi (katsuo/kombu) and dressed with pork-belly chashu and fishcake; the curry version is sweetened with braised wagyu beef. No plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A survivor of postwar Kyoto's neighborhood shokudo culture, this is honest machi-chuka 中華そば: a clean, amber chicken-gara soup softened with the kombu-and-katsuo dashi of an udon shop, finished with a gentle shoyu tare. Its famous curry-chukasoba buries those same noodles under a thick, dashi-forward Japanese curry an — comfort food rather than a specialist's bowl.

places.name: 廣東餐館 鳳飛 (Kanton Sankan Houhi)
places.gf_detail: The headline dishes here are wheat-noodle plates — からしそば (mustard noodles) and 中華そば — served with house-made wheat noodles, and the kitchen leans on soy-based seasonings. Spring rolls, shumai, gyoza and fried noodles are all wheat-based as well. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation, so this is not a safe choice for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-seafood Chinese kitchen: the からしそば is built on chicken and shrimp, signature plates include からし鶏 (fried chicken), juicy pork shumai, and 酢豚 (sweet-and-sour pork), and broths/stir-fries rely on animal stock. No vegan menu or dedicated plant-based options are advertised; a vegan diner would have very little to eat.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-13:30, 17:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:50). Closed Tuesday and Wednesday; additional closures on the first and third Sunday-through-Wednesday of each month.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 鳳飛 is part of Kyoto's celebrated 鳳舞系 — the lineage of Chinese cooks who trained under master 高華吉 at the legendary 鳳舞, which opened in 1967 and closed, much mourned, in 2009. 高 is credited with inventing 京町中華: he reworked Chinese cooking for Kyoto palates and the kimono-clad clien

roles: keeper of the 鳳舞系 からしそば tradition
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (北区 紫野, on Horikawa near Kitaoji)
chef_bio.background: 鳳飛 is part of Kyoto's celebrated 鳳舞系 — the lineage of Chinese cooks who trained under master 高華吉, a Cantonese immigrant, at the legendary 鳳舞, which opened in 1967 and closed, much mourned, in 2009. 高 is remembered as a pioneer of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking who reworked Chinese food for local palates into something light, restrained and broth-forward — chicken- and kombu-based, with the oil and aggressive aromatics pulled back. Disciples who struck out on their own carried the 鳳 (phoenix) character of 鳳舞 into their own shop names; 鳳飛 is regarded as a direct heir of that tradition. (The lineage is sometimes traced further back through 高's earlier restaurants 第一樓 and 飛雲, and ultimately the Taisho-era 支那料理ハマムラ.)
chef_bio.philosophy: Gentle, unshowy, deeply Kyoto: minimal oil, no aggressive aromatics, prices kept low enough for neighbors to eat here regularly — Chinese food rebuilt as everyday comfort food rather than a feast.
chef_bio.specialty: からしそば — a noodle dish found essentially only at 鳳舞系 shops: boiled Chinese noodles tossed with mustard and bound in a lightly sweet, glossy chicken-and-kombu sauce, topped with chicken, shrimp, vegetables and scallion. The version here is known to run notably spicier than at sibling shops such as 鳳泉. The kitchen is also loved for its bamboo-shoot-packed spring rolls, shumai, からし鶏 (mustard-spiced fried chicken), and a light, never-greasy fried rice.

anecdotes.text: You order by writing the dish numbers on a paper slip; regulars line up before opening to claim the first seating in the short lunch window (roughly 12:00–13:30).
Diners describe the cooking as gentle and tender — light, lightly seasoned plates whose flavor deepens bite after bite, in keeping with the restrained Kyoto-Chinese style.
からしそば is a Kyoto noodle dish that exists essentially only within the 鳳舞 lineage of shops, of which 鳳飛 is a direct heir.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Brutus confirms からしそば exists only in Kyoto's 鳳舞系, whose 鳳舞 was founded in 1967 by Cantonese immigrant 高華吉; syu-mai.com traces the lineage (支那料理ハマムラ→第一樓/飛雲→鳳舞, closed 2009). The otaku-son blog and sakura394 confirm 鳳飛's Kitaoji-Horikawa location, paper-slip ordering, the ~90-minute lunch, its からしそば running spicier than 鳳泉's, and its gentle, light cooking as a direct 鳳舞 heir.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kyoto-Chinese noodle and stir-fry dishes here are seasoned with soy-based sauces, which in Japan are standard wheat-containing shoyu; assume gluten is present throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature plates are chicken-, pork- and shrimp-based (からし鶏, pork shumai, 酢豚, and the chicken-and-shrimp からしそば), so animal ingredients run through the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-reservations neighborhood counter where you order by writing dish numbers on a slip of paper; little to no English, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will smooth the visit.

noodles.notes: Thin, supple noodles chosen to cling to the thickened gravy of the karashisoba; in the plain chuka-soba they sit soft and pliable in a clear chicken-kombu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashisoba (からしそば / 撈麺) — thin noodles under a glossy chicken-and-kombu ankake gravy loaded with pork, small shrimp, bamboo shoot and pepper, finished with a sharp lift of Japanese mustard (karashi).
ramen.chashu: The karashisoba carries sliced pork simmered into the gravy rather than a sit-on-top chashu; the gomoku ramen adds yakibuta-style roasted pork slices. No aburi or smoked loin — restrained Kyoto-Chinese pork.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles and a soy-based seasoning; the karashisoba gravy is also thickened with wheat-adjacent starch and built on a shared kitchen — no documented rice/konjac-noodle or GF option.

vegan.note: The broth and gravy are built on chicken bones (with kombu) and the noodle dishes carry pork and shrimp; no documented plant-only bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A direct descendant of the fabled Houmai school of Kyoto town-Chinese, this kitchen speaks in the gentle, dashi-forward dialect of 京中華 — chicken bones quietly married to kombu, almost no garlic, very little surface oil. Its soul is the karashisoba: a clear-souled chicken-kombu lineage thickened into a silky ankake and lit by a clean stripe of yellow mustard, restraint that lets every shrimp, pork slice and bamboo shoot taste of itself.

places.name: なぶら (Nabura)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen and tsukemen built on wheat noodles, and the mackerel karaage topping (tatsuta-age) is wheat-floured and fried; tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is built on a mackerel-and-kombu fish dashi (鯖と昆布の出汁) with a fried-mackerel topping, so the broth and signature dishes are fish-based and not vegan. The shop notably avoids animal/pork products and chemical seasonings (無化調), but it is a fish-broth specialist, not a vegan one.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30), Tuesday-Sunday & holidays; closed Mondays (shifted to the following day if Monday is a holiday).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nabura was opened on 8 August 2022 by a chef who spent about 35 years cooking traditional Kyoto-style Japanese cuisine (kappo/washoku) before turning that craft to ramen, in the Kitano Tenmangu / Kamishichiken area of Kamigyo-ku.

chef_bio.origin: The owner is a keen fishing enthusiast, and the shop name 'Nabura' refers to a school of small fish leaping at the sea surface as they are chased by larger fish - a fisherman's sign of opportunity and good fortune. The name was suggested by a fishing-loving staff member, and the staff wear Nabura T-shirts as a maritime good-luck symbol.
chef_bio.background: Nabura was opened on 8 August 2022 by a chef who spent about 35 years cooking traditional Kyoto-style Japanese cuisine (kappo/washoku) before turning that craft to ramen, in the Kitano Tenmangu / Kamishichiken area of Kamigyo-ku.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop deliberately keeps salt low and the broth clean and digestible so that anyone, regardless of age or gender, can eat it as an everyday meal - the owner says you can finish all the soup and still feel light afterward (誰でも美味しく食べられる).
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a completely additive-free (無化調) ramen whose broth is drawn only from kombu (kelp) and saba (mackerel) - no fish powder, no chemical seasoning - finished with a piece of fried mackerel tatsuta-age floating in the soup. Tsukemen is also a mainstay, including a rich seafood dipping broth.

anecdotes.text: The owner condensed 35 years of experience as a Kyoto Japanese-cuisine chef into this ramen, building the entire broth from just kombu and mackerel with no chemical seasonings.

The name 'Nabura' was chosen by a fishing-loving staff member after the term for a school of small fish breaking the surface while chased by bigger fish - an auspicious sign at sea - and staff wear Nabura T-shirts to carry that good-luck symbolism.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Leaf Kyoto confirms Nabura opened 8 Aug 2022 in the Kitano Tenmangu/Kamishichiken area of Kamigyo-ku; that its owner condensed ~35 years as a Kyoto washoku chef into an additive-free (無化調), deliberately low-salt broth drawn only from kombu and mackerel and finished with fried mackerel tatsuta-age; and that the name refers to a school of surfacing fish, suggested by a fishing-loving staffer, with staff wearing Nabura T-shirts.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles are the base of every bowl and the kitchen handles wheat flour for the mackerel tatsuta-age topping, so flour and gluten are present throughout the cooking surfaces.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are fish-based (mackerel and kombu dashi); there is no vegan broth, so no vegan bowl is possible even setting cross-contact aside.

positives.text: Broth is completely additive-free (無化調) using only kombu and mackerel, with no chemical seasonings, fish powder, or animal/pork products - and the kitchen keeps salt low for a lighter, cleaner bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only neighborhood counter ramen shop run by a veteran Japanese-cuisine chef; no reservations and likely Japanese-only service, so a guide or translation help is useful for ordering and asking about ingredients.

noodles.notes: Flat, crimped thick noodles (ramen) sourced from an established Kyoto noodle maker; the tsukemen uses a chewy whole-wheat flat noodle. Built to grip the dense saba broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Saba ramen (鯖ラーメン) — the thick, condensed mackerel-and-kombu broth crowned with saba tatsuta-age
ramen.chashu: No pork chashu; the protein centerpiece is saba tatsuta-age (lightly battered, fried marinated mackerel) set atop the bowl in place of a meat slice.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen and tsukemen noodles, plus a battered fried-mackerel topping (tatsuta-age uses flour), make this unsafe for celiacs. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire identity of the bowl is a mackerel (saba) and kombu dashi, and the signature topping is fried fish. Despite using no pork or land-animal stock, it is fundamentally fish-based and not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original built by a 35-year washoku chef: instead of pork or chicken, the soul here is a single fish — saba — simmered with kombu into a dense, potage-thick broth with no chemical seasoning, no fish powder, and no animal stock at all. You taste pure mackerel umami rounded by kelp, a clean-yet-rich bowl that stands apart from Kyoto's usual se-abura shoyu lineage.

places.name: 麺ビストロ Nakano (Men Bistro Nakano)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The ramen is built on wheat noodles, and the shoyu-based tare is almost certainly wheat-containing soy sauce; broths and creative dishes are not allergen-controlled. No celiac-safe accommodation is indicated on any source. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. Core bowls use chicken broth, dashi (bonito/fish-based 節), shoyu tare, and dairy-rich potage soups (cream-based), plus meat dishes (pork, chicken) and seafood-leaning items. No dedicated plant-based ramen is offered. A vegan could not reliably eat the signature dishes.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sun & holidays 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 18:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Mondays. (Some sources list lunch starting 12:00.)
places.notes: Men Bistro Nakano is run by a chef who came up through French kitchens and now pours that training into a bowl of noodles. The shop took over the space of a former French restaurant, and it kept that room's calm, grown-up feel rather than turning it into a typical narrow ramen co

chef_bio.origin: The space was previously a French restaurant, and it was opened by someone who had run a French establishment — so the bistro atmosphere is not a gimmick but the literal history of the room.
chef_bio.background: Men Bistro Nakano is run by a chef who came up through French kitchens and now pours that training into a bowl of noodles. The shop took over the space of a former French restaurant, and it kept that room's calm, grown-up feel rather than turning it into a typical narrow ramen counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is a genuine fusion of French, Japanese, and ramen — treating a bowl of ramen with the same care, plating, and finish as a bistro plate. You can pair it with a glass of wine, start with small plates like ratatouille or roasted chicken, and end with a little dessert, which is almost unheard of at a ramen shop.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen builds broths that nod to Japanese cooking — using katsuobushi-style dashi (節) — and finishes them with French herbs and technique. The white and black shoyu bowls anchor the regular menu alongside a tripe-and-tomato ramen and a spicy mazesoba, while seasonal creamed 'potage' noodles (corn, new-onion, edamame, spinach, vichyssoise) rotate through and have become the shop's signature draw. The flavored soft-boiled egg is singled out by regulars as one of the best they've had.

anecdotes.text: Water is served in wine bottles, and the room is so elegant that one writer joked it could go back to being a French or Western restaurant tomorrow — it is notably welcoming even for a solo diner, unusual for a ramen shop.
It is described as a rare 'ramen shop where you can drink wine,' with glasses from around 500 yen and sparkling by the glass, in a space far roomier than a classic ramen counter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (menbistronakano.net) states the shop is run by a former French chef offering a fusion of French, Japanese and ramen, with seasonal potage noodles, à la carte plates, wine and dessert. kyoto-gourmet confirms the former-French-restaurant space, water served in wine bottles, a solo-friendly elegant room, the black/white shoyu, tripe-tomato ramen and mazesoba, and singles out the soft-boiled egg. kyotopi confirms wine by the glass (~500 yen, sparkling ~800) and the roomy, non-counter layout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls are white and black shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen, and shoyu tare in Japan is virtually always made with wheat-containing soy sauce; treat the tare as a wheat source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths lean on chicken stock and Japanese fish dashi (節), and several signature noodles are cream/dairy-based potages, so vegan cross-contact is inherent to the kitchen, not just incidental.

positives.text: Table-only seating in a calm, bistro-style room with small plates and wine makes for an easy, unhurried meal, though the menu itself is not built around dietary restrictions.

cultural_comfort.note: An unusually relaxed, refined ramen room with table seating and an open, bistro-like feel, so a foreign visitor can settle in comfortably; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way.

noodles.notes: Flat, high-hydration wavy noodles with a soft, mochi-like chew that drink up the light broth; pale rather than the usual yellow kansui color. The black-shoyu bowl reads a touch more medium-thick and springy. One source described thin noodles, but most accounts point to flatter medium strands.

ramen.signature_bowl: 白しょうゆ麺 (White Shoyu Men) — a clear, refined French-style chicken-and-seafood broth seasoned with pale white tamari shoyu
ramen.chashu: Light chicken chashu — gentle and low-fat, tuned to sit under the delicate white broth rather than dominate it. The retro black-shoyu bowl instead uses an old-school grilled pork (yakibuta) style.

gf.note: Chinese-style wheat-and-kansui noodles throughout, and the shoyu tare is wheat-based. No rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on French chicken fond (fonds de volaille) plus fish/seafood stock and bushi dashi, with chicken or pork chashu. No plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented. The seasonal creamy potage noodles still use dairy and a chicken-stock base.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto creation from a French-trained chef who builds his soup like a classical kitchen — a clear chicken fond de volaille married to Japanese bushi dashi, then seasoned with pale white tamari shoyu so the bowl drinks like an elegant consommé rather than a ramen. You taste the French-Japanese lineage in the soup: refined, herb-touched, and a world away from Kyoto's usual back-fat shoyu.

places.name: 真打 英多朗 (Shinuchi Eitaro)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core menu is wheat: the signature yuzu ramen uses wheat noodles (a low-hydration, low-kansui country-style noodle developed with a noodle maker), and the shop is also a hand-pulled Sanuki udon house, so wheat flour is heavily used throughout the kitchen. The tare/broth is built on soy sauce infused with the udon-shop dashi, and ordinary Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and high airborne-flour and shared-water cross-contamination risk from on-site noodle making. Anyone with celiac disease should avoid; a wheat-allergic diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan/vegetarian-friendly. The signature broth is a 7-hour chicken-bone stock combined with katsuo (bonito) first dashi and Hokkaido kombu, and the yuzu ramen is finished with pork back fat (背脂). Soy-sauce tare carries fish/animal dashi as well. Evening menu centers on wagyu shabu-shabu and udon-sukiyaki hot pots. No advertised plant-based bowl. A vegan would need to ask, and realistically there is no safe option.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat & holidays: 11:30–14:00 (last order 14:00), 17:30–21:00 (last order 21:00). Closed Sun (with occasional special opening days).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Eitaro began roughly four decades ago as a Kyoto noodle shop and built its name first as a hand-pulled Sanuki udon specialist before its owner — a devoted ramen lover — folded a second craft into the kitchen. After years at Shijo-Karasuma where it drew a loyal following and media

chef_bio.background: Eitaro began roughly four decades ago as a Kyoto noodle shop and built its name first as a hand-made Sanuki udon specialist before its owner — a devoted ramen lover — folded a second craft into the kitchen. After around forty years at Shijo-Karasuma where it drew a loyal following and media attention, the shop closed in June 2024, and reopened in November 2024 just steps from Shijo-Omiya Station, bringing back the bowl people kept asking for.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cook so every guest leaves thinking mata tabetai — I want to eat that again. The owner spent years tasting ramen across Kyoto and reportedly three years persuading a noodle maker to produce his low-kansui straight noodle before he was satisfied enough to put the bowl on the menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Yuzu ramen — the shop's signature bowl. The broth is a 7-hour chicken-bone stock layered with katsuo (bonito) first dashi and Hokkaido kombu, seasoned with a soy tare carried over from the udon side of the house, enriched with pork back fat, and finished with a generous squeeze of fresh yuzu so the citrus cuts through the richness. Alongside it the shop still serves hand-made Sanuki udon and, in the evening, hot-pot courses including wagyu shabu-shabu and udon-sukiyaki.

anecdotes.text: The shop closed in June 2024 and only came back because customers kept saying mata ano aji o tabetai — they wanted that flavor again — which pushed the owner to reopen near Shijo-Omiya in November 2024.

The signature yuzu ramen was born from the owner's own obsession: as a ramen lover running an udon shop, he spent years researching ramen around Kyoto before merging his udon dashi expertise with a ramen of his own.
Over its decades in business the shop became known as a spot frequented by entertainers and celebrities.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-eitaro.com (official) confirms a ~40-year Kyoto udon/ramen shop whose owner walked Kyoto eating ramen before creating a yuzu ramen using multiple bonito types and Hokkaido kombu, with daytime noodles and evening hot-pot/izakaya service and celebrity patronage. Tabelog Magazine confirms the June 2024 close and Nov 2024 reopen near Shijo-Omiya by popular demand, the 7-hour chicken broth with bonito dashi, back fat and fresh yuzu, and ~3 years spent getting a maker to produce the reduced-kansui straight noodle. kyotopi confirms the prior 四条烏丸 (Shijo-Karasuma) location.

gf_cross_contamination.text: On-site hand-pulling of Sanuki udon means heavy wheat flour use in the kitchen, with airborne flour and shared boiling water creating cross-contamination risk for any wheat-sensitive diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen tare is built on a soy sauce infused with the shop's udon dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the seasoning base is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone stock plus bonito (katsuo) dashi and kombu, and the yuzu ramen is finished with pork back fat; evening menu centers on wagyu shabu-shabu and sukiyaki, so animal products are pervasive.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, long-running neighborhood noodle shop where a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; English support is not guaranteed.

noodles.notes: Straight thin noodles made from domestic wheat with deliberately low kansui content, custom-made by a noodle maker the shop courted for years; clean finish with good grip on the broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Yuzu Ramen (ゆずラーメン) — a refined chicken-and-bonito shoyu bowl finished with abundant back-fat and a generous squeeze of fresh yuzu.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork shoulder loin (kata-rosu) so tender it crumbles when lifted with chopsticks; sourced from a noted Kyoto butcher.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; this is a noodle house (also serving udon), so no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken bones and bonito first-dashi, with pork back-fat and braised pork chashu throughout; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: Born from a veteran udon master's own dashi craft, this bowl marries a seven-hour chicken-bone stock with bonito first-dashi and a soy tare, then lights it up with fresh yuzu — its soul is the Kyoto se-abura shoyu lineage softened and brightened, where sweet pork back-fat and citrus acidity meet over a clear, fragrant soup. It is less a regional copy than a Kyoto original: the depth of a noodle shop's broth wearing a coat of yuzu.

places.name: 亜喜英 (Akihide)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles and an ultra-thick pork-bone (tonkotsu/kotteri) broth with a tare that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, options, or accommodation are advertised, and the single small kitchen makes cross-contact unavoidable. Celiac diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The shop is a specialist in extremely rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth and tops its ramen with chunks of charred pork chashu; the entire concept is animal-based. No plant-based ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: Basically closed; opens on irregular, unannounced days. When open, dinner service is often around 18:00-20:00 with last order around 23:00. Opening days/times are posted on Twitter/X roughly 30 minutes beforehand (@eigyoakihide for lunch, @eigyoakihide_2 for dinner).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Akihide is a cult ramen shop tucked along Ichijoji's famous Ramen Street in Sakyo-ku, northeast Kyoto. It is one of the area's most talked-about bowls precisely because it is so hard to catch: the shop is closed by default and opens only on sporadic days that the owner announces

chef_bio.background: Akihide is a cult ramen shop tucked along Ichijoji's famous Ramen Street in Sakyo-ku, northeast Kyoto. It is one of the area's most talked-about bowls precisely because it is so hard to catch: the shop is closed by default and opens only on sporadic days that the owner announces on Twitter/X, often just before the doors open. With a single L-shaped counter of nine seats, no phone, no reservations, no parking, and cash-only service, it has the feel of a hidden, owner-run obsession rather than a conventional restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop bills itself as a specialty house for the 'rich-broth tribe' (濃厚民族専門店) — an unapologetic pursuit of maximum richness and intensity over conventional ramen balance, made for diners who want the heaviest, most concentrated bowl possible.
chef_bio.specialty: An ultra-thick kotteri tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen so concentrated that a ladle is said to stand upright in the soup. The broth has the consistency of a sauce rather than a soup, paired with notably thick, chewy noodles and chunks of soft, fragrantly charred chashu pork that crumble when eaten. The intensity and texture sit somewhere between ramen and tsukemen.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps no regular hours at all — it is 'basically closed,' and the only way to know it is open is to follow its Twitter/X accounts, which post the operating day (lunch via @eigyoakihide, dinner via @eigyoakihide_2), sometimes roughly half an hour before opening. On the days it does open it draws lines.
The broth is described as so dense that it 'stands a ladle upright,' resembling a thick sauce more than a soup — the shop pitches itself directly to the 'rich-broth tribe.'
A visitor tracked the shop's Twitter to catch an open day during a rainy spell and ate the kotteri bowl topped with two chunks of tender roasted pork and a large sheet of nori.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms Akihide is a 9-seat L-shaped-counter Ichijoji shop that is closed by default and opens only on sporadic days announced on X ~30 minutes ahead (lunch @eigyoakihide, dinner @eigyoakihide_2), with no phone, reservations or parking and cash only; it bills itself a 濃厚民族専門店 serving an ultra-thick kotteri tonkotsu 'so viscous the spoon stands upright,' with thick chewy noodles and soft charred chashu. kyoto-kanko corroborates the tracking-the-Twitter visit and toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat Japanese-only counter shop with no fixed hours; you'll need to track its Twitter/X in Japanese to know when it's open, so it suits diners comfortable navigating a Japanese-language local ramen experience.

noodles.notes: Substantial chewy (mochi-mochi) extra-thick straight noodles, around 180g standard, built to stand up to and grab the spoon-coatingly thick broth; some accounts say it eats almost like tsukemen. A thinner noodle is also mentioned at times.

ramen.signature_bowl: Jun-nōkō ramen (the single ultra-rich house bowl) — choose your soup density from mellow (maroyaka) up to dorodoro (sludge-thick), and optionally add garlic or a hit of yuzu fragrance.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds in one bowl: a hanging-roasted/char-grilled (tsurushi-yaki, aburi) pork with fragrant charred edges, plus a braised (nibuta) pork — both thick-cut and falling-apart tender.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu-leaning tare over a long-simmered pork/chicken broth — no rice, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire identity is a pork-bone and chicken-bone broth with pork chashu and pork fat — no plant-based broth or bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto temple of gokunō (ultra-thick) kotteri, where pork and chicken bones are boiled down until a spoon stands upright in the bowl — broth so concentrated it reads more like a sauce than a soup. This is not old-school Kyoto se-abura shoyu but a modern obsession with sheer density, the lineage you taste being pure rendered bone reduced to its richest possible form.

places.name: ラーメン あかつき (Ramen Akatsuki)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional back-fat shoyu ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is essentially always brewed with wheat, over a pork-bone and chicken broth. There is no published gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and shared boiling water for the noodles. Not suitable for celiacs; a wheat-sensitive diner should treat this place as off-limits.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's identity is meat- and fish-based: a pork-bone and chicken broth finished with a Japanese-dashi soy tare, topped with thick hand-cut/braised char siu pork, plus sides like gyoza and karaage. There is no published vegan or vegetarian ramen, and even a plain bowl rests on an animal broth. Not a vegan-friendly option.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–03:00 next day (last order 02:45 next day). Open daily.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Akatsuki is a long-running neighborhood ramen-ya in Kyoto's Kita-Shirakawa quarter of Sakyo Ward, a stone's throw from the Kitashirakawa-kōmae city-bus stop and within walking distance of Mototanaka Station. Reports describe it as approaching its 30th year in business — a g

chef_bio.background: Ramen Akatsuki is a long-running neighborhood ramen-ya in Kyoto's Kita-Shirakawa quarter of Sakyo Ward, a stone's throw from the Kitashirakawa-kōmae city-bus stop and within walking distance of Mototanaka Station. Reports describe it as approaching its 30th year in business — a genuine local fixture rather than a trend shop. It runs from late morning straight through to around 3:00 a.m. every day, making it a dependable late-night bowl for students and locals alike.
chef_bio.philosophy: A straightforward, satisfying bowl that lets a clean pork-and-chicken broth and generous back-fat do the talking — comfort-food Kyoto ramen built for everyday, all-hours eating.
chef_bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu ramen built on a long-simmered pork-and-chicken broth finished with a Japanese-dashi soy tare and crowned with a scattering of sweet back-fat. Despite the rich-looking, glistening surface, the soup drinks surprisingly clean and light, with no heavy off-notes. The thick, tender braised char siu is treated as a star in its own right, and regulars are pointed toward extra char siu or the loaded 'Akatsuki Special.'

anecdotes.text: Despite the rich, oily-looking back-fat surface, the broth is described as drinking considerably lighter than it looks, with no distinctive smell or off-flavor.
Beyond the standard bowl, the menu stretches to a loaded 'Akatsuki Special,' a spicy version, and a fish-based ramen, plus sides like gyoza and karaage and set meals.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms Akatsuki is a nearly-30-year Kita-Shirakawa (Sakyo) shop open 11:30–3:00 daily with parking, serving a back-fat pork-and-chicken shoyu ramen that drinks lighter than its glistening surface, with thin straight noodles, melt-in-mouth chashu, and variants including the Akatsuki Special, a spicy version and a fish-based bowl plus gyoza/karaage; Tabelog corroborates the location and menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water, and the broth is finished with a soy-sauce tare that is essentially always wheat-based — pervasive gluten with no separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a shoyu (soy sauce) ramen finished with a Japanese-dashi soy tare; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base soup is a pork-bone and chicken broth with back-fat and braised pork char siu; there is no animal-free preparation, so all bowls carry animal product.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills neighborhood counter ramen-ya open into the small hours; little to no English, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will smooth the visit.

noodles.notes: Special-order thin, straight noodles with firm chew (koshi); aged. A slim noodle that carries the back-fat-glossed soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu ramen (or the loaded "Akatsuki Special") — the standard pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu bowl crowned with extra back-fat and sliced chashu; sizes run from kids/chibi up to jumbo.
ramen.chashu: Tender braised pork laid over the bowl — described as fatty, melt-in-the-mouth slices fanned out like flower petals; sources vary between loin and belly, both braised rather than grilled.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a soy-sauce (wheat) tare over a pork/chicken broth — no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is a pork-bone and chicken broth finished with pork back-fat and braised pork chashu — no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood institution of Kyoto's signature se-abura shoyu lineage: a pork-bone-and-chicken soy broth, simmered overnight and rested a full day, then glossed with sweet pork back-fat that pools on the surface yet drinks far lighter than its creamy look suggests. The clean koku of that soup over slim straight noodles is the taste of old Kita-Shirakawa.

places.name: 特製ラーメン 大中 本店 (Tokusei Ramen Daichu Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles (straight-cut noodles from a noodle maker) served in a pork-and-chicken-bone broth seasoned with a shoyu (soy sauce) tare, which is virtually always wheat-based. Recommended toppings include garlic chips and gyoza (wheat dumplings) are on the menu. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, tare, or any allergen accommodation. Treat as off-limits if you must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire identity of the shop is its rich animal broth — large quantities of pork bones (tonkotsu) and chicken bones simmered continuously for 24 hours. Bowls come with chashu (pork) and a soft-boiled egg is a topping; the broth itself is meat-based with no plant-based option mentioned. There is no vegetable or vegan ramen on offer.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–24:00 (last order 23:30). Open daily year-round.
places.neighborhood: Fushimi-ku, Momoyama (right by Kintetsu Momoyama-Goryō-mae Stn, under the Fushimi-Momoyama railway viaduct)
places.notes: A Fushimi institution tucked beneath the elevated railway tracks right outside Kintetsu Momoyamagoryomae Station, Daichu has been serving its rich tonkotsu since opening in 1995. The narrow, corridor-shaped space — counter up front, tables behind — keeps a nostalgic Showa-era atm

chef_bio.background: A Fushimi institution tucked beneath the elevated railway tracks right outside Kintetsu Momoyamagoryomae Station, Daichu has been serving its rich tonkotsu since opening in 1995. The narrow, corridor-shaped space — counter up front, tables behind — keeps a nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere, marked from the street by a yellow signboard and a big red lantern. In a district full of izakaya, it runs from late morning until around 2:00 a.m. daily, drawing a steady stream of locals from lunch through late night.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen prides itself on a flavor 'you can't get at a chain' — breaking the bones in-house and simmering large batches of pork and chicken bones for a full 24 hours rather than reconstituting a pre-made base. The shop even posts daily plaques naming that day's noodle-maker and broth-maker, a small signal of how seriously it takes the craft behind each bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: The house bowl is the 大中ラーメン, a 濃厚豚骨醤油 — an intensely rich pork-bone-and-chicken broth seasoned with soy tare, thick enough that it is said a chopstick can stand upright in it, yet finishing with umami rather than heaviness. There is also a 特濃 (extra-rich) version for those who want it even more concentrated. The shop is generous with free customization: noodle firmness, broth richness, extra bean sprouts and green onion, soft-boiled egg, and kimchi, with burnt-garlic oil and garlic chips offered to round out the flavor. Chashu comes in different cuts (shoulder, belly, loin), and a lunch-set adds a side bowl of fried rice, karaage, gyoza, or chashu-don.

anecdotes.text: The broth is famously viscous — described as thick enough to stand a chopstick in — and ranks among Kyoto's most intensely rich kotteri ramen.

Set beneath the railway viaduct with a retro Showa feel, the shop is especially evocative in the evening; the noodles are straight-cut to grab the concentrated broth, and garlic chips are recommended 'to complete the flavor.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: palplan (official) states Daichu breaks bones in-house and simmers large batches of pork and chicken bones 24 hours daily to pursue a flavor 'unavailable at chains,' with all toppings free; v-trip confirms the 1995 founding, the 24-hour tonkotsu simmered to a potage, and the displayed name plaques for the day's noodle- and soup-makers. kyotopi confirms the under-viaduct location, yellow signboard/red lantern, chopstick-standing 濃厚豚骨醤油, 特濃 version, chashu cuts and lunch sets; goriluckey confirms the Showa setting, straight-cut noodles and garlic chips.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a pork/chicken broth with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; gyoza (wheat) and garlic chips are on the menu. No gluten-free option or allergen handling indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is 濃厚豚骨醤油 (rich pork-bone soy sauce), meaning a wheat-based soy tare is integral to the bowl.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made by simmering pork bones and chicken bones for 24 hours; there is no plant-based broth or vegan bowl available.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, no-reservations local ramen joint where a few polite Japanese words and pointing at the customizable order sheet will carry you through; staff are used to a steady all-hours crowd rather than overseas guests.

noodles.notes: Custom-milled slightly-thin straight noodles (from a local noodle maker) built to resist softening and to grip the heavy broth. Firmness is one of the free customization choices.

ramen.signature_bowl: Daichu Ramen — the rich pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu tonkotsu; the "Tokuno" extra-concentrated version is the move for devoted kotteri fans, with thin straight noodles, a selectable pork cut, and free add-ons (bean sprouts, scallion, egg, kimchi).
ramen.chashu: Braised Kyushu pork with selectable cuts — melt-on-the-tongue belly (bara), shoulder-loin (kata-rosu), and lean loin (rosu); relatively lean, soaking up the sauce-thick broth. Diners can choose or combine cuts.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is pork bone plus chicken frames simmered 24 hours, with pork chashu and an optional fish-pork variant. No plant-based broth bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyushu-style tonkotsu transplanted to a Fushimi railway underpass: pork bones and chicken frames are cracked in-house and boiled hard for a full day into a white, potage-thick broth so dense a chopstick can stand in it, then seasoned with shoyu for a soy-tonkotsu soul. It drinks rich and clinging yet finishes surprisingly clean — among the most kotteri bowls in all of Kyoto.

places.name: 元祖熟成細麺 香来 壬生本店 (Ganso Jukusei Hosomen Kourai Mibu Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen shop. The signature bowls are built on wheat noodles (their custom aged thin noodles), a soy-sauce-based tare (typically brewed soy sauce contains wheat), and a chicken-and-pork bone broth simmered for hours. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tamari tare, or allergen handling. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here. No dedicated gluten-free preparation exists.
places.vegan_detail: All core bowls are animal-based: the broth is chicken-bone and pork-bone simmered for about 12 hours with back fat, and bowls are topped with chashu pork. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option indicated. Even the lighter white-shoyu bowl uses Kyoto vegetables but is built on the same animal broth and finished with bonito flakes. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:00-03:00 (next day); Wed closed. Note: from June 2026, open on public holidays and closed the following day instead.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kouri is a well-known purveyor of nostalgic Kyoto-style chuka soba in the lively Shijo-Omiya / Mibu quarter, a dense pocket of affordable, beloved eateries near Nijo Castle and a roughly five-minute walk from Hankyu Omiya station. The Mibu shop is the flagship (honten) of the bra

chef_bio.background: Kourai is a well-known purveyor of nostalgic Kyoto-style chuka soba in the lively Shijo-Omiya / Mibu quarter, a dense pocket of affordable, beloved eateries near Nijo Castle and a roughly five-minute walk from Hankyu Omiya station. The Mibu shop is the flagship (honten) of the brand.
chef_bio.philosophy: A devotion to the aged thin-noodle style the shop is named for (熟成細麺, 'aged thin noodle'), pairing custom noodles with a long-simmered broth that balances vegetable sweetness, soy richness and back-fat depth.
chef_bio.specialty: Their signature is the classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen: custom-ordered aged thin noodles with a springy, clean-cutting bite, in a soy-sauce broth made from carefully prepped chicken and pork bones and aromatic vegetables simmered about 12 hours, finished with back fat. It is typically crowned with tender, melt-in-the-mouth chashu.

anecdotes.text: Beyond the classic bowl, the shop has introduced contrasting shoyu variations: a dark 'black' soy ramen with concentrated, robust broth, thicker chashu and menma, and a refined 'white' soy ramen with a clearer broth, Kyoto vegetables and bonito flakes.
The kitchen leans on custom-ordered aged thin noodles prized for a springy texture and clean bite that carries the broth well.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms Kourai in Mibu/Shijo-Omiya (~5 min from Hankyu Omiya) serves a Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu chuka soba on house aged thin noodles (熟成細麺), with a broth of carefully prepped chicken and pork bones plus aromatic vegetables simmered about 12 hours and topped with melt-in-mouth chashu; it also lists a robust dark 'black' soy ramen (thicker chashu, menma) and a refined 'white' soy ramen with Kyoto vegetables and bonito flakes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; brewed Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat, and no wheat-free/tamari alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared broth is chicken-bone and pork-bone based and bowls are topped with chashu pork; there is no separate vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, casual neighborhood ramen counter open very late; comfortable for solo diners, but expect a Japanese-only menu and minimal English, so come ready to point and order simply.

noodles.notes: The shop's namesake: jukusei hosomen, thin noodles aged about four days for a firm, glossy bite with a clean throat-feel. Other bowls (Korai-men, miso tsukemen) use flat or thick noodles, but the flagship shoyu bowl is built on these aged thin straight noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (the standard chuka-soba): a chicken-and-pork shoyu broth simmered around twelve hours with aromatic vegetables, finished with a scattering of pork back-fat (se-abura chatcha), poured over the house aged thin noodles.
