ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork loin chashu; on the chashu-men it is fanned petal-like around the rim in the old Kyoto presentation, and a richer toro-niku (fatty cut) version is offered as a limited topping.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui aged noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is chicken and pork bone simmered with vegetables, the tare is shoyu, and back-fat and chashu are pervasive. No plant-only bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's beloved se-abura shoyu lineage in the round, descended from the old Takaraya/Daio school: a deep chicken-and-pork soy soup blanketed with floating pork back-fat for a sweet, clinging richness over an otherwise clear base. The house twist is the aged thin noodle that gives the bowl a brisk, glossy bite where most Kyoto back-fat shops lean heavier and chewier.

places.name: 中華そば笑麺 (Chuka Soba Shomen)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles in a chicken-bone broth finished with back fat and an aged soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no indication staff accommodate gluten avoidance. Anyone with celiac disease should not eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The signature and essentially only style is a chicken-bone broth enriched with pork back fat and topped with braised pork chashu, so the broth, fat and toppings are all animal-derived and there is no plant-based ramen on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–21:00 (last order 20:30). Closed Mon.
places.neighborhood: Horikawa shopping street / Kamigyō-ku (Horikawa-dōri, Shimodachiuri–Marutamachi; Marutamachi Subway Stn / toward Imadegawa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Open since 1993, Shomen is a small, well-worn ramen counter tucked into the Horikawa Shopping Street on the west side of Horikawa-dori, a short walk toward Marutamachi and Imadegawa. It belongs to the Kyoto back-fat shoyu tradition that traces back to the famous Masutani style —

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan — a neighborhood町中華 (town-Chinese / local ramen) shop serving the surrounding residents and after-work salarymen.
chef_bio.background: Open since 1993, Shomen is a small, well-worn ramen counter tucked into the Horikawa Shopping Street on the west side of Horikawa-dori, a short walk toward Marutamachi and Imadegawa. It belongs to the Kyoto back-fat shoyu tradition that traces back to the famous Masutani style — a lineage some regulars argue Shomen executes even better than the original.
chef_bio.philosophy: An almost obsessive devotion to the back fat — the fat is sourced from Kyushu — so that the rich, collagen-laced sheen on the surface is the defining character of the bowl rather than an afterthought.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka soba: thin, straight noodles in a chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) broth finished with a glistening layer of pork back fat (背脂) and an aged soy-sauce tare, topped with chashu braised until meltingly tender (とろとろ) and firm menma. A bowl runs about ¥650; the ramen-and-fried-rice set about ¥850.

anecdotes.text: A long-time reviewer rates Shomen as the high point of the Kyoto Masutani-style lineage, saying its devotion to the back fat surpasses even the original Masutani shop.
First-time visitors are served the noodles on the softer side by default; regulars who like them firmer just ask for harder noodles when ordering.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Horikawa Shotengai page confirms Shomen's signature chicken-bone back-fat shoyu ramen. A detailed Tabelog review confirms it opened in 1993, works in the ますたに (Masutani) back-fat lineage (which the reviewer rates above the original Masutani), has its back fat direct-shipped from Kyushu, defaults first-timers to softer noodles (firmer on request), and prices a bowl ~¥650 and a ramen-fried-rice set ~¥850, with tender chashu and firm menma.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small ramen kitchen built around wheat noodles boiled in shared water; no gluten-free protocol or separate prep is indicated, so cross-contact is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with an aged soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, making the seasoning a wheat source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone based, enriched with pork back fat and served with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or toppings exist, so there is no vegan path on the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local Kyoto ramen counter where ordering is straightforward but everything is in Japanese and the welcome is brisk and old-school; a little Japanese or pointing at the menu goes a long way.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight low-hydration noodles built to carry the back-fat-laden aged soy soup; described as a bowl you never tire of even daily.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (chuka soba) — chicken-bone soy ramen crowned with melted back fat; upgrade to the 笑麺 / chashu-men for extra pork and ajitama.
ramen.chashu: Melt-in-your-mouth braised pork chashu, tender and softly stewed rather than char-grilled.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based aged soy tare; no rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soup is built on chicken bones with pork back fat and braised pork chashu; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook bowl of Kyoto's beloved se-abura shoyu lineage: a chicken-bone broth darkened with aged soy sauce and blanketed in glistening, collagen-rich pork back fat. The fat reads rich to the eye yet the soup underneath stays clean and moreish — exactly the kind of nostalgic, never-tiring Kyoto noodle the neighborhood has leaned on since 1993.

places.name: 麺屋 一麺専心 (Menya Ichimen Senshin)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop; the noodles are wheat, the shoyu (soy sauce) tare almost certainly contains wheat, and the kitchen boils flour-based noodles throughout. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no published allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is animal-based: the signature bowl is a super-concentrated tonkotsu made from roughly 1kg of pork bones plus water per bowl, and the other mainstay is a chicken paitan. Even the complimentary warmed vegetables share a meat-broth kitchen.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-23:00 (Thursday lunch only, no dinner service on Thursdays)
places.neighborhood: Kamigyō-ku, Senbon-Marutamachi (northwest of Nijō Castle) / ~11 min walk from JR Nijō Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A newcomer to Kyoto's ramen scene, Menya Ichimen Senshin opened in March 2024 in a roomy former eatery space in Kamigyo Ward, a short walk northwest toward Nijo Castle. The name carries the spirit of devoting oneself wholeheartedly to a single bowl of noodles.

chef_bio.background: A newcomer to Kyoto's ramen scene, Menya Ichimen Senshin opened on 9 March 2024 in a roomy former eatery space in Kamigyo Ward, a short walk northwest toward Nijo Castle. The name carries the spirit of devoting oneself wholeheartedly to a single bowl of noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: Single-minded focus on the broth: the tonkotsu is built from bones and water alone, with no extra additives, letting pure pork umami carry the bowl. Diners are also offered free extras on request such as garlic, onions, and a side of warmed vegetables.
chef_bio.specialty: The headline bowl is a super-concentrated tonkotsu ramen made from nothing but pork bones and water, with roughly 1kg of bones per bowl, yielding a thick, viscous, deeply porky broth with restrained sweetness. Alongside it is a chicken paitan (tori-paitan) and a special shoyu ramen priced at just 700 yen.

anecdotes.text: The signature ultra-rich tonkotsu is made with bones and water only, using about 1kg of pork bones per bowl for a thick, high-viscosity soup.
A 700-yen special shoyu ramen is called out as exceptional value.
Warmed vegetables are served alongside the ramen, an unusual touch for a ramen shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: noodlers confirms the ultra-rich tonkotsu made from only pork bones and water; kyo-soku (2024/03/29) confirms the 9 March 2024 opening in Senbon-Marutamachi and the ~1kg-of-bones-per-bowl broth alongside a tori-paitan; osumituki and retty confirm the bones-and-water tonkotsu, the 700-yen special shoyu and the free garlic/onion plus warmed-vegetable extras.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free offering; wheat noodles are boiled throughout the kitchen, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu includes a special shoyu (soy sauce) ramen; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are animal-based, a pork-bone tonkotsu and a chicken paitan, and even the complimentary warmed vegetables come out of a meat-broth kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally focused independent ramen shop with no sign of English support, so a little Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick, high-hydration wavy noodles from the Kyoto maker Teigaku (Teikaku); chewy and slurpable, built to drag up and cling to the thick broth, with throat-feel prioritized over bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ultra-rich tonkotsu ramen (超濃厚豚骨らーめん) — pork bone and water only, around 1 kg of bone per bowl; the chicken paitan (鶏白湯) is the lighter, collagen-rich second pillar.
ramen.chashu: Tender braised pork belly, richly fatty so the fat melts juicily in the mouth; the tonkotsu bowl is piled generously, with one thick slice plus several thin slices.
notable_toppings: side plate of warm vegetables (cabbage, bean sprouts, carrot, onion) to add as you like

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare; nothing rice- or konjac-noodle based is offered, so this is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on long-simmered pork bone or chicken broth with pork belly chashu and a shoyu tare; no plant-only broth is offered. The complimentary warm vegetable plate is the only animal-free element.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto bone-broth specialist that does the rare double act of an ultra-thick pork-bone soup — boiled down from roughly a kilo of bone per bowl until it turns gluey and viscous, in the spirit of Kyoto's famously dense kotteri lineage — alongside a smoother, collagen-rich chicken paitan. The pork tonkotsu, not Hakata-thin but heavy and emulsified, is the bowl that defines the shop's soul.

places.name: らーめん 遊貯 (Ramen Yucho)
places.gf_detail: Specialist ginger soy-sauce ramen shop. The single menu item is built on wheat noodles (from Menya Teikoku) in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is normally wheat-based, over a fish/dashi broth (horse mackerel, bonito, mackerel). No gluten-free noodle or tare option and no indication of allergen accommodation, so not safe for celiacs. Anyone strictly avoiding gluten should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: The bowl is fundamentally a fish/dashi-based broth (horse mackerel, mackerel and bonito stock) topped with chashu pork and roast beef. Not vegetarian or vegan, and there are no plant-based options on the menu, which serves only the ginger soy-sauce ramen.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:30 daily (sources note irregular closures rather than a fixed weekly day off)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Yucho opened on 18 February 2025 on the second floor of the Iidaya Building, a short walk from Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station in the busy Shijo-Kawaramachi quarter. Styled as a tucked-away hidden-gem shop with a clean, modern Japanese interior and a U-shaped counter, it set out

chef_bio.background: Ramen Yucho opened on 18 February 2025 on the second floor of the Iidaya Building, a short walk from Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station in the busy Shijo-Kawaramachi quarter. Styled as a tucked-away hidden-gem shop with a clean, modern Japanese interior and a U-shaped counter, it set out to do one thing well in a city where ginger-forward ramen is genuinely uncommon.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focused, single-bowl shop: pour everything into one warming, refined ginger soy-sauce ramen rather than a long menu, with broth and ginger meant to leave you physically warmed from the inside.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature and essentially only dish is the Kyoto ginger soy-sauce ramen. A crystal-clear seafood broth is drawn from three kinds of fish dashi (horse mackerel, mackerel and bonito), then driven by ginger both dissolved in the soup and laid on top as fresh needle-cut strips. It is served with thin, supple straight noodles from the Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teikoku, and topped with chashu pork, roast beef, naruto, Kujo green onion, menma and spinach. Diners choose Single, Double or Triple by the number of chashu and roast-beef slices, and a side of ginger dashi rice is also offered.

anecdotes.text: The shop leans into the rarity of ginger ramen in Kyoto, and the broth's pronounced ginger is widely described as leaving diners warmed up and toasty from the inside out.
Diners customize the bowl by selecting how many slices of chashu pork and roast beef they want, scaling from Single (900 yen) to Double (1,250 yen) to Triple (1,700 yen).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-shiyakusho-lunch confirms Ramen Yucho opened 18 Feb 2025 on the 2F of the Iidaya Building near Kyoto-Kawaramachi, a hideaway shop serving a Kyoto ginger soy-sauce ramen: a clear broth of three fish dashi driven by dissolved and needle-cut ginger, on Menya Teikoku (麺屋帝国) noodles, topped with roast beef, pork-shoulder chashu, naruto, Kujo green onion, menma and spinach, scaled Single/Double/Triple at ¥900/¥1,250/¥1,700 with a ¥200 ginger-dashi rice side. osakajinrock corroborates the ginger rarity and warming effect.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the soy-sauce tare is normally wheat-based, so the dish is not gluten-free and no GF accommodation is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a ginger soy-sauce ramen built on a special ginger shoyu tare, which in Japanese ramen is virtually always brewed from wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a fish/dashi base (horse mackerel, mackerel, bonito) and toppings include pork chashu and roast beef, so cross-contact with animal products is inherent to every bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly modern counter ramen shop near Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station where ordering is simple (essentially one bowl, choosing topping count), so a little Japanese and a friendly greeting go a long way.

places.name: 麺屋 鼎（KANAE） (Menya Kanae)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a Jiro-style shop built on extra-thick wheat noodles with salt or soy (typically wheat-based) tare, served in or with a beef-fat/dashi base — not safe for celiac diners. No gluten-free option is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house style centers on wagyu beef fat, beef Achilles tendon, and a dashi-based broth; the wheat noodles and tare make this unsuitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-23:00; closed Wed & Thu.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Kanae opened in September 2025 in the Shijo-Omiya / Mibu area of Kyoto, tucked along a quiet alley off Koin-dori in a renovated machiya (traditional townhouse) known as the 'Wagyu Kaikan.' You slip off your shoes to enter a snug counter space with a retro, handmade feel, th

chef_bio.background: Menya Kanae opened in September 2025 in the Shijo-Omiya / Mibu area of Kyoto, tucked along a quiet alley off Koin-dori in a renovated machiya (traditional townhouse) known as the 'Wagyu Kaikan.' You slip off your shoes to enter a snug counter space with a retro, handmade feel, then order from your smartphone by QR code — an appealing contrast of old-house atmosphere and modern convenience.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's idea is that wagyu beef fat brings a refined umami and a clean sweetness that pork back-fat lacks, giving a salt-based dashi described as having 'no sharp edges' — a more elegant, mellow spin on the famously heavy Jiro genre. Sourcing such beef fat is difficult, which the shop frames as a mark of its commitment to quality.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is 'KANAE no Jiro' — a new-school take on Jiro-style ramen. In place of the usual pork back-fat it uses the fat of wagyu (Kobe and Omi beef), and instead of pork chashu it tops the bowl with tender, gelatinous beef Achilles tendon, with no cabbage. Extra-thick, wide noodles from veteran maker Tieoh (棣鄂) carry the bowl, which can be ordered with soup (ari) or brothless (nashi), and seasoned with salt (shio) or soy (shoyu).

anecdotes.text: Students get a free upsize of noodles and bean sprouts.
Diners can also order select dishes from the sister izakaya 'Toyo-ta' alongside their ramen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms Menya Kanae opened Sept 2025 in a renovated machiya ('Wagyu Kaikan') off Koin-dori in Shijo-Omiya/Mibu, with shoes-off counter seating and QR smartphone ordering; its 'KANAE no Jiro' replaces pork back-fat with wagyu (Kobe/Omi) fat for a refined, edge-free salt dashi, and uses 棣鄂 (Tieoh) thick wide noodles, with the free student noodle/bean-sprout upsize and access to dishes from sister izakaya Toyo-ta. Tabelog corroborates the location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop offers a soy-sauce (shoyu) seasoning option; Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat, so this is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The bowls are built on wagyu beef fat, beef Achilles tendon, and a dashi-based broth, so the kitchen is not vegan-safe.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual counter ramen shop with QR-code smartphone ordering; little English is to be expected, but the self-service ordering eases the language barrier.

noodles.notes: Thick, wide, curly Jiro-style noodles from the Teian maker, made specifically for jiro-kei — chewy with good elasticity, built to drag up fat and seasoning. Said to be relatively approachable for Jiro beginners.

ramen.signature_bowl: KANAE no Jiro (鼎の二郎) — a Jiro-style bowl piled with yasai (bean sprouts, some cabbage), pork and abura, finished with premium wagyu (Kobe/Omi) beef fat. Choose shio or shoyu and soup or no-soup (mazesoba) at the ticket machine; call your garlic/yasai/abura. Shio is the quieter, fat-forward recommendation.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork slices in the standard bowl (two pieces); the shop also leans into beef, with long-simmered gelatinous beef achilles featured as a signature protein in some bowls.

gf.note: Jiro-style wheat-and-kansui noodles plus shoyu/shio tare and a pork-and-beef fat broth — no documented rice or konjac noodle, not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Built on pork tonkotsu with rendered wagyu beef fat, pork chashu and beef achilles — no plant-based broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto take on the Tokyo-born Jiro lineage — the mountain of bean sprouts, garlic and glistening fat is pure jiro-kei — but the soul here is the swap of ordinary pork back-fat for premium wagyu (Kobe/Omi) beef fat, which lends the rich tonkotsu base a sweeter, more elegant, less leaden finish. You taste that beefy refinement riding over the usual Jiro stomp.

places.name: 新進亭 一乗寺店 (Shinshintei Ichijoji)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop whose signature is white miso ramen; miso tare and the broth setup mean wheat is integral, and there is no published gluten-free option or dedicated handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The ramen is built on dashi and pork/meat elements with chashu and other meat toppings, and no vegan or vegetarian bowl is advertised. Plant-only diners cannot reliably be accommodated here.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–16:00 (lunch only). Closed Mon and the 3rd Sun.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shinshintei is widely credited as the birthplace of Kyoto-style white miso ramen. The lineage began as a humble food stall around 1960 and grew into a brick-and-mortar shop opened in 1972 in the Fushacho area of central Kyoto. Over nearly four decades it built a devoted following

roles: successor (founder's younger brother carrying on the recipe)
chef_bio.background: Shinshintei is widely credited as the birthplace of Kyoto-style white miso ramen. The lineage began as a humble food stall around 1960 and grew into a brick-and-mortar shop opened in 1972 in the Fushacho area of central Kyoto. Over nearly four decades it built a devoted following before the original first-generation owner retired and closed that location in 2009 due to age. Rather than let the recipe disappear, the founder's younger brother inherited the name and the noren and reopened the shop in the Ichijoji district, where it carries the tradition forward today as a lunch-only neighborhood institution.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep the original white-miso recipe alive and unchanged - a mellow, comforting, deeply flavored bowl made the same way it has been for decades, served simply at lunch.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is white miso ramen (白味噌ラーメン) - a gentle, mellow, slightly sweet white-miso broth that is distinct from the heartier Sapporo style. It pairs a concentrated dashi with a light white miso (淡めの白味噌), is piled with plenty of vegetables, and is served with straight Kyoto-style noodles that meet the smooth broth.

anecdotes.text: The original Fushacho shop became famous enough to draw notable guests, said to include singer Miyuki Nakajima and actor Keanu Reeves, before it closed in 2009.
When the first-generation owner retired, the founder's younger brother took over the name and reopened in Ichijoji to keep the white-miso ramen tradition going (一乗寺で暖簾を受け継いで開業したのが今の新進亭).
Regulars praise the long-unchanged, mellow and deeply flavored miso ramen as something you can only really experience in Kyoto.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki records the 1960 stall, 1972 Fushacho shop, 2009 closure due to the founder's age, the younger brother reopening in Ichijoji, the light white-miso bowl, and the celebrity guests; v-trip confirms the shop as Kyoto's white-miso originator with a long-unchanged mellow flavor and straight noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's core product is white miso ramen with wheat noodles and a miso-based tare; miso and the broth components typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free preparation is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on dashi and meat elements with chashu and other meat toppings; no vegan or vegetarian broth is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional neighborhood ramen counter where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed lunchtime visit go a long way; English support is unlikely.

noodles.notes: Thin (chu-hoso) straight noodles with a crisp, slightly chewy bite, cooked to a firm finish; they carry the mellow white-miso soup without overwhelming it.

ramen.signature_bowl: 白味噌ラーメン (Shiro-miso / white miso ramen) — the house origin bowl: a mellow, gently sweet Kyoto white-miso soup lifted by a whisper of chili and Sichuan pepper.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu, served alongside seasoned ground pork that melts into the soup; the bowl's protein heart is as much the minced meat as the sliced chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-fermented miso tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The standard bowl carries pork chashu and seasoned ground pork; no documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake) broth or animal-free version. A vegan diner would need to ask, with no guarantee the miso base is free of animal dashi.

ramen.pantheon: Often credited as the original white-miso ramen, this Ichijoji standby trades Sapporo's punchy lard-and-garlic miso for Kyoto's softer soul — a mellow, faintly sweet Saikyo-style white miso soup whose richness is rounded rather than aggressive. A flicker of chili and Sichuan pepper keeps the sweetness honest, and the whole bowl tastes unmistakably of Kyoto's gentle palate.

places.name: 龍鳳 (Ryuho)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The karashi soba uses wheat noodles, a wheat-based mustard/soy ankake sauce, and Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat); gyoza and spring rolls are wheat-wrapped. There are no celiac-safe options and no gluten-free accommodation here.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan place. The signature karashi soba is built on a chicken-bone and kombu broth with chicken, shrimp, and animal-based ankake; the wider menu is meat- and seafood-centric town-Chinese. No vegan ramen or vegan set is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–19:30 (last order 19:00, last entry 18:30). Closed Wed. Note: some sources list lunch-only hours (11:30–16:00), so confirm before visiting.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 龍鳳 is a small, unpretentious Kyoto town-Chinese counter tucked into the Rokkaku lane just off the Shinkyogoku arcade, where it has operated for roughly four decades. Its proprietor, Terada Yasuhiro, trained under two pillars of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking — 第一樓 (Daiichiro), the p

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto-style Chinese (京都中華), Cantonese-rooted but refined and lightly seasoned, in the 鳳舞 (Houmai) / 第一樓 (Daiichiro) lineage.
chef_bio.background: 龍鳳 is a small, unpretentious Kyoto town-Chinese counter tucked into the Rokkaku lane just off the Shinkyogoku arcade, where it has operated for decades. Its proprietor, Terada Yasuhiro, trained under two pillars of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking — 第一樓 (Daiichiro), the postwar restaurant opened around 1951, and the legendary 鳳舞 (Houmai), widely regarded as the fountainhead of 京都中華. He worked as a skilled chef at both before opening his own shop, carrying forward that lineage alongside sister-spirit establishments like 鳳泉.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithful preservation of the original Daiichiro recipe and taste. The dish was born as a course finale at Daiichiro because it travelled to the table more easily than soup noodles; Terada keeps that heritage intact while cooking his version longer to deepen the broth's umami, and historically swapping in seasonal ingredients (matsutake for shiitake in season, fresh bamboo shoots over canned).
chef_bio.specialty: からしそば (karashi soba) — straight noodles pre-coated with mustard paste under a gentle, mildly sweet soy-based ankake, topped with chicken, shrimp, dried shiitake, komatsuna greens and scallion. The mustard gives a sharp 'tsuun' hit to the nose that plays against the sauce's sweetness — a grown-up, unpredictable heat the shop calls a 大人の味.

anecdotes.text: The owner, Terada Yasuhiro, trained at both 第一樓 (Daiichiro, opened around 1951) and the legendary 鳳舞 (Houmai) before opening 龍鳳, and keeps the original Daiichiro karashi-soba recipe alive.
Karashi soba was originally created at Daiichiro as the closer to a Chinese course because it was easier to carry to the table than soup noodles — 'カラシソバはコースの〆によう出してたんです。スープ麺より運びやすいから僕らも重宝してね。'
A craftsman of this trade for a lifetime, the chef is described as over 70 years old yet still running the shop himself — 'ご主人はこの道ひと筋で、御歳70オーバー'.

The dish is served in a nostalgic Showa-era room still set with vintage tabletop condiments — the pepper shaker, soy-sauce dispenser and vinegar bottle that reviewers single out for their retro charm.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Brutus documents Terada Yasuhiro's training at 第一樓 (opened ~1951) and 鳳舞, the karashi-soba course-closer origin quote, longer cooking to concentrate the chicken-and-kombu ankake, and seasonal matsutake/bamboo swaps; kyotopi confirms the over-70 owner devoted to the craft, the Rokkaku/Shinkyogoku location, and the nostalgic condiment-set room.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Karashi soba is wheat-noodle based with a wheat mustard/soy ankake; gyoza and spring rolls are wheat-wrapped, and a small shared kitchen means no gluten isolation is possible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dish uses a soy-based ankake sauce (Japanese soy sauce contains wheat).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone and kombu based, with chicken and shrimp in the dish; no vegan preparation exists, so all cookware contacts animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, old-school Showa-era neighborhood Chinese counter run by a veteran chef; very little English, so a few Japanese phrases and a relaxed, respectful manner go a long way.

noodles.notes: Yellow kansui (alkaline) wheat noodles, served straight-cut. In the signature karashi soba they are tossed with prepared mustard (neri-garashi) before the thick ankake gravy is ladled over.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashi soba (からしそば) — straight yellow noodles tossed with hot Japanese mustard, blanketed in a gentle, delicate shoyu ankake (thick starchy gravy) loaded with vegetables and seafood-chicken.
ramen.chashu: No braised ramen-style chashu in the signature bowl; the protein is sliced chicken and shrimp folded into the gravy. The shop's fried rice does use diced char siu, but the soba bowl is chicken/seafood-forward.

gf.note: Yellow kansui wheat noodles, a wheat-based shoyu gravy, and a flour-thickened ankake all carry gluten. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The light gravy is built on chicken and seafood (shrimp, likely katsuo/Chinese stock), and bowls carry chicken and shrimp. No documented plant-only broth option.

ramen.pantheon: This is not ramen in the broth-and-tare sense but the soul of 京都中華 — the gentle Cantonese-derived town-Chinese cooking born from the legendary Houmai (鳳舞) lineage. Its karashi soba lays straight yellow noodles under a mild, translucent shoyu ankake of chicken, shrimp and vegetables, then jolts the whole bowl awake with a nose-tingling hit of mustard — delicate Kyoto restraint with one deliberate spike of heat.

places.name: 濃厚らーめん 驚麺屋 (Noko Ramen Kyomenya)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkotsu ramen specialist: wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth, with the usual ramen tare. No gluten-free options are offered and there is no indication of allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a heavy pork-bone (and, in some variants, seafood or chicken) broth, with pork back fat as a customizable topping. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30–14:30, 18:00–21:30 (last order 30 min before closing). Closed Tue/Wed/Thu/Fri. Currently open only Sat/Sun/Mon. Additional irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Sakyō-ku, Ichijōji / Chayama (Chayama-Kyoto Geijutsu Daigaku Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kyomenya opened in December 2016 along Ichijoji's famously competitive ramen street in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, just south of the well-known Ramen Yokozuna. It carved out its niche by going against the grain: while Ichijoji leans toward lighter chicken-based broths, Kyomenya planted i

chef_bio.background: Kyomenya opened on December 12, 2016 along Ichijoji's famously competitive ramen street in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, near the well-known Ramen Yokozuna. It carved out its niche by going against the grain: while Ichijoji leans toward lighter chicken-based broths, Kyomenya planted its flag on unapologetically heavy, ultra-rich tonkotsu.
chef_bio.philosophy: The aim is a broth that is intensely rich and creamy — dorodoro thick — yet kept smooth and balanced rather than merely heavy (濃厚ですがくどさはありません), with the pork bone simmered long and combined with seafood stock for depth.
chef_bio.specialty: The flagship bowl is the Kyo-no Tonkotsu (驚濃とんこつ) — a viscous, cream-thick pork-bone broth rounded out with seafood dashi, using roughly three times the pork bone of an ordinary tonkotsu ramen (通常の豚骨ラーメンの約3倍). The lineup also includes the Mega Tonkotsu (メガとんこつ, water and pork bones only, simmered for hours), a chicken-enriched Tori Tonkotsu (驚濃とりとんこつ), a milder Maroyaka Tonkotsu (まろやかとんこつ), and mazesoba.

anecdotes.text: The shop earned a runner-up (準グランプリ) in the tonkotsu category of 'Ultimate Ramen 2021' (究極のラーメン2021), a nod to how seriously its dorodoro broth is taken among ramen enthusiasts.
Diners customize each bowl at ordering — noodle firmness, amount of green onion, and amount of pork back fat (① 麺の固さ ② ねぎの多さ ③ 背油の量).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki 84814 confirms the Dec 12 2016 opening near Ramen Yokozuna, the ~3x pork-bone signature 驚濃とんこつ, and the full tonkotsu lineup; gcjapan lists the three order-time adjustments (noodle firmness, green onion, back fat) and the clean pork-plus-seafood broth; the 究極のラーメン2021 tonkotsu 準グランプリ is corroborated via web search.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills local counter ramen shop where ordering and customizing your bowl in Japanese will go most smoothly; little to no English support should be expected.

noodles.notes: Default bowl uses crimped, medium-thick wavy noodles built to grab and carry the gluey broth; firmness is adjustable to order. Kaedama (refill) noodles are thin and straight for a contrasting bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: 驚濃とんこつ (Kyono Tonkotsu) — the ultra-concentrated pork-bone bowl, simmered with roughly three times the bones of an ordinary tonkotsu into a doro-kei (thick, gluey) soup. Tune it to taste with kaedama, extra negi, and your choice of back-fat level.
ramen.chashu: Tender, lightly sweet braised pork — meaty rather than fatty, simmered so it carries a gentle sweetness without greasiness.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a pork-bone broth seasoned with a wheat-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire menu is built on a heavy pork-bone-and-seafood (bonito/anchovy) broth with pork chashu and back-fat; no plant-based broth or bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto outpost of the doro-kei (mud-thick) tonkotsu school — the bowl chases the ultra-concentrated, throat-coating pork-bone style of Nara's Mutetsupo lineage rather than Kyoto's own sé-abura shoyu tradition. The soul lives in a soup boiled down with triple the bones, layered in recent years with a stronger bonito-and-anchovy dashi so the seafood threads through the pork richness without turning harsh.

places.name: 家系ラーメン斎藤家 一乗寺店 (Iekei Ramen Saitoya Ichijoji)
places.gf_detail: This is Yokohama-style iekei ramen: wheat noodles from Sakai Seimen, a wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) tare, and a pork-bone/chicken broth — all standard wheat-containing components. There is no gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. Rice bowls are available as sides but are prepared in the same kitchen alongside wheat ramen.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken-oil broth with chashu pork, egg, and a soy-sauce tare. There are no vegetarian or vegan ramen options. Toppings such as spinach, seaweed, and negi exist but only as additions to the meat-based bowl. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 18:00–23:00; Sun & holidays 11:00–21:00; Closed Mondays (or the following Tuesday if Monday is a public holiday). Note: some listings show Fri 11:00–21:00 as well; lunch service is most reliable on Sundays/holidays.
places.neighborhood: Sakyō-ku, Ichijōji / Takano (Eizan Railway Ichijōji Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Saitoya is a Yokohama iekei ramen shop whose owner trained at Tagamiya (田上家), a respected Yokohama family-ramen house founded in 2014, and whose lineage traces back to Yoshimuraya (吉村家), the founding shop of the entire iekei genre. The original Saitoya opened in Yokohama's Negish

chef_bio.background: Saitoya is a Yokohama iekei ramen shop whose owner trained at Tagamiya (田上家), a respected Yokohama family-ramen house founded in 2014, and whose lineage traces back to Yoshimuraya (吉村家), the founding shop of the entire iekei genre. The original Saitoya opened in Yokohama's Negishi district in August 2022; this Ichijoji shop, opened July 27, 2024, is the third Saitoya location and the brand's first venture into the Kansai region.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner brews the broth in-house and embraces the iekei idea that the bowl naturally shifts through the day — the flavor you get at the lunch opening differs from the late-evening pour, which they treat as a feature of honest, freshly-simmered cooking rather than a flaw.
chef_bio.specialty: Chashu-men: a rich tonkotsu-and-chicken-oil broth with a soy-based tare, served over Sakai Seimen noodles and topped with tender smoked pork chashu, spinach, nori seaweed, and egg. Diners can customize noodle firmness, oil level, and tare strength in classic iekei fashion, and add cabbage or white negi the Yokohama way.

anecdotes.text: Brought direct-lineage Yokohama family ramen to Ichijoji, one of Kyoto's most competitive ramen districts, billed as the area's first authentic iekei specialist.
Uses Sakai Seimen's premium A-grade noodles, which are reserved for recognized iekei shops in the lineage.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki confirms the owner trained at Yokohama's Tagamiya (est. 2014, drawing on Yoshimuraya), the Negishi original of Aug 2022, the July 27 2024 Ichijoji shop as the third branch and first authentic iekei in the district, the in-house broth, the smoky chashu, and the A-grade Sakai Seimen noodles; noodlers confirms the July 27 2024 Kansai-first opening, self-boiled broth, Sakai Seimen noodles and spinach/nori toppings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles (Sakai Seimen) and a wheat-based shoyu tare are core to every bowl; there is no gluten-free preparation and no separate handling, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a soy-sauce base, which in iekei ramen is standard wheat-containing shoyu; reviewers specifically praised the soy-sauce flavor of the broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-oil based with pork chashu and egg; all bowls are animal-based with no vegan option.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese and there is no English menu — comfortable for travelers used to Japanese ramen shops, but English support should not be expected.

noodles.notes: Sakaiseimen-style thick, straight, short-cut noodles — the textbook Iekei choice. Sturdy and chewy, built to stand up to the rich tonkotsu-shoyu broth and carry it upward; firmness is customizable (kcatame on request).

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (チャーシューメン) — the full Iekei bowl with extra braised pork. Order it with the classic three adjustments: noodle firmness (men no katasa), broth richness (aji no nosa), and oil amount (abura no ryo). A char-grilled pork rice bowl makes the ideal side.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork — tender, generously sliced (about four pieces), seasoned to pair beautifully with rice. A premium extra-thick chashu topping is available.
notable_toppings: free condiments: grated ginger, crushed garlic, vinegar, pepper, chili paste

gf.note: The noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the tare is a soy-sauce (wheat) base over a pork-and-chicken bone broth — not celiac-safe. No rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-bone-and-chicken broth finished with chicken oil and soy tare, topped with braised pork — animal-based throughout. No plant-broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful slice of Yokohama Iekei that traces its soul back to the Yoshimuraya lineage — a creamy pork-and-chicken bone broth bound by a sharp soy tare and a glossy slick of chicken oil, the genre's signature trinity. Rare for the Kansai region, it lets Kyoto diners taste the genuine Kanto Iekei pedigree, leaner and cleaner than expected yet deep in body.

places.name: 本気つけ麺山下 (Honki Tsukemen Yamashita)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. This is a dedicated tsukemen specialist: both bowls are built on wheat noodles (described as thick, whole-grain-flour-containing noodles) dipped in a soy-sauce-based fish broth (gyokai shoyu) or a sesame-soy-milk tantan broth, both of which use wheat-based soy sauce tare. The wheat noodles plus wheat shoyu mean neither bowl is safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners, and no gluten-free preparation is offered or mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The fish-soy tsukemen broth is made from bonito (katsuo) and dried sardines (niboshi/iriko), and the sesame-soy-milk tantan bowl is topped with meat-miso (niku-miso); both are animal-based. Although the tantan broth uses soy milk (tonyu), it is not a vegan preparation. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Sundays. (Tabelog lists the lunch/dinner blocks as 11:00-14:00 and 17:00-21:00; blog sources list 14:30/21:30 close with L.O. at 14:00/21:00.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 本気つけ麺山下 opened on May 25, 2026 in the heart of Ichijoji, the neighborhood widely regarded as Kyoto's most fiercely competitive ramen district. It took over the space of the well-loved shop "Tsurukame Ichijoji," which closed at the end of March 2026, refreshing the storefront into

chef_bio.background: 本気つけ麺山下 opened on May 25, 2026 in the heart of Ichijoji, the neighborhood widely regarded as Kyoto's most fiercely competitive ramen district. It took over the space of the well-loved shop "Tsurukame Ichijoji," which closed at the end of March 2026, refreshing the storefront into a clean, bright counter room of eight seats. The proprietor is understood to have trained at a well-known ramen establishment before striking out on his own here.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop keeps its focus deliberately narrow, offering only its two tsukemen and letting the noodles and dip do the talking, with a stated wish to become a place loved by many.
chef_bio.specialty: A two-bowl tsukemen specialist. The signature 魚介醤油つけ麺 pairs thick, springy noodles with a concentrated bonito-and-dried-sardine fish broth balanced by an aromatic soy tare. The second pillar, 胡麻豆乳担々つけ麺, folds rich roasted sesame and creamy soy milk into a tantan dip crowned with meat-miso for a richness-meets-balance bowl.

anecdotes.text: The thick, straight noodles are from a little-known maker called 'はなき' (Hanaki) and are a particular point of pride, praised by reviewers for how well their texture pairs with the rich dip.
The shop occupies the former premises of the popular Tsurukame Ichijoji, which closed on March 31, 2026.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki and kyotopi confirm the May 25 2026 opening in the former Tsurukame space, the two-tsukemen-only concept (魚介醤油 and 胡麻豆乳担々), the 8-seat brighter counter, and an owner who trained at a well-known but undisclosed shop; Tabelog reviewers confirm the noodles come from the little-known 'はなき' (Hanaki) maker and praise their texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Both tsukemen use wheat noodles dipped in a wheat-soy-sauce-based tare, so there is no celiac-safe option and gluten is intrinsic to every bowl.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The fish broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in Japanese ramen is standard wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish broth uses bonito and dried sardines; the tantan bowl is topped with meat-miso, so animal products are central to both dips.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter shop with mobile-order, cash-only service in Kyoto's ramen-mad Ichijoji district; little English is expected, so come ready to order by phone-app and pay cash.

noodles.notes: Whole-wheat (zenryu-fun) thick noodles with a notably chewy, mochi-like bite and smooth slip — sturdy enough to carry the heavy dipping broth. Sourced from an unusual specialty maker.

ramen.signature_bowl: 魚介醤油つけ麺 (gyokai-shoyu tsukemen) — thick whole-wheat noodles served cold beside a concentrated, fragrant bonito-and-niboshi dipping broth finished with bold shoyu and a lift of yuzu. (The other half of the menu is a creamy 胡麻豆乳担々つけ麺 — sesame-soymilk tantan with house chili oil and meat miso.)
ramen.chashu: Moderately thick-cut braised pork shoulder loin — moist and tender, with a good balance of fat and lean. Aburi (seared) chashu is available as a paid add-on.

gf.note: Wheat-based thick noodles (with whole-wheat flour) and a wheat-containing shoyu dipping broth make this unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The signature broth is built on bonito and dried sardine with pork chashu; the second option leans on meat miso. No documented plant-only broth, so neither bowl is vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tsukemen specialist that narrows its whole focus to two dipping bowls and pours everything into the soup: the flagship pulls deep, almost jammy umami from bonito and dried sardine, lacquered with assertive shoyu and brightened by yuzu so the richness never tips into fishiness. You taste a contemporary gyokai-tsukemen lineage here rather than any single regional dialect — heavy, glossy broth made to cling to fat, chewy whole-wheat noodles to the very last drop.

places.name: 麺屋 練之助 (Menya Rennosuke)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowl uses house-made wheat noodles (Kyoto "Setokirara" wheat), so the ramen contains gluten by definition. The tare/seasoning is unspecified but standard ramen tare typically contains wheat shoyu. No gluten-free menu or noodle option is advertised, and the kitchen is a single small counter operation with shared equipment. Anyone needing strict gluten avoidance should not eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Although the broth is unusually animal-free for ramen — a 100% seafood dashi from two kombu varieties, Ibuki iriko (dried sardines) and saba/urume/katsuo bushi (fish flakes) with no pork or chicken stock — it is fish-based, not plant-based, and the bowl is topped with pork chashu (京の都もち豚). There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option advertised. The kombu component is plant-derived but the dish as served contains fish and pork.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:30) / 17:30–20:30 (last order 20:00). Closed Thu.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, Murasakino-Shimokashiwano-chō (Kitano-Hakubaichō / Kitano Tenmangū area, residential district)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef who trained at 猪一 (Inoichi), a Michelin-recognized Kyoto dashi-soba/ramen establishment, before opening Menya Rennosuke in April 2023 in a renovated traditional wooden house tucked into a quiet Murasakino residential alley.

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef who trained at 猪一 (Inoichi), a Michelin-recognized Kyoto dashi-soba/ramen establishment, before opening Menya Rennosuke in April 2023 in a renovated traditional wooden house tucked into a quiet Murasakino residential alley.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef set out to 'ラーメン屋というイメージを払拭したかった' — to dispel the everyday image of a ramen shop — pursuing refined, additive-free (無化調; no chemical seasoning) cooking that expresses Kyoto's water and terroir, served in a minimalist, jazz-scored space with Kiyomizu-ware bowls. He also plans seasonal evolution such as cold noodles in summer.
chef_bio.specialty: らぁ麺 — a delicate clear seafood-dashi bowl. The broth is 100% fish-based, built from two kinds of kombu and Ibuki iriko (dried sardines) layered with saba, urume and katsuo bushi, with no animal stock at all. House-made straight noodles use Kyoto 'Setokirara' wheat with Inner Mongolian natural kansui and Kiribati salt, cut at #22. The chashu is Kyoto 京の都もち豚 aged about four days, sliced thin and nearly shabu-like to showcase the pork's natural sweetness, and dessert is a house-made wasanbon pudding.

anecdotes.text: Opened April 2023 and within its first year became the only newly selected ramen shop in the Michelin Guide Kyoto-Osaka 2024, earning a Bib Gourmand — recognition it has retained in the guides since.
Hidden in a residential alley near Kitano, the shop is famously hard to find even with GPS, and its stylish, izakaya-like counter with jazz feels unexpected for a ramen establishment.
The chef apprenticed at Michelin-recognized Inoichi (猪一) before going independent.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotokurasu names owner 帯刀, his training at a Michelin shop, the April 2023 Murasakino wooden-house opening, the 100%-fish additive-free broth, and 京の都もち豚 chashu; kyotopi 0yOVx confirms the hidden Kitano alley, jazz counter, the two-kombu/Ibuki-iriko/saba-urume-katsuo broth, the Setokirara-wheat / Inner-Mongolian-kansui / Kiribati-salt / #22 noodles, and the 4-day-aged pork; the yahoo expert article confirms the shop as the only newly selected ramen pick in the Michelin Guide Kyoto-Osaka 2024 with a Bib Gourmand.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small 10-seat counter kitchen built around wheat ramen noodles; no separate gluten-free preparation is advertised, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare/seasoning composition is not published; standard ramen tare typically contains wheat-based soy sauce, so assume wheat unless the shop confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Although the dashi itself is animal-free (kombu, iriko and fish flakes), it is fish-based and the bowl is topped with pork chashu; no plant-based bowl is offered, so the kitchen is not set up for vegan service.

positives.text: Cooking is explicitly 無化調 — no chemical seasonings, yeast extract or preservatives — which some additive-sensitive diners value, though this does not make it gluten-free or vegan.

The broth is unusually a 100% seafood dashi (no pork or chicken stock), which is relevant for diners avoiding meat/poultry stock specifically — but it still contains fish and the bowl includes pork chashu.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter shop run quietly by its owner-chef in a residential alley; basic Japanese helps and a calm, attentive manner is appreciated in such an intimate space.

places.name: 拉麺 夢 (Ramen Yume)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. This is a Jiro-style ramen shop built around house-made wheat noodles (the owner invested in a professional noodle machine specifically to make her own wheat noodles), with a typical wheat-soy-sauce tare and pork-based broth. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. Jiro-style ramen is built on a pork broth with pork (buta) toppings; the signature bowls feature large pork slices. No vegan ramen offering was found.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner spent years as a hospital nurse, earning her nursing credentials while raising her family and returning to study around age 38 after her children grew more independent. Working grueling night shifts, she found that a bowl of ramen eaten after coming off a night shift re

chef_bio.background: The owner spent years as a hospital nurse, earning her nursing credentials while raising her family and returning to study around age 38 after her children grew more independent. Working grueling night shifts, she found that a bowl of ramen eaten after coming off a night shift restored both her stamina and her spirit. That comfort became a calling: at 53 she set out to open her own shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: She refused to cut corners with wholesale noodles, investing in a professional noodle machine (from Daikoku Manufacturing, around 2.7 million yen) so she could make her own noodles in-house, because she wants customers to taste the difference of truly handmade noodles. Sitting near the universities, she also wants to send students and young people off well-fed and happy, an extension of her caretaking instincts from nursing into feeding her community.
chef_bio.specialty: Jiro-style ramen with generous pork and vegetable toppings; the signature is the 小豚ラーメン (kobuta / small-pork ramen). Bowls are customized at order time by noodle quantity and the classic Jiro toppings of garlic, extra vegetables, and oil.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 夢 (yume, dream) carries a double meaning: it is both her dream of running her own ramen shop and the name of her beloved pet dog.
Her path to ramen began on the mornings after night shifts, when a restorative bowl of ramen moved her so deeply that it eventually inspired a mid-life career change at 53.
She crowdfunded her dream of serving house-made (自家製麺) noodles, buying her own noodle machine rather than relying on store-bought noodles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Campfire crowdfunding page confirms owner Noriko Fujita (藤田典子), age 53, a former hospital nurse who returned to study at ~38, was restored by ramen after night shifts, invested ~2.7 million yen in a Daikoku noodle machine for house-made noodles, named the shop 夢 after both her dream and her dog, and located near Kyoto's universities to feed students well.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Built entirely around house-made wheat ramen noodles; flour is handled and cooked on-site, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Jiro-style tare is soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Articles describe a soy/salt-balanced broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork broth and pork toppings are central to the bowls; no vegan or vegetarian preparation found.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run counter shop with ordering by ticket machine and no English presence; basic Japanese and familiarity with Jiro-style ordering (noodle quantity and the "yasai/ninniku/abura" toppings call) will make the visit smoother.

noodles.notes: House-made (jisei-men), built on crowdfunded noodle-making equipment. Slightly thinner and rounder with gentler waviness than the classic flat, brutally thick Jiro noodle — an intentionally more approachable, eat-easy take on the style.

ramen.signature_bowl: 小豚ラーメン (Ko-buta Ramen) — the 300g 'small' bowl piled with extra braised pork; portions also run petit 200g / regular 400g / large 500g. Free call (mashi) for vegetables, back-fat and garlic at the counter.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised (nikomi) pork that turns meltingly tender and shreds apart in the mouth; two generous slices, cut larger and more rustic than a typical neat chashu.

gf.note: Jiro-style bowl: house-made wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy (shoyu) tare and pork broth — wheat throughout. No rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option documented.

vegan.note: Pork-bone broth, soy tare, pork back-fat and braised pork are the core of every bowl. No plant-broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Jiro-inspired bowl with a softer soul — the pork-bone broth carries real porky umami and a sweet roundness rather than the genre's usual brutal heaviness, its soy edge sharpened by a fine layer of melted back-fat. Run by a former nurse who trained at Jiro-kei shops, it reads the mountain-of-pork-and-sprouts ritual through a gentler, more approachable lens while keeping the lineage unmistakable.

places.name: しらかわ (Shirakawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-sauce tare (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat) over a pork-and-chicken back-fat broth, so the signature dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or dedicated handling has been documented, and a shared broth/noodle-boiling workflow makes cross-contact likely. Not suitable for celiac diners; if avoiding gluten you should not rely on this shop.
places.vegan_detail: The core product is a meat-based bowl: a pork/chicken-bone broth finished with back fat (背脂チャッチャ系) and topped with chashu and green onion. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian ramen, and the broth itself is animal-based, so there is effectively nothing for a strict vegan here. A vegan diner should plan to eat elsewhere.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–22:00 (closed Fri)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shirakawa is a small independent Kyoto-style ramen shop tucked into the Jodoji neighborhood west of Ginkaku-ji, within the Ginkakuji-michi / Kitashirakawa stretch of Sakyo-ku famous as the heartland of Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen. The owner trained in the renowned Masutani (ますたに)

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Sakyo-ku, Jodoji), in the Masutani back-fat shoyu ramen tradition
chef_bio.background: Shirakawa is a small independent Kyoto-style ramen shop tucked into the Jodoji neighborhood west of Ginkaku-ji, within the Ginkakuji-michi / Kitashirakawa stretch of Sakyo-ku famous as the heartland of Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen. The owner trained in the renowned Masutani (ますたに) lineage — the Kitashirakawa house generally credited as the origin of Kyoto's back-fat, soy-sauce-and-pork-bone style — before going out on his own to run this counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithful to old-school Kyoto ramen: an unpretentious, affordable everyday bowl served in a homey, diner-like room. A telling detail noted by writers is that vinegar is set out on the counter even though gyoza is not on the menu — a sign the owner understands that a splash of vinegar rounds out and lightens this rich Kyoto broth, a small nod to genuine Kyoto-ramen know-how.
chef_bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu ramen (背脂チャッチャ系): a pork-and-chicken-bone broth blanketed with melted back fat, soy-sauce tare, wheat noodles, chashu and green onion. Compared with Masutani, regulars describe Shirakawa's bowl as having lighter back fat and a slightly stronger, saltier soy edge — a recognizably personal take on the house style.

anecdotes.text: Diners come specifically because they have heard it is run by someone who trained at Masutani: a reviewer opens with 『ますたに』で修行した人がやってるラーメン屋さんと聞いて ("I heard this is a ramen shop run by someone who trained at Masutani").

Writers point to the counter vinegar as proof of the owner's Kyoto-ramen sensibility: 餃子がメニューにないのに、お酢を置いているということは、店主がちゃんと京都ラーメンにお酢が合うことをわかっている証拠 (the fact that vinegar is offered despite no gyoza on the menu shows the owner knows vinegar suits Kyoto ramen).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: retty confirms the shop is run by someone who trained at Masutani and that its back-fat shoyu bowl runs lighter on fat but saltier than the reviewer expected; kyoto-gourmet places it in Jodoji near Ginkaku-ji, describes the pork-and-chicken back-fat shoyu broth, and highlights the counter vinegar despite no gyoza as evidence of the owner's Kyoto-ramen know-how.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A back-fat shoyu ramen shop with a single shared workflow (one pot of wheat noodles, shared utensils, soy-based tare); cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable for a gluten-sensitive diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen; Japanese soy sauce used for ramen tare almost always contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth itself is animal-based (pork/chicken bone with back fat), so even non-meat menu items would share an animal-stock kitchen; no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school neighborhood Kyoto ramen counter run by a local owner; English is unlikely, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will help, but it is a casual and welcoming spot.

noodles.notes: Thin noodles with a surprisingly springy, chewy bite — described as somen-like but with added mochi-mochi resilience that stands up well to the back-fat soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the back-fat shoyu Kyoto bowl; upgrade to the chashu-men for a fuller helping of pork belly.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork belly, three slices to a bowl, on the firmer side with a clean, classic soy-braise flavor rather than fatty melt.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-containing soy (shoyu) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork and chicken bones, the bowl carries pork back-fat and pork-belly chashu, and the tare is soy-based with animal stock. No plant-broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's home-grown soul food: a back-fat-and-soy chuka soba poured over a pork-and-chicken broth, in the lineage of nearby Masutani that fathered the city's se-abura shoyu style. What sets this bowl apart is the splash of vinegar worked into the soup itself, rounding the fat into something mellow and clean rather than heavy.

places.name: らぁ麺 櫻井 (Raamen Sakurai)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The noodles are wheat, the shoyu and miso tare are almost certainly wheat-based, and the bowls are built on a chicken broth — none of which is gluten-free. No gluten-free noodles, gluten-free tare, or celiac accommodation is advertised anywhere we could find. A celiac diner should not eat here; there is no safe bowl.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a 100% domestic-chicken broth (clear chicken dashi or whipped chicken paitan), and chashu toppings use chicken breast and Kyoto pork. There is no vegetable- or plant-based broth and no advertised vegan or vegetarian option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:30, 17:30–02:00 next day. Closed Sun.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent ramen counter that opened in the summer of 2022 a short walk west of the Shijo-Omiya intersection, in the Mibu neighborhood of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward. With just eight counter seats and a kitchen open until 2 a.m., it has built a following as a late-night spot fo

chef_bio.background: A small independent ramen counter that opened in the summer of 2022 a short walk west of the Shijo-Omiya intersection, in the Mibu neighborhood of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward. With just eight counter seats and a kitchen open until 2 a.m., it has built a following as a late-night spot for a carefully made bowl of chicken ramen.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen is committed to an additive-free bowl built on 100% domestic chicken, cooking the broth without chemical seasonings (muka-cho) and topping it with chicken breast and Kyoto pork.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the chicken-paitan shoyu ramen: a creamy chicken broth that the kitchen whips with an immersion blender so it turns foamy and emulsified, concentrating the savory depth, paired with fine noodles, chicken-breast chashu and green onion. The shop also pours a lighter clear chicken-dashi shoyu and shio, plus a chicken-paitan miso.

anecdotes.text: The creamy chicken broth is deliberately whipped with a hand blender until it foams, a technique meant to promote emulsification and intensify the umami in each bowl.
The shop leans into its location and hours as a rare additive-conscious ramen stop that stays open into the small hours near Shijo-Omiya.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms the summer 2022 opening near Shijo-Omiya in Nakagyo Ward, the 8-seat L-shaped counter, hours to 2am, the 100%-domestic-chicken additive-free broth, the immersion-blender-foamed chicken paitan, dual chicken-breast and Kyoto-pork chashu, and the four varieties (chicken shoyu, shio, paitan shoyu, paitan miso).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a shoyu (and miso) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce and miso contain wheat, and no wheat-free tare is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth is made from 100% domestic chicken and chashu uses chicken breast and pork, so all bowls contain animal products with no plant-based option.

positives.text: The kitchen states its broth is additive-free, made without chemical seasonings from 100% domestic chicken.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter ramen bar where Japanese is the working language; basic phrases and pointing at the menu will carry you, but expect little to no English support.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick wavy noodles with a chewy, mochi-like elasticity, chosen to grip the chicken broths. The shop pairs different noodles to its clear versus paitan bowls.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏白湯しょうゆ (chicken paitan shoyu) — the creamy, espuma-foamed chicken paitan finished with soy tare; the shio and a miso paitan are also offered, alongside lighter clear-broth 鶏出汁 shoyu/shio bowls.
ramen.chashu: Two cuts: tender poached domestic chicken breast and braised Kyoto-pork shoulder loin (kata-rosu) that renders a rich, fatty sweetness.
notable_toppings: white negi (scallion, from the shop's own farm)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus wheat-based shoyu/miso tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on 100% chicken broth with chicken/pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto chicken house that puts the bird front and center: a 100% domestic-chicken, additive-free kitchen offering both a clean six-hour clear dashi and a seven-hour creamy paitan whipped into a cloud-like espuma foam. It is part of the contemporary tori-paitan wave rather than Kyoto's classic se-abura shoyu lineage, and you taste that pure-chicken soul in every silky sip.

places.name: 中華そば おにぎり ◯△ (Chuka Soba Onigiri Maru-Sankaku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The thin noodles are made with wheat (and contain 全粒粉/whole-grain wheat flour), the tare uses aged wheat-based soy sauce (老舗蔵元の再仕込み熟成醤油), and the onigiri are an additional wheat-free side but the ramen itself is built on wheat. Broth is chicken-and-seafood based. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or menu; a celiac diner should treat all the ramen here as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. Both signature bowls are animal-based: the ginger chuka soba is a domestic-chicken broth with chicken oil and rare chashu pork; the kai-dashi (shellfish) bowl uses scallop, clam, dried flying-fish (ago) and bonito-style seafood stock. No advertised vegan or vegetarian ramen. A vegan would have essentially nothing here beyond possibly a plain rice ball, which should be confirmed ingredient-by-ingredient in person.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–22:00, open daily (no fixed closed day listed).
places.neighborhood: Yamashina-ku, Takehana-Takenokaidō-chō (in front of Keihan Yamashina / JR Yamashina Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened December 27, 2023 right by Yamashina Station, this compact ramen-and-onigiri counter is a produce/concept shop developed by the Nagoya-based food company F-Lead (株式会社エフリード). The idea is unusual and very deliberate: a bowl of chuka soba designed expressly to be eaten one ha

roles: operator: 株式会社エフリード (F-Lead)
chef_bio.origin: Yamashina, Kyoto — a casual local spot a few minutes' walk from JR / Keihan Yamashina Station.
chef_bio.background: Opened December 27, 2023 right by Yamashina Station, this compact ramen-and-onigiri counter is a produce/concept shop developed by the Nagoya-based food company F-Lead (株式会社エフリード). The idea is unusual and very deliberate: a bowl of chuka soba designed expressly to be eaten one hand on the chopsticks, the other holding a freshly formed rice ball.
chef_bio.philosophy: Build a ramen whose flavor is tuned specifically to pair with rice — fragrant and savory rather than heavy — and serve loosely hand-formed, crumble-in-the-mouth onigiri with carefully chosen rice and generous fillings alongside it.
chef_bio.specialty: The headline bowl is the 生姜中華そば (ginger chuka soba): a domestic-chicken broth finished with aged re-brewed soy sauce, with ginger aroma drawn into the oil by low-temperature infusion so it reads, as one reviewer put it, 'like ginger-pork sauce with the sweetness taken out.' Thin, firm whole-grain noodles, rare-sliced chashu, red onion and menma round it out. The second pillar is the 貝だし中華そば, a clearer bowl layering scallop and clam stock.

anecdotes.text: The onigiri are intentionally formed loosely so they fall apart softly in the mouth, with fillings ranging from truffle-kombu to salmon-and-roe (oyako) and rare chashu with egg yolk.

Opening was announced for December 27, 2023 under the tagline 'eat ginger chuka soba with a fluffy onigiri in one hand' (ふんわりおにぎり片手に中華そばを啜る), framing the rice ball as a core part of the meal rather than a side.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: prtimes confirms F-Lead (株式会社エフリード) as operator, the Dec 27 2023 Yamashina opening, the onigiri-in-one-hand concept and tagline, the 生姜中華そば (domestic chicken, aged soy, low-temp ginger oil) and the shellfish 貝だし中華そば (scallop and clam); kikishouse confirms the loosely formed onigiri and fillings (truffle-kombu, salmon-and-roe, rare chashu with egg yolk), the 'ginger-pork with sweetness removed' ginger note, and the thin firm whole-grain noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is described as aged re-brewed soy sauce from an established brewery (老舗蔵元の再仕込み熟成醤油), a wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Both signature broths are animal-based (domestic chicken with chicken oil; or scallop/clam/flying-fish/bonito-style seafood stock), so shared pots and water mean cross-contact with animal ingredients is inherent.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, brisk neighborhood ramen counter with a ticket-machine ordering system and cash-only payment, so a little basic Japanese and self-service comfort go a long way; English support should not be assumed.

noodles.notes: Whole-wheat-blend thin to medium-thin straight noodles with a crisp, clean (patsu-patsu) bite and a faintly chewy, mochi-like body; cut to drink in the ginger-forward broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoga (ginger) chuka soba — an aged-shoyu chicken clear soup spiked with fresh ginger and floated with melting back fat, the bowl built to be eaten alongside one of the shop's hand-formed onigiri.
ramen.chashu: Two cuts in one bowl: a large, lightly seared rare pork slice that stays tender and pink, plus a deeply braised, fall-apart, more strongly seasoned piece.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based aged-shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. The onigiri are plain rice but the ramen itself is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soup is chicken-based with pork back-fat and pork/animal chashu, and the shellfish variant adds seafood dashi; no documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake) broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A Yamashina take on Kyoto's beloved se-abura shoyu lineage, where pools of melting back-fat ride a clear, aged-soy chicken soup — but here a bright shot of fresh ginger cuts the richness, lending the broth a gingery-savory snap that practically begs for a rice ball. You taste the city's fat-and-soy soul re-tuned for a lighter, more aromatic finish.

places.name: Oike Café × HACHINOBO（オイケカフェ ハチノボウ） (Oike Cafe x Hachinobo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The house bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare over a rich pork-bone broth — both the noodles and the tare contain wheat, so the standard bowls are not suitable for celiacs or those strictly avoiding gluten. No dedicated GF menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no allergen accommodation is documented. Anyone needing gluten-free should treat this as unsafe and confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan specialist. The signature bowls use pork-bone broth and pork/chicken chashu. The original 八の坊 was known for a "ベジそば" (vegetable soba), and the revived shop's broader menu includes vegetable-leaning items, curry and rice dishes, but there is no confirmed fully plant-based ramen — any "vegetable" bowl may still use animal-based broth or tare. A strict vegan must ask in person whether the broth, tare and toppings are entirely plant-based before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 (last order 21:00) / from 11:00 until sold out. Open daily.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyō-ku, Karasuma-Oike (1F of Park Hotel Kyoto Oike)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is the second life of a beloved Kyoto ramen shop. The original 八の坊 (Hachinobo) opened in Enmachi on February 1, 2015 and was one of the shops that introduced Kyoto to the foam-style (泡系) ramen pioneered in Fukuoka. It later reworked itself as 八ノ家, leaning into a Yokohama-ins

chef_bio.background: This is the second life of a beloved Kyoto ramen shop. The original 八の坊 (Hachinobo) opened in Enmachi on February 1, 2015 and was one of the shops that introduced Kyoto to the foam-style (泡系) ramen pioneered in Fukuoka. It later reworked itself as 八ノ家, leaning into a Yokohama-inspired family-style tonkotsu, before closing on September 30, 2023. On August 24, 2024 the name and its signature 'cappuccino' bowl were revived as Oike Café × HACHINOBO on the ground floor of Park Hotel Kyoto Oike near Karasuma-Oike.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tame the heaviness of tonkotsu by aerating it: the frothed broth keeps the famous pork-bone aroma restrained and gives a creamy, fluffy mouthfeel, so the bowl reads as rich-but-refined rather than punishing.
chef_bio.specialty: Cappuccino Ramen — an ultra-rich pork-bone broth whipped into a fluffy cappuccino-like foam, served with thick curly noodles, two kinds of chashu (tender chicken and pork loin), diced onion, extra-thick menma, and a seasoned soft-boiled egg. A spicy soy-based 'RED' bowl (inspired by Katsuura tantanmen) is the other standout.

anecdotes.text: The original Enmachi shop set diners loose with a row of free counter condiments — yuzu vinegar, chili oil, black pepper and a spicy crunchy topping — to remix the bowl to taste.
The shop is credited with helping bring Fukuoka's foam-style ramen to Kyoto, and the cappuccino bowl's fluffy foam is its calling card.
Reopened August 24, 2024 inside Park Hotel Kyoto Oike, a few minutes' walk from Karasuma-Oike station, in a spacious café setting that doubles as the hotel's restaurant.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki records the original 八の坊 opening Feb 1 2015 in Enmachi with foam-style ramen and free counter condiments (yuzu vinegar, chili oil, black pepper, crunchy topping), its rebrand to 八ノ家 Yokohama family tonkotsu and Sept 30 2023 closure; magazine.tabelog ties the shop to Fukuoka's foam-style ramen; kyo-soku confirms the Aug 24 2024 revival as Oike Café × HACHINOBO in Park Hotel Kyoto Oike near Karasuma-Oike, the cappuccino ramen with chicken and pork-loin chashu, menma and egg, and the RED spicy bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles cooked in shared water, with a soy-sauce tare that contains wheat, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent to the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare over pork-bone stock; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls center on pork-bone broth and pork/chicken chashu; any vegetable-leaning dish shares a kitchen that is fundamentally pork-based, so vegan separation is not assured.

cultural_comfort.note: Set inside a hotel café with a street-facing entrance and walk-in seating, it is welcoming and easy to enter; a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will carry you comfortably.

noodles.notes: Thick, high-hydration noodles with a springy, snappy bite that hold up against the heavy broth coating; described variously as curly/crinkled and supplier-made (Teigaku). A few accounts call them straight, so shape is reported with mild uncertainty.

ramen.signature_bowl: Cappuccino Ramen — a thick pork (tonkotsu) paitan broth whipped into a fine, airy cappuccino-like foam, finished with shoyu tare, thick high-hydration noodles, two kinds of chashu and diced onion, with a side of spicy takana pickles.
ramen.chashu: Two styles served together: a tender chicken chashu and a juicy braised pork loin (rosu) chashu. The lighter "tanrei" bowl instead features a richer three-layer braised pork belly.
notable_toppings: spicy takana (pickled mustard greens) on the side

gf.note: Standard ramen is wheat noodles with a shoyu (wheat) tare, so the core bowls are not celiac-safe. The menu does list a Laksa made with rice noodles, but its coconut-shrimp broth and seasonings are not verified gluten-free, and cross-contact in a shared kitchen is likely — ask staff before relying on it.

vegan.note: Bowls are built on a rich pork-bone broth with shoyu tare and pork/chicken chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's standard-bearer for "awa-kei" (foam-style) ramen: a concentrated Hakata-leaning pork paitan blitzed into a silky cappuccino froth, so the dense pork umami arrives light and creamy on the tongue rather than heavy. A shoyu tare and a hit of raw onion and spicy takana keep the richness bright, making it a modern, design-forward take on tonkotsu.

places.name: 麺ショップ この味出来太蔵 (Men Shop Kono Aji Dekitazo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop spanning jiro-kei (extra-thick wheat noodles), 中華そば (Chinese-style soba), and tsukemen, with soy-forward tare and pork-bone/chicken broths — all standard sources of wheat. No celiac-safe options, no dedicated equipment, and no allergy accommodation are mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for someone who must strictly avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The kitchen is built on animal stock — a chicken-and-pork-bone broth — across the menu, plus chashu, pork belly, and karaage sides. No plant-based bowl is offered or noted.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 22:00). Closures irregular (one local guide lists Wednesday as the closed day; confirm before visiting).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner spent years inside Kyoto's ramen world — local accounts cite stints at established names such as Takaradoya, Chingyu and Daichu — before going independent and opening this shop in June 2022, taking over a former ramen tenant on the Nakadachiuri-Chiekoin block of Kamigyo

chef_bio.background: The owner spent years inside Kyoto's ramen world — local accounts cite stints at established names such as Takaradoya (宝屋), Chingyu (珍遊) and Daichu (大中) — before going independent and opening this shop on June 17, 2022, taking over the former 雁木 (Gangi) ramen space on the Nakadachiuri-Chiekoin block of Kamigyo Ward.
chef_bio.philosophy: Broad appeal over purist gatekeeping: the jiro-style bowl is positioned as a 'light jiro' with no intimidating call-out ordering system, so newcomers can try the genre without knowing the ritual. The kitchen keeps the menu wide and rotates seasonal specials (cold noodles, thin miso, extra-rich abura-soba) to stay a place anyone in the neighborhood can enjoy.
chef_bio.specialty: An all-rounder bowl lineup. The 中華そば (Chinese-style soba) is the everyday signature — a chicken-and-pork-bone broth (鶏ガラ豚骨ベース) with quiet depth, melt-in-the-mouth chashu and straight noodles tuned to be savory but easygoing. Alongside it the shop runs a deliberately approachable 'light jiro' (二郎系) bowl and tsukemen, plus salt, miso, seafood and niboshi variations.

anecdotes.text: The jiro-style bowl is framed as a beginner-friendly 'light jiro' — generous sprouts, cabbage and thick pork belly over extra-thick wavy noodles, but with grated (not minced) garlic and no complicated 'call' system, so first-timers aren't put on the spot.
Opened June 2022 in the former 雁木 (Gangi) ramen space, the shop trades on the owner's apprenticeship at well-known Kyoto houses and keeps Southern All Stars playing through the room.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan confirms the June 17 2022 opening in the former 雁木 (Gangi) space in Kamigyo, the owner's training at 宝屋・珍遊・大中, the 鶏ガラ豚骨 中華そば signature, and that the owner is a Southern All Stars fan whose music plays in the shop; kyotopi confirms the beginner-friendly 'light jiro' with sprouts/cabbage/thick pork belly over thick wavy noodles, grated garlic and no call system.
