soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are soy-forward (a soy-tare jiro and soy-based Chinese soba), and ramen tare is conventionally made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (chicken-and-pork-bone), prepared in a single small kitchen alongside pork belly, chashu and karaage, so a plant-only request cannot be isolated.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual neighborhood counter shop run in Japanese with no listed English support, but the no-call ordering and walk-in format make it approachable.

noodles.notes: Noodles supplied by the renowned Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku (麺屋棣鄂). The standard chuka-soba uses straight medium noodles; the jiro-style variant swaps in thick, crinkled, wari-wari (sturdy, chewy) noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) — the shop's foundational bowl, a chicken-and-pork-bone broth simmered around eight hours with a clean shoyu tare. Add the ajitama for the 味玉中華そば.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, slow-cooked until meltingly tender (toro-toro) and deeply seasoned.
notable_toppings: wonton (on the koku-umami shoyu wonton soba)
bean sprouts and cabbage (on the jiro-style bowl)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free seasoning option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on a chicken-and-pork-bone broth with pork chashu and back-fat; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A friendly all-rounder in the Kyoto idiom: its soul is a chicken-and-pork-bone soup coaxed over roughly eight hours into something light yet quietly deep, finished with a crisp shoyu tare and a glint of back-fat in the local se-abura tradition. From that base the kitchen ranges confidently across miso, shio, a seafood chanpon-style bowl, dipping noodles and a gentle, garlic-laced Jiro-style bowl.

places.name: 更科 聖護院支店 (Sarashina Shogoin-shiten)
places.gf_detail: Everything central here is wheat-based: the chuka-soba noodles are Chinese wheat noodles, the soba/udon are wheat-containing, and the bowls feature wheat-gluten fu (umefu and dried fu) plus a Japanese-style broth that almost certainly relies on wheat-containing shoyu. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is indicated. Not suitable for a celiac diner.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba/udon shokudo whose dishes center on fish-and-meat dashi, pork chashu, kamaboko fish cake, and donburi over rice. The signature chuka-soba has a light broth but is topped with simmered pork chashu, menma, and grilled fish cake, and the kitchen makes no mention of plant-based options. There is no vegan offering; a vegan diner would have essentially nothing safe here.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 16:30–18:00 (Sunday is not continuous service but follows the same lunch/dinner split). Irregular closures, roughly 3–4 days per month.
places.neighborhood: 聖護院・岡崎（左京区）— deep down a narrow covered alley off Yoshida-Hi
places.cuisine: Old-school machi-chuka chuka-soba in a Meiji
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sarashina traces its roots to a Meiji-era soba house centered on a main shop in Kyoto's Shinkyogoku shopping district; this Shogoin branch grew out of that lineage and today runs as its own quiet, independent neighborhood shokudo. You find it by walking some ten meters down a cov

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Meiji-era Sarashina lineage out of Shinkyogoku)
chef_bio.background: Sarashina traces its roots to a Meiji-era soba house centered on a main shop in Kyoto's Shinkyogoku shopping district; this Shogoin branch grew out of that lineage and today runs as its own quiet, independent neighborhood shokudo. You find it by walking some ten meters down a covered alley barely a meter wide — easy to stroll right past — until a low old-fashioned door opens onto a small room of rustic, folk-craft furnishings and a single mat of raised tatami seating that feels unchanged for decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: Unfussy, neighborly cooking: an old soba shokudo that quietly serves nostalgic chuka-soba and home-style donburi at gentle prices, prizing warmth and consistency over trend.
chef_bio.specialty: The bowl regulars come for is the chuka-soba: pale, straight-cut Chinese noodles in a gentle, light broth that carries a subtly Japanese, dashi-leaning character rather than the heavy richness of a modern ramen-ya. It arrives piping hot and generously dressed — simmered chashu pork, menma, scallion, nori, grilled kamaboko fish cake, and pieces of wheat-gluten fu (including a pickled umefu) — the kind of comforting, everyday bowl meant to be eaten often rather than to impress.

anecdotes.text: The entrance is so hidden — down a covered alley under a meter wide, about ten meters in — that it is genuinely a 'know-it-if-you-know-it' spot locals say you can walk straight past.
Beyond the chuka-soba the shop is known for homey standards like nabeyaki udon and konoha-don, the everyday dishes of a long-running soba house.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle and soba/udon kitchen with shared boiling water and prep; no gluten-free handling indicated, so cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The light, Japanese-leaning broth and donburi seasonings almost certainly use standard wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare or seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are built on pork chashu and fish cake; even the noodle bowls are not vegetarian, and no separate vegan prep exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional family-run neighborhood shokudo tucked down a narrow alley with no indication of English support, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will go a long way.

noodles.notes: Pale, relatively white straight Chinese noodles that suit the light, gentle broth rather than fight it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — order the (上) jo version for extra chashu.
ramen.chashu: Thin slices of pale, gently boiled (not braised or seared) pork — five to six slices in the jo bowl — soft and mild rather than caramelized.

gf.note: Chinese noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the shoyu tare is wheat-based; on top of that this is a soba/udon house where wheat is everywhere. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth leans on a Japanese-style stock (fish dashi heritage) and the bowl is dressed with pork chashu and fish-cake kamaboko. No documented plant-only option.

ramen.pantheon: A relic of old Kyoto machi-chuka: a Meiji-era soba house whose Chinese noodle bowl is built on the gentle, soba-stock-derived wafu soul of the shop, so the soup tastes more like a clear, kind Kyoto dashi-shoyu than any modern ramen. Pale broth, pale noodles, soothing rather than bold — the kind of everyday comfort bowl that has nearly vanished.

places.name: 大豊ラーメン 木屋町店 (Taiho Ramen Kiyamachi)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle Kyoto ramen shop with a pork-bone soy (shoyu) base, so the noodles and the tare both contain wheat and there is no indication of gluten-free options or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork-bone/chicken broth ramen, chashu pork, and karaage; no vegan or vegetable-broth bowl is offered. Not a vegan option.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 18:30-6:00 next day / Fri, Sat 17:00-6:00 next day / Sun & holidays 18:00-6:00 next day (late-night hours)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Taiho Ramen is a late-night ramen standby tucked away deep in a narrow alley between Kiyamachi and Pontocho, Kyoto's nighttime entertainment district, near a police box. The Kiyamachi shop is open from evening until 6:00 the next morning, bustling with locals and travelers seeking a post-drinking finish.

roles: 独立系の小規模ラーメン店
chef_bio.background: 大豊ラーメンは、京都の夜の歓楽街・木屋町と先斗町のあいだの細い路地の奥、交番のそばにひっそりと構える深夜ラーメンの定番店。木屋町店は夕方から翌朝6時まで営業し、飲んだあとの〆を求める地元客や旅行者で賑わう。
chef_bio.philosophy: 見た目の黒さに反して意外とあっさりとした、くせになる一杯を深夜に提供し、歓楽街の〆の一杯としての役割を担う。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は特製チャーシューラーメン。真っ黒に見える濃色の醤油スープ（鶏の旨味も効いた褐色）に、花びらのように盛られた薄切りチャーシュー、たっぷりの青ねぎ、ストレートの細麺を合わせる。

anecdotes.text: ビジュアルは真っ黒だが、スープは見た目に反してあっさりとしており、鶏の旨味がたっぷり効いていると評される。
厨房を囲うL字型カウンター（＋小さなテーブル）の狭い空間で、飲んだ後の客たちの人間模様が見られる、木屋町ナイトライフの〆ラーメンの代表格。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is built on a pork-bone soy (shoyu) base; standard Japanese soy sauce tare contains wheat, and wheat ramen noodles are cooked in shared water.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone/chicken based and bowls are topped with chashu pork; no separate vegan preparation is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny late-night counter shop down a Kiyamachi alley with no English menu noted; ordering is simplest with a little Japanese, though it draws plenty of post-drinking visitors including travelers.

noodles.notes: Old-school low-hydration straight noodles (described thin to medium-thin) with a firm bite that stands up to the heavy, soy-soaked broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men — the pork-bone soy ramen piled with the shop's black-pork chashu and a heap of shredded kujo negi.
ramen.chashu: Black-pork (kurobuta) chashu, soy-braised and boldly seasoned; served both as thin loin slices and as diced bits mixed through the negi.
notable_toppings: diced chashu folded into the negi

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles plus a heavy soy-based tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is pork-bone based with a pork chashu and soy tare; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A late-night Kyoto institution tucked in a Kiyamachi back-alley, this is the city's beloved 'shime' bowl: an inky, pork-bone soy broth so dark and salt-forward it practically demands you've been drinking. You taste the Kyoto se-abura-shoyu lineage in the rendered pork fat and the aggressive shoyu tare, brightened by the signature crunch of shredded kujo negi.

places.name: 中華そば専門店 なか小路 (Chuka Soba Senmonten Nakakoji)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is a traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop: wheat ramen noodles (Kondo Seimen / Kondo noodle factory), a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that almost certainly contains wheat, and chicken/pork back-fat broths. There is no indication of gluten-free menu items or accommodation, and the kitchen shares boiling water and surfaces with wheat noodles. A celiac or strict gluten-free diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core bowls are built on chicken and pork (back-fat) broth with chashu (pork), and the shop also serves beef-tendon rice bowls, karaage, and ginger-pork sets. No plant-based broth or vegan ramen is offered. Vegans cannot be accommodated.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. ~20:45); weekends and holidays 7:30-14:30 / 17:00-21:00. Monday is lunch only (11:00-14:30). Sources vary slightly on the morning/weekend start.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner came up through two pillars of Kyoto's back-fat ramen tradition: he trained at the legendary Shinpuku Saikan and then served as manager (tencho) at Chinyu in Ichijoji before striking out on his own. The shop opened on November 1, 2021, in a quiet residential pocket of M

chef_bio.background: The owner came up through two pillars of Kyoto's back-fat ramen tradition: he trained at the legendary Shinpuku Saikan and then served as manager (tencho) at Chinyu in Ichijoji before striking out on his own. The shop opened on November 1, 2021, in a quiet residential pocket of Minami-ku's Yoshoin district, taking over a former ramen storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: To serve honest, old-school Kyoto ramen (昔ながらの京都ラーメン) at neighborhood prices: the bolder, back-fat-forward seasoning that south-Kyoto locals favor, paired with the snappy medium-thin noodles that define the city's style. It is built as an approachable, family-friendly local shop rather than a destination.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a classic back-fat soy-sauce bowl (背脂醤油ラーメン) on a robustly seasoned chicken-based broth, finished with green onion, two slices of chashu, and menma. The kitchen also runs a back-fat mazesoba and a chicken bowl, plus hearty sides — beef-tendon rice bowls, karaage, and ginger-pork sets — and weekend morning ramen service.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at Shinpuku Saikan and worked as manager at Chinyu in Ichijoji before opening Nakakoji on November 1, 2021.
Noodles are the snappy, medium-thin Kyoto style sourced from Kondo Seimen, served firm by default; the shop also pours notably cheap draft beer and runs weekend morning ramen.
A local-favorite shop in a residential corner of Minami-ku, praised for back-fat 'chacha' Kyoto ramen that has depth without heaviness and Kyoto green onions that turn sweet from the heat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles (from Kondo Seimen) are boiled in shared water; a celiac-safe preparation is not possible here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) back-fat ramen; standard Japanese shoyu tare contains wheat, and no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken- and pork-based with back fat, topped with pork chashu; sides include beef tendon, karaage, and ginger pork, so no dish is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood ramen counter serving local families; ordering is straightforward but staff and menu are Japanese-only, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps.

noodles.notes: Snappy, crisp-biting straight medium-thin noodles (chuusai) with a powdery, low-hydration 'patsu-patsu' texture, served firm by default. Sourced from Kondo Seimen. The brittle bite is a hallmark of the classic Kyoto chuka-soba style.

ramen.signature_bowl: Buta-soba — the back-fat soy-sauce pork bowl (se-abura chatcha). The chicken version, tori-soba, is the salt-forward sibling.
ramen.chashu: Two slices of pork belly chashu, briefly torch-seared (aburi) to order so the fat caramelizes at the edges; balanced lean-and-fat marbling.
notable_toppings: large chunks of pork back-fat (se-abura) floating on top

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based soy tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs, and no rice or konjac noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The broth is built on pork (and chicken) bones with a soy tare, and bowls are dressed with back-fat and pork belly chashu — no plant-based option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood torchbearer of old-school Kyoto chuka-soba: a clear, deeply simmered pork-and-chicken broth pulled tight with dark soy and crowned with big melting chunks of back-fat — the se-abura chatcha lineage running back through Shinpukusaikan and the Ichijoji ramen scene. You taste the city's working-class soul in the snappy thin noodles and that comforting, savory-sweet fat slick that never tips into heaviness.

places.name: らぁ麺や ふぢとら (Raamenya Fujitora)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles made fresh each morning, and the shoyu version uses a tare blending six soy sauces (wheat-based Japanese shoyu), served over a chicken-and-pork broth. No gluten-free noodle, tare, or menu option is mentioned anywhere, and the kitchen also hand-makes wheat-skin wontons, so wheat is pervasive. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here. Anyone needing strictly gluten-free should not rely on this shop without contacting the owner directly first (担々麺・らぁ麺ともに小麦麺・小麦醤油使用).
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every bowl is animal-based: the broth is built from jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork and pork back bone, finished with chicken oil (鶏油). Standard toppings include pork chashu, ground pork (tantanmen), and meat-filled wontons. There is no vegetable/vegan broth or noodle option described. A vegan would have nothing safe to order here.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Sun: 11:00-14:00 (lunch only); Wed-Sat: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:00; Tue closed. May close early once sold out.
places.cuisine: Chicken-broth shoyu & shio ramen and dandanmen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner-chef discovered ramen as a film student at university in Kyoto, falling for the city's celebrated bowls. He went on to train for roughly six years at らぁ麺 とうひち, a Michelin Bib Gourmand shop often hailed as one of Kyoto's very best, before becoming its first graduate to o

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef fell for Kyoto's celebrated ramen while a university student in the city, and went on to train for roughly six years at らぁ麺 とうひち, a Michelin Bib Gourmand shop often hailed as one of Kyoto's very best, before opening his own place. He launched Fujitora in the quiet Uzumasa-Yasui neighborhood near Hanazono Station in September 2023.
chef_bio.philosophy: Do as much as possible by hand, every day. Fuchihara makes not only the soup but the noodles and the wontons himself, milling fresh noodles each morning so the wheat aroma is at its peak, and is described as cooking earnestly and meticulously. The shop carries forward the とうひち lineage while standing on its own.
chef_bio.specialty: A clean yet deep chicken-forward bowl: the broth is built from jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork and pork back bone into a clear (chintan) soup. The shoyu version is finished with a tare blending six soy sauces (with Wakayama's Kaneiwa and Hyogo's Suehiro shoyu at its base) and served over straight, sharply-cut house-made thin noodles; a shio version runs alongside it. The tantanmen, with its own slightly flatter house-made noodles, sesame-rich and gradually building heat brightened by cilantro, has become a signature draw in its own right.

anecdotes.text: The chef trained about six years at the Michelin-listed らぁ麺 とうひち before going independent, carrying its lineage into his own kitchen.
He first fell for ramen while a student at university in Kyoto, working part-time at Tohichi during those years, which set him on the path to opening his own shop.
On opening, congratulatory flowers arrived from his master chef and other prominent figures in the ramen world, reflecting high expectations for the new shop.
He hand-makes the noodles fresh every morning in the shop to maximize their wheat fragrance, and also makes the wontons by hand.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are milled and cut fresh in the shop every morning, so wheat flour handling is central to the kitchen and cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tare blends six soy sauces (based on Kaneiwa of Wakayama and Suehiro of Hyogo) — standard wheat-fermented Japanese shoyu — so the soy ramen is wheat-based at its core.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork and pork back bone and finished with chicken oil; toppings include pork chashu, ground pork and meat-filled wontons, so no bowl is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small Japanese neighborhood ramen counter with no indication of English menus or staff, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the ticket/menu will smooth the visit.

noodles.notes: House-made fresh daily using Kyoto Izawa Seifun wheat for a strong toasted-wheat aroma. Slightly flattened (hira-uchi) straight noodles with a clean rectangular cut, smooth tsurutsuru throat-feel and a chewy yet crisp bite. The cold shoyu bowl uses a thicker, linguine-like flat noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Shoyu Raamen (special soy-sauce chicken ramen) — the flagship; the Shio version showcases the same delicate chicken broth, and the soupless tantanmen is the third pillar to chase.
ramen.chashu: Two low-temperature (sous-vide) cuts served together: a thin rolled pork belly and thicker slices of shoulder loin, cut from Kagoshima kurobuta pork, kept rosy and tender rather than heavily braised.

gf.note: Wheat house-made noodles (kansui) and a soy-sauce tare built on a six-soy blend; the broth also carries dried-fish dashi. No rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork bones and dried fish, with pork chashu and wonton throughout. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A refined chicken-forward shoyu in the modern delicate (tanrei) lineage carried out of a celebrated Kyoto kitchen: clear amber soup from black jidori chicken and kurobuta bones, lifted by dried fish and a layered six-soy tare, with a whisper of yuzu and chicken fat. The soul is restraint and clarity — you taste the bird and the soy, not grease — while a separately built soupless tantanmen shows the same hand with sesame and spice.

places.name: 麺処 かわ乃 (Mendokoro Kawano)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The noodles are wheat (Hokkaido kitahonami wheat flour / whole-wheat noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker) and the duck broth is finished with soy-sauce tare, which is virtually always wheat-based in Japan. Duck clear-broth ramen of this style is not celiac-safe; no gluten-free menu, no dedicated prep, and no indication staff accommodate gluten. A strict celiac should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Every signature bowl is built on duck — duck stock (made from duck and water), low-temperature duck loin, and duck fat — and the menu also features shellfish (clam/scallop) variants, flavored egg, and duck rice/yukhoe bowls. There are no vegetable- or plant-based broth options listed.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (LO 14:30), 17:30-20:30 per one listing; another lists 11:00-15:00 (LO 14:30) / 17:00-22:00 (LO 21:30). Sat/Sun reported as 11:00-20:30. Holidays irregular.
places.cuisine: Duck soba / duck-and-shellfish chuka-soba / duck mazesoba
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kawano is the younger sibling of 貝だし麺きた田, the popular shellfish-broth noodle shop just north of Kyoto Station. When the team opened this second place near Nijo Station in February 2025 (in a space that previously held a light-style ramen shop), they deliberately did not repeat th

roles: owner/operator (same team as 貝だし麺きた田)
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto — sister shop to 貝だし麺きた田 near Kyoto Station; this branch sits in the Jurakukai/Senbon-Marutamachi neighborhood near Nijo Station.
chef_bio.background: Kawano is the younger sibling of 貝だし麺きた田, the popular shellfish-broth noodle shop just north of Kyoto Station. When the team opened this second place near Nijo Station in February 2025, they deliberately did not repeat the shellfish formula — they set out to 'try something unconventional, with a Japanese sensibility,' and chose duck as their canvas.
chef_bio.philosophy: Restraint and refinement over heavy ramen convention — the kitchen treats the duck like washoku, controlling temperature and timing so the meat stays tender and pink, and lets the broth deepen as the noodles slowly release into it.
chef_bio.specialty: Kamo soba (duck noodles): a clear, delicate duck broth made from just duck and water, served over Hokkaido wheat noodles and topped with low-temperature duck loin, white and green scallion, and bamboo shoots, finished with duck fat. Variations include duck-and-shellfish Chinese noodles (shoyu or shio), duck mazesoba, and duck tsukemen, plus duck rice and duck-yukhoe bowls.

anecdotes.text: The owners describe the project as wanting to challenge something unorthodox while keeping a Japanese aesthetic, choosing duck precisely because the sister shop already owns the shellfish lane.
The duck broth is built from only duck and water; the flavor is meant to evolve in the bowl as the noodles dissolve into it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The duck broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce tare; Japanese soy sauce is almost always wheat-fermented, so the broth should be treated as containing wheat/gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from duck and water and finished with duck fat; bowls include duck loin and shellfish (clam/scallop) variants, so there is no plant-based base in the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood noodle shop with ticket-machine ordering, so a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way, though pointing at the machine gets you fed.

noodles.notes: Thin, faintly wavy straight noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker (Menya Teigaku), milled from whole-grain wheat; prized for a crisp, snappy, clean bite (patsu-patsu) that suits the delicate clear duck broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kamo soba (鴨そば) — the pure duck bowl: a clear duck-and-water broth finished with mellow shoyu tare and a slick of duck fat, topped with low-temperature duck loin. The special (tokusei) adds extra duck chashu and an ajitama. The duck-and-shellfish chuka soba (鴨と貝の中華そば, shoyu or shio) is the richer alternative.
ramen.chashu: Low-temperature-cooked duck loin (kamo rosu), sliced thin and rosy — tender and moist with a refined, gamey-sweet duck umami rather than pork. The special version piles on extra slices; a braised pork-shoulder chashu also accompanies the tokusei.
notable_toppings: two cuts of negi (sliced rings + fine white shreds)

gf.note: Wheat ramen noodles and a shoyu (soy/wheat) tare make these bowls unsuitable for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. The shio version still uses wheat noodles.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on duck stock (with shellfish and beef bone in the chuka soba), finished with duck fat and topped with duck and pork; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto specialist that builds its soul on duck rather than the usual pork or chicken: the flagship kamo soba is drawn from duck and water alone, a crystal-clear chintan whose gentle, refined sweetness is restrained rather than cloying — you taste the bird, clean and direct. A second line deepens the duck with clam, scallop and beef bone for a fuller, shoyu-or-shio chuka soba, marking it as a refined-tradition (tanrei) duck house rather than any regional gotōchi style.

places.name: 熟成麺屋 神来 西院本店 (Jukusei Menya Jinrai Saiin Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle shoyu ramen shop. The noodles are custom-made wheat noodles from Menya Teikaku, the broth is a soy-sauce-based chicken-and-pork-bone base (soy sauce tare almost always contains wheat), and signature sides are wheat-batter karaage and fried rice. No gluten-free options, no celiac-safe preparation, and no dedicated equipment are indicated anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The core product is an animal-based bowl: chicken-and-pork-bone (鶏ガラ豚骨) broth with floating back-fat, topped with house-made chashu (pork), menma, and Kujo negi. Soy-sauce tare and animal broth are intrinsic to every ramen here. No vegan or plant-based ramen is offered, and no vegan preparation is mentioned. Not suitable for vegans.
places.cuisine: Chicken-bone & tonkotsu shoyu ramen, rich back-fat (seabura) style
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Jinrai opened its Saiin flagship on February 2, 2012, right by Saiin station, and grew from this single counter into a small Kyoto-area group of six shops (Ichijoji, two Aeon Mall locations, the Kyoto Racecourse stand, and Aeon Mall Takanohara in Kizugawa). Despite the expansion,

chef_bio.background: Jinrai opened its Saiin flagship on February 2, 2012, right by Saiin station, and grew from this single counter into a small Kyoto-area group of six shops (Ichijoji, Aeon Mall locations, the Kyoto Racecourse stand, and Aeon Mall Takanohara in Kizugawa). Despite the expansion, the Saiin honten remains the original neighborhood ramen-ya that built the name.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner frames running the shop as 'enjoying the business' — treating service as a kind of friendly competition where staff read each customer and move as one unified team to keep the line moving and the bowls hot.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a soy-sauce-based chicken-and-pork-bone ramen with a layer of aromatic back-fat (seabura) floating on top — rich and kotteri but described by regulars as surprisingly easy to drink. It is finished with Kyoto's traditional Kujo negi and house-made chashu and menma, on thin, springy custom wheat noodles made to order by the respected noodle maker Menya Teikaku. The Stamina Ramen (Chinese cabbage, thin pork, chives) is another house draw.

anecdotes.text: Beyond ramen, the shop is famous for two sides that locals come back for: karaage (fried chicken) marinated in a secret soy-based sauce, and a fried rice (yakimeshi) that staff reportedly need rigorous training to cook properly.
Reviewers describe it as a back-fat (seabura) shoyu shop that somehow stays 'light' and keeps a constant flow of customers — an oddly more-ish bowl that explains why the small shop is always busy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a soy-sauce-based (醤油ベース) bowl on custom wheat noodles; soy-sauce tare of this style is virtually always made with wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen is built on chicken-and-pork-bone broth with back-fat, pork chashu, and menma — no plant-based broth is offered or separated.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, no-frills local ramen counter where ordering is quick and Japanese-only; come prepared to order simply and pay cash, ideally with a guide or phrase help if you don't speak Japanese.

noodles.notes: Thin straight noodles custom-made by a renowned Kyoto noodle producer; fragrant with wheat aroma and a springy, firm bite (コシ) despite their slender gauge, built to be slurped fast and to sit lightly in the soy backfat broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 特製炙りチャーシューメン (Tokusei Aburi Chashu-men) — the standard chicken-and-pork-bone soy backfat ramen loaded with extra torched chashu, Kujo negi and house menma; the plain ラーメン is the everyday entry point.
ramen.chashu: House-made pork chashu: blocks slow-simmered with aromatic vegetables and steeped in the shop's own soy marinade; the premium bowl serves it aburi (lightly torched/seared) so the edges caramelize and the fat softens over the hot broth.

gf.note: Wheat ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare make this unsafe for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with pork backfat, a soy tare, and pork chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook Kyoto se-abura shoyu bowl: a chicken-and-pork-bone soy broth crowned with a float of fragrant pork backfat, yet it drinks far lighter than it looks — clean and almost Kansai-udon-dashi in spirit rather than heavy. The backfat lends koku and gloss while the soy tare keeps the soul of the soup squarely in Kyoto's old-school back-fat lineage, with thin springy noodles and sweet Kujo negi sealing the local accent.

places.name: 麺家 しょうりん (Menya Shourin)
places.gf_detail: A chicken-pork paitan ramen shop with no gluten-free offerings. Noodles are wheat (sourced from the well-known Teigaku/棣鄂 noodle maker), broth and tare are standard, and mazesoba/fried-chicken sides involve wheat. Not suitable for celiacs; no GF accommodation noted on any source.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on an animal base — a rich emulsified chicken-and-pork-bone (鶏豚骨) white broth, with meat toppings and a chicken paste stirred in mid-meal. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options; sides include fried chicken and rice. No plant-based menu noted.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, Kamigamo (Nikenchaya / north of Kamigamo Shrine, near Kyoto Sangyo University)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Shourin opened on February 10, 2020 in the leafy Kamigamo district of Kyoto's Kita Ward, just north of Kamigamo Shrine near Kyoto Sangyo University. The owner trained at the well-known Kyoto ramen group Kirameki no Tori (キラメキノトリ) before striking out on his own.

chef_bio.background: Menya Shourin opened on February 10, 2020 in the leafy Kamigamo district of Kyoto's Kita Ward, just north of Kamigamo Shrine near Kyoto Sangyo University. The owner trained at the well-known Kyoto ramen group Kirameki no Tori (キラメキノトリ) before striking out on his own.
chef_bio.philosophy: Careful broth-making aimed at a rich umami with no off-flavors (雑味のない、まろやかな味), served in a clean, café-like space welcoming to students, families and locals.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the niku-paitan soba (肉白湯そば) — an emulsified chicken-and-pork-bone (鶏豚骨) white broth that is full-bodied yet clean and mellow, finished with coarse black pepper. A special chicken paste is meant to be stirred in partway through to deepen the flavor. Straight, medium-thin noodles come from the respected Teigaku (棣鄂) noodle maker. The menu rounds out with a tori-chuka soba and a spicy kara-niku mazesoba.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in a nature-rich setting north of Kamigamo Shrine; counter seats wrap around an open kitchen and the room is bright and clean, almost like a café.
The owner is an alumnus of the popular Kirameki no Tori ramen group and opened Shourin in February 2020.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard ramen tare and an emulsified chicken-pork broth are used; no allergen breakdown is published, but a wheat-based shoyu tare is typical for this style.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are animal-based (chicken-pork bone broth, meat toppings, chicken paste); no vegan or vegetarian preparation exists, so cross-contact is unavoidable for plant-based diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A bright, casual neighborhood ramen counter popular with students and families; no English service is indicated, so a Japanese-speaking guide or translation help makes ordering smoothest.

noodles.notes: Straight, medium-thin gauge from the venerable Kyoto noodle maker Teiigaku (棣鄂); wheaty and supple with good throat-feel, boiled slightly firm to stand up to the thick emulsified broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 肉白湯そば (Niku Paitan Soba) — a thick, emulsified chicken-and-pork-bone white broth finished with a special chicken paste you stir in to deepen the richness, topped with a pepper accent.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin — thick-cut, tender and moist, drinking up the broth's essence; rich, chew-rewarding meat rather than fatty belly or aburi char.
notable_toppings: special whipped chicken paste (stirred into the broth for extra koku)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is an animal paitan from chicken and pork bones, dressed with pork chashu and a chicken paste; no plant-broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on tori-paitan: instead of the city's old-guard se-abura shoyu, this bowl pours a glossy, lip-coating white broth simmered thick from chicken and pork bones, its richness dialed up tableside by stirring in a savory chicken paste. The lineage you taste is contemporary kotteri paitan, smoothed and refined rather than gamey.

places.name: 麺や 賀ら茂ん (Menya Garamon)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional chuka soba / seb-abura shoyu ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat, over a pork-bone, chicken, kombu and seafood broth. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or accommodations are mentioned in any source, and there is no indication the kitchen handles celiac-level allergen requests. Not suitable for anyone needing to avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on a pork-bone, chicken and seafood (fish/kombu) broth and are topped with seared chashu pork. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options described anywhere; the menu centers on animal-based broths and meat toppings.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 11:30-18:00; Sun & holidays 11:30-15:00; closed Wednesday. Hours can change month to month; the owner posts a monthly calendar on X (Twitter).
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, near Misonobashi / Kamigamo, closest station area K
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Garamon began life not as a ramen shop but as a charcoal-grill yakitori izakaya called 炭焼賀ら茂ん (Sumiyaki Garamon). Tucked into a quiet residential block south of Misonobashi near Kamigamo, it later changed its business format to a ramen shop while keeping the same name and its sho

chef_bio.background: Garamon began life not as a ramen shop but as a charcoal-grill yakitori izakaya called 炭焼賀ら茂ん (Sumiyaki Garamon). Tucked into a quiet residential block south of Misonobashi near Kamigamo, it later changed its business format to a ramen shop while keeping the same name and its shoes-off, lived-in izakaya feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: An owner-run, single-counter shop designed so one person can comfortably walk in and eat well for cheap; quiet, residential, no-frills, with the kitchen's attention going into the dashi-forward broth and the seared pork rather than spectacle.
chef_bio.specialty: Two bowls anchor the menu, both at the same modest price: a lighter 中華そば (chuka soba) and a richer 背脂醤油 (sebabura shoyu) with back-fat and added oil. The broth blends pork bone, chicken carcass, kombu and seafood, leaning unusually far toward a Japanese dashi character so that it edges toward udon/soba territory while keeping ramen body and sweetness. The signature topping is a thick slice of aburi (seared) chashu, individually torched and brushed with glaze for aroma.

anecdotes.text: The ramen concept reportedly grew out of an off-menu (裏メニュー) chicken-based chuka soba that the owner served back when the place was still a yakitori izakaya.
You take your shoes off to step up to the small L-shaped counter, a holdover from its izakaya days that gives the room a homey, almost living-room atmosphere.
Tabletop condiments let you tune your bowl as you go: minced garlic, spicy miso, ichimi chili powder and black pepper.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's two core bowls are shoyu-based ramen (chuka soba and sebabura shoyu). Soy-sauce tare in Japanese ramen is conventionally brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tare is mentioned by any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made from pork bone, chicken, kombu and seafood and the kitchen is built around animal-based ramen, so vegan diners face unavoidable contact with meat and fish stock throughout.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny shoes-off counter shop run by its owner in a quiet residential pocket; a little Japanese and easygoing solo-diner etiquette go a long way, and English is unlikely.

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight noodles with a clean wheat aroma and a satisfying bite; their slim profile lets the soy broth cling well. The lighter chuka soba and the back-fat shoyu share the same noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Seabura Shoyu (背脂醤油) — the rich version of the house soy bowl, ladled with pork back-fat (and a touch of chili oil) over the same pork-chicken-kombu-seafood stock; order the aburi chashu as a 1-slice add-on. A lighter Chuka Soba (same broth, fat skimmed and finished with scallion oil) and a Seabura Miso round out the menu.
ramen.chashu: Large, tender sheets of pork with a creamy, sweet fat layer, finished by blow-torching the surface (aburi) for a fragrant, lightly charred top while staying soft and almost crumbly inside.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare made with regional soy sauce; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken carcass and seafood/kombu dashi, the tare is animal-laced shoyu, and the bowls carry pork back-fat and torched pork chashu. No plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood bowl in the Kyoto se-abura shoyu lineage, where a clear pork-chicken-and-seafood broth is seasoned with a regional Kyotango soy sauce that gives a deep, faintly bitter savor lifted by a whisper of ginger. The rich version crowns that clear soup with melting beads of pork back-fat — soul of the city's old-school se-abura shoyu, where fat rides on clarity rather than clouding it.

places.name: 麺屋 坦坦軒 (Menya Tantanken)
places.gf_detail: This is a tantanmen specialist: wheat ramen noodles in a pork-and-chicken sesame broth, with shrimp wonton on the menu — not suitable for celiac diners. The noodles and the sesame/soy-based seasonings both contain wheat, and no gluten-free preparation or menu has been found. Anyone strictly avoiding gluten should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: The bowls are built on an animal base — the broth is described as chicken-forward with a little pork, and the menu features shrimp wonton. No vegetarian or vegan version has been found. This is not a vegan-friendly shop.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-21:00 (last order ~30 min before closing), closed Wed
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This Kinugasa noodle shop opened in October 2025 in the very space that was long home to a beloved local Chinese restaurant known for its tantanmen. The earlier shop had built a quiet reputation around the neighborhood for its dan dan noodles; when the room was reborn as a dedica

chef_bio.background: This Kinugasa noodle shop opened in October 2025 in the very space that was long home to a beloved local Chinese restaurant known for its tantanmen. The earlier shop had built a quiet reputation around the neighborhood for its dan dan noodles; when the room was reborn as a dedicated tantanmen specialist, the recipe was carried forward and refined, and the dining room was brightened into something that feels a touch more like a polished Chinese restaurant than a quick noodle stop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing the fiery, mala-heavy style, the shop leans into a mild, comforting take on tantanmen and puts the heat in the diner's hands: the tables are set with chili oil, garlic chili oil, chili powder, Sichuan pepper, black pepper, salt, soy sauce and vinegar so each guest can dial the bowl up to taste.
chef_bio.specialty: The house bowl is a deliberately non-spicy, Kyoto-style tantanmen, offered both with broth (汁あり) and brothless (汁なし). The soup is described as smooth and creamy with a gentle sweetness, built on a chicken-forward base with just a little pork and vegetable depth, and served essentially without heat or numbing tongue-tingle. A shrimp wonton noodle bowl rounds out the short menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies the former premises of a neighborhood Chinese restaurant that had earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand nod and was locally loved for its tantanmen; the new shop continues and refines that signature bowl while opening for lunch as well as evenings.

The tantanmen arrives with effectively no spiciness, and the table is stocked with a full lineup of condiments (chili oil, garlic chili oil, chili powder, Sichuan pepper, black pepper, salt, soy sauce, vinegar) so guests can build their own level of heat and aroma.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A tantanmen specialist serving wheat noodles in sesame/soy-seasoned animal broth alongside shrimp wonton; gluten is central to the menu and no gluten-free handling has been described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is provided as a table condiment and the seasoning base is sesame/soy-style; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tamari is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is animal-based (chicken with some pork) and shrimp wonton is on the menu, so the kitchen is not set up for vegan separation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, welcoming neighborhood noodle shop with polite service where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through; no English menu has been confirmed.

places.name: あらじん (Arajin)
places.gf_detail: Ramen here is wheat noodles (medium-thick noodles from a noodle factory) in pork-bone broth flavored with miso or soy tare, which typically contains wheat. No gluten-free options and no indication of allergy accommodation; not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A pork-bone (tonkotsu) ramen specialist; the signature bowls are built on rich pork broth with chashu, egg, and back fat. There is no vegan or vegetable-based ramen offering.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–14:30 (lunch), 18:00–21:00 (dinner); closed Tuesdays. Note: some sources list the dinner close as 22:00.
places.cuisine: Rich miso tonkotsu / soy sauce tonkotsu ramen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Arajin opened on September 10, 2014, on a corner of the Ichijoji intersection — one of Kyoto's most intensely competitive ramen districts. It is a compact, clean, counter-only shop run as essentially a one-person operation, where guests buy a ticket from the machine before orderi

chef_bio.background: Arajin opened on September 10, 2014, on a corner of the Ichijoji intersection — one of Kyoto's most intensely competitive ramen districts. It is a compact, clean, counter-only shop (about 12 seats) run as essentially a one-person operation, where guests buy a ticket from the machine before ordering.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking leans on careful craftsmanship — a broth made rich without becoming heavy, and medium-thick, hand-kneaded-style noodles specially ordered from an established noodle maker for good chew and slurp. The result is described as crave-worthy and habit-forming.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the rich miso tonkotsu ramen (濃厚味噌豚骨ラーメン): a creamy, full-bodied pork-bone broth carrying the depth and aroma of miso, finished with back fat, and rounded out with green onion, thin-sliced chashu, half a seasoned egg, nori, menma, and sesame. A shoyu tonkotsu version is the other pillar of the menu.

anecdotes.text: The medium-thick noodles are specially ordered from an established noodle factory, chosen for a firm, chewy texture that stands up to the thick broth.
A side of charred char-siu mayo rice (炙りチャーマヨ飯) rounds out the menu alongside the ramen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tonkotsu bowl uses a soy-sauce-based tare, which in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat; the miso bowl likewise sits on wheat noodles and pork broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen is built on pork-bone broth with back fat and chashu; broth and noodles share the same single-operator kitchen, so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small ticket-machine counter ramen shop run by one person in a busy ramen district; basic Japanese and the usual order-by-ticket flow make it easiest, though the routine is simple enough to manage by pointing.

places.name: 中華そば たく味 (Chuka-soba Takumi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional chuka-soba (ramen) shop: wheat noodles in a pork-bone/soy-sauce broth, with a wheat-based shoyu tare. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. A celiac or strictly gluten-free diner cannot safely eat the ramen here, and nothing on the menu (ramen, tsukemen, gyoza) is gluten-free. Treat as not suitable.
places.vegan_detail: The house broth is built on long-simmered pork bones (tonkotsu) and the bowls are crowned with chashu pork, so the core offering is not vegan or vegetarian. There is no listed plant-based broth or noodle option. A vegan diner has no suitable bowl here.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu-Sun 11:00-24:00 (some sources say until 2:00 the next day), closed Wed. New Year holiday Jan 1-3.
places.cuisine: Tonkotsu-soy sauce and soy sauce chuka-soba
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood ramen counter tucked near the Hyakumanben crossing west of Kyoto University, Takumi has been feeding locals and students for close to two decades. It is the kind of unpretentious machi-chuka spot you slip into for a satisfying, affordable bowl, including late at ni

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood ramen counter tucked near the Hyakumanben crossing west of Kyoto University, Takumi has been feeding locals and students for close to two decades. It is the kind of unpretentious machi-chuka spot you slip into for a satisfying, affordable bowl, including late at night.
chef_bio.philosophy: Comfort over flash: carefully drawn-out broth, an almost extravagant amount of negi, and a friendly counter feel that has kept regulars coming back for years.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the Takumi Chashu-men: a bowl piled with thin slices of chashu pork arranged like overflowing flower petals, set over a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy-sauce broth that is at once viscous and mellow, without any heavy or off-putting edge. A generous heap of Kujo-style green onion crowns the bowl, cutting the richness so it eats clean and bright. A lighter, clear shoyu chuka-soba is also offered for those who want something more delicate.

anecdotes.text: The shop's most-loved dish is the Takumi Chashu-men, known for chashu sliced thin and fanned out like flower petals spilling over the rim of the bowl, atop a creamy yet clean pork-bone soy-sauce broth.
Open for nearly 20 years and beloved by the neighborhood, the shop runs late, making it a go-to for a post-drinking bowl, and the owner's warm manner is part of why regulars keep returning.

The defining feature is the mountain of green onion: onion lovers rejoice, while those who dislike negi are warned to take note, since the abundant Kujo negi lightens the broth and is the bowl's signature.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard ramen tare for a shop of this style is made with wheat-containing soy sauce, and no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is long-simmered pork bone and bowls are topped with chashu pork, so all cooking equipment and broth contact animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locals-and-students counter shop near Kyoto University with no English presence; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will make ordering smooth.

places.name: 鳳舞楼 京都本店 (Houmairou Kyoto Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature からしそば and other noodle dishes use wheat noodles, and the chicken-and-kombu ankake (gravy) tare is seasoned with soy sauce, which is standard wheat-brewed shoyu. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or dedicated prep is mentioned by any source. A celiac diner should treat this as not suitable; even non-noodle dishes (shumai, sweet-and-sour pork, spring rolls) are likely to involve soy sauce and shared wheat-handling surfaces. Ask only if cross-contamination risk is acceptable to you.
places.vegan_detail: This is a Kyoto-Chinese house built around a chicken-bone and kombu broth, with pork (chashu, sweet-and-sour pork, shumai) and seafood (shrimp) running through the signature dishes. The from-scratch ankake gravy that defines the からしそば is chicken-based, so even vegetable-heavy plates are not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian menu is advertised. Not suitable for vegans; a strict vegetarian would need to ask, but options would be very limited.
places.hours_raw: [Mon, Thu–Sun] 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); [Tue, Wed] closed (may open on major holidays).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 鳳舞楼 carries forward one of Kyoto's most beloved culinary lineages: that of 鳳舞 (Houmai), the legendary Kyoto-Chinese restaurant that closed in 2009 after generations of family celebrations were held within its walls. That tradition traces back to Takahashi Kakitsu (高華吉), the chef

chef_bio.background: 鳳舞楼 carries forward one of Kyoto's most beloved culinary lineages: that of 鳳舞 (Houmai), the legendary Kyoto-Chinese restaurant opened by chef 高華吉 (Kō Kakichi) in 1967 and closed in 2009, within whose walls generations of Kyoto families held their celebrations. Kyoto-style Chinese itself traces back further, to 支那料理ハマムラ (Hamamura), the city's first Chinese restaurant, opened in Gion in 1924 by 濱村保三; it was there that Kō Kakichi honed the approach of lightening Chinese dishes with Japanese dashi to suit Kyoto's refined, geisha-district palate before founding his own 鳳舞. After the original Houmai shuttered, its apprentices fanned out across the city to keep the style alive, a network locals call the '鳳舞系' (Houmai lineage). 鳳舞楼 is part of that lineage, now settled in a bright, modern room steps from Imadegawa Station with views toward the Kyoto Imperial Palace and Doshisha University.
chef_bio.philosophy: あっさり上品な京中華 — light, refined Kyoto Chinese. The cooking leans on kelp-and-chicken broth for depth instead of heaviness, an approach meant for unhurried, multi-generational meals rather than a fast bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: からしそば (karashi soba) — the dish that made the Houmai name. Rather than a soup noodle, it arrives closer to a glistening seafood-and-vegetable ankake over noodles, the gravy built on chicken bones and kombu dashi, with Japanese mustard worked through so the heat rises gently up the nose rather than hitting the tongue. Alongside it the kitchen turns out refined Kyoto-Chinese standards: shumai, spring rolls, sweet-and-sour dishes, and chashu noodles.

anecdotes.text: The からしそば has drawn national attention as a uniquely Kyoto noodle dish, featured on television as a regional specialty that puzzles outsiders and delights locals.
Longtime Kyotoites describe coming to the Houmai lineage for the same celebratory family dinners they remember at the original 鳳舞 before it closed in 2009.
The Houmai style is defined by a chicken-and-kombu broth gravy — the key difference from mainstream Chinese cooking — a technique associated with 鳳舞 founder 高華吉 (Kō Kakichi).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken/kombu ankake gravy are central to the menu; no gluten-free preparation or separate handling is described by any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The savory ankake gravy is built on chicken-and-kombu dashi finished with soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-brewed, and no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core broth and gravy are chicken-and-kombu based, and signature dishes feature pork and shrimp; no plant-based broth or vegan menu is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, family-friendly Kyoto-Chinese dining room near Imadegawa Station where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the famous からしそば will carry you comfortably; menus and staff are oriented to local diners rather than English speakers.

places.name: 総代 麺家 あくた川 (Sodai Menya Akutagawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a Yokohama iekei (家系) ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat noodles in a pork-bone-and-chicken-bone shoyu broth whose tare is soy-sauce-based (wheat-containing shoyu), topped with pork chashu. No gluten-free noodles, broth, or accommodations are mentioned anywhere. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A tonkotsu (pork-bone) and chicken-bone broth shop. The signature bowl includes pork chashu, and the broth itself is animal-based, so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The free rice and bean-sprout sides are not a meaningful vegan path here. No plant-based ramen offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-23:00 (closing-day info varies: Tabelog lists daily 11:00-15:00 and 17:00-23:00, while one guide source notes closed Sundays)
places.neighborhood: Tanaka-Monzencho, Sakyo Ward (Hyakumanben / Demachiyanagi, in front of Kyoto University's main gate)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founder Akutagawa and his colleague Hirayama (who became a later shop manager) met working at Ramen Musashi-ya, the parent shop in the iekei (家系) tradition. The two set out to bring real Yokohama-style iekei ramen to Kyoto, scouring the city by bicycle until they found the spot o

chef_bio.origin: Yokohama iekei lineage via Ramen Musashi-ya (ラーメン武蔵屋)
chef_bio.background: Founder Akutagawa and his colleague Hirayama (who became a later shop owner) met working at Ramen Musashi-ya, the parent shop in the iekei (家系) tradition. The two set out to bring real Yokohama-style iekei ramen to Kyoto, scouring the city by bicycle until they found the spot on Imadegawa, just east of the Hyakumanben intersection by Kyoto University's main gate, opening on September 8, 2018.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep it honest and accessible: Akutagawa held the bowl to the parent shop's price point even as he sourced ingredients carefully, absorbing higher costs through tight management so students and locals could afford a serious bowl. He framed the work around how much he could give back to customers.
chef_bio.specialty: The standard bowl (並ラーメン) of rich pork-and-chicken-bone tonkotsu-shoyu broth, built iekei-style by continuously replenishing the stock (継ぎ足し). It comes with thick noodles, thick-cut pork loin chashu, spinach, and sheets of nori, with free bean-sprout namul and refillable rice on the side. Diners are asked their preferences for noodle firmness, broth strength, and back fat.

anecdotes.text: Akutagawa and his former Musashi-ya colleague searched Kyoto by bicycle for the right property before settling on the Imadegawa location near Kyoto University.
He deliberately matched the parent shop's low base price (about 650 yen for a standard ramen) despite costlier ingredients, managing costs elsewhere so the bowl stayed affordable.
Since opening, the shop has drawn near-constant lines and become a go-to among Kyoto University students at the Hyakumanben corner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Iekei ramen here uses wheat noodles boiled in shared water and a soy-sauce (wheat shoyu) tare, so gluten is intrinsic to the dish and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone soy-sauce (豚骨醤油) base typical of iekei, meaning wheat-containing shoyu is in the tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-bone based with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or noodles are available, so the dish is inherently non-vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A fast, no-frills student ramen joint with a ticket machine; ordering is smooth with a few words of Japanese (staff ask noodle firmness, broth richness, and back-fat level), but no special accommodation should be expected.

places.name: 夜鳴きや (Yonakiya)
places.gf_detail: A classic wheat-noodle chuka soba: the noodles are wheat, the soy tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu, and the broth uses pork and chicken. No gluten-free menu, no published allergen handling, and no indication of any wheat-free preparation. Not safe for a celiac diner.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop whose single signature bowl is built on a pork-bone and chicken (豚骨・鶏ガラ) broth finished with sweet pork back-fat (背脂) and a soy tare. The menu centers on this one animal-based ramen, so there is no vegan option. A plant-based diner cannot eat here.
places.hours_raw: Tuesday-Friday 11:00-15:00; closed Monday, Saturday, Sunday. Closes early when the day's noodles/ingredients run out. Note: some guides list a longer through-service window of 11:00-18:00 until sell-out; the shop sells out frequently, so arriving early is advised.
places.neighborhood: Otsuka-Minamimizocho, Yamashina Ward (Higashino Stn / Keihan Yamashina area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yonakiya is one of the old guard of Kyoto ramen, a shop that has stood along the Nara-kaido road in the Otsuka district of Yamashina for decades and is treated as one of the originals of the city's ramen lineage. It was founded by the previous master and has since passed to the n

chef_bio.background: Yonakiya is one of the old guard of Kyoto ramen, a shop that has stood along the Nara-kaido road in the Otsuka district of Yamashina for decades and is treated as one of the originals of the city's ramen lineage. It was founded by the previous master and has since passed to the next generation, who carries on the same single-bowl tradition.
chef_bio.philosophy: A one-bowl shop in the truest sense: it does essentially one thing, the old Kyoto chuka soba, and has done it the same way long enough that decades of consistency on a single corner are taken as proof of the cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka soba: relatively thin, springy straight noodles in a soy-based broth drawn from pork bone and chicken (豚骨・鶏ガラ), topped with sweet pork back-fat (背脂) that floats on the surface. The clean, delicate dashi is carried by the strength of the soy tare, so a bowl that looks rich eats surprisingly light. The shop is known for slicing its big chashu fresh to order.

anecdotes.text: Regulars treat it as a kind of phantom shop because it serves only in daytime and closes the moment the day's noodles run out, so getting a bowl is partly a matter of timing.
The founding master handed the shop to the next generation, who now runs it while keeping the same chuka soba at the center of the menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-product wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free menu or published allergen handling; wheat noodles, wheat-based soy tare and a shared broth/water setup make cross-contamination effectively certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining seasoning is a soy (醤油) tare, which in a traditional Kyoto chuka soba shop is virtually always standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single bowl is built on pork-bone and chicken broth with pork back-fat and chashu; there is no plant-based preparation and no separate vegan handling.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, long-running local counter shop with a Japanese-only menu and no English support; a few words of Japanese and an easygoing, order-and-eat attitude will serve you best.

places.name: メントメシ ザコヤ (Mentomeshi Zakoya)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop with house-made noodles, and the tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodle, or allergen accommodation was found on any page. Not suitable for celiacs. The broth is fish-based rather than pork, but the noodles and tare make the bowl unsafe for gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Despite being meat-free (no pork or chicken), every dish is built on fish and seafood — dashi from sea bream, scallop, ribbonfish and dried sardines, with fish toppings and a seasoned egg. Not vegan or vegetarian.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mentomeshi Zakoya is part of the Zakoya group (ZACOYA), a Kyoto seafood-focused hospitality brand running a cluster of fish-centric restaurants around Karasuma, Sanjo and Gion. The ramen concept began at a Hyakumanben main store and a Gion branch; both have since closed, and the

chef_bio.background: Mentomeshi Zakoya is part of the Zakoya group (ZACOYA), a Kyoto seafood-focused hospitality brand running a cluster of fish-centric restaurants around Karasuma, Sanjo and Gion. The ramen concept began at a Hyakumanben main store and a Gion branch; both have since closed, and the shop relocated to its current Higashidoin-Rokkaku spot near Karasuma-Oike in February 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately meat-free take on ramen: the whole bowl is built from the sea rather than pork or chicken, staying true to the Zakoya group's fish-forward identity. A playful touch is the chopstick rest - a dried sardine you can eat on its own or drop into the broth.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is ryoshi-soba (fisherman's soba), a clear, light seafood ramen offered in salt (shio) and soy (shoyu) versions. The dashi is drawn from premium fish - sea bream (tai), hamo (pike conger) and kinmedai (splendid alfonsino) - built on the Zakoya group's prized fish-scrap (ara) stock, with house-made noodles and toppings of marinated, dried and seared sea bream, yuan-grilled scallop, ribbonfish (tachiuo), a seasoned egg, minced red onion and nori. The companion ryoshi-meshi rice bowl (about 1,200 yen for the regular size, 2,500 yen for the premium), with around a dozen seafood ingredients, is eaten in four stages: as a seafood bowl, then with egg yolk, then poured with hot dashi as ochazuke, finished alongside sea bream sashimi.

anecdotes.text: The chopstick rest is an actual dried sardine (niboshi) that diners are invited to eat directly or add to the ramen broth.
The ryoshi-meshi rice bowl is designed to be enjoyed in four progressive stages, finishing as a dashi ochazuke with sea bream sashimi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat ramen noodles are the base of the dish; no gluten-free option was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A soy-sauce seasoned seafood bowl and shoyu ramen are on the menu; the tare is presumed conventional wheat-based soy sauce. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely seafood-based kitchen with dashi from sea bream, scallop, ribbonfish and dried sardines plus fish toppings and a seasoned egg, so any dish involves fish and likely egg.

positives.text: All dishes are meat-free (no pork or chicken), built entirely on fish and seafood.

cultural_comfort.note: A small Japanese-language counter-and-table ramen shop in a Kyoto business district; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, but don't expect English support.

places.name: 天下一品 総本店 (Tenkaippin Sohonten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The kotteri broth is built on chicken bones and vegetables, but the ramen is served with standard wheat noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare, and the kitchen also fries gyoza, karaage and other wheat items in shared equipment. No gluten-free noodles, broth, or accommodation is offered, and there is no dedicated fryer. A celiac diner cannot eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature broth is a long-simmered chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) and vegetable kotteri base, and the flagship-only specials are beef-tendon (牛すじ) ramen and pork rice (豚重). There are no plant-based broth, noodle, or topping options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-1:00 the next day (L.O. 0:45, 15 min before closing). No regular closing day (closed only on New Year's Day).
places.neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo Ward (Chayama / Kyoto University of the Arts Stn)
places.notes: This is the sohonten — the original headquarters — of Tenkaippin, the cult chicken-broth ramen chain that began in Kyoto. In November 1971, founder Tsutomu Kimura, having lost his job when the gallery company he worked for went bankrupt, had a friend build him a ramen cart out of

chef_bio.background: This is the sohonten — the original headquarters — of Tenkaippin, the cult chicken-broth ramen chain that began in Kyoto. In November 1971, founder Tsutomu Kimura, having lost his job when the gallery company he worked for went bankrupt, had a friend build him a ramen cart out of scrap metal and started pulling a yatai (food cart) around the Ginkaku-ji area. On opening night he reportedly sold only 11 bowls. He kept experimenting, and after three years and nine months he perfected the now-legendary kotteri broth. With the goodwill of a stone-mason whose lot he was using, the cart was fixed in place and grew into his first storefront in August 1975 — on this Ichijoji site, which remains the chain's spiritual home.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kimura's idea was a broth that is 'eaten, not drunk' — a slow-cooked chicken-and-vegetable kotteri so concentrated it behaves like a sauce. The beef-tendon special was developed personally by the chairman over many trial batches, balancing the sweet-savory tendon to harmonize with that signature soup, and the recipe is kept exclusive to the sohonten.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the kotteri (こってり) bowl: a thick, almost potage-like broth simmered from chicken bones (鶏ガラ) and eleven kinds of vegetables until it clings to the noodles — described as a soup you eat rather than drink, yet lighter on the palate than its richness suggests. The Ichijoji flagship is also the only Tenkaippin where you can order the chairman's own creations: 牛すじラーメン (sweet-savory braised beef-tendon ramen), 牛すじキムチラーメン, and the 豚重 pork-rice set.

anecdotes.text: Founder Tsutomu Kimura started in 1971 with a roughly 37,000-yen scrap-metal cart and sold just 11 bowls on his first night before refining the recipe over three years and nine months.

The flagship-only beef-tendon ramen was developed by chairman Kimura himself more than a decade ago; staff note the tendon seasoning is blended specifically to match the kotteri soup, and it became popular as soon as it appeared on the menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles and wheat-based shoyu tare are core to every bowl; the same kitchen also handles gyoza, karaage and fried rice, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard ramen tare is soy-sauce based, which in Japan is normally brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is simmered from chicken bones; flagship specials use beef tendon and pork, so no preparation surface or stock is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, high-volume chain flagship open until 1 a.m.; ordering is simple and tourist-friendly, but staff communication is mostly in Japanese so a few basic words or pointing at the menu goes a long way.

places.name: とことんとりコトコト (Tokoton Tori Kotokoto)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses wheat noodles (from Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku/棣鄂) and the broth is built on chicken and vegetables with a typical tare; donburi sets add chashu and other components. No gluten-free menu, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation is mentioned by any source. Treat as unsafe for celiac diners; ask in person if highly sensitive.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The signature bowl is a chicken-based paitan broth (chicken simmered with vegetables); donburi sets include house-made chashu, egg yolk and shrimp oil. Although a large quantity of vegetables goes into the broth, it is fundamentally animal-based. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
places.hours_raw: Tue, Fri-Sun: 11:00-15:00; Wed-Thu: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 15:00) and 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Monday. Lunch service daily except Monday; dinner service only on Wednesday and Thursday.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A husband-and-wife team who began as a pop-up in the Fukakusa area, running for about three months before opening this permanent shop near Toji Station in January 2021, taking over a former Chinese-restaurant space in Minami Ward. The couple draw on their own experience raising c

chef_bio.background: A husband-and-wife team who began as a pop-up in the Fukakusa area, running for about three months before opening this permanent shop near Toji Station in January 2021, taking over a former Chinese-restaurant space in Minami Ward. The couple draw on their own experience raising children to run a warm, family-friendly counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name itself is the philosophy: to thoroughly and patiently slow-simmer the chicken. The owners describe themselves as still refining the recipe, treating the bowl as a work in continual improvement.
chef_bio.specialty: An exceptionally concentrated chicken paitan (torigara hakutou) — the signature 'Tokoton Tori'. Chicken and a generous amount of vegetables are simmered thoroughly and gently for a long time (the literal meaning of the shop's name) into a broth so thick a ladle can stand upright in it. It is rich in collagen — enough that it sets when cooled — yet the vegetables keep the finish surprisingly clean rather than heavy. Flat noodles from Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku cling to the dense soup; sets pair the bowl with a donburi of house-made chashu, scallion, egg yolk and fried-onion shrimp oil, and fish-powder, chili and mazesoba variations are also offered.

anecdotes.text: The shop name spells out its method — 'tokoton' (thoroughly) plus 'tori' (chicken) simmered 'kotokoto' (gently bubbling away) — capturing the long, slow cook that gives the broth its body.
Started as a three-month pop-up in Fukakusa before settling into a former Chinese restaurant near Toji Station in January 2021.
The broth carries so much collagen that it solidifies when cooled, yet the vegetables simmered alongside the chicken keep it from feeling oily or cloying.

cultural_comfort.note: A small family-run counter ramen shop where ordering and a few words of Japanese will go further than English; a friendly, child-welcoming spot but not set up for English support.

places.name: 中華そば専門店 ふくなが (Chuka Soba Senmonten Fukunaga)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto-style rich-soy chuka soba shop. The noodles are wheat, the tare is a soy-based shoyu (almost certainly containing wheat), and the broth is a concentrated pork-bone (tonkotsu) stock. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodles, and no published allergen accommodation. Not suitable for anyone requiring strict celiac-level avoidance of wheat.
places.vegan_detail: Every signature bowl is built on an animal foundation: a rich pork-bone and soy broth, topped with chashu (pork). Side dishes (fried rice, gyoza, karaage, roast pork bowl) are also meat-based. The "京都タンメン" is a vegetable ramen but is served in the same animal broth, so it is not vegan. There are no published plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 10:00-15:00, 17:00-20:00 / Wed 10:00-15:00 / Sun 09:00-15:00 / closed Mon. Note: some sources say Fri and Sat evenings run late into the night (until 2:00 the next day, L.O. 1:30).
places.neighborhood: Muko City / Mukomachi (JR Mukomachi Stn / southwest Kyoto, Kuze, Minami Ward, Kyoto City)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Before opening his own shop, the owner spent roughly ten years training at one of Kyoto's most storied ramen institutions, Shinpuku Saikan (新福菜館) at Takabashi — the legendary home of jet-black soy chuka soba. He carries that Kyoto rich-soy lineage forward while adding his own ref

chef_bio.origin: Opened in 2020 in the Kuze district of Kyoto's Minami-ku, on the edge of Mukou City near JR Mukomachi Station.
chef_bio.background: Before opening his own shop, the owner spent roughly ten years training at one of Kyoto's most storied ramen institutions, Shinpuku Saikan (新福菜館) at Takabashi — the legendary home of jet-black soy chuka soba. He carries that Kyoto rich-soy lineage forward while adding his own refinements.
chef_bio.philosophy: All vegetables and ingredients are domestically sourced, and the broth itself is the product of constant tinkering — described on the shop's own site as a rich-soy soup 'developed through improvement upon improvement' to land on a deep flavor with a light, clean finish.
chef_bio.specialty: The flagship bowl is the 醤油中華そば (rich-soy chuka soba), a concentrated pork-bone-and-soy broth that drinks surprisingly clean, finished with Kyoto's prized Kujo green onions. Other draws include the 宇宙一のまぜそば ("best-in-the-universe" maze soba), 濃厚担々麺, the vegetable-laden 京都タンメン, and a much-loved fried-rice set.

anecdotes.text: The owner apprenticed for about ten years at the famous Shinpuku Saikan in Takabashi, Kyoto, before striking out on his own in 2020.
Unusually for the area, the shop offers morning ramen (asa-ra) — opening as early as 9:00 on Sundays and 10:00 most other days — so locals can start the day with a bowl.
The shop frames its signature broth as the result of repeated refinement: 'a rich-soy soup, light on the finish, developed through improvement upon improvement.'

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a rich soy (shoyu) tare; Japanese soy sauce of this style virtually always contains wheat. No wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls share a pork-bone broth and the kitchen handles pork chashu, gyoza and karaage; there is no separate vegan preparation.

positives.text: All vegetables and ingredients are domestically sourced, and the shop makes its own noodles in-house.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling local ramen counter where ordering is straightforward but staff communication is in Japanese; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu makes for a smooth visit.

places.name: 第一旭 熊野店 (Daiichi Asahi Kumano-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional ramen and gyoza shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone broth with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is almost certainly wheat-based, and gyoza wrappers are wheat — so the menu is not suitable for celiac diners. No gluten-free menu, ingredient labeling, or allergy accommodation was found in any source. A celiac diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's identity is chashu (braised pork) ramen over a pork-bone broth; gyoza are pork-filled and the bowls are topped with pork and egg. There are no vegetable or plant-based broth options noted anywhere. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: Shogoin, Sakyo Ward (Jingu-Marutamachi Stn / in front of Kumano Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Despite carrying the famous Daiichi Asahi name, this Kumano shop is not part of the celebrated honke (head shop) founded in 1947 by Kyoto Station's Takabashi. It is a survivor of an old chain expansion: when the original franchising company folded, individual shops like this one

chef_bio.background: Despite carrying the famous Daiichi Asahi name, this Kumano shop is not part of the celebrated honke (head shop) founded in 1947 by Kyoto Station's Takabashi. It is a survivor of an old chain expansion: when the original franchising company folded, individual shops like this one carried on independently, keeping the name and the recipes they had been built around. A yellowed signboard from that era — roughly four decades old — still hangs out front, a tangible piece of Kyoto ramen history.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious, value-driven neighborhood ramen-ya: hearty, chashu-forward bowls under 1,000 yen in a retro counter setting that locals have leaned on for years.
chef_bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style chuka soba: a pork-bone broth that reads surprisingly light and clean despite its richness, carrying a soy tare and a faint sheen of back-fat in the classic Kyoto manner. Bowls come piled with generous chashu — diners can choose leaner thigh or richer belly cuts — over medium-thin straight noodles, with bean sprouts, scallion and a soft egg. Spicy miso can be stirred in partway through to shift the flavor.

anecdotes.text: The shop is best understood as an independent descendant of the Daiichi Asahi lineage rather than a branch of the famous head shop — when the old chain company went under, locations like Kumano became their own establishments while keeping the name.
A roughly 40-year-old, yellowed signboard from the original chain era still hangs at the shop, evoking its place in Kyoto's ramen history.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare in the Kyoto chuka-soba style; standard shoyu is wheat-based, so the broth should be treated as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around a pork-bone broth, pork chashu and pork gyoza; there is no separate vegetable preparation, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school neighborhood counter shop where ordering in Japanese (or by pointing at the set menus) is easiest; no indication of English support.

places.name: 中華料理 鷭 (Chuka Ryori Ban)
places.gf_detail: A classic neighborhood town-Chinese counter serving wheat-noodle 中華そば (ramen) and fried rice. The bowls rely on wheat noodles and, almost certainly, a wheat-based shoyu tare in a pork/Chinese-style broth, with shared woks and a fry station — not celiac-safe and no gluten-free preparation or menu is offered. No allergy program advertised; a celiac should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg town-Chinese spot: the ramen and fried rice are built on pork/animal broth, lard, egg and Chinese seasonings. No vegan or clearly plant-based dish is offered or noted, and there is no separate-pan handling, so vegans cannot reliably eat here.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 11:30-13:50 / 17:30-20:45 (L.O. 19:50), Fri 11:30-13:50 (lunch only), closed Sat and Sun
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward / Demachiyanagi (Yoshida-Ushinomiyacho, near Kyoto University / ~9 min walk from Demachiyanagi Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A weathered, easy-to-miss town-Chinese shop tucked into the old streets behind Demachiyanagi, near Kyoto University.
