Its plain machiya-style frontage blends into the neighborhood, but the room inside is surprisingly roomy with tables and a tatami area, and it draws a steady crowd

chef_bio.background: A weathered, easy-to-miss town-Chinese shop tucked into the old streets behind Demachiyanagi, near Kyoto University. Its plain machiya-style frontage blends into the neighborhood, but the room inside is surprisingly roomy with tables and a tatami area, and it draws a steady crowd of students and locals.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, filling, wallet-friendly cooking with no pretension - everything sits comfortably under 1,000 yen, and the shop keeps a calm, focused mealtime atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Cheap, generous everyday Chinese: a simple Chinese-style ramen and fried rice, often ordered together as a set, plus big-portion plates like a heaping tenshinhan (crab-omelet rice).

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a hidden-gem Chinese place found while wandering from Demachiyanagi - unassuming outside, roomier than expected inside, with kind staff and a calm mood.
The shop posts firm house rules and asks that customers not take photos inside or post about the food on social media, keeping the focus on eating.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Town-Chinese kitchen using shared woks/pans and a single fry station, so wheat and other allergens cross freely; no gluten-free handling indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chinese-style ramen and stir-fry seasoning almost certainly use standard wheat-based shoyu/Chinese sauces; no wheat-free tare or sauce noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth, fried rice and plates are built on pork/animal fat and egg; no separate vegan preparation available.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local Chinese counter near Kyoto University with cash-only ordering, set lunch/dinner routines, and posted house rules (including no photos or social media inside), so it is easiest to enjoy with some Japanese and a quiet, follow-the-flow approach.

places.name: らーめん 蔵 (Ramen Kura)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free offering. The signature 京出汁醤油らーめん uses crimped hand-kneaded wheat noodles from Kyoto noodle maker 麺屋棣鄂, and the soy-sauce tare is made from honjozo/hon-mirin soy sauce (wheat-containing). Broth, noodles, and tare all contain wheat, and the cold つけ麺 is likewise wheat-based. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The Kyoto dashi base is built on kombu plus fish/seafood stock (魚介・魚節) and white dashi, and bowls are topped with pork-belly chashu and Kansai-style aburakasu (fried beef intestine). No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-21:00 (hours and closing days subject to change)
places.cuisine: Kyoto-dashi soy sauce ramen; chilled tsukemen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] らーめん 蔵 opened on March 9, 2024 in central Kyoto near Karasuma, tucked into a Taisho-era kura (storehouse) with no street entrance of its own — diners pick up a numbered wooden tag and walk in through the BIGOLI pasta restaurant's storefront to reach the hidden eight-seat counter

chef_bio.background: らーめん 蔵 opened on March 9, 2024 in central Kyoto near Karasuma, tucked into a Taisho-era kura (storehouse) with no street entrance of its own — diners pick up a numbered wooden tag and walk in through the BIGOLI pasta restaurant's storefront to reach the hidden eight-seat counter out back.
chef_bio.philosophy: An almost playfully unpretentious place that leans on customer integrity: the whole shop runs on self-service — you order by lifting an antique black rotary telephone on the wall that rings straight to the kitchen, pay and make your own change, wipe down your table, and collect a 10-yen cashback yourself. The aim is honest, refined Kyoto flavor at a remarkably low price for the city center.
chef_bio.specialty: 京出汁醤油らーめん in three 'bloom' grades — つぼみ (bud, no chashu), 五分咲き (half-bloom, some chashu) and 満開 (full bloom, generous chashu) — built on a gentle Kyoto-style dashi of kombu and fish stock with white dashi, finished with a honjozo soy-sauce tare and a touch of fragrant mayu burnt oil. Hand-kneaded crimped wheat noodles come from the well-known Kyoto maker 麺屋棣鄂, and bowls are topped with thin pork-belly chashu, green onion and Kansai-style crispy aburakasu (fried beef intestine). A cold つけ麺 rounds out the lineup.

anecdotes.text: There is no door to the street — you take a numbered wooden tag and pass through the BIGOLI pasta restaurant to a hidden back-garden storehouse to find the ramen counter.

Ordering is done by picking up an old black rotary phone mounted on the wall that connects directly to the kitchen; the whole experience is completely self-service down to making your own change and clearing your table.
The entry-level つぼみ bowl is priced around 690 yen — strikingly cheap for a refined Kyoto-dashi shoyu ramen in central Kyoto.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is made from honjozo (conventionally brewed, wheat-containing) soy sauce combined with kombu, fish stock and white dashi, so the shoyu base contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The Kyoto dashi broth is built on kombu plus fish/seafood stock and white dashi, and bowls carry pork chashu and fried beef intestine, so the kitchen handles animal products throughout.

cultural_comfort.note: An unusual fully self-service spot with a quirky black-rotary-phone ordering system and Japanese-only signage, so a little Japanese or comfort with figuring out an unconventional local system helps a lot.

places.name: スタメン (Stamen (Start With Men))
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use medium-thick wheat ramen noodles (from producer Teigaku) and a shoyu (soy sauce) tare over a pork broth, plus gyoza, fried rice and karaage on the menu. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and there is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tare, or dedicated handling. Not suitable for celiac diners; treat as not GF-safe.
places.vegan_detail: A pork-broth (豚清湯) ramen specialist whose signature is back-fat shoyu ramen with chashu pork; the brand explicitly centers on its soup and its hand-prepared roast pork (焼き豚), and sides are gyoza, fried rice and karaage. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered. Vegans cannot eat here.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri 11:00-15:30 / 17:00-21:30, Sat and Sun 11:00-21:30 (continuous), closed Mon. Official site lists 11:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Closes once chashu sells out.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Stamen (stylized START WITH MEN) opened in January 2023 in the Kashihara residential district of Nishikyo Ward, a roughly 20-minute walk from Hankyu Katsura/Rakusaiguchi. It is the second brand and third shop of the well-loved Kyoto ramen maker Ramen Mugyu, whose flagship sits in

chef_bio.background: Stamen (stylized START WITH MEN) opened in January 2023 in the Kashihara residential district of Nishikyo Ward, a roughly 20-minute walk from Hankyu Katsura/Rakusaiguchi. It is the second brand and third shop of the well-loved Kyoto ramen maker Ramen Mugyu, whose flagship sits in the Enmachi (Marutamachi) area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Described as a brushed-up, new-generation take on classic Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu ramen — refined and approachable rather than extreme, aimed at pleasing both young diners and families. The parent brand sums up its priorities as 自慢のスープ、こだわりの焼き豚 (a soup it is proud of and meticulously made roast pork), and the kitchen wraps up service once the day's chashu runs out.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a back-fat shoyu ramen: a clear pork stock (豚清湯) generously crowned with chunks of melted back fat, balanced by negi, bean sprouts and chashu, served with medium-thick slightly-wavy wheat noodles from the established maker Teigaku. A wonton-men (ワンタンメン) and a richer 濃厚 bowl round out the lineup, with gyoza, fried rice and karaage as sides.

anecdotes.text: Mugyu publicly introduced Stamen as its 三号店 (third shop) on the brand's official Instagram, framing it as part of the Mugyu family rather than a standalone newcomer.
Local food media described the bowl as a 'new-type' update of classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen, built on a clear pork broth where the sweetness of the back fat blends smoothly into the umami.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a shoyu (soy sauce) tare style; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat, so the broth/tare should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork stock (豚清湯) and the brand centers on its roast pork (焼き豚); bowls are topped with chashu, so no component is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood ramen counter in a residential pocket of Nishikyo; no English is indicated, so a guide or translation help is useful, though the menu is simple and ordering is straightforward.

places.name: 辛獅子 本店 (Karajishi Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The spicy bowl uses either curly egg noodles (wheat) or "konnyaku" noodles that are in fact made primarily from buckwheat/wheat flour rather than pure konjac, plus a spicy minced-meat tare and toppings such as garlic and scallion. There is no indication of a wheat-free broth, tare, or dedicated preparation, so this is not safe for celiac diners. Treat as not gluten-free; anyone highly sensitive should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The kitchen is built around spicy noodles topped with minced meat and egg, and the celebrated sides are braised pork cartilage (nankotsu) and a pork-cartilage rice bowl. Eggs feature throughout (egg noodles, raw/soft egg toppings). No plant-based ramen is advertised. A "0辛" (no-spice) version exists but only changes heat, not the animal content.
places.hours_raw: 09:00-15:30 (L.O. 14:50, closes once soup runs out). Irregular days off (often Fri).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small specialist ramen counter in Kyoto's Nishioji-Sanjo / Gozen-Sanjo area, devoted entirely to karaimen — Korean-influenced spicy noodles built around a fiery minced-meat, garlic, scallion and egg broth.

chef_bio.background: A small specialist ramen counter in Kyoto's Nishioji-Sanjo / Gozen-Sanjo area, devoted entirely to karaimen — Korean-influenced spicy noodles built around a fiery minced-meat, garlic, scallion and egg broth.
chef_bio.philosophy: '辛いが旨い' — spicy, but delicious: the heat is meant to amplify flavor, not bury it.
chef_bio.specialty: The house spicy bowl, where diners dial in heat from 0辛 (no spice) up through extreme levels (each step past the base adds a small charge), choosing either curly egg noodles or the shop's signature thin or thick 'konnyaku' noodles. The braised pork-cartilage rice bowl (なんこつ丼) — pork rib cartilage simmered until meltingly tender and finished with a ginger-grilled-style sauce — is a celebrated second act.

anecdotes.text: Spice runs from a gentle 0辛 all the way to extreme heat, with the price climbing by small increments as the level rises, so regulars can chase a personal-best burn.

Beyond the noodles, the kitchen is proud of its slow-braised pork-cartilage (nankotsu), served as a side in ponzu, ginger-grilled or spicy styles or piled over rice in small, regular and 'mega' bowls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The spicy tare and seasoning are built on a minced-meat, garlic and scallion base typical of Japanese spicy noodles, which generally relies on wheat-containing soy/seasoning; no wheat-free tare is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All noodle and broth preparations share a kitchen centered on pork cartilage, minced meat and egg, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual local counter-and-table shop where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the spice-level chart go a long way; no English menu is indicated.

places.name: 魔界系ラーメン三冠馬 (Makaikei Ramen Sankanba)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-based (wheat shoyu) tare and a thick pork-bone and fish broth, so they are not safe for celiacs. No GF menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. All three signature bowls are animal-based: pork-bone/fish broth and chicken broth, with pork chashu and egg toppings. No plant-based or vegetable broth option is offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat: 11:00-14:30 / 18:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:45). Sun & holidays: 11:00-14:30. Closed Wednesdays and the 3rd Tuesday of each month.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-run ramen shop that opened in September 2022 in the Saiin/Nishikyogoku area of Kyoto's Ukyo ward. The interior is filled with horse-racing memorabilia, plush toys, and posters reflecting the owner's deep love of the sport.

chef_bio.origin: The owner trained with and was supported by the popular Sendai shop 'Makai Ramen Gekko' (魔界ラーメン 月光) when opening, which is the source of the 'makai-kei' (魔界系) name describing the intense, rich broth lineage.
chef_bio.background: A small, family-run ramen shop that opened in September 2022 in the Saiin/Nishikyogoku area of Kyoto's Ukyo ward, in a former Chinese-restaurant space. The interior is filled with horse-racing memorabilia, plush toys, and posters reflecting the owner's deep love of the sport.
chef_bio.philosophy: An at-home, easygoing neighborhood spot where the owner's passion for both ramen and horse racing sets the tone.
chef_bio.specialty: Three signature bowls: '衝撃の豚' (a thick, creamy pork-bone and fish shoyu ramen), '黄金の鶏' (a golden chicken ramen), and '皇帝の油そば' (an emperor's aburasoba/brothless mixed noodle). The pork-bone fish bowl is praised as concentrated and mellow yet clean, with backbone-deep umami and none of the heaviness you might expect.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a horse-racing fan and named the shop '三冠馬' (Triple Crown horse). Remarkably, after opening, a racehorse the owner had backed as a fractional (one-mouth) owner — Liberty Island — went on to win the Triple Crown.
The '衝撃の豚' chashu was recently changed from pork belly to Iga-no-mochi pork loin, served in marbled slices alongside a soft-yolk seasoned egg.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls use a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen is virtually always wheat-containing soy sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-focused neighborhood ramen shop with a warm, family-run feel; a little basic Japanese and patience with the small space go a long way.

places.name: 笑福屋 (Shofukuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop whose broth is a pork-bone/chicken-carcass base finished with a fish-infused soy-sauce (shoyu) tare — the tare is wheat-based shoyu and the noodles are wheat. No celiac-safe options were found in any source, and the kitchen makes no allergen claims. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The ramen broths are animal-based (pork bone + chicken carcass, plus dried-fish/niboshi dashi), and the signature side dishes are seafood (marinated yellowtail zuke-don, sashimi). No plant-based or vegetarian ramen was found in any source.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shofukuya is a small neighborhood ramen shop near Hankyu Nishikyogoku station, run by an owner who trained as a sushi/Japanese-cuisine chef (元板前) before opening here. That washoku pedigree is what sets the place apart: locals and writers consistently point to the kitchen's precis

chef_bio.background: Shofukuya is a small neighborhood ramen shop near Hankyu Nishikyogoku station, run by an owner who trained as a sushi/Japanese-cuisine chef (元板前) before opening here. That washoku pedigree is what sets the place apart: locals and writers consistently point to the kitchen's precision with fish and rice as the reason a ramen shop turns out sushi-restaurant-quality side dishes.
chef_bio.philosophy: A ramen shop seen through a sushi chef's eyes — restrained, balanced broth rather than heavy intensity, and the same care with fish and rice you'd expect at a sushi counter.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen built on a pork-bone and chicken-carcass base finished with a fish/dried-sardine dashi — light and approachable on the first sip but deepening into a quiet umami richness. A delicate shio (salt) ramen is offered in the evenings. But the dish the shop is most loved for is the zuke-don: vinegared sushi rice topped with nori, fresh wasabi, and thick-cut marinated yellowtail (hamachi), often available as a 1,000-yen ramen-and-zuke-don set and sold in limited quantity until it runs out.

anecdotes.text: Writers describe the zuke-don as being at the level of a sushi restaurant's lunch — vinegared rice with wasabi and marinated yellowtail — and explicitly credit the owner's background as a former sushi chef for that quality.
The standard ramen is characterized as light at first but, sip after sip, revealing real depth without being thin or cloying — a pork-bone base with dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi added.
The zuke-don uses plump, thick-cut yellowtail with an exquisitely judged soy seasoning and a touch of fresh wasabi, and is made in limited quantity each day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in the shared pot used for all ramen; no gluten-free preparation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen is a fish-infused soy-sauce (shoyu) flavor; Japanese shoyu tare is wheat-based, so the broth itself contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (pork bone, chicken carcass and dried-fish dashi); no separate plant-based stock is used.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood ramen counter run by a former sushi chef; expect Japanese-only menu and service, so a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 熟成細麺 宝屋 西小路五条店 (Jukusei Hosomen Takaraya Nishikoji-Gojo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The shop's identity is built on wheat "熟成細麺" (aged thin wheat noodles) served in a back-fat pork-bone shoyu broth, and the tare is a wheat-based soy sauce. Noodles, broth tare, and gyoza all contain wheat; cross-contamination from a shared noodle-boiling pot and wok is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core broth is a pork-bone (and chicken-bone) shoyu base finished with back fat (背脂), and signature bowls are topped with char siu pork. There is no plant-based ramen or vegetable broth on the menu; even a plain bowl is built on animal stock.
places.neighborhood: Saiin / Nishikyogoku (Ukyo Ward) / ~10 min walk from Hankyu Nishikyogoku Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Takaraya was once a fast-growing name in Kyoto's ramen world, with a flagship in Pontocho and branches in Fushimi-Momoyama, Mibu, the Kyoto Ramen Koji food hall and beyond. When the operating company went bankrupt in 2011, most of those shops closed or changed hands, and the surv

chef_bio.background: Takaraya was once a fast-growing name in Kyoto's ramen world, with a flagship in Pontocho and branches in Fushimi-Momoyama, Mibu, the Kyoto Ramen Koji food hall and beyond. When the operating company went bankrupt in 2011, most of those shops closed or changed hands, and the survivors reopened under new names such as Kourai (香来), Shinrai (神来) and Miyako (京). The Nishikoji-Gojo branch is the one and only shop still trading under the original 宝屋 name.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep serving the unchanged, honest taste of an old-Kyoto ramen at an everyday price — bowls have long started around 600 to 800 yen — so the neighborhood always has its classic back-fat bowl to come home to.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a Kyoto-classic back-fat pork-bone shoyu bowl: a glossy 背脂 (back-fat) shoyu broth on a pork- and chicken-bone base, paired with the shop's namesake 熟成細麺 — aged thin noodles that stay firm with a springy, shikoshiko bite. Beloved companion orders include the char-siu ramen (チャーシューメン), the fatty toro-niku char-siu bowl, and the layered char-siu rice bowl (チャー丼).

anecdotes.text: After the Takaraya chain's operating company collapsed in 2011, this Nishikoji-Gojo shop became the sole storefront to keep the 宝屋 signboard, while its former siblings carried on as Kourai, Shinrai and Miyako.

Regulars describe it as a surviving piece of the old 背脂ちゃっちゃ (back-fat) Kyoto ramen lineage, with a retro wood-heavy interior and a 'Kyoto royal-road' bowl that hasn't changed; the layered chashu-don even reveals more chashu as you eat down through it.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in a shared pot, making the broth and any bowl unsafe for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a shoyu (soy-sauce) tare base, which in standard ramen contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on a pork- and chicken-bone broth finished with pork back fat; no plant-based stock is available.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where staff and regulars speak Japanese; little to no English, so a translation guide or pointing at the menu helps.

places.name: 大勝軒 千代原口店 (Taishoken Chiyoharaguchi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Tsukemen and ramen built on thick wheat noodles, with a chicken-pork-seafood dipping broth that is almost certainly seasoned with wheat-based shoyu tare. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, broth, or any allergy accommodation. This shop is not safe for celiacs; cross-contamination from a shared wheat-noodle kitchen is unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: A classic meat-and-fish tsukemen house: the broth combines chicken bones, pork bones and seafood, and bowls come topped with chashu (pork), menma and narutomaki. There are no vegan or vegetarian options noted.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun and holidays 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-21:00. Closed Mon (also closed when Mon is a holiday).
places.neighborhood: Kamikatsura / Chiyoharaguchi (Nishikyo Ward) / near the Chiyoharaguchi intersection on National Route 9, within walking distance of Hankyu Kamikatsura Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is a Kyoto branch (暖簾分け) of the legendary 東池袋大勝軒, the Tokyo shop in the Higashi-Ikebukuro district credited as the birthplace of tsukemen. The dish was born by accident in the 1950s: while working at the original shop, owner Kazuo Yamagishi would eat noodles dipped in broth,

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo / Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken lineage, adapted for Kyoto
chef_bio.background: This is a Kyoto branch of the legendary 東池袋大勝軒, the Tokyo shop in the Higashi-Ikebukuro district credited as the birthplace of tsukemen. The dish was born by accident in the 1950s: while working at the original shop, owner Kazuo Yamagishi would eat noodles dipped in broth, zaru-soba style, as a quick staff meal; a regular customer saw it and asked to try the same, and from 1955 it joined the menu as tsukemen.
chef_bio.philosophy: A defining Kyoto touch: because tsukemen began as a staff meal meant to be eaten fast, the dipping broth was traditionally served only lukewarm, but here it is served piping hot for local tastes. The dip is lighter and a touch sweeter than today's intensely thick tsukemen, loaded with chashu, menma and narutomaki, with seafood aromatics that bloom when you add soup to the leftover broth at the end.
chef_bio.specialty: Tsukemen (dipping noodles) — chewy, thick-cut wheat noodles served alongside a sweet-leaning dipping broth that blends chicken bones, pork bones and seafood. The signature pair is the 元祖つけ麺 (original) and the 濃厚つけ麺 (rich, finished with fish powder); an atsumori option serves the noodles piping hot.

anecdotes.text: Tsukemen was reportedly invented by accident: owner Yamagishi ate broth-dipped noodles as a quick staff meal at the original Tokyo shop, a regular asked to try it, and it became a menu item from 1955.
Unlike the original lukewarm dip, the Kyoto shop serves the dipping broth piping hot to suit local preferences.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat tsukemen/ramen noodles are the core product, prepared in a shared kitchen, so flour cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth blends chicken, pork and seafood and is sweet-savory, consistent with a wheat-based shoyu tare; no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based (chicken and pork bones plus seafood) and bowls are topped with pork chashu, menma and narutomaki; no vegan preparation exists here.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills roadside tsukemen counter where service is kept to the essentials and there is no sign of English support, so a Japanese-speaking companion or a clear pointing-and-ordering plan helps.

places.name: 鴨LABO (Kamo LABO)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The noodles are made with wheat (one source notes they contain whole-wheat flour / 全粒粉), and ramen tare/sides such as gyoza and teriyaki-style duck rice bowls typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated allergy handling is mentioned on the official site or any source reviewed. A gluten-sensitive diner should treat this shop as off-limits.
places.vegan_detail: This is a duck specialist: every ramen is built on a duck-based broth and the menu centers on duck meat (sliced duck, minced duck soboro, loin), duck fried rice, duck gyoza, and duck rice bowls. No vegan or vegetarian bowl is offered or advertised. Not a destination for plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (irregular days off)
places.neighborhood: Fushimi Ward / near Jonangu Shrine (~16 min walk from Takeda Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 鴨LABO grew out of a duck wholesaler's move into ramen — a shop opened beside the company's processing operation in Minami Ward, then relocated in 2022 to its current home on National Route 1 in Fushimi, beside Jōnangū Shrine, for easier access and a roomier space. The name reads

chef_bio.chef_name: 原田浩司（株式会社GRACE 代表取締役）
chef_bio.origin: 京都市南区（創業地、2021年末まで）→ 2022年に伏見区へ移転
chef_bio.background: 鴨LABO grew out of a duck wholesaler's move into ramen — a shop opened beside the company's processing operation in Minami Ward, then relocated at the turn of 2021-2022 to its current home on National Route 1 in Fushimi, beside Jōnangū Shrine, for easier access and a roomier space. The name reads as a 'laboratory' for exploring what duck meat can be.
chef_bio.philosophy: Operating as a duck wholesaler's own restaurant, the shop treats itself as a 'lab' for the possibilities of duck — sourcing its own birds and serving everything from ramen to roast duck, minced duck, and takeaway duck hot-pot/shabu sets, so guests can enjoy duck casually day-to-day or as a small occasion.
chef_bio.specialty: Ramen built on domestic crossbred duck (aigamo / 国産あい鴨): the broth carries the deep umami and natural sweetness of duck yet stays light and refined rather than heavy. It runs across shio, shoyu, miso, and a signature kamo tantan-men with minced duck; the duck salt ramen (鴨塩ラーメン) is the reviewer favorite. Rice bowls include duck-loin, duck-chashu and minced-duck (soboro) don.

anecdotes.text: The shop is the ramen arm of a duck wholesaler that 'knows duck inside out,' giving it direct access to domestic aigamo duck and letting it sell duck chashu, minced duck, and duck hot-pot/shabu sets to take home alongside the bowls.
Reviewers describe the duck salt ramen as a mellow, refreshing bowl with a 'healing' (癒し) quality, served in a relaxed, spacious roadside space just south of Jōnangū Shrine's west torii on Route 1.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Ramen noodles are wheat-based (one account notes whole-wheat / 全粒粉 flour), so the bowls are not gluten-free and there is no indication of separate gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and tantan/miso bowls plus duck teriyaki rice bowls and gyoza rely on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and seasonings; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are duck-based and the menu is centered on duck meat throughout, so there is no plant-based broth to protect from cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, roadside ramen shop with parking and no English-specific service noted — a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.

places.name: 大黒ラーメン 本店 (Daikoku Ramen Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a classic pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu ramen shop: the bowls use wheat (medium-thin) noodles and a soy-sauce-based tare that in this style is virtually always wheat-containing, and the popular yakimeshi (fried rice) is also seasoned with soy sauce. There is no published allergen information and no celiac-safe option. Not suitable for someone who must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The single signature ramen is a pork-bone and chicken-bone broth, topped with char siu (pork), and the house yakimeshi contains char siu and egg. There are no vegetable or vegan ramen options. A vegan would not have a meal here.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun 11:00-22:30, closed Mon
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood ramen institution that opened in 1983 and has anchored the old Nara highway in Fushimi for roughly four decades. The shop occupies a renovated former sake brewery (旧酒蔵を改装), fitting for the famous sake district, and is marked by a tricolor red-yellow-navy awning. It

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood ramen institution that opened in 1983 and has anchored the old Nara highway in Fushimi for roughly four decades. The shop occupies a renovated former sake brewery (旧酒蔵を改装), fitting for the famous sake district, and is marked by a tricolor red-yellow-navy awning. It is the original 本店 (main store) of a small local chain.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep it simple and generous: serve one well-made bowl at an affordable price so customers leave full, and welcome them back with a 100-yen discount voucher. As the shop puts it, they want people to eat their fill of good ramen at a reasonable price.
chef_bio.specialty: A single, focused bowl of tonkotsu-shoyu (豚骨醤油) ramen: a golden broth simmered from pork and chicken bones, kept clean and light-bodied (サラッとした味わい) rather than heavy, paired with medium-thin noodles. The other star is the yakimeshi (fried rice) of diced char siu, egg and green onion fried to a fluffy, separated texture; locals famously order ramen and yakimeshi together.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1983 with a four-decade single focus on tonkotsu-shoyu ramen, the shop hands every departing customer a 100-yen discount voucher to encourage their return.
The yakimeshi is so beloved that nearly every customer orders it alongside their ramen; the shop operates out of a renovated former sake brewery on the old Nara highway.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and bowls use a soy-sauce-based tare; no gluten-free handling is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone soy sauce) base; the shoyu tare in this style is virtually always wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is simmered from pork and chicken bones and bowls are topped with char siu pork; there are no vegan items and no separate preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, no-frills local ramen counter with no English support or allergen info, so a guide is recommended for diners with strict dietary needs.

places.name: らーめん門扇 伏水酒蔵店 (Ramen Monsen Fushimi Sakagura-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (sourced from the Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku) and the chicken broth is almost certainly seasoned with wheat-based shoyu tare; the signature sake-lees (kasu) topping is fermented from rice but the noodles and seasoning are wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen handling is mentioned by any source. A celiac diner should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house broth is a rich chicken-bone (tori-paitan / tori-gara) stock simmered with vegetables, and bowls are topped with chicken and other animal items. The sake-lees variations are also built on the same chicken base. No vegan or vegetarian ramen option is advertised.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays (open if Tuesday is a public holiday).
places.neighborhood: Fushimi Ward / Fushimi-Momoyama (inside Fushimi Sakagura Koji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Monsen is a Kyoto chicken-broth ramen house whose flagship is in Gion, with branches in Kiyamachi and here in Fushimi. The Fushimi Sakagura-ten sits inside Fushimi Sakagura Koji, a nostalgic food-court alley of around nine eateries in Fushimi-Momoyama, one of Japan's three great

chef_bio.origin: Fushimi, Kyoto (within the Fushimi Sakagura Koji complex)
chef_bio.background: Monsen is a Kyoto chicken-broth ramen house whose flagship is in Gion, with branches in Kiyamachi and here in Fushimi. The Fushimi Sakagura-ten sits inside Fushimi Sakagura Koji, a nostalgic food-court alley of around nine eateries in Fushimi-Momoyama, one of Japan's great sake-brewing districts, where you can cross-order dishes between shops over a glass of local sake.
chef_bio.philosophy: A bowl that could only exist in Fushimi: letting guests taste the character of the surrounding sake breweries through the lees that finish a carefully simmered chicken broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Their signature is the 'kuramoto-ga-eraberu sakekasu ramen' (brewery-selectable sake-lees ramen), a bowl exclusive to this Fushimi branch. A long-simmered chicken-bone-and-vegetable tori-paitan broth is finished with sake lees that lends a rich, remarkably smooth body and a gentle sake aroma; diners pick their lees from a rotating lineup of local Fushimi breweries (about eight on offer, such as Kizakura, Tsukinokatsura, Hoshuku, Tamanohikari, Eikun, Kyohime, Miyakotsuru and Tomio). Thin, curly noodles from the established Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku cling to the broth.

anecdotes.text: Letting customers choose their sake lees from the rotating lineup of nearby breweries is described as a service unique to Fushimi, where so many brewers are clustered together.

This branch carries Fushimi-only items not found at other Monsen shops, including the 'Montetsu ramen' (門鉄らーめん) done in collaboration with the neighboring teppanyaki spot 伏水 焼鉄 and a lighter 'sake-tori ramen' balancing chicken and sake-lees flavor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source documents the tare composition, but a chicken-broth ramen of this style is conventionally seasoned with wheat-based shoyu; not confirmed gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a chicken-bone (tori-gara/tori-paitan) stock simmered with vegetables, so no bowl is free of animal product even before toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual counter-and-table ramen spot inside a lively Fushimi food-court alley; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you, though dedicated English support is not advertised.

places.name: ら～麺処 克享 (Ra-mendokoro Katsukyo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop: house-blended wheat noodles (fine to thick), miso and shoyu tare, and broths built on pork bone, chicken, and dried-fish/seafood stocks. Miso and soy-based tare typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no allergen labeling is published. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. Every broth is animal-based — domestic pork bone, free-range chicken, and seafood/dried-fish stocks — and signature bowls feature seared chashu pork, fish, and tuna rice sides. No plant-based broth or vegan ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sun: 11:00-14:00 and 17:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Tue: 11:00-14:00 only; Sat: closed.
places.neighborhood: Fushimi Ward, Ryukokudaimae-Fukakusa Stn (Inari / Fushimi Inari)
places.notes: Katsukyo began in Shizuoka in October 2006, opened a second shop in Machida, Tokyo in June 2017, and brought the operation to its current spot in Fukakusa — on Shidan-dori between Fushimi Inari and Ryukoku University — in October 2019. It sits in a student-heavy neighborhood and

chef_bio.origin: Shizuoka, then Machida (Tokyo), now Fukakusa, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Katsukyo began in Shizuoka in October 2006, opened a second shop in Machida, Tokyo in June 2017, and brought the operation to its current spot in Fukakusa — on Shidan-dori between Fushimi Inari and Ryukoku University — in October 2019. It sits in a student-heavy neighborhood and has built a steady local following.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated creed is that 'dashi is Japanese culture' (出汁は日本の文化). Broths are built entirely by hand — pork bone, chicken, and dried-fish/seafood stocks each simmered and refined over one to two days, strained meticulously and rested in cold storage to mature, with no chemical seasonings or instant products (無化調), aiming for a lower-sodium, body-friendly bowl. Ingredients are chosen with care: wild southern (minami) tuna sourced from Yaizu in Shizuoka, naturally sun-dried nori, domestic pork and free-range chicken, house-made menma, and a custom noodle blend rested three days.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a rich, deep miso ramen (the 特製味噌 / special miso) layered with pork-bone, seafood and vegetable umami, served over house-made thick, flat wavy noodles and topped with seared chashu, two kinds of green onion, mizuna and nori. The lineup also runs to a seafood-tonkotsu, a shoyu-tonkotsu line and tsukemen, with rice sides such as a seared-mackerel and a wild tuna (toro maguro) bowl.

anecdotes.text: The owner carried the shop across the country — founding it in Shizuoka in 2006, moving to Machida in Tokyo, then resettling in Fukakusa, Kyoto in late 2019 — before landing in this student quarter near Ryukoku University.
Its wild southern tuna is brought in directly from Yaizu in Shizuoka, a nod to the shop's Shizuoka roots, and appears on the menu as a toro-maguro rice bowl alongside the ramen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water; the kitchen handles wheat noodles, miso/soy tare and flour-thickened broths throughout, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-tonkotsu bowls and the miso tare rely on standard Japanese soy/miso seasoning, which typically contains wheat; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (pork bone, chicken, seafood/dried fish) and there is no separate plant-based stock, so no dish can be made vegan.

positives.text: Broths are made entirely from scratch with no chemical seasonings or instant products, simmered and matured over one to two days, with a stated low-sodium, body-friendly aim.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only counter ramen shop with a ticket vending machine and no English menu published — basic Japanese or pointing at the ticket machine makes the visit smooth.

places.name: らーめんや たみん (Ramenya Tamin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a standard ramen shop: wheat ramen noodles in a rich chicken/pork-bone broth. There is no published indication of gluten-free noodles or any allergen accommodation, and the shop is a one-person operation with a single counter, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is animal-based (chicken / chicken-pork bone, with one source noting a sea-bream-and-chicken base) and bowls are topped with pork chashu and menma. No plant-based or vegetable ramen is offered. Vegans cannot eat here.
places.hours_raw: Business days and hours are irregular and announced on the owner's Twitter as they go. As a rough past guide: lunch ~12:00-14:00, dinner ~18:00-21:00 (varies with sourcing and prep). Checking social media before visiting is essential.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] らーめんや たみん is a one-person neighborhood ramen counter tucked beside Ryukoku University Mae-Fukakusa Station in the Fukakusa area of Fushimi, occupying a spot once home to a shop called 金太 (Kinta). It is the kind of tiny, owner-run place that locals seek out rather than stumble upo

chef_bio.origin: 京都市伏見区深草（旧「金太」の場所）
chef_bio.background: らーめんや たみん is a one-person neighborhood ramen counter tucked beside Ryukoku University Mae-Fukakusa Station in the Fukakusa area of Fushimi, occupying a spot once home to a shop called 金太 (Kinta). It is the kind of tiny, owner-run place that locals seek out rather than stumble upon.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop runs on the owner's own rhythm: just five counter seats, a food-ticket system, and operating days announced as the prep allows on Twitter rather than a fixed calendar. Quality and freshness drive the schedule, and the owner personally warns each guest that the bowl arrives extremely hot.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a 濃厚ラーメン (rich, concentrated ramen) built on a thick, glossy chicken-and-pork-bone (鶏豚骨) broth, topped with notably generous, tender chashu sliced over a centimeter thick, scallion, and seasoned menma. Reviewers describe the soup as deeply umami yet gently salted rather than heavy-handed.

anecdotes.text: One regular food blogger wrote that it took roughly a year of attempts before he finally managed to get in, because the shop opens only on irregular days posted to Twitter.
On serving the rich bowl the owner cautions diners 「熱いので気を付けて下さい」 (please be careful, it's hot) — the concentrated broth comes out scalding.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small counter run by one person serving wheat-noodle ramen; no separate preparation area, so gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on animal broth (chicken / chicken-pork bone) with pork chashu; no plant-based options, and everything shares the same pots.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny five-seat counter shop with irregular hours announced only on Twitter and a food-ticket system; comfortable for a confident solo or pair visit with some basic Japanese, less suited to groups or those needing English support.

places.name: らぁめん みや (Raamen Miya)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto-style shoyu-tonkotsu ramen shop. The noodles are wheat (medium-thick straight noodles supplied by Menya Tiekun), and the soy tare almost certainly contains wheat, served over a pork-bone broth. There is no gluten-free menu, no mention of allergen handling, and the kitchen is a single small operation with shared boiling water — not safe for celiac diners. No gluten-free options found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The entire concept centers on a pork-bone (豚骨) broth and an abundance of chashu (roasted pork belly) — the signature draw is a bowl piled with pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen, and no plant-based options are mentioned in any source.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun 07:00-14:00, closed Mon (closes once sold out)
places.neighborhood: Takeda, Fushimi Ward (Fukakusa-Nishiuracho, under the Meishin expressway overpass)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] After more than ten years of training at Kyoto's legendary Honke Daiichi Asahi Honten near Kyoto Station, the owner struck out on his own and opened Raamen Miya in September 2023, on the former site of a beloved ramen shop (Banrai-ya) that had closed. It sits tucked beneath the M

chef_bio.background: After more than ten years of training at Kyoto's legendary Honke Daiichi Asahi Honten near Kyoto Station, the owner struck out on his own and opened Raamen Miya in September 2023, on the former site of a beloved ramen shop (Banrai / ばんらい) that had closed. It sits tucked beneath the Meishin Expressway overpass, between Keihan Fujinomori and the Takeda subway/Kintetsu station, slightly toward Takeda.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner deliberately lightened the broth compared to the richer Daiichi Asahi original so it would suit early-morning eating, leaning into the Kyoto tradition of 'asa-ra' (morning ramen). The menu is kept simple and honest: ramen, chashu, rice, and a chashu rice bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: An 'Akira-style' (Daiichi Asahi lineage) original Kyoto ramen: a clean pork-bone broth finished with a crisp, fragrant soy tare and crowned with sweet, fatty pork-belly chashu. The signature is the meat-heavy bowl (肉増し / chashu ramen) where slices of chashu are fanned across the surface like flower petals, mixing fatty and lean cuts — reviewers joke the chashu never seems to run out.

anecdotes.text: Despite opening at 7:00 a.m., the counter fills to capacity during breakfast hours — one writer arrived before 9 a.m. to find nearly every seat taken.
The shop opened in September 2023 on the former premises of the much-missed ramen shop Banrai (ばんらい).
The owner trained for over ten years at the famed Honke Daiichi Asahi Honten before opening his own shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the soy-based tare typically contains wheat, served over a pork-bone broth — not safe for gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy (shoyu) tare in the Daiichi Asahi lineage; Japanese shoyu almost always contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is pork-bone (豚骨) based and bowls are topped with abundant pork chashu; no plant-based preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locals-first morning ramen counter where ordering in basic Japanese is smoothest, though an English menu is available to help you point to what you want.

places.name: 鳳舞楼 (Houmairou)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The shop's identity is its 自家製麺 (house-made wheat noodles), and the signature カラシソバ combines those wheat noodles with a mustard-vinegar-soy-sauce dressing (soy sauce normally contains wheat) over a chicken/kombu sauce. Dumpling wrappers (shumai, spring rolls) are also wheat. There is no gluten-free menu and wheat is central to nearly every dish.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. The kitchen is a Chinese eatery built around pork (酢豚), meat-filled spring rolls and shumai, and a chicken-and-kelp-based stock for the noodle dishes. The signature カラシソバ rests on a chicken/kombu sauce. No plant-based menu is advertised; a strict vegan would have nothing reliable to order here.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo Ward / Karasuma-Imadegawa (right by Imadegawa subway Stn / near Kyoto Imperial Palace and Doshisha University)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner, Tetsuo Aiba, entered an apprenticeship at age 16 at the celebrated Kyoto Chinese restaurant 鳳舞 (Houmai), where he handled the noodle-making. 鳳舞 — a landmark of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking — closed in 2009 (Heisei 21), and 鳳舞楼, opened in December 2015, carries on its li

chef_bio.background: The owner, Tetsuo Aiba, entered an apprenticeship at age 16 at the celebrated Kyoto Chinese restaurant 鳳舞 (Houmai), where he was in charge of the noodle-making. 鳳舞 — a landmark of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking — closed in 2009 (Heisei 21), and 鳳舞楼, opened in December 2015, carries on its lineage. Within that 鳳舞 family of successor shops, 鳳舞楼 is noted as the only one that makes its own noodles in-house.
chef_bio.philosophy: Aiba speaks of wanting to carry the flavor he inherited from his 鳳舞 master to people nationwide, not only in Kyoto, and refuses to compromise on the house-made noodles and the mustard seasoning.
chef_bio.specialty: カラシソバ (Karashi Soba) — the shop's number-one dish. House-made, very thin crinkled noodles — springy and made in-house, a rarity among 鳳舞's successor shops — are dressed with a mustard-vinegar-soy seasoning over a chicken-and-kombu-stock (鶏ガラ昆布) sauce loaded with vegetables; the sharp mustard kick is what makes it addictive.

anecdotes.text: The signature flavor was originally devised by Aiba's master at 鳳舞, the shop where he began training at sixteen; he has preserved it as a one-of-a-kind taste.
Even the shumai are wrapped in an ultra-thin house-made skin (自家製の超薄皮), reflecting the shop's commitment to making its noodles and wrappers from scratch.

In March 2024 the shop moved from its quieter Shinmachi-dōri spot to a far more accessible location right at the Karasuma-Imadegawa crossing, above the subway's Imadegawa station and diagonally across from Doshisha University.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature カラシソバ is dressed with a mustard-vinegar-soy-sauce blend; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the sauce as well as the noodles carry gluten.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local Kyoto-Chinese counter eatery where ordering and dietary requests are easiest in Japanese; English support should not be assumed.

places.name: 廣東料理 平安 (Kanton Ryori Heian)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The signature karashi soba is made with wheat noodles, and Chinese-style dishes here use soy-sauce/seasoning bases that typically contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free menu items, separate preparation, or celiac-aware handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan/vegetarian establishment. The signature karashi soba contains shrimp, chicken, and a chicken-and-kombu broth, and the kitchen is built around meat and seafood Cantonese cooking. No documented plant-based ramen or vegan options. Vegans cannot be reliably accommodated.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun: 12:00-14:00 (lunch), 17:30-20:00 (dinner). Closed Wednesday and Thursday.
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama-ku, Gion / Yasaka Shinchi (near Gion-Shijo Stati
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A husband-and-wife team runs this tucked-away Cantonese kitchen in an old Gion building, carrying on the lineage of Hōbu (鳳舞), the legendary Kyoto Chinese restaurant often called the source of "Kyo-chuka" (Kyoto-style Chinese cooking).

chef_bio.background: A husband-and-wife team runs this tucked-away Cantonese kitchen in an old Gion building, carrying on the lineage of Hōbu (鳳舞), the legendary Kyoto Chinese restaurant often called the source of "Kyo-chuka" (Kyoto-style Chinese cooking).
chef_bio.philosophy: Quiet, homey hospitality over flash: a deliberately inconspicuous door, an intimate counter, and an owner who checks in on diners mid-meal so the place feels like eating at someone's home rather than a restaurant.
chef_bio.specialty: Karashi soba (カラシソバ) — wheat noodles tossed in mustard and topped with a savory sauce of shrimp, chicken, green onion, bamboo shoot, lettuce, and wood-ear mushroom, built on a chicken stock and Rishiri kombu base, offered in three spice levels playfully named like school grades (中学 middle-school / 高校 high-school / 大学 university).

anecdotes.text: The karashi soba descends from a famous dish at the now-closed Hōbu, a foundational name in Kyoto Chinese cuisine, so a bowl here is a taste of that vanished lineage.

The mustard is potent enough that even the medium level can make your nose tingle; diners pick their heat by 'school grade,' and the couple's own cartoon likenesses appear on the menu, said to resemble them closely.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature karashi soba uses wheat noodles and the kitchen is a small Cantonese operation cooking many wheat-containing dishes in shared woks, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cantonese/Kyoto-Chinese seasonings and sauces here are based on standard soy sauce and Chinese condiments, which normally contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dish and broth contain shrimp, chicken, and chicken-kombu stock; the kitchen centers on meat and seafood, so any dish risks animal-product cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, husband-and-wife hidden-gem in an old Gion mixed-use building with a warm, homey feel; little to no English, so basic Japanese or a translation aid and a relaxed attitude help a lot.

places.name: ラーメン大蔵 一乗寺店 (Ramen Okura Ichijoji-ten)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese ramen shop with no gluten-free offering. Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, the shoyu/shio tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce, and broths are pork/seafood based — not celiac-safe. There is no published allergen menu, dedicated prep, or GF noodle option. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan/vegetarian-friendly shop. The broths are animal-based (pork/chicken and a seafood-blend "shin-gyokai" stock made from ten kinds of fish), toppings center on chashu pork, and sides include karaage and gyoza. No plant-based ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-05:00 (next day) daily; lunch sets available 11:00-14:00. (Note: the English Tabelog listing shows an older 11:00-22:00 with Tuesday closed; HotPepper/Gnavi list 11:00-05:00 open year-round.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Okura sits on Ichijoji's famous ramen street in northeast Kyoto, a short walk from Eizan Ichijoji Station. It's a small, no-frills counter shop marked by a large red lantern and a neon sign, the kind of place that runs from lunch deep into the early morning for students, ni

chef_bio.background: Ramen Okura sits on Ichijoji's famous ramen street in northeast Kyoto, a short walk from Eizan Ichijoji Station. It's a small, no-frills counter shop, the kind of place that runs from lunch deep into the early morning (to 5 a.m.) for students, night workers and ramen-crawlers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Generous portions and honest value: lunch sets (until 14:00) add just 50 yen to a bowl and come with free rice refills, and the U-shaped counter is built so a solo diner can slip in and eat well without ceremony.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen lets you pick your broth: shoyu, shio, a punchy garlic bowl, a rich kotteri, and a 'new seafood' (shin-gyokai) ramen built on a stock blended from ten kinds of fish for a clean, collagen-rich umami. The back-fat shoyu is a Kyoto-standard animal-based bowl; the garlic ramen is a local favorite.

anecdotes.text: The 'new seafood' ramen leans on a broth made from ten types of fish ground to a powder and simmered in, giving a fish-forward, collagen-rich bowl the shop markets as especially popular with women (女性におすすめ).
Beyond ramen the menu spreads into pork rice bowls, karaage, gyoza and kimchi — set up as a late-night neighborhood spot rather than a single-bowl specialist.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and shio tare at a standard Japanese ramen shop are made with conventional wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (pork/chicken and a ten-fish seafood blend) and the shared kitchen also fries karaage and cooks gyoza, so no plant-based bowl can be assumed clean.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat late-night counter on Ichijoji's ramen street with no English support or allergen handling — comfortable to visit with a guide who can navigate the cash-only ordering and language.

places.name: ラーメンながた (Ramen Nagata)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen: straight wheat noodles in a chicken broth seasoned with a shoyu (wheat-containing soy sauce) tare, plus an abura soba (wheat noodle) option. No gluten-free items or accommodations are indicated. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on chicken broth (the shop uses female local chicken bones), with pork chashu, and the broth/tare contain animal products throughout. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-14:00 (L.O. ~14:30 per Tabelog) and 17:00-21:00; Sun & holidays 11:00-15:00; closed Monday. Sources vary slightly on lunch close (14:00 on blogs, 15:00 on Tabelog) and evening open (17:00 vs 17:30).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner took up ramen seriously later in life, studying the craft on his own after turning 50, and opened this small shop in a quiet Kitashirakawa residential corner just south of the famous Ichijoji ramen district on October 14, 2021. Despite the weathered, old-fashioned lante

chef_bio.background: The owner took up ramen seriously later in life, studying the craft on his own after turning 50, and opened this small shop in a quiet Kitashirakawa residential corner (near Chayama Station on the Eizan line, just south of the famous Ichijoji ramen district) on October 14, 2021. Despite the weathered, old-fashioned lantern signage that makes it look like a decades-old institution, it is a relatively new arrival.
chef_bio.philosophy: He set out simply to become the neighborhood's beloved ramen shop, building bowls on pure chicken stock with female local chicken and refusing to muddy the soup. Offering a plain kake ramen for just 500 yen is read as quiet confidence in the broth and noodles alone.
chef_bio.specialty: Chicken-forward ramen made entirely from poultry stock. The clear shoyu chuka-soba is the chef's calling card, with a clean, transparent broth and medium-straight noodles, while the rich tori-paitan (chicken white broth) is praised as so deep it tastes almost like it contains pork bones. The Half & Half pairs a transparent shoyu seitan broth beneath the creamy chicken paitan in a single bowl that grows richer as you stir.

anecdotes.text: The chef began studying ramen independently after turning 50 and opened the shop on October 14, 2021, driven by the wish to become a local favorite.

The signature shoyu broth is made from female chicken bones and described as a clear, nostalgic Showa-era soup with a sharp soy finish, served with tender grilled chashu; an oversized soy-and-garlic karaage is a beloved side.
The Half & Half layers a clean shoyu seitan broth beneath a rich chicken paitan, so the bowl transitions from light to increasingly rich as the diner mixes it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu (soy sauce) tare that flavors the broth is standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood counter shop with no English menu noted; basic Japanese or a translation app will help.

places.name: 京都ラーメン きんざん 七条本店 (Kyoto Ramen Kinzan Shichijo Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac/strict gluten-free diners. The ramen is wheat noodles (from Kondo Seimen) in a pork-bone broth seasoned with wheat-based aged soy sauce tare. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and shared wheat-noodle boiling water create unavoidable gluten exposure. No published allergen accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The house specialty is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth finished with back fat and topped with two kinds of pork chashu; even the soy tare sits on an animal base. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 / 18:00-23:30 (last orders 14:30 and 23:30). Closed Sundays plus occasional irregular closures. Note: an English listing alternatively shows 11:30-14:00 / 18:00-02:00 daily, so confirm before a late visit.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kinzan opened in 2013 in the quiet Nishioji-Shichijo residential pocket of Shimogyo-ku, a roughly 15-minute walk from JR Nishioji Station. The owner trained at the respected Takabashi Ramen before striking out on his own, and the shop has since grown into a local favorite popular

chef_bio.background: Kinzan opened in 2013 in the quiet Nishioji-Shichijo residential pocket of Shimogyo-ku, a roughly 15-minute walk from JR Nishioji Station. The owner trained at the respected Takabashi Ramen before striking out on his own, and the shop has since grown into a local favorite popular enough to open a second branch near Kyoto City Hall (Shiyakusho-mae).
chef_bio.philosophy: A Kyoto-rooted approach: classic local-style tonkotsu-shoyu done with carefully chosen regional ingredients (Kyotanba/Kyoto Highland pork from Hiyoshi Farm, Kujo negi, aged soy from the Koyama and Goko breweries, Kondo noodles), cooked in an open kitchen so guests can watch the bowl come together.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a 'Kyoto black shoyu' (京都黒醤油) ramen: a glossy, near-jet-black soup built on a pork-bone base, given sharpness and depth by aged soy sauce and a mellow sweetness by back fat. It is finished with two styles of Kyoto Highland pork chashu (lean and fatty, torched to order), Kujo green onion, bean sprouts, menma, and a slick of back fat. Straight medium-thin noodles from the long-established Kondo Seimen give a firm, wheaty bite.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at the well-regarded Takabashi Ramen before opening Kinzan in 2013.
The chashu is cut from Kyoto Highland (Kyotanba) pork raised at Hiyoshi Farm and torched to order, and the noodles come from the long-established Kondo Seimen.
Students get a discount (about 100 yen off, with a free size-up).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi confirms the 2013 founding, an ex-Takabashi Ramen owner, the 京都黒醤油 signature, Kyoto Highland pork from Hiyoshi Farm, Kujo negi, Kondo Seimen noodles and Koyama/Goko soy; a directly-run second shop (市役所前店) opened near City Hall in autumn 2023.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water, and the soy-sauce tare is wheat-based, so gluten contact is inherent to every bowl.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The black soup is seasoned with aged soy sauce (from Oyama and Goko breweries), which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and finished with back fat; pork chashu tops the bowls, so there is no vegan-safe preparation line.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy neighborhood ramen counter with ticket-style ordering and an open kitchen; little to no English, so a few basic Japanese phrases (or pointing at the menu) make it smoother.

places.name: 701 TARESOBA KYOTO (701 TARESOBA KYOTO)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The dish is wheat ramen noodles dressed with a soy-sauce-based tare (soy sauce contains wheat), white vinegar and chili oil, topped with pork chashu. No GF noodles, GF tamari, or allergen accommodation is advertised anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. Every taresoba bowl comes with pork-belly chashu, and toppings/sides lean on meat (assorted chashu, mini chashu rice bowl) and egg (onsen tamago). The tare is soy-sauce based with no advertised plant-only build. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Osamu Shinkai, a Tokyo native, spent years as a head chef in hotel Chinese and yakiniku kitchens before relocating to Kyoto in 2023 to open his own counter shop devoted to a single idea.

chef_bio.background: Osamu Shinkai, a Tokyo native, spent years as a head chef in hotel Chinese and yakiniku kitchens before relocating to Kyoto in 2023 to open his own counter shop devoted to a single idea.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than building a bowl around a rich broth, the shop strips the soup away entirely so the noodles themselves take center stage, with a sauce blended in a 'golden ratio' meant to flatter the noodle and let diners boldly mix and customize to taste.
chef_bio.specialty: Taresoba (soupless mixed noodles): the signature soy-sauce bowl tops springy noodles with pork-belly chashu, chopped onion, white sesame, scallion and bamboo shoot, which you mix vigorously with a tare of special soy sauce, oil, white vinegar and umami chili oil before eating; spicy and chilled mustard-sauce versions round out the lineup.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened on September 22, 2023 on a quiet lane near Hankyu Karasuma in central Kyoto's Nakagyo-ku, bringing a Tokyo-born take on soupless ramen to the city center.
Diners are encouraged to fine-tune noodle firmness and topping amounts, and to add the table-side white vinegar and rayu themselves before mixing.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is built on a special soy sauce, which in Japan is standard wheat-brewed shoyu; combined with wheat noodles this bowl is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is finished with pork-belly chashu and shares a counter kitchen with meat and egg toppings, so no plant-only preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 11-seat counter shop where ordering is simple (pick a sauce, mix it yourself) and a few words of Japanese plus pointing will carry you through comfortably.

places.name: MAZEMEN プルダ (Mazemen Puruda)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle mazesoba shop. The signature bowl is built on wheat ramen-style noodles tossed with a savory tare, topped with chashu (pork), soft egg, menma, Korean seaweed and scallion. Wheat noodles plus a typically wheat-based shoyu-style tare and pork toppings make this unsuitable for celiac diners. No gluten-free preparation, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen handling is advertised. Not safe for gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The only documented bowl is animal-based: chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, menma and Korean seaweed over wheat noodles, with no advertised vegetarian or vegan option. There is no indication any plant-based version is available. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (lunch only); the same premises operates as a Korean restaurant in the evening. Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Higashi-kujo, Minami-ku (Kyoto Station Hachijo exit area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A daytime-only mixed-noodle counter near Kyoto Station's Hachijo exit that shares its kitchen with a Korean restaurant. By day the six seats are devoted to mazesoba; by night the same room becomes the Korean spot Hanbaibo Puruda (韓杯房プルダ). The mazesoba operation opened on July 3,

chef_bio.background: A daytime-only mixed-noodle counter near Kyoto Station's Hachijo exit that shares its kitchen with a Korean restaurant. By day the six seats are devoted to mazesoba; by night the same room becomes the Korean spot Hanbaibo Puruda (韓杯房プルダ). The mazesoba operation opened on July 3, 2025.
chef_bio.specialty: Mazesoba (mixed noodles), regular size around 1,000 yen, topped with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, menma, Korean seaweed and shredded scallion, with an optional soft-cooked egg add-on. The Korean-seaweed topping nods to the kitchen's evening Korean identity.

anecdotes.text: The mazesoba shop opened July 3, 2025, occupying the lunch hours of a space that serves as the Korean restaurant Hanbaibo Puruda in the evenings.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 6-seat counter lunch spot with no English advertised; a little Japanese and pointing at the single mazesoba goes a long way.

places.name: 京都鶏白湯そば 純 四条烏丸 (Kyoto Toripaitan Soba Jun Shijo-Karasuma)
places.gf_detail: This is a chicken-paitan ramen specialist built around custom medium-thick wheat noodles served in a creamy chicken-and-pork-bone broth, with seasoning tare and shoyu/miso variants that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free noodle, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not safe for celiacs; a strictly gluten-free diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on an animal base: a rich chicken (tori-paitan) broth made primarily with Kagoshima chicken, supplemented with Miyazaki and Oita pork bones, and topped with rare pork chashu. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options listed. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Wednesdays per local guides; the official site states year-round open with no Obon closure, so hours may vary — confirm before visiting.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner Sakakimoto came to ramen the long way around, spending some fifteen years training in kappo (Japanese fine-dining) and sushi kitchens before striking out on his own. That fine-dining grounding shows in every detail of the bowl.

chef_bio.origin: This is the second outpost of the Jun chicken-paitan brand, following the original near Marutamachi; it opened in the Shijo-Karasuma area on August 13, 2025.
chef_bio.background: Owner Sakakimoto came to ramen the long way around, spending some fifteen years training in kappo (Japanese fine-dining) and sushi kitchens before striking out on his own. That fine-dining grounding shows in every detail of the bowl.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sakakimoto has said that if he was going to open his own place, he wanted to do something only he could do — hence the labor-intensive, single-serving emulsified broth and the kappo-trained attention to finish and presentation.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a tori-paitan (chicken white-soup) soba in an espuma-like, foamed broth. Rather than ladling from one big pot, the kitchen emulsifies the chicken soup bowl by bowl in a blender, whipping it into a mild, airy, almost cappuccino-like froth. The base is built primarily on Kagoshima chicken, rounded out with small amounts of Miyazaki and Oita pork bones, and paired with custom medium-thick, chewy noodles and rare pork chashu.

anecdotes.text: The bowl is built to be played with: a trio of flavored vinegars (including apple and orange) let you tune the soup as you go, and regulars finish by adding rice to the leftover broth for a carbonara-like second act.
Unlike the original Marutamachi shop, this branch adds wontons and rare chashu to the lineup, with a yuzu-salt foamed chicken paitan and a miso version among the headline bowls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu and miso seasoning variants of the chicken-paitan soba are based on tare/sauces that conventionally contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (chicken paitan plus pork bones) and bowls are topped with pork chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation; cross-contact with meat and animal stock is inherent.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, tidy counter shop comfortable for newcomers and overseas visitors, though staff likely operate mainly in Japanese, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu go a long way.

places.name: とよ二郎 (Toyojiro)
places.gf_detail: This is conventional Jiro-style ramen: wheat noodles plus a soy-sauce (shoyu) or salt tare, served in an animal-fat broth. Wheat noodles and standard shoyu both contain gluten, and no gluten-free option or gluten-handling practice is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs; treat as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: A Jiro-style ramen shop built around a beef-fat broth (rendered fat from 48-month-old female cattle), wheat noodles, and beef-tendon (bokkake) toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl here; the entire concept is meat-forward, so a plant-based diner has nothing safe to order.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00, open daily (no regular closed day listed). Note: the ramen offering reportedly runs alongside the evening izakaya operation; lunchtime is recommended for the ramen without a mandatory drink order.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward / Kyoto-Kawaramachi (~5 min walk from Hankyu Kyoto-Kawaramachi Stn, Yaoyacho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Toyojiro opened on December 1, 2024 in the Kawaramachi area of Kyoto, growing out of the popular Kiyamachi tuna-and-wagyu izakaya Toyota and sharing its premises. It represents that team's leap from sake-side beef and tuna plates into a dedicated Jiro-style ramen concept.

chef_bio.background: Toyojiro opened on December 1, 2024 in the Kawaramachi/Kiyamachi area of Kyoto (Yaoyacho, Nakagyo-ku), growing out of the popular tuna-and-wagyu izakaya Toyota (とよ田) and sharing its premises. It represents that team's leap from sake-side beef and tuna plates into a dedicated Jiro-style ramen concept.
chef_bio.philosophy: To elevate the famously rough, fat-heavy Jiro genre with high-grade wagyu fat rather than ordinary pork back-fat, aiming for a smooth, deep umami that gives ramen fans a more refined take on the style.
chef_bio.specialty: A wagyu-forward Jiro-style bowl: a broth built on rendered fat from 48-month-old female cattle from Okazaki Ranch, finished with a soy-sauce or salt tare and crowned with premium wagyu fat. The house signature carries over the izakaya's bokkake (slow-stewed beef tendon) as a topping, giving a bokkake-jiro that fuses wagyu and ramen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is an offshoot of the tuna-and-wagyu izakaya Toyota and runs from the same Yaoyacho premises, carrying over that bar's signature bokkake (stewed beef tendon) into the ramen.
The broth's defining ingredient is fat from 48-month-old female cattle from Okazaki Ranch, chosen for its melt-on-the-tongue quality and deep umami.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are offered in a soy-sauce (shoyu) or salt tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made from rendered beef fat and the toppings are beef tendon and wagyu fat, so the entire kitchen workflow is meat-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A small counter ramen shop sharing premises with an izakaya; no English information is available, so a visitor will likely need a guide or translation help to navigate ordering and the Jiro-style customs.

places.name: 麺屋一基 (Menya Ichiki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles and is topped with fresh fu (生麩, wheat gluten), so there are multiple unavoidable wheat sources in the dish itself. The broth is a white-miso tare with bonito/kombu dashi (miso typically contains wheat or barley as well). No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is indicated. A celiac diner cannot eat the ramen here safely.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. Both signature bowls are built on animal stock — bonito (katsuobushi)/kombu dashi or a shellfish (kai) dashi — and are topped with smoked chashu pork and a soft-boiled egg. While the yuba (tofu skin) and fresh fu toppings are plant-based, the broth foundation is fish/shellfish-based, so the ramen is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. No vegan version is advertised.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:30 (may close once soup runs out). Closed days irregular - specific weekday closures not published, so the same hours are listed for all days with an irregular-schedule flag.
places.neighborhood: Kita Ward / Murasakino-Higashinocho (Daitokuji / Kenkun Shrine, Kitaoji area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Ichiki bills itself as a Kyoto white-miso ramen specialist. It first opened on October 6, 2025 as a tucked-away shop in the Nishijin area of Kamigyo Ward, then relocated and reopened on April 22, 2026 in a renovated Kyoto townhouse (machiya) in Shikino Higashino-cho, in the

chef_bio.background: Menya Ichiki bills itself as a Kyoto white-miso ramen specialist. It first opened on October 6, 2025 as a tucked-away shop in the Nishijin area, then relocated and reopened on April 22, 2026 in a renovated Kyoto townhouse (machiya) in Shikino Higashino-cho, Kita Ward, along Kitaoji-dori near Kenkun Shrine and Daitoku-ji.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop leans into local Kyoto identity — white miso, yuba and fu — to set its bowls apart from typical tonkotsu or shoyu ramen, and keeps a calm, welcoming room where even solo diners are at ease.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 京の白味噌らーめん (Kyo no Shiro-miso Ramen): an original tare blending several Kyoto white misos for a gently sweet, creamy soup, married to a bonito-and-kombu dashi. A variation, the 京の白味噌貝だしらーめん, swaps in a shellfish (scallop) dashi for a mellower umami. Bowls come dressed in distinctly Kyoto ingredients — fresh yuba (tofu skin) and fresh fu (wheat gluten) — alongside smoked chashu pork, a soft-boiled egg, white negi and bonito flakes. The chashu is torched just before serving to coax out its aroma and umami. Thick, straight noodles with a firm chew round it out. A rice-and-fried-chicken set can be added.

anecdotes.text: Promoted as one of Kyoto's first white-miso ramen specialty shops, leaning on Kyoto white miso, fresh yuba and fresh fu for a distinctly local bowl.
Opened October 6, 2025 as a hidden-gem shop in a Nishijin residential neighborhood, then relocated and reopened April 22, 2026 in a renovated machiya near Kenkun Shrine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish itself contains multiple wheat sources — wheat ramen noodles plus a fresh fu (wheat gluten) topping — so cross-contamination is moot; the ramen is inherently not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a white-miso tare; Kyoto white miso commonly contains wheat or barley, and the dashi is bonito/kombu or shellfish based. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on bonito/kombu or shellfish dashi and bowls include smoked chashu pork and egg; plant toppings (yuba, fu) sit in an animal-based soup, so a vegan-safe bowl is not offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood ramen specialist in a renovated machiya; menus and service are in Japanese, so a translation app or some basic Japanese helps, though ordering a signature bowl is straightforward.

places.name: 麺屋 かがり 木屋町店 (Menya Kagari Kiyamachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: A standard ramen shop: wheat noodles with a chicken/seafood (and tonkotsu) broth and a soy-based tare, which means wheat is present in both the noodles and the seasoning. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or allergy accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: All bowls are built on animal broths (chicken paitan, seafood, tonkotsu) and topped with seared chashu pork, so nothing on the menu is vegan. The fresh yuba (tofu skin) topping that is the shop's signature is itself plant-based, but it is served in meat/fish broth, so it does not make any bowl vegan. No vegan option is offered.
places.hours_raw: 21:00-06:00 (through to the next morning). One source lists daily operation; another notes closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Kamiyacho / Kiyamachi, Nakagyo Ward (~3 min walk from Hankyu Kyoto-Kawaramachi Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Kagari's Kiyamachi branch is a small, hard-to-find ramen shop tucked into a back alley off Kiyamachi Street, just a few minutes from Kyoto Kawaramachi Station. It keeps unusual late-night-into-dawn hours (roughly 9 p.m. to 6 a.m.), which has made it a quiet institution for

chef_bio.background: Menya Kagari's Kiyamachi branch is a small, hard-to-find ramen shop tucked into a back alley off Kiyamachi Street, just a few minutes from Kyoto Kawaramachi Station. It keeps unusual late-night-into-dawn hours (roughly 9 p.m. to 6 a.m.), which has made it a quiet institution for night-shift workers winding down, drinkers looking for a closing bowl, and early risers wanting ramen for breakfast.
chef_bio.philosophy: A neighborhood late-night refuge built around comforting, broth-forward bowls and a hidden-gem atmosphere rather than flash.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen offers a rich, opaque chicken paitan alongside a cleaner chicken-and-seafood soy broth and a heavier tonkotsu. Its most distinctive bowl is a Kyoto-style ramen finished with fresh yuba (tofu skin): the delicate sheets soak up the soup and add a soft, local character you rarely see on a ramen counter. A charred-garlic black ramen rounds out the late-night lineup.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a hidden gem worth the hunt down a Kiyamachi side alley near H&M, prized for its fresh-yuba ramen that pairs surprisingly well with the soy broth.
Locals favor it as a late-night and post-drinking spot as well as an early-morning meal, thanks to its 21:00–06:00 hours.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's soy-based (shoyu) ramen uses a standard soy tare, which in Japanese ramen virtually always contains wheat; combined with wheat noodles this bowl is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on chicken, seafood, and tonkotsu (pork) broths and topped with seared chashu pork; the plant-based yuba topping is served in these animal broths.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, locals-oriented late-night counter shop with no listed English support, so basic Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will make ordering easiest.

places.name: 熊本ラーメン 肥後もっこす (Kumamoto Ramen Higo Mokkosu)
