places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kumamoto-style tonkotsu ramen shop. The noodles are wheat, and the tare/seasoning and accompaniments (gyoza, karaage, paiku rice simmered in soy sauce) are wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised; not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built on a rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with pork toppings, plus chicken karaage and soy-braised pork rice. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is offered.
places.neighborhood: Kita Ward / Kitayama (right by Kitayama Stn on the Karasuma subway line, near Kyoto Botanical Gardens)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small ramen counter sitting steps from Kitayama Station and across from the Kyoto Prefectural Botanical Garden, serving the Kumamoto style of tonkotsu in a city better known for lighter Kyoto ramen. The name itself is a wink to its roots: 'mokkosu' is Kumamoto dialect for a stu

chef_bio.background: A small ramen counter sitting steps from Kitayama Station and across from the Kyoto Prefectural Botanical Garden, serving the Kumamoto style of tonkotsu in a city better known for lighter Kyoto ramen. The name itself is a wink to its roots: 'mokkosu' is Kumamoto dialect for a stubborn, headstrong, true-to-yourself sort of person.
chef_bio.philosophy: Diners describe an owner with a meticulous, conscientious touch in the service and presentation, leaning into a robust, full-bodied bowl rather than the lighter Hakata-style tonkotsu.
chef_bio.specialty: Kumamoto ramen: a thick, milky-white tonkotsu (pork-bone) broth finished with fragrant burnt-garlic ma-yu oil, served with thin, straight noodles. A favorite side is the paiku rice (パイクごはん): pork rib cartilage braised sweet-and-spicy until meltingly tender, collagen-rich and juicy, served over rice.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'mokkosu' comes from Kumamoto dialect meaning a stubborn, headstrong person, signaling its proudly Kumamoto identity in Kyoto.
Its tonkotsu is noted as concentrated and rich yet surprisingly clean-tasting, finished with burnt-garlic ma-yu oil that tames any pork-bone odor.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyoto-gourmet and Tabelog confirm a Kumamoto tonkotsu shop by Kitayama Station opposite the Botanical Garden, with 焦がしニンニク油 (ma-yu), thin straight noodles, and パイクごはん = braised pork rib cartilage over rice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water; not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Paiku rice is pork simmered in a soy-based sauce, and tonkotsu tare typically uses wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with pork and chicken items throughout the menu; no vegan-safe preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood ramen counter; expect Japanese-only ordering, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps.

places.name: 濃厚豚骨ラーメン 岩本家 (Nōkō Tonkotsu Ramen Iwamotoya)
places.gf_detail: This is a Yokohama iekei (家系) ramen shop built on wheat ramen noodles (from Tokyo's Sugano Seimen / 菅野製麺所), a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a thick pork-and-chicken bone broth — none of which is gluten-free. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or menu options are advertised, and there is no indication of allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: A pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken broth iekei specialist; the signature bowl is topped with chashu pork, spinach, and nori. The broth, tare, and toppings are animal-based with no vegan or vegetarian ramen offered. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-1:00 the next day (L.O. 0:30, closes once soup runs out), open daily
places.neighborhood: Saiin, Ukyo Ward (Saiin-Tatsumicho, ~2 min walk from Hankyu Saiin Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Iwamotoya occupies the former 麺匠 一粒万倍 西院店, a branch of a Kyoto ramen group known for lighter chicken-based clear shoyu bowls. In August 2025 this Saiin location was completely reimagined and reopened on August 11, 2025 as a dedicated Yokohama-style iekei (家系) tonkotsu-shoyu shop,

chef_bio.background: Iwamotoya occupies the former 麺匠 一粒万倍 西院店, a branch of a Kyoto ramen group known for lighter chicken-based (Oyama jidori and duck) clear shoyu bowls. That Saiin location — the one shop in the group that had long served iekei — closed on August 3, 2025 and reopened on August 11, 2025 as a dedicated Yokohama-style iekei (家系) tonkotsu-shoyu shop aimed at late-night and younger diners in the Saiin area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than leaning on surface oil, the shop builds its identity on a genuinely concentrated, dense broth that still keeps a proper drinkable fluidity — an honest take on iekei conventions that aims for real depth of bone-and-marrow character rather than formulaic repetition.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a straightforward iekei 'Ramen' (around 950 yen): a thick pork-and-chicken bone broth with a soy tare, topped in classic family-style fashion with a slice of chashu pork, spinach, and three sheets of nori. The noodles are sourced from Tokyo's well-known Sugano Seimen (菅野製麺所) and served a touch shorter than typical iekei noodles, with a rice pairing available.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a full rebrand of the former 麺匠 一粒万倍 西院店, swapping that group's signature light chicken-shoyu style for a bold tonkotsu iekei concept when it reopened on August 11, 2025.
A ramen critic praised the broth as '濃厚で濃密な味わい' (concentrated, dense), noting the shop achieves legitimate originality within iekei conventions while staying authentically in the category.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat ramen noodles in a shared pot, with a wheat-based shoyu tare and animal broth; no gluten-free preparation or separation is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: An iekei tonkotsu-shoyu shop whose tare is soy-sauce based; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken based and bowls are topped with chashu pork; no vegan or vegetarian option exists, so meat-free dining is not viable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen information or accommodation is noted in available reviews or shop information.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, cash-only late-night ramen counter where ordering is quick and simple; a guide or basic Japanese helps, as English support and allergen accommodation should not be assumed.

places.name: 来山軒 (Raizanken)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a classic Kyoto-style chuka soba shop: wheat ramen noodles in a chicken-and-pork shoyu broth, where the soy-based tare almost certainly contains wheat. Sides (gyoza, fried chicken, egg-rice sets) are also wheat/flour-based. Not suitable for celiacs; no dedicated handling found.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature bowls are built on a chicken-bone and pork-bone broth with a touch of back-fat, and the menu centers on chashu ramen, gyoza, and meat/egg sets. No plant-based ramen or vegan options were found.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri and holidays (excluding Sun) 11:30-14:30, 17:30-23:30 / Sat and holidays 11:30-14:30, 17:30-23:00
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing neighborhood machi-chuka (town Chinese) diner sitting almost on top of Goryo Station on the Kyoto subway Tozai line, the kind of homey, street-side counter spot where local families and solo regulars have eaten for years.

chef_bio.background: A homey neighborhood noodle-shop (shokudo) standing right in front of Goryo Station on the Kyoto subway Tozai line in Yamashina Ward — the kind of street-side counter spot, with 16 seats (an 8-seat counter and two four-tops), where local families and solo regulars have eaten for years.
chef_bio.philosophy: Approachable, homemade comfort food at honest prices, welcoming to solo diners, families, and groups alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Budget neighborhood noodle-shop fare: a light shoyu ramen alongside a wafu (Japanese-style) ramen and a curry ramen, plus gyoza and affordable rice-and-set combinations, with most bowls coming in under about 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits directly in front of Goryo Station in Yamashina and serves classic, budget-friendly noodle-shop fare — shoyu and wafu ramen, curry ramen, gyoza and rice sets — in a 16-seat room.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and Gnavi list Raizanken as a ramen/udon diner (食堂) directly in front of Goryo Station in Yamashina, 16 seats, serving shoyu/wafu/curry ramen, gyoza and rice sets under ~1,000 yen; it is not documented specifically as a Chinese (machi-chuka) specialist.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the broth/tare is built on a wheat-containing soy base; no gluten-free preparation exists here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-forward Kyoto-style tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and there is no indication of a wheat-free alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken- and pork-bone based with back-fat; menu is meat- and egg-centric, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, no-frills neighborhood diner run for locals; expect Japanese-only service and a casual, all-smoking room, so a little Japanese and easygoing manners go a long way.

places.name: 油そば 大革命 金閣寺店 (Aburasoba Daikakumei Kinkakuji-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle abura soba / ae-men shop. The noodles are wheat, the tare (sauce that coats the bowl) is soy-sauce-based and almost certainly contains wheat, and toppings include pork. There is no indication of any gluten-free option, gluten-free noodle, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: The core dish is built on pork (chashu / pork belly, meat-topped bowls) and a soy-based tare; meat-miso and meat-topped bowls are the signatures. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl on the menu and no plant-based broth option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (irregular days off)
places.neighborhood: Kinugasa-Tenjinmoricho, Kita Ward (Kinkakuji area / Randen Kitano-Hakubaicho Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A short walk from the famous Golden Pavilion (Kinkaku-ji), this is a casual, student-favorite noodle counter that started life in August 2021 as a branch of the abura-soba franchise Kirin-ji (きりん寺). In April 2025 the shop broke from the franchise and went independent under the bo

chef_bio.background: A short walk from the famous Golden Pavilion (Kinkaku-ji), this is a casual, student-favorite noodle counter that started life in August 2021 as a branch of the abura-soba franchise Kirin-ji (きりん寺). In late April 2025 the shop broke from the franchise and went independent under the bold new name 大革命 ("Great Revolution"). True to the name, the owner kept tinkering: around August 2025 the menu shifted away from classic oil-based abura soba toward oil-free あえ麺 (ae-men), where the sauce is mixed through the noodles rather than pooling with oil at the bottom of the bowl.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hearty, junky, build-your-own-flavor comfort food that proposes 'a new way to enjoy abura soba' — a deliberate contrast to Kyoto's refined kaiseki tradition, aimed at filling up students and travelers cheaply and boldly.
chef_bio.specialty: The shop made its name on the 肉盛り油そば (meat-piled abura soba) — chewy, slightly wavy wheat noodles under a generous heap of soft, tender pork, eaten by mixing in chili oil (rayu) and vinegar to taste, with grated garlic and spicy meat-miso on the table to fine-tune each bite. In the ae-men era this carries over into hearty bowls like a niku-miso (spicy meat-miso) ae-men, often paired with karaage and rice as a set.

anecdotes.text: The name 大革命 ('Great Revolution') marks the shop's independence from the Kirin-ji franchise in spring 2025, and the owner lived up to it by later dropping oil entirely and reworking the dish into mixed ae-men.

A Yahoo! News ramen expert highlighted the roughly ¥1,000 niku-mori abura soba as a 'junk and wild' Kyoto gem within walking distance of Kinkaku-ji, praising the tender, generous pork and chewy noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is built on a soy-sauce-based tare mixed through wheat noodles; standard Japanese soy sauce tare contains wheat, making the sauce as well as the noodles unsuitable for gluten avoidance.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls center on pork (chashu / pork belly) and meat-miso, with karaage on set menus; the kitchen is meat-forward and offers no plant-based bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, no-frills counter noodle shop popular with local Ritsumeikan students; staff are unlikely to speak much English, so order by pointing or a few Japanese words and you'll be fine.

places.name: くそオヤジ最後のひとふり 河原町三条店 (Kuso Oyaji Saigo no Hitofuri Kawaramachi San)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen: wheat (medium-thick straight) noodles plus a soy-sauce-based tare (typically wheat-containing). Not celiac-safe and no gluten-free menu is indicated. The broth itself is clam dashi, but the noodles and shoyu tare make the dish unsafe for gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: This is a shellfish-broth ramen specialist — every bowl (asari/shijimi/hamaguri) is built on a clam dashi with soy-sauce tare and contains clams; there are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 daily (last order around 22:30–23:30 depending on source; press release states 11:00–24:00 L.O. 23:30; Tabelog lists 11:00–23:00)
places.neighborhood: Ebisucho / Sanjo-Kawaramachi, Nakagyo Ward (near Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae subway Stn)
places.notes: This is the first Kyoto location of くそオヤジ最後のひとふり, a clam-broth ramen brand under Osaka's UNCHI Co., the company behind the celebrated 'Jinrui Mina Menrui' (人類みな麺類). The original Kuso Oyaji opened in Osaka's Juso district in April 2013, and the brand has since reached overseas mar

chef_bio.background: This is the first Kyoto location of くそオヤジ最後のひとふり, a clam-broth ramen brand under Osaka's UNCHI Co., the company behind the celebrated 'Jinrui Mina Menrui' (人類みな麺類). The original Kuso Oyaji opened in Osaka's Juso district in April 2013, and the brand has since reached overseas markets including New York, China, Korea and Nepal. The Kawaramachi-Sanjo shop opened September 15, 2023, set into the Sanjo shopping arcade with a refined Japanese-style interior — counter seats on the first floor and tables on the second (about 19 seats in all).
chef_bio.philosophy: The parent company's stated guiding idea is to 'change the world through the power of ramen,' and the broths are made with custom water-purification equipment to draw out a clean shellfish umami meant to be finished to the last drop.
chef_bio.specialty: Clam-broth ramen, with the あさりラーメン (asari/short-neck clam ramen, around 980–990 yen) as the signature bowl — a clean clam dashi balanced with a blend of dark and light soy sauce, served with chewy yet firm medium-thick straight noodles and clams as a topping. The shijimi (corbicula) and a limited hamaguri (clam) ramen round out the lineup.

anecdotes.text: The Osaka original opened in Juso in April 2013 and the brand has since expanded internationally to New York, China, Korea and Nepal before this first Kyoto outpost.
About a week after opening, an evening visit around 7 PM found roughly ten groups waiting in line — a sign of the strong early buzz around the Kyoto debut.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: PRTimes (UNCHI press release) confirms April 2013 Juso origin, 'change the world through ramen' mission, custom water-purification broth, three clam varieties, ¥980 asari ramen, overseas branches (NY/China/Korea/Nepal) and the Sept 15 2023 Kawaramachi-Sanjo opening. Tabelog confirms 19 seats split 1F counter (10) / 2F tables (9). A local blog notes ~10 groups queuing a week after opening.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare — described as a blend of dark and light soy sauce over clam dashi — which typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen is shellfish-based (clam dashi with clam toppings); there is no vegan/vegetarian preparation, so cross-contact with shellfish is inherent to the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, popular ramen counter where you queue and order at the counter; little English support is expected, so a non-Japanese speaker may want a translated menu or guidance, but pointing at the clam-ramen options works fine.

places.name: 焼飯と麺 京都 (Yakimeshi to Men Kyoto)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The signature bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (a Hokkaido "yume-chikara" wheat blend with whole-grain flour) in a pork-and-seafood shoyu broth, so the noodles, the soy-based tare, and the fried rice all contain wheat. There is no indication of any GF menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is a pork-and-dried-fish (niboshi/katsuobushi) double stock, the bowls feature rare char siu (pork), and the fried rice contains roast pork and egg. There are no plant-based ramen or rice options noted. A vegan would have nothing suitable to order.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon–Fri): 11:00–15:00 and 17:30–21:00. Saturdays, Sundays and holidays: 11:00–21:00 (continuous). Oil-based ramen (abura soba) served only from 15:00. Last order around 21:00.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward / Sanjo Meitengai shopping arcade (just west of Kawaramachi-Sanjo / near Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae subway Stn and Keihan Sanjo Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yakimeshi to Men Kyoto opened on April 2, 2025 in the Sanjo-meiten-gai arcade just west of Kawaramachi-Sanjo, as the second location of a brand built by the people behind the well-regarded Osaka-area ramen shop Chuka Soba Fumoto (中華そば ふもと) in Hirakata. The concept is right in the

roles: Operated by the team behind the Osaka-area shop 中華そば ふもと (Fumoto)
chef_bio.background: Yakimeshi to Men Kyoto opened on April 2, 2025 in the Sanjo-meiten-gai arcade just west of Kawaramachi-Sanjo, as a sister shop from the team behind the well-regarded Osaka-area ramen shop Chuka Soba Fumoto (中華そば 麓/ふもと). The concept is right in the name and refreshingly honest: 'we want to eat fried rice AND we want to eat noodles' — so rather than treating the rice as an afterthought side, the shop puts equal craft into both.
chef_bio.philosophy: Take both halves of the classic machi-chuka pairing seriously — neither the noodles nor the rice plays second fiddle. Yuzu powder from Kochi and a grape-sansho (Japanese pepper) oil from Wakayama are set out so diners can tune the bowl to taste, and an evening-only abura soba (oil noodles, from 15:00) gives a different way to enjoy the same house noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the char siu ramen paired with a half portion of fried rice (chashu-men half-yakimeshi set). The bowl is a pork-and-dried-fish double-stock shoyu ramen — pork depth lifted by niboshi (dried sardine) and a finish of shaved bonito — served with house-made straight noodles blending Hokkaido 'yume-chikara' wheat with whole-grain flour, boiled firm. It is crowned with petal-thin rare char siu (pork shoulder loin marinated in a red-wine-and-soy tare). The fried rice is its own point of pride: shredded roast pork and fluffy egg cooked to be moist yet crumbly, with dedicated rice cooked to order per portion.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name literally translates to 'Fried Rice and Noodles,' born from the idea that you shouldn't have to choose between the two — so the half-fried-rice set was made the headline order.
It sits in the Sanjo-meiten-gai arcade and quickly became part of a wave of new ramen shops turning the Sanjo–Kawaramachi blocks into a ramen battleground.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyo-soku and Osumituki confirm the April 2, 2025 opening, that it is run by/sister to Osaka's Chuka Soba Fumoto (麓), the chashu-men half-yakimeshi set, the pork+niboshi+bonito double broth, and house-made Hokkaido 'yumechikara' whole-grain noodles. Kyotopi calls it a sister shop (姉妹店) to Fumoto/Menfu, not a numbered branch. Sources describe the petal-thin rare pork-shoulder char siu in a red-wine soy marinade and yuzu-powder/grape-sansho-oil condiments.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles and wheat-based shoyu tare are central to every bowl, and fried rice is cooked on the same line — heavy shared wheat presence throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen and the char siu is marinated in soy sauce; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, so the tare and marinade should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-and-dried-fish (niboshi, bonito) stock and dishes feature pork char siu and egg; no plant-based preparation is mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, busy arcade ramen shop with no English menu noted; a friendly greeting and pointing at the signature set will carry you through, but staff are unlikely to navigate detailed dietary requests in English.

places.name: 麺心 よし田 (Menshin Yoshida)
places.gf_detail: A tsukemen specialist whose entire identity is its house-made wheat noodles (stone-ground whole-grain flour from Nagano blended with refined wheat). The dipping broths are wheat-thickened/seasoned and rich in shrimp, chicken and fish. There is no gluten-free noodle or menu, no dedicated equipment, and noodles are boiled in shared water — not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is animal-based: the signature dip is built on more than a kilogram of sweet-shrimp heads, and the standard option is a chicken-and-seafood (鶏魚介) broth. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:30). Closing days announced each month (irregular).
places.neighborhood: Nishiotecho, Fushimi Ward (near Keihan Fushimi-Momoyama Stn / Kintetsu Momoyama-Goryomae Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺心よし田 is the shop Fushimi locals name first when the talk turns to tsukemen. Tucked just west of Fushimi-Momoyama / Momoyama-Goryomae stations, past the Otesuji shopping arcade, it has built a devoted following over years — regulars describe coming back dozens of times since thei

chef_bio.background: 麺心よし田 is the shop Fushimi locals name first when the talk turns to tsukemen. Tucked just west of Fushimi-Momoyama / Momoyama-Goryomae stations, past the Otesuji shopping arcade, it has built a devoted following over years — regulars describe coming back dozens of times since their student days for noodles that taste reliably the same.
chef_bio.philosophy: The heart of the shop is the house-made noodle — milled from Nagano stone-ground whole-grain flour blended with refined wheat, giving a soba-like fragrance. The kitchen deliberately leaves the cold-rinsed noodles slightly moist, believing that texture carries the wheat aroma best, and serves regular and large portions at the same price.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a rich shrimp tsukemen (海老味噌つけ麺): a dipping broth built from more than a kilogram of sweet-shrimp heads simmered with vegetables, yielding a deep, almost French-bisque-like richness that earns it the nickname of a 'French-inspired' tsukemen. The classic alternative is a chicken-and-seafood (鶏魚介) dip.

anecdotes.text: The shop hands diners a posted 'way of eating' (流儀): start with the noodles as-is, then layer in onion and citrus to shift the flavor in stages, and finish the leftover dip as a consommé-style soup.
The shrimp dip is built from over a kilogram of sweet-shrimp heads, lending the broth its distinctive concentrated, bisque-like body.
A longtime regular writes of having visited dozens of times since student days, praising the unwavering consistency of the bowl.
anecdotes.source: https://minaju.info/lunch/2-16-京都市伏見区-伏見桃山-麺心-よしだ-つけ麺/

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Totteoki Kyoto (official tourism site) confirms the Fushimi-Momoyama/Otesuji location, house noodles from Nagano stone-ground whole-grain flour, the rich shrimp tsukemen built from 1kg+ of sweet-shrimp heads (French-bisque-like), the chicken-seafood dip, the posted 流儀 eating method (noodles first, then onion/citrus, finish the dip as soup), and same-price regular/large portions. A local blog corroborates the devoted-regular following.
sources: https://minaju.info/lunch/2-16-京都市伏見区-伏見桃山-麺心-よしだ-つけ麺/

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are wheat-based (whole-grain + refined wheat flour) and boiled in shared water; no gluten-free option exists.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (sweet-shrimp heads, chicken, seafood); shared cookware means no vegan-safe preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locals-beloved counter shop with no English menu noted; basic Japanese and a willingness to follow the posted eating-order instructions will make the visit smooth.

places.name: 麒麟園 (Kirin-en)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found. This is a Cantonese Chinese restaurant whose signature is a chili-laden tantanmen — wheat noodles in a sesame/chili broth, almost certainly built on a wheat-based shoyu/miso tare and a pork base. No allergen or gluten-free information is published on the official site, and nothing indicates celiac-safe handling. A celiac diner should treat this place as not safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. A traditional Cantonese kitchen with a meat- and seafood-forward menu (the famous tantanmen and black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork are both meat dishes). No vegan ramen or vegan set is advertised, and tantanmen broth is typically pork/sesame based. A vegan would need to ask, with little likelihood of a fully plant-based bowl.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00; Dinner 16:30–23:00 (last order 22:30). Closed Tuesdays. (Tabelog lists lunch as 11:00–14:00; the official site states 11:30–14:00 — the official 11:30 start is used here.)
places.neighborhood: Muko City, Teradocho — about a 3-minute walk from Hankyu Hig
places.notes: Kirin-en opened in 1967, when authentic Cantonese cooking was still a rarity in the then-farmland district of Muko. Founder Miyaji Tamotsu set out to let local residents enjoy genuine Guangdong cuisine casually, and the place has stayed family-run, with Miyaji Ryo now serving as

chef_bio.background: Kirin-en opened in 1967, when authentic Cantonese cooking was still a rarity in the then-farmland district of Muko. Founder Miyaji Tamotsu (宮路保) set out to let local residents enjoy genuine Guangdong cuisine casually, and the place has stayed family-run, with Miyaji Ryo (宮路亮) now serving as store manager.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house motto for its spicy dishes is 「辛くて旨い」— 'spicy but delicious' (うま辛) — chasing flavor and craft rather than heat for its own sake.
chef_bio.specialty: A fiery tantanmen built on four kinds of chili plus a house-made meat-miso for depth, which became the restaurant's calling card during Muko City's spicy-food (gekikara) town-revitalization push. In developing it the kitchen reached for the bhut jolokia ('jolokia') pepper — once billed as the world's hottest — and the bowl can be ordered across escalating heat levels (roughly 1 to 5), with the mild steps served without the jolokia powder and the hottest tiers dosed with it.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1967 by Miyaji Tamotsu to bring real Cantonese cooking to a Muko neighborhood that was still mostly farmland.
The tantanmen earned the Gold Grand Prix at the very first KARA-1 spicy-food competition, the contest run by Muko City's gekikara shopping district.
A quieter favorite is the black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork (黒酢酢豚), whose depth beneath the tang makes it a regulars' pick.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kirinen.com and the Otokuni-Kyoto guide confirm the 1967 founding by Miyaji Tamotsu (宮路保) with son/manager Miyaji Ryo (宮路亮), authentic Cantonese cooking in then-farmland Muko, the KARA-1 Grand Prix gold-winning tantanmen (four kinds of chili + house meat-miso, bhut/jolokia pepper 'world's hottest at development', selectable 1-5 heat levels), the 「辛くて旨い」うま辛 ethos, and the black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-noodle tantanmen cooked in shared pots in a Chinese kitchen that also fries and wok-cooks; no gluten-free handling is described anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Cantonese restaurant and a tantanmen specialist, dishes rely on standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce and likely wheat-based tare; no wheat-free soy is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat- and seafood-forward kitchen with a pork/sesame tantanmen base; no separated vegan prep is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running neighborhood Chinese restaurant where Japanese is the working language; a little prepared Japanese or a translation app will smooth ordering, and staff are used to spice-seekers from across Japan.

places.name: ラーメン てんぐ 西陣店 (Ramen Tengu Nishijin)
places.gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen shop. The signature bowl is thin wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth blended with Japanese dashi, almost certainly built on a wheat-based shoyu tare, with chashu and menma. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, a dedicated preparation area, or allergen handling, and broth/tare are shared. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The base of every bowl is an animal broth (pork bone blended with Japanese dashi), and toppings center on braised pork chashu. No vegetable or vegan broth, no plant-based ramen, and no meatless menu is described. Not a vegan-friendly option.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (Tabelog) / some articles list 11:30-23:00. Closed Wednesday (some reports say irregular closures).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A modest, locally beloved ramen shop in the Nishijin district of Kyoto's Kamigyo ward, set along Chieko-koin street near the old textile quarter. It is the second location of Ramen Tengu, whose main shop is in Uzumasa in western Kyoto, and it carries a nostalgic Showa-era chuka-s

chef_bio.background: A modest, locally beloved ramen shop in the Nishijin district of Kyoto's Kamigyo ward, set along Chiekoin-dori (知恵光院通) near the old textile quarter. It is the second location (2号店) of Ramen Tengu, whose main shop is in Uzumasa in western Kyoto, and it carries a nostalgic Showa-era chuka-soba feel with a hand-painted sign and a cozy room of wrap-around counter and a few low tatami seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep it simple and let the broth do the work - a clean, comforting Kyoto-style bowl that regulars return for rather than a flashy specialty shop.
chef_bio.specialty: The house bowl, Tengu Ramen, is built on a pork-bone broth blended with Japanese-style dashi: it looks rich and is dressed with back-fat, yet drinks surprisingly clean and stays balanced to the last sip. It comes over thin, springy noodles with menma, nori and a chashu so slow-braised it falls apart and melts on the tongue. A generous heap of fresh, crisp scallions is served on the side so you can pile them in to taste, with an extra-scallion (追いネギ) option for about 50 yen.

anecdotes.text: The chashu is so tender that visitors joke about how many hours it must be simmered - it crumbles apart when lifted with chopsticks and enriches the broth as it dissolves.
The scallions arrive on a separate dish so diners can add as many as they like; an extra-scallions option (追いネギ, ~50 yen) lets you load the bowl up further.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi confirms the Nishijin (Kamigyo) location, that this is the 2号店 with the honten in Uzumasa (Ukyo-ku), the original pork-bone + Japanese-dashi broth that drinks light despite the back-fat, thin broth-clinging noodles with menma and nori, melt-apart chashu, and green onions served separately with a 追いネギ ¥50 add-on.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the broth is a pork-bone/dashi base with a presumed wheat shoyu tare; no gluten-free handling is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is described as a shoyu-style (soy-sauce) ramen, which in a shop like this is virtually always a wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on animal broth (pork bone plus Japanese dashi) and topped with pork chashu; no vegan or vegetable broth is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, local Nishijin ramen counter where ordering in Japanese and paying cash will go most smoothly; no English service is indicated.

places.name: ラーメン京都てんぐ 常盤店 (Ramen Kyoto Tengu Tokiwa-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop: the noodles are thin low-alkaline wheat noodles (mirroring the Sugichiyo lineage), the tare is soy-sauce based (shoyu, which in Japan virtually always contains wheat), and gyoza/shumai/shoronpo sides are wheat-wrapped. The pork-bone/chicken broth itself plus heavy shared-pot/shared-fryer use means cross-contamination is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or noodle substitute is offered.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian establishment. The signature bowl is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken-gara broth finished with back fat (背脂), topped with chashu pork. The broth and the standard bowls are animal-based, so there are no genuine vegan or vegetarian ramen options here. A plant-based diner would have no suitable main; ask staff before ordering, but expect no accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Lunch and dinner 11:30-2:00 the next day (L.O. 1:40). No regular closing day.
places.neighborhood: Tokiwa / Uzumasa, Ukyo-ku (near Randen Tokiwa Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2002 by a chef who had been the head cook (店主／元料理長) at Sugichiyo (杉千代), a legendary Uzumasa ramen shop that drew lines for its then-revolutionary bowls some two decades ago. When Sugichiyo's original shop was gone, Tengu became the refuge for its devoted fans, carrying

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2002 by a chef who had headed the kitchen (店長／元料理長) at Sugichiyo (杉千代), a legendary Uzumasa ramen shop that drew lines for its then-revolutionary bowls some two decades ago. When Sugichiyo's original shop was gone, Tengu became the refuge for its devoted fans — described as the sole shop carrying Sugichiyo's DNA forward — and it is the original 2002 shop of the Ramen Kyoto Tengu group.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carry the Sugichiyo lineage faithfully rather than chase trends: an honest, additive-conscious pork-bone shoyu bowl in a modest storefront, kept welcoming for families, children and women rather than dressed up as a destination shop.
chef_bio.specialty: Tengu Ramen (てんぐラーメン): a pork-bone and chicken-gara shoyu broth finished with back fat (背脂) that drinks lighter than it looks, paired with thin low-alkaline straight noodles in the Sugichiyo mold and chashu so tender it falls apart when lifted with chopsticks. A basket of chopped green onion is provided to season the bowl to taste, and rice is famously all-you-can-eat.

anecdotes.text: The owner had headed the kitchen at the legendary Sugichiyo and founded Tengu in 2002, becoming the home for orphaned Sugichiyo fans.
The thin low-alkaline straight noodles deliberately mirror Sugichiyo's, and diners are handed a basket of chopped green onion to customize the bowl; the chashu is so soft it crumbles when picked up.
Regulars describe it as carrying on the lineage of the now-gone famous Sugichiyo ramen shop in Uzumasa, with all-you-can-eat rice as a signature touch.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Genjitsutouhi and Retty reviews confirm the Tokiwa (Ukyo-ku) shop was founded in 2002 by a former Sugichiyo (杉千代) head from Uzumasa, that it carries Sugichiyo's 'DNA' as the shop fans turned to, its pork-bone+chicken shoyu broth with back fat, thin low-alkaline Sugichiyo-style noodles, melt-apart chashu, a basket of chopped green onion, and all-you-can-eat rice (ライス食べ放題).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles cooked in a shared boiling pot, plus wheat-wrapped gyoza/shumai/shoronpo on the same line, make gluten cross-contamination unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a shoyu (soy-sauce) style ramen; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so the tare is a wheat source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-gara based and finished with pork back fat; chashu pork tops the bowl, so no dish is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, unpretentious neighborhood ramen shop with tatami seating and a family-friendly feel; no English is advertised, so a guide or a few Japanese phrases will make ordering smoother.

places.name: ラーメン専門店 大輝 (Ramen Senmonten Daiki)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto-style ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat noodles in a pork-based clear broth seasoned with a light soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is wheat-based. Both the noodles and the tare contain wheat/gluten, and there is no gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and no indication of allergy accommodation. This is not safe for celiacs or those avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a pork bone broth (made from Miyazaki-raised pork) and topped with fatty chashu pork, so the soup itself is animal-based. There are no vegan or vegetarian options on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri 11:00-15:00 / Sat and Sun 11:00-15:00, 17:30-20:00 / closed Mon and the 3rd Sun
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small ramen specialist tucked into the Umezu neighborhood of Kyoto's Ukyo ward, near the Hankyu Matsuo-taisha line on the Arashiyama side of the city. The shop has been quietly perfecting one thing for over four decades, opening around 1981-1982 and, in the owner's own words, b

chef_bio.background: A small ramen specialist tucked into the Umezu neighborhood of Kyoto's Ukyo ward, on the Arashiyama side of the city near the Hankyu Matsuo-taisha line. The shop has been quietly perfecting one thing for over four decades, opening around 1981 and, in the owner's own words, building it up steadily over roughly 45 years in a corner of Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is simple and warm: once a customer comes in, to earn their return and become a shop they patronize for a lifetime - 'a lifelong asset.' Guests are invited to step in without hesitation and give the ramen a try.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a Kyoto-style shoyu ramen in the Daiichi-Asahi (第一旭) mold: a pork clear-broth (tonkotsu seitan) made from Miyazaki-raised pork, seasoned with a light soy-sauce tare. Unlike many Kyoto bowls it skips the heavy back-fat float yet still drinks rich, with a generous layer of oil on top - mild in flavor but satisfyingly hearty. It comes crowned with soft, fatty melt-in-the-mouth chashu, thin bean sprouts, and Kujo green onion over thin, low-hydration straight noodles.

anecdotes.text: The chashu is made from fatty pork sourced from Miyazaki and is so tender it falls apart and melts in the mouth - a detail regulars single out as the highlight of the bowl.
The owner describes the shop as something built up steadily over about 45 years in one corner of Kyoto, and warmly invites newcomers to come in without hesitation and taste it once.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Osumituki (Kyoto ramen guide) places Daiki in Umezu, Ukyo-ku, dates it to roughly 1981 (~36 years as of 2017, i.e. ~45 now), and describes a Daiichi-Asahi-style Kyoto shoyu on Miyazaki pork with an oil float (not heavy back-fat), fatty melt chashu, fine bean sprouts, Kujo negi and thin low-hydration straight noodles. The shop's own site voices the 'lifelong patronage' aim.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The ramen uses wheat noodles cooked in shared water and a wheat-based light soy-sauce tare; there is no gluten-free preparation or separate handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is seasoned with a light soy-sauce (薄口醤油) tare, which is wheat-based as standard Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork bone stock and the bowl is topped with pork chashu, so all menu items are animal-based; no vegan options exist.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only, counter-only neighborhood shop with no English menu or service; some basic Japanese or pointing at the simple ramen-and-chashu menu will make ordering smooth.

places.name: 龍門 本店 (Ryumon Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The noodle dishes (scallion-oil chicken noodles, hot-and-sour noodles) use wheat noodles, and Chinese cooking here relies heavily on wheat-based soy sauce and shared frying/wok surfaces. There is no evidence of allergen handling or a dedicated setup, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. This is a full Sichuan Chinese restaurant built around pork, chicken, seafood and egg dishes. A few vegetable-forward plates exist (e.g. spicy stir-fried eggplant, eight-treasure mixed vegetables, garlic eggplant), but these are typically cooked with meat-based stock, oils, soy/oyster-style seasonings and shared woks, so they are not reliably vegan. Plant-based diners would need to ask, and even then options are narrow.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists 17:00–23:00 daily; the official site and local press describe the main branch as open 17:00 until 05:00 the next morning, a well-known late-night spot. No regular closing day; open year-round (one source notes Sundays closed, but most sources indicate daily operation — confirm by phone).
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama-Sanjo / Higashiyama Station area (subway Tozai L
places.notes: Ryumon is widely regarded as one of Kyoto's pioneers of authentic Sichuan cooking, with its original Higashiyama-Sanjo location anchoring a small family of branches across the city. The main shop is run with a genuinely Chinese kitchen and floor — Mandarin is spoken throughout, a

chef_bio.background: Ryumon is widely regarded as one of Kyoto's pioneers of authentic Sichuan cooking, with its original Higashiyama-Sanjo location anchoring a small family of about four branches across the city. The main shop is run with a genuinely Chinese kitchen and floor — Mandarin is spoken throughout, and the cooking balances heat with depth rather than chasing pure spice.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, homestyle Sichuan food cooked the way it is in China — real Sichuan chilies and peppercorns, big flavors, late into the night — served in an unpretentious neighborhood setting.
chef_bio.specialty: The scallion-oil chicken noodles (葱油鶏面, ¥650) are the house signature — an inexpensive bowl that regulars describe as authentic enough to bring a tear to your eye. Alongside them, the fiery Sichuan-peppercorn stir-fried eggplant (¥950) and a properly numbing mapo tofu are the dishes locals come back for.

anecdotes.text: The shop draws not only Kyoto locals but visitors from across Japan and even diners from China seeking a taste of home, a marker of how authentic the cooking is.
The dining room is so thoroughly Chinese in feel that one writer joked the inside is 'practically China,' with Mandarin flying between the staff and Sichuan aromatics filling the air.
Regulars single out the 650-yen scallion-oil chicken noodles as 'delicious enough to cry over' and praise it as the real, local flavor.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi and Sotokoto confirm Ryumon as a Kyoto pioneer of authentic Sichuan cooking at Higashiyama-Sanjo (open until ~5am) with roughly four branches, a Chinese kitchen speaking Mandarin, the ¥650 scallion-oil chicken noodles ('泣くほどおいしい'), the Sichuan-peppercorn stir-fried eggplant (¥950), spicy mapo tofu, and diners from across Japan and China. No source states a specific 'Chengdu' style, so that qualifier was removed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan Chinese cooking of this style relies on wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings; no gluten-free soy alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes are cooked in shared woks alongside pork, chicken and seafood and commonly use meat-based stock and animal-derived seasonings; no vegan-separation practice is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, authentic late-night Chinese spot where Mandarin flies between staff; a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way, and the lively, no-frills atmosphere is welcoming rather than precious.

places.name: 瀘川 (Rosen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision found. The house specialty is tantan-men, which is wheat noodles in a sesame-rich, meat-based sauce, and the kitchen is a Sichuan/Chinese operation that relies on wheat-based soy sauce and fermented bean paste (doubanjiang). Mapo tofu, yakisoba, and other listed dishes are all gluten-bearing. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no allergen labeling, and no cross-contamination controls were documented anywhere. Treat as not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian-oriented shop. The signature tantan-men contains pork mince and meat/sesame sauce, and the broader Sichuan menu (mapo tofu, Peking duck, daily lunch sets) is built around meat and animal stocks. No vegan or vegetarian options were identified on any page. A vegan diner could not be reliably accommodated here.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:30-21:30 (L.O. 21:00); closed Wednesdays. (Some listings show 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-21:30; Tabelog gives the 11:30/17:30 times.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Rosen is a small, calm one-story house restaurant in the Kashihara neighborhood of Kyoto's Nishikyo Ward, a short ride from Hankyu Katsura Station. The proprietor trained at well-regarded Sichuan kitchens in the Kansai region and continues to travel to China, bringing that ground

chef_bio.background: Rosen is a small, calm house restaurant in the Kashihara neighborhood of Kyoto's Nishikyo Ward, a short walk from Hankyu Katsura Station. Owner-chef Fukaya (深谷信人), a Kameoka native, trained from his teens — culinary school, then the celebrated Sichuan restaurant Kato Hanten (華都飯店) that once sat in Kyoto's Holiday Inn, followed by kitchens in Osaka and Nishinomiya — before going solo to Shanghai to study authentic Chinese cooking. He named the shop after Luzhou (瀘州) in Sichuan and opened in Kashihara about a dozen years ago, and he still hand-makes his doubanjiang, tianmianjiang and douchi and cooks with Kashihara- and Kameoka-grown vegetables.
chef_bio.philosophy: Authentic Sichuan and Chinese cooking served generously and affordably in an unfussy, settled space — confident portions and honest pricing rather than show, built on house-made seasonings and local produce.
chef_bio.specialty: Tantan-men is the house signature, offered in white-sesame and black-sesame versions (with a curry tantan-men alongside). The black-sesame bowl is the standout: a rich, deeply aromatic sesame sauce with gentle sweetness and restrained heat over springy noodles — mellow rather than fiercely spicy. The Sichuan mapo tofu comes in mild and tongue-numbing spicy versions, and a salt yakisoba on wide flat noodles finished with a touch of oyster sauce is a quieter favorite.

anecdotes.text: Owner-chef Fukaya, from Kameoka, worked his way through the famed Sichuan restaurant Kato Hanten (華都飯店) and kitchens in Osaka and Nishinomiya, then trained solo in Shanghai, naming the shop for Luzhou (瀘州) in Sichuan.
Lunch sets stay under about 900 yen and the dining room fills at midday, drawn by the fragrant tantan-men and the mapo tofu offered in both mild and tongue-numbing spicy versions.

A reviewer singled out the black-sesame tantan-men for its mellow, sweet-leaning sesame richness and good noodle bounce, and praised the salt yakisoba made with wide flat noodles finished with a touch of oyster sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hankei Kyoto Shimbun profiles owner-chef 深谷信人 (Fukaya, 42, from Kameoka): culinary school from his teens, the Sichuan restaurant Kato Hanten (華都飯店) in the former Kyoto Holiday Inn, then Osaka and Nishinomiya, then solo study in Shanghai; the shop is named for Luzhou (瀘州) in Sichuan, opened ~12 years ago in Kashihara, hand-makes doubanjiang/tianmianjiang/douchi and uses Kashihara/Kameoka vegetables. Kyotopi and Gourpass confirm the white/black-sesame and curry tantan-men (mellow black-sesame standout), mild/spicy Sichuan mapo, salt yakisoba with oyster-sauce finish, and sub-¥900 lunches.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House specialty is wheat-noodle tantan-men prepared in a shared Sichuan/Chinese kitchen alongside other wheat-containing dishes (yakisoba, etc.); no gluten-free handling was documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan and Chinese cooking here relies on standard wheat-based soy sauce and fermented bean paste; no wheat-free seasoning alternatives were mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tantan-men contains pork mince and the menu centers on meat and animal stocks; no vegan/vegetarian preparation or separation was noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood Chinese restaurant with no indication of English support, so arriving with your order chosen and a few Japanese phrases ready will make things smoothest.

places.name: とんこつらぁ麺 嘉晴 (Tonkotsu Raamen Yoshiharu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop — straight thin wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth, with gyoza (wheat skins) and fried rice/karaage on the menu. The tare and accompaniments almost certainly include wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation is advertised on any source. A celiac diner should not eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire identity of the shop is a pork-bone (plus chicken carcass and chicken-feet) broth simmered 24+ hours, topped with chashu pork. There are no vegetable- or vegan-broth options noted anywhere. Side dishes are rice/gyoza/karaage, also non-vegan. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Lunch: Tue-Fri 11:00-14:30, Sat-Sun/holidays 11:00-14:45. Dinner: Tue-Sat 17:30-22:00, Sun/holidays 17:30-21:30. Closed Mondays (or the following Tuesday when Monday is a holiday). Note: some listings show slightly different hours (e.g., dinner to 23:30 on Tabelog); the official site hours are used here.
places.neighborhood: 中京区 西ノ京 (near Nijō Castle / Sanjō-kai shopping arcade)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Shoichi Iwasaki spent roughly 15 years training at a celebrated tonkotsu ramen shop in Minoh, Osaka, before opening Yoshiharu in 2013. After leaving the kitchen where he learned his craft, he spent extensive trial-and-error developing the broth that would become the sh

chef_bio.origin: Opened March 2013 in the Nishinokyō neighborhood of Kyoto's Nakagyō Ward, near Nijō Castle and the Sanjō-kai shopping arcade.
chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Shoichi Iwasaki (岩﨑昭一) spent roughly 15 years training at a celebrated tonkotsu ramen shop in Minoh, Osaka — also his hometown — before opening Yoshiharu on March 11, 2013. After leaving the kitchen where he learned his craft, he spent extensive trial-and-error developing the broth that would become the shop's signature.
chef_bio.philosophy: Iwasaki aims for a broth that suppresses the heavy off-odor often associated with pork bone, delivering deep umami and richness with a clean, light finish — a balance of 'umami, richness, and depth.' The shop frames its mission as making ramen — broth, noodles and chashu — that cannot be replicated elsewhere.
chef_bio.specialty: A 'Minoh-style' tonkotsu — distinct from Kyushu tonkotsu — built on a creamy white broth of pork skull and knuckle bones, chicken carcass and chicken feet (momiji), finished with quality back fat, pork skin and plenty of vegetables, then simmered for 24+ hours. The flagship bowl is the special tonkotsu ramen with straight thin noodles, soft sweet chashu, green onion and bean sprouts; the kitchen also offers shoyu, miso, and mayu (burnt-garlic-oil) variations.

anecdotes.text: The shop is regarded as a rarity in Kyoto for being a dedicated pork-bone (tonkotsu) specialist rather than the city's more common lighter styles.
The broth is built from pork skull and knuckle bones plus chicken carcass and chicken feet, back fat, pork skin and vegetables, simmered for 24+ hours.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site and Osumituki confirm owner-chef 岩﨑昭一 (Iwasaki Shoichi), the March 11 2013 opening in Nishinokyo (Sanjokai arcade, near Nijo Station), ~15 years' training at a tonkotsu shop in Minoh (箕面), Osaka — his hometown, which he honors by naming the style 'Minoh tonkotsu' — the 24-hour broth (pork skull/knuckle, chicken carcass, chicken feet/momiji, back fat, pork skin, vegetables), the off-odor-suppressed 'umami/richness/depth' aim, and its rarity as a Kyoto tonkotsu specialist.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood ramen counter with a mobile-phone ordering system and self-serve sides; Japanese-language menu and a little Japanese will make ordering smoother, though pointing works fine.

places.name: ichiren KYOTO（イチレン） (ichiren KYOTO)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop: the signature bowls use thin/thick wheat noodles in pork-and-chicken or fish-dashi broths, and standard ramen tare contains wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no allergen statement, and no celiac-safe handling is published. Not safe for celiacs; even the soy milk yuzu bowl is served over wheat noodles.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan shop. The two core bowls are pork-bone/chicken broth (一蓮) and fish-dashi broth (紗), each topped with tsukune (meatball) and chashu/egg. The 豆乳柚子 (soy milk yuzu) bowl is creamy but is finished with roasted bacon, so it is not vegan, and no vegan-modified version is advertised. A diner could ask, but no plant-based ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (changes by season / details announced on Instagram). Tabelog lists 11:30-18:00. Hours change seasonally.
places.neighborhood: 東山区桝屋町 — Ninenzaka, just below Kiyomizu-dera / Kodai-ji (nea
places.cuisine: Yuzu ramen / chicken paitan; tonkotsu-chicken bone
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ichiren is a newer yuzu-ramen specialist tucked into an alley just off Ninenzaka, a couple of minutes' walk below Kiyomizu-dera and Kodai-ji, opened in May 2021. It is a fresh brand launched by the family behind Motomachi Ramen (元町ラーメン), a Kyoto ramen name carried across three ge

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto — a new concept from the three-generation Motomachi Ramen family, set in a renovated traditional wooden machiya on Ninenzaka.
chef_bio.background: ichiren is a newer yuzu-ramen specialist tucked into an alley just off Ninenzaka, a couple of minutes' walk below Kiyomizu-dera and Kodai-ji, opened May 16, 2021. It is a fresh brand launched by the family behind Motomachi Ramen (元町ラーメン), a Kyoto ramen name carried across three generations, so the bowls draw on a long local lineage while being reworked into something refined and contemporary.
chef_bio.philosophy: To carry forward Kyoto tradition while evolving it into a refined, distinctly Kyoto ramen — built around yuzu and a calm white-and-yuzu-toned interior meant to be welcoming for solo diners and especially women.
chef_bio.specialty: Yuzu-forward Kyoto ramen. The namesake 一蓮 (Ichiren) is a rich double broth of pork bone and chicken bones brightened with yuzu; 紗 (Sara) is a clearer fish-dashi bowl (grilled flying fish, bonito, mackerel, sardine) also scented with yuzu; and the 豆乳柚子 (soy milk yuzu) bowl is creamy yet light. Bowls come with tsukune, and tables offer tororo kombu, yuzu-shichimi and yuzu-kosho to shift the flavor mid-meal.

anecdotes.text: A new brand from the three-generation Kyoto ramen house Motomachi Ramen (元町ラーメン), aiming to inherit tradition while offering a more refined bowl.
Designed by a female owner around a soft, inviting white-and-yuzu-colored space so that solo diners and women feel comfortable.
Lanterns in the shop were made by Kojima Shoten, a well-known Kyoto lantern atelier, reinforcing the machiya atmosphere.
The signature yuzu ramen is presented as a refined Kyoto experience, and tables provide tororo kombu, yuzu-shichimi and yuzu-kosho to vary the taste.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ichiren's official site and Kyorism confirm the May 16 2021 opening on Ninenzaka (near Kiyomizu-dera/Kodai-ji) in a renovated machiya, the three-generation Motomachi Ramen (元町ラーメン) lineage, the female owner and women-welcoming white-and-yuzu interior, the yuzu-forward 一蓮 (pork+chicken) / 紗 (fish dashi: flying fish, bonito, mackerel, sardine) / 豆乳柚子 bowls at ~¥1,000 with tsukune, the Kojima Shoten (小島商店) lanterns, and the tororo-kombu/yuzu-shichimi/yuzu-kosho condiments.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core bowls are wheat-noodle ramen in pork/chicken and fish-dashi broths; no gluten-free noodles or gluten-free menu are offered, so the kitchen is wheat-heavy and not celiac-appropriate.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even the plant-leaning soy milk yuzu bowl is finished with roasted bacon, and broths are animal-based; no vegan preparation is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished, tourist-area yuzu-ramen shop near Kiyomizu-dera designed to welcome solo diners and women; staff are used to visitors, though a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa help.

places.name: あきちゃんラーメン (Akichan Ramen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. The dish is wheat ramen noodles (flat noodles from Shinjuku Daruma Seimensho) in a soy-seasoned broth (wheat-based shoyu tare typical of the style). No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodations are mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. The signature chuka soba is built on a pork-based clear broth (清湯) with sharp soy/salt seasoning and is topped with chashu pork belly, so the broth and standard bowls contain animal products.
places.hours_raw: Sun-Thu 11:00-19:00; Fri, Sat and holidays 11:00-21:00. Open year-round including New Year.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Akichan Ramen opened on September 4, 2025, a short walk from Ono Station near Zuishin-in temple, becoming what local press called the first 'chan-kei' (ちゃん系) ramen shop to land in Kyoto. It belongs to the Chan-noren guild (ちゃんのれん組合), a federation of Tokyo shops such as Kanda Chie

roles: independent ramen shop / chan-noren guild member
chef_bio.background: Akichan Ramen opened on September 4, 2025, a short walk from Ono Station near Zuishin-in temple, becoming what local press called the first 'chan-kei' (ちゃん系) ramen shop to land in Kyoto. It belongs to the Chan-noren guild (ちゃんのれん組合), a federation of Tokyo shops such as Kanda Chie-chan, Shinjuku Ecchan, and Ikebukuro Hiro-chan that brought this style westward.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithfully recreate the Kanto 'chan-kei' bowl — layered umami, rendered pork fat richness, and a clean clear broth — for Kyoto diners, with noodles kept slightly separate from the broth for texture.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka soba (中華そば) — a Tokyo-style clear, fat-rich pork broth with sharp soy-and-salt seasoning, served with generous chashu pork belly, deeply flavored bamboo shoots, and white negi over flat, smooth specialty noodles from Shinjuku Daruma Seimensho. A favorite move is adding a raw egg to mellow the saltiness, often paired with negi rice.

anecdotes.text: Billed by local food media as Kyoto's first taste of the 'chan-kei' ramen wave that had been spreading through the Kanto region.
Uses flat, smooth specialty noodles sourced from Shinjuku Daruma Seimensho, a hallmark of the chan-kei guild shops.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi confirms the Sept 4 2025 opening near Ono Station/Zuishin-in (Yamashina), the billing as Kyoto's first 'chan-kei' shop, membership in the Chan-noren guild (ちゃんのれん組合: Kanda Chie-chan, Shinjuku Ecchan, Ikebukuro Hiro-chan), the clear fat-rich pork chuka-soba with chashu pork belly, seasoned bamboo shoots and white negi, flat smooth noodles from Shinjuku Daruma Seimensho, and the raw-egg add-in.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka soba is seasoned with a sharp soy-and-salt tare typical of the style; soy sauce in Japanese ramen is normally wheat-based, so the broth should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-based clear consomme and bowls are topped with chashu pork belly, so all standard items contain animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local ramen counter with ticket-machine ordering and no reservations or English menu noted, so a little Japanese or pointing helps, though the system is straightforward.

places.name: 夢は力なり (Yume wa Chikara Nari)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free diners. This is a Jiro-style ramen shop built around house-made ultra-thick wheat noodles and a soy (shoyu) tare, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. The broth is pork-based and bowls are loaded with wheat noodles, so gluten is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen accommodation is advertised. Abura soba (brothless) is also wheat noodles. Treat as off-limits if you require strict GF.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. Every bowl is built on a pork-bone/pork broth with large pork chunks (chashu), and standard toppings include a boiled egg. There is no vegetable or plant-based broth option advertised. The free "call" add-ons (extra vegetables, oil, garlic) sit on top of an animal-based bowl and do not make it vegan.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, holidays: 10:00-14:00, 17:00-21:00; Wed, Sat, Sun: 10:00-14:00 (lunch only). Opened 2024-07-23. Hours can shift per the shop's social media.
places.neighborhood: Fukakusa, Fushimi-ku — near JR/Keihan Fujinomori Station and
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner is a well-known local Jiro-style ramen proprietor who has long been tied to this Fushimi corner. He previously ran 'Ramen Dai' and later 'Gatts K Ramen' at this same location, and also did a stint training at 'Yume o Katare,' the famous Jiro-style shop. After stepping a

chef_bio.origin: Fushimi-ku, Kyoto — a ramen battleground near Ryukoku University
chef_bio.background: The owner is a well-known local Jiro-style ramen proprietor long tied to this Fushimi corner on Shidan-dori. Over the years he has run several shops in the area — 'Ramen Dai,' 'Gatts K Ramen,' and 'Ryu no Sakura' — and completed a short training stint at 'Yume o Katare,' the celebrated Jiro-style shop. After stepping away, he returned to the neighborhood, on the spot that once housed 'Ramen Dai,' to open this shop in summer 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop name '夢は力なり' means 'a dream is strength' — an aspirational banner from an owner who left and came back to feed his old neighborhood, carrying the lineage of the Jiro-style shops he trained in and ran before.
chef_bio.specialty: A non-emulsified Jiro bowl: a clean, sharply soy-forward, non-emulsified shoyu broth (rather than the thick cloudy emulsified style), paired with house-made wavy ultra-thick chewy noodles, big chunks of pork, a boiled egg, and a hit of black pepper. Diners customize with the free 'call' system — extra vegetables, oil (abura), and garlic — over generous portions (roughly 300g standard). Aburasoba-style eating is part of the appeal.

anecdotes.text: Local food press framed the 2024 opening as a 'famous proprietor returning' to the ramen-dense Fushimi area, on the corner that once held his earlier shop Ramen Dai.
The broth is described as soy-sharp and clearly defined — a non-emulsified style, distinguishing it from the heavier emulsified Jiro bowls — over self-made thick, wavy, springy noodles.
The owner completed a short training stint at 'Yume o Katare,' a celebrated Jiro-style shop, before returning to run this Fushimi counter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kyotopi (kyotopi.jp/articles/l1ige) reports the summer-2024 opening by a well-known local proprietor formerly of Ramen Dai, Gatts K Ramen and Ryu no Sakura, who trained briefly at Yume o Katare; it describes a non-emulsified shoyu Jiro broth, house-made thick wavy noodles, ticket-machine ordering, counter seating and a free topping 'call' system.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a soy (shoyu) tare-based Jiro ramen; Japanese soy sauce / shoyu tare is brewed with wheat in nearly all cases, so the broth seasoning itself contains gluten in addition to the wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on pork/pork-bone broth with pork chashu and egg; no plant-based broth or vegan option is offered, so there is no vegan-safe preparation to protect from cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, cash-only Jiro-style counter shop where ordering runs on the Jiro "call" system (vegetables/oil/garlic) in Japanese — comfortable if you know the basic ramen ritual, but no English support is advertised.

places.name: マルタ商店 (Maruta Shoten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found. The ramen is built on wheat noodles with a wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) tare over a pork clear broth, so the signature bowls are not celiac-safe. Evening yakitori items often carry wheat-based tare/seasoning as well. No allergen handling or dedicated equipment is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. The core ramen uses a pork-bone clear broth (豚骨清湯) with pork chashu, and the evening menu centers on chicken yakitori, tsukune, and grilled pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen or dishes were found.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:45, 17:30-22:30 (lunch ramen / evening izakaya); Sunday closed. Some sources list lunch ending around 14:00.
places.cuisine: Neighborhood Chinese; Asahi-style clear soy sauce chuka-soba
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained at a ramen shop before opening Maruta Shoten in late May 2025, then built it as a two-faced (二毛作) neighborhood spot near Maruta-cho Shichihonmatsu in Kyoto's Nakagyo ward.

chef_bio.background: The owner trained at a ramen shop before opening Maruta Shoten on May 30, 2025, then built it as a two-faced (二毛作) neighborhood spot near Maruta-cho Shichihonmatsu in Kyoto's Nakagyo ward.
chef_bio.philosophy: By day a straightforward ramen counter, by evening a yakitori-focused izakaya — one small kitchen serving comforting, restrained Kyoto flavors from midday to night.
chef_bio.specialty: A nostalgic Kyoto-style pork clear-broth (豚清湯) shoyu chuka-soba, restrained and lightly seasoned in the classic Kyoto manner — reviewers liken it to the city's pork-bone shops of 30 years ago — and piled generously with chashu, sometimes arranged petal-style (花びらチャーシュー). The owner's ramen-shop training also shows up at night in the grilled pork.

anecdotes.text: The shop runs as a 二毛作 (two-crop) operation: lunch (11:00–14:00) is a dedicated light pork clear-broth ramen service, while evenings turn into a charcoal yakitori izakaya.
Reviewers describe a nostalgic, lightly-seasoned Kyoto pork clear-broth shoyu bowl with thick straight noodles and an abundant chashu portion, including a petal-arranged (花びらチャーシュー) version.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapan-kyoto.com and osumituki.com both date the opening to May 30, 2025 in Nakagyo-ku near Maruta-cho Shichihonmatsu, describe the 二毛作 lunch-ramen / evening-yakitori model, a nostalgic Kyoto pork clear-broth (清湯) shoyu chuka-soba with thick straight noodles and generous (petal-style) chashu, and roughly 30 seats.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is described as a 醤油 (soy sauce) shoyu tare over Kyoto pork clear broth; standard Japanese shoyu is wheat-based, so the tare should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone clear soup and toppings include pork chashu; evening dishes center on chicken and pork, indicating heavy animal-product use throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood spot with no indication of English service; basic Japanese or a translation app will help, especially as it shifts to an izakaya in the evening.

places.name: 麺家ろまん (Menya Roman)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen: medium-thin straight wheat noodles from the long-established Kyoto noodle maker 麺屋棣鄂 (est. 1931), plus a soy-based (shoyu) tare and table-side soy sauce — all wheat-containing. No gluten-free noodles or menu noted; not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The house soup is a chicken-bone-and-vegetable paitan (鶏ガラ・野菜) using Satsuma Chiran chicken, and signature bowls include chicken char siu, Kyoto nine-scallion chicken ramen and wagyu items — all animal-based. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is advertised.
places.neighborhood: In front of Kyoto Stn, west side of Kyoto Tower (Higashi-Shiokojicho, Shimogyo Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺家ろまん opened on January 29, 2026, in the space that previously housed the standing izakaya "すたんど ろまん家" near Kyoto Station, just west of Kyoto Tower. It is a ramen conversion of that yakitori-izakaya operation (the Kyoto "浪漫家／ろまん家" group), reborn as a chicken-paitan specialist.

roles: Former standing izakaya converted to ramen by the Kyoto 浪漫家／ろまん家 group
chef_bio.origin: 下京区東塩小路町 — a few minutes west of Kyoto Station / Kyoto Tower.
chef_bio.background: 麺家ろまん opened on January 29, 2026, in the space that previously housed the standing izakaya "すたんど ろまん家" near Kyoto Station, just west of Kyoto Tower. It is a ramen conversion of that yakitori-izakaya operation (the Kyoto "浪漫家／ろまん家" group), reborn as a chicken-paitan specialist.
chef_bio.philosophy: An approachable, Kyoto-leaning take on chicken paitan built around quality branded chicken and a respected local noodle maker, served as easy, sit-and-slurp comfort food a short walk from the station.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 鶏白湯ラーメン (chicken paitan, 1,000 yen): a chicken-bone-and-vegetable potage-style broth made with Satsuma Chiran chicken (さつま知覧どり), finished with a soy-based tare and paired with medium-thin straight wheat noodles from the historic Kyoto noodle house 麺屋棣鄂 (founded 1931). The broth leans gentle and creamy, opening up as you stir the bowl and add pepper or the table soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a direct conversion of the group's standing izakaya すたんど ろまん家, which is why it keeps the "ろまん" name from the parent yakitori-izakaya brand.
The chicken paitan uses Satsuma Chiran chicken (さつま知覧どり) for the broth, and the noodles come from the long-running Kyoto noodle maker 麺屋棣鄂, established in 1931.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com (224634) confirms the Jan 29, 2026 opening in Shimogyo-ku (東塩小路町) near Kyoto Station as a conversion of the すたんど ろまん家 standing izakaya, a ¥1,000 鶏白湯 built on chicken bones and vegetables with さつま知覧どり and a soy tare, and medium-thin straight noodles from 麺屋棣鄂 (est. 1931).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy-based (shoyu) seasoning and soy sauce is available at the table for adjustment — standard wheat-containing shoyu, not gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in ramen counter by Kyoto Station with QR-code/mobile ordering, so a non-Japanese-speaking diner can manage, but there is no confirmed English support or allergy handling, so come prepared to point and self-cater.

places.name: 鴨町らーめん (Kamomachi Ramen)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The noodles are wheat-based egg noodles (たまご麺) and the broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is virtually always made with wheat. Combined with the pork/chicken broth, every bowl contains gluten, and there is no gluten-free option. People with wheat allergy or celiac disease should avoid this shop.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The broth is simmered from pork bones and chicken carcasses, the noodles are egg noodles, and the tare is soy-sauce based, so the bowls are fundamentally animal-based. There is no advertised vegan or vegetable-broth ramen.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat and days before holidays 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:50) / ~17:00-22:00 (some sources say 22:30); holidays 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Sun (Mon closures vary by month, posted monthly on the official site).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 鴨町らーめん is a tiny counter-only ramen shop tucked into the Demachi neighborhood a minute's walk from Demachiyanagi Station, on a stretch with surprisingly few eateries that makes it a reliable local stop. It has been a fixture of this student-heavy corner of Sakyo Ward, beloved mor

chef_bio.background: 鴨町らーめん is a tiny counter-only ramen shop (about nine seats) tucked into the Demachi neighborhood a minute's walk east of Demachiyanagi Station, on a stretch with surprisingly few eateries that makes it a reliable local stop. It has been a fixture of this student-heavy corner of Sakyo Ward, beloved more by neighbors and Kyoto University-area regulars than by tourists.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop sums itself up as 「味よし 麺よし 心よし」 — good taste, good noodles, good heart — and the spirit shows in small touches like complimentary house-made kimchi offered on request and uncooked take-home noodles to enjoy at home.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a mild tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: pork bones and chicken carcasses, carefully cleaned, are simmered for hours with several kinds of aromatic vegetables into a broth that is slightly thick and mellow on the palate, with the pork richness leading over the soy. It is paired with straight medium-thin noodles and fatty, naturally sweet braised chashu. The menu is broad for such a small shop — miso (a blend of several misos), Hakata-style, tantanmen and hearty set meals among them.

anecdotes.text: Regulars and writers single out the hospitality as much as the bowl: the owner and staff are described as approachable and as giving 「丁寧で気持ちがよい接客」 (courteous, pleasant service) — fitting the shop's mellow tonkotsu-shoyu reputation.
Ask and they'll set out free house-made kimchi alongside table condiments like spicy miso and pepper; they also sell uncooked ramen to take home and prepare yourself.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-gourmet.info and kyotopi (SiMPC) describe a counter-only Demachiyanagi shop with pork-bone-over-soy broth, medium-thin straight noodles, fatty-sweet chashu, free house-made kimchi and take-home noodles; the official site (kamomachi.com) carries the motto 味よし麺よし心よし and confirms a pork-bone/chicken-carcass broth simmered for hours with aromatic vegetables, ~9 counter seats and a miso-blend option.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone soy-sauce) base; soy-sauce tare of this kind is virtually always made with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is simmered from pork bones and chicken carcasses, so all ramen here is animal-based and unsuitable for vegans/vegetarians.

positives.text: Noodles are explicitly egg noodles (たまご麺) — relevant for both egg and wheat allergies.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local counter shop in a student neighborhood with warm, attentive hosts; little to no English is expected, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will go a long way.

places.name: キラメキ☆JAPAN (Kirameki Japan)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop built around house-made noodles, with a chicken-paitan broth and shoyu/shio tare. House-made wheat noodles plus a typically wheat-containing soy tare make every signature bowl unsafe for celiac diners, and no gluten-free option is offered.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is centered on chicken paitan (chicken white broth) and chashu pork, with egg toppings; there is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu and no plant-based broth alternative.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); daily, with irregular closures
places.notes: キラメキ☆JAPAN is one of the founding Kyoto shops of the Kirameki brand operated by Kirameki no Mirai Co., Ltd., a group that has grown to more than thirty ramen shops across Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga, Nara and even Shanghai. This counter-only Demachiyanagi spot opened in January 2016 and

roles: Kirameki no Mirai Co., Ltd. (operating company / brand)
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, part of the Kirameki group founded and headquartered in the Kansai region (headquarters and noodle factory relocated in August 2023).
chef_bio.background: キラメキ☆JAPAN is one of the Kirameki brand's Kyoto shops, operated by Kirameki no Mirai Co., Ltd., a Kansai group that has grown to more than twenty ramen shops across Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga and Nara — plus locations in Shanghai — and openly aims at a '30-shop' target. This counter-only Demachiyanagi-area spot (Tanaka-Monzencho, Sakyo Ward) opened on January 15, 2016 with about twelve seats, built around the company's signature chicken-paitan style.
chef_bio.philosophy: The group's stated belief is that the star of ramen is the noodle itself — 'ラーメンの主役は麺' — which is why every shop continues to make its noodles in-house rather than buying them in.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori-paitan ramen — a creamy, rich-yet-not-heavy chicken white broth paired with house-made noodles, alongside crowd favorites like the 醤油のキラメキ (shoyu) bowl and the hearty キラメキ二朗 (Jiro-style) bowl.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen finishes its chicken-paitan bowls with bright touches such as a slice of lemon and chopped raw onion, so the soup reads creamy and full from black pepper yet stays refreshingly light rather than heavy.
Fans describe the tori-paitan as having an almost carbonara-like richness from the creamy broth clinging to the house-made noodles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog (26026867) dates the opening to Jan 15, 2016, a ~12-seat counter-only shop in Tanaka-Monzencho (Sakyo-ku) with tori-paitan and 醤油のキラメキ / キラメキ二朗 bowls finished with lemon and raw onion. The Kirameki no Mirai company site states the '主役は麺' philosophy, an Aug-2023 HQ/factory move, a shop list spanning Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga, Nara and Shanghai (about two dozen locations) and a '30-shop' goal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are the centerpiece of the shop, so flour and wheat are handled throughout the kitchen and shared boiling water is used for all bowls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature bowls include a shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen, and Japanese ramen soy tare almost always contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken-based (tori-paitan) and bowls are topped with pork chashu and egg, so all shared soup, surfaces and utensils carry animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy 12-seat counter ramen bar where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through; no special foreigner-facing service should be expected.

places.name: こういうもん家 Ro-ji (Kouiumonya Ro-ji)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The tantanmen uses wheat noodles (thin noodles from Kushiro Seimen) and a sesame-based tantan tare that, like virtually all Japanese ramen, relies on wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated prep is mentioned on any source. Celiac diners should not eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature tantanmen is built on a meat broth with large chunks of ground pork, and sides are pork karaage and chicken wings. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or advertised.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: 11:00-22:30 (Mon-Sun and holidays). Official / Leaf KYOTO: 11:00-14:00, 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:45). Irregular days off.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] こういうもん家 Ro-ji opened on August 5, 2019, tucked down a narrow alley (the "ro-ji" of its name) just a minute south of Demachiyanagi Station. The space was originally a private company warehouse, reborn as a half-open, yatai (food-stall) style counter with no partitions — the kind o

chef_bio.background: こういうもん家 Ro-ji opened on August 5, 2019, tucked down a narrow alley (the "ro-ji" of its name) just south of Demachiyanagi Station. The space was originally a company warehouse, reborn as a half-open, yatai (food-stall) style counter — the kind of hidden-alley spot you stumble onto and feel you've found a secret.
