chef_bio.philosophy: A creamy tantanmen tuned to please across age groups rather than to chase extreme spice — approachable comfort food served stall-style, equally happy as a quick lunch or a late-night drinking spot.
chef_bio.specialty: Creamy, mild tantanmen. The house bowl leans on a rich sesame aroma with gentle, not-overpowering heat, topped with ground pork; on request the kitchen will hold back the finishing chili oil (rayu) for milder palates. Variations include a spicy 大辛担々麺, a tomato トマ担麺, and a curry カレー担々麺, and a popular half-size (the local move is to order izakaya snacks first and close the night with a half bowl, or turn the leftover soup into 'tantan rice').

anecdotes.text: Thin noodles are sourced from Kushiro Seimen, a Fushimi-based noodle maker, and served slightly soft; the counter sets out scorched garlic and sansho pepper so you can shift the flavor as you eat.
The shop doubles as a casual izakaya in the evening, with house-made karaage (fried chicken) as a signature side and popular takeout item alongside the noodles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com (127167) and leafkyoto.net confirm the Aug 5, 2019 opening in a converted warehouse as an alley yatai near Demachiyanagi, a mild-and-creamy sesame tantanmen with half-size and 'tantan rice,' Kushiro Seimen (Fushimi) noodles and scorched-garlic/sansho condiments; the official ro-ji.com lists the spicy/tomato/curry variations, the option to omit the finishing rayu, and karaage as a signature/takeout item.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tantanmen uses wheat ramen noodles (Kushiro Seimen) boiled in shared water; no gluten-free handling is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source specifies the tare ingredients, but a sesame tantan tare of this style standardly uses wheat-based soy sauce; treat as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is meat-based with ground pork topping; no vegan preparation described.

positives.text: Kitchen will omit the finishing chili oil on request, so the bowl can be made milder.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, half-open food-stall counter where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; no English service is advertised, so a few Japanese words help.

places.name: 中華そば みみお (Chuka Soba Mimio)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature 中華そば uses wheat straight noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken-and-dashi broth (soy sauce is normally wheat-based), with pork-belly chashu. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The shop is built around a chicken-bone and fish/dashi broth with domestic pork-belly chashu; the soy-sauce tare is animal-adjacent and side dishes include pidan (century egg). No vegetable/vegan broth or plant-based bowl is offered or mentioned.
places.hours_raw: Wed-Sun 12:00-14:00, 18:00-23:00 / closed Mon and Tue (hours should be confirmed)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mimio is a Kyoto institution that spent many years as a beloved late-night, after-drinks ramen haunt in the Gion entertainment district, where it quietly drew a following that included entertainers and professional chefs. In March 2016 it relocated across the city to a quieter sp

chef_bio.background: Mimio is a Kyoto institution that spent many years as a beloved late-night, after-drinks ramen haunt in the Gion entertainment district, where it quietly drew a following that included entertainers and professional chefs. In March 2016 it relocated across the city to a quieter spot in Yoshida, Sakyo Ward, near Jingu-Marutamachi, where it carries on the same bowl.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than the usual fast-turnover ramen counter, the owner deliberately designed Mimio as a place to settle in — with counter, tables and a tatami room — so guests can slowly enjoy a drink alongside the bowl. The broth is portioned so you can comfortably finish it to the last sip.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 名物中華そば is a clear, gentle soy-sauce ramen: a broth drawn carefully from chicken bones and dashi with select ingredients, paired with thin, springy straight noodles and a standout chashu of domestic pork belly sourced from a long-established butcher, simmered until it melts on the tongue. Pidan (century egg) is a popular small plate, and the same broth is even turned into a curry.

anecdotes.text: Before its move, Mimio was a long-loved spot in Gion to cap off a night of drinking, frequented by celebrities and fellow chefs.
The noodles are straight noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker founded in 1879, and the chashu uses domestic pork belly from an established butcher, braised until tender.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mypl.net (newopen_sakyokita, sid=47151) documents the Mar 9, 2016 relocation to Yoshida/Sakyo-ku after long years in Gion (loved by celebrities and chefs), counter/table/tatami seating for lingering over a drink, a broth built to be drunk to the last drop, straight noodles from an 1879 noodle maker, domestic pork-belly chashu from an established butcher, plus pidan and a curry.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowl is a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken-and-dashi ramen; soy sauce tare is normally wheat-based, so the broth should be assumed to contain wheat/gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on chicken bones and fish/dashi with pork-belly chashu, and pidan (egg) is a popular side - no vegan-safe preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood ramen-and-drinks spot run by a hands-on owner; little English is expected, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will go a long way.

places.name: 麺屋 神楽 (Menya Kagura)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (medium-thick straight noodles made with whole-grain wheat flour from a local noodle factory) and almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu tare, served in fish/chicken broth. Nothing on any page indicates gluten-free accommodation, and this is a tiny solo-run counter shop unlikely to manage cross-contamination. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every bowl is animal-based: niboshi (dried sardine), bonito, kombu, clam and chicken stock, with chicken oil and chashu (pork) toppings. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 / 17:30-21:30 (Tabelog lists lunch 10:30-14:30 L.O.14:15, dinner 17:30-20:00 L.O.19:45). Closed Wednesdays; occasional irregular closures.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Kagura opened on February 3, 2021 in the Jodoji / Ginkaku-ji area of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, taking over the address of Menya Himawari, a beloved salt-ramen shop that had operated there since 2006 before relocating to Shizuoka in late 2020. The new shop is run by family of the

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Jodoji / Ginkaku-ji area, Sakyo Ward), Japan
chef_bio.background: Menya Kagura opened on February 3, 2021 in the Jodoji / Ginkaku-ji area of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, taking over the address of Menya Himawari, a beloved salt-ramen shop that had operated there since 2006 before relocating to Shizuoka in November 2020. The new shop is run by the former Himawari owner's brother and opened under the former owner's supervision, carrying the lineage forward in the same little space.
chef_bio.philosophy: A fish-driven ramen specialist standing apart from Kyoto's pork-bone-heavy norm, offering deliberately different lunch (lighter, delicate shio/shoyu) and dinner (richer niboshi) styles from one small counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Niboshi (dried sardine) ramen. The kitchen builds its stock from multiple dried sardines, thick-shaved bonito, kombu, clams and chicken. The signature 濃厚煮干そば is a mild-but-rich niboshi bowl with controlled bitterness and a clean sweetness, while the lighter 淡麗醤油そば and 淡麗塩そば show off fish-forward umami with aromatic chicken oil on top. Medium-thick straight whole-grain wheat noodles from a local noodle maker (麺屋棣鄂) come with generous, well-seared chashu, nori and scallion.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in the exact former location of Menya Himawari, a salt-ramen shop open since 2006 that moved to Shizuoka in November 2020; Menya Kagura opened there in February 2021 under the former Himawari owner's supervision.

In a Kyoto ramen scene dominated by pork-bone broths, Kagura's dedicated niboshi/seafood focus near Ginkaku-ji and Kyoto University is described as a rare specialty, with the owner running service solo.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com (145534) confirms the Feb 3, 2021 opening in the former Menya Himawari space (salt ramen since 2006, moved to Shizuoka Nov 2020), operation by the previous owner's brother under his supervision, a sardine/bonito/kombu/clam/chicken stock, 濃厚煮干 and 淡麗 (醤油/塩) bowls, chicken oil, and medium-thick whole-grain straight noodles from 麺屋棣鄂 with chashu, nori and scallion.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are built on wheat noodles and a fish/shoyu base; the shoyu tare is the standard wheat-containing type with no gluten-free alternative mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths use animal stock (dried sardine, bonito, kombu, clam, chicken) with chicken oil and pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian bowl exists, so shared pots make any vegan accommodation impossible.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny solo-run neighborhood counter shop near Ginkaku-ji where ordering is straightforward but staff likely speak little English; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu goes a long way.

places.name: 美田 (Biden)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard ramen shop built on wheat. The bowls use medium-thin wheat noodles (from a Kyoto noodle maker), and the chicken-paitan broth is almost certainly seasoned with a wheat-based shoyu tare. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The house style is a rich chicken (tori-paitan) broth, and the signature bowl is loaded with chicken chashu, chicken tsukune meatballs, and smoked poultry. The broth itself is animal-based, so even a no-toppings request would not be vegan. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is offered. (A cold tomato noodle and an oil/carbonara-style bowl appear on the menu but are not described as plant-based.)
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 15:00), 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00). Sunday lunch only per Tabelog (11:30-15:00). Closed Sundays per shop guides; one source also notes Monday evenings closed.
places.neighborhood: 出町柳 / 今出川 (north of the Kyoto Imperial Palace)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Biden opened on July 16, 2021, just north of the Kyoto Imperial Palace in the Demachi neighborhood near Demachiyanagi Station, an area with very few ramen shops. The owner had previously cooked at a ramen restaurant over in the Saiin district before striking out on his own. The s

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Demachi / north of the Imperial Palace)
chef_bio.background: Biden opened on July 16, 2021, just north of the Kyoto Imperial Palace in the Demachi neighborhood near Demachiyanagi Station, an area with very few ramen shops. The owner had previously cooked at a ramen restaurant over in the Saiin district before striking out on his own. The shop took over the premises of a former izakaya called YAOMON, which had closed after its previous owner fell ill, and Biden kept those izakaya bones — the tatami room and the counter — while reinventing it as a ramen specialist by day.
chef_bio.philosophy: A chicken-paitan style that is rich but elegant, a style the local guides note is rare in Kyoto, served in a relaxed, lived-in room.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori-paitan ramen (around 800 yen): a creamy-looking chicken broth that drinks surprisingly light and refined rather than heavy, paired with straight medium-thin wheat noodles from a long-established Kyoto noodle maker that give the 'patsun' snap locals favor. The bowl is unusually colorful and generous, topped with thin-sliced smoked chicken chashu, chicken tsukune meatballs, sauteed king oyster mushrooms, bell pepper, sweet thick-cut grilled scallions, and daikon sprouts.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a film and arcade-game enthusiast, and the walls of the shop are decorated with movie posters.

The space was a former izakaya (YAOMON) that closed when its previous owner became ill; Biden reused its layout, with tatami seating on one side and the counter and kitchen on the other, and still flips into an izakaya/omakase service in the evenings.
The signature feature is the thick-cut grilled scallion topping, described as a novel touch that turns sweet when chewed.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com (160101) confirms the Jul 16, 2021 opening near Demachiyanagi, the owner's prior work at a Saiin ramen shop, the takeover of the former YAOMON izakaya (closed after the owner's illness) keeping tatami and counter, a light-yet-refined ~¥800 tori-paitan with straight medium-thin Kyoto-maker noodles and a colorful topping array, plus the owner's film/arcade posters; kyoto-gourmet.info highlights the thick-cut grilled scallion.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood shop with no indication of English-language service; basic Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.

places.name: 福仙楼 北白川店 (Fukusenro Kitashirakawa-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The signature bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, and the broth is a pork-bone/chicken (tonkotsu-shoyu) base seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, tamari, or any allergen protocol, and noodles are boiled in shared water. Treat as off-limits if you require strict GF.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around a pork-bone and chicken broth with back fat, braised tendon, and minced Kyotamba pork; even the simplest bowls carry animal stock and pork toppings. No vegetable or vegan broth option is advertised.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 / dinner 18:00-21:30 (last order). Closed Tue and Wed (consecutive). Note: Tabelog lists closed Wed (with a Tue-Wed consecutive closure in Jan 2026), so closing-day info is inconsistent.
places.neighborhood: Kitashirakawa, Sakyo Ward (Chayama / Kyoto University of the Arts Stn)
places.cuisine: Tonkotsu-soy sauce ramen; Taiwanese street-stall-style ramen; tsukemen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 福仙楼 is the台湾屋台 (Taiwanese street-stall) sibling of the well-known Kitashirakawa ramen shop 東龍, opened on April 30, 2009 along the same stretch of 北白川. The team behind it studied Taiwanese street-stall noodle cooking and brought that style back to this quiet student quarter near K

roles: Taiwanese street-stall sibling of 東龍 北白川本店
chef_bio.background: 福仙楼 is the 台湾屋台 (Taiwanese street-stall) sibling of the well-known Kitashirakawa ramen shop 東龍, opened on April 30, 2009 along the same stretch of 北白川 and sharing the 第2青山荘 building (both under the parent 有限会社 潜龍). The team studied Taiwanese street-stall noodle cooking and brought that style to this quiet student quarter near Kyoto University of the Arts, running it as a counterpart to 東龍. The two brands have grown deeply intertwined over the years — the 福仙楼そば and 屋台そば bowls have at times been folded into 東龍's own lineup, so its identity as a fully separate shop has blurred; check current status before a visit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Generous, hearty Taiwanese-street-stall-style bowls at student-friendly prices — comfort ramen made to fill you up rather than to chase trends.
chef_bio.specialty: 福仙楼そば — a rich pork-bone-and-chicken broth finished with back fat and a soy (shoyu) tare, poured over medium-thick, flat, straight house-made noodles and crowned with slow-braised tendon and juicy minced Kyotamba heritage pork, plus bean sprouts, scallion and garlic. The menu runs from the base 屋台そば up through larger 福スペシャル and チャーシュー麺 versions that pile on more pork.

anecdotes.text: The shop's identity is rooted in Taiwanese 屋台 (street-stall) cooking: its base bowl is literally called 屋台そば, and the house style was developed by studying Taiwanese street-noodle technique before adapting it to Kyoto.
福仙楼 and 東龍 are sister operations sharing the same 北白川 building, so the creamy 東龍そば and the soy-rich 福仙楼そば trace to steps from each other.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com (191010) dates 福仙楼's opening to Apr 30, 2009 as the Taiwanese-street-stall sibling of 東龍 sharing the 第2青山荘 building, describes a pork-bone/chicken broth with back fat, shoyu tare, braised tendon and minced Kyotamba pork, and states it closed May 10, 2020 with its menu absorbed into 東龍; the official tonryu.net still lists 福仙楼 with its 屋台そば / 福仙楼そば bowls, hence the hedge on current status.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; there is no gluten-free noodle or separate-pot option mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone/chicken base finished with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat, so the soup itself is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on animal stock (pork bone, chicken, back fat) with pork and tendon toppings; no vegan or vegetable broth is offered, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood ramen counter run by a local Kyoto operator; menus and signage are in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.

places.name: RAMEN嵐山竹林亭 (RAMEN Arashiyama Chikurintei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. All bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (sourced from Kiyokawa Seimen, including "whole-grain" noodles used for the vegan bowls — whole grain still means wheat, not gluten-free), and the tare is naturally-fermented shiro-tamari or soy sauce, both of which are wheat-based. There is no indication of a gluten-free noodle or a wheat-free seasoning, and no dedicated handling for celiac diners. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Genuine vegan bowls are a fixed part of the menu, not an afterthought: a Kyoto-vegetable salt ramen, a "tonkotsu-style" vegetable ramen (plant-based, mimicking pork-bone richness), and a 100% vegan Shinshu-miso ramen, all on whole-grain (wheat) noodles, around 2,000 yen. However this is a small counter shop whose core trade is chicken-clear, chicken-paitan and spiny-lobster-paitan broths with pork chashu, so the vegan bowls are prepared in the same kitchen alongside animal broths. Good for plant-based diners by ingredient, but ask about shared equipment if you need strict separation.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-19:00 (hours and closing days may be irregular)
places.neighborhood: Saga-Arashiyama, Ukyo Ward (in front of Saga Shakado, near the Bamboo Grove path)
places.cuisine: Chicken clear-broth; chicken paitan; spiny-lobster paitan ramen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] RAMEN Arashiyama Chikurintei opened in December 2023, just off the famous bamboo grove in Saga-Arashiyama, run by a trained Japanese-cuisine (washoku) chef and his wife. The couple bring a kaiseki-style attention to stock-making and ingredient sourcing to a bowl of ramen.

chef_bio.background: RAMEN Arashiyama Chikurintei opened in December 2023, just off the famous bamboo grove in Saga-Arashiyama, run by a trained Japanese-cuisine (washoku) chef and his wife. The couple bring a kaiseki-style attention to stock-making and ingredient sourcing to a bowl of ramen.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality ingredients and meticulous, additive-free preparation: named-breed chicken bones, kombu and bonito for the stocks, additive-free Kyoto pork for the chashu, and specially-milled noodles from Kiyokawa Seimen. The kitchen also keeps a real plant-based line so vegan and international visitors are not left out.
chef_bio.specialty: Their signature is the ise-ebi (spiny lobster) paitan ramen, a creamy broth built from Nagoya Cochin and Daiyama chicken bones and Ibaraki pork and finished with extract pressed from spiny-lobster heads, served with additive-free Kyoto-pork rare chashu and a shrimp terrine; the clear chicken (tori chintan) bowl, seasoned with Guerande salt or naturally-fermented shiro-tamari, shows the same restraint.

anecdotes.text: The spiny-lobster paitan gets its depth from broth pressed out of the lobster heads, layered over a chicken-and-pork base.
Run by a washoku professional and his wife, the shop is described locally as standing out as the best ramen in the Arashiyama area.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com (201250) dates the opening to Dec 18, 2023 near the Saga-Arashiyama bamboo grove, run by a washoku chef and his wife; it details the ise-ebi paitan (Nagoya Cochin + Daiyama chicken bones, Ibaraki pork, lobster-head extract, additive-free Kyoto-pork rare chashu, shrimp terrine), a tori-chintan seasoned with Guerande salt or shiro-tamari, Kiyokawa Seimen noodles, a vegan line, and calls it the area's best ramen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ramen, including the vegan bowls, uses wheat noodles; the seasoning is wheat-based shiro-tamari or soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or tare exists here, so the kitchen has no gluten-free workflow.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare is naturally-fermented shiro-tamari (a white tamari/soy) or soy sauce, both wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan ramen (vegetable salt, vegetable 'tonkotsu-style', and 100% vegan Shinshu-miso) is genuinely plant-based by recipe, but it is cooked in the same small kitchen as chicken-clear, chicken-paitan and spiny-lobster broths with pork chashu, so shared pots/utensils are likely.

positives.text: Multiple dedicated vegan bowls are a permanent menu item, and the kitchen emphasizes additive-free ingredients, which is reassuring for plant-based diners by ingredient.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run counter shop in a heavily-touristed area with a smartphone ordering system and English-friendly vegan options, so a few words of Japanese and a polite manner go a long way, but you will likely manage.

places.name: セアブラノ神 slurp (Seabura no Kami slurp)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature tsukemen uses straight wheat noodles (which include wheat bran) from a noodle maker, and the tare is a blend of two soy sauce varieties (wheat-based) cut with kombu water. Broth is pork-bone and chicken collagen. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated-prep option is indicated; a celiac should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. Every signature bowl is built on animal stock — pork-bone/chicken-collagen broth, Iberico pork belly and low-temperature pork chashu, and soy-sauce tare. There is no plant-based ramen on the menu.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward / Sanjo-Karasuma (right by Karasuma-Oike subway Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] slurp is the fourth shop from the Kansai ramen group behind セアブラノ神 (Seabura no Kami), a name that built its reputation popularizing niboshi-and-tonkotsu style ramen in Kyoto through its Mibu flagship and the Fushimi-Gōriki and Nishiki branches. Opened in 2020 near Sanjō-Karasuma,

chef_bio.background: slurp is the fourth shop from the Kansai ramen group behind セアブラノ神 (Seabura no Kami), a name that built its reputation on niboshi-and-tonkotsu style ramen topped with pork back fat. Opened in 2020 near Sanjō-Karasuma, slurp was the group's first venture into tsukemen, deliberately styled less like a traditional ramen shop and more like an upscale dining bar in black tones so that even a solo diner — including women dining alone — feels at ease walking in.
chef_bio.philosophy: Treat a single bowl of tsukemen as an entertaining, multi-course experience, and make the room approachable enough that anyone — including women dining alone — feels welcome.
chef_bio.specialty: The Iberico pork tsukemen — pitched as 'one bowl, five ways to enjoy it.' Straight noodles (from 麺屋棣鄂) are first soaked in kombu (kelp) water and eaten plain to taste their fragrance and umami, then dipped into a clear-yet-rich broth of pork bone and chicken collagen finished with premium Spanish Iberico pork; a squeeze of citrus refreshes the midpoint, and the leftover broth is cut with bonito dashi and finished over rice like a soup or risotto.

anecdotes.text: slurp was the established Seabura no Kami group's first move into tsukemen, designed as a stylish dining-bar-like space rather than a conventional ramen counter so solo and first-time diners feel comfortable.

The signature Iberico pork tsukemen is built to be eaten in stages — plain kombu-water noodles, then dipped, then brightened with citrus, then the broth finished with bonito dashi over rice — described as 'five ways to enjoy one bowl.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog Magazine (article 420882) identifies slurp as the Seabura no Kami group's fourth shop and first tsukemen, opened 2020 near Sanjo-Karasuma with a black-toned dining-bar design welcoming to solo diners and women; it details the Iberico pork tsukemen served 'five ways,' with kombu-water noodles from 麺屋棣鄂, a pork-bone/chicken-collagen broth, Iberico pork, a citrus refresher and a bonito-dashi rice finish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsukemen tare is described as a blend of two soy sauce varieties cut with kombu-infused water; standard soy sauce is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is a pork-bone and chicken-collagen base finished with Iberico pork belly, and the bowl is topped with pork belly and sous-vide pork chashu — animal-based throughout.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished, design-forward lunch-only tsukemen counter intended to feel welcoming even to solo diners; a friendly greeting and pointing at the signature bowl will carry you, though staff likely operate mainly in Japanese.

places.name: あけてもくれても (Akete mo Kurete mo)
places.gf_detail: This is Jiro-style ramen: thick wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth (or as a brothless 汁なし bowl) built on a soy-based tare. Wheat is present in both the noodles and the soy seasoning, and there is no indication of any gluten-free option or accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone strictly avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: A pork-centric Jiro-style shop: pork-bone broth, large slabs of chashu, and pork back-fat are core to every bowl, with mountains of bean sprouts and raw garlic as standard toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl, and no indication that one can be made.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 18:00-22:00, closed Sun (open on holidays)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop sits in the lineage of "Yume Wo Katare Kyoto," the dream-fueled Jiro-style ramen project that opened in this Senbon-Nakatachiuri neighborhood in 2017. True to the Yume Wo Katare ethos, where a location either closes or is reborn under a new name after a set run, the seco

chef_bio.background: The shop sits in the lineage of "Yume Wo Katare Kyoto," the dream-fueled Jiro-style ramen project whose second location in this Senbon-Nakatachiuri neighborhood (Yume Wo Katare Kyoto II) opened on November 29, 2017. True to the Yume Wo Katare ethos, where a location is restructured or reborn under a new name after a set run, the shop was rebranded on April 1, 2025 as あけてもくれても after owner Teru took the whole place over, carrying the same hulking bowls into a new identity.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carry forward the Yume Wo Katare spirit of generous, soul-filling ramen and the discipline of reinventing the shop rather than letting it stagnate, while keeping the experience honest, hands-on, and built around the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: A classic Jiro-style (Ramen Sou-lineage) bowl: ultra-thick, chewy wheat noodles in a sweet soy-and-pork-bone broth, crowned with a fat slab of chashu, a mountain of bean sprouts, back-fat, and garlic. The house also leans into a signature 汁なし (brothless) version, a nod echoed in the playful name.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, あけてもくれても ("whether it opens or closes / day in, day out"), winks at both its rebirth from Yume Wo Katare and its embrace of the brothless 汁なし bowl.
It traces directly back to Yume Wo Katare Kyoto II, opened November 2017, whose tradition of renaming or restarting a shop after its run led to this April 2025 incarnation under Teru.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com confirms the shop name changed from 'Yume Wo Katare Kyoto II' (opened 2017-11-29) to 'あけても くれても' on 2025-04-01, Jiro/二郎系 style with sweet pork broth and 汁なし options, in Kamigyo-ku's Senbon-Nakatachiuri. noodlers.jp confirms current owner てる (Teru) took over the whole shop under the Yume Wo Katare 'restructure after a run' philosophy, that it specializes in 汁なし, and the name's play on words.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a sweet soy-based tare over wheat noodles, so wheat is present in both the seasoning and the noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork-bone broth, large chashu, and pork back-fat are standard across the menu, with no meat-free preparation noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, locals-and-students Jiro-style counter where you order outside, wait to be called in, and call your toppings ("ニンニク・アブラ・ヤサイ") in Japanese at the counter, so first-timers without a guide or some prep can find the ritual intimidating.

places.name: 極太清流らーめん (Gokuboso Seiryu Ramen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a Jiro-inspired ramen shop built around extremely thick wheat noodles, served with a sweet wheat-shoyu-based tare and a pork broth — the entire concept is wheat-noodle-centric. There is no indication of GF noodles, GF tare, allergen handling, or a dedicated preparation area. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The bowls are built on pork (chashu/meat toppings) and an animal-based broth with a soy/wheat tare; signature orders are explicitly "with meat" (肉あり). No vegan ramen option is mentioned anywhere in the sources.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri 11:00-14:00 & 18:00-22:00; Sat/Sun 10:30-14:00; closed Wednesdays (and holidays). Note: the owner sometimes closes on short notice when he judges the noodle quality is not up to standard.
places.neighborhood: 下京区 西大路八条（near JR Nishi-Oji Station）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner cut his teeth at Nagoya's famed Jiro-inspired shop「ら けいこ」before bringing that gut-punching, big-portion style to a small storefront in southwest Kyoto, a short walk north of Nishi-Oji Station.

chef_bio.origin: Nagoya, via the Jiro-inspired ramen shop「ら・けいこ」(Rakeiko)
chef_bio.background: The owner cut his teeth at Nagoya's Jiro-inspired shop「極太濁流ラーメン ら・けいこ」before bringing that gut-punching, big-portion style to a small storefront in southwest Kyoto's Shimogyo ward, a short walk north of Nishi-Oji Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Noodles first, no compromise: the owner will close the shop on the spot if he judges the day's noodles aren't good enough — announcing closures on social media, a quality-over-convenience stance that keeps the schedule a little unpredictable but the bowls honest.
chef_bio.specialty: Extra-thick (極太), chewy straight noodles in generous, hearty bowls — a sweet soy-based tare clinging to the noodles, piled with vegetables, meat and garlic. The shop runs both broth (汁あり) and no-broth (汁なし) versions, with a standout no-broth bowl named シルナシノ神 ('the god of soupless'), served with fat-coated noodles, thick tonkatsu-style pork loin, greens, crushed nuts, a fried egg, crisp bean sprouts and minced garlic.

anecdotes.text: Regulars know the shop as a 'those-in-the-know know it' hidden gem, and that the owner has been known to post that he is closing because the noodles didn't come out right that day — quality over keeping the doors open.
The defining feature is the gut-punch (ガツン系) extra-thick noodle, a direct inheritance of the Nagoya「ら・けいこ」lineage the owner trained in.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: guts-rentacar.com (Nishioji) confirms the owner trained at a Nagoya ramen shop, the addictive chewy extra-thick Jiro-inspired noodles, sweet soy broth, ~3-min walk north of Nishioji Station, and the owner closing when noodle quality drops (announced via SNS). Web search of Tabelog/Retty/nagoya-meshi confirms the Nagoya training shop is「極太濁流ラーメン ら・けいこ」and that the signature no-broth bowl is シルナシノ神 (~750 yen).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are dressed with a sweet soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in this style is wheat-containing; combined with the wheat noodles there is no celiac-safe path here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on pork and meat toppings with an animal-based broth; no vegan preparation is mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter shop with ticket-machine ordering and no listed phone or English support — comfortable for a visitor with some Japanese or willingness to point and gesture, but plan to order by machine and dine quickly.

places.name: 萬盛 (Mansei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. The chuka-soba is wheat Chinese noodles in a chicken-bone soy (shoyu) broth, and the menu centers on wheat-based udon, soba, ramen, yakisoba, curry and fried tempura items. Soy-based tare and seasonings are standard Japanese shoyu (wheat-containing). There is no indication of allergen handling or a dedicated fryer, so this is not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature chuka-soba uses a chicken-bone (tori-gara) soy broth with roasted pork and kamaboko (fish cake) toppings, and dishes broadly rely on dashi, egg, meat and seafood. No vegan options are advertised.
places.hours_raw: Monday–Saturday 11:00–19:30; Sunday closed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mansei is a much-loved old-style neighborhood shokudo (taishoku-do / machi-chuka) in the Nishinokyo–Enmachi area of Kyoto, near Enmachi Station and Hanazono University. It is the kind of culturally-valuable corner eatery (described locally as a 文化財的な街の食堂, a culturally-treasured t

chef_bio.background: Mansei is a much-loved old-style neighborhood shokudo (taishoku-do / machi-chuka) in the Nishinokyo–Enmachi area of Kyoto's Nakagyo ward, near Enmachi Station and Hanazono University. It is the kind of culturally-valuable corner eatery (described locally as a 文化財的な街の食堂, a culturally-treasured town diner) that has fed the surrounding neighborhood for generations, with neatly kept food-sample cases in the window, an aged wall menu, manga to read while you wait, and a relaxed table-seat layout. Delivery to local homes and workplaces is part of its everyday rhythm.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, affordable, comforting everyday food for the neighborhood — the appeal is the nostalgic, unpretentious shokudo experience rather than any single trend.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the chuka-soba: a simple, body-warming chicken-bone soy (tori-gara shoyu) broth topped with kamaboko fish cake, roast pork, bean sprouts and chopped green onion, served with firm, low-hydration Chinese-style noodles. Beyond the chuka-soba the kitchen turns out a wide home-style range — udon, soba, yakisoba, rice bowls, curry, omurice and stir-fries — at gentle prices, with the tenjoji-don (天とじ丼, egg-bound shrimp-tempura rice bowl, around 680 yen) a noted favorite.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the chuka-soba as a simple, comforting chicken-bone soy broth that seems to soak into your body (シンプルで、身体に染み入る鶏ガラ醤油の中華そば), with kamaboko, roast pork, bean sprouts and green onion over firm, low-hydration noodles.
The shop is praised as a culturally-valuable town diner with carefully kept food samples, a well-aged wall menu and manga to browse, with table seating that encourages a leisurely meal.
The tenjoji-don (天とじ丼) is highlighted: two shrimp tempura and rice enveloped in plenty of dashi-infused egg (海老天ぷら2尾とごはんを出汁を含んだたっぷりの玉子が包み込む), around 680 yen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi.jp calls Mansei a 文化財的な街の食堂 ('a place we hope stays forever'), with vintage food samples, an aged wall menu, manga/TV, table-only seating, home & workplace delivery, and the 680-yen 天とじ丼 (two shrimp tempura bound in egg over rice); address 京都市中京区西ノ京北壺井町62, near Enmachi/Hanazono University. v-trip.net confirms the chuka-soba's chicken-bone soy broth, kamaboko/roast pork/sprouts/green onion toppings and firm low-hydration noodles, plus a broad shokudo menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared kitchen turning out wheat noodles (udon, soba, ramen, yakisoba), tempura and curry; no gluten-free handling indicated, so high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba is a chicken-bone soy (shoyu) broth; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat and no gluten-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and dishes rely on chicken-bone stock, dashi, pork, fish cake and egg; no vegan preparation indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-standing local neighborhood diner with handwritten/wall menus in Japanese and no English signage indicated, so a little Japanese or pointing at the food samples will smooth the visit.

places.name: 麺スター む・ラッキー (Men Star Mu-Lucky)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use standard wheat ramen noodles (from a Kyoto noodle maker) plus a soy-sauce-based tare in the shoyu bowls, and the chicken broths are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, noodle substitution, or celiac accommodation is indicated. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Every signature bowl is built on chicken broth (tori-shoyu, tori-shio, tori-paitan) and topped with pork chashu; the mazesoba and side sets (fried rice, karaage, gyudon) are also meat-based. No vegan or vegetarian option is indicated.
places.hours_raw: Listed lunch hours 11:30-14:30 (some sources note an earlier 11:00 open and an evening service ~18:00-20:00/21:00); closed Sundays. Hours have varied between sources, so evening availability is uncertain.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A chicken-broth ramen specialist that opened in July 2023 in the Jujo-Omiya neighborhood of Kyoto's Minami ward, just north of a local yakitori shop and a short walk from Kintetsu Jujo Station. It is described as having been opened by someone who trained at a popular Kyoto ramen

chef_bio.background: A chicken-broth ramen specialist that opened on July 17, 2023 in the Jujo-Omiya neighborhood of Kyoto's Minami ward (Nishi-Kujo Morimoto-cho), just north of a local yakitori shop and a short walk from Kintetsu Jujo Station. It is described as having been opened by someone who trained at a popular Kyoto chicken-paitan ramen shop near Nishi-Oji.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on the clean umami of chicken - both a light chicken clear broth and a richer chicken paitan - served in a bright, clean, casual room with springy medium-thin straight noodles from the respected Kyoto noodle maker 麺屋棣鄂 (Menya Teigaku).
chef_bio.specialty: Chicken-forward ramen in three styles - tori-shoyu (chicken-soy), tori-shio (chicken-salt) and the signature tori-paitan (creamy chicken broth), plus a mazesoba. The tori-paitan is the recommended bowl, with extra hand-cut scallions a popular add-on; the tori-shoyu pairs a sweet soy tare with chicken-and-bonito dashi.

anecdotes.text: Opened July 17, 2023 by someone said to come from a popular Kyoto ramen shop, with chicken clear broth and chicken paitan as the signature menu.

The tori-paitan is recommended with negi-mashi (extra scallions, +50 yen); the fresh hand-cut scallions are praised for their sweetness and pleasant bite, over a gentle broth with four slices of chashu and sprouts.

The tori-shoyu uses a sweeter soy tare that pairs well with a light chicken clear broth and bonito dashi, finished with bonito flakes and benina radish sprouts; the half fried-rice set is praised for its fluffy, made-to-order wok-fry.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyo-soku.com and osumituki.com date the opening to 2023-07-17 in Minami-ku's Nishi-Kujo Morimoto-cho, with とり醤油 and とり白湯 as signatures; web search (Tabelog/kyotopi) corroborates the owner previously worked at a popular Nishi-Oji chicken-paitan shop. kyotopi.jp confirms four noodle items (tori-paitan, tori-shio, tori-shoyu, mazesoba), +50-yen hand-cut negi-mashi, four chashu slices, and noodles by テイガク (麺屋棣鄂); noodlers.jp confirms the same 麺屋棣鄂 noodles, the tori-shoyu build (soy tare, chicken clear broth + bonito, benina sprouts) and the made-to-order half fried-rice set.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tori-shoyu bowl is built on a sweet soy-sauce tare, which in Japanese ramen is virtually always wheat-based soy sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual neighborhood ramen counter with no indication of English support, so a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will go a long way.

places.name: 京都金閣寺 らーめんなか澤 (Kyoto Kinkakuji Ramen Nakasawa)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls center on wheat ramen noodles (thin straight noodles), and the broths are paired with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare and salt versions — shoyu is wheat-based unless stated otherwise, and no gluten-free noodle or tare option is mentioned anywhere. No dedicated handling for celiac diners. A celiac traveler cannot safely eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu is built on animal stock — a chicken/pork/clam/fish "quadruple" paitan broth — and bowls are topped with multiple kinds of chashu pork and egg. No vegetable-based or vegan broth is offered.
places.neighborhood: Kita Ward / Hirano (Kitano-Hakubaicho / around Kinkakuji and Kitano Tenmangu). ~1 min walk from Waratenjin-mae bus stop, within walking distance of Kitano-Hakubaicho Stn.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nakasawa spent about 18 years cooking ramen across Toyama and Tokyo before coming home to open his own shop near Kinkaku-ji in October 2023. By his own account he never set out to be a chef in the abstract — as he puts it, for him there was only ever ramen.

chef_bio.origin: Born in Kyoto; his family moved to Toyama when he was in middle school.
chef_bio.background: Nakasawa spent about 18 years cooking ramen across Toyama and Tokyo before coming home to open his own shop near Kinkaku-ji in October 2023. By his own account he never set out to be a chef in the abstract — as he puts it, for him there was only ever ramen.
chef_bio.philosophy: He originally planned a clear-broth (chintan) ramen, but mindful of the nearby Ritsumeikan University crowd he reworked it into a creamy-yet-not-overpowering paitan aimed at winning over younger diners. The care shows in details like charcoal-grilled chashu whose smoky aroma carries into the soup — he has been known to ask guests whether they can taste the char.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a chicken-paitan bowl built on a so-called 'quadruple' soup: Kyoto Akaji-dori chicken bones, pork bones, Shinji-ko shijimi clams and a seafood blend (niboshi, scallop, bonito and mackerel), finished with a three-year-aged shoyu tare. The flagship 特製 鶏パイタンらーめん comes with three kinds of chashu (rare pork shoulder, charcoal-grilled belly, thin-sliced loin), a soft-set egg made with Fukuchiyama eggs, and fried enoki mushrooms — a topping the shop bills as rare, possibly a first, in Kyoto.

anecdotes.text: Nakasawa runs the kitchen solo, preparing every bowl himself. A noodle-making machine sits visible from the street, reserved for his plan to eventually make the shop's own noodles in-house; for now the thin straight noodles come from Menya Teigyaku (棣鄂).
The fried enoki topping on the paitan bowl is promoted locally as a Kyoto first, alongside the unusual four-component 'quadruple' broth.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: magazine.tabelog.com profiles chef 中澤敬士 (36): born in Kyoto, moved to Toyama in middle school, ~18 years cooking ramen in Toyama and Tokyo, opened near Kinkakuji Oct 2023; the chicken-paitan uses a 'quadruple' base (京赤地鶏 chicken bones, pork bones, Lake Shinji shijimi, seafood) with 3-year-aged shoyu, charcoal chashu, Fukuchiyama egg, fried enoki, and 棣鄂 noodles, run solo. osumituki.com dates the opening to 2023-10-17, confirms the quadruple broth, the 特製 bowl's three chashu types, and fried enoki as a likely Kyoto first.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are dressed with a three-year-aged soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; soy sauce of this type is wheat-based, making the shoyu bowls unsuitable for gluten-free diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared broth is an animal stock (chicken, pork, clam, fish); there is no separate vegetarian or vegan preparation, so no bowl can be made vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly solo-run counter shop where a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way; English support is unlikely, so pointing and basic phrases are the comfortable way to order.

places.name: ラーメン彩心 (Ramen Saishin)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen shop: wheat noodles served in pork/chicken broth with (typically wheat-based) shoyu or miso tare. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation found in any source. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: All bowls are built on a chicken-and-pork-bone (tori-tonkotsu) broth; toppings include chashu pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen option is described. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat 11:30-14:30 & 18:00-22:00; Wed 18:00-22:00; Thu 11:30-14:30 & 18:00-23:30; Sun closed. (Sources vary; some report later night hours to ~01:00-01:30. Irregular closures occur — check ahead.)
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku, Omiya / Misonobashi (near Kamigamo Shrine and Kyoto
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Saishin opened on February 19, 2017 in the Misonobashi area of Kyoto's Kita Ward, taking over the premises of a previous ramen shop called Ramen Akashin that had closed a month earlier. The new operation kept the predecessor's layout, giving the place the lived-in feel of a

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Kita-ku, near Kamigamo Shrine and Kyoto Sangyo University)
chef_bio.background: Ramen Saishin opened on February 19, 2017 in the Misonobashi area of Kyoto's Kita Ward, taking over the premises of a previous ramen shop called Ramen Akashin that had closed about a month earlier. The new operation kept the predecessor's layout, giving the place the lived-in feel of a long-established neighborhood shop, and inherited its taste and some recipes.
chef_bio.philosophy: Mellow, refined broths rather than heavy or aggressive richness — a simple, balanced bowl that locals describe as easy to finish, with thicker noodles paired to the miso and thinner noodles to the shoyu.
chef_bio.specialty: A chicken-and-pork-bone (tori-tonkotsu) ramen offered in shio (salt), shoyu (soy sauce), and miso versions. The salt bowl is notable for a smooth, pale, almost corn-potage-colored broth in which chicken umami leads over the pork-bone richness, served with medium-thin straight noodles, a single thick slice of charred chashu, menma, nori, bean sprouts and scallions.

anecdotes.text: The shop carried forward a peppery nira (Chinese chive) side dish inherited from the previous tenant, Ramen Akashin.

Run by two women, the counter is described as welcoming for solo diners, and it is a lunchtime favorite of nearby Kyoto Sangyo University students — fried-chicken (karaage) sets come with generous rice.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com confirms Saishin opened 2017-02-19 in Kita-ku's Misonobashi in the premises of Ramen Akashin (closed ~2017-01-19), kept the layout, inherited the taste and a peppery nira side, and serves tori-tonkotsu in shio/shoyu/miso with thicker noodles for miso and thinner for shoyu, near Kamigamo/Kyoto Sangyo University. kyoto-gourmet.info describes it as run by two women (女性2人), welcoming to solo diners, popular with Kyoto Sangyo students, with a pale corn-potage-colored chicken-forward salt broth (~700 yen), medium-thin noodles and one thick charred chashu slice.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood ramen counter run by local staff with a student clientele; little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will help.

places.name: 今出川まぜそば戦車 (Imadegawa Mazesoba Sensha)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle mazesoba shop. The thick flat noodles are wheat-based, and the sweet-spicy tare is almost certainly soy-sauce-based (wheat-containing). There is no indication of any gluten-free option, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The standard bowl includes pork (肉), and the signature 戦車スペシャル adds extra pork plus fish powder (魚粉); a raw egg is also a standard topping. Toppings such as scallions, bean sprouts, garlic, and pickled ginger are plant-based, but the base dish and tare are not vegan and there is no advertised vegan option. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:45). Lunch-only operation inside a live-music venue basement. Operates roughly 20 days a month with irregular closures — check the shop's SNS for the day's status.
places.neighborhood: Kamigyo Ward / Kitakoji-Muromachi (Imadegawa-Karasuma, 2 min walk from Imadegawa Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened October 2, 2021, this is the Kansai-region outpost of a mazesoba concept produced by Saitama's 大宮まぜそば誠治 (Omiya Mazesoba Seiji). It runs as a daytime tenant operation in the basement of a live-music venue (PUB VOXhall / ファーストコート御所室町) near Imadegawa-Karasuma, in the heart of

chef_bio.origin: Concept originates from Saitama (大宮まぜそば誠治); this is its first Kansai location.
chef_bio.background: Opened October 2, 2021, this is the Kansai-region outpost of a mazesoba concept produced by Saitama's 大宮まぜそば誠治 (Omiya Mazesoba Seiji). It runs as a daytime sub-tenant operation in the basement of a live-music venue (PUB VOXhall) near Imadegawa-Karasuma in Kamigyo ward, in the heart of the student district.
chef_bio.philosophy: Customization-first: a mild, sweet-savory sauce as a canvas, with 10-plus toppings and free add-ons (garlic, pickled ginger, oil, raw egg) so each diner builds their own bowl — staff even suggest trying it once without garlic to taste the sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 戦車スペシャル (Sensha Special): the base mazesoba of flat thick wavy noodles from Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂) tossed in a sweet-spicy tare, piled with extra pork (肉増し), chopped scallions (刻みネギ), and fish powder (魚粉), then finished with a 追い飯 rice refill so you can mop up the leftover sauce.

anecdotes.text: Diners crack a raw egg over the noodles and eat it sukiyaki-style, then add a rice refill to finish the remaining sauce.
It has reportedly become an unofficial campus cafeteria for nearby Doshisha University students.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: life-info.co.jp confirms Sensha is the Kansai debut of Saitama's 大宮まぜそば誠治, operating as a basement sub-tenant of PUB VOXhall near Imadegawa-Karasuma (Kamigyo-ku); signature 戦車スペシャル uses 麺屋棣鄂 noodles with 10+ toppings, a sukiyaki-style raw egg and a 追い飯 rice refill, and it has become popular with Doshisha University students (M 300g ¥1,040 / S 200g ¥800).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mazesoba is served in a sweet-spicy tare (甘辛いタレ); such tare is standard soy-sauce-based and wheat-containing, though the shop does not publish its ingredients.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Base bowl and signature special contain pork and fish powder, and raw egg is a standard add-on; no vegan preparation is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-and-QR student-district lunch counter run in Japanese with no reservations and irregular hours — comfortable for an independent visitor who can order in Japanese and roll with a casual, no-frills setup.

places.name: 瑞秦 (Zuishin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision found. The signature bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (and the crispy 揚げそば on fried wheat noodles) in chicken-and-seafood broth, with Chinese-style sauces that typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian or vegan venue. The kitchen centers on chicken-bone and seafood broth, pork gyoza, and meat/seafood Chinese dishes; no vegan ramen or confirmed plant-based options were found. A vegetable stir-fry (野菜炒め) appears on the menu but is not confirmed meat/animal-stock free.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Tuesdays. (Some listings note lunch to 14:30 and dinner to 20:50.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Zuishin is a small, hard-to-find Chinese restaurant tucked deep within a quiet Yamashina residential neighborhood, reached through narrow back streets a short walk from Nagitsuji Station. Locals describe it as a hidden gem that carries the quiet confidence of a long-respected est

chef_bio.background: Zuishin is a small, hard-to-find Chinese restaurant tucked deep within a quiet Yamashina residential neighborhood (Nagitsuji-Nishiura), reached through narrow back streets about a 10-minute walk from Nagitsuji Station. Locals describe it as a hidden gem that carries the quiet confidence of a long-respected establishment, with a bright, energetic owner.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen positions itself as authentic Chinese cooking (中国料理) rather than the Japanized 中華 stereotype, leaning on the aroma of its ingredients - for example gyoza scented with genuine star anise - rather than heavy seasoning.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the tori-soba (chicken soba): a refined, clear (清湯) soup built on chicken-bone stock rounded out with seafood umami, pairing mellow body with a clean finish. Silky noodles are matched to the soup. The crispy fried noodles (揚げそば), made by deep-frying flat wide noodles for a fragrant crunch, and the curry fried rice are other house favorites.

anecdotes.text: Diners note the soup balances chicken-bone richness with added seafood umami for a flavor that is both mellow and crisp, with the tori-soba treated as the standout.
The crispy fried noodles are flat, wide noodles deep-fried until fragrant, served with vegetables and pork in sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumituki.com places Zuishin in a hard-to-find residential street of Yamashina-ku (椥辻西浦町, ~10 min from Nagitsuji Station), serving authentic 中国料理: gyoza with genuine star anise (八角) aroma, delicate crispy 揚げそば (flat wide noodles), curry fried rice and ramen (~¥650), with a bright, energetic owner (name not published). The tori-soba clear soup and seafood-rounded chicken stock are treated as the standout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Authentic Chinese dishes and tare here typically use wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny neighborhood Chinese restaurant tucked deep in a Yamashina residential block; service is in Japanese and best approached with a guide or some Japanese, with simple points-and-smile ordering otherwise.

places.name: 辛麺屋 鯱輪 京都今出川店 (Karamenya Shachirin Kyoto Imadegawa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a house chili tare (Japanese chili-pepper/soy tares are typically wheat-containing) over a meat broth, plus minced pork, chashu and back fat. There is no published allergen information and no dedicated handling, so this should be treated as unsafe for celiac diners. Expect wheat throughout.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature spicy bowls are topped with stir-fried minced meat, seared chashu pork and pork back fat, finished with a beaten egg, over a meat-based broth. No vegetable or plant-based ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:45), 18:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Closed every Tue and the 2nd and 4th Wed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shachirin opened on July 15, 2023 on Imadegawa near the Doshisha University campus, bringing the 'Nagoya karamen' style to Kyoto as a branch of the Nagoya brand 名古屋辛麺 鯱輪. The whole 11-seat counter shop leans into its theme — walls, chairs and towels are bright red, as if the room

chef_bio.background: Shachirin opened on July 15, 2023 on Imadegawa near the Doshisha University campus (about 5 minutes from Imadegawa subway station), bringing the 'Nagoya karamen' style to Kyoto as a branch of the Nagoya brand 名古屋辛麺 鯱輪. The whole 11-seat counter shop leans into its theme — walls, chairs and towels are bright red, as if the room itself were a bowl of spicy ramen.
chef_bio.philosophy: House-developed chili heat built on three kinds of chili pepper blended for sweetness, aroma and spiciness, prepared cup by cup, layered onto a savory umami base.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is the umami-spicy bowl (旨辛にんにくラーメン) that fuses Nagoya Taiwan-style ramen with Miyazaki karamen: stir-fried minced meat, garlic, Chinese chives, bean sprouts and green onion over noodles, with optional seared chashu and sweet back fat, and a beaten egg to mellow the heat. Diners pick a spice level from normal up through 5 and then several steps beyond, and many pour the leftover spicy soup over a free bowl of rice.

anecdotes.text: The recipe was supervised by Naoto Niiyama (新山直人), head (総大将) of the acclaimed Nagoya ramen shop Menya Hanabi (麺屋はなび), the shop credited with originating Taiwan mazesoba.
The interior is deliberately all-red — walls, chairs and towels — so the whole shop reads as a giant bowl of spicy ramen.
Noodle dishes come with free rice refills, and customers commonly finish by pouring the spicy broth over rice.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: leafkyoto.net confirms the Kyoto Imadegawa branch opened 2023-07-15, the all-red 11-seat room, the 3-chili blend, and roots in the Nagoya brand 名古屋辛麺 鯱輪. Official shachirin.com states the brand's flavor was supervised by 新山直人, the 総大将 of Taiwan-ramen originator 麺屋はなび, and describes the pork/chicken/vegetable base and Nagoya-Taiwan + Miyazaki-karamen fusion; osumituki.com confirms free rice refills.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles in a shared spicy-ramen kitchen; no gluten-free preparation is mentioned (per Leaf Kyoto).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu includes a soy-sauce ramen and a chili-based spicy tare; Japanese soy sauce and chili tares are typically wheat-containing, though the page does not list allergens (per Osumituki).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowls are topped with stir-fried minced meat, seared chashu pork, back fat and a beaten egg over a meat broth; no plant-based option is offered (per Leaf Kyoto).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 11-seat counter ramen bar with no English menu or allergen info; a knowledgeable guide is recommended, though a GF/vegan diner realistically cannot eat here.

places.name: めん馬鹿一代 (Menbaka Ichidai (Menbaka Fire Ramen))
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→options. The 'pure wheat, no GF' prior is wrong — a real GF program exists (rice-flour noodles, GF soy sauce tare, dedicated pots/utensils on request; multiple celiac reviewers report safe visits). Too much substance for 'no.' BUT doesn't clear 'high': printed menu reportedly says GF noodles boiled in the SAME water as wheat noodles then rinsed (not celiac-safe), safe handling conditional on declaring celiac, staff-vs-menu accounts conflict. Single small tourist kitchen, not dedicated. Open. — Prior note: The shop's official site states it serves gluten-sensitive guests using premium rice-flour noodles and dedicated pots and utensils "to minimize cross-contamination." This is a genuine, intentional gluten-free offering — unusual for a ramen shop, where noodles, shoyu tare and broth are normally wheat-based. However, it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, and the GF/vegan special bowls must be booked in advance via the TableCheck reservation (the reservation-only "special set menu"); the regular walk-in menu is standard wheat ramen. Highly sensitive celiacs should reserve the GF set, state their needs clearly, and confirm cross-contamination handling on arrival.
places.vegan_detail: A full vegan version of the fire ramen has been offered since 2020: a plant-based soy-sauce soup with no animal broth, custom firm noodles, and tofu/vegetable substitutions, plus vegan sides such as gyoza and karaage (mock meat). Vegan orders are tracked with a colored number card at the seat. The vegan bowls are part of the reservation-only special set menu, so book ahead via TableCheck rather than relying on walk-in.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 daily (Tabelog/official). Lunch service approx 11:00-15:30 and dinner approx 17:00-21:00 per the official reservation page; the kitchen notes the meal takes considerably longer than a typical ramen shop due to the fire performance.
places.neighborhood: Nakagyo Ward, Nijojo-mae Stn (Senbon-Kyunijo, northwest of Nijo Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1984 in Kyoto, this counter ramen shop began serving conventional bowls before its owner stumbled onto the idea that would make it world-famous. Wanting to build a ramen around Kyoto's prized Kujo green onions, he experimented with how to keep the noodles screaming-hot

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1984 in Kyoto, this counter ramen shop began serving conventional bowls before its owner stumbled onto the idea that would make it world-famous. Working with Kyoto's prized Kujo green onions, he sought a way to keep the noodles screaming-hot while showcasing a mound of fresh raw scallions on top.
chef_bio.philosophy: Treat ramen as both craft and spectacle: preserve traditional Kyoto shoyu ramen flavor while turning each serving into a joyful, memorable performance, and welcome guests from everywhere — the shop states it offers vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free and Muslim-friendly (no-pork) options prepared with the same care.
chef_bio.specialty: Negi Ramen, better known as Fire Ramen: a light yet deeply savory chicken-and-seafood soy-sauce broth piled with fresh Kujo scallions, finished tableside when the cook pours scallion oil heated past 300 degrees Celsius over the bowl. The oil ignites into a column of flame that chars the onions, drawing out their sweetness and aroma while keeping the bowl piping hot.

anecdotes.text: The flames are not just theater: scallion oil heated above 300C throws up a pillar of fire that chars the Kujo onions and keeps the bowl scalding hot. After a 2019 renovation the counter was rebuilt with fire-resistant stainless-steel seating, and staff give a safety briefing and paper aprons before lighting each bowl.

About a year after opening the founder discovered that pouring flaming oil over freshly chopped green onions produced an extraordinary aroma and flavor, and that discovery became the shop's signature Fire Ramen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official fireramen.com credits founder Masamichi Miyazawa, dates the shop to 1984 near Nijo Castle, describes Fire Ramen's shoyu base and the ~1-year-after-opening discovery of flaming scallion oil, and lists stated vegan/vegetarian/gluten-free/Muslim-friendly options (dietary safety not independently asserted). livejapan.com confirms the 300C+ scallion oil, the flame pillar that chars Kujo scallions, the 2019 stainless-steel renovation, the safety briefing and paper aprons, and the chicken-and-seafood soy broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop says it uses dedicated pots and utensils to minimize cross-contamination for gluten-free guests, but it is a shared kitchen that also cooks standard wheat ramen, so it is not a fully dedicated gluten-free environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The regular menu is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen; standard shoyu tare is typically wheat-based. The gluten-free version is a separate reservation-only preparation rather than the default bowl.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan orders are marked with a colored number card placed at the seat so staff serve the correct plant-based bowl, but the shop does not publish details on separate cooking surfaces for vegan items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian and no-pork (Muslim-friendly) bowls are offered as a reservation-only special set menu booked in advance via TableCheck; the regular walk-in menu cannot be swapped for these, so dietary guests should reserve ahead and state needs clearly.

positives.text: Rare among ramen shops, this kitchen intentionally caters to gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian and Muslim-friendly diners, with premium rice-flour noodles for gluten-free guests and a fully plant-based vegan fire ramen offered since 2020.
English website, English-speaking staff and online reservations make it straightforward for foreign diners to arrange and confirm special dietary bowls before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a famous tourist-facing spot with an English-language website, English-speaking staff, online reservations (TableCheck) and printed safety/allergy guidance, so foreign visitors are very comfortable here.

gf_review.why: The 'pure wheat, no GF' prior is wrong — a real GF program exists (rice-flour noodles, GF soy sauce tare, dedicated pots/utensils on request; multiple celiac reviewers report safe visits). Too much substance for 'no.' BUT doesn't clear 'high': printed menu reportedly says GF noodles boiled in the SAME water as wheat noodles then rinsed (not celiac-safe), safe handling conditional on declaring celiac, staff-vs-menu accounts conflict. Single small tourist kitchen, not dedicated. Open.
red_flags: printed menu disclaimer: GF noodles boiled in same wheat water then rinsed
safe protocol only triggered if you declare celiac; default is shared water
conflicting menu-vs-staff accounts, no written guarantee
single small non-dedicated high-throughput tourist kitchen
gf_review.plain_rice: GF fried rice available (made with GF soy); plain steamed white rice not explicitly documented

places.name: 麺と酒の寅 (Men to Sake no Tora)
places.gf_detail: A tantanmen specialist built on wheat ramen noodles (the soup-less version uses thick flat crimped wheat noodles), a Sichuan sesame-and-ground-pork sauce, and chili/Sichuan-pepper seasonings. No gluten-free noodle or tare option is mentioned anywhere, and tantanmen of this style is essentially never celiac-safe. Treat as not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-forward shop: the signature bowls center on a ground-pork/meat-miso (肉味噌) topping and a rich creamy sesame broth, and side dishes include beef tataki, gyoza, and fried chicken. No vegan or vegetarian tantanmen is advertised. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:00 (lunch and dinner service daily; closing days irregular)
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward / Demachiyanagi (Yoshida / near Kyoto University)
places.cuisine: Soup and soupless tantanmen (dan dan noodles)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked down a side street just west of Higashioji-dori between Imadegawa and Higashi-ichijo, a short walk from Demachiyanagi and Kyoto University, this little tantanmen counter occupies a space that used to be a neighborhood sake shop with a standing-drink corner. It was reborn i

chef_bio.background: Tucked down a side street just west of Higashioji-dori between Imadegawa and Higashi-ichijo, a short walk from Demachiyanagi and Kyoto University, this little tantanmen counter occupies a space that used to be a neighborhood sake shop with a standing-drink corner. It was reborn in May 2025, reworked under the guidance of Kinshiro (金鵄楼), a Gosho-minami tantanmen specialist that drew lines soon after it opened in 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: True to its name 'Noodles and Sake' (麺と酒の寅), the place is built around the idea of 'have a drink, then close the night with a bowl of tantanmen' — a casual student-and-local hangout where you tune your own bowl by circling choices on a paper order slip (noodle firmness, scallions, scallion oil, fried onion, meat miso, chili-oil heat, and how much Sichuan pepper).
chef_bio.specialty: Sichuan-style tantanmen in two forms: a rich, creamy soup version and a soup-less (mazesoba-style) version built on thick, flat, crimped wheat noodles that grab the sauce. The flavor leans on a fragrant sesame base lifted by the tingle of Sichuan pepper (huajiao), with a meat-miso topping blended for a deeper, more layered umami than the parent shop.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a Kinshiro-supervised spinoff; rather than copy the parent, it deliberately uses thick flat crimped noodles for the soup-less bowl and a re-blended meat miso to create its own deeper, more complex flavor.

The concept is 'a drink first, then finish with tantanmen' (一杯やって担担麺で〆る), aimed in part at the nearby Kyoto University student crowd, with beef tataki, gyoza, and karaage to go with the sake before the noodles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi.jp confirms the shop opened May 2025 in a former Yoshida (Sakyo-ku) sake shop with a standing-drink counter near Kyoto University, supervised by Gosho-minami tantanmen specialist 金鵄楼 (Kinshiro, opened 2024, quickly popular); it serves soup and soup-less Sichuan tantanmen with thick flat crimped noodles and a re-blended, more complex meat miso, paper-slip customization (firmness, negi, scallion oil, fried onion, meat miso, chili oil, Sichuan pepper), the '一杯やって担担麺で〆る' concept, ~16 seats, and sides of beef tataki, gyoza and karaage.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, student-friendly local noodle counter where you order by circling options on a paper slip, so basic Japanese (or pointing) makes ordering much smoother; no English service is advertised.

places.name: （純）中国料理 麒麟園 (Kirin-en)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision is published. The signature dish is wheat-noodle tantanmen, and the kitchen is a full Chinese restaurant relying on wheat-based soy sauce, broths and house-made meat miso. The hallmark heat comes from chili blends, not from any allergen-controlled prep. A celiac diner should treat this as unsafe; no dedicated equipment or GF menu is indicated.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-seafood Chinese restaurant. The famous tantanmen is built on a house-made pork "meat miso" (自家製肉味噌) over a chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) broth, and other signatures include black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork. No vegan ramen or vegan menu is advertised; a vegan diner would need to ask, with little to expect.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:00, Dinner 16:30-22:00 (official site lists dinner to 23:00 / L.O. 22:30). Closed Tuesdays (next business day if Tuesday is a holiday).
places.neighborhood: Muko City (Hankyu Higashi-Muko Stn / JR Mukomachi Stn, Kyoto Gekikara (super-spicy) Shotengai)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kirin-en opened in 1967 (Showa 42), when the area around Mukocho was still mostly farmland and authentic Chinese food was a rarity. The founder set out, in his words, to let the people of Mukocho enjoy real Cantonese cooking, and the place has carried on as a family-run restauran

chef_bio.background: Kirin-en opened in 1967 (Showa 42), when the area around Mukocho was still mostly farmland and authentic Chinese food was a rarity. The founder set out, in his words, to let the people of Mukocho enjoy real Cantonese cooking, and the place has carried on as a family-run restaurant ever since, today led by Tamotsu Miyaji (宮路保) with manager Ryo Miyaji (宮路亮).
chef_bio.philosophy: Their stated aim with the famous bowl was not heat for its own sake but 美味くて辛い - delicious AND spicy - with a spectrum running from mild to brutally hot so anyone can find their level.
chef_bio.specialty: The house gekikara (extreme-spice) tantanmen: a chili blend once built around jolokia, then famed as the world's hottest pepper, layered over a house-made pork meat-miso for body and a chicken-bone broth finished with sesame for depth. It won the Gold Grand Prix at the very first KARA-1 contest. Beyond the noodles, the owner's own favorite is the black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork, made with traditional bottle-fermented black vinegar prized by regulars.

anecdotes.text: The tantanmen was born out of Mukocho's community-revitalization push that became the Kyoto Gekikara (extreme-spice) shopping street; Kirin-en's bowl took the inaugural KARA-1 Gold Grand Prix.
Founder's recollection that he resolved to bring authentic Cantonese cuisine to Mukocho residents back when the neighborhood was largely farmland.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: otokuni-kyoto.sakura.ne.jp confirms Kirin-en opened 1967 (Showa 42) in Mukocho when it was largely farmland, with the founder's mission to bring authentic Cantonese cooking; the family business is led by 宮路保 (representative) and 宮路亮 (manager); the jolokia-based gekikara tantanmen (with house meat-miso and sesame-finished chicken-bone broth) won the inaugural KARA-1 Gold Grand Prix, and the black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork is a signature. Near JR Mukomachi / Hankyu Higashi-Mukо station.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a full Chinese restaurant, dishes rely on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and seasonings; no wheat-free tare or sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature tantanmen is built on house-made pork meat-miso over a chicken-bone broth, so shared pots and broths make vegan separation impractical.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, family-run neighborhood Chinese restaurant with a roomy tatami second floor and no English noted, so a little guidance on ordering and on the spice scale helps.

places.name: 味の天徳 京都四条木屋町店 (Aji no Tentoku Kyoto Shijo Kiyamachi)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Abura soba is built on wheat noodles tossed in a soy-based tare (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat). The "plant-based oil" point refers to the dressing oil being vegetable oil rather than lard — it has nothing to do with gluten. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen handling. Gluten-avoiders should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Despite using plant-based (vegetable) oil rather than animal fat, the standard bowl is topped with chashu (pork) and the soy-based tare typically contains animal-derived components. No vegan/vegetarian version is advertised. The "healthier plant oil" framing is a marketing point about fat type, not a plant-based menu. A vegan diner could ask whether a no-chashu bowl is possible, but the tare itself is not confirmed plant-based, so it cannot be recommended as vegan.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 18:00-23:00 / Sat, Sun 17:30-23:00, irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Shimogyo Ward, Kyoto-Kawaramachi Stn / Shijo Kiyamachi (along the Takase River)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Aji no Tentoku is an abura soba specialist chain originating in Kanagawa (Kakio main shop), operated by Tenki Co., Ltd. In Kyoto it has the Shijo Kiyamachi shop and the Gion shop; the Shijo Kiyamachi shop is a small counter shop along the Takase River, a very short walk from Hankyu Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station in a corner of the entertainment district.

chef_bio.origin: 運営は株式会社天輝（油そば味の天徳ブランド、関東発・京都進出）
chef_bio.background: 味の天徳は神奈川（柿生本店）発祥の油そば専門チェーンで、株式会社天輝が運営する。京都には四条木屋町店と祇園店を構え、四条木屋町店は下京区真町、阪急京都河原町駅から徒歩すぐの繁華街にあるカウンター8席ほどの小さな店。
chef_bio.philosophy: 油そばを『第三の麺類』と位置づけ、動物性の脂ではなく植物性の油を使うことでヘルシーさを打ち出している点が京都での売り。客が酢・ラー油で自分好みに仕上げる参加型の一杯。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は油そば。中太のちょい縮れ麺を醤油ベースのタレと植物油で和えたスープのない一杯で、メンマ・ねぎ・刻み海苔・チャーシューがのる。途中で酢とラー油を加えて味変し『二度美味しい』のが定番の食べ方。醤油だれと塩だれが選べる。

anecdotes.text: 『京都初の油そば専門店』をうたい、植物性の油を使うためあっさり食べられると紹介されている。
途中から酢とラー油を足して二度美味しい、というのが店の定番の食べ方として案内されている。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 株式会社天輝の公式店舗ページで運営会社・神奈川柿生本店発祥・京都四条木屋町/祇園店の存在を確認。Rettyで『途中から酢とラー油を足して二度美味しい油そば』の食べ方を、ぐるぱす等で『京都初の油そば専門店・植物性の油使用』の標榜を確認。店主名は非公開のためnull。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat abura soba noodles dressed in a soy-based tare; no gluten-free option or allergen separation indicated anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The abura soba uses a 醤油ベース (soy-sauce-based) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard bowl is topped with chashu (pork); no vegan/vegetarian preparation is advertised.

positives.text: Uses plant-based (vegetable) oil rather than animal fat in the dressing — a lighter-fat bowl, though this does not make it gluten-free or vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only counter abura soba shop where ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English and there is no allergen support, so basic Japanese and self-direction help.

places.name: 料理屋 てら戸 (Ryouriya Terado)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The noodles are wheat (the curry tsukemen broth is also thickened to a heavy wheat-flour consistency), and a kappo kitchen serving sashimi, sake, and grilled fish relies on standard wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu or any stated allergen accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering. This is a Japanese kappo whose signature noodle dish is a curry tsukemen built on a bonito (katsuobushi) dashi and served with chicken and egg; the broth, tare, and toppings are animal-based, and the evening menu centers on sashimi and fish. Nothing on the menu is plant-based by design, and there is no indication staff can prepare a vegan version.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-22:00. Closed Mondays. (Note: a daytime curry tsukemen has historically been offered, but the official site currently describes the shop as a dinner-only, reservation-based kappo with very limited seatings; lunch availability is uncertain.)
places.cuisine: Japanese kappo / curry tsukemen / dashi rame
places.notes: A small owner-run Japanese kappo on Tominokoji just north of Shijo, a few minutes from Kawaramachi. It opened in December 2019 in a space that had previously housed the curry-tsukemen specialist Menya Itadori (麺屋虎杖), which closed at the end of September 2019. After a temporary cl

chef_bio.background: A small owner-run Japanese kappo on Tominokoji just north of Shijo in Shimogyo Ward, a few minutes from Kawaramachi. It opened in December 2019 in a space that had previously housed the curry-tsukemen specialist Menya Itadori (麺屋虎杖), which closed at the end of September 2019, and it continues to operate today.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner frames the place around wholehearted Kyoto hospitality (真心込めて、おもてなし) and lively, enjoyable time shared with family and friends ('てら戸でご家族や友人と一緒に賑やかで楽しい時間をお過ごしください').
chef_bio.specialty: By night it is a kappo for sashimi, grilled fish, and sushi paired with sake and shochu; its signature crossover dish is a curry tsukemen whose dipping broth is built on a strong katsuobushi (bonito) dashi so the Japanese dashi flavor holds its own against the sweet, old-soba-shop-style curry, finished with aonori and served with thick noodles, summer vegetables, chicken, and a soft egg.

anecdotes.text: The shop carried on the curry-tsukemen lineage of the address: the previous tenant was Menya Itadori, a curry tsukemen specialty shop that closed on September 30, 2019, and Terado opened the following December offering its own kappo take on the dish.

What makes the bowl distinctive is the deliberate balance of a heavily bonito-forward Japanese dashi against a sweet, aonori-dusted curry, an intentionally kappo-minded reworking of a casual lunch noodle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト(ryouriya-terado.com)で店主『寺戸 昌規』と『ご家族や友人と賑やかで楽しい時間を』というもてなしのコンセプトを確認。osumitukiで前テナント麺屋虎杖(2019/9/30閉店)、2019年12月開業、鰹出汁の効いたカレーつけ麺(青のり・夏野菜・鶏・半熟卵)を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature noodles are wheat and the curry dipping broth is described as having a thick wheat-flour consistency.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A kappo serving sashimi and grilled fish; no wheat-free soy sauce or seasoning is indicated, so standard wheat-based soy sauce should be assumed.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run kappo with counter seating and reservation-based, limited daily seatings; comfortable for a guest who can manage basic Japanese, with no indication of English support.

places.name: 油そば・まぜそば ロマン 百万遍店 (Aburasoba・Mazesoba Roman Hyakumanben-ten)
