places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowls use house-made whole-wheat (全粒粉入り) thick noodles and a special shoyu tare, both wheat-based, with no gluten-free noodle or tare option found. The kitchen is built around these noodles, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Not suitable for anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Abura soba and maze soba are built on a meat-based formula — chashu pork, spicy minced meat, soft-boiled or raw egg yolk, and a shoyu tare with oil/fish dashi notes. No vegan or vegetable-only bowl was found. A plant-based diner would have no safe main dish here.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward / Demachiyanagi / Hyakumanben (near Kyoto University)
places.notes: A Kyoto outpost of the Osaka-born abura soba and maze soba specialist Roman (株式会社ロマン), this shop opened on May 16, 2022 along Imagawa-dori in the Hyakumanben district, a short walk from Kyoto University. Oil noodles and mixed noodles were still relatively uncommon in Kyoto at the

chef_bio.origin: Branch of the Osaka-headquartered Roman brand (parent company 株式会社ロマン, founded November 2020, HQ in Higashinari-ku, Osaka), which has grown to more than a dozen locations across Osaka and Kyoto.
chef_bio.background: A Kyoto outpost of the Osaka-born abura soba and maze soba specialist Roman (株式会社ロマン), this shop opened on May 16, 2022 along Imadegawa-dori in the Hyakumanben district, a short walk from Kyoto University. Oil noodles and mixed noodles were still relatively uncommon in Kyoto at the time, which made the new student-friendly counter a notable arrival in the neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's stated belief is 'シンプルだからこその素材の味を大事にしています' — because the dish is simple, the flavor of the ingredients matters most. That focus shows in the whole-wheat (全粒粉) thick noodles the brand uses for its noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Abura soba (brothless oil noodles) and maze soba — thick noodles tossed with a special shoyu tare and seasoned oil. The abura soba comes piled with chashu, menma, soft-boiled egg, and nori; the maze soba layers spicy minced meat, chives, green onion, bamboo shoots, and egg yolk over the same noodles. Diners add vinegar and the house chili oil and mix everything vigorously before eating.

anecdotes.text: The shop courts its Kyoto University neighbors directly with a student discount, and noodle portions scale up through 大盛・ダブル・トリプル all the way to a 'challenge' size.
An opening promotion gave the first 50 customers each day a free large portion or topping through the launch week (until May 22, 2022).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumitukiで2022/5/16開業・左京区百万遍(今出川通沿い、京大近く)・大阪発の油そばまぜそば専門店・学割・大盛〜チャレンジまでの増量・先着50名無料キャンペーン(〜5/22)を確認。roman-japan公式で『シンプルだからこその素材の味を大事にしています』の理念と全粒粉太麺、company頁で創業2020年11月・大阪本社を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on house-made whole-wheat thick noodles boiled in shared water, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Both signature bowls are dressed with a special shoyu (soy sauce) tare, which is wheat-based and a core component of the dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls include chashu pork, spicy minced meat, and egg, with shared utensils and prep surfaces; no plant-based preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, fast-paced student noodle counter near Kyoto University where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu get you through ordering comfortably.

places.name: 中華そば 尋 (Chuka Soba Hiro)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are wheat ramen noodles (flat medium-thick) in a pork-bone shoyu broth, and soy sauce (containing wheat) is central to both the tare and the recommended free-rice topping. No gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and no gluten-free claims appear on any page. A celiac or gluten-avoidant diner cannot eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The single broth style is pork-bone soy (豚骨醤油), and toppings/condiments revolve around chashu (pork belly), boiled egg, and meat-based components. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered or mentioned. Wakame, menma, and green onion exist as toppings but the broth itself is animal-based.
places.neighborhood: Minami Ward, Nishikujo / To-ji (~4 min walk from To-ji Stn / just under 10 min walk from Kyoto Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chuka Soba Hiro is a morning-ramen shop that opened March 28, 2026, in the redeveloping Kyoto Station / Hachijoguchi area. Known as the sister shop of the popular 'Ramen Hiro' in Saiin, its entrance is set deep in an alley to the left of the building.

chef_bio.origin: 京都・西院の本店「尋」の流れを汲む姉妹店。
chef_bio.background: 中華そば 尋（ひろ）は、2026年3月28日、再開発が進む京都駅・八条口エリアにオープンした朝ラーメンの店。西院で人気の「ラーメン 尋」の姉妹店として知られ、ビル左手の路地奥に入口を構える。
chef_bio.philosophy: 朝7時から開け、一杯ごとにごはんを無料で添える——朝ラーを気軽に楽しんでもらうための、気前のよいもてなしを大切にしている。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板は一杯の中華そば。澄んだ黄金色のクリアな豚骨醤油スープで、豚骨の力強い旨味を感じさせつつ塩分や醤油感は控えめに仕立て、朝に食べても重くならない一杯に仕上げている。麺は高加水の平打ち中太麺で、ぷりっとした弾力と滑らかな喉ごしが特徴。チャーシューは注文ごとに切り出す豚バラで、薄切り・厚切りの食感を楽しめる。

anecdotes.text: おすすめの食べ方は、無料のごはんにチャーシューとおろしにんにくをのせ、醤油をひと回し。卓上にはおろしにんにく・一味・醤油・きゅうりの漬物が用意されている。
メニューは中華そば・チャーシュー麺・わかめ中華そばの3種。基本トッピングはわかめ・メンマ・ねぎ・ゆで卵。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopiで2026/3/28開業・京都駅八条口圏(南区西九条池ノ内町)・西院『ラーメン尋』の姉妹店・7:00開店・ラーメン1杯につきごはん無料・黄金色の澄んだ豚骨醤油・高加水の平打ち中太麺・3種メニュー(中華そば/チャーシュー麺/わかめ)・ごはん+チャーシュー+にんにくの食べ方を確認。店主名は非公開でnull。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; there is no gluten-free option or separate preparation noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone shoyu (豚骨醤油) tare, and soy sauce is also part of the recommended free-rice topping and the tabletop condiments — soy sauce in Japan typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single broth is animal-based (pork bone), prepared alongside chashu (pork belly) and egg toppings; no vegan preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual ticket-machine (食券制) morning-ramen counter where a few words of Japanese and a friendly greeting go a long way; no English service is indicated.

places.name: 豚骨魚介ラーメン 穣 (Tonkotsu Gyokai Ramen Minoru)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The signature bowls are built on self-made wheat noodles, and a pork-bone-and-fish (tonkotsu gyokai) ramen of this style almost always uses a wheat-based shoyu tare. No gluten-free option is advertised and no allergen handling is described.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is a heavy pork-bone (uses Niwa Kogen pork) and fish/dried-sardine broth with chashu pork toppings; there is no plant-based bowl.
places.neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo Ward (near Chayama / Kyoto University of the Arts Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner is a graduate of the celebrated Kyoto ramen house Raamen Touhichi (らぁ麺とうひち), a Michelin Bib Gourmand name, where he is described as the first disciple and trained for around seven years. He went on to serve as manager at Raamen Suguru (らぁ麺すぐる), then deepened his craft o

chef_bio.background: The owner trained at the celebrated Kyoto ramen house Raamen Touhichi (らぁ麺とうひち), a Michelin Bib Gourmand name. He went on to serve as manager (店長) at Raamen Suguru (らぁ麺すぐる), then broadened his craft outside ramen at the yakiniku restaurant Nanzan (南山) before opening Minoru on his own on May 18, 2026 in Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku.
chef_bio.philosophy: He values handmade quality, making his own noodles and using domestically sourced ingredients where he can. He is confident enough in the seasoning balance that no table condiments are offered, asking guests to taste the bowl as built.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a rich-yet-mellow tonkotsu-gyokai (pork-bone and seafood) ramen at 1,100 yen with 180g of house-made medium-thick straight noodles, alongside tsukemen at 1,300 yen with 250g of noodles. A special ramen (1,500 yen) tops the bowl with four kinds of chashu made from Niwa Kogen (丹羽高原) pork, plus seasoned egg and extra charred nori.

anecdotes.text: It quickly became one of 2026's talked-about new Kyoto ramen openings, the proprietor opening 満を持して (with great anticipation) after his time at Touhichi.
The L-shaped open-kitchen counter (about 10 seats) gives a live-cooking feel, and the broth shows layered umami — 'ガツンと来る豚の凝縮された旨味' — over a calm, Japanese-style counter with no table condiments.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gcjapanで『らぁ麺とうひち出身の店主』、2026/5/18開業、ラーメン1100円/180g・つけ麺1300円/250g・特製1500円で丹羽高原豚の4種チャーシューを確認。kyotopiで経歴(とうひち出身→らぁ麺すぐる店長→焼肉南山→独立)、卓上調味料なし、L字カウンター約10席のオープンキッチン、厚みのある旨味のスープを確認。『一番弟子』『約7年』の記載は両ソースになく削除。

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are central to every bowl, so a shared boiling pot and wheat-laden kitchen make cross-contamination effectively unavoidable for a celiac.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tare/seasoning ingredients are published, but a tonkotsu-gyokai ramen of this style almost always relies on a wheat-based shoyu tare; assume the seasoning contains wheat unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on pork bone and fish/dried sardine and toppings are pork chashu, so there is no plant-based preparation and animal products are throughout the kitchen.

positives.text: Noodles are made in-house and the shop emphasizes handmade quality and domestic ingredients, so staff know their components well even though no formal allergy program is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, newly opened counter ramen shop where ordering in basic Japanese is easiest; no English support is indicated.

places.name: 中村商店 京都拉麺小路店 (Nakamura Shoten Kyoto Ramen Koji)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles in a chicken-and-seafood shio broth, with a salt tare that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce/seasonings. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodation is advertised on the shop's pages. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Every bowl is built on animal-based broth — chicken bones, whole chicken, kombu, and dried sardines (niboshi) — and topped with chashu pork. There is no plant-based ramen on the menu.
places.neighborhood: Shimogyo Ward / Kyoto Stn (Kyoto Station Building 10F, Ramen Koji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nakamura Shoten is one of the ramen brands of the Kinsei group, founded in Takatsuki, Osaka in 2001 as 彩色ラーメンきんせい ("colorful ramen Kinsei") — named for the wish to make ramen that shines vividly, like Venus in the night sky. The group has spent decades refining chicken-and-seafoo

chef_bio.origin: Takatsuki, Osaka (Kinsei group); Kyoto Station branch
chef_bio.background: Nakamura Shoten is one of the ramen brands of the Kinsei group, founded in Takatsuki, Osaka on November 8, 2001 as 彩色ラーメンきんせい ('colorful ramen Kinsei') — named for the wish to make vividly colored ramen that shines like Venus in the night sky. The group has spent decades refining chicken-and-seafood broths and handmade noodles, and its Nakamura Shoten line carries that work into Kyoto. This branch sits on the 10th-floor Ramen Koji of the Kyoto Station Building.
chef_bio.philosophy: The group builds each bowl around a carefully balanced broth and handmade noodles, with a stated aim of bringing smiles and comfort through a single bowl of ramen ('ラーメンで笑顔に').
chef_bio.specialty: 金の塩 ('gold salt') — a clear, golden shio bowl built on chicken umami and Japanese dashi, given a refined ('お上品') presentation. The menu also runs to chicken-forward and chuka soba bowls; this is Takatsuki-style ramen 'where you can fully savor Japan's distinctive dashi'.

anecdotes.text: The parent brand was founded on November 8, 2001 in Takatsuki with the wish to create 'ramen as vividly colored as Venus shining in the night sky' ('夜空に輝く金星のような鮮やかな色のラーメンを創りたい') — the origin of the 'colorful ramen' (彩色) name.
At the group's Takatsuki home, the 金の塩 is praised for a light, clear chicken-bone broth ('あっさり！鶏ガラの旨味がよいですね！') and a refined, 'お上品' presentation — counter-only and queued at lunch.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kinseigroup公式historyで彩色ラーメンきんせいの創業=平成13年(2001)11月8日・高槻市栄町、『金星のような鮮やかな色のラーメンを創りたい』という店名由来、自家製麺・鶏塩の先駆けを確認。takatsuki-scrambleで金の塩(黄金色・鶏ガラのあっさり・お上品・カウンターのみ・昼は行列)を、京都駅ビル拉麺小路頁で10F出店・『だしを存分に味わえる大阪高槻ラーメン』・看板金の塩を確認。旧引用vacanは410 Goneのため差し替え。

cultural_comfort.note: A busy station food-court ramen counter where ordering is typically done at a ticket machine and a few words of Japanese plus pointing will carry you through comfortably.

places.name: ラーメン まきさだ (Ramen Makisada)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen profile: wheat straight thin noodles, a soy-sauce (typically wheat-based shoyu) tare, and an animal-based broth. No gluten-free accommodation is indicated; not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The single ramen is an animal-based (dōbutsu-kei) shoyu broth topped with thin-sliced pork chāshū; the side shumai are pork. No vegan or vegetarian option is indicated.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Makisada is a ramen specialist opened April 27, 2026, at Takakura-dori Nijo Nishi-iru in Nakagyo Ward, close to Karasuma Oike, created by 'Kyo-Chuka Makisada,' a renowned creative-Chinese establishment in Kyoto. A small shop with only 10 calm counter seats reminiscent of a kappo restaurant, its signature bowl is a ground-up reconstruction as ramen of the negi soba that was originally served as a staff meal at Makisada.

chef_bio.origin: 京都・中京区。母体である「京中華 牧定」の系譜を受け継ぐ新店。
chef_bio.background: ラーメン まきさだは、烏丸御池からほど近い中京区高倉通二条西入に2026年4月27日にオープンした、京都の創作中華の名店「京中華 牧定」が手がけるラーメン専門店。割烹を思わせる落ち着いたカウンター10席のみの小さな店で、看板の一杯はもともと牧定でまかないとして振る舞われていたネギそばを、ラーメンとして一から再構築したもの。
chef_bio.philosophy: 店主は世代を超えて愛される地域の店を目指している――「幼い頃に親に連れられて来ていた子どもが、大人になって今度は自分の家族を連れて来る。そんなふうに世代を超えて愛され、地域に根付いた店にしたい」と語る。
chef_bio.specialty: まきさだラーメン（1,100円）。たっぷりのネギと細切りチャーシューがのった京都風の醤油ラーメンで、動物系の出汁の旨みをしっかり感じつつ後味はすっきり。パツッと歯切れのいいストレート細麺と、じんわり広がるスープのコク、香り高いネギが身上。セット（ごはん・焼売）は+300円で、焼売はクワイ入りのジューシーな一品。夏には冷やしラーメン（1,200円）などの季節限定も登場する。

anecdotes.text: 看板の『まきさだラーメン』は、母体の創作中華店『京中華 牧定』でまかないとして出していたネギそばを、ラーメンとして再構築した一杯。
ラーメンは『まきさだラーメン』の一種類のみという潔い構成で、ごはんと焼売のセットを付けられる。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: leafkyotoで2026/4/27開業・中京区高倉通二条西入・『京中華 牧定』が手がける・カウンター10席・まきさだラーメン1100円(牧定のまかないネギそばを再構築)・ネギと細切りチャーシューの京都風醤油・セット+300円(ごはん/焼売)・クワイ入り焼売・夏の冷やし1200円・世代を超えて愛される店をという店主の言葉を確認。shiyakusho-lunchでラーメンは一種のみ・セット・立地を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 看板のまきさだラーメンは醤油ラーメンで、醤油（しょうゆ）ベースのタレを用いる。通常のしょうゆは小麦を含むため、グルテン回避者には不向き。

vegan_cross_contact.text: スープは動物系の出汁で、トッピングは細切りチャーシュー（豚）、サイドの焼売も豚肉。植物性のみの選択肢は確認できない。

cultural_comfort.note: A small 10-seat counter ramen shop run by a long-established Kyoto Chinese restaurant; staff likely operate in Japanese, so basic Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.

places.name: ラーメン 豚やどっ (Ramen Buta Yado)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (中太の角ストレート麺 with egg white added for a wheat aroma), seasoned with Kyoto Goko soy sauce tare, which is wheat-based. No gluten-free noodle or tare option is offered and no allergy/cross-contamination handling is described anywhere. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot safely eat the ramen here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire concept is pork-centric: the broth is made solely from animal ingredients (豚ウデ肉・背脂 — pork arm meat and back fat), and the shop's identity is generous chashu (sliced pork). The soy-sauce tare and side dishes are pork-based as well. There is no vegetable broth or plant-based bowl.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened February 7, 2026 in the Nishi-Kamogamo / Omiya-Yakushiyama corner of Kita-ku, taking over a space that had previously housed another ramen shop. It is a small independent counter built around one idea: a Kyoto bowl you could eat every single day.

chef_bio.background: Opened February 7, 2026 in the Nishi-Kamogamo / Omiya-Yakushiyama corner of Kita-ku, taking over a space that had previously housed another ramen shop. It is a small independent counter built around one idea: a Kyoto bowl you could eat every single day.
chef_bio.philosophy: "毎日食べられるラーメン" — ramen you can eat every day. The seasoning is kept gentle on purpose, the chashu is cooked soft so it appeals across all ages, and the noodles are made in-house: medium-thick angular straight noodles at 25% hydration, rested a day, with egg white worked in to push the wheat aroma that Kyoto diners favor.
chef_bio.specialty: The house pork-shoyu ramen. The broth is a pork clear-soup (豚清湯) made from only pork arm meat and back fat — deliberately no pork bone — so it lands light yet rich ("あっさりコクうま"), then seasoned with Goko (五光), a traditional Kyoto soy sauce, for that familiar nostalgic Kyoto sharpness. The signature move is chashu, piled without restraint: the standard Buta-yado Ramen (¥950) carries 150g, and the Buta-yado Chashu-men (¥1,200) goes to a 210g mountain of tender, thinly-sliced pork arm. A lighter Buta-yasumi Ramen (¥700) keeps just 30g for a quieter bowl.

anecdotes.text: The owner names Daiichi Asahi and Ramen Fuji — pillars of old-school Kyoto ramen — as the inspiration for the style, positioning Buta-yado as a torch-carrier for the traditional Kyoto bowl rather than a trend chaser.

The broth is described as evoking nostalgia for Kyoto residents — 'no off-smell or funk, with pork umami, richness and the sharpness of soy' — and regulars flag the chashu portions as a destination for people who simply want a lot of tender pork.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumitukiで2026/2/7開業・北区西加茂/大宮・『毎日食べられるラーメン』・豚清湯(腕肉と背脂のみ、豚骨不使用)・五光醤油・自家製麺(加水25%・一晩寝かせ・卵白入り)・チャーシュー量(700円30g/950円150g/1200円210g)・第一旭/ラーメンふじを手本に、を確認。kyo-sokuで立地・豚清湯の懐かしい味わい・チャーシューの多さを裏付け。店主名は非公開でnull。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles and a wheat-based Kyoto soy-sauce (Goko) tare; everything passes through the same shared noodle water and kitchen, so gluten is unavoidable and cross-contact is pervasive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is built on Goko, a traditional Kyoto soy sauce — a standard fermented soy sauce that contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made exclusively from pork (arm meat and back fat) and the shop's whole identity is heaped pork chashu; there is no plant-based option and animal product is in every bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual neighborhood Kyoto ramen counter aimed at locals; no English is indicated, so a few words of Japanese or a pointing-at-the-menu approach will make ordering smoother.

places.name: 麺屋 月 (Menya Tsuki)
places.gf_detail: This is an abura-soba and ramen shop: the noodles are wheat (made by long-established Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku) and the signature dish is dressed in a house sweet-spicy soy (shoyu) tare, which is wheat-based. There is no published gluten-free menu or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on abura-soba topped with pork chashu and stewed pork (kakuni), plus chicken-broth and dashi (fish/seafood) noodle bowls and Okayama-style fried chicken. No vegan or vegetarian option is advertised.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30, 17:00-1:30 the next day (irregular days off, closures announced on Instagram)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺屋 月 is a small abura-soba specialist that opened on February 22, 2026 in Kyoto's Shimogamo neighborhood, on Kitaoji-dori near Kyoto Prefectural University in what is a competitive local ramen district. It is an intimate counter-and-table spot with just over a dozen seats.

chef_bio.background: 麺屋 月 is a small abura-soba specialist that opened on February 22, 2026 in Kyoto's Shimogamo neighborhood, on Kitaoji-dori near Kyoto Prefectural University in what is a competitive local ramen district. It is an intimate spot of about seven tables seating roughly fourteen guests.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop leans on quality where it counts: the thick noodles come from Menya Teigaku, a venerable Kyoto noodle maker founded in 1931, and the abura-soba is built to be enjoyed first as-is, then re-seasoned partway through with chili oil and vinegar to change the flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is abura-soba (brothless mixed noodles): extra-thick noodles tossed in a house-made sweet-and-spicy shoyu tare and fragrant aroma oil, finished with chashu or stewed pork belly (kakuni), menma, soft-boiled egg, shredded nori and white negi. Portions step up generously by weight, from 200g up to 500g for the same modest price band.

anecdotes.text: The extra-thick noodles are supplied by Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂), a Kyoto noodle maker established in 1931, and are praised for a firm, chewy bite that holds up to the heavy tare.
Abura-soba is offered in four sizes at the same low price ladder (200g ¥850, 300g ¥900, 400g ¥950, 500g ¥1000), making it easy to size up for a bigger appetite.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: osumitukiで2026/2/22開業・左京区下鴨/北大路通(京都府立大近く)・7卓14席ほど・自家製の甘辛醤油ダレと香味油の極太麺油そば・麺は麺屋棣鄂(1931年創業)製・トッピング(チャーシュー/角煮/メンマ/味玉/海苔/白ネギ)・200g850円〜500g1000円の増量・そのまま→ラー油と酢で味変、を確認。店主名は非公開でnull。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature abura-soba is dressed in a house-made sweet-spicy soy (shoyu) tare, which is wheat-based; combined with the wheat noodles this dish is not gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, recently opened neighborhood noodle counter where ordering in basic Japanese (and pointing at the menu) will be smoothest; no indication of English service.

places.name: 京ラーメン さだかず (Kyo Ramen Sadakazu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. The bowl uses thin, low-hydration straight wheat noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that almost certainly contains wheat, plus a chicken/pork broth. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergy information published. This is a tiny single-owner late-night counter shop and should be treated as unsafe for anyone avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. The signature bowl is built on a chicken-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth finished with pork back-fat (seabura) and chashu pork, so it is fundamentally animal-based. No vegetable or vegan ramen is offered, and no sources indicate any plant-based accommodation. A vegan diner has nothing suitable here.
places.hours_raw: 22:00-1:00 the next day (Mon-Sat); occasional irregular daytime service. Closed Sun and holidays. Open only for about three late-night hours, and often sells out at around 35 servings.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sadakazu is a late-night Gion institution that earned the nickname "幻のラーメン" (phantom ramen) because it opens only for about three hours after 10 p.m. and often sells out after roughly 35 bowls. The shop relocated from the Bohana area of Fushimi Ward to the basement of the Gion Fi

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Gion, Higashiyama-ku); relocated from Fushimi-ku, Kyoto.
chef_bio.background: Sadakazu is a late-night Gion institution that earned the nickname "幻のラーメン" (phantom ramen) because it opens only for about three hours after 10 p.m. and often sells out after roughly 35 bowls. The shop relocated from Fushimi Ward to the basement (B1F) of the Gion First Building, a move into prime Gion territory that — along with television exposure — turned it into a sought-after late-night and post-drinking destination. It is a solo-run six-seat counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The broth is a mellow soy flavoring with deep umami — a back-fat 'seabura chaccha' style that stays drinkable rather than heavy ('背脂ちゃっちゃ系だが、そんなに油はきつくない。まろやかな醤油ダシ'), the kind of bowl meant to cap off a night out in Gion.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is a Kyoto-style back-fat soy-sauce ramen (背脂醤油ラーメン, 'seabura chaccha'): a clean chicken-bone shoyu broth blanketed with pork back-fat, paired with thin, low-hydration straight noodles and chashu (¥700). A 'special' version (¥900) upgrades the chashu, and a chilled ramen appears in summer.

anecdotes.text: Open only about 22:00–01:00 and run entirely by one person from a six-seat counter, the shop typically sells out after roughly 35 bowls, which is why it is called a 'phantom' ramen that is hard to actually catch open.
It moved from Fushimi Ward into the basement of the Gion First Building, and that move plus TV appearances drove its breakout popularity as a late-night/after-drinking ramen.
The bowl became well-known enough that Ace Cook released a cup noodle 'supervised by owner 樹下真一' recreating the shop's signature 背油醤油ラーメン (released Oct 18, 2021).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopiで『幻のラーメン』・22時台〜25時頃の約3時間営業・一人営業・約35杯で売切・伏見区から祇園ファーストビルB1へ移転・TV出演・背脂ちゃっちゃのまろやか醤油・細麺低加水・700円/特製900円/夏は冷やし、を確認。cupmen-reviewでエースコックの監修カップ麺(2021/10/18)と店主名『樹下真一』を確認しchef_nameに追記。'坊花'の地区名は未確認のため伏見区に丸めた。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water; the dish is built on a wheat-containing soy-sauce tare. No gluten-free handling is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in standard Japanese ramen contains wheat-fermented soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone based and finished with pork back-fat and chashu pork; there is no separate vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, late-night, Japanese-only counter run solo by the owner — expect to order simply in Japanese, eat quickly, and not linger; little to no English support.

places.name: 祇園京都ラーメン (Gion Kyoto Ramen)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle shoyu ramen shop: the noodles are wheat, the tare is soy-sauce based (Japanese shoyu typically contains wheat), and the broth uses pork and back fat. No gluten-free menu, noodle, or preparation is indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Both signature bowls are animal-based: the 特選醤油ラーメン uses a pork/back-fat soy broth with pork loin slices, and the 鶏どろラーメン is a rich chicken-based broth. No vegan or vegetable-only option is indicated.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-5:00 (L.O. 4:30), closed Sun
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A late-night ramen shop sitting right in the heart of Gion on Hanami-koji Street, just a short walk from Gion-Shijo Station. It is built for the after-hours crowd, opening into the evening and running deep into the night for diners winding down after an evening out in one of Kyot

chef_bio.background: A late-night ramen shop sitting right in the heart of Gion on Hanami-koji Street, just a short walk from Gion-Shijo Station. It is built for the after-hours crowd, opening into the evening and running deep into the night for diners winding down after an evening out in one of Kyoto's most storied entertainment districts.
chef_bio.philosophy: An accessible, comforting bowl tuned for late-night Gion: a clean, drinkable broth rather than a heavy one, finished to each diner's liking with the condiments on the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 特選醤油ラーメン (special soy-sauce ramen): a light, easy-drinking Kyoto-style shoyu broth with just a touch of back fat and a gently restrained soy depth, served with medium-thick noodles, thin slices of pork loin, flavorful menma, and white and green onions. Generous table condiments — spicy miso-garlic and grated garlic — let you build the bowl to taste. A richer 鶏どろラーメン, a thick chicken-based ramen, is the other house bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop is laid out with a central cooking area ringed by counter seats and table seating along the opposite wall, giving it a more spacious feel than a typical late-night ramen stand right in the middle of Gion.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-gourmet.infoで祇園・花見小路(祇園四条駅近く)の深夜営業店、特選醤油ラーメン(背油少量のあっさり京都醤油・中太麺・ロース肉・メンマ・白/青ネギ)、辛味噌にんにく・おろしにんにくの卓上薬味、鶏どろラーメン、中央厨房を囲むカウンター+壁側テーブルのレイアウト、を確認。店主名は非公開でnull。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen; Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat, and the noodles are wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are pork/back-fat (shoyu bowl) and chicken (鶏どろ bowl); shared pots and counter make a vegan meal impractical.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual late-night neighborhood ramen counter in the heart of Gion; ordering is simple and welcoming, but expect a primarily Japanese-language, locals-and-night-crowd setting.

places.name: 鶏そば あやむや 烏丸店 (Torisoba Ayam-YA Karasuma)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard ramen and tsukemen shop built on wheat noodles, with shoyu, shio and miso bowls whose tare typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or gluten-free preparation is advertised, so it is not suitable for celiac diners. Halal does not mean gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The house style is tori-paitan (rich chicken broth) and the signature bowls are chicken-based, so the standard menu is not vegan or vegetarian. The kitchen is halal (no pork, no alcohol) and avoids chemical seasonings, which makes it ingredient-conscious, but a plant-based diner would need to ask directly whether anything meat-free can be prepared and should not assume a vegan option exists.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu-Sun and holidays 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30). Closed Wed. Also closed the 1st and 3rd Tue, plus irregular days off.
places.notes: Ayam-YA grew out of a ramen company whose very first shop opened not in Japan but in Seattle. When the team brought the concept home to Japan's hyper-competitive ramen scene, they struggled to draw crowds, until an Indonesian exchange student asked a simple question that changed

chef_bio.background: Ayam-YA grew out of a ramen company whose very first shop opened not in Japan but in Seattle. When the team brought the concept home to Japan's hyper-competitive ramen scene, they struggled to draw crowds, until an Indonesian exchange student asked a simple question that changed everything: 'Do you use only chicken?' That was the moment the founders learned that many visitors could not eat ordinary ramen for religious reasons. Determined to let Muslim travelers in Kyoto enjoy a real bowl of Japanese ramen with peace of mind, they reformulated the recipe and pursued halal certification through the Malaysia Halal Corporation. 'Ayam' means 'chicken' in Indonesian and Malay.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make a genuinely delicious Japanese ramen that observant Muslims and curious travelers alike can eat with peace of mind, by keeping the kitchen free of pork and alcohol and building flavor honestly from chicken rather than additives.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori-paitan (rich chicken) ramen served as the basic shoyu (soy) chicken soba and a richer shio (salt) chicken bowl, with miso also offered; the broths lean on chicken with a seafood aroma for depth, finished with finely minced onion and scallion, and made without chemical seasonings.

anecdotes.text: A turning point came when an Indonesian exchange student asked whether the broth used only chicken, which first made the team aware that some customers could not eat ramen for religious reasons and led them to pursue halal certification.

Owner Saifullah — from Sri Lanka and originally a teacher — came to Japan to study and wished to stay; a devout Muslim, he keeps a prayer space, and the name 'Ayam' is the Indonesian/Malay word for chicken.
The chicken broth carries depth from a seafood aroma, with finely minced onion and scallion adding a herbaceous lift, all achieved without chemical seasonings.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: groovyjapanで『最初の店はシアトル』『日本で苦戦→インドネシア人留学生の“鶏だけ?”の問いが転機→マレーシア・ハラル・コーポレーションでハラル認証』『Ayam=鶏(インドネシア/マレー語)』『店主サイフラ、礼拝スペース』を確認。web検索で店主Saifullahがスリランカ出身の元教師で留学来日と裏付け。japanhalalで四条烏丸のハラル認証4号店・魚介の香りのコク・玉ねぎとネギのミジン切り・化学調味料不使用を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat ramen and tsukemen noodles with shoyu/shio/miso tare; no gluten-free preparation is offered, so cross-contamination with wheat is inherent to the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature basic bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) chicken soba; Japanese soy sauce tare is normally wheat-based, so the soy bowls should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house broth is tori-paitan (chicken) and the menu is chicken-centered, so a meat-free or vegan bowl is not advertised and would share equipment with chicken broth.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop is halal-certified through the Malaysia Halal Corporation and keeps the kitchen free of pork and alcohol, indicating a kitchen used to strict ingredient control, though this addresses religious requirements rather than allergens.

positives.text: Cooks without chemical seasonings and builds umami from chicken and seafood aromatics, with no pork and no alcohol in the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A halal-certified shop accustomed to international and Muslim travelers, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, though full English is not guaranteed.

places.name: 夜来香 西京極店 (Yeraishan Nishikyogoku-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a Japanese-style Chinese diner built on wheat: noodles, gyoza wrappers, and sauces thickened/seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and likely doubanjiang. The signature mapo Tianjin rice rides on a mapo sauce that almost certainly contains wheat soy seasoning. There is no indication of gluten-free menus, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering. The kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward: pork features in the mapo sauce, gyoza, fried chicken, and broths; dishes use animal-based stocks. Even otherwise plant-leaning items (fried rice, ankake) are cooked with meat/egg and shared woks. No vegetarian or vegan menu is advertised.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu-Sun & holidays: 11:00-14:00 and 17:00-21:00 (lunch open also listed as 11:30 on some sources). Closed Wednesdays. Open during holidays, Golden Week and Obon. Some sources note occasional irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: 右京区・西京極畔勝町 (Nishikyogoku, Ukyo Ward); moments from the east
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood machi-chuka (Japanese-style Chinese) restaurant that has fed the Nishikyogoku district for roughly 40 years, just east of Hankyu Nishikyogoku Station. It draws a cross-section of locals, from solo diners on their lunch break to families, in a bright, big-windowed r

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood machi-chuka (Japanese-style Chinese) restaurant that has fed the Nishikyogoku district for roughly 40 years, just east of Hankyu Nishikyogoku Station on Hanayacho-dori. It draws a cross-section of locals, from solo diners on their lunch break to families, in a bright, big-windowed room with a warm, unpretentious feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, generous, affordable home-style Chinese cooking for the neighborhood — big portions, courteous service, and prices that keep regulars coming back day after day.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the maabo-tenshinhan (mapo Tianjin rice): a fluffy egg over rice blanketed in a generous, glossy mapo sauce. The sauce is built on house-made fermented black bean paste (豆鼓醤) and several chili pastes with ginger and plenty of garlic, given depth by coarsely minced salted fermented black beans (豆鼓の粒) and finished with house chili oil, with finely minced pork for richness — kept addictively savory rather than punishingly hot, served around 1,050 yen with soup and pickles.

anecdotes.text: The maabo-tenshinhan reportedly began life as a staff meal (まかない) before customers loved it enough that it became the house signature.
The mapo sauce poured over the Tianjin rice contains minced pork and aromatics but no tofu (豆腐は入っていない), giving it a concentrated, umami-rich punch against the mild egg-and-rice base.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based machi-chuka kitchen: noodles, gyoza, fried items and sauces share woks, fryers and prep surfaces, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chinese-diner cooking here relies on standard soy-based and bean-paste seasonings, which in Japan are wheat-containing; the signature mapo sauce is built on house doubanjiang and chili bean pastes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg and animal stocks run throughout the menu (mapo sauce, gyoza, fried chicken, fried rice), cooked in shared woks, so no dish can be considered free of animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, at-home neighborhood Chinese diner where a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way; English support is not advertised, so come with basic phrases or this guide.

places.name: 中国料理 菜格 (Chugoku Ryori Saikaku)
places.gf_detail: A Sichuan Chinese kitchen built around wheat. The tantanmen and dumpling skins are wheat-flour, the chili/sesame tare and seasonings used in dishes like the mapo tofu lean on soy-based and bean-paste condiments that typically contain wheat, and there is no gluten-free menu or protocol. Not suitable for celiacs. A coeliac diner could ask whether any single rice- or vegetable-based plate could be prepared plainly, but cross-contact in a one-cook wok kitchen is unavoidable — treat as not GF-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan specialist. The signature bowls (tantanmen, mapo tofu, boiled dumplings, sweet-and-sour pork) all use pork and/or animal-derived broth and condiments. There is no marked vegan option. Because it is a small a-la-carte Sichuan kitchen run by one chef, a vegan might be able to ask in advance for a plain vegetable stir-fry, but nothing is confirmed plant-based, so reserve and ask directly before relying on it.
places.hours_raw: Dinner 18:00-21:00 (L.O. ~20:30). Lunch not currently served (as of recent listing). Closed Sunday; Monday occasionally closed (~twice monthly); irregular temporary closures possible.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, refined Sichuan-leaning Chinese kitchen tucked into a quiet stretch of Nijo-dori just south of the Imperial Palace, opened in 2013 and run single-handedly by its chef-owner. He came up through the produce markets before cooking, and that background shows in his sourcing

chef_bio.background: A small, refined Sichuan-leaning Chinese kitchen tucked into a quiet stretch of Nijo-dori just south of the Imperial Palace, opened in 2013 and run single-handedly by its chef-owner. He came up through the produce markets before cooking, and that background shows in his sourcing — a cook with a reliable eye for ingredients who keeps the menu deliberately compact.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep the cooking simple and let quality ingredients carry it — the kind of place where, as one chef put it, everything you order somehow tastes good. Heat is balanced and aromatic rather than punishing.
chef_bio.specialty: Sichuan-style cooking pitched a notch upscale rather than fiery for its own sake: a fragrant tantanmen (the lunchtime dry 汁なし version is especially praised), a mapo tofu where the Sichuan pepper and chili oil are rounded out by the sweetness of the ground pork, plus boiled dumplings and sweet-and-sour pork.

anecdotes.text: Run entirely by one person, so the chef notes service may run slow — part of the charm of a true counter operation.
A fellow Kyoto chef of the same age describes the owner as a former market worker with a trustworthy eye for produce, whose relatively simple cooking nonetheless makes everything taste delicious.
Recognized as a Bib Gourmand selection in the Michelin Guide Kyoto 2023 (Nakagyo-ku Chinese).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, wheat dumpling skins, and wheat-containing Chinese seasonings (soy/bean pastes) are central to the menu and share a single one-cook wok station; assume heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan dishes such as the mapo tofu and the chili/sesame tantanmen tare rely on soy- and fermented-bean-based seasonings that typically contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use pork and animal-based broth/condiments throughout; no dedicated plant-based prep is indicated, and a single chef cooks everything in shared woks.

positives.text: Tiny 14-seat counter with one attentive chef-owner who takes reservations, so allergy questions can be raised directly when booking — though no formal allergen handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run Sichuan counter where one chef cooks everything, so Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking companion) and a prior reservation make for the smoothest visit; come ready to be patient and order in Japanese.

places.name: ラーメン大戦争 京都河原町店 (Ramen Daisenso Kyoto Kawaramachi)
places.gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen specialist whose signature bowls are built on soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. Both the noodles and the seasoning base contain gluten, and no gluten-free menu or noodle option is indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: All four signature bowls are animal-based: the broths are made from chicken bones (Nagoya Cochin), scallop, and fish/dashi elements, and chashu (pork) is a centerpiece. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-00:00 (L.O. 23:30), open daily. (Some local listings note 11:00-22:30; the official store information on the listing shows until midnight.)
places.neighborhood: Sanjo-Kawaramachi, Nakagyo Ward (around Sanjo Stn / Kyoto-Kawaramachi Stn, Yamazakicho)
places.cuisine: Tanrei (light, clear) style; seafood-tonkotsu-soy sauce ramen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Daisenso ("Ramen World War") is the Kyoto outpost of the acclaimed Osaka ramen group behind Jinrui Mina Menrui, the much-queued shop that turned shoyu ramen into something theatrical. This branch opened in October 2022 on the first floor of a renovated Kawaramachi building

chef_bio.origin: 大阪（人類みな麺類系列）
chef_bio.background: Ramen Daisenso ("Ramen World War") is the Kyoto outpost of the acclaimed Osaka ramen group behind Jinrui Mina Menrui, the much-queued shop that turned shoyu ramen into something theatrical. This branch opened in October 2022 on the first floor of a renovated Kawaramachi building (formerly a sushi restaurant) and marked the group's first move into Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: A noodle-first house: the noodle-making room is visible from the street, signalling pride in house-made noodles, paired with generous service — diners can add up to five chashu slices and get a free half-portion noodle refill (kaedama).
chef_bio.specialty: The flagship bowl is the Kansai-dashi shoyu ramen named 'Pistol' (ピストル) — a clear, light-bodied shoyu broth built on Nagoya Cochin chicken bones and scallop, with deep umami and a faint natural sweetness. The lineup of four soy-based bowls also includes 'Heiwa' (平和), 'Sabita Katana' (錆びた刀, a brothless/soupless shoyu), and 'Jiyu' (自由).

anecdotes.text: The shop names its bowls after evocative wartime-and-peace imagery — 'Pistol,' 'Peace,' 'Rusty Sword,' 'Freedom' — fitting the playful 'Ramen World War' branding of the group.
Its arrival was billed as the Osaka queue-shop's first expansion into Kyoto, opening on 4 October 2022.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the kitchen is built around wheat-based shoyu tare; gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature bowls are Kansai-dashi shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen; Japanese shoyu tare is brewed from wheat, so the seasoning base contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from chicken bone and scallop with fish/dashi elements, and chashu pork is central — no plant-based option and pervasive animal-product use.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, queue-friendly counter-and-table ramen shop in central Kawaramachi; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the photo menu will carry you through ordering.

places.name: 人類みな麺類 つけ麺専門店 京都店 (Jinrui Mina Menrui Tsukemen Senmonten Kyoto-)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The house dipping noodles are 全粒粉入りの極太麺 — extra-thick wheat (whole-grain) noodles — and the rich pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth almost certainly carries wheat through the tare. There is no gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation. Anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu is built on a 豚骨魚介 (pork-bone and fish/seafood) broth, with a pork rice bowl (豚丼) as the main side. No vegetable or plant-based broth option is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:30 / 17:00-20:00, closed Wed (local reports). Note: Tabelog lists '11:00-20:45, open daily,' likely reflecting changes since opening, so confirm the latest hours before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Shimomaruyacho, Nakagyo Ward (1 min walk from Kyoto Shiyakusho-mae Stn / 6 min walk from Keihan Sanjo Stn)
places.notes: This is the Kyoto branch of 人類みな麺類, a tsukemen specialty offshoot of one of Osaka's most famous ramen brands. The original 人類みな麺類 opened in Nishinakajima, Osaka in 2012 and built a reputation for weekend lines of fifty-plus people. The dedicated tsukemen shop launched as a new su

chef_bio.origin: Osaka (Nishinakajima); brand expanded to Kyoto in 2025
chef_bio.background: This is the Kyoto branch of 人類みな麺類, a tsukemen specialty offshoot of one of Osaka's most famous ramen brands. The original 人類みな麺類 opened in Nishinakajima, Osaka in 2012 and built a reputation for weekend lines of fifty-plus people. The dedicated tsukemen shop launched as a new sub-brand in Osaka's Yodogawa ward in April 2025; this Kyoto-shiyakusho-mae location opened on September 1, 2025 as its second outpost, just steps from Kawaramachi-dori.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand name 人類みな麺類 — playfully glossed in English as 'Human beings, everybody noodles' — captures founder Matsumura's idea that noodles are something all of humanity shares. The kitchen states its aim plainly: to deliver the highest performance it can currently offer and to keep building the finest shop it can.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick whole-grain wheat dipping noodles served with a rich, mellow pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) dipping broth lifted with scallion-oil richness. The signature lineup is three broths — 原点 (the 'origin'), macro, and micro — with noodle portions of 200g, 300g and 400g all at the same price. The Kyoto-only bowl is the 九条ネギつけ麺, finished with fragrant local Kujo green onion. A small pork rice bowl and a finishing-rice option round out the dip.

anecdotes.text: Matsumura has said the name was born when he was a high-schooler: after a part-time shift he went for ramen with friends, warmed his hands on the bowl, and the thought struck him — 'humanity is all noodles' (人類はみな麺類や) — which became the name of his first shop.
The dipping noodles are made extremely long — eaters use scissors at the table to cut them as they go — a hallmark carried over from the tsukemen brand.
The Kyoto-exclusive 九条ネギ tsukemen was announced as the local-only special when the shop opened on September 1, 2025.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, popular tsukemen counter where a few words of Japanese and following the eat-as-you-go rhythm (the noodles are very long and you cut them with scissors at the table) will make ordering smooth.

places.name: 博多長浜ラーメン みよし (Hakata Nagahama Ramen Miyoshi)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen shop built around wheat noodles (ultra-thin Hakata straight noodles) in a pork-bone broth, with kaedama (noodle refills) as a core part of the experience. There are no gluten-free noodle or broth options, and no dedicated gluten-free preparation. Not suitable for celiac diners. No allergen handling information was found.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth — the shop's identity is its pork broth, kept going continuously. There is essentially one ramen, animal-based, with toppings like chashu (pork). No vegan or even vegetarian bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon/Tue/Thu/Sun 11:00–01:00 (next day); Fri/Sat 11:00–03:00 (next day); weekday mid-afternoon break around 15:00–17:00; closed Wednesday. Note: several blog/listing sources describe much later late-night hours (roughly 11:00 until early morning) and state no regular closing day, so actual closing time runs late and can vary.
places.neighborhood: Ishiyacho / Sanjo-Kiyamachi, Nakagyo Ward (around Sanjo Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 博多長浜ラーメン みよし opened in 1983 in Kyoto's Kiyamachi/Sanjo entertainment district and has become an old-school local institution — the kind of place locals say everyone in Kyoto knows by name. It has been described as a味 (taste) protected for roughly four decades by the family runnin

chef_bio.background: 博多長浜ラーメン みよし opened in 1983 (昭和58年) in Kyoto's Kiyamachi/Sanjo entertainment district and has become an old-school local institution — '京都の人間でその名を知らぬ者はいない' (there is no one in Kyoto who doesn't know its name). For roughly four decades it has carried the same Hakata Nagahama taste, its small open counter running late into the night.
chef_bio.philosophy: A purist, no-frills tonkotsu shop: keep the pork-bone broth going, add nothing unnecessary, and let each diner build their own flavor with the tabletop condiments. Regulars even treat certain moves (like adding pepper or curry powder) as 'forbidden,' and finishing by pouring extra broth over rice is part of the ritual.
chef_bio.specialty: Essentially one bowl done one way: Hakata Nagahama-style tonkotsu ramen — a clean, gently sweet pork-bone broth paired with ultra-thin Hakata straight noodles. Diners customize at the table with around eleven free toppings (beni-shoga pickled ginger, coarse ichimi chili, tenkasu tempura scraps, sesame, etc.) and can order kaedama noodle refills, which is treated as part of doing Nagahama ramen 'your way.'

anecdotes.text: Often cited as a place 'everyone in Kyoto knows' (京都の人間でその名を知らぬ者はいない) — an old-establishment late-night ramen counter running since 1983.

Famous celebrity regular: the gourmet entertainer GACKT, who reportedly rarely eats ramen, is said to have been a loyal customer since his university days and to finish with extra broth poured over rice (替えスープ).
Recommended order from fans: ramen with 'barikata' (extra-firm) noodles, customized with beni-shoga, coarse ichimi, and tenkasu — with pepper and curry powder considered the 'forbidden' moves (禁じ手).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat ramen noodles in a shared tonkotsu broth with kaedama (noodle refills), so a single shared noodle/broth workflow — high cross-contamination risk for gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is built on a continuously simmered pork-bone broth; there is no separate non-animal broth, so all bowls share an animal-based base.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, retro 16-seat counter-only late-night ramen counter in Kyoto's Kiyamachi nightlife district run by a longtime local family; little to no English, food-ticket ordering, and best approached with basic Japanese and casual ramen-counter etiquette.

places.name: めんきや 京都壬生本店 (Menkiya Kyoto Mibu Honten)
places.gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen shop built on a pork-bone-and-back-fat soy-sauce broth. The thin noodles are wheat, the soy-sauce tare is virtually certainly wheat-based shoyu, and the broth is pork-derived — none of which is celiac-safe. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tare, or menu item, no dedicated equipment, and shared pots/cooking surfaces make cross-contamination unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and back-fat broth with pork chashu and toppings such as a boiled egg; sides include karaage and gyoza. There is no vegan or even vegetarian ramen on offer and no plant-based broth alternative noted. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-2:00 the next day (listed as 11:00-26:00), L.O. 1:30. Closing days differ by source: New Year holiday only (Shijo-Omiya Shotengai official) / 2nd and 4th Mon plus Jan 1-5 closed (Tabelog).
places.neighborhood: Shijo-Omiya (right by Hankyu Omiya Stn / the Shijo-Omiya intersection, inside the Shijo-Omiya Shotengai)
places.cuisine: Kyoto-black-style back-fat soy sauce tonkotsu ramen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A late-night neighborhood ramen counter sitting right inside the Shijo-Omiya shopping arcade, a few steps from Hankyu Omiya Station. It runs from late morning straight through to around 2 AM, shifting from a lunchtime rush, to evening drinkers ordering sides with beer, to a sudde

chef_bio.origin: 四条大宮商店街 / 壬生, central Kyoto
chef_bio.background: A late-night neighborhood ramen counter sitting right inside the Shijo-Omiya shopping arcade, a few steps from Hankyu Omiya Station. It runs from late morning straight through to around 2 AM, shifting from a lunchtime rush, to evening drinkers ordering sides with beer, to a sudden surge of customers chasing the last trains.
chef_bio.philosophy: The whole shop is built around one idea — 毎日でも食べられるラーメン, 'ramen you could eat every day' — leaning on accessible, balanced flavor rather than heavy gimmickry, using selected ingredients like Kyoto Tanba highland pork and Kujo negi.
chef_bio.specialty: Their core bowl is a classic Kyoto-style se-abura shoyu (back-fat soy-sauce) tonkotsu ramen: a pork-bone broth finished with back fat over thin, easy-slurping noodles, topped with chashu, with branch menus extending to shoyu-pork, chashu-men, and a miso ramen made with miso shipped from Hokkaido.

anecdotes.text: A boiled egg comes with the bowl and a cup of cold matcha is served as an after-meal gesture — small touches that fit the everyday-comfort idea.

It is documented as a 深夜も賑わう地元の繁盛店 — a locally bustling shop that stays busy late, with after-work diners ordering karaage and gyoza alongside an economical fried-rice set, then a last-train crowd queuing outside.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, and shared cooking equipment make this unsafe for celiac diners; no gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowl is a back-fat shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen; the tare is virtually certainly standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and back fat with pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian option is offered, so all bowls share an animal-based base.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, locals-first neighborhood ramen counter where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though detailed dietary questions in English may be hard.

places.name: 京都北山元町らーめん (Kyoto Kitayama Motomachi Ramen)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional soy sauce ramen shop: wheat noodles in a pork-and-chicken-bone broth seasoned with a tamari-style soy sauce tare, which is wheat-based. There is no gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no published allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is built on pork bones blended with chicken bones, and the signature bowls feature back fat, beef tendon and pork toppings. A "vegetable ramen" (野菜らーめん) is on the menu but it is served in the same animal-based broth, so it is not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based broth is offered.
places.cuisine: Black soy sauce; back-fat soy sauce ramen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop traces its roots to a Chinese-noodle (中華そば) food cart in Kyoto's Kitayama Kitamachi that ran for roughly 50 years before settling into a storefront in the Shijo Karasuma area. The 'Motomachi' name and its dark soy-sauce style carry that half-century of cart lineage forwa

chef_bio.background: The shop traces its roots to a Chinese-noodle (中華そば) food cart that stood in a parking lot in Kyoto's Kitayama Shinmachi (北山新町); the business marks 創業50年 (fifty years since its founding) and carries that cart's dark soy-sauce flavor forward into its Shijo-Karasuma storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carry forward the single signature flavor born on the old cart while serving generous, satisfying bowls late into the night for the downtown crowd.
chef_bio.specialty: A rich, dark soy-sauce ramen made with a tamari-style soy sauce of the kind traditionally used for eel sauce (うなぎのタレ), plus a Kyoto-style back-fat (背脂) soy sauce variant with fat drizzled over the top. Beef-tendon ramen (牛すじらーめん), made with sweet-savory domestic lean beef tendon, is a signature bowl.

anecdotes.text: The current shop grew out of a 中華そば food cart that operated in a parking lot in Kyoto's Kitayama Shinmachi, and it still builds its bowls on that same dark soy-sauce flavor (創業50年 屋台の味).
Its calling card is a 'concentrated soy sauce ramen' using a tamari-style soy sauce, offered alongside a Kyoto-style back-fat soy sauce version.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is seasoned with a tamari-style soy sauce tare, which is a wheat-based fermented seasoning.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local late-night ramen counter where ordering is simple but staff and signage are Japanese-only, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps.

places.name: ラーメン 天 本店 (Ramen Ten Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Ramen uses wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare; the kitchen also fries chicken karaage. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated, and cross-contamination is unavoidable for a celiac.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Broth is pork-bone based; ramen is topped with sliced pork. No plant-based ramen or sides are offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-26:00 (2:00 the next day, L.O. 25:30), closed Thu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood ramen institution that has been running since the Showa era — locals describe it as a 30-plus-year veteran shop that relocated to its current spot a few years back and quickly filled its 37 seats again at both lunch and late night.

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood ramen institution that has been running since the Showa era — locals describe it as a 30-plus-year veteran shop that relocated to its current Yamashina spot (from beneath the Tokaido Shinkansen overpass) a few years back and quickly filled its 37 seats again at both lunch and late night.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, generous everyday ramen for the neighborhood, served late into the night.
chef_bio.specialty: A classic Kyoto bowl: a light yet satisfying soy-seasoned pork-bone broth, paired with firm, snappy straight noodles topped with thin-sliced pork, bamboo shoots, bean sprouts and green onion. The bargain A-set adds a big bowl of rice and fried chicken.

anecdotes.text: Regulars rave that the firm straight noodles are quintessentially Kyoto — 'この麺嫌いな京都人おらんやろ' (no Kyoto person could dislike this noodle) — and that the spicy miso served alongside pairs beautifully with the broth.
It is one of the rare Yamashina spots open until 2 a.m., making it a go-to for late-night eaters.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the shop also fries chicken karaage, so gluten exposure is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a soy (shoyu) tare ramen; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and ramen is topped with pork, so no dish is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, no-frills local ramen counter where Japanese will smooth ordering; little to no English is expected, but pointing at the menu works for a simple bowl.

places.name: 龍門 太秦店 (Ryumon Uzumasa)
places.gf_detail: A neighborhood Sichuan/Chinese restaurant. The signature 鶏肉の葱油かけそば and other noodle dishes are built on wheat noodles, and Chinese cooking here leans heavily on soy sauce, oyster/bean-based sauces, and shared wok/fryer surfaces. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners; assume cross-contamination throughout.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood-forward Chinese kitchen — the house specialty centers on fried chicken and chicken oil, and stocks/sauces are animal-based. No vegan menu or documented plant-based broth. While a few vegetable stir-fries (e.g. spicy eggplant) exist on the menu, they are not prepared vegan (soy/oyster-style sauces, shared woks, likely meat-based stock), so no reliable vegan option can be confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-22:30, closed Wed
places.neighborhood: Ukyo Ward / Uzumasa (Randen Katabiranotsuji / around JR Uzumasa Stn)
places.notes: 龍門 is a small Kyoto chain of authentic Sichuan / neighborhood-Chinese (町中華) restaurants run by 株式会社長城, with the Honten near Sanjo-Higashioji and branches in Okazaki and here in Uzumasa, in Ukyo-ku near the Arashiyama (Randen) line and JR Uzumasa. It has built a reputation as the

roles: Independently operated local Chinese restaurant / 町中華
Part of the small 龍門 group in Kyoto
chef_bio.background: 龍門 is a small Kyoto chain of authentic Sichuan / neighborhood-Chinese (町中華) restaurants run by 株式会社長城, with the Honten near Sanjo-Higashioji and branches in Okazaki and here in Uzumasa, in Ukyo-ku near the Arashiyama (Randen) line and JR Uzumasa. It has built a reputation as the kind of unpretentious place that draws not just Kyotoites but diners from across Japan and even visitors from China itself, seeking the real thing at gentle prices.
chef_bio.philosophy: Serve genuine, full-flavored Chinese cooking — fragrant with Sichuan pepper and star anise — at everyday prices in a lively, no-frills room.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is 鶏肉の葱油かけそば — crisp fried chicken over straight medium-thick noodles in a chicken-oil-rich bowl, finished with scallions and chili and a pour of sizzling hot oil. Alongside it, the spicy stir-fried eggplant (茄子のピリ辛炒め) is often called a dish that all but represents the restaurant.

anecdotes.text: 龍門 is described as a 町中華 popular not only with Kyoto locals but with diners from all over Japan and even Chinese visitors from the mainland, who come for an authentic taste.

The 鶏肉の葱油かけそば is an original noodle dish made with authentic Chinese technique — crisp fried chicken and chicken oil in the broth over straight medium-thick noodles, topped with scallion and chili and finished with hot oil.
The spicy eggplant stir-fry is singled out as a dish that it would be no exaggeration to call representative of 龍門.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature and most dishes use wheat noodles and wheat-based Chinese sauces; shared woks and a shared fryer mean pervasive gluten cross-contact. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Chinese cooking here relies on soy sauce and bean/oyster-style sauces, which are wheat-containing; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (the signature bowl centers on fried chicken and chicken oil); vegetable dishes are cooked in shared woks likely with animal-based stock/sauces, so no reliable vegan preparation can be confirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, locals-and-visitors neighborhood Chinese spot where staff may speak Chinese as much as Japanese; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, but expect little English.

places.name: 油珉 (Yumin)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for gluten-free or celiac diners. The entire concept is abura-soba built on thick house-made wheat noodles (made from 100% "オーション" strong wheat flour), dressed in a soy/oil tare that is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu, with pork back fat. There is no published allergen information and no gluten-free option. Avoid if you require celiac-level safety.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a Jiro-style abura-soba shop centered on pork back fat (背脂), wheat noodles, and a savory oil/soy tare; standard toppings include raw or soft-boiled egg, pork fat, mentaiko mayo, and cream cheese. No plant-based or vegetarian bowl is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:30-22:30 (Fri close 22:00). Note: opening-period hours, subject to change — confirm via Instagram.
places.neighborhood: Sakyo Ward / Tanaka-Monzencho (Demachiyanagi / Hyakumanben, near Kyoto University)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 油珉 (Yumin) opened on October 10, 2025 in the Hyakumanben university quarter, just north of the Hyakumanben intersection and a short walk from Demachiyanagi and Kyoto University. It is the second brand of びし屋 (Bishiya), the popular, queue-forming ramen shop in Kyoto's Ichijoji — t

roles: Sister/second brand of びし屋 (Bishiya), Ichijoji
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Sakyo-ku, Tanaka Monzencho), as a spin-off of the Ichijoji ramen shop びし屋
chef_bio.background: 油珉 (Yumin) opened on October 10, 2025 in the Hyakumanben university quarter, just north of the Hyakumanben intersection and a short walk from Demachiyanagi and Kyoto University. It is the second brand of びし屋 (Bishiya), the popular, queue-forming ramen shop in Kyoto's Ichijoji — the staff here are the Bishiya team, who set out to make a dedicated abura-soba (brothless mixed noodle) specialist for the student neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: Affordable, high-volume, punchy noodles aimed squarely at hungry students — リーズナブル prices and a 'wash-wash' (ワシワシ) chewy texture with the wallop of back fat.
chef_bio.specialty: Jiro-style abura-soba. The standout is the 背脂ヤサイあぶらそば (back-fat & vegetable abura-soba) — extra-thick house-made noodles milled from 100% strong 'オーション' wheat flour, piled with pork back fat, vegetables, menma and naruto, finished tableside with minced garlic and chopped onion. The lineup is three bowls: あぶらそば (750 yen), 辛味あぶらそば (spicy, 850 yen) and 背脂ヤサイあぶらそば (850 yen), with portions scalable from 180g up to a 540g triple.

anecdotes.text: Visitors realized the people running it were the crew from the famous Ichijoji ramen shop びし屋 — 「中の人たちは一乗寺のラーメン店『びし屋』の方々でした」.
The thick house noodles are described as 'オーション100％の極太麺' with a snappy 'ブリンブリン' and chewy 'ワシワシ' texture and strong wheat flavor — the heart of its 二郎系 character.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Built entirely on wheat: thick house-made noodles from 100% strong wheat flour ('オーション100％の極太麺'), in a shared boiling-water/noodle workflow typical of a noodle shop.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dressing is a strong soy-based oil tare with prominent garlic ('醤油ベースのオイルだれ'), which in this style is virtually always wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls center on pork back fat (背脂) and the standard toppings include raw/soft-boiled egg, pork fat, mentaiko mayo and cream cheese; no plant-based preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, student-oriented Jiro-style noodle counter with a ticket-vending-machine ordering system and no published English support, so basic Japanese (or the menu photos) helps; the casual, generous portions make it easy and welcoming once you've chosen.

places.name: ラーメンムギュ Vol.2 烏丸蛸薬師店 (Ramen Mugyu Vol.2 Karasuma Takoyakushi)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use medium-thin straight wheat noodles in a soy (shoyu) tare broth, so both the noodles and the seasoning base contain wheat/gluten. No gluten-free noodles or menu are mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on a chicken (Nagoya Cochin) and dried-bonito broth and are topped with thin-sliced pork belly chashu. The soup is animal-based and there is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:00–22:30 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sunday.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked into a narrow alley just off Takoyakushi-dori near Karasuma, Ramen Mugyu Vol.2 is the second outpost of the original Ramen Mugyu, which began in the Maruyama area. The shop occupies a converted townhouse-style space with a long counter and a relaxed, almost izakaya-like fe

chef_bio.background: Tucked into a narrow alley just off Takoyakushi-dori near Karasuma, Ramen Mugyu Vol.2 is the second outpost of the original Ramen Mugyu, whose Vol.1 shop is in the Enmachi (円町) area. The shop occupies a converted townhouse-style space with a long counter and a relaxed, almost izakaya-like feel; guests are handed paper aprons on arrival to keep the splatter off.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans into a clean, sharp Kyoto-style shoyu profile — proper soy richness with a subtle seafood undertone and a clear chicken backbone — that diners have compared to the old-school Kyoto tradition of Shinpuku Saikan.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the "Onibara Kuro" (鬼腹黒) shoyu ramen: a soy-forward broth built on purebred Nagoya Cochin chicken (純系名古屋コーチン) and aged dried bonito (本枯れ節), brightened with a re-steeped premium soy sauce from Ehime's Kajita shoyu, and finished with medium-thin straight noodles and a fan of extremely thin-sliced pork belly chashu that blankets the bowl. A lighter "Onibara Shiro" (白, white) version and a rich chicken soba (濃厚鶏そば / nōkō torisoba) round out the lineup.

anecdotes.text: Regulars and reviewers liken the soy-based, crisp and salty broth to the venerable Kyoto institution Shinpuku Saikan (新福菜館).
Diners are given a paper apron on entry to protect against splashes, and the counter stretches lengthwise with a notably wide, open kitchen.
The bowl is topped with very thin slices of pork belly chashu that, despite covering the whole surface, stay light on the palate.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls use a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare, and conventional shoyu is brewed with wheat — this is the seasoning backbone of the broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual neighborhood counter ramen shop tucked in an alley; a friendly greeting and pointing at the bowl you want will carry you through, though staff likely speak little English.

places.name: キラメキノ青空 (Kirameki no Aozora)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard ramen and mazesoba shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, with a wheat-based shoyu/tare element and a rich chicken broth, so they are not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. There is no gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no indication of any allergen-handling or dedicated preparation. Even the "shio" (salt) version of the chicken paitan is served over wheat noodles. Treat this as not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The house style is built around a thick chicken (tori-paitan) broth, with chicken chashu and other meat toppings; the Taiwan mazesoba and dan-dan dishes use minced meat. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on offer and no plant-based broth alternative. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-24:30 (L.O. 24:00) / open daily (may close on an ad hoc basis). Tabelog lists 10:30-00:30 daily; official site lists 11:00-24:30 (L.O. 24:00).
places.neighborhood: 下京区・四条室町/烏丸 (Shijo-Muromachi, near Shijo subway and Karasuma
places.notes: キラメキノ青空 is a Kyoto-grown ramen and mazesoba brand run by the Kirameki Group (キラメキノ未来株式会社), known across Kansai as one of the pioneers of Taiwan-style mazesoba. The group built its name in student-heavy neighborhoods like Kawaramachi-Marutamachi, Ichijoji and Yamanouchi before exp

roles: Kirameki Group (キラメキノ未来株式会社) operator
chef_bio.background: キラメキノ青空 is a Kyoto-grown ramen and mazesoba brand run by the Kirameki Group (キラメキノ未来株式会社), known across Kansai as one of the pioneers of Taiwan-style mazesoba. The group operates a spread of shops through Kyoto — in student and downtown neighborhoods such as Yamanouchi (山ノ内) and Tanaka near Kyoto University — and out into Osaka, Shiga and Nara as part of a stated '30-store project'; this Shijo-Muromachi shop (下京区鶏鉾町), a short walk from Shijo-Karasuma, opened as one of its newer locations.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop leans into bold, concentrated flavor and customization: diners are encouraged to tune their bowl with a lemon wedge and the tabletop condiments to shift the heavy chicken broth across the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: A thick, intensely rich tori-paitan (chicken white broth) ramen, offered in both a 'shio no Kirameki' salt version and a shoyu version, built on chicken paitan with niboshi and topped with chashu, menma and green onion, with a lemon wedge for mid-bowl 味変. The kitchen also turns out Taiwan mazesoba and a newer dan-dan mazesoba.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers compare the broth's density to Tenkaiippin's famous pottage-thick soup — 'スープは天下一品みたいなドロドロのポタージュ系' — a heavy, almost gloopy chicken paitan (ぼってりと重い鶏白湯スープ) that rewards using the table condiments and lemon to vary the flavor.
The shop draws a young crowd into the evening — patrons arriving even before 11 p.m. (23時前でも若者がどんどんやってくる) — with a touch-screen ticket vending machine set up outside the storefront for ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard ramen and mazesoba shop: bowls are built on wheat noodles with a wheat-based shoyu/tare element, so wheat is a core ingredient throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around chicken broth and meat toppings (chicken chashu, minced meat in the mazesoba), so there is heavy meat contact across all preparations.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, fast-paced late-night ramen spot with a self-service ticket machine and Japanese-only menu, so a little guidance on ordering helps, but no special etiquette is required.

places.name: 第一旭 桂店 (Daiichi Asahi Katsura-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto-style chuka-soba shop: the ramen is built on wheat noodles in a pork-bone-and-soy-sauce (shoyu) broth, and the soy-sauce tare is almost certainly wheat-based. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated handling, or allergen accommodation is indicated anywhere. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The house broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base finished with soy sauce, and signature bowls are topped with chashu/roast pork. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen offering noted; even a plain bowl would carry the pork broth. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun 11:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30), closed Mon. Some sources note it stays open until around 3:00 in the morning.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Katsura-neighborhood outpost of the long-running Kyoto ramen name Daiichi Asahi, this shop carries on a recipe and reputation built over more than fifty years, passed down through the noren-wake tradition of opening a branch under the founding house's name and methods.

chef_bio.background: A Katsura-neighborhood outpost of the long-running Kyoto ramen name Daiichi Asahi, this shop carries on a recipe and reputation built over more than fifty years, passed down through the noren-wake tradition of opening a branch under the founding house's name and methods.
chef_bio.philosophy: Despite the richness of the pork-and-soy foundation, the broth leans on plenty of simmered vegetable stock to finish clean and light on the palate, the balance Daiichi Asahi is known for. Long daily hours (to around 3am) and a no-frills counter make it the kind of place the neighborhood drops into at any hour, including after a night out.
chef_bio.specialty: Kyoto-style chuka-soba: thin straight noodles in a deeply flavored shoyu broth set on a pork-bone base, crowned with sliced roast pork and a generous heap of green onion. The hearty Special Ramen is the bowl regulars reach for, and the house yakimeshi (fried rice) has its own devoted following.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a noren-wake branch of a Kyoto ramen house with over fifty years of history, carrying the name and recipe of the original.
Regulars single out the fried rice (yakimeshi) as exceptionally good alongside the ramen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gourpass confirms a noren-wake branch of a 50-plus-year Kyoto ramen house, pork-bone/rich-shoyu broth with abundant vegetable stock, Special Ramen (¥650), yakimeshi (¥450), 24 seats, and hours to 3am near Katsura Station.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is described as a pork-bone base seasoned with a deeply flavored soy sauce (shoyu), which in this style is virtually always wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base and bowls are topped with roast pork, so animal products are integral to the dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, cash-only neighborhood ramen counter where a few words of Japanese will go a long way and an English menu should not be assumed.

places.name: らーめん屋台 (Ramen Yatai)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The core dish is Taiwan ramen built on wheat ramen noodles in a pork/minced-meat-and-chili broth, and the menu (gyoza, fried chicken "Apache," yakitori) is wheat- and soy-sauce-heavy. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature Taiwan ramen relies on minced pork and a meat-based broth, and the broader menu centers on chicken, pork, and seafood items. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is advertised.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-23:00 (Tabelog); some listings show evening close at 22:30. Closed Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: 大将軍・北野白梅町 (Kita Ward, near Kitano-Hakubaicho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] らーめん屋台 (Ramen Yatai) started in the Yoshouin neighborhood of Kyoto's Minami Ward and relocated in April 2025 to the Taishogun area near Kitano-Hakubaicho, in the direction of Kinkaku-ji and Enmachi. It is a small, independent neighborhood ramen-and-izakaya hybrid.

chef_bio.background: らーめん屋台 (Ramen Yatai) started in the Yoshoin neighborhood of Kyoto's Minami Ward and relocated in April 2025 to the Taishogun area (Kita Ward) near Kitano-Hakubaicho, in the direction of Kinkaku-ji and Enmachi. It is a small, independent neighborhood ramen-and-izakaya hybrid.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is 台湾ラーメン (Taiwan ramen): a spicy bowl where heat and umami intertwine over a deeply rich broth, finished with garlic, chili, minced meat, and Chinese chives (nira) that you mix thoroughly before eating. Its other famous item is the 'アパッチ (Apache)' — crispy-outside, juicy-inside deep-fried chicken-wing pieces with a lingering spicy seasoning, said to pair perfectly with both beer and the Taiwan ramen.

anecdotes.text: The shop relocated from its original Yoshoin location to the Taishogun/Kitano-Hakubaicho area in April 2025.
Beyond ramen the kitchen serves gyoza, yakitori, oden, and assorted small plates with alcohol, giving it a casual izakaya feel.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms the April 2025 relocation from Yoshoin (Minami-ku) to 北区大将軍東鷹司町 near Kitano-Hakubaicho, the Taiwan ramen and Apache (手羽中唐揚げ) specialties, and gyoza/yakitori/oden izakaya offerings.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the shop is described as welcoming to families, couples, and solo diners, so non-Japanese-speaking visitors should manage comfortably.

places.name: 中国料理 くれたけ (Chugoku Ryori Kuretake)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The ramen and tantanmen are built on wheat noodles, and the broths use a soy-sauce (wheat shoyu) and sesame base over chicken/pork/vegetable stock; the sake-lees ramen dissolves sake lees into a soy-ramen base and is topped with char siu. As a town Chinese kitchen, gyoza, fried items, and stir-fries all involve shared wheat/soy. No celiac handling indicated. A coeliac diner should not eat here without a verified, separately-prepared dish — and none is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan place. The signature noodles use chicken/pork/vegetable broth and char siu pork, and the tantanmen is topped with sweet-miso minced meat. Some vegetable dishes (e.g., a mixed-vegetable set, mapo tofu) exist but are not stated to be meat/animal-free and a shared meat kitchen applies. Vegans would need to ask, with no documented vegan option.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 (Tabelog); some sources list 11:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30) with through-service / weekday lunch specials roughly 11:00–14:00. Confirm directly.
places.neighborhood: Fushimi Ward / Fushimi-Momoyama (in front of Fushimi-Momoyama Stn / Momoyama-Goryomae Stn, corner of Otesuji-dori and Kyomachi-dori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-loved neighborhood Chinese kitchen (町中華) standing at the northwest corner of Oote-dori and Kyomachi-dori, right in front of Fushimi-Momoyama Station and a minute from Momoyama-Goryomae. It has become a fixture for locals who come for serious cooking served in an unfussy, d
