chef_bio.background: A long-loved neighborhood Chinese kitchen (町中華) standing at the northwest corner of Oote-dori and Kyomachi-dori, right in front of Fushimi-Momoyama Station and a minute from Momoyama-Goryomae. It has become a fixture for locals who come for serious cooking served in an unfussy, drop-in setting, with a wood-floored dining room and an adjacent same-name café for Western dishes and coffee.
chef_bio.philosophy: No flashiness, just reliable depth — whatever you order is dependably good, and the heat is kept mild enough that even spice-averse diners can enjoy the tantanmen.
chef_bio.specialty: Sesame-forward tantanmen and, in the cold months, the house's signature sake-lees ramen and sake-lees tantanmen — a nod to Fushimi's sake-brewing heritage. The sake-lees ramen dissolves sake lees into a clear (清湯) chicken-, pork- and vegetable-based soup, finished with char siu and vegetables; the sake-lees tantanmen layers the lees over a rich house-sesame paste and house chili oil, topped with sweet-miso minced pork.

anecdotes.text: The winter-only sake-lees bowls (roughly November to early March, tracking the new-sake brewing season) use sake lees from Fushimi's breweries to add a smooth, warming, complex umami — described as rich and body-warming ('身体ポカポカ').
Locals describe it as a long-established town Chinese place beloved in the neighborhood, where the mapo tofu and tantanmen carry a deep sesame aroma without being overly spicy.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: totteoki and kyotopi confirm the Fushimi-Momoyama location, clear (清湯) sake-lees ramen and sake-lees tantanmen available roughly Nov–early March using Fushimi sake lees (Tamanohikari), the rich-sesame-paste tantanmen with sweet-miso minced pork, mild spice, and the body-warming quality; freelife confirms the beloved 町中華 status and sesame-forward mapo tofu/tantanmen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are wheat and the broths use soy sauce (wheat shoyu) plus a sesame base; the kitchen is a general town-Chinese kitchen making gyoza, fried rice and stir-fries, so wheat and soy are pervasive. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sake-lees ramen is explicitly a soy-sauce ramen (醤油ラーメン) with sake lees dissolved in, indicating standard wheat-containing soy sauce in the tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowls use chicken/pork/vegetable broth and char siu, and the tantanmen is topped with minced-meat miso; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local town-Chinese spot with no English presence indicated, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will make ordering smooth.

places.name: 麺屋 栩羚廚 壬生本店 (Menya Craze Mibu Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (from the Teigaku/棣鄂 noodle maker), a tonkotsu broth finished with what is almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a flour-dredged karaage side. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation or separate handling, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is an ultra-rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth, with chashu, marinated egg, and other animal toppings; even the side dishes are meat. No plant-based broth or noodle option is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-22:00 (last order 30 min before each close), closed Tue (also irregular days off - check Instagram)
places.neighborhood: Mibu-Babacho, Nakagyo Ward (Mibu area / Nijo Stn, Shijo-Omiya, Senbon-Sanjo)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 麺屋 栩羚廚（くれいず）opened on September 12, 2024 on Kouin-dori between Shijo-Omiya and Sanjo-Senbon, an area of central Kyoto in the middle of a wave of new ramen openings. It is a small, design-conscious counter shop that put down roots fast: within months it had drawn a steady followin

chef_bio.origin: 京都市中京区壬生馬場町（壬生エリア）
chef_bio.background: 麺屋 栩羚廚（くれいず）opened on September 12, 2024 on Kouin-dori between Shijo-Omiya and Sanjo-Senbon, an area of central Kyoto in the middle of a wave of new ramen openings. It is a small, design-conscious counter shop that put down roots fast: within months it had drawn a steady following and, in February 2025, opened a second branch in Tokyo's Nakameguro.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'material-commitment' approach paired with careful plating — a richly concentrated pork broth that locals rate among the thickest in Kyoto yet keeps the typical tonkotsu off-smell in check, served with attentive hospitality in a stylish little room.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is an extremely thick, creamy Kyoto-pork tonkotsu. Two core styles anchor the menu — a 濃厚豚骨醤油 (rich tonkotsu-shoyu) and the 極濃豚骨, a paste-thick 'doro-kei' broth poured with a layer of flavorful tare on top that you stir in before eating; a 旨辛豚骨 spiced with ichimi togarashi sits between them. The signature bowl is the limited 極濃豚骨味玉 (¥1,250), built on flat, chewy wheat noodles from the respected Teigaku (棣鄂) noodle maker and finished with squared-cut chashu, a marinated egg, bamboo shoots, and scallion.

anecdotes.text: Diners are offered complimentary burnt-garlic oil (焦がしにんにく油) to add partway through the bowl, deepening the umami and giving the broth a second character mid-meal.
The shop's popularity grew quickly after its September 2024 opening, leading to a Tokyo (Nakameguro) branch in February 2025.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyo-soku confirms the Sept 12 2024 opening on Kouin-dori, the ドロドロ-thick broth rated top-class in Kyoto, the 極濃豚骨味玉 (¥1,250) on 棣鄂 noodles, and the burnt-garlic oil; noodlers confirms the Feb 2025 Tokyo Nakameguro second branch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls use wheat ramen noodles (from the Teigaku/棣鄂 maker) cooked in shared boiling water, and the karaage side is flour-dredged and deep-fried; no gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The core 濃厚豚骨醤油 and the tare layered over the 極濃豚骨 are soy-sauce based, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is built on a pork-bone broth with animal toppings (chashu, egg); there is no separate vegan prep area or plant-based option.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny eight-seat counter shop run on a ticket-vending-machine system, so you can order with almost no Japanese, though a friendly greeting and a little patience go a long way in such a small room.

places.name: 屋台ラーメン 京都中華そば 徳光 (Yatai Ramen Kyoto Chuka Soba Tokumitsu)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free. This is a classic Kyoto-style chuka soba: wheat noodles in a pork-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth with back fat, and the shoyu tare almost certainly contains wheat. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation is indicated, and as a single-operator truck-bed yatai there is no separate prep space. Avoid if you require gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. All bowls are built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy broth with back fat and are topped with chashu (pork). No vegetable or vegan broth option is offered. There is nothing on the menu suitable for a strict vegan or vegetarian diner.
places.hours_raw: ~19:00 to ~25:00 (1:00 the next day). Irregular days off. As a street stall, its operating days and location vary; the latest info is announced on Instagram.
places.neighborhood: Ukyo Ward / Saiin / around Saiin Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A rare survivor of an almost-vanished Kyoto tradition: the late-night ramen yatai. Tokumitsu launched on 28 November 2023, run by a young owner out of the cargo bed of a converted kei-truck, after test runs around Shichijo-Karasuma and in front of the Join Building in Saiin. Rath

chef_bio.background: A rare survivor of an almost-vanished Kyoto tradition: the late-night ramen yatai. Tokumitsu launched on 28 November 2023, run by a young owner out of the cargo bed of a converted kei-truck. Rather than a fixed shop, it rolls out after dark and parks at spots around the Saiin crossing, announcing each night's location on social media.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「店舗さながらの味を屋台で」 — to deliver shop-quality ramen from a food cart. The owner leans into the romance of the form: an unmarked truck under a red lantern, a handful of spots, and a warm bowl eaten outdoors on a cold night.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 背脂チャッチャ (seabura chaccha) chuka soba — old-school Kyoto-style ramen: a pork-bone soy-sauce broth crowned with a generous shower of back fat, topped with chashu, with the salty-sharp snap of shoyu over slightly soft, chewy noodles (~780 yen). Alongside it are a lighter 昔ながらの京だし (Kyo-dashi) chuka soba and a punchy 旨辛ニンニク辛麺 (spicy garlic) bowl (~800 yen).

anecdotes.text: Because the cart roves and keeps irregular hours, regulars treat finding it as a small stroke of luck — 神出鬼没 (appearing and vanishing) is part of its charm; you track it down via its Instagram (@yatairamen_tokumitsu).
The whole appeal is a hot bowl eaten outdoors under a cold sky; one diner wrote that despite the bitter cold the warm ramen was especially comforting and delicious (寒い中体もあったまって美味しかった).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyo-soku and kyotogourmet confirm the Nov 28 2023 launch, the converted kei-truck with a young owner, the roving 神出鬼没 operation around Saiin with locations posted on Instagram, and the three bowls (背脂チャッチャ ~¥780, 昔ながらの京だし, 旨辛ニンニク辛麺 ~¥800). The exact wording of an owner quote about cold-weather ramen could not be verified, so a source-traceable diner sentiment is used instead.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-operator truck-bed yatai serving wheat-noodle ramen in one shared pot/workspace; no separate gluten-free prep is possible and none is advertised.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is a pork-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) base — 「豚骨醤油のスープにたっぷりの背脂」 — and the shoyu tare typically contains wheat. Not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is pork-bone broth with back fat and chashu; there is no vegan/vegetarian option and all prep shares the pork-based broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A late-night Japanese street-cart ramen run with a young local owner and only a few spots at the truck — basic Japanese and an easygoing, go-with-the-flow attitude (it moves and keeps irregular hours) will serve you best; little to no English expected.

places.name: 麺匠 一粒万倍 河原町店 (Menshou Ichiryu Manbai Kawaramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat: ramen noodles, tsukemen noodles, and mazesoba noodles are all wheat-based (the noodles even use whole-grain wheat flour for flavor), and the shoyu tare is wheat-based soy sauce. Broths are chicken and duck stock. There is no published gluten-free menu, no rice-noodle option, and no indication of allergen accommodation. A celiac diner should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. Every signature item is animal-based: the broth is chicken-and-duck (鶏と鴨) clear soup, toppings include chicken and pork chashu, and the mazesoba uses chicken oil and minced chashu. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu and no plant-based broth alternative.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-3:00 the next day (L.O. 26:30), continuous service; reported to be open daily
places.neighborhood: Kawaramachi, Nakagyo Ward (Sanjo / near Kiyamachi and Pontocho, within walking distance of Hankyu Kyoto-Kawaramachi Stn and Keihan Sanjo Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A late-night ramen specialist in central Kyoto's Kawaramachi district, tucked into the World Building on Daikoku-cho near the Kiyamachi and Pontocho nightlife streets. The shop is part of the small Ichiryu Manbai noodle group, which also runs a Teramachi branch nearby.

chef_bio.background: A late-night ramen specialist in central Kyoto's Kawaramachi district, tucked into the World Building on Daikoku-cho near the Kiyamachi and Pontocho nightlife streets. The shop is part of the small Ichiryu Manbai noodle group, which also runs a Teramachi branch nearby.
chef_bio.philosophy: Care put into balance and texture: thin curly noodles cut with whole-grain wheat flour to carry more aroma, paired with a clean but assertive shoyu broth and carefully prepared toppings (chicken and pork chashu, bamboo shoots, soft egg, mitsuba/parsley).
chef_bio.specialty: A clear (清湯) double broth built on chicken and duck, finished with a crisp, sharp soy-sauce tare for the signature shoyu ramen (らぁ麺). The kitchen also turns out a tsukemen and a mazesoba that mixes thick flat noodles with chicken-oil sauce, minced chashu, red onion, and a truffle-oil-dressed egg yolk with lemon to shift the flavor partway through.

anecdotes.text: The mazesoba is described by a noodle blogger as having a sticky, clinging, oil-forward texture close to an abura-soba, brightened at the table with truffle-oil egg yolk and a squeeze of lemon.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: noodlers confirms the Kawaramachi/Nakagyo location and Teramachi branch, the clean duck-and-chicken broth らぁ麺 (¥900), thin whole-grain-wheat noodles, chashu (chicken and pork)/bamboo/mitsuba/red-onion toppings, and the oil-forward mazesoba (¥880) with truffle-oil egg yolk and lemon.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen/mazesoba noodles (including whole-grain wheat flour) are boiled in shared water; not safe for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tare flavoring the broth is wheat-based soy sauce; allergen note lists wheat in both noodles and soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-and-duck stock and toppings include chicken and pork chashu and egg; no plant-based preparation, so all items carry animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, ticket-machine ramen counter open late into the night; a little Japanese helps with the vending-machine ordering, but pointing and basic greetings will get you through.

places.name: つけめんTETSU イオンモールKYOTO (Tsukemen TETSU AEON Mall KYOTO)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowl is extra-thick wheat noodles (blended with udon flour) dipped in a rich pork-bone-and-seafood broth, with a wheat-based shoyu/miso tare. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated prep, and as a busy mall food-court stall there is no allowance for managing cross-contamination. Avoid if you have celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core product is built on a pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) broth, topped with chashu pork and finished with animal-derived tare; menma and noodles are cooked in shared equipment. There is no vegan or vegetarian option advertised.
places.notes: TETSU began in 2005 as a tiny 10-tsubo (about 33 sqm) shop in Sendagi, Tokyo, founded by Kazunori Komiya. Starting with just 3 million yen in capital, he grew it into a tsukemen brand of roughly 30 shops centered on Tokyo. This AEON Mall KYOTO branch opened on October 17, 2023, m

chef_bio.origin: Sendagi, Tokyo (founded 2005); this branch is a Kyoto outpost of the national TETSU chain.
chef_bio.background: TETSU began in 2005 as a tiny 10-tsubo (about 33 sqm) shop in Sendagi, Tokyo, founded by Kazunori Komiya. Starting with just 3 million yen in capital, he grew it into a tsukemen brand of roughly 30 shops centered on Tokyo. This AEON Mall KYOTO branch opened on October 17, 2023, marking the brand's return to Kyoto more than a decade after its earlier Kyoto Ramen Koji shop inside the Kyoto Station Building closed in 2011.
chef_bio.philosophy: An uncompromising obsession with keeping the broth hot to the very last bite — the brand's trademark hot-stone (焼き石) service, in which a scorching stone is dropped into the dipping broth to keep it piping hot. Note that this hot-stone service is a signature of TETSU's standalone shops and is not offered at this AEON Mall food-court outlet.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a rich pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) tsukemen — a triple soup of chicken bones, pork bones and bonito/mackerel — served with extra-thick straight noodles that stand up to the heavy dipping broth. Miso and spicy-miso dipping versions are also popular here, and the kitchen rounds out the menu with a chashu rice bowl and a chuka-soba.

anecdotes.text: TETSU's trademark move solves tsukemen's classic weak point of broth going lukewarm: a stone heated until scorching is dropped into the dipping broth partway through the meal so it stays piping hot until the final drop — though this hot-stone service is not available at the AEON Mall food-court branch.
Founder Kazunori Komiya turned a 3-million-yen, 10-tsubo Sendagi shop opened in 2005 into a roughly 30-location tsukemen chain.
The Kyoto branch opened in the AEON Mall KYOTO food court on October 17, 2023, returning the brand to Kyoto after its station-building shop had closed in 2011.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: pasonacareer confirms founder 小宮一哲 (b.1976), the 2005 Sendagi start with ¥3M capital and growth to ~30 shops; 1goten confirms the 焼き石 hot-stone concept and 極太ストレート麺 (no udon-flour claim); atpress confirms the Oct 17 2023 AEON Mall KYOTO opening, the triple pork-chicken-seafood broth, miso/spicy-miso versions, the 2011 closure of the Kyoto Station Building shop, and that the food-court outlet does NOT offer the hot stone.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles boiled in shared water in a single open food-court kitchen; no gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsukemen broth/tare uses wheat-based shoyu and miso; the noodles themselves are wheat (blended with udon flour).

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on pork-bone-and-seafood broth with pork chashu; no separate vegan prep area.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual mall food-court stall where you order at the counter and carry your tray, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu is all you need.

places.name: イワサキタンメン (Iwasaki Tanmen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Tanmen is built on wheat ramen noodles in a salt (shio) tare; the broth and house salt sauce are not documented as wheat-free, and there are no gluten-free noodle or accommodation options on the menu. No allergen handling is documented. Anyone with celiac disease or wheat allergy should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The signature tanmen is a chicken-bone and pork-based salt broth topped with stir-fried pork and vegetables; pork and a chicken/pork stock are integral to every bowl. No plant-based broth or vegetable-only version is documented. A diner could potentially ask about a vegetable-heavy customization, but the base soup is animal-derived.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:30, Dinner 18:00-22:00 (per Tabelog). One blog lists dinner as 18:00-21:00; treat evening close time as approximate. No regular closed day listed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Iwasaki Tanmen opened its doors on March 1, 2023 in the Ono neighborhood of Yamashina, just steps from Ono Station, taking over a storefront that previously housed another ramen shop (Shioemon). It is a rarity in Kyoto: a shop devoted to tanmen, a vegetable-piled salt ramen far m

chef_bio.background: Iwasaki Tanmen opened its doors on March 1, 2023 in the Ono neighborhood of Yamashina, just steps from Ono Station, taking over a storefront that previously housed another ramen shop (Shioemon). It is a rarity in Kyoto: a shop devoted to tanmen, a vegetable-piled salt ramen far more common in the Kanto region of eastern Japan than here in Kansai.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make-it-your-own simplicity: diners can dial the vegetables, pork, noodle quantity, garlic and spice to taste, and garlic is served on the side so each guest controls their own portion. Sides like gyoza, karaage and takana rice round out an honest, affordable bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: The house tanmen is the signature: a mound of lightly stir-fried vegetables — cabbage, bean sprouts and more — over medium straight noodles in a chicken-and-pork salt (shio) broth. It arrives looking hearty and Jiro-esque, yet the soup is surprisingly clean and light, with no heavy back-fat. A pot of house chili-miso (kara-miso) sits at hand so you can stir in heat and depth gradually as you eat.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers several tanmen styles beyond the original — a shio-tonkotsu, a rich miso and a spicy version — so regulars can rotate through the lineup.
One local blogger called the proprietor very kind and admitted he came back years after his first visit because he simply could not forget the tanmen.
The bowl starts around 690 yen, keeping it genuinely budget-friendly.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyotopi confirms the March 2023 opening in Yamashina-Ono near Ono Station in the former Shioemon space, the Kanto-style tanmen (rare in Kyoto) on a light chicken-pork shio broth, four tanmen varieties (classic/shio-tonkotsu/miso/spicy), customizable veg/pork/garlic-on-the-side/spice, kara-miso, gyoza/karaage/takana-rice sides, ~¥690, and 16 seats.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish uses wheat ramen noodles in a shared salt broth; there is no published gluten-free option and the kitchen is a small single-line operation, so cross-contact with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's tare is described as a house-made salt (shio) sauce rather than a soy-based one; however, its full composition is not published, so wheat content cannot be confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetables are stir-fried together with pork, and the broth is chicken/pork based, so vegetable toppings share animal ingredients and equipment.

positives.text: Diners can customize vegetables, pork, noodle amount, garlic and spice level, with garlic served separately for portion control — useful for tailoring a bowl, though not an allergen accommodation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly neighborhood counter-and-table shop where Japanese phrases for ordering and customizing your bowl will go a long way; English support is not documented.

places.name: だし料理 十 (Dashi Ryori Jifu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature bowl centers on house-made noodles cut from domestic wheat and egg, so the core dish contains gluten. The broth itself is a clean fish dashi (Rishiri kombu, sawara/sagoshi, bonito) seasoned with salt only and no soy sauce, which removes one common wheat source — but the wheat noodle makes this unsafe for celiacs. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is advertised; a celiac diner would need to ask directly and likely cannot be accommodated.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The dashi is entirely animal-based (sawara/Spanish mackerel, bonito flakes, plus kombu), and the noodles contain egg. There is no plant-based bowl on offer.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Thu, Sun 09:00–15:00; Fri–Sat 11:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Mon–Tue closed. From around 16:00 the space shifts to a dashi retail shop / sake bar on the late-evening days.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is the latest evolution of a Kyoto noodle maker who has spent years chasing dashi rather than richness. The lineage runs from a ramen shop, Isao, that built its bowls on Japanese dashi, which the owner then renamed and reframed around 2014 as Odashi to Komugi 130 (おだしと小麦

chef_bio.origin: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto — on the city's famous Ichijoji ramen street, but a deliberate step away from the heavy-broth pack.
chef_bio.background: The shop is the latest evolution of a Kyoto noodle maker who has spent years chasing dashi rather than richness. The lineage runs from a dashi-based ramen shop, Isao (イサオ, opened 2010), which the owner renamed and reframed in 2014 as Odashi to Komugi 130 (おだしと小麦 一三〇) to put the dashi and the wheat front and center. In June 2022 the concept was distilled again into だし料理 十.
chef_bio.philosophy: The whole place is built so customers can actually understand dashi — you can watch it being made, taste it, and talk it through with the owner. Seasoning is pared back to almost nothing so the stock itself carries the bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the breakfast-only o-dashi no new-men: a clear soup built from just three things — Rishiri kombu, sawara (young Spanish mackerel / sagoshi), and bonito flakes — seasoned with a single gram of Iwate salt and no soy sauce at all. The noodles are made in-house from domestic wheat and egg, cut to roughly 1mm square and dusted with Hokkaido corn flour, then finished with hand-shaved bonito and a few pieces of salt-cured pepper.

anecdotes.text: By late afternoon the shop sheds its noodle-counter identity and becomes a dashi retail shop and small sake bar, selling drinkable dashi (nomu o-dashi) and easy dashi kits so people can take the craft home.
Tasters describe being unexpectedly moved by the bowl — 出汁のふくよかな香りとまろやかな口当たり (the rich aroma and rounded mouthfeel of the dashi genuinely move you).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: life-info and Tabelog (former name 'Odashi&Wheat isao') confirm owner Inoue, the lineage Isao (2010) -> おだしと小麦 一三〇 (2014) -> だし料理 十 (June 2022), the breakfast-only o-dashi new-men (Rishiri kombu, sawara, bonito, 1g Iwate salt, no soy; ~1mm domestic wheat-egg noodles with Hokkaido corn flour; hand-shaved bonito and salt-cured pepper), and the afternoon dashi-retail/sake-bar with nomu o-dashi and dashi kits.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The house-made noodles are cut from domestic wheat (and egg) in-house, so flour is handled on-site; a shared-kitchen wheat environment should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dashi bowl uses no soy sauce at all — it is seasoned with about 1 gram of Iwate salt only — so the usual wheat-shoyu tare risk is absent from the broth (the wheat comes from the noodles instead).

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is animal-based throughout (sawara/Spanish mackerel and bonito alongside kombu), and the noodles contain egg, so there is no clean vegan or vegetarian path.

positives.text: Because the owner makes and explains the dashi in front of guests, it is easy to ask exactly what is in the bowl, and the seasoning is unusually transparent: kombu, sawara, bonito, salt, and house wheat-and-egg noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run dashi specialist where the proprietor talks you through the craft; some Japanese (or a translation app) goes a long way, and a quiet, curious attitude fits the room.

places.name: 京都北山 元町ラーメン 四条烏丸本店 (Kyoto Kitayama Motomachi Ramen Shijo-Karasum)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. The ramen uses wheat noodles, a wheat-based tamari/black soy sauce tare (sourced from a long-established Kyoto soy sauce maker), and pork/beef broth. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no indication staff accommodate gluten allergies.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. The broth is a rich black soy sauce ramen built on pork/beef bases (beef tendon is a signature), the noodles are wheat, and the tare uses wheat-containing tamari soy sauce. Nothing on the menu is plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-24:30 (L.O. 24:00), closed Sun. Closes once soup runs out.
places.neighborhood: Bandoyacho, Nakagyo Ward (~5 min walk from Exit 18 of Hankyu Karasuma Stn, down an alley across from the Higashinotoin exit of Daimaru Kyoto)
places.cuisine: Rich black soy sauce; back-fat ramen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kyoto Kitayama Motomachi Ramen traces its roots to a street cart (yatai) that the first-generation owner — a native of Kanazawa — set up on Shinmachi-dori in Kyoto's Kitayama district roughly 50 years ago. Drawing on the rich, dark soy-sauce style of nearby Toyama Black, he built

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (Kitayama district yatai origins); founder from Kanazawa
chef_bio.background: Kyoto Kitayama Motomachi Ramen traces its roots to a street cart (yatai) that the first-generation owner — a native of Kanazawa — set up on Shinmachi-dori in Kyoto's Kitayama district roughly 50 years ago. Drawing on the rich, dark soy-sauce style of nearby Toyama Black, he built a Kyoto take on the bowl using tamari soy sauce from a long-established local soy-sauce maker. The business later moved into a permanent shop, settling at its current location in an alley off Higashinotoin in central Kyoto, where the cart's original flavor is carried on by a later (third) generation.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honor the original yatai flavor while continuing to refine it; serve until the broth is gone and then close for the day.
chef_bio.specialty: A rich black-soy-sauce ramen finished with plenty of back fat (背脂醤油らーめん), with beef-tendon ramen (牛すじらーめん) as a second signature. The defining element is a specialty tamari soy sauce of the kind used for eel sauce, sourced from a 160-year-old Kyoto soy-sauce maker.

anecdotes.text: The founder, from Kanazawa, based his recipe on the dark, bold style of Toyama Black but reinterpreted it for Kyoto using local tamari soy sauce.
The shop began as a Kitayama Shinmachi street cart about 50 years ago, and a later (third) generation continues the cart's original taste at the current shop.
The tamari soy sauce is supplied by a 160-year-old established Kyoto soy-sauce shop (Sawai) and is of the type used for eel sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms the 創業50年 Kitayama Shinmachi yatai origin, a Kanazawa-born first-generation owner inspired by 富山ブラック, the specialty たまり醤油 (eel-sauce type) from a 160-year-old Kyoto maker (Sawai), the 背脂醤油らーめん (¥900) and 牛すじらーめん (¥1,100), the Higashinotoin 京町家 location, and continuation by a third generation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is built on a tamari/black soy sauce tare; tamari and standard soy sauce here are wheat-containing and central to the broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are pork/beef based (beef tendon is a signature), so no item is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, counter-style local ramen shop with no English service signaled; basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother.

places.name: Au petit Foreau pâtissière (オ・プチフォロ・パティシエール)
places.gf_detail: The shop's own materials state it relaunched in September 2021 (令和3年9月) as a plant-based & gluten-free specialty patisserie where every item is made without wheat, white sugar, or animal products (全商品グルテンフリー＆プラントベース). All cakes, tarts and baked goods are built on rice flour (米粉) instead of wheat, so no wheat flour is handled on the premises. It is not a daily shop — the Nijo location opens only about 2–3 irregular days per month, so a date-check and pre-order are essential.
places.vegan_detail: Every product is vegan: no eggs, dairy, butter or any animal-derived ingredient (動物由来のものすべて不使用). The signature 0% cheesecake replaces dairy with soy-milk yogurt, and the shop describes its mission as making 'cute and delicious vegan sweets that nourish the heart' (心が満たされるかわいらしく美味しいヴィーガンスイーツ). Sweeteners are unrefined (beet sugar), not white sugar.
places.hours_raw: Opens only ~2–3 irregular days per month (reservation/pre-order only); on open days approx. 11:00–18:00. Monthly 'Open DAY' dates are announced on Instagram @petitforeau0903 and the online shop (petitforeau.stores.jp). Not a walk-in daily shop.
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] A dedicated wheat-free, plant-based (vegan) patisserie: every product is gluten-free rice-flour based, so cross-contact from wheat is structurally minimized. CRITICAL for planning: this is NOT a daily storefront — it opens only about 3 days per month on irregular dates and works essentially on a pre-order/pickup basis. Check the current Open DAY schedule on Instagram (@petitforeau0903) or the online shop first, and reserve at least one week ahead (Instagram DM, LINE, or petitforeau@icloud.com); payment is on the pickup day. No eat-in — takeout only.

chef_bio.origin: 京都府南丹市 (Nantan City, Kyoto Prefecture)
chef_bio.background: Mika Nakatani is a French-trained pastry chef from Nantan City in Kyoto Prefecture. Japanese sources report she trained in France and, back in Japan, worked as a pâtissière — including a stint at the celebrated Kyoto restaurant Gion Sasaki (祇園さゝ木) and at patisseries and hotels in Kyoto and Osaka — before going independent around 2016. She converted the shop in September 2021 into a dedicated vegan and gluten-free patisserie, rebuilding her entire repertoire around organic, plant-based, rice-flour ingredients. Her signature is the '0%チーズケーキ' — a cheesecake with gluten, white sugar and animal ingredients all at 0%, built on soy-milk yogurt and cashews.
chef_bio.philosophy: Aims to create 'cute and delicious vegan sweets that nourish the heart' (心が満たされるかわいらしく美味しいヴィーガンスイーツを目指します); shifted to plant-based & gluten-free for health and environmental reasons, using organic ingredients and unrefined (beet) sugar.
chef_bio.specialty: 0%チーズケーキ (0% cheesecake) — gluten/white-sugar/animal all 0%, soy-yogurt & cashew based; plus seasonal rice-flour tarts, shortcakes and Mont Blanc.

anecdotes.text: The 0% cheesecake gets its cheese-like richness and refreshing tang from soy-milk yogurt and lemon juice rather than dairy (しっとり上品な口あたり。チーズのコクに豆乳ヨーグルトやレモン果汁が、さわやかな酸味をプラス).
Beyond the Nijo pop-up days, she supplies her plant-based/GF sweets to mumokuteki cafe and to 素食カフェRen (Cafe Ren), which sells the 0% cheesecake by pre-order.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kyoto-ethical-market confirms she is a France-trained pâtissière from Nantan who worked at Kyoto/Osaka hotels before going independent ~2016 and relaunched the shop in Sept 2021 as a fully gluten-free, white-sugar-free, plant-based rice-flour patisserie with the flagship 0% cheesecake; japanese-heart independently confirms a prior pâtissière role at 祇園ささ木 (Gion Sasaki) alongside her France training. Specific French patisserie names given by some profiles could not be verified and were dropped.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entire shop is wheat-free: all products are gluten-free and rice-flour (米粉) based, with no wheat flour used on the premises (全商品グルテンフリー＆プラントベース). This structurally minimizes wheat cross-contact, but no explicit celiac-grade cross-contamination protocol is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Patisserie only — no savory soy-sauce dishes. The 0% cheesecake uses white miso (白味噌) and kuzu; miso can be a hidden wheat/barley source, so confirm the miso is GF if highly sensitive.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No animal products are used anywhere in the shop (fully plant-based), so animal cross-contact is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owner-run allergy-focused patisserie; individual products are also flagged for specific allergies (e.g. ヴィーガン・小麦フリー, 乳・小麦フリー) on the owner's blog, indicating allergy awareness. Orders are handled directly by the chef via pre-order.

positives.text: Dedicated gluten-free + plant-based facility using rice flour, organic ingredients and no white sugar; signature 0% cheesecake is gluten/white-sugar/animal 0%.
Trusted enough by the vegan/GF community that mumokuteki cafe and Cafe Ren resell her sweets.

places.name: Hirose Okomeya Cafe (廣瀬米穀店 お米屋カフェ)
places.gf_detail: A 99-year-old Kyoto rice merchant (廣瀬米穀店, est. 1926) that sells no wheat products, with an attached café whose owner is a certified 米粉マイスター (rice-flour master) and 五ツ星お米マイスター. Everything is made with 100% rice flour and no wheat flour or white sugar: 米粉シフォンケーキ (rice-flour chiffon) is sold Tuesday–Saturday and お米パン / こめやパン (rice bread) is baked and sold on Wednesdays. Because it is a food shop and rice mill rather than a certified dedicated gluten-free kitchen, celiac guests should still confirm on the day that no wheat is handled or stored on-site. In practice the entire product line is rice-based, so cross-contact risk is low.
places.vegan_detail: The お米パン / こめやパン rice bread is fully plant-based — made only from rice, rice oil, maple, salt, rice starter (米種), yeast and water — so it is suitable for vegans (sold Wednesdays / by pre-order). The signature 米粉シフォンケーキ and its rusk contain egg and are not vegan. Small baked snacks (scones, snowballs) may contain dairy, so ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00. Closed Sun, Mon and holidays (rice-flour chiffon cake Tue-Sat, rice bread on Wed).
places.neighborhood: 南区東九条 / Higashi-Kujo (near Kyoto Station, south)
places.notes: [GF] A genuinely useful stop for celiacs just south of Kyoto Station: a century-old rice merchant with an attached café where everything is 100% rice flour and the shop sells no wheat products at all. Grab a 米粉シフォンケーキ (rice-flour chiffon, Tue–Sat) or, on Wednesdays, freshly baked お米パン rice bread. Confirm the day's baking schedule (chiffon Tue–Sat, bread Wed) and that lunch/café-gohan service is running, as it has been paused at times. Small neighborhood shop — assume cash.

chef_bio.chef_name: 廣瀬弘香 (Hirose Hiroka) — 米粉マイスター / 五ツ星お米マイスター
roles: 五ツ星お米マイスター Prof (five-star rice master, professional grade)
米粉パンマイスター (rice-flour bread master)
chef_bio.origin: Originally from Okayama; studied fine art at a design university, then married into the third-generation Hirose rice-merchant family in Higashi-Kujo, Minami-ku, Kyoto.
chef_bio.background: 廣瀬米穀店 was founded in 昭和元年 (1926) and is now in its third generation, giving it roughly 99 years of history as a Kyoto rice merchant. Around 2014, after the shop stopped wholesaling to a large facility, an empty space opened up in the store; the owner's encounter with rice flour and the question 「米屋にしかできないことってなんだろう？」 (what can only a rice shop do?) led to the 2017 opening of the attached café, お米屋カフェ・ふぇ〜る. 廣瀬弘香 — originally from Okayama and trained in fine art before marrying into the family — holds the 五ツ星お米マイスター qualification (with the professional 'Prof' instructor grade) plus 米粉マイスター and 米粉パンマイスター certifications, and the shop is a 「五ツ星お米マイスター認定店」. The rice used is milled in-house (自家精米) from selected varieties, and all café products are made with 100% rice flour and no wheat flour or white sugar.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner wants to spread rice bread 「グルテンフリーのパンとしてではなく、生米から作る一つのおいしい食べ物として」 — not merely as gluten-free bread, but as a delicious food in its own right made from raw rice. She distinguishes 米粉パン (from rice grown for milling into flour) from her お米パン made from top-grade (一等上質米) whole rice, emphasizing the aroma and natural taste of freshly milled rice.
chef_bio.specialty: 米屋の米粉シフォンケーキ, お米パン（こめやパン）, 米粉ラスク

anecdotes.text: 2014年ごろ大型施設への卸販売を中止して店内に空きスペースが生じ、米粉との出会いから『米屋にしかできないことってなんだろう？』という問いが2017年の『お米屋カフェ・ふぇ〜る』開業につながった。
2023年に京都女性起業家賞・優秀賞、京都商工会議所主催の知恵-1グランプリ チャレンジ部門グランプリを受賞。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: アサヒパック広報のnote記事と公式サイト・米粉マイスター協会の紹介により、廣瀬米穀店は昭和元年（1926年）創業の3代目の米屋で、店主・廣瀬弘香（岡山出身・美術系大学卒で家業に嫁いだ）が五ツ星お米マイスター（Prof）等の資格を持ち、2017年に併設カフェ『お米屋カフェ・ふぇ〜る』を開業、全商品が米粉100％で小麦粉・白砂糖不使用。2023年に京都女性起業家賞優秀賞・知恵-1グランプリを受賞。お米パンは米粉パンと区別される。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 全商品を米粉100％で製造し、小麦粉・白砂糖は一切使用していない（Made with 100% rice flour; no wheat flour or white sugar used at all).

It is a rice merchant / rice mill and food shop (自家精米), not a certified dedicated gluten-free kitchen — the product line is entirely rice-based and the shop sells no wheat products, but celiacs should confirm on the day that no wheat is handled or stored on-site.

vegan_cross_contact.text: お米パン／こめやパンは米・米油・メープル・塩・米種・酵母・お湯のみで焼く植物性の食品（rice bread is plant-based). シフォンケーキには卵が含まれる（chiffon contains egg).

staff_allergy_handling.text: 店主は米粉マイスター・五ツ星お米マイスターの有資格者で、グルテンフリーを明確に掲げている（Owner is a certified rice-flour master and explicitly markets the shop as gluten-free).

positives.text: 米屋なので小麦製品を扱わず、カフェのお菓子・パンはすべて米粉100％（A rice shop that handles no wheat products; all café sweets and bread are 100% rice flour).
自家精米したての新鮮な米から作るグルテンフリーのお米パンとシフォンケーキ（Gluten-free rice bread and chiffon made from freshly in-house-milled rice).

places.name: Ikariya Beika Kyoto (イカリヤベイカ京都)
places.gf_detail: A rice-flour baumkuchen specialist that sources raw rice from farmers (non-glutinous rice from Shiga Prefecture, etc.), mills it in-house at the workshop, and bakes baumkuchen using only freshly ground, fresh rice flour. The brand does not push a gluten-free label to the fore ('rather than proclaiming gluten-free'), placing the taste and texture of freshly ground rice flour front and center. The baked-goods production line uses no wheat, but whether the drinks and accompaniments served at the small in-store eat-in area at the back are entirely free of wheat products should be confirmed at the time of visit.
places.vegan_detail: Because the baumkuchen is baked using egg, butter (dairy), and sugar, no vegan-friendly items could be confirmed. It is gluten-free thanks to rice flour, but it is not plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00, open every day (open year-round, closed only January 1)
places.neighborhood: 中京区 高倉通三条 / Karasuma-Oike (central)
places.notes: [GF] Central, tourist-friendly machiya workshop 5 min from Karasuma-Oike station; a self-milling 米粉 (rice-flour) baumkuchen specialist where the entire baked line is wheat-free — an easy, low-risk GF gift/snack stop for celiacs. Everything is built on freshly milled rice flour rather than wheat. There is a small eat-in space in back; if eating in, confirm no shared wheat items accompany drinks. Cards accepted; reservations possible for pickups.

chef_bio.background: 運営は京都・大阪でフレンチ『イカリヤ食堂』やビストロ『ikariya523』、ウェディング事業などを手がける株式会社Ludico（代表：鳥井和機）で、IKARIYA BEIKA KYOTOはその食ブランド群のひとつ。ブランド名の『BEIKA＝米菓』は米粉菓子を指す（実際の煎餅製造業由来ではない）。物語は『ひとりの菓子職人が抱いた好奇心』から始まり、築100年超の京町家を改装して工房兼店舗を構えた。農家から生米を仕入れ、店内で挽きたてに自家製粉した米粉でバウムクーヘンを焼く。挽きたて米粉は含む水分量が違い、しっとりした質感が最大のこだわり。2023年3月29日にはJR京都伊勢丹で期間限定出店し、米粉のグルテンフリーバウムクーヘンを販売した。
chef_bio.philosophy: グルテンフリーというラベルより、挽きたて自家製粉の米粉ならではの味・しっとりした食感を主役にする（味先行、GFは結果）。
chef_bio.specialty: 自家製粉 rice-flour baumkuchen — 定番6種（フワフワ/ザクザク/白あんマロン/ショコラ/ほうじ茶ワヅカ茶 ほか）+ 季節限定（レモン・焼アンノウイモ・七福バウム等）

anecdotes.text: 運が良ければ京町家の外から職人がバウムを焼く工房の様子を眺められる。
毎週日曜14時から店頭に並ぶ『朝焼きバウム』（1カット220円）は実店舗のみの限定商品で、定番よりさらにふんわりした食感が味わえる。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト・食べログ・アットプレス各リリース・KYOTOLIFE・life-info・まげわっぱブログ、および株式会社Ludicoのリリース群を総合すると、母体はイカリヤ食堂やikariya523等を運営する株式会社Ludico（代表 鳥井和機）。滋賀県産うるち米などを生米で仕入れ工房内で挽きたてに自家製粉し、グルテンフリーの米粉バウムクーヘンを焼く専門店。2023年3月29日のJR京都伊勢丹期間限定出店・オンライン販売あり。煎餅製造業由来という説は日本語一次情報では裏付けられなかった。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 焼き菓子の製造ラインは自家製粉の米粉のみで小麦粉を使用しない専門工房のため、製品自体の小麦クロスコンタミ・リスクは低い。
個人ブログでは『メニューはすべて米粉を使ったグルテンフリー』と報告されているが、店奥のイートインで供する飲み物・付け合わせに小麦品がないかは公式には未確認。

vegan_cross_contact.text: バウムクーヘンは卵・乳(バター)を含むため、ヴィーガン品との交差接触管理に関する情報はなく、そもそもヴィーガン提供品が確認できない。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 米菓職人が自家製粉した米粉で丁寧に焼き上げると明記されるが、個別の小麦アレルギー対応プロトコルの記載は見当たらない。

positives.text: 専門店として全焼き菓子が米粉ベースの小麦不使用で、グルテンフリーの土産・スイーツとして京都のGFガイドでも紹介されている。
生米を工房内で自家製粉するなど原材料管理にこだわり、JR京都伊勢丹でもグルテンフリーバウムとして販売実績がある。

places.name: Mushiyashinai (むしやしない)
places.gf_detail: The operator states 「2023年10月には完全グルテンフリー化の為、生産工場と店舗をリニューアルオープンしました」 (In October 2023 they reopened both the production factory and the shop after renovating them for complete gluten-free conversion), and describes the venue as 全商品グルテンフリー (every product gluten-free). Allergen scope is unusually deep: a dedicated 「ミラクル＊ヴァンヌフ 29」 line is free of 29 designated allergens (29品目アレルゲン物質除去), building on the wheat/dairy/egg-free (小麦・乳・卵不使用) base of all cakes. Principal caveat for celiacs: the kitchen is soy-centric (豆乳/おから), and the signature 豆乳醤油プリン uses 醤油 — confirm they use a gluten-free tamari, though a fully-GF-converted facility would be expected to.
places.vegan_detail: The house concept is explicitly 「グルテンフリー＆ヴィーガン」. Because the cakes are built on 米粉 (rice flour) and 豆乳 (soy milk) instead of wheat, dairy and egg, a large share of the sweets — puddings, roll cake, brûlée, okara cookies, the 29-allergen ミラクル cakes — are plant-based. Not everything is guaranteed vegan (seasonal afternoon-tea components and some savory items are unconfirmed), so vegan diners should ask per item.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (eat-in L.O. 17:30). Closed Mondays and Tuesdays (regardless of holidays). Some listings show a 10:30 open; as of 2026 in-store offering leans toward pre-ordered whole cakes and baked-good assortments.
places.cuisine: Gluten-free soy-milk patisserie - cakes, baked sweets, puddings, light savory items
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] Kyoto's rare fully-gluten-free patisserie: in October 2023 the owner rebuilt the production factory and the Ichijoji shop specifically to eliminate gluten, and now markets every product as gluten-free. Cakes use rice flour and soy milk; a dedicated line removes 29 allergens. Celiac risk from cross-contact is minimal by their own claim, but the kitchen leans heavily on soy — one of the 29 they otherwise track — and the soy-sauce pudding should be confirmed as GF tamari. Reservations/pre-orders drive whole cakes; eat-in seats are not reservable.

chef_bio.chef_name: うのゆきこ (Uno Yukiko) — 豆乳パティシエ / soy-milk patissier
chef_bio.background: Uno Yukiko styles herself as 「世界で唯一の豆乳パティシエ」 (the world's only soy-milk patissier) and opened Mushiyashinai in Ichijoji around 2006. In December 2009 an encounter with a boy who had 23 food allergies (23品目の食物アレルギー) pushed her to develop allergy-friendly cakes made from 米粉 and 豆乳 rather than wheat and dairy, and by 2020 she completed a cake free of 29 allergens (29品目のアレルゲン不使用). In October 2023 she renovated both the production factory and the shop expressly to convert the whole operation to gluten-free. This Ichijoji patisserie is a distinct venue from the operator's soy-milk ramen line (branded 'UNO RAMEN'); the sweets shop, not the ramen, is the celiac-relevant location.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「すべての人を笑顔にするために」— pursuing 食のバリアフリー (barrier-free food) across social, health and religious dietary needs; plant-based, gluten-free, allergen-conscious baking.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-milk & rice-flour cakes, 焼き菓子, 豆乳プリン, ブリュレ, おから (soy-pulp) sweets and light savory items

anecdotes.text: The allergen mission traces to a December 2009 meeting with a boy carrying 23 food allergies, which led to the 29-allergen-free 「ミラクル＊ヴァンヌフ 29」 cake by 2020.
In October 2023 the factory and shop were renovated and reopened expressly to convert the whole operation to gluten-free.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 648471.com（公式）と ことりっぷ（co-trip 701134）、ふるさとチョイス／ふるなび、食べログ が一致して、2006年開業のうのゆきこ主宰の豆乳パティスリーであること、2009年12月の23品目アレルギーの男の子との出会い、2020年の29品目アレルゲン不使用ケーキ完成、2023年10月に完全グルテンフリー化のため生産工場と店舗をリニューアルオープンしたことを裏付ける。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Own claim of 完全グルテンフリー化: the production factory and shop were rebuilt in Oct 2023 to be entirely gluten-free, and all products are marketed as 全商品グルテンフリー — implying no wheat is handled on-site, so cross-contact risk is minimal.
Corroborated: 豆乳などを活用することで全商品グルテンフリーの身体に優しいパティスリー.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature 「SOY豆乳醤油プリン」 is served with a 醤油 (soy-sauce) caramel; ordinary soy sauce contains wheat. Given the facility's stated complete gluten-free conversion they would be expected to use a wheat-free tamari, but this is not explicitly documented — confirm directly for celiac safety.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen uses no dairy in cakes (豆乳 substitutes milk) and offers wheat/dairy/egg-free lines, reducing animal-product contact, but eat-in afternoon-tea and some savory items are not confirmed fully vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Deep allergen literacy: shop developed 29-allergen-free (29品目アレルゲン物質除去) cakes and frames itself around 食のバリアフリー for allergy sufferers since 2009.

positives.text: One of very few fully gluten-free-converted production facilities in Kyoto, purpose-rebuilt in Oct 2023.
Card / e-money / QR payments accepted; parking and eat-in café available.

places.name: Oshokuya Kappa (京都イタリアン 欧食屋Kappa)
places.gf_detail: Japanese sources resolve the conflict in favor of a full conversion: since September 2024 the kitchen uses no wheat flour at all (小麦粉を一切つかわず), replacing wheat pasta and focaccia with house-made rice-flour (米粉) pasta and bread — the whole menu is now gluten-free. The owner's own blog does NOT admit wheat is still served; the sentence about wheat pasta/focaccia is a past-tense counterfactual ('we could have kept using them with no problem, but chose to convert'). Order anything, or ask for the グルテンフリーコース (50-vegetable GF course); note the restaurant still posts a contamination disclaimer (コンタミの可能性があるので小麦アレルギーに対応しておりません), so a strict celiac should self-identify and confirm seasonings/no cross-contact.
places.vegan_label: Limited — vegetable-forward, few strictly vegan dishes; ask
places.vegan_detail: This is a vegetable-sommelier restaurant with strong produce focus, but it is emphatically NOT vegan — the menu is heavy on meat (Kameoka beef, lamb, chicken), fish/seafood and cheese, and the owner's blog worries customers wrongly assume it went vegan. Plant-forward options like the vegetable spaghetti/risotto and green salad can likely be made vegan on request (confirm no butter, cheese or dashi); arrange vegan needs in advance.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed day reported inconsistently across sources (empacede: Tuesdays + irregular; some listings show Wednesday or Sunday) - treat as irregular and confirm.
places.notes: [GF] As of Sept 2024 the entire kitchen is wheat-flour-free — a genuine 100% conversion, not shared with a wheat line; pasta is rice-flour and bread is 米粉パン. Celiacs can eat broadly here, and the 50-vegetable グルテンフリーコース is the safest showcase order. The one caveat: the restaurant posts a contamination disclaimer and says it cannot formally guarantee against a wheat allergy, so self-identify as celiac and confirm cross-contact and sauces. Not vegetarian/vegan-oriented — plenty of meat, seafood and cheese.

roles: 野菜ソムリエ / Vegetable Sommelier
chef_bio.background: Ishibashi Kenji is the owner-chef of Oshokuya Kappa, described as the only Japan Vegetable Sommelier Association (日本野菜ソムリエ協会) certified restaurant in Kyoto. In a 14 September 2024 blog post he announced the restaurant's pivot to the concept 'グルテンフリーで叶える至福のイタリアン' (blissful Italian achieved through gluten-free), switching the kitchen to use no wheat flour. He is explicit that the change was voluntary — his business was thriving on wheat pasta and focaccia — motivated by learning about gluten's effects rather than being forced by an allergy.
chef_bio.philosophy: Delicious first, gluten-free second — as a craftsman he says '美味しく無いならグルテンフリーにする必要無い' (if it isn't delicious there's no point going gluten-free); he pledges the food is as tasty as before and centers seasonal Japanese vegetables via his sommelier training.
chef_bio.specialty: GF pasta (rice-flour), 米粉パン (rice-flour bread), and a 50-vegetable グルテンフリーコース

anecdotes.text: Cites tennis player Novak Djokovic overcoming gluten intolerance via a gluten-free diet and rising to world No.1 as inspiration for the switch.
Says he isn't wheat-allergic himself (only mild skin irritation) and is running a personal experiment of quitting wheat to see what changes.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The owner's own 2024-09-14 fcblog post announces a full conversion to gluten-free Italian using no wheat flour; the homepage repeats '小麦粉を一切つかわず' and the empacede GF guide rates it ★★★★★ with 'すべてのメニューがグルテンフリー' since Sept 2024. Note: empacede also states the shop does NOT accept wheat-allergy orders due to cross-contamination risk (コンタミの可能性) — so 'gluten-free' here means recipe-level, not celiac-safe.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Restaurant states 'コンタミの可能性があるので、小麦アレルギーに対応はしておりません' — cross-contamination is possible, so they do not formally accommodate a wheat allergy, despite the kitchen using no wheat flour.
Homepage repeats they serve gluten-free dishes with no wheat flour but explicitly '小麦アレルギーに対応はしておりません' due to contamination risk (likely supplier/seasoning-level).

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant openly declines to guarantee safety for a medical wheat allergy (小麦アレルギー未対応) even while marketing itself as fully gluten-free; strict celiacs should self-identify and confirm.

positives.text: Kitchen converted entirely to gluten-free in Sept 2024, using no wheat flour; wheat pasta and focaccia were replaced with house-made rice-flour pasta and 米粉パン.
Curated Japanese GF guide rates it ★★★★★ with 'all menu items gluten-free' (すべてのメニューがグルテンフリー).
Signature 50-vegetable gluten-free course by a certified vegetable sommelier.

places.name: Senza X kyoto
places.gf_detail: Senza X describes itself as 「小麦・グルテン・卵・乳製品を使わないアレルギー対応のイタリア料理店」— a fully allergy-adapted Italian restaurant that uses no wheat, gluten, egg or dairy anywhere on the menu. Pasta is imported from Italy and made from corn and rice rather than wheat (「もちろんパスタはグルテンフリー」), and bread is baked from rice flour (「パンは米粉を使用したもの」), so the entire kitchen is free-from rather than offering modified dishes. IMPORTANT — current status: the venue is TEMPORARILY CLOSED (休業). Its Tabelog listing carries the banner 'Currently, we are temporarily closed due to staff shortages,' and its own booking system stopped accepting reservations from September 2025 onward; it has not been reported as permanently closed. Confirm reopening via the official site or Instagram before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: The whole kitchen is egg- and dairy-free, and owner-chef Shiga's stated aim was a restaurant 'enjoyable by all, vegans and allergy sufferers,' so plant-based choices exist — e.g. 植物性乳酸菌のジェラート (plant-based probiotic gelato) and fruit sorbet (いちごのソルベ). However, most pasta and antipasti feature fish or meat (anchovy, sardine, clams, beef cheek, lamb), so it is options rather than a full vegan menu; ask when booking.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:00–17:30 (L.O. 16:30); closed Mondays and the day after public holidays. NOTE: temporarily closed since ~mid-2025 due to staff shortage — check Instagram/website for reopening.
places.neighborhood: 左京区岡崎円勝寺町 / Okazaki (Heian-jingu area)
places.notes: [GF] Whole-menu free-from Italian: no wheat, gluten, egg or dairy used anywhere, corn+rice pasta and 米粉 (rice-flour) bread, so cross-contamination risk is very low and it is one of Kyoto's safest celiac options when open. The old wood-fired pizza oven inherited from Da Yuki remains on site but is 「今のところ使用されていない」(not currently in use), so no wheat pizza is made. CURRENT STATUS (verified 2026-07-02): temporarily closed (休業) due to staff shortages, not permanently closed — reservations were suspended from Sept 2025; verify reopening before relying on it. Mention your allergy when booking.

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Shiga opened Senza X on 9 February 2024 in the former premises of Pizzeria Napoletana Da Yuki in the Okazaki area of Sakyo-ku, after Da Yuki relocated ~200m east to a larger space. Shiga worked as sous-chef and sommelier at Da Yuki, a Michelin Bib Gourmand pizzeria also listed on Tabelog's 'Pizza 百名店.' Senza X ('senza' means 'without' in Italian) is a whole-menu free-from Italian restaurant using no wheat, gluten, egg or dairy. It seats 18. The stone pizza oven from Da Yuki has been preserved on site with the stated hope of one day serving pizza as well.
chef_bio.philosophy: Shiga says he held a dream for over 20 years to build a restaurant 'that can be enjoyed by all, vegans and allergy sufferers,' making the entire kitchen free-from rather than offering ad-hoc modifications; he also runs Senza X as a place to nurture young cooks toward their own future ventures.
chef_bio.specialty: Corn-and-rice gluten-free pasta imported from Italy, rice-flour (米粉) bread, and multi-course allergy-adapted Italian lunch

anecdotes.text: The wood-fired stone oven inherited from Da Yuki is kept on site with the goal of one day offering pizza, though it is currently unused.
The name SENZA means 'without' in Italian — reflecting the without-wheat/egg/dairy concept.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログマガジン（magazine.tabelog.com/articles/369395）・Leaf Kyoto・kyotopi（FdcL1）は、シェフ志賀氏がミシュラン・ビブグルマンかつ食べログ『ピッツァ百名店』の姉妹店『ピッツェリア・ナポレターナ ダユキ』の副料理長・ソムリエで、2024年2月9日にその跡地（左京区岡崎）で18席のSenza Xを開業したと伝える。全メニューが小麦・グルテン・卵・乳製品不使用で、パスタはイタリア産のとうもろこしと米から、パンは米粉。20年来の夢だったと語り、ダユキの石窯は残されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entire kitchen is free-from — no wheat, gluten, egg or dairy used anywhere, so celiac cross-contamination risk is inherently minimal. Pasta is gluten-free ('もちろんパスタはグルテンフリー') and bread is rice-flour.
The inherited wood-fired pizza oven remains but is 'not currently in use' (今のところ使用されていない), so no wheat flour is handled for pizza.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen uses no egg or dairy at all; plant-based probiotic gelato (植物性乳酸菌のジェラート) is offered as a dessert.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Tabelog advises guests with allergies to state them when booking; the owner-chef built the restaurant specifically for allergy sufferers.

positives.text: Celiac blogger mamehana praised the pasta as tasting authentically like real pasta, unlike ordinary commercial gluten-free pasta, and confirmed the whole restaurant is 小麦・グルテン・卵・乳製品不使用.
Purpose-built allergy-adapted Italian: no wheat/gluten/egg/dairy on the entire menu, corn+rice pasta, 米粉 bread.

places.name: Vegan Sweets Courier (クーリエ)
places.gf_detail: The shop's own site states its sweets are 「グルテン、卵、乳製品、白砂糖を使わず、米粉で作ったスイーツ」— made with specially-cultivated rice flour (特別栽培の米粉) and containing no gluten, egg, dairy or white sugar. It is a dedicated gluten-free and vegan production facility (株式会社クーリエ) where no wheat flour is used anywhere, so there is no wheat in the kitchen to cross-contaminate. The physical storefront in Higashiyama opens Saturdays only (11:00–18:00); the business is primarily nationwide mail-order shipping from its Kyoto factory. Purchases on non-Saturday days are possible by advance reservation.
places.vegan_detail: Describes itself as a 「プラントベースの米粉お菓子専門店」(plant-based rice-flour confectionery specialist). All products are made from carefully selected plant ingredients — no eggs, no dairy (casein-free), no animal products — using organic soy milk and organic maple syrup in place of dairy and refined sugar. Every item in the range is 100% vegan.
places.hours_raw: Storefront Saturdays only, 11:00–18:00 (closed all other days; purchases possible other days by reservation). Mainly mail-order.
places.neighborhood: 東山区福稲御所ノ内町 / near Higashiyama
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] Dedicated gluten-free + vegan rice-flour sweets factory (株式会社クーリエ) at 京都市東山区福稲御所ノ内町2番地14, near Tofukuji/Higashiyama — no wheat is used anywhere in production, so it is celiac-appropriate. IMPORTANT for visitors: the physical storefront is open Saturdays only (11:00–18:00); the business is primarily nationwide mail-order, so plan around Saturday hours or order online / reserve ahead for other days.

roles: representative / president (株式会社クーリエ)
chef_bio.background: The founder turned to a gluten-free and plant-based diet after struggling with health problems following marriage and childbirth, and began studying nutrition and diet from scratch. Wanting guilt-free vegan sweets — free of wheat (gluten-free), dairy (casein-free), eggs and white sugar — to be more widely available, they began manufacturing and selling such confections. Vegan Sweets Courier launched in August 2015, and the business is now run as 株式会社クーリエ with 藤戸義晶 as representative. The company operates a Kyoto factory shipping nationwide, with a small Saturday-only storefront in its Higashiyama (福稲御所ノ内町) premises.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「大切なのは摂ることよりも抜くこと」— what matters is not what you add but what you take away; removing unnecessary ingredients (gluten, egg, dairy, white sugar, artificial colorants) to make sweets that can be enjoyed without guilt.
chef_bio.specialty: rice-flour vegan sweets, no gluten/egg/dairy/white sugar

anecdotes.text: The signature 'くるれ' cakes (French for 'vivid') get their colors entirely from natural ingredients via a special production method that uses no artificial colorants (色素を使わない特別な製法).
The founder began the gluten-free lifestyle out of personal necessity after post-childbirth health issues, then launched the brand in August 2015 in the role of development director.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: vegansweets.jp（公式）・shop.vegansweets.jp・kameoka-up・giftmall によれば、代表 藤戸義晶が、結婚・出産後の体調不良をきっかけにグルテンフリー・ヴィーガンの食生活を始め、「小麦（グルテンフリー）・乳製品（カゼインフリー）・卵・白砂糖を使わない罪悪感のないヴィーガンスイーツ」を広めたいと2015年8月にVegan Sweets Courierを立ち上げた（株式会社クーリエ、京都・東山区福稲御所ノ内町）。理念は「大切なのは摂ることよりも抜くこと」。着色料不使用で自然な色を出す『くるれ』が看板。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Dedicated gluten-free facility: the entire product line is made with rice flour (米粉) and no wheat flour is used anywhere, so there is no wheat in the production environment to cross-contaminate. Own claim: 「グルテン、卵、乳製品、白砂糖を使わず、米粉で作ったスイーツ」.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not applicable — this is a sweets-only maker; no soy sauce or savory wheat-containing seasonings are involved. Products use organic soy milk and organic maple syrup, not wheat-based ingredients.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fully vegan facility — no eggs or dairy are used in any product (casein-free), so there is no animal-product cross-contact risk in the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The brand was founded specifically for people avoiding wheat/gluten, dairy and eggs, and markets its sweets as suitable for those with wheat allergy and following gluten-free/casein-free diets; gift and mail-order packaging is a core part of the business.

positives.text: Whole facility is gluten-free AND vegan by design — no wheat, egg, dairy or white sugar anywhere, using 特別栽培の米粉, 有機豆乳 and オーガニックメープルシロップ; colors come from natural ingredients with no artificial colorants.

places.name: Warewarewa Arashiyama (和レ和レ和 アラシヤマ)
places.gf_detail: A rare fully gluten-free traditional teishoku/udon café in the Arashiyama tourist core. The operator states 「すべてのメニューがグルテンフリーで、京都の食材を多く使い、ランチで提供する『うどん』は玄米、揚げ物の衣も米粉を使ったグルテンフリーに仕上げました。」 — the lunch udon is made from brown-rice (玄米) noodles and all fried items use rice-flour (米粉) batter, so no wheat flour is used anywhere on the menu. A curated Japanese GF guide independently confirms it is prepared 「完全に独立した厨房で調理」 (in a completely separate/independent kitchen) and that 「小麦不使用の調味料を厳選」 (wheat-free condiments, i.e. tamari-style soy, are used), which supports celiac-safe status. Caveat: the dashi is a traditional kombu-and-bonito (かつお) stock, so it is gluten-free but not vegan; travelers with allergies beyond gluten are told to confirm in advance.
places.vegan_label: Limited vegan · one kombu-water salad udon
places.vegan_detail: The menu is fish-forward: nearly every udon and teishoku uses a bonito/kombu dashi (出汁) plus egg (だし巻き, 京鴨てりたま), duck (京鴨), or cod roe (明太子). The 昆布水サラダうどん (kombu-water salad udon) is described in press coverage as the plant-based/vegan-friendly option. Tofu-based とうめし may be adaptable but likely uses a dashi tare, so vegans should confirm. Not a vegan-focused venue.
places.hours_raw: 8:00-18:00 daily (food L.O. 17:00); irregular closures. Opened 2024-09-09 with a three-part schedule - morning set meals (teishoku) 8:00-11:00, lunch udon 11:00-15:00 (until sold out), and evening light drinking (choi-nomi) 15:00-21:00 - but the current official/Tabelog listing is 8:00-18:00.
places.cuisine: Gluten-free Japanese teishoku & brown-rice udon café
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] All items are gluten-free with a separate/independent kitchen and wheat-free (tamari-style) seasonings, making it one of the few genuinely celiac-safe sit-down spots in central Arashiyama — a 2–3 min walk from Randen (Keifuku) Arashiyama Station, beside Tenryū-ji. Practical notes: no reservations accepted, cashless payment only (cards/IC/PayPay, no cash), and udon can sell out; arrive early. Dashi is bonito-based (GF, not vegan); confirm any non-gluten allergies with staff.

roles: Operating company: 株式会社 日と々と (Hitobito Inc.)
Parent brand: パンとエスプレッソと (Bread, Espresso &)
chef_bio.origin: Omotesandō, Tokyo bakery-café group expanding into Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Warewarewa Arashiyama is operated by 株式会社日と々と (Hitobito Inc.), the company behind the well-known bakery-café brand パンとエスプレッソと (Bread, Espresso &), which runs roughly 32 outlets nationwide. The Arashiyama shop opened on 2024-09-09 as a new gluten-free Japanese-set-meal concept rather than a bakery, positioned to serve growing post-pandemic inbound demand for gluten-free dining. It is a company-run concept store, not an independently owned restaurant, though its kitchen is operated as a fully gluten-free, separate cooking environment. The 和レ和レ和 (Warewarewa) name plays on 和 (Japanese/harmony). No individual head chef is publicly credited.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「すべてのメニューがグルテンフリー」— an all-gluten-free Japanese teishoku café using many Kyoto ingredients (京鴨 Kyoto duck, 九条ネギ, 嵯峨豆腐森嘉 tofu/aburaage), making healthy wheat-free washoku accessible to travelers.
chef_bio.specialty: 京鴨昆布水つけうどん (Kyoto-duck kombu-water dipping udon on brown-rice noodles); だし巻き定食 (fluffy 4-egg dashimaki set using 蘭王卵); 6種の天ぷらうどん with rice-flour batter.

anecdotes.text: The signature dashimaki is made with four eggs (蘭王卵) and marketed as a 'must-eat' Arashiyama breakfast, drawing early-morning crowds.
Positioned as a gluten-free café responding to rising inbound interest in gluten-free dining after the pandemic reopening.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 日本語各紙（PR TIMES／ことりっぷ／ファッションプレス／食べログマガジン）は、パンとエスプレッソと（株式会社日と々と、約32店舗）が2024年9月9日に嵐山（右京区嵯峨天龍寺）にオープンした『和レ和レ和 アラシヤマ』を、全メニュー・グルテンフリー（玄米うどん・米粉衣）の和定食カフェとして紹介。GF専門ガイドは『完全に独立した厨房で調理』『小麦不使用の調味料を厳選』とし、セリアック対応の安全性を評価している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: GF guide states the food is 「完全に独立した厨房で調理されており、グルテンフリーの安全性を確認しています」 (cooked in a completely independent kitchen, gluten-free safety confirmed).
Operator claim: 「すべてのメニューがグルテンフリー」— no wheat flour anywhere; udon is 玄米 (brown rice) and fry batter is 米粉 (rice flour), so the fryer handles only GF batter.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: GF guide notes 「小麦不使用の調味料を厳選」 (wheat-free condiments carefully selected), indicating tamari-style / wheat-free soy and tsuyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi is a traditional bonito (かつお)/kombu stock and egg is used widely; only the kombu-water salad udon is flagged plant-based, so vegan cross-contact with fish stock is likely across most dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: GF guide advises: 「グルテンフリー以外のアレルギーがある場合は、事前にお店へ確認してください」 (for allergies other than gluten, confirm with the shop in advance).

positives.text: Whole menu is gluten-free with a separate/independent kitchen — a rare celiac-safe sit-down option in the Arashiyama tourist core.
Run by an established 32-store café group (パンとエスプレッソと / 株式会社日と々と), suggesting consistent operating standards.

places.name: Bakery House Creek (ベーカリーハウス クリーク)
places.gf_detail: Creek is a large everyday neighborhood bakery in Saiin that runs a genuine 米粉100％ (100% rice flour) gluten-free line alongside roughly 80 conventional wheat breads. The shop advertises 米粉を100％使用したグルテンフリーのパン and describes its signature 米粉100％グルテンフリー食パン as 卵・乳製品・小麦粉を一切使わず (using absolutely no egg, dairy, or wheat flour). Because the very same premises bakes and slices wheat bread all day, airborne wheat flour and shared equipment mean cross-contact is realistic — this is an OPTIONS venue, not a dedicated celiac-safe one. Celiacs should treat the rice-flour items as allergen-recipe products, not contamination-controlled ones.
places.vegan_detail: The flagship 米粉100％グルテンフリー食パン is explicitly 卵・乳製品・小麦粉不使用 (no egg, dairy, or wheat), so it is incidentally vegan, and some rice-flour cookies may follow. Most of the ~80 breads, however, are standard wheat products with butter/dairy, so vegan choices are limited to a handful of the rice-flour line. Confirm ingredients per item, as recipes vary.
places.hours_raw: 8:00–22:00, open daily (no regular closing day). Note: some listings cite 7:00–20:30 — confirm before visiting.
places.notes: [GF] Ask staff directly for the 米粉100％ (100% rice flour) items — the gluten-free 食パン (loaf), rice-flour bagels, cookies and cheesecake. These are made to an egg/dairy/wheat-free recipe, but everything is produced in a shared, high-volume wheat bakery with visible flour dust, so this suits gluten-sensitive/allergy-aware diners rather than highly reactive celiacs. Buy sealed/whole items rather than open-shelf loose bread to reduce cross-contact, and go early as the GF line sells out.

chef_bio.background: Bakery House Creek is a long-running neighborhood bakery on the Saiin shopping street, about three minutes on foot from Hankyu Saiin Station (near Randen Saiin). It is a high-volume everyday shop offering around 80 kinds of bread daily at budget prices (mostly ¥100–200). Japanese coverage notes the shop expanded its rice-flour, gluten-free range over time in response to customer requests, particularly from people with wheat allergies. No individual owner or head-baker name is published in the sources reviewed.
chef_bio.philosophy: An affordable, approachable everyday bakery that has added a dedicated rice-flour gluten-free line so wheat-allergic and health-conscious customers can still buy bread.
chef_bio.specialty: 米粉100％グルテンフリー食パン (100% rice flour gluten-free loaf) and rice-flour bagels, alongside ~80 conventional wheat breads.

anecdotes.text: The gluten-free 米粉食パン is made 卵・乳製品・小麦粉を一切使わず (with no egg, dairy or wheat) to a house recipe, giving a moist, chewy (しっとりもちもち) texture prized by wheat-allergy customers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 日本語記事（kyotokurasu.jp、kyotogourmet.jp、Tabelog）によれば、西院の商店街にある約80種類のパンを扱う庶民的なベーカリー（阪急西院から徒歩約3分）で、米粉100％のグルテンフリーのパン（卵・乳製品・小麦粉不使用、しっとりもちもち）や米粉ベーグルが名物。オーナー個人名は記載なし。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Full wheat bakery (~80 wheat breads baked and sliced daily on-site) sharing space with the rice-flour line — realistic airborne wheat-flour and shared-equipment cross-contact. OPTIONS tier only.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan-friendly 米粉100％ loaf is baked in the same premises as dairy/butter wheat breads; no separation stated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No formal allergen-handling protocol published; GF items are recipe-based (卵・乳製品・小麦粉不使用) rather than contamination-controlled. Ask staff which items are 米粉100％.

positives.text: Genuine, clearly labelled 米粉100％ gluten-free line: 米粉100％グルテンフリー食パン made 卵・乳製品・小麦粉を一切使わず, plus rice-flour bagels, cookies and cheesecake.

places.name: coconotane (ここのたね)
places.gf_detail: Tiny (~2-tsubo) takeout-only shop billed as a 米粉シフォンケーキ専門店 (rice-flour chiffon-cake specialty store) using 国産米粉100% with 米油 (rice-bran oil) and きび砂糖 (unrefined cane sugar); their materials say additives are avoided — "小麦粉だけではなく添加物は極力使わず" — implying no wheat flour in the recipe. Because everything is rice-flour chiffon, the risk of wheat on-site is low, but there is no explicit gluten-free / no-wheat-on-premises statement on their own channels, so this stays at options pending direct confirmation. Note some varieties use 牛乳/生クリーム (dairy). Celiacs should message the shop to confirm a fully wheat-free kitchen before relying on it.
places.vegan_detail: Base chiffons use 豆乳 (soymilk), 米油 and きび砂糖 rather than butter, so some flavors are plant-based; however certain items use 牛乳 (dairy milk) and the cream-sandwich versions use whipped cream. Vegans should check each flavor.
places.hours_raw: Thursday & Saturday only, 10:30–14:00 (closes when sold out)
places.neighborhood: 久我森の宮町 (Kuga Morinomiya-cho), Fushimi-ku, Kyoto — residential, near 下久我 bus stop
places.notes: [GF] Rice-flour (米粉) chiffon-cake specialist, takeout only, open Thu & Sat until sold out. Recipe uses 国産米粉100%, 米油 and きび砂糖 with no wheat flour, but there is no explicit dedicated/no-wheat-on-premises claim — kept at options. Celiacs: DM the shop to confirm the whole kitchen is wheat-free, and note some flavors contain dairy.

chef_bio.background: coconotane opened on 16 October 2021 as a two-tsubo (約6.6㎡) takeout stand built in the garage of a residential neighborhood in Fushimi-ku's Kuga (久我) area. Owner Kajiki Miyuki runs it single-handedly as a 米粉シフォンケーキ専門店 under the motto "心に笑顔の種を撒く" (sowing seeds of smiles), using 国産米粉100%, 豆乳, 米油 and きび砂糖, no baking powder, and keeping additives to a minimum. The shop became a viral hit (a short video reportedly reaching ~13 million / 1300万回 views) and now regularly sells out before noon. It operates only Thursdays and Saturdays.
chef_bio.philosophy: "心に笑顔の種を撒く" — safe, gentle rice-flour sweets made with 国産米粉100%, 豆乳, 米油 and きび砂糖, no baking powder, keeping additives to a minimum.
chef_bio.specialty: Cut rice-flour chiffon cakes (plain, hojicha, espresso, etc.) and chiffon cream sandwiches

anecdotes.text: 住宅街のガレージに建てた2坪の小さなお店で、2時間ほどで売り切れる人気店 (a 2-tsubo garage shop that sells out in about two hours).
2021年10月16日オープン。店主は梶木美夕紀さん。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: まいぷれ（店舗ページ＋伏見人vol.059）・kyotopi・osumituki・kameoka-up によれば、店主・梶木美夕紀さんが2021年10月16日に伏見区久我のガレージで始めた2坪のテイクアウト米粉シフォンケーキ専門店。国産米粉100%、豆乳、米油、きび砂糖使用、ベーキングパウダー不使用、添加物は極力不使用。木・土のみ営業で午前中に完売。動画1300万回再生の人気。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 米粉シフォンケーキ専門店 making only rice-flour chiffon; 国産米粉100%. Low likelihood of wheat on-site, but no explicit no-wheat-on-premises statement published, so cross-contact is unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Base uses 豆乳/米油/きび砂糖, but some flavors use 牛乳 (dairy) and cream sandwiches use whipped cream.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owner-run micro takeout stand; contact via Instagram/LINE to confirm allergen/kitchen details.

positives.text: "小麦粉だけではなく添加物は極力使わず"; 油は米油、砂糖はきび砂糖、国産米粉100%.
"添加物は極力使わない、安心安全な米粉スイーツ".

places.name: COCO KYOTO (COCO KYOTO 本店)
places.gf_detail: Bean-to-bar chocolate and sweets shop whose products lean gluten-free: their own materials say "白砂糖・添加物を使用しないチョコレート" and "グルテンフリーやオーガニックカカオといった素材にもこだわり," and they sell 米粉 (rice-flour) cakes plus vegan-friendly chocolate. However, "グルテンフリー" here is a product/ingredient claim, not a facility one — the shop is a full patisserie (roll cakes, baumkuchen, sandwich cookies) and there is no statement that wheat is absent from the kitchen or that there is no cross-contact. For celiacs this stays at options: confirm item-by-item and ask about shared equipment before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: They advertise ヴィーガン対応 chocolate and Raw Botanical / Raw ケークショコラ items, but much of the cake lineup contains dairy cream, butter and eggs. Vegan buyers should confirm which specific chocolates/raw cakes are plant-based.
places.hours_raw: Sun–Thu 10:30–18:00 (LO 17:30); Fri–Sat 10:30–18:30 (LO 18:00); closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 西ノ京三条坊町 (Nishinokyo Sanjobocho), Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto — near Nishioji-Oike
places.notes: [GF] Rice-flour (米粉) cakes and gluten-free-leaning chocolate, white-sugar-free and additive-free per the shop, but it is a full patisserie that also makes wheat-flour goods with no dedicated/no-wheat-on-premises claim. Celiacs: verify each item and ask about shared equipment/cross-contact before buying.

chef_bio.background: COCO KYOTO is a Kyoto bean-to-bar chocolate atelier that controls its chocolate from cacao-bean selection through manufacturing (カカオ豆の選定から製造まで一貫). The shop built its identity around 白砂糖不使用 (no refined sugar), 無添加 (additive-free) and グルテンフリー sweets, positioning indulgence without guilt under the slogan "おいしさに罪悪感はいらない." Its main store sits in Nakagyo-ku's Nishinokyo Sanjobocho near Nishioji-Oike. The brand has been recognized at the International Chocolate Awards for eight consecutive years.
chef_bio.philosophy: "おいしさに罪悪感はいらない" — delicious sweets that are refined-sugar-free, additive-free and gluten-free, using organic cacao and Kyoto ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Bean-to-bar and raw chocolate, rice-flour (米粉) cakes, chocolate terrines and roll cakes

anecdotes.text: インターナショナルチョコレートアワードを8年連続で受賞している (International Chocolate Award winner eight years running), including World and Asia-Pacific medals.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: coco-cacao.jp と kameoka-up.net によれば、白砂糖・添加物不使用、グルテンフリー、オーガニックカカオにこだわった京都・中京区西ノ京三条坊町のビーントゥバー・チョコレート店で、カカオ豆選定から製造まで一貫。インターナショナルチョコレートアワード8年受賞。米粉ケーキやヴィーガン対応チョコも販売。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shop advertises グルテンフリー as a product/ingredient claim but is a full patisserie also making wheat-flour items (roll cakes, baumkuchen, sandwich cookies); no no-wheat-on-premises or cross-contact statement found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many cakes contain dairy and eggs; vegan applies only to specific chocolates/raw items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen-handling protocol; buyers must confirm per-item and about shared equipment directly.

positives.text: "白砂糖・添加物を使用しないチョコレート" and "グルテンフリーやオーガニックカカオといった素材にもこだわり"; sells 米粉ケーキ.

places.name: EVERYSOY (エブリソイ)
places.gf_detail: This soy-milk specialty cafe run by long-established Kyoto yuba maker ゆば庄 makes its signature choux pastry from rice flour and soy cream. The menu page states the ソイシュークリーム base is 「米粉のシュー生地と豆乳クリーム」and is 「乳製品不使用・グルテンフリー」— but note the chocolate flavor is an exception: 「ショコラのチョコレートの中には一部乳成分が含まれます」(contains some dairy). It is a small takeout-focused counter (テイクアウト中心の小さなカフェ) that also sells non-gluten-free ライトミール from the same shared counter, so this is a dessert/drink stop with genuine GF options, not a fully GF venue; celiacs should treat cross-contact from shared prep/counter as possible.
places.vegan_detail: Everything is built on soy milk and the shop centers on plant-based soy, but the rice-flour choux pastry is likely egg-based and some flavors contain dairy (chocolate). No explicit vegan labeling was found, so vegans should confirm egg/dairy per item.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 9:00-20:30 / weekends and holidays 10:30-20:30
places.neighborhood: 京都市下京区 四条烏丸 (Shijo-Karasuma, LAQUE四条烏丸 1F)
places.notes: [GF] Soy-milk cafe by Kyoto yuba maker ゆば庄. The signature 米粉シュークリーム (SOY chou à la crème) is stated 乳製品不使用・グルテンフリー — a reliable GF treat (avoid the chocolate flavor, which contains some dairy). Because non-GF light meals are prepared and sold from the same small counter, treat it as a GF-options dessert/drink stop rather than a celiac-safe kitchen.

roles: parent company: ゆば庄 (Yubasho / 京湯葉の老舗)
chef_bio.background: EVERYSOY is a soy-focused cafe brand created by ゆば庄 (Yubasho), a long-established Kyoto yuba (tofu-skin) specialist. The shop's own site states 「京都で長年湯葉をつくり続けてきた京湯葉の老舗『ゆば庄』が、厳選したフレッシュな豆乳を使ってつくっています」. The concept ('Stop, Soy'n Go') is to help people enjoy nutritious soy in everyday, approachable forms — drinks and sweets — rather than only traditional yuba. It runs a small takeout-centered counter cafe at its 四条烏丸 branch near the Shijo-Karasuma crossing.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make high-nutrition soy an everyday pleasure via fresh soy milk drinks and dairy-free sweets.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-milk drinks and the rice-flour, dairy-free ソイシュークリーム (SOY chou à la crème) and ソイティラミス.

anecdotes.text: The SOY chou à la crème pairs a rice-flour choux shell with soy-milk cream, marketed as 『とっても軽くてクリーミー』.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: everysoy.com（公式ブランド・メニュー・店舗ページ）は、京湯葉の老舗『ゆば庄』が厳選フレッシュ豆乳で手がけるソイカフェで、米粉・乳不使用のソイシュークリームやソイティラミスを提供と記載。四条烏丸に店舗（食べログ EVERYSOY 四条烏丸店）。KBS京都ラジオも特集。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small takeout counter that also sells non-GF ライトミール from the same counter; shared prep space means cross-contact is possible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dessert/drink focus; no wheat soy sauce implicated in the signature items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Chocolate flavor contains some dairy: 「ショコラのチョコレートの中には一部乳成分が含まれます」.

positives.text: Signature ソイシュークリーム explicitly labeled 乳製品不使用・グルテンフリー with a 米粉 (rice-flour) choux base.

places.name: LOCO CHICKEN (ロコチキン)
places.gf_detail: A Hawaiian-style mochiko/rice-flour chicken specialist. The Japanese GF guide glutenfree.empacede.co.jp (rated ★★★★) states 「米粉チキン専門店です。米粉チキンは、小麦粉の代わりに米粉を使っており、グルテンフリーです」— the karaage is battered in rice flour, not wheat. Burgers can be made GF: 「ハンバーガーのバンズは、プラス200円で、グルテンフリーのバンズに変更できます」and tacos/taco rice are inherently GF. The guide notes wheat is present on-site only for standard burger buns and for beer; a shared fryer is likely at a general fried-chicken shop, so confirm dedicated oil and no wheat in the seasoning before ordering, and note the on-premises beer keeps this out of dedicated-GF territory.
