places.vegan_detail: A fried-chicken and burger shop; core menu is chicken. No vegan mains identified.
places.neighborhood: 京都市下京区 四条河原町 (Shijo-Kawaramachi)
places.cuisine: Hawaiian-style rice-flour (mochiko) fried chicken, burgers, plates, tacos
places.notes: [GF] Rice-flour (mochiko) chicken specialist near Shijo-Kawaramachi. Per JP GF guide, the karaage/chicken uses rice flour instead of wheat (グルテンフリー), burgers swap to a GF bun for +¥200, and tacos/taco rice are GF. Wheat on-site is limited to standard buns and beer — but it is a small (5-seat) general fried-chicken shop, so confirm the fryer oil isn't shared with any wheat item and that seasonings are wheat-free before you order.

chef_bio.background: LOCO CHICKEN is a Hawaiian-influenced 米粉チキン (rice-flour / mochiko chicken) specialist that opened around 30 September 2021, about a 3-minute walk south of Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station in the Shijo-Kawaramachi area (河原町会館). It is a tiny first-come, first-served counter shop (5 seats) doing dine-in and takeout, blending Hawaiian 'mochiko chicken' style with Kyoto tastes; the Kyoto-native owner drew inspiration from Saipan's 'LOCO & TACO.' The signature is thin, light, crisp rice-flour-battered chicken served as burgers or over-rice plates.
chef_bio.philosophy: Light, crisp mochiko/rice-flour chicken in a casual Hawaiian plate-lunch format.
chef_bio.specialty: 米粉唐揚げ (rice-flour karaage) plates and chicken burgers with an optional gluten-free bun (+¥200).

anecdotes.text: Menu spans classic, yangnyeom, Nashville-hot and yuzu-pepper tartar chicken burgers plus 3- and 4-piece rice plates.
米粉チキンは小麦粉の代わりに米粉を使いグルテンフリー。ハンバーガーのバンズはプラス200円でグルテンフリーのバンズに変更でき、席数はカウンター5席のみ。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: glutenfree.empacede.co.jp（京都GFリスト、★★★★、米粉チキン専門店・GFバンズ+200円・カウンター5席）、osumituki.com（2021/9/30オープン・住所・価格・サイパンLOCO & TACO由来）、食べログ/Retty 掲載が一致。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small general fried-chicken shop; a shared fryer is likely. Guide states wheat on-site is limited to burger buns and beer, but fryer/seasoning cross-contact is unconfirmed — ask before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sauces/glazes (yangnyeom, teriyaki-style) may contain wheat soy sauce; confirm per item.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Offers a paid GF bun swap (+¥200), indicating some allergy awareness, but no formal cross-contact protocol documented.

positives.text: 米粉チキン専門店です。米粉チキンは、小麦粉の代わりに米粉を使っており、グルテンフリーです — rated ★★★★ by JP GF guide.

places.name: THE NICOLE / Nicole Naturelle (ザ・ニコル / Nicole Naturelle)
places.gf_detail: THE NICOLE is a conventional Kyoto patisserie that also runs a separate allergen-free sub-line, Nicole Naturelle, whose sweets are made with 100% domestic rice flour and no wheat. The venue's own site states 『卵、乳製品、小麦粉、動物性油脂は使用しておりません』 and describes the line as 『卵、乳、白砂糖不使用』『グルテンフリー』『ヴィーガンスイーツ』 using 国産米粉100%. However, the SAME shop bakes ordinary wheat flour cakes and serves 欧風スパイスカレー (European-style spice curry, typically a wheat-roux base), so the kitchen is shared and cross-contact with wheat flour is a real risk — celiacs must confirm the item is from the Naturelle line and ideally order it pre-made/sealed. Because of the shared wheat kitchen this stays an 'options' venue, not a dedicated GF facility.
places.vegan_detail: The Nicole Naturelle line is fully plant-based — 卵・乳・動物性油脂不使用, using 京豆腐 (Kyoto tofu), 豆乳ヨーグルト (soy yogurt), soy-based 'butter', coconut oil and maple syrup in place of dairy and eggs. These items are explicitly marketed as ヴィーガンスイーツ. The parent THE NICOLE shop, though, also sells conventional cakes with egg/dairy and non-vegan curry, so only the Naturelle line is vegan — choose from it specifically.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-16:00; closed Mondays and Tuesdays (per the venue's own site). Nicole Naturelle allergen-free sweets are by reservation / online order only. Hours differ between sources (Tabelog lists Mon-Fri 10:30-18:00, Sat 10:30-17:00, closed Sun) - confirm before visiting.
places.cuisine: Patisserie & café — rice-flour vegan/gluten-free allergen-free sweets (Nicole Naturelle line), plus conventional wheat cakes and spice curry
places.notes: [GF] The Nicole Naturelle sub-line uses 国産米粉100% and no wheat, but it is baked in the same kitchen as THE NICOLE's ordinary wheat cakes and wheat-roux spice curry — treat as cross-contact risk, not celiac-safe. Order Naturelle items specifically (they are reservation/online-order only, sold mostly as whole tarts, chocolate and sablé tins) and confirm the item is from the allergen line. Cash only; small counter shop in the Horikawa shopping arcade.

chef_bio.background: Owner-pâtissier 堀口微笑琉 was born in 1979 in Kyoto's Nishijin district and trained at several patisseries between 1999 and 2006 before opening his own shop, 洋菓子屋Sorcier, in Umeda in 2008. The business relocated and evolved over the years — becoming Kitchen&Cake NICOLE in 2015 and rebranding as THE NICOLE at the current 西陣 (Nishijin) location in 2019. In 2022 he added a spice-curry operation (麺屋 微笑琉 / NICOLEカレー, a once-a-week spice-curry-udon venture), and in 2023 he launched the Nicole Naturelle allergen-free, vegan, gluten-free sweets line. The shop's stated aim is 「食材の制限がある方も楽しめるスイーツ」— sweets that people with dietary restrictions can also enjoy.
chef_bio.philosophy: A hybrid patisserie-and-curry café built around inclusivity and low-sugar / low-fat, 'body-friendly' sweets, with the Nicole Naturelle line created so that people with egg, dairy and wheat restrictions can still enjoy cakes.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour (米粉) vegan gluten-free tarts, 京豆腐 tofu-based raw-chocolate confections and 黒糖 sablé tins; also European-style spice curry.

anecdotes.text: The venture is a 洋菓子×スパイスカレー hybrid — a Western-confectionery shop that also serves a meat-free 'Nicole curry' deriving umami from vegetables, plus rotating daily creative spice curries.
The allergen-free Nicole Naturelle line was launched in 2023, years after the base patisserie, specifically to serve customers with dietary restrictions.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: the-nicole.com（自社サイト沿革）に、オーナー堀口微笑琉（1979年西陣生まれ、1999-2006修業、2008 Sorcier梅田、2015 Kitchen&Cake NICOLE、2019 THE NICOLE西陣、2022 麺屋微笑琉、2023 Nicole Naturelle）、Nicole Naturelleの「卵・乳製品・小麦粉・動物性油脂不使用」「グルテンフリー」「ヴィーガン」「国産米粉100%」の記載あり。kyotopi.jpは洋菓子×スパイスカレーのハイブリッド業態を紹介。食べログは住所・営業時間・現金のみを裏付け。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The Nicole Naturelle allergen-free sweets are baked in the same premises as THE NICOLE's conventional wheat-flour cakes and a wheat-roux European-style spice curry, so shared-kitchen cross-contact with wheat is a genuine risk despite the rice-flour recipes.
Venue's own claim for the line: 『卵、乳製品、小麦粉、動物性油脂は使用しておりません』／『グルテンフリー』／国産米粉100%. No dedicated-facility or cross-contamination protocol is stated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not applicable to the sweets line; however the shop's spice curry is not part of the gluten-free line and should be assumed to contain wheat (欧風カレー roux) — do not treat curry as GF.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Nicole Naturelle items are made in a kitchen that also handles dairy, eggs and animal fats for conventional cakes; the Naturelle line itself is 卵・乳・動物性油脂不使用 but shared equipment means trace animal-product contact is possible.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop positions itself as making 「食材の制限がある方も楽しめるスイーツ」and takes Naturelle items ご予約のみ (by reservation), suggesting allergy awareness, but no formal celiac cross-contact handling procedure is documented.

positives.text: Explicit written wheat-free claim using 100% domestic rice flour: 『卵、乳製品、小麦粉、動物性油脂は使用しておりません』『グルテンフリー』, a dedicated recipe line rather than incidental GF items.
Naturelle line is fully vegan (京豆腐, 豆乳ヨーグルト, ココナッツオイル), doubling as a good option for plant-based celiacs.

places.name: OUBON (オーボン)
places.gf_detail: OUBON is a conventional Western pâtisserie near Fushimi Inari that sells regular wheat cakes AND a separate allergy-oriented 米粉 (rice-flour) line, so wheat is handled in the same kitchen. The shop's own claim for the rice-flour line reads: 「全てに米粉を使用していますのでアレルギーをお持ちの方でも、もちろん食べていただけます」 ("Everything uses rice flour, so even those with allergies can of course eat it"). Because the same bakery also produces wheat pastries daily, cross-contact with gluten is a real risk and this cannot be treated as celiac-safe — confirm with staff and buy only the labeled 米粉/グルテンフリー items.
places.vegan_detail: The rice-flour items are not vegan: the shop lists 卵 (egg) and 乳 (dairy) as allergens on its gluten-free products. The signature 豆乳米粉ロール is described as 「グルテンフリー商品となっており、アレルギー物質は卵、乳」 and uses Hokkaido cream (whipped with soymilk), so egg and dairy remain. No vegan line is advertised.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–20:00, closed Wednesdays (some listings show Wed 12:00–18:00). Tel 075-632-8287.
places.neighborhood: 京都市伏見区深草鈴塚町 (Fukakusa, Fushimi-ku — near Fushimi Inari)
places.cuisine: Western pâtisserie / cakes & baked sweets (rice-flour GF line)
places.notes: [GF] Not celiac-safe — this is a shared wheat kitchen. OUBON bakes conventional wheat cakes alongside its 米粉 (rice-flour) allergy line, so gluten cross-contact is likely; only the explicitly labeled 米粉/グルテンフリー sweets (e.g. 豆乳米粉ロール, 米粉フィナンシェ, 米粉マドレーヌ) are wheat-free by recipe, and even those are baked in the same premises. Also note the GF items contain egg and dairy. Tabelog currently lists the shop's operating status as unconfirmed/on-hold, so phone ahead (075-632-8287) to confirm it is open before visiting.

roles: Head pastry chef (シェフパティシエ)
chef_bio.background: OUBON opened in December 2017 a few minutes' walk from Fushimi Inari station in Fukakusa, Fushimi-ku. Head pastry chef and owner Nobuo Kakine (垣根信夫) is described as a veteran patissier with 40 years of experience in Kyoto, having served as head pâtissier at a well-known hotel and other shops before opening his own. The shop is credited as being run by a pâtissier who helped pioneer Japanese-style (和風) sweets. For its gluten-free line OUBON sources rice flour from Asahi Seifunjo (朝日製粉所) in the Kyoto Tamba/Nantan area.
chef_bio.philosophy: To make rice-flour cakes so close to wheat versions that they are hard to tell apart in a blind test, so that people with wheat allergies can enjoy ordinary-tasting Western sweets alongside everyone else.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour (米粉) gluten-free cakes and baked sweets — signature 豆乳米粉ロール, plus financiers and madeleines — alongside a conventional wheat pâtisserie range.

anecdotes.text: The shop says its rice-flour sponge is so like wheat that even a blind taste test can't reveal the difference: 「小麦を使っているケーキと食感は全く同じで、味もブラインドテストをしてもわからないほど」.
OUBON's gluten-free cakes are made with rice flour supplied by Asahi Seifunjo, a Kyoto Tamba (Nantan) flour mill that profiled the shop and its 40-year-veteran chef.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: オーボンは2017年12月に伏見稲荷近くにオープンした洋菓子店。シェフパティシエの垣根信夫氏は京都で40年の実績を持つベテランで、有名ホテルのチーフパティシエを歴任。通常の小麦のケーキに加え、朝日製粉所の米粉を使ったアレルギー対応・グルテンフリーのケーキを製造。元祖和風スイーツを編み出したパティシエとして紹介されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: OUBON is a conventional pâtisserie that handles both wheat cakes and rice-flour cakes: 「通常の小麦を使ったケーキや洋菓子、グルテンフリーなケーキやスイーツなどを扱っているお店」. Shared wheat kitchen means airborne-flour and shared-equipment cross-contact risk; not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not applicable — this is a dessert/patisserie shop; no savory soy-sauce dishes on offer.

vegan_cross_contact.text: GF items are not vegan by recipe — egg (卵) and dairy (乳) are listed as allergens on the rice-flour products, so vegan cross-contact is moot.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop markets the rice-flour line as 「アレルギー対応の米粉ケーキ」 (allergy-conscious rice-flour cakes) and states allergens for the roll as egg and dairy, indicating some allergen awareness, but no dedicated-line or celiac protocol is claimed.

positives.text: A named, clearly-labeled 米粉/グルテンフリー range (roll, financiers, madeleines, shortcake, lemon cake) with rice flour sourced from Asahi Seifunjo, made by a 40-year-veteran chef — a convenient wheat-free sweet stop right by Fushimi Inari.

places.name: 土佐屋 室戸 (Tosaya Muroto)
places.gf_detail: A Tosa (Kōchi) izakaya built on charcoal yakitori and seared bonito (katsuo tataki) — soy sauce, yakitori tare and ponzu (all typically wheat-based) run through the menu, so it is not celiac-safe by default. Some items can be gluten-free if confirmed: sashimi, salt (shio) skewers, and salt-style katsuo tataki without ponzu. But the shared charcoal grill, shared fryer and wheat-containing sauces make cross-contamination likely — order shio, skip the tare/ponzu, and show the celiac card.
places.vegan_detail: A Tosa seafood-and-yakitori counter: seared bonito, charcoal chicken, and shamo (game-fowl) hot pot. Bonito dashi and fish run through almost everything, so even vegetable dishes likely carry animal-derived stock. A few plain vegetable skewers or pickles may be possible, but confirm dashi/bonito with the chef. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–23:00. Closed Sunday. (Some listings also note the 2nd & 3rd Monday closed and lunch by reservation only — confirm before visiting.)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop — a Tosa master, ~50 years at the counter] A tiny Kōchi (Tosa) izakaya on Kiyamachi just south of Sanjo — up on the 2nd floor above an Italian restaurant, with a ~10-seat counter plus one small tatami (6–8). The chef, a Tosa native with roughly half a century behind the grill, cooks yakitori over Tosa binchōtan charcoal and serves Kōchi's signature seared bonito (katsuo tataki), shamo (game-fowl) hot pot, and seasonal Tosa dishes. Reviewers praise thick, odorless bonito and carefully grilled skewers, and a charming old-Kyoto exterior; it draws locals and food-loving visitors alike. Reservations accepted (HotPepper). Address: 河原町通三条下ル大黒町49 (entrance on Kiyamachi-dōri). Instagram @tosayamuroto.

chef_bio.origin: Tosa (Kōchi Prefecture); the shop is in Kiyamachi, Nakagyo Ward, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: A Tosa (Kōchi) native who by most accounts has spent roughly fifty years cooking, running a small counter izakaya devoted to the food of his home region. He grills over Tosa binchōtan (the prized white charcoal of Kōchi) and centers the menu on Kōchi's most famous dish, seared bonito — the kind of veteran, single-counter kitchen where the chef works in front of you and the seasons drive what's served.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal yakitori over Tosa binchōtan and Kōchi's signature katsuo (bonito) tataki, plus shamo (game-fowl) hot pot and seasonal Tosa dishes.

anecdotes.text: Regulars single out the bonito tataki as thick, generous and completely free of any fishy odor — the hallmark of well-handled katsuo — and note that each yakitori skewer is grilled with real care over binchōtan.

The shop is described as an izakaya serving 'the finest yakitori in Kyoto' by a chef with about 50 years of experience, originally from Tosa in Kōchi Prefecture, with a charming, old-Kyoto exterior that draws both locals and foreign guests.
It sits on Kiyamachi just south of Sanjo, upstairs on the 2nd floor above an Italian restaurant — an easy-to-miss counter that rewards those who seek it out.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese listings (Tabelog 郷土料理/焼き鳥, Retty, hitosara, navikyo, HotPepper) describe 土佐屋室戸 as a small Kōchi (Tosa) izakaya at 河原町通三条下ル大黒町49, entrance on Kiyamachi, on the 2nd floor above an Italian restaurant, with a ~10-seat counter and one small tatami room (6–8). Open Mon–Sat 17:00–23:00, closed Sunday; phone 075-221-3056; reservations accepted. The taishō, a Tosa native, is credited with ~50 years of experience; signatures are binchōtan yakitori, katsuo (bonito) tataki, and shamo hot pot, with seasonal Kōchi fare.

cultural_comfort.note: A small veteran-run counter on Kiyamachi that does see foreign diners and takes online reservations — but it's a Japanese izakaya led by a ~50-year chef, with little to no English. A reservation, a few words of Japanese, and easy counter manners go a long way.

places.cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate ~10-seat counter run by a veteran Tosa chef. It's more visitor-accustomed than a true locals-only spot (it's on the Kiyamachi nightlife strip, welcomes food-loving travelers, and accepts online reservations), but don't expect an English menu or English service. Book ahead, arrive on time, order at the counter, and let the chef guide you — a little Japanese or a translation app makes the evening far smoother for everyone.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a yakitori-and-izakaya counter, not a celiac-safe kitchen. Skewers are grilled over a shared charcoal grill alongside tare-basted (wheat soy sauce) items, and any battered/fried dishes share oil, so cross-contamination with wheat is likely across the menu.

Naturally gluten-free choices may exist if confirmed — sashimi, salt (shio) skewers, and salt-style katsuo tataki without ponzu — but they must be ordered explicitly shio/no-sauce and confirmed with the chef.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare, ponzu for the katsuo tataki, and nabe broths are built on Japanese soy sauce, which typically contains wheat. Assume these sauces contain gluten unless the chef confirms a wheat-free alternative; ordering shio (salt) avoids the tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A fish- and meat-forward Tosa izakaya (bonito, chicken, game-fowl hot pot) with bonito dashi throughout — even vegetable dishes likely carry animal-derived stock. There is no documented vegan menu; confirm each item with the chef.

positives.text: A tiny, owner-run counter where the chef cooks directly in front of you, so you can ask about ingredients and preparation face-to-face — easiest with some Japanese or a translation app.
